The Seerah

The Life of the Prophet ﷺ

The seerah in 104 chapters, and what each day asks of our faith

Before you begin

This is not a summary. It is an invitation to walk the whole life of the Prophet ﷺ, from before his birth to his passing, slowly, one chapter at a time, and to let each day move something in our own hearts.

Each chapter is retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ (Memphis Islamic Center), with every Qur'anic verse taken from its canonical translation and verified via quran.ai. Where a report is weak or disputed, the Sheikh's caveat is kept. Read one slowly.

Buruja · buruja.com

The Seerah · 01 of 104

The specialities of the Prophet ﷺ, part 1

What Allah gave him and no one else

This is day one of a long walk. One hundred and four days through the life of the single greatest human being who has ever walked the face of this earth, and before the story begins, before the lineage, the birth, the cave, and the first trembling word of revelation, Dr. Yasir Qadhi opens his landmark seerah by refusing to rush. He will not start with what happened to the Prophet ﷺ. He starts with who the Prophet ﷺ is. Because, he says, when you study the specialities first, everyone already moved to study this life is moved more, made hungrier, more in love with the road ahead. The scholars call these the khasais, the things that belong to him ﷺ and to no one else. Today is a first handful of them, and by the end you will understand why a hundred more days with him will not feel like enough.

So where does a person even begin describing the one whom Allah chose above the whole of His creation? How do you do justice to a man when Allah Himself has already done it, when the praise of heaven is on him and no words of ours are left to add? You begin, the Sheikh says, where Allah began. You begin with the fact that Allah raised his name with His own hand.

Raised in remembrance

Allah says of His Messenger ﷺ something that no other human being can claim, that He lifted up his very mention in the world.

And raised high for you your repute.Qur'an 94:4

Ibn Abbas and the early scholars read this short verse and noticed something you can test for yourself this very hour. Allah raised the Prophet's ﷺ remembrance so high that wherever Allah is mentioned, His Messenger ﷺ is mentioned right beside Him. Listen for it. It is in the shahada, where you cannot bear witness to the Lord without bearing witness to the slave He sent. It is in the adhan that calls a city to prayer. It is in the tashahhud of every single prayer on every continent, where after praising Allah you turn at once to send peace upon the Prophet ﷺ. There is hardly a khutbah, hardly a lesson, hardly a gathering of the faithful, that praises Allah and does not, in the next breath, praise him.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi asks you to stop and actually picture the scale of this. Not one second passes on this earth in which there are not millions, tens of millions, hundreds of millions of human beings praising this one man ﷺ: a soul sitting in tashahhud, a muezzin partway through the adhan, a tongue moving in salawat, a teacher naming him, a writer setting his name down and following it with peace. There is no millisecond of history empty of his praise. And here is the thing to understand: that is not fame. Fame fades, and even the most famous die out of the mouths of men within a generation or two. This is something else. This is Allah keeping a promise. He said He would raise the name, and He raised it past the reach of time.

Mercy, sent walking

And then there is the way Allah described the purpose of sending him at all.

And We have not sent you, [O Muḥammad], except as a mercy to the worlds.Qur'an 21:107

Read the wording closely, because the Sheikh lingers on it. Allah did not say He sent the Prophet ﷺ with mercy, as though mercy were a parcel handed to a courier. He said the sending itself is mercy. So follow where that leads. He ﷺ is mercy. His message is mercy. His teachings are mercy. Believing in what he brought, and acting upon it, is mercy reaching down to you personally, fourteen centuries downstream from the man who carried it. He is everything associated with mercy: the source of it, the channel of it, the proof of it. There is nothing he touches that does not carry the same watermark.

Hold that through all hundred and four days. Everything you are about to hear, the years of quiet persecution, the boycott in the valley, the blood at Uhud, the grief of burying his own children, the long road back to a city that drove him out, every last scene sits inside this one verse. A mercy to the worlds. Even the hardest chapters are written in that ink.

The names Allah gave him

People gave the Prophet ﷺ many names across the centuries, the Sheikh tells us; one early scholar gathered more than two hundred and fifty of them. But the names the people gave only carry so far. The names Allah gave are the primary ones, the ones with the deepest meaning, because they come from the One who knows him best. And in a hadith the Prophet ﷺ himself counted some of these out. I am Muhammad, he said, and I am Ahmad. I am al-Mahi, the one through whom Allah erases disbelief. I am al-Hashir, the gatherer, at whose feet mankind will be raised. I am al-Aqib, the one who comes at the very end, after whom there is no prophet at all. And he named himself the prophet of repentance, the one through following whom people are forgiven, and the prophet of mercy.

Of all of them, the Qur'an itself uses only two as proper names. The first is Muhammad, which appears four times. The second is Ahmad, and the only place it ever appears is on the tongue of Isa, the son of Maryam, announcing the one to come after him centuries before he ﷺ was ever born.

And [mention] when Jesus, the son of Mary, said, "O Children of Israel, indeed I am the messenger of Allāh to you confirming what came before me of the Torah and bringing good tidings of a messenger to come after me, whose name is Aḥmad." But when he came to them with clear evidences, they said, "This is obvious magic."Qur'an 61:6

Both names grow from the same root, hamd, and hamd is not just any praise. Here the Sheikh draws a distinction worth keeping for life. There is shukr, the thanks you give a person in return for a favor; if someone helps you and you call him generous, that is shukr, a kind of transaction, something given back for something received. And then there is hamd, which sits higher: praise given not in return for anything, but because of what a being simply is, the praise owed to perfection regardless of what it has or has not done for you. It is the first word of al-hamdulillah. And both of the Prophet's ﷺ most common names are built from it, because Allah praises him, the angels praise him, the earlier nations praised him, every prophet praised him, and all of mankind praises him whether they know it or not. The believers praise him directly with their tongues. And those who reject him with their tongues are still forced to admire the qualities he came with, the mercy and the gentleness and the truth, so that even denial cannot help but be a kind of praise.

The two names are not the same. Muhammad, the Sheikh explains, carries the sense of praise repeated, again and again and again, praise without end: praise in quantity, the kind that fills every second of time from the beginning until the Day of Judgment and beyond. Ahmad carries the sense of the finest, the highest praise there is: praise in quality. And one classical scholar saw a quiet wisdom in which nation was told which name. The followers of Musa were the largest believing nation before this one, vast in number, and they were given word of Muhammad, the abundantly and continuously praised, a name fitting a great multitude. The true followers of Isa were the opposite: few, hunted, thrown to the lions and torn with combs of iron by pagan Rome, small in number but immense in devotion and quality. To them came the name Ahmad, the finest praise, a name fitting the precious few. Quantity for the many, quality for the few, and both names, in the end, belong to him ﷺ.

The station everyone will praise

Now comes a scene the Sheikh builds slowly, and it deserves to be told slowly, because it is the meaning of the name made visible. It is anchored in a promise Allah made about a praised station.

And from [part of] the night, pray with it [i.e., recitation of the Qur’ān] as additional [worship] for you; it is expected that your Lord will resurrect you to a praised station.Qur'an 17:79

Picture the Day of Judgment. Fifty thousand years long, and the standing and the waiting are themselves a kind of punishment. The sun is near, the reckoning has not begun, and humanity, believer and denier alike, is so crushed by the tension of the wait that they want it over, whatever the cost, just let the judgment start. So they go looking for someone to stand before Allah and plead with Him to begin.

They go first to Adam. You are our father, they say, Allah created you with His own hand and breathed into you of His spirit; look at the state of your children and go to your Lord for us. And Adam remembers a single slip, the one mistake he should not have made, and he says, myself, myself, go to another. They go to Nuh, and he remembers the day he prayed for his drowning son, and he too sends them on. They go to Ibrahim, the friend of the Most Merciful, and he remembers three things he had once said, things that were not even truly lies, and the memory of them still makes him tremble, and he says, go to another. They go to Musa, who remembers the man he struck down in anger. They go to Isa, who simply says, go to another. One after another, the very greatest souls Allah ever created, every one of them, says some version of the same small, honest, frightened word: not me.

And then all of humanity comes to Muhammad ﷺ. And he does not flinch, and he does not recite a list of his fears, though no one had a more careful relationship with his Lord than he did. He says: this is mine. I am the one for this. He goes forward, he falls down in prostration before Allah, and he is given what was given to no one else in all of creation, permission to raise his head and to intercede, so that the reckoning may at last begin. And on that day, every human being who has ever lived will praise him for it. Not only the believers. Even the ones who mocked him in this world, who cursed him, who drew their ugly caricatures of him, on that day they will praise him and send peace upon him, because on that day they will need him and they will know exactly what he is. That, the Sheikh says, is al-maqam al-mahmud, the praised station this verse promised: the name Muhammad, the continuously praised one, paid out in full and in public, before the whole of creation, on the only day that finally counts.

Given to him alone

Then come the specialities themselves, the items that appear on no other prophet's list, and they begin with the most decisive of them all.

Muḥammad is not the father of [any] one of your men, but [he is] the Messenger of Allāh and seal [i.e., last] of the prophets. And ever is Allāh, of all things, Knowing.Qur'an 33:40

He is the final prophet. The long chain of revelation, thousands of years of messengers sent nation after nation, ends, deliberately and forever, with him ﷺ. There can be only one last prophet, and Allah chose him for it, and said so plainly. And this was no late decision, no honor awarded at the end of a life well lived. When a companion asked him when exactly he was decreed to be a prophet, he answered: while Adam was still between the spirit and the clay, before the soul was even breathed into the first man. The last messenger was written into creation before the first human drew breath.

He is also the only prophet sent to the whole of humanity. Every messenger before him was sent to his own people and no further. Someone might object that Adam was sent to all the people of his time, and Nuh too, and that is true, but the Sheikh answers it cleanly: that was an accident of early history, not a commission. Adam was the first man, so the only humanity there was happened to be his own children; Nuh was sent when there was a single town and no other, so when that town was destroyed the world was destroyed with it. Neither was given the title of messenger to all mankind; it simply happened that all mankind, in their day, was one family or one village. No prophet after them, once the earth had filled with cities and nations, was ever sent to more than his own. He ﷺ alone was sent, on purpose, to every nation at once. And further still, he was sent to the jinn, which no prophet before him ever was. There is a night in Makkah the series will reach, the night of the jinn, when a company of believing jinn came and asked him to teach them their religion, and he taught them.

And Allah aided him with a weapon no army has ever carried. He ﷺ said that Allah supported him with awe, a fear cast into the hearts of his enemies across the distance of an entire month's journey, so that they were terrified of him before any rider of his came into view, before a single sword was drawn. Empires felt him coming before they ever saw him.

An ummah like the horizon

He ﷺ was shown the nations of the prophets in a vision, the Sheikh recounts, and the sight of it is staggering. He saw a great multitude of people, stretching as far as the eye could reach, and he wondered whether this enormous nation was his own. He was told: no, this is Musa and his people. Then he was shown another multitude, and this one filled the whole horizon, swallowing the edge of his vision entirely, and he was told: this is your ummah.

On another day he turned to his companions and asked them a question. Would it please you, he said, to be a third of the people of Paradise? And they called out, Allahu Akbar. Would it please you to be half of the people of Paradise? Allahu Akbar, they cried again. Then he said: by Allah, I hope that you will be two thirds of the people of Paradise.

Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi stops you and tells you to do the arithmetic honestly. When the Prophet ﷺ spoke those words, there was exactly one city of Muslims on the earth, Madinah, and perhaps fifteen hundred believers in all the world, a tiny gathering surrounded by empires that counted their subjects in the millions. He said this to maybe fifty companions in his masjid, that they would be two thirds of all the people of Paradise. In the moment he said it, it was not common sense. It was a prophecy, and an audacious one. Now look around. His followers are counted in the billions, and the number has not stopped climbing for fourteen unbroken centuries. We hear the hadith today and think it obvious. When he said it, it was a miracle. We are simply alive late enough to watch it come true with our own eyes.

And the greatest of the miracles he carries is the one that outlives every other kind. Think, the Sheikh says, of the miracles of the prophets before him. The sea split for Musa. Isa raised the dead by the permission of Allah. These were tremendous signs, but they were spent in a single moment, witnessed by one generation, and gone. You and I were not standing on the shore when the water parted, nor at the grave when the dead man walked out; for us they are things we believe in, not things we can touch. But the Qur'an is the one miracle a Muslim can physically place into the hands of a doubter and say: here, this is the miracle of my Prophet, read it, hear it for yourself. It is a living miracle, recited on a million tongues, renewed in the air every time it is read, available to every human being in every century. No prophet before him was given anything remotely like it, and we will spend our own weeks with it as the series goes on.

The first, everywhere

Of all the human beings who have ever lived, only one was ever summoned up through the heavens into the presence of Allah.

Indeed, We have granted you, [O Muḥammad], al-Kawthar.Qur'an 108:1

On the night journey, a night the series will one day give whole lectures to, the Prophet ﷺ was raised up, and there came a boundary at which even Jibril, the mightiest of all the angels, halted and said: my permission ends here, beyond this you go alone. And he ﷺ went on alone. Musa, the greatest of those before him in this respect, was spoken to by Allah while standing on a mountain of the earth. The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ was the only one ever called up.

He is, by his own words ﷺ, the sayyid of the children of Adam, the master and leader of all humanity, and he said so without arrogance, simply as a fact entrusted to him. And on the Day of Judgment the order of everything bends toward him. His is the first grave to crack open when the trumpet is blown the second time. He is the first to be clothed. He is the first to cross the bridge over the fire, leading his ummah behind him. He is the first to knock on the gate of Paradise, and when the keeper asks who is there and hears his name ﷺ, the answer comes back: for no one before you was I commanded to open. His name is the password to the door.

And his ummah, the very last nation to arrive in the order of time, will be the first nation through that gate, not for anything in us, but purely because we are his and Allah honors us for his sake. Waiting beyond it is the hawd, his pool, so immense that a single one of its four sides runs the span of half the Arabian Peninsula. And there is al-Kawthar, the river named in this surah, the great fountain of Paradise from which all its other rivers branch away. So that, the Sheikh points out, anywhere in all of Paradise that a soul bends to drink, it is drinking from a gift that Allah gave to him ﷺ.

A level meant for one

Picture Paradise, the Sheikh says, as something that narrows as it climbs: no crowding anywhere in it, but the lower levels hold the many, and the higher you go the fewer can reach. And at the very top, directly beneath the Throne of the Most Merciful, there is an entire level of Paradise prepared for exactly one servant of Allah. One. It is called al-wasilah.

The Prophet ﷺ told his companions about this place, that Allah had set it aside for a single one of His slaves, and then he added something that should stop your heart. He said: and I hope that I am that servant. He hoped. There was never anyone else it could possibly be, no other creature fit to stand on it, and still, out of the depth of his humility, he would not simply claim it. He hoped for it, and he asked Allah for it, and he asked us to ask for it on his behalf.

And so it is, fourteen centuries later, that every time the adhan finishes anywhere on the earth, the ummah he loved raises its hands and begs Allah to grant Muhammad ﷺ al-wasilah and the lofty station and to raise him to the praised place He promised him. Hundreds of millions of voices, five times in every day, praying not for themselves but for him. It is day one of the seerah, and you already have your first assignment.

What this day asks of our faith

It is tempting to read a list of specialities like a list of trophies, to be impressed, to admire from a safe distance, and to move on. That would be to miss what the Sheikh has done by opening here. He did not begin with the khasais to make us marvel at a man beyond our reach. He began here to make us fall in love before we even meet him, because a heart that loves will walk a hundred and four days gladly that a curious mind would abandon by the second week. So before the story starts, let these gifts ask something of your own faith.

He is the most praised being in creation, and Allah Himself raised that praise and keeps it rising every second without pause. So consider what your share in it is. When you send salawat upon him ﷺ, you are not beginning something. You are joining a river of praise that has never once stopped since Allah promised to raise his name, that was running before you were born and will run long after you are gone. Most of us send peace upon him out of habit, half-asleep at the end of a prayer. Knowing what you now know, you could send it on purpose, awake, glad to be one more voice in a chorus that fills all of time.

He carries the one living miracle, the Qur'an, the sign that did not expire with the generation that saw it. You were not on the shore when the sea split, but the miracle of your Prophet ﷺ sits in your home and fits in your hand. Ask yourself, honestly, how you treat it. The one proof every century can hear with its own ears, the gift that no nation before us could hold, deserves more from us than a beautiful cover on a high shelf. It asks to be opened, recited, understood, and lived, because to neglect it is to neglect the very thing Allah chose as the standing evidence of the man you claim to follow.

And then there is al-wasilah, the level meant for one, and the way he reached for it: not by claiming it, though it was his by every right, but by hoping, and asking, and asking us to ask. That is the posture this whole day teaches. The most deserving being in creation modeled humility before his Lord, and then handed us a way to love him in return that costs us almost nothing and means almost everything. He asked us to pray for him after the adhan: five small moments in a day, already on your lips, already part of your routine, and he asked that you make them his. There is hardly an easier act of love in the whole religion, and hardly one we forget more often.

So take one thing from this first day down into your ordinary life. The next time the adhan ends, do not let the du'a run past you on its way to nowhere. Stop, lift your hands, and ask Allah to give Muhammad ﷺ al-wasilah and the praised station He promised him, because the most praised soul in all creation once turned to people like us, in a small masjid in a small city, and asked us to. Believe in his nearness before the story even begins. Hold his living miracle the way you would hold something irreplaceable, because it is. And let love, not duty, carry you into the hundred days to come. May Allah send His praise and His peace upon Muhammad ﷺ, raise him to al-wasilah and the praised station, let us drink from his hand a drink after which there is no thirst, and gather us, the last nation in time, among the first through the gate, under his banner, on the Day the nations are gathered.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 1: the specialities of the Prophet ﷺ (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (94:4, 21:107, 61:6, 17:79, 33:40, 108:1), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of them has been followed.

The Seerah · 02 of 104

The specialities of the Prophet ﷺ, part 2

The beauty they could not describe

The story of his life has not begun yet. For one more evening, before the first revelation and the long road to Madinah, Dr. Yasir Qadhi keeps us standing in front of the man himself ﷺ. Last week counted a first handful of the things Allah gave him and no one else, perhaps fifteen of close to fifty. Tonight the gift is told from a different angle: not what was given to him, but what he was. What he looked like. How he slept. How he laughed. And how fiercely he loved people he would never meet in this world, a group that includes, by name, you.

Begin with a rule the Sheikh lays down before anything else, because it frames the whole night. It is the way of Allah, His sunnah with His prophets, to send each one with the most perfect character and the most perfect form, inner and outer, so that no human being is ever left an excuse to turn away. A messenger comes beautiful so that nothing about him repels. Yusuf was given half of all beauty, and most scholars read that as half the beauty of all mankind. But there is a second reading, and it points exactly where this evening is going: half, some said, of the beauty of Muhammad ﷺ himself, because in their eyes the most beautiful of all creation is the man this entire series is about.

They reached for the sun and the moon

So how did the people who actually saw him describe him? They could not, and that turns out to be the most eloquent description of all. They reached past the things of earth and grabbed at the sky.

Rubayyi bint Muawwidh was an old woman by the time her son pressed her to tell him what the Prophet ﷺ had been like. Everything she had seen with her own living eyes collapsed into a single sentence, preserved in at-Tabarani's Mu'jam: my dear son, had you seen him, you would have thought the sun had risen. Ka'b ibn Malik, when he tried, said that when the Prophet ﷺ was glad, his face would light until it was a piece of the full moon. One companion hands him the sun, another the moon, each one offering the most beautiful thing his world contained and finding it still not enough.

Then comes the report the Sheikh names among his favorites, the kind that catches in the throat. Amr ibn al-As had been one of the noble enemies of Islam. He belonged to that strange category of men who opposed the truth for years and yet never stooped to the gutter of it, the same breed as Khalid ibn al-Walid and Ikrimah the son of Abu Jahl: enemies, but not vile ones, men with a real nobility in their hearts. And Allah, who knew the good that was in those hearts, guided every last one of them to Islam. Amr accepted it late, and had the company of the Prophet ﷺ for barely more than two years. Listen to what those two years did to a hardened statesman who went on to live a life of power and grandeur. There was nothing, he said, sweeter to me than to gaze at the face of the Prophet ﷺ, and I could never get my fill of looking at him. Yet were you to ask me to describe him, I could not. Men do not normally speak this way about another man, and that is the entire point. Two forces worked on Amr at once. A beauty that pulled his eyes upward, and an awe, a reverence the Arabs called hayba, that pushed them back down. He spent his time with the Prophet ﷺ caught between the pull and the lowering, which is why the face he loved most in all the world is the one face he could never put into words.

The portrait the young ones drew

That awe explains something the Sheikh wants us to notice: the most detailed portraits of the Prophet ﷺ come not from the great elder companions but from the youngest, the ones too small to be pressed down into lowering their gaze.

Anas ibn Malik was a boy when his mother brought him and gave her son to the Prophet ﷺ as a servant. For ten years he would run to that house at dawn, spend the day at his service, and go home at night. So it is Anas, in the description Tirmidhi keeps in his Shamail, who dares the details. He ﷺ was neither so tall that he stood above a gathering nor so short that he was overlooked, but exactly in the middle. His hair was neither tightly curled nor flat, but firm and full, worn down to the earlobes. And Anas adds the two things only a child underfoot would ever know: I never touched silk or velvet softer than the palm of his hand, and I never smelled any musk or perfume more fragrant than his sweat. The household knew that last one intimately. One of the wives would gather his sweat in a small bottle as he slept, and keep it as perfume, and use it as medicine, a drop in water to drink.

His coloring needs a translator, and here the Sheikh slows the pace. The narrations say white with a reddish tinge, but the Arabs did not use the word white the way English does. The shade English calls white they called yellow: they named the Romans the people of yellow, the way some peoples of the Americas said paleface, because it was not a color they reckoned among themselves. When an Arab called a man white, he meant a luminous light brown. That is him ﷺ, neither the pallor of the Romans nor a deep ruddy brown, but a glowing lightish brown between the two.

Al-Bara ibn Azib gives the frame: medium in stature, broad in the shoulders, a full beard, and that famous thickness of hair, a detail at least five separate companions narrate. He saw the Prophet ﷺ once at night in a red cloak and pronounced his verdict on the spot: I never saw anything more beautiful than him. And Ali ibn Abi Talib, who grew up at his side and married into his house, fills in the face itself: not fleshy, not fully round, but gently oval. The eyes large, the pupils jet black, the lashes long. The joints large, the upper back broad, no hair across the body save a fine line running from the chest to the navel. He walked briskly, leaning forward as a man does coming down a slope, and some scholars said it was as though the earth itself were folded humble beneath his stride. And one habit every teacher should steal from him: when a person addressed him, he never answered over his shoulder. He turned his whole body and gave the speaker all of himself. The Sheikh confesses, as an aside, that he has read stacks of modern books on public speaking, and every technique they sell he keeps finding fourteen centuries early in the ﷺ conduct of the Prophet. Ali ends where everyone ends. Whoever saw him without warning stood in awe of him. Whoever kept his company came to love him. And whoever tried to describe him surrendered with the same words: I never saw anyone like him, before him or after him.

The seal, and the face that could not lie

Between his shoulder blades sat one thing no other human being carried: the seal of prophethood. Not a wound and not a blemish, but a small raised gathering of hair, a different color from the rest, about the size of a pigeon's egg, growing in a place where hair simply does not grow. The Prophet ﷺ bore it as a physical sign, set on his body by Allah, so that the people who kept the older scriptures could know the one they had been told to wait for.

The series will one day give Salman al-Farsi a night of his own, but here is the foretaste. Salman's Christian teacher had told him the final prophet would carry a seal between his shoulders. So Salman came testing, sign by sign. The first held. The second held. The third lay hidden beneath a garment, and how does a man ask a prophet to bare his back? Salman circled around behind him, hoping to steal a glimpse, and the Prophet ﷺ, reading the stranger exactly, said not a single word and simply let the garment fall from his shoulders. Here is what you came for. Salman saw it, and accepted Islam.

His face passed the same kind of test, again and again. Jabir ibn Samura was walking home one clear night under a full moon when he crossed the Prophet ﷺ in that same red cloak, and he did the thing only an eyewitness ever could. He looked at the moon, then at him ﷺ, back and forth between the two, and swore afterward that he was more beautiful in my eyes than the full moon. And on the Prophet's ﷺ very first day in Madinah, Abdullah ibn Salam, the chief rabbi of its Jews and the most respected scholar among them, came out only to see what the commotion was about. He was not shopping for a religion; he came to inspect a claim. One look undid him. As soon as I saw his face, he said, I knew it could not be the face of a liar. One conversation later, the rabbi was a Muslim.

A bed that left marks

Beauty like that, you would think, sleeps on silk. He ﷺ slept in a small bare room beside the masjid that held a jug of water and a mat, and the mat was woven not from the soft leaves of the date palm but from its hard fibers, with nothing between his body and the weave. Umar ibn al-Khattab walked in on him there in the seventh or eighth year after the hijrah, when the Prophet ﷺ was the undisputed leader of much of Arabia and the wealth had begun to pour in. The Prophet ﷺ sat up to greet him, and Umar saw the weave printed red into his side, and broke down weeping. Messenger of Allah, he said, look at Caesar of Rome, look at the emperor of Persia, look at how they live; surely you, of all people, deserve better than this. The answer came back with an edge. Umar, is this what we are here for? Are you not content that they should have this world, and we the next?

Aisha said his bed was a skin of leather, sometimes stuffed with palm leaves to soften it a little, and leather is something you lay over a saddle, not something you sleep on. One night a wife folded the bedding double to give him a touch of comfort, and he slept longer than usual, and woke asking who had done it. Return it the way it was, he said; the softness had cost him his night prayer.

The food matched the bed. Six weeks could pass, Aisha said, without a fire lit for meat in the house, and when her nephew Urwah asked her decades later how on earth they had lived, she answered: on the two black things, dates and water. Long after the Prophet ﷺ was gone and the wealth of the ummah had finally come, the people who had loved him could not bring themselves to enjoy a rich meal. Abdur Rahman ibn Awf, by then among the wealthiest men of Madinah, was set before a platter of meat and bread and wept over it: the Prophet ﷺ left this world without once eating his fill of bread, and not his family with him, and here we have remained to see all of this. Aisha wept the same way over fine food across the forty years she outlived him, repeating that he ﷺ never once filled himself even with coarse bread, not one day of his life.

And then there is the noon no one who hears it forgets. Umar stepped out into the midday furnace, the hour when no one walks the streets, and found the Prophet ﷺ sitting outside. What brings you out at this hour? The same thing that brought you, he ﷺ answered: there was nothing to eat at home. Abu Bakr drifted out for the very same reason, and the three best human beings then alive sat together in the heat with empty stomachs, until Abul Haytham, hurrying home from his work, came upon them and could not bear the sight. He owned one old goat, past the age of milk. He slaughtered it, his wife cooked, he kneaded the dough with his own hands, and his three guests ate meat and bread, the luxury of that world. When the meal was done, the Prophet ﷺ turned to his two companions: by Allah, you left your homes hungry, and you will be asked about this blessing on the Day of Judgment. The Sheikh lets that sentence rest on all of us. Three meals a day pass through our hands, and we scarcely stop once to thank Allah for any of them.

The leader who walked his turn

He ﷺ owned a camel and had the means for a horse, and still rode donkeys through Madinah without a flicker of embarrassment. At Badr the believers numbered around three hundred and fourteen, with roughly seventy five camels among them, which worked out to three men per camel, riding in turns and walking in turns. The Prophet ﷺ was placed in a group with Ali and Abu Lubaba, and the two of them tried to give up their shares the gracious way: Messenger of Allah, we are both young men, you ride and we will walk. Any commander on earth takes that offer, and rank alone would have excused him; a leader, the Sheikh notes, is owed extra protection and extra respect quite apart from religion. He ﷺ smiled and refused. Neither of you is any stronger than I am, and I am no less in need of the reward than the two of you. He wanted the reward of the walking too. So the leader of the army climbed down on his turn and walked his shifts across the sand like every other man in it.

Then the testimony of the boy who watched him backstage. Anas served him ﷺ for ten years, indoors and out, the years in which a servant learns every flaw a man has. His verdict: in ten years he never once said uff to me, not the smallest syllable of irritation in the language. He never asked, why did you do this? He never asked, why did you not do that? The Sheikh turns it on us gently. It is easy to be a saint at the masjid and at work; the person your own family receives is the real you. For ten years, the private Prophet ﷺ was the gentlest man in Madinah. Anas would know. Sent once on an errand, he found children playing in the street and forgot the errand entirely. The Prophet ﷺ came out searching, found his little messenger lost in the game, and took him by the ears from behind, and Anas turned around into a smile, not a scolding. And when his mother had first brought him, she had asked for one thing only: make dua for your little servant. He ﷺ asked Allah to bless Anas in his life, his wealth, and his children. Anas lived past a hundred and ten in an age when thirty was a full life, grew so rich that people joked any rock he turned over had gold beneath it, and lost the count of his own descendants while he yet lived. One dua, paid out across a century.

Gentle with curses, first toward danger

A group of the Jews of Madinah came to him once with a poisoned greeting, winking at one another as they twisted the words: as samu alaykum, may death be upon you, in place of peace. He ﷺ answered with two words, wa alaykum, and upon you, and let it lie. Aisha, listening from behind the curtain, erupted: may you be cursed, may Allah destroy you. And the Prophet ﷺ calmed his wife with a sentence the ummah has carried ever since: gentleness is not placed in a thing except that it beautifies it, and harshness is not placed in a thing except that it makes it ugly. When she asked how he could swallow such an insult, he showed her that he had not swallowed it at all: did you not hear what I said back? And upon you. Whatever they sent was returned to its sender, and his dignity never once left the room.

But do not mistake the gentleness for softness. One night Madinah woke to a frightening sound in the dark, and the people crept out of their doors in nervous knots to learn what it was. They met the Prophet ﷺ already riding back toward them, alone, on Abu Talha's horse with no saddle on its back, his sword slung at his neck, calling out: do not be afraid, I have checked it, there is nothing. He had gone toward the danger before the rest of the city had finished deciding to go and look. Ali would later say of Badr that when the fighting grew fierce, the companions would seek shelter close to the Prophet ﷺ himself.

And he could not bring himself to say no. The companions said that any little girl of Madinah could take the Prophet ﷺ by the hand and lead him wherever she needed his help, with no fear of being turned away. Once, when his own garment had worn through with holes, a companion gave him a fine new one, and he had hardly stepped outside in it when a man asked him for it as a gift. Yes, he said, and went back in, and came out again in the old patched cloth. The companions rounded on the man: you knew he never refuses anyone, how could you ask? The man's answer ended the rebuke. I did not ask in order to wear it. I asked so that I might be buried in cloth that had touched him ﷺ. It became his shroud.

Even his jokes were true

All of that majesty, and he ﷺ joked. The Sheikh dwells here on purpose, because humor reveals a man's humanity, and the humor of the Prophet ﷺ never once stepped outside the truth. An old woman came asking him to pray that Allah admit her to Jannah, and he answered with a perfectly straight face: my dear aunt, have you not heard? No old woman enters Jannah. She burst into wailing on the spot, and only then did he let the rest of it land: do not cry. No old woman enters Jannah, because Allah will first remake her young, and young and fair she will enter it. Then he recited the proof:

Indeed, We have produced them [i.e., the women of Paradise] in a [new] creationQur'an 56:35

The punchline was Qur'an.

He joked even on his own deathbed. In his final days, with perhaps four or five left to live, Aisha lay nearby with a pounding headache, moaning, oh my head. The reply came from his own sickbed: rather, oh my head, for mine is worse than yours. Then, teasing her: and what would you lose if you were to die before me, when the one to wash you, shroud you, pray over you, and lay you in the earth would be me? She fired straight back: I am sure you would like that; you would come home from my funeral and spend the evening with your other wives. Days from death, and the two of them are still sparring with smiles. And because he never spoke anything but the truth, even in jest, the scholars later mined that very moment for law: ash-Shawkani built an entire chapter of fiqh, on a spouse washing the body of a spouse, out of a dying man's joke.

And there was Zahir, a simple believer of the desert whom history barely recorded, except for the one fact that matters most: the Prophet ﷺ loved him. Finding Zahir in the market one day shouting his wares, the Prophet ﷺ slipped up behind him and locked him in an embrace. Zahir struggled, let me go, who is this, until he caught sight of who was holding him, and then went limp and pressed himself back to gather all the blessing he could from that body. And the Prophet ﷺ called out over the souk: who will buy this slave from me? Zahir laughed that the buyer would be cheated: by Allah, you would find me poor merchandise. Back came the answer the ummah has never let go of: but in the sight of Allah, you are not cheap; you are dear. Even the prank held nothing false, the Sheikh notes. Zahir was no slave of any man, but the slave of Allah he truly was, and his Lord had priced him high.

Ummati, ummati: the dua he never spent

Ar-Rauf and ar-Rahim are two of the names of Allah Himself. And in one ayah, the Sheikh points out, Allah takes both of His own names, strips away the definite article that fences them off as His alone, and drapes them over His Messenger ﷺ:

There has certainly come to you a Messenger from among yourselves. Grievous to him is what you suffer; [he is] concerned over you [i.e., your guidance] and to the believers is kind and merciful.Qur'an 9:128

Rafa is the close, protective tenderness a mother and father feel for a child, the kind of mercy that cannot rest until the loved one is safe and well. That is the word Allah chose for what he ﷺ feels toward the believers, the overwhelming majority of whom he never met in this life. You are inside the meaning of that verse.

One night he ﷺ was reciting and came to the words of Ibrahim, handing his people over to his Lord:

My Lord, indeed they have led astray many among the people. So whoever follows me - then he is of me; and whoever disobeys me - indeed, You are [yet] Forgiving and Merciful.Qur'an 14:36

And then to the words of Isa, doing the same for his:

If You should punish them - indeed they are Your servants; but if You forgive them - indeed it is You who is the Exalted in Might, the Wise.Qur'an 5:118

Two prophets, each pleading for his nation. And the thought of his own nation rose up and overcame him ﷺ. He lifted his hands and wept and could say only this: Allahumma, ummati, ummati. O Allah, my ummah, my ummah. Allah, who knew already why His beloved was weeping, sent Jibril down to ask, and then sent him back carrying one of the tenderest sentences in all of revelation, kept for us in Sahih Muslim: go to Muhammad and tell him, We shall please you concerning your ummah, and We shall not grieve you.

Now the teaching the Sheikh asks you to carry, not only for tonight but for the rest of your life. Every prophet was granted one dua that Allah guaranteed He would answer, one certain request held in reserve. And one by one, the prophets spent theirs in this world. Nuh, after nine hundred and fifty years of mockery, spent his against his people, asking that not one household of the deniers be left upon the earth; the flood answered him, and mankind began again from the ark. Musa spent his against Firawn, the genocidal tyrant of his age, the slaughterer of infants: O Allah, do not guide him. And Firawn was never guided; his deathbed faith came too late to be counted. Sulayman spent his on a request no one has matched:

He said, "My Lord, forgive me and grant me a kingdom such as will not belong to anyone after me. Indeed, You are the Bestower."Qur'an 38:35

So the wind carried him a month's journey and home again, and the jinn built for him and dived for him, and every garbled legend of flying carpets and genies sealed in bottles is a dim memory of what Allah truly gave that prophet. Even Ibrahim spent his outside himself, on a people not yet born:

Our Lord, and send among them a messenger from themselves who will recite to them Your verses and teach them the Book and wisdom and purify them. Indeed, You are the Exalted in Might, the Wise.Qur'an 2:129

The Prophet ﷺ would later say: I am the answer to the dua of my father Ibrahim. And what of his own guaranteed dua, the one certain request of the most beloved of all creation? He told us himself. Every prophet hastened to use his dua in this world, except me; I have held mine back, and saved it for my ummah, and I will call upon it for them on the Day of Judgment: O Allah, forgive my whole ummah. Every soul who ever believed in him stands inside that one sentence, however stained, however late. By that dua, the Sheikh reminds us, every believer in this Prophet ﷺ will eventually reach Jannah, even if he must first be purified, because the most beloved man to ever live held a guaranteed answer from Allah in his hand for his entire life, through everything they did to him, and never once spent it on himself. There is no greater sacrifice than that, and no plainer proof of love.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read an evening like this as a gallery of admiration, a beautiful man set so high above us that he has nothing left to ask of our ordinary lives. That would be to miss what the Sheikh built the whole night toward. Every brushstroke in this portrait is something a person can copy, and together they put a question to our iman.

He was gentle where we are sharp, and the rule he gave was his own: gentleness beautifies whatever it touches, and harshness makes it ugly. He absorbed a curse with two calm words and kept his dignity whole, and his measure of a man was never the face he wore at the masjid but the one his family received behind a closed door. Ten years, and never an uff. Ask honestly which version of you your spouse and your children actually meet, and whether the private you is the best you, because that, and not the public performance, is the real one.

He was content with little while holding the keys to much. The leader of Arabia slept on a weave that printed marks into his side and would not let it be softened, lest the comfort steal his prayer, and he reminded two hungry friends that even a single meal of meat and bread would be asked about on the Day of Judgment. Set that beside the tables that pass through our hands every day, untouched by gratitude. His life asks whether we could be content with enough, and thankful for more than enough, the way he was content and thankful.

And then there is the dua he never spent, which is the heart of the night. He had one guaranteed answer, the thing each of us would seize for our own rescue, and he carried it untouched through persecution and loss and grief, and laid it down at last not for himself but for people he would never see, ourselves among them. That is what rafa means, the tenderness Allah lent him from His own names: to want for others, before you have even met them, more than you want for yourself. The man who loved us like that asked almost nothing back. He asked that we love him in return, and proved that the love is the whole road, when a man came confessing thin deeds and thick love and was told, plainly, that a person is with the one he loves. The companions said they never rejoiced over any word as they rejoiced over that one, because they had feared their deeds could never lift them to his side, and suddenly the bridge was love itself. It still is. And Allah Himself sealed where that love must lead:

Say, [O Muḥammad], "If you should love Allāh, then follow me, [so] Allāh will love you and forgive you your sins. And Allāh is Forgiving and Merciful."Qur'an 3:31

So take one thing from this evening down into your ordinary days. Make the private you gentler than the public you, the way he was. Be content with enough and thankful for more, the way he was. And love him on purpose, not as a feeling that visits and leaves but as a daily choice that shows itself in following him, because that love is the bridge to the very face his companions could not describe. May Allah send His finest peace and blessings upon His Messenger ﷺ, who held his one dua for us and wept ummati, ummati into the night, gather us in the company of the man we believe in without having seen, count us among the ummah he saved that dua for, and let our eyes one day rest on the face that was more beautiful than the rising sun.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 2: the specialities of the Prophet ﷺ, part 2 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (56:35, 9:128, 14:36, 5:118, 38:35, 2:129, 3:31), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own telling has been followed.

The Seerah · 03 of 104

Why Study the Seerah

Why we walk in his footsteps

Before the story begins, there is a question to settle, and it is not a small one. You are about to give a hundred days of your life to the life of one man ﷺ. Why? Why spend evening after evening on dates and journeys and battles that ended fourteen centuries ago, in a desert most of us will never see? Dr. Yasir Qadhi does not rush past this. He stops at the threshold and asks it plainly, because everything that follows depends on the answer. And the first piece of the answer is hiding inside the word itself.

Seerah comes from the Arabic verb sara, which means to travel, to set out on a journey. The Arabs called a person's biography a seerah for exactly that reason: to read a life is to travel it, to walk a road someone walked before you, to follow in his footsteps. So the word tells you, before a single event is narrated, what these hundred days actually are. You are not gathering facts about the Prophet ﷺ the way you might memorize the dates of a war. You are walking behind him. And there is a quiet honour folded into the word, because the Arabs once used it for anyone's biography, but the moment the Prophet ﷺ came, the scholars of Islam kept it for him alone. Say seerah now, in any century and any land, with no name attached, and every scholar on earth knows whose journey you mean. One word, reserved for one life.

Commanded to know him

Begin with the reason that removes all choice from the matter. Knowing this man ﷺ is not a hobby for the devout or a luxury for those with spare evenings. It is an obligation Allah laid upon us. In more than fifty places the Qur'an commands the believer to take the Prophet ﷺ as his example, and the most famous of them is a single, weighty verse:

There has certainly been for you in the Messenger of Allah an excellent pattern for anyone whose hope is in Allah and the Last Day and [who] remembers Allah often.Qur'an 33:21

Uswa hasana, the words say: an excellent pattern, a beautiful conduct, a model laid out so completely that you can hold your own life up against it and see where the lines match and where they fall short. But a pattern you have never studied is no pattern at all. The command to follow him is, underneath, a command to learn him.

And here Dr. Yasir Qadhi draws out a mercy most of us pass over. Some among the Arabs balked at the very idea of a human messenger, a man who ate and slept and grieved like them, and the Qur'an answers them: had angels been the ones walking the earth in settled peace, an angel would have been sent down to them. But it is not angels who fill the earth. It is us. So in the perfection of His wisdom, Allah sent flesh and blood, a man born of a woman, who married and raised children and buried some of them, who knew hunger and fear and loss, only chosen and guided. Had the example been an angel, every one of us would have had the perfect excuse ready: we cannot be like that, this is not our kind. No human being will ever say that of the Prophet ﷺ. He was a man, and so the door he walked through is a door we can walk through too.

And there is no angle from which the example fails. Study him ﷺ as a worshipper, and you learn how a servant stands before his Lord. Study his manners, his gentleness, his mercy with the weak and the wronged. Study him as a leader, as a husband, as a father, as a friend, as a man under siege and a man in victory. Whatever role you woke up inside this morning, he was there first, and he was there perfectly.

Love wants the details

Here the lecture turns warm and personal, and it catches you off guard. Dr. Yasir Qadhi describes how, when he travels away from home, he calls his children and asks what they did that day, what they had for breakfast. He says it himself: who cares what they had for breakfast? It changes nothing in his day. And that, exactly, is the point. Love wants the details. Wanting to know everything about someone, down to the most useless little fact, is simply what loving a person looks like. We do not interrogate the lives of people we are indifferent to.

Now turn that lens around onto ourselves, and it stings. Our children can name film stars and athletes without pausing for breath, and many of them, many of us, cannot name the children of the Prophet ﷺ, or say how old he was when revelation first came, or when he made the hijrah, or why. If a person claims to love this man ﷺ, the most beloved of all creation, and yet never once cares to open his story, never wants a single new fact about him, what kind of love is that? It is not a love any human being would recognise in any other corner of life. So the study is two things at once. It is the proof of the love, because love that never seeks the beloved is only a word. And it is the fuel of the love, because the more you learn of him the more you love him, and the more you love him the more you ache to learn. It is a circle, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi notes the wonder of it: this circle only ever spins upward.

The first generations set that circle turning inside their own homes. The great-grandson of the Prophet ﷺ, raised in Madinah among those who remembered, said that they would teach their children the campaigns of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ exactly as they would teach them a surah of the Qur'an. Two subjects, one curriculum, at the foundation of a child's life. That is the place the seerah once held in a Muslim household, and the place it is quietly waiting to hold again, starting, perhaps, with the bedtime stories told in your own house tonight: not the old pagan legends, but the true story of the best of mankind ﷺ and the companions who loved him.

The surah that needs its story

Travel back with the lecture to the first year of revelation. The wahy had begun, and then it stopped. For weeks, nothing descended. The heavens went quiet, and into that silence crept the worst of thoughts: that perhaps his Lord had grown angry, perhaps he had been abandoned, perhaps the One who had begun this had now turned away. This is early, the most fragile season of the whole mission, and shaytan presses on exactly the place that hurts. And then the answer came down, and notice how it opens. Not with a rebuke, not with an argument, but with daybreak itself:

By the morning brightness And [by] the night when it covers with darkness, Your Lord has not taken leave of you, [O Muhammad], nor has He detested [you]. And the Hereafter is better for you than the first [life]. And your Lord is going to give you, and you will be satisfied.Qur'an 93:1-5

Sit with the opening word. Ad-duha is the forenoon brightness, the light that climbs after dawn, and to every people in every age, the breaking of the morning has meant one single thing: a new day is coming. The dark is ending. So before Allah says a word of comfort, He swears by the very image of comfort. Your Lord has not left you. He is not displeased with you. And what lies ahead of you is better than what you are walking through now, until at last you will be given, and given, until you yourself are satisfied. Now ask yourself, honestly, how much of that you felt the last time Surah ad-Duha passed by in your prayer. For most of us, the answer is almost none. The words went by as sound.

This is one of the great gifts the seerah hands back to you, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi presses it hard: the Qur'an was revealed inside a story. It did not float down complete and context-free. It came ayah by ayah, into real moments, answering real fears, consoling a real and frightened heart, and without the story the depths of the words stay locked. Know the weeks of silence, know the whisper of abandonment, and these few short verses open into something almost unbearably tender: Allah Himself reaching down to steady His beloved ﷺ, and through him, every believer who has ever lain awake fearing he has been forgotten.

Then the door swings the other way, because the Qur'an is not only illuminated by the seerah. It is the first and surest source of the seerah, the source Dr. Yasir Qadhi says most people skip straight past on their rush to the history books. It touches nearly every major event of his ﷺ life, and reaches past both ends of it: back to the army of the elephant that Allah turned away in the year of his birth, and forward to among the very last words ever sent down, the verse that declared the religion complete. And it records what no chronicle on earth could ever record. A historian writes down what happened; the Qur'an writes down what happened inside. It tells us of the day eyes shifted in fear and hearts climbed into throats, of the hidden cowardice and the hidden faith, things only the One who fashioned those hearts could ever publish. And yet, the Sheikh notes, the Qur'an is not arranged in the order things occurred, and it rarely names the battle or the crisis it is answering. So the two need each other completely. You need the seerah to understand the Qur'an, and the Qur'an to understand the seerah. They walk hand in hand, the whole way.

Stories that steady the heart

Why is so much of the Qur'an given over to the stories of the messengers who came before? Nuh and Ibrahim, Musa and Yusuf, told and retold, their trials laid out across surah after surah. Allah tells His Prophet ﷺ the reason in His own words:

And each [story] We relate to you from the news of the messengers is that by which We make firm your heart. And there has come to you, in this, the truth and an instruction and a reminder for the believers.Qur'an 11:120

Stop on that, because it is staggering. The strongest, purest, most certain heart ever placed inside a human chest, and Allah chose to steady it with sacred biography, with the journeys of the prophets before him. The seerah of the earlier messengers was the medicine prescribed for the heart of the final Messenger ﷺ himself. So put the question to yourself, the way the lecture does: if his heart was held firm by these stories, what do you imagine yours is meant to run on?

This is the benefit Dr. Yasir Qadhi presses most urgently for our own moment. Muslims in the West, he says, are feeling the heat: the suspicion, the slander, the hostile cycle of news. And then he does something striking. He refuses to let the word persecution anywhere near it, because when you set our discomfort beside what the Prophet ﷺ and the first believers actually endured, the comparison is, in his word, embarrassing. So walk through their worst years, and two things happen at once. Your own trials shrink to their true and smaller size, and your hope swells to its true and larger size. The seerah is where this ummah has always gone, in every dark century, to refill on optimism, and the supply has never once run dry.

And consider the company you keep along that road. The seerah is the story of the best generation that ever walked the earth, the companions, radiyallahu anhum, the people of whom Allah declares, in eight or nine places, that He is pleased with them and they with Him. Ibn Mas'ud said that Allah looked into the hearts of all His servants and chose the purest of them to be the heart of His Prophet ﷺ, then looked again and chose the purest hearts after his, and made them his companions. Abu Bakr and Umar, Anas and Jabir, and a hundred more, each one a legend, each one a living model of what the message makes of a human being. A famous report likens them to the stars: whichever one you follow, you will be guided. Its wording is somewhat weak, the Sheikh is careful to note, but what it points to is sound and true. Somewhere in that generation is a role model fitted exactly to you.

There is even a blueprint folded into all of it. Every movement and faction today carries its own grand theory for how the ummah might be revived, its own ideology, its own programme. Dr. Yasir Qadhi offers a simpler thought. Why not begin where the Prophet ﷺ began? He started from nothing, literally from zero, a single man in a city that would soon want him dead, and within one human lifetime the entire world had turned. Whoever truly dreams of rebuilding the house ought to go and sit, first, with the architect.

The life that is itself the miracle

Ask a Muslim to name the miracles of the Prophet ﷺ and the hand reaches for the famous ones: the moon split in the sky, water flowing from between his fingers, the tree that moved and the food that multiplied. Tonight points somewhere closer and easier to miss. His whole life is a miracle, beginning to end. The Andalusian scholar Ibn Hazm swore that if the Prophet ﷺ had been granted no miracle whatsoever other than his life and times, his seerah alone would have been proof enough that he is a messenger of Allah. Weigh that claim, because it holds. An unlettered man who could not read or write, raised a shepherd among a people with no script, no libraries, no civilisation worth the name, stands up out of the middle of all that and brings a scripture of unmatched eloquence, a message no enemy could explain or imitate. Within his own lifetime, Arabia was remade from the ground up. Within a hundred years, the empire of Persia had collapsed before his followers and Rome was reeling. No one alive could have predicted it, and nothing in the natural order accounts for it. Nothing explains it but Allah.

And inside that great miracle sits a quieter one that Dr. Yasir Qadhi lingers over. The Prophet ﷺ ended his life holding a degree of power and devotion that kings would murder to possess; his companions would have sacrificed anything at a single word. And it changed nothing in him: the same simplicity, the same plain food and patched cloak and floor to sleep on. Power exposes every human being it ever touches; it pulls the true self up to the surface. It touched him, and there was nothing there to expose but more of the same. And that, too, the Sheikh says, is part of the proof: no man survives that much power so intact without a pure and divine sincerity holding him steady from the inside.

That knowledge is also, in our times, your armour. His ﷺ honour has been under attack from the very first day. The Quraysh, cornered by a Qur'an they could not account for, threw every label they could invent: a magician, a madman, a poet, anything that might make him and his words go away. The propaganda never stopped. It only changes its vocabulary from age to age, and the slanders of our own day, that he was a man of violence, a man of appetites, are the same old method dressed in modern clothes. And here is the hard truth the lecture will not soften: you cannot defend the Messenger of Allah ﷺ if you do not know his life. You will not know which charges are pure fabrication, which events truly happened, and what their context was that makes sense of them. The defence of his honour has always run straight through the seerah, and there is no other road to it.

Put every reason side by side, and the summary writes itself: this is the study of the best of the best of the best. The best human being who ever lived ﷺ, the master of the children of Adam, as he said of himself, not in boast but as plain fact reported to him by his Lord. The best generation that ever was. The best places on earth, Makkah and Madinah, the second of them made sacred on his own lips when he declared, by Allah's leave, that he was making Madinah holy as Ibrahim had made Makkah holy. The best of all eras. Three and twenty years of prophethood, out of which everything we hold today has flowed.

How his story reached you

Fourteen centuries is a long way for a story to travel without distortion, so the honest mind asks: where does the seerah actually come from? The first source you have already met tonight, the most prized and most overlooked of all: the Qur'an itself, revealed in the thick of the events. The second is the hadith, because every authentic narration is, in truth, one snapshot of his ﷺ life, and the six famous collections, with dozens of books beside them, hold thousands upon thousands. The third is an entire bookshelf written for the seerah alone, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi traces its beginning to a touching place: the homes of the companions' own children. Picture growing up with a father who had walked beside the Prophet ﷺ, and you understand at once why the sons reached for the pen. Urwa, the son of az-Zubayr and the nephew of our mother Aisha radiyallahu anha herself, could sit with his aunt and ask what no other man was permitted to ask, and so he became one of the great carriers of her memories of the Prophet ﷺ. Those earliest little books survive now only inside the larger works that came after: in an age when owning a book meant copying it out by hand, the scribes copied the fullest one, and the small pamphlets quietly dissolved into the libraries they had fed.

The biggest one came early. Muhammad ibn Ishaq, born in Madinah around eighty-five years after the hijrah, grew up among the children and grandchildren of the companions, in the very city where the Prophet ﷺ had lived and died and been buried. He gathered everything, travelling to other cities to chase down the reports that had scattered with the companions across the new lands. Then he did what the earlier writers had not: he arranged it all, for the first time, in the order it had happened. And he did it the uniquely Islamic way, the way no other religion or culture on earth ever built: every report carried its isnad, its chain of named narrators running back, link by link, to the eyewitness, with each name's memory and honesty known and graded. We did not simply pass our sacred history down as fable and rumour; we asked, at every step, who told you this, and who told him, all the way back to the one who was there. His book ran to some ten or fifteen volumes. A generation later, Ibn Hisham trimmed it into the edition the world still reads, and the discipline of what he did is the point: he added nothing and rearranged nothing, he only cut, and mostly what he cut was long stretches of poetry and the endless lineages stretching back to Nuh.

Then, centuries on, came the vindication. Ibn Ishaq's original had been lost, until the great Indian scholar Dr. Hamidullah went hunting through the manuscript collections of Europe, where so many of this ummah's treasures had ended up, and recovered roughly a quarter of it. Scholars laid the rediscovered original beside Ibn Hisham's version, and the comparison confirmed him completely: cuts, and only cuts, no additions, exactly as he had claimed. So when you open Ibn Hisham today, Dr. Yasir Qadhi tells us, you are reading, in substance, a book composed roughly a hundred years after the Prophet ﷺ passed away, generations before Bukhari and Muslim ever compiled their hadith. Around these stand the other genres: the shama'il books that preserve how he ﷺ looked and carried himself, the dala'il books that gather his miracles, and the histories of the companions and of the two holy cities.

It is from these wells that this whole series drinks. Dr. Yasir Qadhi teaches from the sources directly, weighing the chains as he goes, refusing the convenience of the modern retellings, and pausing after each incident for the question that justifies the entire journey: not merely what happened, but what it asks of us now. He has taught the seerah four or five times across his life, he confesses, and never once reached the end of it in English, because every time he refuses to water it down and the years run out before the story does. That is not a flaw in the teaching. Some roads are meant to be walked slowly.

Two rivers of Arabs

The last stretch of the night turns from the why and the how to the stage itself: who were the Arabs that the Prophet ﷺ was sent among? Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi reaches back further than most expect, because you cannot understand a people without their origins. The oldest layer of Arabs is gone entirely. The scholars call them the extinct Arabs, nations like Ad and Thamud, the earliest peoples known to have lived in the peninsula. The Qur'an still recites their stories as a warning, you can still walk today into the dwellings they carved out of the rock, and modern archaeology dates the flourishing of Thamud to some five thousand years ago. Ibn Khaldun preserves a memory that they had wandered out from the ancient city of Babel after some great strife. They rose, they defied the prophets sent to them, and they vanished. History keeps them now only as a lesson.

The Arabs who remained flow down in two great rivers. The first is Qahtan, the line of the south, called the original Arabs. Tradition traces the very word Arab, and the Arabic tongue itself, back to Qahtan's son Ya'rub, though Dr. Yasir Qadhi adds the sensible caveat that languages grow over generations, they are not minted whole by a single man in an afternoon. From this southern river came the storied kingdoms whose names still echo, like Saba, whose sign the Qur'an still recites. And from this same river, much later, two tribes named Aws and Khazraj would settle in a northern town called Yathrib. Hold their names lightly in your memory, because the seerah will come back for them.

The second river runs, astonishingly, through a single baby left crying in a barren valley. Ismail, the Sheikh reminds us, was not an Arab by blood at all. His father Ibrahim came from Iraq, and his mother Hajar was from Egypt. The Prophet ﷺ never forgot that Egyptian grandmother. You will conquer Egypt, he ﷺ foretold his companions, and when you do, treat its people well, for you have with them a bond of kinship. When Ibrahim, by his Lord's command, left mother and infant son in the empty valley that would one day become Makkah, a wandering tribe of Qahtan called Jurhum passed through and settled near the water that sprang for them. Ismail grew up among them, married into them, and took up their tongue. And so his descendants became the Arabized Arabs, the bloodline of Ibrahim speaking the language of Qahtan. Generations down that line a man named Adnan was born, and from Adnan spread the tribes of the north, among them, in time, Quraysh. From Adnan to the Prophet ﷺ, Dr. Yasir Qadhi notes, the genealogists count exactly twenty generations, and on this single point, for once, they do not disagree at all.

And here, at the close of the night, the planning of Allah shows its hand. Settled at the crossroads of the whole peninsula, the Arabized Arabs met every passing tribe, traded with every dialect, and absorbed the best of all of them, until in time they grew more eloquent and more honoured than the original Arabs from whom they had borrowed their very speech. The students surpassed the teachers. And it was from this line, the line that had taken a borrowed language and perfected it, that Allah chose His final Messenger ﷺ. None of this is dry genealogy to be skimmed and forgotten. These tribes, their fierce pride, their old rivalries and shifting alliances, are the chessboard on which the entire seerah will be played out. And beneath all of it lies one story still waiting to be told: Ibrahim, Ismail, and the empty valley that became the most beloved city on earth.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to treat a chapter like this one as throat-clearing, a list of reasons to get through before the real story starts tomorrow. That would be to miss the question it is actually putting to you. Strip the night down to its heart, and it is not really about why to study at all. It is about how to love, and whether your love for the Prophet ﷺ is the kind that does something, or only the kind that says something.

He told us that love wants the details. The man who loves his children calls home to ask what they ate for breakfast, not because the answer matters but because the wanting to know is the love itself. So the question lands on us with nowhere to hide: can you name the children of the Prophet ﷺ? Do you know how old he was when the angel came, or when he left Makkah, or why? If the honest answer is no, that is not a small gap in your knowledge. It is the measure of a love that has never once gone looking for its beloved. And the cure is the gentlest thing imaginable. Learn one true thing about him today, then one tomorrow. The circle only spins upward.

He showed us that the Qur'an was revealed inside a story, and that the seerah is the key that unlocks it. Most of us recite the same short surahs a thousand times and feel almost nothing, because we read the words without the moment they were sent into. Surah ad-Duha is only sound until you know the weeks of terrible silence it broke, and then it is Allah Himself bending down to console a frightened heart ﷺ, and yours along with it. So pair your recitation with his life, and watch the locked doors swing open one by one.

And he reminded us that Allah steadied the strongest heart ever made with the stories of the prophets who came before, and asked, in effect, what we imagine our own weaker hearts are meant to run on. This is the deepest thing the night offers. When your times feel heavy, when the suspicion and the slander press in and the news runs dark, the medicine has been sitting on the shelf the whole time. Walk through the worst years of the people who came before you, and your trials will shrink to their true size while your hope rises to its true size, because theirs were so much heavier and Allah carried them through every one. That is what the seerah has always been for this ummah: not a museum to admire, but a fire to warm yourself at when the world goes cold.

So take one thing from this threshold down into your ordinary days, before the journey even begins. Decide that you will not merely read these hundred days but walk them, asking of each one where his footsteps ﷺ are pointing your own. Learn the names of his children this week if you have never known them. And the next time your heart feels small and besieged, reach for the lives of those who endured far worse and were not abandoned, because the word for following a beloved down the road he walked first is seerah, and it is a road still open to anyone who wants it. May Allah make us of those who study him until we love him, who love him until we follow him, and who follow him until we are gathered in his company; may He steady our hearts with his story ﷺ as He steadied his heart with the stories of the prophets; and may He send His peace and His blessing upon him, his family, and his companions, until the Day we meet him at last.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 3: why study the seerah and pre-Islamic Arabia (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (33:21, 93:1-5, 11:120), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 04 of 104

The World Before Islam

The darkness that made the light unmistakable

Before the seerah tells you who he ﷺ was, before the lineage and the birth and the cave on the mountain, it stops and turns you around to look at the world he was sent into. Not its politics, not yet, but its soul: what people believed, who they bowed to, and how far they had wandered from the truth. Dr. Yasir Qadhi opens this night with a reason as simple as it is exact. We know white by black and day by night; we understand a thing through its opposite. So if we want to feel the weight of the mercy Allah sent in His Prophet ﷺ, we must first feel the dark it arrived in. Walk back into that dark on purpose. It is the only honest way to reach him already grateful.

The Arabs had a prophet too

It is tempting to imagine the Arabs of the desert as a people who had never been told the truth, who simply never knew. That is not the case, and the Qur'an is plain about it.

Indeed, We have sent you with the truth as a bringer of good tidings and a warner. And there was no nation but that there had passed within it a warner.Qur'an 35:24

Every nation, Allah says, had its warner. The Arabs were no exception, and their warner was Ibrahim, peace be upon him, together with his son Ismail. This is why the Qur'an again and again calls the path back to them the religion of your father Ibrahim, and why the Arabs themselves still spoke his name with reverence. He was a man of pure tawhid, one God and not a single idol, and so was his son after him.

And Ibrahim left them more than a creed. He sanctified Makkah itself, made it a haram, a sanctuary, a word from the same root as that which is forbidden, because so much that is permitted everywhere else becomes forbidden within its bounds. Inside the haram a man may not hunt the wild game, may not so much as tear a leaf from a tree, because it is all Allah's creation and untouchable there. Allah says of it that whoever enters it is safe, and the ruling stands in our law to this day: a criminal who flees to the haram seeking refuge is not to be touched within it. The idol-worshippers themselves upheld this so completely that Ibn Abbas remembered men of the days of ignorance saying a person could come upon the very murderer of his father performing tawaf and would not lay a hand on a hair of his head. Ibrahim instituted the four sacred months, when all hostility had to cease and every warring tribe was bound to peace, a mercy that steadied society and let its trade breathe. And he laid down the hajj entire: the tawaf around the House, the running between Safa and Marwa, the sacrifice, the animals garlanded and led to Makkah so that everyone along the road would know they had been dedicated to the poor of the sanctuary. For thousands of years these rites stood.

Which sets up the one question the whole night turns upon. If the father of the Arabs was Ibrahim, and Ibrahim worshipped one God with no idol at all, and Ismail the same, then where on this earth did all the idols come from?

How one man changed a religion

Sheikh Yasir pauses here over an objection the orientalists are fond of: that Islam simply borrowed its rites from the pagans, who already circled the Kaaba, who already ran between the two hills, who already sacrificed at the sanctuary. It is all a matter of where you are standing, he answers. Is the glass half empty or half full? The non-Muslim looks and sees a Prophet ﷺ adopting paganism and adding his own touches to it. We look and see the reverse: a Prophet ﷺ who invented none of these rites but resurrected the original worship of Ibrahim and washed the paganism back out of it. The rites were always Ibrahim's.
