It was the idols that were the intrusion. For if Ibrahim taught tawhid, the shirk had to enter from somewhere, and the Prophet ﷺ told us exactly where.

In an authentic report in Sahih Muslim, he ﷺ said he saw Amr ibn Luhayy al-Khuzai dragging his own entrails through the Fire, punished in a humiliating way, for he was the first to change the religion of Ismail. He was also the first to invent the superstitions of the dedicated animals, the bizarre rules of which beast must be set loose or slaughtered for the gods according to which female bore which calf, the kind of charms and omens that Allah condemns in the Qur'an:

Allah has not appointed [such innovations as] bahirah or sa'ibah or wasilah or ham. But those who disbelieve invent falsehood about Allah, and most of them do not reason.Qur'an 5:103

The story handed down is that Amr traveled north to Syria, to the land of the Amalekites, a powerful and ancient people the histories describe as giants, undefeated, builders of great structures. He found them worshipping idols and asked what these were. These, they told him, are our sources of power: in drought and in hunger and when the enemy attacks, we pray to them, and miracles come. So he asked them for one to carry home. They gave him an idol called Hubal, and Hubal became the first idol of the Arabian Peninsula and the chief idol of the Quraysh, planted by Amr in front of the Kaaba. By the Sheikh's careful reckoning, since the Arabs kept no calendar and dated nothing, Amr belonged roughly to the first century of the Christian era, some five hundred years before the Prophet ﷺ was born. Five centuries of slow rot, then, for that one imported stone to spread its idolatry across all of Arabia.

But the Sheikh will not let you leave it as a curiosity of history. He slows down and presses the harder question onto you directly: how could a single man overturn the faith of an entire people, the faith of Ibrahim and Ismail? He draws out three reasons, and warns that they are not safely buried in the past. The first was an inferiority complex. Amr was dazzled by a mighty civilization, its architecture and its undefeated armies, and assumed that a people so powerful must be right about everything else as well. They were not. A nation can lead the world in technology and in conquest and be utterly lost in its theology and its morals, and to borrow a people's creed merely because they are winning is a trap. The second was credentials. Amr was no ordinary man but the respected chieftain of Khuzaah, the tribe that then held Makkah, a generous and victorious leader whom his people loved, so that when a man of his standing imported a belief, the people followed without question. And the third was ignorance. Two thousand years lay between Ibrahim and Amr; the prophets had become a distant rumor, the message diluted, and a people who no longer know their own religion are easy to move. The Sheikh names these three plainly and then turns the mirror on us: we face the very same three pressures today, whenever our religion is pushed to remake itself to suit whoever in our own age happens to be powerful, eloquent, and credentialed.

When the stones became gods

The Qur'an traces idolatry on the earth back further still, past Amr, all the way to the people of Nuh and the names they would not abandon.

And said, 'Never leave your gods and never leave Wadd or Suwa or Yaghuth and Yauq and Nasr.'Qur'an 71:23

Ibn Abbas explained how it began, in an age when there was only a single settlement on all the earth. These five, Wadd and Suwa and Yaghuth and Yauq and Nasr, were righteous men, and when they died their people carved images of them so as to remember their piety and be moved by it. That was all it was at first, a memorial. But generation by generation the remembering curdled, until the children no longer recalled the men at all and worshipped the statues themselves. It is always a stepping stone, the Sheikh notes, never a leap, and Shaytan is patient. And the strangest thing is that these very same five names, unworshipped for untold ages, surfaced again among the tribes of Arabia in the Prophet's ﷺ own time, one tribe clinging to Wadd, another to Suwa, another to Yaghuth, as though the oldest idolatry on earth had been disinterred.

From Hubal at the Kaaba the idols multiplied in ways that should stop you cold. Caravans leaving Makkah took to chipping a stone from the Kaaba itself and carrying it off, setting it down wherever they camped and worshipping it, as though the building blocks of the House were holy in themselves. They are not, the Sheikh insists, and this is a thing many Muslims never pause to think about: it is the place that is sacred, the patch of earth Allah chose, not the marble of the floor nor the silk of the covering nor the bricks of the walls. The Kaaba has been taken down and rebuilt many times across the centuries; the structure comes and goes, and only the ground abides. But the people of that age forgot the difference, and so the worship slid from the House to its stones, and from its stones to any stone at all. One companion, Abu Raja al-Utaridi, remembered it in his own words in a report in Sahih Muslim: before Islam we used to worship rocks, and if we came upon a finer rock than the one we had, we would throw the first away and take up the better; and if we were traveling in the open desert and could find no rock at all, we would heap up a mound of sand, milk a goat over it to pack it firm, and make tawaf around that.

It is, as the Sheikh says, mind-boggling that intelligent people could do such a thing, until you remember that there are corners of the world where men still pour offerings before a painted stone, and then you understand it is not so far from us as we would like. On the two hills of Safa and Marwa themselves stood a pair of idols, Isaf and Naila, which the people would touch in passing as they walked between them. So when Islam came, the Muslims hesitated over the very rite Ibrahim had given: how could they run between two hills that the pagans had defiled with idols? And Allah settled it for them.

Safa and Marwa belong to Allah

Indeed, as-Safa and al-Marwah are among the symbols of Allah. So whoever makes hajj [pilgrimage] to the House or performs umrah - there is no blame upon him for walking between them. And whoever volunteers good - then indeed, Allah is Appreciative and Knowing.Qur'an 2:158

The two hills were sacred long before any idol was ever set upon them, the Sheikh draws out, and they remained Allah's signs after the idols were gone. There is no blame, the verse reassures the hesitant believer, in walking between them. The paganism had always been the stain laid over the rite, never the rite itself, and Allah simply lifted the stain away and handed Ibrahim's worship back, clean.

By the time the Prophet ﷺ would one day conquer Makkah, there were some three hundred and sixty idols ranged around the Kaaba, of every shape and size, most of them half-human and half-animal, the stuff of children's fairy tales given an altar to stand on. The Quraysh had even reached the point of deciding that the angels were the daughters of Allah, and they worshipped them as such. And yet, the Sheikh points out, beneath all of it there was no real creed, no settled doctrine at all. Idolatrous societies rarely have one. Ask ten of them what they believe and you may get ten different answers, each man with his own gods and his own private notion of what they can and cannot do for him, all of it equally permitted because nothing is fixed. Many of the Arabs believed in a Day of Judgment; many denied it; there was no agreement to be had. The one thing they all clung to was the oldest excuse of every idolater who has ever lived, the excuse that the idols only bring us nearer to Allah.

The few who would not bow

It was not total. Even in that long darkness the books of sira preserve a scattered handful of people called the hunafa, from a word meaning to turn away: they turned away from shirk and toward Allah, groping back toward the pure way of Ibrahim with no living prophet anywhere to guide them. Allah Himself calls Ibrahim a hanif in verse after verse, more than eight times, and the Arabs knew the word and what it meant, and these few were searching, deliberately, for the religion of Ibrahim by that very name.

One of them was Quss ibn Saidah, an old man of the distant tribe of Iyad, whom the Prophet ﷺ saw with his own eyes preaching against idolatry at the hajj when he ﷺ was still young, for the hajj drew all of Arabia together. Quss was among the most eloquent of the Arabs, and his speech, it was said, came nearer to the cadence of the Qur'an than any other man's. Decades later, in the ninth year after the hijrah, when the tribe of Iyad came to accept Islam, the Prophet ﷺ asked after Quss and was told he had died long ago, and still he remembered him upon his red camel and asked them to remind him of the man's words, and they did, and he loved them: O people, listen and understand; whoever lives will die, and whoever dies is gone, and everything decreed will surely come to pass. Where are Thamud and Aad? Where are your fathers and your grandfathers? Who will reward the one who did good but was never thanked, and who will punish the one who did wrong but was never punished? With no scripture in his hands, Quss was reaching for the Day of Judgment, and saying aloud that there must be a religion better than the one his people were on.

Ibn Hisham preserves a quieter scene, one the Sheikh lingers over with affection. The Quraysh held a great festival around their idols, and the whole city poured out to it, exalting the idols and sacrificing to them, until four men found themselves left behind, each one hanging back, each too ashamed before the others to take any part in it. And realizing in that moment that they were all of one mind, they made a pact: let us stay friends in this, and let us tell no one, and let us go out and search for the true religion of Ibrahim, for these stones can neither harm nor help. Then their paths scattered across the world. One of the four was Waraqa ibn Nawfal, the cousin of Khadijah, who studied the scriptures, learned Hebrew, and embraced Christianity. He is the same blind old scholar to whom Khadijah would one day rush the Prophet ﷺ after the first revelation, the learned man of Makkah who recognized the angel at once as the very one who had come to Musa and to Isa, and who wept that he was not young enough to stand beside him ﷺ on the day his own people would drive him out. Waraqa died soon after that meeting, and in a sense that the Sheikh marks plainly, this man who knew the truth before the call had even gone public was the first believer in Makkah to pass away. Of the four companions of that pact, the transcript's telling leaves one or two of the names indistinct, and faithful to the discipline of this series we will not guess at what it garbles. What matters is the thing itself: that even with no prophet alive, sincere men went out searching, and were not all of them lost.

Zayd, a nation of one man

The most striking of those searchers was Zayd ibn Amr ibn Nufayl, a cousin of Umar ibn al-Khattab. He went to the Jewish rabbis and to the Christian priests and was unconvinced by both, telling them to their faces what he was sure of: this is not the religion of Ibrahim, and you know it. So he came home to Makkah and stood among the Quraysh and declared that there was not one soul left upon the religion of Ibrahim in the whole of the city but himself. Asma bint Abi Bakr, then still a small child, remembered seeing him with her own eyes, leaning his back against the Kaaba and rebuking his people for their idols, for the meat they fed to stones, and for the daughters they buried alive. And when any man of the Quraysh meant to kill his infant girl, Zayd would intercept him: give her to me, he would say, I will take her, I will raise her in my own household as my own. And he did, gathering up the daughters that Makkah meant to murder, forbidding the killing, sheltering them.

The Prophet ﷺ, himself a young man who had never once in his life bowed to an idol nor eaten of what was sacrificed to one, met Zayd and found in him a kindred spirit, asking him what stood between him and his people. But Zayd died about five years before the revelation came, and never reached it. Years later his son Saad ibn Zayd, one of the ten given the glad tidings of Paradise in their own lifetimes, came to the Prophet ﷺ and asked, knowing his father and what he had stood for, what his fate would be. And the answer the Prophet ﷺ gave is among the most beautiful things in the whole of this night: he will be raised, on the Day of Judgment, as a single nation entirely by himself, an ummah of one man, with no prophet of his own, saved by the sincerity of a heart that simply knew the idols were a lie. And after the Night Journey the Prophet ﷺ told Saad that he had entered Paradise and seen his father there, blessed with not one garden but two. There is the first comfort of the night, and you should hold onto it: even buried alive in the dark, a sincere heart is never abandoned by Allah.

The world outside Arabia

Lift your eyes beyond the peninsula and the picture does not brighten; it only widens. Persia and its great empire followed Zoroastrianism, tending a perpetual fire that was never permitted to die and dividing all of reality between a god of light and a god of darkness, which from the eyes of tawhid is one more paganism in another dress. Rome was Christian, but a Christianity already remade by human hands. Within a generation or two of Isa, peace be upon him, his followers had divided. There were those who held to what he had actually taught, that he was a servant and a messenger of Allah, sent to the Children of Israel and bound himself to the Law, circumcision and the rest. And there was a newer theology, traced by the historians to a man who had never walked beside Isa in his life, which lifted Isa up toward divinity, set the Law aside as no longer binding, and seeded the idea of a trinity.

For three centuries it was argued back and forth, the Sheikh recounts, the believers persecuted and thrown to the lions all the while, until a Roman emperor at last converted and gathered a great council to settle on one official version of the faith. The version chosen was the remade one, and it carried more than a little of the old Roman paganism quietly into it. From that point the original monotheists were the ones hunted and scattered, their books gathered up and burned so that only the approved few survived, and the Christianity that went on to conquer Rome was no longer the Christianity of Isa. Two hundred and fifty years before the Prophet ﷺ was born, true monotheist Christianity had all but vanished from the empire.

Almost. The seerah keeps two witnesses to just how little of it was left. The first is Salman al-Farisi, who tells his own story in an authentic report: the son of a Persian fire-keeper, set to tending the sacred flame, who one day heard a Christian monk at prayer and was so moved that he abandoned the fire and the religion of his father and went out, teacher to teacher, across the Christian world. Each of his masters, as he came to die, would point him onward to another who still held the old, undiluted way, until the last of them told Salman there was nearly no one left upon it anywhere on earth, but that the awaited prophet was about to come at last: he would rise in a land of date palms, he would carry the seal of prophethood between his shoulders, and he would accept a gift but never take charity. Salman set out toward Arabia and was betrayed on the road, kidnapped and sold into slavery, and ended up enslaved in the very town that would one day become Madinah. There, after long years of toil, the Prophet ﷺ arrived on his hijrah, and Salman tested the three signs upon him one by one, the dates, the refusal of charity and the acceptance of a gift, and at last the seal upon his back, which the Prophet ﷺ uncovered for him without being asked, and Salman wept and kissed his hands and feet. The second witness is the emperor Heraclius, who in a report in Sahih al-Bukhari interrogated Abu Sufyan when a trading caravan from Makkah happened to be in Jerusalem, recognized from his own hidden scriptures that this was the foretold prophet, and openly confessed as much, and then could not bring himself to surrender his throne to follow him, and died upon his old faith. Two men who clearly saw the very same truth, and between them the whole of the human heart laid bare: one who surrendered everything to it, and one who could not let go of the world.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read the world before Islam as mere background, the dim stage the real story is acted upon, and move on quickly to the birth. That would be to miss the question this night is built to put to us. Into all of the darkness you have just walked through, the Prophet ﷺ was sent. In a report in Sahih Muslim he describes what Allah saw when He looked upon the earth in those days, and it is not a gentle picture: He despised them all, in the harshest of terms, their idolatry and their ignorance, save only a remnant of the People of the Book, the very teachers Salman had spent his life chasing from land to land. That is the darkness. That is precisely why we call it jahiliyyah. And because of him ﷺ, the Prophet promised, there will never come a jahiliyyah like it upon the earth again. He is the light, the Sheikh closes the night, and the mercy to all the worlds, sent so that the long night could finally break.

Does man think that he will be left neglected?Qur'an 75:36

Carry three things out of this night, because they were never only about the seventh century. The first: guidance comes from Allah alone. For two thousand years no philosopher, no genius, no proud and powerful civilization could reason its way back to the truth; the world stayed lost in the dark until revelation came down from above it. We are not free, then, to bend the Qur'an and the Sunnah to the taste of an age, to make halal today what was haram yesterday because the times have moved on. If the greatest minds of mankind could not find their way to guidance until Allah gave it, neither can we manufacture it for ourselves now. The second: most people follow the crowd. The Quraysh knew in their hearts that the idols were not the religion of Ibrahim, and worshipped them anyway, because everyone did, and it was simply too hard to break away alone. To stand with the truth has almost always meant standing apart from the many. And the third, the gift hidden inside Salman's entire wandering life: whoever is truly sincere will be guided. It did not matter that he was raised tending a fire in Persia, surrounded from birth by everything false; a heart that genuinely wanted the truth was pulled, teacher by teacher, kidnapped and enslaved and still pulled, all the way to the feet of the Prophet ﷺ. So when you find yourself surrounded by falsehood, or lost in a darkness of your own, the night asks you only to want the truth sincerely, and to trust the promise sealed in the verse: that man was never created to be left neglected, and that the same Allah who guided Salman out of the dark is more than able to guide you out of yours. May Allah show us the truth as truth and grant us to follow it, show us falsehood as falsehood and grant us to leave it, and send His peace upon His Prophet ﷺ, the light sent to a world like ours, and gather us under his banner among the sincere.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 4: the religious status of the world before Islam (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (35:24, 5:103, 71:23, 2:158, 75:36), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 05 of 104

The lineage of the Prophet ﷺ and the Year of the Elephant

The chain Allah chose, and the army He turned to straw

The prologue is over. The classical books of seerah never opened with a birth; they opened with a question and a chain: who are the Arabs, and through which line did Allah send His final Messenger? It can look like the driest possible way to begin a story you already love, a recitation of grandfathers, a wall chart of names. But sit with it the way Dr. Yasir Qadhi sits with it, and the names begin to move. For five generations before the Prophet ﷺ was born, Someone was quietly assembling a family, a city, and a House for him, plank by plank, like a stage built in the dark before the lights come up. And the day this assembly ends arrives with one of the most cinematic scenes the ancient world ever produced: an army marching on the Kaaba behind war elephants, in the very year that the Prophet ﷺ lay hidden in his mother's womb.

A map of the Arabs, and twenty certain names

Begin where the old scholars began, with the race itself. None of what follows is science; it is legend handed down generation to generation, and the honest teacher says so plainly. There were the extinct Arabs, the most ancient peoples of the peninsula, nations the Qur'an itself names, Aad and Thamud among them, gone now save for their stories. Then there were the Arabs who remained, in two branches: the pure Arabs of Qahtan, the old civilisations of Yemen, and the arabized Arabs, the descendants of Ismail, who were not born to the tongue but took Arabic from the southern tribes and made it their own. Many generations down from Ismail comes a man named Adnan, and from Adnan, exactly twenty generations later, comes Muhammad ﷺ. Dr. Yasir Qadhi cannot resist dating him out loud: one early genealogist records that Adnan's son lived in the time of Isa, peace be upon him, and five hundred and seventy years across twenty generations lands at roughly thirty years a generation, which is simply how human families run.

So the lineage of the Prophet ﷺ falls into three zones, and their colours of certainty are themselves a lesson. From him ﷺ back to Adnan: twenty names, agreed upon by everyone, set in stone, memorised by the ummah for fourteen centuries, Muhammad, son of Abdullah, son of Abd al-Muttalib, son of Hashim, son of Abd Manaf, son of Qusayy, and on up. From Adnan back to Ismail: folklore, honestly admitted, one classical source gathering seven different opinions, seven names in one list, forty-one in another. And from Ismail back to Adam there is a single source, the Old Testament, which the Muslim does not rely on.

So those wall charts that run the Prophet's ﷺ ancestry all the way to Adam in fifty-five tidy steps, the ones framed in so many Muslim homes? Dr. Yasir Qadhi grades them with a smile that has become one of his signatures: half fact, half myth, half fiction, never mind that the fractions refuse to add up. The first third is certain, the second is legend, the last is borrowed from a book we cannot trust. And that same chart quietly smuggles in a claim, that humanity is only about six thousand years old, which Islam never signed; carbon dating and cave paintings place human beings tens of thousands of years back, and our tradition, he insists, leaves room for all of it. Imam Malik was once told of a man who could trace his pedigree to Adam, and disapproved sharply: how could anyone know? And the Qur'an, naming the vanished nations, simply says of the ages between them:

And [We destroyed] ʿAad and Thamūd and the companions of the well and many generations between them.Qur'an 25:38

When Allah says many, the Sheikh argues, He does not mean ten or fifteen. The beginning recedes into a depth we cannot measure, and the believer is free to say, of everything before Adnan, the honest words our scholars said: Allah knows best.

The chain Allah chose

Inside those twenty certain names, a hadith in Sahih Muslim draws the circles tighter and tighter, like a hand closing. The Prophet ﷺ said that Allah chose Kinanah from the descendants of Ismail, and chose Quraysh from Kinanah, and chose Banu Hashim from Quraysh, and chose him from Banu Hashim. Choice within choice within choice, until the chain narrows to a single man. For the Arabs of his day, everything rode on lineage, a man's standing, his honour, the very right to be heard, and so Allah gave His final Messenger the noblest line mankind has known. The companions wielded it openly: a companion standing before the emperor of Persia declared, without flinching, that his land was the best of lands, his lineage the best of lineages, his tribe the best of tribes, and the man Allah had sent the best of them all.

But keep the balance the Sheikh insists on, because here the unwary stumble. Your father's name is worth precisely nothing on the Day you stand before Allah; no pedigree drags a single soul into Paradise or out of the Fire. And yet in this world, lineage opens doors, and to pretend otherwise is naive. Even America, a land of immigrants almost allergic to aristocracy, still makes way for a Kennedy or a Rockefeller. So there is no contradiction in the honour Islam leaves with Quraysh: zakat is held beneath the Prophet's ﷺ descendants, leadership sat among Quraysh for a thousand years of khilafah, and none of it, not one strand of that noble lineage, will save the unrighteous among them on the Day they meet their Lord. Honour in the world, and weightlessness before Allah, held together without strain. Quraysh, by the way, is itself a title, not a birth name, and the strongest opinion settles it on Fihr, because every clan of the tribe in the Prophet's ﷺ Makkah, and the lineages of all ten companions promised Paradise, converge exactly at him.

Qusayy gathers the scattered

Now the story tightens onto Makkah itself, and onto a man without whom there would have been no city for the Prophet ﷺ to be born into. Ismail had married into Jurhum, the tribe that held the sacred valley for centuries, until they grew unjust and bled the pilgrims with taxes. The tribe of Khuzaah rose against them and threw them out, and on their way into exile Jurhum committed one act of pure spite that would shadow the city for three hundred years: they buried the well of Zamzam so thoroughly that no amount of digging could ever find it again, leaving the city of the sacred well to haul its water in from distant places. And Khuzaah pushed out the descendants of Fihr as well, so that the original Quraysh lived scattered in encampments outside their own holy city, exiles at the edge of the House their forefather had served.

Enter Qusayy, around the year 400, some hundred and seventy years before the Prophet's ﷺ birth, his great-great-great-grandfather and the true beginning of the rise this whole series is about. His method was patience dressed as marriage. He wedded the daughter of the chief of Khuzaah, settled inside the ruling house, and so plainly outshone the chief's own sons that when the old man died the people wanted the son-in-law over the blood heirs. From inside the family he summoned the scattered clans of Fihr, and some say it is this very gathering that earned them the name Quraysh, the gatherers. He went to war, expelled Khuzaah, and took Makkah back for his people. The ascent of Quraysh begins with him; five generations later, when the Prophet ﷺ arrives, they stand at the very pinnacle of their power.

And Qusayy built like a man who fully intended to be remembered. He raised the Dar an-Nadwa, Arabia's first parliament, on the very ground the Prophet's ﷺ own Makkah would still be using generations later. He took custody of the Kaaba and carved its service into offices, the watering of the pilgrims, their feeding, the keys of the House, duties his descendants still held in the Prophet's ﷺ lifetime. And he invented the great Hajj fundraiser, rising every season to remind his tribe: you are the neighbours of Allah's House, and these pilgrims are His guests, and they deserve your hospitality. A good man and a good politician at once, which is rarer than it should be.

Hashim puts Makkah on the grid

Qusayy's son Abd Manaf was the kind of man a city loves, handsome and capable, beloved for the way he carried his responsibilities. But it is Abd Manaf's son whom history refuses to call by his given name. He was born Amr; everyone remembers him as Hashim, the crumbler, because in years of hunger he would crumble bread into broth and feed a whole city of pilgrims from the pot. It is said that Hashim never once ate a meal alone.

His masterstroke was born out of horror. A drought came so merciless that a man would dig a grave for his own family and sit down in it to wait, because there would be no one left strong enough to bury them later. Hashim decided this could not go on, and the idea he struck upon reshaped Arabia: two caravans a year. In winter, south to Yemen, where the ships docked carrying the goods of India and Africa; in summer, north to Busra near Damascus, a doorway onto the Silk Road. Dr. Yasir Qadhi, who cannot resist a good business case, sums it up the way a merchant would: location, location, location. The whole world already came to Makkah every Hajj; Hashim simply plugged his city into the planet's two great supply lines, with the Kaaba squarely in the middle. Makkah was on the grid.

And the caravans crossed a lawless desert untouched, because no one robbed the neighbours of the House; even the pagans felt these people were too sacred to raid. Hashim sealed treaties with the kings along the routes, the wealth poured in, and he poured it straight back into feeding the pilgrims. Then Allah Himself sealed the whole arrangement into revelation, an entire surah on the two journeys, the feeding against hunger, the safety against fear, and the only fitting response a heart could make to it:

For the accustomed security of the Quraysh - Their accustomed security [in] the caravan of winter and summer - Let them worship the Lord of this House, Who has fed them, [saving them] from hunger and made them safe, [saving them] from fear.Qur'an 106:1-4

Three seeds were planted in Hashim's life that would shape everything to come. His wealth lit a jealousy in his brother Abd Shams and his nephew Umayyah, and that rivalry of Banu Hashim and Banu Umayyah would run on into Islam and far beyond it. He married a woman of Yathrib, and that single marriage is why, three generations later, the Prophet ﷺ would arrive in Madinah to find distant cousins waiting among the Ansar; Allah plans in decades while men plan in days. And Hashim died far from home, trading in Gaza, which has been called Ghazzat Hashim, the Gaza of Hashim, ever since, where the Prophet's ﷺ great-grandfather rests to this day.

The boy they mistook for a slave

Because Hashim died on the road, his most important son was born in Yathrib, in his mother's town, after his father was already in the grave. The child came with a streak of white in his newborn hair, so they named him Shaybah. His mother kept him quietly hidden from his Makkan uncles, and you can understand why: in that world custody went to the stronger, and Quraysh were stronger. Years later his uncle al-Muttalib passed through Yathrib, took one look at the boy, and knew his own blood. He coaxed the lad onto his camel with talk of the great ancestors and the honour waiting in Makkah to be reclaimed, and rode hard for home. The Makkans who saw a dusty boy riding behind al-Muttalib drew the obvious conclusion, that the chief had bought himself a new slave: Abd al-Muttalib, they said, the slave of al-Muttalib. The nickname stuck, and stuck so hard that the most prestigious chieftain Arabia would ever know carries it to this day, his real name all but forgotten beneath it.

He had to fight his own uncles for his father's share of the inheritance, and he won it. And then came the dream. Night after night he was shown the same thing, dig in such and such a place, between this idol and that marker, and you will find Zamzam. He ignored it at first, then could ignore it no longer. He had one son at the time, al-Harith, and the two of them went out with a shovel and an axe while Quraysh gathered to mock: you will find what no one has found in three hundred years? He kept digging until he struck something Dr. Yasir Qadhi calls more precious than gold and more precious than oil. He struck water.

The clans crowded in at once: the well belongs to all of us. He refused; he had been shown it, he had dug it, the right was his. The standoff sharpened toward bloodshed, and in that pressure Abd al-Muttalib made the vow that would shadow his house: O Allah, if You ever grant me ten sons grown strong enough to defend me, I will sacrifice one of them to You. The dispute was eventually arbitrated in his favour, and the well of Zamzam has run in his line's care, and the ummah's, ever since.

Allah gave him far more than ten sons. Eighteen of his children reached adulthood, and he lived to nearly a hundred years. A man of his word, he gathered the sons and told them of the vow, and the lot fell on Abdullah, the most beloved of them, sixteen or seventeen years old, the future father of the Prophet ﷺ. He took the boy toward the Kaaba to fulfil his oath, and Quraysh physically refused to let it happen, terrified the sacrifice of so beloved a son would harden into a custom that would one day take their own. Sent to a priestess for a way out, they were told to ransom him, and the ransom settled at one hundred camels. And that number walked straight out of the desert and into the Shariah: to this day, the Sheikh reminds you, the blood money for a human life is reckoned at one hundred camels. Abdullah lived. He married Aminah. And every single day that remains in this series flows out of that ransom.

The year of the elephant

Far to the south, a man named Abrahah governed Yemen for the Negus of Abyssinia, the father of the very Najashi this series will one day meet again. Year after year he watched his Christian subjects stream north, and he asked them where they were going. To Hajj, they told him, to the House of Allah. So Abrahah resolved to build a rival, a colossal cathedral, stained glass hauled across Arabia at vast expense, meant to be the greatest temple of Christianity in the peninsula, and decreed that pilgrimage would now come to him. One Bedouin answered for all the Arabs: he travelled to the cathedral and relieved himself inside it. Abrahah's rage hardened into a vow to match Abd al-Muttalib's own: he would tear the Kaaba to the ground, so the world would have nowhere left to go but to him.

He marched north with an army and with African war elephants, beasts Arabia had never laid eyes on, led by a great tusker named Mahmud. An Arab sold him the road, a hired guide named Abu Righal, whose treachery so disgusted the Arabs that they made a proverb of his name: more treacherous than Abu Righal. As the army neared Makkah it seized livestock, among it more than two hundred camels belonging to Abd al-Muttalib, a small sign of how far the family's fortunes had risen.

Then the chief of Makkah came to the invader's tent, and the books pause over him: a man over six feet tall in an age that rarely produced such height, handsome, unmistakably a leader, so striking that Abrahah rose from his throne and came down to sit on the floor beside him out of sheer respect. I have no quarrel with you, Abrahah offered; leave the city, and I will destroy only your House. And Abd al-Muttalib answered: I did not come to talk about the House. I came about my camels. Abrahah nearly laughed in his face. I have come to demolish your holiest sanctuary, and you come to me about livestock? And then came the sentence that has outlived both men. I am the lord of the camels, said Abd al-Muttalib, and it is my job to protect them; the House has its own Lord, and He will protect it.

He got his camels back. Then he stood at the Kaaba and pleaded with the only One who could help: O Allah, we cannot fight this army, they are far too strong for us; You take care of Your House. And Quraysh withdrew into the mountains, leaving the House to its Lord. Below them, the army turned Mahmud toward Makkah, and the great elephant knelt. They whipped him, they beat him, they bled him; he would not take a single step toward the House, yet turn him any other direction and he walked freely off.

And then the sky filled. Birds in flocks, each one carrying stones, stones the Sheikh describes as a piece of Jahannam shown openly in this world: wherever one struck, flesh dissolved, man and beast collapsing into ruin before the watching eyes of Quraysh, until Allah, in the surah every Muslim child learns by heart, made the proudest army in Arabia like eaten straw.

Have you not considered, [O Muḥammad], how your Lord dealt with the companions of the elephant? Did He not make their plan into misguidance? And He sent against them birds in flocks, Striking them with stones of hard clay, And He made them like eaten straw.Qur'an 105:1-5

Abrahah himself was granted the worst of it. Carried back toward Yemen dissolving as they marched, he died at the very edge of home, denied even the mercy of reaching it. And Makkah kept the memory for generations: one companion, asked in old age whether he was older than the Prophet ﷺ, answered that he ﷺ was greater, but that he himself was born first, for he remembered his mother showing him the dung of the elephants, dried and yellowed, when he was a boy.

The womb the army never knew about

Now step back with Dr. Yasir Qadhi and look at the whole chain at once, because this is the reading the entire night was building toward. Qusayy built the politics. Hashim built the wealth. Abd al-Muttalib restored the water and crowned the prestige, the chieftain whose dua Allah answered with birds out of the sky. Every single ancestor establishes something momentous, as if a stage were being assembled plank by plank. And the man it was assembled for ﷺ knew what he stood upon: at Hunayn, years later, when the lines broke and the newest Muslims of Quraysh wavered, he steadied the field by calling out, I am the Prophet, this is no lie; I am the son of Abd al-Muttalib, reaching for the very grandfather they were proudest of, the man who would raise him ﷺ for his first eight years.

Two closing observations, and they are the kind a person carries for days. First: the chain is bracketed at both ends by ransom. At its head stands Ismail, ransomed from beneath his father's knife by the ram Allah sent down; at its foot stands Abdullah, ransomed from beneath his father's vow for a hundred camels. The beginning and the end of the lineage were both bought back by Allah, as if the line itself were marked in His own hand: this family is being kept for something. Second: look closely at who fought the war of the year 570. A Christian king attacked a pagan city, and the pagans could not defend the House, did not in the end even try; they handed it to its Lord and climbed the hills. Allah defended His own House Himself, and in doing so declared two things at once: that none of them were worthy of it, and that someone worthy was coming, who would one day empty it of its idols and return it to what Ibrahim built it to be.

And then the quietest detail of all, the one the whole day was hiding. Living that year beside the Haram was a young wife named Aminah, and she was expecting a child. When the stones fell from the sky, the Prophet ﷺ was already in her womb; he would be born only a few months later, in that same Year of the Elephant. So the very first miracle of protection in the seerah happened before its subject ﷺ had drawn a single breath. Allah was not only defending a building of stone that year. He was defending the child who would one day fill it with the worship of Him alone, and the proudest army in Arabia had marched up to the edge of Makkah and dissolved into straw without ever knowing that the true reason for the day slept, unborn and unguarded by any human hand, in the house just beyond the rocks where the elephant knelt.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read a day like this one as background, a family tree to be skimmed before the real story starts tomorrow with the birth. That would be to miss the question it puts to us. Stripped to its meaning, this day asks our own iman three things.

It asks whether we can see Allah's hand in the long, slow preparation of a life. Nothing about the Prophet's ﷺ arrival was an accident. Qusayy won back the city a century and a half before he was born; Hashim plugged it into the trade routes of the world and married, almost at random it must have seemed, a woman of a distant town that would one day shelter his great-grandson; Abd al-Muttalib followed a recurring dream to a patch of forgotten ground and dug until lost water rose to meet him. Plank by plank, decade by decade, a stage was being built for a Messenger nobody yet knew was coming. If Allah prepared the world that carefully for him ﷺ, then nothing in the long, frustrating preparation of your own life is random either. The years that feel like waiting are often the years He is building the stage.

It asks us to learn the one sentence at the heart of the day and live by it. I am the lord of the camels, and it is my job to protect them; the House has its own Lord, and He will protect it. There is an entire school of tawakkul folded into that line. Abd al-Muttalib did not abandon his camels to fate and call it trust; he went and got them back, because guarding them was his to do. And he did not march out to fight an army he could not beat, because defending the House was never his to do; it belonged to its Lord. The whole art of faith lives in telling those two apart: doing, with everything in you, the thing that is genuinely yours, and then handing to Allah, without anxiety, the thing that was never in your hands to begin with. We tend to invert it, exhausting ourselves over outcomes we cannot control while neglecting the small duty in front of us. His grandfather got the order right, and the House still stands.

And it asks us to believe, down in the marrow, that what Allah intends to protect cannot be reached. No wall saved the Kaaba that year, no army, no clever negotiation in a tent. Birds saved it. The Arabs dated their calendars from the Year of the Elephant, certain they had witnessed the great event of their age, with no idea that the real event of that year weighed less than a single stone of hard clay and slept in the house of a woman named Aminah. When you are small and unseen and the odds against you look like elephants, this day asks whether you truly believe that the One who turned that army to straw is the same One who holds your fragile, hidden things, and that He loses nothing He has chosen to keep.

So take one thing from this day down into your ordinary life. Trust that the slow seasons are preparation, not waste. Do the small duty in front of you with all your strength, and then leave to Allah the outcome that was always His. And the next time you feel hidden and outmatched, remember the womb the army never knew about, and rest. May Allah, the Lord of this House, who prepared the whole world for His Messenger ﷺ before he ever drew breath, send His finest praise and peace upon him, ennoble us by a measure of love for him and his blessed line, and protect what is unseen and fragile in our lives as He once protected, behind the rocks of Makkah, the child who would fill His House with the worship of Him alone.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 5: genealogy and the Year of the Elephant (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (25:38, 106:1-4, 105:1-5), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 06 of 104

The Birth of the Prophet ﷺ

The du'a of Ibrahim, answered

For five lessons the seerah has walked the world that waited for him: the tribes and their idols, the long descent of a chosen line, the grandfather who once stood ready to sacrifice his own son and was given him back. Now, at last, he ﷺ is about to arrive. And yet the lecture does not hurry to the cradle. Before the child is born there is a question worthy of the moment, one Dr. Yasir Qadhi insists we sit with first: of all the lands on the earth, why this one? Why did Allah pass over Rome, the mightiest civilization of the age, and Persia, the most ancient, and choose for the final messenger a desert with no empire, no script, no army, no government at all?

The answer reads less like an accident of history than like a plan laid down long before anyone in Makkah drew breath. And when the birth itself arrives, it arrives the way this series promises to tell everything: weighed, sifted, stripped of fairy tale, until what is left standing is one Monday, one light, and a name no one in the city had ever carried.

The shirk that knew Allah by name

One debt is left over from the last lesson, and it is worth paying carefully, because it shows what this whole religion came to restore. The pagans of Makkah were not strangers to Allah. Ask them who created the heavens, who sends down the rain, who provides their sustenance, and the Qur'an itself records their answer, by that very name: Allah. They carved idols of everything they revered, and never once an idol of Allah, because they knew He could not be represented and could not be contained. Their shirk was subtler than the paganism of other nations. The idols were stepping stones, intermediaries; we are too sinful to approach the Holy One directly, they reasoned, so we route our worship through holy beings who will carry it up on our behalf.

Unquestionably, for Allah is the pure religion. And those who take protectors besides Him [say], "We only worship them that they may bring us nearer to Allah in position." Indeed, Allah will judge between them concerning that over which they differ. Indeed, Allah does not guide he who is a liar and [confirmed] disbeliever.Qur'an 39:3
And they worship other than Allah that which neither harms them nor benefits them, and they say, "These are our intercessors with Allah." Say, "Do you inform Allah of something He does not know in the heavens or on the earth?" Exalted is He and high above what they associate with Him.Qur'an 10:18

And where do such holy beings come from? Consider al-Lat. Before al-Lat was an idol, al-Lat was a man, a generous soul who sat on the road to Makkah grinding barley and feeding soup to tired pilgrims, named for the grinding itself. When he died the people raised a monument over his grave to honor a good man. Travelers began to touch it, then to seek blessings from it, and slowly, surely, a righteous man's grave became a god worshipped beside Allah. This, Sheikh Yasir notes, is exactly why our religion forbids raising monuments over graves: the slope is slippery precisely where the man was good. Almost no one worships an evil being, while billions invoke Isa, one of the greatest of all the prophets, lifted by love above the place that was his.

The warning lands close to home, and the lecture refuses to soften it. The very sentence of the jahili Arabs, that we are too sinful to reach Allah directly, lives on wherever a Muslim routes his worship through a saint, or even through the Prophet ﷺ himself. He is the most beloved of all creation and the role model for every soul, but never a doorway your prayers must pass through. Islam came to cut every intermediary away. Between a servant and his Lord stands no one at all.

Why Arabia: the empty stage

Two lessons have now catalogued how dark jahili Arabia truly was, so the question asks itself with force: why there? The wisdoms gather one by one, and laid side by side they look like preparation.

Begin with the map. Arabia sat wedged between the two superpowers of the age, the Byzantines and the Sasanid Persians, who had bled one another for four hundred years. Connected to both, ruled by neither. Had the message risen in distant China it could never have toppled them; rising on their very doorstep, it conquered both empires within decades of the Prophet's ﷺ passing. And nobody saw it coming, because the Arabs, forever busy fighting one another, had never once threatened the powers around them. When the first Muslim envoys arrived, it is said the Persian emperor treated them like children: go home, take some gold coins, do not be ridiculous. It was as though someone today pointed at the poorest country on the index and announced it the next superpower of the world.

Even the emptiness was a gift. The Arabs had poetry but no written literature, courage but no architecture; the great church of Constantinople already stood, a century old, and the palaces of Persia stand to this day, while Arabia raised nothing that lasted. So when Islam came there was no entrenched civilization to fight it, only a vacuum, and Islam filled it with a civilization entirely its own: its language, its script, its coinage, its architecture, all of it new. The first time in history the Arabs were ever united was under the Prophet ﷺ. And the Qur'an told them plainly what had happened to them, that a people with no legacy had been handed one through which the nations would forever speak their name:

We have certainly sent down to you a Book [i.e., the Qur'an] in which is your mention. Then will you not reason?Qur'an 21:10

The first House, and a people kept ready

Deeper than the geography lay something older. Makkah was the place of Ibrahim and Ismail, the home of the first house ever raised on the earth for the worship of Allah. Every revelation before this one had been local. Musa was sent to the Children of Israel, and Isa after him to the very same; no prophet had ever been sent to the whole of mankind. Islam is the first universal religion, and so it was only fitting that the first call meant for every nation should rise from the valley of the first House built for them all.

Indeed, the first House [of worship] established for mankind was that at Bakkah [i.e., Makkah] - blessed and a guidance for the worlds.Qur'an 3:96

And the people, for all their darkness, had been kept strangely ready. They were simple, unclouded by philosophies and convoluted ideas, and simple hearts accept the truth fastest; in every society, the Sheikh observes, the first to believe are the sincere, never the ones tangled in their own cleverness. They were bred to hardship, able to cross impossible distances on a little water and less food, exactly the stamina the early conquests would demand while the armies of Rome and Persia marched with their pampered troops and long supply lines. They were brave, and proud in the way that pride can be honorable.

Above all, they could not bear to lie. Abu Sufyan, still a pagan and an enemy of the message, once stood before Heraclius, the emperor of Rome, his own caravan placed at his back and instructed to signal the instant he spoke a falsehood. He confessed afterward what held his tongue: had I not feared my people would brand me a liar, I would gladly have invented lies against the Prophet ﷺ. A man's word was the whole of the contract in Arabia; there were no written agreements until Islam brought them, because none had ever been needed, and treachery ranked among the ugliest disgraces a man could carry. Add the finest horses and riders alive, praised by the Prophet ﷺ himself in authentic hadith and prized to this day, and a Semitic tongue so concentrated that a single verb root unfolds into two hundred words, an eloquence fit to carry a final revelation, and the choice of this land stops looking like chance and starts looking like a thing prepared.

The du'a that was waiting for him

Then comes the reason Sheikh Yasir saves for the very end, because in his weighing it outweighs all the rest. Long before that Monday, Ibrahim and his son Ismail stood raising the walls of the Ka'bah, and as they built they prayed:

Our Lord, and send among them a messenger from themselves who will recite to them Your verses and teach them the Book and wisdom and purify them. Indeed, You are the Exalted in Might, the Wise."Qur'an 2:129

Ibrahim already knew that prophethood would run through his other son. The glad tidings had named Ishaq, and a prophet beyond him, and so it went: every prophet after Ibrahim, we believe, rose from the line of Ishaq, generation upon generation, a privilege Allah granted them and which we affirm. Every prophet except one. From the line of Ismail came a single prophet, the last of them all, and Ibrahim's prayer that morning at the foundation of the House was reaching forward across the centuries toward him.

So when the Prophet ﷺ later described himself, he reached past his parents and his clan to that scene at the Ka'bah. I am the du'a of my father Ibrahim, he said in an authentic hadith, and the glad tidings of Isa. For Isa had told the Children of Israel that one would come after him, and the lecture pauses on a remarkable trace: even the New Testament still carries farewell words in which, as some Muslim theologians read them, Isa says he must depart so that another can come. A du'a reaching forward from the foundation of the House, a prophecy reaching forward from the last prophet of Israel, and both of them arriving at one child in Makkah.

Even the bloodline had been narrowed like a beam of light. In a hadith in Sahih Muslim the Prophet ﷺ said that from the children of Ismail, Allah chose Kinanah; from the children of Kinanah, Quraysh; from Quraysh, Banu Hashim; and from Banu Hashim, He chose him ﷺ. The noblest lineage of the noblest house, selected stage by stage, until everything that had been prepared came to rest upon a single marriage.

Abdullah and Aminah: a week of marriage

Of his ﷺ parents we hold barely a few lines, and the honesty about why is itself a lesson. Abdullah and Aminah both died in their early twenties, perhaps younger, decades before any soul on the earth knew their son would be a prophet; no one records the life of an ordinary young couple of Quraysh. And by the time Islam stood stable enough to write down its memory, more than half a century had passed. The proportions tell the story: the fifty-three Makkan years of the Prophet's ﷺ own life fill a single volume of chronicles for every three filled by the ten years of Madinah, because a persecuted people, dragging Bilal through the streets and watching Ammar tortured, is far too busy surviving to record. If even his own years were thinned like that, what chance did his parents' brief lives have?

Here is what we do know. Abdullah was the son Abdul Muttalib had nearly sacrificed and then ransomed, the story this series has already told. Soon after the rescue, Abdul Muttalib went looking for a bride for him and chose Aminah, daughter of Wahb, the chieftain of Banu Zuhrah, another of the clans of Quraysh: a chieftain's son for a chieftain's daughter. Abdullah was a young man, eighteen by one report, in his twenties by another. They married only days before the caravan season, and he spent perhaps three days with his bride, five at the most, before he rode north with the caravan toward Syria. He never came back.

One story survives about him that is not authentically narrated, and the Sheikh flags it exactly so, because this is the discipline the series lives by: people have always wanted to add legends to this story and make it more flowery than it is, and our task is to weigh the chain of transmission, not let our emotions sway us. Yet there is no harm, he says, in telling this one, because nothing in it is wrong. Abdullah, it is said, was strikingly handsome, with a brightness resting in his face, and the young women of Quraysh had openly hinted that they would gladly become his bride; he answered only that he would follow whatever his father wished. After his wedding to Aminah those same women lost all interest, and when he asked them why, they told him: the light that was in your face is gone. Where it had gone, the tale wants us to understand, was into the child Aminah now carried. Allah knows best, and there the matter rests.

On the road home from Syria, Abdullah fell gravely ill. At Yathrib he told the caravan: I am slowing you down; I have relatives here, leave me with them until I recover, and carry on to Makkah. He had kin in that city because Abdul Muttalib's own mother had come from Yathrib, and the Sheikh asks us to pause on that, for Quraysh almost never married outward, and yet Allah had quietly planted a family tie for the Prophet ﷺ in the very city that would one day shelter him and his religion. The caravan reached Makkah without him. Aminah, carrying a child her husband in all likelihood never even knew of, was told first that he was recovering, and then that he was gone, dead at twenty or twenty-two, buried somewhere in Yathrib in a grave no one has ever located. She was a widow at eighteen or nineteen, and the child she carried beneath her heart was the seal of the prophets.

A Monday in the year of the elephant

So when was he ﷺ born? Begin where the Sheikh begins, with the Prophet's own words. A man once asked him why he fasted every Monday, and he answered, in the famous hadith of Sahih Muslim, that this was the day he was born and the day revelation first came down upon him. A Monday, then, on his ﷺ own authority, and that much is beyond dispute.

The year comes to us through the memory of old men. Someone once asked one of the oldest of the living Companions whether he was older than the Prophet ﷺ, and the old man smiled at the wording and corrected it: the Prophet ﷺ is greater than me, but I am older than him. He was born in the year of the elephant, and I remember my mother carrying me out of Makkah as a small boy to see the elephants' dung, still green, drying outside the city. Another Companion said it more simply still: the Prophet ﷺ and I were born in the same year, the year of the elephant. The Arabs kept no running calendar; they hung their years upon events, the year of the drought, the year of the invasion, the year of the elephant, counting forward and back from each until the next great thing happened. Piecing the evidence together, the great majority of historians place that year at 570 of the Common Era. And one faint whisper survives about the hour itself, through a narrator of the first century whose father had lived in the age of the Companions: he ﷺ was born at high noon. There is a small gap in its chain, but it is the only report we have on the hour, and its symbolism is lost on no one. At the very moment the sun stood at its highest and brightest, there entered the world the one who would leave nothing around him dark.

Then there is the day of the month, and here Sheikh Yasir does a thing he jokes may sound to some like blasphemy: he opens the earliest books and simply counts. Ibn Ishaq, the most famous author of the seerah of all, writing roughly two centuries after the birth, states without any chain of narrators that the Prophet ﷺ was born on Monday the 12th of Rabi al-Awwal. But the next earliest book of seerah records other voices entirely: some said the 10th of the month, others the 2nd. Count across the early books and there are more than ten opinions in all, the 2nd, the 8th, the 10th, the 12th, the 17th, the 22nd, and even Ramadan, the view of az-Zubayr ibn Bakkar, the first scholar ever to write a history of Makkah. Weighed honestly, the 2nd, the 8th, and the 10th rest on earlier and stronger authority, with Imam Malik of Madinah among those who held the 8th and a descendant of the Prophet ﷺ himself among those who held the 10th, while the famous 12th hangs upon Ibn Ishaq alone, late and without a chain.

So why did the 12th conquer the calendar? Two reasons, says the Sheikh, who has written a detailed history of the mawlid. First, nine of every ten books on the seerah do little more than summarize Ibn Ishaq, so his date became the date by sheer repetition. And second, a festival. For five full centuries no Muslim celebrated the Prophet's ﷺ birthday at all; the Arabs did not so much as record birthdays, and many a grandmother to this day does not know the year she was born. The first known mawlid appears around the year 517 of the hijrah under the Fatimids of Egypt, a dynasty of extreme Shia theology that staged dozens of public festivals a year, festivals being then what they remain now, occasions of commerce and of popularity for the ruling house, and the date they chose was the 12th. A century and a half later some Sunni governors in Iraq imported the celebration, rulers began competing over whose festival was the grandest, with free meat and bread and gifts handed out, and under the weight of public enthusiasm the floodgates opened. The date lodged itself in the mind of the ummah, even though, weighed academically, it is the very weakest of all the candidates.

His counsel at the close of it is the gentlest correction of the whole night. If you truly wish to celebrate the day the Prophet ﷺ was born, then do with it what he himself did: fast on Mondays, because that was his own answer when he was asked about the fast. To love him with a single festival once a year, the Sheikh says plainly, is a very easy way out. Real love does not gather on one day and disperse; it shows itself every day of the year.

One light, and the legends set aside

And the birth itself? Here the series shows you its spine. Of everything ever narrated about that morning in Makkah, Sheikh Yasir counts exactly one report as academically sound, and he says so without flinching. That the child was born already circumcised. That he fell into prostration the instant he arrived. That he lifted a tiny finger to the sky in testimony of the oneness of Allah. The earliest books, hungry as they were for every scrap of detail, know none of this; books written seven centuries later somehow know it all, the volumes swelling fatter with each retelling. Use the mind Allah gave you, he asks: who exactly was in the room with Aminah to witness such things and carry them down to us? We do not need to invent fairy tales to praise the Prophet ﷺ. Allah has praised him enough, and the facts are enough, and dressing the truth in fabrications only makes the religion look less dignified than it truly is.

The same sieve catches the rest. The temples of the pagans toppling across the earth on the night he was born: no recorded history anywhere mentions it, and the Sheikh does not believe it himself. The jinn barred from eavesdropping on the heavens: that, he says, did happen, but the stronger opinion places it at the coming of revelation, when he ﷺ was forty, and not at his birth. It was then, as the Qur'an describes in the words of the jinn themselves, that they found the sky transformed:

And we have sought [to reach] the heaven but found it filled with powerful guards and burning flames. And we used to sit therein in positions for hearing, but whoever listens now will find a burning flame lying in wait for him.Qur'an 72:8-9

And a band of them, hearing the Qur'an recited, returned to their people believing.

What does survive the sieve is his ﷺ own testimony, an authentic hadith in the Musnad of Imam Ahmad. When my mother gave birth to me, he said, and in another version, while she was carrying me, she saw a light go out from her that lit up the palaces of Busra in the land of Sham. No witness needs hunting here; he is telling us of it himself. And the light, of course, is him.

Why should a newborn's light point toward Syria? Because the geography is itself a prophecy. Sham is a land our religion holds blessed, the land of the prophets of Israel, the land where Isa will one day descend at the end of time. And Sham, the right arm of Byzantium, was the first province Islam would open beyond the Peninsula, with Busra among the very first of its cities; Damascus, the jewel of the Romans, would within a single generation become the capital of the Muslims. A light from Aminah's room had drawn the map of everything that was to come. Teaching this lesson in 2011, with Sham itself bleeding, the Sheikh stopped in the middle of his words to beg Allah to free that land from the tyranny it was enduring and to restore it to its old glory, a du'a worth carrying still in our own prayers.

The seventh day: a name and a freed slave girl

Seven days later, Makkah did for him ﷺ what it did for every newborn, and the very normalcy of it is the point. Ibn Ishaq records it as a matter of fact: his grandfather circumcised him on the seventh day. Only the books written seven centuries later insist that he was born already circumcised, inventing a wonder where the earliest sources saw simply a normal child, of a normal marriage, and a normal birth. Abdul Muttalib held a feast for the boy, and at it he announced a name that puzzled the whole of Quraysh: Muhammad. It was known among the Arabs, the stronger opinion holds, but vanishingly rare, and borne by no one at all in Makkah. Why not a name of your fathers and forefathers, they asked him. The old chief answered: I want him to be praised by the people of the earth, as I want him to be praised by the people of the heavens.

The first day of this series taught what the name carries: the one praised again and again, without end. A grandfather chose it as a wish. Allah wrote it as a fact. Fourteen centuries on, not one second passes without that name being praised somewhere upon the earth.

And one more soul rejoiced that week, and his story is the strangest mercy of the night. Abu Lahab, an older half-brother of the dead Abdullah, was not yet the enemy he would one day become; perhaps he felt the pull of a younger brother's orphan, his own full brother long gone. When his slave girl Thuwaybah came running with the news that his brother's son had been born, he freed her on the spot for sheer joy. Years after Abu Lahab died an enemy of everything that child brought into the world, his brother Abbas, radiyallahu anhu, saw him in a dream, suffering the severest of punishments. Did your kinship to the Prophet ﷺ avail you nothing at all? he asked him. Nothing, said Abu Lahab, except that because I freed Thuwaybah at the good news of his birth, I am given a few drops of water from between these two fingers. Sit with that exactly where the lecture leaves it. A single flicker of joy at his ﷺ arrival, from a man who would spend the whole of his life opposing him, was still not forgotten, still answered with a mercy across the barrier of death. What, then, of a love chosen deliberately, returned to daily, by a believer who means it?

What this day asks of our faith

It is tempting to read a day like this one as pure narrative, a tender opening to a great story, and to take from it nothing but warmth. That would be to miss the question it puts to us. Strip the day down to what actually stands after the sifting, and it asks something of your iman that does not stay safely in the seventh century.

It asks you, first, to keep your worship pure of every middleman. The pagans of Makkah believed in Allah by name, affirmed Him as Creator and Sustainer and Lord, and lost Him anyway, because they could not believe He would receive them directly, and so handed their hearts to intermediaries they imagined would carry their case upward. The tragedy did not begin in wickedness; it began in reverence for the good, in a man who fed pilgrims soup and ended up a god. That same instinct waits inside everyone who has ever felt too small or too sinful to approach Allah without something, or someone, in between. Love the righteous. Love the Prophet ﷺ above all of creation. And then turn, in your prayer, to Allah alone, with no one in the doorway, because that is the whole of what this religion came to restore.

It asks you, second, to trust that Allah is preparing what you cannot yet see. Look at how much was laid in place before the child ever drew breath: an empty stage cleared between two exhausted empires, a people trained by the desert to be truthful and tireless, the valley of the first House ever built for mankind, a family tie planted quietly in a far city against a future need, and beneath it all a du'a spoken at the foundation of the Ka'bah and left waiting through the long centuries. None of it was improvised, and the Sheikh's point reaches straight off the page into your own life: nothing in his story was an accident, and nothing in yours is either. The tie you do not understand, the door that closed, the years that felt like waiting, may be the very scaffolding of something you have not been shown.

It asks you, third, to love him the way he himself taught, and not the way that is easiest. Asked about the day he was born, he did not call for a festival; he pointed to a fast, every Monday, quietly, for the rest of his life. The honest measure of love for the Prophet ﷺ is not a grand evening once a year that costs a little money and a few hours. It is the sunnah carried into an ordinary week, the salah prayed, the character softened, the salawat sent when no one is watching. One day of devotion is a thing the heart can fake. A devotion that returns every single week is a thing only love can sustain.

And it asks you, finally, to believe that no turning toward him is ever wasted. Here is the most staggering mercy of the whole night: Abu Lahab, who would become one of the bitterest enemies the message ever had, felt one instant of genuine joy at the birth of his nephew and freed a slave girl over it, and that single instant followed him past his own death and bought him drops of water in his torment. If a flicker of joy from a man bound for ruin was still recorded and still answered, then consider what is being recorded for you, who love him on purpose, who choose him daily, who send peace upon him with a believing heart. Nothing you give him is lost. Nothing you do for his sake falls through. What the careless world would never count, your Lord is writing down.

So carry one thing from this day down into your ordinary life. Pray to Allah directly, today, about the very thing you have been routing through everyone and everything else. Trust that the waiting and the closed doors are scaffolding, not abandonment. And love the Prophet ﷺ in a way that survives past a single day, by reviving one sunnah this week that no one but Allah will see. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, the answer to the du'a of Ibrahim and the glad tidings of Isa; may He let that beloved name be praised in our homes as He has raised it in the heavens; may He free the land of Sham that his first light touched; and may He gather every one of us in his company on the Day the light is real and full.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 6: the birth of the Prophet ﷺ and why Arabia (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (39:3, 10:18, 21:10, 3:96, 2:129, 72:8-9), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 07 of 104

The early childhood of the Prophet ﷺ

Orphaned three times, raised under Allah's eye

By the time this chapter opens, he ﷺ has barely learned to walk, and the sources go quiet. Dr. Yasir Qadhi begins with an honest accounting, because the honesty is itself part of the lesson. Gather every report we hold from the fifty-three years the Prophet ﷺ spent in Makkah and it amounts to less than half of what we know about ten years in Madinah, and almost all of even that begins after the age of forty. The first four decades reach us as a scattering of moments and long silences. Who, after all, was going to sit in a widow's house and write down the games and the weeping of a fatherless boy?

The believer does not despair at that silence. He takes consolation in a conviction the Sheikh names as the theological footing of it all: whatever we truly needed to know of our Prophet ﷺ, Allah preserved. The few moments we do hold are not leftovers; they are exactly what we were meant to keep, and they are not small: a desert tribe and a milk-mother, an angel who wrestled a four-year-old to the ground, a grave on the road home, a blind chieftain's throne with room for one small boy. This is the chapter that shows why the most beloved of Allah ﷺ was orphaned three times before the age of nine, and why not one of those losses was neglect. Every one was a raising.

Why the nobles sent their sons to the desert

The first thing we know of his life after the birth is strange to modern ears: his mother gave her newborn away, to be nursed and raised out in the open desert. To us it sounds like the opposite of love. To the nobility of Quraysh it was custom, almost a mark of rank, and there was hard, long-term thinking underneath it.

The city bred disease, and in an age when most infants did not survive their first years, the surest protection was distance: a child kept among two or three people in the clean desert air had a better chance of living than one passed from hand to hand in a crowded town. They wanted hardy children, too. Raise a boy in austerity, the reasoning went, and the hardships of Makkah will one day feel like luxury, for children adapt to their circumstances far more easily than grown men can. The desert also kept a boy from the doting of grandparents, uncles, and aunts, the relatives who lovingly undo every rule a parent sets. But the reason the Arabs prized above all was language. City Arabic was being corrupted, loanwords creeping in from every passing caravan, while the pure, ancient tongue survived only in the desert, among certain tribes famous for guarding it unmixed. The most famous of all was Banu Sa'd ibn Bakr: the tribe that came to Makkah for the children of the elite, and that would carry away the son of Amina.

He ﷺ would later fold his entire beginning into a single sentence. Asked to tell something of himself, he said that he was the du'a of his father Ibrahim, the glad tidings of his brother Isa, and that he had been nursed among Banu Sa'd ibn Bakr. The du'a is the prayer Ibrahim made as he raised the very first house of worship, setting the stones of the Ka'bah in place and asking his Lord for a messenger out of his own descendants:

Our Lord, and send among them a messenger from themselves who will recite to them Your verses and teach them the Book and wisdom and purify them. Indeed, You are the Exalted in Might, the Wise.Qur'an 2:129

That prayer, made over the foundations of the Ka'bah, was answered with this child. And the glad tidings were the announcement of Isa, who foretold a messenger after him. Some Muslim theologians, the Sheikh notes, find it a plausible reading that the word gospel, which in its own language means simply good news, was always pointing here: the good news Isa kept preaching was him ﷺ.

The orphan nobody wanted

Halima of Banu Sa'd tells her own story, in the first person, preserved in the books of hadith and of sira. She and her husband were crushed by poverty, and that, plainly, is why the desert women made their yearly journey into Makkah: a nursling from a wealthy family meant money, and you had to be a new mother yourself, with milk already flowing, to take one on. Halima came that year with a newborn son of her own, whose name the Sheikh went looking for in the earliest books and could not find recorded, and with an older daughter of perhaps seven or eight, Shaima, who was about to become the foster sister of the best of creation ﷺ.

In Makkah one of the newest babies came with a warning attached: the orphan child. His father was already dead, and you do not take an orphan for free, because the whole point was the fee. His grandfather was the chieftain of Quraysh, yes, but a man with ten sons and a crowd of grandchildren was not going to pay handsomely for one fatherless boy. Some women never even knocked on Amina's door. Halima went, saw the poverty, and moved on like the rest. By the end of the week every one of her companions had a baby in her arms, and the only child left in all of Makkah was Muhammad ﷺ.

She told her husband she was ashamed to ride home empty-handed. And he answered with the sentence that turned a transaction into a destiny: take the orphan, perhaps Allah will bless us through him. They were both pagans, and yet good-hearted enough to know that sheltering an orphan is a noble thing. The narrations agree on what followed, and the Sheikh sees no difficulty in it: the blessings began at once. The old goat that had long gone dry came back full of milk that very evening, and the worn-out mount that had barely carried the two of them became the swiftest animal in the returning caravan. Allah blesses whomever He chooses, and this poor desert household was now carrying His Messenger ﷺ.

The custom was two years of nursing, and then the child went home. But Halima had seen what her house became with him ﷺ in it, and when the time came she could not give him back. She invented one excuse after another: he is still so young, the city is full of plagues, let him stay a little longer. Amina could never have matched the other families' fees, but by now it was not about the fee, and seeing how cherished her son was, she let him stay on.

The day Jibril opened his chest

Of everything in the childhood, this is the one event the lecture holds with complete certainty, and it is emphatic about why: the Qur'an points to it, Anas ibn Malik radiyallahu anhu narrates it in Sahih Muslim, and the Companions, decades later, could still see the mark it left on his body. This is no embellished legend; it is bedrock.

He ﷺ was around four, out playing with the children of Banu Sa'd, when Jibril came. The other children took one look and ran. The four-year-old stood his ground, and when the mightiest angel Allah created took hold of him, he struggled, fighting an angel he could not possibly overpower, because even at four he ﷺ would not be taken without resisting. Jibril pinned him gently to the ground, opened his chest, drew out his heart, and removed from it a black clot, saying: this is Shaytan's portion of you.

Pause on that clot, because it is about you as much as about him. The Prophet ﷺ taught that Shaytan pricks every child at the very instant of birth, which is why a newborn enters the world screaming, an enemy already lunging out of ancient envy of the children of Adam. He taught, too, that every person is assigned a qareen, a companion devil whose lifelong work is to whisper. The Companions once asked, even you, Messenger of Allah? Even me, he answered, except that Allah helped me against him, so that my companion only urges me toward good. For the rest of us the whisperer remains. But hear exactly what the whisper is, and is not, for the Qur'an preserves Shaytan's own confession to his followers when the matter is over:

And Satan will say when the matter has been concluded, "Indeed, Allah had promised you the promise of truth. And I promised you, but I betrayed you. But I had no authority over you except that I invited you, and you responded to me. So do not blame me; but blame yourselves. I cannot be called to your aid, nor can you be called to my aid. Indeed, I deny your association of me [with Allah] before. Indeed, for the wrongdoers is a painful punishment."Qur'an 14:22

He has no control panel. No one can say Shaytan forced me, because Shaytan forces nothing.
