The vile thought that ambushes you, the blasphemy that surfaces from nowhere, the sudden ugly desire, is not you and not your iman; it is a call, and a call can be refused. For the Prophet ﷺ, that wire was cut clean at four.

Then Jibril washed the heart in a vessel of gold filled with Zamzam, set it back, and sealed the chest with a pass of his hand. Dr. Yasir Qadhi observes, and means it quite literally, that the first open-heart surgery in history was performed by Jibril upon the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. Allah, who can heal without leaving the faintest trace, deliberately left a visible line, so that no one could ever explain the event away as a dream; Anas, looking at him ﷺ near sixty, said he could still see the traces of that line upon his chest. Meanwhile the other children had run shrieking to the tents: our brother has been killed, a man has taken him. The household rushed out in terror and found him sitting alone, his small face pale and shaken, and yet not wailing, not screaming, holding the fear in: the bravest heart the world would ever carry, keeping itself still at four years old.

It would happen once more, some forty-five years later, on the night he ﷺ was taken up through the heavens: the same opening, the same washing in Zamzam, and one difference, no black clot the second time. There was nothing left to remove; it had been taken when he was a child. The scholars of tafsir overwhelmingly read one ayah as a reference to this very cleansing:

Did We not expand for you, [O Muhammad], your breast?Qur'an 94:1

A second reading sits alongside it, that the opening of the breast means the opening to guidance and the light of Islam, and the two do not quarrel. By this washing he ﷺ was being prepared for the purest, most dignified life a human being has ever lived; the prophets are ma'sum, protected from every major sin, human enough to slip in small things, angry as Musa grew angry, hasty as Yunus was hasty, but guarded utterly where it matters. Halima, though, had now seen enough strangeness in her tent. Frightened for the child, she quietly carried him ﷺ back to his mother.

He never forgot the desert

Hold Banu Sa'd in your mind and leap forward fifty years, because the lecture leaps with you. After the battle of Hunayn, one of the last campaigns of his life, the captives included people of Banu Sa'd, and one woman insisted to the soldiers that she was the sister of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. They brought her forward, and he asked how he could possibly know. She answered: I still carry the marks of the bite you gave me, when I carried you on my back as a child. Only one person alive could have known that. It was Shaima. He ﷺ freed her and sent her home laden with gift after gift.

And some time later an old woman of the desert came to visit him, when he was in his late fifties with all of Arabia at his feet. He recognized Halima the instant he saw her. He rose to his feet, pulled off his own rida, the cloak from his back, spread it on the ground, and seated his foster mother upon it. The woman who had taken the baby nobody else would bid on received back, half a century later, the cloak off the shoulders of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. Of all men who ever lived, he was the least capable of forgetting a kindness.

A grave at al-Abwa

Of the years after Halima returned him to Amina's care, exactly one journey survives; the Sheikh combed the classical works for anything more and found nothing else he could stand behind. When he ﷺ was around six, his mother took him north to Yathrib to visit relatives, traveling with Umm Ayman, the servant girl whom, it is said, Abdul Muttalib had given to Abdullah at his wedding.

Notice the geography, because the Sheikh wants you to see the hand of Allah in it. The Prophet's ﷺ own great-grandmother had been a woman of Yathrib. Of all the towns on earth, the only one this small child ever traveled to, the single place where he had any family ties, was the very village that would one day be renamed Madinah and open its arms to him when his own city cast him out. Coincidence? Allah has a plan. Some books of sira even mention that fifty years later, arriving as an emigrant, he recognized places he had known as a boy.

They stayed some months. And on the road home, at a small settlement called al-Abwa, which stands to this day, Amina fell ill, and there she died. Umm Ayman buried her where she had fallen and carried the six-year-old the rest of the way to Makkah. Nearly everything we know of these months we know only because Umm Ayman lived to tell it: she accepted Islam, remained at his side ﷺ, and outlived him into the caliphate of Abu Bakr radiyallahu anhu. The Sheikh lets out the historian's quiet sigh: if only someone had written down all she witnessed, we would possess a whole chronicle of these silent years.

Now the scene that no one reads with dry eyes, recorded in Sahih Muslim. Decades on, traveling with his Companions, the Prophet ﷺ suddenly turned his mount off the road and walked into the wilderness. Not one of them asked a single question; whatever he did, they did. He came to a grave, sat down beside it, and wept until the tears ran down into his blessed beard and soaked it through. Most of them had never once seen him cry, and not knowing why, not daring to ask, the entire company wept with him out of nothing but love. Then he explained: I used to forbid you from visiting graves, but I asked my Lord for permission to visit my mother's grave, and He granted it. So visit the graves, for they remind you of death.

Hold both edges of that moment. He ﷺ would not visit even his own mother's grave without first seeking Allah's permission, and that is precisely what it means that he is the Messenger, a man who does not lift a finger except by his Lord's leave. And the permission your own feet rely on every time you walk into a graveyard entered the Sharia through Amina, through a grown son standing at al-Abwa, weeping for a mother he had lost when he was six.

The chieftain's platform

The orphan now passed into the care of Abdul Muttalib, by this time past eighty and blind, still the chieftain of all Quraysh. From these two years only a couple of glimpses survive, carried by the two most ancient sira sources we possess, Ibn Ishaq and Ibn Sa'd, and both say the very same thing about the old man's heart.

The first: a platform used to be spread for Abdul Muttalib in the shade of the Ka'bah, the seat from which he discussed the affairs of Quraysh every afternoon. It was the closest thing Makkah had to a throne, and not one of his many sons would dare to sit on it. The little boy would come running and climb straight up to sit beside his grandfather. His uncles reached to pull him back down, because no one sat there, and the blind old chieftain stopped them: leave him, this is my child, let him remain. Of all his grandchildren, the only one Abdul Muttalib seated beside himself on the throne of Quraysh was Muhammad ﷺ.

The second: the uncles once sent the boy, perhaps seven or eight, out alone into the desert to search for strayed camels. Why send a child? Ibn Sa'd preserves the reason: he never set his hand to a task except that it succeeded, and the desperate uncles knew it. But the boy was slow returning, and when Abdul Muttalib learned they had sent the child alone into the wilderness, he was furious, pacing until at last the boy appeared. Then he threw his arms around him and swore: from this day on, I will never let you out of my sight.

He could not keep that promise. When the Prophet ﷺ was eight, Abdul Muttalib died, and the orphan was orphaned a third time: the father before his birth, the mother at six, the grandfather at eight, three bereavements stacked on one small boy before the age of nine. A Companion once asked whether he remembered Abdul Muttalib, that legend of Arabia, and he answered: yes, and I was eight when he passed away (a report with a slight weakness in its chain, the Sheikh notes, but no harm in it, and the thing that fixes his age for us). On his deathbed, the old chieftain chose the next guardian with care. Not Abu Lahab, not the line of Hamza; those were only half-brothers of the boy's father. He entrusted him ﷺ to Abu Talib, the one full brother of Abdullah still living, who would shelter and guard the Prophet ﷺ for more than forty years, until the Prophet was past fifty, and this story will owe him many an evening yet to come.

Why Allah chose an orphan

Now the question the lecture refuses to walk past. If Allah had willed it, His Messenger ﷺ could have been born into luxury and kept both parents at least until he was grown. Why, instead, three griefs heaped one upon another onto a single small child? Why so hard a childhood for the most beloved of all creation?

The scholars' first answer is found in what Allah said to Musa: a helpless baby cast by his own mother into a chest and set adrift on a river, washed up at the door of the very tyrant slaughtering the children of his people, raised inside the palace of his enemy. And Allah told him, long after, what all of that drifting and danger had truly been:

[Saying], 'Cast him into the chest and cast it into the river, and the river will throw it onto the bank; there will take him an enemy to Me and an enemy to him.' And I bestowed upon you love from Me that you would be brought up under My eye [i.e., observation and care].Qur'an 20:39
And I produced you for Myself.Qur'an 20:41

If that is what Allah says of Musa, the Sheikh reasons, how much more of Muhammad ﷺ. The stripping away of father, mother, and grandfather meant that no parent, no patron, no powerful tribe could ever stand up and claim: I made him, he owes his greatness to me. Allah took the raising of His final Messenger as His own work, directly, under His own eye, a soul produced for Himself.

The orphanhood was also equipment for the road ahead. A pampered childhood does not forge a man for a life of sacrifice; it forges the opposite. The Sheikh points to a rule every parent has watched come true: the child raised in hardship matures years beyond his peers, carrying responsibilities at six that softer households would not hand a sixteen-year-old. And hardship lived young tends to breed mercy rather than selfishness; it is the rich, he says bluntly, who lean toward stinginess, not the poor. The persecution and crushing burdens that lay ahead needed a heart trained early by loss, and trained by that loss toward tenderness for everyone weak.

So when he ﷺ later said that he and the one who cares for an orphan would be together in Paradise like this, holding up two fingers side by side, and taught that you should pass your hand gently over an orphan's head, do you not think he was remembering a particular boy in Makkah? The Sheikh turns it directly onto you: find an orphan, and sponsor that orphan. Thirty, forty, fifty dollars in a month, less than many of us pour into coffee, and you may stand beside the Prophet ﷺ in the Garden. Our religion set the care of the orphan near its very center, and the Messenger who set it there had himself been the orphan no one wanted to take.

And the desert paid one last dividend. Raised on the unmixed, ancient tongue of Banu Sa'd, he ﷺ grew into the most eloquent of all the Arabs, and he said of himself that he had been given jawami al-kalim, speech gathered tight, a few words that can be unfolded for hours. The eloquence that would one day carry the revelation of Allah to the world was planted in a tent in the desert, by the deliberate design of the One who was raising him.

The monk story, weighed and set aside

One famous tale remains from the childhood, and the chapter ends by laying it honestly on the scale, because how we handle it teaches something larger than the story itself. As it is told, the boy, eleven or twelve, traveled with Abu Talib in a caravan toward Syria, and along the route a monk named Bahira, who had never given a passing caravan the time of day, came out, laid a feast, and studied the boy: I saw the clouds shading him, I saw the trees leaning to shelter him; this one will be a prophet, so guard him well. In the version recorded by Tirmidhi, seven Roman soldiers then appear, hunting the foretold prophet to kill him; Bahira turns them away, and the boy is hurried back to Makkah, escorted, the narration claims, by Abu Bakr and Bilal.

Most scholars of our tradition passed the story along at face value. But the most critically minded stopped cold, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi, whose own specialty is the science of hadith, walks through their objections with relish. Adh-Dhahabi, with Ibn Kathir and Ibn Sayyid an-Nas beside him, asked: how can this be? Abu Bakr was himself only a child at the time, and Bilal not yet born, not acquired by Abu Bakr until long after Islam came. And the sharpest question of all: why, when he ﷺ stood up thirty years later and all of Makkah called him mad, did no one, not Abu Talib, not the caravan, not the man himself, ever mention that a monk had announced his prophethood to the world? Why does the man who came down trembling from the cave of Hira, running to Khadijah, needing Waraqah to explain that this was the angel who had come to Musa, look nothing like a man told since boyhood to expect exactly this? Adh-Dhahabi's verdict was blunt: I feel this story is fabricated. The Sheikh sides with the critics: tell the story only to make clear that it does not stand.

And the stakes, he insists, are not small. Non-Muslim writers have seized on Bahira as the missing link they were hunting for, the one afternoon in which an unlettered boy, from a city with no Jews, no Christians, no scriptures, and no libraries, supposedly absorbed the entire sacred history of the Children of Israel he would later recite as Qur'an. Even at face value the theory collapses: an eleven-year-old, in one half-hour feast, memorizing an encyclopedia of prophetic history and reproducing it flawlessly forty years on. But the Qur'an had already answered the charge directly, long before any orientalist raised it:

And you did not recite before it any scripture, nor did you inscribe one with your right hand. Then [i.e., otherwise] the falsifiers would have had [cause for] doubt.Qur'an 29:48

And of the histories he brought, histories his people had never known:

That is from the news of the unseen which We reveal to you, [O Muhammad]. You knew it not, neither you nor your people, before this. So be patient; indeed, the [best] outcome is for the righteous.Qur'an 11:49

The closing lesson reaches well past Bahira. Throughout the history of this ummah people fabricated reports, many of them, the Sheikh stresses, with sincere intentions, like the man who invented a separate virtue for every single surah of the Qur'an, hoping to draw people back to reciting it, and who confessed exactly that when caught. This is the very reason the science of hadith exists, and the reason the seerah itself must be told from what is authentic. The raised finger at his birth, the claim that his body cast no shadow, the legends piled on legends by people who only wished to exaggerate his greatness, none of it honors him ﷺ. It does the opposite: it hands his enemies their ammunition and makes onlookers ask what kind of religion traffics in fables. He is the greatest human being who ever lived, and, as the Sheikh puts it with finality, what we authentically know of him is more than enough.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read these first eight years as a string of misfortunes to be pitied and left behind. That would be to miss the question they put to us. Look at the whole of it in one frame. The baby nobody would bid on. The four-year-old pinned to the desert ground by an angel and washed with Zamzam. The six-year-old walking home behind his mother's fresh grave. The eight-year-old at the deathbed of the only throne in Makkah that had ever made room for him. The world looks at that and says: a childhood of catastrophe. The Qur'an teaches you to say what Allah said over the cradle of Musa: brought up under My eye, produced for Myself. Every door this world could close on a child was closed on him ﷺ, so that it would be unmistakable, forever, that Allah alone raised him. When the next hard season closes a door in your own life, his childhood asks whether you can hold open the possibility that the closing is itself a raising.

And the boy raised by so much loss did not grow hard; he grew tender. That is the second thing this day asks of you. He became the man who rose to his feet for an old desert woman and spread his own cloak in the dust for her, who freed his foster sister at the mere memory of a childhood bite, who promised Paradise side by side with himself to anyone who shelters an orphan. Loss made him merciful instead of bitter, openhanded instead of guarded, and that was no accident; it was the training. So the question turns on your own wounds: are they hardening you, or are you letting them soften you toward everyone weak the way his softened him? The proof he asks for is not a feeling. It is an orphan in your keeping, sponsored and remembered, for less than the price of your coffee, so that you might stand where he promised you could stand.

There is a third thing, quieter, threaded through the chapter. He would not visit even his own mother's grave without first asking Allah's leave. We move on our own wants and ask afterward, if at all, whether Allah had opened the way; his life runs the other direction: seek the permission, then act. And the last thing the day asks is a kind of guarding. The whisper in your chest, the ugly thought, the sudden pull, is only ever a voice, never a command; Shaytan was given an invitation and no authority at all, and the heart washed clean at four shows what a heart can be when it is given over to Allah. Guard that heart with His remembrance, and guard his story too, on your own tongue, from every fable that would dress him in borrowed legend, for the truth of him is already more than enough.

So take one thing from these eight years into your ordinary days. When a door closes hard, hold open the thought that Allah may be raising you through the loss. Find one orphan and make his care a fixture of your life. Seek your Lord's leave before you move, and refuse the whisper as the empty call it is. That is how the most beloved of Allah ﷺ was raised, in loss turned to mercy, in dependence on his Lord alone, and it is a way still open to anyone who wants it. O Allah, send Your peace upon Your Messenger ﷺ, who knew loss before he knew letters; raise us and our children under Your care as You raised him, put orphans in our keeping and mercy in our spending, and gather us with him on the Day every mother and every child is gathered.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 7: the early childhood of the Prophet ﷺ (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (2:129, 94:1, 14:22, 20:39, 20:41, 29:48, 11:49), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 08 of 104

The Early Manhood of the Prophet ﷺ

The quiet years that built the man Makkah would trust

Of the forty years the Prophet ﷺ lived before the first revelation, history kept almost nothing. There was no writing in Makkah and no records, and no one suspected that the orphan boy in front of them was someone who would one day need remembering. By the time the world wanted to know how he had spent his youth, the people who had watched him grow were mostly gone. Dr. Yasir Qadhi opens this chapter with a question that lands closer to home than any date: how much do you really know of your own father at eight, at twelve, at nineteen? You knew him, and still, of his childhood, all you hold is one story here and one story there. That is exactly what we hold of the Prophet's ﷺ early manhood, fourteen centuries on. A handful of episodes survived, and the striking thing, when you sit with them, is that they are precisely the ones a believer needs.

So this is not a chapter of dates. It is a chapter of formation: a monk on the road to Syria and the honest questions the scholars learned to ask about him, a first job paid in the smallest coins the city had, a war he ﷺ helped in and never once regretted, and a pact sworn in an old man's house that he ﷺ said, decades later as a Prophet, he would not have traded for a herd of red camels. Watch these years closely, because they teach you how Allah builds the men who carry His word: not by dropping greatness out of the sky, but by raising it, slowly, in the lowest and loneliest schools.

A monk on the road to Syria

He ﷺ was around nine or ten or eleven, nobody recorded which, when his uncle Abu Talib readied a caravan for the long haul north to Syria. The plan was to leave the boy behind. But this was a child who had already buried a mother and then a grandfather, who had learned what it was to be the one left, and he could not bear it. Who are you leaving me with, he asked his uncle. How can you leave me here alone? Abu Talib wept at the words, and took him along. The route ran the way Quraysh rode it every year, and on the northern road it passed the cell of a Christian monk, one of those hermits who walled themselves off from the world entirely, nothing day and night but worship.

Caravans had filed past that cell for years, and the monk had never once stirred for them. This time, the story goes, he came out and called them down. The books give his name as Bahira. He looked at the boy, and then he told Abu Talib what he claimed to see: a cloud that moved to keep the child in its shade, trees that lowered their branches toward him, the very signs of the awaited prophet of the Arabs foretold in the scriptures he kept. He asked after the boy's father. I am his father, said Abu Talib. That cannot be, the monk answered, this boy's father cannot be living. I am actually his uncle, Abu Talib admitted, and the monk nodded, because that, too, fit the signs. And he sent them off with a warning: guard this boy from the Romans, and in another telling the Jews, for if they recognize what he is, they will try to kill him.

Every Muslim child has heard some version of that story, repeated lovingly, and it is exactly here that this chapter becomes unforgettable, because here the Sheikh stops the caravan.

When a beautiful story meets the scholars

It is time, Dr. Yasir Qadhi announces, for a little critical thinking, a little analysis of the chains. And he does something a softer telling never would: he turns the beloved story over and examines its seams. One version puts Abu Bakr in that caravan at the Prophet's ﷺ side, but Abu Bakr was himself a boy of about ten in those years, with no recorded friendship between them that young. Another places Bilal there as a slave of the company, but Bilal had not yet even been born, let alone been bought and freed, which lay some thirty years down the road. The chain of the report has weaknesses, and so does its content.

He is not the first to frown at it. Classical scholars raised these objections centuries ago, Ibn Kathir among them, and adh-Dhahabi, whose questions still land with full force. Why would the trees need to bend down and shade the boy when the same report already has a cloud overhead doing exactly that? If a monk had announced this child's prophethood before a whole caravan, why did Quraysh find his message so strange decades later, and why did the Prophet ﷺ never once turn to his hesitating uncle and say: do you not remember what the monk told you on the Syria road? And the heaviest question of all: when the angel Jibril came to him in the cave of Hira, why did he ﷺ come down the mountain shaking with fear, fleeing to Khadijah, wondering what had seized him, if he had spent thirty years knowing an angel was coming? A man told at ten that he will be a prophet does not tremble at thirty when it arrives. adh-Dhahabi's verdict, which the Sheikh hands on without flinching: I think the story is fabricated.

Someone will ask, reasonably, why be so strict with a tale that only praises him ﷺ, where the clouds shade him and the trees bow. Because of what hangs on it. The reports of the seerah, the Sheikh reminds us, were never held to the iron standard demanded of matters of law. Imam Ahmad would clench his fist tight for what is halal and haram, and open his hand loose for the looser histories, and most nights the Sheikh lets the gentler details pass the same easy way. You stop, he says, only when a story creates a problem. And this one does. For more than a century, the people who deny his prophethood have leaned on this very monk to answer the question that corners them: where did Muhammad ﷺ get all of this? One famous orientalist of the last century wrote that Bahira fired up the boy's imagination with tales of Musa and Isa, which the young man then regurgitated, garbled, three decades later. The Qur'an answers him directly. The Arabs had not seen a prophet in the thousands of years since Ismail; the very idea of prophethood reached Makkah as a stranger. He ﷺ did not know the names in those stories, did not know the concept, until it was given to him from above:

And thus We have revealed to you an inspiration of Our command. You did not know what is the Book or [what is] faith, but We have made it a light by which We guide whom We will of Our servants. And indeed, [O Muhammad], you guide to a straight path.Qur'an 42:52

So love him with the truth. That is the lesson the Sheikh draws out of a story he is willing to set down: his greatness needs no props that cannot bear their own weight, and the scholars who questioned the tale were not doubting the Prophet ﷺ, they were guarding him.

A shepherd in the valley of Ajyad

From about ten to fifteen the record goes almost completely silent, and then his first job arrives on the most solid footing a report can have, a hadith in Bukhari. Allah never sent a prophet, he ﷺ told his companions, except that he tended sheep. They went looking for an exception in him: even you, O Messenger of Allah? Even me, he said. I used to tend the flocks of the people of Makkah for a few small coins. Notice the texture of that, the Sheikh says: his own companions did not know this about him until he told them himself. If the men closest to him did not know he had been a shepherd, imagine how much of those quiet years has reached no one at all.

He never carried it like a secret. Years later, on an expedition, he ﷺ passed some shepherds and stopped to advise them: take from the darker branches of the arak tree, it is better for your flock. They were astonished. How does the Messenger of Allah know which branch of which tree, in which season, is best for sheep? I was a shepherd, he answered. And in another narration he named the company he kept in that trade: Musa was sent as a prophet while he was a shepherd, Dawud was sent while he was a shepherd, and I tended sheep at Ajyad. Ajyad is the small valley directly behind the Haram. To this day the hospital nearest the Kaaba carries its name, and if you stand there, you are standing in the first workplace of the best of creation ﷺ.

Hold the pattern up to the light, because the Sheikh wants you to see it. Musa kept another man's flock in Madyan as the price of his marriage, and was called from the grazing grounds to face Pharaoh. Dawud was a shepherd boy nobody rated, until he stepped out alone against the giant Jalut with a sling, and the kingship and the prophethood found him in the field. Allah trains the men who will carry His word in the same low, lonely school. It is not an accident that befell them; it is a curriculum.

What sheep teach a future prophet

If Allah had willed it, the Sheikh observes, money would have been poured over His beloved ﷺ from his childhood, and he would never have had to lift a finger. Instead Allah chose for him the lowest-paid and most menial labor in all of Makkah. Why? Dr. Yasir Qadhi, who says plainly that he has never herded a flock but has sat with the shepherds of Arabia and asked them, walks through the wisdoms. Solitude, first: a shepherd spends his days far from people, out under the open sky with his animals and his thoughts, and that emptiness becomes room to think about why we are here at all. Hearts that live close to the creation of Allah stay soft, while hearts buried in the noise of this world slowly harden until they can ignore their Maker entirely. It is no coincidence, the Sheikh notes, that the denial of God grows loudest in the most opulent places, while the people closest to the soil and the sky tend to keep their faith.

Then there is the flock itself. To a stranger the sheep all look alike; but the shepherd knows every single one: this one stubborn, that one gentle, this one a leader, that one a follower, and he handles each according to its nature. That is leadership rendered small enough to learn, and it is exactly the eye a prophet must bring to people, every one of whom is carried differently. The work shapes a soul in two directions at once: tender, because these helpless creatures are yours to keep alive, and brave, because you stand alone between your flock and the wolf. He ﷺ would later say that pride rides with the owners of horses, and a coarse arrogance with the owners of camels, while sakina, a settled humility, lives with the people of sheep.

The tenderness never left him; decades on, a camel groaned and wept at the sight of him ﷺ, and he soothed it quiet, then told its owner to fear Allah in the beasts placed in his hand. And the dignity never left him either. A boy of fourteen or fifteen who would not freeload off his uncle grew into the Prophet ﷺ who taught that the purest wealth a person can hold is what his own hands have earned, and who pointed to Dawud, a king and a prophet, eating only from the labor of his own hands. He was never embarrassed by the bottom rung. Once he had reached the very top, he announced it.

And spread over the whole of these years, the early sorrow and the slow climb out of it, Surah ad-Duha reads almost like a biography. Your Lord has not taken leave of you, nor has He detested you, and the Hereafter is better for you than this first life, and your Lord is going to give you, and you will be satisfied. The Sheikh unfolds the promise simply: every coming stage of his ﷺ life would be better than the one before. Then the surah names the very shape of his childhood:

Did He not find you an orphan and give [you] refuge? And He found you lost and guided [you], And He found you poor and made [you] self-sufficient.Qur'an 93:6-8

And then the surah turns that hard beginning outward, into standing orders for everyone who would ever read it: so as for the orphan, do not oppress him; and as for the one who asks, do not turn him away; but as for the favor of your Lord, speak of it. The harsh start was not cruelty. It was the making of a soft heart, so that the man who had been the orphan and the pauper would know, in his bones, how to treat them.

The war they named for sin

He ﷺ was a teenager, most likely around fifteen, when war broke out between two great confederations of Arab tribes: Kinanah, the larger branch to which Quraysh belonged, and Qays Aylan, the branch that held Hawazin and Ghatafan, names that will return in this story years from now. A man of Kinanah killed a man of Hawazin, and the days of ignorance ran on a brutal arithmetic: you kill one of mine, I kill one of yours. Hawazin came for their vengeance, and the Kinanah tribes fell back into the Haram of Makkah, because the ancient law of that sanctuary, the law our religion still keeps, is that whoever enters it is safe.

But Hawazin were too enraged to honor it. They attacked inside the sacred precinct, and that single act tilted everything. The first wrong had been on Kinanah's side, the Sheikh is careful to say; both had blood on them. But killing a man outside the Haram is one kind of crime, and violating the sanctuary itself a far graver one. Now Quraysh declared all-out war. The fighting went against them, then turned in their favor, and a treaty finally closed it. The Arabs themselves named these years with unsparing honesty: the wars of al-Fijar, from fujur, sin, because both sides had done evil and, above all, because the sanctity of the House of Allah had been broken.

And he ﷺ was there. In an authentic report he remembered it himself, long afterward: I would gather the arrows and hand them back to my uncles. An arrow was ammunition you could use twice, and the boy, still too young to be given a sword, kept his family supplied. It is also narrated that on the days he was present, Quraysh would gain ground, and on the days he was absent they would lose it, until Abu Talib swore the boy would not leave his sight again. And here is how he ﷺ weighed it as a Prophet: I do not regret it. A war with no religion in it at all, idol worshippers on both sides, and still he had helped his own people on the side that stood closer to right, and he refused, ever, to disown having done so. There is a whole world of guidance in that, the Sheikh notes, for a believer living among people who are not believers, watching conflicts that are not about faith, and wondering whether justice is still his to defend.

One poem against all of Quraysh

A few years pass, and he ﷺ is in his early twenties now, one report says exactly twenty, when the second of these surviving episodes unfolds. It was one of the sacred months, the season when Makkah filled with pilgrims and merchants. A trader arrived from Zubayd, a tribe of distant Yemen, which in the cold ledger of Makkah meant two things at once: low standing, and no kinsmen near enough to make a powerful man afraid. He sold his goods to al-As ibn Wa'il, a chieftain of Quraysh, a career statesman and a wealthy one, the father of the Amr ibn al-As whose name will come later. Pay you after the Hajj, said al-As. Then, when the man returned: come back tomorrow. Then tomorrow again, until the stranger understood, with a sinking heart, that he was being robbed, slowly and politely, by a man no one in this whole city would dare to cross.

So he went from door to door through the clans of Quraysh, Banu Hashim among them, and every door gave him an excuse, because that is the other arithmetic of the days of ignorance: justice reached exactly as far as your tribe's swords could carry it, and this man's swords were a month's journey away in Yemen. Trapped, he reached for the one weapon left to a man with a voice. Poetry, the Sheikh smiles, was the news media of that world, its front page and its viral post at once, except that nobody was counting characters, and a sharp enough verse outran any horse.

At the hour when all of Makkah was gathered before the Kaaba, the wronged man stood up and sang his case to the city. O family of Fihr, he called them, naming Quraysh by their oldest and proudest name. A man has been wronged, he cried, here in the valley of Makkah, far from his home and any who would help him, still in the white cloth of his pilgrimage, his hair uncombed and his Umrah unfinished, robbed between the Hijr and the Black Stone. Sanctity, he flung at them, belongs to people of honor; there is no sanctity for a cheat in a clean pilgrim's robe. He was calling the holy city's bluff to its face, and the verses spread like fire through dry grass.

Perfume on the Kaaba

One man heard it and decided the shame of it was unbearable: az-Zubayr ibn Abdul Muttalib, an uncle of the Prophet ﷺ himself. He convened the senior houses of Quraysh in the home of a man whose name, the Sheikh tells you, is worth memorizing, because it recurs again and again in the seerah: Abdullah ibn Jud'an, the most honored elder of Makkah in his day, legendary for his hospitality, the one the city brought its problems to. He would die before the call to Islam ever began, but in his house, that day, the chiefs swore an oath the Arabs had never heard the like of before. We will stand, they pledged, with the oppressed against the oppressor, whoever the two of them turn out to be, until the wronged man is given back his right, even if the wrongdoer is one of our own and the wronged a stranger from the farthest corner of Yemen.

There were no signatures, because there was no paper and almost no one who could read or write. So they sealed it the way Makkah sealed things that mattered: they walked to the Kaaba, proclaimed the pact aloud before everyone, dipped their hands in perfume and pressed them to the wall of the House. From that it took one of its names, hilf al-mutayyabin, the pact of the perfumed. Its other name carries the better story. When al-As ibn Wa'il, the very cheat who had started it all, heard what they had sworn, he snapped that they had meddled in fudul, in matters none of their business. The insult stuck, and was worn instead as a badge of honor: hilf al-fudul, the pact of the people who made justice their business. Its first piece of business was the stranger from Zubayd, who got his money.

Now look carefully around that room. Among the gray heads and the seasoned chieftains stands the youngest participant of them all, a man of about twenty, dipping his hand in the perfume beside the elders of his city: Muhammad ﷺ, invited into that gathering before a single word of revelation had come to him, because they could already sense the leader he was going to be. And here is the line that should be carried out of that house. Decades later, a Prophet now with the whole world rearranging itself around him, he ﷺ still spoke of that day: I witnessed in the house of Abdullah ibn Jud'an a pact I would not trade for a herd of red camels, the most coveted wealth an Arab could name, and were I called to it in Islam, I would answer.

That, the Sheikh suggests, is the quiet headline of all these years. The age of ignorance was dark, but it was never hollow. The Arabs still carried real veins of nobility within them, and on their best day they could gather, idol worshippers all, against an injustice that touched none of their own tribes, and seal their stand for a stranger with perfume on their hands. It was into exactly that buried, waiting goodness that Allah chose to send His final Messenger ﷺ, to revive the virtues already sleeping in his people. Forty years he ﷺ lived before revelation, and these few episodes are essentially all that history kept of them, and that, the Sheikh reads as a mercy: Allah preserved precisely what we need, and let the rest fall quietly away.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read these years as a charming prelude, a few sweet anecdotes on the way to the real story. That would be to miss what they ask of us. Strip them down, and each one puts a question to your own iman.

Love him ﷺ with the truth. The scholars who took the beautiful story of the monk and set it aside were not doubting him; they were protecting him, because his proof never rested on a stranger's tale, and a faith propped up by something fragile is weaker, not stronger, for the prop. Be as careful with what you pass on about him as they were with what they were willing to narrate. To honor him with a story that cannot stand is not devotion; to honor him with the truth, even when the truth is plainer, is.

There is no dishonor in the bottom rung, and no shortcut around it. The best of creation ﷺ tended other people's sheep for a handful of the smallest coins Makkah had, and then announced it from the summit of prophethood without a trace of shame. The purest wealth, he taught, is what your own hands earn. We are forever hunting the inheritance, the fluke, the success that skips the climb, when the order of his life was the reverse: start low, work honestly, and let Allah raise the stages one by one. And the hidden wage of those years was time alone under the open sky that kept his heart soft and awake. Our age is engineered against exactly that; the noise never stops, and a heart buried in it hardens by degrees, without noticing the moment it stopped feeling. Find the valley, whatever yours is, where your soul can hear itself. And over whatever Allah has placed in your care, keep the shepherd's eye: every soul has its own nature, and he who tends them is tender toward the flock and brave before the wolf.

And make justice your business. He ﷺ joined a pact sworn by idol worshippers for the sake of one wronged stranger who shared none of their blood, and said as a Prophet that he would rise to its call again. Oppression is oppression, the Sheikh insists, wherever it lands; it does not become someone else's problem because the one suffering it is not one of yours. A believer who stands publicly with the wronged, no sermon attached, is offering dawah of the very highest order, the kind that makes the faith itself look as sincere as it is. So take one thing from these silent years down into your own. Bless the work of your hands. Keep a little solitude where your heart can stay soft. And pick one fight against an injustice that has nothing to do with you, and show up for it. The years no one bothered to record were not empty; they were a school, and none of your own quiet years are empty either, if you spend them the way he ﷺ spent his: working honestly, thinking deeply, and standing where justice stands. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, the shepherd of Ajyad and the youngest hand on the pact of honor, soften our hearts in solitude, bless what our hands earn, and write us among those who stand with the wronged until He is pleased with us.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 8: the early manhood of the Prophet ﷺ (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (42:52, 93:6-8), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 09 of 104

Marriage to Khadijah and the rebuilding of the Ka'bah

The honest shepherd, the woman who chose him, and the stone no one could lift alone

These are the last two stories the seerah has before everything changes. Dr. Yasir Qadhi reminds us how thin the ground is: for forty years of his life ﷺ before prophethood we have perhaps a dozen incidents and no more, and after these two the next time we meet him a voice will be filling the cave of Hira and the world will divide into before and after. Both of these scenes are about a single thing, and it is the same thing in each: a man so honest, so gentle, so plainly trusted by everyone around him, that a wealthy woman would hand him her entire caravan and a city on the edge of war would hand him its holiest decision, and not one verse had yet been revealed to him.

Watch that thread, because the Sheikh draws it through the whole evening. Before there was a prophet, there was already al-Amin. The trust came first.

The shy shepherd and the wages

He ﷺ was a shepherd, probably twenty-three or twenty-four years old, and shepherding in Makkah was the work of the poor: a man with no flock of his own would take other people's animals out to graze the hills for a meager wage. It so happened that Khadijah's older sister, whom some reports name as Hala, owned a herd, and she hired two young men to take her animals out beyond the city, the Prophet ﷺ and another whose name we do not know.

When the grazing was finished and the wage was due, they had to go back into town to collect it, because in those days you were paid the moment the work was done. His companion said, come, let us go and ask Khadijah's sister for our wages. And he ﷺ answered with the line that opens this whole story: you go on my behalf and collect for both of us, because I am too shy to go to her, since she is a woman. So the young man went alone.

Khadijah happened to be in her sister's house when he arrived. He asked for his wage, and she said, where is Muhammad? He has earned half of this. He was too shy to come and ask you himself, the man explained. And at that, Khadijah's sister began to praise him, and could not seem to stop. She had never met a man more shy, she said, more honorable, more noble and modest in the way he dealt with her; she had watched him lower his gaze and carry himself with a quiet dignity she had never seen in a worker at so humble a job.

This, the narrator notes, was the first time Khadijah heard his name spoken to her in that way, and as with any human heart, when a man is praised so highly, something stirred. It was honesty that opened the door, before anything else. Not his wealth, for he had none, nor his standing, for he was an orphan working for hire. Only the way a poor shepherd conducted himself when no one important was watching.

A caravan placed in trustworthy hands

Khadijah was the richest woman in Makkah, and it is worth understanding how, because it tells you why she was so often cheated. In the days of jahiliyyah women did not, as a rule, inherit. But Khadijah's second husband was a wealthy merchant who died leaving no surviving siblings and no children with her, so the fortune, in a rare turn, came to her. She then did with it what a shrewd merchant does: invested it in the two great trade routes, sending goods to Syria and Yemen in the season of Hajj, buying there and selling back in Makkah on the cycle of the caravans.

But because she was a woman she could not travel with her own goods, so every journey she had to hire a man, and paid him not a flat wage but a share of the profit. This arrangement, mudaraba, where one party provides the capital and the other the labor and they split the gain by an agreed percentage, is a transaction the Sheikh notes the Sharia has fully endorsed. The trouble was the men. Time after time the merchants she hired, with no real stake in her interest, would lie, steal, and shortchange her of what she had earned. She kept her dignity and a good portion of her wealth, but never the share she actually deserved.

So when she heard her sister praising this honest young man, she made an unusual decision. She would entrust her caravan to him, though he had never made a single business journey in his life. He was inexperienced and young, when caravans were normally led by seasoned men in their forties and fifties who knew every route and market. She set all of that aside for one reason: his honesty. And there is wisdom in her reading of him, the Sheikh observes, for it is human nature that a man so decent and restrained in how he treats a woman is likely decent in the rest of his character too.

The offer was generous, half the profit for him and half for her, and here comes a detail the Sheikh lingers on. He did not seize it. He went to his uncle Abu Talib and said, my uncle, Khadijah has sent me such and such an offer, what do you think? He did not need permission; he was a grown, independent man. But he asked anyway, out of respect, and Abu Talib told him this was a blessing from Allah, far better than herding sheep, and not to refuse it. So he accepted.

He ﷺ took the caravan north to Busra, a market town on the very edge of the Roman lands, perhaps a hundred kilometers short of Damascus, where the Arab traders typically stopped rather than push deeper into the empire. Khadijah sent a servant named Maysarah to travel alongside him and watch. And when they returned, Maysarah told her everything: the care he had shown on the road, the honesty in every dealing, and a profit many times what she had ever seen, what the early Arabic calls ad'af, multiplied over. Some of the older books even mention a cloud that shaded him on the journey. The Sheikh is careful here. If it happened, it happened, he says, but there is no authentic report of it, and we must be cautious with such signs before the prophethood, for had people clearly seen a miracle, that would itself have been a public sign of what was coming, and the seerah records none. What is not in doubt is that whatever this man touched, Allah blessed, just as blessing had poured into the household of Halima who nursed him as an infant. The caravan came home richer than Khadijah had ever known it, and her feelings, already stirred, deepened.

The woman who chose him

By now Khadijah's heart was clear, and the Sheikh pauses to say plainly what some of us feel too shy to say. There is nothing wrong in what she felt. She was a single woman, he ﷺ was an eligible young man, and to be drawn to him was no fault at all. As he tells his own students, falling in love is not haram; it is what a person does with that love that makes it one thing or the other. Khadijah did the thing that honors it. She pursued it through marriage.

And understand what kind of woman was choosing him. Khadijah had been married twice and then closed the door. A number of noble men had proposed, drawn by her pure Qurayshi lineage and, the Sheikh notes bluntly, by her wealth, since under jahili law a woman's fortune passed into her husband's hands the moment he married her. She turned every one of them away, believing none would treat her as she deserved. This is the woman who now wanted a poor orphan, and wanted him for the one thing none of those wealthy suitors had.

Every version of how the proposal came about, the Sheikh stresses, differs in its details but agrees on a single point: Khadijah was the one who set it in motion. A man proposes to a woman, then and in Islam, but there is nothing wrong, within proper limits, with a woman first making her interest known. In one account she sent an older woman named Nafisa to him, who asked, why do you not marry? And he ﷺ answered, and who would marry me? I am an orphan and a poor man of the Quraysh. So she said, what if it were Khadijah? He fell silent, and then said: but why would she want me? Notice, the Sheikh says, what he did not say. He never said he was not interested. He only wondered, with that same humility, why a woman like her would want a man like him.

About three months after his return, in the month of Safar, the marriage took place. The Sheikh deliberately raises a second version only to set it aside, and it is worth seeing how he handles it, because it is the discipline of the whole seerah. There is a report, on a weak chain, that Khadijah's father was alive, opposed the match, was made drunk and tricked into agreeing, and woke too late to undo it. The Sheikh will not let it stand. It is not a true story, he says, and it is contradicted by stronger facts: Khadijah's father had already died, which is precisely why she controlled her own wealth, for a woman with a living father or brother or husband would not have held the money herself. The reliable version, in more books than the one that carries the tale of the wine, is that her uncle Amr ibn Asad gave her in marriage. This is why we must sift these early histories so carefully, he explains: many were collected within two or three centuries as loose stories without chains, and a tale that happened to someone else, or to some pagan, could drift until a confused narrator pinned it to the Prophet ﷺ.

Abu Talib came with him ﷺ and delivered the sermon, preserved in the early books. He praised Allah, then spoke of the noble lineage of the Quraysh, the descendants of Ibrahim and Ismail, caretakers of the Ka'bah and people of Makkah. And of his nephew he said there was no young man in all of Makkah to compare with him in character, honor, or nobility. The mahr was modest and respectful, twelve and a fraction in pieces of silver, a sum the Sheikh ballparks at a few hundred dollars, not a fortune and not a token but the dignified amount that suited him. Khadijah's uncle accepted, saying this was a young man who could not be refused.

What Khadijah was to him

How old was Khadijah when they married? The famous figure is forty. But the Sheikh lays out the stronger case that she was younger, around twenty-eight, and it is worth following because it shows how the seerah is actually built. The age of forty comes from al-Waqidi, a narrator who ranks lower than those who give a different number. Against him stand Hisham al-Kalbi, who gives twenty-eight, and, more weightily, Ibn Ishaq, the foundational authority of the seerah, reported through al-Hakim, who also gives twenty-eight; al-Bayhaqi and the great Ibn Kathir indicate she died in her fifties, not at sixty-five. And then the plain human evidence the Sheikh finds clearest of all: she bore him at least six children. Six children from a woman in her late twenties is entirely natural, one every year and a half or two over a dozen years; the same from a woman married at forty strains belief. And the lesson we always draw remains untouched, he adds: he ﷺ married a woman older than himself, twice widowed, with a child of her own, and remained faithful to her alone for twenty-five years, not beginning to marry his other wives until he was past fifty, after she was gone. This was not a man ruled by desire.

And what a wife she was. She was the first to believe in him ﷺ when the revelation came, comforting him in the first terror of it and thinking to take him to Waraqa ibn Nawfal to understand what was happening. Hers was the only household Jibril would enter; of all his wives in the years to come, none would have that. Once, while the Prophet ﷺ sat with her, Jibril came and told him that Allah was sending Khadijah His own greeting of peace, with the glad tidings of a house in Jannah of hollowed pearl, in which there would be no noise and no toil. Look closely at her answer, the Sheikh says. She did not say "and upon Allah be peace," for Allah is As-Salam, peace itself and its source, and one does not return the greeting to its source. She said instead that Allah is peace, and sent peace back upon Jibril and upon her husband ﷺ. It is a small thing and an enormous one, the quick theology of a brilliant heart.

Years after she died, when Aisha radiyallahu anha was the beloved of his later years, Aisha said she was never more jealous of any woman than Khadijah, whom she had never met and who had died before Aisha was born, because she could see how deeply he ﷺ still loved her. He would slaughter a sheep and send portions of the meat, a luxury then, to Khadijah's old friends, ten years and more after she was gone. Once, irritated, Aisha asked how long he would keep mentioning this old toothless woman when Allah had given him someone younger and better. And the Sheikh has us feel the edge in his reply. No, by Allah, he said, Allah did not give me better than her. She believed in me when the people rejected me. She gave me of her wealth when they deprived me. And Allah blessed me with children only through her, none of my other wives. After that, Aisha said, she never mentioned Khadijah again. And there is the tenderest scene of all: when Khadijah's older sister, the same one who had first praised him, later came to Madinah and her footstep sounded outside the door, his face changed at once, because it was so like Khadijah's walk, and all the memory came back. When Khadijah passed, a companion said, they did not see him smile for months.

Al-Kawthar, and the enemy cut off

All of his children were from Khadijah, and there were at least six. His firstborn was al-Qasim, from whom he took his kunya, Abu al-Qasim; the boy lived long enough to reach the age when children ride a camel, six or seven or eight, before he died, and that is nearly all we know of him. Then came four daughters, Zaynab, Ruqayyah, Umm Kulthum, and Fatima the youngest. And then a second son, Abdullah, also called at-Tayyib and at-Tahir, the pure one, born after the revelation had begun, who died in infancy. (Some count at-Tayyib and at-Tahir as two more sons, the Sheikh notes, but the stronger view is that these were simply nicknames for Abdullah.)

When Abdullah died, and the Prophet ﷺ was left with no living son, his enemy Abu Jahl ran through the streets of Makkah cheering. Batara Muhammad, he cried, Muhammad is cut off, severed, with no male heir to carry his name. To the Arabs a man without sons was as good as erased. Imagine it, the Sheikh says, and sit in how cruel it is: your infant son has just died, and your worst enemy is running through your city rejoicing aloud at the death. And into that grief Allah sent down a whole surah, three short ayat that became more beloved to him ﷺ than this world and everything in it:

Indeed, We have granted you, [O Muḥammad], al-Kawthar. So pray to your Lord and offer sacrifice [to Him alone]. Indeed, your enemy is the one cut off.Qur'an 108:1-3

We have given you al-Kawthar, Allah told him, the abundance, the river of Jannah, the fountain of all good; do not grieve over the one thing taken when you have been given everything. So turn to your Lord and worship. And it is your mocking enemy, not you, who is truly the one cut off. The Sheikh reads the verdict of it straight off the centuries. Fourteen hundred years later, the only people on earth who even speak the names of Abu Jahl and Abu Lahab are the ones who despise them. The man they jeered at as abtar, severed, became the most remembered human being who ever lived, his name raised in every adhan and on every tongue, and they are the ones the world cut off. The promise of the surah came true to the letter.

Tested by loss, and shaped by it

The Sheikh steps back here over the whole span of his life ﷺ, because once you see the pattern you cannot unsee it. The most painful thing that can befall a child is to lose his parents, and the most painful thing that can befall a parent is to lose a child, and he tasted both, over and over. Orphaned of his father before birth, then his mother, then his grandfather, then in time the uncle who shielded him. And then he buried his own children, al-Qasim and Abdullah and three of his four daughters with his own hands, only Fatima outliving him, and even she by mere months. Much later he would have one more son, Ibrahim, born not of a wife but of Maria, and Ibrahim too died, at around eighteen months, which any parent will tell you is the sweetest age of all.

Why would the greatest of all creation be handed the heaviest of all griefs? Because, the Sheikh teaches, it is precisely through trial that the heart is forged, and the Prophet ﷺ needed that forging more than anyone. When life is comfortable the heart hardens; it is loss that throws a person's hands up to the sky, that teaches true servitude and the patience a prophet would need beyond all other men. And there is a mercy folded into the pain: a man who has known poverty feels for the poor, and a man who has been an orphan tends to the orphan. It is no accident, the Sheikh says, that he ﷺ left us so many words about caring for the orphan, even telling us that if we have nothing to give, we should at least pass a gentle hand over the orphan's head, for he knew from the inside exactly what that child carries.

And he named a wisdom in having no surviving son that goes deeper still. Of Ibrahim he said that had the boy lived he would have been a prophet, for it is not possible that a son of the Messenger ﷺ be anything less; but there is no prophet after me, and so the line of sons could not continue, because the prophethood had to be sealed. Look, the Sheikh adds, at what the ummah did even with grandsons through Fatima, the lengths some went to in exalting them. Had there been a direct son, people might well have made him into a god walking the earth. So even this loss, the cutting off the world mocked, was in truth a mercy and a protection.

The Ka'bah, rebuilt by a shipwreck

Now the years jump forward. He ﷺ was about thirty-five, and in those ten years of marriage the seerah has preserved for us not a single story; we trust, the Sheikh says, that Allah withheld no benefit we needed. Then comes the rebuilding of the Ka'bah, and it begins with two disasters. First a fire: a woman was cooking close by, for in those days the houses of Makkah stood only a few feet from the House, not the vast open courts of today, and an ember caught the cloth that had draped the Ka'bah since jahiliyyah, weakening the structure though it did not bring it down. Then, later in the year, a great flood. Makkah sits in a basin among mountains and floods every so often, something the Sheikh says he has waded through himself, water to his chest in the streets on one of his own pilgrimages. This flood tore off the roof and broke the walls, and between the fire and the water the Quraysh resolved that the House had to be torn down and rebuilt entire.

But they had no fine timber and no marble; they lived in a desert that produced neither. And here, the Sheikh marvels, is the planning of Allah laid bare. The emperor of Rome had sent the choicest materials and a skilled craftsman by ship to rebuild a church in Yemen that the Persians had destroyed in their long war, the Christians against the fire-worshippers. Allah sent a wind off the coast of Jeddah that wrecked the ship, yet did not sink it; it limped into the harbor, because Allah had a plan for its cargo. And there, suddenly for sale, was the finest marble and grandest wood the people of Makkah had ever laid eyes on, with a master builder trained in the palaces of Caesar beside it, needing now only the fare home. The Quraysh pooled their wealth, bought all of it, and carried back to Makkah materials and a craftsman more skilled than any in Arabia, the whole of it financed, without his knowing, by the most powerful man on earth. Wa makaru wa makarAllah: they planned, and Allah planned.

Even tearing down the old House terrified them, for this was the House of Allah and no hand had ever been raised against it. By one report of Ibn Ishaq, a great snake that had long lived in the well of Zamzam used to come out and hiss at any who approached, until Allah sent a bird to carry it off behind the mountains, and they took that as the sign to begin. Still no one dared lift the first axe. Finally al-Walid ibn al-Mughira stepped forward, the very nobleman of whom Allah would later reveal:

Leave Me with the one I created aloneQur'an 74:11

He took up the axe and, in words even Ibn Ishaq preserves, spoke to Allah in his pagan way as he swung: O Allah, do not be alarmed, do not be angry, we mean only to help and rebuild Your House. He brought down part of one wall, and not a single other man helped him that day. Then someone proposed using him as the test: if he lives through the night, we will join him; if something befalls him, then one fewer of us to worry over. He woke unharmed, and so the whole of the Quraysh fell to the work, dividing the walls among the tribes, the noblest branches taking the most prestigious sides.

The stone no one could lift alone

When the building rose back to the corner of the Black Stone, the work stopped dead. The stone belonged to no single wall but to the corner shared between them, and the Arabs had always known it for a sacred stone, so every tribe demanded the honor of setting it back. Banu Abd Manaf, the Prophet's ﷺ own noble branch, and Banu Makhzum, the rival house from which Abu Jahl came, dug in hardest, and the others refused to be passed over. For five days construction halted while the argument grew hot enough to draw blood. Banu Makhzum secretly bound themselves to other tribes in a pact to fight to the death over the stone, and to seal it, having no perfume to dip their hands in, they dipped them in the blood of a slaughtered animal and swore. What jahiliyyah, the Sheikh says: grown men ready to widow their wives and orphan their children over the honor of placing a stone, all for what later Arabs might say of them. A whole city stood on the edge of war over the corner of a wall.

Then the oldest man among them, Abu Umayya ibn al-Mughira, proposed the way out: there will be no bloodshed; let the matter go to whoever next walks in through the gate. To them this was the luck of the draw, for each tribe was privately certain that whoever entered would naturally favor his own people, since in their minds it was simply impossible that a man would choose anyone but his own. And the one who walked in through the gate was him ﷺ.

Here the Sheikh has us stop and feel the wonder of it, because it is the whole point of the evening. Every tribe rejoiced. Not only his own clan, but all of them, even Banu Makhzum, even the bitterest rivals, were glad it was him, because every single tribe was certain this was a man who loved them and would surely choose their side. That is the measure of how he was loved before he was ever a prophet: a man each rival house was sure was secretly theirs. He likens it to what Amr ibn al-As would say decades later, that the Prophet ﷺ was so warm to him that Amr was convinced he was the most beloved of all the companions, though Amr came to Islam late and was not in the first ranks; that was simply how he made every person feel.

But he chose no tribe. He called for a cloak, laid the Black Stone in its center, and told the chieftains of every clan to take hold of its edges and lift together. They raised it as one, every rival house with a hand on the cloth, and when it reached the level of the wall he ﷺ set the stone into place with his own hands. Banu Makhzum had sworn to fight to set the stone, and in lifting their corner of the cloak they kept their oath without spilling a drop; every tribe was honored, not one above another, and the war died in the cradle. The same thread the Sheikh drew through the marriage runs straight through this: decades before a word was revealed, this was already the man everyone trusted, already the one who could turn rival houses into a single pair of hands.

And the changes they made that day held. They had not enough of the costly material to build on the old foundation of Ibrahim, which was a rectangle, so they raised a smaller square and marked off the remainder, the Hijr, with a low wall, which is why a portion of the original House sits outside the Ka'bah to this day. They doubled its height, raised the door off the ground so the keepers among Banu Abd Manaf could control who entered, and fitted a water spout to carry off the rain that had ruined the roof. Years later, after the conquest of Makkah, the Prophet ﷺ would tell Aisha that were her people not so new to Islam, he would have torn the House down and rebuilt it on the foundation of Ibrahim with its door at ground level, but he feared unsettling new hearts, and so he left it. So it has stood, square, with the Hijr beside it and its door raised high, from that day to this.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read these two stories as charming preludes, a happy marriage and a clever solution to a quarrel, and move on toward the cave. That would be to miss what they ask. Set side by side, the wedding and the stone make one quiet argument about the man the revelation was about to find, and through him, about us.

Honesty opened every door, and it still does. A poor shepherd, too shy even to collect his own wage from a woman, was lifted into the most trusted hands in Makkah, not by wealth, which he did not have, not by experience, which he lacked entirely, but by how he carried himself when no one important was watching. Khadijah overlooked his every disadvantage for the single thing that outweighed them all. We spend so much effort polishing our credentials and so little tending the plain trustworthiness that, in the end, decides what people will place in our hands. Ask what you are when there is no audience, because that is what you actually are. And notice, beside it, how he asked his uncle's permission for a decision entirely his to make. He needed no one's leave, and he sought counsel anyway, because consulting those who love you is not weakness but the manner of the noble.

Then there is Khadijah, and the way he never once let her go. She believed in him first, gave to him first, comforted him first, and bore him every child but one, and decades after she was in the ground he was still sending meat to her friends and turning pale at the sound of her sister's footstep. No, by Allah, Allah did not give me better than her. There is a whole ethic of loyalty in that one sentence. The people who stand by us when we have nothing have a claim that does not expire when their usefulness does, or even when their lives do. Keep faith with those who kept faith with you. And let the loss in his life reshape how you read your own, for he was given the heaviest griefs precisely so that his heart would be softened, not hardened, by them. When comfort makes us forget Allah and hardship drives us back to Him, we have misread which one is the gift. The trial that breaks you open to your Lord, and to everyone else who is hurting, may be the most merciful thing that ever happens to you.

And last, the cloak. A city had drawn its swords and dipped its hands in blood over the honor of a single stone, and a thirty-five-year-old man with no rank to enforce and no army at his back ended the war not by choosing a winner but by giving every side a corner to hold and a stone to raise together. He could have taken the honor for his own clan, and every tribe braced for exactly that. Instead he made rivals into one pair of hands. Most quarrels in our own lives are smaller versions of that corner: a thing two proud parties both want to claim alone, that could be lifted, if someone humble enough walked in, by everyone at once. Be the one who reaches for the cloak.

So carry these two scenes down into an ordinary day. Be honest where no one can see it, and let that be enough. Keep faith with the people who kept faith with you, long after the world says you may stop. When loss comes, and it will, let it bend you toward Allah and toward the broken rather than toward bitterness. And when you find a thing too heavy for any one of you to lift alone, lay it on the cloak and lift it together. May Allah send His peace and blessings upon Muhammad ﷺ, whom the people trusted before a single verse was sent down, be pleased with Khadijah who stood with him when the world stood against him, make us honest in private and loyal in fidelity, and let us lift, together, the things too heavy to carry alone.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 9: marriage to Khadijah and the rebuilding of the Ka'bah (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (108:1-3, 74:11), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 10 of 104

Zayd ibn Harithah and the first revelation

Loved before he was followed

The quiet years are almost over. The Kaaba has been rebuilt on the foundations of Ibrahim, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi reads the sign out loud before moving on: the house of tawhid renewed on its old stones, five years before the very religion of Ibrahim is renewed, by the man whose arbitration had just saved Makkah from a war over a single black stone. The foundations stay the same; the building is made new. But before the cave, there is one last story from the years before revelation, and it may be the strongest character witness the whole seerah owns. It is not a miracle. It is a kidnapped boy, offered his freedom in front of his own weeping father, who refuses to go home.

So this is really two days folded into one. A man named Zayd chooses Muhammad ﷺ over his own blood, years before a single verse exists. And then, in his ﷺ fortieth year, in a crevice of rock above the city, the verses begin to fall.

The boy stolen at the fair of Ukaz

Zayd ibn Harithah was a child of Yemen, from the Qahtani branch of the Arabs, the other great limb of the family tree, the one the Prophet ﷺ and Quraysh do not come from. His mother, Su'da, once took him, seven or eight years old, to visit her own tribe. While they were there a small skirmish flared between her people and her husband's, the kind of love-hate quarrel those Yemeni tribes carried for years. And some of her own relatives, wanting to wound the father's tribe, did something cruel even by the standards of Jahiliyyah: because in their world a son belongs to the father and takes the father's lineage, they stole the boy from his mother to spite the man, carried him off, and sold him into slavery, just as the brothers once sold Yusuf. He was put up at Ukaz, the greatest fair of Arabia, the vast marketplace that opened after the Hajj season, and sold for a hefty price.

The buyer was Hakim ibn Hizam, the nephew of Khadijah, sent to the market with her money to find a servant for her household. So a stolen Yemeni boy landed, of all the doorsteps in all of Arabia, at the door of Khadijah radiyallahu anha, and when she married Muhammad ﷺ she gifted the young servant to her husband as part of what she brought to the marriage. All of this, the Sheikh is careful to stress, is years before there is any Islam at all. It is Allah quietly carrying a child across the peninsula, into the one household on earth where his small story would be folded into the largest story ever told.

His father, Harithah, meanwhile, was going out of his mind. He sent the boy's description through the tribes the way Yaqub once wept for Yusuf, crying out across the lands: a boy of our people, these are his features, if anyone sees him come and tell me. And one Hajj season it worked. Some pilgrims from his part of Yemen noticed a young Arab slave in Makkah whose features did not belong there, since most slaves came from Ethiopia or other lands and this one was an Arab of the south. They asked him a few careful questions and carried the news home: your son is alive, he is in the holy city, a servant to one of the grandsons of Abdul Muttalib, a man named Muhammad. The father gathered every coin he could touch, took his brother, and the moment they had enough, they rode.

More than a father and an uncle combined

They found him ﷺ in the Haram, near the Kaaba, and opened with every honor Jahili courtesy owned. O Muhammad, son of Abdul Muttalib, you are of the noblest lineage, a people of generosity and trust, and Allah has blessed you. They praised and praised him, though he was not yet a prophet, because they wanted something. Then the plea: our son was stolen and sold unjustly; name your ransom, and be gentle with us, for we can only afford so much. Notice what they could not do, and the Sheikh wants you to notice it. Jahiliyyah has no court and no judge to hear that a free child was kidnapped. There is no magistrate, no law, no order. Power belongs to the strongest, and a man's only appeal is to mercy.

His answer ﷺ was better than the law they did not have. He set the ransom aside entirely. The matter is his, he said. Let Zayd choose. If he chooses you, take him back, and I want no money for him at all. But if he chooses me, then I am not a man who turns his back on someone who has turned to me; if he has chosen to stay with me, I cannot send him away. The father and uncle nearly sang with relief. O Muhammad, they said, you have done marvelous, you have done far more than we dared hope.

Zayd was called. He was a grown man now, about twenty five, and more than ten of those years had been spent at this master's side, and before that with Khadijah. Do you know these two men? Yes, he said: this is my father, and this is my uncle, I know them. The choice was laid before him plainly, go with them or stay, and Zayd gave the answer that still stops this ummah fourteen centuries later: I could never choose anyone over you. You are to me more than a father and an uncle combined.

His father erupted. Harithah cried out: Zayd, have you gone mad? You would choose to stay a slave in a strange land, with no rights and no honor, with a man who is not even of your blood, over your own father, your own tribe, your own freedom? And Zayd answered without flinching: yes, I have made my choice, for I have seen from this man what I could never choose anyone over. Here Sheikh Yasir stops the story and makes you sit inside it. A son's bond to his father is fitrah itself; there are men who first meet their fathers at forty and feel the love seize them on sight. Zayd was not a baby when he was taken. He remembered his father, and his memories were good ones. Wallahi, the Sheikh says, and he says it knowing it sounds hard, no man on earth chooses a master over a remembered father, unless the one he is choosing carries what only prophets carry. No verse had come down. There was no religion yet to win him, no paradise promised, no command from heaven. There was only forty years of character, and this single scene, the Sheikh says, tells you more about the manners of Muhammad ﷺ than whole chapters of praise ever could.

Zayd ibn Muhammad

What he ﷺ did next, no one had asked of him. He took Zayd by the hand, walked to the Hijr, the open arc of the Kaaba where the announcements of Makkah were made, and called out to the city: O people of Makkah, bear witness that Zayd is from this day a free man, and that I have adopted him as my son; he will inherit from me, and I from him. He staged it deliberately, in front of the father and the uncle, so that they would not ride home feeling they had lost everything, but rather at ease: their boy was no longer a slave, he was now adopted into the noblest house of Quraysh, given its name and its standing if not its blood. A father who had come for a slave rode home having gained a son of Banu Hashim.

From that day the city knew him by one name only. Abdullah ibn Umar, who was himself only a young man in early Makkah and had never known Zayd as a slave boy, said: we never knew him as anything but Zayd ibn Muhammad. That was simply who he was to us. It stayed that way for decades, until Allah revealed an ayah of Surah al-Ahzab, some thirty years later in Madinah, returning adopted sons to the names of their true fathers as the more just way before Allah.

Call them by [the names of] their fathers; it is more just in the sight of Allāh. But if you do not know their fathers - then they are [still] your brothers in religion and those entrusted to you. And there is no blame upon you for that in which you have erred but [only for] what your hearts intended. And ever is Allāh Forgiving and Merciful.Qur'an 33:5

So he became Zayd ibn Harithah again: a name corrected, and nothing else lost. The love did not move an inch. And when revelation finally came, the man who had chosen him ﷺ before prophethood did not hesitate after it. Ibn Shihab az-Zuhri, one of the great scholars of the first generations, held that Zayd was the very first person to accept Islam. Others name Khadijah, Abu Bakr, or Ali, and the scholars reconcile the reports gently: the first woman to believe was Khadijah, the first free man Abu Bakr, the first child Ali, and the first freed slave Zayd. Order the list however you like; Zayd is always on it.

The only name in the Qur'an

Pull on the thread of Zayd's life and the love simply never runs out. Back in the days of Jahiliyyah he had married Umm Ayman, the woman who had served the Prophet's ﷺ mother Amina and, when Amina died, had stayed on to mother the orphaned boy herself, a foster mother who never suckled him but raised him. She was many years older than Zayd, and both of them were slaves when they married, as slaves could. Their son was born inside the Prophet's ﷺ own home, for there were no hospitals then, and that child was Usama ibn Zayd, who grew up the darling of the household.
