The companions called him the beloved of the Prophet, and when they wanted something they would send Usama to ask, because they knew he ﷺ could never say no to him; he loved Usama, and he loved his father. Later the Prophet ﷺ matched Zayd to his own cousin, the noblewoman Zaynab bint Jahsh, precisely to dissolve the last social residue of slavery, so that no stigma of having once been a slave could cling to him. That marriage strained and ended, and around its ending hangs a verse and a story the series will face squarely when it reaches the fifth year after the hijrah, refusing every distortion that has been laid upon it. What matters today is what came after: Zayd married again among the women of Quraysh, more than one, and no one any longer thought of him as anything but one of them.

Count what Allah kept giving him. On every expedition Zayd ever rode, and there were at least ten, he was placed in command; not once in his life was anyone set above him. At Mu'tah, against the Romans, the Prophet ﷺ did something unprecedented, because he knew how hard the battle would be: he named three commanders in sequence, Zayd first, then his cousin Ja'far ibn Abi Talib, then Abdullah ibn Rawaha, and all three fell that day, one of the most painful days of the Prophet's ﷺ entire life. The leaderless army rallied behind Khalid ibn al-Walid, a Muslim of barely a month, an infantryman who took the banner and brought it home, and the Prophet ﷺ gave him the name the world would remember: Sayfullah, the sword of Allah. And the love outlived Zayd. The Prophet ﷺ set young Usama, barely sixteen, over the next army marching north, and passed from this world as that army was setting out. When some pressed Abu Bakr radiyallahu anhu to replace the boy with an older man, he refused to undo what the Prophet ﷺ had tied: how could he remove someone the Messenger of Allah ﷺ himself had appointed? And once, when Abdullah ibn Umar complained to his father that Usama drew a higher stipend than he did, Umar the khalifa answered his own son with fearless honesty: he was more beloved to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ than you, and his father was more beloved to him than your father.

And above all of it sits one honor no other companion shares. Allah names Zayd in the Qur'an itself, in Surah al-Ahzab, the only one of the thousands of companions whose name is written there. Even Abu Bakr is present only by inference, the second of two in the cave:

If you do not aid him - Allāh has already aided him when those who disbelieved had driven him out as one of two, when they were in the cave and he said to his companion, "Do not grieve; indeed Allāh is with us." And Allāh sent down His tranquility upon him and supported him with soldiers you did not see and made the word of those who disbelieved the lowest, while the word of Allāh - that is the highest. And Allāh is Exalted in Might and Wise.Qur'an 9:40

Abu Bakr is unmistakably there, and yet unnamed. Zayd is written by name. Some of the companions said that had Zayd lived to see the Prophet ﷺ pass away, he would have been the khalifa, so beloved was he. Allah chose otherwise, and gave him something rarer still: a kidnapped slave boy whose name this ummah will recite from its Book until the Day of Judgment.

The quiet making of a prophet

With Zayd's story told, the file on the quiet years closes. These are nearly all the incidents we have from before revelation, and every sign is now in place: a rebuilt Kaaba, a city united around one trusted man, old seekers among the Hunafa whispering that a prophet is due, the house saved in the year of Abraha. What happens next is narrated in the hadith that opens the very first chapter of Sahih al-Bukhari, the chapter on the beginning of revelation, and the narrator is Aisha radiyallahu anha, who was not yet born when any of it took place. Savor that detail, the Sheikh says, because it tells you something tender about the marriage: she can recount it as vividly as an eyewitness because her husband ﷺ told her, in long unhurried conversations, everything those days had held. A loving husband talks to his wife about his early life, and so the woman who never saw the cave can describe what happened in it.

In his fortieth year he ﷺ began to withdraw to the cave of Hira, high on the mountain that Makkah now calls Jabal al-Nour, the mountain of light, a name it did not carry in Jahiliyyah and earned only from what came down upon it. Dr. Yasir Qadhi has climbed up and sat inside it himself, and his description undoes the picture in your head. It is barely a cave at all, more a crevice, a working in the rock you have to crawl into, with an opening front and back and room for exactly one person. And when you sit in it, you are forced to face one direction only: the Kaaba. You can see the House clearly from the opening, far below, the summit breeze moving past, as if Allah had carved the little room for this one appointment. Ibn Ishaq records that Abdul Muttalib had found the spot a generation earlier and would sit there to contemplate in private. The grandfather discovered the room; the grandson kept the appointment.

He ﷺ would carry up food and water and spend nights in remembrance, then come down to Khadijah to resupply and see to the family before climbing again. And as he climbed, strange mercies began. He later told the companions, in a hadith Muslim preserves: before the revelation came to me, I would hear the rocks and stones greet me with salam as I passed, and I would turn and find no one. There is a stone in Makkah, he said, that used to give me salam, and I know it to this day.

Then came the dreams. Every single night he ﷺ would see what the next day would bring, and it would arrive exactly as he had seen it, true the way the dawn is true; you know the sun will rise tomorrow, and he came to know that what he saw would be. He brought it to Khadijah, and she answered like the woman Allah had chosen for this house: this is a good sign from Allah. Bukhari preserves the timing, and here the Sheikh does an arithmetic that gives you chills. The dreams ran six full months. The Qur'an began descending in Ramadan, on the night it names itself, the Night of Decree.

Indeed, We sent it down during the Night of Decree.Qur'an 97:1

Count six months back from Ramadan and you land in Rabi al-Awwal. The prophethood would last twenty three years, and six months is exactly one part in forty six of it. Then open Tirmidhi: the Prophet ﷺ said that nothing remains of prophethood except the true dream, and the true dream is one of the forty six parts of prophethood. The fraction is exact, down to the number. The scholars read those months two ways, and both are true at once: as notice, that something immense was coming and he must be made ready for it, and as training, because a man is not handed the weight of revelation cold. Allah was walking him ﷺ into it gently, dream by dream, stone by stone.

Read, in the name of your Lord

Some narrations outside the two Sahihs add a prelude. On a Saturday in Ramadan he ﷺ saw a light and heard a sound and found nothing there. On Sunday, the same. Most men would never have climbed back; that he stayed, with a light and a voice and no visible source, was its own quiet courage. Then came Monday, and we know it was Monday from his own mouth, in Sahih Muslim, when he was asked why he fasted that day: on a Monday I was born, and on a Monday revelation was sent down to me. It was one of the last ten nights of Ramadan, the night this ummah still rises every year to seek.

In the cave the angel came to him and said: iqra. Read. And he ﷺ answered the honest truth of an unlettered man: ma ana bi qari, I am not one who can read. Then Jibril seized him and pressed him until, in his ﷺ own telling, all his strength was squeezed out of him, until he thought he could not bear it, and then released him. Read. I am not one who can read. A second crushing embrace, to the very edge of what a body can hold. A third command, and this time the words came, the first five of heaven's last Book.

Recite in the name of your Lord who created - Created man from a clinging substance. Recite, and your Lord is the most Generous - Who taught by the pen - Taught man that which he knew not.Qur'an 96:1-5

Why the crushing embrace? There is no authoritative answer, the Sheikh says, only readings the scholars venture with care. Perhaps so he would know beyond any doubt that this was no dream, for pain is how the dreamer tests whether he is awake. Perhaps his body was being braced for the sheer weight of what it would carry, for Allah would soon tell him plainly:

Indeed, We will cast upon you a heavy word.Qur'an 73:5

Receiving the wahy would remain a draining, physical ordeal for the rest of his ﷺ life. And some scholars saw in the three squeezes a sign of three coming trials that would each feel like the end: the boycott, when the believers nearly starved for years; the plot and the hijrah, from which only Allah's protection brought him through; and Uhud, when he stood bleeding and all but surrounded. Each one crushing, each one opened by Allah at the last moment. And Allah knows best.

Now hear the word itself. Iqra in Arabic carries two meanings, to read from a page and to recite from the heart; even today a child told iqra al-Quran will ask, from the page or from memory? The unlettered Prophet ﷺ heard the first meaning and answered honestly, for he had never been taught letters. But Jibril meant the second: not from a parchment, directly from your Lord. And notice, the Sheikh says, what the first revealed words are not. They are not bismillah. They are iqra bismi rabbik, recite in the name of your Lord, by Him, with His help, your recitation coming not from yourself but straight from Allah. Who created. Created what? The sentence is left open on purpose, the Sheikh points out, because there is nothing to complete: He created everything. Yet out of all of creation, one creature is singled out and named: man, made from alaq, a clinging thing, the embryo suspended in the womb of its mother. The One who shaped that speck is now choosing to speak to it.

And the command lands twice, iqra and then iqra again, and the scholars heard two libraries in the doubling. Recite what comes from your Lord, and read what human beings write with the pen: the deen and the dunya, both of them His gift. This was said to a nation with perhaps five literate men, whose very script had no dots yet, closer to hieroglyphics than to the Arabic we know. Go read what others have written, Allah tells them, broaden your knowledge, but remember that even this I gave you, for it was He who taught by the pen. Within a century they were the foremost power on earth; within a few more, students were riding from England, Italy, and France to study medicine and science in Muslim lands and carry it home. The Prophet ﷺ said the first ever to write with the pen was the prophet Idris: even the pen had to be taught, even speech was taught to Adam, all knowledge tracing back to its Giver. And from this very verb, qara'a, the Book takes its name: the Qur'an, the recitation, preserved whole in human memory and in ink at once, like no other book humanity has ever held.

Cover me, cover me

Then the cave was empty and a man was coming down the mountain in the dark, his heart pounding. He ﷺ burst in upon Khadijah with two words: zammiluni, zammiluni. Cover me, cover me. She wrapped him until the trembling eased. Stand in this moment, the Sheikh urges, because it answers a slander before anyone has the chance to utter it. This is not how impostors begin. The false claimants of prophethood compose magnificent, premeditated accounts of their own commissioning, polished to persuade. This man ﷺ came home terrified, cold, shaking, and utterly human, into the arms of his wife. He did not run to Abu Talib or to his cousins; he ran to Khadijah, because the comfort a man needs at such an hour is the comfort of the woman Allah chose for him.

When he could speak, he told her everything, and added the phrase Bukhari preserves: I fear for myself. The scholars have stacked up more than a dozen readings of that fear, and the two that hold are simple and human: that the embrace had nearly killed him, or that he feared for his mind, seeing and hearing what other people could not. Khadijah did not pause to philosophize. She swore an oath: no, by Allah, never. Allah will never disgrace you. You keep the ties of kinship, you carry the burdens of others, you give to those who have nothing, you honor the guest, you stand with every good cause. Out of a heart that had never heard a single verse came perfect theology, the Sheikh marvels: the One who made you this good will never hand you over to harm. Do good, and Allah does not waste it. This is precisely why Allah chose her for him.

One lesser known report says she first took him to Addas, the only true Christian then living in Makkah, a slave of Roman background whom the seerah will meet again years later in Ta'if, holding out a plate of grapes; he gasped, the angel of God, in this heathen city of idols? Then, in the version Bukhari carries, she took her husband ﷺ to her own cousin Waraqa ibn Nawfal, the eldest of the four Makkans who had walked away from the idols years before in search of the religion of Ibrahim, a man who had studied the scriptures of the Jews and the Christians and copied them out in Hebrew, his own quiet category of believer, now old, blind, and near the end of his life.

Waraqa heard the whole story and lit up: this is the Namus, he said, the keeper of the secret, the very one Allah sent down to Musa. Not Isa, the Sheikh notes, but Musa, most likely because this prophet, like Musa, would have to lead a nation and not only teach it. Then the old man's joy bent into longing: how I wish I were a young man, that I might be alive to help you on the day your people drive you out. The Prophet ﷺ had absorbed everything else without flinching, but this stopped him: will my people really expel me? Yes, said Waraqa. No man has ever brought what you bring without his people turning on him, ridiculing him, persecuting him, driving him out; and if I live to see your day, I will help you with all my strength. Aisha says Waraqa died only a short while later, a believer who had barely heard a verse. And years afterward the Prophet ﷺ said he had seen Waraqa, in white robes, amid gardens, in Jannah.

Stand up and warn

After Iqra, there was silence. The wahy paused, and he ﷺ did not see Jibril for a stretch the reports measure differently, some saying months, the claim of a full year judged unreliable, and life steadied for a while. Then one day, walking, he heard a sound, looked up, and saw the same angel of Hira seated on a throne that filled the whole space between heaven and earth. The terror returned, and he ﷺ hurried home with the same plea on his lips: cover me. And the revelation that answered him retired those words forever.

With iqra, the scholars say, he became a nabi, a prophet who receives. With qum fa andhir, arise and warn, he became a rasul, a messenger sent out. Read the opening of al-Muddaththir as the marching order it is.

O you who covers himself [with a garment], Arise and warn, And your Lord glorify, And your clothing purify, And uncleanliness avoid, And do not confer favor to acquire more.Qur'an 74:1-6

O you wrapped in your cloak: the days of comfort and seclusion are over; rise, and warn. And as you warn, keep magnifying your Lord, for takbir is where the strength will come from. Keep your garments pure, and your record purer, untouched by the very filth you warn the people against. Abandon every idol. And give without keeping a ledger, never doing a favor in order to harvest a larger one in return: work for Allah alone, and look to Allah alone for the reward.

And mark one correction this episode insists upon, the one the Sheikh is most emphatic about. Once revelation came, he ﷺ never returned to Hira, not once. So whoever turns the cave into a method, who tells people to seal themselves into caves and rooms with their dates and water and meditate for months, has the order of events exactly backwards. The seclusion belonged to the time before the message; the message belongs to the streets. The believer who still craves that sweetness of solitude has a door left open to him, and it is not a cave: it is tahajjud, the night prayer, when the world sleeps and the servant rises to stand before his Lord. The seerah's quiet years end here. From this day forward the story is a man ﷺ on his feet, warning with mercy, and the series turns next to how the wahy itself would come.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read day ten as two charming episodes, a touching story and a famous one, and leave them in the past. That would be to miss the question they put to us together. Set them side by side, because the order is the lesson. On one side of this day stand forty years of character so complete that a kidnapped boy, handed his freedom in front of his own weeping father, chose the man ﷺ instead. On the other side stand the first five verses of the Book that would remake the earth. And Allah arranged them in that sequence on purpose. He let Makkah watch the man for forty years before He asked anyone to trust the message; and when the message came, the two souls who knew him best, his wife and the cousin who knew the scriptures, believed without a moment's hesitation. People believed the man ﷺ before they ever heard the message, and the message never had a better witness than the life that preceded it.

So Zayd asks you the first thing. Your character preaches long before your words are given permission to. Zayd chose him with no religion on offer, nothing to gain, only conduct to go by; and the people nearest you are reading your conduct now, in the years before you ever say a word about your faith. Be the kind of person whose mere manners would make someone choose you over their own blood.

Khadijah asks you the second thing, and it is the steadiest sentence in the whole episode. When fear shook the greatest man who ever lived, she did not hand him a theory; she handed him a mirror. Look at the good Allah has already put in your hands, she said, the ties you keep, the burdens you carry, the poor you feed, the guest you honor, and know that the One who made you good will not abandon you to harm. Do good, and Allah will not disgrace you. The next time dread comes for you in the dark, steady yourself the way she steadied him: not by arguing yourself out of the fear, but by remembering Whose you are, and trusting Him with what you cannot see.

And the cave asks you the last thing. Six months of dreams as true as dawn, stones offering salam, one part in forty six measured to the number: Allah trains before He tasks. Nothing heavy lands in a faithful life unannounced. Learn to read the slow, quiet preparations Allah is running in your own days, and answer them the way he ﷺ did, by climbing back up the mountain even after a light and a voice gave you every reason to stay home. Then read, and read in His name, knowing that revelation and the pen are both His gift, and that the first command of your religion was to seek knowledge and to seek it bismi rabbik. And when living your faith begins to cost you, remember the very first thing old Waraqa told the new Prophet ﷺ: no one has ever brought the truth without being made to pay for it. If your faith is costing you comfort, you are not off the path. You are on the oldest one there is. May Allah teach us by His pen what we do not know, give us hearts that choose His Messenger ﷺ the way Zayd chose him, tongues that steady the fearful the way Khadijah steadied him, and on the Day the nations are gathered, gather us in his company, never parted from his nearness.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 10: Zayd ibn Harithah and the beginning of revelation (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (33:5, 9:40, 97:1, 96:1-5, 73:5, 74:1-6), verified via quran.ai. The narration is the Sheikh's; the phrasing is Buruja's.

The Seerah · 11 of 104

The Second Revelation

Arise, and warn

When we left him ﷺ, the cave of Hira was behind him, Iqra had been pressed into his chest by an angel he had no word for, and Khadijah had wrapped his shaking body and carried him to the one man in Makkah who might understand. Waraqah ibn Nawfal looked at what he described and named it without hesitation: this is the very angel that came down upon Musa, this is prophethood, and you, by the One in whose hand my soul rests, are the prophet of this nation. And then, almost as soon as he had said it, Waraqah died.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi pauses over a question that troubles people in this narration: how could a prophet go to a man who was not a prophet, to be told what was happening to him? The answer reframes the whole scene. At that moment he ﷺ did not yet know he was a prophet. The concept itself had been lost to the Arabs; they had forgotten how Ibrahim was inspired and what Ismail had carried, the way whole religions in our own time have no notion of a human being who speaks with God. Waraqah came from a tradition that still remembered. So a man overwhelmed by something with no name went to the one person who could name it, and that, the Sheikh notes, is the dignity of knowledge: even a prophet, before he knows, learns from the one who knows. Waraqah believed by declaring him a prophet, and died before he could pray a single prayer, and years later the Prophet ﷺ would say he had seen him in Jannah, clothed in beautiful garments. The first man to die upon this faith died at its very dawn, before there was even a creed to recite.

And then the most important thing in the world simply stopped. No angel. No voice. Just silence over Makkah.

When the sky went quiet

The scholars have a name for what came next. They call it the fatrah, the gap, the long pause in which no revelation came at all. Jabir ibn Abdullah carries the thread of the story forward, and Ibn Abbas fills in the ache of it: for days the Prophet ﷺ would wander the valleys and the slopes of Makkah, climbing back toward Hira, wanting only to see Jibril again, and finding nothing. Some scholars stretched the silence to two or three years, but Dr. Yasir Qadhi sets that aside as far too long. The firmer reports give a shorter span: Ibn Abbas is narrated to have said forty days, another companion simply many days. Working from the fact that the first revelation fell in Ramadan, the likeliest picture is something close to a month and ten days, the silence settling over the month of Shawwal.

Picture that month from the inside, because the seerah asks us to. He ﷺ had seen something that filled the horizon, a creature of Allah enthroned between heaven and earth, and then it was gone, and would not come back. He climbed the mountain expecting the angel and came down with empty hands, day after day after day. In the narration he says, plainly, that he feared for himself. He wondered whether something had gone wrong with his own mind, whether he was hallucinating, whether the thing he had witnessed was a sickness rather than a summons. Sit with how human that is. This is not the diary of a man assembling a legend. This is a person alone with something too large to carry, waiting on an answer that would not come, half afraid of his own sanity. The silence was its own kind of test, and he passed it the way the sincere pass every test, by enduring it without inventing a comfort he had not been given.

The name in the empty air

Then, one day, coming down off the mountain, he heard his name.

He looked ahead along the path: no one. He looked behind him: no one. He looked again, and again, and the voice kept coming, clear and close, with nobody there to speak it. Dr. Yasir Qadhi draws out a small, true thing about this moment. When someone calls your name, the very last place you think to look is straight up into the air; it is simply not in our nature to search the sky for a voice. So he kept turning over the ground around him, scanning the empty path, until at last he raised his eyes.

And there was the angel he had seen at Hira, the same one, enthroned between the heaven and the earth, filling the whole of the sky. He had wanted exactly this. For a month and more he had begged the mountain for it. But wanting the angel and surviving the sight of him turned out to be two different things entirely. He began to tremble. In one report his knees gave way beneath him and he fell, struck down by the sheer weight of what he was looking at. He got up and he ran, the way a body runs from something it understands before the mind has caught up, all the way down and home to Khadijah, calling out as he came: cover me, cover me. That kind of fear, the Sheikh notes, is the kind that turns a person physically cold, that sets the limbs shaking on their own. May Allah protect us from ever knowing it. He had looked on the angel of revelation in its true form, and there was nothing in him that wanted to perform serenity for the sake of the story. He wanted a blanket and his wife's arms.

Arise, and warn

Here is a detail worth slowing down for, because almost everyone reads past it. The revelation did not come on the mountain, in the open, at the sight of the angel. It came in the house. He ﷺ had already run home, already been wrapped in his cloak, already buried himself in the warmth of the shawl in Khadijah's house, and it was there, under the garment, that Jibril spoke. Which means the angel had followed him home. There was no outrunning this. Wherever he fled, the mission had already arrived ahead of him and was waiting under the blanket.

And the first word was tender before it was heavy.

O you who covers himself [with a garment], Arise and warnQur'an 74:1-2

O you who is wrapped up, O you with the shawl drawn close around you. The address itself is gentle, almost intimate, naming him by the very comfort he had run to. And then the command lands inside the tenderness, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi unfolds the quiet poetry of it. You are comfortable. You are sheltered. You have your safety blanket around you, the way even a child clings to one, and the time for that has ended. Qum, stand up. Get out from under the cloak. Leave the warmth, leave the ease, leave the sheltered life you have been living, and go out to the people. Arise and warn. The man in the cloak is being told, in the first breath of his mission, that he no longer has the luxury of staying still. There is a world outside the blanket that has to be warned, and he is the one who has to do it.

Five commands for a lifetime

After the command to warn come five more verses, and they read less like the opening of a passage than like a whole code of life handed down in a single breath. The Sheikh walks through each one, and behind the literal meaning of each he finds a second meaning aimed at the soul.

And your Lord glorify And your clothing purify And uncleanliness avoidQur'an 74:3-5

Glorify your Lord: as you go out to warn the people, do not for one moment stop worshipping the One who sent you; keep magnifying Him, praising Him, returning to Him, even while your hands are full of the work. Purify your clothing: keep your garments literally clean and presentable, the way a man who calls others to God ought to look, and behind the literal sense, keep the soul itself clean, let no sin pollute the one who summons others to purity. Abandon all uncleanliness: leave every idol, walk away from the false gods entirely, while you preach the true God to a city drowning in them.

Then comes a harder verse, and the Sheikh lingers on it precisely because the translation is so easy to misread.

And do not confer favor to acquire moreQur'an 74:6

The word at its heart, the Sheikh explains, is about reminding someone of a kindness you did for them. And why does a person do that? Almost always to collect on it. You lend a man money, and a few months later you happen to mention the loan, and then you ask to borrow his car for a week. The favor was a hook all along. Allah tells His Messenger ﷺ not to do this, not to do good in order to be repaid, not to give in order to be given back. Do everything for the sake of Allah alone, and nothing for the sake of what people might return to you. Dr. Yasir Qadhi ties this directly to sincerity: when a person acts purely for Allah, no one on earth can impugn his intention, but the moment a caller is suspected of wanting something back, money, status, a debt repaid, his whole message is tarnished. This, the Sheikh notes, is exactly why the Prophet ﷺ was never permitted to take charity, never allowed to receive a wage or a payment for the religion. So that no one, then or ever, could say he was in it for what he could get.

And then the last verse to descend in this passage, and the gentlest words in it carry the hardest warning.

But for your Lord be patient.Qur'an 74:7

For the sake of your Lord, be patient. There is a calamity folded inside that command, the Sheikh says. The instruction to be patient is itself a quiet announcement that there will be something to be patient about. The road ahead will be hard. There will be suffering and loss. And the only thing that will hold a human being steady through it is doing the whole of it for Allah, not for applause, not for visible results, not for any reward that people can hand back. For Allah alone. Notice what this means. On the very first day of the mission, in the very first instructions he received, he was already told that it would cost him. The work and the price of the work were handed to him together, in the same seven verses.

The proof inside the trembling

Why the silence at all? Why a month of nothing before the angel returned? The Sheikh gives the scholars' answer: the gap was a mercy. It was a pause to let him recover from the overwhelming first encounter, to gather his strength, to settle his nerves, and even to make him eager, to let the longing for the angel build until he was actively climbing the mountain to find him. And note what happened in spite of all that preparation. Even after a month of mounting hope, even ready and waiting and wanting it, he was still struck to the ground with terror when Jibril appeared. Imagine, the Sheikh says, if it had come without any warning at all. The mercy of the gap was real, and it still left him trembling.

But Dr. Yasir Qadhi pulls something deeper out of that trembling, and it is one of the load-bearing arguments of the whole seerah. A fraud does not write himself this way. Look at the men who have claimed prophethood across history, even in recent centuries, even one who appeared in America and another in India barely a hundred years ago. The stories they tell are always grandiose, always majestic, always with themselves cast as the serene and knowing hero at the center. The seerah gives you the exact opposite. A man who did not plan this, did not seek it, did not want it; a man who when it came was so shaken that he fled down a mountain and begged his wife to bury him in a blanket. That reaction, the Sheikh argues, could only come from someone telling the truth. A liar inventing a religion does not invent his own panic. The fear is not an embarrassment to be explained away. The fear is the evidence.

How revelation came

Seeing the angel directly, as he did at Hira and again now coming down the mountain, was only one of the ways revelation reached him, and the Sheikh takes what he calls a necessary tangent to walk through the others, drawing on the careful list of Ibn al-Qayyim. There are, he explains, several distinct forms in which wahy descended, not one.

The lowest was the true dream, and it is the only form still open to anyone at all today. He ﷺ saw true dreams before prophethood and after it, and true dreams are not the preserve of prophets; any righteous believer may be given one. Above that was a kind of inspiration cast into the heart, the sort Allah gave to the mother of Musa when He inspired her, and to the mother of Isa, an inspiration that emphatically did not make either woman a prophetess. The Sheikh dwells here, because people reach for these verses to argue for female prophets, and he answers them on their own ground: seeing an angel does not make one a prophet, or the companions who saw Jibril in human form would all be prophets; and being addressed by inspiration does not make one a prophet either, or the mother of Musa would be one. The mufassirun who held the majority view restricted prophethood to men sent from among the people of the towns, and the Sheikh lays out both the majority and the minority positions plainly rather than flattening them.

Then there was Jibril appearing as a man, often in the form of a strikingly handsome companion, speaking plainly so that the Prophet ﷺ understood him with ease and the companions sometimes saw the visitor without knowing who he was. And then the hardest form of all: Jibril remaining in his angelic reality while something descended on the Prophet ﷺ that no one around him could see, a state so heavy that on a cold day it would break him into a sweat, heavy enough that when revelation came upon him while he sat on a camel, the animal sank to the ground beneath the weight, heavy enough that once, when his head rested on a companion's thigh as it came, the companion felt the pressure build until he feared his own bone would crack. The Prophet ﷺ described two of these to a questioner: sometimes the angel comes as a man and I understand him, and sometimes it comes like the ringing of a bell, and that is the hardest upon me. Above all of these stood the rarest form, when Allah spoke to him directly, without any angel between them at all, a height reached only once, years later, on the Night Journey above the seven heavens, where even Jibril said: go on, I cannot pass beyond this.

Allah Himself named the weight of it.

Indeed, We will cast upon you a heavy word.Qur'an 73:5

A heavy word. Aisha and the companions watched it press down on him, again and again, every time the words came. That is what it cost him, each time, to receive the verses you and I now recite at our ease, lying down, walking, half attentive. The Sheikh wants us to feel the distance between the comfort of our recitation and the weight of its arrival.

The first to believe

With the command to warn, the mission begins, and at first it is small and quiet, kept among the people closest to him. Dr. Yasir Qadhi is careful about the order, because the order itself honors people. The first to believe was Khadijah, who had accepted before there was even a shahadah to pronounce, on nothing but her knowledge of her husband, certain that Allah would never humiliate a man so kind and so true. The second was Waraqah, who believed by naming him a prophet and died before a single prayer was prescribed. After them, the Sheikh resolves an old dispute the gentle way: the first child to believe was Ali, raised in the Prophet's own household; the first freed servant was Zayd, whom the Qur'an itself would one day name; and the first grown free man was Abu Bakr. All three are first, each in his own category, and respect is given to each.

And Abu Bakr did not pause. The Sheikh relays the Prophet's own words, spoken years later in Madinah when a quarrel flared among the companions and he gently rebuked Umar: Allah sent me to you with the truth, and you all said you lie, and Abu Bakr alone said you have spoken the truth. In another report he said there was no one he ever invited to Islam who did not hesitate, who did not pause to weigh it, except Abu Bakr, who accepted the instant he was asked. This is why, by the consensus of Ahl al-Sunnah, Abu Bakr is the greatest of the companions; and it is why Allah refers to him in the Qur'an as the second of two when the two of them were in the cave. The moment he believed, he became a door, and through that door came a procession of names that would each grow into a world of their own: Uthman ibn Affan, Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas, az-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, Abd ar-Rahman ibn Awf. The Sheikh makes a point of it: every one of these early converts became a legend, a mountain of a man, an entire nation in himself, precisely because they came when coming was costly.

Two of their stories he lingers on, and they belong here whole. Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas was perhaps sixteen, from a great family, and when he believed his mother tried first to torture the faith out of him and then turned to a slower weapon: she swore she would neither eat nor drink until he returned to the idols, and she would die in front of him, slowly, and her death would be on his hands. She wasted away on what looked like a deathbed. And Sa'd, riled and grieving and immovable, said to her: by Allah, my mother, if you had a hundred souls and they left you one by one before my eyes, I would not leave this religion. And she ate. Allah sent down the verse about exactly this:

But if they endeavor to make you associate with Me that of which you have no knowledge, do not obey them but accompany them in [this] world with appropriate kindness and follow the way of those who turn back to Me [in repentance]. Then to Me will be your return, and I will inform you about what you used to do.Qur'an 31:15

Obey them in nothing that severs you from your Lord, and be beautiful to them in everything else. The other story is of Ibn Mas'ud, a young servant tending another man's flock, who was approached by two strangers asking for milk. He answered with the honesty that would mark his whole life: the animals are not mine to give from. So one of them took hold of a barren goat, one long past milk, ran his hand over it, and it filled before the boy's eyes, and they drank. Ibn Mas'ud asked who they were, and learned he was looking at Muhammad ﷺ and Abu Bakr, and believed where he stood. He would one day say he had taken more than seventy surahs directly from the mouth of the Prophet ﷺ, and the Prophet ﷺ would say that whoever wished to recite the Qur'an as fresh as the day it came down should recite it as Ibn Mas'ud did.

Come back when I have prevailed

One last story belongs to this early, hidden dawn, and it carries the whole spirit of the moment in it. A man from Yemen, Amr ibn Abasa, had already worked out in his own heart that idol worship was a lie and that his people were lost in misguidance. Word reached him in Yemen that someone in Makkah was saying the very thing he had been thinking, so he made the entire journey north to find him. And here the Sheikh marks a detail he promises to return to: when Amr found him, he found him hidden, keeping low, the dawah still private and close. Amr was gentle with him, drew near, kept talking, and finally asked: what are you? A prophet, he ﷺ said. And what is a prophet? One whom Allah has sent. Sent with what? To keep the ties of kinship, to break the idols, and to worship Allah alone, with nothing beside Him. No prayer yet. No fasting, no hajj, no law. Just this, at the very beginning: mend your kinship, smash your idols, worship your one Lord.

Who follows you, Amr asked. One free man and one slave, he answered, meaning Abu Bakr and Bilal, though there were already a few more, naming the smallness of it honestly. And Amr, convinced, wanted to join him then and there. The Prophet ﷺ turned him away, gently. You cannot do this now, he told him. Do you not see my situation? I am hiding from my own people. You are not of Quraysh; if you stay and stand with me now, you will be killed for nothing. And then the line that ought to stop the reader cold: go back to your people, and when you hear that I have prevailed, come to me. At the very beginning of everything, with two followers and a secret and an angel he had met only twice, he already knew how it would end. Amr went home to Yemen and waited, certain, and when at last he heard the news that the Prophet ﷺ had migrated and his cause had risen, he traveled all the way to Madinah and presented himself and asked, do you recognize me? And he ﷺ said: yes. You are the one who came to me from Yemen.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read the second revelation as a single dramatic scene, the angel in the sky, the trembling, the famous opening verses, and to leave it there. That would be to miss what it actually hands us, which is a whole life folded into seven verses, and a question for every one of them.

He was told first to get up. The very first word after the silence was qum, arise. He was wrapped in the warmth of the cloak, sheltered, comfortable, and the command was to leave it, to stand and go out into the day. Our faith does the same thing if we let it: it does not want to stay wrapped in the warm and private place where it costs us nothing. At some point it has to rise from the blanket and walk out into a world that needs warning. Ask yourself honestly how much of your religion is lived under the cloak, in comfort, where no one is troubled by it, and what it would mean to stand up.

He was told to keep his garments clean, and underneath, his soul. The one who calls others to good is asked first to scrub his own heart, quietly, in the place no one sees. And he was told not to confer a favor in order to be repaid, to do good for Allah and never for the credit, because the instant good is done to be seen or returned, it is tarnished, and sincerity is the only thing in this life that no one can ever take from you. There is a freedom in that worth seizing: spend on Allah alone, want nothing back from anyone, and you place your treasure somewhere no person can reach it.

And he was told, in the gentlest words of all, to be patient for the sake of his Lord, which was Allah's quiet way of telling him on day one that it would hurt. The difficulty was promised at the very outset, before a single blow had fallen. And the only patience that survives a thing like that is the patience anchored in Allah, not the patience that waits for results or applause and collapses when they do not come. Hold all of this beside the trembling, the knees giving way, the run home, the cry to be covered, and take from it the mercy that the Sheikh found there: being shaken to your core by something enormous is not proof that you are unfit for it. Sometimes it is the very proof that you are sincere. The man who came down that mountain afraid was the truest man who ever lived.

So take one thing from this day down into your ordinary one. Rise from the comfortable place when your Lord calls you to rise, and go with your whole heart. Keep the inside clean where only He is watching. Do your good for Him and refuse the wage of anyone's praise. And when the difficulty comes, as it was promised it would, be patient for His sake and no one else's. O Allah, who told Your beloved ﷺ to arise and warn, and then said to him, my Lord, expand for me my breast and ease for me my task, expand our chests as You expanded his, make us of those who stand up rather than stay wrapped, accept from us what we do for You alone, and grant us the patience that is for Your sake and no one else's, until we are returned to You. Ameen.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 11: the second revelation (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (74:1-7, 73:5, 31:15, 20:25-26), verified via quran.ai. The narration is the Sheikh's, the phrasing is Buruja's.

The Seerah · 12 of 104

The Declaration of Prophethood

Three years a whisper, then a cry from Safa

For three years the most important message ever entrusted to a human being moved through Makkah without once being shouted. A friend told a friend. A household prayed behind its own locked door. Word of it traveled the length of Arabia, all the way to Yemen, and still the man who carried it ﷺ had never stood before his city and said it out loud. A seeker came from Yemen because rumor of a new prophet had reached even there, and the Prophet ﷺ sent him home with a strange instruction: not now; go back, and when you hear that I have prevailed, return to me. The message existed. The whole peninsula could feel it stirring. But it had not yet been proclaimed, and that was deliberate.

This is one of the hinge days of the seerah, the day the whisper became a cry, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi walks it scene by scene. A miracle served at a family dinner. The first public sermon of Islam, delivered from a hilltop that served Makkah as its town hall. The first open curse flung at the Prophet ﷺ, answered from above seven heavens by name. And one old man, with no army and no allies, standing alone between his nephew ﷺ and the whole of Quraysh. Before any of that, though, there is a word to get right.

Private, never secret

The Sheikh is careful about a single word, and the care is worth borrowing. People often call these first years a secret dawah. That is the wrong word. Secret would mean nobody knew. What the Prophet ﷺ practiced was a private dawah, and the difference is everything. He did not stand before the Kaaba and announce his prophethood to the crowds, nor preach to the pilgrims streaming into the city, nor even to the relatives he suspected would only mock him. He went instead to the friends he trusted and the hearts he knew, one soul at a time, and left the public square untouched.

Because he touched nothing public, there was nothing public to oppose. Abu Lahab heard the rumors; Abu Jahl heard them. But what was there to answer? He was not interfering with the city's trade, not challenging anyone in the street, not denouncing the idols at the Kaaba. So for three full years, the proper opinion the Sheikh settles on, there was no ridicule, no torture, no persecution at all. And that quiet was not a failure of nerve; it was the point. Three years without persecution meant three years to learn the faith, to deepen iman, to let a brotherhood knit itself together before the fire came. It was exactly this season of unhurried teaching that braced those first believers for everything about to fall on them.

There is a teaching in that for every age. When the climate is one of real danger, the Sheikh says, dawah is permitted to stay private. He points to the lands where, within living memory, a man could be killed for simply naming himself a Muslim. Our religion does not demand that a believer there stand in the square and volunteer for martyrdom; you may keep a low profile and live your faith quietly. What you may never do is lie about it: when a sincere seeker comes wanting the truth, you cannot hide the truth from him. Private, never secret. The religion is realistic.

And look at who answered in those quiet years. A handful came from the elite: Abu Bakr, Uthman, Zubayr, Abdurrahman ibn Auf, Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas, and Zayd, counted among the noble through the Prophet's ﷺ own household. But the bulk were the weak and the low: Ammar ibn Yasir and his parents Yasir and Sumayya, Bilal, Khabbab ibn al-Aratt. Years later the Roman emperor Heraclius would quiz Abu Sufyan about precisely this, whether the strong followed this prophet or the weak. The weak, Abu Sufyan admitted. And the emperor read it correctly: that is the very signature of true faith, for the powerful have privileges to protect and a status quo to defend, while the poor can afford to see the truth plainly. Every one of those early names became a pillar; of more than a hundred thousand companions, history preserved barely five thousand names and full life stories for only a few dozen, and an outsized share of that handful are the believers of these three silent years. Somewhere in this same period, it seems, Jibril came down and taught the Prophet ﷺ wudu and salah: two rakat at a time, voluntary then, a gift before it was ever a command. No single authentic report fixes the date, the Sheikh notes, but the pieces point this way, and the five daily prayers would wait for the night journey still to come.

The five stages of the dawah

Before the story moves forward, the Sheikh stops and hands the listener a map you can lay over the whole of the seerah and the whole history of the ummah after it. The dawah of the Prophet ﷺ passed through five distinct stages, and each was the right wisdom for its hour.

The first was the private dawah just described, three years long, a small and exposed community simply living its faith without confrontation. The second was public dawah carried by the tongue alone: roughly ten years of open preaching in Makkah with no permission to raise a hand in return, even as the persecution sharpened into torture, exile to Abyssinia, the journey to Ta'if, the boycott, and repeated attempts on his ﷺ life. The command for that decade was patience: turn away from the ignorant and keep speaking, and do not strike back even when one of your own is killed, for you have not the power and it is not yet the time.

Then Madinah changed the equation, in steps. The third stage permitted fighting, but against one enemy only, the Quraysh and whoever allied with them, for the first six years after the hijrah. The fourth, after the treaty of Hudaybiyyah, held real power but deliberately sheathed it: open dawah to all, arms drawn only in defense. It looked to many like surrender and proved one of the greatest victories Islam ever won, the victory Umar himself could not understand until he saw its fruit. And the fifth, in the Prophet's ﷺ final years, was open dawah carried with armies to any power that physically barred its path, the stage upon which he ﷺ passed away and which his companions inherited, until the conquests reached Persia and Rome and beyond.

The Sheikh draws the map for a reason that is the exact opposite of what the fearmongers assume. Conquest was never the goal of the religion; it was a tool the ummah took up only when it needed to, and for most of its history it settled on the fourth stage, open preaching without aggression. For Muslims living as minorities today, the Sheikh is emphatic: the second stage is the model. Speak the truth plainly, harm no one, reject anyone who preaches violence in the religion's name, and never say one thing in the masjid and another in public. Every community, guided by its scholars, reads its own hour. All five stages are his ﷺ sunnah, and the heart of each is the same: preach the message, by the best means available, and leave the outcome to Allah.

The command comes down

Three years in, the order from heaven changed. The first of the two verses that turned the whisper into a cry was from Surah al-Hijr, and its opening word, fasda, means to come out with it, to break forth and proclaim openly, hiding nothing and paying no mind to what the ignorant will do in return.

Then declare what you are commanded and turn away from the polytheists.Qur'an 15:94

Then a second verse made the starting point unmistakable, naming the very first audience he was to warn.

And warn, [O Muḥammad], your closest kindred.Qur'an 26:214

The word for kindred here, ashira, is wider than uncles and aunts; it reaches to kith and kin, the whole web of related clans that was Quraysh itself, the people of Makkah being in truth a single extended tribe. The Prophet ﷺ understood: the time of the private dawah was over, and the message had to be carried into the open. But it would go out the way wisdom goes out, in rings. First his own clan, the Banu Hashim. Then the whole of Makkah. Then, in time, every nation on earth. The order itself is the lesson the Sheikh underlines, because Allah commanded the warning of the nearest kin before He revealed the verses addressed to all mankind: the first people any of us answers for are our own.

One dish, forty guests

So the Prophet ﷺ asked Ali ibn Abi Talib, then a young boy in his household, to prepare a meal. The food was almost nothing, a single leg of lamb and one cup to drink from, but the guest list was not: more than forty grown men of the Banu Hashim and the sons of Abd al-Manaf. The Prophet ﷺ blessed the food and ate first, and then, the narrator remembered, every one of those forty men ate his fill from the one small dish and drank his fill from the single cup, as if each had had the whole of it to himself. One of the first miracles of the prophethood, served quietly at a family dinner.

Abu Lahab sensed what the evening was for. Before his nephew ﷺ could say a word, this senior uncle rose, made an excuse, and left, and with one of the elders gone the gathering broke up. The Prophet ﷺ read the tactic for what it was, and answered it with patience: he told Ali to cook the same meal again. Days later the same faces returned, and this time he ﷺ rose before anyone could slip away. He opened with praise of Allah arranged as no Arab had heard it, the khutbah of need that Muslims still begin their gatherings with to this day, and then said it plainly. O sons of Abdul Muttalib, I know of no man who has ever come to his people with anything better than what I bring you. I bring you the good of this world and the next: leave your idols, turn to Allah alone, and He will give you both.

Abu Lahab muttered his contempt to the room, not daring to address his nephew directly, that this was an unworthy business and that a young man had no place overturning the ways of the forefathers. The rest neither accepted nor refused; it remained, after all, a private family matter. But one of the source books preserves a single voice that rose in answer. Ali, the youngest person in the room, radiyallahu anhu, stood and said: I will help you, O Messenger of Allah. A boy, against the silence of all his elders. Anyone who knows the rest of Ali's life is not the least bit surprised.

The cry from Safa

Then came the morning Makkah never forgot, a scene preserved in Sahih al-Bukhari. Safa, the hill nearest the Kaaba, stood far taller then than fourteen centuries of climbing feet have since worn it down, and it carried one fixed civic meaning: nobody climbed Safa unless he had an announcement that could not wait. So when the Prophet ﷺ climbed it and began calling the clans by name, O Banu Abd al-Dar, O Banu Abd Manaf, the small town, perhaps a thousand souls, dropped everything and assembled. A cry from the top of Safa was old Makkah's town hall bell, and everyone came.

He ﷺ began not with a claim but with a question. What do you know of me, he asked. Have you ever once heard me tell a lie? A voice answered for the crowd: nothing but good; you are al-Amin, the trustworthy. He pressed further. If I told you that an army was massed behind this hill, ready this moment to fall upon you, would you take my word for it, unseen, and act? We would, they said. We have never known you to lie. He had built his ground carefully, on the honesty they had just sworn to before the whole city, and now he laid the message on it: then know that I am a warner sent to you before a severe punishment. Save yourselves from the Fire, for I cannot avail you against Allah at all.

And then he made it personal in the most piercing way. He called the clans from the furthest related inward, ring by ring, until he reached his own blood, and named his uncles and aunts one by one. O Hamza ibn Abdul Muttalib. O Safiyya bint Abdul Muttalib. Save yourselves from the Fire; I cannot help you before Allah. And last, the dearest soul of all to him, his own daughter: O Fatima. For her alone he added a sentence he gave no one else: ask me whatever you wish of my wealth and it is yours, but I cannot save you from Allah. If nearness to him ﷺ could have rescued a single person, it would have rescued her. The message at Safa was for every soul, standing utterly alone: save yourself.

The first public ridicule of Islam came, fittingly, from inside the family. Abu Lahab scooped up a fistful of dust and flung it toward his nephew, the old Arab gesture of utter contempt, and snarled: tabban laka, may you perish! Is this why you gathered us here? In the privacy of the dinner he had only been cold; here, before the whole city, he performed a bravado he felt he had to show. And Allah answered him from above seven heavens, by name and measure for measure: he had said tabban laka, and Allah revealed a whole surah against him, opening with his very words turned back on him.

May the hands of Abū Lahab be ruined, and ruined is he. His wealth will not avail him or that which he gained. He will [enter to] burn in a Fire of [blazing] flame And his wife [as well] - the carrier of firewood. Around her neck is a rope of [twisted] fiber.Qur'an 111:1-5

A surah that this ummah recites against him until the Day of Judgment. From that morning the dawah lived in the open: at the Kaaba, in the markets of Mina, on the roads where the pilgrims came. And from that same morning, so did the opposition.

Stop him, or hand him over

Makkah had no king; its clans were too proud to crown one. What it had instead was the Dar al-Nadwa, a council of chieftains, one for each clan, and beneath all its customs ran one iron law: no one lays a hand on a man of a tribe unless that tribe's own chief surrenders him. The chief of the Banu Hashim was Abu Talib. So the leaders of Quraysh went to him, politely the first time: your nephew curses our idols and ridicules the ways of our forefathers; surely you cannot allow this to continue.

Notice the arithmetic in that complaint, the Sheikh says, for it is the same dishonest sum used against Islam in every age since. The Prophet ﷺ had cursed no idols at all. The Qur'an itself forbids insulting them, lest the insult rebound onto Allah:

And do not insult those they invoke other than Allāh, lest they insult Allāh in enmity without knowledge. Thus We have made pleasing to every community their deeds. Then to their Lord is their return, and He will inform them about what they used to do.Qur'an 6:108

All he had said was that tawhid is the truth and idolatry is foolishness. But hatred takes one and one and makes twenty: if idolatry is foolish, then our forefathers were fools, and if you call them fools, you curse them, and on it builds. It is precisely how the Islamophobe works in our own time, magnifying a small true thing into a vast invented threat. Abu Talib gave the delegation gentle words, promised them nothing, and sent them on their way.

They came back harder: stop him, or hand him over to us, and we will deal with him ourselves. No chief of the Arabs had ever been cornered by his own clan like this, and here the Sheikh draws out a quiet truth about power: every leader, however mighty he looks, is inwardly dependent on the people who carry him, and must keep appeasing them or cease to lead. Only Allah is King without a court. So the old man called his nephew and pleaded: my people have come to me with such and such. Have mercy on yourself, and on me; do not load me with more than I can bear. Emotional blackmail at its finest, the Sheikh calls it, and from the one man whose plea could truly wound, the uncle who had been father and mother both to him ﷺ since he was eight.

The Prophet ﷺ, overwhelmed, gave the answer the ummah has carried like a banner ever since. O my uncle, by Allah, if they placed the sun in my right hand and the moon in my left so that I would abandon this matter, I would not abandon it, until Allah makes it prevail or I perish in its path. And in another report, the one the Sheikh judges probably the more authentic, he ﷺ pointed instead at the sky: do you see the sun, my uncle? I have no more power to stop what I carry than you have to light your stick from its flame. Read either version twice. The light he bore was brighter than the sun and moon together and could not be eclipsed, and an offer of the heavens themselves could not buy it back, because it had never been his to put down.

Abu Talib looked at his nephew, saw the certainty settled in his face, and gave way. Go, he said, and say whatever you will, for by Allah, I will never come to you about this again. And he kept that word through everything that followed, all the way into the years of the boycott, where he would choose to starve in the valley beside the believers rather than abandon the boy he had raised.

But Quraysh tried once more, and the offer they brought reveals the whole soul of jahiliyyah. They came this time as a full delegation, the entire city now and not merely the Banu Hashim, and brought a young man with them: Umarah, son of al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah, by their reckoning the finest youth in all of Makkah. Take him, they said. Adopt him as your own son. And in exchange, hand us your nephew, and we will do with him as we please. Abu Talib's reply had his poet's edge in it: what an evil bargain you offer me! You give me your son to feed and raise, and I give you mine to kill?

Then Mut'im ibn Adi rose, the most senior man of Quraysh then alive, the elder remembered for averting bloodshed years before when the Kaaba was rebuilt, and even he sided with the city: O Abu Talib, your people have offered everything that could reasonably be asked; take one of their offers. The stage was now set for a thing the Arabs had never done, the deposing of a living, able chief, a coup against one's own blood. Abu Talib saw the script for what it was and told Mut'im so to his face: this is a plot you have hatched. Then he made his stand. Do as you please, he said. I will not budge. One old man with not a single sword behind him, against the assembled whole of Quraysh, armed with nothing but his dignity and the bedrock certainty that you do not hand your own blood to its killers. He poured that defiance into verse so powerful that Ibn Hajar and others would rank his poetry above the seven famed odes that hung in the Kaaba. And Quraysh, holding every visible advantage, backed down. Nothing carried the day but conviction, and the will of Allah.

Hold on to the name Mut'im ibn Adi, the Sheikh urges, because the Prophet ﷺ did. This day was Mut'im at his worst. But in the years to come he would do the Muslims good after good, and long after his death, standing over the seventy two captives taken at Badr, the Prophet ﷺ would say: had Mut'im ibn Adi been alive and spoken to me for these prisoners, I would have released them all for his sake. The lesson is drawn without flinching: those who do not share your faith are not one undifferentiated mass to be hated alike. Some are noble in their dealings, some stand up for the wronged, and the Prophet ﷺ remembered such people and honored them. In our own day, the Sheikh says, there are non-Muslims who do more for justice than many Muslims do. They are the Mut'ims of our time. Find them, and honor them too.

The phenomenon of Abu Talib

Now the question that aches through the whole episode: why did the man the Prophet ﷺ loved most never come to believe? He had grown up with no father and no mother, and Abu Talib had been both to him, beside him until he was fifty three years old. And in his heart, the Sheikh says, Abu Talib knew. He had seen too many signs, and above all he knew his nephew, and he knew his nephew did not lie. On his deathbed the Prophet ﷺ bent over him and begged: my uncle, say one kalima, just one, and I will plead for you with it before Allah. The old man's lips began to part. And Abu Jahl, standing watch at the bedside, struck at the one thing Abu Talib treasured even above his nephew: will you abandon the religion of your father, the way of Abdul Muttalib? The mouth closed. He died the son of Abdul Muttalib, and nothing more, for it was his lineage, that one supreme claim to fame and power among the Arabs, that he could not let go.

The Prophet ﷺ, grief-stricken, vowed to keep asking Allah's forgiveness for him unless he was forbidden, for the prophets do not act except by permission. And he was forbidden. The verses came down drawing the line with both love and iron:

It is not for the Prophet and those who have believed to ask forgiveness for the polytheists, even if they were relatives, after it has become clear to them that they are companions of Hellfire.Qur'an 9:113

And another, telling him that even the best of all creation ﷺ does not own the gift of guidance:

Indeed, [O Muḥammad], you do not guide whom you like, but Allāh guides whom He wills. And He is most knowing of the [rightly] guided.Qur'an 28:56

He could warn, he could weep, he could love with his whole heart; he could not carry his own uncle across the line. And here Dr. Yasir Qadhi opens what he calls the phenomenon of Abu Talib. Suppose the dua had been answered the easy way, and Abu Talib had spoken the kalima and believed. That same hour he would have ceased to be chief of the Banu Hashim. The chieftainship, the standing, the immunity that shielded his nephew, all of it rested on his being the honored heir of Abdul Muttalib in the eyes of pagan Makkah, and the protection would have died the instant his paganism did. For the dawah to survive its Makkan years, the one man who could shield it had to remain, painfully, outside it. The proof followed swiftly: only when Abu Talib died, and Abu Lahab took the clan, did Makkah become unlivable and the road to Madinah finally open. Allah knew what His Messenger ﷺ needed better than His Messenger knew it himself, and both the wisdom and the tears are true at once. It is also, the Sheikh notes, the clearest proof that the Prophet ﷺ was a man and not a god: he could not guide even his own beloved uncle, because guidance belongs to Allah alone.

The ache never left him ﷺ. At the conquest of Makkah, Abu Bakr radiyallahu anhu led his own white-bearded father, Abu Quhafah, to pledge Islam at last, and then wept: by Allah, O Messenger of Allah, I would have given even this, my own father's hand in yours, to have seen the hand of Abu Talib there instead. And when al-Abbas later asked whether he had been able to benefit the uncle who shielded him all those years, he ﷺ answered, in a hadith Sahih Muslim carries: yes. Because of my dua he has been moved to the shallowest edge of the Fire; were it not for me, he would be in its depths. No one in the Fire bears a lighter punishment than Abu Talib, and still the gate of the Garden is shut to him, because he died upon shirk, and shirk that goes unrepented is the one debt Allah has told us He will not forgive. It is mercy and justice in the same breath, and the sternest sentence in all the seerah.

The episode closes with four sons of Abdul Muttalib laid side by side, four full uncles of one Prophet ﷺ. Hamza, sayyid al-shuhada, above whom no martyr stands. Abbas, a Muslim of the righteous, beloved and honored, though beneath the rank of the very foremost. Abu Talib, never a Muslim, yet no one outside the faith in all our history holds a higher honor or a lighter sentence. And Abu Lahab, the one enemy of Islam cursed by name in the Qur'an, recited against until the end of time. The same father, the same blood, the same nearness to the best of creation ﷺ, and four utterly different ends, because lineage decides nothing and deeds decide everything. Which is exactly where the first public sermon of Islam had begun, on the hill of Safa, with the gentlest man who ever lived ﷺ telling even his own daughter: I cannot save you from Allah. Save yourself.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this day as a stirring sequence of scenes safely sealed in the past: a miraculous dinner, a speech from a hilltop, a famous defiance. That would be to miss the questions it puts directly to us. Strip the day down and three remain.

The first is the question of the stage. For three years he ﷺ kept the dawah private, then went public the moment Allah commanded it, and even then he moved outward in rings, his own family before his city and his city before the world. We are quick to broadcast and slow to begin at home; we will lecture the distant before we have warned the people sleeping under our own roof. The day asks us to reverse it: say the truth you have been keeping quiet, gently, to the people nearest you first, because they are the ones you will be asked about first. And to keep your faith low when real danger surrounds you is not cowardice but a stage of the same sunnah; what is forbidden is to say one thing in private and another in public. Private, never secret, in your life as in his ﷺ.

The second is the question of Safa, and it is the hardest. Standing on that hill, the most beloved man to ever walk the earth told his own daughter that he could not save her from Allah, that she must save herself. If nearness to him could not rescue Fatima, then no name we carry, no lineage we inherit, no proximity to the righteous, will rescue us. On the morning when names and bloodlines fall away, the only currency left will be deeds. So the day asks, quietly and without flattery, what you are actually doing, beyond what you were born into and whom you happen to know.

And the third is the question of Abu Talib, the one that aches. Here was a man who gave the Prophet ﷺ everything, who would starve beside him in the boycott, who stared down a whole city for him, and who still died without the one word that would have saved him, and the Prophet ﷺ, for all his love and all his weeping, could not carry him across. Most of us have an Abu Talib: someone we love deeply who has not yet answered. The day teaches us our part and its limit at once. Speak to them. Love them. Make dua for them with everything in you, as he ﷺ did. Then leave their hearts to the One who owns hearts, because you do not guide whom you love, and despair over a beloved soul was never your assignment.

So carry one thing from Safa into your ordinary days. Begin your dawah at home, gently, with the nearest first. Build something of your own that the Day of Judgment will recognize, because no name will stand in for it. And tonight, name before Allah the person you love who has not yet answered, the Abu Talib of your own dua list, and ask for them what only He can give. O Allah, send Your praise and Your peace upon the one who proclaimed what he was commanded ﷺ, make us of those who answer him, guide through us the hearts we love, for You alone are the Guide, and gather us with him at the hawd on the Day when names and lineages fall away and nothing remains but what our hands have sent ahead.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 12: the declaration of prophethood (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (15:94, 26:214, 111:1-5, 6:108, 9:113, 28:56), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 13 of 104

Opposition from the Quraysh

Every weapon they owned, tested against one voice

The dawah is public now, and Makkah has a problem it does not know how to solve. The chiefs leaned on Abu Talib last week and lost; his protection still holds, so the Prophet ﷺ cannot simply be silenced or sent away. The message is loose in the city, and people are listening. So the Quraysh open a new file, and what they reach for, one tactic after another, is the whole arsenal of a society that owns everything except an answer. They will drown the recitation in noise, beat the man who has no tribe to avenge him, mock, slander, dare him to bring miracles, and when all of that fails, reach for the checkbook. Dr. Yasir Qadhi walks their playbook tactic by tactic, and the strange thing running underneath the whole campaign is this: the harder they fight the recitation, the closer they creep toward it. This is the day the enemies of the Qur'an cannot stop confessing to it.

They tried to shout it down

Their first new weapon was the simplest thing in the world: noise. Ibn Abbas preserved the picture of it. Whenever the Prophet ﷺ raised his voice with the revelation in front of the Kaaba, the Quraysh would erupt, shouting and jeering and cursing the One who sent it down and the one ﷺ it was sent down upon, until the words could not be heard. Anyone who wanted to listen had to pretend he was not, straining to catch a phrase over the din. It was crude and deliberate, and the astonishing thing is that the Qur'an recorded their strategy in their own mouths, so that the scheme to bury the recitation is now read out, forever, as part of the recitation it tried to bury.

And those who disbelieve say, "Do not listen to this Qur'an and speak noisily during [the recitation of] it that perhaps you will overcome."Qur'an 41:26

The Prophet's ﷺ own instinct was to answer noise with volume, to lift his voice above the mob. And Allah corrected him toward something better than a shouting match. Not a roar, not a whisper, a path laid carefully between the two.

Say, "Call upon Allah or call upon the Most Merciful. Whichever [name] you call, to Him belong the best names." And do not recite [too] loudly in your prayer or [too] quietly but seek between that an [intermediate] way.Qur'an 17:110

Do not let the hecklers set your volume, the verse tells him. Recite the way you were commanded, ignore the racket, and the message will carry. And it did. There is a quiet principle buried in that correction: falsehood is forever trying to set the terms, to drag truth down into its own register of shouting. Truth does not have to accept the invitation. You do the work you were given, at the pitch you were given, and let it travel.

The volunteer at Maqam Ibrahim

With the recitation effectively banned in the open air, the believers gathered and faced a plain fact: in all of Makkah, no one but the Prophet ﷺ himself had ever recited the Qur'an aloud in public. It was simply not done; no one had the courage. So the question went around the room. Who will volunteer? And a hand went up that the others immediately tried to push back down. Abdullah ibn Mas'ud, radiyallahu anhu, said he would do it, and they said, in effect, not you, pick someone else. Their reasoning was the brutal arithmetic of that world. Ibn Mas'ud was not of the Quraysh; his roots ran back to the tribes of Yemen; he had no clan inside the city to come avenging if he were harmed. In Makkah your lineage was your passport, the Sheikh explains, the thing you pulled out when trouble started. Ibn Mas'ud had no such name, and no one. And he answered them: I am putting my trust in Allah, and Allah will protect me.

If anyone had earned the errand, it was him. This is the man the Prophet ﷺ would one day single out for the Qur'an itself: whoever wishes to recite it fresh and ripe, the way it first came down, let him recite it the way of Ibn Umm Abd, which was Ibn Mas'ud. He had taken more than seventy surahs directly from the Prophet's ﷺ own mouth to his, with no teacher in between; his voice was the voice of a qari, the reciter among the companions.

So the next morning he went down at the hour the town gathered in the shade around the Kaaba to talk and trade gossip, their version of the coffee shop, the Sheikh smiles. He stood at the Maqam of Ibrahim and lifted that beautiful voice with Surah ar-Rahman, one of the earliest and loveliest of all the revelations.

The Most Merciful. Taught the Qur'an. Created man. [And] taught him eloquence.Qur'an 55:1-4

A crowd drew in before it understood what it was hearing. These were the masters of Arabic speech, and yet nobody spoke like this: the long held vowels, the hum and roll of it, the strange music that belongs to the Qur'an and to nothing else on a human tongue. The whole science of recitation, the Sheikh points out, was made for this book; the Arabs had never heard their own language carry like this. They sat and listened and asked one another what this was. Then somebody placed it: this is what Muhammad ﷺ claims has come down on him. The spell broke. They fell on Ibn Mas'ud, fists and sandals, one man with no tribe and no defender, and beat him until the recitation stopped. He had not managed two full pages.

He came back to the believers bloodied and swelling, and they met him with the obvious: this is exactly what we feared for you. And his answer is the kind of sentence you carry for life. By Allah, nothing has grown in me today except contempt for the enemies of Allah, and I am ready to do the very same thing again tomorrow. They told him: enough. You have made them hear what they could not bear to hear.

Three shadows at fajr

Banned in the daylight, the Qur'an became irresistible in the dark. The Prophet ﷺ prayed tahajjud every night and recited aloud, and Makkah was a small, quiet village, dead silent at three and four in the morning, when a voice reciting in a house carries to the alleys outside. And so it happened, in a report the Sheikh grades authentic, that three of the senior men of Quraysh slipped out of their beds on the same night to stand in the dark near his ﷺ house and listen: Abu Jahl, Abu Sufyan, and al-Akhnas. Three architects of the war on the message, each one alone, each certain he was the only man out there. When the recitation stopped near dawn they turned for home down the main alley and collided, and there was a flurry of excuses, and each knew exactly why the other two were out. They swore it would not happen again. The second night, the same. The third night, the same, until, terrified of what it would do to their standing if the town found out, they made a solemn pact never to come back.

Sheikh Yasir stops the lecture here and asks you to feel the weight of it. These are men in their fifties, leaders of the city, leaving warm beds for the chill of the night to stand in darkness outside the house of the very man they are trying to destroy, three nights running, drawn by a recitation they have outlawed in public. That is what this Qur'an does to people who hate it. And then he turns the thought toward us. If recordings of our own great reciters move a believer the way they do, what must it have been to hear the Prophet ﷺ himself? He breaks off to make a dua, that Allah let him, and all of us, hear that tilawah with our own ears in Jannah, a dua worth making your own.

The morning after the third night, al-Akhnas went looking for verdicts. He came first to Abu Sufyan, who was too cautious to commit and made the other man speak first. Al-Akhnas finally said it plainly: by Allah, it seems to be the truth. Abu Sufyan then hedged exactly as the politician he was, the founder, in time, of a dynasty: some of it I understood, and some was beyond me. No verdict, no clean quotation to be used against him. He was the most sympathetic of the three, and the one who would, years later, accept Islam.

Then al-Akhnas knocked on Abu Jahl's door and got the most honest thing Abu Jahl may ever have said, a confession the Sheikh notes he repeated, in almost the same words, to another man on another day. We and the clan of Abd Manaf, said Abu Jahl, have raced each other for honor for generations. They fed the pilgrims, so we fed them; they gave water, so we gave water; they showed valor, so we showed valor, until we were like two racehorses neck and neck at the line. And now they say there is a prophet among them, revelation coming to him out of the heavens. When will we ever catch that? By Allah, as long as I live I will never believe in him. Notice what is missing from that speech, the Sheikh says. Not one word that it might be false. The wall between Abu Jahl and faith was never made of evidence; it was lineage, rivalry, the old jahili pride, the very same wall that held Abu Talib back from the other side of the line. Snobbery damns as surely as hatred does.

A taunt, and the dawn that answered it
