The next tactic was ridicule: turn the man ﷺ and his followers into a joke, and you never have to answer them at all. Fourteen centuries later, the Sheikh notes dryly, this method still has not retired. There came a stretch when revelation paused for weeks, and one of the women of Quraysh, perhaps the wife of Abu Jahl, no one is certain, taunted him in the open: I see your shaytan has finally abandoned you, meaning the angel that brought the words. He ﷺ was a human being, and it cut him; he was grieved by it. And heaven itself came down to answer a street taunt, not with an argument but with an oath, sworn over the morning and the night.

By the morning brightness. And [by] the night when it covers with darkness. Your Lord has not taken leave of you, [O Muhammad], nor has He detested [you]. And the Hereafter is better for you than the first [life]. And your Lord is going to give you, and you will be satisfied.Qur'an 93:1-5

A promise of being given until he is content, sworn to him in the very middle of being mocked: the Lord of the worlds pausing to console one grieving heart against the cruelty of a taunt in the market.

Abu Jahl, meanwhile, ran the campaign of abuse like a system tuned to each man's weak point. A convert of high standing got public scorn, the loaded question thrown in his face: are you better than your father, better than your grandfather, the founder of your tribe? A merchant got his trade boycotted. A man with no protector got fists. But the smearing kept misfiring, and never more so than on the open roads. In the pilgrimage season Abu Jahl, and sometimes Abu Lahab, would post themselves outside the city to warn the incoming travelers: there is a madman in there, a magician; when you meet him, plug your ears, or he will bewitch you. Most obeyed. But one visitor heard the word madman and thought, I am a healer, I will go and cure him. He came to the Prophet ﷺ, who told him plainly, I am not mad, and then opened with the words of praise his ﷺ addresses always began with, all praise belongs to Allah, we praise Him and seek His help. The man stopped him before he reached a single line of the message. Say that again, he said. By Allah, I have never heard anything more beautiful. And he accepted Islam on the spot. The warning had become the advertisement.

The Shakespeare of Makkah

When mockery had run its course they reached for outright lies, and the Qur'an preserves the whole catalogue, because the Quraysh could never even agree on which lie to tell. He is mad. He is a soothsayer. He is a magician, no, he is the one bewitched; they argued among themselves which it was. He is a poet. And the Qur'an's replies sit right beside the slanders. To the charge of madness: I lived a whole lifetime among you before this, will you not use your reason? To the charge of poetry, his entire biography: he had never written a line before the revelation came, and to the end of his life, the Sheikh notes, he never once completed so much as a full couplet of verse, half a line at the most. And here the Sheikh makes an observation he wants every one of us to keep. To this very day, every rejection of the Prophet ﷺ still has to reach for one of these same labels: deluded, or a deceiver, or some marvelous natural genius of language. The list has not added a single new item in fourteen hundred years.

Then comes the story the Sheikh asks every one of us to know and memorize, because it shows exactly how they were forced to deal with this book. Al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah was the chieftain of Banu Makhzum, as Abu Talib was chieftain of Banu Hashim, and the father of Khalid ibn al-Walid. He was also the unrivaled poet of the city, its Shakespeare, as the Sheikh calls him: when Quraysh sent a champion against the other tribes in verse, al-Walid was the one they sent to the final. One day he caught the Prophet ﷺ reciting by the Kaaba and, for the first time, with no shouting to cover it, listened all the way to the end. He walked away a changed man, and what he muttered ran through gossip-hungry Makkah by nightfall. By Allah, I have heard from Muhammad speech that is not the speech of men and not the speech of jinn. It has a sweetness to it; the height of it bears fruit and the depth of it gushes water. It rises over everything, and nothing rises over it.

Quraysh panicked: their own laureate, undone by a single hearing. Abu Jahl said, leave him to me, and went to al-Walid with a threat wrapped in concern: your people will not be satisfied with you until you say something against it. Hold that sentence next to last week's, the Sheikh urges. Quraysh said those identical words to Abu Talib, and the old chief stood his ground and let them do their worst; al-Walid hears the same pressure and folds on the spot. That is the whole difference between a leader and a politician, says the Sheikh: a leader carries his people, while a politician is carried by them, servile because he fears the crowd and not Allah. As Ibn Taymiyyah put it, the people of politics are not leaders at all; they are followers of the whims of the masses.

What follows is the strangest script meeting in the history of Makkah: the greatest wordsmith alive begging to be told what to say. Call him a madman, says Abu Jahl. But he is not mad, al-Walid answers; we have seen madmen, and there is none of it in him. A soothsayer? None of their muttering. A magician? No spells, no knots. Then call him a poet. By Allah, says al-Walid, none of you knows poetry better than I do, and this is nothing like poetry. Every lie dies in the expert's own mouth. At last he asks for a few days, paces his house frowning, and lands on the only label with any plausibility left: magic, but the kind handed down, words passed from teller to teller that bewitch a man and split him from his father and his brother.

He never got to publish it. Before al-Walid could announce his verdict, Allah announced His, reciting back to all of Makkah the very scene from inside the man's house, the thinking, the frowning, the scowling, the turning away in pride, things not even his own family had witnessed. And it opens, the Sheikh reads almost in a whisper, with Allah turning to face the man alone.

Leave Me with the one I created alone. And to whom I granted extensive wealth. And children present [with him]. And spread [everything] before him, easing [his life]. Then he desires that I should add more.Qur'an 74:11-15

And then the private deliberation, read out in public before the man could open his mouth.

Indeed, he thought and deliberated. So may he be destroyed [for] how he deliberated. Then may he be destroyed [for] how he deliberated. Then he considered [again]; Then he frowned and scowled; Then he turned back and was arrogant. And said, "This is not but magic imitated [from others]. This is not but the word of a human being."Qur'an 74:18-25

Allah, one against one, with a man He had given wealth upon wealth and sons standing at his side, who repaid it by calling His speech secondhand sorcery. The verses are themselves a challenge, the Sheikh notes: do you think you can beat this, al-Walid, you who know poetry better than anyone alive? Al-Walid died on his paganism, true to the same pride that damned Abu Jahl. And both his elder sons, Khalid among them, would one day accept Islam.

Show us a miracle

The next tactic was the dare. If you really are a prophet, then prove it; bring us something to see. Surah al-Isra preserves their whole list of demands. We will not believe, they said, until you split a spring of water out of this ground; until you own a garden of palms and grapes with rivers bursting through it, grapes being the one luxury Makkah's rock could never grow; until you drop the sky on us in fragments; until you bring Allah and the angels here in front of us. And Allah taught him ﷺ a single sentence for the entire catalogue.

Or you have a house of ornament or you ascend into the sky. And [even then], we will not believe in your ascension until you bring down to us a book we may read." Say, "Exalted is my Lord! Was I ever but a human messenger?"Qur'an 17:93

So why were the dares refused? The Sheikh gives the reasons in order. First, they were not all refused. Makkah asked for a sign in the heavens and was given the moon itself, split in two before their eyes, one half on each side of the mountain, and they called it sorcery on their vision.

The Hour has come near, and the moon has split [in two]. And if they see a sign, they turn away and say, "Passing magic."Qur'an 54:1-2

Second, the greatest miracle was already in their ears every time the Prophet ﷺ opened his mouth. Every other sign in history expired with the people who saw it; the Qur'an is the one miracle that renews itself for every generation that hears it, which is why no other was needed. Third, Allah knew the asking was stubbornness and not searching. Were the angels themselves to come down, and the dead to rise and speak, and every created thing be gathered before their faces, they still would not believe; Allah says as much, knowing what they would do with a sign before they do it.

And there is a heavier reason, with a warning folded inside it. With Allah, a granted sign becomes a verdict: witness the very miracle you demanded, reject it anyway, and the punishment comes down with no second chance. The only reason Allah held the signs back, the Qur'an says, is that the peoples before had demanded them, then denied them, and been destroyed. So when Quraysh begged the Prophet ﷺ to turn Safa into solid gold, and Safa then stood several times the size of the rise you see today, gold enough to drown the markets of the world, he ﷺ felt that this time they might genuinely be sincere, and he made the dua. The answer came down, in a report the Sheikh grades authentic. If you wish, Safa becomes gold, and if they then disbelieve, they are destroyed where they stand, for Allah does not deal in games; or, if you wish, the door of mercy and repentance stays open. And the Prophet ﷺ chose. Leave the door open.

Sit with that choice, because the Sheikh does. A mountain of gold, traded away for nothing more than the bare chance that his own tormentors might one day be guided, and the door he ﷺ held open that day is the very one their sons and grandsons would walk through.

One word

The seventh tactic was the deal, and it opened with a theological compromise of the kind that always sounds so reasonable in a council chamber: let us simply alternate. One day we all worship your God; the next, you worship ours. Society is stitched back together, everyone keeps his face, the converts stop tearing families apart. The answer came down with no soft edge at all, a short surah of pure and total separation.

Say, "O disbelievers, I do not worship what you worship. Nor are you worshippers of what I worship. Nor will I be a worshipper of what you worship. Nor will you be worshippers of what I worship. For you is your religion, and for me is my religion."Qur'an 109:1-6

Pause on the puzzle in it, because the Sheikh does. The Quraysh did worship Allah, over the heads of their idols; how then can the surah tell them you do not worship what I worship? Because worship routed through idols, the Sheikh explains, is not worship of Allah at all. And the surah's strange doubled refrain, which has drawn some ten scholarly opinions, carries a precision he unpacks through Ibn Taymiyyah's reading: the first pair denies any sharing in the One who is worshipped, the second any sharing in the way He is worshipped. Their rites, Allah testifies elsewhere, were whistling and clapping around His House. Object and method, both sealed off. There is no timeshare in tawhid.

So they tried once more, this time face to face, through a delegation: name your price, they said, and all of this stops. And he ﷺ answered: I want one word from you. Abu Lahab leapt at the apparent bargain, hearing one word and thinking one small condition: one word? We will give you ten! And then the word came. La ilaha illa Allah. And the council recoiled, in the very phrase the Qur'an preserved of them.

Has he made the gods [only] one God? Indeed, this is a curious thing."Qur'an 38:5

One word was the one thing all their wealth was offered to avoid.

Everything a man could want

The most famous offer of all came through Utbah ibn Rabi'ah, an elder of the wider house of Abd Manaf, a distant cousin of the Prophet's ﷺ own line, wealthy and weighty and, by Makkah's standards, a genuinely decent man. Remember the name, the Sheikh says, because it returns: this is the man in whose garden the bleeding Prophet ﷺ will one day rest on the road back from Ta'if, and the man about whom, on the morning of Badr, the Prophet ﷺ will say that if there is any good left in the Quraysh they will listen to the man on the red camel, as Utbah gallops between the lines begging his people not to kill their own brothers and fathers, before he is shouted down and dies in the duels that open that battle. A man of real wisdom, the Sheikh observes, spending it all on the wrong side.

In the council, with the chiefs trading curses and dead-end schemes, it is Utbah who asks the question nobody had asked: has anyone actually tried to negotiate with the man? He is my nephew, more or less; send me. He found the Prophet ﷺ sitting alone by the Kaaba and began gently: my nephew, you know your lineage and your standing among us. Then the knife slid in. You have split your people, shattered their unity, made them a laughingstock among the Arabs. Are you better, or your father Abdullah? Are you better, or your grandfather Abdul Muttalib? They are trick questions, and the Prophet ﷺ answered them with silence. The Sheikh holds that silence up as wisdom our own arguments badly need. A believer cannot lie, and the true answer, that yes, he ﷺ was better, would have been blasphemy in Utbah's jahili ears and only set the room aflame; so he ﷺ refused the bait. We keep one face in private and public both, the Sheikh teaches, but that does not oblige us to answer every trap, especially when the questioner could never receive the true answer. Utbah pressed on with an Arabic idiom for a city about to tear itself apart: we are all waiting for the cry of the pregnant woman, the scream that the civil war has begun. He was right, the Sheikh notes quietly; it was only a few years off.

Then came the menu, the complete catalogue of everything Utbah's world believed a man could want. If it is wealth, we will pool our fortunes until you are the richest man among us. If it is honor, we will make you our king, a crown the proud Quraysh had never granted anyone. If it is women, name the one you want and she is yours. And if some spirit has touched you, we will spend everything on physicians until you are cured. Money, power, women, and a doctor's note. He finished. And the Prophet ﷺ, who had not interrupted him once, asked: are you done, Abu al-Walid? Anything more? No. Then listen to me.

He ﷺ began to recite from the opening of Surah Fussilat, and you can watch Utbah change as the verses come: the lounging ease goes out of him, his hands brace on the ground behind him, his breath shortens. The Sheikh begs us to go home and read this surah ourselves and feel the tempo build. Then came the verse of warning.

But if they turn away, then say, "I have warned you of a thunderbolt like the thunderbolt [that struck] 'Aad and Thamud.Qur'an 41:13

And Utbah lunged forward and clapped his hand over the Prophet's ﷺ mouth, begging him: I beg you, by Allah, and by the kinship between us, stop. He could not bear to hear the destruction named over his own city.

He walked back to the men who had sent him and gave Quraysh the most clear-eyed counsel it ever received and ever ignored. Leave this man alone. I have heard speech the like of which I have never heard; I could not grasp all of it, but he is going to be a man of enormous consequence. If the other Arabs cut him down, other hands will have rid you of him and your own are clean. And if he prevails over them, his kingdom is your kingdom, his honor your honor, his victory your victory. They laughed at their own ambassador: he has bewitched you too, Abu al-Walid. But Utbah had only heard, decades early, what Allah had already written down.

We have certainly sent down to you a Book in which is your mention. Then will you not reason?Qur'an 21:10

Without this Book, the Sheikh closes the thought, the Arabs were headed for the footnotes of history. Because of it, they would carry light to the world and outrun Rome and Persia both. Utbah glimpsed the whole trade in a single recitation, and he could never again unsee it. He simply lacked the will to act on what he saw.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this day as a parade of villains safely defeated long ago. That would be to miss the question it puts to us. Look again at who testifies for the Qur'an across this single episode, and notice that not one of the witnesses is a friend. Al-Akhnas, in the dark before dawn: it seems to be the truth. Abu Jahl, of all men: how could we ever compete with revelation? Al-Walid, the poet laureate of the enemy, after one hearing: nothing rises above it. Utbah, sent to buy the Prophet ﷺ off with a kingdom: his victory is our victory. The whole opposition of the Quraysh, the Sheikh shows us, was never once a counterargument. It was a long confession, made through noise and fists and slander and gold, that no counterargument existed. They threw everything a civilization owns at one reciting voice, and the closest any of them came to refuting the Qur'an was to press a hand over the mouth reciting it.

So take the first lesson from the man with no tribe. Ibn Mas'ud had no clan, no protector, no name to pull out when the fists came, and he went anyway, on a single sentence: Allah will protect me. He came back bloodied and entirely unbroken, his only growth that day a deeper contempt for falsehood and not a trace of self-pity for his wounds. Most of us measure our courage by the protection we can count on. He measured his by Allah alone, and it held.

Then take the lesson the Prophet ﷺ teaches against every one of these tactics: let the Qur'an do the arguing. The chiefs crept out at night to hear it; the city's own poet swore it was beyond the speech of men and jinn; the ambassador begged for it to stop. Nobody in this entire episode actually resisted the recitation itself, only the pride and fear wrapped around their refusal to submit. We are quick to hand people our debate and slow to simply hand them the recitation and let it do what it did to al-Walid and Utbah. And learn his adab, which was a weapon too: he let Utbah finish every last word of the bribe before he answered, and met the trick questions with a silence no lie could corrupt. A believer keeps one face, owes no trap an answer, and never has to interrupt.

And then sit with the mountain of gold he ﷺ refused. Offered Safa turned to solid gold, enough to make him the richest man on earth, he saw the price underneath, that his tormentors would be destroyed the moment they rejected it, and chose instead to keep the door of mercy open over them. The man being cursed was quietly protecting his cursers from their own dare. This is the mercy that plays the long game, that would rather lose a mountain of gold than slam a door someone's grandchild might one day walk through, and walk through it they did. So take this day down into your ordinary life. Trust like the man with no tribe; argue less and recite more; keep your one face and your patience under insult; and when you are wronged, leave the door of mercy open the way he ﷺ left it, for you never know whose child will need it later. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, who stood unbought and unprovoked against every weapon Makkah owned, make the Qur'an the spring of our hearts, grant us the trust of Ibn Mas'ud and the patience of His Prophet ﷺ, and let us hear that beloved recitation with our own ears in Jannah.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 13: opposition from the Quraysh (Memphis Islamic Center, 2011). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (41:26, 17:110, 55:1-4, 93:1-5, 74:11-15, 74:18-25, 17:93, 54:1-2, 109:1-6, 38:5, 41:13, 21:10), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 14 of 104

The persecution of the weak

The ones with no tribe, and the God who took their side

By the time this evening of the seerah arrives, the dawah is years old and fully public, and Quraysh has been working steadily through its toolbox. They have tried mockery, and it failed. They have tried negotiation, dangling wealth and rank and a turn at the kingship, and it failed. And lately they have tried something stranger: borrowed riddles, fetched from the rabbis of distant Yathrib in the hope that a true prophet of Israel's line could trip up this Arab who claimed to be one. That tactic was the eighth, and it died as the others died. What Quraysh reached for next was the ninth, and it was the oldest weapon the strong have ever turned against the truth: pain. Not aimed at the Prophet ﷺ, whose uncle's protection still held, nor at Abu Bakr, who had a clan behind him, but aimed with cold precision at the believers whose blood no tribe was obliged to avenge. The slaves. The freedmen. The ones nobody would miss.

So this day belongs to the people history usually forgets. Bilal under the boulder. Khabbab under his own forge iron. The family of Yasir on the point of Abu Jahl's spear. Suhayb, the richest freedman in Makkah, robbed of every coin on the road north and arriving on his hands and knees. Dr. Yasir Qadhi hangs this whole heavy evening on a single Arabic maxim and asks his listeners to memorize it before the night is out: al-jaza min jins al-amal, the recompense matches the deed. Allah repays a person in his own currency: what you spend for Him comes back in kind, and so does what you spend against Him. Hold that sentence; it is the thread that will run through every story here, and it will turn an hour of unbearable cruelty into one of the most quietly triumphant chapters of the whole seerah.

Riddles from Yathrib, before the whips

Before the whips, the riddles. To understand them you have to understand what the Arabs of Makkah did not know. Prophethood was simply not their world. They knew poetry, trade, and idols; but the very category of a man who speaks for God had grown faint among them, because the legacy of Ibrahim had faded to almost nothing in their valley. The only neighbors who still spoke the language of prophets, who could tell a true messenger from a false one, were the Jews. Dr. Yasir Qadhi underlines the point: recognizing a prophet was a Jewish competence, not an Arab one, and so when Quraysh wanted this man tested, they sent north to the people of the Book. Give us questions, they asked, that only a real prophet of Israel could answer, and we will unmask him.

One delegation came back with a challenge built on lineage: ask him about Yaqub, who is Israel himself, and about his father and his sons, and what passed within that family. These were the private affairs of the children of Israel, and an Arab merchant could not possibly know them. The answer arrived not as a sentence but as an entire surah, Surah Yusuf, the inner struggle of Yaqub's household laid open in detail the questioners themselves could not have supplied, and Allah sealed it with a verse that named exactly what had happened:

That is from the news of the unseen which We reveal, [O Muhammad], to you. And you were not with them when they put together their plan while they conspired.Qur'an 12:102

A man who had never been there had just told Israel's children their own family secrets. A second delegation carried three questions, and these are the famous ones: ask him about young men who entered a cave and slept across long ages; about a traveler who crossed the earth from east to west and saw wonders; and about the ruh, the spirit, what it is and where it comes from. The report comes through Tirmidhi with a slight weakness in its chain, the kind the scholars tolerate for a narration like this, and the Sheikh lets the caveat travel honestly with it. The Prophet ﷺ, certain of his Lord, answered: come back tomorrow and I will tell you. He did not say insha'Allah. And revelation went silent, not a day but some two weeks, while Makkah jeered that whatever spirit had spoken to him had now plainly deserted him. The pause was itself the teaching: there is the Rabb and there is the slave, and even the most beloved slave waits at his Master's door without a guarantee. And the word slave is no insult here. Allah Himself calls His Prophet ﷺ His abd, His slave, at the very summits of the Qur'an, four times in all, and means it as the height of honor, for there is no rank a human being can climb higher than being the true worshipper of his Lord. Then Surah al-Kahf came down and answered everything: the sleepers of the cave, in detail the rabbis had never possessed, and the world-crossing traveler, Dhul-Qarnayn (who was not, the Sheikh pauses to add, Alexander the Great, a known pagan tutored in the gods of the Greeks by Aristotle himself; whether prophet or just king, Allah knows best, but he was not the Macedonian).

The third question was the trap inside the trap. The rabbis knew the nature of the ruh is knowledge Allah has kept to Himself, so a confident lecture on the soul would have exposed a false claimant on the spot. The answer that descended was the only true one there is:

And they ask you, [O Muhammad], about the soul. Say, "The soul is of the affair [i.e., concern] of my Lord. And you [i.e., mankind] have not been given of knowledge except a little."Qur'an 17:85

Fourteen centuries on, no laboratory has improved on it. Every clever weapon Quraysh owned had now been spent and broken. What they reached for next needed no cleverness at all.

The architecture of cruelty

To feel the horror of what follows, you have to understand the machinery that made it possible. Makkah had no state. No police, no court, no magistrate to appeal to. Your safety in that society was worth exactly as much as the tribe obliged to avenge you, and not one dirham more. The Prophet ﷺ himself stood inside that shelter, and the Sheikh lingers over the words Ibn Mas'ud chose to describe it: Allah protected him through his uncle. Not Abu Talib protected him, but Allah protected him through his uncle, the old man only ever the instrument in a hand far higher than his own; and this is the wisdom of why Abu Talib was left a pagan to the end, the God-given wall around the dawah in its most fragile years. Abu Bakr likewise had his people. But the full, unwitnessed weight of Quraysh came down on the believers nobody was bound to avenge: the slaves, and the mawali. A mawla is a freed slave, bound by a client relationship to a former master, more liberty than a slave but an allegiance owed, and a protection that could be revoked like a cancelled debt. Between the slave and the mawla you have the whole class of people whose lives, in the brutal accounting of that society, were cheap.

How bad did it get? Sa'id ibn Jubayr put exactly that question to Ibn Abbas, a living authority in an age before the books were compiled. They were beaten, Ibn Abbas answered, and starved, and denied water until they could no longer sit upright, until a man would be asked, is al-Lat your god, is al-Uzza your god, and he would say yes, just to make it stop. Until an insect crawled past, and they would ask, is this insect your god, and he would say yes, this too, anything, only stop. Hold the horror and the mercy of it together, because the religion does: Islam forgives every syllable of that. A word pried off a tongue by fire does not touch a heart that stayed standing underneath it, and before this evening ends, Allah Himself will write that mercy into the Qur'an.

The engineer of the program had a name, and the Prophet ﷺ gave him a title: Abu Jahl, the Firaun of this ummah, viler in this story than even Abu Lahab. His method moved precisely by class. A convert from the nobility could not be touched, for his tribe would answer the blow, so Abu Jahl gave him taunting and shame. A merchant who lived by trade was strangled where he breathed: a boycott, no one buys from you, no one sells to you. And a slave, who had no one at all, was handed to the mob, with Abu Jahl lending his own hands to the beating. Ibn Mas'ud counted seven who wore their Islam openly in those first days: the Prophet ﷺ, Abu Bakr, Ammar and his mother Sumayyah, Suhayb, Bilal, and al-Miqdad. Of these, the unprotected were seized, wrapped in coats of iron, staked out on burning sand, and every last one of them, in the end, gave the torturers some word of what they demanded. Every one except a single Abyssinian slave. And who among us, the Sheikh asks, would dare be surprised at the others? Allah forgave them all. We would have broken long before they did. The wonder of this night is not that men bent. It is the one who would not.

Ahad, Ahad

His name was Bilal ibn Rabah, radiyallahu anhu, an Abyssinian slave of Umayyah ibn Khalaf, one of the cruelest men in Makkah, and in that city's racial arithmetic an African slave stood on the very lowest rung there was. Ibn Mas'ud explained why the full storm gathered over him: Bilal had stopped pricing his soul. He counted it as worth nothing beside Allah, was willing to give it up entirely, and so there was nothing left in him for a torturer to bargain with. A man who will not name a price cannot be bought at any, and because he would not bend by a single word, they did to him what they did to no one else.

Umayyah had a sadist's patience. He would take Bilal out at the hottest hour, pin him to the floor of the valley, have a great boulder rolled onto his chest, and leave him there, no water, no shade, while the sun climbed to its height. On other days Bilal was handed to the ruffians of Makkah, the idle and cruel that every city keeps, who looped a rope around his neck and dragged him up and down the lanes for sport. And through all of it Makkah heard one word coming back, from under the rock, from behind the rope, from a throat cracked with thirst: Ahad. Ahad. One. One. He was not pleading and not arguing. He was reciting the oneness of his Lord at the precise place where it cost the most.

The Sheikh stops over two details it would be easy to race past. The first is economic, and startling: a slave cost more than a camel, more than a house, a fortune in any age's money. When Umayyah tortured Bilal he was methodically destroying his own most valuable property, taking a loss on it day after day, which tells you the depth of a hatred willing to pay that much. The second is darker: an entire city watched a human being dragged through its streets by the neck and did not blink, and the only way a society reaches that point is by first deciding the victim is not quite a human being at all. Reclassify a group as less than people, and anything may be done to them in daylight while the neighbors look away. The Sheikh draws the line forward without flinching, to the death camps of the last century, where the same machinery ran: first the dehumanizing, then the atrocity it licenses. This is exactly what Islam came to tear out by the root. No nobility of lineage, no nobility of skin. The whole jahili ranking, demolished. The witnesses never forgot: Hassan ibn Thabit, still a pagan, marveled that Bilal was somehow alive; a chief of Quraysh remembered him on rocks so hot raw meat would have cooked, saying still, I reject al-Lat and al-Uzza, and I believe in Allah; and Urwah recorded the simple final tally of the long contest. Through every day of it, Bilal never gave them even one word they wanted.

The recompense matches the deed

Now the maxim begins to pay out, and this is where the night turns. Al-jaza min jins al-amal, the recompense matches the deed. It is not a hadith, though many assume it is; it is a rule the scholars trace through the whole fabric of the religion. Do good, and Allah returns you good of the same kind; do evil, and the evil comes back in its own coin. So ask of Bilal: what, exactly, had he spent? A voice, crying the oneness of Allah in the one place and at the one cost where it could buy him nothing but pain.

Then look at what Allah did with that voice. When the call to prayer was given to this ummah in Madinah, shown first to a companion in a dream, the Prophet ﷺ said: teach it to Bilal, for he has the most beautiful voice among you. The throat that had croaked Ahad under a boulder became the appointed caller of the whole ummah, the muadhdhin of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ himself. And consider who stood in that voice's audience: for more than a decade in Madinah, five times every day, the Prophet ﷺ listened to Bilal call. Now place that beside another of his teachings. In a hadith in Ibn Majah, he said that everything within earshot of a muadhdhin, wet or dry, living or stone, jinn and man and tree, will stand as a witness for him on the Day of Judgment, which is why he told even a lone shepherd in the empty hills to call the adhan aloud, so the wilderness itself would testify for him. Now put one and one together, as the Sheikh asks. The man whose voice is heard must be witnessed for by all who hear it; and the man who heard Bilal's voice, day in and day out for years, was the Prophet ﷺ. The witness list being assembled for that tortured slave is not like yours and mine.

And then the scene that closes the circle. The eighth year of the hijrah, Makkah conquered, the idols toppling inside the Ka'bah, and the Prophet ﷺ gives a command: Bilal, climb onto the roof of the House of Allah. From the top of the Ka'bah, over the same valley where boys had dragged him by the neck through the dust, the same voice gives the first adhan those sacred precincts had ever heard. Tortured at the very bottom of the city; raised to the very top of it. No wonder the Prophet ﷺ said that if people only knew the reward in the call to prayer and the first row, and could find no other way to settle who got them, they would draw lots; no wonder he said the muadhdhins will have the longest necks on the Day of Judgment, the old Arabic image for the one to whom every eye must look up in honor. Every muadhdhin on earth, until the end of time, is standing inside a tortured slave's inheritance.

The blacksmith and the fire

Khabbab ibn al-Aratt, radiyallahu anhu, was among the first ten to enter Islam: an Arab slave, which in that race-ranked society bought marginally less savage treatment than Bilal's, and a swordsmith by trade, hammering blades for the fighting men of Makkah. He was owned by a woman, Umm Anmar. When she learned what her blacksmith had become, she gathered a gang of Quraysh and sent them to his workplace. Khabbab remembered coming back to find them waiting, and they pressed and taunted him until he confessed his Islam, then beat him so thoroughly he came to bruised and bloodied with no memory of how it ended. After that, his own forge supplied the instrument. Umm Anmar would take the iron her slave had just drawn glowing from the fire, the very metal he had been shaping into swords, and press it to his back. And Khabbab could not lift a hand, for to strike a Qurashi woman was to forfeit his life on the spot.

There is a hard symmetry in what came of him. Decades later, in Madinah, Umar ibn al-Khattab, by then khalifah, asked Khabbab to tell him what Umm Anmar used to do. Khabbab said nothing. He turned around and lifted his shirt, and Umar said: I have never seen anything like what I see today, and seated him beside himself in honor. The symmetry is in who was asking, because in the secret early years it had been this same Khabbab, a slave with almost nothing to give, who slipped into the house of Fatima bint al-Khattab to teach her the Qur'an, the very thread Allah would pull to bring her brother, Umar himself, into Islam. The man Umar honored for his scarred back had once carried the faith into Umar's own family.

And the fire went back to its sender. The Prophet ﷺ passed the shop one day while the screams were coming out of it, saw Umm Anmar at work with the heated iron, and raised his hand: O Allah, help Khabbab against his enemy. Within days she was seized by a disease that left her panting and crawling about her house like a rabid animal, her senses gone, and the doctors of Makkah prescribed the one remedy they knew: cauterization, the burning of the skin. She died under the burning iron. The recompense, again, paid back in the deed's own coin. And a teaching rides alongside it, for the Prophet ﷺ taught that no one may punish with fire except the Creator of fire; even a lawful executioner is forbidden it, because no torment is crueler.

It was this same Khabbab who, in the hadith Bukhari preserves, came to the Prophet ﷺ as he sat in the shade of the Ka'bah and could bear no more: how long? Will you not ask Allah for us? And the Prophet ﷺ, who had been reclining, sat upright and answered in three movements the seerah never forgets. The believers before you were sawn in two and torn with iron combs and did not leave their religion. By Allah, He will complete this affair until a woman travels alone from Hadramawt to San'a fearing nothing but Allah, and the wolf over her sheep. And then the rebuke, gentle and immense: but you are being hasty. Khabbab lived to test the whole promise. He marched in every expedition and drew one of the highest stipends in Umar's state, for Umar built his pay scale on precedence in Islam, a scale that placed Umar himself below the very slaves he had once watched bleed. Yet on his deathbed he wept, and not from fear of meeting Allah. He wept at the comfort around him, terrified his reward had been paid out early, here, in soft living, while his companions had gone to Allah having tasted none of this world's sweetness; and when they brought his shroud, a fine broad cloth, he remembered Hamza, buried in a sheet so short that to cover his face was to bare his feet. The men Makkah could not break with rock or iron spent their final breaths afraid of nothing but ease.

The first family of martyrs

Among all the stories of this evening, one belongs not to a man but to a whole household, and the Sheikh tells it as the one that owns him personally. Yasir, his wife Sumayyah, and their sons Abdullah and Ammar, radiyallahu anhum, were a client family bound to a Makkan tribe, with no blood of their own in the city to make their suffering costly, and every one of them had believed. And here is what sets their story apart. Everyone else suffered alone; this family was tortured together, in front of one another, a husband made to watch his wife, a mother made to watch her sons, each one's agony doubled by being made to witness the rest. Dr. Yasir Qadhi calls it the nightmare of nightmares, and then tells why this household has a claim on him: his own father named him for this family, Yasir, and he in turn named his own son Ammar. Fourteen centuries later, scholars are still signing their children into this house.

The Prophet ﷺ would pass them as they were staked out under torture, and could do nothing to stop it. Feel what that helplessness must have cost him ﷺ, to walk past a family being destroyed for believing in him and have no power in his hand to save them. What he could give them was a sentence, preserved in an authentic hadith and among the most famous of the seerah: Sabran, Al Yasir, fa inna mawidakum al-Jannah. Patience, family of Yasir, for your appointed meeting place is Paradise. Notice what he did not promise: not rescue, not relief, not an end to the pain in this life. He promised them something larger and further away, and they believed him.

Yasir died first under the torture. The reports differ on how, whether dragged through the streets by ropes or torn apart between horses; the sources here are scant, the way history is always scant about what is done to slaves, and all that is certain is that Quraysh took some brutal pleasure in it. Then Sumayyah, grieving and unbroken, rebuked Abu Jahl to his face, a slave woman refusing the man who thought he owned her soul, and the Firaun of this ummah took a spear and drove it up through the most private part of her body, a killing so vile the books still relate it with an apology on the tongue. One after the other, Yasir and Sumayyah became the first two martyrs of Islam. And then the mob turned on Abdullah and killed him too.

Ammar was perhaps fifteen, perhaps younger. He had watched his father killed, his mother killed, his brother thrown into a ditch, and when they turned the iron on him the boy said the words they demanded, and, being the youngest and now alone, was let go. He ran to the house of al-Arqam and stood before the Prophet ﷺ sobbing, his parents' deaths almost blanked from his mind by a terror that felt worse than grief: Ya Rasulallah, I spoke words of shirk, what is to become of me now? The Prophet ﷺ asked one question: how do you find your heart? They could force my tongue, Ammar answered, they could change this, but they could not change this: I find my heart at peace, secure with iman. Then if they do it again, the Prophet ﷺ told him, you do it again. And heaven co-signed the ruling, sending down the verse that would shelter every tortured believer until the end of the world:

Whoever disbelieves in [i.e., denies] Allah after his belief... except for one who is forced [to renounce his religion] while his heart is secure in faith. But those who [willingly] open their breasts to disbelief, upon them is wrath from Allah, and for them is a great punishment;Qur'an 16:106

The exception was carved into the Qur'an forever: the one forced, while the heart stays firm, bears no sin at all. Ammar grew into one of the giants of the companions. The Prophet ﷺ said iman had filled him to the very top, up past his heart to his neck, overflowing; he called him Ibn Sumayyah, his mother's son, the man who, shown two roads, always took the truer of the two. And one prophecy followed him down a long life: the rebel party will kill you, the Prophet ﷺ had told him. Forty years later, when the ummah tore itself apart in the strife between Ali and Muawiyah, the old man fell at the Battle of Siffin, fighting on the side of Ali, and the hadith quietly settled which side had stood nearer the truth. Keep here exactly the manners of Ahlus-Sunnah the Sheikh keeps. Both camps intended good. We say no evil of any companion of the Prophet ﷺ, radiyallahu anhum; we leave what passed between them to the mercy of Allah, who will forgive them. And yet, for the sake of truth and never of partisanship, with the clarity only hindsight allows: the closer cause was Ali's, and the body of Ibn Sumayyah marked the line.

Do not send them away, and the most profitable sale

Three of this evening's names moved through Makkah together as a band of friends, young men in their twenties and thirties, and the sira keeps mentioning them shoulder to shoulder: Ammar, Suhayb, and Bilal. One day Abu Jahl and a circle of the chiefs were deep in conversation with the Prophet ﷺ, and he ﷺ allowed himself to hope their hearts were finally softening, when the three happened to pass nearby. The mood curdled on the instant. If what you brought were genuine, the chiefs objected, would the likes of these have reached it before us? Send these people away, and then perhaps we will sit with you, but we will not enter a religion as the equals of slaves. The answer did not come from the Prophet ﷺ at all. Allah Himself answered, and He came down on the side of the slaves:

And do not send away those who call upon their Lord morning and afternoon, seeking His face [i.e., favor]. Not upon you is anything of their account and not upon them is anything of your account. So were you to send them away, you would [then] be of the wrongdoers.Qur'an 6:52

Weigh exactly what is on that scale. On one side, the chiefs of Makkah, with their gold and swords and the whole following of the city, offering their entire allegiance as the prize. On the other, a freedman's bow, a blacksmith's branded back, a strangled cry of Ahad from beneath a rock. And the scale of heaven comes down, flatly and forever, on the side of the weak. To dismiss them, Allah told His own beloved Messenger ﷺ, would be to join the wrongdoers. The poor and the enslaved of this day are not a footnote to the seerah. They are the reason this verse exists.

And one of those three friends bought himself the last and largest place in this day's ledger. Suhayb ar-Rumi, the Roman, was never Roman at all. He was an Arab boy from Iraq, carried off in a Byzantine raid when he was small and raised so long in Roman lands that Latin had crowded the Arabic out of his mouth and only the memory of his roots remained. He worked his way back toward home, came to Makkah as a slave, and landed in the household of Abdullah ibn Jud'an, one of the gentler masters, who saw the quick and literate mind in his slave, made him his business manager, and freed him in his will. In time Suhayb became the wealthiest freedman in all of Makkah. His own torture was lighter than the others', for his late master had treated him well, though Abu Jahl and men like him made sure he was not spared entirely. But what Quraysh could not forgive him, in the end, was his fortune.

When Suhayb finally slipped out to make the hijrah, riders of Quraysh overtook him on the road, and he turned in the saddle and gave them the only speech he had left. I am the finest archer among you, he told them, and by Allah you will not lay a hand on me until every arrow in this quiver has found flesh, and then until my sword breaks on your bones. Come, ten of you against one of me. They chose a different arithmetic. You came to us with nothing, they said, a penniless slave, and you grew rich among us; the wealth is ours, not yours. Leave it and go. And the Sheikh notes how old this is, how the same resentment still circles the stranger who arrives with empty hands and prospers, as though his success were a theft from those who watched it. So Suhayb told them where his fortune lay buried, handed over even the camel beneath him, and walked. The books of sira record that the only companion known to have made the hijrah on foot came crawling at last into Quba on his hands and knees, emaciated, caked in dust, very nearly finished.

The Prophet ﷺ wiped the dust from him with his own hands, gave him food and water, and smiled before Suhayb could explain a thing. Rabiha al-bay, Suhayb. The sale has profited. The transaction was the best you ever made. Suhayb could only stare: no one could have brought you that news yet, ya Rasulallah, no one but Jibril. And when revelation opened again in Madinah, the very first that came down, Surah al-Baqarah carried his receipt:

And of the people is he who sells himself, seeking means to the approval of Allah. And Allah is Kind to [His] servants.Qur'an 2:207

Among all people, there is the one who sells his whole self, asking nothing in return but the pleasure of his Lord. And here the threads of the whole evening draw together into a single ledger. Bilal sold his body to the rock and the rope. Khabbab sold the skin of his back to the iron. Sumayyah and Yasir and Abdullah sold their very lives. Suhayb sold every coin he had ever earned. This entire day of the seerah is an accounting of those purchases, scream by scream and dirham by dirham, and the verdict it returns on every single one is the same. Not one of them, in the books of Allah, made a losing trade.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this evening as a catalog of someone else's suffering, safely sealed in the deep past, and to close the book moved but untouched. That would be to miss the questions it puts directly to us, and the Sheikh draws each of them straight out of the wounds.

The first is about the heart, and where iman actually lives. Almost every believer tortured on this day eventually said what Quraysh demanded, and Allah forgave every one of them; the verse revealed for Ammar shelters every forced tongue behind which the heart stayed at peace with faith. So be slow, very slow, to judge anyone's breaking point, including your own. We who have never felt the heated iron are quick to imagine we would have held where they bent, when the truth is that we would have broken long before any of them did. The wonder of faith is not that the body fails under fire. It is that the heart, in the right person, does not.

The second is the maxim, now that we have watched it pay out again and again. Al-jaza min jins al-amal, the recompense matches the deed. Bilal's strangled Ahad came back as the adhan from the rooftop of the Ka'bah; Khabbab's burned back came back to his tormentor as her own doctors' fire. Allah repays in kind, in this life often and in the next without fail. So look hard at what you are spending, and in what coin, because it is coming back to you in the same.

The third is the warning the Sheikh refuses to soften. Cruelty becomes possible at the exact moment a human being stops being seen as one. Makkah could watch a man hauled through the streets by a rope and feel nothing, because it had first decided he was not quite a man; and that same mechanism runs under every atrocity from that valley to the camps of the last century. Islam answered it at the root, abolishing the nobility of blood and of color outright. So watch your own eyes. Whenever a whole class of people begins to shrink in them, to become a category instead of a soul, this day is the alarm bell, and you should hear it ringing. And running alongside it is the fourth lesson, in the verse heaven sent down to protect three slaves from the chiefs of a city: Allah does not trade His weak servants for your powerful ones. Any gathering, any dawah, any heart that courts the strong by quietly sidelining the poor is bargaining against revelation itself, and will lose.

There is a last question, the one Khabbab left ringing in the shade of the Ka'bah, the one the seerah will answer on another night: why? Why would Allah let His most beloved servants bleed like this, when a single word from Him could have handed them the victory on the first day? That answer is promised its own evening. But this day has already proved the half of it we most need to carry: that not one scream went unheard, not one burn went unrepaid, not one forced word was ever held against a faithful heart. So take this evening down into your own ordinary, comfortable life. The next time the adhan reaches you, hear the inheritance folded inside it, a tortured man's reward made audible five times a day over every city on earth. O Allah, You heard Bilal when he said One; You saw Ammar's heart while his tongue was breaking; You kept Your appointment with Sumayyah and Yasir at the meeting place You promised them. Make our hearts firm without ever requiring their trials of us. Never let us trade Your weak servants for the approval of the strong. Write us among those who sell themselves seeking nothing but Your face. And send Your peace upon Your Messenger ﷺ, who would not send the slaves away, and who walked past the fire he could not put out, holding only a promise of Paradise, until the day Allah completed the affair, exactly as he said He would.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 14: the torture and persecution of the weak (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (12:102, 17:85, 16:106, 6:52, 2:207), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry a weaker chain, the Sheikh's own weighing of the narrations has been followed.

The Seerah · 15 of 104

Harm Reaches the Prophet ﷺ, and the First Hijrah

The cost of one sajdah, and a ship to a just king

The last gathering belonged to the tortured. Bilal flattened under a boulder on the burning sand, Khabbab branded with iron from his owner's own furnace, the enslaved believers whom Makkah's tribal code did not cover, because a slave had no clan to avenge him and so could be broken in the open. They bled because they had no one. The question Dr. Yasir Qadhi opens this day with follows so naturally you almost ask it before he does: and what of the Prophet ﷺ himself? He was a nobleman of Quraysh, a Hashimi, a man with a clan at his back. Did the violence ever actually reach him, or did his standing keep him untouched while lesser men paid?

The honest answer is layered, and the Sheikh refuses to flatten it. Physically, yes, the Prophet ﷺ and the Quraysh believers as a class were relatively protected; nothing here approaches what was done to Bilal. But relatively protected is not the same as immune, and these early Makkan years hold a string of incidents in which harm came to him directly, building eventually toward open talk of assassination. Here is the shape the Sheikh draws across all of it, the lens for everything that follows: sometimes, by a wisdom He alone knows, Allah shielded His beloved ﷺ with wonders no eye could explain, and sometimes, by a wisdom He alone knows, He let the blow land. Both happen on this day. Neither is an accident. A protection you can see and a protection that looks like its opposite are both, in the end, His hand.

The sajdah the angels guarded

Abu Jahl was boasting, as he loved to do, in the nadi: the open air council beside the Kaaba where the elders of Quraysh sat to talk and be seen, the city's parliament. He swore an oath to his peers. By al-Lat and al-Uzza, if I see Muhammad praying there one more time, I will plant my foot on his neck while he is in sajdah and grind his face into the dust. It was not an idle threat. Most believers prayed hidden in their homes, but the Prophet ﷺ was among the very few who prayed openly at the Kaaba, in full view, and that openness was exactly what enraged them, so the chance came quickly.

The scene is carried to us by Abu Huraira. The Prophet ﷺ came, stood, and went down into prostration, and Abu Jahl moved toward him to make good on his oath. Then the watchers saw something they could not account for. The tyrant stopped short, backing away, shoving at the empty air with both hands, fending off a thing none of them could see. When he returned to the circle they pressed him: what happened to your oath, we saw you reach out, why did you turn? And Abu Jahl, shaken, told them the truth as he had lived it: between me and him was a trench of fire, and a terror, and wings. When the Prophet ﷺ was told of it afterward, he named the wings. They were angels. Had Abu Jahl taken one more step, he said, they would have torn him apart, limb from limb.

Heaven did not let the scene pass without a word, and here the Sheikh shows you how a surah can lock onto a single street. The very chapter that had opened all of revelation in the cave of Hira, the surah of Iqra, its first five verses the first words ever sent down, received its conclusion over this confrontation, and once you know the story every line drops into its slot. The one who forbids a servant from praying: there he stands in the flesh. Does he not know that Allah sees the whole of it? Then an image desert Arabs felt in their hands before they understood it in their minds. Seize a beast by the forelock, the nasiyah, the tuft at the front of the skull, and the entire animal goes wherever you drag it, helpless. A lying, sinning forelock. Let him call his nadi, the very council he sat boasting in; Allah will summon the angels of Hell. And then the surah turns from the tyrant to the beloved ﷺ and settles the whole matter by ignoring the threat entirely.

Have you seen the one who forbids A servant when he prays? Have you seen if he denies and turns away - Does he not know that Allāh sees? No! If he does not desist, We will surely drag him by the forelock, a lying, sinning forelock. Then let him call his associates; We will call the angels of Hell. No! Do not obey him. But prostrate and draw near [to Allāh].Qur'an 96:9-10, 96:13-19

Do not obey him. Prostrate, and draw near. The man who tried to stop the sajdah is handed to the angels of Hell, and the one who made the sajdah is told to keep making it, and to come nearer to his Lord by exactly the act they were trying to stop. The threat had no answer worth giving. The answer was worship.

Choked at the house of Allah, and a carcass on his back

A generation later there lived a young man named Urwah ibn az-Zubayr, born just too late to ever meet the Prophet ﷺ whose memory surrounded his whole childhood, nephew of Aisha herself. Like any child raised on a story he wanted the eyewitness account, so he went to one of the men who had been there, Abdullah ibn Amr ibn al-As, with a blunt question: tell me the worst thing you ever saw the pagans do to him. If you had lived then, the Sheikh notes, you would have asked exactly this.

The answer was this. The Prophet ﷺ was praying beside the Kaaba when Uqbah ibn Abi Mu'ayt came up behind him, pulled the garment off his own back, looped it around the Prophet's ﷺ neck, and twisted it tight. He throttled him there, at the house of Allah, while the crowd watched and did nothing, until word reached Abu Bakr: your companion is being killed. He came running, threw himself on Uqbah, beat him back, and cried out the whole time, will you kill a man for saying, my Lord is Allah? The Sheikh lingers on what became of that one sentence, because its afterlife is astonishing. The cry Abu Bakr raised in the dust stands now in the Qur'an, word for word, in the mouth of the believing man of Pharaoh's household who rose to shield Musa against the same murderous logic, and it is recited in prayer to this hour.

And a believing man from the family of Pharaoh who concealed his faith said, "Do you kill a man [merely] because he says, 'My Lord is Allāh' while he has brought you clear proofs from your Lord? And if he should be lying, then upon him is [the consequence of] his lie; but if he should be truthful, there will strike you some of what he promises you. Indeed, Allāh does not guide one who is a transgressor and a liar.Qur'an 40:28

Abu Bakr is one of only two companions the Sheikh notes Allah quotes by name in His Book, the man Allah Himself calls, in the verse of the cave, the Prophet's ﷺ companion. And the words he spoke to save the man he loved were the very words revelation had already placed in the mouth of the best believer of Pharaoh's court. He did not know he was reciting scripture.

But the worst single thing came later, and Abdullah ibn Mas'ud saw it himself; Bukhari preserved his telling. He watched from the edge, rigid and helpless, because he too was of the slave class, and one move would have cost him his life. The Prophet ﷺ was praying at the Kaaba again, because they always chose the most public stage; the entire point was humiliation. A camel had been slaughtered the day before, its carcass left rotting at the refuse ground outside the city, and Abu Jahl threw down a dare to his circle: which of you will go to that carcass, bring back its filth, and dump it on Muhammad's back when he goes into sajdah? The worst of them rose. Uqbah ibn Abi Mu'ayt, again, a robed nobleman of Quraysh plunging his hands into a rotting body and carrying the reeking entrails back through his own streets. Whatever clung to his hands, what lived in his heart was filthier. He waited for the prostration, then emptied the whole load onto the back of the Prophet ﷺ as he lay before his Lord. The weight pinned him down; he could not lift his head. And the men of Quraysh laughed until they fell against one another, helpless with it.

He ﷺ stayed in sajdah, because he could not rise, and someone, his name lost to us, some last ember of mercy, ran to fetch Fatima. She was a small girl then, eight or nine years old. She came running and weeping, and with her own small hands she pulled the filth off her father's back while the men of her city laughed on. He finished his prayer. Then he stood, faced them, and lifted his finger toward the heavens, and the laughter died as they watched. He prayed against them by name, each name three times: Abu Jahl ibn Hisham, Utbah and Shaybah the sons of Rabi'ah, al-Walid, Umayyah ibn Khalaf, Uqbah ibn Abi Mu'ayt, and a seventh whose name Ibn Mas'ud could no longer recall. The blood drained from every face. And Ibn Mas'ud swore to the sequel: by the One who sent Muhammad ﷺ with the truth, I saw every one of those seven men dead on the day of Badr, their bodies dragged to the well.

Now pull back and look at the attackers as a kind, because here the Sheikh hands you one of his signature observations. The enemies of the Prophet ﷺ came in two broad sorts. There were honorable opponents, dangerous and angry but who kept it above the belt and never stooped to torturing the helpless: men like Umar ibn al-Khattab before his Islam, Khalid ibn al-Walid, Abu Sufyan. And there were the vile: Abu Jahl, and Uqbah, perhaps the lowest of all. Run both lists to the ends of their lives and the pattern stuns you. The honorable enemies, very nearly to a man, were eventually guided to Islam and became its lions. The vile ones died on their hatred, most of them face down in the well of Badr, guided to nothing. How a person fights tells you what he is made of, and a residual nobility even in an enemy is soil in which guidance can still grow, while a heart that will torture the defenseless has already sealed itself.

Why the best of people are tested

Now stop, because the Sheikh stops, and asks the question this whole season of suffering forces up out of the heart. We believe this man ﷺ is the most beloved of all Allah's creation. We believe his companions were the finest body of believers the world has held. So why? Why is the carcass allowed to land on that back, of all the backs in creation? Why not simply hand them Makkah on the first day, let the people believe and grant them safety and a state at once? And it is not only our story, the Sheikh insists; the true followers of Musa bled under Pharaoh, and the sincere followers of Isa were butchered under pagan Rome. The best of people, the worst of treatment, again and again across the prophets. What is Allah doing?

The answers come in layers, and the first reaches all the way back to why we are here at all. Allah did not create us to be comfortable in this world. He created death and life to test which of us is best in deed, and the place where the test is administered was never meant to feel like the prize at the end. The suffering is not Allah forgetting His beloved ﷺ; it is the examination hall doing what an examination hall is for. The second layer is mercy dressed as hardship: Paradise is simply too precious to be purchased by a lifetime of deeds, no matter how heavy, so it is granted by mercy, and trials are where Allah magnifies the small deeds of a servant until, by His grace, they outweigh what they could never have outweighed alone. The third layer is that a claim must be tested or it stays a claim. Iman that has never met hardship has never been proven, even to the one who carries it, and so the test sorts the truthful from the one who only said the words.

Do the people think that they will be left to say, "We believe" and they will not be tried? But We have certainly tried those before them, and Allāh will surely make evident those who are truthful, and He will surely make evident the liars.Qur'an 29:2-3
Or do you think that you will enter Paradise while such [trial] has not yet come to you as came to those who passed on before you? They were touched by poverty and hardship and were shaken until [even their] messenger and those who believed with him said, "When is the help of Allāh?" Unquestionably, the help of Allāh is near.Qur'an 2:214

These verses, the Sheikh says, were the daily bread of those Makkan years. And there is a last layer, one that buys something time can never take back: the trials proved the rank of that first generation forever. No believer who suffers in any century afterward can say it would have come easy to the companions, that they had it soft and so cannot judge our pain. They had it harder than almost anyone since, did not bend, and every cornered believer in every age has found in them a lamp still burning.

It reached the point where Khabbab ibn al-Aratt, the blacksmith whose owner pressed hot iron from her furnace into his back, came to the Prophet ﷺ as he sat in the shade of the Kaaba and asked it aloud: how long? Will you not ask Allah to help us? The Prophet ﷺ sat up from where he reclined, marking with his body the weight of what he was about to say. Among those before you, he answered, a man would be combed with combs of iron until the flesh was stripped from his bones, and it did not turn him from his religion. Another would be sawn in two from the crown of his head, and it did not turn him from his religion. By Allah, Allah will complete this affair, until a woman will travel alone with her flock from Hadramaut to San'a fearing nothing but Allah, and the wolf for her sheep. But you are being hasty.

Notice precisely what the answer was and was not. It was not a denial that help was coming; he ﷺ never doubted the victory for the length of a heartbeat. It corrected the clock, not the promise. The iron combs and the saw were not flourishes either: this is exactly what pagan Rome later did to the sincere followers of Isa, believers the Sheikh calls the Muslims of their own age, persecuted worse than Makkah ever managed and unbent by it; Nero burned them as living torches to light his streets. The shepherdess walking unafraid came true as well, in century after century of Muslim peace across those lands. We are asked only to trust the promise while the clock runs, and to know the ease is already on its way, traveling toward us inside the very hardship, the way the verse pairs them: with the difficulty there comes ease.

A safe house hiding in plain sight

By now the believers numbered around twenty or thirty, and many of them were secret: slaves who had not told their masters, sons who had not told their fathers. They needed a place to gather, to learn, to breathe, and there was none. The first true masjid of Islam, Quba, was still years away on the far side of the hijrah; the Kaaba was ringed with idols and watching eyes. So as the dawah went public and the persecution began in earnest, the Prophet ﷺ chose a house. And the choice, the Sheikh says with real delight, is a masterclass in reading the seerah like an analyst, because the books simply record that he chose the house of al-Arqam without telling you why, and the why is quiet genius from first detail to last.

He chose the house of al-Arqam ibn Abi al-Arqam, and look at what that carried. Al-Arqam was from Banu Makhzum, the clan of Abu Jahl himself, the great rival house of Banu Hashim, and no mind in Makkah would imagine a Makhzumi sheltering the Hashimi prophet; the tribal rivalry made the safe house invisible. He was among the first ten to accept Islam, so he could be trusted absolutely with what had become the single most dangerous secret in Arabia. He was a young man, perhaps eighteen, holding a house inherited from his father, and nobody suspects a teenager of hosting the headquarters of a revolution. And by one report his own Islam was still unannounced, one more layer of cover over all the others.

But the masterstroke is the location, and it runs against every instinct. You would expect a secret base on the empty edge of town, where no one would see you come and go. Al-Arqam's house sat behind Mount Safa, a stone's throw from the Kaaba, in the busiest quarter of the entire city, precisely where every Makkan walked every single day to trade, to socialize, to circle the haram. And that is the genius of it. Forty people drifting toward downtown raise no suspicion at all, because everyone goes downtown; four people walking out to a lonely house on the outskirts would draw every eye in the valley. The believers could slip into an alley, sit with their Prophet ﷺ, and walk back out into the crowds as though coming from the Kaaba itself. Hidden in plain sight, under the noses of the men hunting them. And read analytically, one more fact falls out for free: since dozens met there at a time, the house must have been a large one.

Hold the address in mind, because the seerah keeps returning to this door. In the house of al-Arqam, Ammar ibn Yasir embraced Islam, and Suhayb ar-Rumi embraced Islam. And one day soon, the most feared young man in all of Makkah, Umar ibn al-Khattab, sword on his hip, will arrive at that very door, and what happens inside will change the dawah forever. The most intelligent human being who ever lived ﷺ chose nothing at random, and the lesson runs through the whole seerah like a spine: this careful, layered planning was no crack in his trust in Allah. Planning and tawakkul were never rivals. He tied every camel he could, and trusted his Lord with the rest.

Fifteen souls toward the sea

Barely a year and a half had passed since the dawah went public, and that year and a half held everything you have just read, the choking and the carcass and the iron and the laughter, compressed into eighteen months. So in the month of Rajab, in the fifth year of the dawah, the Prophet ﷺ made an announcement to his people. This land has grown too tight for you, he told them. Whoever wishes, let him go to the land of Abyssinia, for there is a Christian king there, a just king, and no one is wronged for his worship under his rule.

Feel the size of what he was asking, because the Sheikh makes sure you do not read it through modern eyes. There were no passports and no way to wire your wealth ahead of you. Your tribe was your government, your police force, and your insurance all at once, and one step beyond its reach you could be robbed, enslaved, or killed with no one obliged to answer for it. For most of human history, the Sheikh reminds us, the overwhelming majority of people were born, lived, and died within a short walk of one spot. And these believers were being invited to cross a sea to a land of another tongue, another people, another religion, leaving behind property they could not even sell, because to sell it would announce the escape. That they preferred all of that to staying is the truest measure we have of what Makkah had become.

Fifteen went, eleven men and four women, down to the port we now call Jeddah, two days away, and from there onto a ship. At the head of them, the very first couple to emigrate in the history of this ummah: Uthman ibn Affan and his wife Ruqayyah, the daughter of the Prophet ﷺ himself. With them traveled names that would become pillars of the religion: az-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, Abdur-Rahman ibn Awf, Mus'ab ibn Umayr, Uthman ibn Maz'un, Abu Salamah and his wife Umm Salamah. Umm Salamah radiyallahu anha would remember those days with a simple, grateful sentence preserved in Bukhari: we lived in a good land, with a good neighbor, safe in our religion, and we worshipped Allah and were not harmed.

Then run your eye down that list again, the Sheikh says, because the observation it yields is unsentimental and it cuts. Every single name on it is Qurashi nobility. The ones who needed escape most desperately, Bilal, Khabbab, Ammar, Ibn Mas'ud, the tortured of the slave class, could not go at all, because leaving was a privilege of the protected; you needed standing and means even to flee. The lesson he draws keeps its mercy without flinching: when you cannot save everyone, you still save whom you can, and the believer who has a way out is not obliged to stay and burn in solidarity with those who have none. As for why Abyssinia in particular, his reasons are four, and the first is the heart of it: a just king under whom worship was free. Then a land Quraysh already knew through trade, so it was no leap into the wholly unknown; an easy passage, two days to the port and a short sail beyond; and a people of the Book whom the Qur'an itself describes as the nearest of all people in affection to the believers.

You will surely find the most intense of the people in animosity toward the believers [to be] the Jews and those who associate others with Allāh; and you will find the nearest of them in affection to the believers those who say, "We are Christians." That is because among them are priests and monks and because they are not arrogant.Qur'an 5:82

The day Layla saw Umar soften

Did the emigrants slip away in secret or leave in the open? The reports differ, and the Sheikh notes there is nothing strange in an open departure, because at this stage Quraysh had not yet thought to block the road. One report, carried by Tabarani, points to at least one departure in plain daylight, and it hands us a scene worth the whole episode. Layla, the wife of Amir ibn Rabi'ah, one of only four couples among the fifteen, was loading her camel for the journey when a shadow fell across her packing. It was Umar ibn al-Khattab, in those days still one of Islam's fiercest enemies. Traveling somewhere, are you? he asked. Where to?

And Layla, exhausted, frightened, packing up her whole life, and finished with being polite to the men who had made it unlivable, snapped at him. Yes, by Allah, she said. We are leaving, and it is because of you. Your persecution of us, only because we worship Allah, has driven us out to seek a land where we can worship Him in peace. She braced for his rage. Instead, something crossed his face she had never once seen there: compassion. Has it really come to that? he said quietly. And then: may Allah be with you. And he turned and walked on.

She carried the moment home like a rumor of a miracle. When her husband heard it he snorted in contempt. Are you actually hoping he becomes Muslim? By Allah, the donkeys of his father's house will accept Islam before Umar ibn al-Khattab ever does. Within about two years, the donkeys still as they were, Umar was praying shoulder to shoulder with the Prophet ﷺ. The lessons are gentle and enormous at once. The hardest shells often guard the softest hearts; Umar's inner mercy was real years before his shahada caught up to it, the same softness we will see again in a coming episode when he strikes his own sister and then, at the sight of her blood, breaks. And guidance belongs to Allah alone, who guided the man even his own future brothers in faith would have bet against. So never condemn a living soul to the Fire, the Sheikh urges; never close the book on anyone while they still draw breath. The persecutor leaning over your camel today may be standing beside you in the prayer line tomorrow, and that file is Allah's, not yours.

Fourteen years under a just king

The king they sailed toward is remembered by a title, not a name: the Najashi, the Negus, just as Rome's ruler was always the Caesar and Persia's the Kisra, the office swallowing the man. His own name, the Sheikh tells us, was As-hama. And weigh the two words the Prophet ﷺ chose to describe him, because the Sheikh builds a whole vision of justice out of them: a just king. What made a Christian ruler just, in the Prophet's ﷺ own framing? One thing above all: that under him no one was wronged for his worship. Freedom to worship Allah, on the tongue of the Messenger ﷺ himself, is offered as the very measure of a kingdom's justice.

And the refuge worked. Muslims lived in Abyssinia for some fourteen years, the Sheikh stresses, long after Madinah was founded and a full Islamic state existed there; seven years into the hijrah there was still a believing community there under Ja'far ibn Abi Talib, which is why Ja'far missed the battle of Badr, and only after Khaybar did the Prophet ﷺ send a letter calling them home. The Sheikh turns this long sojourn into one of his clearest lessons for our own age. Not all non-Muslim lands are the same. Some persecute, as Makkah did, and some guarantee your worship, as Abyssinia did, and where you have that freedom you live as the Abyssinian Muslims lived: grateful, law abiding residents who entered on what amounted to a visa, plotted nothing against the state that sheltered them, and sought nothing beyond the freedom to pray. Against the voices who declare it forbidden to live in such lands at all, the Sheikh holds this community up as the standing refutation, a model for Muslims who live as minorities in the West, citizens under a government whose laws they obey, asking only the freedom the Abyssinian believers were granted.

Then he presses one turn further. If the Prophet ﷺ praised a Christian king as just precisely because he protected the worship of others, could the very law the Prophet ﷺ brought turn around and deny that same protection? It could not, and does not. Egypt has remained home to its Coptic Christians through fourteen centuries of Muslim rule; the Zoroastrians of Persia, fire worshippers and not people of the Book, were granted protected standing by the consensus of the early Muslims when their land was conquered; and scholars of the stature of Ibn Taymiyyah extended that guarantee to followers of any religion at all. The airless utopia some overzealous young voices imagine, a land scrubbed of every other form of worship, is simply not the Prophet's ﷺ Islam, and never was.

Why did this one king see the truth so clearly? Here the Sheikh shares a theory, flagged honestly as only a theory, and Allah knows best. It may be that the Christianity of Abyssinia still carried traces of an older faith from before the Council of Nicaea, the way of those who held Isa to be the Messiah and a servant of Allah, kept alive in scattered pockets after a man named Arius refused the creed Nicaea imposed and fled south. If a remnant of that faith had survived there, then when Ja'far stands in that court in a coming episode and recites the Qur'an, the king's famous reaction will need no explaining: he will weep and say that what he has just heard and what Isa brought come from one and the same source. The grave of the Najashi is known in Abyssinia to this day. The first land that ever opened its arms to this ummah was ruled by a man who simply refused to wrong people for their prayers.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this day as a catalogue of cruelty and a happy escape at the end, and to leave it there, safely sealed in the seventh century. That would be to miss every question it is pressing into your hands. Lay the two images of it side by side and do not look away from either. A man pinned to the earth beneath the rotting insides of a camel while his entire city laughs until it falls over, and the same man ﷺ, minutes later, on his feet with one finger raised toward heaven, utterly unbroken. The trench of fire was real on the day Abu Jahl charged, and the filth was real on the day no fire came, and the Sheikh's deepest point is that both days were the same care of the same Lord at work: one a shield, the other a crown. The God who could wall His beloved ﷺ behind angels chose, on some days, to let the blow land, not because He had looked away, but because the blow was building something a rescue could not have built.

So ask what it was building, because the answer is the question this day puts to your own faith. It was building the proof that this deen traveled into the world on patient, bleeding backs and not on ease, bought and not given cheap.
