It was building the rank of the best generation, settled forever beyond all argument. And it was building a lamp for you, for the exact night you find yourself cornered and the help of Allah feels impossibly far. Khabbab asked how long, and so will you. The answer he received is the answer you will receive: not a denial of the promise, never that, but a correction of the clock. You are being hasty. The help of Allah is near, the verse says, even while the hardship is still pressing on the back. You are asked only to trust the timing to its Owner.

And there is a quieter pair of asks underneath the great one. It asks you to plan like the house behind Safa, choosing the rival clan and the trusted youth and the door on the busiest street, using every lawful means with intelligence, never imagining that careful planning betrays trust, because the most intelligent man who ever lived ﷺ planned to the last detail and trusted to the last breath and saw no contradiction in it. And it asks you, perhaps hardest of all, to write no one off. The donkeys of his father's house will accept Islam before Umar will, said a good man in real pain, and within two years Umar was Muslim and would one day lead this ummah. Guidance is Allah's file, not ours, and the mercy was already in Umar's eyes by the loading camel, years before his tongue caught up to it.

So take this brutal, hopeful day down into your own. When the carcass lands, stay in your sajdah and finish your prayer; the One who let it fall is the One who will raise you from it. When you ask how long and hear only you are being hasty, believe the promise and surrender the clock. Be grateful to the land that lets you pray, and keep your heart from closing the book on any soul Allah may yet guide. May Allah send His peace and blessings upon the one who was choked at the house of his Lord and would not stop praying, who stood beneath the filth of his enemies and rose unbroken, and who opened for his people a sea road to a just king. Make us firm as You made his companions firm, and patient as You made him ﷺ patient, and when our own narrow places close around us, let the ease that You have hidden inside the hardship reach us in Your time, and not a moment before it is good for us.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 15: torture to the Prophet ﷺ and the first hijrah to Abyssinia (Memphis Islamic Center, 2011). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (96:9-10, 96:13-19, 40:28, 29:2-3, 2:214, 5:82), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing has been followed.

The Seerah · 16 of 104

The so-called Satanic Verses

One real prostration, one planted tale

Some days of the seerah you study because they are beautiful. This one you study because, sooner or later, someone is going to quote it at you. So Dr. Yasir Qadhi does something here that he does almost nowhere else in the series: he puts the verdict before the story. The tale that some have called the Satanic Verses has no authentic chain back to the Prophet ﷺ. An early master of hadith, of the very generation that built the science, called it a fabrication concocted by the enemies of Islam to try to destroy it. And the one version of that night which survives every test of scrutiny contains nothing satanic at all. Hold that conclusion like a lamp, because we are about to walk into a room that has frightened a great many Muslims, and the only thing that ever frightened them in it is the dark.

The honest way to teach this, the Sheikh says, is the hard way. It would be comfortable to announce that the whole affair was invented by missionaries and orientalists and be done with it. It was not. Some version of these reports really does sit in a few of our own books, and our religion does not permit us to bury a truth because it is inconvenient; it commands us to face it, weigh it, and answer it in the open. Walk it slowly, and by the last page you will know this story better than the people who weaponize it.

The rumor that crossed the sea

Pick the thread up where the previous week set it down. In Rajab of the fifth year of the dawah, the Prophet ﷺ had given a small band of believers permission to emigrate across the Red Sea to Abyssinia, to a Christian king under whom they could worship Allah without torture. About fifteen of them went, eleven men and four women. And then, astonishingly, within roughly three months, by Shawwal, they came back. Back across the water, back to Makkah, back to the city of whips and branding irons they had just barely escaped. Why would anyone do that?

Anyone who has ever moved far from home already knows half the answer. The hardest stretch of any migration is not the danger you left; it is the first arrival. No house yet, no friends yet, a strange language, strange food, a whole new set of norms pressing in from every side. The muhajirun were safe in Abyssinia, but they were strangers there, and the heart of a stranger leans homeward with a force that has nothing to do with logic. Into that ache dropped a rumor almost too good to be true: Quraysh have accepted Islam. Fifteen people in a foreign land had no way to send a delegation back to verify it, and some news the heart simply wants to believe. So they packed and started home, and only on the road did the truth catch up with them. Not a lie, the Sheikh is careful to say. An exaggeration. A rumor leaves home a hand span long and comes back the length of an arm.

But underneath every exaggeration there is usually a kernel of something real, and underneath this one there was a real night in Makkah. That night is not in the obscure books. It is in Sahih al-Bukhari.

The night Makkah fell into prostration

Ramadan of the fifth year, roughly two months after the migrants had sailed. The Prophet ﷺ stood and recited Surah an-Najm from its first verse to its last, perhaps fifteen or twenty minutes of recitation, with Muslims and idol worshippers gathered in the same audience. If you know the surah, you already feel what that room was feeling. The verses are short and sharp, the questions landing like hammer strokes. It moves through the great nations of the past, Aad and Thamud and the people of Nuh and the overturned cities of Lut, each one mightier than Quraysh and each one swept away. This is a warner, the surah declares, of the same kind as the warners of old, and the Hour is drawing near, and none but Allah can lift its veil. The pitch climbs verse upon verse until, near the very end, the surah wheels around and addresses the listeners themselves:

Then at this statement do you wonder? And you laugh and do not weep While you are proudly sporting? So prostrate to Allāh and worship [Him].Qur'an 53:59-62

And he ﷺ fell into prostration. The Muslims fell with him. And then, in the report of Ibn Abbas, radiyallahu anhu, preserved in Sahih al-Bukhari, the mushrikun went down too. The very men who had mocked him, who had hounded his followers across the sea, beaten for one moment by the sheer power of what they had just heard, found themselves on their faces before Allah behind Muhammad ﷺ. In one narration nothing that heard the surah was left standing, neither human nor jinn. For the first time in the whole history of the dawah, the entire city bowed together, and there was no idol anywhere in that prostration. Just one old man was too proud to go down. He scooped a handful of dust, pressed it to his forehead, and said: this is enough for me. In another telling it was one of the two staunchest enemies of the Prophet ﷺ who refused, but the detail is a footnote to the scene, not its heart.

That, says Dr. Yasir Qadhi, is the entire authentic event, and his first point about it is exactly that simple: the Bukhari version needs no far-fetched tale bolted onto it, because the surah itself is the whole explanation. Once you have felt the momentum of an-Najm you stop being surprised that it bent the knees of its enemies. That is all that happened. And watch what the rumor mill made of it. By the time the scene had crossed the sea to Abyssinia, Quraysh prostrated behind him had swollen into Quraysh have entered Islam. That single arm's length of exaggeration is what emptied the first hijrah and sent fifteen homesick believers sailing back toward the swords.

A bird, an orientalist, and a borrowed name

So where do the so-called Satanic Verses come in? The first thing to know is where they do not come in. Not in Bukhari. Not in Muslim. Not in any of the six canonical books of hadith. Not in the Musnad of Imam Ahmad. Not even in Ibn Ishaq and Ibn Hisham, the two standard early sources for the sira. The added details live somewhere else entirely: in the vast catch-all collections that gathered up everything, with at-Tabari chief among them. And Tabari, to his credit, warned his readers himself in the introduction to his history. I am going to record everything I hear, he said in effect, authentic or not; my task is simply to report it. Bukhari was a critical collector who sifted and rejected; Tabari announced openly that he was an uncritical one, an open channel passing along whatever reached him. Finding a report in his pages, then, proves only that somebody, once, told that story. It is the unfiltered wire feed of Islamic history, not the vetted front page.

Even the unforgettable name is borrowed from outside the tradition. You will not find the phrase Satanic Verses anywhere in the Qur'an, in the Sunnah, or even in the weak reports themselves. It was coined roughly a century before this lecture by Sir William Muir, a Presbyterian minister who became a professor of Islamic studies in Scotland and one of the first orientalists to write a major sira in English. He gave the episode that chapter title, and the label stuck. A novelist much later took the same phrase for a work of fiction built upon the very worst version of the tale, and the storm around that book is its own separate history, not this one. What did the Islamic sources that do carry the reports call it? Something else entirely: qissat al-gharaniq, the story of the gharaniq. The gharaniq is a bird. Modern Arabic linguists differ over its exact English equivalent, the heron, the crane, the pelican, but they agree on the shape of it: a bird with a long neck, the kind that climbs high into the sky. Remember that bird. The whole inflated tale turns on it.

And here is the Sheikh's signature move, the reason this episode is taught at all rather than quietly skipped. He refuses to pretend the whole thing was simply manufactured by Islam's enemies, because it was not, and a teacher who shades the truth has already lost the argument. The reports sit in some of our own sources, and our religion tells us not to bury that fact but to bring it into the light and examine it. So examine it we will.

What the weak reports claim

Version two reaches back to a famous and respected narrator of the generation after the Companions, a man who, crucially, never once saw the Prophet ﷺ. Fix that detail in your mind, because it is the master flaw: the chain is broken at exactly the link that matters most, the link to the Messenger ﷺ himself. Here is what the report claims. Early in Surah an-Najm the recitation names the three great idols of the Arabs in order to tear them down:

So have you considered al-Lāt and al-ʿUzzā? And Manāt, the third - the other one?Qur'an 53:19-20

The surah's own argument here is withering: you assign these goddesses to Allah as daughters, while for yourselves you crave sons and bury your daughters in the sand, so you hand your Lord the very thing you despise. But at that exact moment, the report claims, Shaytan cried out two extra lines in his own voice, lines that praised the idols instead of demolishing them: these are the exalted gharaniq, the high-flying cranes, and their intercession is to be hoped for, idols that would carry your prayers upward the way the long-necked birds climb the sky. According to this version, the mushrikun heard the interjection clearly. The Muslims, the report insists, heard nothing at all.

For Quraysh, two lines like that would change everything. This is the point the seerah keeps having to make about them, because we forget it: Quraysh never had a quarrel with Allah. They believed in Allah. Their entire war with the Prophet ﷺ was over the idols he kept rejecting, the gods of their fathers he kept calling worthless stone. So if those idols had suddenly been honored as intercessors, then in their minds Muhammad ﷺ had at last made room for their gods, and the dispute was over. That, says version two, is why the city bowed when he prostrated: we are all in agreement now, so we will go down with you. And that is why the news racing across the sea sounded so much like a mass conversion.

Version three is uglier, and it is the one the enemies of Islam reach for, because it alone gives them what they want. Here it is not Shaytan shouting over the recitation from outside; it is the Prophet ﷺ himself who, in a pause of the revelation, mistakes Shaytan's interjection for the voice of Jibril and recites the two corrupt lines with his own tongue. Later, when Jibril reviews the surah and arrives at those lines, he disowns them: I never brought you these. The Prophet ﷺ is left grieved and shaken, and in this telling Allah then sends down the verse of Surah al-Hajj about Shaytan casting something into the recitation of the prophets. Understand plainly why this is the version the missionaries and polemicists and the novel were built upon. If it stood, it would mean a prophet who could not tell an angel from a devil, and a preacher who trimmed his message to court a crowd, one theology today and another tomorrow. That is what is actually at stake in something as dry-sounding as the grading of a chain. The whole assault stands or falls on whether these broken chains can carry weight.

The scholars take their stations

A great company of scholars crossed versions two and three off the page from the very beginning. Ibn Khuzaymah, an early giant of hadith who worked at the same exacting level as Bukhari and Muslim, was asked directly about the story of Shaytan inspiring the Prophet ﷺ, and his answer was flat: this is a fabrication, the work of the enemies of Islam to try to destroy it. From the earliest generations, serious critics were already naming it for what it was. Open almost any reliable modern book of sira and you will find it either absent or stamped a fabrication. In 1966 an entire international conference of scholars convened in Cairo over this one story, and its conclusion was the same word: fabricated. And in our own era al-Albani, whom the Sheikh calls the greatest scholar of hadith of recent times, devoted a whole booklet to nothing but this story, chasing the report through every source it appears in and showing, chain by chain, that every last one is weak.

Now comes the part that takes scholarly honesty: there are enormous names on the other side. Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani, the greatest commentator Sahih al-Bukhari has ever had, accepted version two. He conceded that each of the five or six reports is individually weak, but argued from a genuine and accepted principle of hadith science: that enough independently weak chains, all telling the same story without copying one another, can prop one another up until the report as a whole becomes acceptable. Al-Albani's booklet is aimed at exactly this argument. The principle is sound, he answers; but it carries conditions, and he walks through, in technical detail, why this story fails them, so that the weak chains here cannot be stacked into strength. That debate, between two masters who agree on the rule and split on its application, is the real scholarly heart of the matter.

Ibn Taymiyyah went further still and accepted even version three, anchoring it in the verse of Surah al-Hajj:

And We did not send before you any messenger or prophet except that when he spoke [or recited], Satan threw into it [some misunderstanding]. But Allāh abolishes that which Satan throws in; then Allāh makes precise His verses. And Allāh is Knowing and Wise.Qur'an 22:52

The word the verse uses, tamanna, can mean to wish, but Ibn Taymiyyah argued, citing pre-Islamic poetry and a reading carried from Ibn Abbas himself, that its older meaning is to recite. Read it that way and the verse seems to describe the gharaniq incident exactly: the prophet recites, Shaytan throws something in, and Allah erases it and firms up His own verses. And the verse immediately after, he argued, settles the case:

[That is] so He may make what Satan throws in [i.e., asserts] a trial for those within whose hearts is disease and those hard of heart. And indeed, the wrongdoers are in extreme dissension.Qur'an 22:53

A trial for those whose hearts are diseased, Ibn Taymiyyah reasoned, means people who heard something with their ears out in the world, not something that passed silently through a prophet's private thoughts. The scholars who reject the story read the same two verses in a completely different key: tamanna as wishing, Shaytan forever scheming to tamper with the hopes and intentions of the prophets, and Allah wiping out his every interference, with no connection to the gharaniq at all. As for the claim that Surah al-Hajj came down over this very night, the Sheikh notes that most scholars place that surah's revelation around the time of the hijrah, years later, though he adds that we rarely possess a firm chronology for individual verses, so an early date for this one cannot be ruled out. But read those two verses once more and notice what both camps agree on. Whatever Shaytan throws, Allah abolishes. Then Allah makes His verses precise. The verse at the dead center of the storm is, on every reading, a promise of protection.

Can a prophet be deceived?

For most of the scholars who rejected the story, though, the deciding court was never the chains at all. It was theology. One scholar of our own era put it with startling force: even if the chains were as bright as the sun, he said, I would still not accept it. Qadi Iyad, whose beloved ash-Shifa fills two volumes with the rights, ranks, and miracles of the Prophet ﷺ, argued as much from the heart as the head: how could anyone who truly loves him ﷺ believe this of him? The principle they were all defending has a name, ismah, the divine protection Allah wraps around His prophets. If revelation could become a channel that Shaytan edits, even for two lines, then every verse in the Book is suddenly negotiable, and the whole structure of trust collapses. This is exactly the line at which Ibn Hajar drew his own border. Version two, he held, leaves the Prophet ﷺ untouched, because a devil shouting over the recitation from outside is the devil's deed and not the Prophet's; but version three, where the Prophet ﷺ recites the corrupt lines with his own tongue, Ibn Hajar would not carry. He kept the one and discarded the other precisely to guard the Prophet's ﷺ ismah.

So how could Ibn Taymiyyah, of all people, a titan of orthodoxy, accept version three? Because his map of ismah is drawn with finer borders, and the Sheikh walks his students through it fairly. Prophets, on Ibn Taymiyyah's understanding, can never lie; lying is impossible for them. They can never commit major sins, and never anything vulgar or indecent. But they can make judgment calls, ijtihad, that Allah then corrects, the way revelation reviewed the Prophet's ﷺ decision to take ransom from the prisoners of Badr. And they can slip in small, genuinely human ways, on two unbreakable conditions: they never persist, and they repent at once. The proof Ibn Taymiyyah reaches for is the one no Muslim can argue with. Adam, a prophet by consensus and a rank of creation above us all, was told not to eat from the tree, and he ate. And to our own Prophet ﷺ Allah said:

That Allāh may forgive for you what preceded of your sin [i.e., errors] and what will follow and complete His favor upon you and guide you to a straight pathQur'an 48:2

A promise of forgiveness, past and future, implies there is something there to be forgiven. Then Ibn Taymiyyah adds the sentence the Sheikh pauses over, because it sounds alarming aloud and rings true the moment you sit with it. In that immediate repentance, he says, lies the very perfection of the prophets, for had Allah made them angels, they could never have been role models for us at all. They are human, not superhuman, and their humanity is as perfect as humanity gets. You do not have to follow Ibn Taymiyyah the whole way into version three; the Sheikh does not, and says so plainly. But that one sentence about the prophets is worth carrying for life.

And then Ibn Taymiyyah, a scholar the Sheikh says he admires immensely, takes the whole weapon and turns it around. Suppose, he says, the story were true. It would prove the exact opposite of everything the enemies of Islam want from it. It would not show the Qur'an corrupted; it would show the Qur'an protected, because an attempt at corruption was made and Allah Himself rebuffed it, erased it, and firmed up His verses so cleanly that no generation of Muslims since has ever been confused about what the Qur'an contains. The attempt was made and defeated in full view, and the defeat is the point. And it would not show the Prophet ﷺ a deceiver; it would show him the most truthful of all who ever spoke. Because a man inventing a scripture buries his embarrassments, and this story, if it happened at all, could only ever have reached us because he ﷺ reported it against himself, the one tale that appeared to impugn him. A forger hides the thing that makes him look bad. He broadcast it.

Ibn Taymiyyah proves the pattern from a verse no one disputes. The Qur'an once corrected the Prophet ﷺ in public, over the affair of Zayd and Zaynab, radiyallahu anhuma. It told him openly that he had concealed within himself what Allah was going to disclose, that he had feared the people when Allah had more right to be feared, and then revelation went further and performed the marriage itself, We married her to you, a nikah pronounced in the Qur'an and recited by the ummah ever since. There is a report in Bukhari that if the Prophet ﷺ had been one to hide any part of the Qur'an, he would have hidden that verse, the one recording his own correction. He hid nothing. Allah is not embarrassed by the truth, and neither was His Messenger ﷺ. And if he did not bury the verse that corrected him over Zaynab, why would he have buried the gharaniq, had it occurred?

The Sheikh marks these two, the gharaniq and the story of Zaynab, as the two most sensitive episodes in the entire seerah, and his policy toward both is identical. He does not believe in hiding the truth. He says it would almost be easier to skip them, especially for an audience in a Muslim land, and simply teach the gentle, comfortable parts. But we do not live in that world. A Muslim who never hears these stories from a teacher who loves them will one day hear them from an enemy who does not, dressed in their ugliest telling, with no defenses prepared and no answers ready. He has met such Muslims, hit cold with the worst version and left reeling. Ignorance, he says, is not bliss here. Knowledge is power. The believer's armor against this story is not to look away from it but to know it down to its chains.

Where the Sheikh lands

Having laid the giants out fairly, Ibn Taymiyyah on one flank, Ibn Hajar holding the middle, and the long line of rejectors from Ibn Khuzaymah down to al-Albani on the other, Dr. Yasir Qadhi gives his own verdict with a student's adab. Who is he, he asks, to compete with men of that rank? And yet a student may give his considered opinion, and his is this: version one is all we need, and versions two and three can be crossed out. He offers seven reasons in all, and the two he leads with carry most of the weight.

First, the purity of revelation. Allah has guaranteed the wahy, verse after verse insisting that no falsehood can force its way into His Book. And even with Ibn Taymiyyah's careful explanation in front of him, the Sheikh's heart will not accept that Shaytan's voice could thread itself into that channel for even two lines; he grants Ibn Taymiyyah his answer, that the channel was cleared again at once, but his own heart inclines toward a purity that was never breached at all. Second, and more decisively, there is simply nothing here to accept. Not one version of the gharaniq story carries an authentic chain. Not one runs unbroken back to the Prophet ﷺ. Every single report is weak. A story that exists only in broken chains, and stands in tension with a guarantee Allah made about His own speech, does not get the benefit of the doubt.

The questions from the floor that night finished the job, and the Sheikh lets them. Did any scholar ever claim those two lines were part of the Qur'an? No one, not a single Muslim, not even the scholars who entertained the story, ever held that they belonged in the mushaf; their very wording does not sound like the Qur'an. How much power does Shaytan truly have over a person? As much, the Sheikh answered, as you give him, and the verse itself says his throwing becomes a fitnah only for hearts that already carry disease and hardness. And as one questioner put it with the plainest logic of all, the Prophet ﷺ reciting praise of idols would contradict every syllable he had been sent to say. Strip the weak versions away, then, and nothing at all goes missing from the history. Bukhari's night explains the prostration. The prostration explains the rumor. The rumor explains the migrants sailing home. The day stands complete and whole, and there is not one satanic syllable anywhere inside it.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to leave this chapter as a clever piece of scholarly defense, a debate you can now win, and miss the worship buried inside it. So before you close it, take the day down into your own chest, because it asks three things of you.

It asks you to meet hard things with knowledge instead of fear. The Sheikh refused to skip the most dangerous story in the books precisely because he knew that one day, in a dorm room or a comment thread or across a dinner table, someone would set it down in front of you in its worst form and watch your face. The Muslim who has never heard it crumbles. The Muslim who has walked it slowly, who knows where the reports live and how the chains break, simply answers. That is the difference between a faith that panics and a faith that stands, and the only thing between the two is that you bothered to learn. Ignorance was never safety. Knowledge is the armor. Learn the answer before you ever need it, and then it is there when you do.

It asks you to trust the Qur'an to defend itself. Read the verse at the center of the storm one final time and feel where it lands: whatever Shaytan throws in, Allah abolishes, and then Allah makes His verses precise. Both camps of scholars, the ones who linked it to this night and the ones who read it broadly, arrive at the identical promise. The Book is guarded. Fourteen centuries have passed, and with everything this ummah has ever disagreed about, there has never once been a disagreement about what the Qur'an actually is. You are holding something the enemy tried, and failed, to alter by so much as a line. Let that steady you the next time a doubt is dressed up to look like scholarship.

And it asks you to love the recitation enough to let it move you. Underneath all the chains and conferences and refutations, the only authentic core of this entire night is breathtakingly simple: a man recited Surah an-Najm from the depth of his heart, and the Book bent the knees of its own enemies. No sword forced them down, no state, no army, not even a friendly crowd. The words alone, falling on hard pagan hearts, pulled the mockers of Makkah onto their faces beside the believers. That is what the Qur'an can do to a human being met with an open heart, and it is exactly what Shaytan's whispering cannot do, because the verse tells us his throwing only ever snags the heart already diseased and hardened. So keep yours soft. Give him nothing to catch on. Recite an-Najm tonight, slowly, and remember what the Book once did to the very people fighting it, and ask Allah to do the smaller, quieter, greater thing to you: to make you firm upon His Book as He has made its verses firm, to keep your heart soft and free of the disease that turns a trial into a fall, to let the recitation of His words bend you the way it bent Makkah on that Ramadan night, and to send His finest praise and peace upon the most truthful of all who ever spoke, our master Muhammad ﷺ, al-amin, the trustworthy, who carried the Book whole and hid not one letter of it, and upon his family and his companions, ameen.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 16: the incident of the so-called Satanic Verses (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (53:59-62, 53:19-20, 22:52, 22:53, 48:2), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 17 of 104

The second migration to Abyssinia

The truth stands before a throne

The believers who sailed home from Abyssinia came back for a Makkah that did not exist. A rumor had crossed the sea ahead of them, the kind of news a hunted people lets itself believe: that Quraysh had finally accepted Islam. It had grown out of one real moment, the day the disbelievers of Makkah fell into prostration at the sheer power of Surah an-Najm, and around that true moment a false tale had wrapped itself, the story Dr. Yasir Qadhi weighed the week before and set aside as having no sound basis at all. Whatever its source, the report reached the exiles, and they packed up their exile and turned for home. On the road, hearts running ahead of them, they asked the first caravan they met the only question that mattered: have our people entered Islam? And the answer came back like a stone dropped into still water. You have heard falsely. Makkah is exactly as you left it, and the new religion is still being hunted down.

Day seventeen is the story of what they did when the door home turned out to be locked, and it carries three treasures that belong together: Abu Bakr handing back the only protection he had rather than pray his Qur'an in silence; a second migration, more than four times the size of the first, setting sail for a foreign king; and Ja'far ibn Abi Talib, radiyallahu anhu, standing in a court already bribed against him, refusing to lower his head, telling a Christian king the truth about his Prophet ﷺ so beautifully that the king wept. It is a day, beginning to end, about what la ilaha illa Allah costs in a world where every kind of safety has a price, and about a God who keeps His servants when every earthly protection has been spent.

Seventeen come home to nothing

The reports differ on how many made that first migration, somewhere between fourteen and seventeen, and the strongest count the Sheikh gives is twelve men and five women. They were a tiny vanguard, now caught between two worlds. Makkah lay one ridge ahead, with their kin, their homes, everything they owned; behind them lay a land of strange speech they had only just escaped. When the caravan told them the truth, some said it plainly: let us turn around now, it was a mistake to come back. But stand where they stood, the Sheikh asks, with home that close, and you understand why they pressed on the last step to see for themselves.

Only there was no simply walking back in. In a world built entirely on the question of who protects whom, these believers had broken from their tribes to make that journey; in the Sheikh's modern image, they had cancelled their own passports, and now they were trying to re-enter a city that owed them nothing. Leaving your land in that age was so heavy a thing that exile stood in later Islamic law as a punishment in its own right, a sentence handed down for certain crimes; the world has changed so completely, the Sheikh observes, that what was once a punishment now sounds to us like a holiday. But these believers had inflicted that punishment on themselves, willingly, for the sake of one sentence: there is no god but Allah. And now each of them, to set foot in Makkah again, needed a new patron, an entry visa in human form. Much of this day is the story of how three of them went looking for that protection, and what they did when they found it.

The protection of Allah is enough

Rewind to the day the first migrants set out, because the greatest of all the companions had meant to go with them. Of the four men we think of first among the early believers, only Abu Bakr and Uthman ibn Affan, radiyallahu anhuma, could have migrated at all: Umar had not yet accepted Islam, and Ali was a child of nine or ten. Abu Bakr had loaded his camels and joined the caravan for the sea when they passed the lands of Ibn ad-Daghinah, a chief of a neighboring tribe and an old trading partner who knew him personally, and when he learned a man like this was leaving, he refused to accept it. Someone like you does not deserve to be driven out, he said. Go back, and let me extend my protection over you. It was not a thing he could simply declare, for Makkah was not his city; offering his protection to a man of Quraysh was like one country quietly naturalizing another's citizen, the Sheikh explains. So Ibn ad-Daghinah came to the Kaaba at the hour the chiefs gathered, announced his protection over Abu Bakr, and Quraysh, unwilling to snub a neighboring chief, agreed, on one condition: Abu Bakr must not pray in public. He was of the elite of Quraysh, and before Umar accepted Islam there were only a handful brave enough to pray openly at the Kaaba, the Prophet ﷺ chief among them. That public prayer, Quraysh said, has to stop.

So Abu Bakr went home and did something the books record as a first in this religion. He could pray in his own house, the agreement allowed that, so he extended his house, built a room onto it, and made that room a place of worship, the first masjid ever built, it is said, only a man reading the loopholes of his agreement to the letter and finding room in them to worship his Lord. There he prayed aloud, because it was his house and the words said his house. But his recitation would not stay behind his walls. His daughter Aisha, radiyallahu anha, tells us he was easily moved to tears, that when he recited the Qur'an he wept from the fear of Allah; Ibn Ishaq describes the women and children of Quraysh gathering in amazement to listen. Picture the smallness of that city, the houses close, the nights silent; a recitation like that carried for blocks, and Quraysh could feel their own families leaning toward the sound.

They could not have it. They sent for Ibn ad-Daghinah: Abu Bakr could not pray even inside his own house. The chief brought him the only two options Quraysh had left, stop praying or release me from my word, and Abu Bakr did not hesitate. I return your protection to you, he said, and I am content with the protection of Allah. From that day until his hijrah he lived in Makkah under no human cover at all. And here is why that matters so much, the Sheikh reminds us, pointing back two weeks in the story: it was in this very period of exposure that Abu Bakr threw himself between the Prophet ﷺ and a man choking him, crying out, would you kill a man for saying, my Lord is Allah? The mob turned on Abu Bakr and beat him until he could not rise for over a week, and no one in Makkah was obliged to lift a finger, because the man who had given everything for the protection of Allah had nothing of this world left to shield him.

The eye that grew jealous

The returnees from Abyssinia ran the same calculation, one by one. Uthman ibn Maz'un, one of the famous early companions with an early martyrdom waiting for him, reached out to an old friend from before Islam: al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah, one of the most powerful men in Makkah, a rival to Abu Jahl, the father of the great Khalid ibn al-Walid, and the very man the Qur'an describes in the surah of the wrapped one, who turned the revelation over and over searching for some word to throw at it and at last, defeated, called it a strange kind of magic.

Leave Me with the one I created alone And to whom I granted extensive wealth And children present [with him]Qur'an 74:11-13

That was al-Walid: created alone, then granted stretching wealth and sons all around him, and still he set himself against the truth. He announced his protection over Uthman publicly, and it held perfectly. Not a hair on Uthman's head was touched.

It held too well. Ibn Ishaq tells us Uthman would move through the city in his untouchable safety and watch the other believers tormented, Bilal, Ibn Mas'ud, the ones who had returned with no powerful name, and the very security he enjoyed began to eat him alive. There was nothing sinful in his situation; he had done nothing wrong. But his conscience would not rest. How are they suffering, he asked, while I sit safe behind a friendship left over from the days of ignorance? So he went to al-Walid: my uncle, take your protection back. The old man was bewildered. Has anyone harmed you? Have I fallen short? Neither, said Uthman. I cannot bear to enjoy a safety my brothers and sisters do not share. They went to the Kaaba and dissolved the pact in public, Uthman insisting the choice was his alone and no failing of al-Walid's.

Walking home, free now and unprotected, Uthman passed a gathering of Quraysh around a guest of honor: Labid ibn Rabi'ah, one of the towering poets of Arabia, who would one day accept Islam but had not yet. Listen, said Labid, and he recited: truly, everything besides Allah is worthless. You have spoken the truth, Uthman called out, for the Prophet ﷺ himself would later say it was the truest line a poet ever spoke, one of the very few he ﷺ ever quoted. But then Labid went on: and every blessing, without doubt, must one day vanish. You have lied, said Uthman, the bliss of Paradise never ends. A stir went through the crowd; their guest had been heckled, and Labid, with no idea this man was a believer, asked Quraysh since when they let visitors be insulted. A man rose in anger and struck Uthman across the face so hard his eye swelled shut. Al-Walid came rushing: do you see what leaving my protection costs you? Come back under it. And Uthman gave the answer the seerah has never let go of: by Allah, my other eye is in need of what this eye has just received. He would not go back.

But the Sheikh holds a balance here, and we must hold it with him. Not every companion gave back his protector, and there is no blame on any of them; people stand at different levels of strength, and the religion has room for every one of them. The lesson is not that comfort is a sin. It is only that we must never let comfort be the thing that chooses our level for us.

One who kept his protector was Abu Salamah, the husband of Umm Salamah and a cousin of the Prophet ﷺ through his mother. By tribe he was Banu Makhzum, the clan of Abu Jahl himself, the last door he could knock on, so he sent instead to his uncle Abu Talib, already carrying the heaviest protection in Makkah, and Abu Talib simply said yes. Banu Makhzum arrived in a fury: you have taken your nephew Muhammad from us, and now one of our own too? If I am going to protect one nephew, Abu Talib answered, why can I not protect the other? They surrounded the house, the argument heating, and then help rose from the one direction no one would expect. Abu Lahab stood up. Have you not troubled this old man enough? Let him be, or by God I will stand at his side in everything. And because his weight could not be brushed aside, Banu Makhzum backed down. Abu Lahab was not defending Islam; what moved him was the same tribal love that moved Abu Talib himself, the kind that saves no soul, the affection that sheltered the Prophet ﷺ a whole life without ever accepting what he taught. And yet, the Prophet ﷺ would say in Madinah years later, Allah sometimes strengthens this religion through a person who does not even believe in it. The day Banu Makhzum walked from that door, the living proof of that hadith stood there wearing Abu Lahab's face.

Eighty sails south

Makkah refused to change. The returnees stayed two or three weeks, long enough to confirm with their own eyes that the persecution was exactly as the caravan had said, and long enough for something quiet and decisive to happen. Every Muslim in the city now heard, firsthand, from people they trusted, what Abyssinia had actually been. A strange land, yes, a strange tongue, culturally difficult in every way. But there were no whips there. The migrants had lived with food in their hands and safety in their streets, and those two things, the Sheikh reminds us, are after iman itself the very greatest blessings Allah gives anyone: to wake in the security of your home, with the sustenance of your day before you.

Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi offers a reading he is careful to mark as his own opinion. Perhaps, he says, this is precisely why Allah allowed that first migration to end in a false rumor and a journey that looked entirely wasted. Because nothing on earth convinces a human heart like word of mouth. No announcement could have moved a third or even half of Makkah's believers across the sea to a foreign land; but seventeen returning eyewitnesses, telling their neighbors with their own mouths how safe that land had been, could move them, and did. When the second migration set out, more than eighty believers went, more than four times the first count, and at their head walked Ja'far ibn Abi Talib, the Prophet's ﷺ own cousin. The wasted journey had not been wasted at all. It had been the seed.

Not everyone crossed a second time. Of that first seventeen, some chose to stay; the most famous was Uthman ibn Affan, who remained and would later make his hijrah to Madinah, fighting at Badr and after. Mus'ab ibn Umayr, the gentle young man whose own parents had tortured him more cruelly than almost anyone, stayed at the Prophet's ﷺ side, and within a few years would become the very first man he ﷺ sent ahead to Madinah. But weigh the departing number the way the Sheikh insists. When the seerah says tribe, do not picture thousands; picture ten, for Banu Hashim was ten brothers, the sons of Abdul Muttalib, and the sub-tribes of Quraysh were small like that. The whole of Makkah held at most perhaps a thousand souls, very possibly only five or six hundred, so eighty believers walking out meant a tenth of the population or more, simply leaving. There was no precedent for it anywhere in Arabia, and it had landed on the proudest tribe of all, the custodians of the sacred house. It was an unbearable public shame, and Quraysh resolved the bleeding had to be stopped.

Leather, bribes, and a king who listens

Their weapon was the oldest one in the world: money. Quraysh chose two envoys, and chief among them was Amr ibn al-As, not yet a Muslim and already the shrewdest political mind the tribe possessed. The Sheikh pauses to be just to him: once Amr accepts Islam we say nothing of him but good, for he became a companion and carried that same statecraft into the service of the religion; but at this stage that cunning was working for Quraysh. With him went a second man whose name the scholars dispute, the gentler of the two. They were loaded with Makkah's most prized export, the fine camel leather the city was famous for, the kind of luxury Abyssinia could not produce, and pointed at the court of the Najashi. Najashi, the Sheikh explains, is not a name but a title, like Caesar for Rome or Pharaoh for Egypt; this king's own name was As-hama, and his grave is still known in that land, a place friends of the Sheikh have stood at.

Umm Salamah, radiyallahu anha, who lived these years and became the chief narrator of all that happened in Abyssinia, tells what came next, her report preserved in Sahih al-Bukhari. The Muslims had been received with the best of hospitality, granted security in their religion, free to worship without the smallest hindrance; we did not hear so much as one word of ridicule, she says with wonder, knowing how a minority among a majority is usually treated. Then the envoys arrived and worked the court before they ever worked the king. They went to every minister, one by one, pressing leather and rich gifts into their hands with the same whisper: tomorrow we will ask for our runaways; counsel the king to give them back, and remember this gift. Open bribery, the Sheikh calls it, and he lingers, because in our own age the same transaction wears a respectable name: pay a public servant directly and we call it corruption; pay into his campaign and we call it lobbying. Islam forbade it outright, lest the gift bend the judgment of one who has no claim on the public's money beyond his wage. And here were Quraysh, greasing every hand in the court.

The next day they came before the throne, and their pitch touched every nerve a ruler has. These are foolish youths, they said, who abandoned the religion of their own people without entering yours or any we know; they have invented something new, rebelled against their elders, and we, who know their faults better than anyone, have been sent to take them home. They frightened the king on every front at once: religiously, that these were fanatics of neither faith; socially, that they were rebels against their fathers; and beneath it the hint that the trade between the two lands and the king's grip on a band of unknown foreigners were better served by handing them back, for every government fears an unknown. And the bribed ministers rose on cue: our king, what these men say makes sense; these are strangers; return them.

And they [i.e., the disbelievers] planned, but Allah planned. And Allah is the best of planners.Qur'an 3:54

The Najashi refused to be hurried. No, by Allah, he said: people who chose my land over every other land, who came to me and asked for my protection, will not be handed over until I summon them and hear what they have to say. He was a king with no earthly reason to listen to a band of refugees over the loaded envoys of a wealthy trading partner, and he insisted on hearing both sides anyway. The Prophet ﷺ had called him a just king, and here, before the believers had spoken a word, was the proof: they planned, and Allah planned.

We will say what our Prophet ﷺ taught us

The summons terrified them. None had ever stood before the king, and now the palace wanted them all at once, their entire future hanging on the answer. What will we say? And Ja'far gave them the sentence that became the spine of the whole day: we will say what our Prophet ﷺ taught us, and we will not change the truth, whatever comes of it. They entered a hall crowded with the king's viziers and his patriarchs. Amr and his companion prostrated before the throne, as everyone did before the kings of that age; to men who bowed before stones, bowing before a living emperor cost nothing. Ja'far walked in upright and would not lower his head by an inch. The ministers were scandalized: how dare you enter without prostrating to the king? And Ja'far answered, with eighty lives hanging on the audience, our Prophet ﷺ has taught us that we prostrate to no one but Allah. They opened the most important meeting of their lives by declining its etiquette.

The king asked the obvious thing: what is this religion of yours, that has made you leave the faith of your people without entering mine, or the Judaism or Christianity we know? And Ja'far, through a translator, with no warning and no time to prepare, delivered one of the most beloved speeches in the whole of our history. O King, we were a people of jahiliyyah. We worshipped idols, we ate the flesh of dead animals, we committed every indecency, we cut the ties of kinship, we wronged our neighbors, and the strong among us devoured the weak. We lived like that until Allah sent us a Messenger from among ourselves, a man whose lineage we knew, whose house we knew, whose truthfulness we knew, who had never told a single lie in his life. He called us to worship Allah alone and to throw away the stones our fathers served. He commanded us to speak the truth, to keep our trusts, to join the ties of kinship, to be good to our neighbors; he forbade us bloodshed and false testimony, the devouring of the orphan's property and the slander of chaste women; he ordered us to pray and to fast and to give in charity. So we believed in him and followed him. And for that our own people fell upon us and tortured us, to force us back to the idols, and when their cruelty overwhelmed us, we came out to your land, and we chose you above every other king, hoping that in your presence we would not be wronged.

Sheikh Yasir slows over this speech the way you would over a jewel turned in the light. Composed on the spot, in a hostile court, in a language the speaker had to send through a translator, and it reads its single listener perfectly: to a Christian king it speaks of a Messenger, a category the king's own scripture had trained him to love, and within five minutes the accused have become the wronged and the bribed envoys the persecutors. This is exactly why the Prophet ﷺ placed Ja'far at the head of the eighty: leadership is personality and eloquence, and Ja'far carried both. Then came the request that turned the audience. Do you have anything, the king asked, of what this Messenger brought from Allah? Yes, said Ja'far. And of everything in all the revelation he might have chosen, he chose the opening of Surah Maryam, the surah of the virgin, the cradle, and the child who spoke from his mother's arms.

[Jesus] said, "Indeed, I am the servant of Allah. He has given me the Scripture and made me a prophet. And He has made me blessed wherever I am and has enjoined upon me prayer and zakah as long as I remain alive And [made me] dutiful to my mother, and He has not made me a wretched tyrant. And peace is on me the day I was born and the day I will die and the day I am raised alive."Qur'an 19:30-33

For long minutes the recitation rolled through that hall, the voice of Ja'far booming and breaking against the walls of the palace while the elite of a kingdom sat hushed. Ibn Ishaq records that the patriarchs wept without understanding a word of the Arabic, moved by the sound alone, and that when it was translated the Najashi wept with them and said: by God, this and what Musa brought, and what Isa brought, have all come out of one and the same lamp. Then he turned on the two envoys: go. I will not hand these people over to you, and do not even think of asking me again.

A twig, a parchment, and a funeral prayer

Amr ibn al-As was not finished. Leaving the hall in defeat, he whispered to his companion that he had one final trick. His gentler companion tried to talk him out of it, whatever else these people are, they are still our relatives, but Amr would not. The next day, to a king who said he had already decided, he said: there is one thing we forgot. They say something monstrous about Isa the son of Maryam. Ask them. And now the king was caught, for this touched the heart of his own faith, and a fresh summons went out that frightened the Muslims worse than the first, because it came with no delay: come now. Again they asked Ja'far what to say, and again he held the line: we will say exactly what our Prophet ﷺ taught us, whatever comes of it.

Asked about Isa, Ja'far answered: we say that he is the servant of Allah, and His Messenger, and a spirit from Him, and His word, which He cast to Maryam the chaste virgin. Every word true, and not one word more. The Sheikh dwells on the wisdom of that restraint: Ja'far said plainly what the Qur'an says of Isa, neither lying to please the king nor marching forward to call the Trinity a blasphemy, though that is the Muslim's belief. He stopped before that line and let the king draw his own conclusion. Firm on the principle, wise in the wording, never groveling and never picking a fight the moment has not asked for.

The Najashi bent down, picked up a small twig from the floor, and held up the tiny distance between his fingers. By God, he said, what you have just said about Isa does not exceed what Isa himself said by even this much. For the king had never truly believed Isa was the son of God or the third of a trinity; Ja'far's words had not threatened him, they had named what he already secretly held. He turned on the envoys: be gone, and take your gifts with you; I have no need of them. The day before he had kept their gifts; now even the leather already in the ministers' houses was sent back. Umm Salamah remembered the two men leaving debased, carrying home everything they had come with, and what came after: we stayed in the best of lands, with the best of neighbors, until we returned to the Prophet ﷺ.

But the king paid for that twig, and the way he paid it is one of the quietest treasures in the story. Word spread that the Najashi had abandoned the religion of his fathers and become a heretic, and a faction began moving against him. Warned by his loyalists, he met it head on. He wrote on a small parchment that he bore witness none deserves worship but Allah and that Isa is His servant, His Messenger, and His word cast to Maryam, tucked it inside his robe against his heart, and summoned the agitators. Have I not been a good king to you, a just ruler? Then what is your grievance? When they spoke their creed, that Isa is the son of God, he laid his hand flat over the hidden parchment and swore: by Allah, this is exactly what I believe, and let them hear it however they wished. They went away satisfied; he had told the literal truth and saved both his throne and his faith in a single sentence. He had even sent secretly to the Muslims that if they heard of his death, a ship lay waiting to take them wherever they pleased; he won, and the ship was never needed. Years later a rival royal house dragged the land toward civil war by the Nile, and the believers, never more terrified, begged Allah to keep the just king on his throne, until Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, radiyallahu anhu, came back from watching the distant battle beaming: rejoice, the Najashi has won.

The believers stayed in his land some twelve years in all. From Madinah the Prophet ﷺ later wrote to invite the Najashi formally to Islam, and al-Bayhaqi records the reply: I am a Muslim; I believe in you and in everything you say; and if you command me, I will come to Madinah and serve you. Then one day the Prophet ﷺ came out to his companions with news no caravan could possibly have carried yet, a death two weeks' journey away announced the very hour it happened: Jibril has informed me that a righteous brother of yours has died in a distant land. He lined the companions up and prayed the funeral prayer over the absent king, the only janazah over an absent body that he ﷺ prayed in his entire life. Why the Najashi alone, when other beloved companions died far away without it? Dr. Yasir Qadhi inclines to the simplest answer: because in that Christian land, where his faith had stayed hidden to the end, there had been no one to pray over him. The king who would not sell eighty strangers for all the leather in Makkah was sent out of this world by the takbir of the very Prophet ﷺ those strangers had followed.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this day as a thrilling courtroom victory and leave it there, the clever cousin outdueling the clever envoy before a fair-minded king, a good story safely shut in the past. That would be to miss every question it is asking us.

Look first at what these believers were willing to pay. Abu Bakr handed back his only protector rather than recite his Qur'an in a whisper, and walked his own city for years with nothing between him and the mob but Allah. Uthman ibn Maz'un gave back a safety nobody had asked him to surrender, and when the blow came he asked for a second one, because his conscience could not bear a comfort his brothers did not share. Eighty families sailed into a land of strangers a second time, and a king, far away, risked his throne for a sentence of truth he hid against his heart. None of them broke, and not one was abandoned. La ilaha illa Allah has a price, it always has, and the question is not whether you will be asked to pay but what you are willing to spend. Most of us are never asked for our lives; we are asked for far smaller coins, a comfort, an approval, a silence, and we guard them more tightly than Abu Bakr guarded his life. Ask which of your safeties you would hand back as he handed back his, content, like him, with the protection of Allah alone.

Look next at how Ja'far won. He did not boycott the court of a non-Muslim king or stand on the purity of staying out of a worldly system; he walked straight into it, pleaded inside it, and won his people's freedom through it, and the Sheikh draws the line to our own day sharply: the believer fights oppression with the lawful tools of the land he lives in, the courts, the press, the public square, the vote, and there is nothing in our religion that tells us to sit on our hands while a sympathetic system stands open. But notice the manner he won by. He would not prostrate, would not lie, would not soften by a hair what his Prophet ﷺ taught about Isa, and he also would not volunteer a single insult the moment did not require. Firm on the principle, wise in the wording. That is the register this day hands you for every hard room you will ever enter: tell the truth, standing up, and do not pick the fight the truth itself has not asked for.

And then hold the whole day under the ayah the Sheikh leaves in your hands. Quraysh did everything cunning could do: they chose their sharpest man, loaded him with their finest wealth, bribed every minister in a foreign court, and frightened a king on every front he can be frightened. They had the money, the brilliance, every visible advantage, and they walked home with their gifts in their arms and nothing else. The believers had no leverage at all, only the truth and a cousin willing to stand and speak it, and they kept their land, their freedom, and their king. They planned, and Allah planned, and Allah is the best of planners. Carry that the next time you find yourself in the weaker chair, outspent and outmaneuvered, with every earthly advantage on the other side of the table. Do every lawful thing in your power, as Ja'far did, and then rest in the one fact that held this scene steady: the planning of Allah overrules the planning of everyone else, and it has never once failed the servant who stood up for His truth.

May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, whose name carried a band of strangers safely across the sea into the heart of a just king; make our conscience louder than our comfort, our truth upright in every hard room, and our hearts content, as Abu Bakr's heart was content, with the protection of Allah above every protection of men; be pleased with Ja'far and Abu Bakr and Uthman ibn Maz'un and Umm Salamah and the Najashi and all who sailed, and gather us with them under the banner of His Prophet ﷺ on the Day when no protector remains but Him.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 17: the second migration to Abyssinia (Memphis Islamic Center, 2011). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (74:11-13, 3:54, 19:30-33), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 18 of 104

Hamza, Umar, and the boycott

Two lions, then the starving valley

By the sixth year of the dawah the arithmetic of the believers looks like defeat. The second flight to Abyssinia has drawn off the bulk of the community, and the few who remain in Makkah can almost be counted on the fingers: by one precise reckoning, only thirty seven or thirty eight men. Out of more than a hundred who had once gathered, the streets now hold a thin remnant, and Quraysh, looking around at a Makkah it seems to have won back, has every reason to think the matter nearly finished. It is exactly here, into a season that looks emptied out, that Allah sends two men in the space of a single week. Both are famous for their strength. One swings a bow across the face of the fiercest enemy of the dawah; the other sets out through the city with a drawn sword to murder the Prophet ﷺ, and by the end of the week both are kneeling among the believers. Then, in the very next breath of the story, Makkah reaches for the cruelest weapon it has left and seals the whole clan of the Prophet ﷺ into a valley to starve. This is a hinge in the Makkan years, and it asks to be held in both hands at once: the sweetest conversions of the period, followed without a pause by its hungriest stretch.

A refuge held in reserve

Before we turn to Makkah, there is one thread from Abyssinia worth tying off, because of what it reveals about the man at the center of everything. The Muslims who fled across the sea did not come home when the danger eased. They stayed ten or eleven years in all, through the hijrah and through Badr and Uhud and the Trench, the Prophet ﷺ telling them to remain; only after Khaybar, when Madinah at last stood fully secure, did he ﷺ send word to Jafar to bring everyone back. Why keep a whole community parked in a foreign kingdom for a decade while he was fighting for survival and short of every pair of hands? Dr. Yasir Qadhi reads it the only way that makes sense to him, and Allah knows best: Abyssinia was the Prophet's ﷺ plan B, a refuge held in reserve in case Madinah did not work out, released only when it was no longer needed. The man of perfect tawakkul was at the same time the man who tied his camel, trusting Allah completely and planning years ahead in case the obvious plan failed.

The day Hamza's blood boiled

Back in Makkah, Hamza, the uncle of the Prophet ﷺ, was a renowned archer and hunter who would disappear on long expeditions into the wild. His habit on returning was the habit of Makkah's men: before his own door, he went first to the Kaaba to make tawaf. It was on one of these trips, while Hamza was away, that Abu Jahl came upon the Prophet ﷺ near the hill of Safa, in a foul temper, and emptied it onto him: cursing him, his faith, his forefathers, a long and public stream of contempt, never laying a hand on him, while the Prophet ﷺ stood and answered not a single word, until Abu Jahl wore himself out and went on his way.

The women of the Banu Hashim had watched it all, and Muslim or not, this was a matter of blood, not religion: to insult the son of the clan was to insult the clan. So when Hamza came striding back into the city with his bow on his shoulder, they met him with a taunt meant to sting: what kind of uncle are you, what kind of leader of the Banu Hashim, when your own nephew is abused in the open before all of Makkah and not one man rises to defend him? Hamza asked one question. Did people see it? All of Makkah saw it, they told him.

He never made it home. He marched straight to where Abu Jahl sat among his clansmen of the Banu Makhzum, raised his bow, and brought it across Abu Jahl's face hard enough to open a gush of blood. How dare you curse my nephew, he said, and then, in the heat of it, heard himself say a thing he had not planned and did not yet mean: and I too am a follower of his religion. He had meant only to defend his nephew, and now, in front of everyone, he had declared himself for a faith he did not hold. The men of the Banu Makhzum rose to defend their chief, but Abu Jahl waved them down. Leave him be, he said. By Allah, today I cursed his nephew like I never have before. Even Abu Jahl understood what he had lit.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi pauses to name what actually moved in Hamza that hour, because the Qur'an has a name for it: hamiyyah, the hot, baseless clan pride of the days of ignorance, the pride that defends a bloodline simply because it is yours. Hamza had stood up for a nephew, not for a Prophet. And so that night, the strongest man in Makkah went home dazed and trapped, and out of his confusion came one of the most honest duas in the whole seerah. O Allah, he said, You know that I am one of the leaders of Quraysh, and I have now said a thing I cannot take back. So if this matter is true, then guide my heart to it; and if it is not, then let me die. He spent the most miserable night of his life turning it over. In the morning he went to his nephew ﷺ and told him everything, not as a believer but as a man caught in a snare. And the Prophet ﷺ spoke with him, reasoned with him, called him to la ilaha illallah, until at last Hamza said words that this time rose from somewhere real: I testify that you are truthful, and I never want to return to the religion of my forefathers.

So a man who began by defending his family ended by submitting to his Lord, and the dawah gained its first elder, a son of Abdul Muttalib and a brother of the chieftain Abu Talib, the most senior man yet to enter Islam. Quraysh noticeably softened its abuse, because to insult the Prophet ﷺ now carried a cost. And Allah took the man whose pride had pushed him through the door and set him on a road that ends at Uhud, where he falls as sayyid ash-shuhada, the master of the martyrs.

A dua for one of two men

A few days later, three days only by one report, Allah followed the gift with a second. To feel the shock of it you have to remember who Umar ibn al-Khattab was in the sixth year of the dawah. Together with Abu Jahl, he was one of the two fiercest enemies the believers had: mighty in body, mighty in lineage, a kind of measuring stick for the persecution itself. And yet there had been one small crack in the wall. When Layla bint Abi Hathma was loading her camel for the second journey to Abyssinia, Umar passed by and she turned on him bitterly: it is because of you that we must leave our land to worship Allah in a foreign place. She braced for something harsh, and instead, for once, Umar wavered. Has it really reached this level, he said, and then: may Allah give you barakah wherever you go. Her husband refused to read a thing into it. By Allah, he said, his father's donkeys will accept Islam before he does.

What no household in Makkah knew was that the Prophet ﷺ had already carried Umar's name into his night prayers. In a dua preserved in Sunan at-Tirmidhi he ﷺ had asked: O Allah, bring honor to Islam through the more beloved of these two men to You, Abu Jahl or Umar ibn al-Khattab. The two strongest, best connected, most dangerous men in the entire city, and he ﷺ asked his Lord for either one, whichever Allah loved more. Allah loved Umar, and gave him to the religion.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi stops here to hand us two lessons before the story moves on. The first is that guidance belongs to Allah alone, so we must never stamp a final verdict on any soul: the two most hopeless cases in all of Makkah were each one dua away from the truth. The second is that it is not flattery but wisdom to carry the dawah to people of influence, because a society moves when its symbols move; his own example is the boxer Muhammad Ali, who did more for the image of Islam in America than a million scholars could, simply by living his faith in public. The Prophet ﷺ understood that fourteen centuries before us, and prayed accordingly.

More than one account is told of how the answer came, and when that happens, the Sheikh notes, the truth is usually all of them together: blow after blow landing on a single heart until it cracks open. The first blow fell on a night Umar loved to retell for the rest of his life; his first person account is preserved in the Musnad of Imam Ahmad. He was in his mid twenties and fond of his wine, and one night the craving sent him out into the dark to find his drinking companions. They were not at the first house, nor the second, and even the wine seller was gone. So, with nowhere else to take his restlessness, he gave up and went instead to make tawaf of the Kaaba.

The sanctuary had no lamps and stood empty except for one man: the Prophet ﷺ, alone in the middle of the night, standing in prayer before the Kaaba with no guard and no companion, reciting. Umar's first thought was the old, familiar one: here is my chance, with no one watching, to do him harm. He crept up close behind him. And then the recitation reached his ears, and the plan simply dissolved, because for the first time in his life the man who had only ever hated stopped, and listened. The surah was al-Haqqah, and Umar said afterward that he was amazed at the way it was composed, the short verses landing one after another like blows.

Then came the part he could never afterward explain away. A thought crossed his mind: this must be the speech of a poet, just as Quraysh keep saying. And the very next words off the Prophet's ﷺ tongue answered the thought:

And it is not the word of a poet; little do you believe. Nor the word of a soothsayer; little do you remember. [It is] a revelation from the Lord of the worlds.Qur'an 69:41-43

A soothsayer, then, Umar thought, reaching for the next accusation, and the recitation had already closed that door too. Then what if the man is inventing it, he wondered, and the surah answered that as well:

And if he had made up about Us some [false] sayings, We would have seized him by the right hand; Then We would have cut from him the aorta.Qur'an 69:44-46

Allah was answering Umar's private, unspoken thoughts in real time, through the mouth of a Prophet ﷺ who did not even know he was there. That, Umar said, was the first time Islam entered my heart. He did not submit that night; he shook it off and slipped into the dark. But the Word had touched him, and the Word does not let go easily.

His sister's blood

The famous day that followed began with a bounty. Abu Jahl rose before Quraysh in a rage: this man has cursed our religion, mocked our forefathers, condemned our gods, and who will rid us of him? A hundred camels, the choicest in all of Arabia, and a hundred pouches of silver besides, to whoever does the deed. Umar stood up for the price, greed and old grievance moving together in him, strapped on his sword, and walked out through Makkah with the blade bare in his hand. On the way he met Nuaym ibn Abdullah, a man who had quietly accepted Islam and told no one, and who now read death in Umar's face. Where are you going? To kill Muhammad ﷺ. Have you lost your mind, said Nuaym; do you think his clan will let you walk the earth after you kill one of their own? And if you are so eager to set your own house in order, start there: your own sister and her husband have left your religion and followed his.

It was true. Fatima bint al-Khattab and her husband Saeed ibn Zayd had both accepted Islam, and inside that house at that very hour sat a teacher: Khabbab ibn al-Aratt, the former slave whose back the idolaters had once burned over hot stones, now assigned to teach the new believers their faith. Dr. Yasir Qadhi will not let this detail slip past. Even here, in the depths of persecution, in total secrecy and under threat of torture, the dawah was running an education program: every person who converted was assigned someone to teach him the Book and the prayer. Then the Sheikh looks straight at us. Fast forward to our own time, and a convert gets a warm hug at the masjid and is too often never heard from again. Knowledge was the lifeline of this religion at its weakest hour, and we have let it become optional at our strongest.

Umar heard the low hum of recitation through the door and began pounding on it. Khabbab slipped into hiding, and Fatima pushed the written page beneath her and all but sat on it. What was that humming I heard, he demanded. Nothing, they said. By Allah, I know what I heard, he said; the two of you have followed him. He lunged at Saeed, and Fatima threw herself between them, and the blow meant for her husband fell on her instead and split her lip open. And then, bleeding in front of the very brother she had been hiding from, she stopped hiding. Yes, she said, we have accepted Islam, we believe in Allah and His Messenger ﷺ, so do whatever you please.

The sight of his sister's blood, and the courage behind it, broke something open in him. Here were the two layers of Umar that the ummah would later know across his whole khilafah: granite on the outside, and beneath it a heart that tore easily. Show me what you were reading, he said, suddenly quiet. Not while you are in this state, she said; you are a mushrik, and this Book is not touched until you have purified yourself. Even in the sixth year of the dawah, the Sheikh observes, the believers already knew that the Book of Allah is so honored that one cleans oneself before laying a hand on it. So Umar made ghusl, and only then took up the page and read the opening of Surah Taha:

Ṭā, Hā. We have not sent down to you the Qur'an that you be distressedQur'an 20:1-2

And Islam entered his heart, and he knew that this was the truth. He had one question. Where is he now?

Allahu Akbar from the house of al-Arqam

Saeed saw that the rage had drained out of his brother-in-law's eyes, and gave up the secret: the Prophet ﷺ is in the house of al-Arqam. So Umar walked there, the sword still in his hand, and knocked. One of the companions looked out through a crack in the door, saw who stood there with a drawn blade, and came back into the room shaking. It is Umar, with his sword. And it was Hamza, only days into his own Islam, who steadied the room. Let him in, he said. If Allah intends good for him, he will accept it; and if not, the very sword in his hand will be the end of him.

They opened the door, took him by an arm on each side, and brought him before the Prophet ﷺ, who reached out, took hold of Umar's garment, and pulled the most feared man in Makkah toward himself. What has brought you here, son of al-Khattab, he said. By Allah, if you do not stop, Allah will send His punishment down upon you. And Umar answered: O Messenger of Allah, I have come to believe in Allah and in His Messenger, and to testify to the truth. The Prophet ﷺ cried Allahu Akbar so loudly that everyone in the house knew at once, without being told, that Umar ibn al-Khattab had become a Muslim.

And then something happened that had never happened before. Around forty believers formed themselves into two rows and walked out to the Kaaba in the open light of day, Hamza at the head of one row and Umar at the head of the other, the two newest converts leading because no one in Makkah now dared lay a hand on either of them. And there, for the first time, the Muslims prayed publicly at the House of Allah. Years later Ibn Abbas asked Umar where his title had come from, and Umar pointed to that day: the Prophet ﷺ named him al-Farooq, the one who separates truth from falsehood, because his Islam had dragged the truth out of hiding. And decades later, as Umar lay dying, Ibn Masud measured a whole era by him: we have remained in honor ever since Umar accepted Islam, for until that day we could not even pray at the Kaaba.

Umar being Umar, the secrecy itself offended him. He went in person to Abu Jahl's house, informed his old ally face to face that he now believed in Allah and His Messenger, and the door slammed shut on a curse. Then he sought out the man in Makkah least able to keep a secret and confided his news; the gossip was running through the streets shouting it before Umar, walking calmly behind, could even correct the wording for the crowds. And of this convert the Prophet ﷺ would later say, as recorded in Bukhari: among the nations before you there were men who were spoken to though they were not prophets, and if there is any such man in my ummah, it is Umar.

The pact hung inside the Kaaba

Look at the year through the eyes of Quraysh: the bulk of the believers have slipped overseas beyond your reach, and in a single week the dawah has taken your two most promising men. So in the seventh year of the dawah the clans gathered and reached the only consensus they had left: Muhammad ﷺ must be killed. They went to Abu Talib one final time with a cold offer. Hand over your nephew, name whatever blood money you wish, and we will even give you our word that the man who strikes him will not be one of us. Or be cut off from Quraysh entirely. Abu Talib's fury answered before his words did. Do as you please, he told them. I will never hand him over.

So Quraysh did a thing Arabia had never seen. They wrote the threat down. A formal pact: no one would buy from or sell to the Banu Hashim, no one would marry into them or give them daughters, no one would so much as sit and deal with them, no food, no water, no trade, until they surrendered the Prophet ﷺ. They sealed the document and hung it inside the locked Kaaba, dressing a siege of starvation in the robes of the sacred.

Then the Banu Hashim and their twin clan the Banu al-Muttalib, believer and idol worshipper together, withdrew into a valley the people of Makkah knew as the shib of Abu Talib. Sit with how strange and how telling that is, the Sheikh says: most of those who went hungry beside the Prophet ﷺ for the next years did not believe in him at all. They went into exile out of pure loyalty of blood, an old tribal pride that would not see their own kin treated this way, and Abu Talib, still upon the religion of his fathers, presided over the whole camp the entire time. Only one man of the clan refused: Abu Lahab, unwilling to give up the comforts of the city, publicly broke with his own people and defected to Quraysh.

Two years, some say three, in that valley were the hungriest of the whole seerah. They lived on rainwater and the leaves of the shrubs, and one of those who endured it remembered that their droppings became like the droppings of goats. The sources fall strangely quiet about this stretch, and the Sheikh reads the silence itself: few who lived through it survived into the later years when history came asking, and those who did, being human, did not love returning to the most painful season of their lives. But we do know who kept them alive. Every so often Mutim ibn Adi, the most decent of the Prophet's ﷺ non-Muslim countrymen, would load a camel with grain and water in the dead of night, strike its flank, and send it walking in through the valley's single entrance; Khadijah's nephew Hakim ibn Hizam did the same in secret. Years later, after Badr, the Prophet ﷺ would say that had Mutim been alive and asked him for all seventy captives of that battle, he would have handed them over with no ransom at all, for he ﷺ never forgot a kindness. In the other direction, when foreign traders arrived in the hajj months who had not heard of the boycott, Abu Lahab would call out, do not sell to these people, and I will pay you double. And still, every hajj season the Prophet ﷺ would walk out of the valley to meet the visiting tribes and go on giving dawah while his own family chewed leaves.

Eaten away, all but the name of Allah

The boycott broke at last under three blows that fell together. The first came from the Prophet's ﷺ own lips. He prayed against them: O Allah, send upon them a famine like the famine of Yusuf.
