And a drought settled over Makkah until its people too were reduced to eating carcasses and chewing dead hides, and they understood whose dua had done this to them, and messengers began to move, feeling for some way back.

The second was a quiet conspiracy of decency. Hisham ibn Amr, tied to the besieged families through his mother, had had enough, and he built a coalition: Zuhayr ibn Abi Umayyah, Mutim ibn Adi, Abu al-Bakhtari and a fifth man, the small handful of Quraysh whose hearts had never truly signed the pact. The next day, when the assembly gathered by the Kaaba, Zuhayr rose first, the one whose lineage placed him farthest from suspicion. How long, he said, are we going to starve our own kith and kin to death? This is an evil thing. Abu Jahl shot back that the pact had been agreed by everyone, and the trap sprang shut. Hisham rose: I never agreed, this was your idea. Mutim rose: no, you forced this on us. Then the fourth man, then the fifth, each from a different corner of the gathering, until Abu Jahl looked around and said the truest thing he ever said: by Allah, this is a plan that was hatched in the night. It was, and it had worked. The public mood had turned.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi draws out the lesson the Prophet ﷺ was already living: every nation holds within it some who are cruel and some who are decent, and the wisdom of the dawah is to welcome the consciences that hate injustice. These five men were idol worshippers to the last, and yet they could not stomach what their own people were doing, and the Prophet ﷺ took their decency and used it against their own bigotry. So too, the Sheikh says, must his ummah, wherever it lives among others.

The third blow no human being could have staged. The Prophet ﷺ came to his uncle in the valley with news: Allah has informed me that He has set the termites upon their pact, and they have eaten every word of it except the opening, Bismika Allahumma, in Your name, O Allah. The Kaaba was locked; the document was sealed inside its cloth; no human eye could possibly have checked. Your Lord has told you this, Abu Talib asked. Yes, said the Prophet ﷺ. Then, said the old chieftain, I stake my whole case upon it. And for the first time in years he walked into the Haram with his clansmen behind him and laid a wager before Quraysh. My nephew says his Lord has informed him that your treaty has been devoured, all but the words Bismika Allahumma. Bring it out. If he is wrong, I will hand him to you this very hour; if he is right, you will end this. They agreed, certain of an easy victory, unsealed the cloth, and found there was nothing left to read but: in Your name, O Allah. They spat the word sorcery, but a promise given before all of Quraysh is a promise, and the boycott was dead. Around the tenth year of the dawah, with the Prophet ﷺ nearing fifty, the two clans walked home.

And out of those starving years came, of all things, the era's greatest poem: the Lamiyyah of Abu Talib, around a hundred verses each landing on the letter lam, the old man's furious indictment of Quraysh for what they had done to their own kin in the very shadow of the sanctuary, judged by Ibn Kathir finer than the seven hanging odes. But the seerah holds its breath here, because the relief is brief. Close ahead wait the deaths of Abu Talib and of Khadijah and the bitter road to Taif: the lowest valley of the entire seerah, and the very doorway through which Madinah will at last appear.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this stretch as two thrilling rescues and a grim siege, a dramatic week sealed safely in the past. That would be to miss the question it puts to us. Held together as the seerah insists on holding them, these scenes form a single lesson about the shape of a believing life, and it cuts in several directions at once.

Begin with the two conversions, and what they say about how we judge other people. On the morning Umar buckled on his sword, no soul in Makkah stood farther from Islam; by nightfall he was praying at the head of a row before the Kaaba. Hamza came to faith sideways, through clan pride and a swing of his bow, and Allah made him the master of the martyrs. The Prophet ﷺ had prayed for the two most dangerous men in the city by name, because he wrote no one off. So make the dua you are tempted to think is wasted. Pray for the hardest heart you know, the relative who mocks, the friend who sneers, the public figure beyond reach, because guidance was never in your hands or theirs but in the hands of the One who turned a drawn sword into the first row of the prayer. And carry the message, with respect and without flattery, to the people others actually follow.

And then there is the valley, and what it does to our idea of what a good life with Allah should feel like. For two or three years the family of the Prophet ﷺ chewed leaves inside a sealed valley while a document of starvation hung in the holiest building on earth, and most of those who suffered did not even believe. This is the seerah refusing to let us imagine that nearness to Allah buys an easy road: the same week that brought the sweetest strength brought the hungriest exam, and the same Lord, in His wisdom, authored both. So when your own doors are sealed, do everything decency and effort can do, as Hisham's five quiet men did and as the Prophet ﷺ did walking out to the tribes on an empty stomach, and then rest in the knowledge that not even a termite moves except by the command of Allah. The men who wrote that pact held the Kaaba, the wealth, the trade routes, the power of an entire city, and the thing that undid them was an insect they could not see, eating their cruelty away in the dark until nothing was left but the name of Allah.

So take both halves of this day into your ordinary life, because the seerah will not let you take only the sweet one. Despair of no one. Make worship safer for someone weaker than you with whatever strength you hold, whether muscle or money or simply a voice. Seek knowledge and pass it on. And when the world seals its doors, hold fast to the certainty that the One who set the termites upon a sealed pact is more than able to open what has been shut against you. May Allah, who turned Umar from the road of the sword to the front row of the prayer and ate away the pact of the oppressors with the smallest of His creatures, guide the hearts we have given up on, make us a shelter for His weakened servants, keep us patient when the doors close, and gather us beneath the banner of His Messenger ﷺ with Hamza and Umar on the Day of Gathering.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 18: the conversions of Hamza and Umar and the boycott of Banu Hashim (Memphis Islamic Center, 2011). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (69:41-43, 69:44-46, 20:1-2), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 19 of 104

The Year of Sorrow

Two graves, forty days apart

Some years are remembered by a number, and some by a feeling. This one is remembered by its grief. The books of seerah do not file the tenth year of the dawah under a date; they file it under a word, Aam al-Huzn, the Year of Sorrow, because within about two months the Prophet ﷺ lost the two people who had made Makkah survivable: the uncle who shielded him in the street and the wife who held him together at home. Dr. Yasir Qadhi gives the year a full evening, and it is the heaviest evening of the Makkan story so far.

Walk back into the city with them first. The boycott was over. After perhaps a year and a half of hunger, the reports only guess at the length, sympathetic voices inside Quraysh had torn up the pact pinned to the Kaaba, and Banu Hashim came home from the valley they had been penned in. It was the tenth year of the dawah, two and a half, maybe three years before the hijrah. After the ravine, the streets of Makkah must have tasted like mercy.

The relief lasted about six weeks. Then Allah willed three great calamities, one after another, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi is careful with the wording: you cannot rank them, cannot say each was heavier than the one before, because each was a wound of its own. All three fell inside roughly two months, and together they gave the whole year its name. This day carries the first two. The third is a road out of the city, and it waits at the end.

The first blow fell exactly where the whole mission leaned. Barely five or six weeks after the return, Abu Talib fell sick, and the pangs of death came upon him. He had spent the last ten years of his life standing between his nephew ﷺ and a city that wanted him gone. Now he was leaving, in the month of Shawwal, and the question of what he would say at the end was about to become the most painful sentence in the Makkan years.

The word that would not come

The chiefs of Quraysh reached the sickbed before death did. Abu Jahl, Utbah ibn Rabi'ah, the old guard of the opposition came to the dying chieftain with a final plea: you are about to leave us, settle this quarrel before it turns into civil war. Let your nephew keep to himself and stop reviling our idols, and we will keep to ourselves; no one will harm him in your lifetime. The Sheikh weighs the timing the way the Makkan years force him to, because the reports arrive without a chronology: some books set such an embassy at the start of the mission, others at the deathbed, and the elders had come to Abu Talib more than once. But it rings true that a city staring at a future with no Abu Talib in it would try him one last time.

So the old man put it to his nephew: my people have surrounded me, give them this one thing. And the Prophet ﷺ answered with the offer he had carried through thirteen years of rejection: I will give them what they ask, if they give me a single word, one kalima, and with it the Arabs and the non-Arabs will be theirs. Abu Jahl leapt at it: we will give you not one but ten! Then say la ilaha illallah, the Prophet ﷺ told them, and I will fall silent, and all the kingdoms of the Arabs and the others will be under your hand. And the generosity died in Abu Jahl's mouth. Ten conditions, yes; that one word, never.

Sahih Bukhari preserves the hour itself in a single detailed paragraph, the most authentic report we have of any death in this period. The Prophet ﷺ entered the room where his uncle lay dying and found Abu Jahl and Abdullah ibn Abi Umayyah already seated there, like sentries posted over a soul. O my uncle, he ﷺ pleaded, say la ilaha illallah, one word that I may argue for you with before Allah. And Abu Talib was about to say it. But every time he leaned toward the kalima, the two men leaned in with him: will you abandon the religion of Abdul Muttalib? Your father's religion. They had found the one rope that still held him, and they hauled on it, back and forth, until the very end. The last sentence the old man spoke kept him on the religion of Abdul Muttalib, and the word never came.

The Prophet ﷺ left that room carrying a vow that love had spoken ahead of any command: I will keep asking Allah to forgive you, he told his uncle, until I am forbidden to. Sit with the weight of that. The whole order of a prophet's life is that he does not move on his own desire; he waits upon the commandment of his Lord. Here, for once, emotion outran the order. He ﷺ had no instruction to seek forgiveness for a man who died refusing tawhid, and resolved to do it anyway, unless heaven stopped him. So heaven stopped him. And notice, Dr. Yasir Qadhi says, exactly how the answer is worded, because the gentleness of it is the point:

It is not for the Prophet and those who have believed to ask forgiveness for the polytheists, even if they were relatives, after it has become clear to them that they are companions of Hellfire.Qur'an 9:113

There is no rebuke in it. No how could you, no you have made a grave mistake. Just a boundary drawn with mercy: you are My Prophet, you know he did not die upon tawhid, and once that much is clear about a soul, however beloved, the asking has to stop. And he ﷺ, in his perfect obedience, stopped.

The man who knew

Here is what makes Abu Talib ache in the memory of this ummah forever: he knew. He was not a man groping in the dark for a truth he could not find. Ibn Ishaq records that as the chiefs left, he told his nephew ﷺ almost plainly that were it not for the fear that his people would say he embraced Islam out of dread of death, he would have said the word and given him the joy of hearing it. And his own poetry, preserved in the books of sira, confesses even more: I have come to know that the religion of Muhammad is the best of the religions of mankind, and were it not for the blame, the dread of being mocked. The line stops exactly where his life stopped.

He had even staked his entire prestige on his nephew's ﷺ truthfulness once, in public, and won, when he carried the Prophet's ﷺ claim about the worm-eaten boycott parchment straight to Quraysh and it proved true before the whole city. He did not worship the idols; his poetry is full of contempt for them. He recognized, with his own eyes, where the truth lived. He simply could not survive one sentence: he left the religion of his father. Abdul Muttalib was the legend of the Arabs, and Abu Talib was its keeper, the eldest living son and the heir of the mythology, and keeping it mattered to him more than the truth he admitted in verse. So much good and so much evil, the Sheikh marvels, can share one chest. The one real crime of this man, who fed and defended the Messenger of Allah for a decade, was the oldest sin in Arabia: the arrogance of ancestry, pure jahiliyyah.

And out of that deathbed Dr. Yasir Qadhi draws one of the deepest theology lessons of the entire series, the lesson the year was sent to teach. Iman was never information. Islam does not mean knowing; the very word means surrendering, and to merely know the truth has never once made a person a Muslim. The proof is the most terrible example imaginable. Iblis knows everything Abu Talib knew and a great deal more. He affirms that Allah is his Lord. He does not deny that Allah sends prophets. He is so certain of the Day of Judgment that he begs to be granted life until it comes. And all that knowing leaves him a kafir, because when the command came to prostrate, he refused, too proud to bow. Knowledge is not the dividing line. Surrender is.

Then the Sheikh holds up the mirror and turns it toward us. What of the one who calls himself a Muslim and never prays, never fasts, never submits a single corner of his life to the law of this religion? Set him beside Abu Talib. Both are convinced the Prophet ﷺ spoke the truth, and both refuse to let the conviction cost them anything. The difference almost runs the wrong way: the one who says I am not a Muslim is at least honest about what the word demands, while the one who says I am, and submits to nothing, is claiming a surrender he has not made. The kalima was never a fact to nod along with. It is a flag to live under. This is why, the Sheikh adds, it is a mistake to translate iman simply as faith: you can say Iblis has faith, you can say Abu Talib had faith. Faith is one part of iman, not the whole of it. Iman is faith that has bent the knee.

You cannot guide the one you love

It was Ali, radiyallahu anhu, who carried the news, and a report in the Sunan of Abu Dawud keeps the rawness of his words. He did not say my father. He said your uncle, and in one version your misguided uncle, has died. Ali bore the double wound of a believing son whose own father had refused the truth, and there is more sharpness in his grief than in the Prophet's ﷺ, who had only gentleness left to give. Go and bury him, he ﷺ said. But he died a mushrik, Ali objected. Go and bury him, came the answer again, and come straight back to me when it is done, do nothing else on the way.

Ali went, dug the grave, laid his father in it with his own hands, and came back with the dust of it still on his body. And the Prophet ﷺ made du'a for him, long and unhurried, solace poured over a young man who had just buried his father a disbeliever. Ali never let go of it: I would not exchange those du'as, he said, for this world and everything in it.

And in those same days the verse came down, the one this whole year exists around:

Indeed, [O Muhammad], you do not guide whom you like, but Allah guides whom He wills. And He is most knowing of the [rightly] guided.Qur'an 28:56

Read it against the room it was sent into. The most beloved of all creation ﷺ could not guide the one human being he loved most in this world. Guidance was never in his hand. It sits in no hand at all but Allah's. And here the Sheikh draws a line he will not let blur: if the Prophet ﷺ did not possess the power to place faith in a single beloved heart during his own lifetime, then whoever is tempted to lift him toward the station of his Lord should stand in this room and watch the matter being taken out of his hands. His rank is the highest of creation. It is the rank of a created servant, and this verse is the proof.

But read the wording one more time, because there is a mercy folded into it that the year needs you to find. Whom you like. Whom you love. Allah Himself affirmed the love. The Qur'an did not scold the Prophet ﷺ for loving a man who died refusing Islam; it redirected his istighfar and never once touched his heart. And from that single word the episode untangles a knot our own century keeps retying. Some have taken a few verses about loyalty and twisted them into a doctrine that a Muslim must hate every non-Muslim, even his own parents. That, the Sheikh says flatly, is a complete misreading. Allah here is openly affirming the Prophet's ﷺ love for his pagan uncle. The loyalty that belongs to Allah, His Messenger ﷺ, and the believers, and is never given to those who set themselves against them, is a religious loyalty, an allegiance rooted in someone's stand for Allah's cause:

You will not find a people who believe in Allah and the Last Day having affection for those who oppose Allah and His Messenger, even if they were their fathers or their sons or their brothers or their kindred.Qur'an 58:22

That is the allegiance of the heart, and it is reserved. But the natural love of a parent, a child, a spouse who do not share your faith is a different thing entirely, human and real and permitted, and the proof is as simple as a marriage contract. Islam allows a Muslim man to stay married to a Christian or a Jewish woman; it could hardly be commanding him, in the same breath, to hate his own wife. Love your people; reserve your allegiance. The two were never the same.

The shallows of the fire

Later, after the grief had settled, al-Abbas, the Prophet's ﷺ uncle, asked the question the whole clan must have carried in silence. O Messenger of Allah, did you benefit your uncle at all? He used to protect you and grow angry on your behalf. Yes, the Prophet ﷺ answered. He is in the shallows of the fire, and were it not for me, he would be in its depths. It was the intercession of the man Abu Talib had defended that lifted him from the bottom to the rim.

A report in Sahih Muslim states it with terrible precision: the one punished most lightly of all the people of the fire is Abu Talib, made to wear two sandals of fire from whose heat his brain boils. And the episode keeps the distinction exactly where the texts keep it. That is the lightest sentence among those who remain in the fire forever. The believing sinners who will pass through Jahannam and then be brought out, may Allah shield us from ever being among them, are an altogether different matter. Abu Talib's is the gentlest of the everlasting punishments, and even that gentleness reaches him only through his nephew ﷺ. The protector ended his life needing the protection of the one he protected.

Why would such an ending be written for such a man? Dr. Yasir Qadhi traces one wisdom out loud, and then stops, deliberately, at the edge of the rest. The very thing that let Abu Talib shield the dawah for ten years was his standing as the true heir of Abdul Muttalib, a chieftain his pagan city could still claim as its own. The day he said the kalima, Quraysh would have stripped the chieftainship from under him, and the shield would have fallen with it, years too early. His refusal, inside the strange perfection of the decree, held the door of belief open for everyone who came after. But then the harder question: fine, let him stay a disbeliever through the years when his protection was needed, but why not at the very end, on the bed, with nothing left to lose and a lifetime of love to reward? There the tracing stops, and the stopping is itself the lesson.

He is not questioned about what He does, but they will be questioned.Qur'an 21:23

We do not audit our Lord. We may grasp a wisdom when He shows us one and rest in His wisdom when He does not, but to stand over the decree of Allah and demand that He justify it is, the Sheikh says, literally satanic, because that is precisely what Iblis did: I know better than You. A heart with iman in it lays that habit down. Why Abu Talib was not given his last word, in the end, only Allah knows, and a believer can live inside that not-knowing.

Forty days later, Khadijah

The household had barely learned to breathe again when the second calamity came, no less heavy than the first and in some ways heavier. Ibn al-Jawzi reckons the gap at less than forty days. Abu Talib died at the beginning of Shawwal; Khadijah, radiyallahu anha, passed away on the tenth of Ramadan, a month and a handful of days later. She had stood beside the Prophet ﷺ from the first trembling night, when he came down shaking from the cave and she wrapped him and steadied him and swore that Allah would never disgrace him. His wife, his closest friend, his first believer, his first support. If Abu Talib had guarded him outside the house, Khadijah had guarded him within it.

There was no janazah for her, and the reason stops you cold: the salah itself had not yet been revealed. The funeral prayer as we know it did not exist to be prayed over her. So the Prophet ﷺ did what love could still do without a ritual to carry it. He took charge of the burial himself, went down into her grave, and laid her body into the earth with his own hands.

The companions said that after Khadijah died, they did not see the Prophet ﷺ smile for months. Set the two losses side by side and you understand at last why this year, alone in the whole seerah, is named after a feeling rather than an event. One loss took the protection of the street and the council chamber; the other took the comfort waiting behind his own door. Within forty days both were in the ground, and a grief so total settled over everything that remained that the year could only be called by its name: Aam al-Huzn, the Year of Sorrow.

No one better than her

Years later, in Madinah, long after the grave in Makkah had closed, a single knock reopened it. Khadijah's sister Halah called out for permission to enter, and her voice was so like her sister's that the Prophet ﷺ, lying down half asleep, started upright, shaken. For one unguarded heartbeat he thought it might be Khadijah. Aisha, radiyallahu anha, watched the fluster cross his face, and a jealousy she could not contain boiled over, jealousy for a rival she had never once laid eyes on. Khadijah had died at sixty five; Aisha was a young woman who knew her only as the name her husband ﷺ would not stop honoring.

So she said what jealousy says, in words she herself admitted went too far: how long will you go on remembering this old woman of Quraysh, toothless with age, when Allah has given you someone better than her? The first part, perhaps, he ﷺ could have let pass. The second he would not. No, by Allah, he said, Allah has not given me anyone better than her. She believed in me when no one else did. She supported me when the people rejected me. She spent on me when the whole city had boycotted me. And Allah gave me children through her when He gave me children through no other wife.

Aisha said: after that day, I never opened my mouth about Khadijah again. Look closely at what the Prophet ﷺ was defending, because it was not nostalgia. It was a debt, and a loyalty that did not expire with the person who earned it. Years had passed, a whole new life had been built in a new city, and still Khadijah's rank in his heart was simply not up for discussion. Whoever had stood with him in the hardest chapter of his life was written, permanently, into every chapter that came after.

The last door in Makkah

Now feel the ground shift under the whole mission. Abu Talib had been, in the Sheikh's own image, the Prophet's ﷺ visa to live in Makkah at all, and the visa had just expired. Ibn Ishaq records that with the old man gone, Quraysh raised their persecution to a height they had never dared before; an early scholar put it plainly: what they had once been forced to do in hiding, they could now do in the open.

The series has told the story of the camel's entrails in an earlier setting, but Dr. Yasir Qadhi argues it truly belongs here, and his evidence is the silence around it. No protector appears anywhere in the report, and no one is punished afterward, which fits a Makkah with no Abu Talib left in it far better than the earlier years. Uqbah ibn Abi Mu'ayt gathered the slaughter waste of a camel with his own hands, the blood, the dung, the entrails, and dumped the whole reeking load across the Prophet's ﷺ shoulders while his forehead was on the ground in sajdah. When he ﷺ finally rose with the filth and the blood clinging to him, he turned to the only protector he had left. O Allah, he said, I complain to You. And then, name by name, he handed them over: O Allah, deal with Utbah ibn Rabi'ah; deal with al-Walid; deal with Abu Jahl. Seven men, counted out before their Lord. Within a few years, by the morning of Badr, every name on that list had fallen.

Then, for one strange week, shade fell from the unlikeliest tree in Makkah. Abu Lahab, the eldest pagan son of Abdul Muttalib still living, inherited the leadership of Banu Hashim by default, Hamza and al-Abbas being Muslims, and the office briefly rearranged the man. His given name was Abd al-Uzza; Makkah called him Abu Lahab, father of flame, for the handsome face that seemed to glow, and the Qur'an had already promised, in a surah bearing his nickname, that the flame would one day be no metaphor at all:

He will [enter to] burn in a Fire of [blazing] flameQur'an 111:3

When a man from another clan abused the Prophet ﷺ, the new chieftain bristled with clan pride and went to his nephew with an offer that sounded almost like love: live as you lived in Abu Talib's day; while I am alive, you shall be as you were. But it was not love, and Abu Lahab said so himself. The Sheikh draws a sharp little lesson from it: a man can boast about how he will act, and then responsibility comes and he settles into the seat and behaves like a chieftain whether he means to or not. When the city whispered that Abu Lahab had converted, he crushed the rumor in person. No, he said, I am no Muslim. I am only protecting my flock.

Abu Jahl could not tolerate even that much cover, and he and Uqbah coached the perfect poison. Go to your nephew, they told Abu Lahab, and ask him where your father, Abdul Muttalib, is now, the father in whose name you are protecting him. So Abu Lahab asked, and the Prophet ﷺ, cornered between a truth he would not deny and a cruelty he would not commit, gave the answer that refuses both lying and savagery: he is with his people. Abu Lahab took it for reassurance and went away content, straight to Abu Jahl, who turned the knife exactly where he had aimed it. You fool, where are his people, by your nephew's own preaching, except in the fire? And the rage did the rest. You say my father is in the fire, Abu Lahab stormed back, while I protect you in his name? By Allah, never again. It had taken Abu Jahl one week to pry away the last protection in the city.

So the day ends with the Prophet ﷺ standing in his own birthplace with no uncle, no Khadijah, and no clan cover at all, and the persecution finally wearing no mask. What he ﷺ did next tells you who he was. He did not soften the message to buy safety, and he did not sit down inside his grief and stop. He lifted his eyes past the rim of the valley toward the nearest city that might yet listen: Ta'if. That road is where the series walks next, and it is a hard one. But it begins here, with a man who had buried his shield and his comfort in forty days and would not let the mission be buried with them.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this year as a stretch of bad luck the Prophet ﷺ simply endured, and leave it sealed in the past. That is to miss what it asks of us. The year heaven named for grief was sent down on the most beloved soul in all creation ﷺ, and watching him carry it is meant to change how each of us carries our own.

He was not spared loss; he was carried through it. Within forty days the mission lost its outer wall and its inner one, and Allah let it happen to the one He loved most. Sit with that the next time your own year turns dark. You are not being singled out by your sorrow; you are walking a road he ﷺ walked first, all the way down to loving someone the truth could not reach. Loss is not a sign that Allah has turned away. It was poured, in fuller measure than we will ever taste, over the one He had chosen.

At the very center of the year is the deathbed, with the wrong voices in the room. Abu Talib knew. He said in his own poetry that the religion of Muhammad ﷺ was the best of all religions, and the knowing saved him nothing, because iman was never information and the kalima was never a fact to agree with. It is a flag to live under, a surrender that costs you something, and Abu Talib would not pay the one price he had: the pride of his ancestry. So ask yourself honestly which of the two you are doing, agreeing that the kalima is true, or actually living under it. And watch who you let sit beside you, because it was Abu Jahl's voice in that room, not the dying man's own heart, that kept la ilaha illallah off his tongue at the end. Keep near you the people who would whisper the kalima to you in your last hour, not the ones who would whisper your pride.

Then there is the verse the whole year exists to teach, and the mercy hidden inside it. You do not guide whom you love; Allah guides whom He wills. The most beloved of creation ﷺ could not guide the uncle he loved most, and neither can you guide the ones you are aching over. So make du'a for them and never stop, the way he ﷺ would have gone on asking until heaven drew the line, but lay the outcome where it has always belonged. Love them with all the natural love Allah Himself affirmed and never asked the Prophet ﷺ to surrender; only do not confuse the love that is yours to give with the guidance that was never yours to grant, nor the Messenger's ﷺ station with his Lord's.

So leave this day holding the kalima a little tighter, because two graves just showed you what it is worth: not a sentence to nod at, but a flag to die under with no voice in the room strong enough to talk you out of it. May Allah give us hearts that do not merely know the truth but surrender to it, let la ilaha illallah be the last word on our tongues, grant us the loyalty of Khadijah in our homes and the gentleness of His Prophet ﷺ in our grief, carry us through our own years of sorrow as He carried His beloved ﷺ through his, and gather us with him on the day that sorrow ends for good.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 19: the deaths of Khadijah and Abu Talib (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (9:113, 28:56, 58:22, 21:23, 111:3), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 20 of 104

The incident of Ta'if

The day worse than Uhud, and the mercy that answered it

Years later, settled and safe in Madinah, Aisha radiyallahu anha asked her husband ﷺ a tender question. Was there ever a day, she wanted to know, that was harder on you than the day of Uhud? She was asking from the only suffering she had seen with her own eyes. She had lived through Uhud, watched the wounds open on his body, watched the day he very nearly lost his life, and in all her memory of him she could imagine nothing worse. He ﷺ answered her at once. Yes. Your people, he said, have hurt me a great deal, and the worst of it that I ever bore from them was on the day of Aqaba. He named a day she had never heard of, a day from a Makkah she had never known, outside a green city in the mountains called Ta'if.

This chapter is that day. It comes only weeks after the Year of Sorrow, while the graves of Khadija and Abu Talib are still fresh, when the protection that sheltered him for ten years has fallen away and Makkah has become a city with no one left to answer for him. Almost every Muslim first meets this story as a child, and almost no one ever outgrows it, because it is here, with blood drying in his sandals and not a single soul on his side, that the mercy of the Messenger ﷺ shows its full and unbearable size. Hold it gently, and walk it slowly, from the rejection all the way to the answer that came down from heaven.

Why Ta'if, and not anywhere else

For ten years he ﷺ had never once thought of leaving Makkah. Not through the long years of insult, not through the boycott that nearly starved his clan, not through the seasons of being ignored. Dr. Yasir Qadhi draws a quiet, weighty rule out of this before the journey even begins: a person stays with his own people, in the society he belongs to, for as long as it is humanly possible. Even an evil society, even one that puts barriers between you and your faith, you do not simply throw down the towel and walk out of; Nuh alayhi salam stayed among his people nine hundred and fifty years, never giving up. So the Prophet ﷺ stayed. It was only when Abu Talib died and Abu Lahab, after a few short weeks, withdrew the protection he had briefly extended, leaving his nephew with no legal standing at all, a man anyone could harm and no one would avenge, that he began, for the first time, to look toward another city.

The city he turned to was Ta'if, and the Sheikh explains why it was the natural choice. It was the nearest large city to Makkah, a twin and a rival to the Quraysh, in a relationship of love and hate by turns, too close for outright war and too near to ever fully trust. The two knew each other intimately, family by family, in a way no distant city could; if he went to Ta'if, the people would already know who he was and who his people were. And the Qur'an records how the people of those days thought of these two cities. When the deniers sneered that revelation, if it were real, should have come down upon some grand and powerful man, this is the complaint Allah preserved:

And they said, "Why was this Qur'ān not sent down upon a great man from [one of] the two cities?"Qur'an 43:31

The two cities are Makkah and Ta'if, and the words, the Sheikh notes, were spoken by al-Walid, the father of the same Khalid ibn al-Walid who would one day be the Sword of Allah. There was even a thread of blood: one of the three chieftains of Ta'if had married a distant aunt from the wider clan of the Quraysh, so that the man he was going to meet was, in some far branch, kin. If anywhere on earth would listen, it stood to reason that it would be here.

The walk, and the tying of the camel

He went on foot, and he went in secret, taking with him only Zayd ibn Haritha radiyallahu anhu, the freed man he loved like a son. Ta'if sits high in the mountains; to this day, the Sheikh reminds us, it is an hour and a half by car up precarious winding roads, and to walk it is a day and a half, perhaps two, of climbing. A mount would have spared his body, and nothing forbade him an animal. He took none. To be seen leaving Makkah on a camel would have raised suspicion, and suspicion at that moment was deadly; had the Quraysh understood he was leaving to seek a new base, they might have stopped him, or worse, before he reached the edge of the city. So he slipped out quietly, the two of them, as though he were only going for an evening stroll, with nothing about him to draw a second glance.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi lingers here, because this scene cuts straight against a lazy and dangerous idea of faith. Trusting Allah, he says, does not mean acting foolishly and calling the result destiny. The man with the most complete trust in Allah who has ever lived still planned to the last detail, crossed every t and dotted every i, plotted his exit so carefully that no one in Makkah knew he had gone. You do everything in your power, perfectly, and only then, in your heart, do you place the outcome with Allah. That is what trust actually looks like: it comes after the plan, never instead of it.

And by walking out at all, he was doing something far heavier than travel. With his protection stripped away, he had been left in a kind of legal limbo, a grey area where no clan stood behind him, and to make any move at all was itself a statement: an acceptance of the status Abu Lahab had abandoned him to, a cutting of the last fraying tie to the Quraysh. Both literally and in symbol, he was turning his back on the only home he had ever known and walking toward a city that might, just might, say yes.

Rejected, and stoned out of the city

Ta'if belonged to the tribe of Thaqif, the old rival of the Quraysh, and it was ruled by three brothers, the sons of Amr, who on their father's death had made a strange pact: rather than fight over the chieftaincy, all three would hold it together and rule as one. He ﷺ arranged to meet them, presented to all three the message of Islam, and called them to believe. They rejected him, and they did it with the cruelest sarcasm the Sheikh records anywhere in the seerah. One said that if Allah had truly sent him as a prophet, he might as well tear down the covering of the Kaaba, so absurd did he find the claim. The second said: could Allah not find anyone better than you to send? And the third refused to speak to him at all, saying that if he were really a prophet then he was too lofty to be addressed, and if he were a liar then he was too low to be worth the breath of a reply. Three men, three mockeries, the worst kind of contempt.

Even as he rose to leave, he ﷺ was still planning, still tying his camel. He asked them one thing on his way out. If you will not accept me, he said, then at least do not tell the Quraysh that I came to you. He was guarding the little ground he had left, knowing word of this errand reaching Makkah would only make his standing there more impossible, and it appears they granted him that much, for the Quraysh did not hear of it at once. Then, according to the stronger reports, he did not simply turn and leave. He stayed in Ta'if for roughly a week, working the marketplace, going down to the common people now that the leaders had refused him. This, the Sheikh says, is wisdom: you court the powerful, the ones the masses follow, but you never write off the ordinary people, and never turn one away who comes to you. Later converts would remember, years afterward, the Prophet ﷺ preaching in their streets, and no one answering his call.

And when at last it seemed that one or two might actually be listening, the leaders panicked. They gathered the riffraff of the town, the idle and the ruffians with nothing better to do, and set them on him with orders to drive this man out with stones. They pelted him until the blood ran. Zayd threw his own body into the path of the rocks and was wounded from head to foot trying to shield him, but two men cannot cover each other from a whole city while both are running for their lives. By the time the mob let them go and they staggered free of Ta'if, his sandals were soaked through with his own blood.

To You I complain

He walked out of that city in what we would honestly call a state of shock. By his own telling, he did not come back to himself, did not even register where he was, until he had reached a place miles outside Ta'if. And notice how little he ever said of it, even to the wife who loved him: he did not whine, did not parade his wounds, but kept it generic and left the details for others to tell, for our honor comes from Allah, not from displaying our injuries to people.

There, bloodied and utterly alone but for Zayd, he sat down in the shade of a tree beside a garden wall and raised the words that Ibn Ishaq preserved for us. The Sheikh notes their standing honestly: this du'a does not come with a complete chain, there are missing links in it, it is not in Bukhari or Muslim. And yet, as one of our own scholars said, it needs no chain, because the wording itself tells you it came from the heart of a prophet. It deserves, the Sheikh says, to be pondered and even memorized.

O Allah, to You I complain of my weakness, my helplessness, and my lowliness before people. O Most Merciful of those who show mercy, You are the Lord of the oppressed and the weak, and You are my Lord. To whom would You entrust me? To a distant stranger who will meet me with a hard face, or to an enemy You have given power over my affair? If You are not angry with me, then I do not care, but Your wellbeing is wider and easier for me. I seek refuge in the light of Your Face, by which all darkness is lit and the affairs of this world and the next are set right, from Your anger descending upon me or Your displeasure alighting upon me. To You belongs the right to reproach until You are pleased, and there is no power and no strength except by You.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi asks the reader to notice what is, and is not, in this plea. There is no question of why. Complaining to Allah, he explains, is not only permitted, it is sunnah. When Ya'qub's grief broke open and his children mocked how long he mourned, his answer was a complaint lifted upward:

He said, "I only complain of my suffering and my grief to Allāh, and I know from Allāh that which you do not know."Qur'an 12:86

There are, the Sheikh says, two utterly different kinds of complaint. There is the complaint of Iblis, the why are You doing this to me, the rejection of Allah's decree and His wisdom, which is not merely a sin but a kind of disbelief. And there is the complaint of the prophets, the look at me, Ya Allah, have mercy on me, the turning to Allah for Allah's own mercy, which is the very essence of tawhid. Every line of this du'a is the second kind. And then the Sheikh points to the thing that should stop the reader's breath. Listen for what frightens him most. It is not the blood on his hands, not the stones, not the abandonment. His one fear is a single question: have I done something to displease You? As long as You are not angry with me, he says, I can bear all of it. What caused him distress was never the pain of the body, but the bare possibility that he might have earned the displeasure of his Lord.

And notice, against a misunderstanding held by some, that he still asks for relief: Your wellbeing is wider for me. To be content with Allah's decree does not mean a person may never plead for the trial to be lifted; he ﷺ accepts what Allah decreed and in the same breath asks for something gentler, and there is no contradiction in it. He closes with the words he called a treasure from the treasures of Paradise: there is no power and no strength except by You. Du'a, the Sheikh says, is the weapon of the believer, and here is a man with no city, no protector, not even food in his belly, who reaches for the one weapon that is never taken away. As long as you have du'a, you are never trapped; you will carve a way out of any wall.

A bowl of grapes, and a man from Nineveh

It was as if the very instant he said there is no power except by You, the answer began to arrive. The garden whose wall he was sitting against belonged, though he did not know it, to two of his own distant relatives from the Quraysh, Utba and Shayba, the sons of Rabiah, who kept a plot of land in green Ta'if as many of the Makkans did. They had watched from a distance, seen their kinsman pelted, seen the blood, and felt the stir of the old tribal blood, an unwillingness to see one of their own treated this way by Thaqif. So they sent their servant out to him with a bowl of grapes, and the Sheikh has the reader pause over the timing. Grapes are among the most soothing of fruits, then as now, a fruit he did not even have in Makkah, set before him the moment his plea ended, as though Allah answered instantly, physically, with comfort in the hand before another minute could pass.

The servant was a Christian named Addas, from Nineveh, far away in the land of Iraq. He carried the grapes over and said they were a gift, and because it was a gift and not charity, which the Prophet ﷺ did not accept, he could take it. When he ﷺ reached for the grapes and said Bismillah, Addas startled. What is this phrase? he asked. That is not something the people of this land say. He ﷺ told him it was something his Lord had taught him, and asked where he was from. From Nineveh, said Addas. The Prophet ﷺ smiled. From the city of Yunus ibn Mata, he said, and then: he is my brother, for he too was a prophet of Allah. Addas was astonished. How do you know Yunus ibn Mata, he said, when no one in all this desert has ever heard the name of Jonah? And the man bent down and began to kiss the head and hands and feet of the Prophet ﷺ, the way the Christians honored their priests, and he believed in him then and there.

His two masters stared in disbelief that the slave they had sent with a bowl of fruit was now kneeling and kissing this stranger. They told him he had been bewitched, that his own religion was better than this man's; he answered that there was no one on earth better than this man, for he had told him things only a prophet could know. But the Sheikh draws the lesson this whole scene was built to teach, small and enormous at once. One little sunnah, a single Bismillah said openly and without the least embarrassment, not even an obligatory act, opened a heart; you never know which small, unhidden mark of your faith Allah will use to guide a soul. And there is a quiet symbolism in who He chose to send: a Christian from the furthest edge of the known world. Even as those nearest to him, Makkah and Ta'if, turned away, the farthest corner of the earth was already recognizing the truth.

The angel of the mountains, and a mercy refused

Then comes the part of the day that no human eye on earth could have witnessed, the part that returns us to the soundest of sources, to Bukhari. As he sat there, he looked up and saw a cloud shading him; if his people had stoned him out into the open sun, his Lord had sent a cloud to shelter him. And in that cloud was Jibril, who called to him: O Muhammad, your Lord has heard what your people said to you and their rejection of you, and He has sent to you the angel of the mountains, to do with as you command. Then another voice gave him salam, the angel of the mountains himself, who said: command me as you wish. If you want, I will fold the two mountains over them, for Ta'if lies in a valley between two great mountains, and the city would be crushed between them and gone.

Here, the Sheikh says, is the heart of the whole day, and he will not let the reader rush past it. Understand where he is standing. The sarcasm of the three chieftains was still ringing in his ears, the if you are a prophet I might as well tear down the Kaaba, the has Allah found no one better than you, words barely days old. His own blood was still wet on his skin. The angel of all the mountains was at his disposal, offering to erase the city that had done this to him. He had every reason, and by every measure of human justice every right, to say yes. And he ﷺ said no. No, do not do that, he said. Rather, I hope that Allah will bring forth from their loins those who will worship Him alone and associate nothing with Him.

The miracle, Dr. Yasir Qadhi insists, is not that the angel came down, nor that the mountains could be folded together; for Allah those are easy things. The real miracle, the thing the reader is meant to stagger under, is that a man treated like this, fresh from this exact rejection and this exact wound, still had enough mercy left in his heart to refuse, and to ask instead that the descendants of his torturers be guided to worship Allah. If someone says one sharp word to us, the Sheikh says, we will nurse it for ten years. He was stoned out of an entire city, and he prayed for that city's unborn children. This, exactly this, is what it means that Allah sent him as a mercy to all the worlds. And his du'a was answered to the letter. Barely ten years later he ﷺ himself would bring Ta'if into Islam, and the Sheikh, who has stood there, tells us that the very ground where he was tortured is, to this day, a masjid where Allah is worshipped through the day and the night. Had he willed it, there would be no city there at all.

The jinn of Nakhlah

The journey home was long, and the wounds did not wait for it. On the road back he camped in a small desert grove in the valley of Nakhlah, a place that still exists, with the whole trip there and back stretching across some ten days. He had not yet washed the blood from his body; the scars had not healed, he was not yet home, he had every excuse a person could have. And as was his unbroken habit, he stood up in the cold of that desert night to pray tahajjud. He never once abandoned that prayer, not in travel, not in injury, not while bleeding in the middle of nowhere, and his night prayer was not like ours but two and three hours of standing. Most of us, the Sheikh observes plainly, can barely manage the five daily prayers in all our comfort.

And as he recited the Qur'an into the dark, something happened that no eye of any person on earth could see, that only Allah could tell us about afterward, and did. He turned a company of the jinn toward him to listen. It was no accident of their wandering; He had directed their path to carry them past a prophet praying alone in the night.

And [mention, O Muḥammad], when We directed to you a few of the jinn, listening to the Qur'ān. And when they attended it, they said, "Listen attentively." And when it was concluded, they went back to their people as warners.Qur'an 46:29

When the world of men had mocked him, the Sheikh says, the world of the jinn fell silent. Listen attentively, they told one another, and they did not stir until he had finished. Then they turned back to their own people, and the Qur'an is precise about what they became: not merely believers, but warners, little messengers carrying the message onward to their kind.

O our people, respond to the Caller [i.e., Messenger] of Allāh and believe in him; He [i.e., Allāh] will forgive for you your sins and protect you from a painful punishment.Qur'an 46:31

The Sheikh reads the symbolism plainly, and after the week this man has had it is almost overwhelming. The seen had rejected him, and now the unseen, the jinn of Nakhlah, stopped dead in their tracks to listen. The people of Makkah could put a barrier between him and a soul or two in their streets, but who could chain the jinn, or forbid the truth from reaching the worlds beyond the sight of men? The truth is not contained by the reach of human hands, and no power on earth can wall in its spread. This was the first company of jinn to accept Islam, and not the last; they would return, the Sheikh notes from the report of Ibn Mas'ud, to learn their religion from the Prophet ﷺ himself.

How he came home

There remained the hard practical matter of getting back into Makkah at all, a city he had, by leaving it, effectively renounced. Zayd asked the obvious, aching question: O Messenger of Allah, how will we even enter Makkah now that they have driven you out? And he answered: O Zayd, Allah will surely make for us a way and a relief; Allah will help His Prophet and not abandon us. This, the Sheikh says, is finally where tawakkul takes its rightful place. Stranded in the desert with no city behind him and none ahead, having done everything in his power and fallen short in nothing, now, only now, he rests the rest with Allah: I do not know what will happen, but I know that Allah will not forsake me.

He sent quietly to two or three men of the Quraysh whom he judged might be sympathetic, men not of the same hard heart as Abu Jahl, asking their protection so that he might re-enter the city. Dr. Yasir Qadhi notes these were chosen with care, not at random, for several of them would in time accept Islam. The first declined, pleading too weak a position to give protection. The second declined too, with a flimsy excuse about precedent that was plainly not the real reason; and the Sheikh reads the embarrassment itself as a sign, for a person gives a feeble excuse when he knows he ought to act and cannot, which tells you his heart was not as hard as the others. The third said yes. It was Mut'im ibn Adi, chief of one of the clans of the Quraysh, the very man who years before had broken the boycott against Banu Hashim, sending loaded camels of food to the besieged at his own great expense. Mut'im did not merely send word back. He armed his four sons, sent them out dressed for battle to escort the Prophet ﷺ into the city, then stood at the Kaaba and announced to all of Makkah that Muhammad ﷺ was under his protection. Even Abu Sufyan, the Sheikh recounts, asked whether Mut'im had become a follower or was merely granting protection, and accepted it only when told it was protection alone.

Mut'im died a pagan; he never accepted Islam. And yet years later, after the battle of Badr, when more than seventy prisoners of the Quraysh were in the Prophet's ﷺ hands, he ﷺ said that if Mut'im ibn Adi were alive and had asked him for these prisoners, he would have released every one of them for his sake. The Sheikh draws the last lesson of the chapter here, and it is a striking and generous one. Not all who reject the faith are the same. A person may not share your belief and still stand for justice and the protection of the weak, and Allah can grant victory to this religion through such a person, for the Prophet ﷺ taught that Allah aids His religion even by means of people who have no faith. There is no shame in honoring such people for the good they do, in forming alliances with the Mut'ims of every age, so long as the line is kept clear: you honor the justice, not the disbelief. He ﷺ would not pray over Mut'im or make du'a for his soul; religion remained religion. But the decency of the man he repaid with a praise that has outlasted Mut'im's own grave.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read Ta'if as a sad story safely sealed in the past and close it there. That would be to miss what it puts to us. There is a reason he named this, and not Uhud, the worst day his people ever gave him. Uhud wounded the body; Ta'if wounded the heart, and a wound to the heart, as the Sheikh says, is the heavier of the two. And the whole weight of the day is in what he did with that wound.

He complained, and he complained upward, taking his grief to the only One who could lift it instead of carrying it sideways to people who could only watch. When your own day of Ta'if comes, and it will, that is the first question it asks: the complaint of Iblis on your tongue, or the complaint of Ya'qub. And he feared the right thing. Bloodied and abandoned, his single fear was not the stones but whether he had displeased his Lord. Here is a measure to lay against every hard day you are given: not how much it hurt, but whether Allah is pleased with you in the middle of it, a question beside which most of our troubles shrink to their true size. And he tied his camel before he trusted, planning to the last detail and only then resting the outcome with the One in whose hands it always was.

But the thing to carry out of this chapter above all the rest is the mercy. He was handed the destruction of the city that broke his body, an angel waiting on his single word, and he refused it, asking instead that the children of his attackers be guided. When you are most wronged is exactly when mercy weighs the most and costs the most, and exactly then that it counts the most. The ground where he was tortured became a place where Allah is worshipped day and night, because a man on the worst day of his life chose to pray for a future he would not see rather than avenge a wound he could still feel. Every mercy you have ever been shown runs downstream from a heart that could do this.

So take this day down into your ordinary ones. When the walls close in, complain only upward, in his words: O Allah, to You I complain of my weakness and my helplessness and my lowliness before people; You are the Lord of the oppressed and You are my Lord; if You are not angry with me, I do not care, but Your wellbeing is wider for me. Fear His displeasure more than your pain, do everything you honestly can and trust Him with the rest, and when the chance comes to crush or to forgive, choose, as he chose, the harder and higher road. May Allah send His peace and blessings upon His Messenger ﷺ, who was wronged as no one has been wronged and answered it with mercy, gather us behind him, and make the very ground we are broken upon, in the end, a place where He alone is worshipped. Ameen.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 20: the incident of Ta'if (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (43:31, 12:86, 46:29, 46:31), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 21 of 104

The Night Journey and the Ascension, part 1

After the hardest year, the heavens opened

By the time this night arrives, the Prophet ﷺ has buried Khadijah, radiyallahu anha, the woman who believed him first and steadied him through the worst of Makkah. He has buried Abu Talib, the uncle whose protection kept the swords sheathed for a decade. And he has limped back from Ta'if, where he went hoping for shelter and was answered with stones until the blood filled his sandals. One year took almost everything a man can lose, and the early Muslims called it what it was, the year of sorrow.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi opens this episode the way the religion itself answers grief, with the promise stitched into every trial Allah sends: He never tests a servant who meets the test with patience except that ease comes with the hardship and after it. So after the lowest valley of his life ﷺ, it was only fitting that the next thing should be one of the highest peaks. The Sheikh goes further than fitting. Some scholars, he notes, hold that what came next was the single greatest miracle the Prophet ﷺ was ever granted personally. The Qur'an was the miracle given to the whole ummah, a sign meant to last to the end of time. This night was the miracle given to him alone. The Qur'an was the gift to us. This was the gift to him.

The gift after the year of sorrow

Two words name the night, and the names carry the shape of the journey. Al-Isra is built from a word that means to travel by night; it is the ride from Makkah to Jerusalem, a journey across the surface of this earth. Al-Mi'raj is something else. It is not the ascent itself but the instrument of ascent, the apparatus you rise by, the way you might say a lift or an elevator: a thing Allah made for climbing from Jerusalem up and out through the heavens, a journey that has nothing to do with this world at all. Allah references both in the Qur'an, the Isra in the surah that bears its name, the Mi'raj in Surah an-Najm.

And listen to how He opens the surah that carries this night. He begins with the word He reserves for the most exalted of all things.

Exalted is He who took His Servant by night from al-Masjid al-Haram to al-Masjid al-Aqsa, whose surroundings We have blessed, to show him of Our signs. Indeed, He is the Hearing, the Seeing.Qur'an 17:1

Subhan: glory be to the One free of every imperfection, who has no defect and no flaw. He praises Himself, and the Sheikh asks us to feel the weight of that. Why is glory due to Him here? Because of this journey. He is announcing that He is worthy of being exalted on account of what He is about to tell you. That is how immense this night is: Allah opens by praising His own perfection because of it.

And notice what He calls the one He carried: not by name, but by title. His Servant, abdihi. The Sheikh stops here, because this is the highest praise Allah ever gives him. Allah tells us why He made us:

And I did not create the jinn and mankind except to worship Me.Qur'an 51:56

So when Allah calls a man His servant, His slave, He is naming the one who perfected the very reason creation exists, the one who worshipped Him as no one before or since. Among ourselves, we address the great by title rather than name, Mr. President, Your Highness, because the title outranks the name. So it is here, and more so: more often than Allah names him ﷺ in the Qur'an, He titles him. To be called by name is honor; to be called His Servant is the summit.

And the ayah states the journey's one purpose plainly: to show him of Our signs. Surah an-Najm answers it from the far side of the night, that he certainly saw of the greatest signs of his Lord. Promised here, delivered there. Notice for whom this night was meant. Not for Quraysh, who would do nothing but mock it. Not even for us, who were not there and can only believe what we are told. The whole journey was a personal gift to him ﷺ; we are simply permitted to overhear it. And it came when it came for the reason this series keeps arriving at: no one passes through a trial with patience except that the sweetness is already on its way.

Twenty narrators and one night

Before the first step, the Sheikh pauses for an honest word about sources, the place where Dr. Yasir Qadhi the storyteller makes room for the academic. The night journey, he says, is among the most heavily narrated events in the whole seerah: more than twenty companions carried pieces of it, six of them in Sahih al-Bukhari alone. Here is the fact he asks us to sit with: gather every authentic report about the entire Makkan period except this night into one volume, and gather every report about this single night into another, and the volume about one night would be the thicker of the two.

That abundance brought trouble. Professional storytellers fell in love with this story in the earliest generations, men who made a living going masjid to masjid spinning tales the crowds loved, much of it pure invention dressed in vivid, impossible detail. The scholars rose against them and pushed until storytellers were banned from the masjids outright, but by then some of those fabrications had soaked into the popular books. And even after the authentic reports are sifted from the false, a labor the Sheikh says he has spent long hours on himself, a second problem remains. What is left is a box of true puzzle pieces with very little to fix their order. It is, he says, like asking twenty friends to retell one very long day: each starts somewhere different, each remembers a different handful of moments, each tells it in his own words. To reconstruct the exact sequence with certainty is, in honesty, impossible.

The knots were real enough that great scholars wrestled with them. Imam an-Nawawi, among others, allowed in one verdict that perhaps there had been more than one night journey, because certain reports seemed impossible to reconcile otherwise, and this was no fringe view. But the Sheikh stands with the majority and reads the answer straight out of the ayah: Allah said He took His servant by night, laylan, one servant, one night. One journey, and the small seams between narrations are to be reconciled, not multiplied.

As for when it happened, no companion ever recorded a date. The earliest writers of sira, Ibn Ishaq foremost among them, placed it about a year before the hijrah, and that is where most of the early authorities landed; others guessed two years, even five, but one authentic clue closes the window. Khadijah passed away before the five daily prayers were made obligatory, and the prayers were made obligatory on this very night. So the journey came after her death, which means after the year of sorrow, in the last stretch of Makkah. The month is genuinely unknown; the Sheikh found five claims among the early scholars, the most famous being Rajab, and not one rests on an authentic chain. And there is wisdom in that silence. The companions, who preserved the smallest details of the night, simply let the date fall away, because the date was never the point. What happened matters; when it happened does not. To mark a particular evening as the night of the Isra, when we do not even know the month, is to commemorate what the people closest to it deliberately did not.

The roof opens, the heart is washed

Where did the night begin? Bukhari preserves two answers, and the Sheikh shows how they fit together rather than fight. In one report the Prophet ﷺ says he was lying in the Hijr, the low semicircle of wall beside the Ka'bah; that space was once part of the House itself, left outside the walls when Quraysh rebuilt the Ka'bah and their lawful funds ran short, which is why to pray in the Hijr is to pray inside the original Ka'bah. In another report he says he was in his house when the roof opened and Jibril descended. The scholars read the two as one story: taken from his house, brought first to the Hijr. Why, the Sheikh asks, would a man with a home in Makkah be sleeping out in the open beside the Ka'bah? Not every difference is a contradiction.

There Jibril opened his chest. He brought a basin of gold, and the reports describe what filled it two ways: one says Zamzam, another says iman, faith itself. Both are true at once, the Sheikh says, for whatever was in that basin was poured in to carry faith into him, and it was Zamzam too. Jibril washed his heart and returned it to its place. This was the second washing of his life. The first came when he was a small boy in the desert, and that time a black speck was drawn out of his heart and discarded. This time there is no speck to remove. So if the heart was already cleansed long ago, why wash it again? Because this washing is not a cleansing. It is a fortifying.

And it had to be, because of what came next. He ﷺ was about to step clean out of the world as we know it. It is related that if another man had been shown even a fraction of what the Prophet ﷺ was about to see, he would have lost his mind on the spot. So Allah strengthened the heart before the seeing, and the Qur'an testifies to the result.

The heart did not lie about what it saw.Qur'an 53:11
The sight did not swerve, nor did it transgress its limit. He certainly saw of the greatest signs of his Lord.Qur'an 53:17-18

The eye did not flinch, did not wander, did not look away in fear. He gazed steadily, with a heart made strong for the purpose, at sights no human being had ever been permitted to see.

A creature named lightning

Then Jibril brought the mount. The Prophet ﷺ describes it with care: a riding beast, pure white, smaller than a mule and larger than a donkey, and by a report in Tirmidhi it wore a harness and a saddle. Its name was al-Buraq, grown from the root that gives the word for lightning, and its stride is the detail no one forgets. Each hoof came down at the farthest point its eye could see. Run whatever numbers you like, the Sheikh says: if the horizon is ten miles off and a single step carries you there, and the next the same, then in a heartbeat you have crossed twenty miles, and in a minute a distance the mind cannot hold. This is a creature faster than anything we have ever built.

And notice what is absent from the description: wings. The winged horse of the later paintings and legends was never narrated. The hadith describes a creature of flesh and blood, and it behaves like one. When the Prophet ﷺ moved to mount it, the Buraq startled and reared, the way any animal shies under an unfamiliar rider. Jibril seized the harness and rebuked it: are you not ashamed? No one more honored in the sight of Allah has ever ridden you than the one who rides you now.

Sit with that sentence, the Sheikh says, because of what it quietly admits. Others have ridden this creature before, riders we are told nothing about, on errands of Allah we know nothing of. The Qur'an tells us that Allah creates what we have no knowledge of; even the jinn, we learned not long ago, have their own beasts. Whole orders of creation move through worlds beyond our small catalogue, and tonight one of them stood saddled in Makkah, shying at its noblest rider ﷺ before carrying him out of the world.

The masjid only he could see

The Buraq carried him ﷺ to Jerusalem, and the Sheikh asks us to picture the city honestly, as it truly was in that year and not as it is now. There is no dome on the hill, no minaret, no sanctuary, no structure of worship at all. Rome rules Jerusalem, and Rome is now a Christian empire, and its rulers, out of an old contempt for the Jews, had deliberately turned the site of the ancient Temple into a refuse heap, a dumping ground for the city's filth. To every other eye in Jerusalem that night, the holiest hill on earth after Makkah was rubble and sewage.

He ﷺ did not see what the city saw. He says: I tied the Buraq to the ring that the prophets tie their mounts to. A hitching post, worn by the prophets who had prayed there before him, at a masjid the world had buried under garbage. Allah unveiled to him the house of worship as it truly is, the original sanctuary no other eye could find. Then he walked in and prayed two rak'ahs. Greeting a masjid with prayer on entering it would only be legislated years later, in Madinah, and the five daily prayers did not yet exist at all; yet here he ﷺ was already living what he would one day be commanded to teach.

And he was not alone. He turned and found them, or perhaps had known all along, for the narrations carry it both ways: the prophets, gathered, standing in prayer. He describes them the way a man points across a room at faces he can see. Musa: tall, dark, powerfully built, like the men of the tribe of Shanu'ah. Isa: of a reddish-white complexion, shorter, his hair glistening as though he had just stepped from a bath. And of Ibrahim he said the simplest and most striking thing of all, that of all people no one resembled him more closely than he himself did.

Then the call to prayer was given, and he ﷺ was put forward to lead. The Sheikh asks us to weigh this slowly. We were told, many lessons ago, that the prophets number around a hundred and twenty thousand; here they stand, well over a hundred thousand souls in a single unbroken row behind him, stretching beyond the reach of the eye. And every prophet is the leader of his own ummah, so the imam of all the prophets is, by extension, the leader of every ummah that ever was. He ﷺ would later say it plainly, that he is the master of the children of Adam, and he added at once: and it is no boast. It is simply what happened that night in Jerusalem.

One more thing about that row holds the night together. He had already passed Musa on the way, standing in prayer in his grave; here Musa prayed in the row in Jerusalem; soon, in the sixth heaven, they would meet a third time. Death had not stopped a single one of them from praying. The same prayer, toward the same Lord, carrying the same message: one family at one task across all of time, some of them preferred above others, and the one leading them all preferred above every one.

Two cups, one ummah

After the prayer, Jibril held out two vessels. One narration places this moment in Jerusalem, another during the ascent; a small seam, and the Sheikh says it changes nothing. In one was wine. In the other, milk. And remember exactly where we stand in the story: wine has not yet been forbidden. The believers of Makkah still drink it without sin. So this was not a test of law. It was a test of instinct.

Jibril said: choose, and choose for your ummah, for this choice will not stop at you. Fourteen centuries hung on the reach of one hand. He ﷺ took the milk, and Jibril told him: you have chosen the fitrah. The Sheikh lingers on the symbolism, because it runs deep. Milk is the Qur'an's own image of purity.

And indeed, for you in grazing livestock is a lesson. We give you drink from what is in their bellies, between excretion and blood, pure milk, palatable to drinkers.Qur'an 16:66

Pure milk drawn clean from between filth and blood, wholesome and nourishing, the drink the Prophet ﷺ loved, and, as a hadith notes, the one thing that serves as both food and drink at once. Wine is the opposite story told backward: something once pure, left to ferment and rot until it steals the very mind that milk would have fed. And the Sheikh relays the word of one of the companions, that even if wine had never been prohibited at all, a man of sound intelligence would have walked past it on his own.

You have chosen the fitrah, Jibril said, and your ummah will be upon it. The Prophet ﷺ taught that every child is born upon the fitrah, the clean original setting, good at its core, and Jibril used that very word for the milk. So that night, long before the laws of permitted and forbidden came down one at a time, the direction of this whole ummah was set: purity as its default, the fitrah as its home.

Doors that ask who is there

The Buraq stays tied at the post in Jerusalem; from here the second journey begins, the Mi'raj, the rising. At the lowest heaven there is a door, and it is locked. Jibril asks for it to be opened, and from the other side a gatekeeper answers: who is this? Jibril. And who is with you? Muhammad ﷺ. Has he been sent for? Yes. Only then does the door open. And the same exchange repeats, word for word, at the second heaven, and the third, all the way to the seventh.

Hold that picture, the Sheikh says. There are doors in the heavens, and they are locked, and each has an appointed keeper, and no one passes without permission, not even Jibril. And the only credential required is a word, because the angels cannot lie. No paper, no stamp, no visa; the angel simply says that Allah has commanded it, and an angel does not deceive. In a world without falsehood, your word is your passport.

What are these seven heavens? Here the Sheikh slows down, because two words get tangled in people's minds: the samawat and the jannat. The samawat are seven created skies, layered one above the next, each enclosing the one below it. And in his considered view, everything astronomy has ever seen, every star in every telescope, every galaxy billions of light years out, the whole observable universe, is only the first of the seven, the nearest heaven.

And who created seven heavens in layers. You do not see in the creation of the Most Merciful any inconsistency. So return your vision to the sky; do you see any breaks?Qur'an 67:3
And We have certainly beautified the nearest heaven with lamps and have made from them what is thrown at the devils and have prepared for them the punishment of the Blaze.Qur'an 67:5

The lamps are the stars, and Allah calls the sky He set them in the nearest heaven, the lowest of the seven. Study even the most basic astronomy and the mind begins to lose its footing, the Sheikh says, at the distances, the light years, the galaxies so far off that what we see is what they were ages ago. Every larger telescope only finds more, and still we have not reached the edge of it. And all of that is heaven number one. Six more stretch beyond it, each wrapping the last, and the jannat, the gardens of Paradise, lie at the very top of them all.

Which untangles a confusion before it can start. To meet Adam in the first heaven does not mean Adam dwells on the lowest level of Paradise; the samawat are not the jannat, and in any case no one is in Paradise yet. It stands empty until the Day of Judgment. The prophets he ﷺ is about to meet were brought out to the heavens for one purpose on this night alone: to welcome him.

Seven welcomes, seven messages

Behind the first door stood a tall, towering man, and Jibril said: this is your father Adam, greet him. Adam returned his salam and welcomed him: welcome, O righteous son and righteous prophet. And here the Sheikh clears away the most common mistake about this scene. The order of the heavens is not a ranking of the prophets, lowest to highest; these are welcoming parties. When a dignitary arrives, men of honor receive him at each stage of his coming, and Allah sent out some of the noblest of all His prophets to receive this one guest ﷺ. And each greeter, read rightly, is a message addressed to him, a man on the eve of being driven from his home.

Adam first, fittingly, since he is the father not only of the prophets but of all mankind, and because his story is the Prophet's ﷺ own in miniature: chosen for the holiest of homes, made to leave it, and promised a return. Within a year the son before him would be driven from the holiest city on earth, expelled the way Shaytan once drove Adam out, with the Abu Lahabs of Makkah playing the old part, and like his father he would one day return. The second heaven held Isa and Yahya, the sons of two sisters, the prophets nearest him in time, both savaged by their own people; Yahya was killed outright, a thing recorded in their own scriptures, and Isa escaped only because Allah raised him up. The message: you are not the first whose own people turned on him. The third heaven held Yusuf, given half of all beauty, wronged by his own blood brothers, who lived to hear them confess and forgave them. The Prophet ﷺ never forgot that one; years from this night, standing over a conquered Makkah while Quraysh waited to learn their fate, he would reach for Yusuf's exact words.

He said, "No blame will there be upon you today. May Allah forgive you; and He is the most merciful of the merciful."Qur'an 12:92

The fourth heaven held Idris, of whom the Qur'an tells us essentially one thing.

And We raised him to a high station.Qur'an 19:57

A single line about a high station, spoken now over a man being raised through the heavens themselves, who stands in the fourth of seven. The fifth held Harun, despised by his people and then beloved by them, another rejection that ended in love.

The sixth held Musa, and no prophet's road resembled his ﷺ more closely: the trials, the stubborn people, the long hard years, and the second largest ummah ever gathered behind a prophet. All his life the Prophet ﷺ would steady himself by him; when some injury cut deep, he would say that Musa was harmed more than this and was patient, and keep walking. And as the Prophet ﷺ passed beyond him in the heavens, Musa wept. Asked why, he said: this young man was sent after me, and more of his ummah will enter Jannah than mine. A young man, he called him, though the Prophet ﷺ was past fifty; but Musa had lived to a hundred and twenty and more, so to him this was a man half his age. And the tears were not a grudge.
