They were ghibtah, the believer's envy, which grieves not that another has something but that another carried more good home to Allah. It is the one jealousy this religion smiles upon: to wish you too had recited more, given more, drawn nearer.

And the seventh heaven held Ibrahim, resting his back against al-Bayt al-Ma'mur. He alone greeted the Prophet ﷺ as Adam had, as righteous son and righteous prophet; every other prophet had said brother, but the two fathers said son, and you can hear the pride of a father in it. Ibrahim is the khalil, the intimate friend, and the Prophet ﷺ said Allah took only the two of them for that rank. As for the house at his back, it is the frequented house, the Ka'bah of the heavens, set so exactly above the Ka'bah of earth that were it to fall it would land upon it. The one authentic hadith about it tells us that seventy thousand angels enter it every day to worship and never return, a fresh seventy thousand every day since creation began; do that arithmetic until the mind gives out. And why is Ibrahim the one resting there, at ease against a house an angel may enter but once in all eternity? Because he built the house below. The reward takes the shape of the deed: raise Allah's house on the earth, and you are given to rest against Allah's house in the seventh heaven.

One quiet note rings ahead out of all these meetings. Of every prophet he met in the heavens, only Isa is still alive, raised up and not yet returned, and so only Isa had to come down to meet him that night, as he will come down once more at the end of time.

What this night asks of our faith

It would be easy to read the night journey as a wonder safely sealed in the past, a story of a white mount and seven skies, and to leave it there. That would be to miss what it puts to us. The Sheikh ends this first ascent with the journey still climbing, and we will leave the Prophet ﷺ exactly where he left him, standing in the seventh heaven with Ibrahim beside the frequented house and above them what no created eye had yet been shown. But take three things down the stairs into your ordinary days.

The first is that patience gets paid, and sometimes sooner than you dare hope. The year of sorrow, the burials, the blood at Ta'if: these were not the end of the story but the eve of the highest night of his life. Allah does not test a servant who endures with patience except that ease is already written on the far side of the trial, often nearer than the next sunrise. When your own hardest season closes around you, hold that as the literal pattern of his life ﷺ: the lowest valley was the doorstep of the highest peak.

The second is the choice of the milk. Two cups sat in front of him, and the wine was still entirely permitted that night; no law forbade it yet. He chose the milk anyway, and Jibril named the choice the fitrah. Permissible, the Sheikh shows us, is not the only filter a believer uses. There is a higher one: what keeps the heart and the mind clean, what fits the original setting Allah made you on. We spend our days asking only whether a thing is allowed; he ﷺ chose by a deeper instinct, and chose it for all of us. When two cups sit before you and both are lawful, reach for the one that keeps you pure.

And the third is the prayer. Look at where he found it on this night: Musa standing in prayer in his grave, the prophets standing in prayer in Jerusalem in a single row, a greeter standing in prayer behind every door in the heavens. Salah is the family trade of the prophets, the thing not even death interrupts, and it was on this very night, after the year of sorrow, that the five daily prayers were bound to this ummah as a gift. The companions could not preserve the date of the night, and did not try; they preserved the prayer instead, and so should we. Guard it like the inheritance it is, handed down from the night the heavens opened.

The ascent is not finished. Tonight he ﷺ stands at the seventh heaven, and tomorrow the climb continues past it, into what no eye has seen and no heart could have invented. O Allah, as You carried Your Servant ﷺ by night and raised him through the heavens, raise our love for him, fortify our hearts as You fortified his, keep us upon the fitrah he chose for us, and on the Day You open every locked door, gather us behind him in the row of the prophets. Ameen.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 21: the Night Journey and the Ascension, part 1 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (17:1, 51:56, 53:11, 53:17-18, 16:66, 67:3, 67:5, 12:92, 19:57), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 22 of 104

The Night Journey and the Ascension, part 2

Past the last tree, where the prayer was given

Yesterday we left him ﷺ at the seventh heaven, Ibrahim resting his back against the frequented house, the seven welcomes of the seven prophets behind him. Tonight the ascent does not stop there. There is one more tree to pass, one more boundary at which even the mightiest angel of Allah must stay behind, and then a meeting whose details were kept private from us all, except for the single gift that was sent down from it and placed into your ordinary day.

This is the heart of al-Isra wal-Mi'raj, the middle of three nights of telling. Dr. Yasir Qadhi is honest at the very start, the way the sound study of the seerah forces a person to be honest. The reports of this night reach us as scattered fragments, each one a true piece, but with very little to fix their exact order. We have one narration in which the prophets are deep in conversation, and we cannot say for certain where it happened: in Jerusalem, in the masjid before the ascent, or somewhere on the climb itself. Where the order is unknown, the Sheikh simply says so, and so will we. What is certain is the shape of the journey: up, past the last created thing, to the Lord above the veil, and back down again with the prayer in hand.

What the prophets spoke of

Somewhere on this night, the narrations do not pin down exactly where, the prophets fell into conversation, and Allah preserved a piece of it for us through Abdullah ibn Masud, radiyallahu anhu. They were speaking of the one matter none of them could be casual about: the Day of Judgment. Ibrahim was asked first, and he said he had no knowledge of it. Musa was asked, and he too said he had no knowledge. Then they turned to Isa, and Isa had an answer, because Isa had been told his own part in what was coming.

He said that Allah would send him back to this earth, and that he would be the one to kill the Dajjal. After that, he said, the peoples would scatter to their various lands, and Yajuj and Majuj would pour out from every direction, drinking dry every water they passed until nothing was left, until he would pray against them and Allah would destroy them in a single night, the whole earth left reeking with their decay as far as a person could smell. Then, he said, he would pray again, and Allah would send rain from the sky to wash the world clean of them. And after all of that, he said, the Hour would be like a pregnant woman at the very end of her term: everyone knows the birth is near, no one knows the hour it comes. The Prophet ﷺ confirmed it the way he confirmed so much that night. This, he said, is found in the Qur'an, and he recited one of its references to Yajuj and Majuj.

Until when [the dam of] Gog and Magog has been opened and they, from every elevation, descendQur'an 21:96

Sheikh Yasir pauses on what this small scene quietly explains. So much of what the Prophet ﷺ would later teach his ummah about the end of time, about Isa returning, about the Dajjal, about Yajuj and Majuj, has a source you can now point to: Isa told him directly, on the night he was carried up. One conversation out of who knows how many was preserved for us. What else did the prophets discuss that night? Allahu alam. The rest is in the unseen, and the believing thing to do is to leave it there, exactly where Allah left it.

A father's salam, and a garden's empty soil

There was another exchange that night, and it is a gift handed down to every single one of us. The Prophet ﷺ said: I met Ibrahim on the night I was taken, and he said to me, O Muhammad, convey my salam to your ummah. Hold that for a moment before you move past it. Our father Ibrahim, the khalil of Allah, the intimate friend of the Most High, sending his peace forward across the centuries to a people not yet born, and sending it through the one descendant of his who would carry it to them. So receive it tonight exactly as it was sent: peace from Ibrahim, to you.

And he sent advice along with the greeting. Tell your ummah, he said, that the soil of Jannah is the most beautiful and fertile soil there is, but that it is a flat, barren plain, with nothing planted in it yet. Then he told them how its trees come to grow. Every time a servant says subhanallah, alhamdulillah, la ilaha illallah, Allahu akbar, a tree is planted for him in that rich ground. The soil is guaranteed to bear fruit; what it is waiting for is your seed. You are not guaranteed a single tree until you plant it with your own tongue.

It is the gentlest possible reading of dhikr. The remembrance you scatter through an ordinary day, muttered under your breath in traffic, repeated after the prayer, half-asleep on the pillow, is not vanishing into the air. It is being planted, in the richest soil that exists, in a garden you are quietly growing while you are still on your way toward it. Ibrahim wanted you to know that, and he asked the Prophet ﷺ to make sure the message reached you. Sheikh Yasir lingers here on the sheer kindness of it: a prophet who saw Jannah with his own eyes, and the one thing he chose to send back was an instruction for how the rest of us might fill ours.

The angel who has never smiled

On this night he ﷺ was also brought to meet Malik. Not the angel of death, whose name is Malak al-Mawt; Malik is someone else, the keeper of the Fire, the gatekeeper the Qur'an names. Jibril said to him: this is Malik, give him your salam. The Prophet ﷺ turned to greet him, and before he could even speak, Malik greeted him first. And he noticed something at once. Every other angel that night had carried a brightness, a warmth, a visible joy at meeting him. Malik did not. He met the best of all creation with a face that held no smile at all, somber and grave.

So the Prophet ﷺ asked Jibril why this one was different, and Jibril answered: he has never once smiled or laughed since the day he was created, because of what he was made to guard. Once Malik had seen the Fire, he never smiled again. Then Jibril added a line that should land softly on your heart: had Malik ever been going to smile for anyone, he would have smiled for you, O Muhammad ﷺ. The keeper of the Fire, somber since the beginning of his existence, makes his one exception in his heart for the Messenger of Allah, and still cannot smile, because of the weight of what he stands over.

There is a courtesy in this scene the Sheikh does not want you to miss. The Prophet ﷺ would be shown the Fire that night, which means that if he had wished, he could have passed by Malik at his post and greeted him there. Instead, the arrangement was the reverse: Malik was brought up to him, and Malik was made to greet first. From this, Sheikh Yasir draws one of the wisdoms of the whole night. The whole arrangement is a statement about distance. The Messenger of Allah ﷺ is as far from that Fire as the seventh heaven is from the lowest of creation. Even its noble keeper comes up to him, and pays him the respect a person pays a superior, greeting him first and waiting to be answered. The gap between this man and that place could not be drawn more clearly than by who was brought to whom.

The Lote Tree of the Utmost Boundary

Then, having passed the seventh heaven and all the prophets, Jibril continued up with him until they came to it: Sidrat al-Muntaha, the Lote Tree of the Utmost Boundary. The sidr was a tree the Arabs knew well, a desert tree prized for its wide, shading branches, its sweet fruit, its pleasant scent. And muntaha means the very end, the utmost limit. That is precisely what this tree marks. It is the edge of creation as we know it. Everything that rises up from the earth, Sheikh Yasir explains, ascends to here and goes no further: the souls, the prayers, the supplications, the righteous deeds, the wholesome word, all of it carried up and absorbed at the Sidra. And everything that Allah sends down to the earth, His mercy and the rain and every decreed thing, begins its descent from here. The Lote Tree is the great threshold between our world and whatever lies above it.

He described it the only way it could be described to people who had never seen it: in the language of things they already knew. Its fruit were like the great clay water jars of the town of Hajar, storage vessels so large the people he spoke to could picture them at once, though we have lost track of their size. Its leaves were like the ears of elephants, vast and broad, even though he ﷺ had never seen an elephant in his life. And then the description stops being like anything at all. The tree was being enveloped, covered over, by something, and the Qur'an itself will not name what.

When there covered the Lote Tree that which covered [it].Qur'an 53:16

Colors ran up and down it that he ﷺ said he could not describe, colors beyond any our eyes were built to register, beyond the small band of the spectrum we know. Butterflies of gold drifted around it. It did not hold still; in one report he said it kept changing until no one could describe it at all. He was no longer looking at anything of this world. The Sheikh notes here, in passing, that this small phrase, colors I cannot describe, fits quietly with what we now know of light and energy beyond the visible, not as a thing to be forced into a scientific proof, but as one more sign that he had crossed into a dimension our senses are not made for.

At the Lote Tree of the Utmost Boundary - Near it is the Garden of Refuge [i.e., Paradise] -Qur'an 53:14-15

And the Qur'an's verdict on what he saw there is the seal upon it. Allah Himself counts the Lote Tree among His major signs, the ayat al-kubra. So pause on the arithmetic the Sheikh leaves with you, the same arithmetic the Qur'an invites. If a single tree, at the very edge of the universe, is named among the greatest signs of the Lord, then what is greater still, waiting just beyond it, was about to summon him ﷺ on alone.

Two rivers you can see, two you cannot

At the Lote Tree he ﷺ saw four rivers flowing, two of them hidden and two of them open to view, and he asked Jibril what they were. Jibril said: the two hidden rivers are in Jannah, rivers you will not see in this world; and the two you can see are the Nile and the Euphrates. Two of the rivers of Paradise touch this earth, and they are the very rivers along which the oldest civilizations of mankind have always lived. Sheikh Yasir cannot help but dwell on this small fact: the cradle of human life as we know it sits on the Euphrates and the Nile, watered and blessed from a source higher than any map could ever trace. As for the two rivers of Jannah themselves, they are al-Kawthar, the abundance Allah granted him, and Salsabil, the fountain the Qur'an names.

Indeed, We have granted you, [O Muḥammad], al-Kawthar.Qur'an 108:1
[From] a fountain within it [i.e., Paradise] named Salsabeel.Qur'an 76:18

And here, somewhere in this stretch above the heavens, three more of Allah's major signs were unveiled to him, one after another. He saw al-Bayt al-Mamur, the frequented house, the Kaaba of the heavens that Ibrahim had been resting against, set so exactly above the earthly Kaaba that were it to fall it would land squarely upon it. Seventy thousand angels enter it every single day to worship and never return, a fresh seventy thousand the next day, and the next, since the beginning of creation, until your mind gives out trying to count them. And he saw Jibril in his true and original form, the form in which Allah first created him, six hundred wings spread across the whole of the horizon, pearls and corals dripping from his feathers.

Sheikh Yasir gathers the three signs into a single thought. The Lote Tree is the highest reach of this universe, beyond which nothing of creation extends. The frequented house is the heavens at worship, the angels' own Kaaba, the spiritual counterpart of the one we circle. And Jibril in his form is the noblest of all the angels of Allah, the one preferred above the rest, which is why he was given six hundred wings when the Qur'an describes other angels with two or three or four. The greatest of the angels and the greatest of the children of Adam ﷺ, seeing each other as they truly are, at the boundary of everything created. Of all that he was shown that night, the Sheikh notes, the Prophet ﷺ saw Jibril in that original form only twice in his entire life, and this was one of the two.

Carried up alone, to hear the Pen

Now comes the boundary that even Jibril does not cross. Up to the Lote Tree, the narrations keep Jibril at his side, leading, naming, explaining each thing as they rise. At the Sidra, that changes. The Qur'an describes the encounter with the angel, the one intense in strength who rose to his true form at the highest horizon, and then the verbs shift. The Prophet ﷺ stops speaking of Jibril altogether and says, in the singular: then I was caused to ascend. Reading the reports as carefully as he can, and finding no clear statement either way, Sheikh Yasir concludes that the legitimate thing to say, the thing the evidence will bear, is this: the Prophet ﷺ went on beyond the Lote Tree, beyond Jibril, beyond the last living creature, to a level no created being has ever reached.

Taught to him by one intense in strength [i.e., Gabriel] - One of soundness. And he rose to [his] true form While he was in the higher [part of the] horizon.Qur'an 53:5-7

What was up there? In one hadith he said: I rose until I could hear the scratching of the Pen. This is the same Pen that Allah created first of all things, and commanded to write everything that would be until the Day of Judgment, writing in the Book that is with Allah above the Throne. No angel stands at that height. No creature does. He ﷺ rose to where the only ones present are the Owner of the Throne and the Pen recording His decree, and he heard it being written. It was, the Sheikh says, an audience the like of which we know of no other being ever receiving, an elite gathering of one servant before his Lord, with the universe itself left behind below him.

Then he approached and descended And was at a distance of two bow lengths or nearer. And he revealed to His Servant what he revealed [i.e., conveyed]. The heart did not lie [about] what it saw.Qur'an 53:8-11

And of what passed there, Allah told us almost nothing, and there is a wisdom in the silence. The conversation between the Lord and His Messenger ﷺ was something too private and too precious to be handed out to a crowd. The Qur'an gathers it into one merciful, undisclosed line, and he revealed to His servant what he revealed, and leaves it veiled. Sheikh Yasir is careful to clear away the legends here, the most common being that the words of the tashahhud we say in every prayer are a transcript of this conversation. That, he says plainly, is a fabrication with no basis in any sound book; do not carry it. What is sound is that only one thing was reported back to us from that meeting. Only one. And it was the most important thing that could possibly have come down.

Fifty, lowered to five

The one gift that came down was the prayer. Think about what that means before you hear the rest of it. Every other command of this religion, the zakah, the fasting, the hajj, the night prayer, the Qur'an itself, all of it was sent down to him ﷺ through Jibril, in the ordinary way of revelation. For one commandment alone, the Messenger was summoned up, past the heavens, past the Lote Tree, into the very presence of the Divine, to receive it directly from his Lord. That one commandment is the salah. If nothing else in the entire seerah told you where the prayer stands in this religion, the Sheikh says, this alone would be enough.

Allah prescribed fifty prayers a day. On the way back down, the Prophet ﷺ passed Musa, who asked him what his Lord had enjoined. Fifty prayers, he said. Musa told him to go back: your ummah cannot bear fifty, I have more experience with people than you, I have tested the Children of Israel before you, go and ask Him to lighten it. And here the Sheikh stops on a detail he loves, because it is so human. The Prophet ﷺ glanced toward Jibril, as if asking his opinion, and Jibril nodded to him: yes, do it. The best of mankind, seeking a second opinion from the best of angels, in the middle of the heavens. So he went back, and Allah lowered the number. And he came down, and Musa sent him back again, and again, and again, the same counsel each time and the same mercy each time, at least five journeys up and down by the soundest reckoning, until at last the Prophet ﷺ said: I have gone back to my Lord until I am ashamed to ask again. I am content.

And the moment he said it, a voice called out: I have decreed My obligation and I have made it light upon My servants; it is five, but it shall be rewarded as fifty. Five prayers on the scale, fifty in the reward. Allah had known from the very start that it would settle at five; Musa and the Prophet ﷺ, the only two men who had each been spoken to by their Lord, were the only two present to hear it settle. The going up and the coming down was mercy made visible, His easing of the burden shown to us step by step, so that we would see with our own eyes how far He was willing to lower it.

He has chosen you and has not placed upon you in the religion any difficulty.Qur'an 22:78

Sheikh Yasir leaves you with the ache of it, and with its other half. The ache first: if Allah had truly wanted fifty, fifty would have stood, for the original command was a window onto what worship could be, a life that is almost nothing but prayer, the way the angels themselves never tire of it. He lowered it because He does not want hardship for you, because, as the verse says, He has not placed any difficulty upon you in the religion. The five are already the light version. And still, the Sheikh says with sorrow, it costs us less than twenty minutes of an entire day, this gift carried down from beyond the Throne, and so many cannot find even the five. Then the other half of the picture, lest the lightening be mistaken for laxity: the Prophet ﷺ himself, knowing the original fifty, would pray close to fifty units a day of his own accord, the obligations and the sunan and the night prayer and the forenoon prayer adding up almost exactly. The five were lightened for us. He kept reaching for the fifty.

We stop here, with the prayer in his hands and the descent not yet finished. Tomorrow the journey comes the rest of the way down, past Jannah and the Fire, and then the hardest part of the whole night arrives: the morning after, when he ﷺ must stand before Makkah and tell them what he saw.

What this night asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this middle stretch of the ascent as a catalogue of wonders, a tour of trees and rivers and angels meant to leave us amazed and nothing more. That would be to miss the question it puts to us, because the most astonishing thing about this night is not how high he went, but what he was sent back down with.

Look again at the climb. Past the seventh heaven, past a tree that Allah names among His greatest signs, past the mightiest of the angels and the boundary that even he could not cross, all the way up to where the Pen could be heard writing the decree, to a meeting Allah kept private out of its sheer preciousness. The whole universe was crossed in one direction. And what came back down from that unrepeatable height was not a secret to dazzle the crowds, nor a wonder to silence the doubters. It was an instruction for your day. Five prayers, lowered from fifty by a Lord who refused to make the religion hard, and still credited to you as fifty. That is the measure of what the salah is worth: the entire creation was traversed to place it in your hands. When you stand to pray, you are holding the one thing Allah chose to deliver to His Messenger ﷺ in person.

And there is Ibrahim's message, waiting to be acted on the moment you put this down. The soil of Jannah is rich and ready, and it is empty, and it is yours to plant. Every word of remembrance is a seed in ground that cannot fail to bear fruit. The dhikr you have always treated as filler, the muttered subhanallah between tasks, is the most productive thing your tongue does all day. Begin treating it that way. And measure your distance from the Fire by the company you keep: Malik, its keeper, was brought up to the best of creation and made to greet him first, to show how far the beloved of Allah stands from that place. Walk toward him ﷺ, follow his way, and you are walking in the direction that distance points.

So carry three things down from this night into your ordinary days. Guard the five prayers as what they are, a gift carried by hand from beyond the Throne, and never let them become the thing you fit around your day instead of the thing your day is fitted around. Plant your Jannah with your tongue, because the soil is already prepared and only your seed is missing. And let Allah's easing of the burden humble you rather than relax you: He lowered fifty to five as a mercy and rewards the five as fifty, and the only fitting response to a Lord that generous is to give Him, gladly, the little He asked for. May Allah, who carried His servant ﷺ past the Lote Tree and gave him the prayer from above the Throne, make us of those who guard the five, plant for us in the soil of Jannah with every word of His remembrance, keep us as far from the Fire as its own keeper could not smile beside, and gather us with His Messenger ﷺ in the garden he was shown.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 22: the Night Journey and the Ascension, part 2 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (21:96, 53:14-15, 53:16, 108:1, 76:18, 53:5-7, 53:8-11, 22:78), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 23 of 104

The Night Journey and the Ascension, part 3 ﷺ

The morning after the heavens

The heavens have been climbed. The prophets have been greeted and led in prayer. The fifty prayers have been received and lightened to five, beyond the seventh heaven, at the very edge of the created order. And now the descent begins, and with it the part of the miracle that nobody warns you about: the morning after. The man who hours earlier had stood where no created being had ever stood, at the veil of light, must now come back down to the streets of his own small city and stand in front of Abu Jahl.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi opens this final night of the arc with a warning about its very shape. The descent, and indeed most of the Night Journey, is almost impossible to lay out in exact order. We do not possess one connected narrative; we have a scattering of separate reports, each sound in itself, the sequence of which only Allah knows. So from here on the honest way to tell it is to gather the incidents as they come down to us, dwell on each, and confess freely that we cannot always say which came first. What we can say is that every one of them, on the way home, was preparing him for the hardest conversation of his life ﷺ, and preparing the ummah for everything that came after.

A fragrance on the road

Somewhere on the night of the journey, and the reports do not tell us where, the Prophet ﷺ caught a fragrance so sweet that he had to stop and ask. Jibril, what is this beautiful scent? The answer, carried in a long hadith in Musnad Ahmad, opens a window onto a nation that had passed away centuries before: this is the fragrance of the woman who used to comb the hair of the daughter of Firawn, and of her children.

She was a servant in the palace of the tyrant who told his court that he knew of no god for them but himself, the same Pharaoh whose arrogance the Qur'an records in his demand for a tower to the heavens:

And Pharaoh said, "O eminent ones, I have not known you to have a god other than me. Then ignite for me, O Haman, [a fire] upon the clay and make for me a tower that I may look at the God of Moses. And indeed, I do think he is among the liars."Qur'an 28:38

One day, as she combed the princess's hair, the comb slipped from her fingers and she said, in the reflex of a believing heart, bismillah. The little girl looked up: surely you mean the name of my father? For a child raised in that palace, her father was god. No, the woman said. My Lord, and your Lord, and the Lord of your father, is Allah. Shall I tell him you said this? Tell him.

Firawn summoned her. He had built his whole rule on the claim that there was no lord above him; the Qur'an even has him ask his people whether he had ever taught them of another god. Now he asked this slave woman the same thing: do you have a lord other than me? And with the same steadiness she had shown his daughter she answered the tyrant to his face: yes. My Lord and your Lord is Allah. So he ordered a great cauldron of water set to boil over fire, and told her she would throw her own children into it, one by one, or take him as her lord. She asked for one thing only, that her bones and her children's bones be gathered afterward and buried together in a single place, not scattered. He granted it. One by one her children went into the fire, until the only one left was the baby still nursing at her breast, and there, for one human moment, she faltered. Then the infant spoke: my mother, go forward, for the punishment of this world is nothing beside the punishment of the next. And she went. Ibn Abbas would later count this child among the very few who ever spoke from the cradle, alongside Isa and the boy in the story of Jurayj.

Sheikh Yasir lingers here on the thing easiest to miss: we do not even know her name. Her own people never recorded it. Her story survives in no older scripture; it is not in the Torah, not in the traditions of the children of Israel, even though it happened among them. Allah willed instead that her courage travel up through the centuries and meet His final Messenger ﷺ on the greatest night of his life, as a fragrance on the road, and that the largest ummah ever raised would carry her memory to the end of time. Firawn kept his empire for a season and lost his name to a curse. A nameless slave woman lost her body to the fire and became perfume in the heavens.

Tents of pearl, and the fires below

After the prayer had been prescribed, the Prophet ﷺ said: then I entered Jannah. He saw tents made of pearl, and a soil of pure musk, the most precious of perfumes. The order of it is itself a lesson the scholars noticed, and the Sheikh draws it out: first the meeting with Allah, the entire purpose of the ascent, and only afterward the sights He had prepared. The guest is brought to the Host before he is shown the house.

But the word entered raises a question, because other reports say that no one truly enters Jannah until after the Judgment, and that he ﷺ himself will be the very first to enter it then. The scholars offer two readings, and Sheikh Yasir leaves both honestly on the table rather than forcing one. Either this night was an exception written for him alone, an entry now and an entry forever, with no contradiction in his being first after the Resurrection too; or the word is to be read as surveying rather than dwelling, for some versions say only I saw Jannah, just as he saw the Fire that night without entering it, and indeed the fact that he described the Fire in the same breath suggests a beholder looking in, not a resident. Even the martyr, remember, does not yet live in Jannah: his soul roams it in the form of a green bird, hung from its chandeliers, near it but not settled in it. Either way the point stands untouched: he ﷺ was shown what no eye had ever been shown.

And he was shown the other side. Gathering the reports he gave at different times, the picture is terrifying in its precision, and the Sheikh sets them out one by one. People with faces disfigured, swallowing coals of fire that passed straight through their bodies: these were the devourers of the wealth of orphans, exactly as the Qur'an warns:

Indeed, those who devour the property of orphans unjustly are only consuming into their bellies fire. And they will be burned in a Blaze [i.e., Hellfire].Qur'an 4:10

People raking their own faces and chests with nails of copper: the backbiters, who in life had torn at the honor and flesh of others, so on that Day their own flesh is torn. People seated before fresh, wholesome meat, turning from it to eat from rotten, stinking meat instead: those who left the halal Allah had given them and went chasing the haram. People with bellies so swollen they could not stand, left on the ground for beasts to trample them where they lay: the consumers of riba, fattened by greed, shown at last what all their hoarded wealth had earned them. And people cutting their own lips and tongues with shears of fire: the preachers who commanded others to good and forgot themselves. At each scene he asked Jibril, who are these? And at each answer there is only one response left for us, which is to seek refuge in Allah from being among any of them.

On that night he ﷺ also saw the Dajjal, and described him to his companions with a bluntness, the Sheikh notes, that no prophet before him had used with his people. He is one-eyed, the bad eye like a swollen, floating grape, plainly deformed, so that you would know him at a glance. The ummah that will one day face the great deceiver was given, in advance, his very face.

Home before the night was over

Of the way down we have only fragments. The detailed, heaven-by-heaven narrations all belong to the ascent; for the return we have no such thing. At Bayt al-Maqdis the Buraq stood waiting where it had been tied, because the Buraq was the mount of the earthly miles, the creature that carried him from Makkah to Jerusalem, while the climb through the heavens was the mi'raj itself, a different ascent altogether. So when he came back down to Jerusalem he mounted the Buraq once more and rode for home.

On that road, in reports the Sheikh grades as less than the highest but with nothing in them to reject, he passed three caravans of Quraysh, and in a city where everyone knows everyone, he recognized them. In the first he saw faces he could name. From the second, or another of them, feeling thirst on the way, he drank from the large common vessel the travelers kept their water in, a public store, not anyone's private property. And a third he found out searching the dark for a camel that had wandered off and could not be found. Small, ordinary details, the kind no one bothers to invent, and by sunrise they would matter more than anyone in Makkah could imagine.

Then he slept, and he woke in the Haram. That, the Sheikh explains, is precisely why the reports that have him sleeping that evening in his own house, or in the house of Umm Hani, are read the gentle way: he was resting there, then Jibril brought him first to the sacred mosque, and the journey itself ran exactly as the Qur'an draws it, masjid to masjid:

Exalted is He who took His Servant [i.e., Prophet Muhammad (ﷺ)] by night from al-Masjid al-Haram to al-Masjid al-Aqsa, whose surroundings We have blessed, to show him of Our signs. Indeed, He is the Hearing, the Seeing.Qur'an 17:1

The Qur'an had drawn the route from the sacred mosque to the farthest mosque and back. The night had filled it in.

The morning he dreaded

Now the scene this whole episode turns on, and we have it in the first person, from the Prophet's ﷺ own mouth: when I woke in the morning, I felt a heavy anxiety, for how was I going to tell the people what had happened to me? Sheikh Yasir pauses on the logic buried in that sentence. He ﷺ never spoke of his own accord and never acted without command, so the very fact that he felt obligated to tell them means Allah had commanded him to tell them, and he already knew exactly what Makkah would do with it. Sit with the image. The man who only hours before had stood at the veil of light, where no creature had ever stood, now sat in front of the Kaaba, grieved and worried, like any one of us before the hardest conversation of our lives. The seerah keeps showing us his humanity ﷺ precisely so that we never lose it: the joys and the fears, the highs and the lows, a Messenger of God who still felt the knot in the chest that we feel.

Abu Jahl passed by and saw him sitting in that state and asked, in a sneer, whether something had happened. And because he ﷺ would not lie, not even to Abu Jahl, he answered yes: last night I was taken to Bayt al-Maqdis. To Jerusalem, and here you are among us this morning? Abu Jahl, the Sheikh notes, did not know whether to ridicule him on the spot or to bait a larger trap, and he chose the trap, lest his enemy retract before a crowd could gather. If I call your people, he said, will you tell them exactly what you just told me? Yes, said the Prophet ﷺ. So Abu Jahl ran through the town shouting, summoning the clans by the name of their forefather: come, gather, there is an announcement.

Makkah is small, and the strange sight of these two enemies hosting one announcement together pulled the people out of their houses. They sat, curious, around Abu Jahl standing and the Prophet ﷺ seated. Tell them, Abu Jahl demanded, what you told me. You promised. And the Prophet ﷺ told them: last night I was taken to Bayt al-Maqdis, and I prayed there. Notice, the Sheikh says, that he mentioned only the Isra, the journey to Jerusalem, and not the ascension; the heavens he would entrust to the believers later. Some in the crowd began to clap in mockery, some put their hands to their heads, some snickered, and underneath it all ran a current of genuine confusion, because the one man in Makkah who had never in his life been caught in a lie was saying this in deadly earnest.

Then a man who had actually traveled to Jerusalem stood up with the obvious test: describe it for us. He ﷺ began to describe the place, and then they pressed him on specifics, and here the seerah shows us his humanity once more. He had entered the city at night, prayed, and left; who memorizes a city in the dark? As the questions narrowed, he said afterward, such distress seized him as he had never felt before; the word the report uses means terrified. The questions were fair from their side and impossible from his, for he had not gone as a tourist counting buildings. And then came Allah's rescue. Bayt al-Maqdis itself was raised up before his eyes, set in the distance just beyond the house of his cousin, the very house in which he had grown up, and he answered every question while gazing straight at the city, though the crowd around him could see nothing at all. At last the man who had set the test conceded: as for the description, he has it exactly right.

He gave them more than they asked. He offered them signs on their own road: three of their caravans, described one by one, the people in the first, the lost camel of another, the water he had drunk from the third, and the nearest of them, he said, was about to enter the city. While they were still arguing it out, word came in that the caravan was arriving, exactly as he had said. Abu Jahl went out, saw it with his own two eyes, and came back with the only door a refusing heart has left to it: this is plain sorcery. The eye had confirmed everything. The heart declined anyway.

If he said it, then it is true

Before the Prophet ﷺ could even reach his closest friend, one of the Quraysh ran ahead to Abu Bakr's house, delighted to be first with the scandal. Do you know what your companion is claiming now? That he went to Jerusalem, a month's journey there, and came back, another month, all in a single night. Abu Bakr, radiyallahu anhu, did not gasp and did not stumble. His first words, the Sheikh observes, are a masterclass in where faith actually lives: if he said it, then it is true. He did not say yes, that happened, for he had not yet heard it from the Prophet ﷺ himself and would not vouch for the messenger's wording; he verified the source. And this source had never once failed.

You believe him, in something like this? And then the answer that named him for the rest of history: I believe him in something far more amazing than that. I believe that revelation descends to him from above the seven heavens in an instant. Weigh the two, the Sheikh says, the way Abu Bakr weighed them. If you already accept that Allah speaks to this man across the entire span of the heavens, then a night's ride to Jerusalem and back is the smaller wonder, not the larger one. From that morning he was as-Siddiq, the one who declared the truth true, and the title never left him.

One famous line from that morning, though, Sheikh Yasir stops to examine, because the examining is part of the seerah's honesty. Some books of sira say that when the news spread, a number of Muslims could not bear it and left Islam. That phrase, he points out, appears only in Ibn Hisham, with no chain of narration behind it, and in none of the six canonical books. And it collides head-on with something firmly authentic. Years later the Byzantine emperor Heraclius would interrogate Abu Sufyan, at the time still a sworn enemy of the Prophet ﷺ, with a long string of questions, and among them: does anyone abandon his religion, in resentment, after entering it? No, said Abu Sufyan, who would dearly have loved to answer yes. And Heraclius told him that this is exactly what true faith looks like once it settles into hearts: it does not leak away. Not a single apostasy is recorded in the entire Makkan period. So the famous line, the Sheikh concludes, simply should not be relied upon. The Night Journey thinned no ranks. It sorted the mockers from as-Siddiq.

Did he see his Lord?

The Qur'an houses this night in two surahs: the journey itself in the opening verse of Surah al-Isra, and the scenes of the ascension in Surah an-Najm. And out of an-Najm rose the first of the two great questions the ummah has asked of this night ever since: did he ﷺ see Allah?

The answer plays out in one of the most vivid classrooms in our history, and the Sheikh tells it almost as a scene. Aisha, radiyallahu anha, the mother of the believers, taught from behind a real curtain, for the Qur'an had commanded an extra screen for the wives of the Prophet ﷺ, a veil above and beyond ordinary covering, a physical barrier between them and any man. Her student Masruq gathered his courage and spoke from the other side of it: O my mother, let me ask you something, and do not be angry with me. Did Muhammad ﷺ see his Lord? She answered that his words had made the hair on her body stand on end: whoever tells you that he saw his Lord has uttered an enormous lie against Allah. But Masruq had his proof ready, the verses of an-Najm themselves: does the Qur'an not say that he saw him?

While he was in the higher [part of the] horizon. Then he approached and descended and was at a distance of two bow lengths or nearer.Qur'an 53:7-9

Aisha did not retreat an inch. She answered from the highest authority anyone in the world could hold on the matter: I was the very first person of this ummah to ask the Messenger of Allah ﷺ about these verses, and he told me that the one he saw, filling the horizon, was Jibril, the angel in his true and original form. The seeing in an-Najm was the seeing of Jibril, not of Allah. And she sealed it with the Book, citing first that vision itself cannot contain Him:

Vision perceives Him not, but He perceives [all] vision; and He is the Subtle, the Aware.Qur'an 6:103

And then the verse that fixes the only ways Allah addresses a human being, none of which is an unveiled face seen with these eyes:

And it is not for any human being that Allah should speak to him except by revelation or from behind a partition or that He sends a messenger [i.e., angel] to reveal, by His permission, what He wills. Indeed, He is Most High and Wise.Qur'an 42:51

Abu Dharr, radiyallahu anhu, once put the question to the Prophet ﷺ directly, and the answer is preserved in Sahih Muslim: there was light; how could I see Him? That light, the scholars explain from another hadith, is the veil of Allah itself. His veil is light, and were He to remove it, the glories of His Face would burn through everything His sight reaches, which is to say all of creation. Our veils are made of darkness and covering; His veil is made of light. And the Sheikh points to the mountain, where this was taught once already, to Musa, peace be upon him:

And when Moses arrived at Our appointed time and his Lord spoke to him, he said, "My Lord, show me [Yourself] that I may look at You." [Allah] said, "You will not see Me, but look at the mountain; if it should remain in place, then you will see Me." But when his Lord appeared to the mountain, He rendered it level, and Moses fell unconscious. And when he awoke, he said, "Exalted are You! I have repented to You, and I am the first [among my people] of the believers."Qur'an 7:143

If a mountain could not stand the unveiling and crumbled to flat ground, no creature in this world can bear to look upon the Lord of the worlds. And what then of the narration from Ibn Abbas that he did see Him? Either Aisha's word stands above it, for she asked the Prophet ﷺ in person and Abu Dharr asked him in person, or, as another report from Ibn Abbas himself clarifies, he saw Him with his heart, a seeing of an entirely different kind, not the seeing of the eyes. The vision of Allah that the believers are promised belongs to the next world, to a new and perfect order of creation made able to bear it, not to these mortal eyes in this life. And yet hold what even this restraint means for our Prophet ﷺ: he was carried past every created thing, to the very veil of light, the one creature ever brought so near.

Awake, in body and soul

The second great question: was any of it a dream? One or two early voices leaned on the phrase I woke up and read the whole night as a vision, but for more than a thousand years the ummah has answered with a single voice, and the Sheikh gathers the proofs that, as he says, nearly argue themselves. If it were a dream, where is the miracle in it? Any of us can fly to the heavens in our sleep and no one in Makkah would care; if it were only a dream, why would he have dreaded telling them at all, and why would they have erupted? Why a Buraq, a physical mount to be saddled and mounted and tied at a post, if there were no body to carry? Why did he grow thirsty on the road home and drink real water from a real caravan, an act of a waking body if ever there was one? The Quraysh's fury is itself a witness that he claimed a journey of flesh and bone, not a thing seen behind closed eyes. The Isra is carried by some forty or fifty companions and stated plainly in the Qur'an, so to deny it is to deny the Book and the soundest Sunnah at once. As for the modernists of our own age who find miracles embarrassing and would shrink the night down into a vision, they have, the Sheikh says, missed the entire point: the gift was precisely that it really happened, body and soul together.

And why was it given at all? Here is the reading this series keeps returning to, and the Sheikh states it outright: the Isra and Mi'raj was first and foremost a personal gift from Allah to His Messenger ﷺ. Most miracles are aimed outward, to convince the doubters of a prophet's truth. This one was aimed inward, at his own heart. It is the same mercy we saw with Musa at the sacred valley, shown the staff turned to a serpent and his hand shining white with no audience present, miracles whose only witness was the prophet himself, given to fill his certainty to the brim before the heaviest of roads. Even Ibrahim, peace be upon him, who never doubted, asked to see the dead brought back so that his heart might be at rest. So our Prophet ﷺ was shown, with his own eyes, that everything he had been preaching is real: the angels, the prophets, the Garden and the Fire, the Lord above the veil.

And the timing tells the rest of the story. It came unannounced, with no sign that it was coming, at the lowest point of his life: Khadijah newly buried, Abu Talib newly buried, the wounds of Taif still raw. The lowest of the low was met with the highest of the high. This is the law the whole arc has been teaching: with hardship comes ease, and the greater the hardship, the greater the ease, so that whatever Allah puts a faithful soul through, what comes back is proportionate to what was borne, or more.

One last detail, and it may be the most staggering of the entire night. Ibn Kathir wrote that if any other man had seen even a fraction of those sights, he would have come back out of his mind. The Prophet ﷺ saw the heavens opened, the Fire, the prophets, the veil of light, and then came home, lay down, and slept. The Qur'an's own praise of that composure is the verse from an-Najm:

The sight [of the Prophet (ﷺ)] did not swerve, nor did it transgress [its limit].Qur'an 53:17

He carried what he saw with a steady eye, and he carried it for us. He once told his companions that if they knew what he knew, they would laugh little and weep much. He knew, and he stayed standing.

What this day asks of our faith

The Sheikh closes the three nights of this arc the way the classical scholars close it, by counting what the night left in the ummah's hands. Begin with this: in one journey the Prophet ﷺ met every pillar of iman with his own senses. He reached the veil of Allah and was spoken to. He saw the angels, and Jibril in his true form. He met the prophets, prayed at their head, and spoke with them of the Last Day. He saw Jannah and the Fire, already created, already real. He even heard the scratching of the pen writing the decree. For one man, on one night, faith in the unseen became faith in the seen. And the question that leaves for us is whether we will believe, on his testimony, what he was shown so that we would not have to be.

So take a few of its lessons down into your ordinary days. Believe like as-Siddiq: verify the source once, and then trust it completely, the way Abu Bakr checked who was speaking and never flinched again, because a heart that has examined its grounds does not crumble at the next strange-sounding truth. Deliver the message and do not edit it: he ﷺ woke dreading the telling and told them anyway, even Abu Jahl, because the command was not his to soften, and our task is to carry the religion as it is and not trim it to fit the approval of the room. Say it to yourself first, before you say it to anyone, remembering the preachers in the Fire shearing their own lips, who called others to a good they would not walk toward themselves. And trust that Allah keeps the names that matter: a slave woman whom no historian on earth bothered to record is remembered above the seven heavens, her fragrance laid across the Prophet's ﷺ own path, which means nothing you offer for the sake of Allah, in the dark, unseen, is ever lost.

And then there is the heart of all three nights, the reading the Sheikh returns to again and again: the journey came unannounced, at the lowest point of his life, and answered the worst season he had ever known with the highest honor any creature has ever received. The year of sorrow lay behind it; the road to Madinah lay ahead of it; and Allah lifted His Messenger ﷺ above the heavens precisely there, at the hinge, as if to show him before the hardest decade of his life that the One sending him into it sees everything and hears everything. That is the comfort the Night Journey hands to every believer standing in a narrow place: the prayer he brought back, the only command in our religion handed down above the heavens, is in your hands five times today, a daily ascent of your own; and the Lord who carried him through the dark and brought him home before the night was over is the same Lord, near and hearing, in your own dark. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, whom He took by night from the sacred mosque to the farthest mosque and raised beyond the heavens; grant us the certainty of as-Siddiq, the courage of the woman whose fragrance filled his path, eyes that do not swerve from the truth He shows us, and prayers that never leave us, until He gathers us with His Messenger ﷺ in the home he was shown.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 23: the Night Journey and the Ascension, part 3 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (28:38, 4:10, 17:1, 53:7-9, 6:103, 42:51, 7:143, 53:17), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 24 of 104

Planting the Seeds of Yathrib

Six strangers, and a door no empire could open

For three weeks the seerah had been suspended in the heavens, walking with the Prophet ﷺ through the Night Journey and the seven skies. Now it has to come back down to earth, because he did. Just as he ﷺ returned from the Isra and life in Makkah resumed, the story resumes too, and it resumes at the lowest visible point of his entire life in that city. Khadijah is in her grave. Abu Talib is in his grave. The shield of the Banu Hashim that had covered him from birth is gone, and the man living under it now is living on a courtesy that everyone in Makkah knows will not last. A lesser man would have grown quiet and waited for the end. Instead he ﷺ walked into the single largest gathering on the face of the earth and began knocking on tents.

This is the chapter Dr. Yasir Qadhi calls the planting of seeds. By every measure a human eye can take, the Prophet ﷺ is at his weakest here, refused by superpowers, turned away by the wise, hunted out of one city and abandoned by his own. And it is exactly here, in the hour that looks most like defeat, that the single most important door in the seerah quietly swings open, not where he was pushing on it with all his strength, but in a field no one was watching.

Living on borrowed protection

Step back to where the thread was cut. Abu Talib had died, and with his death the lifelong protection of the Banu Hashim came to an end. For a week or two his uncle Abu Lahab felt the weight of tribal obligation and extended his cover over the Prophet ﷺ, and then he rescinded it, cancelled it, withdrew it within days. So who covered the Prophet ﷺ after that? Not his own clan. A man from outside the Banu Hashim altogether: Mut'im ibn Adi, an elderly chief who never accepted Islam and yet took the Prophet ﷺ under his guardianship, so that he could move through the streets of Makkah and live in it at all.

It was a precarious arrangement, and everyone felt how awkward it was. His own tribe had abandoned him; his protector was from another tribe entirely, and the Quraysh were not pleased to see it. And Mut'im was very old. Within roughly a year, before the Battle of Badr, he would be dead, and the thin paper of protection over the Prophet ﷺ would tear with him. The Prophet ﷺ understood exactly what that meant. Makkah was closing around him like a fist, and he needed a way out before it shut completely. He had already turned to Ta'if and been stoned out of it. So now he turned to the one place on earth where the whole of Arabia came to him.

Knocking on every tent

Every year the tribes streamed into Mina for the Hajj, a pilgrimage reaching back to Ibrahim, peace be upon him. The way pilgrims in our own time gather by nation, with the Americans in one quarter and the Nigerians in another and the Iranians in a third, the Arabs of that age gathered by tribe. Every clan had its known patch of ground in Mina, its customary camp that everyone recognized as theirs. And the Prophet ﷺ, year after year, used the simple fact that all of Arabia had come to him. He moved through the camps.

He had been doing this for years already, but for one purpose only: to call them to the worship of Allah alone, to preach tawhid tent by tent. After the death of Abu Talib the request changed and doubled. Now it was not only embrace this message; it was shelter me, adopt me, transfer your allegiance and take me into your tribe so that I can convey the words of my Lord, because the Quraysh have stopped me from conveying them. The Arabs had a rare custom by which a man could change his tribe, usually for a marriage or a political cause, and the Prophet ﷺ reached for that custom now. Who will take me in, he asked the camps, who will adopt me, so that I may carry the speech of my Lord, for the Quraysh have prevented me.

We are not guessing at how this looked, because there were eyewitnesses, and not one of them was a Muslim at the time. One of them, Rabia ibn Abbad, recalled it from his boyhood. He remembered coming to Mina as a child at his father's side, and seeing a man, young-looking, his beard still black, moving from tent to tent calling the people to worship Allah and abandon their idols. And he remembered a second man following close behind, an older man in a Yemeni cloak with two braids of hair, waiting for the first to finish and then stepping forward at each tent to undo every word: O people, this man is calling you to abandon your gods and to leave the way of your forefathers, so do not listen, cling to the way of your fathers. The boy asked his father who the two of them were. That, his father said, is the one who claims to be a prophet, and the man behind him is his own uncle, Abu Lahab. Even his rejection had become a family business. His name and his claim had spread across the tribes of Arabia before he ever reached most of their tents.

The price the great tribes named

He went to the heavyweights first, and the answers they gave are a lesson in what people want from a movement they think is just beginning to rise. He approached Kinda, a tribe that had once held a kingdom of its own, four or five centuries before, and still ached for that vanished glory. One of its chiefs listened with sharp attention, then said: this is interesting, come with me, I will give you an audience with the other elders, and what you have said to me, say to them. So the Prophet ﷺ followed him in, and the man convened the gathering, and after the Prophet ﷺ had preached, this chief stood and spoke to his own people in plainly political terms. If we adopt this man of the Quraysh, he told them, we will have a message through which we can devour the rest of the Arabs. Then he turned to the Prophet ﷺ and put the real question on the table: if we follow you, and Allah gives us victory over your enemies, will the rule pass to us after you?

The answer the Prophet ﷺ gave is the answer of a man who could not be bought even when buying might have saved his life. The kingdom, he said, belongs to Allah; He grants it to whomever He wills of His servants. Not yes. Not no. A condition I have no right to accept. The chief heard it for exactly what it was and waved him off: so we are to stretch out our necks for you to be cut in war, and then when victory comes the rule goes wherever Allah pleases? We have no need of this. And so Kinda walked away, one of the true superpowers of the peninsula, perhaps the single strongest card on the table, and the Prophet ﷺ let it go rather than promise it a throne.

Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi makes us stop. This was an opportunity that most of us would have seized with a far smaller compromise than the one being offered. The Prophet ﷺ was in a situation that, if you called it desperate, you would not be far from the truth. He needed a sponsor. Kinda was a heavyweight that had once outweighed even the Quraysh. And still he would not bend the message a single degree, because he understood two things that the rest of us forget under pressure. The first is that he was not permitted to barter what Allah had revealed; you do not win the help of Allah by trading away a part of His religion. The second is sharper still: a people who came for power would never be the people through whom Allah aids His faith. Allah helps this religion through those who believe in it, not through the might of Kinda or anyone else.

Then he came to Banu Shayban ibn Thalaba, and that meeting went very differently, for these were not fools. The Prophet ﷺ would usually travel with Abu Bakr, radiyallahu anhu, at these seasons, because Abu Bakr was the great master of Arab genealogy, who knew the lineages of the tribes better than any man alive. Before the Prophet ﷺ would speak, Abu Bakr would brief him: this is so-and-so son of so-and-so, these are their strengths, these are the men among them to watch. He was, in effect, his ambassador, opening the doors and managing the introductions. There is a quiet lesson buried in that habit, and the Sheikh draws it out. The Prophet ﷺ is the Messenger of Allah, inspired in everything that touches religion and worship and the unseen, but in the ordinary affairs of this world he had no objection to leaning on the knowledge of those around him. Abu Bakr had studied genealogy for more years than the Prophet ﷺ had, and so the Prophet ﷺ let him advise on it. If the Messenger of Allah himself benefited from the expertise of others, where do the rest of us stand who are too proud to be taught.

To Banu Shayban the Prophet ﷺ gave a short sermon and then recited a verse so complete in its foundations of faith and conduct that it would later become a fixture of the Friday khutbah for generations:

Say, "Come, I will recite what your Lord has prohibited to you. [He commands] that you not associate anything with Him, and to parents, good treatment, and do not kill your children out of poverty; We will provide for you and them. And do not approach immoralities, what is apparent of them and what is concealed. And do not kill the soul which Allah has forbidden [to be killed] except by [legal] right. This has He instructed you that you may use reason."Qur'an 6:151

Their elders were impressed, and they were honest. The Quraysh have lied about you, they said, your message is beautiful, this is the best of character and conduct. But we cannot abandon the way of our forefathers on a single first meeting, having never met you before; this would be a hasty decision we might come to regret, and we have left people behind at home whose counsel we are bound to seek. Come back next year, and we will return too. It was, the Sheikh notes, an intelligent answer, exactly the answer of the wise and noble people Abu Bakr had described, where no single man rushes to decide and the elders are consulted before anything is done.

A prophecy in a desperate hour

But their military leader, Muthanna ibn Haritha, added the real obstacle, and in adding it he revealed how the whole world looked from inside that hour. We hold two sets of treaties, he explained. One with the Arab tribes around us, and those we are not worried about; whatever we choose to do, we can manage our own affairs against the Arabs. The other is with Kisra, the emperor of Persia, and that is a different matter. We have a pact with him to stay neutral in the affairs of the peninsula, and this message of yours does not look like the sort of thing the kings will tolerate. If we were to accept you, it could only be on the Arab side. We could help you against the Arabs, but never against Persia.

Watch what the Prophet ﷺ does with that excuse, because it is one of the most astonishing moments in the whole Makkan period. Banu Shayban are afraid of Persia, the Sasanian empire that had bloodied Rome for three and a half centuries, an empire whose ruined palaces at Ctesiphon still stop anyone who sees them, pillars rising fifty meters out of the bare desert. They tremble before it. And as the Prophet ﷺ rises to leave, a refugee with no army, no city that will keep him, and a protector who will be dead within the year, he turns and says: and what if I were to tell you that Allah will give you the kingdom of Kisra, his land and his wealth and his palaces? Their young men say of course, if that were so, we would follow you. But they do not believe it. How could a scattering of Arabs topple the power that even Rome, in three hundred and fifty years, could not break?

Hold that scene against what history actually did. The same Persia they feared is the empire that, within a handful of years of the Prophet's ﷺ death, the Muslims would pour into and dissolve so completely that historians to this day are mystified by it. The Sasanian state that had stood against Rome for centuries crumbled in roughly two years and became mere tales of the past, and the men who did it were, in many cases, the very Bedouin of tribes like the one he was speaking to. The Sheikh ties it to the famous opening of Surah ar-Rum, revealed in this same Makkan struggle, where Allah foretold that the Byzantines, defeated then, would themselves rise again:

The Byzantines have been defeated In the nearest land. But they, after their defeat, will overcome Within three to nine years. To Allah belongs the command [i.e., decree] before and after. And that day the believers will rejoiceQur'an 30:2-4

He could see all of it from inside the worst hour of his life, because his certainty was never built on his circumstances. It was built on his Lord. They asked him for safety from an empire; he offered them the empire itself, and they were not ready to believe him. Years later, when the Prophet ﷺ sent his letter to Kisra and the emperor tore it to pieces, the Prophet ﷺ said that Allah would tear his kingdom apart as he had torn the letter, and so it was. The Sheikh points out the precision of it: the promise was the kingdom of Kisra, not of Caesar, and Allah's promise is exact, for the empire of Persia fell utterly while the realm of Rome did not.

No such thing as half a Muslim

So there was a deal on the table from the wisest tribe in the region, and it was a partial one: Muslims toward the Arabs, neutral toward Persia, faith on the half of the map that was convenient and silence on the half that was dangerous. And the Prophet ﷺ refused it as firmly as he had refused Kinda its throne. He praised their honesty first. Your answer has been good, he told them, and you have been sincere and fair in naming your fears. But then came the line that closed the door: the religion of Allah is only ever aided by those who embrace it in full. There is no such thing as half a Muslim. A faith held on the convenient side of a man's life is no faith that moves anything at all.

This is a thread the Sheikh shows Allah drawing again and again through the revelation. It is the very command of the Book:

O you who have believed, enter into Islam completely [and perfectly] and do not follow the footsteps of Satan. Indeed, he is to you a clear enemy.Qur'an 2:208

The Quraysh themselves had once tried the same bargain in Makkah, worship our gods for a year and we will worship yours for a year, and the answer had come down from heaven just as cleanly, with no room left to negotiate:

For you is your religion, and for me is my religion.Qur'an 109:6

So the Prophet ﷺ stood up and walked away from the most intelligent tribe in the region, leaving its wisdom behind, because its wisdom had a ceiling and his message did not. He would sooner go home empty-handed than carry away a follower who was only following with one foot.

The seed in a city he half-remembered

Then the seerah turns, and it turns on a city the Prophet ﷺ barely knew. Yathrib was not on his radar. He had distant kin there, on his mother's side, and had visited it only once or twice in his life, and it was on the way home from one such visit, as a small boy, that his mother had died on the road. The place carried for him a child's memory and a grave. Yathrib held three peoples in those days: two Arab tribes, the Aws and the Khazraj, and a large Jewish population, one of the only such communities in all of the Hijaz, the others being at Khaybar and, far to the south, in Yemen. And without anyone in Makkah noticing, two things had been quietly preparing that city's soil for years.

The first was a war. The Aws and the Khazraj had torn at each other for over a decade in a grinding civil war, the small Bedouin quarrels over land and water and grazing that swell, one killing at a time, into something enormous. It climaxed a few years before in the Battle of Buath, the most savage day those two tribes ever knew, which killed off the bulk of their senior leadership and left the survivors a younger generation, sick to death of blood and hungry for something new. Aisha, radiyallahu anha, would later say a thing the Sheikh lingers over: the day of Buath was a gift that Allah gave to His Messenger ﷺ. A strange gift, a battle. But it shattered the old order of Yathrib so completely, decimated the entrenched leaders so thoroughly, that there was room left for a new order to take root, and a generation young enough to want it. Only one senior figure of standing had survived Buath, a man named Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul, and it was precisely his survival, his seniority and unspent ambition, that would one day make him the leader of the hypocrites; had it not been for Islam, he would have ruled the city unopposed.

The second thing was stranger, and it was the very arrogance of their neighbors that had done it. Living for generations beside the Jews of Yathrib, the Aws and the Khazraj had absorbed a vocabulary no other Arabs possessed: a prophet, a book, a law, a reckoning, a God who sends messengers. The Arabs as a whole were a people to whom no warner had come, who did not know what a prophet was. But these two tribes did. And in their quarrels their neighbors would taunt them with a deliverer who was coming: a prophet is about to be sent, they would say, and when he comes we will follow him and destroy you with him, down to the last man. The Qur'an records exactly this turn of theirs, this praying for a victory through a prophet they expected and then rejecting him when he came, because he came from the Arabs and not from among themselves:

And when there came to them a Book [i.e., the Qur'an] from Allah confirming that which was with them, although before they used to pray for victory against those who disbelieved, but [then] when there came to them that which they recognized, they disbelieved in it; so the curse of Allah will be upon the disbelievers.Qur'an 2:89

So the Aws and the Khazraj had been hearing the prophecy of a coming prophet for years, thrown in their faces as a threat by people who held themselves superior. When an Arab actually appeared claiming prophethood, the idea was not strange to them at all. It was the very thing their neighbors had been promising, suddenly within their own reach, and the chance to seize for themselves the book and the law and the dignity that had been flaunted over them for a hundred years.

Six men at Aqabah

And so the meeting that changes everything happens almost without ceremony. The Prophet ﷺ is working the great tents as always, concentrating on the heavyweights, Kinda and Banu Hanifa and the rest, because it is only common sense to give your fullest effort where one conversion might bring a whole tribe. On his way among them he notices a small group resting at Aqabah, the place near Mina where the stones are thrown at the Hajj. No tent. No entourage. No prestige of any kind. Just six men sitting on the ground. He asks them who they are. We are from the Khazraj, they say.

This time Abu Bakr is not at his side to brief him, and so the Prophet ﷺ searches his own memory for the thread. Which Khazraj? He reaches for the one connection he has. The Khazraj who are the neighbors of the Jews? Yes, they answer, those Khazraj. From Yathrib. He asks if he may sit and speak with them, and they make room, and he gives them everything. The call to worship Allah alone, the warning against shirk, the recitation of the Qur'an, the same fire and sincerity and zeal he had spent on Kinda and Banu Shayban and every glittering tribe before them. Six poor men with nothing to offer, not even a tent, received the full measure of his sincerity, exactly as if they had been the strongest tribe in Arabia. And Allah, who chooses where His religion will take root, had chosen them. They believed.

The Sheikh presses on the wonder of it. The first converts to Islam from beyond Makkah were not the superpowers the Prophet ﷺ had so carefully courted. They were an obscure handful from a tribe he could barely place, from the poorer of Yathrib's two clans, the farmers rather than the merchants, men of no rank the world would ever have noticed. And it was through them, not through the might of Kinda or the wisdom of Banu Shayban, that Allah had decided to plant His religion. The Prophet ﷺ himself would later voice the lesson: in Makkah he had been shown that he would emigrate to a land of palm trees and greenery, and he had assumed it must be Yemen, with its civilization and its great tribes, never once guessing it was Yathrib. He was simply scattering seeds across every field he could reach, the way a farmer broadcasts seed not knowing which will take, never trivializing a single soul, and Allah grew His entire religion from the field no one was watching.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this chapter as a string of near-misses and one lucky break, a discouraging slog redeemed at the last moment, and to leave it there. That would be to miss the question it is putting to us. Strip it down and you will not read a failing season of your own life the same way again.

He never once trivialized a small opening. The Prophet ﷺ poured himself into the heavyweights, as wisdom demanded, but when he passed six tentless strangers on the ground at Aqabah, men with nothing to give him and no power to protect him, he gave them the identical passion he had given the kings, and Allah grew His whole religion out of them. We are forever doing the opposite, measuring people by what they can do for us, saving our sincerity for the rooms that look important and offering the leftovers to everyone else. You do not know, and you cannot know, which small good and which overlooked soul your Lord has already chosen to change everything. Give the six men at the mountain pass the same fire you would give the kings.

He would not barter the message for the win, even when the win looked like survival. Kinda offered him an army for a promise of a throne; Banu Shayban offered him loyalty on the convenient half of the map. He refused both, from inside a situation so tight that any of us would have called it desperate. Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi will not let us pass quickly, because the temptation is so human: this was a deal many of us would have taken for a fraction of the compromise. But the Prophet ﷺ knew that some doors are not worth the key they demand, that you cannot purchase the help of Allah by selling off a piece of His religion, and that a people who come to you for power will never be the people through whom Allah aids His cause. Ask what you have quietly been willing to trade away for a door that looked like rescue.

And his certainty was never a reading of his circumstances; it was a reading of his Lord. With nothing in his hands, his wife and his uncle buried, his city closing around him and his protector dying, he looked a frightened tribe in the eye and promised them the empire of Persia, the very power that had broken Rome for three centuries, and it came true exactly as he said. That is the difference between faith and optimism. Optimism counts up the situation and hopes; faith looks past the situation to the One who owns it. The men hunting his religion held the kingdoms, the armies, the wealth, the whole visible weight of the world, and within a generation it was all carried into the masjid of a city that, on this day, was not even on his map.

So take this one thing down into your ordinary days. When your own efforts seem to be failing on every front you can see, when the powers you approached have all said no and the doors you pushed on hardest have all stayed shut, remember which tent Allah chose. The way out for the Prophet ﷺ came from precisely where he never thought to look, not from Yemen, not from Kinda, not from the heavyweights of the peninsula, but from six strangers at a mountain pass:

And whoever fears Allah, He will make for him a way out, and will provide for him from where he does not expect.Qur'an 65:2-3

Six men with no tent went home to Yathrib and began to talk, and the next season more than double their number came, and the season after that the number multiplied again, until a city that had never been on his radar was begging the Prophet ﷺ to come and be its refuge and pledging its very neck to protect him. Three and a half years, from six men on the ground to an entire city opening its gates. The relief your Lord is preparing for you may already be growing, quietly and unseen, in a field you have not even thought to look at. May Allah, who makes a way out for those who fear Him and provides from where they never expect, open for us the doors we cannot see, grow His good in us beyond our knowing, and never let us trivialize a single soul or a single small chance to do good for His sake. And may He send His peace and blessings upon His Messenger Muhammad ﷺ, who scattered the seeds in every field and trusted his Lord with the harvest.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 24: planting the seeds of Yathrib (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (6:151, 30:2-4, 2:208, 109:6, 2:89, 65:2-3), verified via quran.ai. The narration is the Sheikh's; the phrasing is Buruja's.

The Seerah · 25 of 104

The Pledges of Aqabah

The night a city took his hand

For more than ten years he ﷺ had walked the fairgrounds of the Hajj season carrying the same question from tent to tent. Who will shelter me, so that I can convey the words of my Lord? Season after season the great tribes of Arabia had looked him over and said no. A man here believed, a man or two there, never enough to be a base to stand on. And then, in the season before this one, he ﷺ had passed a small knot of strangers camped on the plains of Mina, six men he did not recognize, and stopped to ask who they were. They were from Yathrib, of the Khazraj, not a tribe whose name carried far. He sat with them and gave them the dawah, and something opened. They did not all convert on the spot, and the histories are honest that we cannot be sure whether their hearts settled there or later, back home. What we know is the thing that had not happened in ten years: they did not say no. They said, let us think about this affair, and we will come back to you next year.

This is where Dr. Yasir Qadhi picks the thread up, and it is worth holding the smallness of that beginning, because everything that follows grows out of it.
