Six men who did not say no, and a city not yet called Madinah, for it was still Yathrib and would only take its new name once the man they had just met came to live in it. They went home, and for one full year they did the one thing that costs nothing and changes everything: they spoke. There is a new prophet, they told their people, preaching that Allah is one and the idols are nothing. Then the next Hajj came, and they returned for the pilgrimage bringing converts.

Twelve hands in broad daylight

Twelve men came from Yathrib in the eleventh year of the dawah, two years before the hijrah, and twelve sounds like almost nothing until you set it against the ten years behind it. Never, the Sheikh says, had twelve people accepted Islam at once from any group outside Makkah; it was the largest single conversion the dawah had yet seen from the wider world of the Arabs. But inside the twelve sat a quieter wonder. Ten were of the Khazraj and two of the Aws, and the two tribes were enemies, fresh from five years of civil war that had decimated both; they had never once stood together under anything. Now they stood in a single row under Islam, in a city the religion had not even reached yet, and it was already doing the work it would never stop doing: cementing the bonds tribalism had severed, making people forget the wars of their fathers and remember they were brothers. A positive beginning, the Sheikh says, and you can read the whole future of the Ansar in it.

There was nothing to hide. Twelve pilgrims talking with a preacher drew no one's eye, so this first covenant happened in broad daylight, on the plains of Mina, in the campground the people of Yathrib always took near the great Jamarah at a pass the Arabs called Aqabah, which is why the meeting carries the name. And a clarification matters, because people confuse the two: what happened the year before, the six men and their conversation, is not counted as a covenant at all, for nothing was formally sworn. This was the first. Ubadah ibn as-Samit, radiyallahu anhu, one of the twelve, narrates it in Bukhari, and it is he who gives it its famous name. He calls it the pledge of the women, not because women took it, but because of what was in it, and what was not.

For there was not one political clause in it. No armies, no protection, no alliances, nothing about defending him if he were attacked. It was an oath of theology and morality and nothing more: to worship Allah alone, not to steal, not to fornicate, not to kill their children as the people of jahiliyyah did, not to live immoral lives, and to obey the Prophet ﷺ in all that was good. These are the very terms on which the Qur'an, when it came, would set the pledge of the believing women, terms of the heart and the limbs and no more, and so the name fit it exactly.

O Prophet, when the believing women come to you pledging to you that they will not associate anything with Allah, nor will they steal, nor will they commit unlawful sexual intercourse, nor will they kill their children, nor will they bring forth a slander they have invented between their arms and legs, nor will they disobey you in what is right - then accept their pledge and ask forgiveness for them of Allah. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.Qur'an 60:12

And the Prophet ﷺ attached to it a promise that is the whole shape of this religion in miniature. Whoever fulfills this, he told them, his reward is with Allah, and Allah will give him Paradise; whoever falls short and repents, Allah will forgive him; and whoever falls short and does not repent, his affair is with Allah, who may punish or forgive. Notice how young the religion still was. The five daily prayers had been legislated by now, brought down on the Night Journey, but there was no Ramadan to fast, no zakat, no Hajj with its rites, and wine was still lawful and would stay lawful for years. To become a Muslim in those days was tawhid and an upright life, and nothing else yet. The Sheikh lingers here for a reason: Allah builds His religion the way a wise builder builds a house, foundations first, and the foundations are always the heart turned to its Lord and the limbs kept from sin.

One teacher for a whole city

The twelve asked for one thing before they scattered home. Send us someone, they said, who can teach us the Qur'an and lead us in prayer. They had the message now; they needed a teacher to plant it. And the choice the Prophet ﷺ made tells you something about how Allah's religion travels. There were at least two hundred and fifty Muslim men in Makkah by now, some years deep in their faith. Out of all of them he chose one young man, Mus'ab ibn Umayr, radiyallahu anhu, and sent him north alone. One teacher for a whole city.

What that one young man did in a few weeks is almost hard to believe. Within weeks of Mus'ab reaching Yathrib, forty people had embraced Islam, and word came back from the Prophet ﷺ: you may now establish the Jumu'ah prayer. Sit with that, because it rearranges something you might have assumed. The first Friday prayer in the entire history of Islam was not prayed in Makkah, and was not led by the Prophet ﷺ. It was prayed in Yathrib, at his command but far from his presence, because in Makkah the believers still could not so much as pray in public without being set upon and beaten. The very first khutbah was delivered by Mus'ab, in the house of As'ad ibn Zurarah, radiyallahu anhu, a large house with a grove of date palms, and forty worshippers standing in it. This is how things start, the Sheikh says. A handful of people in a living room, and a religion takes root.

From that garden the dawah moved outward, street by street. Their society was divided into subtribes, each living in its own district, and slowly, the Sheikh says, there was hardly a locality left without at least one household that had entered Islam. But two conversions above all would tip the whole city, and both began the same way: with a man marching up to a door, spear in hand.

Two spears at the door

The civil war that had bled Yathrib white had also emptied its leadership, and into that vacuum two young chiefs were rising: Sa'd ibn Muadh and Usayd ibn Hudayr, radiyallahu anhuma. When word reached them that a Makkan stranger was converting their people in the house of As'ad ibn Zurarah, Sa'd was caught in an awkward bind, for As'ad was his own cousin. So he turned to his friend Usayd. Go, he said, and get rid of this man. Who is he to preach against the religion of our forefathers? And Usayd picked up his spear, the open sign of war, and marched.

As'ad saw him coming. This is one of our leaders, he whispered to Mus'ab; he comes with his spear, make du'a. Usayd came in hot. Why have you come to our land, he demanded, to corrupt the weak-minded among us, to take our women and children from the ways of our fathers? Leave this city, if you value your life. And Mus'ab answered with the thing the Sheikh keeps returning to, a calmness that only iman can put into a man. Why not sit, he said, and listen? If you find it agreeable, take it; and if not, I will stop. Usayd, angry but not unreasonable, sat down, spear and all, and listened. And only after the simple message had been laid before him did his heart crack open. What you have said makes complete sense, he told Mus'ab. How does a person enter this faith? The man who had come to kill him was now asking where to sign. Go, said Mus'ab, wash, say the shahada, and pray. So he did. And then he handed Mus'ab the key to the whole city. There is a man behind me, he said, and if you can convince him of what you have just convinced me, not a soul in Yathrib will hold out against you.

Sa'd saw his friend returning and read his face from a distance. By Allah, he said, this is not the same man who left us; when a person leaves paganism for this religion, the Sheikh notes, they change, and it shows. Usayd did not argue theology. He reached instead for the one lever that would move a chief, letting it be known that another clan was plotting to kill As'ad, Sa'd's own cousin, while Sa'd did nothing. A rumor with likely a grain of truth in it, stretched just enough, the way Yusuf's brothers stretched the truth about the wolf. And it moved Sa'd at once. He took up his weapons and marched on the house himself.

He came in furious, speaking past Mus'ab to his cousin. Were you not my kin, he said, these weapons would not still be hanging at my side. Rid us of this guest and his pollution. And Mus'ab, before a man enraged enough to strike his own cousin, made the very same offer, word for word. Sit and listen; if you find it agreeable, good, and if not, I will stop. The Sheikh marvels at the nerve of it, a confidence that is not overconfidence but the certainty of a man who knows what he carries. And Sa'd, looking at this young man who would not waver, not the least afraid even of a chief ready to kill his own blood, decided the offer was fair, and sat. And this time Mus'ab did not only explain. This time he recited, the Qur'an itself, the living miracle handed straight to the ear of the angriest man in the district. And it finished Sa'd completely. The Qur'an is the miracle, the Sheikh underlines, and having heard it, Sa'd asked the same question Usayd had: how do I convert? What do I do?

With those two names, the city turned. The entire subtribe of the two chiefs, the clan of Abd al-Ashhal, followed them into Islam within weeks, the largest mass conversion the dawah had yet known. Hold both men in your mind, for the Sheikh will return to them. Usayd ibn Hudayr, whose recitation would one day be so beautiful that lights descended from the sky, which the Prophet ﷺ told him were the angels come to hear his Qur'an. And Sa'd ibn Muadh, at whose death the Throne of the Most Merciful itself would shake. Both walked into this religion past one young teacher's refusal to flinch.

And there is a footnote to that clan the Sheikh cannot resist, for it carries its own lesson in Allah's mercy. One man of the tribe, Amr, refused for years, until the morning of Uhud, when he changed his mind at last, took his shahada, entered the battle, and was killed before a single prayer ever came due on him. Abu Hurayrah, radiyallahu anhu, would later quiz his students with him as a riddle: who entered Paradise without praying a single rak'ah? Of this man the Prophet ﷺ said, he did very little, and he was rewarded much.

Why Yathrib said yes

The next Hajj season came, in the twelfth year of the dawah, only months before the hijrah, and Mus'ab came back to Makkah, not with twelve but with around seventy-five Muslims arriving as pilgrims, and behind every one of them, the Sheikh reminds us, stood two or three more who had stayed home. In one year, one young man had brought somewhere between two hundred and fifty and three hundred people into Islam, a community now rivaling in sheer numbers everything thirteen years of preaching had gathered inside Makkah itself. The question almost asks itself. Why did Yathrib open so fast when Makkah had bolted itself shut for thirteen years?

The Sheikh slows down here, because the answer teaches. There was no persecution there, for one thing; the message could be heard on its own terms. The hearts of that city already leaned, for another, toward the idea of one God. But the biggest reason was also the saddest, and it was the civil war. The old guard who would have defended paganism had largely killed one another off, and the generation coming up behind had watched the old ways bankrupt the whole city. Half their people dead, all their leadership gone, and for what? They had seen the foolishness of the old gods with their own eyes, tasted its emptiness in their own grief. So when a message arrived preaching one God, an end to bloodshed, and clean upright lives, the young were ready for it in a way comfortable, undisturbed Makkah never was. And there was something in the stock itself, the Sheikh adds. The Aws and the Khazraj were Yemeni Arabs by descent, of the line of Qahtan, and the Prophet ﷺ praised the people of Yemen as the softest of heart and gentlest of soul. Iman is Yemeni, he said, and wisdom is Yemeni.

Jabir ibn Abdillah, radiyallahu anhu, who witnessed what came next, folds those long Makkan years into one aching sentence. The Prophet ﷺ stayed more than ten years in Makkah, he said, going to the people in the Hajj season, asking, who will shelter me, who will support me, so that I may convey the message of my Lord? And he would find no one, only a man here, a man or two there, never enough. Until at last Allah guided us, the people of Yathrib, and we believed in him and began to recite the Qur'an. And then, Jabir says, Allah gave us an idea. Mark his words, the Sheikh insists, for Jabir will not take the credit. He does not say we thought of it. He says Allah gave us the idea: for how long will we leave the Messenger of Allah ﷺ to be driven from valley to valley, fearing for his life? So the invitation went from them to him: come to us, live among us, be one of us. It came from the people of Yathrib. He ﷺ never imposed himself on the city that would one day cradle his grave.

Out of the tents, one by one

So a meeting was set, and this one could not be held in daylight. Seventy-five Muslims hidden among pagan relatives was no longer a curiosity; it was a danger. The word went quietly around: the last night of the Hajj, in the last third of the night, in the valley beside Aqabah, behind the place where they camped. Ka'b ibn Malik, radiyallahu anhu, was there, the same Ka'b whose later repentance Allah Himself would send down in the Qur'an, and he tells it like a memory you can still feel. We hid our Islam from our pagan relatives, he said. We lay down among them, and when the night had grown deep we began to slip out of our tents one by one, so as not to wake suspicion, and gathered in the dark to wait for the Messenger of Allah ﷺ.

He came with exactly one companion, and the companion tells you something tender about the hour. He came with his uncle al-Abbas, still upon the religion of his people, not yet a Muslim. That he came at all says a great deal. Like Abu Talib before him, Abbas loved his nephew with a fierce and natural love, and that bond would last years yet before Abbas finally entered Islam, late, well after Badr. He had come, the Sheikh explains, to negotiate something like a transfer of guardianship: if Makkah's protection of his nephew was ending, then Abbas, as the senior man of the clan who still acknowledged him, would at least see the boy handed to these strangers on terms he could trust. But the elders of Yathrib he had known from his trading days were dead now, gone in the civil war, and when he looked around that dark valley he recognized not one face, and it unsettled him.

So Abbas spoke first, and he spoke pure tribe. He addressed the whole gathering as Khazraj, though Aws stood among them too, because eyes like his could not yet see Muslims, only Khazraj and Aws, the categories of jahiliyyah. You know the standing of this man among us, he said. We have protected him from his own people, though we follow their same religion. He has honor among us, and protection. But he has resolved to leave us and join you. So if you are certain you can live up to what you promise him and shield him from those who will come against him, take on his burden; and if not, leave him now. Hear the overcompensation in it, the Sheikh says. Honor and protection was a generous account to give of a city whose chiefs would, within weeks, plot the man's assassination; but Abbas was embarrassed that his own nephew had to leave the Banu Hashim at all, and reached for the old tribal pride to cover the shame. The believers let the old diplomat finish, and answered simply. You have spoken, they said. Now let the Messenger of Allah ﷺ speak.

My blood is your blood

There were no torches in that valley, only whatever light the waning night allowed, for the moon was past its full. The Prophet ﷺ stood in that dimness and began to recite the Qur'an, calling them to Allah. And then he laid down his condition. I will take your pledge, he said, on this: that you protect me the way you protect your own, your wives and children and families. That you make me one of you. And one of them seized his hand almost before he had finished. We are a people of war, he said, we have inherited it father to son; no one will harm you, stretch out your hand. Haste and iman in the same breath.

But before the pledging began, one man asked a question first, and the Sheikh holds it up like a jewel. It was Abul-Haytham ibn at-Tayyihan, radiyallahu anhu, the same man who would one day cook his family's last old goat because he could not bear to find the Prophet ﷺ, Abu Bakr, and Umar with stones bound against their bellies for hunger. Messenger of Allah, he said, between us and the Jews of our city there are treaties, and by taking you in we will be severing them. And then, when we have done this for you and Allah grants you victory, will you return to your own people and leave us? Hear what is hidden inside that fear, the Sheikh says. Here are seventy-some men about to take on the whole of Arabia for one hunted refugee, and this man's dread is not death. He is so certain Allah will give His Messenger victory that the only thing he fears is the day after it, the day he might lose him. What a frank, almost dangerously bold question to put to a prophet, and what a freedom there was among them, that a man could ask it without fear.

And the Prophet ﷺ smiled, and answered with a phrase in Arabic that the Sheikh says loses everything in English. Rather, he said, my blood is your blood, and my ruin is your ruin. I am of you, and you are of me. We will live together, and we will die together. Once I cut my ties to Makkah, I am one of you. And mark how completely he kept that word. Years later, on the day Makkah opened to him as its conqueror, his whole tribe now Muslims around him, he could have moved back to his hometown. Instead he turned his back on Makkah and walked home to Madinah. He lived there, died there, and is buried there. He told these men the truth in the dark of that valley.

Set this night beside the tribe of Kindah from only a few months before, the Sheikh insists, and you will see what made these people the Ansar. Kindah too had been offered the chance to shelter him, and had named a price: make us the rulers, once Allah gives you victory. They were turned away, the irony being that had they asked for nothing, the rule might well have come to them. A religion taken up for the sake of a throne is no religion. The Ansar asked the very opposite of a price; their one condition was that they would never be left. Then he ﷺ named the terms of this second pledge, no longer the gentle clauses of the first: that you hear and obey in ease and in hardship, that you spend of your wealth in the way of Allah, that you command the good and forbid the evil, that you speak the truth fearing no one's blame, and that when I come to you in Yathrib, one of the last times he would call it by that name, you protect me as you protect your own wives and children. These were political conditions now, an oath of war and alliance and sacrifice. And out of the dark a single voice called the question every heart was holding. And what, it asked, is in it for us? And the Prophet ﷺ gave them the one word they wanted. I cannot promise you wealth, he said in effect, nor fame nor spoils. Al-Jannah. Paradise. And that was all they wanted.

Your necks will meet swords

They rose to give the pledge, and then came one of the most striking interruptions in the whole seerah. As'ad ibn Zurarah, Mus'ab's own host, one of the first believers of the city, pressed the Prophet's ﷺ hand down before a single man could take it. Wait, he said, and turned to his own people. O people of Yathrib, we have not crossed all this distance except that we already know, in our hearts, that this man is the Messenger of Allah. But understand what you are about to sign. To take him from his people means war. Once they expel him and he is with us, the Arabs will come against us with swords, and the best of you will be killed; fathers will lose their sons, and sons their fathers. So if you are ready for your necks to meet the swords, take his hand, and your reward is with Allah. But if you fear, leave it now, and perhaps Allah will excuse you. The Sheikh calls this the intelligence of As'ad: he did not want a single man entering this oath half-understanding what it would cost, standing before Allah having sworn a pledge he had not weighed. So with the hand of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ already reaching out, As'ad held one last exit wide open.

And their answer has carried fourteen centuries down to us. Take your hand off the hand of the Messenger of Allah, they told him. We want this pledge. And one by one, seventy-two men placed their hands in his ﷺ, and one by one he promised each of them Paradise in return. What a blessed company that was. There were two believing women in that valley as well, and from them he ﷺ took the pledge by word alone, never touching the hand of a woman not related to him, as was his way his whole life long.

And when it was finished, al-Abbas looked over the crowd one last time, still worried, and said what was on his mind. These are people I do not know, not one of them; they are all young, all strangers to me. And he was right, the Sheikh grants. But if Abbas did not know them, Allah knew them; if the old diplomat could not place a single face in that dark valley, Allah and His Messenger had counted every name in it. Three weeks from that night, the chiefs of Makkah would gather in their hall to plot the Prophet's ﷺ murder. Within two months, the road these unknown young believers had just opened with their own necks would carry the whole ummah out of Makkah and into a new home. The emigration begins tomorrow. And the highest rank among all the Ansar, the Sheikh notes, belongs to those who were here, at the two pledges: those who said yes in the open when twelve was a crowd, and those who slipped out of their tents to say it again in the dark.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read the pledges of Aqabah as a clever piece of political timing, a refugee securing a base, and leave it there. That would be to miss the question the night puts to us. Everything in the seerah before this is asking; everything after it is building. This is the hinge, and the hinge turns on hearts.

Look first at how the religion entered that city, because it is the opposite of how we imagine these things move. It came not on the back of an army or a king, but in the calm of one young man with no weapon, who, when a chief stood over him with a spear and a death threat, answered only: sit and listen, and if it displeases you, I will stop. He said it twice, to two different men come to kill him, and twice it disarmed them, because the confidence that iman gives does not need to raise its voice. We so often think our faith must argue and shout and win, when the thing that actually opened a whole city was a quiet certainty, and a Book. Notice what Mus'ab reached for when explanation had done its work: he recited the Qur'an, the miracle you can place before the angriest man in the room and let it do what nothing else can. Ask how much you have let it speak, to others and to your own hard hours.

Look next at what the Ansar asked for against what Kindah asked for, because the two requests are a mirror held up to us. Kindah wanted thrones, was refused, and lost everything. The Ansar wanted one thing only, never to be left, and they were given him ﷺ, and promised Paradise besides. What you ask of this religion reveals what you actually love. Do you come to it for what it can make of your worldly standing, or do you come, like Abul-Haytham, so sure of Allah's promise that your only fear is being parted from His Messenger? A man of that same city believed on the morning of Uhud and fell before one prayer came due on him, and he ﷺ said he did very little and was rewarded much, because Allah weighs the heart before the deeds. The resume is not the point. The heart that swore the oath is.

And then there is the line the whole night turns on, called from the dark by a voice that spoke for them all: and what is in it for us? He ﷺ could promise nothing the eye could see. He gave them one word. Al-Jannah. And seventy-two men decided, with their necks on the line and As'ad's warning still ringing that their best would be killed, that one word was worth everything they had. That is the trade the pledge struck, and it has never once expired. Hear and obey, spend, command the good, speak the truth no matter who it angers, shelter the deen with your own body, and the return is the same single word it was in that valley. The day quietly asks each of us what we have decided that word is worth, and whether, when our own hand is asked for, we will reach for his ﷺ the way they did, or hold back and hope to be excused.

So take this night down into your ordinary days. Carry your faith with the calm of Mus'ab, who never had to shout. Ask of your religion only what the Ansar asked, and watch what you secretly want from it. And when the cost of obedience comes due, as it always does, remember the seventy-two who heard the price named honestly, the swords and the fatherless sons, and answered, take your hand off the hand of the Messenger of Allah, we want this pledge. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, whose blood became their blood and whose home became their home, be pleased with the Ansar who took his hand in the dark and never let go, and gather us with them, and with him ﷺ, in the Jannah he promised and they asked for.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 25: the pledges of Aqabah (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translation is from Sahih International (60:12), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 26 of 104

The early emigration to Madinah

Every road out of Makkah had a price

It is the last night of Hajj, in the twelfth year of the dawah, and in a fold of the valley behind the jamarat of Mina something is about to be sworn that will redraw the map of the world. More than seventy men and two women from a northern town named Yathrib have come down under cover of dark to a man ﷺ their own city's poets have not yet heard of. A year before, a smaller delegation had given him a gentle, private oath: no politics in it, no protection, no promise to lift a sword. The Arabs would come to call that first pact the covenant of the women, precisely because it asked nothing a woman could not give. This second night is different. This is the covenant of war.

So begins one of the most underread stretches of the whole seerah. We are taught the famous cave, the spider, the pursuit, all of which belong to tomorrow's story. What we are rarely taught is the quiet exodus that ran ahead of it: believers slipping out of Makkah in ones and twos toward a city not yet called Madinah, each paying a different toll at the gate, a wife, a child, a fortune, freedom itself. Dr. Yasir Qadhi spends this episode with the ones who left first, and by its end the road has come to matter as much as the arrival.

A scream over the sleeping camps

Picture the place exactly as it was: the middle of the night, the last night of Hajj, every camp in Mina dead asleep, and the desert so still that a single voice can carry clear across the valley. The Prophet ﷺ had arranged this meeting like a man who knows that trusting Allah and tying the camel are one instruction, not two: the hour chosen for when Makkah would least suspect it, his cousin Ali and his friend Abu Bakr posted as lookouts, his uncle Abbas brought along at his side. The terms were plain. You will protect me as you protect your own women and your own children. They asked what they would receive in return. He ﷺ answered with a single word: Jannah.

Before a hand was raised, one of their own stood and begged them to slow down, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi lingers on this man, for he shows the marks of a leader thinking past the night in front of him. Do not be hasty, he told them. You have traveled all this way knowing he is the Messenger of Allah; we do not doubt that. But understand what you are saying yes to. Say yes to this and you will bury your sons; you will make widows of your women; the whole of Arabia will come at you as one bow drawn back. If you are ready, give the oath. And if you are not, excuse yourselves tonight, and perhaps Allah will excuse you, for you never made the promise; do not pledge and then break. They shouted him down: remove your hand, and let us pledge. One by one, more than seventy hands took the hand of the Prophet ﷺ, while the two women among them gave their oath by word alone, for his ﷺ hand never in his life touched the hand of a woman who was not his to touch, not even in the sealing of an oath as grave as this one. Off to one side stood Abbas, displeased, still doing the arithmetic of the old world of tribe and elder and protection: I see only young faces, he muttered; I cannot say whether they will keep faith. He was righter than he knew. They were new faces because a new age was beginning.

Then the scream. From somewhere out in the valley a voice tore over the tents: O people sleeping in your camps, do you not know that a band of the blameworthy has gathered with the one who left his religion to make war on you? The Prophet ﷺ knew at once whose voice it was. He named the shaytan of that valley by his strange, knotted name, for the names of the shayatin, the Sheikh notes, run always to twisted syllables, and answered him aloud: I swear by Allah, I will deal with you. Sit with what this moment is. The entire craft of shaytan is concealment; he whispers, he hides, he almost never shows his hand. This night he was so overwhelmed by what had just been pledged that he abandoned his own method and screamed into the human world for someone to do something, because he himself could do nothing. Allah had warned the believers exactly what he is:

Indeed, Satan is an enemy to you; so take him as an enemy. He only invites his party to be among the companions of the Blaze.Qur'an 35:6

At Aqabah you watch that enemy, named in the Book, lose his composure and cry out in the dark.

The blood of the men of Yathrib was up. Should we not fall on them tonight? We are seventy and armed; they sleep unarmed in their tents. It was an honest question, and the Sheikh holds us inside it, for this is the first time in thirteen years of beatings and boycotts and fresh graves that the Prophet ﷺ has had anything resembling a fighting force at his back, and every human instinct in the valley said now. His answer set the shape of everything to come: I have not been commanded to do this. No falling on sleeping men, not even these men, not even on the night they had plotted his death. The meeting dissolved into the dark as quietly as it had gathered. By morning Quraysh had caught the scent of something and sent delegations storming from camp to camp, and at the tents of the Khazraj the pagans of the tribe swore by Allah, truthfully, that they knew of no such meeting, while the Muslims among them held their silence behind that oath, unwilling to lie even to save their own skins.

The women who kept the pledge

Do not let those two women pass by unremarked, because the Sheikh refuses to. The Prophet ﷺ did not tell the men of Yathrib to leave their women in the tents. The women came, the women pledged, and what they pledged was not a softer version of the men's oath. The first covenant had been the covenant of the women because it carried no commitment to fight. This was the covenant of war, and the promise the two women gave was the very same promise the men gave, word for word, weight for weight. Their oath was counted in full, they were held to its terms, and, as their lives would prove, they held to it.

Of one of them, history kept barely a line: she was a cousin of Muadh ibn Jabal, the companion the Prophet ﷺ would one day call the most knowledgeable of this ummah in the lawful and the unlawful, and beyond that the record falls silent. The other was Nusaybah, and she turned one sentence sworn in a dark valley into half a century of proof. At Uhud she fought with her own body to shield the Prophet ﷺ and walked off that field carrying more than a dozen wounds. Years later, when the rumor flew that he ﷺ had been killed and he called the believers to renew their pledge, her hand was again among them. Her son grew into a soldier and was cut to pieces before her eyes by Musaylimah the false prophet, and she marched against the very man who killed him. And in her sixties she fought at Yarmuk in the land of the Romans, and there her hand was severed from her arm. Then even the record of her loses the thread. She gave the oath of Aqabah once, as a young woman in the dark, and spent the rest of her life paying it as though it had been sworn that very morning.

Here the Sheikh pauses over a truth at once sorrowful and strangely consoling. There were tens of thousands of companions, and for the overwhelming mass of them we do not possess two facts; for many, not even a name. Think how impossible it would be to document a single generation of one city in our own time; then know that the same forgetting fell over them. Whole lifetimes of sacrifice are filed with Allah alone. The handful of stories we do have, a Nusaybah here, an Umm Salamah there, are not the exceptions. They are the visible edge of a buried treasury, the few coins lying atop a hoard we will not see the bottom of until the Day we are shown it.

Were it not for the hijrah

Understand what the people of Yathrib were truly afraid of that night, because it was not what you would expect. It was not death; they had just volunteered for that with open eyes. Their fear was more tender: when Allah gives you victory, they asked, and your own city of Makkah, which drove you out, finally wants you back, will you leave us and return to them? And the Prophet ﷺ answered with an oath of his own, an old formula of the Arabs that means we are now one people and one fate: my blood is your blood, my ruin is your ruin. Notice the whole direction of this thing. He ﷺ never imposed himself on Yathrib; they came to him, asked to adopt him, offered the protection. And he kept faith with them to the end: even after Makkah, the most beloved of cities to him, lay conquered and open at his feet, he ﷺ turned around and went home to Madinah, because he had given them his word. The Sheikh lets us watch that promise tested years ahead of our story. After a later campaign, the Prophet ﷺ gave enormous gifts to newly conquered chiefs whose faith was still soft; to one such man, an entire valley of sheep, a gift so staggering that the man went home and told his people to embrace Islam, saying: this is the giving of a man who has no fear of poverty; this is a prophet. The Ansar, who had done the hard fighting, felt the sting of it, for they were human, exactly as we are. So the Prophet ﷺ gathered them alone, no Muhajirun in the tent, and asked: are you not content that these people go home with sheep and dirhams and dinars, and you go home with the Messenger of Allah ﷺ? And the men who had once pledged their lives in a dark valley wept where they sat. It was there that he ﷺ spoke the words Bukhari and Muslim preserve: were it not for the hijrah, I would have been a man of the Ansar; and if the Ansar went down into one valley and all of mankind into another, I would go down into the valley of the Ansar. He taught that loving the Ansar is a part of iman, and hating them a sign of hypocrisy. Fourteen centuries on, that ruling has not expired.

Now rewind, the Sheikh urges, and see how patiently Allah built all of this. It began with six men of the Khazraj, a delegation so minor they did not even have a tent of their own at Hajj. The Prophet ﷺ did not trivialize them, and here Dr. Yasir Qadhi presses what is, in his own estimation, one of the great lessons of the entire seerah: wallahi, be genuine and sincere at every opportunity, however small, for you do not know who is standing in front of you or what he will become. His eyes were on the great tribes; yet he planted seeds in six tentless men, and those seeds grew into an Islamic republic. The six became twelve; a teacher was sent north and Islam entered house after house in a single year; the next season more than seventy came back to pledge. And underneath it all, Allah had been preparing the city before His Prophet ﷺ knew he would need one: a long, bloody civil war between Yathrib's two great tribes had raged while Abu Talib still lived and Makkah paid it no mind, a gift wrapped and set aside years in advance. There is even a bitter irony stitched in. The People of the Book of Yathrib had boasted for generations over their pagan neighbors that a prophet was coming, and that with him they would crush those neighbors; the very neighbors they mocked heard the message first, recognized it from the mockery itself, and believed, while the ones who had carried the prophecy refused it. The boast fell upon the boasters. These, the Sheikh says, are the ways of Allah.

Stand back here and count the phases you have walked through in twenty-six days: a secret dawah, a public preaching that bore persecution with patience, a political asylum under a just king in Abyssinia, and now a political emigration to a city that has invited him ﷺ to lead it, with treaties and open wars still ahead. A question rises that echoes into our own century, and the Sheikh poses it head on: is the seerah a ladder, each phase abrogating the one below it, so that only the final phase binds? Or a corridor of doors, every one still valid, each opened by the circumstances that fit it? A small, forceful minority said ladder, the Andalusian Ibn Hazm most fiercely, and the Sheikh asks us to notice when he wrote, at the beginning of the decline of al-Andalus, a desperation bleeding into the deduction. But the majority across the centuries answered: nothing here was abrogated; every phase remains alive, ready for the situation that calls it forth. And here Dr. Yasir Qadhi speaks frankly, in a judgment distinctly his own: to impose one unified program on the whole ummah is, to put it gently, naively foolish, for what is unfolding in one Muslim land is not what is unfolding in another, and none of it is what is happening for Muslims in the West. The scholars of every land must open the seerah fresh in the light of their own street, and for small Muslim minorities in the West he points, with the caution that no model ever fits like a glove, to the closest fit: Abyssinia, believers with no design on the throne, asking only the freedom to worship Allah openly. He said it from a minbar in Memphis in 2012, and it reads as though spoken this morning.

A land of date palms and black stone

After Aqabah, the Prophet ﷺ gathered the Muslims, most likely in the house of al-Arqam where so much of this story has already quietly happened, and made the announcement they had waited thirteen years to hear: Allah has shown me the land of your emigration, a land of date palms lying between two plains of black volcanic rock. For a little while the destination hung unnamed; in one telling he ﷺ wondered at first whether it might be one of Arabia's other famous date countries, for only a few places grew palms in such number. Then certainty came: it is Yathrib. And the Sheikh adds an aside you will not forget on your next journey to the Prophet's city: fly into Madinah in daylight and look out the window as you near it, and you will see them still, the two great harras, fields of porous, ragged, black lava rock that neither hoof nor foot can cross, the exact frame the Prophet ﷺ described before he had ever laid eyes on the place.

Permission was given, and the exodus began the only way it safely could, in ones and twos, by night. Things in Makkah had reached their worst, and Quraysh would tolerate nearly anything sooner than stand and watch their problem simply walk away from them. The books record that the first to leave was Abu Salamah, a man already stamped twice over for the sake of Allah: he and his wife Umm Salamah are counted among the few who made both hijrahs, to Abyssinia first, and now this one. He was not himself of Quraysh; he had settled in Makkah among his wife's people, and so he reasoned that his leaving was no one's business but his own, and packed in the open: one camel, his wife, and their small son set upon it.

Quraysh met him on the road, armed. You may go, they said; we wash our hands of you. But your wife is one of ours, a daughter of Quraysh, and you will not take her. Her clansmen dragged her back. Then his own kinsmen arrived, their pride stung in turn: in that case the boy is ours, our blood. And there, between the two clans, a child barely two years old was pulled this way and that in an actual tug of war, each side hauling on a small body, until his little arm was wrenched clean out of its joint and his mother cried out for them to take him, only stop. Abu Salamah rode into exile utterly alone: his wife held in one house, his injured son carried to another, everything he loved torn into pieces between two tribes who wanted none of it, only the winning of it.

Umm Salamah, radiyallahu anha, tells the rest herself. Every single day, she said, for a year and a half, she would walk out to al-Abtah, the farthest point from which Makkah is still in sight, and there she would sit and weep, a husband gone in one direction and a son held fast in the other, and herself able to do nothing. At last some of her cousins were cut to the heart by the sight of her and shamed the elders into mercy, and she was released, her boy placed back in her arms. And this young mother set her child upon a camel and walked out into the open desert toward a city two weeks distant, with no guard, no guide, and no plan beyond the only one she ever named. Asked on the road who was with her, she gave the answer that is the spine of this whole day: no one. I have Allah.

The noblest escort in Arabia

Just beyond the last rooftops she was seen by Uthman ibn Talhah, a young man of Quraysh riding home from an expedition, and at this point in his life not a Muslim at all. A woman alone with an infant in the open desert, past the edge of safety, in a country of wolves and worse. Where are you going? To my husband, in Yathrib. And who is with you? No one, she said, but Allah. He said: by Allah, you shall not be left like this. And he took the halter of her camel into his hand and began to walk.

He walked the whole of it, Makkah to Yathrib, two weeks on foot, leading her camel by the hand, and the Sheikh marvels at the manners of the man: he did not speak a single word to her the entire road. When it came time to rest he would kneel the camel, then turn his back and walk well away so that she could dismount unseen; he slept apart, by the animal; in the morning he readied her mount and turned his back once more. At the edge of Yathrib he pointed: your husband is in that village. Then he turned and walked the two weeks home again, with no camel now and no reward, having asked her for nothing. Umm Salamah said it plainly for the rest of her long life: I have never seen, among all the Arabs, a nobler gentleman than Uthman ibn Talhah. Imagine, the Sheikh says, doing even a fraction of that, and he a pagan, with nothing in it but the bare honor of the deed.

Now watch how Allah repays a debt that not one human ledger even bothered to record. The woman this pagan honored on the road was to become a Mother of the Believers: Abu Salamah would in time die of his wounds, and the Prophet ﷺ would marry his widow, so that the stranger Uthman had shielded so carefully was, all along, the future wife of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, though no soul on earth knew it that day. And for Uthman himself, Allah held a gate quietly open: he embraced Islam in the very last batch of converts before the conquest of Makkah, slipping in among a company the Qur'an itself sets a degree above the rest:

And why do you not spend in the cause of Allāh while to Allāh belongs the heritage of the heavens and the earth? Not equal among you are those who spent before the conquest [of Makkah] and fought [and those who did so after it]. Those are greater in degree than they who spent afterwards and fought. But to all Allāh has promised the best [reward]. And Allāh, of what you do, is Aware.Qur'an 57:10

Then came the conquest itself, and the great honors of the Kaaba were being handed out. The Prophet's ﷺ own uncle Abbas, already entrusted with giving the pilgrims their water, asked to be given the keys of the House as well, to hold both honors at once. And it was in that moment that this ayah came down:

Indeed, Allāh commands you to render trusts to whom they are due and when you judge between people to judge with justice. Excellent is that which Allāh instructs you. Indeed, Allāh is ever Hearing and Seeing.Qur'an 4:58

Render the trusts to their rightful people. The keys of the Kaaba had belonged to Uthman's family, and so the Prophet ﷺ sent Ali to place them back into his hands, with words like the deed to an estate: take them, with you and your descendants until the Day of Judgment, and none shall take them from you but a wrongdoer. Fourteen and a half centuries have passed, and the family that opens the door of the Kaaba to this very day traces its line back to him: the man who once walked a stranger's camel across the whole of Arabia and never once let his eyes rest on her.

The man whose trade could not lose

Suhayb's entire life had been one long climb. An Arab boy of a northern tribe, captured in the raids of the days of ignorance and sold into slavery among the Romans, he grew up far from his own people: fluent in Latin, the only companion who ever was, fair and ruddy like the people who raised him, speaking his native Arabic with a foreigner's accent, which is the whole reason Makkah took to calling him Suhayb the Roman though there was not a drop of Roman blood in him. Sold back into Arabia, he worked his way to the house of Abdullah ibn Jud'an, bought his own freedom with the labor of his own hands, and built himself into a small businessman with savings buried safely in the earth, the way a man banked his money in a world that had no banks. He accepted Islam among the earliest believers, on the same day as his closest friend Ammar, and from that day the two were scarcely ever apart.

When Suhayb at last rode out for Yathrib, a militia of Quraysh caught up and ringed him round, one man alone against a posse. So he stood his ground, drew his bow, and made them a cold accounting. O people of Quraysh, you know I am among your best archers, and by Allah you will not reach me until I have loosed every one of the forty arrows in this quiver, and after the arrows comes my sword, and none of you will lay a hand on me until it has first run him through. They believed him to the letter, for life is sweet and not one of them cared to be first. Then he offered the other ransom. What if I told you where all my wealth is buried? Would you let me pass? Yes, they said; it is a deal. So he told them the place where the earnings of an entire lifetime lay hidden, the freedom money, the long climb, every coin of it, and walked on toward Madinah with nothing but the clothes on his back.

And here is the moment Allah pinned, like a banner, over the whole exodus. When the Prophet ﷺ saw Suhayb arriving, he smiled before a word of explanation was even possible, and said: your trade has profited, your trade has profited. No rider on earth could have outraced Suhayb to Madinah with the news; Jibril had carried it ahead of him, and revelation had already framed its meaning:

And of the people is he who sells himself, seeking means to the approval of Allāh. And Allāh is Kind to [His] servants.Qur'an 2:207

Stand in his sandals at the gate of that city, penniless twice over, having handed away the savings of a whole hard life, and hear heaven itself announce, through the smile of the Prophet ﷺ, that you got the better end of the bargain.

A mother's vow and a brother's camel

When Umar prepared his own hijrah, he made a pact with two companions, Ayyash and Hisham: they would meet at dawn at a named valley outside Makkah, and whoever was missing when the light came would be understood to have been seized and stopped, and the others must ride on without him. Dawn came, and Hisham was not there. Umar and Ayyash rode north together and made it through.

Then two riders came up from Makkah behind them: Abu Jahl and his brother, who were, through one shared mother, the half brothers of Ayyash. And they had brought a story sharpened on a whetstone. Your mother has sworn an oath to Allah that no shade will touch her head until she has seen your face again; she sits in the burning sun, she will not eat, her hair has matted and crawls with lice, she is wasting away with grief for you. Umar saw straight through it: they are tricking you; by Allah, if hunger truly bites your mother she will eat, and if the sun burns her she will take the shade. And he offered his own wealth to hold his friend in place: if it is money you are riding back for, take from me whatever you want, only do not go. But Ayyash's heart had already gone soft for his mother, and he would go. So Umar gave him the last protection he had left: take my own camel, for she is stronger and faster than theirs; at the first scent of treachery, turn her around and fly. Look, the Sheikh marvels, at what is happening: a man's own blood brothers come to entrap him, while the one who is no blood of his at all, only his brother in faith, offers him all his money and then his own swift camel, anything, only do not walk into the trap.

On the road, the brothers let the long miles and the easy chatter loosen Ayyash's guard, and then Abu Jahl sighed that his own camel had gone weary. Brother, let me ride up behind you on yours, and we will rest mine a while. The very moment Ayyash knelt his camel, the two were upon him. They bound him hand and foot, carried him back, and paraded him into Makkah in his ropes, crying to the city: this is how we deal with our fools. There he found Hisham already taken, exactly as the dawn had foretold, and the two were thrown into a dungeon Quraysh had set up for them alone: a private prison for two believers whose only crime in the world was the road they had tried to take.

The Prophet ﷺ made his own hijrah soon after, and that is tomorrow's story, but from Madinah he did not forget the men left behind in the dark. He ﷺ would rise from the bowing in the prayer itself and call out for them by name: O Allah, rescue Ayyash; O Allah, rescue Hisham; O Allah, rescue the weak and the oppressed among the believers; O Allah, lay the weight of Your grip upon Quraysh. And he kept asking the Muslims aloud: who will go and bring them back to me? To volunteer meant walking alone into the very midst of Quraysh to pry two prisoners loose, and for a long while no one would. At last one man stood: the older brother of Khalid ibn al-Walid, the same blood from which that bravery would later run. He went down into Makkah alone, by night, found a dungeon whose location was no one's public knowledge, came in over the roof, cut the ropes of both men, and brought them home to Madinah. That is the whole of the detail history kept, and the Sheikh laughs that Hollywood could not have scripted it better; this, he says, is the real stuff. Hisham would live on to die a free man's death years later, fighting the Romans at Yarmuk.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read the early emigration as a string of dramatic escapes and leave it there, a set of adventures safely closed in the past. That would be to walk past the door it is holding open for us. Strip these stories down to what they actually cost the people who lived them, and you will not read a hard season of your own obedience the same way again.

Begin with the night in the valley, with seventy swords drawn and the enemy asleep and unarmed within reach. I have not been commanded to do this, he ﷺ said, and turned the moment down. Here is a thing worth carrying out into a violent and suspicious age: at the first taste of strength he had ever known, the answer was restraint. Our religion, the Sheikh says, is not bloodshed and the slaughter of the sleeping, not even when the sleeping are the men who plotted your death and you hold every visible advantage. Most of us are never tested with a sword in a dark valley. We are tested with smaller power: a sharp word we are entitled to say, a revenge that is technically ours to take, a person unguarded against us. Strength, this night teaches, is never the same thing as permission. The question is never can I; the question is have I been commanded.

Then look at the prices, laid side by side. Umm Salamah paid in her own family, torn into three. Suhayb paid in the buried gold of a lifetime, handed over at arrow point. Ayyash paid in his freedom, chained in a private cell. Nusaybah paid in her blood, on installments, across fifty years, down to the hand she left at Yarmuk. Not one of them could see, while they were paying, the receipts Allah was quietly writing: a Mother of the Believers, a trade announced by revelation before any messenger could arrive, a rescue out of legend, the keys of the Kaaba kept in one family until the end of time. They paid in the dark and could not see, and Allah published every payment forever. This is the quiet promise folded inside the day. Obedience always costs something; there is no road out of any Makkah without a toll at the gate. Ask honestly what yours is costing you, the family that does not understand the turn you have made, the money the haram would have brought, the comfort you surrender, and then pay it, and pay it gladly, knowing that the One who carried Umm Salamah across the desert and ransomed Suhayb's trade keeps an exact account and lets nothing fall.

And do not miss the smallest seed of all, six men without a tent, whom the Prophet ﷺ did not trivialize, and out of whom grew everything. You do not know who is standing in front of you, the Sheikh says, or what Allah intends to make of the opportunity He has set, unannounced, in your path. Be genuine in the meeting that seems to lead nowhere, faithful in the work no one is watching, because the gift is so often wrapped long before you feel the need of it, the way a civil war prepared a city for a Prophet ﷺ who did not yet know he would need one. So take these things down into your ordinary days. When strength comes to your hand, ask first whether you have been commanded, and choose mercy. When obedience presents its bill, pay it without flinching, and trust the account. And be wholly sincere in the smallest open door, for you are never told in advance which seed becomes the tree. May Allah make us of those who sell themselves seeking His pleasure, carry us across our own deserts as He carried them, rescue the weak and the oppressed among the believers in every land of ours, and send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, who, with seventy swords at his back, chose restraint, upon the Muhajirun who paid the price of the road, and upon the Ansar who made of their city a home.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 26: the early emigration to Madinah (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (35:6, 57:10, 4:58, 2:207), verified via quran.ai.

The Seerah · 27 of 104

The Hijrah

Two in the cave, and Allah the third

By the time this night arrives, Makkah is almost empty of believers. For three and a half months they have been slipping out under cover of dark, family by family, house by quietly locked house, until the only ones left are the Prophet ﷺ, Abu Bakr, the young Ali, and a scattering of women and children. Every other adult Muslim has gone. He ﷺ alone is still waiting, and the reason he waits is the heart of this whole story: for him, leaving Makkah required permission from heaven, and heaven had not yet given it.

The hijrah is not one neat tale. Dr. Yasir Qadhi reminds us that the seerah was never written down as a single connected narrative; it has to be pieced together, report by report, weighing each chain as it comes. So this night reaches us in fragments, some from the most authentic books and some from the gentler, looser histories, and the honest way to tell it is to hand on each piece as it was handed down: the firm as firm, the beloved as beloved, and none of them, in the end, the slightest bit difficult for Allah.

Almost everyone left in secret, at night, telling no one. There was one exception, and only one: Umar ibn al-Khattab, radiyallahu anhu, who strapped on his sword, made tawaf seven times in full view of Quraysh, and announced to the chiefs of the city that whoever wished his mother to mourn him should follow him out beyond the valley, for he was making hijrah. Not a man moved. It was the answer to a dua the Prophet ﷺ had once made, that Allah strengthen Islam with whichever of the two men named Umar was more beloved to Him; Allah loved this one and gave him to the religion. The other man bore a name from the very same root, and history would call him Abu Jahl. On this very night he will be working in the dark for the religion's destruction.

The visitor at the hottest hour

The soundest thread of these days belongs to a child. Aisha, radiyallahu anha, was around six or seven, and her memory of it is preserved in Bukhari. She could not remember a single day on which her parents were not Muslims, nor a day on which the Prophet ﷺ did not come to visit their house, morning or evening. When the command to emigrate came, Abu Bakr made a camel ready and asked his permission to leave, because the companions asked his permission before everything. Wait, he ﷺ told him. I hope that Allah will give me permission too. Abu Bakr heard what lay underneath the words and asked it plainly: are you hoping for my companionship? Yes, the Prophet ﷺ answered. Companionship.

So Abu Bakr did something that reveals the whole texture of his love. He prepared not one camel but two, and fattened them for the desert: penned so they would not burn their strength, fed and salted so they would drink deep and store the water. He did this, Aisha remembered, for some four months, keeping a second camel ready against a hope he could not be sure would ever be answered.

Which day was it? Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi is careful. No hadith names the date. What the sources give is the day of the week, for Ibn Abbas reports in Sahih Muslim that the great markers of the Prophet's ﷺ life fell on Mondays: born on a Monday, made a prophet on a Monday. Working the calendars backward, modern researchers arrive at Monday the 26th of Safar, in the thirteenth year of the dawah. And notice: the hijrah did not fall in Muharram. The companions would later open their calendar at Muharram for reasons of their own; the journey itself belongs to Safar.

The day announced itself strangely. It was noon, the furnace hour, when the heat of Arabia shutters a city and sends everyone indoors to the midday nap the Prophet ﷺ himself encouraged. Through the deserted streets a figure came walking, his turban wound across his face so that no feature showed. The household knew him anyway, the way you know someone you love by the rhythm of his walk. No one came at that hour, Aisha remembered, unless something grave had happened. He ﷺ asked Abu Bakr to clear the house. Messenger of Allah, said Abu Bakr, there is no one here but your own family. The nikah to Aisha had already been contracted, though years would pass before she joined his household; behind the curtain of that small house, a little girl sat listening to history walk in the door. Then he ﷺ said it: Allah has given me permission to leave. And Abu Bakr begged him, by my mother and my father, has permission been given for me to go with you? Yes, said the Prophet ﷺ. Companionship.

Aisha said afterward that she had never known a person could weep for joy until she saw her father weep that day. Then Abu Bakr offered the gift he had prepared in silence for months: two camels ready for the road, and one of them is yours. The Prophet ﷺ, who in all his life in Makkah had never owned a camel, gave an answer the Sheikh lingers over: only if I pay its price. He would not ride the greatest journey of his life on a borrowed good deed. Part of it was the perfection of his manners, a man who never once leaned on his station to take a favor. And part of it was a hunger of another kind: he wanted the reward of the hijrah whole, paid from his own pocket and shared with no one, because to help another in a good deed is to take a share of it, and this reward he wanted undivided.

She of the two belts

The house became a departure point within the hour, and it ran on its women. Aisha was too young to do anything but watch, but Asma, radiyallahu anha, her elder sister by more than a decade, was a grown woman, and the work fell to her. She bundled the food and provisions, and when she came to tie the saddlebag she found nothing to bind it. So she reached for her own belt, tore it in half with her teeth, kept one half to hold her garment and lashed the Prophet's ﷺ provisions shut with the other. Heaven liked the trade. From that day she was Dhat an-Nitaqayn, she of the two belts, and the name has stayed on her to the end of time.

Abu Bakr, meanwhile, was emptying his house of money. Ibn Ishaq's account remembers that he had forty thousand dirhams on the day he accepted Islam, and only five thousand remained now; the missing thirty-five thousand had gone to buy persecuted slaves out of the hands of their owners, Bilal among them. Nearly ninety percent of his wealth, given for the sake of Allah. Now he gathered the last five thousand, every coin, and took it with him. His reasoning was love guided by a clear head: Makkah will not let two girls starve, but the desert ahead has no neighbors and no guarantees, and there is no telling who along that road may need to be paid.

The bill for that generosity came due at once, and Asma paid it twice. First came her own grandfather, Abu Bakr's father, Abu Quhafa, an old man, blind, still a pagan, and famously sharp of tongue. He came taunting: your father has run off and abandoned you girls, and taken every last coin with him. Look at Asma's faith and her quickness. She filled the empty money pouch with pebbles, wrapped it in cloth, took the blind old man's hand and laid it on the bundle: no, grandfather, see, he has left us all this. He felt the reassuring weight and went away satisfied. By Allah, she said later, he left us nothing at all; I only wanted to settle the old man. Then came the second visitor, and he did not come to taunt. Abu Jahl came hunting the fugitives, and when Asma would not tell him where her father had gone, he struck her across the face until she bled. She did not give him a single word.

Midnight in their parliament

Ibn Abbas pointed at one verse of the Qur'an and said simply: this is that night.

And [remember, O Muhammad], when those who disbelieved plotted against you to restrain you or kill you or evict you [from Makkah]. But they plan, and Allah plans. And Allah is the best of planners.Qur'an 8:30

While the camels stood fattened and ready, the chiefs of Quraysh met in secret session in Darun-Nadwa, the assembly hall that served Makkah as its parliament. Every clan sent its elders, every clan but one: Banu Hashim, the Prophet's ﷺ own clan, was not invited. And the Sheikh pauses over two more empty seats, because even a council convened for murder kept a strange residue of its own rules. Abu Lahab was kept out, for all that his heart would gladly have voted with them; he could not be seen to bless the killing of his own nephew, and what a man does not officially know, he can never be made to answer for. And Mut'im ibn Adi was kept out, the man whose word of protection still formally covered the Prophet ﷺ; you do not invite a chief into the room where you mean to tear up the guarantee he has given. Their treachery, to the last, was wrapped in a tribal sense of honor.

A knock interrupted the secrecy. An old stranger asked to be let in, a shaykh of Najd, he said, come to profit them with his counsel. Ibn Abbas named him without flinching: it was Shaytan himself, come to make sure the night ended in blood. The Sheikh hands this on as he found it, noting the chain skips a generation, even as the night to come makes the conclusion easy to believe. The options went around the room. Chain him in a house? His words would seep through the very stones, and his followers would carry them out of Makkah. Exile him? They would only be handing him to his supporters, and he would return stronger. Then Abu Jahl said aloud the thing every man was thinking and none had the courage to name: why do we not simply kill him?

Even for them it was almost unsayable, for the Arabs did not murder one of their own in cold blood, and the shame would cling to Quraysh for generations. So Abu Jahl engineered the shame away. Let every clan send one strong young man, and let them strike together as one hand, so that the blood lies on every sword in Makkah at once and no single house can be blamed. Banu Hashim could never declare war on the whole city; they would have no choice but to accept the blood money, and the matter would be closed. The stranger from Najd pronounced it the only intelligent plan in the room. The young men were chosen on the spot and sent that same night to wait outside his ﷺ door. And here is the line the Sheikh wants carried out of that hall: the timing of the hijrah was this meeting. Their plot set the clock for the escape Allah had already written. They plan, and Allah plans.

Walking out through Yasin

That same evening Jibril came down with the order to go now, tonight, and told the Prophet ﷺ exactly what the parliament had decided. The famous details of the next hour come through Ibn Ishaq without a connected chain, and the right way to tell them, the Sheikh says, is to keep them just as they were handed on: beloved, less than certain, and not one of them hard for Allah to have done. Ali, the young man the Prophet ﷺ had raised in his own household, now eighteen or nineteen, lay down in his bed, so that any eye pressed to the opening of the house would see a sleeper and relax. And the Prophet ﷺ stepped out through his own front door, walked through the cordon of chosen swordsmen, recited the opening verses of Surah Yasin, and let a handful of dust fall on their heads as he passed.

And We have put before them a barrier and behind them a barrier and covered them, so they do not see.Qur'an 36:9

Not one of them saw him ﷺ go. They found the dust in their hair, and the decoy in the bed, only after he was long gone.

He made his way through the dark to Abu Bakr's house, and the two of them rode out. Where the last shops on the edge of the city gave way to open land, he stopped, turned, looked back, and spoke to Makkah itself: you are the most blessed of Allah's lands and the most beloved of them to me, and had my own people not driven me out, I would never have left you. He would not set foot in the city again until he entered it as its conqueror, eight and a half years later.

Stand at this hinge a moment, because the Sheikh points to it. Thirteen and a half years earlier, in the first days of revelation, the old scholar who recognized the truth of him had said he wished he could be alive on the day the Prophet's ﷺ own people drove him out. Will my own people really drive me out? he ﷺ had asked, astonished. Yes, came the answer, for no man ever brought what you bring without being made an enemy. Now, at the city limits, in the dark, the words were coming true to the letter. Prophecies in this story do not rust.

Three quiet jobs and a hired guide

Madinah lies due north of Makkah. The cave of Thawr lies due south, a hard climb of nearly three hours in exactly the wrong direction. That was the plan's first and cleverest stroke: turn your back on your destination and let the hunt wear itself out racing north. They would lie hidden in the rock for three days and nights until the search cooled, then loop down toward the coast near what is today Jeddah and pick up a back road north that only the desert tribes knew. Fourteen centuries later, engineers laid the modern Makkah to Madinah highway along the easiest natural line through the land, and it runs close to the route of the hijrah. The back road, it turns out, was the best road all along.

Around the cave, Abu Bakr's long planning unfolded as three quiet assignments. His son Abdullah, a sharp boy whom no one thought to watch, moved through the markets of Makkah by day with his ears open, then climbed to the cave each night with food, water, and the day's intelligence, so the two men in the rock could change their plan if the search turned toward them. A man named Amir ibn Fuhayra, a servant Abu Bakr had once bought out of slavery and freed, grazed a flock of sheep back and forth across the boy's tracks until the sand kept no testimony of who had passed. And the secret of the back road was held by a hired guide: a Bedouin of a distant tribe, not of Quraysh, and not a Muslim.

Sit with that last fact, because the Sheikh does. The two most wanted men in all of Arabia placed their lives in the hands of a pagan, for a fee that was a small fraction of what his silence was worth, and he kept faith with them. Scholars went looking for this man across the centuries and found almost nothing; we do not even know for certain whether he ever accepted Islam. What we do know is the judgment the Prophet ﷺ and Abu Bakr made of him: he was honest, and honesty is real wherever it is found. The Qur'an itself says as much of the People of the Book, that among them is the one who will return a fortune untouched and the one who will cheat you of a single coin:

And among the People of the Scripture is he who, if you entrust him with a great amount [of wealth], he will return it to you. And among them is he who, if you entrust him with a [single] coin, he will not return it to you unless you are constantly standing over him [demanding it]. That is because they say, "There is no blame upon us concerning the unlearned." And they speak untruth about Allah while they know [it].Qur'an 3:75

Faith and trustworthiness, the Sheikh says plainly, are two separate tests. Belief does not by itself make a person honest, nor does its absence make a person a liar. For this road, the two travelers needed proven trustworthiness, and that is what they hired.

Two in the cave, Allah the third

Quraysh woke to dust in the hair of their swordsmen and a cousin asleep in the bed, and the bounty went out across the desert: a hundred camels for the two of them, dead or alive, no questions asked. A camel in that Arabia was what a car is to us; imagine a hundred offered for one fugitive who, until that very week, had never owned a single one. Riders combed every road to the north and found nothing, because the trail ran south. So Quraysh hired a master tracker, a reader of footprints and camel sign, who followed the traces from Abu Bakr's door to the foot of Thawr, and there he stopped: from here the mountain takes them, and I can read no further.

It was a guess, but it was the best lead in three days, and so the chiefs themselves climbed the mountain, Abu Jahl among them. Understand the shape of the place. The cave of Thawr is not a chamber you walk into; it is a crevice you wiggle through, with room for barely two men, and its mouth opens at the level of a standing man's feet, looking like nothing more than a crack in the rock. That ordinariness is exactly what saved them. Abu Bakr, lying in the dark, could see the sandals of the men standing above. He whispered: if even one of them looks down at his feet, he will see us. And the answer came back, calm as a man sitting in his own home, a sentence preserved in Bukhari that deserves to be memorized and carried: what do you think of two, when Allah is the third of them?

And what of the spider and its web across the mouth, the leaning tree, the pigeons nesting at the entrance? Here the Sheikh weighs rather than flattens. The story of the spider's web comes in Musnad Ahmad with a slight weakness, and it is the strongest of those reports; the leaning tree and the nesting pigeons arrive with whole generations missing from their chains. There is no harm in narrating them, and no certainty in them, and nothing in any of them would have been the least bit difficult for Allah. But notice what the soundest report makes the true shield that day. Not silk, not feathers. A single sentence of tawakkul, and eyes that Allah simply did not permit to look down. The hunters stood on the roof of the cave and walked away. On the morning after the third night, the guide arrived with the camels, and the road north began.

The hunter and the bracelets of Kisra

Word had spread that three riders had slipped away together: the Prophet ﷺ, Abu Bakr, and a guide. One man came within reach of the bounty, and because he lived to become a Muslim, he told the story on himself in the first person for the rest of his life. Suraqa ibn Malik, a chief among the desert clans, was sitting with his tribesmen when one of them returned from a hunt and said he had just seen three riders far off, surely the ones Quraysh wanted. Suraqa wanted those hundred camels for himself, with no partners. Those are not the three, he said smoothly, that is the party of so-and-so. He sat on a while to keep up the show, then slipped home, armed himself, mounted his warhorse, and galloped after them.

What happened next he swore to in his own telling. At his first sight of the riders his horse pitched forward and threw him. He cast the azlam, the divining arrows the pagans consulted before a venture, and the answer came up: do not go on. Greed put him back in the saddle. The horse went down a second time, the arrows refused a second time, and a third time he rode, close enough now to call out, and this time the horse plunged hardest of all, and a smoke rose up between him and the men ahead. I knew then, Suraqa said, that this was a force beyond me, and that this man's affair was bound to prevail. So the bounty hunter began calling out to his prey, begging a promise of safety. He had been watching them as he closed in: one rider could not keep still, wheeling to the rear and spurring to the front, fear wearing him like a cloak, and none of it fear for himself. That was Abu Bakr. The other never once turned his head, settled in the saddle, reciting quietly, as untroubled as a man riding home. That was the Prophet ﷺ.

The guarantee of safety was written out for him on a piece of leather. He offered them provisions, and they took nothing. One request only was made of him, by Abu Bakr: keep our secret. He kept it, and turned for home. But the strangest thing came as he was leaving. The Prophet ﷺ, a hunted refugee with a price on his head and a single newly bought camel to his name, turned to him and said: how will it be with you, Suraqa, on the day you wear the two bracelets of Kisra? Kisra was the emperor of Persia, the way you say the king when, on all the earth, there is reckoned to be only one king, a man whose gold was the envy of the world. Suraqa could barely stammer the name back. The Prophet ﷺ did not even pause to explain himself.

Hold that receipt to the end of the story. Years later, after the conquest of Makkah, Suraqa came forward holding the same worn piece of leather; the promise was honored, and he accepted Islam and became a companion. Within six or seven years of the Prophet's ﷺ passing, the Persian empire buckled and broke, and the treasures of its palaces were carried into the masjid of Madinah. Umar ibn al-Khattab looked at the heaped gold of a fallen empire and said: where is Suraqa? They brought him, an old Bedouin now, and Umar sat him in his own place, found the two bracelets of Kisra in the treasure, and clasped them onto the old man's wrists, and the masjid shook with the takbir. The promise had become a legend while it waited to come true. Praise be to Allah, said Umar, who has stripped these bracelets from the emperor of Persia and dressed a Bedouin of the desert in them.

What the Hijrah asks of our faith

It would be easy to read the hijrah as a thrilling escape and leave it there, a great adventure safely sealed in the past. That would be to miss the question it puts to us. Strip the night down to its mechanics and you will never again read a hard season of your own life the same way.

He waited for permission, and then he moved completely. Every believer in Makkah was free to emigrate, and they did; he ﷺ alone stayed, because he took no major step without permission from his Lord, even with the path obvious and the danger closing in. And the moment the word came, nothing in Makkah could hold him another hour. We are quick to move on our own desires and slow to ask whether Allah has opened the door, when the order of his ﷺ life was the reverse: seek the permission first, then act with everything you have. Our hijrah is no longer a journey across the desert. It is the daily migration from sin toward obedience, and it waits, as his did, on a heart willing to be told when to go.

He insisted on paying for his own camel, a detail small enough to slip past you and large enough to reshape how you worship. Offered the mount of his life as a gift, he would not take the reward of the hijrah at a discount; he wanted it whole, bought with his own wealth, shared with no one. Ask how much of your own good is borrowed, done at someone else's cost or for someone else's eyes, and how much you would still want if the whole reward had to be yours alone, seen only by Allah.

And then there is the sentence at the heart of it all, whispered in the dark with sandals creaking overhead and a hundred camels riding on a single downward glance. Abu Bakr did everything a careful man could do: months of feed for two camels, a son carrying intelligence, a freed servant erasing the tracks, a trusted guide and a back road chosen to deceive the hunt. He planned like a man who knew that planning is part of trust, not its opposite. And when the plan reached its last line, the last line was not a plan at all. What do you think of two, when Allah is the third of them? This is the balance the hijrah teaches and most of us never quite learn: to tie the camel and trust the Lord, to do every lawful thing in our power and then to rest, genuinely rest, in the knowledge that the outcome was never in our hands. The men hunting him held the swords, the bounty, the trackers, the mountain itself. They had every visible advantage, and they walked home empty. They plan, and Allah plans, and Allah is the best of planners.

So take one thing from this night down into your ordinary days. Wait on Allah's permission before you move, and when it comes, move with your whole heart. Do one good thing whose reward is yours alone and whose cost no one else will ever carry.
