And the next time you find yourself in a narrow place, with the walls close and the way out invisible, do everything you honestly can, and then say the sentence that held this entire religion steady when it was two men wide in a crack of rock: Allah is the third of them. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, who left the most beloved of lands for His sake, be with us in our narrow places as He was with them in the cave, and write for each of us a hijrah from sin to obedience that He is pleased to receive.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 27: the Hijrah (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (8:30, 36:9, 3:75), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 28 of 104

Lessons from the Hijrah

What the road taught, and the city that waited

Yesterday the chase ended in the strangest of victories. Suraqa ibn Malik, who had ridden out to collect a hundred camels for the heads of the two fugitives, rode home instead with a promise written on a scrap of leather: that one day he would wear the two bracelets of the emperor of Persia. He could barely stammer the name back. Years later, after an empire had fallen, he wore them. That is where day 27 left the road, and the road is not finished teaching.

Today we walk the last stretch of it with the Prophet ﷺ and Abu Bakr, radiyallahu anhu, and then Dr. Yasir Qadhi does something the seerah does only at its hinges: he stops the story and steps back. Before the palms of his new city part to receive him, the Sheikh asks us to gather up what these days have been quietly handing us, and then to answer the question hiding inside the whole journey. Out of every city on the earth, out of every place the dawah had already reached, why this one? Why Madinah? By the end of today you will know her before he ﷺ ever sets foot inside her: renamed by his own mouth, guarded by angels, soaked in his du'a, and prepared by Allah generations before anyone thought to look.

Milk from a dry goat

Somewhere on that road, in open desert about an hour and a half by car from Madinah today (the place still carries her name, and a highway sign still marks the exit), an elderly bedouin woman sat in a worn tent waiting for her husband to come back from searching for food. She lived the hardest version of the bedouin life, and there was nothing in her tent worth the taking, so when she heard travelers outside asking leave to enter, she let them in without a second thought. She was old, she was poor, and she had no reason to be afraid. She did not know the two men. One was Abu Bakr. The other was the most hunted man in all of Arabia ﷺ.

They asked to buy food, and the Sheikh lingers on that one word, buy. Two desperate travelers with a city's bounty riding behind them, and they would not ask for it free, though desert custom would have excused them completely. This was the manners of the Prophet ﷺ and of Abu Bakr even in flight. She apologized: she had nothing at all, because her husband had gone out for exactly that reason. Then he ﷺ noticed the goat in the corner. An old goat, long past milk and long past bearing young, not even sent out to graze; an animal kept against the day it would be slaughtered for meat, and nothing more. He asked her leave to milk it. She nearly laughed: that day is long gone, there is no milk in her, but do as you like. So he ﷺ mentioned the name of Allah, made du'a, and passed his hand beneath the udder, and the udder filled, right then and there. Abu Bakr milked it until the vessel brimmed over. They drank, she was given her share, and they left the rest for her and her husband.

When the husband came home and found milk waiting, he could not take it in; they had gone days without any. From this goat? So she told him the story, and here she gave history something precious. She described the stranger in detail, handsome, long haired, neither tall nor short, and her account is one of the very few full physical portraits of the Prophet ﷺ that has reached us, because most of the companions never described him in that way, and this came from a woman who had hosted him for an hour. Her husband knew at once: those are the two the Quraysh are hunting, and one of them, you know, claims to be a prophet. She answered from what her own two eyes had just watched happen: that is no claim. He is a prophet. Both of them accepted Islam, and the whole account reaches us in her own voice.

She was not the only one. The reports mention another two or three souls on that road who met him ﷺ and accepted Islam, some of whom did not even leave us their names. Sit with that, the Sheikh says, because it is the quiet miracle of the journey. A man is fleeing for his life with an entire city's wealth set on his capture, and he keeps pausing to invite strangers to Allah. By the end we can count at least four or five who entered Islam on the road of the hijrah itself. No time, no place, and no danger was ever heavy enough to switch off the dawah.

One more meeting on that road called for quick wits. In deep desert two parties of travelers will drift toward one another simply to trade news, and so a caravan veered across to greet the two riders, and someone in it recognized Abu Bakr from an old trading expedition. These were not Quraysh; they had heard nothing of any bounty, nothing of two men fleeing Makkah. They greeted Abu Bakr, traded their news, and then came the question: who is this man with you? Abu Bakr answered without missing a beat: he is my guide, guiding me to the path. The caravan heard a hired guide for the desert road. Abu Bakr meant his guide to Jannah. The scholars call this tawriyah, a double meaning that Islam permits for a reason, what English would call a white lie, except that it is not a lie at all and contains not one false word. And a small detail hides inside the exchange, the Sheikh notes, for by this point their actual hired guide had finished his task and turned back; from here the two of them already knew the way.

Tie your camel, then let go

Now step back and look at the architecture of this whole escape, the Sheikh says, because this is where the lessons begin. Recall how meticulously the hijrah was prepared: two camels force fed for months until their humps stood high and full, a face wrapped so no feature showed, a room emptied before a word of the plan was spoken, the young Ali left in his ﷺ own bed so any eye at the window would find a sleeper tossing and turning, tracks covered, a son carrying the morning's news, food slipped out in secret, a hired guide the Quraysh would never think to suspect. Every detail was locked down, and then locked down again.

And the man at the very center of all that planning, the one human being with more right than anyone alive to lean on Allah and simply do nothing, took every precaution there was, and then let go of all of it completely. Out in the open desert he ﷺ rides calm, untroubled, ungrieved, reciting Qur'an, not so much as glancing left or right. It is Abu Bakr who cannot hold still: now spurring ahead in case danger comes from the front, now dropping behind in case it comes from the rear, his heart pounding, and never once for his own sake. When Suraqa came hunting them, it was Suraqa and Abu Bakr who spotted each other from far off. The hunted man ﷺ never turned his head.

Sheikh Yasir is exact about what this means, because believers get it wrong in both directions. Hiding in the cave was not a failure of tawakkul; it was tawakkul. This is the heart of our religion: you tie your camel, and then you put your trust in Allah. You do everything you possibly can, down to the decoy lying in the bed, because Islam does not ask you to behave like a fool, and then you attach your heart not to the plan but to the One the plan was always for. They did not stand at the mouth of the cave daring Makkah to do its worst. They hid. And when Abu Bakr's heart raced even in the dark (if even one of them looks down at his feet, he will see us), the Prophet ﷺ calmed him, and Allah handled the rest: a dove, the reports say, and a spider's web across the opening, if those reports are authentic, and the Sheikh weighs them honestly rather than flattening them, for there is nothing wrong with citing them. What is beyond any doubt is that men stood close enough overhead to be heard, and Allah simply did not let them look down. Once you have done your part, Allah does His.

The second of two

Years later, that cave received its own ayah, and the timing is half the lesson. The verse came down in one of the very last surahs to be revealed, when the Muslims stood at the absolute peak of their power, after the conquest of Makkah itself. And at that summit, with armies at their command, Allah told them plainly: if you will not help His Messenger ﷺ, it changes nothing at all, for Allah already helped him, back when an entire city was out to kill him and the full census of Islam on that road was two people. The second of two.

If you do not aid him [i.e., the Prophet (ﷺ)] - Allah has already aided him when those who disbelieved had driven him out [of Makkah] as one of two, when they were in the cave and he [i.e., Muhammad (ﷺ)] said to his companion, "Do not grieve; indeed Allah is with us." And Allah sent down His tranquility upon him and supported him with soldiers [i.e., angels] you did not see and made the word of those who disbelieved the lowest, while the word of Allah - that is the highest. And Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise.Qur'an 9:40

Inside the cave he ﷺ had said five words to his friend: do not grieve, Allah is with us. The ayah records what Allah did next. He sent down His sakinah, His tranquility, upon him, so that the hunted man was the calm one, his heart at rest while the searchers stood overhead, and He aided him with soldiers no one could see, the host that many scholars take to include the humble dove and spider. And to the thousands reading this ayah at the very height of their victory, the message lands without a soft edge: none of you were able to help him then, and I did not need you. I sent an army you could not see.

Before moving on, the Sheikh pauses on a treasure buried in the wording: he said to his companion. Abu Bakr is the only human being whose companionship of the Prophet ﷺ Allah Himself affirmed directly in the Qur'an. Every other companion's rank is real, but it is inferred, drawn out indirectly; Abu Bakr's is spelled out in revelation, with no wishy-washiness in it at all. And by the unanimous agreement of every single group that has ever claimed Islam, the man in that cave was Abu Bakr and no one else. So whoever denies that Abu Bakr was the companion of the Prophet ﷺ, the Sheikh says, is not quarreling with history. He is quarreling with the Qur'an.

They call it Yathrib, it is Madinah

Before the Prophet ﷺ enters his city, learn her name, and notice who in the Qur'an refuses to use it. Her original name was Yathrib, an ancient town ringed with old volcanic rock and sitting on a hidden gift of water, a current running beneath the ground, fed from the mountains around her, enough to raise the date groves that made her famous across Arabia. There were only two places in the whole peninsula with palms like hers. And the Prophet ﷺ was shown his new home by exactly those trees. Two years before the hijrah, in the tenth or eleventh year of the dawah, he ﷺ had told the Muslims of a dream: I saw myself emigrating to a land of date palms. He guessed at two famous date towns far across the peninsula in Yemen, and it was neither. He could not place the city until the sign arrived, when the people of Yathrib embraced Islam and invited him to come. Then he knew. The city in the dream was theirs.

He also told them: I have been commanded to migrate to a city that devours all other cities; they call it Yathrib, and it is Madinah. From this hadith the scholars draw a real ruling, the Sheikh explains. They call it Yathrib, but its name now is Madinah, so Islamically the old name is retired, and one narration teaches that whoever says Yathrib should say al-Madinah. And the Qur'an itself underlines the point, for it places the name Yathrib only ever on one set of tongues. When the hypocrites wanted to stoke fear among the believers and scatter them, it was they who cried out:

And when a faction of them said, "O people of Yathrib, there is no stability for you [here], so return [home]." And a party of them asked permission of the Prophet, saying, "Indeed, our houses are exposed [i.e., unprotected]," while they were not exposed. They did not intend except to flee.Qur'an 33:13

So the hypocrites say Yathrib, while Allah Himself calls her al-Madinah, and so does His Messenger ﷺ. That is no accident of vocabulary. It is the city's biography told in two names. The old name, the scholars say, grows from a root meaning blame, or another meaning evil and corruption, and the Prophet ﷺ never let an ugly name stand, not on a person and not on his city. He disliked bad names and changed them everywhere he met them: a woman embraced Islam carrying a name that meant sour, and he renamed her sweet on the spot. A name that carried blame and corruption could not stay on the city of the Prophet ﷺ. So he called her al-Madinah, the City, capital C, as if there were no other, and a pair of sister names besides, both meaning the pure and the source of purity, the exact opposite of all the old name had carried. The Arabs heaped names on whatever they held grand, and no place collected more than she did; the historians of Madinah, as-Samhudi among them, gathered well over a hundred of her names, and one early scholar wrote that no city known to Islam has more. Yet of them all, he ﷺ used only two or three.

A city soaked in du'a

He ﷺ asked Allah for the love of her, in words like these: O Allah, cause us to love Madinah as we love Makkah, or even more. And the du'a took visible root in him: a companion describes how, returning from an expedition, the moment the silhouette of Madinah rose in the distance he ﷺ would grow excited and urge his mount on. Even her landscape was folded into the love; of Mount Uhud he said this is a mountain that loves us and we love it, and counted it among the mountains of Jannah. He prayed even over her economy: O Allah, bless us in our sa' and our mudd, the very scoops and measures of the marketplace, the hadith meaning that food bought in Madinah stretches further, the same measure feeding more mouths.

Then he asked higher still: O Allah, place in Madinah double the blessing You placed in Makkah. That du'a kept the scholars debating, the Sheikh says. Does it mean Madinah is more blessed than Makkah? Imam Malik, himself a son of Madinah and perhaps a little partial to his own city, held her to be the holiest of lands. Others held that the two cannot be ranked against each other, each blessed in its own way. Makkah, in the majority view, was blessed from the day the heavens and the earth were created; she holds the first masjid ever raised for the worship of Allah on this earth, and she carries the far greater reward in prayer, a hundred thousand to Madinah's thousand. Madinah's blessing began the day her Prophet ﷺ moved into her. Two cities, two glories, and the believer is not asked to choose between them.

Then come the protections, and these are not faint whispers from weak books; narration after narration sits in Bukhari and Muslim. Iman itself, he ﷺ said, returns to Madinah the way a desert animal darts back to its burrow the instant it senses a threat. When faith is hunted everywhere else, Madinah is where it runs home; she will be the bastion of Islam. The Dajjal himself will not be permitted to enter her: he will come meaning to destroy her, and two great angels will meet him at her approaches and drive him out, which is why the early teaching says that if you hear of him, head for Madinah. And no plague will ever enter her. Fourteen centuries have tested that sentence: famines have come, for he ﷺ made du'a only against the plague, but the great plagues that swept the world passed her by, the bubonic plague and the Spanish influenza of 1918 that killed perhaps a third of mankind, while pilgrims kept arriving from every infected corner of the earth, and the city stayed untouched. The protection has teeth, too: no one plots against Madinah, he ﷺ warned, except that Allah dissolves him the way salt dissolves in water.

She blesses both halves of a believer's story, the living and the dying. No one leaves her, no longer wanting to live in her, except that Allah replaces him with someone better. And she is a hard land, fierce in her heat and lean in her seasons, and that is exactly the point: whoever bears her difficulties patiently, he ﷺ promised to intercede for him on the Day of Judgment, as he promised for whoever is able to die in her. Umar ibn al-Khattab, radiyallahu anhu, built an impossible du'a on that promise: O Allah, grant me martyrdom in Your cause, and death in the city of Your Messenger ﷺ. Those around him wondered how the two halves could ever meet, for martyrs fall at the far borders of the empire, not at home in their beds. Allah accepted both at once: Umar died a shahid, in Madinah. And her very soil is honored, for al-Baqi is the most blessed graveyard in the world, where over ten thousand companions lie, nine of the Prophet's ﷺ wives (all but Khadijah), his son Ibrahim, and early giants of scholarship. One night a wife of his ﷺ woke to find him gone, and learned on his return that Jibril had told him to go and pray for the people of al-Baqi; so he had left his bed in the dark to stand among his buried companions and make du'a for them. The city is blessed down to her graves.

Only two harams

Madinah is not only blessed; she is a haram, and the Sheikh is careful with the word, because here people get confused. A haram, in the legal sense, is a sanctuary: a stretch of land inside which things that are halal everywhere else become haram, which is precisely why it shares its root with the word for forbidden. Weapons are not carried there except for genuine necessity, like the armed guards who protect the sanctuaries today. Armies do not march in; even the Prophet ﷺ said Allah permitted him to enter Makkah with an army for only a part of a single day, and for no one after him. Blood is not shed. The natural greenery is untouchable, down to a leaf on a tree and the grass underfoot, and you do not even pick up a lost item from its ground, but leave it where it lies. Everything inside is under the protection of Allah.

That is what Makkah had been since the time of Ibrahim, and the Qur'an itself declares it:

In it are clear signs [such as] the standing place of Abraham. And whoever enters it [i.e., the Haram] shall be safe. And [due] to Allah from the people is a pilgrimage to the House - for whoever is able to find thereto a way. But whoever disbelieves [i.e., refuses] - then indeed, Allah is free from need of the worlds.Qur'an 3:97

Even the days of jahiliyyah honored that safety; a man would see the murderer of his own father making tawaf around the Ka'bah and not raise a hand against him, because Makkah was a haram and the Arabs knew it. The ayah goes further still: in the sanctuary of Allah, merely intending wrongdoing, with no need to succeed, invites a painful punishment.

Indeed, those who have disbelieved and avert [people] from the way of Allah and [from] al-Masjid al-Haram, which We made for the people - equal are the resident therein and one from outside - and [also] whoever intends [a deed] therein of deviation [in religion] by wrongdoing - We will make him taste of a painful punishment.Qur'an 22:25

And into this exact category the Prophet ﷺ lifted his new city, making du'a in words like these: O Allah, Ibrahim was Your servant and Your khalil, and he declared Makkah a sanctuary; I am Your servant and Your Messenger, and I declare Madinah a sanctuary, between her two lava plains. Madinah became the second haram of this religion.

Now listen carefully, the Sheikh says, because this next sentence upsets people, and he asks them not to be angry but to hear it out. There are exactly two harams in our religion: Makkah and Madinah. Not three. Jerusalem, the land of al-Masjid al-Aqsa, is beloved and blessed beyond any doubt, a place we should visit and pray in, where the most authentic narration counts one prayer as two hundred and fifty. But blessed and haram are two different words. No scholar of Islam, the Sheikh says, none of them, ever counted Jerusalem a haram in the legal sense. Her animals may be hunted and her trees may be cut, and the rulings of the sanctuary simply do not apply there; the name people use for her precinct is popular vernacular, not the language of fiqh. Hold the rule exactly the way the episode hands it to you: every haram is blessed, but not every blessed place is a haram, and Jerusalem is the clearest proof of it.

Why Madinah?

Step back from the blessings now and ask the planner's question, the way the Sheikh does: there were already Muslims in Abyssinia under a king who had embraced the faith, so why not send the Prophet ﷺ to him? Why this city? The honest answer begins with adab: these are the wisdoms the scholars have traced, and the full ledger of reasons rests with Allah alone. But look at what He had prepared. Madinah sits at a thinking distance from Makkah, seven or eight days by caravan and three and a half for a hard rider, near enough to keep the dawah anchored to the one city that had to be won, yet too far for an army to surprise, and dead center in the Hijaz, which is dead center in Arabia. And her geology reads like fortification poured out in lava ages before there were any Muslims to shelter behind it: volcanic plains east and west that no army can march and no camel train can cross, dense palm groves choking the southern approach toward Makkah, and a single open gap a few miles wide. Hold that fact, the Sheikh says, because when the Battle of the Trench comes, the one ditch the Prophet ﷺ digs will run exactly across that gap. You cannot moat a whole city in a week, but you can moat a few miles, and Allah had left only a few miles open. Add her people, who had never been conquered and so had never had their fighting spirit broken, and the strange gift of the wars of Bu'ath, a civil war dragging on for a generation or more that, just before the hijrah, accomplished what no strategist could: it cleared away the old, power hungry chieftains and left young blood in charge, open minded, sick to death of funerals, ready to welcome a clean leader from outside the bloodstained tribes. Nobody in Makkah was watching the wars of Bu'ath. But Allah has a plan, and while the dots are being drawn no one can read them; only when everything is finished do you connect them and stand amazed.

And the most intimate reason of all is blood. Of all the tribes of Arabia, the Prophet ﷺ had a direct blood tie to this one city, and the story of how is a small saga by itself. His great-grandfather Hashim, the great trader of Quraysh, used to pass through Yathrib on the road to Syria, and there he met Salma, a striking, strong willed businesswoman, daughter of a chieftain, who married and divorced men of her own choosing. He proposed; she set her terms, that she would keep her city and her trade and any child of theirs would stay with her, and Hashim agreed. Their time together was short, for he died on a later journey far from home, in Gaza. Salma was carrying his son and told the Quraysh nothing. The boy was born with a white streak in his hair, so they called him Shaybah, and he grew up a child of Yathrib while his father's people knew nothing of him, until a traveler noticed unmistakable Quraysh features on a Yathrib boy and asked whose son he was. Hashim, the boy answered proudly. The news raced to Makkah, and Hashim's brother al-Muttalib rode quietly north, found the boy of twelve or thirteen, talked him onto a camel with promises of his father's rank, and sped away without facing Salma. As they entered Makkah, people asked: who is this boy, a slave you bought? Al-Muttalib, not wanting the news to spread, kept silent, and so the boy was nicknamed Abd al-Muttalib, the slave of al-Muttalib. Sit with what that means, the Sheikh says: Abd al-Muttalib, the grandfather of the Prophet ﷺ, was raised in the very streets his grandson would one day call home. Is this not Allah's plan working through history?

So when Yathrib chose him ﷺ, she was not choosing a stranger. Through Salma he was a second cousin of the Khazraj, and the Arabs kept their lineage by heart, twenty generations deep; kinship through a mother weighed lighter in that culture than through a father, but blood is blood, and they knew exactly whose blood he was. He ﷺ had even seen those streets once, as a child of five, brought by his mother Aminah to visit their Yathrib kin (it was on the road home that she died), which is why, when he finally entered the city, it was no coincidence that he lodged with Abu Ayyub, his closest relative among the Ansar. Wider still, the Aws and Khazraj were Qahtani Arabs, the Yemeni line he ﷺ praised when he said that iman is Yemeni and wisdom is Yemeni, while the tribes ringing Quraysh were all Adnani; so in the new ummah the two ancient halves of the Arab family fused, and no one could ever again rally the peninsula against the Muslims on tribal blood, a sign that this religion had come to bury ethnic pride altogether. And for two centuries these tribes had lived beside the Jewish tribes of Yathrib, who spoke of prophets and a book and a law and held it over their neighbors as a boast. The boast turned over in Allah's hand: when the awaited prophet came, those who had been waiting for him refused him, and those who had only overheard the waiting recognized the truth and ran to it. Why Madinah? Because long before anyone thought to ask, Allah had already answered.

What this day asks of our faith

Here, on the last stretch of road before the gates, with the city in view but not yet entered, the journey hands over its parting gifts, and they are not relics to admire; they are questions put to us. Tie your camel, and then let go. The hijrah was the most carefully planned escape in the whole seerah, two camels fattened, a wrapped face, a decoy in the bed, tracks erased, and the heart at the center of all that planning was attached to none of it. Plan as though everything depends on the plan; trust as though none of it does. And notice that hiding can itself be tawakkul: they did not stand at the cave's mouth and dare the Quraysh to come; they hid, and Allah turned the searchers' eyes away. Precaution never betrays trust. It is the camel's rope, held lightly, in a hand that knows Who tied the knot that matters.

Then watch how dawah keeps no office hours. With a bounty on his head ﷺ and his life riding on speed and silence, he still paused for a widow's tent and for strangers on the open road, and four or five souls entered Islam during the escape itself. If he could invite while hunted, you can invite while busy, and when the inviting calls for cleverness, let it be clever without ever being false. He is my guide, guiding me to the path: Abu Bakr's tawriyah turned a dangerous question aside without one untrue word in it. A believer's tongue may carry two meanings at once, but it does not lie. And love the city he loved: he ﷺ asked Allah to make Madinah as beloved to him as Makkah, hurried his mount at the first sight of her, and promised his intercession to whoever bears her hardships patiently. If you are ever her guest, stand in her hard and holy streets like a guest of that love.

But the deepest gift of the day is the one buried under the whole question of why Madinah, because it is also the answer to your own life. A widowed businesswoman's terms in a marketplace, a streak of white in a kidnapped boy's hair, a civil war no one in Makkah was watching, a field of lava cooling for ages: dots nobody could read while they were being drawn, until Allah connected them into a refuge for His Messenger ﷺ, complete down to the few open miles a single trench would one day close. Somewhere in your own story the dots are being drawn exactly the same way, and you cannot read them yet, and you are not meant to. So leave today the way the two travelers left every stop on that road: with the work done and the heart handed over. O Allah, You were with them in the cave, so be with us in every narrow place. Cause us to love Madinah as we love Makkah, or even more, write for us a prayer in the masjid of Your Messenger ﷺ, guard our iman the way You guarded her from plague and from plotters, and gather us under his ﷺ intercession on the Day the dots are all at last connected. Ameen.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 28: lessons from the Hijrah and the blessings of Madinah (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (9:40, 33:13, 3:97, 22:25), verified via quran.ai.

The Seerah · 29 of 104

Introducing the Madani phase

A city prepared centuries in advance

The escape from Makkah is complete, and somewhere on the long northern road two riders are coming toward a new life. The story is almost at the gates of the city. And here, with the arrival only minutes away, Dr. Yasir Qadhi stops the narrative cold and refuses to take another step forward until we understand the ground we are about to stand on.

His reason is simple, and he says it plainly. The Madani phase is three times the length of the Makkan one, and almost none of it can be understood without a map: who already lives in this oasis, who holds the money and the fortresses, who owes whom an old debt of blood, and how all of that came to be in the middle of an empty desert. So this is an orientation day. No single dramatic scene, no battle, no miracle, until the very end. Just two peoples, five tribes, one oasis, and the slow revelation that Allah had been furnishing this city to receive His Prophet ﷺ for three hundred years before he ﷺ ever needed it. Learn the map well. Everything that comes after is drawn on it.

Two peoples and a ground rule

Yathrib on the eve of the hijrah held two peoples. There were three Jewish tribes, Banu Qaynuqa, Banu Nadir, and Banu Qurayza, and these are names the Sheikh tells us to memorize now, because they will return again and again across the coming years. And there were two Arab tribes, the Aws and the Khazraj. Nearly everything that happens over the next decade of the seerah, the treaties and the tensions, the betrayals and the mercies, moves along the lines drawn between these five names.

But before any of the history, Dr. Yasir Qadhi lays down a ground rule, and it is one of the most important things he says all day. He knows exactly where this subject leads. How the Prophet ﷺ dealt with the three Jewish tribes is among the most politicized questions in the entire seerah, the basis of the charge that the religion itself is anti-Semitic. It is the kind of question that surfaces every time a Muslim gives dawah to a non-Muslim, alongside the question of his marriages and the age of Aisha. And so it has to be handled, the Sheikh insists, without being apologetic. He pauses to define the word: an apologist is someone who waters Islam down, who shaves off its edges until its critics find it palatable. That is not the method here. The honest method has exactly two steps. First, ask whether a report is actually true; if it is a lie about the religion, say so. And if it is true, do not flinch from it, do not soften it, but explain its wisdom and stand by it, whether or not it sits comfortably with the politics of the age. Did he ﷺ do it? If he did, what was the wisdom in it? That is the whole methodology, and the Sheikh asks us to carry it through every hard chapter still to come.

Then he adds one honesty about the sources themselves. Every detail we possess about these three tribes comes from the Islamic tradition alone; no outside chronicle yet discovered so much as names them. Critics treat this as fatal: the sources are biased, they say, written by people with an agenda to defend the Prophet ﷺ at any cost, so the full truth can never be known and anyone who opposed him must have been painted unfairly. The Sheikh has read where that logic ends. There are articles, he says, that recast Abdullah ibn Ubayy, the man the seerah knows as the very leader of the hypocrites, into a noble gentleman, the true man of honor in Madinah, on no reasoning whatsoever except that whoever opposed the Prophet ﷺ must secretly have been the good one. They flip the entire narrative inside out. Notice that method now, he warns, because you will recognize it every single time the seerah is attacked.

The temple and the two destructions

Where did Jewish tribes come from, deep in the heart of the Arabian desert? The trail runs all the way back to Jerusalem.

There, once, a man was a king and a prophet at the same moment, Sulayman, peace be upon him, and the Jews in his day were a people of Jerusalem. He raised the great temple, the haykal, and nothing on earth was like it, because his workforce was not of this earth. Allah had subjected the jinn to him, and the Qur'an records the kind of labor they did:

And [also] the devils [of jinn] - every builder and diverQur'an 38:37

Every builder among them raised the structure, and every diver brought treasures and pearls up from the depths of the sea to adorn it. It was reputed to be one of the wonders of the ancient world, a feat the likes of which men had never seen, and the reason, the Sheikh reminds us, was simply that Allah had given Sulayman command of the jinn to build it. It stood solid and untouched for four or five centuries.

And the Qur'an had already foretold what would come of this people. Read the opening page of Surah al-Isra, the Sheikh says, and you find mention of two corruptions and two punishments sent upon the Children of Israel:

And We conveyed to the Children of Israel in the Scripture that, "You will surely cause corruption on the earth twice, and you will surely reach [a degree of] great haughtiness."Qur'an 17:4

The majority of the scholars of tafsir, he tells us, have always read these as two corruptions and two punishments long since fallen, sealed in the ancient past. He then passes along a footnote, careful to say he does not adopt it: some modern readers take these verses as prophecy of something still to come, a reading that would change the entire dynamic of the surah. He leaves that tangent with Allah and returns to the majority view, on which the two punishments are history.

The first blow fell in 587 BC. Nebuchadnezzar, the tyrant the Arabic sources call Bukhtanassar, surrounded Jerusalem and, for the first time in that people's history, destroyed the original temple of Sulayman. This was the first diaspora, the first scattering of a people who would come to call themselves the wandering ones, for from that day they would not hold a unified land again. A later king, in time, took pity and rebuilt the temple, but it was his structure now, a rich man's imitation of a prophet's original. And Rome tore that one down too. The emperor Titus destroyed the second temple in 70 CE, after the time of Isa, peace be upon him, and the people scattered a second time. Then, in 132 CE, a group of Jews rose in armed revolt against an emperor who demanded an act of sacrilege from them, the slaughter of a forbidden animal to an idol, and here the Sheikh interrupts himself to say plainly that in this revolt our sympathies are with them. The emperor Hadrian answered with massacre, surrounding the city and killing hundreds of thousands, a third great scattering. It is from these Roman catastrophes, in the Sheikh's weighing the most plausible account, that families fleeing helter-skelter wandered south into the Arabian Peninsula.

The refugees who planted Yathrib

Now Dr. Yasir Qadhi lays the theories side by side and weighs each one in the open.

The first account he reports comes from early Islamic sources: that Musa, peace be upon him, himself sent a small colony of his followers to the land of the Hijaz, knowing the final Prophet ﷺ would one day arise there, so that a body of believers would be waiting to welcome him. It is a tender thought. But the Sheikh finds it strained, with an Allahu a'lam. Why would a prophet send his own followers away from him, when the people of his time should remain with him, and the one he foretold would not come for two or three thousand years? It seems too early. He does not forbid the theory; he simply does not find it sound.

Far more reasonable, and this is the account he leans toward, is the second: that the refugees of Rome's destructions wandered due south from Jerusalem, with no particular destination, until the desert opened before them into a fertile, empty ground thick with date palms and without inhabitants. They stopped, and they settled, and on this reading the Jews did not merely come to live in Yathrib. They founded it. One group halted nearby at Khaybar, and the largest wave kept walking south all the way to Yemen, which became home to the greatest number of Jews in all of Arabia. It is no small detail, for Yemen was the one corner of the peninsula that held both Jews and Christians together, and it is exactly why, years later, when the Prophet ﷺ sent Mu'adh ibn Jabal there, he told him he was going to a land of the People of the Book.

A third account simply runs the river backward: that the Jews of Yathrib came up out of Yemen rather than down from Jerusalem. And here the Sheikh draws the conclusion that survives no matter which theory is right. Whether they came down from Jerusalem and learned their relatives had gone on to Yemen, or whether they were themselves the descendants of those Yemeni Jews, every single version ties Yathrib's Jews to Yemen. That link will matter in a moment.

Then he offers a tangent from modern researchers. Their clue is a strange one: the Jews of Madinah appear to have been not of mainstream rabbinic Judaism at all, but of an ancient scripture-first branch called the Karaites, which rejected the authority of the rabbis. The significance is a matter of dates: rabbinic Judaism, which every Jewish group of our times descends from, became dominant only around 400 or 500 CE. So a Karaite community in Yathrib points to an arrival from before that, which fits precisely with refugees fleeing the swords of Titus and Hadrian, and adds quiet weight to the second theory.

And then Dr. Yasir Qadhi offers a theory entirely his own, and he prefaces it with a humility worth pausing on: Allahu a'lam, he says, and may Allah forgive me for daring to have an opinion. Here is the puzzle that prompts it. Jews do not divide themselves into tribes; that is an Arab habit, not a Jewish one. The twelve tribes of Israel were ancient history by this point, long since merged into one people. So why are the Jews of Yathrib three separate tribes, Qaynuqa and Nadir and Qurayza, distinct enough that they would later fight on opposite sides of an Arab civil war? If they all came from one place and settled together, it makes no sense for them to fracture into three. His suggestion is that there is an element of truth in more than one of the stories at once: that these three tribes represent three different waves of migration, arriving across three different windows of time, each new group too late and too estranged to be folded into the one before it. Take it or leave it, he says; he knows of no one who has proposed it, and the classical sources, even Ibn Ishaq, never thought to ask the question, never mentioning at all where these Jews came from.

What cannot be taken or left, though, is the conclusion the Sheikh draws firmly against a modern claim. Some recent non-Muslim writers deny that these were real Jews at all, calling them instead Arab converts to Judaism, perhaps, he suggests, out of embarrassment over what later befell them, a wish to make the affair not about Jews. The Sheikh calls the theory baseless, for a single clean reason. Every Arab tribe on the peninsula traces its lineage either to Adnan or to Qahtan, without exception; the Arabs preserved their genealogies above almost all else. These three tribes trace to neither. They appear out of nowhere, and names like Qaynuqa and Qurayza were never Arab names. They were Jews. Real Jews, who would in time take on the dress and the tongue and the manners of the Arabs around them, but Jews in faith and in lineage all the same.

The flood that sent two tribes north

So much for the first people. Where, then, did the Arabs of Yathrib come from, the Aws and the Khazraj? Their trail also ends in Yemen, and unlike the Jews, their lineage is fully mapped, because the Arabs kept their genealogies. They were descendants of Qahtan. And their story the Qur'an tells itself.

In the south of Arabia stood Ma'rib, the city of the people of Saba, mentioned by name in its own surah. And Allah records of them a feat the whole world remembers: they were the first human beings ever to build a dam. Part of it still stands outside the city to this day, a wonder of ancient engineering that blocked the water and sent rivers running through the land. That dam turned desert into a sign, and Allah describes the blessing He poured over them:

There was for [the tribe of] Saba' in their dwelling place a sign: two [fields of] gardens on the right and on the left. [They were told], "Eat from the provisions of your Lord and be grateful to Him. A good land [have you], and a forgiving Lord."Qur'an 34:15

Gardens to the right and gardens to the left, rivers flowing between them, a good land and a forgiving Lord. Allah blessed them as He had blessed almost no people of their time. And they were not grateful. So the blessing was withdrawn in the manner the next verse names:

But they turned away [refusing], so We sent upon them the flood of the dam, and We replaced their two [fields of] gardens with gardens of bitter fruit, tamarisks and something of sparse lote trees.Qur'an 34:16

The flood of al-Arim was the bursting of their own dam against them. Most likely around 300 CE, the Sheikh judges, two and a half centuries before the Prophet ﷺ, the waters broke through. The gardens died into bitter scrub, villages were destroyed, and the tribes of Saba were scattered across Arabia in flight. Among the migrants who drifted north were two cousin tribes, descendants of a single ancestor, which is why they always travelled and settled together: the Aws and the Khazraj.

But why Yathrib, of all the oases between Yemen and the north? Here the Sheikh is honest about the limits of knowledge. No one was writing chronicles in 300 CE, he reminds us, so every answer must be held loosely. And yet his earlier thread now pays off. If every origin story ties Yathrib's Jews to Yemen, then the Aws and the Khazraj were not stumbling blindly onto a strange settlement. They were walking toward a place they had likely heard of, settled by a kind of people they already knew how to live beside, because the Arabs of Yemen had lived alongside Jews for generations. That, he suggests, is what drew two southern tribes to this particular northern oasis. Allahu a'lam, but it makes the migration make sense.

More than a coincidence

Now step back from the detail and look at what has quietly assembled itself on the map, because the Sheikh wants us to see a pattern, even as he is careful about how far he will press it.

All Arabs descend from two great fathers: Adnan in the center, whose line mixed with the descendants of Ismail and runs forward to Quraysh and to the Prophet ﷺ himself, and Qahtan, generally of the south. The Hijaz was solidly Adnani, every tribe of it, Quraysh and Thaqif and all the rest. And the single pocket of Qahtani Arabs in the whole region was Yathrib. The two halves of the Arab race, moreover, carried an old animosity stretching back centuries, and were still fighting each other within living memory of the Prophet's ﷺ own day.

So consider what is about to happen. The Adnani Messenger ﷺ is driven out by Adnani Makkah, and given refuge by the only Qahtanis in the land. The two halves of the Arab people fuse together at the very birth of Islam, and from that day no enemy can ever paint his ﷺ religion as the project of a single bloodline. The precursor to uniting all the Arabs under one banner was being laid before a single battle had been fought. The Sheikh is measured here, and his caution is itself a lesson: we do not speak Arabic on behalf of Allah, he says, we cannot claim His purposes with certainty. But this, he allows himself, does not look like coincidence. It looks a great deal more like intention.

And inside the oasis, across the generations, the two peoples had grown into each other. The Jews brought what the desert Arabs lacked: skill in agriculture, in weaving, in trade. The Arabs brought what the harsh land demands: skill in war, and the Arabic language. So the Jewish tribes Arabized. Their tongue became Arabic, fluent and native, though the seerah records that they kept their Hebrew too. They took on the dress of the Arabs, their culture, and their ways of dealing, while remaining in faith and in lineage unmistakably themselves.

Alliances, a long war, and a head count

The two Arab tribes did not arrive as equals, and there is a theory, held loosely as all of this must be, for why. It appears the Aws came first. They won the alliance and protection of the two largest Jewish tribes, Banu Qurayza and Banu Nadir, and with it the best of the land, the most luscious gardens and richest plots of the oasis, for in those days a man lived where his tribe lived. The Khazraj came later, allied instead with Banu Qaynuqa, and settled the lower, leaner ground; this, the Sheikh notes, is why the Aws were the wealthier of the two. And the Jews held more farmland than they could work themselves, so the newcomers worked it for a share of the harvest. Land, wealth, and loyalty were braided together from the very beginning.

Those braids hardened into battle lines. For more than a hundred years the Aws and the Khazraj bled each other in civil wars, the worst of them the war of Bu'ath, which fell just five years before the hijrah. And the Jewish tribes were pulled in behind their respective Arab allies: Banu Nadir and Banu Qurayza financing the Aws, Banu Qaynuqa financing the Khazraj, bankrolling opposite ends of someone else's war. Whether they actually took up arms, the Sheikh says, the sources do not tell us. But that they were divided against one another, lined up on hostile sides, that much is clear.

How many people, in the end, are we talking about? There was never a census, the Sheikh admits, but a careful estimate can be reconstructed backward from the battles to come. Tally the fighting men of the three Jewish tribes as the seerah reports them and you reach roughly two thousand, which at three dependents apiece gives something like six thousand Jewish souls in Yathrib when the Prophet ﷺ arrived. As for the Arabs, the Ansar mustered four to five thousand men at the conquest of Makkah, which puts the Arab population near twelve to fifteen thousand. So the whole oasis comes to roughly twenty-four thousand people, a substantial town for its day. And now hold the single asymmetry that explains so much of what is coming. The Arabs were nearly double the Jews in raw numbers. But the Jews held the power: the money, the best land, and the fortresses, for the Aws and the Khazraj built none. The three Jewish tribes did not even live in Madinah proper; they lived outside it, each behind its own walls. Numbers on one side, strength on the other. Remember that imbalance. It is the key to much of the Madani phase.

O Arabs, your king has arrived

The map is drawn. Now Dr. Yasir Qadhi rejoins the road, and the orientation gives way at last to a scene.

Word had outrun the riders. All of Yathrib knew the Prophet ﷺ was coming; it was only a matter of time now. And so every single morning the believers walked out of the city toward what is now Quba and stood there, staring down the southern road, waiting. And every morning, around ten or eleven o'clock, the sun climbed too high and the heat drove them home to sleep. On the day that finally mattered, they had done exactly this, waited through the morning and given up and gone home by the time the heat won, when in the far distance two figures at last shimmered out of the haze, the Prophet ﷺ and Abu Bakr. And not one of the Ansar was left on the road to see them. They had walked home minutes too soon.

The only eyes still on the horizon belonged to a Jewish laborer at the top of a date palm, high up among the fronds, pulling the harvest. The fever of expectation that had gripped the whole city had caught even him, for that is the way of any community when something long awaited is near: everyone feels it, everyone is swept along. And he could not hold the news in. He cried out, at the very top of his lungs, so the whole oasis could hear: O Arabs, your king has arrived.

The Sheikh stops us on the exact wording, because, he says, the entire Madani phase hides inside it. Your king. Not my king. And not a prophet at all, not even a ruler over him, but a king belonging to the Arabs, their affair to celebrate. The excitement had reached this man fully; the allegiance had not reached him at all. From the very first minute, the Jewish tribes filed the Prophet ﷺ under Arab business, something to shout about from the top of a tree, not something to climb down and follow. We have our affairs and they have theirs, that was the assumption, he will not interfere with us and we will not with him. Slowly, across the years ahead, that assumption would come undone. But on this first day it stands fully intact, announced from a palm tree for the whole city to hear.

And then the city emptied itself into the road. Al-Bara ibn Azib, radiyallahu anhu, whose memory of the day is preserved in Sahih Muslim, describes how the moment the cry went up the Ansar dressed themselves in their finest clothes and poured out in hordes, more than five hundred men, armed, like an honor guard come to escort their Prophet ﷺ in. The women climbed onto the rooftops to see. The children thronged through the crowd. It was a Monday in Rabi al-Awwal, the second of the month by one report and the twelfth by another, in the fourteenth year of the call, the year the ummah would later choose as the first year of the hijrah.

And here the Sheikh asks us to feel the very air change. This is not Makkah. There he ﷺ was persecuted, mocked, tortured, hemmed in. Here, for the first time, there is excitement, there is buzz, there is a freshness in the air, hundreds upon hundreds thronging simply to be near him ﷺ, and every one of them a believer, every one glad of his coming. A new tide is coming in. What the man at the top of the date palm announced as the arrival of a king was, in truth, something far greater than any kingdom: the seed of the first Islamic nation being lowered into the ground. And the story of that seed, its nurturing and its growth and its flourishing, is the whole story of the Madani seerah. It begins tomorrow.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to skip past a day like this one, to treat it as a dry preface of names and dates and theories to be endured before the real seerah resumes. That would be to miss what it is quietly teaching, which is some of the deepest theology in the whole series.

Look first at what the Sheikh has shown you across the centuries. A dam burst in Yemen around the year 300, and two ungrateful tribes were scattered north. Refugees fleeing the swords of Roman emperors, hundreds of years before that, wandered helter-skelter into an empty valley of date palms and made it a city. And by the time the Prophet ﷺ was driven out of Makkah with a price on his head, every piece was in place: a refuge waiting, an Arab people of the opposite half of the race ready to receive him so that no one could call his religion one bloodline's project, a city he could enter not as a fugitive but as a king. None of those people knew they were building it. The Sabaeans did not know their ingratitude was clearing a road. The exiles did not know whose city they were founding. Allah knew. He had been furnishing this refuge for three hundred years while Makkah was busy rejecting the one it was being furnished for. This is what qadar looks like when you can finally see it from above, and it should change how you read the long, slow, seemingly arbitrary arrangements of your own life. Nothing about your refuge, either, is unplanned.

Look next at the method the Sheikh modeled, because it is a discipline as much as the history is a lesson. He refused to be an apologist, and he refused to be a denier, and he held the harder middle: ask whether a thing is true, and if it is, explain its wisdom and stand by it without flinching. And he held his theories with an open hand, weighing this account against that one, offering his own only after begging Allah's forgiveness for daring to have an opinion at all, ending again and again with take it or leave it, and Allahu a'lam. That is how sacred history deserves to be handled, with rigor and humility at once, never watered down to please a critic and never inflated past the evidence. We live in an age that demands we either apologize for our religion or shout it without thinking; the Sheikh shows a third way, the way of people who actually love the truth.

And then there is the man at the top of the palm tree, who may be the quiet heart of the whole day. He saw the Prophet ﷺ before anyone. He was so moved that he shouted the news to an entire city at the top of his lungs. And he stayed in the tree. Your king, he called, not my king, not a prophet, not someone I must climb down and follow. He stood at the very edge of guidance, closer to it in that instant than almost anyone alive, thrilled by it, announcing it, and he did not take the step toward it. It is a frightening thing to sit with, because it is so possible. We can be moved by the truth, celebrate it, even tell others about it, and still keep it at the safe distance of someone else's affair. Excitement is not allegiance. The whole tragedy of the Madani phase, of those who would not believe, begins in that one word shouted down from the fronds.

So take three things from this map down into your ordinary days. Trust that the inconveniences and detours of your life are being arranged by the One who broke a dam in Yemen to seat His Prophet ﷺ in the right city; your refuge is being built before you know you will need it. Handle the truth, especially the hard parts of it, the way the Sheikh did, without apology and without arrogance, asking only what is true and what is its wisdom. And do not be the man in the palm tree. When guidance shimmers out of the haze in front of you, do not shout about it from a comfortable height and call it someone else's business. Climb down. Walk out onto the road with the five hundred. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, the one a whole city dressed itself in its finest to welcome; as He prepared Yathrib for him across three hundred years, let Him prepare our hearts to receive his story, and may He never leave us watching from the edge of guidance, but bring us down into the road with the Ansar, and gather us with him ﷺ in the abode where there is no parting.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 29: introduction to the Madani phase (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (38:37, 17:4, 34:15, 34:16), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 30 of 104

The Madani Era Begins

Allah's house before his own

Begin where the last lecture left off: standing at the edge of an oasis with a map of it in your head. Three peoples now share the place we will learn to call Madinah. The Ansar, who are the hosts. The muhajirun, arriving in waves out of the south. And the Yahud, the three Jewish tribes the previous episode introduced. Count them the way Dr. Yasir Qadhi counts them, because the arithmetic itself carries a lesson. Each of the three Jewish tribes fielded around seven hundred fighting men, roughly two thousand in all, and you reckoned a population in that world by taking a fighting man and multiplying by three for the women and children behind him. So call the Yahud some six thousand souls. The Ansar would one day march four thousand men to the conquest of Makkah, so multiply again and call them twelve thousand. For every one of the Yahud in the oasis, then, roughly two Arabs.

And the muhajirun? The smallest of the three by far. At Badr, when very nearly every emigrant alive marched out, they numbered exactly eighty two men, all but a handful of the whole community. With the women and children, perhaps three or four hundred souls in the entire city: the smallest group in Madinah, and the poorest, for they had walked away from everything they owned. Yet whenever the Qur'an names the two groups side by side, it is the muhajirun who come first. Allah counts differently than a census does: what a person abandoned for His sake outweighs how many stand beside him. Tuck that away, because it returns on the hardest day of the whole story. When the Prophet ﷺ passes, one group of the Ansar will feel it only natural that the majority should lead, outnumbering the emigrants by something like four to one, and through the eloquence Allah will place on the tongues of Abu Bakr and Umar the truth will come out, and the leadership will be handed to the few. The series will reach that day, perhaps a year from now, perhaps three. For now, simply notice that Allah had already decided whose few hundred outweighed whose thousands.

The command that emptied Makkah

For the believers still left behind in Makkah, hijrah was no longer one virtue among many. It had become an obligation laid on every single Muslim, down to the women and the children: leave Makkah, come to Madinah. The Prophet ﷺ himself had been very nearly the last of all to go, and even then he left Ali behind for one unfinished duty. There were no banks in that world, no safety deposit, no secure box; when you owned a precious thing and could not keep it close, you left it with a man you trusted. And here is the detail that should stop you: despite their hatred of him and his message, despite the very plot to kill him, the people of Makkah had kept their valuables in his ﷺ house. The city's safe was the man the city was trying to murder, and he would not leave a single trust unreturned. So Ali stayed to hand the deposits back, and the Prophet ﷺ went.

The command itself came the way Allah's commands always come, which is gradually. Some eight or nine verses across different surahs, each one firmer than the one before, for this is the sunnah of Allah: a law does not descend all at once and strike you down, it arrives by degrees, the first verse coaxing and the last one closing every door. The earliest held the door open with nothing but promise. In Surah al-Hajj, a surah whose verses some scholars place across the very hijrah itself, the invitation is pure, with no threat in it at all:

And those who emigrated for the cause of Allāh and then were killed or died - Allāh will surely provide for them a good provision. And indeed, it is Allāh who is the best of providers. He will surely cause them to enter an entrance with which they will be pleased, and indeed, Allāh is Knowing and Forbearing.Qur'an 22:58-59

Read it the way its first hearers did: emigrate, and even if the road itself kills you, the Best of Providers personally guarantees your provision and an entrance you will love. That is the door held open. Then, a year or two further on, the tone begins to harden. In Surah al-Ankabut, Allah turns to a certain kind of heart:

And of the people are some who say, "We believe in Allāh," but when one [of them] is harmed for [the cause of] Allāh, he considers the trial [i.e., harm] of the people as [if it were] the punishment of Allāh. But if victory comes from your Lord, they say, "Indeed, We were with you." Is not Allāh most knowing of what is within the breasts of the worlds [i.e., all creatures]?Qur'an 29:10

The word for the harm here is a delicate one, the Sheikh notes: it can mean real persecution, but it can mean the slightest irritation, a verbal rebuke and nothing more. And at that first scrape of discomfort, this person treats the pressure of men as though it were the punishment of Allah Himself. He has no priorities; a little squeeze and he collapses. Then the moment victory arrives he reappears to say, we were with you all along. He wants Makkah and iman both, the cake and the eating of it, and Allah ends the passage by reminding him that the contents of every breast are already known.

Who was this aimed at? A small circle, perhaps five or ten souls, of two kinds. Some had been Muslims when the Prophet ﷺ emigrated and simply had not followed, and their names the companions never recorded. Others converted in Makkah after he ﷺ had already reached Madinah, because the dawah did not stop; even after thirteen years the light still went on in a heart here and there. And understand their bind before you judge them. Wealth in that world sat all around you and could not travel; you carried what one camel could carry and abandoned the rest. To emigrate was to choose poverty with your eyes open. So they stayed, hiding their faith, wanting their property and their belief at once, and verse after verse kept arriving to close the gap they were hiding in.

Not one more night in Makkah

Ibn Abbas tells us what staying finally cost them, and the telling turns on one quiet mechanism. There was a secret correspondence running between the Muslims of Madinah and the Muslims still in Makkah, and every fresh revelation was carried in to them. The Qur'an was their lifeline, reaching them verse by verse down that hidden channel. So when the hardest verse of all came down, it traveled to Makkah too:

Indeed, those whom the angels take [in death] while wronging themselves - [the angels] will say, "In what [condition] were you?" They will say, "We were oppressed in the land." They [the angels] will say, "Was not the earth of Allāh spacious [enough] for you to emigrate therein?" For those, their refuge is Hell - and evil it is as a destination.Qur'an 4:97

Ibn Abbas attaches a story to it. When Badr came, those closet Muslims were dragged out to fight on the wrong side, against the believers. They hung back, avoided the fighting as best they could, and still some of them fell to Muslim arrows, killed in the ranks of the enemy. About them this verse descended: the angels at the moment of death asking, in what condition were you? We were oppressed, the dying plead, we were forced. And the angels refuse the excuse: you were not forced to remain in Makkah; was not the earth of Allah wide enough that you could have left?

The verse reached Makkah down the secret line, and it found an old man who had accepted Islam only after the Prophet ﷺ was already gone: so elderly he could not walk, and blind besides, by every reckoning of men precisely the kind of person an excuse is written for. He heard the verse and refused the loophole anyone alive would have handed him. I am not of those who have an excuse, he said. I can emigrate. I will not spend another night in this city. He ordered his servants to lift him onto his bed, a couch of a thing since he could not walk, and to carry him out of Makkah toward Madinah; he would find some way. And as the pangs of death overtook him, just past the edge of the city, he pressed his right hand into his left and said: O Allah, this is my pledge of allegiance to Your Messenger ﷺ, the one I could not give in person; whatever I was able to do, accept it from me. And there, on the very first night of his hijrah, he died, and there he was buried.

When word reached the companions, some of them said: how unlucky, he almost made it. That is how seriously that generation took the command, that they would not even grant him the arrival his body had failed to reach. And Allah Himself answered them, sending down the very next verse:

Except for the oppressed among men, women and children who cannot devise a plan nor are they directed to a way - For those it is expected that Allāh will pardon them, and Allāh is ever Pardoning and Forgiving.Qur'an 4:98-99

It came down a little grudgingly, the Sheikh observes, not as an open blanket but as a narrow pardon, precisely to keep showing how severe the matter was for anyone with no genuine excuse. But it came. And Ibn Abbas read the word in it, asa, perhaps, the way the early scholars always read that word when its speaker is Allah: every perhaps from Allah is a certainty. For Allah's sake, He will do it. So the old man did not almost make it. He made it all the way.

Does the hijrah still bind us?

If hijrah was once an outright obligation on every believer, the lecture turns to ask, what about a Muslim today, in Memphis, in Toronto, in London? The seerah answers its own question. As the believers grew strong, from around the fifth year onward, the strictness began to ease; and when Makkah itself fell, the Prophet ﷺ closed the chapter with one famous sentence: there is no hijrah after the conquest. He did not mean that travel had ended. He meant the Hijrah with a capital H, the specific migration from Makkah to Madinah carrying those Qur'anic rewards, had been brought to its end. Makkah was now part of Islam; the duty to leave it was abrogated. A believer could live anywhere.

Later generations of scholars then debated a further question: may a Muslim freely, by his own choice, live in a land that is not a land of Islam? The majority, the standard Hanafi and Hanbali view and the standard Shafi'i view, permitted it on conditions you can count on one hand: that you are secure in your religion, not tortured, not forced into the forbidden, and able to live an Islamic life. Their proof is a hadith about as explicit as proof gets, from a companion we know only as Fudayk. His own tribe in central Arabia had not accepted Islam, and people were telling him his faith obliged him to emigrate or be destroyed; his very Islam, they said, was not valid while he lived among people upon shirk. So he came to Madinah to ask, and the Prophet ﷺ told him: establish the prayer, leave the sins, and live with your people wherever you like. Ibn Hibban carried it, and the Sheikh tells us plainly that this is his own position too.

The Malikis ruled hardest the other way, and their history explains their fatwa: Andalusia. Muslim Spain was Maliki to its core, and when the Reconquista came and the last kingdom of Granada fell, Ferdinand and Isabella first promised the Muslims freedom, telling them not to worry, that only the Jews need fear. Within a hundred years there was not a public Muslim left. Arabic was outlawed in public, Islamic dress forbidden, the hijab stripped from the women; the Inquisition hunted houses for a hidden mushaf; families were forced to eat pork or vanish. The Andalusian scholars wrote that pain straight into law: never again place yourselves under such a power.

Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi gives his own judgment, with the majority, and then asks for something rarer than a ruling, which is realism. How realistic is it, he says, to import a fatwa from a thousand years ago, built for a world with a real dar al-Islam, into a world that has none? Do the arithmetic. Some two hundred million Muslims in India, a minority. Tens of millions in China, a minority. Forty million and more across the Western lands. Add it up and you reach over half a billion believers living as minorities, fully half the ummah; to tell them all that emigration is obligatory is, with respect, not realistic. And then he turns the question over to land in an unexpected place: every land must be judged by its own scholars, who alone can say whether their situation has become like Makkah's was, and America, he says plainly, with every freedom to pray and fast and live the religion openly, is nowhere near it. Then the line that flips the whole anxiety: the people most worried about whether they should emigrate are usually the most practicing, exactly the ones who should stay and be the best ambassadors of this religion, while the ones who never give it a thought, busy with every sin, are the ones who would actually benefit from a purer place. So memorize Fudayk's hadith and keep it in your pocket: pray, leave the sins, live a good Muslim life, and represent him ﷺ where you stand.

A Friday arrival, and a song returned to its day

So on what date did he ﷺ arrive? Here the Sheikh the academic hands you an honest shrug in place of a fairy tale. Nobody was keeping a calendar, for there was no Islamic calendar yet to keep. Umar would institute one some fourteen or fifteen years later and number the years backward, because the Arabs did not count years, they named them: the Year of the Elephant, the year of drought, each year titled by its great event. This year needed no title; it simply became Year One. As for the beloved 12th of Rabi al-Awwal, stamped by sheer habit onto his birth and his arrival alike, and even, some say, onto the first revelation, which cannot be true, since the Qur'an came down in Ramadan, the Sheikh calls it gently what it is, a small superstition of ours. What can responsibly be said is this: most likely the first week of Rabi al-Awwal, corresponding to September of the year 622.

He had waited at Quba about a week. Quba was the outermost settlement of an oasis that was not a single city at all, but small pockets of homes separated by stretches of desert and date orchard, some clusters a five minute walk apart, others a full hour, the whole conglomeration called Madinah. He waited there for Ali, until every trust in Makkah had been returned, and he waited for the women Abu Bakr had arranged to be brought north by a guide, his daughters Aisha and Asma among them. When they came, the Prophet ﷺ moved on into the city. And it was a Friday.

The Ansar heard he was coming. More than five hundred of them gathered as a delegation and dressed in full armor, swords and all, the parade dress of that age, the way you turn out to honor a king. They marched to Quba and walked their Prophet ﷺ home.

And the song? Generations have grown up picturing the little girls singing Tala'a al-Badru Alayna as he entered the city, and the Sheikh, academic before romantic, has to move the scene. That song welcomes a traveler coming over Thaniyyat al-Wada, the pass on the road toward Sham, which lies on the northern side of Madinah. But a rider out of Makkah enters from the south, the exact opposite end of the oasis; he ﷺ never passed that northern pass on his way in. If the incident is authentic at all, and its chain carries a measure of doubt, it belongs to his return from the expedition of Tabuk, years from now. Let the song go gently back to its own day, because what is certain here needs no decoration: an entire oasis turned out in armor and tears, walking at a camel's pace beside the man they had waited their whole history to meet.

Let the camel be

Now every clan in Madinah wanted him. As the camel made her way through the pockets of the oasis, the Ansar pressed in, each household reaching for her halter and begging: stay with us, Messenger of Allah, ours is the strength, ours the wealth. And to every offer he ﷺ gave one answer: let the camel be, for Allah has taken charge of her; let her go where she is going. The routing was out of human hands, so that no tribe could claim the honor or resent the choice. She knelt at last on a small open lot, not a true date plantation but a bare patch with only a few trees, the ground the local people used to spread their dates out to dry. The Prophet ﷺ understood: this was the place Allah had chosen for his masjid. The lot belonged to two orphan boys, named in one narration as Sahl and Suhayl, who had inherited it from their father and, being young, had not yet planted it, which is likely why it sat empty enough for the neighbors to dry their dates on it. Hold that empty lot in view. Almost everything is about to happen on it.

Where the camel knelt, he ﷺ asked a single question: whose house, of our family, is nearest to here? For Madinah held kin. His own great-grandmother, Salma, was a woman of Banu Najjar, this very neighborhood, and so he had distant cousins all around. A man named Khalid ibn Zayd, known to history forever as Abu Ayyub al-Ansari radiyallahu anhu, was of Banu Najjar, and he answered: mine. Count the lineage out and the two men meet something like six generations up, sixth cousins, and the Arabs carried such genealogies in their heads the way we carry a string of phone numbers. A thread of kinship six generations thin, and the Prophet ﷺ honored it: of all the houses in Madinah, he chose to lodge with family.

By Allah's quiet arrangement, Abu Ayyub's house was one of the few in the oasis built with an upper floor, which is exactly what made it possible for the Prophet ﷺ and Abu Bakr to stay. So the hosts moved upstairs and the guests settled below, and for roughly half a year, while the masjid and his rooms were built, he ﷺ lived there. From those six months only two or three scenes survive, and every one is a portrait of what loving him up close actually looks like.

One night Abu Ayyub turned over in his sleep and knocked the water jar across the upper floor. He woke in a panic, woke his wife, and the thought that froze him was that a drop might seep through the boards and fall on the Prophet ﷺ sleeping below. The two of them spent the rest of that night soaking the spill out of the floor with their own blanket, dreading that one drop might so much as irritate him. Another evening it struck Abu Ayyub cold that he and his wife were walking, their very feet, above the head of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ; they spent that whole night pressed against the edges of the room, feet drawn in, unable to bear what they were doing. In the morning they went down and begged him to swap floors. The bottom is easier for me, he answered: easier for my guests, more private, no stairs to climb. They refused him. We can never stand on a roof above your head, they said, and they would not obey the thing he was begging them to do. It is the only disobedience in the whole seerah made entirely out of respect, and it won. He ﷺ moved upstairs.

And the food. Abu Ayyub would cook and send the whole dish up, keeping nothing back, far more than the Prophet ﷺ would eat, and then he would look for the place on the plate where the Prophet's ﷺ fingers had been and eat from exactly that spot. One day the dish came back down untouched, and Abu Ayyub ran upstairs in alarm: what have I done? Nothing was wrong, the Prophet ﷺ reassured him; nothing was haram. The dish had raw garlic in it, and, he said, I speak with those you do not speak with. The angels kept him company. Garlic is not forbidden, he made clear; cook it and the odor is gone and there is no issue at all. But the one who has eaten it raw should spare the masjid his breath, for the masjid is the place of the angels. Six months of hosting, and what history chose to keep is a soaked blanket, two cramped sleepers, and a studied dinner plate: love, recorded in its smallest units.

Allah's house before his own

Back to the drying yard. The two orphans, already Muslims, understood what the Prophet ﷺ intended and cried out: this is a gift, ya Rasulallah, a gift to Allah from us. He ﷺ refused: I will only take it at its price. He did not operate that way with charity. He negotiated a price as any buyer would and paid it in full, because an orphan's wealth stays fenced off even when the buyer is the Messenger of Allah ﷺ and the project is the house of Allah. The handful of palms on the lot, fewer than a dozen, were cut down, and nothing went to waste: their trunks became two of the masjid's walls, and clay brick the companions made formed the other two. One or two pagan graves, fifty or a hundred years old, were respectfully dug up and the remains moved beyond the grounds, which the Sheikh notes shows that graves may be relocated for a legitimate need, just as the trees of the sacred precinct, normally untouchable down to a plucked leaf, may be cut for a genuine purpose like building.

Then he ﷺ took his place in the labor line. There was a chain of men passing material from the quarry to the site, and he joined it, carrying his load, and when a companion tried to take the work from him and sit him down, he refused. They sang as they worked, the way men everywhere find a rhythm to pass the hours of manual labor, and Bukhari preserved the words, the Prophet ﷺ chanting them along with the rest: O Allah, there is no good but the good of the hereafter, so have mercy on the Ansar and the Muhajirah. Almost two weeks of that, and the masjid stood.

For its day it was vast, by modern estimates something like a hundred feet by a hundred and thirty, closer to a square than the long rectangle of the masjid today. It had at least three main public doors, on the south, the west, and the east. Years later, when it was suggested that men and women should not crowd through the same entrance, the Prophet ﷺ set the eastern door aside for the women, and it became Bab an-Nisa, the women's door; to this day a pillar in the haram of Madinah bears the name. And because a wall in that world was such a luxury that two households shared one between them, the homes built against the masjid opened straight into it through small private doors, half a dozen of them, perhaps a dozen: a single step and you were inside. Remember those doors. On his deathbed the Prophet ﷺ would order every one of them sealed forever except the door of Abu Bakr, and Madinah would understand exactly what he meant by it, that this was the man to lead them after him. The room of Aisha touched the masjid too, separated from it by nothing but a curtain.

Do not picture marble. The roof, where there was any roof at all, was palm thatch, low enough, says one early report from a companion who could reach up and touch it, that a raised hand brushed it. At first it sheltered only the side facing Jerusalem, the first qiblah. When the qiblah turned toward Makkah, the old patch of shade stayed where it was, and the poorest of the believers, the ones with no household at all, made it their address: the People of the Suffah, whom the series will meet properly very soon, named for that shaded place because anyone spending the day in the masjid had to spend it there or burn in the sun.

Even once a full roof was finally raised over the whole masjid, in the middle of the Madani years, it was built to keep out the sun, not the rain. We know this for certain, the Sheikh says, because Bukhari records that one Ramadan night the sky opened and the floor of the unpaved masjid turned to mud, and a companion who was there describes the Prophet ﷺ rising from sajdah with the traces of mud on his forehead and his nose.
