He had pressed the noblest part of himself into the mud without a flicker of embarrassment, because sajdah is the whole purpose of a servant: you lower your face, the most honored part of you, before Allah, whatever lies beneath it, since wet earth is not impure. And hold on to the largest fact of these weeks, the one the Sheikh calls, in plain words, mind-boggling: he ﷺ would not raise a roof over his own head until the house of Allah was built. For half a year the man this city would be renamed for had no house in it, and so did not even live with his family, lodging with Abu Bakr instead, because first the house of Allah, and only then his own.

The stump that wept

Inside the new masjid, his first minbar was one of the felled palms: a stump, and a large one, tall enough that he ﷺ once even prayed his salah up on top of it to show the rows exactly how he prayed, stepping down each time for sajdah and climbing back up. For the first few years every khutbah, every reminder, came from that piece of wood, and it stood close beside him through all of it.

Then, as both Bukhari and Muslim record, he asked an Ansari woman whose servant was a carpenter to have a proper minbar made, and the carpenter built one of three steps and set it a little apart from the old trunk. The first Friday the Prophet ﷺ climbed the new steps to give the khutbah, a sound rose over his words: a whimpering, a crying like that of a baby camel, and the companions traced it and found it was the abandoned stump, weeping.

He ﷺ stopped his khutbah, came down, and took the stump in his arms, and it settled like a child, sniffling itself into silence. If I had not embraced it, he said, it would have cried until the Day of Judgment. He ordered it lifted and buried beneath the new minbar, as close to him as wood could be, so that it would not be parted from him after all. For one moment, the Sheikh reminds you, Allah let the companions hear aloud what creation feels in his ﷺ presence and what it cannot bear to lose; that is the miracle of it.

And one of the early scholars, Hasan al-Basri, after narrating this very hadith, would turn the whole thing on his listeners. Here, he said, was a piece of wood that wept because it longed to be near the Prophet ﷺ. O believers, are you, who claim to love him, not more deserving than a tree of crying to be near him? Where are our hearts, that a stump could ache for his ﷺ closeness and we cannot? Fourteen centuries later, the question still has your name on it.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read the beginning of the Madani era as a relocation, a community simply changing address, and to move on. That would be to miss what the day puts to you. Hold its two buildings side by side, the grave at the edge of Makkah and the masjid rising in Madinah, and they ask you the same question from opposite ends of the same week.

Allah weighs hearts, not headcounts. Three or four hundred emigrants against thousands upon thousands, and still the Qur'an names them first whenever it names the two, because what they walked away from for His sake outweighed the multitude they walked toward. We spend our lives quietly counting the wrong things, our numbers, our backing, our footprint, when the only scale that will matter weighs what we were willing to leave behind for Allah. Ask not how many stand with you, but how much you have been willing to give up.

When the command lands, move that night. A blind old man who could not walk heard one verse and refused the excuse the whole world would have written for him; he died at the city's edge with his pledge pressed between his own two hands, and Allah sent down his pardon in the very next verse. We are quick to find ourselves a loophole and slow to simply obey, and here is a man with the soundest excuse imaginable who would not take it, and who arrived precisely because he refused to. Our hijrah is no longer across a desert; it is the daily migration from sin toward obedience, and it waits, as his did, on a heart that will not bargain when Allah has spoken.

Build Allah's house before your own. He ﷺ slept half a year in a borrowed room, away from his own family, so that the masjid would rise first; he passed its bricks with his own hands and refused to be sat down; and he paid two orphans the full price for land they begged to give him for free. There is a whole order of priorities in that, and a whole ethic of justice: first things first, and fairness even inside an act of charity. Look honestly at what you build first with your hours and your wealth, and ask whether the house of Allah comes before your own.

And then there is the smallest scene of all, which carries the largest weight. A man and his wife awake all night soaking a spilled jar out of the floorboards, terrified that a single drop might reach him; feet drawn back from the space above his head; a dinner plate studied for the marks of his fingers. This is what reverence is actually made of, not grand declarations but the smallest units of behavior, love proven in the dark when no one is watching. And it ends with a piece of dead wood that could not bear the distance from him and wept until he came down and held it. If a stump longed for his ﷺ nearness that much, the early scholar asked, what excuse does a heart have? That is the day's quiet assignment, and salawat is its answer: the refusal of distance from him.

So end the day the way the builders ended theirs, with mud on the floor and the akhirah on their lips. O Allah, there is no good but the good of the hereafter, so have mercy on the Ansar and the Muhajirin, and on us who came late. Send Your peace upon the one who built Your house before his own ﷺ, gather us in his masjid in this life and around him at his pool in the next, and make our hearts like the wood he embraced: unable to bear the distance from him, and quieted only by nearness to him. Ameen.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 30: the beginning of the Madani era (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (22:58-59, 29:10, 4:97, 4:98-99), verified via quran.ai.

The Seerah · 31 of 104

The First Mosques of Islam

Allah's house before his own

He ﷺ has barely dismounted. The camel has knelt where Allah willed it to kneel, and a constellation of little settlements scattered across an oasis has begun, all at once, to think of itself as a single city with a single name. Watch what the Prophet ﷺ does with his very first week in his new home, because the first thing a man builds tells you what he loves. He does not build himself a house. He builds Allah a house. Then he begins a second one. And while the houses of Allah rise out of the dust, he ﷺ sleeps under borrowed roofs and has, for weeks, no roof he can call his own.

This is the week of the first mosques, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi walks it stone by stone: Quba, which the Qur'an itself calls a mosque founded on taqwa; the first Friday sermon of his ﷺ life, short enough to memorize in an afternoon; a joke in the dust that turned out to be one of the heaviest prophecies in the whole seerah; and a call to prayer that arrived in a sleeping man's dream and became law on the Prophet's ﷺ single word. Before any of it, there is a small act of scholarly honesty about a date.

A Monday in Quba

Ask almost anyone when the Prophet ﷺ arrived in Madinah and you will hear, without hesitation, the 12th of Rabi al-Awwal. Dr. Yasir Qadhi turns that famous date in the light until its seams show. One early book of history records that he ﷺ left Makkah on the 1st of that month. The journey took a very fast rider three and a half days and a slow caravan as many as nine, and he ﷺ had also spent two or three hidden nights in the cave of Thawr before turning north at all. Lay those numbers end to end and the 12th will not stretch to cover them; the 8th or the 9th fits far better. The deeper point is not the correction but the reason it is even possible: nobody in that world was keeping a careful calendar, and the precise date of a thing mattered to no one the way it matters to us. What the people of that morning remembered and handed down was not a number on a page but the day of the week. It was a Monday.

And it was nearly noon. The news of his ﷺ departure had outrun him by days, so every morning the believers of Madinah walked out to the southern edge of the oasis and waited on the Makkah road, and every morning the desert sun beat them. By ten or eleven the heat of any desert climate becomes a wall, and these were early risers who gave up the watch when the furnace hour came and went home. The Prophet ﷺ arrived in exactly that emptied hour, a little before or after midday, the welcoming crowds just dispersed and the road deserted. Which is why, as we have already seen, the first voice in all of Madinah to cry out that he had come belonged to a man up a date palm.

The farthest settlement of the oasis out toward Makkah, the first a traveler from the south would reach, was Quba, and so Quba received him ﷺ first. He lodged there with a Companion most of us have never been taught to name: Kulthum ibn Hidm, an elderly man of the Ansar. Search the books, as the Sheikh did when a questioner asked whose house it was, and you find almost nothing. Kulthum was old, he was of the Ansar, and that is nearly the whole record. We know only one thing more, and it stops you. He was the first Muslim to die in Madinah after the arrival; Allah kept this old man alive, it seems, exactly long enough to host His Messenger ﷺ under his roof, and then took him. A whole quiet lifetime, preserved for one honor. By day, some reports add, the Prophet ﷺ received his visitors at the house of Sa'd ibn Khaythamah, a bachelor whose home could absorb a crowd, and returned to Kulthum's house only at night; Abu Bakr lodged with another family of the Ansar.

The mosque founded on taqwa

Here is the wonder that was waiting for him ﷺ in Quba: Madinah was already praying. Before he ever arrived, the believers there gathered in a place not purpose-built, just a patch of ground, and the daily prayers were established and the congregation formed. And they had something more startling still. They had Jumu'ah. From Makkah, where persecution made any public Muslim gathering unthinkable, the Prophet ﷺ had sent word north to establish the Friday prayer, and so for roughly a year the Muslims of Madinah had been praying a Jumu'ah that their own Prophet ﷺ had never once in his life been free to pray. Dr. Yasir Qadhi lingers on the strange sweetness of this. Jumu'ah is one of the very few commands in all of Islam that the Companions lived out before the Messenger ﷺ himself could, an order he gave from inside persecution and could only watch others obey from afar.

He ﷺ stayed in Quba through the week, partly because a traveler was still on the road behind him. Ali, radiyallahu anhu, had remained three extra nights in Makkah to discharge a duty that tells you everything about the man ﷺ they were trying to murder: the Makkans, even as they plotted to kill him, had left their valuables in his keeping, and he would not leave until every deposit was handed back to its owner. Then Ali set out by the short main road, while the Prophet ﷺ had gone south to the cave and looped the long way around, so that Ali, starting three days later but traveling faster, caught up at Quba around the Wednesday or Thursday, as if the two had left together. And in those same days the building began. The reports say the Prophet ﷺ set the first stone of Masjid Quba with his own hands; Ali and Abu Bakr carried the work forward; and the Ansar finished it. It was the first purpose-built mosque in the history of Islam.

The verse came down later, in the language of prohibition. The hypocrites of Madinah would in time raise a mosque of their own, not a house of worship but a weapon, a story the series keeps for further along, and Allah forbade His Prophet ﷺ ever to set foot in it.

Do not stand [for prayer] within it - ever. A mosque founded on righteousness from the first day is more worthy for you to stand in. Within it are men who love to purify themselves; and Allah loves those who purify themselves.Qur'an 9:108

The majority of the scholars of tafsir, the Sheikh notes, read the mosque founded on taqwa here as Masjid Quba, because the hypocrites had built their rival in its very vicinity, so the verse turns the Prophet ﷺ from the one toward the other. And a hadith in Abu Dawud preserves a small, almost comic scene from the day it arrived. The people of Quba were asked what they had done to be singled out for praise by Allah Himself, named in revelation as men who love to purify themselves. Their answer was disarmingly down to earth: water. After answering the call of nature they did not merely wipe, as most of that world did; they washed, every time. The Sheikh adds what the scholars add: no one in Islam ever made water obligatory for this, and dry purification has always been permitted and sufficient. But washing is the better way, and Allah loved these men for reaching for it when they did not have to.

And yet. In another hadith, this one fully authentic, a Companion put the question straight to the Prophet ﷺ, asking which mosque the verse meant, and he answered: by Allah, it is this mosque of mine. So which is it, Quba or his own mosque in the city? The answer, and the reconciliation is itself the teaching, is both. Both were founded upon taqwa from their first day, and he ﷺ said it of his own precisely so that no one would imagine Quba outranked it. He never stopped loving Quba, though. Ali relates that every single week, usually on a Monday, the Prophet ﷺ would go out to Masjid Quba, on foot or riding, and pray two rakaat there. And he left his ummah a standing offer that holds to this day: whoever purifies himself in his own house, then comes to Masjid Quba and prays in it, carries away the reward of a complete umrah.

The first Friday of his life

On the Thursday night he ﷺ announced that in the morning he would at last enter the city itself. The Ansar turned out for him more than five hundred strong, in their ceremonial best, wearing armor and blades the way men wear honor on a day of welcome, a guard assembled to escort the man ﷺ who had ridden out of Makkah hunted, with a single companion and a price on his head. And on that road between Quba and the city, the sun tipped past its zenith and Friday came due. So the first Jumu'ah of the Prophet's ﷺ entire life was prayed in neither of his mosques. He stopped where the hour found him, in the quarter of one of the Ansar clans along the way, and prayed it there. The people of that place later raised a mosque over the very spot. A third mosque, planted by nothing more than a pause for prayer.

Then he ﷺ delivered the first khutbah of his life, preserved by Ibn Ishaq through a chain with a touch of weakness, the gentle sort of report the scholars permit themselves to relate. Before a single word of its content, notice its length. Every khutbah of his ﷺ that has come down to us runs three to five minutes, no more. He ﷺ said outright that it is a mark of a man's fiqh, his real grasp of the religion, that he keeps his sermon short and makes his prayer long, and his own salah was always longer than his speech. Dr. Yasir Qadhi is honest about why we have so completely inverted that order. The Friday sermon is now the only hour in the week that most of the ummah spends inside a mosque at all, indeed the only time many pray in congregation in seven days, so the world's khutbahs have stretched to carry the weight of a whole week's teaching. His ﷺ first listeners needed no such topping up. Their iman ran at a voltage ours does not, and a few minutes were enough.

And what did he ﷺ choose as the theme of the first sermon in his new homeland? Giving. He urged them to be generous, and set that urging between two unanswerable certainties: that death is coming to every one of them, and that Allah will ask each soul what it was given in this world and what it sent ahead for the next. And inside the sermon sits a single line the ummah has never stopped repeating, carried independently in Bukhari as well: whoever is able to shield himself from the fire, let him do it with even half a date; and whoever cannot find so much as that, then with a good word, for every good deed is repaid ten times over. Then he sat down.

Of course the first sermon was a call to sacrifice, the Sheikh says. An entire community, an entire new order, was about to be built out of nothing in a city with almost no resources, and there was never an hour in the whole of Islam when the ummah needed its givers more than at its very birth. And mark the method as much as the message. Hope and fear arrive together in that short khutbah, the promise laced through the warning, Jannah set beside the fire. Some faiths of our own age keep only the warm half of this and call the other backward; the Qur'an keeps both, because human nature is built to answer to both.

The words that converted a chief before the speech began

The second part of the sermon, in Ibn Ishaq's record, opens with the words Muslims still open their sermons with, the khutbat al-hajah: all praise is for Allah, we praise Him and seek His help and ask His forgiveness; we take refuge in Allah from the evils of our own souls and from our wrong deeds; whomever Allah guides, none can lead astray, and whomever Allah leaves to stray, none can guide; and I bear witness that none deserves worship but Allah alone, and that Muhammad ﷺ is His servant and Messenger. There is a small puzzle here the Sheikh flags rather than smooths over: in this report the khutbat al-hajah stands at the head of the second portion, whereas other narrations have the Prophet ﷺ place it at the very beginning. Perhaps the practice settled later, perhaps the parts have been transposed in the telling; he leaves the question open and hands it back to Allah, who knows best.

Do not let long familiarity flatten those opening lines, because they once converted a man entirely on their own, before any sermon had begun. Back in Makkah, a chief of the tribes of Yemen named Dimad al-Azdi, a healer by trade, had been so thoroughly warned about the dangerous sorcerer Muhammad ﷺ, a man possessed, touched by jinn, that he stuffed cotton into his ears so as not to hear the bewitching speech. Then he reasoned himself into courage: I am an intelligent man, he thought, and a physician; if this man is truly mad, I am the one to treat him. He pulled the cotton out, walked up to the Prophet ﷺ, and said his people had warned him but he wished to hear for himself. And the Prophet ﷺ said the khutbat al-hajah. Repeat those words, Dimad demanded, and he ﷺ repeated them. I have memorized the poetry of men and the speech of the jinn, Dimad finally said, and by Allah I have never heard anything like these words; you must be a man whom Allah inspires. He accepted Islam on the spot, before the Prophet ﷺ had spoken a single line of the actual address. Those few opening sentences have since drawn whole books to themselves: Ibn Taymiyyah wrote a treatise on the khutbat al-hajah, Ibn al-Qayyim filled many pages on it, and in our own era al-Albani gave it an entire book.

Where the first half of the sermon had asked the people for their hands, the second half asked for their hearts. The truly successful one, he ﷺ told them, is the one whose heart Allah has made beautiful, whom He has brought into Islam after disbelief and chosen above the rest of mankind for the best of all matters. So love what Allah loves, and love Allah with the whole of your heart. Never tire of the speech of Allah and never tire of His remembrance, for He has chosen the Qur'an and His dhikr to be the best of all deeds; and do not let your hearts grow hard. Worship Allah and associate nothing with Him; be sincere and true to Him in all that you say; and love one another with the spirit of Allah between you, a phrase so singular that Dr. Yasir Qadhi says he has not found it in any other hadith. The last word of the sermon was a warning dressed as a plain fact: Allah hates that His covenant be broken. You have given Allah your word to uphold His religion; do not be the ones who break it.

Action, then spirit. The hand, then the heart. Fear braided through with hope. An entire religion folded twice into a few short minutes. And mark the two things he ﷺ named, and only two, as the daily antidote to a hardening heart: never tire of the speech of Allah, never tire of His remembrance. Reciting the Book and remembering its Author are what keep a heart from turning to stone, and he set that prescription for his new city in its first hour.

A mosque at the center of everything

The khutbah finished, he ﷺ rode on into the city, the camel knelt at the place yesterday's episode left her, and Abu Ayyub al-Ansari's household took him in. And the very first work he ﷺ turned his hand to, before any business of governing the new community, before any home of his own, was the same work he had just left behind in Quba: a mosque. Do the arithmetic with the Sheikh, because here the arithmetic is the sermon. Within roughly five days of reaching his new city, the Prophet ﷺ had begun two mosques, three counting the prayer stop a grateful clan later built over, and he still did not have so much as a roof over his own head. The house of Allah went up before the house of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ.

Allah calls the mosques His own houses, and in Surah an-Nur He shows you what they are for and who fills them.

[Such niches are] in houses [i.e., mosques] which Allah has ordered to be raised and that His name be mentioned [i.e., praised] therein; exalting Him within them in the morning and the eveningsQur'an 24:36
[Are] men whom neither commerce nor sale distracts from the remembrance of Allah and performance of prayer and giving of zakah. They fear a Day in which the hearts and eyes will [fearfully] turn about -Qur'an 24:37

Now watch everything that one plain courtyard of palm trunks and mud became. It was the school, where the circles of knowledge met. It was the council chamber: here, before Badr and again before Uhud, he ﷺ would gather the Companions and ask them openly what the community should do, the nearest thing this young society had to a parliament. It was the social heart of the whole city, where the Companions sat and laughed and told stories of their old days of jahiliyyah while the Prophet ﷺ sat among them; weddings were celebrated inside it; armies formed their ranks within its walls and marched out from its door; and the Qur'an itself spread into the world from this single courtyard.

And it was a roof for anyone who had none. Every homeless newcomer was housed in it, for he ﷺ said, in an authentic hadith, that the masjid is the home of every person of taqwa. Dr. Yasir Qadhi hears both meanings ringing at once. Where does a person of taqwa feel most at home, most himself? In the house of Allah; that is the spiritual sense. And the Muslim with no home at all discovers that Allah's house is his house; that is the plain physical sense, and both are true together. The Prophet ﷺ lived the first meaning out in full view, more present in his masjid than in his own rooms, and soon the mosque would become something more again: a residential university, with students who lived in it, the people of Suffa, whose story belongs to tomorrow.

One correction to the imagination before you leave the building site. Do not picture a single grand mosque serving the whole of Madinah. The oasis was a string of settlements, some an hour and a half apart on foot, too far to walk to a central mosque for each of the five prayers, and so in time at least a dozen neighborhood mosques stood scattered across the city, each clan praying in its own. The Prophet's ﷺ mosque was simply the center and the largest, and, as far as the reports tell us, the only one in which Jumu'ah was ever prayed.

The son of Sumayyah

Back at the building site, the Sheikh adds a story he almost ran out of time to tell, and it carries one of the weightiest prophecies in the whole seerah. The Prophet ﷺ did not stand back and supervise. He labored in the work himself, hauling stone alongside everyone else, and the sight of him ﷺ carrying rock pulled poetry out of the Companions, a line they chanted as they worked: if we sit back idle while the Prophet ﷺ labors, then that, from us, is the deed gone astray.

Through the dust came Ammar ibn Yasir, the young man Makkah had orphaned twice over, for his father Yasir and his mother Sumayyah had been the very first martyrs of this ummah, killed for their faith in the early Makkan years. Ammar was in his twenties now, coated head to foot in dust, carrying two of the great quarry stones at once while everyone else carried one. So he lodged a complaint, the way you complain to someone you love and trust completely: Messenger of Allah, they are killing me, two stones on me and only one on them. He did not mean it, the Sheikh smiles, and the smile carries a point of fiqh. The Companions joked with their Prophet ﷺ, and a joke understood by everyone to be a joke is no lie at all. The only difference, and it matters, is that the Prophet's ﷺ own jokes, unlike ours, never once bent the truth even in play; whatever he ﷺ said in jest was still, somehow, exactly true.

The Prophet ﷺ smiled back, and brushed the dust from his body with his own hand, and answered with words that would take decades to unfold. They are not killing you, son of Sumayyah, he ﷺ said. The ones who will kill you are the transgressing party, al-fi'ah al-baghiyah, the group that has gone beyond the bounds. And he ﷺ set gentler words beside the heavy ones: everyone here today is earning a single reward for his labor, and you are earning two for your two stones. And the last thing you will drink in this world will be a draught of milk. Hear the name he ﷺ chose to call him by. Not Ammar. Son of Sumayyah, lifting up the first martyr of Islam by honoring her in her own son's name; and the Companions, having heard the Prophet ﷺ call him so, called him Ibn Sumayyah ever after.

Decades later, long after the Prophet ﷺ had passed, an old man in his late sixties stood with Ali ibn Abi Talib in the war that broke the ummah's heart, the fighting between the camp of Ali and the forces of Mu'awiyah. In his camp that day Ammar was handed milk and drank it, went forward into the battle, and an arrow found him, and he was killed fighting on the side of Ali. The Companions remembered the building site at once, down to the glass of milk, and read it as the sign it was. This is sensitive ground, and the Sheikh treads it with all the adab the subject demands, because the killers were themselves Companions whom we honor. For Ahlus-Sunnah wal-Jamaah the hadith became a sign that Ali, radiyallahu anhu, was the closer of the two parties to the truth, while Mu'awiyah, radiyallahu anhu, was sincere yet mistaken, and not a single bad word is to be said of either. Some of the greatest Companions refused to take any side at all; asked in an earlier dispute whether he stood with the party of Ali or the party of Aisha, Ibn Abbas gave the answer the Sheikh loves: I am on the team of Muhammad ﷺ. And what no Sunni doubts, Dr. Yasir Qadhi finishes, is the one thing that dusty afternoon proved beyond argument: the Prophet ﷺ was given knowledge no one else had, of things not yet come, down to the last glass of milk a man would drink. Like all such cryptic foretellings, it was not understood when spoken; it snapped into place only after the thing came to pass, and then it was crystal clear.

The adhan arrives in a dream

Meanwhile the prayer itself was settling into the form we now know. Since the night of Isra and Mi'raj, which Dr. Yasir Qadhi places most likely in the tenth or eleventh year of the dawah, the believers had prayed five times a day, but every one of the five had been two rakaat. Aisha, radiyallahu anha, relates in Bukhari that when they came to Madinah the prayers were established as we have them today: Dhuhr, Asr, and Isha rose to four rakaat, Maghrib stood at three, and Fajr kept its two; the original two were preserved as a mercy for the traveler, who still prays them shortened on a journey. Around these obligations the sunnah prayers took their settled places, and the witr of the night that he ﷺ never once abandoned, not even when traveling.

Then, within his ﷺ first month or two in the city, a question arose that years of hiding in Makkah had never permitted: how do you call an entire city to prayer? He ﷺ put it to the Companions in open consultation. One suggested a bell, like the Christians; but the Prophet ﷺ disliked bells, and there are authentic reports that he ﷺ said the angels do not accompany a caravan that carries one. Another suggested a horn, the shofar the Jews sounded; that too was set aside. Other ideas were raised and fell away, and the meeting broke up with nothing chosen.

That night, Allah settled in sleep what the council could not settle awake. Abdullah ibn Zayd of the Ansar dreamed of a man carrying the very instruments they had been debating, and went up to buy one. Why do you want it, the man asked. To call the Muslims to prayer, Abdullah said. Shall I not show you something better, said the stranger, and he taught him the words the earth has not stopped hearing since: Allahu Akbar, Allahu Akbar, on through to the end of the adhan. Abdullah woke with the dream blazing so vividly that he threw on his clothes and rushed straight to the Prophet ﷺ and told him the whole of it. He ﷺ gave his verdict: it is a true dream, in sha Allah. Then: stand with Bilal on the roof of the masjid and cast the words to him one phrase at a time, for his voice is the loudest. And so, the Sheikh smiles, the first adhan was in a sense given twice at once, whispered phrase by phrase by the man who had dreamed it and proclaimed to the whole city by the voice of Bilal. Before it had even finished, Umar ibn al-Khattab came hurrying into the masjid, his lower garment still trailing half-tied: Messenger of Allah, I saw these very same words in a dream. Allah had scattered the gift across more than one sleeper that night, and willed which mouth would reach His Prophet ﷺ first.

Two reflections of the Sheikh's are worth carrying out of this. The first is a point of law. The adhan is the one and only piece of the entire Sharia that arrived through the dream of a Companion, and even here it was not the dream that made it law but the Prophet's ﷺ ruling upon it, his declaration that it was true. This ummah does not take its religion from dreams; had he ﷺ not confirmed it, the dream would have legislated nothing. The second is a quiet mystery the Sheikh confesses he could not solve. He went looking for any scholar who explains why Allah chose this single ritual, out of the whole religion, to arrive this single way, and found no one who had even taken up the question. And he went looking for Abdullah ibn Zayd himself, and found a Companion remembered for almost nothing else: the one of the dream of the adhan, as Ibn Hajar sums up his entire life. You can sense from the narrations that he had hoped to be the one to call the prayer himself; but the calling went to Bilal's voice, and the dream remained his portion. Why him? What was his secret standing with Allah that earned him this one shining honor and so little else in the record? We will never know it in this world, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi names this one of the quiet sorrows of studying the seerah: how many of its heroes survive as a single line, while Allah holds the rest. The Prophet ﷺ portioned the blessings of that morning according to what each man carried, the dream to one and the voice to another; and Allah portioned the honor according to what only He could see.

The mosque took some two or three weeks to raise, and only then, with the house of Allah complete, did he ﷺ build a place for himself, and lodging is the better word than house: rooms set directly against the wall of the mosque, not a dwelling apart from it. He had two wives at this time, in the years between Khadijah's passing and the marriages still to come: Sawdah and Aisha, radiyallahu anhuma. So two rooms went up beside the masjid, the only homes in all of Madinah connected to it; the wives he ﷺ married in later years lived in a separate row of houses with no doors onto the mosque, for by then the city had built itself up close around it. And one undated tenderness from inside those rooms teaches the very giving his first sermon had called for. Sawdah had married him ﷺ in Makkah when she was already elderly, older than him in years. At some point in Madinah she came to him with a gift no one had asked of her: Messenger of Allah, I am an old woman, and I know you prefer the company of Aisha, so I give my night to her. She wanted nothing but his ﷺ pleasure, the Sheikh says, and she knew exactly what would bring it. He ﷺ honored her always, sitting with her by day; but the nights became Aisha's, a doubled portion, so that the room history remembers as the room he ﷺ lived in, the one against the wall of his own mosque, was Aisha's. One more of the blessings of Aisha, placed in her hands by the open palm of an older woman who asked for nothing but to please the Messenger of Allah ﷺ.

What the first mosques ask of our faith

It would be easy to read this week as a pleasant prelude, the settling-in before the real history of Madinah begins. That would be to miss the question it puts to us. The first mosques are not only a story; they are a blueprint, and the blueprint is drawn in the order in which a homeless refugee chose to spend his first five days.

He ﷺ built Allah's house before his own. Two mosques begun, three counting the prayer stop, and still no roof over his ﷺ head. He did not first secure his comfort and then, once settled, attend to the worship of Allah; he reversed the order the whole world takes for granted. We build our lives first and fit Allah into whatever room is left over, a corner of the schedule, a few minutes at the edges of an exhausted day. He ﷺ set the prayer at the dead center of the new city before he had anywhere to sleep, and let everything else arrange itself outward around that center. Ask what sits at the center of the life you are building, and whether the house of Allah goes up first in it or last.

And out of that first sermon comes a prescription with our name on it, untouched after fourteen centuries: never tire of the speech of Allah, never tire of His remembrance, and the heart will not harden. A daily dose of Qur'an and a daily dose of dhikr, in whatever amount you can actually keep, is the medicine he ﷺ handed his city against the slow petrifying of the soul.

And then there is the adhan, and the quiet man who dreamed it. Two lessons sit folded together in that dawn. The first guards the religion: the only piece of the Sharia ever to arrive in a dream became law not by the dream but by the Prophet's ﷺ word upon it, so that we honor our inspirations without ever mistaking them for revelation. The second guards the heart against bitterness. Abdullah ibn Zayd received the dream and not the voice, and is remembered for one line; the old man Kulthum was kept alive just long enough to host the Messenger ﷺ and then died first of all the city's believers; Sawdah gave away her nights and asked for nothing. Allah portioned the honor of that week with a precision none of them could see, each according to what only He knew. Most of His most beloved servants survive in the record as a single sentence, and He holds the rest, and that is enough. So take this week down into your ordinary days. Put the house of Allah first in whatever you are building. Take the daily dose. Honor the small, unremembered acts of giving that no one will ever record but Allah. And when your own portion seems thin beside another's, remember the man of the dream of the adhan, whose name the whole world says five times a day without knowing it, and who carried his secret with Allah quietly to his grave. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, who laid the first stone of the first mosque with his own hands and loved the houses of Allah more than his own comfort; make the masajid home to our hearts as he ﷺ taught they are home to every soul of taqwa, keep our hearts soft with His speech and His remembrance, and gather us behind the callers of his ﷺ call in the house of His generosity that no builder of this world has ever seen.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 31: the building of the first mosques in Islam (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (9:108, 24:36, 24:37), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 32 of 104

The Brotherhood of Madinah

Strangers made brothers, a city made home

The chase is over. The cave is behind them, the bounty hunters have turned for home, and the most beloved of lands lies far to the south. Now the seerah changes its whole texture, and the change is easy to miss because nothing in it explodes. No swords are drawn here, no riders on the horizon, almost no miracles of the kind that make a story race. There is instead the slower work that comes after every deliverance: a community has to be built out of two cities' worth of strangers, refugees who own nothing and hosts who owe them nothing, and somebody has to do it on purpose. Dr. Yasir Qadhi gathers up the quiet founding acts of those first months in Madinah, and they amount to a portrait of prophethood on its ordinary days: mercy organized into systems. How the ummah learned to count its years. How a band of homesick men was taught to love a new home. And how one man ﷺ sat down and turned migration into family, one named pair at a time.

It is a chapter about belonging: why the Islamic calendar starts where it starts, why Madinah feels the way it feels to anyone who has stood in it, and what the Ansar did for the Muhajirun that no people before or since have managed to do.

When time itself was crooked

Begin with a question you have probably never thought to ask: what year did the Arabs believe it was? The Romans had a calendar. The Persians had a calendar. The Arabs had nothing. A shared way of counting time is one of the marks of a civilization, and this was a people who had reached almost none of those marks: no script, no architecture, no settled learning. So each tribe dated its life by its own memories: the year of the death of so and so, two years after that chieftain, three years before the elephant. And because the tribes were not one nation, every tribe kept a different clock: a hundred private calendars and no public one.

Hold that backwardness next to what was coming. Part of the standing miracle of this religion is that Allah took exactly this people, the most uncultivated society on the map, and made them through a single Book into the teachers of the world. The Sheikh anchors the claim in the verse that promised this nation its legacy would live through the Book it was given.

We have certainly sent down to you a Book [i.e., the Qur'an] in which is your mention. Then will you not reason?Qur'an 21:10

And it was worse than having no calendar; the time they did keep was bent out of true. From Ibrahim, peace be upon him, the Arabs had inherited one piece of sacred law: four months of every year are forbidden to war, sanctuaries where no raiding may take place. The number was not theirs to set. Allah had fixed it on the day He made the heavens and the earth, and He says so.

Indeed, the number of months with Allah is twelve [lunar] months in the register of Allah [from] the day He created the heavens and the earth; of these, four are sacred. That is the correct religion [i.e., way], so do not wrong yourselves during them. And fight against the disbelievers collectively as they fight against you collectively. And know that Allah is with the righteous [who fear Him].Qur'an 9:36

Twelve months, four of them sacred, decreed before there was a single human being to keep them. But a sacred month is an inconvenience to a tribe that wants to fight, and the powerful tribes, Quraysh foremost, found a way around it. This is not really Muharram, they would announce, this year it is Safar; and they had the clout to make the smaller tribes accept the swap, so a sacred month became lawful at a chief's word, all to suit a season's ambition. The Qur'an gives this trick its name, the nasi, and refuses to call it a mere adjustment.

Indeed, the postponing [of restriction within sacred months] is an increase in disbelief by which those who have disbelieved are led [further] astray. They make it lawful one year and unlawful another year to correspond to the number made unlawful by Allah and [thus] make lawful what Allah has made unlawful. Made pleasing to them is the evil of their deeds; and Allah does not guide the disbelieving people.Qur'an 9:37

An increase in disbelief: a deliberate editing of Allah's own law to fit human appetite. Move the months at will for generation after generation and they scramble like a jigsaw puzzle, until no one alive can say which month it truly is. That is the wreckage the Prophet ﷺ inherited, and at the Farewell Hajj, the tenth year after the Hijrah, he set it right in a single sentence. By Allah's decree the months had cycled that year back into their original positions, and he ﷺ announced, in a report Bukhari preserves, that time had returned to the shape it held on the day Allah created the heavens and the earth, and from now on, no more swapping. From that declaration to the calendar on your wall this morning, Muharram has followed Dhul-Hijjah and no month has ever been moved again.

Year one, chosen by the companions

The order of the months was fixed by the Prophet ﷺ himself. The counting of the years was not; that part he never instituted, and it surfaced only about seventeen years after the Hijrah, in the most ordinary way imaginable: a lawsuit. Two men stood before Umar ibn al-Khattab, radiyallahu anhu, over a debt. He was to repay me by Sha'ban, said one, and Sha'ban has come and gone. I meant Sha'ban of next year, said the other. And there was no way to settle it, because no document in Arabia could name its own year. The governors wrote in with the same trouble: you order us to finish a project by a certain month, but which year's month? A state cannot keep its promises if it cannot agree on what year it is.

So Umar gathered the companions. Someone suggested adopting the calendar of the Romans or the Persians, and it was dismissed at once: a civilization at its full height does not borrow another people's clock; you follow everyone else, the Sheikh observes, only in your seasons of weakness. Then, which year should be year one? The birth of the Prophet ﷺ, said one. His passing, said another, and the room recoiled from opening a calendar on grief. Badr, said a third. Then Ali ibn Abi Talib spoke, and his reasoning ended the debate: let year one be the year of the Hijrah, because that was the year Allah turned the believers' condition from dhull to izzah, from humiliation to honor. That was the hinge of the whole story, where the new leaf turned. The room agreed as one. Notice what the companions understood, the Sheikh says: every other milestone was arguable, but no one argued about the Hijrah. They knew which moment had remade them.

One question remained. Which month should open the year? Ramadan had its holiness, some said; Dhul-Hijjah its Hajj; Rajab its blessing. It was Uthman ibn Affan who said: Muharram, and the scholars give two reasons, both tied back to the Hijrah. First, plain history: the call to emigrate went out after the pledge of Aqabah, sworn in the days of Hajj, so the believers began streaming toward Madinah in Muharram, the Prophet ﷺ himself slipping out only at the end of Safar. Second, in a report from Uthman himself: the companions made Hajj almost every year, and coming home from it felt like a life washed clean; and since you return at the close of Dhul-Hijjah, the month that begins that new life is Muharram. Later scholars even reached for a Qur'anic anchor, the masjid founded on taqwa from the first day, since Masjid Quba was raised in the first days of the Hijrah.

Do not stand [for prayer] within it, ever. A mosque founded on righteousness from the first day is more worthy for you to stand in. Within it are men who love to purify themselves; and Allah loves those who purify themselves.Qur'an 9:108

The Sheikh smiles at that last argument and calls it an imaginative stretch, though a harmless one. The honest weight rests on the first two reasons. Either way, the year of the Hijrah, opening with Muharram, became the heartbeat of Islamic time, and it became so because the companions, given every other option, kept returning to the one event that had turned their whole world from disgrace to dignity.

The map of the next ten years

Before walking deeper into Madinah, the Sheikh hands us a map. The Makkan era ran thirteen years; the Madinan era will run only ten, and yet the reports preserved for these ten years are roughly three times everything recorded for the first fifty-three years of his life ﷺ. The pace of history is about to triple, and these ten years fall into three clear eras.

The first is the era of consolidation, from the Hijrah to the battle of Ahzab in the fifth year: the slow, dangerous work of removing every threat to a newborn community, within and without, the pagans, the hypocrites, the hostile tribes, and beyond the walls the enmity of Quraysh. Never let the later triumphs fool you into thinking it was ever safe. At Badr the Prophet ﷺ would plead with his Lord through the night: O Allah, if this small band is destroyed, You will not be worshipped on earth again. That is how thin the thread was. Only after Ahzab, when the great alliance that came to erase Madinah broke apart instead, did the tide turn for good. The second is the era of truce, the two and a half years from Hudaybiyyah to the conquest of Makkah, when Islam and its enemies shared the peninsula at peace; the Sheikh underlines a fact the battles tend to bury, that in the era of peace the ummah multiplied fivefold, far more than it ever grew through war. And the third runs from the conquest to his passing ﷺ in Rabi al-Awwal of the eleventh year, when the whole peninsula came into the religion of Allah in waves.

And you see the people entering into the religion of Allah in multitudes,Qur'an 110:2

That verse is the destination of the whole journey. But everything in this chapter belongs to the earliest, most fragile stretch of the first era, the founding weeks, when a homesick, penniless community had first to be made into one people.

Homesick in the city of light

Now return to those first weeks and to a detail the books of seerah are honest enough to keep: the Muhajirun, at first, did not like Madinah. Nothing was wrong with Madinah. Everything was wrong with its not being home. There is no place like home, the Sheikh says; the finest lodging in the world cannot give you what your own lumpy pillow gives you. And the emigrants were not only heartsick; they were physically sick, because greener, wetter Madinah bred fevers that dry Makkah had spared them. Homesickness and illness came down together, and hard.

Aisha, radiyallahu anha, by now in the household of the Prophet ﷺ, went to check on her father after he and Bilal had emigrated, and found both men burning with fever. Abu Bakr, sweating through the worst of it, spoke like a man who feels death at his shoulder, saying that death is nearer to a person than the strap of his own sandal. And Bilal, who had owned almost nothing in Makkah and had been pinned under a rock on its scorching sand for his faith, ached aloud for one more night in its valley among its thorny desert scrub, missing the very thorns of the place that had tortured him because it was home, and his ache turned to anger as he called upon Allah against Shaybah ibn Rabi'ah and Umayyah ibn Khalaf by name, the men who had driven them out. Sit with that picture, the Sheikh says: these were not weak souls but the giants of this religion, and they wept with homesickness raw enough to taste. Loving the place you grew up in is not a flaw in faith; it is part of being human, and the strongest of them felt it.

Aisha carried that heavy scene back to the Prophet ﷺ. He did not answer it with a sermon on gratitude. He raised his hands for all of the Muhajirun at once: O Allah, make Madinah as beloved to us as Makkah, or even more beloved still. He asked for blessing in the city's provision, in its sa' and its mudd, in every scoop of food it measured out. And he asked that its fevers be carried off and cast far away, out to Juhfa, an empty land beyond the city, so that his people would not sicken in it.

And Allah answered. The love of Madinah sank so deep into their hearts that when the Prophet ﷺ traveled away, the companions would count the days until return, and Anas narrates in Bukhari that when he ﷺ saw Madinah rise in the distance he would urge his camel faster, hurrying home. Here the Sheikh sets down the seerah a moment and speaks in his own voice: he lived in Madinah, he says, and its love, its sukoon, the peace that settles on the heart there, is unlike anywhere else on earth. He is convinced, wallahi, that the sweetness every visitor still feels there, and the quiet ache that pulls even at those who have never seen it, is nothing other than this one du'a of the Prophet ﷺ, still being answered fourteen centuries after he raised his hands.

Brothers, chosen by name

Among the very first things the Prophet ﷺ did in Madinah, by one early scholar's reckoning even before the masjid itself was finished, was an act with no real precedent: the mu'akha, the pairing. He took each emigrant and joined him to one of the Ansar and declared the two brothers, not as a figure of speech but so completely that in that first stage the two would inherit from each other, the Ansari heir standing ahead of a man's own blood relatives. Think what that meant for a refugee who had left every coin and every cousin behind in Makkah: he woke up in a strange city with a brother, a household, a place at a table, and a name written into someone's will.

The believers are but brothers, so make settlement between your brothers. And fear Allah that you may receive mercy.Qur'an 49:10

And the pairs were not thrown together at random; they were chosen, one by one, and the Sheikh invites you to test it: take any pairing the books preserve, more than a hundred are known by name, look into the two lives, and the match was made with care. Abu Bakr was paired with Kharijah ibn Zayd, a nobleman of the Ansar. Abdur Rahman ibn Awf, once a wealthy merchant of Makkah and now owning nothing, was paired with Sa'd ibn ar-Rabi'ah, one of the richest businessmen of Madinah, two traders who spoke the same language. Salman al-Farsi was paired with Abu Darda, and the two appear side by side ever after, so close that Salman once made his brother break a voluntary fast to share a meal and lie back down when he rose too early in the night to pray, the easy authority of a real brother. Picture it: the Prophet ﷺ literally sat down, and one after another the men came, and he matched soul to soul, by name; Anas ibn Malik remembered with pride that the pairing was done in their quarter. A true leader, the Sheikh says, does not announce brotherhood in the abstract and walk away; he implements it, person by person, choosing for each newcomer the one companion best fitted to carry him.

Nor was it a single ceremony. The Sheikh corrects a thing many readers of the seerah get wrong: it went on for years. Salman himself did not accept Islam until well after the Hijrah, and was given a brother when he did; when Ja'far ibn Abi Talib returned from Abyssinia in the seventh year, the Prophet ﷺ paired him with Mu'adh ibn Jabal; pairings were still being made after the conquest of Makkah. And from this the Sheikh draws one of the sharpest lessons of the night: the mu'akha is a neglected sunnah, and we should be reviving it. Every convert who walks into a masjid today is a Muhajir without a city, a stranger who knows neither the streets nor the people nor even how to pray, who needs not a friendly handshake but real support, someone assigned to sit with him and teach him until he can stand. It is the founding mechanism by which this religion turned strangers into one body, and the Prophet ﷺ kept it running to the end of his life.

Only one clause of the mu'akha was ever retired. After Badr, Allah revealed in Surah al-Anfal that blood relatives are nearer to one another in the decree of Allah, and inheritance returned to family. Look at the gentleness of the sharia, the Sheikh says: while the Muhajirun had no kin in Madinah, Allah made their brothers their heirs, so no emigrant would die with no one to inherit him; once they married and raised children, He moved inheritance back to blood. The law came down for that founding generation in stages, the same way wine was prohibited in stages, a softness that belonged to them alone, because they were the first Muslims with no community standing yet to lean on. We who come after have the finished example before us, and do not get the staged concessions, because we were handed the whole religion at once.

Half of everything, and a dignified refusal

What the Ansar offered their new brothers has no parallel in history, and the proof is in Sahih Bukhari. They came to the Prophet ﷺ and offered, in earnest, to hand over half of their land, half of the date orchards that were the wealth of Madinah, free and outright. He made du'a for them, and refused. Instead he ruled: the Muhajirun would work in the orchards and the two parties would share the produce. Labor for the newcomers, partnership in place of charity, dignity in place of dependence.

Why turn down half a city handed over for nothing? Because he ﷺ was building a nation, not a dole. Let the emigrant work and earn; guard his self-respect; and close the door Shaytan might one day pry open generations later, the whisper of we gave your fathers everything for free. It was the same spirit that breathed in the first words the city ever heard from him. Abdullah ibn Salam, the foremost rabbi of Madinah and one of the only great Jewish scholars to accept Islam, pressed into the crowd that rushed to see the new arrival, and said afterward, in a report Muslim and Ahmad preserve, that the moment the Prophet's ﷺ face came clear to him he knew this was not the face of a liar; a pure soul can sense a pure soul. And the first instruction he heard from those lips was this: spread the salam, feed people, keep the ties of kinship, and pray in the night while people sleep, and you will enter Jannah in peace. Generosity was the founding law of the city; freeloading was never part of it.

Then comes the scene every student of the seerah carries in his heart. Sa'd ibn ar-Rabi'ah brought his new brother home and laid the whole inventory of his life on the table. I am the wealthiest of the Ansar, he said; take half of my money. I have two orchards; one is yours. And in some narrations he went further still, offering to part with one of his two wives so that this stranger from Makkah could have a household of his own. He meant the word brother with a completeness that ought to stop you mid-sentence.

And Abdur Rahman ibn Awf answered with the other half of iman. May Allah bless you in your wealth and your family, he said. Just show me the way to the marketplace. He went to the suq, bartered, came home with a little butter and wheat, went back the next day, and built. Some while later the Prophet ﷺ caught the scent of perfume on him and teased: have you married? Yes, he said, a woman of the Ansar, with a mahr the weight of a date stone in gold. Then hold a walima, the Prophet ﷺ told him, even if with one sheep. Notice the two faces of one faith, the Sheikh says. One man's iman commanded him to offer everything, down to his own household; the other's commanded him to accept nothing he could earn for himself. Neither freeloaded, neither hoarded. The one gave with his whole heart, and the other, out of his honor and karama, said thank you, and no, and pointed himself toward the market. That, on both sides of the gift, is what real iman looks like.

The home Allah Himself named

When Allah praised the two halves of this new society from above the seven heavens, the Sheikh draws our attention to the exact words He chose. Listen first to what He calls the emigrants.

For the poor emigrants who were expelled from their homes and their properties, seeking bounty from Allah and [His] approval and supporting [the cause of] Allah and His Messenger, [there is also a share]. Those are the truthful.Qur'an 59:8

The poor, al-fuqara. Every Muhajir, even the ones who had been rich, became poor by the very act of hijrah; and Allah binds that poverty in the same breath to the highest of titles, the truthful. Then He turns to the Ansar, and here the Sheikh slows over a single word.

And [also for] those who were settled in the Home [i.e., al-Madinah] and [adopted] the faith before them. They love those who emigrated to them and find not any want in their breasts of what they [i.e., the emigrants] were given but give [them] preference over themselves, even though they are in privation. And whoever is protected from the stinginess of his soul, it is those who will be the successful.Qur'an 59:9

Those who settled ad-dar, the Home. Not their home. Allah does not say their house, though the house was technically theirs; He calls it simply the Home, one bare word wide enough to hold host and guest together, because the Ansar had given half of it away. And the Sheikh notes a thing easy to walk past: in all the Qur'an Allah never once calls the city Yathrib. That old name appears only on the tongues of the hypocrites. The city of the Prophet ﷺ had been renamed from above.

Then the verse testifies to something so rare it is almost frightening: the Ansar found no resentment in their chests at what the emigrants were given, and preferred others over themselves even while they themselves were in need. That is ithar, and Allah swears to its sincerity from over the seven heavens; not only did they give, their hearts were clean as they gave. No society in the history of mankind, the Sheikh says flatly, has been this selfless toward strangers; even blood brothers do not do for one another what these men did for people who walked into their city with nothing.

And it grew so overwhelming that the Muhajirun came to the Prophet ﷺ carrying the strangest worry ever laid before him. O Messenger of Allah, they said, we have never seen a people like these; they share with us in hardship and lavish on us in plenty, until we fear they have carried off all our reward. Notice the beauty of it, the Sheikh says: a complaint wrapped inside a praise. After all the torture of Makkah, the wealth abandoned, the agony of the journey, their fear was not for any of that, but for their deeds, that all of it might be handed to these impossibly generous hosts. And the Prophet ﷺ answered: no, not so long as you keep praising them and making du'a for them. Each keeps his reward whole, for in this religion every soul is repaid for exactly what it has done.

Hold the two verses above in the order Allah placed them, and you have the final balance of the day. Love of the Ansar, the Prophet ﷺ said in what Bukhari and Muslim record, is a sign of iman, and hatred of them a sign of hypocrisy; after the conquest he ﷺ told them that were it not for his own birth and lineage he would have counted himself one of the Ansar, and that if all of mankind walked down one valley and the Ansar walked down another, he would walk the valley of the Ansar. And yet, the Sheikh points out, every time the Qur'an names the two together, the Muhajirun come first; even here, the verse of the emigrants stands one verse ahead of the verse of the helpers. The most honored hosts in history, and the guests still ranked one degree above: that is the weight, in the scale of Allah, of leaving everything behind for His sake.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read past this episode looking for the next battle, and to feel let down that there is no chase in it, no miracle to quicken the pulse. That would be to miss what it is showing you. Strip these first months to their bones and you find three quiet inventions, a calendar, a du'a, and a list of names, and every one of them is still working, and every one puts a question to your own life.

The first is the calendar, and the lesson is in how the companions chose its starting point. Given the Prophet's ﷺ own birth, given the victory at Badr, given every shining option, they passed them all and counted year one from the Hijrah, because that was the year humiliation turned to honor. We tend to date our lives by what was done to us, the loss, the diagnosis, the betrayal, the windfall. The companions dated theirs by the moment they moved toward Allah. Keep time the way they kept it: let the turning points of your life be the migrations of your own heart from sin toward obedience.

The second is the du'a over homesickness, and the lesson is in what the Prophet ﷺ did not do. Abu Bakr and Bilal, two giants of this faith, lay feverish and aching for a home that had abused them, and he did not scold them for ingratitude. He raised his hands and asked Allah to plant love in their hearts where it would not grow on its own, and the answer is still felt by everyone who has ever wept at the sight of Madinah. When a place or a season Allah has decreed for you sits heavy on your heart, do not lecture yourself into contentment and do not pretend the ache away. Name it to Allah, the way Aisha carried it to the Prophet ﷺ, and ask Him to make beloved to you what He has chosen for you.

And the third is the mu'akha, and it asks the most of us, because unlike the calendar and the du'a it is a thing we do with our own hands. That first society held together on two postures of faith facing each other: the open hand of the Ansari who offered half of everything, and the upright honor of the Muhajir who answered, may Allah bless you in it, now show me the marketplace. Real iman knows both. But the Prophet ﷺ did more than praise the spirit; he built the structure, sitting in the dust and pairing strangers into brothers by name. The Sheikh's charge lands in our own masjids tonight: somewhere near you is a new Muslim, a newcomer, a homesick soul with all the loneliness of a Muhajir and no Ansari yet. Do not wait to be asked. Be the one who is assigned, the brother or sister who sits down and stays.

So take these three down into your ordinary days. Date your life by your turning toward Allah and not by what befell you. When a trial weighs on your heart, hand the ache to Allah and ask Him to make it beloved. And find the stranger near you and become his brother, expecting nothing, the way the Ansar expected nothing and were named the successful for it. May Allah make iman beloved to our hearts and beautiful within them as He made Madinah beloved to the ones who wept for Makkah, bind us to one another as He bound the Muhajirun and the Ansar, give us hands that offer like Sa'd and honor that strives like Abdur Rahman, and gather us with His Messenger ﷺ in the Home that no one ever has to leave.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 32 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (21:10, 9:36, 9:37, 9:108, 110:2, 59:8, 59:9, 49:10), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing has been followed.

The Seerah · 33 of 104

The Constitution of Madinah

The first Islamic state, put in writing

The masjid is built. The brotherhood is paired, muhajir to ansari, heart lashed to heart through the houses of the city. And now, in the early months after the hijrah, comes the third act of building Madinah, and unlike the first two it is made of ink. A long document, five pages of it, names every tribe in the new city: the believers who came from Quraysh and the believers of Yathrib, the Jewish clans one by one, even the pagans who had not yet converted. History calls it the Treaty of Madinah, or its Constitution, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi gives it a full evening, because it is significant, and because almost everyone who picks it up manages to misread it. Some inflate it into the secret seed of the American Constitution; others read malice into its clauses. Walk the document itself, the way he walks it, and you find something better than either myth: the first Islamic state, written down.

It is, in one sense, an ordinary administrative act, the kind a city does when it finally has a government, and in another it is one of the quietest wonders in the whole seerah. A man who arrived as a refugee a few months earlier ﷺ takes a city that had bled itself white in civil war for generations and writes its three faiths and forty quarrelsome clans onto a single page as one body, with no army behind the ink and no treasury to enforce it, only the weight of his word and the words of his Lord.

Five pages history almost mislaid

Begin with a strange fact, because the Sheikh begins there. The most important political document of the seerah reaches us without a chain of narrators, and is not even recorded in every early reference book. We all know the most famous book of seerah, the sira of Ibn Ishaq, which most students meet only through Ibn Hisham's later summary; fewer know that Ibn Ishaq is where the full treaty survives, and that he introduces it with nothing more than: it has been narrated to me. Between Ibn Ishaq and the Prophet ﷺ stand a hundred and fifty years, and across that gap there is no connected isnad at all. The Musnad of Imam Ahmad preserves a single line of it with attribution, that the Prophet ﷺ wrote a document between the people of the city in which every group would carry its own debts and affairs, with goodness between them. One phrase, soundly chained; the rest arrives thin, which has led some, then and now, to doubt there was ever a treaty at all.

Here Sheikh Yasir lays out why the scholars of our age accept it anyway, and the reasoning is a small masterclass in how to weigh a source. Look at the language. The text is written in an Arabic so archaic that even in Ibn Ishaq's day, a century and a half on, nobody wrote that way anymore: long, knotted sentences that wander where a modern treaty would march in tidy rows. A forger does not invent in a dead register. Worse for him, the document is cluttered. It does not simply say the Aws and the Khazraj; it walks through some forty or fifty of their subtribes, clan by clan, names so obscure that most have fallen clean out of history, and it handles the Jewish tribes the same way. A man who fabricates a charter makes it smooth, quotable, convenient. Reality is messy, and this is messy. The clutter, the Sheikh argues, is the signature of authenticity. You do not counterfeit a list of forgotten clans.

So he refuses to read it to us clause by crawling clause, and takes it instead in four bundles: what the treaty asks of the Muslims among themselves, what it agrees with the Jewish tribes, what it lays on the pagans who stayed pagan, and what it sets down, last, for everyone in the city at once. Take it in hand that way, in four grips, and a thing that looks like an unreadable inventory turns into the architecture of a state.

One ummah, and the word Yathrib

The first bundle opens with a single sentence that rearranges Arabia. The believers of Quraysh and of Yathrib, and those who follow them and join them and strive alongside them, are one ummah, distinct from all of mankind. One community, set apart from every older loyalty on earth. The Prophet ﷺ is declaring that these people now share a bond no outsider can enter into and no tribe can outrank. He is building the ummah in writing.

Two small words in that opening are themselves proof of how early the parchment is, and the Sheikh lingers on both. It says Yathrib, the old pagan name of the city, the very name the Prophet ﷺ would soon ask the people to retire in favor of Madinah; do not call it Yathrib, he would say, it is Madinah. But a city does not change its name in a day, and a document written in these first months had to speak to people who still had the old word in their mouths. A later forger, writing when everyone said Madinah, would have written Madinah without a second thought. And notice the second tell: it does not yet say the muhajirun, the Emigrants; the word had not yet crystallized, so the text simply says the believers of Quraysh. Two fingerprints, in the first line, of how close to the event this is.

Then the clauses turn practical, because an ummah, however exalted, still has to pay its bills. Each of the forty named subtribes keeps the duties it carried before Islam: its own blood money, the ransom of its own captives, the care of its own poor. Welfare in the Prophet's ﷺ state, the Sheikh points out, was local before it was ever national; a man in need did not appeal to a central office but turned to his own clan. Blood money alone could run to a hundred camels, a fortune no single man could shoulder, so the clan carried it together. The person who falls is carried by the people standing nearest him.

Two more clauses bind the believers to one another, and both reach back into the Prophet's ﷺ own past. All of them, the treaty says, shall stand together against whoever commits injustice, even if the wrongdoer is one of their own. The Sheikh hears in that the echo of an older oath: hilf al-Fudul, the pact of justice the young Muhammad ﷺ had stood in long before prophethood, the league of clans who swore to back the wronged man against the strong even when the strong man was their own. What he had pledged as a youth in Makkah is now law in his city. And then: the protection granted by the Muslims is one. Any believer, even the humblest, can grant a stranger safe passage into Madinah, and once it is given no Muslim on earth may break it. A freed slave can grant it; a child old enough to understand what he is doing can grant it, on the majority view. In the giving of protection, the doorman and the chief hold exactly the same seal.

An ummah alongside the believers

The second bundle belongs to the Jewish tribes, and it refuses to treat them as a faceless bloc. We say there were three Jewish tribes, the Sheikh reminds us, but that is shorthand; inside the three were layers of clans, and the treaty honors them. Clause after clause, around a dozen of them, names a tribe in turn: the Jews of this banu, the Jews of that banu, each by its own name. And over them all stands a line astonishing for its century. These Jews, the document says, are an ummah along with the believers. One community alongside the other.

Sheikh Yasir is careful here, and asks his listeners to be careful with him: do not misquote me, he says. This is not identical standing in every ruling. It is recognized communal standing in one shared city, a status he describes as, in some ways, equivalent. Their religion is their own and the Muslims' religion is the Muslims'. If that, he asks the room, is not the height of equality for its age, then what is? A prophet sent to call all people to one faith writes, into the founding charter of his own state, that the other community's religion belongs to them.

Inside their own walls, that community governs itself. Their disputes, their internal crimes, their own blood money, their quarrels and thefts: their affair, their courts, and the Muslims do not interfere, unless they choose to bring a case to the Prophet ﷺ, or unless the matter crosses the line between the two communities, and then it rises to him. The same financial logic governs them as the Muslim clans: your poor are yours to carry, ours are ours. But let an enemy march on the city and the two ledgers merge. Muslims and Jews are mutual support against whoever makes war on the people of this treaty, and the Jews spend on the defense for as long as the war lasts. Domestic life separate; the defense of the city shared, paid for by both.

Two final clauses complete the picture, and one surprises a modern reader until the Sheikh unpacks it. No one of the Jewish tribes may leave Madinah without the Prophet's ﷺ permission. That sounds severe until you remember what leaving meant. Where you lived was your tribe, your army, your passport, your whole allegiance, all at once; to slip out of the city unannounced was to defect, to carry your loyalty and possibly your intelligence to the other side. So the clause requires what every modern state still requires of a citizen renouncing his citizenship: declare it openly, before the authority. And the last clause guards the other direction: if any Jew chooses, freely, to follow the believers and embrace their faith, he is to be helped and protected, and no injustice done to him. No one may touch a man for the choice he makes about his own soul.

The pagans who stayed pagan

Here is the detail that startles most readers, and the Sheikh names it plainly. In the Prophet's ﷺ own capital, in the first year of the first Islamic state, there were still idol worshippers walking the streets. We forget this. They remained until the Battle of Badr; only when he ﷺ came home victorious did the last of them read the times, see how small a remnant they had become and how the wind had turned, and enter Islam. Until that day they lived in the city as open pagans, and the Constitution legislates for them too.

And here is the part that should stop a person cold: it does not order them to convert. This is the Prophet ﷺ himself, sent to put an end to the very idolatry these men practiced, writing a clause about them, and the clause does not say believe. It says only this: no pagan of Madinah may grant protection to Quraysh, to their persons or their wealth, nor stand between Quraysh and the believers, not for money and not for the old ties of blood. How fair is that, the Sheikh asks. They are idol worshippers in the Muslims' own city, and they are not told to change their religion. What is in your heart is yours, the clause says in effect; keep your faith if you keep it, but your sword and your purse will not serve Makkah's war against us. Believe what you believe. The one thing forbidden is treachery.

This clause would echo down the centuries into a real debate among the jurists: could idol worshippers reside under an Islamic state at all, or only the People of the Book? Everyone agreed the Jews and Christians could stay, and so could the Zoroastrians of Persia. The question was whether the same shelter reached the idolaters. The Hanafi school said yes, and this very treaty is among their proofs; it was precisely this position that later made it workable for Muslim empires, the Mughals among them, to govern entire lands full of them. The Sheikh weighs in without hedging: the broader reading seems to him the stronger case, that under the Shariah the freedom to remain upon your own faith is open to anybody. This clause is where that breadth begins.

A sanctuary with four corners

The final bundle belongs to everyone in the city at once, and it has the feel of a foundation being poured. First, the Prophet ﷺ declares the interior of Yathrib a haram for the people of this treaty, sacred ground the way Makkah is sacred, where things permitted everywhere else become forbidden the moment you cross in. No weapon drawn from its sheath. No wild tree torn down. No game hunted within the lines. And he did not leave the lines to anyone's imagination; he drew them himself, marking four corners around the city: two small mountains to the north and south, and the two great black volcanic plains, the harratayn, to east and west, places the people of Madinah still point to and name today. Inside those four markers, the city is sanctuary.

Second, and this is the keystone of everything raised afterward, a single court of last resort. Whatever dispute breaks out among the peoples of this treaty that threatens to tear the city open shall be referred to Allah and to His Messenger ﷺ. Muslim against Jew, Jew against pagan, pagan against Muslim: when a quarrel grows large enough to endanger the whole, it goes to one address. Pause on that, the Sheikh says, because it is the bedrock under the entire structure. An Islamic state, whatever else it becomes, is at its root a place where the gravest quarrels submit to revelation, where the final word is not a chief's or a faction's but Allah's and His Messenger's ﷺ.

Third, the price of betrayal, and the document turns to thunder. No believer who has agreed to this treaty, and who believes in Allah and the Last Day, may aid or shelter anyone who rises against it; whoever does carries the curse of Allah, of the angels, and of all mankind until the Day of Judgment, and no good deed will be accepted from him. Madinah will be conspired against in the years to come, and this clause is the city's immune system written in advance. Then the treaty exhales into its closing line: whoever stays in Madinah is safe, and whoever leaves is safe, except the one who commits injustice or sin. Come in peace, go in peace, so long as you have committed no crime. And the seal at the very end gathers it all up: Allah protects the pious and the righteous, and Muhammad ﷺ is His Messenger.

Two ways to misread a treaty

Now set the parchment down and deal with its readers, because this document attracts myths from two opposite directions, and the Sheikh handles each in turn. The first inflates it. In the 1980s a paper by a prominent American Muslim academic made the rounds and became, for a while, genuinely famous in Muslim circles, claiming that this treaty was the basis of the American Constitution, that Thomas Jefferson took his very idea of America from it. The Sheikh does not pretend to find this charming. He names it for what he believes it is: a symptom of an inferiority complex, the reflex that cannot admire anything good in the world without rushing to insist that we did it first. It is not merely unacademic, he says; it makes us a laughingstock, because the documents are so plainly different that the claim collapses the moment a serious reader examines it. And the sober truth is grand enough on its own. The treaty was genuinely novel, genuinely unique in its world, and some of its concepts did, over long centuries, travel west and slowly evolve into other things, by what he wryly calls twenty degrees of separation. That is influence. It is not authorship, and we embarrass the truth when we overstate it.

The second myth blackens it, and this charge the Sheikh will not dodge, because among many non-Muslims it has hardened past accusation into assumed fact: that the Prophet ﷺ dealt with the Jews in bad faith from the start, and that this treaty is the opening move, the proof that he set out to wall them off and load them down. The answer sits in the text itself.
