Every single obligation the treaty places on the Jewish tribes rests, literally word for word, on the Muslims as well: carry your own poor, pay your own blood money, ransom your own captives, spend on the city's defense. The same duties, the same protections, and on top of them a recognition no power of that age offered a religious minority anywhere, that they were an ummah alongside the believers, their faith and their courts their own. Read the document and the slander has nowhere to stand.

Then comes the verdict that the next stretch of the seerah will put to the test, and the Sheikh states it carefully. Had the Jewish tribes lived by this treaty, they of all people would have risen with the city. They were its tradesmen and jewelers, its craftsmen and financiers; Madinah's flourishing would have been their flourishing. What happened instead, as the coming chapters will show, is that the agreement was broken, and broken from their side, at its single clearest line: do not side with the pagans against us. So when consequences eventually came, the Sheikh is emphatic that we understand why: for what was done, never for who they were. From our perspective, he says, there is no anti-Semitism in any of it; people were dealt with according to their deeds, not their blood. Hold that distinction, he asks, every time this subject returns, and he promises to take those hard chapters head-on, and frankly, when they arrive.

What one word holds

Step back from the clauses and the deepest revolution in the whole document comes into focus, and it hides inside a single word. Arabia ran on one operating system, ancient and total: you are who your father was. Lineage decided your protector, your rank, your debts, and your grave. This parchment quietly pulls that system out by the root and sets another in its place. The ummah is entered by faith, not by birth; whoever accepts Islam becomes a full member the moment he does, and no chief, no pedigree, no gatekeeper stands in between. Bilal, radiyallahu anhu, a freed slave, and Abu Bakr, radiyallahu anhu, of the noblest lineage in Makkah, stand inside it at precisely the same rank. We take that so completely for granted now, fourteen centuries downstream, that we cannot feel how strange it was. Nobody, the Sheikh says, had ever organized human beings by theology before. Belonging had always been an accident of birth; here it becomes a matter of belief.

And the word chosen to carry this new belonging repays a minute of Arabic, the kind of minute Sheikh Yasir loves to spend. Ummah grows out of umm, mother, as even a non-Arab can hear, but the root runs deeper still, down past mother to a meaning older than both: to aim for, to head toward, to intend a destination. The same root gives us imam, the one who stands in front, toward whom everyone turns and whom everyone follows. A newborn has exactly one direction, one object that gathers all its attention and all its love, and that is the umm. An ummah, then, is a people gathered so completely upon one direction that they might as well share a single mother, a single source they all come from and all face toward. And the Qur'an stretches the word wide enough to lay it over one man alone. Of Ibrahim, who stood against an entire idol-worshipping world by himself, it says:

Indeed, Abraham was a [comprehensive] leader, devoutly obedient to Allāh, inclining toward truth, and he was not of those who associate others with Allāh.Qur'an 16:120

One man, named an ummah, because one chest carried what whole nations are built out of. That is the word written across the top of Madinah's Constitution: not a wary federation of bloodlines holding its breath between wars, but a mother-bond of believers all aimed in one direction, with room inside its walls even for those who had not yet joined it.

Masjid, brotherhood, constitution

So what kind of state did the document actually build? The Sheikh draws its shape before he closes, and three features stand out. It is a state with a head: the treaty makes official what everyone already understood, that the man invited from Makkah, first as a mere arbiter between exhausted factions, is now the accepted ruler of Madinah, the address to which every cross-communal dispute must finally come. It is a state with light hands: each community runs its own internal affairs and keeps its own courts, even its own inherited customs, for the Prophet ﷺ preserved the accepted norms of each tribe wherever they did not collide with revelation, the principle of urf that would become one of the five great maxims of fiqh. Defense and the highest justice belong to the center; almost everything else stays local. Some moderns call it a federal system, and the Sheikh allows the comparison cautiously, but the structure is honestly there: autonomy below, unity above, a government that interfered remarkably little. And it is a state with one law for all: crime is crime, whoever commits it; no one may shelter a criminal; and being a Muslim, he says pointedly, gets nobody off the hook.

Two absences are worth naming, because they too are marks of its honesty. There is no jizya in it, for the simple reason of chronology: the verses legislating it would not come down for years, so the treaty neither contradicts the later ruling nor pretends to anticipate it, one more quiet proof that no one wrote it back into the record afterward. And there is no compulsion anywhere in it. Under the order this document founds, non-Muslims kept their worship, their family law, their own courts; classical fiqh let them keep even their wine among themselves, the Sheikh notes to a slightly startled room, the jurists ruling that a Muslim husband could not forbid his Christian wife what her own religion permitted her. Known limits sat around that freedom, chiefly that one did not proselytize among the Muslims, but the freedom itself was wider than the ancient world had ever thought to offer.

He cannot resist the historical irony here, and it answers a charge thrown at this religion in our own century. The very civilization that calls Islam intolerant spent those same centuries hunting down its own dissenters. From Constantine onward the church fixed a single creed and outlawed every other Christianity, including those nearer to the truth that held Jesus to be the Messiah and not the Son of God; Arius, whose belief sounded strikingly close to Islam, was driven south, it is said as far as Abyssinia, which is how the rulers there came to know a faith so near to ours. Through the medieval centuries, the Sheikh says without exaggeration, the church killed its own in numbers that run into the millions, long before Luther, who himself had to run for his life. And when John Locke, the very philosopher that Jefferson and Franklin and Adams revered, wanted to shame Christendom into tolerance around 1689, he pointed east: look at the Turks, who let creed live beside creed among them. By the testimony of Islam's own critics, the working model of religious coexistence in that world traced back to the order this treaty founded, which the Ottomans would carry for centuries as the millet system, each community judged by its own law.

And then the episode steps back, and the first year in Madinah snaps into a sequence so deliberate it reads like architecture. First the Prophet ﷺ built a masjid: the compass, the daily reminder of why anyone is alive at all. Then he built the brotherhood, pairing emigrant to helper: the hearts. Then he wrote this treaty, fixing the place of every soul in the city, Muslim and Jew and pagan alike: the order. A mosque, a family, a law. On those three foundations the first Islamic state stood up, and the rest of the seerah is the long account of what it did.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to walk past this day. Thirty-three days into the story, the seerah hands us not a battle and not a miracle but a legal document in difficult Arabic, and the temptation is to file it as administration and hurry on. That would be to miss the questions it puts to us.

The first is about who counts as our family. The treaty's opening clause makes belief, not blood, the bond: one ummah, distinct from all of mankind, and whoever enters the faith enters the whole of it at full rank, the freed slave beside the noble. We say we believe this. But the old operating system, you are who your people are, is not dead; it has only changed its vocabulary, and it still whispers that this believer is more truly ours for his passport or his accent, and that one less so. The Prophet ﷺ wrote the antidote into the first line of his city's constitution. Treat every believer as a member of one mother-bond, and let the differences the world is so loud about fall where they belong, beneath the bond.

The second is about whether we will hold one law even against ourselves. The believers, the treaty says, stand together against the oppressor even when he is one of their own. This is the precise opposite of the instinct it was written to bury, the tribal reflex that defends a wrong because the wrongdoer is mine. Justice that bends for our own people is not justice; it is tribalism wearing justice as a mask. Ask, the next time loyalty pulls one way and truth the other, which one the Prophet ﷺ legislated for, and you already know.

The third is about the quiet confidence of a faith that does not need to coerce. He left the idol worshippers of his own capital upon their religion. The man sent to end idolatry, with a city behind him, did not force a single one of them to the shahada; he asked only that they keep out of the war and deal in good faith, and trusted the truth to do its own slow work, which it did, after Badr, when they came of their own accord. Coercion is the move of someone who suspects the truth cannot win an honest argument, and he never suspected that.

And the last thing this day asks is the simplest, and it reaches you exactly where you live, because you too live under agreements: contracts and marriages, citizenships and friendships, the small unwritten treaty of being someone's neighbor. The first Islamic state was built out of kept words. A man with no army and no treasury took feuding tribes and three faiths and forty restless clans and made them one body, and the only thing holding the page together was that his word could be relied upon and his Lord's word stood above it. So take that down into your ordinary days. Make your given word a safe country for whoever holds it. Stand for one law even when it costs you your own side. Carry the people nearest you before you reach for distant causes. And let your certainty be quiet enough that it never needs to force anyone. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, who made one ummah out of strangers, bind our scattered hearts the way he bound his city, make our given word a shelter and not a snare, and gather this ummah under his banner on the Day the nations are gathered.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 33: the treaty and constitution of Madinah (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translation is from Sahih International (16:120), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry the treaty without a connected chain, the Sheikh's own weighing of its language and authenticity has been followed.

The Seerah · 34 of 104

The Change of the Qiblah

The morning every prayer turned home

For about a year and a half in Madinah, every single prayer began with a quiet ache. The Prophet ﷺ would lift his hands, say the takbir, and turn his face north toward Bayt al-Maqdis in Jerusalem, exactly as Allah had commanded him. And the house he loved most on earth, the Ka'bah his father Ibrahim had raised, the first house ever built for the worship of Allah, lay directly behind his back. Not off to one side. Behind him. He never complained about it, and he never once asked out loud to have it changed. He simply carried the ache, prayer after prayer, and waited.

This day is about a direction, but it was never really about geography. Dr. Yasir Qadhi walks through it as one of the great turning points of the Madinan period, and what he uncovers is not a story about which way to point the body. It is a story about what a command is for, about a silent du'a made to the night sky with no witnesses at all, about the first time in the history of Islam that Allah lifted one ruling and put another in its place, and about the tenderness of a Lord who sometimes phrases His decree not as a bare order but as a gift.

A year and a half facing Jerusalem

After the treaty of Madinah, the forward-thinking document that bound the city's communities by faith and covenant rather than by blood and tribe, the seerah goes quiet for a while. The early books, as the Sheikh keeps reminding us, are a series of snapshots rather than a diary; not every day is recorded, and for seven or eight months after the treaty nothing major is preserved. Then comes the next great incident: the change of the qiblah, which probably occurred around fifteen to sixteen months after the hijrah, a year and a half or a little less into the new life of the city.

From the very beginning of the prayer, the command had been to face Jerusalem, and here Dr. Yasir Qadhi notes something easy to miss: that command had never been written in the Qur'an at all. It came through the Sunnah, through revelation Allah gave His Prophet ﷺ outside the recited text. Jerusalem was the holy land of the children of Israel, and the yahood of that age were, with the Christians after them, the largest community then worshipping the one God on earth. For them the holiest land had always been Bayt al-Maqdis. They barely knew Makkah, and to this day they do not regard it as sacred, because the sanctity of Makkah was a thing Allah had blessed Ibrahim and Ismail with, not their fathers.

The true rank of the Ka'bah had stayed, in a sense, a secret kept among the prophets themselves. By the Prophet's ﷺ own report more than seventy prophets made the pilgrimage to the Ka'bah, because Allah had told them of it and commanded them to perform hajj, though their nations never followed them there. Asked once which masjid was built first on earth, he ﷺ answered: al-Masjid al-Haram. And the next? al-Masjid al-Aqsa. And how long between them? Forty years. Who laid the foundations of the second, whether it was Ismail or someone after him, Allah knows best; the temple that Sulayman would later build came many centuries afterward. The first house was Makkah, and Allah wished, through His final Prophet ﷺ, to restore that first house to its rightful place.

In Makkah, a believer could hold both houses at once. The books record that whenever the Prophet ﷺ prayed there, he would deliberately position himself on the side of the Ka'bah that faced Jerusalem, so that a single bowing gathered two qiblahs into one. He could face Jerusalem, as commanded, and still have the Ka'bah directly in front of him. The hijrah broke that quiet geometry. Madinah lies due north of Makkah, and Jerusalem due north of Madinah, all on one straight line, so that to face Jerusalem from Madinah now meant turning a full hundred and eighty degrees away from the house of Ibrahim. The same command that in Makkah had let him face both houses at once now forced his back upon the one he loved.

Like they know their own sons

So why carry that ache for a year and a half at all? Because Madinah held an audience that Makkah never had. The Quraysh owned no scripture and could not even say what a prophet was; when the message came to them they would ask, in genuine bewilderment, what is a prophet, what is a book, we have never heard of such things. But the yahood of Madinah knew both. Here at last were thousands of people who believed in one God, in a chain of prophets, in revealed books. Anyone who has tried to convey faith, the Sheikh says from his own experience, knows the difference between speaking to those who share that framework and those who have no concept of it at all.

And the Qur'an says of their learned ones that they knew him ﷺ the way a parent knows a child:

Those to whom We gave the Scripture know him as they know their own sons. But indeed, a party of them conceal the truth while they know [it].Qur'an 2:146

Only a parent fully understands that verse. You pick out your own child across a crowded field at any distance, in any clothing; you know his cry in a noisy room without turning your head. That is how surely their own books had taught the rabbis to recognize the man now living among them. So the Prophet ﷺ had every earthly reason to be hopeful. Their foremost rabbi, Abdullah ibn Salam, had already embraced Islam. And in those first months the Prophet ﷺ deliberately kept his outward habits close to the People of the Book, wishing to resemble them where revelation allowed, to ease the road between them and make them feel that here was something familiar, not foreign.

But when it became clear that most of them knew the truth and were determined to refuse it anyway, that their refusal was arrogance and not ignorance, the instruction began to reverse. Now be different from them. They pray barefoot, so pray in your shoes. They part their hair on one side, so part yours on the other. They withdraw from their wives completely during menstruation, so sit with your wives, eat with them, share everything but intimacy itself. A new ummah was being given its own face, distinct from those who had come before, and the single greatest mark of that distinction was still on its way. It would be the qiblah.

The du'a he made to the night sky

He ﷺ longed to face Makkah. But a command of Allah stood, and he would not bend it by a hair on his own authority. And here the Sheikh dwells on a scene that ought to be sat with slowly. It is narrated that once, when Jibril came down with some revelation, the Prophet ﷺ confided in him his hope: I wish that I were turned toward Makkah. And Jibril answered him: I am only a slave, just like you. I come only with the command of Allah. I cannot do this for you. If you want it, ask Allah.

Stop and picture that exchange. The most blessed human being ever to walk the earth is speaking with the most blessed inhabitant of the heavens, and each one says the same thing: this is not in my hands, take it to Allah. How utterly lost, then, the Sheikh says, is anyone who directs his du'a or his worship to a prophet or an angel, when the Prophet and the angel both pointed the asking past themselves to Allah alone. If the greatest of the angels could not grant the wish of the greatest of mankind, then every raised hand in creation belongs before Allah and before no one beneath Him.

So he asked. Not from the minbar, not before the companions, but at night, when no one was watching, lifting his face again and again toward the sky. This was the posture of his most desperate pleading. Ordinarily in du'a he would lower his head, but on the rarest and gravest occasions, when the situation truly called for it, he would raise his face to the heavens and implore, as he would later do openly on the morning of Badr. This du'a, though, had no witnesses at all. He made it alone in the dark. And then the entire ummah came to know of it forever, recited until the end of time, because Allah Himself announced it from above the seven heavens:

We have certainly seen the turning of your face, [O Muhammad], toward the heaven, and We will surely turn you to a qiblah with which you will be pleased. So turn your face toward al-Masjid al-Haram. And wherever you [believers] are, turn your faces toward it [in prayer]. Indeed, those who have been given the Scripture well know that it is the truth from their Lord. And Allah is not unaware of what they do.Qur'an 2:144

Read the wording slowly, the Sheikh urges, because the wording is the whole point. Allah had already decreed that the qiblah would move; He did not need to mention His Prophet's ﷺ longing at all. He could have issued a bare command. Instead He opens with the longing itself: We have seen the turning of your face toward the sky, and We will give you a qiblah you are pleased with, tardaha, one that will please you. Not merely an order, but an order wrapped inside a gift. The Lord of the worlds is saying to His Messenger ﷺ: I saw what you wanted, and because you wanted it, I am giving it to you. The change was already written. The tenderness was that Allah chose to frame it as a present for a beloved.

The morning the rows turned

It happened in Rajab or Sha'ban of the second year, a month or two before Badr. And here Dr. Yasir Qadhi pauses to correct a story almost everyone has heard. The familiar image is of Jibril descending while the Prophet ﷺ leads the prayer in his own masjid, and the whole congregation rotating around him in the middle of a rak'ah. That, the Sheikh says plainly, is a myth, a later confusion. The authentic reports are simpler and quieter. The command came down in the early morning. The Prophet ﷺ announced it. He prayed one prayer facing Jerusalem and the next facing Makkah, and from that day forward the front of his masjid became the back and the back became the front, and he prayed toward the Ka'bah until he passed away ﷺ.

The dramatic mid-prayer turning did happen, just not where people imagine. After the Prophet ﷺ prayed the new way, a man who had been with him set off back across the city. By the time he reached the masjid of the tribe of Banu Salamah, that congregation was already standing in prayer, still facing Jerusalem, because the news had not yet reached them. So he called out from the back: I have just come from the Messenger's ﷺ masjid, I prayed with him facing Makkah, the command has come down. And they turned where they stood. The imam walked straight through the rows to the opposite end, every worshipper rotated in place, and the prayer carried on unbroken.

That mosque still stands, and it carries the name the moment earned it: Masjid al-Qiblatayn, the mosque of the two qiblahs. The Sheikh notes, with evident warmth, that it sits within walking distance of the Islamic University of Madinah, where he himself studied for ten years; you leave the university, turn left at the roundabout, and there it is. Not a legend on a page but an address you can walk to.

The first abrogation, and the letters inside it

The turning set the whole city talking, and Allah had warned His Prophet ﷺ in advance that it would:

The foolish among the people will say, "What has turned them away from their qiblah, which they used to face?" Say, "To Allah belongs the east and the west. He guides whom He wills to a straight path."Qur'an 2:142

One of the yahood scoffed: what kind of prophet faces one direction today and the opposite one tomorrow, changing his mind back and forth? Another took offense: was our qiblah not good enough for him? And for the Muslims themselves the confusion ran deeper still, because this was the first time in Islam that Allah had revealed a ruling and then replaced it with another. The scholars call it naskh, abrogation, and the qiblah was its very first case. It was, the Sheikh says, a mini crisis for a young community encountering a concept entirely new to them.

So al-Baqarah, the first of the great surahs revealed in the Madinan period, taught the ummah what abrogation actually is:

We do not abrogate a verse or cause it to be forgotten except that We bring forth [one] better than it or similar to it. Do you not know that Allah is over all things competent?Qur'an 2:106

Nothing is ever lifted, the verse says, except that Allah brings something better, or at least its like. The exchange never runs downward. The classic example the Sheikh gives is the waiting period of a widow: the Qur'an first set it at one full year, then later revealed the four months and ten days that we know today. Both verses are still recited; only one is implemented. That is not a contradiction, for there is no contradiction in the Book of Allah. It is legislation in motion, a Lord raising His people stage by stage. Ahl as-Sunnah affirm abrogation, with more than a dozen authentic cases in the books, even as some later groups denied the concept entirely and tried to reinterpret every such verse away. And abrogation, the Sheikh notes, does not only run from Qur'an to Qur'an. There are four possibilities; revelation can abrogate revelation across its two forms. The Jerusalem command had come through the Sunnah, so Islam's very first instance of abrogation was the Qur'an lifting a ruling that the Sunnah had carried. Two forms of revelation, one Lord behind both.

And folded inside that single command sat sealed letters, a different message for every reader. To the yahood it spoke firmly and yet fairly. For a year and a half this Prophet ﷺ faced your qiblah, which is proof that he stands within the very line of your own prophets and not outside it; he venerates what your prophets told you to venerate. The Sheikh is exact here, because fairness demands it: the Qur'an itself states that Allah preferred the children of Israel over the worlds of their era. They truly were a chosen people. Were. Some Muslims, in their anger, deny even this, but the Qur'an does not, and the emphasis falls on the tense. The turning announced that the season of one nation's exclusivity had now closed, and the trust was returning to the original house, the first ever raised for the worship of Allah.

The yahood answered in the language of law, which the Sheikh observes is where the emphasis of some of them lay, on observance more than on the inner life of the spirit. They said that piety itself is to face Jerusalem, and that whoever faces elsewhere has nothing accepted from him. To this day they pray toward it, and many of the most ancient churches, the Orthodox and Eastern traditions, still build and pray eastward. And Allah's reply to this stands among the most majestic ayat in the whole Book:

Righteousness is not that you turn your faces toward the east or the west, but [true] righteousness is [in] one who believes in Allah, the Last Day, the angels, the Book, and the prophets and gives wealth, in spite of love for it, to relatives, orphans, the needy, the traveler, those who ask [for help], and for freeing slaves; [and who] establishes prayer and gives zakah; [those who] fulfill their promise when they promise; and [those who] are patient in poverty and hardship and during battle. Those are the ones who have been true, and it is those who are the righteous.Qur'an 2:177

Righteousness was never about which horizon your face points to. It is faith, and wealth given away while you still love it, and the prayer established, and the promise kept, and patience when patience costs the most. The direction serves the heart; the heart never serves the direction. And the Prophet ﷺ was consoled in the same passage: bring the People of the Book every sign and they will not follow your qiblah, nor will you follow theirs, nor do they even follow one another's, for the Jews have one and the Christians another. Do not be troubled, then; you have your qiblah and they have theirs, and Allah has chosen the best for you. The Quraysh received their letter too, and it cut deep. The man they had expelled from Makkah now honored its house more than they ever had, making it the center of his worship from exile while they ringed it with idols. The qiblah named them unfit to be custodians of the Ka'bah, and named the one ﷺ who would inherit it and restore the dignity it had held in the days of Ibrahim.

One ayah, three answers

Then came a question that only sincere believers would ever think to ask. The prayer had been obligatory for some years, and in the time since perhaps ten of the companions had already died in Madinah of natural causes, since there had been no battle yet. Every prayer of their entire lives had been offered toward Jerusalem. They never once faced the new qiblah. So the companions came to the Prophet ﷺ troubled: what about them? What about our brothers who have died? Will their salah be accepted? Are their prayers lost?

The answer came down inside one of the tenderest arguments in the whole Qur'an:

And thus We have made you a median [i.e., just] community that you will be witnesses over the people and the Messenger will be a witness over you. And We did not make the qiblah which you used to face except that We might make evident who would follow the Messenger from who would turn back on his heels. And indeed, it is difficult except for those whom Allah has guided. And never would Allah have caused you to lose your faith. Indeed Allah is, to the people, Kind and Merciful.Qur'an 2:143

Notice what Allah called those prayers. Not your salah. Your iman. Never would Allah let your faith be wasted. Imam al-Bukhari hung an entire chapter heading on exactly this point, the Sheikh notes, and Imam Ahmad and generations of classical scholars drew the weighty conclusion from it: Allah called their prayer their faith, which means that without the prayer there is no faith, that the prayer is not an accessory bolted onto iman but part of the substance iman is made of. One classical scholar filled a whole book on this single question, gathering some twenty-two ayat and dozens of ahadith. It is a position Dr. Yasir Qadhi holds firmly and states plainly: a Muslim must keep some living relationship with the prayer, must at some point be falling into sujud before Allah, for by the Prophet's ﷺ own words the one who utterly abandons the prayer has walked out of the faith itself. And yet, with all that weight, see the mercy: the companions feared for their dead, and Allah did not lecture them, He reassured them. He does not discard sincerity offered in good faith on a technicality.

Folded into the very middle of the same ayah came an identity for the new community: thus We have made you an ummatan wasatan, witnesses over mankind. Most readers hear wasat and think middle, or moderate, and the Sheikh grants that this meaning is real, but he insists it is secondary. In its oldest classical usage, wasat named the summit. You speak of the wasat of the mountain meaning its peak, which is at once its highest point and, on a mountain's slope, its middle. When the Qur'an tells of the brothers who lost their garden in Surah al-Qalam, the one it calls their awsat, the brother who had urged them to glorify Allah, is not the middle child but the best and the wisest of them. So hear the ayah as its first listeners heard it: I have made you the summit of the nations, the highest in stature and honor and character. And to the summit nation Allah gave the summit qiblah, the first house, the qiblah of your father Ibrahim. The best direction for the best ummah, and the perfect moment to demonstrate it, precisely when they would no longer face the qiblah of the yahood nor of the Christians.

And the same ayah states the test without flinching: We did not make the first qiblah except to make evident who would follow the Messenger ﷺ and who would turn back on his heels, and Allah Himself admits it was hard, difficult except for those whom Allah has guided. Every commandment, the Sheikh says, is a test, every single one. The content of the command changes from age to age. The question inside it never does. When Allah says turn, do you turn?

The shelter, and the first university of Islam

The turning rearranged the masjid itself, and out of that rearrangement came one of the most beautiful institutions of the entire seerah. What had been the front wall, the old Jerusalem-facing wall, was now the back. And over that retired qiblah wall the Prophet ﷺ ordered a shaded covering raised. Hold that detail against another one the Sheikh sets beside it: his own masjid had no roof at all in those years, and would not have a proper one for years to come; when it rained the worshippers were rained upon, and only later was a leaking cover of date palms laid over part of it. The first roof the Prophet ﷺ built, he built for the poor.

Beneath that shade gathered the men history remembers as Ahl as-Suffah, the people of the shelter. They were muhajirun who had arrived with nothing, new converts, travelers come to learn Islam, living inside the masjid because the homes of the Ansar, for all their famous generosity, had finally run out of room. Up until this point every newcomer could be taken in by some household, but eventually the Ansar were saturated, and so the shelter was built. It seems to have taken shape in these very weeks, shortly before Badr; a man or two are counted on both lists, people of the Suffah and people of Badr.

Their poverty is hard for us even to imagine. Many of them had no proper lower garment at all. There is a hadith in Bukhari in which the Prophet ﷺ instructed the praying women not to raise their heads from sujud until the men had fully sat upright, to spare the men of the Suffah their exposure, because they did not own enough clothing to cover themselves in prostration. And he ﷺ carried these men on his heart constantly. When his grandson Hasan was born, he told Fatimah, radiyallahu anha, to send charity to the people of the Suffah. And once, when she came to him worn out by housework and asked for a single servant from a newly arrived group of captives, he refused the most beloved person on earth to him, and the refusal trembles with the weight of his concern: how can I give you a servant while the people of the Suffah double over with empty stomachs? By Allah, I would sooner sell these captives and spend every coin of the price on them. She never asked again. In his house, the Sheikh observes, nearness of blood bought no privilege, not even for the daughter he loved beyond anyone.

And the city learned to love them as he did. One companion had a simple idea: hang a string between two pillars of the shelter, and whenever the Ansar brought in a date harvest, let the first of it be hung on the string, so that the hungry could come and eat without ever having to beg. The Prophet ﷺ approved it, and it was done. The Sheikh's own elder teachers told him that food was still being hung in the Prophet's ﷺ masjid for those who could not afford it as recently as the 1940s and 1950s. One quiet idea between two companions, and the custom of feeding the poor without humiliating them outlived its own century by more than a thousand years.

But do not picture the Suffah as a mere poorhouse, the Sheikh insists, because it became something the world had never seen before. It was the first university of Islam, with the Prophet ﷺ as its teacher and the masjid floor as its dormitory. Its residents were not only the destitute; some of the Ansar who owned perfectly good homes moved into it anyway, simply to live beside him ﷺ and his teaching, because everything happened in the masjid and he was in the masjid far more than he was in his own house. Its population breathed with the seasons, five or ten men at times, as many as seventy at others, most of them staying for weeks or months. When believers arrived from distant tribes needing to learn the Qur'an and the prayer, the Prophet ﷺ would assign one of the residents to teach the newcomer, and the graduate would carry the religion home to his people.

Its most famous resident reached Madinah only in the seventh year, after Khaybar: a man from Yemen the whole world now knows as Abu Hurairah, radiyallahu anhu. He stayed close to the Prophet ﷺ long enough to out-narrate every other companion, and when a later generation wondered how a latecomer could possibly relate more hadith than the earliest believers, he answered them himself in a report in Bukhari and Muslim. Were it not that Allah condemned those who conceal knowledge, he said, I would not have narrated a single hadith to you. My brothers among the muhajirun were busy with their trade in the markets, and my brothers among the Ansar were busy with their farms, and I stuck to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ with my hungry stomach, present for what they missed, memorizing what they could not. He was not even truly poor: his mother bought a house in Madinah, and he chose the shelter over it, because to remain in the masjid was to remain with the Prophet ﷺ. He admits in another report that he would sometimes ask a companion a question he already knew the answer to, just to draw out the conversation and walk beside the man to his doorstep, hoping to be invited in for a meal, too dignified to beg.

One day the Prophet ﷺ found him collapsed by the road, faint with hunger, and brought him home, where a neighbor had gifted a single cup of milk. Then came the instruction that sank Abu Hurairah's heart: go and call the people of the Suffah. He obeyed, though everything in him recoiled, and gathered the whole shelter, dozens of men, and served the cup to each in turn, watching them drink their fill and certain that nothing whatever would be left for him. When every one of them had drunk, only he and the Prophet ﷺ remained. Then the Prophet ﷺ said: sit. And: drink. And again: drink. And again, until Abu Hurairah protested that by the One who had sent him with the truth, there was no room left in him, and only then did the Prophet ﷺ take the cup and drink last of all. He had known his student's hunger the entire time. It was, the Sheikh says, the closest thing to a loving practical joke: did you really think I would let you starve?

Abu Hurairah would one day govern Bahrain and live comfortably, because the Suffah was a season of sacrifice for the sake of knowledge, not a permanent vow of poverty, and the Sheikh is careful to note that the later mystics who derived a whole spirituality of destitution from the word are mistaken even about its origin. And the shelter's final gift came after the Prophet ﷺ was gone. In the wars of the riddah, so many of the Suffah's Qur'an memorizers fell fighting Musaylimah, more than seventy by one report, that Umar pressed Abu Bakr to gather the Qur'an into a single compiled book before its living carriers were lost. The people of the Suffah were the people of the Qur'an, and the mushaf that sits on the shelf today descends, through that compilation, from the students of that shaded wall.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read the change of the qiblah as a piece of liturgical history, a ruling that changed long ago and need not trouble us now, and to move on. That would be to miss every question the day puts to us. Sit with it a little longer, and it reaches into the most ordinary corners of a believing life.

Take the longing first, and where he ﷺ took it. He did not change the command on his own, and he did not even ask another creature to change it for him. When he confided his hope to Jibril, the greatest of the angels said, I am only a slave like you, ask Allah. If the most blessed inhabitant of the heavens could not grant the wish of the most blessed of mankind, then there is no hand worth raising your du'a to but the hand of Allah. And see where the answer came from: he made the du'a at night, alone, with no witness on earth, and Allah answered it from above the seven heavens in verses recited until the Day of Judgment. The ache you have never said out loud, the one you have told no one, is already seen. He does not need to hear it from your tongue to know it is in your heart, and He may answer it more tenderly than you would ever have dared to ask.

Then take the test that lives inside every command. The ayah says the first qiblah existed only to make evident who would follow the Messenger ﷺ and who would turn back on his heels, and Allah Himself admitted the turning was hard. Every instruction since has carried the same quiet question folded inside it. The thing commanded changes; the question never does. When the prayer arrives and you are tired, when the lawful is plain and the unlawful is sweet, when He says turn and turning costs you something, do you turn? That is the whole of it. The qiblah was the body's version of a turn the heart is asked to make a hundred times a day.

And then take the mercy that ran underneath the test. The companions feared for the prayers of their dead, every one of them offered toward the old qiblah in perfect sincerity, and Allah did not call those prayers wasted. He called them iman, and swore He would never let them be lost. Nothing you have ever offered to Allah in good faith, under a ruling you believed was His, is discarded when the ruling changes. He is, the same ayah says, Kind and Merciful to the people. The God who tested them with the turning is the God who reassured them about their dead in the very same breath, and He named their salah their faith, so that the scholars built an entire doctrine on that one word: guard the prayer as though it were your iman, because it is.

So carry one turning out of this day and down into your ordinary hours. Five times today you will turn your body toward the house of Ibrahim, the first house ever raised for the worship of Allah. Let the heart make the same turn that the body makes: away from whatever it had been facing, toward the One who sees the longing you never speak, who never wastes a sincere deed, and who sometimes, to a slave He loves, phrases His command as a gift. May Allah make us among those who turn wherever He commands, keep our faces and our hearts toward His house, accept every prayer we have ever offered Him in good faith, and never let what we did for His sake be lost. And may He send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, the one He turned toward a qiblah that pleased him.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 34: the change of the qiblah and abrogation in the Qur'an (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (2:146, 2:144, 2:142, 2:106, 2:177, 2:143), verified via quran.ai. Where the Sheikh weighs a popular report, his own judgment of it has been followed.

The Seerah · 35 of 104

The Preparation for Badr

A market drawn in the sand, and permission to fight at last

The constitution has been written, the brotherhood has been built, the qiblah has turned. Madinah is no longer the refuge the Prophet ﷺ slipped into under cover of night; it is a city with his hand on it. And before the seerah arrives at its first great battle, Dr. Yasir Qadhi slows the whole story down to show you something most retellings skip past: a people do not walk out to Badr by accident. They are shaped for it, quietly, over two years, in their markets and their prayers and their hearts, long before a single sword is drawn. The Prophet ﷺ had moved to a new place. It was a new era, and everything was different. Policies became possible in Madinah that had been unthinkable in Makkah, where he had been a persecuted minority with no power to order an economy or a society of his own. Three changes in particular reshaped the young community: the economic, the spiritual, and the political. The first two the Sheikh sketches; the third, the road to war, he opens all the way up. By the last line of this chapter you will be standing exactly where the next seven days begin: on the edge of the wells of Badr.

A market drawn in the sand

In Makkah the Prophet ﷺ had been a minority with no economic policy to call his own. In Madinah he was independent at last, and one of the first things he did was go and look at the markets that already existed. The Arabs of the city, soon to be called the Ansar, were farmers more than traders; when they wanted to do business they went out to the great trading grounds run by the Jewish tribes on the outskirts, for there was no major market inside Madinah itself. So the Prophet ﷺ walked through those outlying markets, and what he saw there, reported in Sunan Ibn Majah, he refused. There was cheating in them, deception built into the way they ran, and he said it plainly: this is not a marketplace for you. Then he went back toward his masjid and, with his own feet, dragged lines into the sand, a line and another and another, until he had marked out the borders of a new market a short walk to the west of the mosque. He gave it two rules, and only two. Let no one encroach on these lines and shrink it. And let no one tax the people who trade in it: no fee, no penalty, no charge for the right to do business there.

What he built in that square of sand was not a tax code, it was a conscience. He tied honesty in trade directly to faith itself, and this, the Sheikh stresses, is the heart of it. He praised the honest merchant and condemned the cheat, teaching that the truthful trader would stand in the highest company on the Day of Judgment, while the one who deceives has stepped outside the circle of the believers altogether. Anyone who understands how an economy actually works, Dr. Yasir Qadhi notes, knows that without ethics and an inner conscience a person with power or wealth can abuse it at will and nothing on earth will stop him; the only true restraint is the one a man carries inside. So the Prophet ﷺ wove commerce and religiosity into a single fabric, and then he policed it himself, walking the very market he had founded. He once came upon a date seller with beautiful fruit heaped at the top of his bag, and pushed his hand down into it, drawing out from underneath the dates that were wet and ruined and meant to be sold hidden beneath the good ones. Whoever cheats us, he said to the man, is not of us. Not of us: not, in that moment, a believer doing even the minimum his faith required of him.

The same instinct ran through his other rulings. He forbade false oaths over goods and the hiding of defects, and he banned a practice that sounds almost innocent until you see the wisdom in it: no resident of Madinah was allowed to act as a broker, a simsar, for a Bedouin coming in from the desert to sell. Let the Bedouin come and sell for himself, the Prophet ﷺ ruled, because the city insider knows the ins and outs of every price and will inflate and deflate and play the game, while the desert trader, ignorant of the market, is content with whatever fair amount he can get. The middleman gets the fat cut and the stranger gets nothing. Most of the profit in any market, the Sheikh observes, flows to people who do little except possess the cunning to position themselves between buyer and seller, and the Prophet ﷺ cut that off at the root. The whole philosophy was unprecedented. People sometimes claim Islam is simply a capitalist system; that is not quite true. Set it beside the great isms of the modern age, and it comes closest, perhaps, to capitalism, but with many real differences, and the honest thing to say is that Islam is its own system, with its own logic. And its first fruit was independence. The market the Prophet ﷺ drew in the sand flourished, so that when the Jewish tribes were later expelled one by one and their markets collapsed, the believers barely felt it; they had their own, up and running, sufficient unto themselves. Financial independence was there from the very first day, and there is a lesson in that, the Sheikh says, for anyone alive in a world of borrowed and tangled economies.

The faith, taught one rung at a time

The same patience that built the market built the worship. Within roughly a year of the hijrah, almost the entire law of worship had come down, the whole shariah of ibadah, everything except the Hajj, and Hajj alone had to wait, because Hajj is performed in Makkah and Makkah was still enemy ground. It would not be revealed until the ninth year, when Abu Bakr would lead it, with the Prophet ﷺ himself performing it only in the tenth. But the rest arrived inside those first two years, and the way Allah brought it is the thing worth holding onto: never the whole mountain on the first morning, always one rung, then the next.

Watch the fasting come down a single step at a time. The Prophet ﷺ reached Madinah, and the first Muharram passed with no law of fasting at all, and the first Ramadan of the hijrah passed with nothing ordained either. Then, on the tenth of the next Muharram, nine or ten months in, he made the fast of Ashura obligatory: a single day, and he decreed that whoever had already eaten his breakfast that morning should simply eat nothing more for the rest of it. One day, to get the body used to the very idea of fasting. Only afterward, that same second year, did Allah reveal in Surah al-Baqarah that whoever among you witnesses the month of Ramadan must fast it, and the whole month became the obligation, while the fast of the tenth of Muharram softened back into a recommendation, a sunnah, which is what it remains. Charity arrived the same way. First came zakat al-fitr, the small and affordable one, in our day the price of a single modest meal that nearly anyone can give, set so that everyone could taste the act of giving. Then, once that habit was set, within a few months came the zakat on wealth itself, the greater obligation. From the easy rung to the harder one. And the prayer was perfected here too. In Makkah every prayer had been two rak'ahs; pray, he had said, as you have seen me pray. In Madinah the Dhuhr and Asr and Maghrib and Isha were lengthened, only the Fajr keeping its two, and the wudu, the rules of major impurity, the whole shape of how the prayer is performed, all of it settled in these first two years.

Hold that pattern, because it is how Allah teaches anyone who is willing to be taught. Not the whole law on the first day. One rung, until your foot is steady on it, and then the next.

Thirteen years, and not one blade drawn

Now the heart of the episode. Here is a fact to sit with before anything else: in all thirteen years of Makkah, the Prophet ﷺ never once authorized a single act of force. Not when Yasir and Sumayya were tortured to death. Not when Bilal was dragged across the burning streets of the city. It would not have been impossible, the Sheikh points out, to send a companion to slip out at night and quietly kill one of the persecutors, a man like Umayya ibn Khalaf. The Prophet ﷺ never did, and not from weakness, but because he understood something most of us never learn to do under pressure: he weighed the harm. He saw that to kill one tormentor would bring down on the whole fragile young community a persecution it could not yet survive. So the suffering of one believer was allowed to stand, deliberately, so that the slaughter of the many could be prevented. He thought ten steps ahead while everyone around him was reacting to step one. This is the calculus the scholars call weighing the masaalih against the mafaasid, the benefits against the harms, and it is one of the deepest forms of wisdom a person can carry.

And here is the turn that lands hardest. It was, in fact, the eager who first wanted blood. Some of the young men of Makkah, full of the zealousness that belongs to the young, were straining to fight, and it was them that Allah held back. Dr. Yasir Qadhi reads the verse over them slowly: those who were told to restrain their hands and pray and give charity, and then, when fighting was finally written upon them, a party of them flinched.

Have you not seen those who were told, "Restrain your hands [from fighting] and establish prayer and give zakah"? But then when battle was ordained for them, at once a party of them feared men as they fear Allah or with [even] greater fear. They said, "Our Lord, why have You decreed upon us fighting? If only You had postponed [it for] us for a short time." Say, "The enjoyment of this world is little, and the Hereafter is better for he who fears Allah. And injustice will not be done to you, [even] as much as a thread [inside a date seed]."Qur'an 4:77

In Makkah their hands were tied: lower your hands, the command had been, just pray and give what you can. Then permission came in Madinah, the thing they had begged for, and suddenly the same group grew afraid. They asked why it had been decreed so soon, why it could not have been delayed a little longer. The Qur'an caught them in the very act, and Allah chastised them: where has your enthusiasm gone, now that I have given you what you wanted? And the lesson, the Sheikh says, lands as hard fourteen centuries later as it did then. Those who talk the loudest are very often the ones who act the least. The grandest critic, the most furious voice, frequently does the smallest thing when the moment to act finally arrives.

Permission, at last

Then the first verses permitting fighting came down, and the Sheikh asks you to read their wording with great care, because the very words carry the entire philosophy. This is Surah al-Hajj, a Makkan surah revealed at the very end of the Makkan era or the very beginning of Madinah, and it is the first concession for armed struggle in the whole of revelation.

Permission [to fight] has been given to those who are being fought, because they were wronged. And indeed, Allah is competent to give them victory. [They are] those who have been evicted from their homes without right - only because they say, "Our Lord is Allah." And were it not that Allah checks the people, some by means of others, there would have been demolished monasteries, churches, synagogues, and mosques in which the name of Allah is much mentioned [i.e., praised]. And Allah will surely support those who support Him [i.e., His cause]. Indeed, Allah is Powerful and Exalted in Might.Qur'an 22:39-40

Notice the very first word, the Sheikh says: permission. Not command, not the summit of the religion, but permission. Allah had withheld them; now He releases them. And the reason is spelled out so plainly that there is no room to twist it: because they were wronged, driven from their homes for no crime except saying our Lord is Allah. Fighting here is the right of an oppressed people against the oppressor who has trampled them, and no court or law on earth, the Sheikh observes, would fail to recognize that right. What else is a people to do when every other avenue has closed and force is the only way left to win back what was stolen? And then the verse widens the principle into a law of the world itself: were it not that Allah restrains one people by means of another, the very houses where His name is remembered, the monasteries and churches and synagogues and mosques, would be torn to the ground. Allah permits war as the means by which oppression and corruption are pushed back, so that the earth does not collapse into chaos. This is, in its own terms, what later thinkers would call a just-war principle, and the believers need not shy away from it: yes, our religion has jihad, and jihad rightly understood is the cause of a persecuted people whose basic freedoms have been denied, fighting back against those who oppressed them.

Abu Bakr, radiyallahu anhu, said that the moment he heard Surah al-Hajj revealed, he knew there would be war. But here Dr. Yasir Qadhi pauses to make something unmistakably clear, and we keep his caution exactly as he gave it, because the times demand it. This is history, not a slogan, descriptive and not prescriptive, a window onto what actually happened in the Prophet's ﷺ own time, and never a license for the terrorism that gets read back into these words today. In an age when a person can be locked up for a sentence taken out of context, this disclaimer has to be spoken plainly. And then he lays out the four stages the scholars trace through the seerah. First: no military fighting at all, through all thirteen years of Makkah, only the jihad of the soul, patience and turning away and leaving the persecutors to Allah. Second: permission granted but not yet an obligation, fighting on a volunteer basis, immediately after the hijrah. Third: fighting made obligatory, but against the Quraysh alone and no other tribe, and this was the bulk of the Prophet's ﷺ time. Fourth, only at the very end: fighting against all the idolaters of Arabia who had warred against the believers, when he ﷺ said he had been commanded to fight the people of his time until they testified that there is no god but Allah, after which the whole peninsula entered Islam. Permission came first. It always came first.

And the cause, the Sheikh drives home, was justice for a people whose rights had been trampled, and he sets it against an irony you cannot unsee once it is pointed out. This very country, he says, went to war and killed soldiers because of a tax raised on tea; if that was reckoned a worthy cause, then how much more worthy, a thousand times over, is the cause of a people who say only that their Lord is Allah, and for that are killed, robbed of their property, expelled from their homes, persecuted for thirteen years. To fight back against that was the very least a wronged people could do.

Why the caravans

So the expeditions began, and they had clear and deliberate aims that the Sheikh sets out one by one. First, to declare to the Quraysh in plain terms that the Muslims had not fled like cowards to live quietly and forgotten in another city; they were an independent political and military force, a people who would stand and reclaim what had been taken from them. Second, and this is the strategy he underlines hardest, to cut off the oxygen the Quraysh breathed. Not the oxygen of the air, he says, but the oxygen of money. The wealth of Makkah was not in the city itself; it rode out in caravans.

For the accustomed security of the Quraysh, their accustomed security [in] the caravan of winter and summer.Qur'an 106:1-2

The caravan of winter and the caravan of summer were the entire lifeline of the Quraysh, the journeys north toward Syria and south toward Yemen that made Makkah rich; without that linkage, Makkah would have collapsed. So cut the artery, and the city that expelled the believers would feel it where it lived. And third, every march out drew a nearby tribe into alliance, expanding the young state mile by mile. The scholars name two kinds of these ventures, and the Sheikh teaches the terms: a ghazwa is an expedition the Prophet ﷺ went out on himself, and so commanded in person; a sariyya is one he dispatched but did not personally join. By his companions' own count he went out on nineteen ghazwas and actually fought in only eight, the rest ending without battle, while the smaller saraya were so many, perhaps thirty or forty, that the scholars do not even try to list them all.

The first ventures barely drew blood. One reached a place called al-Abwa and, finding no battle, sealed the first of many treaties with a local tribe, pushing the reach of the young state a hundred miles beyond the city. On another, an arrow was loosed in the path of Allah for the very first time, shot by Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas, who carried the pride of it for the rest of his life. Then came the one that was secretly the opening act of Badr itself, the expedition of al-Ushayra. The Prophet ﷺ led some hundred and fifty men, by other counts two hundred, out to intercept the mother of all caravans, the great annual caravan going up to Syria, seventy or eighty camels carrying perhaps seventy or eighty percent of the invested wealth of Makkah, for everyone with a coin to spare put it into that caravan, the way Khadijah once had, since there was no other way to make money. At its head was Abu Sufyan, not yet a believer. And they missed it. By the decree of Allah, a man of the caravan strayed and caught sight of the Muslims in the distance, raced back, and Abu Sufyan, a politician to the bone, at once diverted onto another road. The Muslims, with no way to track him, came home assuming they had simply arrived a day too late, never knowing he had seen them coming. But Abu Sufyan knew, and being intelligent, he did not forget it. There was a smaller expedition too, sometimes called the first Badr, on the plains of Badr but not against the Quraysh: it was their allies who had struck Madinah by night like thieves, killing a herder or two and stealing some camels, and the Prophet ﷺ rode out after them with seventy of the Muhajirun and could not catch them. Even the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, the Sheikh observes, was not simply handed easy victory after victory; he came home empty-handed more than once, and there is a wisdom in that we are meant to feel.

The sealed letter at Nakhla

Then the incident the whole episode has been walking toward. One consequence of that first Badr was that the Prophet ﷺ decided he needed eyes on the movements of the Quraysh, and so, for the first time, he began sending out reconnaissance missions. He handpicked eight men, all of them from the Muhajirun and not one from the Ansar, and put them under the command of his own cousin, Abdullah ibn Jahsh. He gave Abdullah a sealed letter and sent the party northeast, the opposite direction from Makkah, with an instruction: travel for two days, and only on the morning of the second day open the letter and read it. When Abdullah broke the seal, the letter ordered him to double back to Nakhla, on the eastern side of Makkah toward Ta'if, to watch the movements of the Quraysh and report their preparations. And it added a mercy: force no one; whoever among you wishes may turn back. There were reasons for so strange a method, the Sheikh explains. The first was secrecy so total that not even the eight men themselves knew their destination until they were already two days out, for war, as the Prophet ﷺ said, is deception, deception that is permitted where outright treachery is not. The second was that the detour was itself the disguise, sending them away from Makkah so that they could curve back undetected. And the third was the mercy in the letter: these men were walking nearly unarmed into the lap of the enemy, eight against a city, and he would compel none of them to it.

Abdullah read the letter to his companions and then said what the moment called for: whoever longs for martyrdom and to meet Allah, come with me, for I do not think I am coming back; and whoever wishes, let him return to Madinah. Not one of them turned around. They had four camels among the eight, two men to a camel, for in those early years the Muslims never had the luxury of a mount apiece. On the way, the camel that Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas and Utba ibn Ghazwan were sharing broke its tether in the night and was lost, and the two of them were left behind in the desert to search for it while the rest pressed on, because the mission had to continue. So six men reached Nakhla. And before they could even pitch a tent and set up their watch, a small caravan of the Quraysh came into view, loaded to the top with spices and raisins and goods, lightly guarded by only three or four men, utterly unsuspecting, for the Quraysh were certain that no one would attack them now.

The six agonized. There were two problems, and the Sheikh weighs both. The first was the Prophet's ﷺ order: he had sent them to gather information, not to fight, though, it is true, he had not forbidden them to fight. But the deeper problem was the date. It was the last day of Rajab, one of the sacred months in which the Arabs did not make war, and only an hour or two of daylight remained before Rajab would end and Sha'ban begin. There was the cruelty of the timing, the Sheikh says, and there is a reason Allah arranged it just so: had it been any other day there would have been no dilemma at all. Wait until sunset and the caravan would pass on and the prize and the chance would be gone forever. Strike now, within that last hour, and they would be guilty of shedding blood in a sacred month. They chose to strike. One man of the caravan was killed, two were taken captive, the first prisoners the Muslims ever took, and they rode hard for Madinah with the goods. And it should be understood, the Sheikh notes, that there was no greed in this; the laws of dividing the spoils of war had not yet been revealed, so the six had no expectation of personal gain. Whatever they seized would go to the young state, not to their own pockets. They could not be accused of plunder.

But the Prophet ﷺ met them with anger. I did not command you to fight, he said, and he refused to touch any of the spoils. He saw at once what a disaster of reputation this would be, and it was. The Quraysh seized on it and broadcast it across the whole peninsula: look at these Muslims, they have violated the sacred month and spilled innocent blood, they have betrayed the very sanctities of Ibrahim. The criticism, in its narrow place, was valid, and the Prophet ﷺ felt the weight of it heavily; the believers had genuinely done a thing that should not have been done. And the Jewish tribes of Madinah watched the friction with quiet satisfaction, and from this point it grew clear which way they would lean, for they were not going to stand with the Muslims. For days, no one knew what to do, and the fitna gathered momentum.

Who is better than Allah in judging

Then the Qur'an itself settled it, in a verse of such breathtaking balance that the Sheikh calls it the very height of eloquence. Allah did not pretend the bloodshed was nothing.

They ask you about the sacred month - about fighting therein. Say, "Fighting therein is great [sin], but averting [people] from the way of Allah and disbelief in Him and [preventing access to] al-Masjid al-Haram and the expulsion of its people therefrom are greater [evil] in the sight of Allah. And fitnah is greater than killing."Qur'an 2:217

There is the honesty first, the full stop the believers expected: fighting in the sacred month is a grave sin, a kabir, and Allah neither sanctioned it nor excused it, for He is al-Haqq and judges by the truth. The Muslims should not have shed that blood, and the Prophet ﷺ had not commanded it. But the sentence does not end there. And who, Allah turns and asks the Quraysh, are you to be the accusers? You who barred people from the path of Allah, who disbelieved in Him, who blocked the Sacred Mosque and drove its rightful people out of their homes: all of that is far greater in the sight of Allah than the single death you point at, and the fitnah you have caused is a greater crime than this killing. Ibn Abbas, radiyallahu anhu, read the word fitnah here as the very disbelief the Quraysh lived in; the Sheikh widens it, rightly, to take in also the chaos they had spread and the families they had torn apart, all of it worse than the blood that had been shed. Ibn al-Qayyim put it as plainly as it can be put: yes, what the Muslims did was a sin, a great sin even, but what you, the Quraysh, are upon is a far greater crime in the sight of Allah. The grammar of the verse, the Sheikh adds, has occupied scholars from the very beginning, its clauses so intricate that whole treatises and even modern academic papers have been written on this single ayah; it cannot really be carried into English at all.

This is the reading that is distinctly the Sheikh's gift in this episode, the place where he reaches straight out of the seventh century into our own moment, and he reaches with a line that Malcolm X left us: listen to the powerful long enough and you will start to believe that the oppressor is the victim and the victim is the terrorist. He said it more than half a century ago, and it has only grown truer. The child throwing a stone becomes the aggressor; the tank in front of him becomes the wronged. The accuser screaming about a single death while standing on thirteen years of his own cruelty is not a relic of a vanished age; he is alive and broadcasting today. And the danger runs both ways, the Sheikh is careful to say: a wrong does not become right merely because a greater wrong provoked it, and terror answered with terror is still terror, and it returns upon the one who sheds it. The point is neither to excuse the small wrong nor to let the great one hide behind its outrage. Allah did exactly that here. He named the believers' act a sin and refused to let the Quraysh pose as the injured party, and He closed the matter with a question that should steady any heart that ever has to weigh a hard thing: who is better than Allah in judging?

With the verse revealed, the Prophet ﷺ accepted the spoils and arranged the ransom of the two captives, but he set a condition: they would be held until his two lost companions, Sa'd and Utba, came home safe, for no one yet knew their fate out in the desert. The two did find their camel and, a week or two later, returned to Madinah, and so the captives were ransomed. And one of those two prisoners, a man named al-Hakam ibn Kaysan, did a remarkable thing. After his ransom was paid, he embraced Islam, chose to stay in Madinah, and later died a martyr in one of the Prophet's ﷺ battles. He was the first of many, the Sheikh notes: again and again an enemy taken captive would accept Islam, but only after the ransom had been paid, so that the wealth would already be in the believers' hands and no one could say he had converted to save his money. So the road was paved. Every small expedition, every near miss, every hard judgment had been leading to one place. Abu Sufyan's great caravan was still out there, and the man had already sent a rider galloping toward Makkah to raise an army, a rider he would instruct to bloody himself and tear his clothes and cry disaster through the streets, so that the Quraysh would gather the largest force they had ever assembled. The believers did not yet know it. They were about to be met by an army out of nowhere. The wells of Badr were waiting, and that is where we go next.

What this day asks of our faith

It is tempting to read a chapter like this one as mere prelude, the throat-clearing before the great battle, and to hurry through it to get to the swords. That would be to miss the questions it presses on us, because nearly everything that matters about a person's faith is decided in exactly the quiet, unglamorous places this chapter lives in: how you earn, how you worship, how you weigh a wrong, how you wait.

He built the inside before he fought the outside. Before a single expedition rode out, the Prophet ﷺ had drawn a market in the sand and ruled it with a conscience, and had let the law of worship come down one rung at a time, a single day of fasting before a whole month, a small charity before a greater one. This is how Allah teaches anyone willing to be taught, and it is how real change is built in a human life: not the whole mountain demanded on the first morning, but one step made steady, and then the next. If you are trying to turn your life toward Allah and you keep collapsing under the weight of everything at once, you have mistaken the method. Build the staircase. And tie your earning to your faith while you climb it, because the honesty you keep when no auditor is watching, the ruined dates you refuse to bury at the bottom of the bag, is itself an act of worship, and whoever cheats us, he ﷺ said, is not of us.

Then there is the long restraint, thirteen years of it, and the discipline it took. He let his own companions be tortured and killed in front of him and did not strike back, not because he could not, but because he had weighed the harm and refused to let the suffering of one trigger the destruction of all. He thought ten steps ahead while the world reacted to step one. Wisdom, this chapter teaches, is not the absence of feeling; it is the refusal to let feeling choose the worse of two outcomes. And notice who flinched when the moment finally came: the same young men who had begged loudest to fight in Makkah grew afraid the instant permission was given. Measure people, and measure yourself, not by what is said while it costs nothing, but by what is done when the order at last lands.

And then the verse that should be carried out of this day into every argument you will ever have about who was wronged and who did wrong. Allah did not pretend the believers' sin at Nakhla was nothing, and He did not let the Quraysh hide their thirteen years of cruelty behind their sudden outrage over a single death. He held both truths at once, named each for what it was, and judged. We are rarely so honest. We excuse the wrongs of the side we love and magnify the wrongs of the side we fear, and the powerful are forever teaching us to mistake the oppressor for the victim. Refuse the inverted story. Look for where the wrong began. And when the matter is finally too tangled for you to judge cleanly, rest where the Prophet ﷺ and his companions rested: who is better than Allah in judging? So take this from a day that has not yet reached its battle: build your faith one rung at a time and tie it to your honesty, weigh the harm before you react, prove yourself by your deeds and not your noise, and never let anyone convince you the victim is the villain. May Allah send His peace and blessings upon His Messenger ﷺ, who built a city's heart before he ever drew its sword, keep our hearts firm upon His religion when the test arrives, never let us mistake the oppressor for the wronged, make us content with His decree, for who is better than He in judging, and gather us under his banner on the Day the nations are gathered.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 35: the preparation for Badr (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (4:77, 22:39-40, 106:1-2, 2:217), verified via quran.ai. The narration is the Sheikh's; the phrasing is Buruja's.

The Seerah · 36 of 104

The Battle of Badr, part 1

A thousand camels, two horses, and a dream

Here is the strangest thing about the way the greatest battle of early Islam begins: nobody in it is planning a battle. A caravan of nearly a thousand camels is coming down from Syria carrying the wealth of an entire city, and the man guarding it is the most nervous merchant in Arabia. A column of three hundred and some believers slips out of Madinah expecting to walk home with recovered goods and no fight at all. And in Makkah, three nights before any rider arrives, a woman wakes from a dream that will come true in front of the whole city, on schedule, to the day. Dr. Yasir Qadhi opens here the chapter every earlier episode has been climbing toward, and the wonder of it is exactly this: watch Allah move every piece on the board while each player believes he is only chasing camels. This is the first of seven days at those wells, and it ends mid story, with a scream in the valley.

The well, the qibla, and a city on edge

First the stage, because the ground itself carries meaning here. Madinah has given the believers what Makkah never did: political freedom. For the first time there is an independent community under the Prophet ﷺ, with its own leadership and its own enemies, and the enemies sit in two directions. Inside the oasis are the hypocrites who would gladly see the old order restored, and tribes still watching to see whether the new arrangement will stumble. Beyond the horizon is Quraysh. Over the coming years, for the first time in the history of the Peninsula, all of Arabia will gather into two great camps, and that polarization, Dr. Yasir Qadhi reminds us, is the painful road toward something the Arabs had never once known: unification. It is why the chroniclers filled the Madani years with one expedition after another; from their seats, the battles were the story worth recording.

Small sparks had already flown. At Nakhlah a scouting party had seized a Quraysh caravan and taken a man's life on the last day of a sacred month, and revelation itself had to answer the uproar that followed. Before that, the Prophet ﷺ had marched out to al-Ushayra to intercept this same great caravan on its way up to Syria, and missed it by a single day. Al-Ushayra was, in effect, the first act of this very battle: the same caravan, the same man, on the journey out, and Badr would be the second act, on the way home. So when Abu Sufyan brought it back down months later, he traveled the way a man travels when he knows he is being hunted, on what we would today call code red.

Between the two cities waits a stretch of ground that nobody yet has reason to notice. Badr is not a town. It is a well, dug generations earlier by a man named Badr ibn Yakhlud of the tribe of Banu Ghifar; his name passed to the water, and the water's name to the whole plain around it. It lies about one hundred and sixty miles southwest of Madinah and two hundred and fifty north of Makkah, three days by caravan then and barely over an hour by car now. It sits closer to Madinah than to Makkah. Hold on to that detail; it will matter when the two forces race for the wells.

One more thing before anyone moves, and the Sheikh will not let it pass. The qibla had changed perhaps three weeks before Badr, and for him that nearness is too close to dismiss as accident, for with Allah there are no accidents. The believers had just turned their faces from Jerusalem toward a Kaaba still crowded with idols, in a city that had cast them out. The change reads like a quiet promise: how can you face Makkah, and turn toward the House while it is polluted with idolatry, and not one day hold it in your hand and cleanse it? Three weeks after the turn, Allah handed this small community its first great victory on the road between the two cities, as though to say that facing the Kaaba spiritually was the first installment of possessing it entirely. And that, in the end, is exactly what came to pass.

An oath sworn beside the Kaaba

War was in the air long before any caravan was sighted, and Bukhari preserves a scene from the first year after the hijrah that proves it. Sa'd ibn Mu'adh, radiyallahu anhu, the brilliant young chief of the Ansar, had an old friendship from jahiliyyah with Umayyah ibn Khalaf, the Makkan who had once owned Bilal: trading partners who hosted one another, Sa'd in Madinah, Umayyah in Makkah. On one visit Sa'd asked for a quiet hour to make tawaf, and Umayyah walked him to the Kaaba at noon, the furnace hour, when Makkah sleeps and no one would see.

They walked straight into Abu Jahl. Who is this with you, he demanded, and the answer did not soothe him, for here was a chief of the very city that now sheltered Muhammad ﷺ and his companions. How is it, Abu Jahl said, that you circle the House in safety after giving protection to the renegades, the apostates from the religion of your fathers? He did not even seem to know Sa'd had embraced Islam; sheltering the believers was crime enough. Were you not the guest of Umayyah, he swore, you would not return to your home in one piece.

Understand what had just broken. Since the days of Ibrahim, alayhi salam, whoever entered that sanctuary was safe, as the verse the Sheikh ties to this moment says plainly:

And whoever enters it [i.e., the Ḥaram] shall be safe.Qur'an 3:97

The Makkans themselves used to boast, Ibn Abbas narrates, that in the days of ignorance a man could make tawaf beside the very killer of his father and no one would touch a hair of his head. Now, for the first time, the rule bent: sacred for everyone, except the Muslims and whoever loved them. Sa'd did not whisper his reply. He raised his voice so the whole of Makkah could hear it: if you cut me off from the House, by Allah, I will cut you off from something more painful to you, your trade road to Syria. The caravan route was Makkah's bloodstream, and now the threat to sever it came not only from the dispossessed Muhajirun but from the Ansar, sworn aloud beside the Kaaba. When the great caravan finally rolled south, more than one reckoning was riding with it.

The caravan every household paid into

Now it is Ramadan of the second year of the hijrah. The caravan that escaped at al-Ushayra is coming home, swollen with a full season of trade, and the Prophet ﷺ wants to know exactly where it is.
