He sends out scouts in waves and treats their reports as state secrets. Sahih Muslim records that when one spy returned, he ﷺ cleared the room of everyone except Anas, his young attendant of perhaps seven, before he would hear a word. Ibn Ishaq names Talha ibn Ubaydillah and Sa'id ibn Zayd among the watchers, who shadowed the caravan until it had passed, then galloped for Madinah with the count.

And the count was almost beyond belief. Nearly a thousand camels under Abu Sufyan's command, the largest caravan Quraysh had assembled in living memory, guarded by only forty men. Later attempts to price the cargo land around fifty thousand gold dinars, which converts into the millions in our money, an entire treasury walking through the desert. Ibn Ishaq adds the detail that makes it personal: there was hardly a household in all of Makkah that did not have something invested on those camels. These were traders, not farmers or wage earners, and once or twice a year a man would pour his wealth into the caravan and wait for the return that fed his family for the year. This was not a shipment. It was a city's whole economy.

Orientalist pens have loved this moment, and used it to sneer: the prophet turned highway robber. Dr. Yasir Qadhi answers the charge without flinching. For thirteen years the believers had been persecuted, tortured, and killed; the survivors had been driven from their homes and their property confiscated without a coin in return, and that seized wealth, he notes, is very likely one reason this caravan was the richest Quraysh ever sent. The Prophet ﷺ targeted no neutral tribe and broke no treaty; he moved against the one city already at war with him, and both sides understood the rules of that war exactly. After everything Quraysh had done, returning their plundered fortune to the refugees they had stripped was the least they could expect.

Whoever is ready, come right now

What happened next moved at the speed that wins wars. The reports seem at first to disagree. In Sahih Muslim the Prophet ﷺ announced no destination, only that there was a task, and that whoever had his riding animal ready should come at once; those who asked leave to fetch a mount were told no, only those ready here and now. In Ibn Ishaq's telling he ﷺ speaks plainly: this is the caravan of Quraysh carrying their wealth, so go out to it, and perhaps Allah will grant it to you. Dr. Yasir Qadhi unties the knot, refusing to drop the Sahih Muslim report most books of seerah quietly skip. Inside the masjid he kept silence, because Madinah still held idolaters and hypocrites and loose tongues, and a destination spoken there could reach Abu Sufyan before the army did. Only once the column was clear of the city did he ﷺ name the target. The two accounts are one: secrecy within the walls, disclosure beyond them.

Notice the pattern, because it runs through the whole of his ﷺ life, from the night of the hijrah onward. He is the Messenger of Allah ﷺ; he could have leaned on heaven and lifted not a finger. Instead he clears rooms to hear intelligence, seals destinations, moves before news can outrun him, and then places his trust in Allah. Tie your camel first, the Sheikh says, and let tawakkul come after the knot, not in place of it.

The urgency tells you what this expedition was, and what it was not. Forty guards on a thousand camels is not a real escort; it is almost an invitation. Show three hundred armed men and that column would scatter like birds. So nobody mustered for war. There was almost no armor in the force, two horses in the entire company, belonging to az-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam and al-Miqdad ibn al-Aswad, and fewer than a hundred camels for more than three hundred men. Badr was never meant to be a battle. It was meant to be a confiscation, swift and bloodless, a sitting target taken before Quraysh could even learn it was in danger. Allah had written something else, for a wisdom that would show itself only on the far side of the coming days, and the whole shape of the expedition is proof that the men who marched had no idea what they were marching into.

Around the twelfth of Ramadan the column slipped out of Madinah, and the city itself was placed in the hands of a man whom the empires of that age would never have considered: Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum, the blind companion. In a world that treated the blind as half a person, fit to be pitied and set aside, the Prophet ﷺ made him the caretaker of his capital, and not for the only time; across his life he would leave him in charge of Madinah perhaps a dozen times. He was simply the wisest man available, and his eyes had nothing to do with the job. Dr. Yasir Qadhi pauses to mark how far ahead of its age this was: a faith that saw the ability and not the disability.

Three hundred and ten and some, three men to a camel

At the first encampment the Prophet ﷺ did what the rush out of Madinah had not allowed: he walked the lines and counted who had come. Two volunteers were younger than fourteen, al-Bara ibn Azib and Abdullah ibn Umar, boys aching to be numbered among the men, and he sent them home; the camp was still close enough that they could walk back in safety. It would not be the last time, for at almost every battle to come children will present themselves at the line and he ﷺ will gently turn them away. The Sheikh draws a word from it for our age: Allah made the age of physical maturity the age of intelligence, and societies that treated a boy of fourteen as a man tended to get men, while we, who have stretched childhood far past it, get the troubles we complain of.

The final muster came to three hundred and ten and some: three hundred and thirteen in some books, fifteen or seventeen in others. Eighty three were Muhajirun, and the rest Ansar, sixty two of Aws and a hundred and seventy of Khazraj, the larger and poorer tribe, who as a rule came to the truth faster than the comfortable. With that muster, the men of Madinah were now openly inside a fight that had until now been Makkah's quarrel with her own exiles. And linger on the number, because the tradition lingers on it: when Abu Dharr, radiyallahu anhu, asked how many messengers Allah had sent, the Prophet ﷺ answered three hundred and ten and some, and it is said the company who stood with Talut against Jalut numbered the same. Allah knows best what is woven into that figure, but the echo is deliberate: small companies, impossible odds, and a help that never came from numbers.

They shared their mounts three men to a camel, riding and walking by turns, and Imam Ahmad keeps in his Musnad the scene that tells you everything about the man ﷺ they were following. The Prophet ﷺ was grouped with Ali, radiyallahu anhu, and Abu Lubabah, and imagine being assigned to share a single camel with the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. Of course both men said it at once: ride, ya Rasulallah, and we will walk.

He could have said yes, and the Sheikh presses on this hard, because the temptation to wave it through is exactly the point. Who would object to the general riding while the foot soldiers walk? Every army that ever existed runs on that arrangement, and he ﷺ was infinitely more than a general; he was the Messenger of Allah, and not a soul could have faulted him for accepting. Instead he smiled and said: the two of you are no younger than I am, and no stronger than I am, and I am in no less need of the reward than you. He was in his mid fifties, the senior of all three, which only sharpens the words. There is no answer a man can make to a sentence like that. So when his turn ended, the Messenger of Allah ﷺ dismounted and walked his share of the Ramadan desert, footsore in the heat like every other man in the line.

Who complains about the heat after looking up to see him ﷺ on foot among them? Rank, in his ﷺ hands, was a thing for carrying people, never a thing for being carried, and the lesson did not stay in that desert: Umar would carry it to the gates of Jerusalem.

The held back and the turned away

Two men begged to march to Badr and were refused, and the reason deserves to be carved somewhere lasting. Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman and his father, al-Yaman, had been seized by Quraysh on the road months earlier. Their captors came close to killing them, then released them on one extracted promise: that they would never take up arms alongside Muhammad ﷺ against Makkah. So when the Prophet ﷺ learned of the pledge, he held them to it, and they stayed behind while the army marched out.

Sit with how radical that ruling is. The promise had been wrung out under fear of death, given to the very enemy now waiting across the sand, and keeping it cost two believers their place at the first and greatest battle of Islam. None of it mattered. A Muslim is bound by his conditions; a Muslim is never a traitor. The Sheikh lands the point squarely in our own century: the covenants you have entered, including the quiet ones, the oaths of citizenship and the visas stamped in your passport, are binding contracts in the sight of Allah. The Qur'an opens an entire surah with the command:

O you who have believed, fulfill [all] contracts.Qur'an 5:1

Nobody forced you to give your word. Keep it, or do not give it; there is no third road. That two believers missed Badr rather than break faith with their enemies is the measure of how seriously this religion takes the word a man gives.

Then came the mirror image. As the army moved out, a pagan of Madinah, famous for his courage in a fight, caught up with the column hoping for a share of the prize, and the companions were glad to see so formidable a warrior join them. The Prophet ﷺ turned to him: do you testify to Allah and His Messenger? No, said the man. Then go back, he ﷺ answered, for we will not seek the help of an idolater. The man caught up again with the same offer and met the same refusal, and a third time, until at last he understood and said: I testify that Allah is my Lord and that you are His Messenger. Then come, said the Prophet ﷺ, and the man rode with them.

The scholars would later weigh this refusal against the times he ﷺ clearly did accept help from outside the faith: Abdullah ibn Urayqit, the pagan guide to whom he entrusted his very life on the night roads of the hijrah, and Mut'im ibn Adi, whose protection had carried him home from Ta'if. Imam an-Nawawi settles the tension as a general rule with exceptions: one does not, as a rule, seek the aid of an idolater's army, but where the trust is real and the need genuine, it may be weighed case by case. But do not miss the quieter lesson. A fortune sat on the horizon, the man's conversion had every worldly motive available to it, and nobody interrogated his heart. This religion judges what is outward and leaves the inward to its Lord; we were never asked to read what is in men's chests. And it is unbothered by imperfect beginnings: come for the wrong reason, the Sheikh says, and given time you will stay for the right one.

Date seeds, a dream, and a screaming rider

Out on the trade road, Abu Sufyan was running an intelligence war of his own, sending scouts to hunt for scouts. Desert nomads told him two riders had camped nearby and watched the road. He went to the spot himself, and in the keenness of a frightened, careful man he broke open the camels' droppings and picked out date stones, and knew them at a glance: these were the dates of Yathrib. His caravan was marked. He made two decisions, and in saving his caravan with them he handed Quraysh its catastrophe. First, he hired a guide and swung the caravan off the inland road toward the coast, bypassing Badr altogether. Second, he sent for help: Damdam ibn Amr al-Ghifari, the fastest rider on the fastest camel, pounding toward Makkah with one message, save your wealth or lose it. Damdam covered it in something like three days, and in saving himself Abu Sufyan summoned to the field the army that would be broken at Badr.

But Makkah had already been warned, and not by any rider. Three nights before Damdam arrived, Atikah bint Abdul-Muttalib, the Prophet's ﷺ full aunt, the sister of his father Abdullah and of Abu Talib, woke in terror and sent for her brother Abbas. She had dreamt of a crier who came into Makkah on a camel and cried out three times: first by the masjid, then from the roof of the Kaaba, then from the peak of Abu Qubays, the mountain that overlooks the sanctuary. O you treacherous ones, he called, go out to your deaths in three days. Then he tore loose a great boulder and sent it crashing down the slope, and at the foot it shattered, and not a single house in Makkah was missed by a flying piece of it.

The interpretation needed no expert. Whatever the crier would announce would carry the deaths of these people within three days, and the boulder splintering into every home meant no household in Makkah would be spared its share of the calamity. Abbas left her shaken. This is a dangerous dream, he told her; tell no one. And then he told his friend al-Walid in confidence, who told his father in confidence, and by the next morning the whole of Makkah was retelling it, which is how secrets work, the Sheikh observes drily: if you want one kept, keep it yourself. Abu Jahl pounced. O sons of Abdul-Muttalib, he sneered before the Kaaba, was a man among you claiming prophethood not enough, that now your women must prophesy too? If three days pass and no crier comes, we will hang a writ on the Kaaba naming you the most lying house among the Arabs. Abbas denied everything. That evening the women of Banu Abdul-Muttalib rounded on him, stung that he had swallowed the insult in silence, and he resolved to face Abu Jahl in the morning.

He never needed the speech. Crossing toward the Kaaba on the third day, Abbas saw Abu Jahl catch sight of him, turn pale, and hurry away, and for one proud heartbeat he credited his own menace. Then he heard it. The crier had come. Damdam stood at the mouth of the valley on a camel he had deliberately mutilated, its nose cut and blood smeared across it, his own shirt torn front and back, turned backward on his saddle, every trick of desert melodrama deployed to make it seem he himself had been attacked, screaming to Quraysh: your caravan, your wealth with Abu Sufyan, Muhammad ﷺ and his companions are upon it, help, help! It was theater; no hand had yet touched a single camel, and the caravan was slipping safely toward the coast. Makkah believed every word.

Did Atikah herself ever embrace Islam? Here the seerah keeps its disagreement honestly, and the Sheikh keeps it with it. Ibn Ishaq, the earlier and weightier authority, counts only Safiyyah among the Prophet's ﷺ aunts as a Muslim; Ibn Sa'd says Atikah accepted Islam and migrated to Madinah; Ibn Hajar leans toward doubt, since after this dream the record falls silent about her, where a believer's deeds would have been preserved. Allah knows best. What no one disputes is what happened next: a city in which nearly every household had wealth riding on one caravan now heard that the caravan was dying, and a dream that had promised a stone through every doorway began, on schedule, to come true. The next six days will tell how.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this opening act as a tale of camels and clever spycraft and miss the questions it presses on us. Strip the day down to its moving parts and almost every one is asking something of your own life.

He cleared the rooms, sealed the destination, moved before the news could run, and only then trusted Allah. That rhythm is the cure for two diseases at once. Some of us mistake planning for a failure of trust, and so we drift, and call our drifting tawakkul. Others plan as though the outcome were ours to manufacture, and grind ourselves to dust over results that were never in our hands. He ﷺ shows the only way: tie the camel with everything you have, and then rest the rope in the hand of your Lord. Do the work. Send the scouts. Then let go.

He refused to ride while his companions walked. Offered the camel full time by two men who meant it with all their hearts, he answered that he was no stronger than they were and in no less need of the reward, and walked his turn through the Ramadan sand. Ask what your own rank is for, in your home or your work or your community. He ﷺ held the highest station any human has ever held, and used it to carry people, never to be carried by them.

And there are the two men who missed Badr rather than break a word given to their enemies under fear of death, the fighter turned away three times until he believed, and the blind man left to govern a city. Keep the word you have given, even when keeping it costs you the thing you most want, for a believer is bound by his conditions and treachery has no fine print. Judge what is outward and leave the secrets of the heart to the only One who reads them. See the ability and not the disability, as he ﷺ saw only that Ibn Umm Maktum was the wisest man for the job. Behind each of these small doors is the same large room: a way of living that takes Allah's arrangement seriously enough to trust it, and a man's given word seriously enough to die holding it.

So stand back tonight and look at the whole board, because this is where the day leaves us, mid story, with nothing yet decided. A terrified caravan is hugging the coastline. A rider on a bleeding camel is screaming before the Kaaba over an attack that has not happened. Three hundred and some believers, two horses among them, are camped in the desert expecting to walk home with no blood spilled. And over all of it hangs a dream that promised a splinter of flying stone through every doorway in Makkah within three days. Not one player on any side has planned what is actually coming: the believers set out to collect a caravan, and Allah had written something immeasurably larger. May Allah, who moved every piece of Badr while His servants saw only camels, teach us to trust what He arranges behind what we can see, keep us truthful to every word we give, set our feet on the road beside those who walk it for His sake, and send His peace and blessings upon the one who refused to ride while his companions walked, our master Muhammad ﷺ, until the day we meet him at his pool.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 36: the Battle of Badr, part 1 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (3:97, 5:1), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 37 of 104

The Battle of Badr, part 2: the march neither side could stop

Two unwilling armies, one appointment

The strangest thing about the most famous battle in Islam is that almost no one in it wanted to fight. We are used to imagining Badr as two hosts straining toward each other, swords already drawn in their hearts. The truth is the opposite. On one road a city marched out in a fury and then spent the whole way arguing with itself about turning back. On the other road a small band rode out for easy plunder and learned, mile by mile, that an army was waiting for them instead. Dr. Yasir Qadhi gives this entire episode to the days before a single blade is drawn, and it is some of the most human ground in the whole seerah: cowards hiding behind hired men, a noble pagan shamed into his own grave, Shaytan marching in a borrowed face, and a Prophet ﷺ who asks his companions one question four separate times, waiting for the right people to answer it.

Last week the story had split in two. A caravan was slipping home along the coast, because Abu Sufyan had sensed the Muslims watching the road and swung his camels toward the Red Sea, while a crier tore into Makkah with an exaggerated scream of alarm that set every street alight. This is the story of what that scream set in motion, and of how Allah walked two reluctant armies, step by step, toward a meeting that neither of them set and neither could stop.

Makkah empties, and the army of the unwilling

The crier's report did exactly what it was sent to do. The Quraysh convened their council, and the verdict was almost unanimous before the debate began: the caravan was not luxury cargo, it was the city's savings, the livelihood of every family that had bought a share, and an army had to ride out at once to protect it. And so there took place the largest and fastest muster in the history of Makkah, preparations that should have eaten up weeks finished inside a day. Ibn Ishaq preserves the detail that gives the gathering its eerie weight: not a single household of Makkah stayed out of it. Every family sent a fighting man, and any family that could not hired a man in its place. Anyone who sat through the first part of this story will feel a chill here, because this is the dream of Atikah coming true in real time. She had seen a rock split from the mountain and a fragment fly to every single house of Quraysh, with no household spared. Now every household was sending out a man, and every household, soon enough, would have someone to grieve.

Look closer, though, and you find this proud host riddled with men who wanted no part of it. Begin at the very top. Abu Lahab, chieftain of Banu Hashim, did not go. He found a man who owed him four thousand dirhams and wiped the whole debt clean in exchange for the man taking his place. The classical books never say why he stayed home, and the Sheikh flags his own explanation as a theory: alongside the plain cowardice of any man who fears being killed, perhaps even Abu Lahab could not draw a sword against his own sub-tribe, for the men across that field would be Banu Hashim, his own blood, and the one they marched against was his own nephew ﷺ. The good that ever flickered in him was never iman. It was tribe.

Then there was Utbah ibn Rabiah, the distant uncle who had once sent a tray of grapes to the Prophet ﷺ as he sat bleeding outside Ta'if. Utbah refused to march at first, for these were his own kin. It was his brother Shaybah who turned him with the oldest argument in Arabia: if we hang back at a moment like this, we will spend the rest of our lives under the mockery of Quraysh. So the two brothers packed for war, and neither understood he was packing for his own funeral. They would be the very first to fall at Badr, in the mubaraza, the duel of champions with which the Arabs opened their battles. Hold on to Utbah, because the days just ahead belong partly to him: when the two armies finally face off, he will gallop through his own ranks on a red camel begging Quraysh not to spill the blood of their own sons and brothers, until Abu Jahl questions his courage and rage accomplishes what wisdom never could, and Utbah throws himself first into the duel and dies for the very battle his own counsel had argued against. His loyalty was never to the truth; it was to the tribe, and the tribe is a cause that cannot carry a man safely home. This is one of the judgments the Sheikh returns to again and again: Islam reserves unconditional following for exactly one human being, the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. Every other person, party, and cause must be weighed against the truth, even when the truth testifies against your own side.

You cannot outrun what is written

Across the whole series, Dr. Yasir Qadhi keeps pointing to a spectrum among the enemies of Islam: dignified ones like Abu Sufyan, who never stooped to something vulgar and whom, as a general rule, Allah eventually guided, because some good remained in them; and crude ones, with no floor beneath them, whom as a general rule He did not. At the very bottom sat Uqbah ibn Abi Muayt, a man so filthy, the Sheikh suggests, that the Qur'an does not even dignify him with a direct mention.

His record speaks. It was Uqbah who crept up behind the Prophet ﷺ as he prayed at the Kaaba and twisted a garment around his neck to throttle him, until Abu Bakr radiyallahu anhu threw himself in and took the beating in his place, crying out words that would themselves become a verse of the Qur'an, the protest of a believing man of Pharaoh's house: do you kill a man merely because he says, my Lord is Allah? It was Uqbah who, when Abu Jahl asked who would heap the entrails of a slaughtered camel on the Prophet's ﷺ back as he lay in sajdah, leapt up to do it, a rich nobleman carrying filth in his own hands until young Fatima came weeping to pull the load off her father. And it was Uqbah who once spat in the face of the Prophet ﷺ, who calmly wiped it away and gave him a single appointment: the first day I meet you outside the valleys of Makkah, I will put you to death. So when the call to Badr came, Uqbah was terrified, because somewhere beneath the filth he believed the promise. A kinsman talked him out the gate: I own the fastest camel there is; even if the whole army breaks, this beast will carry you home. At Badr, when the army broke, that camel was the first creature on the field to bolt, and it bolted without its rider. Uqbah was left alone on an empty plain and taken prisoner; and of all the captives of Badr only two would be executed. Uqbah headed the list. The appointment was kept to the letter.

And there was Umayyah ibn Khalaf, the slave master who had tortured Bilal in the sun: huge, perfumed, a man of money with no stomach for war, who hired a stand-in and settled back, relieved. But Umayyah was one of the five great lords of Makkah, and Abu Jahl could not afford the hole his absence would tear in morale. When flattery failed, Abu Jahl sent Uqbah into the public square with a smoking censer and women's perfume: scent yourself, Umayyah, for surely you are one of the women. The barb landed precisely where it was aimed, and Umayyah, cursing the man who had clearly sent it, bought the finest camel he could and rode out swearing to his wife he would only make a show of it and slip home. But he had forgotten an older reckoning. The day the entrails were thrown on him ﷺ, the Prophet ﷺ had risen and named seven or eight of the lords of Makkah aloud, handing them over to Allah: Abu Jahl among them, Uqbah among them, Umayyah among them. Every single man on that list marched out to Badr. Not one of them marched back.

The whole spectacle had begun with a prayer that doomed those who made it. One of the early works of tafsir records that before the army marched, the Quraysh gripped the rings of the Kaaba and prayed: O Allah, whichever of these two armies is nobler in Your eyes, grant it victory; whichever of the two parties is more honorable, send Your aid down upon it. They thought they were buying divine insurance. They were signing their own sentence, and Allah answered them in Surah al-Anfal, the surah that walks through Badr from its first verse to its last.

If you [disbelievers] seek the decision [i.e., victory] - the decision [i.e., defeat] has come to you. And if you desist [from hostilities], it is best for you; but if you return [to war], We will return, and never will you be availed by your [large] company at all, even if it should increase; and [that is] because Allah is with the believers.Qur'an 8:19

Then they marched: thirteen hundred men, more than a hundred horses, six hundred coats of armor, camels enough to slaughter ten a day, and even their singing girls, drums beating, to boast across the desert. Nothing like it existed in Arab memory, where a tribal war was a hundred men against two hundred; now thirteen hundred moved as one body, the first army in a series that would break its own record again and again, because Allah was accelerating Arabia toward an end none of them could yet see. But watch the niyyah behind the parade, because the Qur'an watched it. Allah recorded their procession in Surah al-Anfal, describing men who came forth from their homes insolently and to be seen by people, and then turned to this ummah, for all time: do not be like them. They imagined they were blocking the path of Allah; they had not grasped that Allah had encompassed them entirely.

Shaytan joins the march

The cracks showed before Makkah was even out of sight. The Qur'an's portrait of the disbelievers fit this army exactly: you think them united, but their hearts are diverse. Barely on the road, a panic rolled through the ranks. There was an old blood feud with the Banu Bakr that the arrival of Islam had frozen but never finished, and now every fighting man of Quraysh was walking away from an undefended city. What if the Banu Bakr fell upon their homes the moment their backs were turned? A large part of the army was ready to wheel around on the spot.

Shaytan could not permit that. Iblis himself, the Qur'an testifies, appeared to them in the form of Suraqah ibn Malik, a trusted chieftain of the Banu Kinanah, the wider tribe to which the Banu Bakr belonged. Do not be afraid, he told them; I guarantee you Makkah is safe from them, and to prove it I will march with you myself. The army cheered the great chieftain's pledge, and the devil kept pace with them the whole road to Badr, polishing their confidence as he went.

And [remember] when Satan made their deeds pleasing to them and said, "No one can overcome you today from among the people, and indeed, I am your protector." But when the two armies sighted each other, he turned on his heels and said, "Indeed, I am disassociated from you. Indeed, I see what you do not see; indeed, I fear Allah. And Allah is severe in penalty."Qur'an 8:48

Keep the ending of that verse beside its beginning. On the morning of the battle, when this false Suraqah saw what no pagan eye could see, the angels of Allah descending rank upon rank, he spun on his heels and ran, shoving away a soldier who grabbed at him so hard that the man flew through the air. And the devil's parting sermon to the congregation he had so carefully gathered was the single truest thing he ever told them: I see what you do not see; I fear Allah. He marched them all the way to the field, and abandoned them on it. That, the Sheikh notes, is the whole career of Shaytan in one ayah: he beautifies the road in, and he is gone at the precise moment you need him.

Every exit refused

Then a rider came up from the coast with Abu Sufyan's second message: the caravan is safe, I will be back in Makkah within days, the army can go home. Notice that even Abu Sufyan, whose goods had set all of this in motion, wanted no battle at all. And Utbah, the voice of plain sense to the very end, said it openly: the job is done; let us turn back. Abu Jahl refused. No, he said, we march on to Badr, camp there three days, slaughter our camels, drink our wine, let the girls sing for us, and all of Arabia will hear that Quraysh is mighty and not to be touched. There was still, even now, no military reason left to fight a soul; this was theater, pride hunting for an audience. The Banu Zahra wanted none of it and turned for home, and others with them, some three hundred to three hundred and fifty men, and the army shrank from thirteen hundred to about a thousand. A full third of the host had walked away.

One more warning was wasted on them on the road. The youngest man of the Banu Hashim contingent woke from a dream shaking: he had seen a rider come crying through the camp that Utbah and Shaybah and Abu Jahl and Umayyah were slain, name after name of Quraysh, and then gash his camel and drive it loose until its blood spattered every single tent. The meaning lay in plain sight, the same warning Atikah had been given, that no household would be spared a loss. They laughed it off as a boy's bad sleep, and kept marching. So the march had become a strange thing: the caravan safe, a third of the army gone home, the wisest man in the camp arguing for retreat, the camp's own dreams screaming at it to stop. And on the other road, as we are about to see, the Muslims wanted no battle either.

[Remember] when you were on the near side of the valley, and they were on the farther side, and the caravan was lower [in position] than you. If you had made an appointment [to meet], you would have missed the appointment. But [it was] so that Allah might accomplish a matter already destined - that those who perished [through disbelief] would perish upon evidence and those who lived [in faith] would live upon evidence; and indeed, Allah is Hearing and Knowing.Qur'an 8:42

Two armies, neither of which chose this meeting, walked toward the same wells anyway, because the appointment was never theirs to set or to cancel.

The army that only wanted a caravan

Now cross to the other road. Some three hundred and fifteen men had ridden out of Madinah expecting an easy raid: a caravan guarded by forty riders, odds of nearly ten to one in their favor, a few days out and back. They had packed provisions for an errand, not armor for a war. Then the rumors began to drift in from travelers met along the way: an army has left Makkah, and it is looking for you. The Prophet ﷺ had been shown a dream that he would meet a fighting force, and had quietly hoped it might be meant for some later expedition. Now he began testing the camp. What do you think, he asked, if we met a force from Makkah that has been warned of our coming? Some answered honestly: Messenger of Allah, we made no preparations for that; we came out for the caravan. The next day he asked again, and the answer came back firmer still. The Qur'an preserves the scene without the smallest flattery.

Arguing with you concerning the truth after it had become clear, as if they were being driven toward death while they were looking on.Qur'an 8:6

And here is the mercy folded inside the rebuke. In the very passage that corrects them, Allah calls them believers; the verse just before names them a party among the believers who were unwilling. There were no hypocrites yet, for the disease of nifaq only appears after Badr makes Islam worth faking. So these arguers were sincere mu'minin who were simply afraid, and Allah records their fear without expelling them from faith. Battle had been prescribed for them while they hated it, just as He says elsewhere: enjoined upon you while it is hateful to you, and perhaps you hate a thing that is good for you. The trembling before a hard duty, the Sheikh notes, is not the sin; surrendering to it is. And beneath the fear sat a promise that had already been given.

[Remember, O believers], when Allah promised you one of the two groups - that it would be yours - and you wished that the unarmed one would be yours. But Allah intended to establish the truth by His words and to eliminate the disbelieversQur'an 8:7

They ached for the unarmed party, the easy spoils. Allah intended the armed one, and with it something larger: that the truth would be set firm by His words, and that this day would earn the name al-Furqan, the criterion splitting truth from falsehood once and for all. So the Prophet ﷺ kept repeating one line: whichever of the two we meet, Allah has promised us that it is ours.

We are from water

Nearing the wells, certainty was still missing, and what the Prophet ﷺ did next is, in the Sheikh's reading of the whole seerah, the only time it ever happened: he went out as his own scout, with no deputy but the one man he trusted beyond every other, Abu Bakr. A scout rides beyond the protection of the army, where any hand may seize him, and that is the courage of it; and he ﷺ would not carry back a word he had not verified with his own eyes, and that is the care of it. They came upon an old bedouin, one of the desert's professional neutrals who survived by knowing everything and belonging to no one. What word, the Prophet ﷺ asked him, is there of Quraysh, and of Muhammad and his army? I will tell you nothing, the old man bargained, until I know which side you are on. Speak, the Prophet ﷺ told him, and I promise to tell you where we are from. So the bedouin reported that Muhammad had left Yathrib on a certain day, which would place him now near Badr, and Quraysh on another, which would place them on the far side of it, and both reckonings were exactly right. Now, said the old man, your end of the bargain: where are you from?

We are from water, said the Prophet ﷺ, and he turned his camel and rode away, leaving the bedouin to run through every clan and valley named Water he had ever heard of. It was not a lie. It was tawriyah, a truth wearing a veil, for Allah asks in Surah al-Anbiya whether the deniers have not seen that He made from water every living thing. The scholars note both its permission and its limit, that a tongue which leans on it too freely will soon be called a liar. But in a scout's mouth, on the rim of a battlefield, it was wisdom, and it kept the secret without costing the truth.

Back at the camp, the Prophet ﷺ stood in a long prayer, and a commotion rose up behind him. The companions had caught two slave boys of the Quraysh army at the water. We are from the army, the boys said, truthfully. But the companions' hearts were still fixed on the caravan, so they beat the boys until they said what was wanted, we are from the caravan, and stopped; and when the truth crept back, the beating resumed. When the Prophet ﷺ finished his prayer he put the absurdity into one sentence: when they tell you the truth you beat them, and when they lie to you, you let them go. Under a stick a man will say anything; the only thing those sticks had silenced was the one fact the companions could not bear to hear. Then he ﷺ questioned the boys himself. How many men are they? We are only slaves, they said, we do not count armies. So he asked a question fitted to their work: how many camels do they slaughter each day? Nine, they said, some days ten. Then they are between nine hundred and a thousand, he said, for a single camel feeds about a hundred men. Two gentle questions had retrieved what all the beating could not. And which of the noblemen are with them? The boys recited a who's who of Makkah, every senior enemy of Islam gathered into one camp, and the companions' faces fell, for if the lords had come, so had their wealth and armor and hired men. But the Prophet ﷺ brightened, because every name was collateral on a promise Allah had already made, and he answered with a phrase Arabic almost refuses to surrender into another language: Makkah has thrown to you the dearest pieces of its own liver, the cream of its crop, the very apple of its eye. Then he began to walk the ground and point. By Allah, he said, here falls Utbah, here Shaybah, here Umayyah, here Abu Jahl. Days later, every one of those men would be found dead on the exact patch of earth his finger ﷺ had marked.

Until the Ansar spoke

One thing remained, and it explains the strangest detail of the whole night. The Prophet ﷺ, who needed counsel from no human being, gathered his companions and asked them: advise me, what should we do? Abu Bakr rose, praised Allah, and said: it is your command, and we are behind you. The Prophet ﷺ thanked him, and asked the same question again. Umar rose and said the like of it, and was thanked, and the question came a third time. So al-Miqdad of the muhajirun stood with fire in his mouth. O Messenger of Allah, he said, we will not say to you what the Children of Israel said to Musa, when they told their prophet in the words Allah preserved in Surah al-Ma'idah to go, he and his Lord, and fight, for they would sit where they were. Rather, said al-Miqdad, go, you and your Lord, and fight, and we are fighting right beside you; take us to the farthest corner of the earth and we will follow you. And still the Prophet ﷺ asked: what do you think we should do? Why keep asking? Because Abu Bakr and Umar were Quraysh, and al-Miqdad was a muhajir, and the pledge of Aqabah had bound the Ansar to one thing only: to defend the Prophet ﷺ inside Madinah as they would defend their own families. Badr was not Madinah, and this was not defense. He ﷺ would not drag them an inch past the letter of their oath; he wanted them to step past it of their own accord. And the chief of the Ansar understood. Sa'd ibn Muadh radiyallahu anhu, the man at whose death, years later, the Throne of the Most Merciful would shake, rose and said: it seems it is us you mean, O Messenger of Allah. He said yes.

Then Sa'd gave the answer the whole night had been waiting to hear. We have believed in you, he said, and borne witness that what you brought is the truth, and we have given you our oaths to hear and to obey. Notice that he never once reached for the exemption he was entitled to claim; he answered the spirit of his pledge and left its letter where it lay. By the One who sent you with the truth, he said, if you were to plunge into this sea we would plunge into it right behind you, and not a man of us would hang back on the shore. We are not afraid to meet the enemy tomorrow; we are patient in war and true at the encounter; and perhaps Allah will show you, through us, what will comfort your eye. So march, with the blessing of Allah. And the face of the Prophet ﷺ lit up like the shining moon. March, he said, and receive glad tidings, for Allah has promised me one of the two parties, and this is the one; and every man of theirs whom I have named will fall. The desert men who could not swim had just promised him the ocean. And so the two armies lay down within reach of the same wells, to wake in the morning at Badr.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this episode as stagecraft before the real drama of the battle, all marching and counting and bargaining, and to wait for the swords. That would be to miss the question it presses on us, the same one the Prophet ﷺ pressed four times on his own companions: when a duty you did not choose is suddenly camped across the valley from you, what will you say?

Look first at the men who came out to silence that question with noise. Utbah argued against the battle with genuine wisdom and then charged into it the instant his pride was pricked, dying for a cause he himself had named as folly. Uqbah secured the fastest camel in the army and it fled without him. Umayyah hired a stand-in and swore to his wife he would only pretend, and fell on appointed ground all the same. Every one of them spent his cleverness trying to bend the outcome, and the outcome did not bend. So the day asks us to stop pouring our strength into outrunning what Allah has written, and to pour it instead into meeting Him well when it comes. And it asks something sharper about where we place our loyalty, for Utbah followed his tribe over the truth he could plainly see, and the tribe carried him straight to his death. Only one human being in all of history is to be followed without condition, the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. Everyone else, every party we are born into and every cause that flatters us, must be laid against the truth and kept only as far as it agrees, even when the truth indicts our own.

Then look at the men on the other road, the ones who trembled and did not run. Allah corrected the companions who argued against marching, and in the very same breath He called them believers. That single word should steady everyone who has ever mistaken his own fear for the failure of his faith. The dread before a hard obedience, the weight of the blanket over Fajr, the racing heart before a first Hajj, is not nifaq and it is not weakness of belief; it is the cost of being human, and it takes nothing from the iman that overrules it. The sin was never in the trembling; it would only have been in staying home.

And then there is Sa'd, offered every honest excuse and refusing all of them. He had pledged to defend the Prophet ﷺ within the walls of his own city, and not a soul would have blamed him for holding to exactly that; instead he reached past the letter of his oath to its spirit, and pledged the Ansar into the open desert and the sea beyond it. So when Allah asks of us only the minimum, will we hand Him the minimum and call the account settled, or answer like the Ansar and give Him the spirit of the thing and not merely its letter? Take this night, then, down into your ordinary days. Follow the truth above your tribe and your own comfort. Spend your strength preparing to meet the decree rather than scheming to escape it. Tremble, if you must, and then march anyway. And when Allah asks more of you than He strictly could have, answer Him as the Ansar did: by the One who sent you with the truth, if you plunged into the sea, we would plunge in behind you. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, make us of those who follow the truth wherever it leads, who fear and obey all the same, and write for each of us the company of the people of Badr in the gardens of His pleasure.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 37: the Battle of Badr, part 2 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International, quoted at 8:19, 8:48, 8:42, 8:6, and 8:7 and referenced from 8:47, 21:30, and 5:24, all verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing has been followed.

The Seerah · 38 of 104

The Battle of Badr, part 3

An army asleep, a Prophet ﷺ awake

The day before this one ended with a pledge. The Ansar had stood and committed themselves to whatever their Prophet ﷺ asked of them, and now the pledge is to be spent. Three hundred and some believers are gathered on the plains of Badr, and across the open sand the host of Quraysh is arriving in the failing light, nearly three times their number, armored, mounted, provisioned for a war the believers had not gone out to fight. This is the third of the seven days the seerah spends inside this battle, and it is the hinge of the whole story. Nothing has clashed yet, and by the time the sun goes down on it, everything that decides the outcome will already have happened, except the fighting.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi builds this episode almost entirely out of Surah al-Anfal, the surah he reads as one long commentary on Badr, and out of a single image the Companions could never afterward shake loose: an entire army stretched out fast asleep under a soft rain, and one man ﷺ awake beneath a tree, pleading until the sky turned grey. Hold that image, because the day is built around it.

Three flags and two flanks

With the Ansar committed, the manpower was his to arrange, and the Prophet ﷺ divided the army into its parts. The primary standard, the flag the whole field would orient itself by, was white that day. He kept no single banner across his life; the color changed from battle to battle, whatever was at hand, sometimes white, sometimes black. This white flag he handed to Mus'ab ibn Umayr, the same Mus'ab who would meet his shahadah years later at Uhud. The right flank, with the Muhajirun, he gave to Ali ibn Abi Talib. The left flank, with the Ansar, he gave to Sa'd ibn Mu'adh. One report adds a small reserve held in the rear under Qays ibn Sa'sa'a, against reinforcement or some shift in the line.

Linger over the choices, because the Sheikh does. The Muhajirun were placed with the Muhajirun and the Ansar with the Ansar, and that arrangement carries a lesson some Muslims badly need. Men fight best beside the men they were raised with; the Muhajirun knew one another, had grown up together, were easy in each other's company, and so were the Ansar among themselves. The instinct of some believers to erase culture and lineage entirely, to pretend that ethnicity simply does not exist, is itself an extreme, and Allah corrects it in His own words:

O mankind, indeed We have created you from male and female and made you peoples and tribes that you may know one another. Indeed, the most noble of you in the sight of Allah is the most righteous of you. Indeed, Allah is Knowing and Aware.Qur'an 49:13

He made us peoples and tribes on purpose, so that we would come to know one another, and so that each of us would carry a recognizable self. If every face and every taste were identical, what would there be to know? Islam does not flatten that variety; it gathers it. And neither does it pretend that all people weigh the same in their communities, for the Prophet ﷺ set a young, respected nobleman over each flank, Ali over the one and Sa'd ibn Mu'adh over the other, men whose own people already looked up to them. In war above all, no soldier should be left wondering why a stranger commands him. Leadership belongs to those a community already respects.

And the flag, the most honored post on the field and the most dangerous, went to the one man both halves of the army could claim. The bearer is not the commander; he is the fixed point the army reads to know which way it faces in the chaos, and he fights one hand always on the staff, while every enemy wants nothing more than to see that flag drop, for a falling flag breaks a watching army's heart. Mus'ab was Qurayshi by blood, of the noblest of them, and yet the most Madani of all the Muhajirun, the teacher sent ahead at whose hands most of the Ansar had entered Islam. No one could have symbolized the whole army better. One flag, deliberately chosen, stitching two peoples into a single ummah.

Is this revelation, or is it opinion?

The Prophet ﷺ reached Badr first, on the sixteenth of Ramadan in the second year of the hijrah, a full day ahead of Quraysh, and made camp at the near edge of the plain. Before the camp had properly settled, a man came forward whose whole reputation was the desert: al-Hubab ibn al-Mundhir, a seasoned traveler who had spent long stretches reading this kind of land. He put to the Prophet ﷺ one of the great questions of the seerah. O Messenger of Allah, this place you have chosen to camp: is it something Allah has commanded you, so that we may not move from it a single step forward or back, or is it your own opinion, the tactics and the strategy of war? No, came the answer, it is my own opinion.

Then this is not the place, said Hubab. Do not camp at the corner of the plain. March on, past the midpoint, until the wells of Badr lie behind us. We will hold the water, drink our fill, and leave Quraysh to whatever their jugs carried from Makkah, and there is no heavier blow to an army's spirit than thirst it can see no end to. In one narration Jibril came and told him to follow Hubab's counsel. The Prophet ﷺ rose and did exactly that, telling Hubab he had pointed them to the better course. He set the wells at his back, the great one and the several smaller, and to be safe he had the smaller stopped up, their water hauled out and poured into the great well now at the center of the Muslim camp, so that Quraysh would reach no water on any side. Then, at Sa'd ibn Mu'adh's suggestion, a shaded headquarters was raised on higher ground from which the Prophet ﷺ could watch over the field.

Sit with what just happened, because it is shura made visible. The Messenger of Allah ﷺ pulled no rank. He took the counsel of a single foot soldier and moved an entire army on the strength of it, one of dozens of such moments in his life; he was never the dictator who must have his own way. But the Sheikh plants a warning here, because this very story gets pulled out of shape. Some people stretch this one war-camp question into a license to sort the whole religion into commands they must keep and opinions they may discard, saying the prayer and zakah bind but the laws of marriage, divorce, inheritance, and dealings were merely his personal view. This is, in the Sheikh's plain word, extremism. Where to pitch a tent at Badr legislated nothing; Badr happened once, and he ﷺ never meant the campsite as a ruling for anyone to follow. The prayer, the fast, the laws he taught the believers of Madinah: that is sharia, and Allah binds us to it, for if you obey him you are guided. And notice that even Hubab did not presume to decide for himself which was which. He asked. Who among us is in any position to do now what Hubab did then? What the Prophet ﷺ said and did is his sharia, and the handful of times a Companion ever asked outright, is this a command or a suggestion, can be counted on the fingers of one hand.

The night Allah put an army to sleep

Night fell, and on the far horizon the believers could watch Quraysh pouring onto the plain. There would be no battle tonight; everyone understood that tomorrow was the day. It is narrated in the Musnad of Imam Ahmad that the Prophet ﷺ spent that entire night awake, standing, pleading, sinking into long prostration, and at the heart of his crying was one sentence: O Allah, if You destroy this small band, You will not be worshipped on earth. He meant it exactly: I am the final prophet, and if this handful falls, there is no messenger to come after me, and the worship of You leaves the earth with us. Ibn Mas'ud, who lived a long life among the believers, said he never in all of it saw a human being plead the way the Prophet ﷺ pleaded that night. And Ali would remember it for years: if only you had seen us at Badr, every one of us dead asleep, and the Messenger of Allah ﷺ alone beneath a tree, praying and making du'a until the morning came.

Feel how strange that sentence is. The night before the first decisive battle of Islam, outnumbered men about to face their own fathers and brothers in the morning, and they sleep like children. The night before any ordinary examination robs us of sleep; how was anyone to sleep before this? But the Qur'an names the sleep itself as the gift, the work of Allah and not of their own calm:

[Remember] when He overwhelmed you with drowsiness [giving] security from Him and sent down upon you from the sky, rain by which to purify you and remove from you the evil [suggestions] of Satan and to make steadfast your hearts and plant firmly thereby your feet.Qur'an 8:11

Read what that single verse loads onto one night. The drowsiness was security straight from Him, and sleep is what makes a body fresh and able the next day. The rain that fell in the dark was not a downpour but a drizzle, just enough, and it did three things at once: it washed them clean, a physical bath for tired and dusty men; it drove off the whispering of shaytan, a spiritual bath beneath the first; and it bound the loose desert sand firm beneath their feet, for dry sand swallows a step and heavy rain turns to mud, while just the right rain packs it hard as pavement. Some books add that Quraysh caught a heavy, miring downpour instead, though Sheikh Yasir notes he found no authentic chain for that detail and hands it on only as it came. What is certain is that one side received sleep and the perfect rain, and the other received neither.

Somewhere in that night, Ibn Kathir holds, the Prophet ﷺ briefly dozed, and in that dozing Allah showed him the dream the Qur'an records:

[Remember, O Muhammad], when Allah showed them to you in your dream as few; and if He had shown them to you as many, you [believers] would have lost courage and would have disputed in the matter [of whether to fight], but Allah saved [you from that]. Indeed, He is Knowing of that within the breasts.Qur'an 8:43

The Sheikh is careful with this, because it could be misread. It was not false information, for Allah never shows what is untrue. If a hundred men are shown as ten, that is a true portion of the hundred; it would only be a lie to show a hundred as a hundred and fifty. He ﷺ was shown a real section of the enemy, and the sight planted confidence where dread had every right to live, a surge of certainty before the victory had even begun, and that surge was itself the mercy. Then the dawn broke, and it was he ﷺ, the one man who had not slept, who walked among the sleepers calling out: O people, the prayer. So the believers were woken for Fajr, and the seventeenth of Ramadan in the second year of the hijrah began. By some modern reckonings this was the seventeenth of March, 624, and it appears to have fallen on a Friday.

Rows like prayer, and a poke repaid

The Arabs of that age fought in the old way they called al-farr wal-karr: surge out, strike, wheel back to recover, surge again, attacking in waves and circling home between them. After Fajr the Prophet ﷺ arranged something Arabia had never drilled. He stood the army in ranks, rows and files like a congregation lined for salah, the few spears set at the front, the swords forming the body, the handful of bows behind, every man with a sword because swords were what they had. He had attended no academy of war, had never seen this done, and yet it simply came to him, and the same Qur'an that loves the worshippers standing in lines names the same shape on the battlefield:

Indeed, Allah loves those who fight in His cause in a row as though they are a [single] structure joined firmly.Qur'an 61:4

A single structure, joined firmly: fourteen centuries later, ranks and files are exactly how every army on earth is taught to stand and to march, the same psychology, the same solidity, and he ﷺ was given it whole, with no precedent behind him.

He walked between the rows with a small stick, straightening the line the way he straightened it for prayer, and found one Companion, Sawad, standing forward out of the line. He prodded his stomach with the stick: get back in line, Sawad. And Sawad, with the battle minutes away, said the unthinkable. You have hurt me without cause, O Messenger of Allah, and Allah has sent you with truth and justice, so I demand my retribution. Picture any other general, in any other army, on any other morning, hearing that from a private over the lightest poke of a stick. The Prophet ﷺ did not pause and did not bristle. He dropped the stick, lifted his garment clear of his stomach, and bared the skin: here, take your retribution. And Sawad fell upon him and pressed his mouth to the bared skin, kissing it. What is this, Sawad? O Messenger of Allah, you see what stands before us today, and I wanted, if I am to die, that my last moment in this world be my skin against yours. The Prophet ﷺ prayed for him and moved on down the line.

The Sheikh draws this out, because it is one of the reasons, he says, that the religion led the world for as long as it did. On the very morning the whole religion could have perished, its justice still ran level over the leader and the led. The man who carried revelation conceded that someone he had poked held a claim against him, and he settled it on the spot, with the enemy in sight. He did not say, not now, there is a war to win. There is no crisis large enough, in his example, to suspend a person's right over the one who leads them.

The man on the red camel

Now cross the field, as the lecture does, to Quraysh. They had arrived late and uncertain of what they faced, so in the early light they sent out their sharpest scout, Umayr ibn Wahb al-Jumahi, to ride wide around the plain and bring back a count. His report was precise enough: around three hundred, no armor, no real cavalry, nothing in their hands but their swords. Then came the assessment no one had asked for. I see catastrophe coming. These are young men of Yathrib with nothing but their blades, and men with nothing fight like the desperate. By Allah, you will not kill one of them before he kills one of you, and if they take three hundred of you, a third of this army, what sweetness is left in life afterward? It was an honest reading. Abu Jahl waved it away: we did not ask your opinion, only the number.

Others were reaching for a way out too. More than a third of the army had already turned back once the caravan was known to be safe, and the doubt had not left those who remained. Hakim ibn Hizam carried a proposal to Utbah ibn Rabi'ah, the senior chief who had opposed this march from the first day and come to it grudgingly. Pay the blood money for Amr al-Hadrami, the man killed months earlier at Nakhlah whose name the war party kept chanting as their grievance, and the pretext for the whole battle dissolves. Utbah, a rich man and a noble one, stood and offered to pay it from his own wealth. If anyone calls you cowards for turning back, he told the army, lay the name on me; say Utbah turned coward. What will you win here? You will kill your own fathers, your own sons, your own cousins, then go home to live as neighbors with the man whose hand killed your brother. Leave this man and his companions to the rest of the Arabs: if the Arabs deal with him, your hands stay clean, and if he prevails, his glory is your glory, for he ﷺ is one of you in the end.

Watching from the Muslim lines, the Prophet ﷺ saw a rider hurrying back and forth on a red camel through the enemy ranks and said: if there is any good in them, it is with the man on the red camel; if they listen to him, they will be rightly guided. He sent Ali forward to call to Hamza, who stood at the front, and learn who the rider was. Sheikh Yasir stops the narrative here for one of the episode's sharpest lessons. Utbah's motive was nothing but tribal jahiliyyah; he did not argue that the believers were upon the truth, he argued from blood, from the horror of a clan killing its own. His reason was pagan, and yet his position was wise, and the Prophet ﷺ named that wisdom as good from clear across a battlefield. A person can be an idolater and still hold to principles worth admiring, worth respecting, even worth supporting. The believers who insist that no cause deserves their backing unless it was born inside revelation, that one must stand wholly apart from any system not their own, have misread their own seerah. Here is the Prophet ﷺ calling an idolater's counsel the one good thing in Quraysh. A just cause is a just cause, wherever it stands.

Abu Jahl made certain wisdom lost. He mocked Hakim as a mere errand boy, and Hakim threw it back, that he carried his own conviction and wanted no bloodshed either. Then Abu Jahl went to the slain man's own brother, shamed him, and pushed him forward to raise the cry for vengeance before any mind could soften, and he told Utbah to his face that the sight of the Muslim ranks had turned his courage to fear. Utbah, who minutes before had volunteered to wear the name of coward for the good of all, could not take the word from this man. He flung a coarse insult back, swore Abu Jahl would soon see who the coward truly was, and called his own brother and his own son out beside him for single combat, then and there. He acted on a wound to his pride, and it killed him. As the lines drew up, Abu Jahl raised his own voice in prayer for all to hear: O Allah, whichever of these two armies has brought the greater evil, cut more of the ties of kinship, and carried the stranger doctrine, destroy it today. On every one of the three counts he had described himself and not the Prophet ﷺ, who carried the ancient creed of Ibrahim while it was Abu Jahl who clung to the newer idols and fought precisely to sever the ties of his own blood. And the Qur'an is Allah's reply to that prayer:

If you [disbelievers] seek the decision [i.e., victory] - the decision [i.e., defeat] has come to you. And if you desist [from hostilities], it is best for you; but if you return [to war], We will return, and never will you be availed by your [large] company at all, even if it should increase; and [that is] because Allah is with the believers.Qur'an 8:19

You asked for a verdict, and the verdict has come.

Three against three

The Prophet ﷺ had his own orders moving down the line as the armies faced off: certain men of the enemy were not to be killed if it could be helped. Among them his uncle al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib, and Abul Bukhtari, who had once stood tall among those who broke the boycott of Banu Hashim. These were men dragged out to this field against their will, and even across a line of battle the Prophet ﷺ refused to flatten an army into a single undifferentiated enemy. Not all enemies are the same, the Sheikh says; even among those marching to kill you, some are better than others, and some may carry no hatred in their hearts at all.

First blood had in fact already been spilled. Al-Aswad of Makhzum, the tribe of Abu Jahl, had come upon the blocked wells in a fury and sworn he would drink from that water or die in the attempt. Hamza met him short of it and cut him down, and he died trying, the first man killed at Badr, though whether it fell on the night of the sixteenth or the morning of the seventeenth the books of sira do not settle.

Then came the mubaraza, Arabia's old curtain-raiser, the duel of champions out in the open ground between the armies before the armies themselves close. Utbah strode forward with his brother Shaybah and his son al-Walid, calling for opponents. Three young men of the Ansar leapt up first, Awf and Mu'awwidh the two sons of Afra, who will return in this story to meet Abu Jahl, and Abdullah ibn Rawahah, eager to prove themselves against Quraysh. Utbah waved them off. We have no quarrel with you, people of Yathrib; we do not even know you. Send us our equals, men of our own blood. Even with the proof of it standing in front of him, the jahili mind could not register that iman had made these strangers closer than blood. So the Prophet ﷺ called the names himself: rise, Ubaydah ibn al-Harith; rise, Hamza; rise, Ali. He sent out his own house, a cousin of his father, his uncle, and the young man raised in his shadow. Ubaydah was the eldest, so the pairing fell out by age, the one Ibn Ishaq records and the only one that makes sense: Ubaydah to Utbah, Hamza to Shaybah, Ali to al-Walid. Hamza and Ali killed their men without taking a single blow, then turned to find that Utbah had sliced through Ubaydah's leg and was about to finish him; together they cut Utbah down and carried Ubaydah back on their shoulders. He died of that wound some days after the battle, an elderly man whose leg could not be saved, a shaheed by its slow effects.

Three of the cream of Quraysh lay dead within minutes, the appetizer, as the Sheikh calls it, of a victory Allah had already decided to give them whole. But notice who one of the three was: Utbah, the one man who had argued against this war from the first day to this very morning, dead now beside his own brother and his own son, all three because he could not bear Abu Jahl saying a single word to his face. These were neither the worst of Quraysh nor the best, but their tribal heat, the bare arrogance of my tribe, right or wrong, burned them up in the end. When a person's morals stand on tribe and pride instead of on the Qur'an and the Sunnah, even the wisdom in them carries a crack, and it splits exactly when it is needed most. Many of the scholars of tafsir read the verse below as descending about these very champions, two sides disputing over their Lord, and Ali himself used to say that he would be the first to kneel in that dispute before his Lord on the Day of Judgment, for he was the first to kill at Badr:

These are two adversaries who have disputed over their Lord. But those who disbelieved will have cut out for them garments of fire. Poured upon their heads will be scalding waterQur'an 22:19

The cloak that fell from his shoulders

It is narrated in Sahih Muslim that as the ranks stood ready and the two armies could at last see one another, the Prophet ﷺ turned to face the qiblah and raised his hands high above his head, palms open and lifted to the sky. This was the rarest of his postures of du'a, the hands raised all the way to the heavens with the face lifted too, kept for the edge of distress and otherwise never used, unlike the common posture of the palms held open before him. He stood in it now, and the plea of the night returned word for word: O Allah, fulfill what You have promised me. O Allah, if this small band is destroyed, You will not be worshipped on earth. He begged so long and sank so deep into the asking that he lost awareness of everything around him, and his upper garment slipped from his shoulders and fell, and he did not notice. He stood there bare-shouldered, the cloak in the dirt, and went on asking.

It was Abu Bakr, radiyallahu anhu, who stooped and lifted the garment, wrapped it back around him, and held him from behind. Enough, O Messenger of Allah. Your Lord will give you what He has promised you. Sheikh Yasir reads this scene as one of the treasures of the whole series: two perfections inside a single embrace. The Prophet ﷺ in that moment is perfecting fear, refusing to take even a divine promise for granted, holding the trembling of a servant who will not presume on his Lord. Abu Bakr is perfecting hope, certain the promise is good and already as good as kept. Both belong in a believer's heart, and each has its hour; and here, with the enemy drawn up and everything about to be decided, fear is the more fitting, and it is the Prophet ﷺ who carries it while Abu Bakr, always the second, carries the hope.

His hands had barely come down when the trance of revelation took him. Remember the hadith in Abu Dawud: when a servant raises his hands to Allah, Allah is shy to return them empty, the way a man of any nobility feels shy to send away a beggar who keeps asking; and to Allah belongs the higher example, so what of these hands, and this servant doing the asking? When the revelation lifted and the Prophet ﷺ turned, Ibn Mas'ud said his face shone like the moon. Rejoice, Abu Bakr, he said. You tell me to be calm, and I tell you to be glad: the help of Allah has come. There is Jibril, wearing his turban, holding the rein of his horse, leading it down through the valley.

[Remember] when you were asking help of your Lord, and He answered you, "Indeed, I will reinforce you with a thousand from the angels, following one another."Qur'an 8:9

A thousand angels, following one upon another, for three hundred men, when a single angel would have been enough. That is the measure of Allah's reassurance: not the help the moment required, but help heaped past all need, so the believers would know they were not abandoned to their own thin numbers. Then the Prophet ﷺ stepped forward reciting a verse of Surah al-Qamar:

[Their] assembly will be defeated, and they will turn their backs [in retreat].Qur'an 54:45

Umar ibn al-Khattab said he had long wondered which assembly that verse meant, and where on earth it would turn and flee. On the morning of Badr, hearing it on the Prophet's ﷺ lips with the host of Quraysh spread out before him, he knew.

You did not throw when you threw

One gesture remained before the armies closed. The Prophet ﷺ bent down, scooped up a handful of pebbles and dust, and flung it toward Quraysh, saying: may these faces be disgraced. One man's throw, across perhaps half a mile of open plain, and physically it could never have carried; yet every soldier in that army found his eyes and his nostrils stung with grit, blinded, forced to stop and claw the dust away. Allah Himself carried what a human hand could not, and then He claimed it in His own words:

And you did not kill them, but it was Allah who killed them. And you threw not, [O Muhammad], when you threw, but it was Allah who threw that He might test the believers with a good test. Indeed, Allah is Hearing and Knowing.Qur'an 8:17

Hold that sentence, because it is this entire day in miniature, and very nearly the whole seerah. You threw, and it was not you who threw. The hand was his; the reach was Allah's. The believers did everything that lay within their power: they took counsel and moved the camp, secured the wells, straightened the rows, bared the skin for justice, sent out their champions, raised their hands. And the sleep, the rain, the firmed sand, the true dream, the thousand angels, and the dust that blinded an army were Allah's alone. The work was real, and so was the help, and the help was the part that mattered. Tomorrow the lines will crash together at last: the melee, the angels seen striking, and the tyrants of Makkah meeting their accounts. Tonight, hold the image the Companions carried out of this day for the rest of their lives, a Prophet ﷺ who would not stop asking for the ummah that would one day include you and me, and a Lord who was shy to send those hands back empty.

What this day asks of our faith

It is easy to read Badr as a great adventure safely sealed in the past, and to miss the question this particular day puts to us. Strip it to its bones and it asks the same thing of our faith that it asked of theirs, only the battlefield has moved indoors, into the heart.

He carried a promise of victory and still pleaded all night as though he had none. This is the first thing to take down into ordinary days. The Prophet ﷺ knew Allah had guaranteed him help, had been told the enemy would be routed, and spent the whole night in sajdah anyway, begging until his cloak fell and he never felt it go. We treat our confidence in Allah as a reason to ask Him for less, as if certainty made the asking unnecessary; he treated it as a reason to ask for more. If the one man on earth holding a divine guarantee would not stop pleading for it, then the surer you are of Allah, the harder you should be knocking at His door, not the softer. And remember which of them, that morning, carried the fear and which the hope. Neither canceled the other. A believing heart is meant to hold both, and the whole of wisdom is knowing which the hour in front of you calls for.

He took a foot soldier's correction and moved an army on it, and minutes from battle he bared his own stomach to settle a debt over the poke of a stick. Leadership that cannot be advised, that cannot be told it has chosen the lesser ground, is not following his example, however much it claims his name. And justice that waits for a calmer day, that tells the wronged person there is a crisis on and their claim can keep, is not his justice either. He let Hubab redraw the battle plan and he let Sawad take his retribution, on the same field, in the same hour, because neither counsel nor fairness was beneath the dignity of the man Allah had chosen, and neither should be beneath ours.

And he pointed across a battlefield at an idolater and called his counsel the one good in Quraysh. A person can be wrong about the greatest thing of all and still be right about a true and decent thing, and the believer who will not stand with a just cause because it was not born in revelation has shut a door the Prophet ﷺ held open in the middle of a war. Take all of it down into your days. Knock harder the surer you are. Hold fear and hope together and know the hour. Stay correctable, and let no crisis postpone your fairness to the people in your charge. And when you find yourself outnumbered in some narrow place of your own, do every lawful thing within your power, straighten your rows and secure your wells, and then raise your hands and leave the throw to Allah, who threw at Badr when His Prophet ﷺ threw and did not let the dust fall short. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, who pleaded through the night for an ummah that had not yet been born, give our nights some portion of his asking and our mornings some portion of Abu Bakr's calm, and answer us, when we are small and afraid, the way He answered the small band at Badr.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 38: the Battle of Badr, part 3 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (49:13, 8:11, 8:43, 61:4, 8:19, 22:19, 8:9, 54:45, 8:17), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 39 of 104

The Battle of Badr, part 4: the fighting, the angels, and the fall of Abu Jahl

Two boys, a thousand angels, and the fall of a tyrant

It is Friday, the seventeenth of Ramadan, in the second year after the hijrah, and the sun is barely up over the sand. This is a date Dr. Yasir Qadhi quizzes his students on, because every Muslim should simply know it the way they know their own name. For three days you have watched the two armies circle one another, watched the rain firm the believers' ground and turn the enemy's to slush, watched the Prophet ﷺ beg in the little tent of branches until his cloak slipped from his shoulders. Now the circling is over. The lines crash together, and from soon after Fajr until midday, Allah separates truth from falsehood on a plain that no one had ever heard of and no one will ever forget.

The Sheikh opens with an honest caveat. You cannot describe a battle of hundreds; you can only describe the duels inside it, the single combats of the old style, one man against one man, and of those perhaps fifteen survive in the record, in an order no one can fully reconstruct. So this day is not a marching narrative. It is a mosaic: a man who threw away his dates, archers told to wait, a rider no one could see, two unknown teenagers keeping a secret oath, a tyrant's last words under a shepherd's foot, and a voice that could not be buried. Walk it slowly. Every tile teaches.

Too long a life to finish these dates

Quraysh surged, and the believers braced to meet them, and even now there was discipline in the small army. The Prophet ﷺ had commanded the archers to hold their arrows until the enemy came within range, then to shower them but keep some in reserve. In the version Abu Dawud records he told them not to unsheathe their swords until the enemy was right upon them: until then, arrows, and eyes fixed on the foe. Somewhere in these opening moments, the Sheikh suggests, belongs the handful of dust you watched him ﷺ scoop up the day before. He flung it into the faces of the advancing army with a curse upon those faces, and the reports insist that every single man of them was blinded for a while, clawing the grit out of his eyes. One throw, a whole army staggered. And the Qur'an itself ruled on whose throw that really was.

And you did not kill them, but it was Allah who killed them. And you threw not, [O Muhammad], when you threw, but it was Allah who threw that He might test the believers with a good test. Indeed, Allah is Hearing and Knowing.Qur'an 8:17

Then came the call that opens the gates of the unseen. As the lines closed, the Prophet ﷺ cried out: rise to a Jannah whose width is the heavens and the earth. One of the companions was chewing a few dates, eating to put strength in his arm for the fight, and at those words an old Arabic exclamation escaped him, one of those classical phrases, long gone from common speech, that a man uses to make light of a thing, to say in effect, is that all? The Prophet ﷺ asked him what he meant. Nothing, Messenger of Allah, he said, only that if a Jannah as wide as the heavens and the earth is truly there waiting, then what use is it to keep sitting here? I want to be of its people. And he ﷺ answered: you are of its people.

The man looked at the dates still in his hand, and said the sentence the ummah has never let go of: if I live long enough to finish these dates, that is too long a life. He flung them away, drew his sword, and charged into the army until he met his shahada. Only some thirteen or fourteen Muslims fell at Badr in all. The people of Badr are the elite of the companions, the Sheikh reminds us, and the martyrs of Badr are the elite of that elite, and this man was among the first of them: a believer who measured the distance to Paradise in half a handful of dates, and judged even that too far to wait.

The closest of us to the enemy

Did the Prophet ﷺ himself fight that day? The early scholars discussed it, and the majority held that in most of his battles he did not, because he was the commander, and a commander by and large does not trade blows in the line; he holds the order of the field. But Badr, it seems, was different.
