Ali ibn Abi Talib would say of it, in a report carried in Muslim and in Ahmad, that on the day of Badr, when the fighting grew fierce, they would seek shelter close to the Prophet ﷺ, and he was the closest of them all to the enemy, the bravest and most courageous of them that day. Sit with that picture. The safest place on the whole field was beside him ﷺ, because no man among them stood nearer to the swords than he did.

And yet the same Ali carries the other half. He slipped back to look in on him ﷺ at the command tent and found him fallen in prostration, pleading with his Lord. Ali returned to the line and fought, came back, and found him still in sujud; a third time he came, and a third time found him on the ground before Allah, and only after that third return was victory written. So Ali narrates both: that he ﷺ was at the very front, and that he was in the tent in prostration. Scholars like Ibn Kathir and Ibn Hajar put the two memories together the only way they fit. The battle lasted some hours, four or five at least, from a little after Fajr until the heat of midday, and all that morning he ﷺ alternated, front line and then prostration, sword and then sujud, the order of the field in one hand and the plea of a beggar in the other. And wherever he moved, Abu Bakr moved with him, guarding his Prophet's ﷺ prayer when he prayed and his back when he fought.

Most of us, when the pressure comes, choose one or the other. We throw ourselves into the doing and forget to ask, or we sink into asking and never lift a hand to do. The morning of Badr is what it looks like to refuse that choice: one man giving the battle his whole arm and his whole forehead in the very same hour, certain that the effort was obligatory and equally certain that the effort, by itself, would decide nothing.

A thousand, rank after rank

The night of pleading you stood through was answered with a number, and the number was exact: one thousand angels. And here Dr. Yasir Qadhi slows down on the single verb Allah chose, because that verb carries the whole theology of the day.

[Remember] when you were asking help of your Lord, and He answered you, "Indeed, I will reinforce you with a thousand from the angels, following one another."Qur'an 8:9

The word is mumiddukum, I will reinforce you, I will supply and extend you. It is not the language of replacement, the Sheikh insists, it is the language of completion. Allah did not say, sit back and I will handle this for you. He said, you have begun the work, and the help I send will carry it through to its end. And the angels came rank following upon rank, host behind host, wave upon wave, until a thousand had come down on one small plain. Then listen to what Allah says, not to the believers now, but to the angels themselves:

[Remember] when your Lord inspired to the angels, "I am with you, so strengthen those who have believed. I will cast terror into the hearts of those who disbelieved, so strike [them] upon the necks and strike from them every fingertip."Qur'an 8:12

I am with you. Even the angels, beings of pure light who have never once disobeyed, are steadied with those words before they are sent in. They are told to make the believers firm, and the believers were made firm: their hearts, their footing, their grip on their weapons. And then the order to strike, with Allah Himself naming where the blow should fall. Imagine it, the Sheikh says: Allah instructing the angels where to strike, and in the same breath telling them, I am with you. How much more help could any army need?

And some of the companions heard them, and some saw. Ibn Abbas narrates, in a hadith in Sahih Muslim, that one of the believers was in hot pursuit of an enemy when he heard the crack of a whip above him and a rider's voice he could not see, urging on a horse by its name: forward, Hayzum. Even the angels name their mounts, the Sheikh smiles. Before the companion's own blow could land, the man he was chasing fell with his face struck open, and when he told the Prophet ﷺ of it, he answered: you have spoken the truth, that was help sent down to you from the third heaven. When a slight, short man of the Ansar brought in al-Abbas, the Prophet's ﷺ uncle and a famous warrior, Abbas would not accept the shame, insisting his captor had been a handsome man with parted hair whom he could not find in the camp; be quiet, the Prophet ﷺ told him, for Allah aided you with a noble angel. And of Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, who wore a yellow turban that morning, he had said, every prophet has a hawari, a devoted helper, and my hawari is Zubayr; it is mentioned that the angels came down dressed as he was dressed, an honor draped over one man by a thousand of Allah's soldiers.

But notice the pattern stamped on every one of these reports, for it is the lesson the whole episode is built around. The believer begins the motion, and the angel completes it. The companion raises the sword, the angel brings the man down. Never once, in any surviving story of Badr, does an angel fight a believer's battle while the believer stands and watches. The help of Allah, at Badr and in your own life, is mumidd. It does not descend on the idle. It meets people already moving.

Why a thousand, when one would have done

Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi stops the narrative entirely to ask the question the numbers beg. When Allah willed the destruction of the people of Lut, the reports say that Jibril, who has seven hundred wings, came in his true form, vast as the eye can see, and struck the earth beside their cities with the edge of a single wing, and the cities were lifted whole and brought crashing down. One angel, one wing, one blow, and a civilization gone. So why send a thousand angels to Badr, when one of them could have ended Quraysh before they finished their breakfast?

Not because Allah was short of power. He was teaching a law that runs through the entire Qur'an and sunnah: nothing is given for free, not even Jannah. Our deeds could never purchase Paradise; its price is beyond anything we own, and the reward is worth infinitely more than the effort we pay for it. And still it is not handed over without the effort. Allah looks at the striving, at its quality and sincerity rather than its quantity, and then His help carries that striving immeasurably further than it could have gone alone. The three hundred had to march, and draw, and walk bodily into the swords first. Only then, with the believers already in the thick of it, their trust placed in Allah the way Ibrahim placed his when the knife was already at his son's throat, did the help descend and the verdict come: you have passed the test.

The proof text the Sheikh reaches for is gentler than any battlefield, and it lands harder for being gentle. Maryam, alone, exhausted, in the pains of labor with Isa, leaning against the trunk of a palm, is not simply handed her provision out of the sky.

And shake toward you the trunk of the palm tree; it will drop upon you ripe, fresh dates.Qur'an 19:25

If anyone in creation was ever owed a miracle with no effort attached, it was she, in that state, in that hour. And still the dates did not fall until she shook the tree. Allah could have let them drop into her lap. He told her to shake. So when you and I sit back and say, if it is decreed for me, it will come, we have the equation backwards. Take the means, the Sheikh says, shake your tree, and what is decreed for you will fall. Ibn Abbas adds one line more, and the Sheikh treats his word on these matters as weighty: only at Badr did the angels actually fight; in every other battle they were presence and reassurance, not swords in the line. Even heaven's own army, when it came, came under the rule of effort.

And sometimes the means Allah asks of you will look absurd in your hand. Ukkasha's sword shattered against an enemy's armor, and he came to the Prophet ﷺ holding the useless stump: I have no other sword. The Prophet ﷺ picked up a branch, some twigs, and said: fight with this. He did not stand there asking what a man does with a stick in a war of steel. He took it, raised it, and in his hand it became a sword, and not just any sword but the finest he ever owned, which he carried in every battle until he died a shahid with it years later. That is what taking the means with full trust looks like: the Prophet ﷺ says fight with this, and you do not argue, you walk.

Two boys and an oath of shadows

Across the field, Quraysh had their own version of the command tent. Abu Jahl, the man who had engineered this entire war, was stationed in a grove of trees, as Ibn Hajar and others describe it, screened by his men, his son beside him, a strong young warrior. And Allah had decreed that this tyrant would not fall to a famous champion. He would fall to two teenagers, perhaps sixteen and seventeen, both of the Ansar, neither of whom had ever set foot in Makkah or seen Abu Jahl's face. There is a humiliation in that meant for him, the Sheikh observes, and an honor in it meant for them and for the Ansar to the end of time.

Abdur-Rahman ibn Awf tells the story himself, and Bukhari preserves it. Standing in the line as the armies closed, he looked right and left hoping for big strong men beside him, the kind who finish their opponent and turn to help you, and was deflated to find a young boy on each side. Then one of them nudged him and leaned in to whisper, so the other would not overhear, for the two were friends and rivals in a secret of their own: uncle, do you know Abu Jahl? What do you want with him, Abdur-Rahman asked. I have heard he insults the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, the boy said, and by Him in whose hand my soul is, if I see him, my shadow will not leave his shadow until the one of us with the shorter life is dead. Barely had he taken that in when the second boy nudged him from the other side and swore the same oath. Two friends, in secret competition, racing each other for one tyrant. I stood between them, Abdur-Rahman said, and was comforted to be there after all.

When he caught sight of Abu Jahl in the distance, he told them: there is your man. And they shot off like two arrows loosed at once, straight through an entire army. Perhaps, the Sheikh notes, their very youth was its own wisdom, for no one recognized two unknown boys or thought to stop them. The first to reach him was Mu'adh, son of a chief of Banu Salamah, a boy who had given his pledge at the second Aqabah. Afraid he would not close the last gap in time, he leapt it with his sword, could not reach Abu Jahl's upper body, and the blow came down instead and took off his leg at a stroke. One narrator, reaching for the only image strong enough, said the leg flew off the way a date stone leaps from beneath the grindstone. It was grievous, but not by itself a killing wound. And Abu Jahl's son, defending his father, brought his own sword down and severed Mu'adh's arm at the shoulder.

The arm hung by a strip of skin, and Mu'adh fought on with it dangling, until it got in his way; so he set his foot on it, bent down, and tore it off, and went on with the other hand. He was perhaps sixteen. If one of us takes a pin to the finger we lose the power to think; this boy ripped his own arm away and returned to the battle. He lived a long life one-armed and died in his bed in the caliphate of Uthman. The second boy was Mu'awwidh, one of the two sons of Afra you were told to remember, the same boy who had leapt up for the duels at the start of the day and been gently waved back. He landed his own blow on Abu Jahl, fought on, and fell shahid before the day was over, he and his brother both, two sons of one righteous mother lying among the fourteen of Badr.

Both boys had raced back to the Prophet ﷺ first, each crying out: I killed Abu Jahl, I killed him. He ﷺ asked to see their two swords, looked at the blades, and gave a verdict that let neither lose his joy: both of you killed him. The wounds together were the death. By the law of the spoils, the personal effects of a slain enemy belong to the one who killed him, and so Abu Jahl's costly armor, his horse and his sword, went to Mu'adh, because Mu'awwidh had already gone on to something far better than armor.

The Firawn of this ummah, and a voice that could not be buried

When the fighting began to wind down, the Prophet ﷺ sent the companions out: find me the body of Abu Jahl. Because he had fallen in that screen of trees they could not find him for a while. The one who found him, breathing his last among the branches, was Abdullah ibn Mas'ud: the slight Yemeni shepherd, the sixth person ever to accept Islam, the man Abu Jahl and his circle had humiliated again and again in the streets of Makkah precisely because he had no clan worth fearing behind him. Of every man Allah could have appointed to stand over this deathbed, He chose that one.

Ibn Mas'ud placed his foot on the chest of Abu Jahl and said: do you finally see that Allah has disgraced you? And the man stayed in character to his last breath. How has He disgraced me, he answered, turning the blame back even now, am I anything more than a man killed by his own people? Then: whose is the victory today? The victory belongs to Allah and His Messenger, said Ibn Mas'ud. And the last words Abu Jahl ever spoke were one more sneer, up at the foot on his chest: you have climbed to a lofty place, you little son of a shepherd. Ibn Mas'ud's own sword had been blunted by the long morning, so the end came, fittingly, by Abu Jahl's own gleaming blade, the fine sword he had carried all day and never used, drawn now from his hand and used on him. Ibn Mas'ud came back and said: Messenger of Allah, I have found the body of Abu Jahl. Do you swear by Allah, he ﷺ asked, and three times Ibn Mas'ud swore it, because this was news that changed the shape of the world. Then the Prophet ﷺ stood over the man who had tortured the weak, starved Banu Hashim in the ravine of the boycott, and driven this whole war into being, and gave him the one title of its kind he ever gave anyone: this was the Firawn of this ummah. Every nation has its Pharaoh; ours had now met his sea.

And there was a second tyrant to fall. You met Umayyah ibn Khalaf days ago: the perfumed lord of Makkah who once pinned Bilal to the burning stones, who tried to buy his way out of marching to this battle and was shamed into coming, because deep in himself he believed the Prophet's ﷺ warning that if he were ever met outside Makkah he would not live. Now Quraysh were breaking, and Umayyah cast about for someone to buy his life. His eye fell on his oldest friend from before Islam, Abdur-Rahman ibn Awf, the one believer who had never harmed him. Abdur-Rahman was carrying off captured armor, a small fortune of it. Umayyah seized his hand, his son at his side: leave the armor, take the two of us as prisoners, and I will give you milking camels beyond counting. A businessman even on the battlefield, the Sheikh smiles, and the deal was permissible, so Abdur-Rahman dropped the armor and began walking his two captives toward the camp.

They never reached it. Bilal, radiyallahu anhu, saw the prisoner's face, and everything Makkah had done to him rose up at once. The head of kufr, he cried, Umayyah ibn Khalaf, if he is saved then let me not be saved. Abdur-Rahman pleaded that these were his prisoners, but Bilal only raised his voice, the voice Allah had built to carry, until a circle of the Ansar gathered, and he told them exactly who this man was. Every Muslim child today knows what Umayyah did to Bilal, the Sheikh reminds us; what then did the Ansar of that day know of it? Abdur-Rahman threw his own body over his old friend to shield him, and the swords went in beneath his arms, one blade catching Abdur-Rahman's own foot, a scar he showed for the rest of his life. Umayyah and his son were killed there, on what was technically still the field of battle, before any law of captives had yet been revealed: a gray hour, the Sheikh is careful to mark, in which the rules that would later govern prisoners did not yet exist, and in which Allah willed that this man not be ransomed. Abdur-Rahman, merchant to the marrow, would say wryly ever after: may Allah have mercy on Bilal; he cost me my armor and my prisoners both. But the end belongs to the justice of Allah. When the dead of Quraysh were gathered for burial, the body of Umayyah alone could not be lifted: every time they took hold of him the flesh came away in their hands. So they left him where he had fallen and heaped the hot pebbles of the desert over him until he was covered. The man who had pressed Bilal down onto the burning stones of Makkah was buried beneath those same stones, and the voice he had tried for years to crush was the very voice that called down his end. As you do unto others, the Sheikh repeats, so it shall be done unto you. This is a law Allah has written through the seerah and through every nation before it.

Fathers, sons, and where loyalty lands

Badr cut straight through families, blood against blood across the lines, and the Prophet ﷺ never let the army forget it. His order stood: al-Abbas and the young men of Banu Hashim were not to be killed, for they had been dragged out against their will, and indeed they surrendered at the first chance without having killed anyone. And on the same protected list stood a man with no tie of blood at all: Abul Bukhtari, who during the long years of the boycott had quietly slipped food and water to Banu Hashim in the ravine and had pushed hardest of anyone to tear the unjust pact apart. In the middle of a battle, the Prophet ﷺ was repaying an old favor. Dr. Yasir Qadhi pauses here and speaks directly to his own congregation. Not all who reject your faith are the same. Those who stand with the Muslims for justice and dignity, who defend the freedom of every faith to be practiced, are owed gratitude and justice in return, never the treatment reserved for those who hate. Their kufr does not cancel the good they do; the isolationist who would lump every non-Muslim together has missed a clear teaching of the seerah. He draws the line from Badr straight to the Muslims living as minorities in his own land.

Abul Bukhtari's end still aches. A companion reached him and called out the reprieve: we have been forbidden to harm you, surrender and you are safe. He asked: and my companion beside me? Him I cannot protect, the man answered. Then Abul Bukhtari spoke the sentence that became his epitaph: I will not have the women of Quraysh say that I saved myself and abandoned my companion. He forced the fight, and was killed, and the companion came grieving to the Prophet ﷺ, who excused him, because he had tried.

Then there is Abu Ubaydah ibn al-Jarrah, the early believer and muhajir, whose own father had taken the field with one fixed obsession: to kill the son who had believed. All day the father hunted him through the lines, and all day Abu Ubaydah slid away, refusing the fight out of respect and love, until at last the father cornered him and attacked outright, and in defending his own life the son's sword fell and the father died by it. The whispers started at once, that he had killed his own father, and the grief of it sat heavy on him. It was answered from above the seven heavens, in the closing verse of Surah al-Mujadila.

You will not find a people who believe in Allah and the Last Day having affection for those who oppose Allah and His Messenger, even if they were their fathers or their sons or their brothers or their kindred. Those - He has decreed within their hearts faith and supported them with spirit from Him. And We will admit them to gardens beneath which rivers flow, wherein they abide eternally. Allah is pleased with them, and they are pleased with Him - those are the party of Allah. Unquestionably, the party of Allah - they are the successful.Qur'an 58:22

Allah testified that hearts like his have iman written into them by His own decree. Loyalty to Allah outranks the tie of blood, and when blood itself takes up a sword against faith, faith holds. There is no room for negotiation in this, the Sheikh says; every other loyalty a believer carries is conditional upon that one.

And one more son. Abu Hudhayfah ibn Utbah had watched the opening duels take his father Utbah, his uncle Shaybah, and his brother Walid within minutes. When the order to spare al-Abbas reached him barely an hour later, something jahili flickered up through the fresh grief and a sentence escaped him: so we kill our own fathers and uncles and brothers, and the uncle of the Prophet is to walk free? He even swore he would seek Abbas out himself. When word reached the Prophet ﷺ, he did not summon the man and shame him. He turned to Umar and said: will the face of the Messenger's uncle be struck with the sword? Umar offered to deal with the speaker in the way only Umar would, and the Prophet ﷺ forbade it, and Umar instead went quietly and set Abu Hudhayfah straight behind the scenes. No punishment followed, because the outburst of a man who had just lost three of his house in an hour was owed mercy: corrected, yes; humiliated, never. Abu Hudhayfah himself never granted himself that ease. I will never feel safe from that one sentence, he said, unless Allah grants me a shahada that wipes it away. And Allah did, years later, at Yamamah.

The episode closes at the gentlest moment of the whole day. When the bodies of Quraysh were being dragged off for the burial that is tomorrow's story, the body of Utbah passed by, and the face of his son drained of all its color. The Prophet ﷺ, only hours after this same son had threatened his uncle, walked over to console him: perhaps it pains you, what you see of your father? No, by Allah, Abu Hudhayfah answered, I do not doubt what my father died upon. But I had known in my father wisdom and forbearance and nobility, this was the man who once sent the grapes at Ta'if, and I had hoped all of that would one day lead him to Islam. That he died upon kufr is what grieves me. And the Prophet ﷺ made du'a for him and blessed him. The morning's threat was forgotten; the evening's grief was honored. That, the Sheikh says, is what a true leader of hearts looks like.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read the fighting at Badr as a thrilling tale of swords and angels and a tyrant brought low, and to leave it there, sealed safely in the past. That would be to miss the single question every tile of this mosaic puts to you. Step back from the whole field, and one law shines through all of it.

The man with the dates moved first, and Jannah received him. The archers held, and then they loosed. The Prophet ﷺ swung a sword and wore out a place of prostration in the same morning. Two boys ran before anyone could stop them. Ukkasha raised a piece of wood, Bilal raised his voice, and in every single case the help of heaven landed on the one already in motion. This is what the Sheikh circles back to again and again, because it is the central lesson of the day. Allah sent a thousand angels to a battle that one wing tip could have ended, so that no believer would ever again be able to stand back and say, my small part does not matter. Your part is the very thing the help comes to complete. Mumiddukum: reinforcement, not replacement. Even Maryam, alone and in labor and owed a miracle if anyone ever was, had to put her hand to the trunk and shake it before the dates would fall. So when you find yourself waiting for life to drop into your lap because surely, if it is written, it will come, hear the correction the seerah offers: take the means, shake your tree, walk into the thing you fear with your trust placed wholly in Allah, and then watch how far three hundred steps of honest effort can be carried.

And keep the second law beside the first, because Badr teaches both in the same breath. Umayyah pressed Bilal onto the burning pebbles, and he died on a bed of them and was buried beneath them, and the voice he tried to silence was the voice that ended him. As you do unto others, so it shall be done unto you. Allah keeps long and exact accounts: a warning to every tyrant who imagines the stone he presses down will never be pressed back, and a balm to everyone who is still, today, lying under someone else's stone. The accounts are not closed. They are simply not yet settled.

So take these down into your ordinary days, where there are no swords and no descending angels, only the same God and the same law. Move first, and trust Allah with the ending. Do the work even when the work in your hand looks as useless as a stick against armor, because it is not the size of your effort that buys the result, it is the sincerity of it that invites the help. Repay the people who were good to you when you were weak, the way he ﷺ repaid Abul Bukhtari in the middle of a war. Correct quietly and console in person, the way he corrected Abu Hudhayfah out of sight and then came to grieve his father with him. And when one bad sentence or one old failure haunts you, remember that a man far better than us carried his single sentence for decades and Allah wiped it clean with mercy in the end. Nothing about where you have been decides where you will land. May Allah, who reinforced His Messenger ﷺ with a thousand angels rank upon rank, complete every good that we begin, finish every swing we take for His sake, write us among those who move first and trust Him with the ending, and send His peace and His blessings upon Muhammad ﷺ, the closest of them all to the enemy on the morning of Badr, and the longest of them in prostration before his Lord.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 39: the Battle of Badr, part 4 (Memphis Islamic Center, 2012). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (8:17, 8:9, 8:12, 19:25, 58:22), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry a detail as a beloved but uncertain report, the Sheikh's own weighing of it has been followed.

The Seerah · 40 of 104

The Battle of Badr, part 5

The deceiver flees, and the victor weeps

By the time this day opens, the swords of Badr have already fallen silent. The lines collided, the tyrants of Makkah met their accounts, and Quraysh broke and ran. What follows is not another clash. It is the three days the army stays camped on the field after the victory, and those days turn out to be their own teacher, because what a man does with winning reveals him as sharply as how he meets losing. These are the days the Prophet ﷺ buries two armies in two different ways, divides the first spoils this ummah ever held, repays a debt to a man already in his grave, and spends the morning after the greatest victory of his life in tears.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi builds this stretch of the seerah out of the verses of Surah al-Anfal that came down on the battlefield itself, the surah he reads as one long commentary on Badr, ayah after ayah linked to the day. And he hangs it on two images that refuse to leave you once you have seen them: a Prophet ﷺ standing over a well packed with the dead chiefs of Quraysh, asking them whether their Lord's promise came true, and the same Prophet ﷺ under a tree the next morning, weeping with Abu Bakr over a verdict that had just descended from heaven.

The day the deceiver ran

To understand the strangest flight in the Qur'an you have to rewind to the march out of Makkah. Quraysh had very nearly turned back, afraid that an old enemy among the subtribes of Banu Kinanah would fall on their undefended rear while they were away. Then a familiar chieftain appeared to settle their nerves: Suraqah ibn Malik of Kinanah, pledging on his honor that no one would touch them. No one can overcome you today, he told them, and I am your protector. He even offered himself as a living hostage, riding with the army so that if Kinanah struck, his own life would answer for it. Except it was not Suraqah at all. The Qur'an lifts the disguise and names the rider:

And [remember] when Satan made their deeds pleasing to them and said, "No one can overcome you today from among the people, and indeed, I am your protector." But when the two armies sighted each other, he turned on his heels and said, "Indeed, I am disassociated from you. Indeed, I see what you do not see; indeed, I fear Allah. And Allah is severe in penalty."Qur'an 8:48

When the two armies came within sight of one another, the false chieftain saw what no eye of Quraysh could see: Jibril descending on his horse, and the angels with him. He spun on his heels and bolted. Al-Harith ibn Hisham caught at him, where are you going, Suraqah, at an hour like this? The answer was a shove so violent that the man flew upward and landed on his back; whatever wore Suraqah's face was no human being. And as it fled it confessed exactly the words Allah preserved: I am free of you, I see what you do not see, I fear Allah. The Prophet ﷺ said that Shaytan was never, since the day Allah created him, more humiliated or more despised than he was on the day of Badr, for what he saw of Allah's mercy pouring down and Jibril marshaling the angels forward.

Mark who came that day. This was not some deputy devil sent to do the whispering. By the Prophet's ﷺ own report, Iblis keeps a throne over the waters and dispatches his shayateen out on his errands; for him to walk in person among an army measures his desperation. And it set the field with a terrible clarity. On one side of a hundred meters of sand stood the being who had refused to bow to Adam, and beside him Abu Jahl, Utbah, Shaybah, and Umayyah. On the other side Jibril came down from the heavens beside the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, Abu Bakr, Umar, and the believers. The Qur'an itself calls Badr yawm al-furqan, the day of the criterion, the day truth was split from falsehood, and the Sheikh holds that no battle since mankind was created has drawn that line so visibly, nor will one until the Day of Judgment. It was as if Allah let an entire army watch the betrayal happen live: the one who guarantees protection right up to the brink and abandons you at it has shown you his colors forever. How, the lecture asks, could you ever again trust a liar who will not even feel ashamed to lie until the very last second? The greatest military victory of the Prophet's ﷺ life, with only the conquest of Makkah standing anywhere near it, opened with the deceiver running off the field.

The rout, and a road left open

How the melee itself unfolded the books barely record. The sira keeps a handful of single combats, the stories of the day before, and then the ending: Quraysh turned on their backs and fled for Makkah. But modern students of the battlefield map notice something the early reports do not pause on. The plain of Badr had one clear corridor of retreat, and the Prophet ﷺ, who had reached the field first and chosen the ground, never moved to block it. Sheikh Yasir passes their reading on as a theory, for no one can know what was in the Prophet's ﷺ mind, yet he weighs it very plausible: an army with no way out fights to the death, while an open valve lets fear do the routing for you. And Quraysh fled precisely through that gap.

Then the arithmetic of the day. Seventy of Quraysh lay dead and some seventy-three or seventy-four more were taken prisoner, around fifteen percent of an army more than a thousand strong, gone in a single morning. From the believers, around fifteen men were martyred, fewer than five in a hundred, and not one was taken captive. And rather than hurry home in triumph, the Prophet ﷺ announced: we remain here three days. To gather and bury the fallen. To recover. To be certain no counterattack was forming. And, beyond the shadow of a doubt, to settle who held the field, because the victor camps on the battlefield while the loser cannot summon the courage to come back even to look.

It was here at Badr that the law of the shaheed first came down. The martyr is not washed; no janazah prayer is offered over him; he is buried where he fell, in the very clothes he bled in, his wounds left untouched. For the Prophet ﷺ taught that on the Day of Judgment the shaheed will rise with his blood still the color of blood, while its scent will be the scent of musk. Madinah was barely a day's journey away, an hour or so by car in our own time, and yet the fifteen were not carried home. They were buried at Badr, each given his own grave; at Uhud, where the dead would be far more, two men would come to share a single grave because digging enough was too heavy a burden, but here, with so few, each had his own. Visit Badr today and the graves of the first shuhada of this ummah are still there in the ground. A shaheed belongs to the earth he fell upon.

A well, and a mound of pebbles

The seventy of Quraysh were not left to the sun either. Even toward the dead of an enemy army this religion sets a floor of dignity. The bodies were gathered, lowered into one of the abandoned wells of Badr, and the sand thrown in over them. It was not the burial the believers received, and it was never meant to be, but it was a covering, deliberate and commanded, so that the slain would not simply rot in the open. Every nation on earth treats its own dead better than its enemy's; the question is whether your sacred law obliges you to do anything at all for the dead of those you fought. Ours does.

One body refused the well. Umayyah ibn Khalaf was a heavy man, and when the companions tried to lift him the flesh came apart in their hands; the body simply would not be carried whole. So they did the only thing left to them: they heaped over him the pebbles of the very spot where he had fallen, a mound of small stones for a grave.

Read that ending as slowly as the lecture reads it. This is the man who used to drag Bilal radiyallahu anhu out into the desert heat and pile the rocks of that ground onto his chest to make him renounce his Lord. Allah wrote his end in the alphabet of his own crime: pebbles over a body, in the sand, forever. As you do unto others, it shall be done unto you, and the Sheikh counts this strange burial among the signs Allah wanted the watching world to contemplate, that everyone present and everyone after might see the justice of it.

Have you found your Lord's promise true

On the third day the caravan packed up for the road to Madinah, and then the Prophet ﷺ turned the whole line of march aside. The well was not on the way home; he diverted toward it on purpose, and the companions, obedient as ever, followed without a single question about why. At its mouth he stopped and began to call the dead out by name, each man with his father's name: O Utbah ibn Rabi'ah. O Shaybah. O Abu Jahl. One after another he named every chief lying in that well, and to each he put the same question. Have you found what your Lord promised you to be true? As for me, I have found what my Lord promised me to be true.

Umar said aloud what the army must have been wondering: O Messenger of Allah, how can you speak to bodies that have no souls in them, that have no cognition to hear you? And the answer came with an oath. By the One in whose hand is my soul, you do not hear what I am saying any better than they do; only, they are unable to answer me back. Qatadah, the famous tabi'i in the chain of this report in Sahih Muslim, added the explanation he carried: Allah restored them to life in that moment so that the words would reach them, humiliation and regret heaped on men who had marched out certain of victory.

Out of this well rises one of Islam's oldest and most courteous disputes: can the dead hear the living? Companions themselves stood on both sides of it. Ibn Umar held that the one in the grave hears the visitor who comes to him, and even that he is pained by hearing his family wail over him. Aisha radiyallahu anha denied it, reaching for the verse:

And not equal are the living and the dead. Indeed, Allah causes to hear whom He wills, but you cannot make hear those in the graves.Qur'an 35:22

She read it alongside its sisters in Surah ar-Rum and Surah an-Naml, where the dead are likened to the deaf who will not hear you when you call. Each camp carried hadith too: those who affirmed hearing pointed to the authentic report that the buried man hears the footsteps of those who walked away from his grave, to the salam the Prophet ﷺ would give the people of the graveyard, and to Amr ibn al-As, who asked his children to linger at his grave so their nearness might steady him for the questioning. The other camp answered each in turn, and behind them the great imams divided for fourteen centuries, with names on both sides.

Sheikh Yasir, in one of the rare matters where he gently parts from Ibn Taymiyyah, leans with Aisha's reading, and says Allah knows best. To him the verses speak plainly, and Badr reads not as the rule but as the exception that proves it. Notice, he says, that the Prophet ﷺ never corrected Umar with do you not know the dead can hear; he carved out an exception, these men, right now, hear me, and the very astonishment of the companions tells you that hearing was not what they understood to be normal. Then he hands you the manners that matter far more than the verdict. The evidences are genuinely strong on both sides; companions and great imams sit in both camps; and by the unanimous agreement of the scholars not one act of worship changes whichever way you hold it. On neither opinion does anyone stand at a grave holding conversations with the buried, coaching the dead through their questioning, or turning to them with his needs. The Sheikh is scornful of the spectacle, seen in many lands, of a man shouting answers down at a fresh grave as though the deceased could cheat on his exam by being fed the lines. Hold the dispute lightly. Hold the scene at the well in awe.

They ask you about the spoils

The field was strewn with everything a fleeing army drops behind it: weapons, armor, mounts, baggage. And the believers stood over it genuinely unsure, because no ummah before this one had ever been permitted to keep the spoils of war. The nations before us would gather what they had captured into a great pile, and a fire would come down from the sky and consume it as the sign that their fighting had been accepted; the practice is remembered in the Old Testament and confirmed in our own hadith. This was the first ghanimah in the history of Islam, and nobody yet knew the rules. Three groups each pressed a claim: we were the ones who gathered it from the field; we were the ones who chased Quraysh down so you could gather it in safety; we were the ones who ringed the Prophet ﷺ with our bodies so that no blade could reach him, and will his guard be left with nothing? And Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas came carrying a beautiful sword he had taken from a man he cut down: give me this sword, O Messenger of Allah, for by Allah I used it in the battle.

The answer came down on the battlefield itself, the opening words of Surah al-Anfal:

They ask you, [O Muhammad], about the bounties [of war]. Say, "The [decision concerning] bounties is for Allah and the Messenger." So fear Allah and amend that which is between you and obey Allah and His Messenger, if you should be believers.Qur'an 8:1

Before a single coin moved, Allah relocated the ownership entirely: it is not your property, it belongs to Allah and His Messenger ﷺ to apportion as He decides. And in the same breath He named what the moment was really testing. Fear Allah, and set right what is between you. A brotherhood that had just been sealed at Badr was not going to be torn over saddlebags and swords; His pleasure outprices every piece of the loot, and greed was not to be allowed to become the engine of the day.

Then came the law of it, the details of which fill chapters in every book of fiqh. One fifth of the spoils is set aside, itself divided into shares: for Allah and His Messenger ﷺ, a portion that ended with his ﷺ passing; for his near kin; for the orphans; for the needy; and for the stranded traveler. The remaining four fifths went to the army that fought.

And know that anything you obtain of war booty - then indeed, for Allah is one fifth of it and for the Messenger and for [his] near relatives and the orphans, the needy, and the [stranded] traveler, if you have believed in Allah and in that which We sent down to Our Servant on the day of criterion [i.e., decisive encounter] - the day when the two armies met [at Badr]. And Allah, over all things, is competent.Qur'an 8:41

At Badr every man drew an equal share. Only later, from Khaybar onward, would the Prophet ﷺ give the man who fought on a mount three times the share of the man who fought on foot. And nine men who never set foot on the field at Badr were written into the shares regardless, every one of them held back by a duty the Prophet ﷺ had himself assigned them.

Sit for a moment with one of those nine. Uthman ibn Affan radiyallahu anhu remained in Madinah by the Prophet's ﷺ explicit order, at the bedside of his wife Ruqayyah, the Prophet's ﷺ own daughter, who lay gravely ill while the army marched. She died, and was buried on the very day the victors of Badr came home. Uthman is counted among the people of Badr until the end of time, a badri who never saw Badr. In this religion the man obeying at a sickbed holds the rank of the man holding a sword.

The debt owed to a man in his grave

It is narrated in Bukhari that the Prophet ﷺ surveyed the seventy-odd captives drawn up before him, the very men who at sunrise had been swinging their swords at the believers, and said: had Mut'im ibn Adi been alive, and had he spoken to me about these rotten ones, I would have released them all to him. Rotten ones, he called them, a word with no softness in it at all for what these men had just tried to do. And yet, every last captive, ransoms that in today's money would run into the millions, all of it would have been released for a single word from a man who had died months before Badr, a mushrik who never accepted Islam. Why?

Rewind to the bleakest stretch of the Makkan years. Ta'if had answered the Prophet ﷺ with stones, and Abu Lahab had revoked his protection, so that the city of his own birth was closed to him; a believer, in a sense, had cancelled his visa, and he could not enter. He camped outside Makkah and sent word to chief after chief, four or five of them, and each one excused himself: not me, I am not powerful enough, this and that. Mut'im ibn Adi did not excuse himself. He armed his own sons, sent them out to escort the Prophet ﷺ into the city under his shield, stood by as he made tawaf, and declared before all of Quraysh: I have given my protection to Muhammad, and whoever harms him has harmed me. Even Abu Lahab bowed his head and said, we will protect the one you have protected. And years before that, Mut'im had been among the men who stood up to tear apart the boycott that was starving Banu Hashim.

So at Badr, with the nobility of Makkah kneeling in ropes before him, the Prophet ﷺ honored that debt at the highest volume he could give it, announced over a fortune in prisoners, for a dead man who would never hear the words: his nation's highest medal of honor, pinned upon a grave. The Sheikh lingers here deliberately, because he says Muslims keep misplacing this lesson. Mut'im was no believer, and Mut'im was owed. There are people who will never share our theology and will still spend their name, their sons, and their own safety in the cause of justice, and the seerah's verdict on such people is honor, gratitude, and partnership, not suspicion. A community that has talked itself into believing it is treachery to stand alongside non-Muslims who stand for truth has read its own Prophet ﷺ with one eye shut, for he repaid a mushrik's nobility in public, and even into the grave.

Softer than milk, harder than stone

Then came the question with seventy lives inside it: what is to be done with the prisoners? Nothing had yet descended about captives, for the ummah had never held any. In the morning these men had been trying to extinguish Islam; by the afternoon they were bound at the believers' feet. So the Prophet ﷺ did what he had done at the wells before the battle: he gathered counsel, and especially from the two men Ali would call his two wazirs, Abu Bakr and Umar.

Abu Bakr said: O Messenger of Allah, they are our relatives, our own kith and kin, the same families as ours; show them mercy for the sake of the blood between us, and take the ransom from them. Umar saw it the opposite way: hand Aqil to Ali to strike his neck, and hand me a man of my own house to strike, and let each of us put his own kinsman to the sword, so that not one of them lives to march on us another day. Two counsels as far apart as counsels can be, and the Prophet's ﷺ reply is one of the tenderest pieces of the whole seerah. Allah, he said, makes some hearts so soft they are softer than milk, and some so hard they are harder than stone. You, Abu Bakr, resemble Ibrahim, who said of his people, whoever follows me is of me, and whoever disobeys me, then surely You are Forgiving and Merciful; and you resemble Isa, who handed his deniers over to Allah:

If You should punish them - indeed they are Your servants; but if You forgive them - indeed it is You who is the Exalted in Might, the Wise."Qur'an 5:118

And you, Umar, resemble Nuh, who prayed against a people who would not believe:

And Noah said, "My Lord, do not leave upon the earth from among the disbelievers an inhabitant.Qur'an 71:26

And you resemble Musa, who asked his Lord to harden the hearts of Pharaoh's people until they should see the painful punishment with their own eyes. Notice that neither companion is scolded. Mercy has prophets standing behind it, and severity has prophets standing behind it; both kinds of heart were forged by Allah, and an ummah will have need of them both. Then the Prophet ﷺ chose: the captives would live and be ransomed, the counsel of Abu Bakr. And here the lecture is careful with a nuance the Qur'an is about to be careful with too. The Prophet ﷺ chose from mercy, and Abu Bakr counseled from mercy, but among the wider army there were also men who simply had their eyes on the ransom money, and heaven saw the difference between the two motives.

The morning the victor wept

The next day, Umar came upon the Prophet ﷺ and Abu Bakr sitting beneath a tree, and both of them were weeping. What Umar said is its own short lesson in love: tell me what makes you weep, O Messenger of Allah, so that if I find weeping in myself I may weep with you, and if I cannot, I will force myself to weep, only to be with you in it. The Prophet ﷺ recited to him what had just come down:

It is not for a prophet to have captives [of war] until he inflicts a massacre [upon Allah's enemies] in the land. You [i.e., some Muslims] desire the commodities of this world, but Allah desires [for you] the Hereafter. And Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise.Qur'an 8:67
If not for a decree from Allah that preceded, you would have been touched for what you took by a great punishment.Qur'an 8:68

It was not for a prophet to hold captives while the believers were still this weak in the land; some of you wanted the goods of this world while Allah wanted the Hereafter for you; and had a decree from Allah not already gone ahead, a terrible punishment would have touched you for what you took. The gentler choice, made in mercy, had not been heaven's preference, not yet, not while Islam could still be strangled in its cradle. Allah let the ransom stand, and later revelation would hand the Muslim ruler a whole range of options for prisoners of war, so that Badr's decision lays down no permanent rule. But the rebuke itself was made eternal, recited in every generation since, and so is the day's most piercing picture: the man who had just won the greatest victory of his life ﷺ spending the morning after it in tears over how the mercy had been weighed, the softest heart of the ummah weeping at his side.

The lecture closes inside the question this scene forces open. Did the Prophet ﷺ exercise ijtihad, his own weighed judgment? A minority of scholars held that his every utterance descended as revelation. But the vast majority, and the plain sense of the seerah, answer that Allah gave him ﷺ the right to weigh and to choose. He said of himself, I am only a human being, I forget as you forget. He chose a campsite at Badr and then accepted a better one when it was suggested; he once advised the farmers of Madinah against a way of pollinating their date palms, and when the crop suffered he told them plainly, you know the affairs of your world better than I do. And here is exactly what guards this point against every modern distortion of it. When his judgment needed adjusting, Allah adjusted it in public, in revelation the world still recites; and when heaven stayed silent over a choice, that silence is itself Allah's approval of it. Either way the outcome carries His seal. That is why his ﷺ command binds every believer, why the Qur'an orders obedience to him in more than sixty places, and why those who whisper that some ruling was merely his ijtihad in order to slip out from under it have, the Sheikh says, stepped outside the religion's plain and agreed teaching altogether. His humanity is not a crack in the proof. It is part of the proof.

The captives are walking toward Madinah now, seventy and more lives suspended between the sword and the ransom, and the Sheikh is not finished with them. The next day stays at their ropes, because what Islam did with seventy enemies held in its hand is its own story.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to file these three days under aftermath, the tidying-up after the real drama of the battle, and move on. That would be to miss the questions they put to us, which are sharper than anything on the field.

Watch the deceiver run, and never trust his guarantees again. He swore he was their protector and rode with them as a hostage, and the instant the angels became visible he turned on his heels, disowned them, and confessed that he feared Allah. Allah let a whole army witness the betrayal in real time so that no believer afterward would take his whispered promises at face value. Every temptation makes the same offer Shaytan made at Badr: safety, advantage, no one can touch you today. And every one of them abandons you at exactly the brink it lured you toward.

Then learn how this religion wins. Seventy of the enemy are covered with earth rather than left to rot; a tyrant is buried in the very instrument of his own cruelty; a fortune in spoils is surrendered to Allah before a single coin is shared, lest greed eat the brotherhood; a debt of plain human decency is repaid in public to a mushrik already in his grave. This is what victory looks like when it belongs to Allah and not to the ego: dignity even for the dead of your enemy, gratitude even to those outside your creed who once stood for justice, and a fierce guarding of the bond between believers against the pull of the loot. Ask which of these your own small victories tend to forget.

And then sit with the two hearts and the tree. Abu Bakr's mercy was likened to Ibrahim and Isa, Umar's resolve to Nuh and Musa, and neither man was corrected, because an ummah needs both and wisdom is knowing which one the hour is asking for. Your tenderness can be prophetic and so can your firmness; what is dangerous is to mistake the one the moment needs. Above all, sit with the weeping. The most successful man who ever lived spent the morning after his greatest success in tears, not over enemies but over whether he had handled a mercy the way his Lord most loved, schooled in public by a revelation we still recite. That is the measure this day leaves in your hands. Not whether you can win, but whether you can win without cruelty and grieve without despair; whether, when heaven corrects you, you receive the correction as he ﷺ did, with tears and submission and not a word of defense. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, who routed the deceiver and made his Lord's promise true at the lip of a dry well and then wept over a verdict from heaven, give us Abu Bakr's mercy and Umar's resolve and the wisdom to know the hour of each, and gather us with the people of Badr under his banner.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 40: the Battle of Badr, part 5 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (8:48, 35:22, 8:1, 8:41, 5:118, 71:26, 8:67, 8:68), verified via quran.ai. Where the matter of the dead hearing is contested, the Sheikh's own weighing of the evidences has been followed.

The Seerah · 41 of 104

The Battle of Badr, part 6 ﷺ

Seventy captives, and a necklace from Khadijah

The swords of Badr are back in their sheaths, and now comes the part of victory that has no drums. Seventy men of Quraysh, bound and breathing, are on the road north to Madinah, and they put to their captors the oldest question a winner ever faces, the one almost every winner in history has answered badly: what do you do with your enemy once he is finally in your hands? Dr. Yasir Qadhi gives this whole lesson to the answer, because for him this is where the religion turns its face to you. Two men met the sword. Everyone else met bread, ransom, release, and a kindness the world had no word for yet. Strictness exactly where it was due, and mercy everywhere mercy could reach: a scale held level when hardly anyone in the long story of men with power over other men has held it steady for even an afternoon.

Forgiveness belongs to the strong

The previous lesson had ended on a decision and a correction. The Prophet ﷺ, taking counsel, had chosen the gentler of the two options, ransom over the sword, and revelation had come down to say that this once the harder option would have been the better one. Today opens inside the why, and the first answer sits in the verse itself.

It is not for a prophet to have captives [of war] until he inflicts a massacre [upon Allah's enemies] in the land. You [i.e., some Muslims] desire the commodities of this world, but Allah desires [for you] the Hereafter. And Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise.Qur'an 8:67

Read it slowly and you find a principle, not a scolding. Forgiveness offered from weakness, the Sheikh explains, is not really forgiveness at all; it is helplessness wearing a kind face. If you pardon a man because you could not have touched him anyway, what exactly have you pardoned? Only the one fully able to strike, and choosing not to, has truly forgiven. Allah was telling a community still small, still hunted, still the lower hand in Arabia, that the hour of clemency is the hour of strength, and that hour had not yet come. Pardon would come, and it would be magnificent, but it would come from the high ground or not mean what mercy is meant to mean.

The other wisdoms are bracingly practical. Spare a fighting man of Quraysh today and you may meet him in next year's army, the same war, the same enemy, and that is exactly what happened: some of the freed men of Badr came back to fight in later expeditions. Why hand your sword's edge back to the throat it was raised against? And there was Umar's reasoning on the day of the counsel, which the Sheikh marks as distinctly his. Give me my own kinsman, Umar had said, give Ali his relative, let every man deal with his own blood, so that all Arabia would see that loyalty to Allah now outranked the tribe. It took Quraysh years to grasp that such a brotherhood could even exist; Islam had become a stronger glue than blood, invisible to men who measured the world by lineage.

Allah accepted His Prophet's ﷺ gentler choice and let the ransom stand, but the verse that followed pressed its full weight onto everyone who had reached for the spoils.

If not for a decree from Allah that preceded, you would have been touched for what you took by a great punishment.Qur'an 8:68

It is reported that Abu Bakr wept over those words; both he and Umar understood the verse to mean that a real punishment had been hovering, near enough that only a prior decree of Allah held it back. Here the Sheikh pauses for one of his signature arguments. Systems that preach turning the other cheek as an absolute law have never once managed to run a society on it, because a rule that collapses the moment someone tests it is not an ideal but a slogan; turn the other cheek always, and every opportunist on earth takes your second cheek and then your coat. Islam, he says, is more honest, and that honesty is itself a sign of its truth: the general rule is mercy, the stated preference is pardon, yet justice stays within reach for the day mercy would be read as an open invitation. This episode holds both at once, mercy and severity in the same hand, and almost no one in history ever has.

Two men the pardon passed over

The battle had been fought on a Friday, the seventeenth of Ramadan. The Prophet ﷺ stayed three days on the plain and turned for home on Monday the twentieth. Somewhere along that road the column halted at an open stretch of ground, and out of the seventy, two prisoners were drawn aside. Some scholars hold that these two are the only prisoners of war ever executed at the Prophet's ﷺ command in all his campaigns, and the Sheikh offers it as the clearest proof of how absolute the rule of clemency was: the single exception in all those years was carved for these two, which tells you what they had made of their lives.

The first was an-Nadr ibn al-Harith, one of the very few of Quraysh with a foreign education. He had lived years in al-Hirah, the magnificent old Arab capital in Iraq, and came home stocked with the legends of the kings of Persia. When the Qur'an began to descend, an-Nadr appointed himself its rival. These are nothing but the fables of the ancients, he would sneer, and I can produce better. When people gathered around the Prophet ﷺ to listen, he would set up nearby: leave his stories, come and hear mine, and then unspool the tales of the ancient heroes and kings of the Persians. It is said that eight or more ayat came down concerning this one man, and the Sheikh reads him into every verse that quotes the sneer about old fables, up to the terrifying one about the man who claims he can produce the like of revelation.

And who is more unjust than one who invents a lie about Allah or says, "It has been inspired to me," while nothing has been inspired to him, and one who says, "I will reveal [something] like what Allah revealed." And if you could but see when the wrongdoers are in the overwhelming pangs of death while the angels extend their hands, [saying], "Discharge your souls! Today you will be awarded the punishment of [extreme] humiliation for what you used to say against Allah other than the truth and [that] you were, toward His verses, being arrogant."Qur'an 6:93

An-Nadr and the second prisoner had even ridden together, in the early days, all the way to the Jews of Yathrib to collect trick questions designed to expose the Prophet ﷺ as a fraud, and they had come back proud and certain, only to watch every question answered and waved away.

The second man you have met before. Uqbah ibn Abi Mu'ayt: the nobleman who rose to Abu Jahl's dare and fetched a rotting carcass with his own hands, the carcass no slave would touch, and lowered it onto the Prophet's ﷺ back as he prostrated, while grown men of Quraysh laughed, until a small daughter ran to pull the filth off her father. The same Uqbah who, another day, wound his cloak around the Prophet's ﷺ neck as he prayed and pulled it tight to choke him, until Abu Bakr threw himself between them with the cry the Qur'an itself puts in the mouth of the believer of Pharaoh's house.

And a believing man from the family of Pharaoh who concealed his faith said, "Do you kill a man [merely] because he says, 'My Lord is Allah' while he has brought you clear proofs from your Lord? And if he should be lying, then upon him is [the consequence of] his lie; but if he should be truthful, there will strike you some of what he promises you. Indeed, Allah does not guide one who is a transgressor and a liar."Qur'an 40:28

Brought out now to die, Uqbah began to beg. Why me, he cried, out of all of them? And Ali answered with the only reason that mattered: for your enmity toward Allah and His Messenger ﷺ. He had already tried to buy his life with money; now he fell to his knees and asked the question begging men ask. Muhammad, who will take care of my children? The reply is one sentence of the seerah you do not forget. The Fire. The scholars read it two ways, the Sheikh says, and both close the door: do not waste your last breath on your children, for you have something infinitely greater than them to worry about now, or, they need nothing from you, for you have already led the way to the Fire ahead of them. Then the Sheikh asks the question that closes Uqbah's file for good: where was this man's tenderness for children on the day little Fatima had to come and clean filth off her father's ﷺ shoulders because not one grown man in Makkah dared move? His bravery and sympathy were selective, spent freely on cruelty and gone the moment his own neck was at stake. Abu Jahl, for all his evil, died hard and never begged. Uqbah begged, and it is said Ali himself carried out the sentence.

The takbir, the whisperers, and a grave

Ahead of the army flew the news. The Prophet ﷺ had sent criers, and the first to reach the city was Zayd ibn Harithah, the man all Arabia had once known as the Prophet's ﷺ adopted son, mounted deliberately on the Prophet's ﷺ own she-camel, a mount every eye in Madinah knew on sight, so the news would arrive bearing its master's seal. Zayd rode in crying Allahu akbar and reciting the names of the Qurayshi dead, a roll call of the lords of Makkah, chieftain after chieftain, the men who had owned the city, gone in a single morning.

The believers erupted in joy. But mark the men who did not. Here and there a whisper started: Zayd has lost his mind, Muhammad must be dead, and his poor servant has fled on the master's camel and is babbling of victories in his grief. They were not weighing evidence; they were mourning a world that had just ended and could not believe the thing that had ended it. The Sheikh points to the mirror image in Makkah, where the first crier of the disaster was likewise called a liar, because there too the news refused to fit the world anyone knew.

And watch what the news did next, because Badr redrew Madinah from the inside. The last pagans of the city quietly gave up their idols. No verse ever commanded them to convert, the Sheikh notes carefully, and no decree was issued; it simply became impossible after Badr to stand in that society holding an idol, and the holdouts dwindled until paganism in the Prophet's ﷺ city was a memory. That was the bright aftershock. The dark one: hypocrisy was born at Badr. Before Badr there was nothing to gain by faking faith and much to lose; now the calculation flipped. Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul, the senior chieftain of Yathrib who had expected the whole settlement to crown him until the Prophet ﷺ arrived, heard Zayd reciting the names of the dead and drew the politician's conclusion aloud: the matter is settled now, this man is here to stay. So he professed Islam with his tongue, and the Qur'an testifies that his heart never followed. From this day the seerah gains a permanent shadow cast, the hypocrites, who will let no opening pass without striking at Islam from within.

And then one footnote of pure grief, set by Allah into the middle of the joy. The same day Zayd's takbir rang through the streets, it is said the very same hour, Uthman ibn Affan, radiyallahu anhu, was finishing the burial of the Prophet's ﷺ daughter Ruqayyah, and had to ask what the shouting was about. The scholars differ over where Ruqayyah fell in the order of his daughters; they do not differ that she was the first of them to die. The happiest day the Muslims had yet lived, and Allah willed that the one household with the most right to its happiness should be standing at a graveside in the middle of it.

Why, the Sheikh asks, and the answer is the seerah's standing lesson about this world: it is a place of testing, and it pauses for no one's celebration. The Prophet ﷺ once sketched it for his companions with two lines drawn in the dust: the long line of a man's hopes running out ahead of him toward all he means to do, and the short line of his appointed death cutting clean across it long before the hopes run out. Every one of us carries that long list, and death does not wait for us to finish it. Even the day of Badr was not permitted to feel like a final home, so that no joy of yours would ever fool you into thinking it was one.

Prisoners under the Prophet's ﷺ own roof

The captives reached Madinah, and the city had no prison to hold them, so they were marched to the mosque, and the Prophet ﷺ distributed them among the men who had captured them, with one standing order: I command you to treat them with kindness. The most senior lords of Quraysh he kept for his own household. Foremost was Suhayl ibn Amr, by sheer attrition now among the highest-ranking leaders Quraysh had left, the same Suhayl who will one day sit across from the Prophet ﷺ as Quraysh's negotiator at Hudaybiyyah. This man slept, ate, and waited for his ransom under the roof of the Prophet ﷺ he had marched out to destroy. Dr. Yasir Qadhi makes the point with full force: search the whole history of mankind for another ruler who took the enemy's senior prisoners of war into his own house, to eat at the same table where he himself slept. There is no Guantanamo here, the Sheikh says. There is none.

Abu Aziz, the blood brother of Mus'ab ibn Umayr, was assigned to a family of the Ansar, and he described his captivity himself. Whenever the household sat to eat, they pressed the bread and the meat into the prisoner's hands and kept the dates and the water for themselves, because their Prophet ﷺ had commanded kindness. Out of embarrassment, he said, he would put the bread back; they would set it before him again, untouched. The Sheikh asks you to date it. Mankind needed until the twentieth century, the Geneva Conventions, to write down rules requiring that prisoners be fed decently, and only bothered because it finally realized it could not go on doing what it had always done to captives. Here were the farmers of Madinah, before any convention on earth, feeding their prisoners better than they fed themselves, on the strength of a single sentence.

But that same family carries the hard edge of the lesson, and the Sheikh sets the two side by side on purpose. When Mus'ab passed his captive brother, Abu Aziz brightened: my brother, help me out now. Instead of softening, Mus'ab turned to the Ansari holding him: bind him well, his mother is a wealthy woman and she will pay the highest ransom for him. Is this how you treat me, Abu Aziz protested. And Mus'ab pointed at the Ansari: he is my brother now, not you. Badr had redrawn kinship itself. Blood opened no doors that day. Faith opened them, and the ransom ledger about to be written would prove the same thing on the Prophet's ﷺ own family first of all.

The day Sawdah forgot herself

Into these crowded houses walked a moment so human the Sheikh slows almost to a stop for it. Sawdah, radiyallahu anha, the wife of the Prophet ﷺ, had been visiting the family of the two young men of the Ansar who cut down Abu Jahl, when word came that the prisoners had reached the city. She hurried home, and this was years before the verses of hijab, so the households of Madinah still mixed freely. She burst into her own room, and there in the corner sat Suhayl ibn Amr, the great chieftain she had been raised from girlhood to revere, his hands bound.

She did not even register that the Prophet ﷺ was standing beside her. The old world rose straight up in her throat: Abu Yazid! You surrendered? You let them take you alive? Could you not have died an honorable death? Then a voice at her shoulder: are you stirring him up to fight against Allah and His Messenger ﷺ? She told the story on herself afterward. By Allah, she said, I did not know what I was saying; when I saw the lord of my people tied like that, I lost hold of who I was, and of all of Islam, and could not control myself, and I fell back into the old days.

Now weigh her words for what they were: a wish, spoken aloud in the Prophet's ﷺ own house, that a pagan champion had fought to the death against Allah and His Messenger ﷺ. Outwardly, the Sheikh says plainly, that is a sentence of kufr, which is exactly why the Prophet ﷺ said she was encouraging Suhayl to fight against Allah and His Messenger. And yet the Prophet ﷺ heard her excuse, accepted it, and never once raised the matter again. The Sheikh sets it beside the famous hadith of the man lost in the desert, dying of thirst, who suddenly finds his lost camel and in the delirium of joy blurts the words backward: O Allah, You are my servant and I am Your lord. The Prophet ﷺ smiled at his madness and excused him, because joy had scrambled his tongue. Emotion trips the tongue, and every one of us has said the thing we did not mean. He ﷺ judged the heart behind the slip and not the slip itself, and in doing so he taught every household among us what to do when the people we love misspeak: take the excuse, close the file, do not keep returning to the wound.

A ransom measured to every man

Word now went to Makkah: come and ransom your men. The narrations on the amounts differ, and the Sheikh weighs them as he always does, settling on the strongest reading, that every prisoner was priced to his own purse. The rich paid heavily, the modest lightly, and the poorest went home owing nothing at all. And notice who set the prices: a Prophet ﷺ who knew every one of these men personally, their families and their trades and, as it turned out, exactly how much money each of them had. Quraysh were, after all, his own relatives. No tribunal in history has been harder to deceive.

O Prophet, say to whoever is in your hands of the captives, "If Allah knows [any] good in your hearts, He will give you [something] better than what was taken from you, and He will forgive you; and Allah is Forgiving and Merciful."Qur'an 8:70

It was tested at once, from inside his own family. The Ansari who had captured al-Abbas, the Prophet's ﷺ own uncle, offered to gift his prisoner back: he is your uncle, Messenger of Allah, take him freely. The answer sealed every door favoritism might creep through: no, do not reduce his ransom by a single coin. Al-Abbas was assessed the steepest price on the whole ledger, four thousand dirhams, and told besides to pay the ransom of two nephews, Aqil and Nawfal, who had no money of their own. He tried the religious argument first. In a report preserved by Imam Ahmad he said: Messenger of Allah, I was already a Muslim, they forced me out to fight. The reply is a whole court ruling in one breath. Allah knows your true state, and if what you say is so, He will repay you and give you something better; but we judge by what we can see, and what we saw was you in the ranks against us. Then he tried poverty: I have no money. And the Prophet ﷺ asked him: then where is the money you and your wife buried on such and such a day, when you told her that if you should fall, this much goes to al-Fadl, this much to Abdullah, this much to each of your sons? Al-Abbas swore on the spot: by the One who sent you with the truth, no living soul knew of that money except the two of us. And he paid.

Al-Abbas himself used to say this very verse came down concerning him, and he lived long enough to test its promise. Years later he would say: I wish the Prophet ﷺ had taken even more from me, for Allah took twenty measures of silver and gave me in their place twenty slaves, every one a businessman trading on my behalf. The man charged the heaviest price at Badr spent his life convinced the exchange had run in his favor. And the refusal of favoritism never wavered: when the day came, years later, that the Prophet ﷺ abolished the dealings of interest, the first debts of riba he declared void were those owed to this same beloved uncle, so no one could ever say his family had kept a privilege.

Then there were the captives with no silver at all. For the literate among them, the Prophet ﷺ named a ransom no society on earth had ever thought to ask: teach the children of the Ansar to read and write, and walk free. This is not legend, the Sheikh stresses; it is authentically preserved in the Musnad of Imam Ahmad. Sit with what it means. In a culture that had never prized the written word, the man ﷺ leading it priced literacy above thousands of dirhams of ransom silver. Islam, the Sheikh says, drawing the wide line he has drawn across this whole series, came to the Arabs as a civilizing force, came to a people who cared nothing for letters or history, and lifted them to be the leaders of the world. A religion that freed a captured enemy in exchange for teaching its children to read has told you, once and forever, what is worth spending on.

Those with neither money nor letters were simply released. Abu Azzah pleaded his case: you know I am a poor man with no powerful family, only daughters to feed and no money, so be generous with me. The Prophet ﷺ freed him on a single promise, never to take the field against the Muslims again, and the man went home and wrote a poem in praise of the Prophet's ﷺ generosity that the books of seerah still carry. And mercy did what no sword could: many of the prisoners of Badr eventually entered Islam, the Prophet's ﷺ own captured kinsmen among the first. Mercy is the general rule, strictness the rare exception, and it was the mercy that won the hearts in the end.

One man refused the entire market. Abu Sufyan, suddenly the most senior leader Quraysh had left and the most humiliated man in Makkah, was told to ransom his son Amr, the older brother of Mu'awiyah. He refused: shall they have my blood and my money both? One of his sons already lay among the dead of Badr; let Amr stay in their hands as long as they please. And here the Sheikh makes an observation quietly devastating: the only reason Abu Sufyan could afford that defiance was that he knew, deep down, his son was in safe hands. No father shrugs at captors who torture. The enemy's coldest calculations were being balanced on the assumption of the Prophet's ﷺ mercy.

How he finally got his son back is uglier. Months later an elderly Muslim of Madinah, a man who had taken no part in Badr, came to Makkah on business, trusting the immunity the sacred precinct had granted every soul since before memory, the very law Quraysh themselves had always upheld so strictly that a man could see his own father's killer in the Haram and not lift a hand. Abu Sufyan seized this old man in broad daylight: my son for your elder. It was blatant extortion, the Sheikh says, and there is no nicer way to say it. Months earlier, when the Muslims had killed a single man of Quraysh near the sacred months, Quraysh had howled their outrage across all Arabia; now one of their own chieftains committed open kidnapping in the sacred city, and not one voice objected. This is what happens when a people answer to no fixed standard from Allah but only to themselves: grand talk of rights, applied to everyone except their opponents. The Prophet ﷺ did not write a manifesto about it. Pragmatic as ever, he swapped the prisoners and brought the old man home, for what was the old man's fault in any of it? And Allah forgave Abu Sufyan even this in the end, for he would one day accept Islam, and Islam erases what came before.

A necklace, and the road out of Makkah

Among the ransom payments that arrived from Makkah, one parcel stopped the room. It had come to free Abul-As, the husband of the Prophet's ﷺ daughter Zaynab, most likely the eldest of his daughters. Abul-As was the son of Hala, Khadijah's own sister; Zaynab had married her cousin in the days before revelation, when there was no enmity between them, and he had loved her honorably ever since, never once standing between her and her Islam, though he himself stayed on the religion of his people. In those years, the Sheikh reminds us, a Muslim woman could still be married to a pagan man; the verses that would separate such couples came later. So at Badr, Abul-As had marched out with Quraysh against the father of his own wife, and lost, and now from Makkah came the ransom to buy him back.

To complete her husband's ransom, Zaynab had emptied her own jewelry into the parcel, and among the pieces lay a necklace. Khadijah had worn it, and had clasped it around her daughter's neck on Zaynab's wedding day, the way mothers do still. When it tumbled out in Madinah, the companions watched the face of the Prophet ﷺ change. This was the man whose eyes would brim at the mere sound of Hala's footsteps, because they fell so like her sister's, the man whose grief for Khadijah was so visible that Aisha would later confess her jealousy of a woman long dead. And now Khadijah's own necklace was lying in a heap of war ransoms in his hand. He mastered himself, and made the gentlest request of the whole campaign: if you see fit, release her husband for her, and return to her this necklace. And who, the Sheikh asks, was going to refuse a request from the Prophet ﷺ? They saw fit.

But the release carried a private condition, and Madinah learned it only later. A month after Badr, the Prophet ﷺ quietly sent two companions to a spot outside Makkah: wait here a few days, and a traveler will come to you. The traveler was Zaynab. Her husband's unpaid ransom had not been money at all; it had been a promise to send the Prophet's ﷺ daughter home. Makkah had been told nothing, but everyone knew Abul-As had paid no coin, and the city did its arithmetic. Hind, the wife of Abu Sufyan, appeared at Zaynab's side, all honey: if you mean to go back to your father, tell me first, and I will prepare your baggage, for women know what a traveling woman needs better than any man. The plan, the Sheikh explains, was to keep her in Makkah as a hostage. Zaynab felt the hook inside the sweetness, told Hind nothing, and packed alone.

Abul-As could not bear to lead his wife back to the father he had just fought, so he sent his brother Kinanah, and Kinanah, brave but not subtle, set Zaynab's camel moving out of Makkah in broad daylight. The city boiled over: weeks after the humiliation of Badr, were they now to let the Prophet's ﷺ daughter parade out in front of everyone? A mob caught up with them, and a man named Habbar drove his spear at the camel. It reared in terror, and Zaynab, who was pregnant, was thrown hard to the ground. She bled where she lay, and she lost the child. Some accounts say the injuries never fully left her, and were among the reasons for her early death a few years later; of all the Prophet's ﷺ daughters, only Fatima would outlive him, and Zaynab carried the last wound of Badr through the few years she had left. Kinanah planted himself over her with his bow drawn: by Allah, the first man who comes near me tastes my arrow, and every one of you knows I do not miss.

The standoff was broken, in the end, by the politician. Abu Sufyan came riding up, scattered the crowd, and scolded Kinanah: you acted like a fool, in broad daylight, after everything that has just happened to us. We gain nothing by keeping this woman, but you cannot parade our humiliation through the streets. Take her back, let the talk die down, then take her out quietly. And that is exactly what happened.
