A few nights later, under cover of darkness, Kinanah led Zaynab out again and placed her reins in the hands of the two waiting companions, who brought the Prophet's ﷺ daughter home to Madinah. The Sheikh pauses on Kinanah deliberately: a man who was no Muslim, yet ready to die rather than break the promise his brother had made. There are honorable people in every nation, he says, and the seerah will not let you forget it.

And Habbar, the man with the spear? Here Sheikh Yasir, who once wrote a university paper on what follows and can barely hide his delight, traces the bloodline forward. Generations later, the descendants of Habbar, Muslims now, rode into Sindh, and from his progeny rose the Habbari dynasty, which ruled there for some two hundred years and minted its own coins in the land that is today Pakistan. The name of the man who speared the Prophet's ﷺ daughter and killed the child she carried survives in history as the name of a Muslim dynasty. That, the Sheikh marvels, is what Islam does to a bloodline, and what the long, patient justice of Allah does with a story.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this lesson as a string of episodes, a little statecraft and a little sentiment, and miss that it is one thing held in one steady hand. Hold the whole day at once and you will see it. The Fire pronounced over Uqbah, and bread pressed into Abu Aziz's palms by captors who ate dates and water themselves. Four thousand dirhams taken from a beloved uncle down to the last coin, and freedom handed to a poor father of daughters for a promise and a poem. A wife's terrible sentence forgiven before she had finished explaining it, a daughter lowered into her grave in the very hour the city shouted for joy, a son-in-law set free at the sight of a dead woman's necklace. This is not a man ﷺ swinging between moods. It is the steadiest scale ever held by human hands: strictness exactly where Allah's right had been openly and arrogantly attacked, on the two who had made war on revelation itself, mercy everywhere else mercy could reach, and grief carried straight through the middle of it without one drop souring into cruelty.

So ask what the day asks of you. First, the lesson of the verse: forgiveness belongs to the strong. We are quick to call ourselves merciful when in truth we simply lack the power to be otherwise, and that is not mercy, it is incapacity. Real pardon is the pardon you could withhold and choose not to, offered from the high ground and not from the ditch. Its companion is to keep your severity rare and exact: two men met the sword, and seventy met bread and ransom and release. Justice existed in that camp to guard the rule of mercy, never to replace it. When you must be firm, be firm with precision, and then return at once to kindness, because the kindness is the rule and the firmness only its rare and reluctant servant.

Then take Sawdah home with you. When the people you love misspeak out of shock or grief or anger or even joy, when the old self rises in someone's throat and says the thing they do not mean, do what he ﷺ did: judge the heart and not the sentence, accept the excuse, and never go back to reopen the wound. Take al-Abbas and the children of the Ansar, and learn what a believer spends on: he ﷺ priced the teaching of reading above ransom silver in a society that did not read, so ask what you are willing to pay for knowledge and for the minds of the young, when the Prophet ﷺ was willing to free his enemies for it. And take Ruqayyah's grave, set in the middle of the happiest morning, and learn to hold your joys loosely. The line of your hopes runs long, and your appointed end will cut across it sooner than you plan; celebrate with gratitude, pack lightly, and let no happiness in this world ever pretend to be your home.

And last, take Kinanah and Habbar, and refuse forever to flatten people into camps. There are honest, decent souls in every nation under heaven, and Allah can take the worst act in a story and, generations on, grow from that very bloodline something that worships Him. May Allah send His peace and His blessings upon Muhammad ﷺ, who fed the men who fought him and freed them for teaching children to read, who forgave a slip of the tongue and wept over a necklace, and who held justice and mercy level in the same hand when no one before or since has managed it for a day. Join him in the highest gardens with Khadijah, with Ruqayyah, and with Zaynab, and mend every grief He decreed for that blessed household; and make us people who forgive from strength, who take the excuse gladly, who pay any price for knowledge, and who, on the day our own long line of hopes is cut, are gathered under his banner and given to drink from his hand a drink after which there is no thirst. Ameen.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 41: the Battle of Badr, part 6 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (8:67, 8:68, 6:93, 40:28, 8:70), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 42 of 104

The Battle of Badr, part 7: the grief of Makkah, the end of Abu Lahab, and the surah of Badr

The city that could not weep, the surah that remembers

Seven days ago this chapter opened with a caravan hugging the coast road and a city riding out as if to a parade. Today it closes, and not on the battlefield. The fighting at Badr is finished; what is left is the echo, and the echo is louder than the swords ever were. Today the news walks into Makkah and breaks it. The worst of the old enemies dies without a single hand raised against him. An empire falls on the very morning Allah had said it would. And then heaven itself retells the whole battle, because Surah al-Anfal begins to come down while the dust of Badr is still on the believers' clothes.

This is the seventh and final day at those wells, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi spends it the way the sources themselves do: half in a grieving, silenced Makkah, and half inside the surah Allah sent down so that Badr would never be remembered wrongly. The battle has been won. Now comes the harder, quieter business of understanding what it meant, and the Qur'an does that understanding for us, while the memory is still warm.

The news walks into Makkah

Here is how certain Quraysh had been of victory: they marched out with no plan for losing. No rallying point, no second rendezvous, no plan B at all, because it had not occurred to them that the lines could break. So when they did break, the army simply scattered and trickled home in shocked little batches.

The first to reach Makkah was a man called al-Haysuman, and the state of him made the streets gather: bloodied, wounded, something plainly wrong. What happened? they asked. And he answered in a flat, drained voice, a list instead of a story. Utbah is killed. Shaybah is killed. Abu Jahl is killed. Zam'ah is killed. Name after name from the first row of Quraysh, the who's who of the entire city, until he had named so many that the listeners decided the only merciful explanation was that the man had lost his mind. He is listing everyone we know, they said. It is not possible.

Safwan ibn Umayyah was sitting in the shade of the Kaaba, inside the wall of the hijr, when the report reached him, and he designed a small test. If the man has gone mad, Safwan reasoned, he will name even the living among the dead. Go and ask him what became of Safwan ibn Umayyah. So they put the question to the broken rider: and Safwan, what of him? The man looked across the distance, saw Safwan seated there, and said: Safwan is sitting right over there. But I watched with my own eyes as they killed his father and his brother.

The test had come back with a witness instead of a madman. And as survivor after survivor confirmed the same names, a grief settled over Makkah unlike anything in its memory. Three times the number of the Muslims, the finest weapons in Arabia, every great house represented in the ranks, and in a single morning the city's entire senior generation was simply gone.

Abu Lahab hears about the angels

One senior name had not marched at all. Abu Lahab had hired a man to fight in his place, and Quraysh had not pressed him to go, for it sat awkwardly even with them that the chief of Banu Hashim should ride out against his own nephew ﷺ. That was against everything their tribal world stood for, and so, unlike the pressure they put on others, they let him stay.

When the deserters' reports reached him he refused to believe them, sank into a black mood, and waited for Abu Sufyan to come back with the truth, telling himself the others had only run in fear. They met at the house of Abbas, and the house itself is part of the story. Abbas, the Prophet's ﷺ uncle, was at that very moment a prisoner in Madinah, bound after Badr; his iman, Dr. Yasir Qadhi reminds us, had finally settled at the battle, when the Prophet ﷺ told him about money he had hidden that no living soul could have known about. His wife, Umm al-Fadl, is said to have been the second woman to believe after Khadijah, and it may well be that her faith is what had first drawn Abbas toward Islam. She was home. So was a slave of the household, a quiet believer, working over the cooking in the corner, where a servant has no business listening and less business speaking.

Tell me face to face exactly what happened, said Abu Lahab. And Abu Sufyan, a man with no reason in the world to flatter the Muslims, gave testimony worth sitting with. By Allah, he said, the moment we met them it was as if they overpowered us without our lifting a hand. They took prisoners as they pleased and they killed as they pleased. And even so, I cannot blame our men. I saw men with white faces on horses black and white, the finest horses of Arabia, hovering between the heaven and the earth, and nothing on the ground could stand before them.

The slave in the corner could not contain himself. By Allah, he cried out, those were the angels! In that one sentence his loyalty stood exposed, and it was the wrong room to expose it in. Abu Lahab's grief snapped into rage. He seized the man, threw him down, and beat him almost to death, and a slave in that house could not so much as raise an arm to defend himself. When Umm al-Fadl came out and physically pulled Abu Lahab off, he turned and began to beat her too. And wounded as she was, she answered him with a fury that has outlived him: the master of this house is away in captivity, and this is what you do to his household? This is the protector you are?

Abu Lahab left humiliated and guilt-ridden, and within days a foul sickness took hold of him. The books of tafsir and sira describe it in different ways, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi will not call it a natural death; his reading is that misery and disgrace killed Abu Lahab as surely as any disease. He was not seen again. Within a week of the battle he was dead, the worst of that first generation of enemies of Islam, and the very last of them, finished without a single Muslim raising a hand against him. It is as if Allah had saved this one for the end, the icing, the Sheikh says, on a bitter cake, so that the destruction of the old guard of Makkah would be complete and not one of them could claim a Muslim's blade had done it.

The city that was not allowed to weep

Ibn Ishaq records that once the survivors were home, Makkah was soon enveloped in wailing, the loud ritual mourning of the jahiliyyah, rising from every quarter of the city. Remember the dream of Atikah from the first day of this chapter, the boulder that struck the mountain and burst, sending a shard into every single house in Makkah? This was the shard arriving. There was no household without its dead.

Then Abu Sufyan gathered Quraysh and decreed something the Arabs had never done before in their history: no woman shall wail. Not out of mercy, but out of spite. He could not bear that word might reach the Muslims that Makkah was weeping, that they might take any pleasure from the sound of Quraysh grieving. So the city was made to swallow its grief whole. One night, al-Aswad ibn al-Muttalib, a distant uncle of the Prophet ﷺ who had lost all three of his sons at Badr, heard a woman wailing somewhere in the dark, and a flicker of hope rose in him. He sent his servant running: has the ban been lifted, so that I may finally weep for my son, for my Zam'ah? The servant came back with the answer. No. She is wailing over a lost camel.

Ibn Kathir, on whom the Sheikh leans throughout this episode, saw in that small cruel scene a punishment folded inside the punishment. Mourning shared is mourning softened; the wailing of the jahiliyyah, for all its excess, gave them comfort, the household gathering, the voices rising together, the grief made bearable by being carried in company. By forbidding it, Abu Sufyan, without meaning to, denied his people even that. They had to grieve the worst day in their history in silence, each behind a closed door. The ban he imposed out of pride became, by Allah's decree, one more weight upon their backs.

The other victory of the same morning

Now widen the lens, because Badr was not the only battle Allah decided that morning. Years earlier, in the middle of the Makkan period, around the sixth or seventh year of the dawah, Persia had crushed Rome so thoroughly that Roman power seemed to be sliding into its grave. And it was precisely then, against every reading of the politics, that Allah revealed the opening of Surah ar-Rum:

The Byzantines have been defeated In the nearest land. But they, after their defeat, will overcome Within three to nine years. To Allah belongs the command [i.e., decree] before and after. And that day the believers will rejoice In the victory of Allah. He gives victory to whom He wills, and He is the Exalted in Might, the Merciful.Qur'an 30:2-5

It is one of the most explicit predictions in the entire Qur'an, and it carried two promises at once. The defeated empire would rise and win within bid' sinin, a handful of years; and on the very day that victory came, the believers would be rejoicing in a victory of their own. Ubayy ibn Khalaf mocked the prophecy to Abu Bakr's face: you truly believe Rome will beat Persia, after a defeat like that? So the two men set a wager, in the days before such wagers were forbidden. The Arabic bid' runs from three to nine, and when Ubayy asked how many years, Abu Bakr radiyallahu anhu took the middle and said six. Six years passed with no Roman victory, and Abu Bakr lost the bet and paid up the camels he had staked.

Then, on the same day the swords met at Badr, more than a thousand miles away, Heraclius of Rome launched a fierce offensive and broke the armies of Khosrow II of Persia. As the qadr of Allah unfolded it, two of the Persian king's generals defected with their entire flanks, and his own family plotted against him; it was as though his right hand had been cut off before the fighting began, and the empire that had looked invincible folded. Both predictions of the surah landed together, on one morning. The believers at the wells did not even know it yet; the news would take two weeks to ride into Arabia, and by then the wager was long settled and Ubayy already dead.

Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi makes one of his signature points. This event is dated in the Roman and Persian chronicles too, written by people who had never heard of Badr and cared nothing for it. Lay the sira beside the recorded history of the world and the dates interlock, incident by incident. For those who doubt whether the sunnah and the sira were truly preserved, here is the answer in the hardest currency there is: the life of the Prophet ﷺ is not legend gilded by his followers. It is preserved, and corroborated, down to the morning. A handful of pebbles changed a desert; on the same sunrise, far away, an empire kept an appointment a Makkan verse had set for it years before.

The best of us

What did one day at those wells make of the three hundred and some who stood it? Jibril himself came down and put the question to the Prophet ﷺ: how do you reckon the people of Badr among you? We count them, he ﷺ answered, the best of us. And Jibril replied: and so do we count the angels who were present at Badr the best of us. Heaven and earth filed the very same ranking. It is why Imam al-Bukhari, generations later, would give the virtues of the people of Badr an entire book within his Sahih, full of hadith on their station.

Some of that rank was bought in blood on the day itself. Harithah ibn Suraqah, a young man struck by a stray arrow that found him before the lines had fully closed, was among the first of the martyrs. His mother came from the Ansar with the only question a mother carries: tell me about my son. Is he in Jannah? And the Prophet ﷺ answered her with a gentleness that multiplied the gift. It is not one garden, he told her. They are many gardens, and your son has reached al-Firdaws, the highest of them all.

And some of that rank would only show its full weight years later, when one of these men stumbled. At the conquest of Makkah, a veteran of Badr named Hatib ibn Abi Balta'ah did something close to unforgivable: he sent a secret letter ahead to Quraysh, warning them that the Muslims were marching, when the whole power of the conquest lay in its being a surprise. Jibril informed the Prophet ﷺ, riders overtook the courier, a woman, and drew the letter from where she had hidden it in the braids of her hair, and there it was in plain words: beware, the Muslims are coming. Treason, by any measure. Umar asked for the man's head, and this time, Dr. Yasir Qadhi notes, the request was by every outward standard fully justified; this was not a sneer or a doubt but plain treachery in time of war. But Hatib's defense was a frightened father's. By Allah, he said, I never doubted Islam for a moment; you know me. But every other man here has a clan back in Makkah to shield his family, and I have none. I hoped that by doing Quraysh this favor I might buy protection for my family, and I knew with certainty that Allah would protect you regardless. The Prophet ﷺ said: Hatib has told you the truth. And when Umar pressed a second time, asking to strike not for unbelief but for the crime, the Prophet ﷺ answered with the words that fixed the station of these men forever: how do you know, Umar? Perhaps Allah looked upon the people of Badr and said, do as you please, for I have already forgiven you.

He was saying this five and six years after the battle, and using Badr to shield a man's life and lift his rank long after the dust had settled. That is what one day of sincerity at those wells could weigh. It is why the classical sira writers, from Ibn Ishaq down through Ibn Hisham to Ibn Kathir, spent page after page listing all three hundred and some of the people of Badr by name, one by one, for no scholarly reason at all except honor.

Before opening the surah, the episode steps back to name what this single morning did to history, and the Sheikh draws three effects. First, the Muslims were now a fact of politics: a separate, independent, legitimate entity, a state that Quraysh and all of Arabia would have to deal with as a tangible reality, no longer a runaway preacher and a band of followers to be wished away. Second, it was the wound from which Quraysh never recovered, and the Sheikh's judgment is blunt: this was the single greatest shock in the whole sira, every later defeat trivial beside it. At every battle afterward they would at least fight knowing the Muslims were a power; only at Badr had they marched out certain they were about to wipe Islam from the earth, and instead lost their entire first row before noon. Third, and closest to home: Badr is the moment the masks inside Madinah began to slip. Until now the grumbling of the munafiqun and the unease of the surrounding tribes had been little more than sarcasm; after Badr, envy began to harden into something colder, and the episodes ahead will have to reckon with both the hypocrites within and the treaty tribes around. Victory, it turns out, does not end your tests. It only changes their address.

Allah retells the battle

Now the heart of this final day. Surah al-Anfal, the eighth surah of the Qur'an, began to come down on the plains of Badr itself, to men whose memory of the battle was measured in hours, and the Sheikh walks nearly the whole surah to show that almost every ayah touches what they had just lived. Imagine, he says, what they understood that we never will: references so fresh and so plain to them that they needed no explaining, lost to us now only because the people who carried that memory are gone. The surah opens exactly where the camp's tension sat: they ask you about the spoils. Say that the spoils belong to Allah and His Messenger, so fear Allah and set right what is between you. The quarrel of the three groups over the war booty, the ones who had chased the enemy, the ones who had guarded the Prophet ﷺ, the ones who had taken the camp, dissolved in a single verse. And immediately the surah lifts the believers' eyes off the money to the real prize:

The believers are only those who, when Allah is mentioned, their hearts become fearful, and when His verses are recited to them, it increases them in faith; and upon their Lord they rely -Qur'an 8:2

Then the surah does something astonishingly tender. It remembers their fear, and does not shame them for it. It recalls how their Lord brought them out of their homes in truth while a party of the believers were unwilling, arguing about it after the truth was clear, as if they were being driven toward death with their own eyes open and watching it come. These were the men who, when the army turned out to be the army and not the unguarded caravan, hesitated and said they had not come prepared. And in the very breath that records that terror, Allah names them al-mu'minun, the believers. Fear, the Sheikh insists, does not cancel iman. Being terrified expelled not a single man from the ranks of faith. The believer is allowed to be afraid, even very afraid; what matters is not whether the fear comes but whether the feet keep moving. And the wording is no rebuke: Allah did not call them Muslimun but mu'minun, the higher praise, even as He corrected them.

Verse after verse reads their own week back to them. The desperate cry for help, and the answer: I will reinforce you with a thousand of the angels, rank upon rank. The strange drowsiness that fell over the camp the night before, when men with an ordeal at dawn cannot close their eyes and these men slept; and the rain that purified them, firmed the sand beneath their feet, and drove off the whispers of Shaytan. The dream in which Allah had shown the enemy as few, and the eyes on the field that saw the same, so that one companion guessed aloud, do you think they are seventy? and another answered, perhaps a hundred, when in truth they were a thousand. The meeting itself: had the two armies tried to set that appointment they would have missed it, but Allah delivered both to the wells so that a matter already decreed would be carried out, that whoever perished would perish upon clear evidence and whoever lived would live upon it. Even Abu Jahl's prayer at the Kaaba, that Allah destroy whichever party had cut the ties of kinship and broken most with the faith of the fathers, is answered to the letter: you sought the verdict, and the verdict has come. And it came against him.

And at the center of it all stands one ayah, the one the scholars of aqidah return to again and again:

And you did not kill them, but it was Allah who killed them. And you threw not, [O Muhammad], when you threw, but it was Allah who threw that He might test the believers with a good test. Indeed, Allah is Hearing and Knowing.Qur'an 8:17

One handful of pebbles, cast by the Prophet ﷺ at an entire army, and somehow it reached every eye. The Sheikh weighs the wording the way the people of aqidah do, because it holds the whole balance of our belief in qadar. Notice, he says, that Allah affirms the throw: when you threw. He did not say you never threw; the Prophet ﷺ genuinely picked up the dust and flung it, by his own will and intention, so we are not puppets, and this is not the doctrine of jabr, that a man is forced and does nothing. And then Allah claims the result: but Allah threw. The reaching of every eye, the effect no human arm could produce, was Allah's alone, so we are not the power either. Had the verse stopped at you threw not, man would be a robot; had it said only you threw, man would be the doer of all things. It says both. You act with everything in you; Allah carries the act to its end.

One verse even carries an address. The disbelievers had boasted, when the verses were recited, that they had heard all this before and could compose its like if they wished, that it was nothing but legends of the ancients. That was the trademark sneer of an-Nadr ibn al-Harith, the storyteller of Quraysh, and an-Nadr was among those executed on the road home from Badr, in the very days these verses were descending. No narration spells the timing out, and the Sheikh flags plainly that this is his own reading, not a transmitted fact, but it may well be that the man lived just long enough to hear heaven quoting his own boast back to him. They had dared Allah, too: if this is really the truth from You, then rain stones down on us, or bring a painful punishment. Badr, the surah says in effect, was that rain. And yet even here a door is held open. Allah would not destroy them wholesale while the Prophet ﷺ was among them, and, in the reading the Sheikh prefers, while there were still people in that city who would one day believe and seek His forgiveness. And most of Makkah, in the end, would.

The commands that walked out of Badr

Al-Anfal does not only remember Badr; it legislates from it, and the new state takes its first laws here, given the swift way the Prophet ﷺ gives them.

O you who have believed, respond to Allah and to the Messenger when he calls you to that which gives you life. And know that Allah intervenes between a man and his heart and that to Him you will be gathered.Qur'an 8:24

Think who first heard the call that gives life. Barely eighty-five Muhajirun stood at Badr, their Ansar brothers around them, and the call they answered looked for all the world like a call to death. Had that small band not answered it, the Sheikh says, the religion would never have reached you; we are alive as Muslims today on the answer they gave that morning. And the verse ends inside the human chest: Allah intervenes between a man and his heart. This is why the Prophet ﷺ never stopped pleading, O Turner of hearts, make my heart firm upon Your obedience. If your heart feels weak, turn to its Owner to strengthen it; and if it feels strong, turn to Him anyway, lest you imagine a strong heart cannot be turned.

The second great law is unity:

And brought together their hearts. If you had spent all that is in the earth, you could not have brought their hearts together; but Allah brought them together. Indeed, He is Exalted in Might and Wise.Qur'an 8:63

All the treasure on earth, spent honestly, could never have joined the hearts of these old feuding tribes; Allah joined them, and it is His gift. And the surah guards the gift with a warning a few verses on: do not dispute with one another, or you will fail and your strength, your wind, will leave you. The Sheikh points one chapter ahead: the very verb of that warning, the root of tanaza'tum, is the word the Qur'an will use in Surah Al Imran for exactly what goes wrong at Uhud, where the believers will argue and lose what they had won. Unity is half of victory, and disputing is precisely how a victory is handed back.

Then come the standing orders of the new state. Prepare against the enemy whatever strength you can, and the Prophet ﷺ himself defined that strength: indeed the strength is in the throw, he said, the strength is archery, marksmanship before mere muscle. But if the enemy inclines to peace, then incline to it and rely on Allah, for war is not the default of this religion; peace is. And never treachery: a Muslim may outmaneuver an enemy in war, deception of strategy is allowed, but a sworn word is sacred, and if a treaty must be ended it is announced openly, thrown back so that both stand on equal footing, before a single move is made. The Sheikh lingers here, because the world still slanders the religion on this point: deception in war is not treason. To feint and maneuver is one thing; to swear an oath and break it is another, and that, the surah says, Allah does not love. Even the fighting ratios were eased within these verses, from one believer against ten down to one against two, a concession written because Allah knew the weakness in His servants: mercy folded directly into the law of war.

And at the last the surah turns to the captives and to the believers still scattered far away. Say to the prisoners in your hands: if Allah knows any good in your hearts, He will give you better than what was taken from you, and forgive you. Abbas would swear in later years that this ayah came down concerning him, that he wished only that Quraysh had taken more from him, for everything Badr had cost him returned many times over, with the gift of Islam on top of it all. And then the brotherhood verses bring the surah to its close. After Badr, every believer still in Makkah was commanded to complete the hijrah; and believers anywhere who call for help in the matter of the religion are owed that help, the Sheikh says, looking up from the page at our own century, within the limits of standing treaties. Brothers always, even where politics constrains what aid can reach them. The bonds of faith are not dissolved by a border.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this seventh day as the satisfying end of a war story, the enemies punished, the prophecy vindicated, the surah revealed, and to close the book there. That would be to miss what the day is actually asking of us. Strip it down and three questions step forward, each one aimed straight at an ordinary life.

The first is the question of fear. Allah looked at men who marched as though watching their own executioner approach, men whose courage was visibly shaking, and He called them, in the same verse, the believers. We tend to think faith means feeling no fear, and so when dread rises in us before a hard and lawful thing, we take it as a verdict against our iman and we turn back. Badr says otherwise. Bring your fear with you. Let it ride beside you to the wells. Only do not hand it the reins; do not let it choose your direction. The men of Badr were afraid, and they are the best of us, because their feet kept moving when their hearts were pounding.

The second is the question at the center of the surah, the handful of pebbles. You did not throw when you threw, but Allah threw. Here is the balance most of us spend a lifetime failing to hold. On one side are those who do nothing and call it trust; on the other, those who do everything and credit themselves. The verse refuses both. The Prophet ﷺ genuinely bent down and threw, with his own hand and his own will, and Allah genuinely carried that throw to every eye on the field. So work. Prepare your strength, learn the archery, tie the camel, send the letters, do every lawful thing within your power as if the outcome depended on it, and then, when you have done it all, attribute none of the result to yourself. You are never a puppet, and you are never the power. You throw; Allah carries.

And the third is the question of the heart, and of the hearts around you. All the wealth on earth could not buy what Allah gave freely at Badr, the joining of hearts that had hated each other for generations, and the same surah that named the gift warned that quarreling is how its wind departs. So guard whatever unity you have been handed, in your home, your masjid, your ummah, like the weapon it is. And guard your own heart most of all, for it lies in the hand of the One who turns it, nearer to it than you are. The men who held the swords, the bounty, the numbers, the certainty of victory, marched home with nothing. The handful who answered a call that looked like death are the reason you pray today.

So carry one thing down into your ordinary days from this last morning at the wells. When the next hard, lawful thing stands in front of you and the fear comes, let it come, and move anyway. Do your utmost, and then rest the result with Allah, who threw when His Messenger ﷺ threw. Keep your given word unbroken and your ranks undivided. And when your own heart feels far from Him, say the plea He taught beside the verse that He stands between a man and his heart: O Turner of hearts, make my heart firm upon Your obedience. May Allah, who joined the hearts of the believers when all the earth's treasure could not, join ours; make us people who answer the call that gives life; and as He looked upon the people of Badr and forgave them, may He look upon us and forgive what He knows of us. And may He send His peace and His blessings upon His Messenger Muhammad ﷺ, and gather us under his banner with the best of them.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 42: the Battle of Badr, part 7 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (30:2-5, 8:2, 8:17, 8:24, 8:63), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 43 of 104

Between Badr and Uhud

A poisoned sword, a broken treaty, and the lines being drawn

Badr is barely a month old, and the ground is still shaking from it. The ransoms are still being haggled over, the shock is still travelling out across Arabia by every road, and into that unsettled season the seerah hands us two stories that belong together even though, on the surface, they could not look more different. In the first, a man crosses the desert with a sword steeped in poison and a secret that only two people on earth know, and he walks out of the masjid a believer. In the second, a treaty dies in a goldsmith's market, and a city learns where its loyalties truly lie. Dr. Yasir Qadhi gathers them as the first events between the two great battles, and the thread that ties them is a single hard fact: after Badr, no one in Madinah can stand in the middle any longer. Every heart has to declare itself, and these two stories are simply the two directions a heart can run.

Two grieving men in the Hijr

The first story begins not in Madinah but in Makkah, in the Hijr beside the Ka'bah, where two cousins sit together with their grief. One is Safwan ibn Umayyah, and his loss is fresh and personal: his father was the famous Umayyah ibn Khalaf, the man who had once owned Bilal and tortured him under the desert sun, and the first rider to return from Badr had found Safwan and told him plainly, by Allah, I saw your father and your brother as they were dragged to their deaths. The cousin beside him is Umayr ibn Wahb, among the most severe of all the people in his hatred of the new religion. His wound is of a different kind: his own son was taken captive at Badr and now sits a prisoner in Madinah, and the father can do nothing but sit in Makkah and think about it.

So the two of them trade their losses back and forth, until Umayr says the sentence that sets the whole story in motion. Were it not for a debt I owe and a family I would have to leave behind, he says, I would ride to Madinah myself and kill Muhammad ﷺ. Safwan does not let those words cool in the air; he sees a golden opportunity, and his father's wealth is behind him. What if I take care of your debt, he answers, and your family lives exactly as my own family lives, so that every gift I give my children, yours receive the same? Will you do it then? Umayr agrees on one condition. Keep this between the two of us. Tell no single soul.

And he tells no one, not his wife, not his servant. He sharpens his sword and steeps it in poison until the merest scratch from its tip would carry death into the body, the way the Arabs knew how to do to their weapons. Then he slips out of Makkah alone, in complete secrecy, and points his camel north. The ordinary traveler covered that road in eleven or twelve days; a man riding alone would take longer, and Umayr rode the better part of two weeks. Sit a moment with the weight of what he is carrying. His own son is a hostage inside the very city he is riding to strike, and he knows exactly what becomes of that boy once the deed is done. There is no road home from this, for himself or for his son. That is the depth of the hatred: he is spending his own life and his child's life both, for one thrust of a poisoned blade.

The secret only two men knew

He enters Madinah the way any traveler enters from the open desert, his face wound up in his turban against the blowing sand, his sword slung from his neck, and he makes straight for the masjid. And there the first man he meets is the last he would have chosen. Umar ibn al-Khattab, radiyallahu anhu, takes one look and knows him by his eyes. That dog, Umar says, the enemy of Allah. By Allah, he has come for nothing but evil. He hurries to the Prophet ﷺ ahead of Umayr: the enemy of Allah is in the city, looking for you. The answer comes back without alarm. Bring him to me. So Umar walked Umayr in with his hand resting on his own sword the whole way, ready to cut him down at the first wrong movement, and quietly told the Ansar to watch this man's every move, because he could not be trusted.

What follows is one of the most extraordinary conversations in the entire seerah. Umayr offers the old pagan greeting, a wish for a pleasant morning, and even now, with an assassin and a poisoned sword before him, the Prophet ﷺ teaches rather than recoils. Allah has given us a greeting better than yours, he says, the greeting of the people of Jannah, the salam. Umayr brushes it aside. Then the questions begin. Why have you come? To negotiate the ransom of my son, Umayr answers; he has his cover ready. Then what, the Prophet ﷺ asks, is this sword hanging from your neck? A man who comes to negotiate does not come armed. Umayr glances at it as though seeing it for the first time. This sword? What good did our swords ever do us at Badr? Tell me the truth, the Prophet ﷺ says. I have told you, Umayr insists.

So the Prophet ﷺ tells him the truth instead. No. You and Safwan sat together in the Hijr and spoke of your dead at Badr, and you said that were it not for your debt and your family, you would come and kill me yourself. And Safwan took your debt and your family upon himself so that you would. But Allah has come between you and your plan.

Umayr does the arithmetic of secrets in a single heartbeat. Two men, and only two, had been in that conversation. He had told no wife and no servant; no traveler had outrun him to carry the tale ahead. There was exactly one way this man could know what was said in the Hijr, and it was not a way of this earth. He says it himself, out loud, in the masjid: we used to call you a liar when you told us revelation came down from the sky, but no one knew of this except me and Safwan, and by Allah, only Allah could have informed you. I bear witness that there is no god but Allah, and that you are the Messenger of Allah. He had come within a sword's length of his target, and there, at the very tip of his reach, he was conquered instead, and then he gave thanks for it: praise be to the One who dragged me to the truth.

Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi stops for a judgment that is distinctly his. Some of Islam's worst enemies, he says, were sincere in their hostility. Some, like Abu Jahl, knew the truth and fought it anyway; but many others genuinely believed this religion false, and for those it sometimes took a miracle as blunt as a slap across the face, the way Pharaoh's magicians, the most hardened of men, fell into sajdah the instant they saw a sign too enormous to explain away. Then the Sheikh turns the point on us. If that was true of people who stood before the Messenger ﷺ himself, we should hold it in mind about the loudest enemies of Islam in our own age: some of them, he has no doubt, genuinely believe the filth they repeat, brainwashed even as they brainwash others. Which is why his general rule stands: gentleness wins what harshness cannot, and a lifetime of hatred can flip in five seconds when it is met the right way.

Two cousins, two shahadas

The Prophet ﷺ turns to his companions and gives three instructions that deserve to be framed on the wall of every masjid. Teach your brother his religion. Help him to memorize the Qur'an. And go and free his captive for him. The son Umayr had come to ransom is released without a single coin, and the would-be assassin's first gift in Islam is the very thing he had used as his cover story. The man who a second earlier was reaching for a poisoned blade now receives every blessing imaginable. And notice which command came first: teach him his religion, help him with the Qur'an. When a person enters the faith, this is the way, that we be as firm as we can without driving him off, even if at the start he reads the Fatiha off a card written in his own letters, the way Sheikh Yasir describes doing for a man who had converted the week before. The Prophet ﷺ assigned a teacher to every new Muslim, every time, and the Sheikh laments openly how completely we have let that simple sunnah lapse, and how easy it would be to revive: a list ready so that the moment a new convert walks in, someone takes him in hand as a teacher and a foster family and folds him into the community.

Umayr stays a while and learns, and then he comes with a request, and the address itself tells you how far he has travelled. The man who walked in saying O Muhammad now says O Messenger of Allah. I spent myself, he says, trying to put out the light of Allah, and I tortured those who embraced Islam. Give me permission to return to Makkah and call them to the very truth I used to bar them from. His blood is safe there; he is senior, pure-blooded Quraysh. Permission is granted. And back in Makkah, Safwan has been telling everyone a surprise is coming, just wait. Then the news arrives: his hired sword has become a believer. Safwan vows to Allah that he will never speak to his cousin again, never look at him, never stand beneath one roof with him, after a lifetime of growing up side by side. Umayr preaches anyway, and Ibn Ishaq records that a number of people accepted Islam at his hands. It is the same message the Prophet ﷺ had been preaching for thirteen years, only now it lands differently because it comes from inside the family. Guidance, the Sheikh reminds us, is rarely the work of five minutes; people are raised inside a way of life, and it can take years, even decades, to think their way out of it.

And the ending is too good to leave for later, so run the story forward six years. Umayr returns to Makkah with the army of the Conquest, a Muslim now. His cousin Safwan, still a pagan, is certain the Prophet ﷺ will never forgive the son of Umayyah ibn Khalaf, and he flees, though in truth he was not even one of the handful of condemned names. And who goes hunting for him? The very cousin he had sworn out of his life. Umayr asks the Prophet ﷺ for an aman, a guarantee of safety, for Safwan, and the Prophet ﷺ, who never refused a man who asked him, grants it. Umayr tracks his old friend down, talks him back into the city, and Safwan says the shahada in front of the Prophet ﷺ himself. Look at what the qadar of Allah has done: the two men who once sat in the Hijr plotting an assassination both end up giving their hearts to the very man they had hired the sword against.

Telling the hardest stories straight

The second half of the episode steps onto what Dr. Yasir Qadhi calls the most politically charged ground in the entire seerah: the dealings of the Prophet ﷺ with the Jewish tribes of Madinah. It is made heavier by the age we live in, by the charge of anti-Semitism, by the ugly phrase of a mini-Holocaust that gets reached for whenever any group of Jews is harmed, so that every such incident, even a purely political one, is chained to a catastrophe none of us defends. Before he tells the first of these stories, the Sheikh sets terms that will govern all of them, and they are worth memorizing. We are not allowed to sugarcoat, and we are not allowed to invent false defenses. He is no apologist; he will not bend the truth to shield the faith. Honesty is the best policy, and it is not his job to defend Allah's decree, only to tell it exactly as it is and to let the sources speak for themselves.

But honesty cuts both ways, and the standing accusation, that the Prophet ﷺ was somehow anti-Jewish, collapses the instant you widen the lens. The claim is so shallow, the Sheikh says, that no serious person can hold it. For century after century, large communities of Jews lived in peace across the Muslim world, in Iraq, Tunisia, Morocco, Yemen, and every time they were persecuted and expelled from Christian lands they found safe refuge in Muslim ones. When Spain expelled them, they crossed by the shipload to Morocco, and the Sultan sent his own vessels: come, we want you. The greatest minds of Judaism flourished under Islam. Maimonides, the single greatest mind in the history of Jewish thought, was raised in the universities of Muslim Andalusia, wrote his masterworks of Jewish law in Arabic so that they later had to be translated into Hebrew, and served as the personal physician of Salahuddin; one recent study even argues that for a period he lived outwardly as a Muslim. And the favorite modern label does not even attach, because the Arabs are themselves Semites, descendants of Ibrahim as the Jews are. An Arab may be called anti-Jewish, perhaps, but anti-Semite is a word meaningless against him.

Then the Sheikh hands over the key he will use for every hard chapter that follows, and asks us to hold it firmly. The Prophet ﷺ never treated these tribes harshly because of who they were. It was always, only ever, because of what they did. A criminal who happens to be a Jew, or a Muslim, or a Christian, punished for his crime, is punished for the crime and not the creed, and the difference is enormous. Yes, harder chapters are coming, he warns plainly, and there is indeed a tribe that will eventually be put to the sword, but the principle does not change, and a heart that wants the glass half empty will always find it half empty without telling an outright lie. Even the Prophet ﷺ, judging the simplest complaint a wife brought against her husband, heard the husband's side before he ruled, and found that every true grievance she stated had a context that changed it. If context matters that much in a marriage dispute, the Sheikh asks, how much more in the story of an expulsion.

The market where the treaty died

Banu Qaynuqa were the largest of the three Jewish tribes of Madinah: seven hundred fighting men, perhaps two or three thousand souls in all, goldsmiths by trade, owners of the biggest market in the city. They were signatories, too, of the treaty of Madinah, by which each community ran its own affairs and all of them stood together against any enemy from outside. The sources preserve very little of what went wrong; Ibn Ishaq gives the whole affair less than a page. The Sheikh offers a humane explanation that has nothing to do with concealment: it is simply human nature, the way a family writing the history of a beloved grandfather lingers on his triumphs and passes over his hard years in a single sentence. There was no impulse among the early Muslims to preserve the gruesome details. But two scenes survive, and they are enough.

The first is public. After Badr the Qaynuqa made no secret of their grief at the Muslims' victory, and their hostility kept rising, until the Prophet ﷺ went himself to their market, gathered them, and reminded them of the treaty. One of their leaders stood up and answered him to his face. Do not be deceived, Muhammad, by your victory. You fought a people who knew nothing of war, a pack of nobodies. Had you fought men like us, you would have learned what fighting really is. That is not a rumor whispered in the dark; it is a declaration, delivered in the open by one of their own chiefs, three and a half weeks after Badr.

The second scene is the spark, and it is crude and ugly. A woman of the Ansar came to their market to sell her goods, and having sold them she sat at a goldsmith's stall to buy some gold. The goldsmith began pressing her to uncover herself, and she refused. So he made a sign to a man behind her, who pinned her garment in such a way that when she rose to her feet it was torn away and she was left exposed, while the men around her laughed. She screamed for help. A Muslim leapt up and struck the head from the goldsmith with his sword, and the crowd closed in and killed the Muslim. By the time the news reached the Prophet ﷺ, a believing woman had been publicly humiliated and a believing man lay dead.

What he ﷺ did next is itself a lesson. There was no night raid and no ambush. He sent the Qaynuqa a formal message: the pact between us is ended. There is no longer a treaty. For this is the law of it in Islam, that you never break a treaty by surprise; you announce to the other side that the agreement is finished before you act. And the very ayah that governs this exact situation had come down only weeks before, in the surah of Badr:

If you [have reason to] fear from a people betrayal, throw [their treaty] back to them, [putting you] on equal terms. Indeed, Allāh does not like traitors.Qur'an 8:58

In Islam, the Sheikh says, a treaty may die, but it dies in daylight, declared openly to the other side, on equal terms. Treachery is never to be answered with treachery.

The siege and the grip on his armor

The Qaynuqa did not expect him to act at all. Call it arrogance, the Sheikh says, or confidence in their numbers, or trust in their fortresses, those thick-walled strongholds the Jewish tribes of the region had mastered the art of building in a way the Arabs never adopted. When the Prophet ﷺ marched, they simply ran inside and locked themselves up. The Muslims had no siege engines, none would appear in the whole story until the very end, at Tabuk, so they did the one thing open to them: they surrounded the fortresses and cut off the supplies. Water does not last forever inside a fort. After half a month the Qaynuqa surrendered, and the Prophet ﷺ ordered the men bound like prisoners of war while their fate was decided.

From the old days of Yathrib the Qaynuqa had two chief allies among the Khazraj, and what those two men did next is the true heart of this episode. The first was Ubadah ibn as-Samit, radiyallahu anhu, and he went straight to the Prophet ﷺ, not to the prisoners, and resigned his old alliance on the spot. O Messenger of Allah, I am finished with them. My loyalty is to Allah and His Messenger and the believers, and I cut my ties to these people. The second ally was Abdullah ibn Ubayy, the man history would know as the head of the hypocrites, and he did the opposite. He marched first to the prisoners and ordered the guards to untie them, and the companion in charge told him that if he so much as touched the ropes he would kill him. So he went instead to the Prophet ﷺ. Muhammad, he said, for he would almost never bring himself to say O Messenger of Allah, be generous with my allies. The Prophet ﷺ said nothing. He said it again. Silence, and the Prophet ﷺ began to turn away. And then Abdullah ibn Ubayy did the thing no companion in the world would ever have dreamed of: he thrust his hand into the collar of the Prophet's ﷺ armor and gripped it tight.

Let go of me, the Prophet ﷺ said, and those present could see the anger rising in his face. He repeated his demand and held on tighter. Woe to you, the Prophet ﷺ said the third time, let go of me. And ibn Ubayy swore he would not. By Allah, I will not release you until you promise to treat my allies well. Seven hundred men, three hundred in mail and four hundred without, who protected me from the white and the black of mankind, and you would mow them down in a single day? By Allah, I fear the turning of fortune. Listen closely to that last sentence, because it is a confession of what was, and was not, in the man's heart. Not one word about Allah, not one word about truth or justice. Only old debts, old protectors, and the superstition of bad luck.

Finally the Prophet ﷺ said: they are yours. Their lives would be spared. And here Dr. Yasir Qadhi is carefully, deliberately honest about the limit of what we can know. The Prophet ﷺ had never actually announced that he would execute these men; he had only kept silent. We cannot say whether he meant to spare them all along, or conceded it in that moment to be rid of an unbearable man, or changed his mind there and then. Allah knows, and we do not. But the wisdom of the outcome we can see plainly. Abdullah ibn Ubayy was still the senior-most chief of old Madinah, with crowds who looked up to him even among the Muslims; it was barely a year and three months since the hijrah, the hypocrisy was not yet nakedly shown, and there was still hope for the man himself. To grant him this publicly cost little and prevented much. And notice what did not happen: a hand had gripped the Prophet's ﷺ armor, a voice had defied him, and no punishment of any kind ever followed. Aisha, radiyallahu anha, testified of him ﷺ that he never once took revenge for himself; if he ever punished, it was for the sake of Allah alone.

The verdict came down: three days to load what they could carry and leave, and whatever they left behind would belong to the Muslims. They begged for more time and were refused. They appealed, of all people, to Ubadah, and his answer showed how far the ground had shifted: do not come to me, for I would not have given you even the three days. It was Ubadah who saw them out. They left with their gold but little else, abandoning their houses and the bulk of their possessions, all of which came to the Muslims. A watching heart with a disease in it could spin that ending however it pleased. The plain reading is far simpler: they mocked, they threatened openly, and they broke the treaty in the most delicate season the young city would ever pass through. If your hatred runs that loud and that dangerous, you go and live somewhere else.

The ayat that drew the line

At this Allah revealed a passage of Surah al-Ma'idah, verses fifty-one through fifty-six, that reads almost like a transcript of the week just past. And because the first of those verses is among the most weaponized in the entire Qur'an, the Sheikh slows down over a single word:

O you who have believed, do not take the Jews and the Christians as allies. They are [in fact] allies of one another. And whoever is an ally to them among you - then indeed, he is [one] of them. Indeed, Allāh guides not the wrongdoing people.Qur'an 5:51

Awliya does not mean friends, the Sheikh insists, whatever the old careless translations say, and the islamophobes have built careers on that one mistranslation, pointing at it to claim the Qur'an forbids a Muslim ever to befriend a Jew or a Christian. It does no such thing. A wali is a protector, a patron, the side you run to and place your loyalty with when the lines have been drawn. And this verse came down in the precise week when one man ran to a tribe that had just broken its treaty and humiliated a believing woman, and another ran to Allah and His Messenger. In a moment like that, the ayah says, you do not hand your ultimate loyalty to the people standing against Allah and His Messenger, and whoever does has joined them.

Then the passage does something that should raise the hair on your arms: it quotes the conversation back from above the seven heavens.

So you see those in whose hearts is disease [i.e., hypocrisy] hastening into [association with] them, saying, "We are afraid a misfortune may strike us." But perhaps Allāh will bring conquest or a decision from Him, and they will become, over what they have been concealing within themselves, regretful.Qur'an 5:52

That is Abdullah ibn Ubayy's own sentence, the fear of a turning fortune he confessed aloud while his fist was knotted in the Prophet's ﷺ armor, lifted out of his mouth and set down in the Book. And against him, the declaration of Ubadah is taken up, praised, and made the creed of every believer who would come after:

Your ally is none but Allāh and [therefore] His Messenger and those who have believed - those who establish prayer and give zakāh, and they bow [in worship].Qur'an 5:55

And the passage closes with a promise about which side, in the end, holds the field:

And whoever is an ally of Allāh and His Messenger and those who have believed - indeed, the party of Allāh - they will be the predominant.Qur'an 5:56

This is the real subject of these in-between weeks, and it is why the two halves of this episode belong to one chapter. Badr split the world in two, and now every heart in Madinah is being forced to declare which half it stands on. The true colors of the hypocrites were beginning to show, the Sheikh notes, even if they would not be fully unmasked until they turned back from the march at Uhud a few weeks later. The lines were being drawn, and a believer in such a moment must choose the side of Allah and His Messenger.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read these weeks as two unconnected anecdotes, a thrilling foiled assassination and then a grim political expulsion, and to file them away as history. That would be to walk past the question they put to us. Strip both stories down and they turn out to be the same story told from two ends, and the question at the center of it will reach every one of us before long.

Look first at what mercy is capable of. A man crossed a desert with poison on his blade and his own son's life forfeit, carrying as much hatred as a human heart can hold, and he was met not with a drawn sword but with a better greeting and the plain truth, and a lifetime of enmity collapsed in five seconds. The Prophet ﷺ did not lecture him about his crimes; he handed back his captive son for free and told the companions to teach the man his religion. That is the rule the Sheikh asks us to carry into our hardest conversations: gentleness wins what harshness cannot. And underneath it lies a humbler thought, that some of the loudest voices set against this faith, then and now, sincerely believe what they are saying, brainwashed even as they brainwash others. We are quick to write a person off as a settled enemy. Allah flipped this one in the time it takes to draw a breath, and stood ready to do the same for Safwan six years on.

Look next at how a believer keeps his word, even toward those who have broken theirs. The Qaynuqa shattered the treaty, and they were not ambushed in the dark for it; the pact was declared dead to their faces, in daylight, on equal terms, exactly as the ayah of al-Anfal commands. A believer's word holds even when the other side's does not, because the standard was never the other side's behavior; it was Allah's command. Carry that into your own contracts and given words. And carry, too, the key the Sheikh pressed into our hands for every hard chapter ahead: the Prophet ﷺ never moved against anyone for who they were, only ever for what they did. A heart that wants a half-empty glass will always find one, even in the soundest report; refuse to be that heart.

And then there is the choice at the very center of it all, the one the Qur'an itself froze in place forever. Two men, the same crisis, two sentences. One pushed his hand into the Prophet's ﷺ armor and bargained for his old allies out of fear for his luck and his business, and Allah recorded his words from above the heavens as the speech of a diseased heart. The other gave up every old alliance in one clean sentence, my loyalty is to Allah, His Messenger, and the believers, and Allah made that sentence the creed of everyone who would ever come after. The same question will reach you, in smaller print, on some ordinary day, in some room where standing with Allah and His Messenger ﷺ carries a quiet cost: a friendship, a contract, a reputation, a seat at a comfortable table. The ayat are already written, waiting to see which sentence you will choose, before a hard day chooses it for you. So meet the people who oppose you with the truth and with gentleness, and leave their guidance to Allah, who guided an assassin. Keep your word in daylight even with those who break theirs. And when the lines are drawn in your own small life, say the sentence the whole religion turned on: my loyalty is to Allah, to His Messenger ﷺ, and to the believers, for the party of Allah, in the end, are the ones who prevail. May Allah make our loyalty His, keep our word unbroken even toward those who break theirs, guide the ones who oppose this faith in sincere error as He guided Umayr, and write each of us, when our own day of choosing comes, among the party of Allah. And may He send His peace and His blessings upon His Messenger Muhammad ﷺ, his family, and his companions.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 43: events between Badr and Uhud (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (8:58, 5:51, 5:52, 5:55, 5:56), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories preserve only fragments, the Sheikh's own weighing of the sources has been followed.

The Seerah · 44 of 104

Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf

A man at war, by his bride's own word

Between Badr and Uhud the seerah does not catch its breath. The great victory has barely cooled before the alarms begin again, one after another, smaller fires lit around a city that has just learned how to win. Raiders circle Madinah to avenge a strangled trade route. Abu Sufyan swears an oath he cannot wash off. A caravan worth a fortune slips out of Makkah along a road no one has ever ridden. And inside the walls, a celebrated poet, rich and handsome and bound by the city's own treaty, decides that treaty ink means nothing at all. Day forty four carries every piece of it, and then arrives at the story Dr. Yasir Qadhi opens by calling one of the most controversial in the entire seerah: the killing of Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf.

Pay attention and take good notes, he tells his students that night, because this one will be quoted at you. Then he models the only honest way to hold it: no denial, no sugarcoating, no apology. It is in Sahih al-Bukhari. It happened. And understood in its own time and its own place, it was the act of a head of state protecting a small city at war for its life. So we will walk it slowly, the small fires first and then the hard story, so that you know exactly what happened, why it happened, and what to say when someone sets it before you as a charge.

Small fires after Badr

Seven days. That is all the rest Madinah was given after Badr before the next alarm. Subsections of two of the largest tribes in Arabia, neighbors whose income had bled away when the Makkan caravans were forced off their old road, gathered some two hundred men to strike the city. Their living had depended on that trade, the way a small town today clamors to have the interstate routed through it, for the money moves when the road moves. With the route diverted their livelihood had dried up, and they meant to poke the Muslims hard enough to make sure it never happened again.

The Prophet ﷺ did not wait to be raided. He marched out to meet them, and when the raiders saw the believers coming over the ground toward them they turned and fled without striking a blow, though they outnumbered the Muslims, leaving tents and herds and baggage where they had pitched them. Every man who marched came home the richer for it, two camels apiece. And the Prophet ﷺ camped on the captured ground for three days, exactly as he had done at Badr, for that was his way.

Two small treasures came out of that bloodless march. The first was a man. An Abyssinian slave named Yasar was taken in the first sweep, and during the days he spent among the believers he watched them closely, saw how they lived with one another, and accepted Islam. The companion who had captured him brought him to the Prophet ﷺ and said, he is yours, Messenger of Allah, for he is a Muslim now. And the Prophet ﷺ did with him what he did with every male slave who ever came into his hand: he set him free. Yasar stayed on anyway, a free man serving by choice the one ﷺ who had freed him. The second treasure was a sentence. Asked why armies kept dissolving at the mere sight of the Muslims, the Prophet ﷺ said that Allah had aided him with awe, so that his enemy is struck with terror of him at a month's journey away. Empires would learn the weight of that sentence in time; the tribes around Madinah were learning it now.

The battle of porridge

Abu Sufyan had sworn an oath of his own after Badr. He would not bathe, not even the purifying bath that the pagans had kept as an inheritance from the religion of Ibrahim, until he had taken his revenge for the dead. Months passed and the water never touched him. At last, unable to bear the weight of his own vow any longer, he gathered around two hundred riders and slipped toward Madinah, and along the way he was sheltered, fed, watered, and resupplied by the Banu Nadir, the second of the Jewish tribes of the city.

Hold that detail, because the seerah will come back for it. The treaty of Madinah bound the Banu Nadir to two clear things: never to aid Quraysh against the Muslims, and to stand as one body with the city if it were attacked from outside. To provision the very man marching against Madinah was no gray area. It was blatant treachery, and it sat on the record waiting to be answered.

The raid itself was small and ugly. With perhaps twenty men Abu Sufyan fell on one of Madinah's date orchards, terrified the women and children there, killed two of the Ansar, set fire to the grove, and ran. The Muslims gave chase, and as the raiders fled they began cutting their saddlebags loose to lighten their camels and gain speed. The bags were full of sawiq, the dried travel food of the Arabs: barley ground and mixed with milk and butter and honey, then dried into packets a man could live on for a long journey. The pursuers came home with a great heap of porridge and no Abu Sufyan, which is why the books, with a perfectly straight face, call this the battle of sawiq, the battle of porridge. It does not sound like much, and it was not. But the vow behind it tells you the temperature in Makkah: a man with a token revenge in hand and a real war already counting down inside him.

A caravan with no road left

By the third year, Quraysh had a problem that no sword could cut through. The annual caravan to Syria was due, and at their council Safwan ibn Umayyah, put in charge of it that year, laid the difficulty out plainly. Muhammad ﷺ and his companions, he said, have blocked our passages, and if we creep along the coast road most of the tribes there have already given him their allegiance and entered his religion. Dr. Yasir Qadhi stops the whole story here, because a sentence like that is worth more than a chapter. The sira books were written in an age that recorded wars, and the Madinan years come down to us as nine parts battle. Yet here, from a single line in the enemy's own mouth, you learn what the chronicles so rarely pause to say: all this while, with no army anywhere near them, Islam was spreading. Tribe after coastal tribe was entering it through dawah, through preaching, through trade, through the simple force of how the Muslims carried themselves. The battles got written down. The quiet conquest of hearts you have to overhear.

The coast was closed to them, then, and the western route by the Yanbu plain was no safer. So one of the elders proposed the only thing left: send the caravan the long way, a great semicircle east of Madinah, up toward the Iraq road, then doubling back northwest to Syria, a route Quraysh had never once used. It was a measure of how desperate they had become, and of how thoroughly Badr had tightened around them. They hired a guide who knew the strange road, loaded the camels with goods and silver, and set out, telling almost no one.

The secret died in a cup. One of the small inner circle who knew the plan was drinking in Makkah, in the days before wine was forbidden, and in his drunkenness he boasted to his companion of a scheme so clever no one could outsmart it, and named the route aloud. His companion happened to be one of the few Muslims still living quietly in Makkah, his Islam concealed from everyone, and within days the word had reached Madinah. The Prophet ﷺ sent Zayd ibn Harithah, radiyallahu anhu, at the head of an expedition (the books call such a mission a sariyya, one sent by him ﷺ but not ridden with him, and they number them in the hundreds). The whole caravan fell into Muslim hands: a hundred camels, more than fifty thousand dirhams of silver besides the trade goods, a fortune by any reckoning, and not a drop of blood spilled on either side.

Now lay one verse beside that morning. At Badr, Allah had promised the believers one of the two parties, the laden caravan or the armed host, and they had wished for the easy prize and been given the hard one.

[Remember, O believers], when Allāh promised you one of the two groups - that it would be yours - and you wished that the unarmed one would be yours. But Allāh intended to establish the truth by His words and to eliminate the disbelieversQur'an 8:7

A year later the caravan came too. He had promised them one of the two, and out of His generosity He gave them both, the army at Badr and now the caravan without a fight. As for Quraysh, every road was now cut: the coast, the west, even the trackless wilderness. The desperation that boiled up out of that strangling is what would soon march them to Uhud.

The man in the fortress

Now the hard story, and it begins with a pedigree. Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf was born of a union of two worlds. His father was a pure Arab and a pagan, a man of a distant tribe who had killed someone among his own people and fled the blood debt, and the Banu Nadir took him in. In time they gave him one of their women in marriage, and from that marriage came Ka'b: a full Arab through his father, and a full member of the Banu Nadir through his mother, for among them lineage in religion ran through the mother. He inherited everything from both sides, the noble Arab line of his father and the standing of his mother's tribe, who counted him among their chiefs, their ashraf. He was rich, a moneylender with his own fortress on the edge of the city, and, the books agree, one of the most handsome men of all Yathrib, for when two peoples mix you often get the best of both. And he was a poet, which in that age meant something we have no exact word for: a gifted poet was a broadcasting station, a voice that carried, a maker of public opinion across the whole of Arabia.

His hostility to Islam showed itself within months of the hijrah, while the religion was still finding its feet in the new city. When the qiblah was turned from Jerusalem toward Makkah, Ka'b was among the scoffers: what kind of religion changes the very direction it prays toward? The Qur'an answered that scoffing in the verse that opens its second juz, naming the kind of voice that says exactly such a thing.

The foolish among the people will say, "What has turned them away from their qiblah, which they used to face?" Say, "To Allāh belongs the east and the west. He guides whom He wills to a straight path."Qur'an 2:142

When charity was commanded, Ka'b went to his old friends among the Ansar who had embraced Islam and worked to talk them out of it. Do not give your wealth away, he told them, you may yet become poor, and who knows how this man's affair will end. The Qur'an described that counsel too, the man who is stingy and presses stinginess on everyone around him.

Who are stingy and enjoin upon [other] people stinginess and conceal what Allāh has given them of His bounty - and We have prepared for the disbelievers a humiliating punishment -Qur'an 4:37

Verse after verse came down around the words of this one man, while he sat in his fortress composing. Rich, and never generous, he hoarded his own wealth and worked to make others hoard theirs, and the Book itself kept turning to answer him.

He pronounced his own verdict

Then came Badr. When the two criers rode into Madinah announcing the dead of Quraysh, name after noble name, Ka'b heard them out and said the sentence that would follow him to his grave. If Muhammad has truly killed these men, the very nobles of the Arabs, then it is better to be inside the earth than on top of it. Hear what he is saying. Better dead than alive. He had pronounced sentence on himself, and he would be given precisely what he asked for.

What he did next put his meaning beyond any doubt. Once the victory of Badr was undeniable, Ka'b rode to Makkah with a band of the Banu Nadir, sat with Abu Sufyan, and the two of them struck a secret pact against the Prophet ﷺ. No book of sira records its terms, for it was a thing between the two of them alone. But ask honestly what a secret pact between two enemies of the same city can possibly be, except coordination for war: a time, a place, a plan. Common sense fills in what the record withholds. And before they parted, Abu Sufyan put a question to him, swearing by Allah as he asked it: which of the two religions is closer to Allah and more beloved to Him, ours or Muhammad's? Sit with the strangeness of the moment. A monotheist by inheritance was being asked by a worshipper of idols to rank idolatry above the worship of the one God. The pagans of Makkah had long felt themselves the lesser men beside the Jews of Madinah, who could read and write and carried a Book, and Abu Sufyan was reaching for reassurance from the very people he felt below. And Ka'b gave it to him. You, he said, the idolaters, are more rightly guided than they are. Allah quoted that private conversation into the Qur'an, where it sits to this day.

Have you not seen those who were given a portion of the Scripture, who believe in jibt [superstition] and ṭāghūt [false objects of worship] and say about the disbelievers, "These are better guided than the believers as to the way"?Qur'an 4:51

And Allah told His Prophet ﷺ of the pact itself. Then Ka'b returned to Madinah, and there came the last straw. He turned his celebrated gift to filth, composing lewd verses about believing women, naming them one by one, crafted to shame them and to drag the imaginations of men toward them. You can find the verses; they are as crude as that sounds, and worse. Even by the loose standards of that age it was past every line a man could cross, and he knew exactly whose city he was doing it in.

One note on the timing, because the Sheikh weighs it in the open rather than smoothing it away. Ibn Ishaq, the earliest authority, places Ka'b's end here, between Badr and Uhud in the third year, and carries an exact date with it. A later master, al-Bayhaqi, whose work on the seerah runs to some twelve volumes, places it instead after Uhud, on the eve of the march against the Banu Nadir, and adds a charge the earlier sources cannot carry: that the idea of inviting the Prophet ﷺ to a poisoned meal had been Ka'b's own. Dr. Yasir Qadhi sides with Ibn Ishaq as the stronger and more authoritative position, while leaving the other honestly on the table, and he offers a thought of his own, flagged plainly as a theory we can never confirm. The books say the Banu Nadir provisioned Abu Sufyan before the porridge raid, but never name who among them arranged it; it would join one and one into two if that host had been Ka'b himself, a few weeks before he rode to Abu Sufyan in person. Either way, the case against the man stands complete on its own.

Say what you must

So the Prophet ﷺ stood before his companions and asked, who will deal with Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf, for he has hurt Allah and His Messenger? Allah is beyond all harm; the word he ﷺ used carries the sense that Ka'b had crossed from mere disbelief into open aggression against Allah and His Messenger. A man of the Aws rose: I will, O Messenger of Allah. His name was Muhammad ibn Maslamah, radiyallahu anhu, and even his tribe was part of the wisdom of the night. In the days of jahiliyyah the Banu Nadir had been the allies of the Aws; had a hand from the Khazraj done this deed, the old buried civil war between the two Ansari tribes might have stirred again in its grave. From the hand of the Nadir's own former allies, no such feud could catch fire.

Then comes the detail that turns a tense story tender. For three days afterward Muhammad ibn Maslamah could not eat or drink. When word reached the Prophet ﷺ and he asked the man why, he answered, O Messenger of Allah, I gave you a promise, and now I do not know whether I can fulfill it. Ka'b was a rich and guarded man in his own fortress with his own entourage, and the weight of the word he had given was crushing him. The Prophet ﷺ untied the knot with a single sentence: all that is upon you is to try; success belongs to Allah. Keep that sentence somewhere you can reach it quickly, for the rest of your life. It calmed him at once. Then Muhammad ibn Maslamah asked leave to say things he did not mean, the small deceptions the ruse would require, and the Prophet ﷺ said, say as you like.

The ruse was built entirely from Ka'b's own appetites. Muhammad ibn Maslamah called on him in private, the way a borrower calls on a moneylender, and complained, just bitterly enough to ring true. This man, he said, meaning the Prophet ﷺ, has brought us nothing but hardship these few years, the Arabs have all drawn up against us as one, and now on top of everything he asks us for our money. Ka'b lit up; here was a Muslim who sounded exactly like himself. This is only the beginning, he promised, just you wait, he will put you through far worse. Then came the loan, and with it the haggling over collateral that every man in Madinah knew was Ka'b's routine. Pledge your women, said Ka'b. By Allah, came the answer, you are the most handsome of men, charming as Yusuf; shall we trust our women near you? Then your sons, said Ka'b. So they grow up taunted in the streets, came the answer, the boys once mortgaged for a sack of barley? Not that either. Then what is left, said the borrower, but our weapons. And the moneylender agreed gladly, because in a time of war weapons are excellent collateral and there was profit waiting for him. See the quiet genius of it, the Sheikh says. Now armed men could walk up to Ka'b's fortress in the dark and raise no alarm at all, because the weapons were the whole pretext. Abu Na'ilah, Ka'b's own foster brother, nursed at the same breast, and two or three others arranged loans on the same terms, so the visit would look like ordinary business. A time was set after dark, and Ka'b, who wanted no witnesses either, agreed; secrecy suited them all.

A full moon, and a man at war
