The fourteenth night of the month, in the third year of the hijrah. The books of sira remember the very sky: a full moon, and not a cloud near it. The Prophet ﷺ walked the small party to the edge of the city and sent them off with his blessing, may Allah help you in your mission.

At the fortress they called up to him to come down. Ka'b had married a new wife not long before and was lying with her when the voices reached him, and as he rose to go she caught his arm. Where are you going at this hour, she asked, and what honest business comes calling in the middle of the night? Then she said the sentence this whole episode turns upon, a sentence we will need again before the night is out: you are a man at war, and I am worried for you. Hold her words. She was no soldier and no party to any of it, and still she understood what her husband's pacts and his poetry had made of him. He waved her off. It is only Muhammad ibn Maslamah, he said, whom I have known since before Islam, and Abu Na'ilah, my foster brother. He pulled free of her, came down to them perfumed, and armored even then, because a man at war dresses like one even to receive friends.

They talked a while in the moonlight, the easy gossip of old Yathrib, and then one of the party leaned toward him. What is this scent on you, he asked, the sweetest perfume I have smelled? Ka'b smiled. I have with me, he said, the most perfumed woman in all of Arabia. Let me smell it, said his guest, and then, drawing closer, it is in your hair, let me smell your head. Ka'b bent his head toward him, and the hand that closed on his hair did not let go. The others struck. It was hard work, for his armor turned the blades, and in the dark one of the companions was badly gashed by his own friends' swords and had to limp home bleeding. The Prophet ﷺ met the party on their return and touched his blessed saliva to the wound, and the books record that it healed.

By morning the plotters of the city understood what had happened, and what it meant. Ibn Ishaq writes that after that night there was not a Jewish tribe in Madinah that felt safe in its treachery; some men did not leave their fortresses for days. Complaints were carried to the Prophet ﷺ, and he did not entertain them, because the order had been his own. But look closely at what was done and at what was not. No tribe was attacked. No quarter was burned. No man was harmed for the faith he held, and Madinah went on housing non-Muslims under its treaty exactly as before. One man, the chief instigator himself, answered for his own treason, and the precision of it was the whole message: the treaty is real, betraying it has a price, and the price falls on the one who earned it.

What this day asks of our faith

Now the framing, because this episode sits near the very top of every hostile pile, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi gives the last stretch of the night to teaching you how to carry it without flinching and without lying. Three things, then one beneath them.

First, it is authentic. Sahih al-Bukhari gives the killing of Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf an entire chapter of its own; Sahih Muslim narrates it; no book of sira omits it. Some Muslims have a way of deleting whatever embarrasses them, and it is not the way of honesty. The one who erases this report has not defended the seerah, he has denied the very sources it stands on, for the same books that carry this carry everything else we hold dear. It happened, the Sheikh says plainly. There is no need to sugarcoat it. It is what it is.

Second, the why. Ka'b was not killed for his disbelief. Nowhere in the entire seerah is a single soul harmed simply for being a kafir, and Madinah itself was full of non-Muslims living peaceably under its treaty, before this night and after it. Nor was he killed for his mockery alone, stinging as it was. He was killed for what any age that has ever known war would name treason: a man bound by his city's own pact who traveled to the enemy's capital and struck a secret military alliance there, who incited the avengers of Badr toward a second war, and who then assaulted the honor of believing women in published verse, by name. There is a line, and the line is not belief. His own bride had already given the true description of him before any blade was drawn. A man at war.

Third, the manner, and here the Sheikh refuses to be apologetic at all. The modern objection is rarely to Ka'b's guilt; it is to the missing courtroom, the absence of judge and jury and trial. But you cannot lower a modern legal system onto a seventh century city-state three years old and fighting for its survival. Madinah had no jails; prisons enter Islamic history generations later. The Prophet ﷺ was, in his own single person, the political, legal, and religious authority of the state, ruler and judge at once, so that his considered order was a verdict, not a vigilante's whim. And the proof that this was authority and not vengeance is Makkah itself: thirteen years of abuse far worse than anything Ka'b did, and nothing of the kind was ever done there, because in Makkah the Prophet ﷺ held no sulta, no executive power. Every young revolution in history, the Sheikh reminds his students, acted before its institutions were built; you cannot judge the third year of Madinah by the laws of an order it had not yet finished raising. And the critic of our own day has a beam in his own eye. Governments that have legalized targeted killings by drone, that have killed even their own teenage citizens with no trial and no jail and called it lawful policy, have surrendered any standing to gasp at this story. You cannot have your cake and eat it too.

Beneath the law sits something warmer, an honor the Arabs name with a word that English does not own: the protective jealousy, the ghirah, that rises in a believing community for those who cannot defend themselves, for the women a poet had dragged into the public square for sport. Whether any later state should imitate the method is a question for states and their scholars, the Sheikh adds, and it binds no one today. What is asked of you is not imitation. It is only honesty. Told whole and told straight, this is not an embarrassment to be explained away in a whisper. It is the portrait of a three year old city of believers, ringed by armies and salted through with traitors, surviving because its Prophet ﷺ was exactly what that hour demanded: merciful wherever mercy could live, and unflinching where treachery threatened every soul under his care.

So take this day down into your own. When a duty frightens you, when you have given your word and lie awake unable to eat for fear you cannot keep it, remember the sentence that loosened the knot in Muhammad ibn Maslamah: all that is upon you is to try, and success belongs to Allah. When this story is set before you as a charge, do not blush and do not deny; know where the line truly is, that it was never belief but treason in a time of war, and say so without apology. And learn the harder, quieter lesson the whole night teaches, that loving the Prophet ﷺ means loving him as he actually was, mercy to the worlds and yet unbending before betrayal, refusing the cheap comfort of a version of him sanded down to please his critics. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, make us truthful about him and unashamed of him, guard this ummah from the betrayal within and the slander without, shield the honor of our women, and let us rise to every frightening duty knowing that the trying is ours and the outcome is His.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 44: the assassination of Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (8:7, 2:142, 4:37, 4:51), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 45 of 104

A summary of the Makkan period

Thirteen years, walked again in one evening

Forty-four days into this walk, the caravan stops and turns around. Badr is behind us, the first open battle won against every expectation, and Uhud waits over the next rise with loss folded into it. When Dr. Yasir Qadhi reached this point in his telling it was the first lesson of a new year, January of 2013, the students back from a break, and instead of pressing forward into the next battle he gave them a gift. He took the whole Makkan period, every year of it, and walked it again in a single sitting, from the days of jahiliyyah to the streets of Quba flooded with joy.

A summary is not a repeat. At walking pace, day after day, you see events: a birth, a marriage, a revelation, a boycott, a flight. From this one hilltop you see what the daily pace hides, which is design. You watch centuries of preparation, a guarded lineage, a neutral land wedged between two tired empires, even the private wars of pagans, all of it bending quietly toward one man ﷺ and one message. So let the road rest a moment, and look back at the whole of it at once. Thirteen years, gathered into one telling.

The stage, centuries in the building

Begin long before the birth, because the Sheikh begins there, and the lesson is in how early Allah starts. Arabia then held three kinds of Arabs: the ancient, extinct tribes, gone without descendants or much history; and two surviving families, Qahtan and Adnan, both tracing back to Sam the son of Nuh. Adnan descends from Ismail himself, the infant once left in a barren valley with his mother Hajar, raised beside the well that saved them, married into Jurhum, the tribe that held Makkah for centuries. Hold onto that one fact: the city at the dead center of this whole story grew up around the household of the Prophet's ﷺ own forefather. Nothing about Makkah was accidental.

Jurhum grew greedy and unjust, taking advantage of the pilgrims, until the tribe of Khuzaah drove them out; on their way into exile they buried the treasures of the sanctuary and let the well of Zamzam be lost with them. Then Khuzaah, now in charge of the Kaaba, did something far worse than Jurhum ever had. Their first chieftain went north to Syria on business, was dazzled by its flowering civilization, and they were idol worshippers; they gifted him one of their idols, Hubal, and he carried it home and stood it before the Kaaba: the first idol in the valley of Ibrahim, roughly four centuries before the man ﷺ who would one day clear it out. He went on to rewrite the rites of Hajj and seed Makkah with the pagan customs the Qur'an would later condemn. The Prophet ﷺ said, in a report Sahih Muslim carries, that he saw that chieftain dragging his own intestines through the Fire: the first man ever to change the religion of Ibrahim.

Idolatry then did what idolatry always does: it splintered them. Tribes turned partisan to their own gods, and the most ancient idols of all, the five that the drowned people of Nuh had refused to surrender, were raised again among the Arabs and parceled out, one to a tribe, fought over alongside wealth and pride. The Qur'an names them, the very gods of Nuh's stubborn people:

And said, 'Never leave your gods and never leave Wadd or Suwa or Yaghuth and Yaʿuq and Nasr.'Qur'an 71:23

Beneath the quarrels, though, a floor was being laid. Qusayy, the great-great-great-great-grandfather of the Prophet ﷺ, married the daughter of Khuzaah's chief around 450 of the common era and brought Quraysh back into the sanctuary, some hundred and fifty years before the birth. The students are made to memorize the chain, and so should we: Muhammad ibn Abdullah, ibn Abd al-Muttalib, ibn Hashim, ibn Abd Manaf, ibn Qusayy. Hashim then tied Makkah into the wider world with his two great caravans, south to Yemen, the gateway of Africa and India, and north to Syria, the gateway of Byzantium, until the city sat wealthy and indispensable on the spine of international trade. Lift your eyes to the larger map and the design grows almost loud: two spoiled, exhausted superpowers, Byzantium and Persia, and wedged precisely between them a hard, free, unconquered people, neutral in politics and religion, never once united. Latent power, the Sheikh calls it, waiting for a word.

Then, in the lifetime of the Prophet's ﷺ own grandfather Abd al-Muttalib, came three signs in quick succession. Zamzam was rediscovered through a dream, after centuries of thirst. His son Abdullah was vowed for sacrifice and then ransomed, the way Ismail had been ransomed before him, so that the child to come would be the son of the two ransomed ones. And an army led by an elephant marched on the House and was turned back in the very year of the birth, the year the Qur'an opens a whole surah to remember:

Have you not considered, O Muhammad, how your Lord dealt with the companions of the elephant?Qur'an 105:1

The Prophet ﷺ would one day trace the whole choosing himself, and the line is worth carrying: Allah chose Kinanah from the children of Ismail, and Quraysh from Kinanah, and Banu Hashim from Quraysh, and chose him ﷺ from Banu Hashim. In a society that worshipped pedigree above nearly everything, that lineage became a shield no enemy dared pierce. To harm him was to bring the deepest shame upon themselves, for he was their prize, the noblest of them. Their own jahiliyyah logic, the Sheikh notes, turned by Allah into Islam's protection.

The orphan Makkah came to trust

He ﷺ was born in the Year of the Elephant. On the exact date there are thirteen recorded opinions, and if any is nearest the truth it is probably the eighth of Rabi al-Awwal. His parents had been together barely a week, some say only three days, before Abdullah left on a journey and never came home; Aminah bore him already a widow. When she gave birth she saw a light go out from her that lit up the palaces of Syria, that bastion of civilization in the age, and the first of the great lands his ummah would one day take. He ﷺ said of himself, in an authentic hadith in Sahih Muslim, that he was the supplication of his father Ibrahim and the glad tidings of Isa; the very word Injil means glad tidings, and he ﷺ is the good news that book was named for.

Like the children of Quraysh's better families, he was sent out to be nursed in the desert, among the Banu Saad: cleaner air, a harder life, purer Arabic. He was four, out with Halimah's people, when Jibril came, opened his chest, washed his heart with Zamzam, and cast away the black speck that is Shaytan's claim on a person. Then loss came in threes. His father he never knew; his mother Aminah died on a journey while he was still small; his grandfather Abd al-Muttalib followed. Hold the why the way this telling holds it, for the Sheikh refuses to leave it as mere sorrow. He was orphaned again and again so that no human being could ever claim a favor over him, and so that Allah Himself would take charge of the boy; because no child matures faster than an orphan, who stands on his own feet early and is never pampered soft; and because the one who tastes the hardest life comes out tender, not bitter, knowing in his own skin what it is to be helpless. Every quality the final Messenger ﷺ would need was being installed in him from the start.

Of his youth we know almost nothing. It is the least documented stretch of the entire seerah, two or three small scenes across some twenty years, and every one points forward. He worked as a shepherd for a few qararit, the Sheikh says, coins of the lowest sort, the most menial job the city had; and on those hillsides he learned what no palace teaches, the patience a flock demands and the truth that every sheep, like every soul, must be handled in its own way. It is the quiet apprenticeship of a leader. And at around twenty he sat as the youngest member of hilf al-fudul, a pact of the clans to stand with the wronged against the wrongdoer whoever he might be, even if the wrongdoer were of Quraysh and the wronged a man of no tribe. He ﷺ remained proud of that pact all his life.

Then came Khadijah, radiyallahu anha. The wealthiest and most sought-after woman of Quraysh, twice widowed and interested in no one, until she heard of his ﷺ shyness and honesty and quality, and quietly, indirectly, set a proposal in motion. With one marriage Allah rewrote his worldly life: yesterday a shepherd, today a merchant managing her wealth, never again pressed for money through all the Makkan years, and given, far above any wealth, the woman who would believe him before anyone else on earth. Into that house came Zayd, a slave boy received as a gift, who, when his own father arrived to ransom him home, astonished everyone by choosing the Prophet ﷺ instead. To honor a love like that, he ﷺ declared before the people, this is my son. For more than twenty years Makkah knew him simply as Zayd ibn Muhammad, until Allah revealed Surah al-Ahzab in the fifth year after the Hijrah and returned adopted sons to the names of their true fathers; Ibn Abbas said they had not even known Zayd was anything other than a son until those verses came down.

One last scene before the cave. When he ﷺ was around thirty-five, a flood damaged the Kaaba and Quraysh set out to rebuild it, and the timber arrived by way of a storm. The emperor of Rome had sent a ship loaded with fine wood to raise a grand church in Abyssinia; it wrecked at Jeddah, and the stranded merchants sold their cargo. Wood meant for the greatest church in the region went instead to the House of Ibrahim, as if heaven were announcing where worship was about to be carried. There was not quite enough of it, so for the first time in its history they shortened the Kaaba from a rectangle to the near-square it has kept ever since, leaving one curved side open, which is why a believer today can stand within that low arc, the Hijr, and pray as though inside the House. And when the clans fell to quarreling over who would set the Black Stone back, that stone from Jannah, the one surviving piece of Ibrahim's own building, and the dispute hardened toward bloodshed, it was Muhammad ﷺ they agreed to trust. He laid the stone on a cloak, let each clan lift a corner, and set it in place with his own hands. Read the sign the way the Sheikh reads it: the man returning Ibrahim's stone to its place was about to return Ibrahim's religion to its place, and the man who dissolved a civil war among Quraysh would one day unite all the Arabs.

Read, then stand and warn

Thirteen years before the Hijrah, around the year 600, he ﷺ turned forty. Every worldly need was met; no caravan called him anymore; and exactly then a restlessness settled over him, a sense that something was missing which he could not name. So he withdrew to a cave to contemplate and to worship. And there Jibril came down with the word that split history in two:

Recite in the name of your Lord who created.Qur'an 96:1

He ﷺ came home trembling, and Khadijah wrapped him and steadied him with that certainty for which she is forever remembered: what you have seen can only be true, for Allah would never disgrace you. It has been said that she believed in him before he believed in himself, the Sheikh allows, and there is an element of truth in it. Such was Khadijah.

Then came the second revelation, and the Sheikh folds the entire religion into its pairing with the first:

O you who covers himself with a garment, Arise and warnQur'an 74:1-2

Iqra is ilm, knowledge: read, learn, study. Qum fa-andhir is amal, action: now stand up and live what you have learned. And Islam, he teaches, is nothing other than the two together. Learn, then rise and act. The first two commands of revelation already carried the whole curriculum, and they carried it in that order, which is not optional.

For about three years the call stayed quiet, and notice the precise word the Sheikh insists on: not secret dawah but private dawah, carried person by person to those whose hearts seemed ready. Within the household they believed at once: Khadijah, and Zayd, and Ali, still only a boy. Outside it, the first adult man was Abu Bakr, of whom the Prophet ﷺ testified that every soul he ever invited hesitated except Abu Bakr alone, who was not so much invited as came asking and accepted without a pause. Through him followed a wave of young Quraysh, and after them the mawali and the enslaved, those with no clan to shield them: Yasir and Sumayyah and their son Ammar, Bilal, and the rest of the famous early names. And from this height the Sheikh maps the whole twenty-three years into five stages of dawah: private invitation; then ten years of open preaching by the tongue alone, the last three carried to every tribe of Arabia; then dawah joined to defensive fighting in the early Madinan years; then the open, weaponless call made possible by the peace of Hudaybiyyah; and finally, for the last year and a half after the Conquest, dawah backed by strength. None of the five abrogates another, he is careful to say; all five remain available, and the leadership of the ummah in every age must weigh which one fits its own land and time.

Out loud, and every tactic they tried

After the years of private invitation came the command to go public, and it came aimed first at his own blood:

And warn, O Muhammad, your closest kindred.Qur'an 26:214

He ﷺ gathered the relatives and told them to their faces what Makkah had so far only heard as whispers: that he was the Messenger of Allah. His uncle Abu Lahab threw his hands in the air, spat that this was the ludicrous thing for which he had been called, and cursed him. Heaven answered the curse with a curse that became scripture, recited in every generation since, while the man's own name survives only inside the verses that condemn him:

May the hands of Abu Lahab be ruined, and ruined is he.Qur'an 111:1

From that day Makkah and the message were openly at war, and the Sheikh gathers the years that follow into the tactics Quraysh tried, one after another, against a man they could not silence. First, pressure at the top. He ﷺ lived under the protection of his uncle Abu Talib, chief of Banu Hashim, so they leaned on Abu Talib, gently and then with threats. When the old man at last pleaded with his nephew to stop, the answer rang down the centuries: by Allah, if they placed the sun in my right hand and the moon in my left to make me abandon this affair, I would not abandon it until Allah makes it prevail or I perish in its cause. Abu Talib made a vow of his own, that whatever they said to him after this he would never again ask his nephew to stop, and kept it to his last breath.

So they turned to ridicule. Madman, they called him. Magician. Possessed. Abu Lahab and Abu Jahl would trail him ﷺ wherever he went and reach every listener first: this is our kinsman, he is mad, do not listen. It wounded him deeply, and the Qur'an returns again and again to console exactly that wound; mockery, the Sheikh observes, is the tax every truth-teller pays, and he ﷺ paid it daily for years.

When mockery failed, they demanded miracles, made to their own order, and the Qur'an records a dozen such demands. Send a river gushing through Makkah. Hand us gold. Bring Allah and the angels down before our eyes. Float a written book out of the sky. Allah did not answer to their menu; He gave signs of His own choosing, and the grandest split the moon itself in two over their heads:

The Hour has come near, and the moon has split in two. And if they see a sign, they turn away and say, "Passing magic."Qur'an 54:1-2

They watched the sky obey and turned away, saying he had bewitched their eyes. Proof, the Sheikh notes, was never their problem. So they offered a merger: worship our gods for a time, and we will worship yours, and meet in the middle. An entire surah came down to bolt that door forever:

Say, "O disbelievers, I do not worship what you worship. Nor are you worshippers of what I worship. Nor will I be a worshipper of what you worship. Nor will you be worshippers of what I worship. For you is your religion, and for me is my religion."Qur'an 109:1-6

When theology failed they tried appetite: we will make you our king, give you all the wealth you want, marry you to whomever you desire, everything a man can dream of, if only you will stop. He ﷺ was not for sale at any price their imaginations could reach. Their cleverest move was borrowed. The Arabs had no concept of prophecy, no notion of a man addressed directly by God, so Quraysh sent to the Jews, who did, and came back armed with examination questions. Ask him about Yusuf. So Surah Yusuf came down in answer, the full story of a prophet with whom the Arabs shared no lineage and about whom they had no books, in a city with no library, recited by a man ﷺ who could not read; and whatever else they asked, revelation answered. Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi pauses for a footnote from our own century: the academy still cannot explain where that knowledge came from, and one prevailing theory simply redates the whole Qur'an a hundred years forward, to Damascus, on the reasoning that an unlettered man in Makkah could not possibly have produced it. Too much knowledge, so they move the goalposts. He calls it ludicrous; the oldest explanation has not budged in fourteen centuries: it came from Allah.

And when every argument was exhausted, there was blood. The torture fell first and worst on those no clan would avenge, the slaves and the foreigners, and in those middle Makkan years Islam was given its first martyrs. Sumayyah was the first soul of this ummah to die for it, and her husband Yasir followed her. Bilal was tortured to the edge of death; so were many of the early believers. The Prophet ﷺ himself, fenced about by his lineage, was not seriously touched until very near the end.

A prostration heard across the sea

When the pain grew unbearable, he ﷺ gave permission for the first hijrah in the history of Islam. Some fifteen believers crossed the sea to Abyssinia, among them Uthman with his wife Ruqayyah, the Prophet's ﷺ own daughter, and Umm Salamah, who would one day be a Mother of the Believers. Barely five months later they came hurrying home, because a report had reached them across the water that all of Makkah had accepted Islam.

What actually happened that day is one of the most slandered scenes in the whole seerah, and the Sheikh's verdict on it deserves to be carried whole. Western writers call it the Satanic Verses; the older books call it the story of the gharaniq, the cranes; and the strongest opinion, the one this telling rules by, is that it simply never happened. No authentic narration carries any whispered, planted verses. What the authentic report in Bukhari preserves is only this: the Prophet ﷺ recited Surah an-Najm, one of the most powerful surahs in the Qur'an, and when its final command came down, everyone within earshot fell into prostration, Muslim and pagan, human and jinn alike:

So prostrate to Allah and worship Him.Qur'an 53:62

You do not need invented verses to explain that scene, the Sheikh says; you only need to hear an-Najm recited, and the people of Makkah were simply overcome. A prostrating Makkah, garbled across the sea, became a believing Makkah, and the homesick emigrants sailed back to find nothing changed.

But Allah had folded a plan inside the disappointment. The returning emigrants reported that Abyssinia was hard in language and culture yet free in worship, and weighing freedom of faith against mere unfamiliarity, a far larger company chose to go: nearly a hundred believers this time, a painful share of a small city, walking out in plain sight. Quraysh felt the humiliation of their own people leaving them, and sent two emissaries with gifts to buy the emigrants back; the emissaries were rebuffed and came home with their gifts. And in those same days, within weeks of each other, two of the most formidable men in Makkah embraced Islam: Hamza, and then Umar. The tide was visibly turning, and Quraysh could feel it.

So they played the cruelest card a tribal society holds: a boycott. The whole of Banu Hashim, Muslim and pagan alike, every soul but Abu Lahab, was cut off and driven out to scrape a living in the desert, for roughly two years so lean that history barely managed to record them. The boycott is hard to date, but reason it out the way the Sheikh does: to sever your own kin was the most drastic act an Arab could commit, the very last resort, which places it late, around the seventh or eighth year of the dawah, after everything else had failed.

The lowest year, the highest night

The boycott ended, and within that very month, by the account this telling carries, Abu Talib died; it is not far-fetched, the Sheikh allows, that two years of hardship had broken the old man's health. It was the tenth year of the dawah, three years before the Hijrah. Less than six weeks later, in Ramadan of that same year, Khadijah followed him. His public shield and his private comfort, gone back to back: the uncle whose standing held back the knives of the city, and the wife who had believed before all the earth believed. He ﷺ was not seen to smile for nearly a year, and the ummah still calls that season by the name his household knew it as, the Year of Sorrow.

One of the evening's quiz questions lands here, and the Sheikh offers it lightly. Of his ﷺ roughly twelve uncles, only four lived to see Islam. The two whose names honored idols, Abd al-Uzza who was Abu Lahab and Abd Manaf who was Abu Talib, refused it. The two who bore the names of lions, Hamza and Abbas, accepted it. Perhaps, he says, just a coincidence.

Abu Lahab inherited the chieftainship of Banu Hashim, and at first, in his brother's memory, kept Abu Talib's policy and shielded his nephew. Then an instigator planted the fatal question: do you know what this nephew of yours says about your own father, about Abd al-Muttalib? Pressed for an answer, the Prophet ﷺ would not lie, and chose the gentlest true words he could, that he is with his people. The point was made; Abu Lahab snapped, withdrew his protection, and overnight the Prophet's ﷺ standing in his own city went gray, among his people but no longer protected by them. That gray, dangerous status is what finally sent him to Taif.

Taif, with Zayd at his side, was meant to be a door; it became the deepest wound of his life. Years later, asked whether any day had been harder than Uhud, he ﷺ answered that the worst day of his life was the day of Taif. He came away bloodied, and on the road back, alone in the middle of nowhere, he made his famous dua; and the answers began at once. Addas, a Christian servant from Iraq, in that age among the farthest of lands, accepted Islam on the spot. That same night a company of jinn stood in the dark, listened to him reciting, and believed. It was as if Allah were telling His Messenger ﷺ: if your own city refuses you, the ends of the earth will come to you, and so will the world you cannot even see. Yet to reenter Makkah now required a guarantee. Word went quietly to chief after chief, and each excused himself, until Mutim ibn Adi said yes. A pagan to his dying day but loyal to an older code of honor, Mutim armed his seven sons, walked the Prophet ﷺ openly to the Kaaba, and declared before all of Makkah that Muhammad ﷺ was under his protection, a gauntlet thrown at the feet of Abu Jahl. He ﷺ never forgot it; after Badr he honored Mutim's memory in words that keep it green to this day, and there is even a hadith in this meaning, the Sheikh notes, that Allah may strengthen this religion through a man who is not of it.

And somewhere in that lowest season, on a night no historian can fix, came the gift. The Isra and Miraj is the single hardest event of the seerah to date, with opinions scattered from the third year of the dawah to the very last. The Sheikh reads it where the evidence leans, after Khadijah's death, and for the most tender of reasons: she appears in no report of the journey, and it bears every mark of a mercy timed for the hour it was needed most. The Year of Sorrow had stripped him of his shield and his comfort, so Allah took him ﷺ Himself: by night from Makkah to Jerusalem, where every prophet prayed behind him, and then up through the seven heavens to the presence of his Lord:

Exalted is He who took His Servant by night from al-Masjid al-Haram to al-Masjid al-Aqsa, whose surroundings We have blessed, to show him of Our signs. Indeed, He is the Hearing, the Seeing.Qur'an 17:1

From the lowest of the low to the highest of the high. It was a personal miracle, the Sheikh says, given to him personally; the rest of us are simply honored to believe in it.

A door opens in Yathrib

He ﷺ kept knocking. He went to the great tribes, the ones with the numbers and the names, the only houses on earth that could rival Quraysh, concentrating on the big names while offering the message freely to all. Every big name said no. And Allah had willed that the help would come from precisely the place no strategist would have chosen, a modest town called Yathrib, tied to him by blood, for his great-grandmother was one of them and he had visited the town as a boy with his mother and lost her on the road home. When Yathrib eventually took him in, it was taking in a relative.

Allah had been preparing that town, too, by means no one would have requested. The wars of Buath, a feud that ran a hundred years out of a quarrel over a grazing camel, had turned so savage in their final five that nearly the entire senior leadership of Yathrib was wiped away, until Abdullah ibn Ubayy was almost the only elder left standing. What remained was the young: fresh minds, sick of the old ways, hungry for something new. Aisha, radiyallahu anha, later said that Allah gifted the wars of Buath to His Messenger ﷺ. While he knocked at the front doors of the mighty, Allah was quietly unlocking a side gate.

Six men of Yathrib met him in the tenth year of the dawah and carried the message home. Twelve came back the next season and gave him the first pledge of Aqabah: to worship Allah alone and abandon the great sins. He sent Musab ibn Umayr north with them as a teacher, and the dawah spread like wildfire, until there was not a tribe in Yathrib without Muslims in it, its rising young leaders among them. By the final months, with perhaps a hundred and fifty or two hundred believers in the town, seventy of them came and gave the second pledge, and this one changed the world. Not theology alone now, but protection: if you come to us, we will defend you exactly as we defend our own families. Defensive, the Sheikh stresses, not offensive; it was Badr, later, that would extend it. The door out of Makkah now stood open.

So the believers began to leave, with difficulty and at cost. Some were stopped; some bought their freedom with promises; Suhayb famously handed Quraysh everything he owned for the right to walk out. Makkah was emptying, and Quraysh, terrified of what he ﷺ might become beyond their reach, at last agreed on the deed that thirteen years of malice had stopped short of: every clan together would strike down one of their own. On his last night in Makkah they ringed his house. Jibril came down: now is the time. Allah covered their eyes, and he ﷺ walked out through them untouched, joined Abu Bakr, who had long been quietly preparing for this very night, and vanished. Three days hidden in a cave, then the long desert road north, miracle following miracle, the story of Suraqah among them, all the way to safety.

And then Quba. Five hundred of the Ansar came out, dressed and armed, to receive him ﷺ; the men and the women climbed onto the rooftops, and the streets flooded with joy. A whole new era was in the air, the Sheikh says, the beginning of a new dawn, the seed of a new millennium, and we have been living inside it ever since. After thirteen years of refusal, Allah's yes filled an entire city in a single morning. Tomorrow, the road bends toward Uhud.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to take a summary like this as a convenience, a quick way to remember the order of events, and miss the thing it is for. Seen at walking pace, the Makkan years are a string of incidents. Seen from this hilltop, in one telling, they are a design, and the design itself is the lesson; it asks something of the way we read our own lives.

Allah prepares long before He commands. Before there was a single believer, there was Zamzam rediscovered, a lineage guarded across centuries, two empires held in stalemate, a free and unconquered people waiting in the middle, even a pagan civil war softening a town the Prophet ﷺ had not yet heard he would need. The stage was set generation by generation before the word iqra was ever spoken. We tend to read the slow, uneventful stretches of our own lives as Allah's absence, as time that does not count; the Makkan period says the opposite. The setup is longer and kinder than it ever looks from inside it, and the quiet years are not empty; they are the floor being laid.

Hardship is a curriculum, not a punishment. The Sheikh refuses to let the orphaning pass as mere grief, or the shepherding as mere poverty. The loss installed maturity; the flock taught patience and the handling of souls; the hard life left him tender rather than bitter, exactly the equipment the final Messenger ﷺ would need. Nothing was wasted. Ask of your own difficult chapters what they are installing, because the same Hand that shaped him in the desert is shaping you in yours, and the soft heart that comes out of real difficulty is one of the rarest things on earth.

And then there is the order of the first two commands, which the Sheikh says is the whole religion in miniature. Iqra came first: learn. Qum fa-andhir came second: now stand and live what you learned. Knowledge that never becomes action is half of what was asked, and action without knowledge is the other half; Islam is the two joined, and joined in that sequence. The cave and the cloak, read together, refuse to let us pick one.

Carry these into ordinary days. Trust the slow preparation of your life the way the believers came to trust the centuries before iqra. Read your hard seasons as a curriculum, especially the years that take from you what Abu Talib and Khadijah were taken in, for it was in exactly that valley that he ﷺ was carried to the highest heaven, the lowest of the low lifted to the highest of the high. And let your knowledge become action and your action rest on knowledge, learning then standing, the way revelation itself was ordered. Quraysh tried everything a city could try, pressure and mockery and miracles demanded and bribes and borrowed riddles and torture and a boycott, and all it bought them was an empty room and a guarded door; the dawn broke somewhere else, in a small town up the road. May Allah, who set the stage for His Messenger ﷺ centuries before his birth, be trusted by us with the slow preparations of our own lives, give us knowledge that becomes action and patience in our years of sorrow and His nearness in the hours we need it most, and gather us, as He filled the streets of Quba with joy after thirteen years of no, in the company of His Messenger ﷺ on the Day the believers are welcomed home.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 45: a summary of the Makkan period (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (71:23, 105:1, 96:1, 74:1-2, 26:214, 111:1, 54:1-2, 109:1-6, 53:62, 17:1), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration or more than one date, the Sheikh's own weighing has been followed.

The Seerah · 46 of 104

The Battle of Uhud, part 1

Three thousand for revenge, seven hundred for Allah

Badr did not end at Badr. It is Shawwal now, the third year of the hijrah, a year and a few weeks since the Quraysh limped home from the well in the desert, and they have spent every day of that year preparing to come back. Dr. Yasir Qadhi opens the story of Uhud the way the sources open it, and the first thing to understand is that it is not the same kind of battle as the one before it. Badr was almost an accident of timing, a reconnaissance party sent to intercept a caravan that swelled into a fight no one had quite planned. Uhud is the opposite, the first time the Quraysh arm to the hilt and march out with a single declared purpose: a war, decided upon a full year in advance. And the whole of the first day at the mountain reaches us not as a clash of swords but as a ledger, a letter, a dream, and a Friday meeting that decided where seven hundred believers would stand against three thousand. Walk it slowly, because nothing that happens on the slopes ahead will make sense without what is set in place now. This is the first of five days at the foot of one mountain.

Revenge, paid for in advance

Why are they coming? The Sheikh counts four pressures, and they push Makkah toward war all at once. First, religion: a year of revelation and a year of defeat had hardened the idolaters' dislike of Islam into a settled animosity, a hatred of this monotheism and of the man ﷺ who preached it. Second, the social debt, and at its center the oldest engine in Arabia: revenge. They had humiliated themselves at Badr and buried the tier of leaders who made Quraysh Quraysh. Abu Sufyan, who had guarded the caravan that started the whole affair, carried the public shame of the disaster; Ikrimah had lost his father, Abu Jahl; Safwan had lost his father, Umayya ibn Khalaf, the man who once owned Bilal. There was blood on their minds now, and blood asks to be answered.

The third pressure was economic, the same one that had loomed behind Badr. Madinah sat astride the trade road from Makkah up to Syria, and the Muslims had made it impassable; even the alternate route had been found out and spoiled. The Makkan economy, the Sheikh reminds us, was a pipeline, Yemen at one end and Syria at the other, and Quraysh grew rich brokering its length; cut one end and the whole line dies, and Madinah had cut the northern end. This was not an inconvenience but the slow strangling of a city. And fourth, the plainly political fear: the Prophet ﷺ was no longer a local irritant but a rising power whose alliances grew by the season. Religious, social, economic, political, four reasons stacked into one necessity. Quraysh felt they had no option left but to fight.

Ibn Ishaq tells us that Abu Sufyan made up his mind the very moment he brought the caravan of Badr safely home. And here is the detail that makes Uhud unforgettable before a single sword is drawn: the war was funded by that very caravan. The Muslims had marched out for the caravan, the lighter and richer of two prizes, and Allah had turned them instead toward the army; so the caravan came home untouched, its luscious profit distributed to every shareholder in Makkah, each man's percentage counted out by Abu Sufyan, its accountant, who knew to the coin who had invested and who had earned. Now he went door to door, with the two grieving sons at his side, Ikrimah ibn Abi Jahl and Safwan ibn Umayya, and at every house that had profited he said the same thing: hand the profit back, we are buying an army with it. The wealth the Muslims had once ridden out to seize came back to ride against them.

The Qur'an had already written this scene. Surah al-Anfal came down in the weeks right after Badr, while the collection plate was still going around Makkah, and inside it Allah described money spent to block His path with three verbs laid out like a sentence being passed.

Indeed, those who disbelieve spend their wealth to avert [people] from the way of Allāh. So they will spend it; then it will be for them a [source of] regret; then they will be overcome. And those who have disbelieved - unto Hell they will be gathered.Qur'an 8:36

They will spend it; then it will become a regret; then they will be overcome. Three movements, in order. The Muslims could not yet have known what it pointed to, but the Sheikh wants you to hold those three verbs through the next five days and watch them pay out one by one.

Three thousand men, and women to sing them forward

Money alone does not win a war; Makkah needed bodies and steel, and had not enough of either on its own. So the Quraysh turned to the great tribes who shared their stake in the caravan road, Kinanah and Tihamah, and asked for everything: men, money, mounts, armor. What assembled was beyond anything the region had seen them field: three thousand fighting men, two hundred horses, and seven hundred coats of mail.

That last number deserves a pause, because it is easy to skim past. Armor was not something an ordinary man owned. The Arabs were not metalworkers; iron mail was imported from Yemen or Syria at enormous cost, bought for a lifetime and perhaps used once in twenty years, so most fighters owned none at all. This is why one of the most prized things a warrior could carry off a battlefield was the armor stripped from a fallen opponent: a fortune lying in the dust. Seven hundred coats meant the wealth of whole tribes poured into this one army, most of the fighters surely Quraysh while Kinanah and Tihamah gave their aid in logistics, the armor and saddles a wealthy trading people had to spare. And so the circle of the conflict was widening: what had begun as a quarrel between one man ﷺ and his own tribe was becoming, tribe by tribe, the first real confrontation between Islam and the paganism of all Arabia.

And the Quraysh brought their women, around two dozen of them, with Hind bint Utbah, the wife of Abu Sufyan, among them; the seerah will have much more to say about her before it is done. The women came to sing, their poetry sharp and taunting and a little sensual, verses to spur the men forward and shame any thought of turning back. But their presence was more than encouragement; it was strategy with a cold logic underneath. An army that runs abandons its women to the enemy. By bringing their wives, these men had made retreat unthinkable on purpose.

Abu Sufyan took the head of the army himself. On the right flank he set Khalid ibn al-Walid, and on the left, Ikrimah ibn Abi Jahl, two names this story will meet again on very different terms. On the seventh of Shawwal they marched out of Makkah, and you can measure their hunger in the distance they ate. A caravan covered the road to Madinah in two weeks at ease, ten days at a steady push; they devoured it in seven, as though, the Sheikh notes, they wished to answer Badr almost on the anniversary of the wound.

The letter that outran an army

But Makkah had a leak, and the leak was the Prophet's ﷺ own uncle. Al-Abbas, radiyallahu anhu, still lived in the city. At Badr he had been dragged out to fight against his will and taken prisoner, and when he protested that he had no wealth for ransom, the Prophet ﷺ named to him the exact sum he had hidden with his wife Umm al-Fadl, and his whispered instructions about who should receive what if he died, knowledge no human being could have carried out of that house. Ya Rasul Allah, Abbas had said, no one could have told you this except Allah. From that moment, the Sheikh says, there is a strong theory, which he flags as one we cannot prove, that Abbas went home a secret Muslim, the Prophet's ﷺ quiet eyes inside Makkah, right up until the eve of the conquest when at last he emigrated openly.

Now, the instant the army marched and the streets emptied, Abbas acted. He sealed a letter with everything in it, the three thousand, the horses, the coats of mail, the whole shape of the force, and handed it to a trusted servant who rode the road to Madinah as fast as a human being on a camel could physically cover it. Three days; you cannot do it in less. The army had a head start of weeks; the letter was faster. He had waited until the city emptied, the Sheikh notes, perhaps because Quraysh never fully trusted an uncle who had shown no open hatred, or simply because a servant slipping out under watching eyes would have been a death sentence.

The rider found the Prophet ﷺ at Quba. Ubayy ibn Ka'b was with him, and since the Prophet ﷺ was unlettered, an nabiyy al-ummiyy, he had Ubayy read the letter aloud, the numbers passing into his ears one by one. Then his first instruction: tell no one until I tell you. And watch how he ﷺ moves, because the Sheikh holds it up as a masterclass in leadership under dread. No public alarm, no panic poured over the whole city at once. First he spoke quietly to a core group, leaders of the Ansar like Sa'd ibn al-Rabi, because Madinah had never faced anything like this and the men who would defend her needed to know first. Then he sent two or three of his own scouts to confirm the news with their own eyes.

Did he doubt his uncle? Of course not; he trusted Abbas completely. But this was news that would move an entire city to war, and a city's fate cannot be staked on a single letter, however beloved its sender, for what if even a sincere man had been deceived? So he verified. The scouts came back grim: the army was real, its herds already grazing barely a day or two off, on the very outskirts. He ﷺ had taught his ummah that deliberation is from Allah and haste is from Shaitan, and here is that teaching lived at the highest stakes: the largest army the Muslims have ever faced is at the gate, the window is perhaps forty-eight hours, and still he verifies before he decides and consults before he commits. Some time was lost, and it was better lost than spent raising a false alarm.

The dream of the broken sword

Somewhere across these tightening days hangs a dream. The Prophet ﷺ saw himself strike with his sword, and the blade snapped; then he struck again, and it came back whole, as good as it had ever been. He saw cows. He saw that Allah is good. And in the version Imam Ahmad carries, he ﷺ saw himself wearing a coat of strong armor, and understood it to mean Madinah.

Every image would be paid out at Uhud. The snapped blade was the blow about to fall on the believers, the calamity of the day to come. The blade restored was what would follow it: victory again, and an ummah fused back into a single piece, for Uhud was about to split this community for the first time into believer and hypocrite, and only after the break would the believers know exactly who stood with them. The cows were the believers who would fall, and Ibn Hajar explains the symbol with a tenderness almost too much to bear: a cow gives benefit while it lives and gives benefit when it is slaughtered, so whichever way the day went for those companions, alive or martyred, Allah had chosen good for them on both sides of it. And the armor was the city itself: stay inside your walls.

Here the Sheikh pauses over a question of sources, and his weighing is part of the lesson. Ibn Ishaq places the telling of this dream before the battle; Bukhari's narration has the Prophet ﷺ relating it afterward, once events had unlocked its meaning, to console a wounded community. The Bukhari placement, the Sheikh argues, simply makes more sense. Read it the other way and you have the Prophet ﷺ announcing the coming defeat to the very army he is about to lead out. That cannot be right. The truer shape is a cryptic dream he could not interpret when it came, understood only in hindsight, and told afterward not to predict the wound but to bind it.

A Friday of shura, and armor that stays on

By now it was Friday, and the city was already gathered for Jumu'ah, and, as Ibn Ishaq notes, a funeral had drawn in even more people than usual, as if Allah willed a full house for the most consequential meeting Madinah had yet held. After his quiet hours with the inner circle, the Prophet ﷺ now opened the floor to everyone. He laid it all before them, no holds barred, the numbers and the danger exactly as they were, and then, before inviting a single voice, gave his own counsel first.

I see myself, he said, in a strong fortress, and the fortress is Madinah. Stay. Let them break against the city. And the geography backed him completely, for Madinah was born fortified: the harrah, the great fields of black volcanic rock that no cavalry crosses, on the east and west; the long wall of Uhud's range to the north; dense date plantations toward Quba that no army marches through. An enemy that forced its way in would drown in alleys it did not know, split into pockets and cut apart by people fighting for their own streets, with women and children raining stones from the rooftops. Fight them, he was saying, where every wall is on your side.

The first voice to agree was Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul, and the Sheikh asks you to remember the name, because the agreement was not love. Ibn Ubayy was the most seasoned soldier in the city, and he backed the plan because it was militarily correct: Madinah, he said, has never been taken while her people stayed inside her. A correct opinion can sit inside a corrupt heart, and this road is about to prove it.

Then the young men spoke, and they would not stop. Most had missed Badr and had spent a whole year aching over it, hungry to show what they were; some of them, Ibn Ishaq notes, would find their shahadah at Uhud. Shall we sit in our houses like women, they said, while they camp at our gates? Lead us out to meet them like men. And the elder companions, who agreed with the Prophet ﷺ, said nothing at all, and their silence was itself a lesson: they would not turn his ﷺ gathering into a bickering match in front of him, even to defend his own stated view. When the weight of the room settled with the young men, the Prophet ﷺ accepted it. The one human being on earth who could have imposed his will and been obeyed with joy chose instead to be bound by his people's counsel. Their affair is consultation among them: he did not merely recite the verse, he governed by it on the brink of war.

And those who have responded to their Lord and established prayer and whose affair is [determined by] consultation among themselves, and from what We have provided them, they spend,Qur'an 42:38

He ﷺ went inside to put on his armor. The moment he was out of earshot, the elders turned on the young men: he gave you his own opinion, and you pressed him out of it until he yielded? Shame landed fast, and they sent in Hamza, radiyallahu anhu, his uncle, the lion of this story who is fated to fall on the very field they had just argued for, to carry the message: they have changed their minds, they will do whatever you wish. But Hamza found him already buckled into his mail. And the answer he ﷺ sent back is preserved in Muslim and in Ahmad: it does not befit a prophet, once he has put on his armor, to take it off again until Allah has judged between him and his enemy. There is a code that binds the prophets and no one else, the way it was wajib upon him alone to pray through the night and he alone could fast days on end without food or water while companions who tried it fainted. The decision had been made by consultation; now it would be carried with iron, with no reversing it.

And Ibn Ishaq adds a detail that preaches by itself. The man whose life Allah had personally guaranteed wore two coats of mail to this battle, one over the other. He ﷺ needed no armor at all; the protection of heaven was promised to him. Yet he doubled it, to teach his ummah forever that you do not fold your arms and call it tawakkul. Allah commanded the believers to take their precautions in war and to carry provisions for the road, and the best provision is taqwa: do the thing with Allah in your heart, yes, but do it with your hands too. To wear no armor and call it reliance is not faith but folly. Tawakkul ties its camel, and then ties it again, in iron, twice over.

And mark what never happened afterward. When Uhud went the way it went, not one of those young men was ever blamed for it, not for the rest of their lives. The decision had been taken properly, with istikhara and istishara, prayer and counsel, and once a matter is decided that way it is sealed. The believers' mouths stayed clean of every did I not tell you. There is a whole discipline of the heart in that silence: decide well, then leave the decision alone.

Three hundred backs turn away, and two tribes Allah held

Late on that Friday afternoon, after the prayer and the counsel, roughly a thousand men marched north out of Madinah under three banners: the Muhajirun behind Mus'ab ibn Umayr, the Aws behind Usayd ibn Hudayr, the Khazraj behind al-Hubab ibn al-Mundhir. Against an army of three thousand they carried a hundred coats of mail among them and a bare handful of horses.

Then, on the road, the rot showed itself. Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul never announced his treachery; he had not even the courage for that. His people simply began to murmur, to drift, to fall toward the rear, until three hundred backs had turned for home, a full third of the army gone in a slow slinking retreat. He listened to the boys, Ibn Ubayy complained, and not to me; why should we throw away our lives for a man who will not take our advice? And Abdullah ibn Amr ibn Haram, the father of the famous Jabir and himself barely a day from his own martyrdom, rode after them with the two appeals a believer makes: fear Allah, and do not abandon your Prophet ﷺ and your people with the enemy at the door; and if you will not fear Allah, at least honor your pledge, for you promised at al-Aqabah to defend him as you defend your own families. He reminded them of Allah and of their own word, because that is finally all a man has.

The answer came back smooth and dead. If we knew there would really be fighting, we would follow you; but we do not think it will come to a battle. And Allah seized that sentence and hung it in the Qur'an forever, naming it exactly what it was.

And that He might make evident those who are hypocrites. For it was said to them, "Come, fight in the way of Allāh or [at least] defend." They said, "If we had known [there would be] battle, we would have followed you." They were nearer to disbelief that day than to faith, saying with their mouths what was not in their hearts. And Allāh is most knowing of what they conceal -Qur'an 3:167

A lie, from men nearer to disbelief that day than to faith, mouths saying what their hearts did not hold. And the verse just before it gives the why of the whole trial, the meaning the Sheikh names as one of the great purposes of the entire battle:

And what struck you on the day the two armies met [at Uḥud] was by permission of Allāh that He might make evident the [true] believersQur'an 3:166

Before a single arrow flies, Uhud is already doing its first work. It is a sieve. The ummah had never yet seen open disunity in its ranks, innocently unaware of how much evil the hypocrites carried in their hearts; Allah willed that the hidden rot become visible now, on this road, so that this community would never be stung from the same hole twice, and at the Trench every believer will already know exactly who Ibn Ubayy is. It is only in fitna that a man's true iman is shown. Whole stretches of Surah Al Imran came down around this day, and sections of Surah an-Nisa, and you have just read why.

Watching a third of the army peel away, the believers' own blood rose, and they fell to arguing: one group said turn back and deal with these traitors first; another said leave them, settle with them later, march on. And here is the remarkable thing the Sheikh draws out: both sides were angry purely for the sake of Allah, and even so the Qur'an reached down and stilled the quarrel, gently, because the anger was sincere, but firmly, because the people whose entire work is division must not be allowed to divide even the believers' condemnation of them.

What is [the matter] with you [that you are] two groups concerning the hypocrites, while Allāh has made them fall back [into error and disbelief] for what they earned. Do you wish to guide those whom Allāh has sent astray? And he whom Allāh sends astray - never will you find for him a way [of guidance].Qur'an 4:88

Do not split into two camps over the hypocrites. Allah has already thrown them back for what they earned; the matter is His.

Because the danger in a walkout is never only the three hundred who leave; it is the wobble that runs through everyone left watching them go. Two whole tribes felt their own feet soften, the Banu Haritha of the Aws and the Banu Salama of the Khazraj, and they came to the very edge of folding back to the city behind Ibn Ubayy. The Qur'an records it without flinching, names them as two parties who nearly gave up, and then says the words that saved them.

When two parties among you were about to lose courage, but Allāh was their ally; and upon Allāh the believers should rely.Qur'an 3:122

But Allah was their ally. They steadied, and they stayed. And here is the part to carry home, the part the Sheikh clearly loves. For generations afterward the Banu Haritha and the Banu Salama would boast of this very verse, the one that preserves the most embarrassing hour in their history, and their descendants boast of it to this day. Why boast of the record of their own near-cowardice? Because the same verse that preserves the stumble preserves Whose hand caught them: wallahu waliyyuhuma, and Allah was their Protector. They would not trade their recorded weakness for anyone else's recorded strength, because their weakness came back to them from Allah with His friendship attached. Let that recalibrate the way you read your own worst hours.

Seven hundred, and a mountain that loves them

So seven hundred reached the mountain, and the choice of ground was its own sermon. Uhud, the Sheikh corrects a common error, is not a single peak; it is a range, a wall of rock more than a mile long across the north of Madinah. The Prophet ﷺ set his camp with its back against that wall and its face toward the city: stone behind him so no one could come at his rear, the flanks pinched tight by the terrain, and on the one open side a single small hill, Jabal ar-Rumah. Hold that name, because fifty archers and one command are going to live or die upon it tomorrow.

What he ﷺ had done, with no academy and no training, was to make seven hundred the largest army the ground could hold. Three thousand men mean little if only a narrow corridor of them can reach you at once; block three sides and the enemy must funnel through the fourth, where your small force meets them on equal frontage. And there was a quieter cruelty folded into the geometry. The Quraysh had come up from the south, so to reach a camp pitched to the north they had to drag their whole army the long way around Madinah, an extra half day's march on legs that had already swallowed two weeks of road in seven days. No military school taught him ﷺ any of this. The One who sent him did.

And of all the mountains of the earth, it is Uhud. The Prophet ﷺ loved this mountain and said so plainly, in words Bukhari preserves from a homecoming, when its face on the horizon was the first sign that Madinah was near: Uhud is a mountain that loves us, and we love it. To love Uhud, the Sheikh says, is a sign of iman, and he ﷺ called it a mountain of Jannah. Once, as Muslim narrates, it trembled beneath him as he climbed it with Abu Bakr, Umar, and Uthman, and he tapped it with his foot: be firm, Uhud, for upon you are only a prophet, a siddiq, and two martyrs. He ﷺ had even drawn the sacred boundaries of Madinah himself, declaring it a haram as Makkah is, between the mountains of Air and Thawr and the two lava plains. Air sits just beyond Uhud, which means tomorrow's battlefield lies inside holy ground.

The Sheikh carries a personal memory here, and it belongs in the retelling. One early morning in the mid-nineties his own teacher, Sheikh Safiur Rahman Mubarakpuri, the author of ar-Raheeq al-Makhtum, with whom he read Sahih Bukhari one-on-one, drove him around this mountain, walked him through his own reading of where the two armies stood, then took him the long way behind Uhud to point out a small rise: that, he said, is Air, the far marker of the haram the Prophet ﷺ named. It was the only time the Sheikh ever saw it. When he teaches you Uhud, he is teaching earth he walked with his sheikh, may Allah have abundant mercy on him.

Now stand at the edge of the camp and do the arithmetic the lecture will not let you skip. Seven hundred men at the mountain. In the entire world that night, counting every secret believer still in Makkah and every scattered soul beyond, there were perhaps not even a thousand Muslim men alive; every able man above fifteen was here, some at fourteen. That is the whole religion of Islam, sleeping at the foot of Uhud. Within fifteen or twenty years these same men, and the sons they would raise, will be knocking on the gates of Damascus and wiping away the empire of Persia; within a hundred, their banners will stand from China to the edge of France. They had no numbers, two hundred fewer horses and six hundred fewer coats of mail than their enemy. What they had, the Sheikh says, is the one thing we so often do not: trust in Allah so total that the mightiest superpowers of the age could not stand against a few hundred of them. They had the promise.

If Allāh should aid you, no one can overcome you; but if He should forsake you, who is there that can aid you after Him? And upon Allāh let the believers rely.Qur'an 3:160

If Allah aids you, no one can overcome you. The believers at the mountain knew that half of the verse by heart. And Uhud, over the days ahead, will teach the ummah the weight of the other half, the half we would so much rather not read: but if He should forsake you, who is there that can aid you after Him?

What this day asks of our faith

The battle has not begun, and already the road to Uhud is heavy with everything the Sheikh wants us to carry. Strip the day to its turning points, and each puts a question to us that does not stay safely in the seventh century.

He verified, even though he trusted. He ﷺ never doubted his uncle for a moment, and still sent scouts before moving a single household, because a decision that will cost lives cannot rest on one report, however beloved its source. We invert this exactly when it matters most, acting fastest under the heaviest pressure, when the pressure is precisely the reason to slow our hands. Deliberation is from Allah; haste is from Shaitan; the bigger the decision, the slower the hands.

He consulted, and then submitted to the consultation, surrendering the correct opinion when the room went the other way, because their affair is consultation among them. The one leader on earth entitled to impose his will refused to. And once the matter was sealed by counsel and prayer, the armor went on and stayed on, and when the day later turned bitter, no mouth ever said did we not tell you. Decide together, decide properly with istikhara and istishara, and then leave the decision alone, win or lose. Then there are the two coats of mail: the man whose safety Allah Himself had guaranteed wore his armor doubled, to brand into his ummah forever that trust in Allah is not the absence of effort but the spirit your effort is made of. We dress up our laziness as reliance and our anxiety as diligence; he ﷺ shows the opposite of both, hands that prepare everything possible and a heart that rests in the One who decides the outcome.

And there is the sieve. Before a single arrow flew, this day exposed three hundred hypocrites the believers had marched out beside without knowing what was in their hearts, and steadied two tribes whose own feet had nearly failed. When a trial thins your ranks, or thins your resolve, read it the way the Qur'an reads Uhud: as Allah making the true thing visible, so the community is never wounded from the same opening twice. And read your weak hours the way the Banu Haritha and Banu Salama read theirs, not as a shame to be buried but, if Allah caught you in them, as a place where His friendship became visible, wallahu waliyyuhuma, a verse worth boasting of because it records both the stumble and the Hand.

So leave the believers there tonight, exactly where the day left them. Seven hundred men, their backs against a mountain that loves them and their faces toward the city they were defending; across the plain, an army three thousand strong, bought with the profit of a lost caravan, led by a man avenging his pride and flanked by two commanders avenging their fathers, sung forward by women who had come to make retreat impossible. Nothing has happened yet, and everything has been decided: the letter believed, the shura honored, the armor fastened and refused, the hypocrites unmasked, the wavering held, the ground chosen with a wisdom no one taught him ﷺ. Tomorrow the field gets drawn like a map, where each army stood and how the fighting opened, and fifty archers will climb a small hill with a single command upon which the whole day will hang. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, who wore two coats of mail over a heart that trusted his Lord completely; make us a people who seek counsel and honor it, who verify before we move and never look back once the armor is on; and if He writes for us any share of loss on the road to Him, may He choose for us in it the good He chose for the people of Uhud.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 46: the Battle of Uhud, part 1 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (8:36, 42:38, 3:166, 3:167, 4:88, 3:122, 3:160), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 47 of 104

The Battle of Uhud, part 2 ﷺ

A mountain at his back, fifty archers on his flank

Uhud does not reach us as a single clean story. It reaches us in fragments, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi is honest about this before he draws the first line on the map. We do not have one companion who painted the whole picture. We have one man who remembered one moment and another who remembered a different one, each handing his children the one or two things from a hard day that he could never forget, and the work of the later scholar is to set those scattered pieces in order and to bridge, as carefully as he can, the gaps between them. Two things make Uhud harder than most. It was chaotic, and it ended in loss, and human nature speaks little of its losses. Open five books of seerah and you will find five different orderings of the same day. So what follows is one reconstruction of the sequence, offered as that and not as the only one, and the incidents themselves are firm even where the thread that ties them is a matter of inference.

And before any sword is drawn, the most striking thing about this day is how much of it was already decided by where the Prophet ﷺ chose to stand. He had already prayed his istikhara of the heart and made his decision; he had put on his armor; and once the armor was on, he would not take it off and walk it back, whatever the second thoughts of those around him. The battle, then, was coming. The only question left open was the ground, and the ground was his to choose.

A march nobody was meant to see

By the Thursday, which the early authorities reckon to be around the thirteenth of Shawwal, the Quraysh and the Muslims were within scouting distance of each other, close enough that the spies of each could learn roughly where the other lay. The Quraysh had come three thousand strong, and they had come for revenge. The Prophet ﷺ made his decision plain: before this army could settle outside the city and choose its own moment to strike, the Muslims would go out and meet it at Uhud, and they would get there first. So as soon as the Friday prayer was done, he moved, and the way he moved tells you everything.

He asked for a guide, not to take them along the open highway to Uhud, but to thread them through the date palm groves and the back lanes between the gardens, a man who knew the lay of the land well enough to keep them off the main road the whole way. The reason is not stated outright; it has to be inferred, and the inference is the point. With enemy scouts watching the highway, he did not want his route or his destination handed to them too early. He wanted to reach Uhud before the Quraysh did and to choose, with his own eyes, exactly where his men would stand. Uhud sits due north of the city, and it is close: a walk of barely half an hour from his mosque. Getting there first was not a small advantage. It was the whole battle.

As the army rustled through one of the gardens, the noise of all those men and horses reached an old blind man whose land it was, a friend of the chief of the hypocrites of Madinah. He understood whose feet were crossing his property, and his hatred rose in him, and he began flinging pebbles and shouting that by God he would not let Muhammad ﷺ and his companions set foot on his ground. One of the companions reached for his sword to be done with him. Leave him, the Prophet ﷺ said: he is blind in his eyes and blind in his heart. What would you gain by killing him? They walked on, and by the early afternoon they held the place they had come for. The Quraysh, learning from their scouts where the Muslims had gone, followed after and made their own camp; and as the night fell with both armies on the field, everyone understood that the morning, the morning of Saturday the fifteenth of Shawwal, would bring battle.

Sent home for being too young

As soon as the ranks were forming, the Prophet ﷺ walked the line, and he looked at every single fighter, putting each man where he belonged. About a dozen boys he turned back. The rule that day was fifteen: you had to be fifteen years old to stand at Uhud, and those younger were sent home to Madinah. Among the ones turned away were names this ummah would one day lean its whole weight upon. Abdullah ibn Umar was sent back. Zayd ibn Thabit, who as a grown man would gather the scattered Qur'an into a single book, was sent back. Usama ibn Zayd was sent back. By the arithmetic of his later life, Usama was perhaps eleven or twelve that day. He had tried to slip into the army unnoticed and keep out of sight until it was too late to send him away, and he was caught right before the fighting and turned around, heartbroken, and made to go home.

Two of the boys argued their way in. Rafi ibn Khadij was fourteen, and the Prophet ﷺ was sending him back when his relatives stepped in to plead for him, because they knew how badly he wanted to stay and how crushed he was at being refused. He is an expert archer, they said: even at fourteen, he need not stand in the front line at all, only let him use his bow. And because he was an archer, Rafi was allowed to remain. The instant he was, Samura ibn Jundub, also fourteen, got to his feet. Messenger of Allah, he said, if you let Rafi stay, then I should stay too, for I am stronger than him, and I have beaten him in wrestling more than once. To prove it on the spot, one report says, he threw Rafi to the ground in front of everyone, and he too was allowed to remain.

Sheikh Yasir asks you to sit with the contrast, because it is one of the quiet judgments of the whole day. Here are boys of fourteen weeping at being kept away from danger and wrestling each other for the right to risk their lives. And elsewhere in this same story stand grey-haired men of experience and standing, men who carried the dignity of years, who will melt away and go home before the first blow is struck, their cowardice laid bare in the Qur'an. The faith was not in the years. It never is. It was because the young men on this side carried this kind of iman that the religion went where it went.

The mountain, and the order to never leave it

Now look at the ground the way the Sheikh lays it out on the map. Ibn Ishaq records that the Prophet ﷺ turned the army a full half-circle, so that their faces were now toward Madinah, the way they had come, and their backs were to the great mass of Mount Uhud. And Uhud is not a single peak; it is a long range, a wall of rock nearly the size of the old city itself, and it sealed the encampment on three sides. There was only one open side, and on that side, by Allah's arrangement, a small hill stood almost exactly in the middle of the gap. In those days it was called Jabal al-Ainayn. Today it is known as Jabal al-Rumah, the mountain of the archers, and the name tells you what happened there. On that hill the Prophet ﷺ placed fifty of his finest archers, and with their bows commanding the open ground, the camp was sealed shut. All that remained exposed, the whole field on which the battle could be joined, was a stretch of perhaps three hundred meters.

The verse Allah would later reveal about this day opens on precisely this hour, the Prophet ﷺ in the early morning setting his men in their places:

And [remember] when you, [O Muhammad], left your family in the morning to post the believers at their stations for the battle [of Uhud] and Allah is Hearing and Knowing.Qur'an 3:121

This, the Sheikh says plainly, is military genius, and he says it as a man who does not hand out that phrase lightly. Outnumbered more than four to one, seven hundred against three thousand and more, the Prophet ﷺ chose a field so narrow that the enemy's surplus thousands became almost meaningless. If only three hundred meters of front are open, it no longer matters how long a line the other side can form; only so many men can come at you across that gap at once. The Quraysh would be forced to thin out and meet the Muslims more or less man for man, in a space that could not be flanked. He had taken a small army and erased most of the disadvantage of being small, cutting the four-to-one down to something close to even, not by adding men, but by choosing the earth they would fight on.

There was exactly one weakness left in the whole position, and he saw it and named it. The archers' hill guarded the only line by which an enemy could come around the back. So he gave those fifty men an order that, of all the many reports of Uhud, comes down to us through the most authentic chains of all, recorded in Bukhari and Muslim and in every single book of seerah. Protect us with your arrows, he told them, for the enemy's horses will never charge into arrows; a horse will not run onto the points no matter how its rider drives it. Hold this place. Then, in words shaped to leave no room for interpretation: even if you see the birds eating our bodies, do not leave your positions until I send for you. In another wording: make sure they do not come at us from behind, and whether you see us winning or losing, stay until I call you. Dr. Yasir Qadhi pauses on the fact that this, of everything that happened that day, is the detail history transmitted most reliably, and on what that tells us: the Prophet ﷺ understood, completely, the single hinge on which the whole battle turned, and bolted it down with an image so graphic, our corpses lying out and the birds at them, that no man could later claim he had misunderstood.

A sword, and what it is owed

With the sun rising on the Saturday, the Prophet ﷺ moved among his men to rouse them for the fighting. Every army has its ways of lifting its soldiers, the Sheikh notes; even modern armies keep whole departments for the morale of their troops. And what greater motivation could there be than Allah and His Messenger ﷺ? Among the ways he stirred them, he drew his own sword and held it out: who will take this sword from me? At once the hands went up and the voices came, I will, Messenger of Allah, and among the first to reach for it was his cousin Zubayr ibn al-Awwam.
