Then he asked it again, but differently: who will take it with its right? Who will give this sword the right that it is owed? And now the hands hesitated. A man named Abu Dujana asked the question the rest were turning over in silence: and what is its right, Messenger of Allah? That you fight the enemy with it, he said, until it bends or breaks and can no longer be used. I take it with that right, said Abu Dujana, and the sword was his.

Abu Dujana was a famous warrior, a fighting machine with a reputation built in the days before Islam, and he owned a red turban he called the turban of death, which he wore only at the fiercest extremity of battle. He tied it on now, and took the bared sword and began to walk out before the lines with a slow, swaggering, deliberate stride, his chest thrown forward, parading himself so that the Quraysh could see exactly what was coming for them. Watching him go, the Prophet ﷺ said: this is a walk that Allah hates, except in a place like this. The Sheikh lets that land, because the lesson in it is double-edged. The arrogant strut, the puffed-out chest, the open display of one's own strength: hateful anywhere else, and you can think without much effort of where you have seen it. But here, on this ground, it had a purpose: to drive fear into the enemy and fire into your own side. And so here, only here, the thing that is hateful everywhere else was exactly right.

An attempt to split the believers in two

Before the clash, the Quraysh tried twice to peel the Ansar away from the Muhajirun, and the strategy was not stupid: the Ansar were the bulk of the army, five or six hundred men, while the emigrants from Makkah were scarcely a hundred. Pull the Ansar out and the rest collapses. So first a herald rode up within shouting distance and called out that Abu Sufyan had sent him: people of Madinah, leave us to our cousins, we have no quarrel with you, go back to your homes safe. The Ansar answered with a volley of curses and insults, furious at the very suggestion that they would walk away and leave the Prophet ﷺ to stand alone. Here the Sheikh notes a point of fiqh in passing: on the battlefield, a harshness and even a roughness of tongue is permitted that would never befit a Muslim in ordinary life. The decorum of the gathering is for the gathering; the field has its own register.

Then a man came forward who believed he could do better, an old chief of the Aws named Abu Amir. Before the hijrah he had been among the most senior and beloved of his tribe, the equal in standing of the great hypocrite of Madinah himself, and his people had honored him with a title: ar-rahib, the monk. But he had refused Islam and abandoned the city rather than live under it, taking a small band of his tribesmen to Makkah to wait for exactly such a day as this, a day on which he could return and reclaim the chieftaincy he had lost. Leave them to me, he told Abu Sufyan, boasting: my people have always respected me, you will see now the power I hold over them. He went out before the lines and called: people of the Aws, it is me, Abu Amir, I have come back. And before he could say another word they cried back that Allah give him no joy and no comfort, that he was not Abu Amir the monk at all but Abu Amir the corrupt, the fasiq, and they would not hear one more word from his mouth. He came back to Abu Sufyan stunned, saying that some sickness had befallen his people, that he no longer knew them. He did not know them, the Sheikh says, because faith had remade them from the inside: their loyalty belonged to Allah and His Messenger ﷺ now, and the old ranks of tribe and lineage that had once commanded them had simply lost their grip. There is a final note the Sheikh will not let pass, and it is a heavy one. Abu Amir's own son was on the other side, with the Muslims, a man this ummah remembers and loves: Hanzala, who within hours would give his life at Uhud and be washed by the angels. Father against son, on opposite banks of faith.

The duel before the battle

Battles in that age opened with single combat, and Uhud opened with Talha ibn Abi Talha, of the Banu Abd al-Dar, the clan that carried the Quraysh banner, striding out into the open to call for an opponent. Ali ibn Abi Talib answered him. Talha was sheathed in full body armor head to foot, every limb covered; Ali wore none at all. Talha struck the first blow, swinging the moment they were within reach, but Ali was faster. He took the full force of the sword on his shield, and before Talha could even draw the blade back, Ali had answered, one hand still lifting to block while the other came down in the same motion. The Sheikh can only reach for the comparison to something out of a film, a speed of hand and mind that none of us in our own lives could begin to match. Finding Talha armored everywhere except below the thigh, Ali struck him there, and with a force that took the leg clean off, Talha collapsed to the ground.

As he fell backward his garment rode up and he lay exposed, his awrah uncovered, and when Ali came forward to finish him, Talha began to beg, pleading by the ties of kinship, for the two of them were distant cousins through Abd Manaf and Abd al-Dar. They were out of sight of the army, no one watching, and Ali felt a stab of shame, some hayaa, some sense of muruwwah, at killing a defenseless man laid bare on his back who had appealed to him by blood. So he lowered the sword and walked away. When the companions asked why he had let the man live, he told them the truth: the kinship, and the shame of it. Talha could no longer fight in any case; his wound would take his life before the battle ended, and he became one of the few of the Quraysh to die that way. That, the Sheikh says, is the mark of a noble warrior: there is a way to fight, and even a way to spare, and the strength that knows the difference is the higher strength. And look at the two faces of one man inside a single minute. One moment he is striding out and calling for a champion; the next he is flat on his back, stripped bare, weeping for his life. Look at the distance between those two moments, the Sheikh says, and learn what this world is worth.

The charge that scattered them

Of the opening assault itself we have only a handful of reports, scarcely more than four or five small tidbits, but every one of them points the same way: it was devastating. After Ali's victory in the duel the Muslims charged, and the Quraysh, for all their four-to-one strength and their heavy arms, could not hold the line. This was exactly what the ground had been chosen to produce. Pressed into that narrow three hundred meters, where the enemy could not bring its numbers to bear and had been forced to thin itself out, the concentrated Muslim ranks hit the weakened Quraysh line like a single bullet driven through a thin layer, and they broke clean through it and drove on toward the far end of the camp.

The proof of how far they drove is in where the fighting reached. The Quraysh banner was the ancient honor of the Banu Abd al-Dar, Talha's clan, and before the battle Abu Sufyan had taunted them over Badr, where their flagbearer had been among the first to run, until they swore to him that as long as one of them drew breath the flag would stay up. And it did. One by one, all ten of them were cut down where they stood, most of them by Hamza, some by Ali, some by Abu Dujana, until the last of the ten fell and the banner came down to the ground, which in the language of that age was the symbolic death of an army. Hamza fought that day with a fury that placed him among those who killed the most of the enemy; in Ibn Ishaq's long list of who killed whom at Uhud, his name runs through it again and again. The Sheikh notes quietly, and leaves it there for now, that this same Hamza, the uncle of the Prophet ﷺ, is about to become the chief of the martyrs, and that his story belongs to the day ahead.

There is one more sign of how completely the charge had carried, and it is the image with which the Sheikh chooses to stop. The women of the Quraysh had come as the army's last line, stationed at the very rear of the encampment, singing the men forward and shaming any who fell back, promising comfort to the brave and scorn to the coward. And the women had to run. Al-Bara ibn Azib, who saw it with his own eyes, remembered it to the end of his life: when we fought them at Uhud, they turned and fled, until I saw with my own eyes the legs of the women as they lifted their skirts and scrambled up the slopes, and I could see their ankle bracelets. Ibn Hisham adds that he remembered seeing Hind herself among them, she and her companions running headlong up the mountain with nothing left to shield them. The fact that the women at the very back were forced to flee tells you that the Muslims had reached all the way to the end of the enemy camp.

The verse Allah revealed about this battle confirms exactly this, that the early promise was kept while the believers struck the enemy down, and it names, in the very same breath, the turn that was still to come:

And Allah had certainly fulfilled His promise to you when you were killing them [i.e., the enemy] by His permission until [the time] when you lost courage and fell to disputing about the order [given by the Prophet (ﷺ)] and disobeyed after He had shown you that which you love. Among you are some who desire this world, and among you are some who desire the Hereafter. Then He turned you back from them [defeated] that He might test you. And He has already forgiven you, and Allah is the possessor of bounty for the believers.Qur'an 3:152

The day, for now, was in their hands; the promise was being kept. And it is exactly here, at the height of the advantage, just before the words until the time when you lost courage and fell to disputing come true, that Sheikh Yasir stops the story, because what comes next, the one weakness, the hill, the order remembered or forgotten, is the turn of the whole battle, and it belongs to the days ahead.

What this day asks of our faith

It is tempting to read the early hours of Uhud as a straightforward triumph and leave them there, a stirring charge safely sealed in the past. That would be to walk past the questions the day is quietly pressing on us, and there are at least three.

The first is in the ground. The Prophet ﷺ won most of Uhud before a single sword was raised, and he won it by where he chose to stand: a mountain sealing his back, three sides closed by rock, one narrow gap left open and commanded by fifty archers on a hill. Notice that he did not pray for the mountain to move or for the three thousand to vanish. He took the means Allah had placed within his reach, the terrain, the timing, the secret march off the highway, the positioning of every man, and arranged them with all the skill he had, and only then did he leave the outcome to his Lord. Our battles are not fought on that plain, but they are fought on some plain, and the question is the same: have we chosen our ground, or are we meeting our trials in the open, exposed on every side, having taken none of the lawful means that were ours to take? Position is decided before the first blow. It was for him ﷺ, and it is for us.

The second is in the boys and the grey-haired men. Children of fourteen wept to be kept from the danger and wrestled each other in the dust for the right to risk their lives, while men of years and standing, men who should by every outward measure have been at the front, slipped quietly home. The fire was never in the age. It was in the faith. We tell ourselves we will do the good thing when we are older, when we are more established, when the time is somehow riper; and here are boys too young to be allowed to fight, breaking their hearts for the chance to give everything. Eagerness for what is good is not measured in years, and the soul that waits to feel old enough, or settled enough, or ready enough, may be waiting on the wrong thing entirely.

And the third is in the sword. Many hands shot up when the Prophet ﷺ offered it; the hands fell still when he asked who would take it with its right. Wanting the honor is the easy part, and we are all quick at it. Abu Dujana asked first what it would cost him, and when he was told, he accepted the cost: to fight with it until it bent or broke. How much of the good we reach for would we still want if the full price came attached, the responsibility and not only the reward? There is a sword in nearly every life worth living, some trust held out that comes with a right that is owed. The believer is the one who asks what its right is, and then takes it on those terms. So choose your ground before the trial reaches you and take every lawful means within your hand; do not wait to feel old enough to do something good; and when a worthy thing is held out to you, ask what it is owed, and take it with its right. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, who chose his ground with a mountain at his back and named the one place it could go wrong; keep our courage when the test comes and our feet where He has told us to stand; and place us never among those who desire only this world, but among those who desire the Hereafter, and gather us under his banner ﷺ on the Day the nations are gathered.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 47: the Battle of Uhud, part 2 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (3:121, 3:152), verified via quran.ai. This chapter follows the Sheikh's own reconstruction of the order of events, which he presents as one careful sequence among several.

The Seerah · 48 of 104

The Battle of Uhud, part 3: the archers leave the hill, and the martyrdom of Hamza

The hill emptied, and the lion of Allah fell

Yesterday the Muslims were winning. The Quraysh had broken and fled, the field below Uhud lay open and scattered with the things a poor city could only dream of, and for one bright hour it must have looked as though Badr had simply happened again. Today is the day all of that turns over. And Dr. Yasir Qadhi opens this third chapter the way he opened the last, with a plain admission about how little of it survives. Uhud does not reach us as one connected account; it reaches us as fragments, one story of Musab, one story of Hamza, one story here and one there, and never a clean picture of the whole. Reconstructing what happened at Uhud, he says, is in his own experience the single hardest task in the entire seerah, because the men who lived through it told only a piece each, and human beings do not like to dwell on their worst memories. So the honest way to tell this day is the way he tells it: scene by scene, the way the scenes were handed down, and around the gaps an open and admitted I do not know.

But the shape of the turning is not in doubt, and Allah Himself names it. The verse that the Sheikh keeps returning to belongs to Surah Al Imran, revealed afterward to teach the believers what their own hands had done.

And Allah had certainly fulfilled His promise to you when you were killing them [i.e., the enemy] by His permission until [the time] when you lost courage and fell to disputing about the order [given by the Prophet (ﷺ)] and disobeyed after He had shown you that which you love. Among you are some who desire this world, and among you are some who desire the Hereafter. Then He turned you back from them [defeated] that He might test you. And He has already forgiven you, and Allah is the possessor of bounty for the believers.Qur'an 3:152

Read it slowly, because the whole disaster is folded inside it, and Allah folds it around one small word. He had kept His promise; the believers were cutting the enemy down by His permission. Until. Until they lost courage and disputed the command and slipped, after He had shown them the very thing they wanted. That is the lesson of the entire day, and the Sheikh draws it out without hurry: while the Muslims were pure and sincere, victory was theirs by right, and the instant a desire for this world crept in among them, everything changed.

When you had what you loved

Down on the plain it looked harmless enough. The Quraysh were gone, the field was strewn with abandoned wealth, and a man would have had to be made of stone not to bend down and gather. We forget, fourteen centuries later, what those spoils were. Good weapons in that world were imported, because the Arabs were not fine smiths; they exported leather and dates and brought their iron in from other lands, and anything imported was costly and rare. A sword could last a generation and be handed to the next. A single camel left behind by a fleeing enemy was a small fortune on the sand. So when the believers saw the field littered with all of it, they laid down their guard, set aside their weapons, and began to collect.

And here is the bitter irony the Sheikh will not let pass. The rules of the spoils, the ghaneemah, had not yet been fully revealed. What the men on the plain did not understand is that wealth left on a fled battlefield is not finders-keepers; it does not belong to the hand that snatches it. It is gathered into one pile and shared among the whole army, and to grab a piece and keep it for yourself is not a clever prize but a theft from the war booty, a major sin. So those who broke their discipline to gather did not even own what they gathered. They reached for the dunya, and they broke the one thing that was winning them the battle to do it, and in the end they kept neither. That, the Sheikh says, is the whole symbolism of Uhud caught in a single image: this is the reality of whoever prefers the dunya over everything else. He grasps for it with both hands and walks away with neither this world nor the next.

The fifty who were told to stay

Behind the army, on a small hill the seerah remembers as Jabal ar-Rumah, fifty archers waited. Their orders had been absolute, and the Prophet ﷺ had left no room in them at all: do not leave this place, whatever you see, whether you watch us winning or watch us being torn to pieces, until I send you word. Hold the hill. That was the whole of the command.

No messenger came. From where the Prophet ﷺ stood the battle was not yet over; it was simply too early to call it, and his instinct, as always, was to wait and to be sure. But from where the archers stood the war looked finished and forgotten. They could see everything from up there, all the way to the end of the field: the tents, the animals, the weapons, the food, every other Muslim below filling his arms while they sat on an empty hill holding a position no enemy seemed to want anymore.

This is exactly where shaytan reached them. Twenty minutes passed, then thirty, then more, and the waiting did its slow work. They began to argue among themselves, and the Sheikh asks us to picture it as it really must have been, not one tidy exchange but a long back-and-forth, because no one changes his mind in an instant. The more men who leaned toward going, the easier it became for the next man to lean with them, because a person stands firm alone with difficulty and follows a crowd with ease. Their commander, Abdullah ibn Jubayr, planted himself against the drift and reminded them of the exact words of the Prophet ﷺ, that they were to hold this place until the command came. By Allah, he said, I will not leave my position until he sends for me. Around forty of the fifty came down anyway. Ten men stayed with their commander on the hill. And the one door that the Prophet ﷺ had bolted shut with an absolute order, the single weak point in the whole arrangement, swung quietly open.

The one who never stopped looking back

There was a man among the fleeing Quraysh who was not running for his life. Khalid ibn al-Walid was retreating the way only a born general retreats, cautiously, glancing back over his shoulder, hunting the whole time for the one mistake the enemy might make. He was barely into his twenties. Uhud was his first real battle; he had not been at Badr. And he was about to become the only man in history ever to inflict a genuine military defeat on the Muslims, before Allah chose him for Islam and he went on to carve out half an empire under its banner. The Sheikh pauses on him here with something close to wonder: Allah gives certain people a talent the way He gives others health or wealth, and this young man on a fleeing horse, watching and waiting, had the talent of war in him in full.

The moment the forty archers came down, his mind moved. The hill was bare. And here the Sheikh sets aside the popular picture, the one many books repeat, of Khalid circling the entire mountain of Uhud to come at the Muslims from the far rear. Look at the mountain, he says, and the picture collapses. To go all the way around it would take hours, hours the battle did not contain; the Muslims would have packed up and left before he ever arrived. The more careful reading is simpler and more brutal. Khalid saw the gap from a distance and swung his horsemen back in along a low watercourse, one of those dry channels that fill only in the rains and run sunk beneath the level of the land, so that a rider inside it cannot be seen by anyone on open ground. Perhaps a hundred horsemen, perhaps a hundred and fifty; enough to do terrible damage, not so few that they would do little, not so many that the slaughter would have been even worse. They poured up through the gap the archers had left, and in one stroke Khalid cut the Muslim force clean in two. On one side, the camp of the Prophet ﷺ; on the other, the scattered men gathering spoils; and his own cavalry driving between them. The very terrain that had protected the Muslims all morning, the high hill and the sheltering channel, had turned in an hour into the trap that caught them.

The voice calling from behind

The first person on the whole field to see the horsemen coming was the Prophet ﷺ himself. He was watchful where everyone else had grown comfortable, uneasy in exactly the place the others felt safe, and so he caught the flank before any other eye did. And then he did the most dangerous thing he could possibly have done.

He was the one target the entire enemy army wanted. Every man there knew that to bring him down was to end the matter. The safe move, the move any of us would have made by instinct, was to slip away quietly, to not draw a single eye, to protect the one head the whole battle turned on. Instead he stood his ground and shouted at the top of his voice, calling out to the believers scattered across the field that the attack was coming from behind them, take your guard, they are at your backs. And every Muslim there knew that voice; there was no mistaking who was calling, and no commander but him. By shouting he handed the enemy his own location, deliberately, to buy the men in front of him the seconds they needed to turn and arm. It was, the Sheikh says plainly, the bravest thing imaginable, and without it the slaughter that followed would have been double or triple what it was.

This is the scene Allah preserved forever in the next verse, the believers fleeing up the slope without turning to look at anyone, and the Messenger ﷺ calling them back from behind.

[Remember] when you [fled and] climbed [the mountain] without looking aside at anyone while the Messenger was calling you from behind. So Allah repaid you with distress upon distress so you would not grieve for that which had escaped you [of victory and spoils of war] or [for] that which had befallen you [of injury and death]. And Allah is [fully] Aware of what you do.Qur'an 3:153

The surprise broke the ranks into chaos. Men were scattered in twos and threes and tens, many of them with their armor off and their swords set aside, because moments before they had been gathering, not fighting. And then a second horror came, of the cruelest kind there is. In the confusion shaytan stirred the two halves of the Muslim force to mistake one another for the enemy in the dust and the helmets and the screaming, and some believers fell to believing swords. The most tragic of these was an old man named Yaman, the father of Hudhayfa, whose own story carries the whole tenderness of the religion. He had been so elderly that the Prophet ﷺ had excused him from the battle entirely, one of only two men sent back to the city for their age. But his iman would not let him sit with the women and children, and he and a companion as old as himself reasoned with each other through the morning: how long have we even got left, a day, an hour? Let us go, and perhaps Allah will seal our lives with the shahada we want. They came late, into the chaos, after Khalid had struck. And there a group of Muslims who had not seen him in the morning ranks and did not know his face surrounded him with their swords. His own son saw it from a distance and screamed, that is my father, that is my father, stop, but no voice carries over a battle like that, and they cut him down before they knew him. When it was over and the men came in grief to ask forgiveness, Hudhayfa said only: may Allah forgive you, and He is the most merciful of the merciful. The Prophet ﷺ ordered the blood-money paid to him for his father, and Hudhayfa gave every coin of it away in charity. He lived, the companions said, in nothing but good after that day, and the Prophet ﷺ later chose him as the keeper of his greatest secret, the one man entrusted with the names of the hypocrites.

The lion of Allah, taken from behind

And now the heaviest loss of the day, the one Dr. Yasir Qadhi handles with the full gravity it has carried for fourteen hundred years: the death of Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib, the uncle of the Prophet ﷺ, his foster-brother, his shield in the worst years of Makkah. You will remember how Hamza came to Islam. He came to it first not out of faith but out of family honor, blurting his belief in a flash of fury the day Abu Jahl had insulted his nephew, declaring himself a Muslim almost before he knew what he was saying, and then refusing on principle to take it back, until the iman he had announced in anger grew real and grew deep, until he became one of the masters of the martyrs. No man on that field could touch him in open combat. He cut down warrior after warrior, and there was simply no honest way to bring him down. So he was not brought down honestly.

There was a slave in the Quraysh army named Wahshi, an expert with the javelin, who had been promised one thing in exchange for one thing: his freedom, if he killed Hamza. His master Jubayr ibn Mutim wanted Hamza dead twice over, once because Hamza had killed Jubayr's uncle at Badr, and once for the sheer symmetry of it, that the Prophet ﷺ should lose an uncle as Jubayr's family had. Wahshi tells the story himself, in the first person, preserved across the years because two of the next generation sought the old man out and asked him. I had no desire to fight anyone, he said, no hatred for Hamza at all; I only wanted to be a free man. So he did not fight like a warrior. He stalked Hamza through the battle, hiding, waiting, slipping from cover to cover, while Hamza fought on ahead of him. And when Hamza had just struck a man down and his back was turned, Wahshi stepped out from behind the brush and threw with all the force he had, aiming, it is said, for the gap in the armor where the rings did not cover, the place a master javelin-thrower would know to find. The spear went clean through him, half its length out the other side.

And here the Sheikh stops, because the next detail is the measure of the man. With the spear already driven through his body, Hamza turned to face his attacker and tried to raise his sword, and only then, his strength gone, did he fall. What a man, the Sheikh says, almost helplessly. There was no other way on earth he could have been brought down. Hamza fell in the first half of Uhud, in the victory, before Khalid ever came around the flank, which means that by the time the tide turned, what was done to his body had already been done. We know what Hind did, and the Sheikh does not hide it, because Allah let our tradition preserve it and there is a wisdom in that. Out of nothing but hatred, out of a will to wound the Prophet ﷺ in the deepest place she could reach, she opened his uncle's body with her own dagger and made of the mutilation a trophy. There is no reason for any of it except the desire to inflict pain on the one she hated. And then, the Sheikh reminds us, gently, almost abruptly, Allah guided her, exactly as He would later guide Wahshi. Both of them lived to enter Islam. Both of them were forgiven. By a wisdom that belongs to Allah alone.

Thirty for one, and the verse that answered him

When the battle was finally over and the Prophet ﷺ walked the field and came at last upon the body of his uncle, he wept. And in the first wave of that grief he said a thing that any of us would understand and that Allah, in His mercy, would not let stand. Were it not for two things, he said, he would have left Hamza where he lay for the birds and the beasts, so that Allah would gather his scattered body on the Day of Resurrection as an honor for him. The two things that stayed his hand were tender and human: that his sister Safiyya, Hamza's full sister by the same mother and father, would grieve unbearably to see him left so, and that the people would take whatever he did as a sunnah after him. And he said, in his sorrow, that if Allah ever gave him victory over the Quraysh, he would mutilate thirty of their dead in answer for this one.

Then Allah sent down the answer from above him, and it is a thing to sit with for a long time. It came in Surah an-Nahl.

And if you punish [an enemy, O believers], punish with an equivalent of that with which you were harmed. But if you are patient - it is better for those who are patient.Qur'an 16:126

If you would punish, punish only with the like of what was done to you, and not a hair beyond it; and if you are patient, that patience is the better thing. When this verse came down, the Prophet ﷺ took back his vow at once and put in its place a command that would outlast him and outlast every one of us: that no dead body is ever to be mutilated, friend or enemy, for as long as this ummah stands. The Sheikh stops here to point at something tender about prophethood itself, something easy to rush past. In a moment of unbearable grief a vow was made, the most human vow there is, and Allah corrected it gently from the heavens, and His servant ﷺ submitted in the same breath and turned the correction into law. Even his sorrow became mercy for us. The one man on that field with every earthly right to revenge, standing over the torn body of the uncle who had loved him and shielded him, was taught to choose restraint, and he chose it, and he made his restraint a permanent gift to everyone who would come after.

The other graves of that day

Hamza did not fall alone, and the Sheikh tells the others so that we feel the full weight of what this ummah was built upon. There was Musab ibn Umayr, who had been the most pampered youth in all of Makkah, the boy whose mother dressed him in new garments and the finest perfumes, who gave up the silk and the scent for chains and hunger when he believed, who was sent ahead as the first emissary to Medina and won whole tribes to Islam by the sheer beauty of how he carried the message. He fell holding the banner. His right hand was struck off and he caught the flag with his left, and his left was struck off and he clutched it against his chest with the stumps of both arms until they killed him with many wounds at once. When they came to bury him he owned a single garment, too short to cover the whole of him; pull it over his head and his feet showed, pull it over his feet and his head showed, and so they covered his head and laid desert grass over his feet. Years later, the companion Khabbab ibn al-Aratt, who had himself been tortured in Makkah and then lived long and was given a house and servants and ease, wept as he told it. Some of us, he said, lived to take our reward in this world. But Musab took nothing here at all. He went with one short garment, and the whole of his reward is waiting for him there.

There was Hanzala, married only the night before, who heard the call and left for the battle without bathing after his wedding night and was struck down within reach of Abu Sufyan himself; the Prophet ﷺ saw the angels washing his body between the sky and the earth and called him forever the one washed by the angels. There was Quzman, a ferocious fighter on the Muslim side whom the companions praised, of whom the Prophet ﷺ said that he was of the people of the Fire, and who, when a single arrow wounded him and the pain became more than he would bear, set the hilt of his own sword against the ground and fell upon the blade and ended his own life, proving the warning true; sometimes, the Sheikh notes, Allah aids this religion even through a corrupt man, but the man's own end is his own. There was al-Usayrim, still a pagan at dawn that morning, who came to the field, accepted Islam on the spot, fought, and was killed, all before a single prayer-time had so much as entered. He never made one sajda in his life, and the Prophet ﷺ said of him that he did little and was rewarded much, and Abu Hurayra would test his students for years with the riddle: who is the man who entered Jannah without ever praying a single prayer? And there was Mukhayriq, of the Jewish tribes of Medina, who held to the treaty when his own people would not, who took up his sword on the Sabbath out of nothing but loyalty to a promise given and told them that if he died his wealth should go to Muhammad ﷺ. He died defending the Muslims, and the Prophet ﷺ called him the best of his people. Loss after loss after loss, and in every single one of them, the Sheikh shows us, something left behind for us to carry.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read Uhud as a tragedy and close the book, a brave army undone by bad luck and a clever enemy, safely sealed in the past. That would be to miss every question it is asking of us. Strip the day down to what actually turned it, and you will not read a setback in your own life the same way again.

It turned on a hill, and on fifty men, and on one instruction that some of them decided did not apply to them that day. Khalid did not break a strong and faithful army; he walked his horsemen through a door that the army's own archers had left open. And those archers were not cowards or traitors. They were believers who had stood through the hard part, who simply grew tired of waiting, who looked at what everyone else was gathering and reasoned that surely the command no longer meant what it had plainly said. That is how the disasters of a life almost always begin: not with some towering, obvious sin, but with one small order from Allah or His Messenger ﷺ that we quietly decide cannot really be meant for us, here, now, in our case. The opening is always in the small disobedience. Guard the small commands, the Sheikh is telling us through the whole of this day, because the hill is lost there, long before the cavalry ever appears.

And it turned on the dunya, on a field of spoils spread before tired and victorious men. Watch what it cost them. They reached for the wealth and broke their ranks to do it, and then the wealth was not even theirs to keep, and the victory slipped away with it, so that they ended the day holding neither. Whoever puts this world ahead of everything, the Sheikh says, often ends with neither this world nor the next. Ask yourself honestly how many times the thing you broke your discipline to grab turned to nothing in your hand, and the better thing you let go of to grab it never came back. The believer is not forbidden the dunya; he is forbidden to let it be the thing he reaches for first, at the cost of the command he was given.

And then there is the grief, and what was done with it. Over the body of the uncle he loved more than almost anyone alive, mutilated by people who hated him, the Prophet ﷺ felt the most justified anger a human heart can feel, and made a vow out of it. And Allah took that vow, gently, and turned it into its opposite, and his servant ﷺ obeyed and made restraint the law forever. This is the hardest thing the day asks, and the most needed. Our most justified anger, the wound we did nothing to deserve, is precisely the place where Allah watches most closely what we do next. Grief does not license cruelty. The greater your right to strike back, the greater the worship in choosing not to, and the man with the most perfect right to revenge in all of history chose patience over the body of the one he loved, and gave that choice to us.

So take this day down into your ordinary life, and do not leave the most hopeful thing in it behind. The hand that threw the spear at Hamza, and the woman who tore his body apart, both lived to say there is no god but Allah, and both were forgiven. Jubayr, who sent the slave to kill him, felt the first stirring of faith in his chest as a prisoner tied to a pillar, hearing the Prophet ﷺ recite in the night the words of Surah at-Tur, until he reached the place where Allah asks the human being who he thinks he is:

Or were they created by nothing, or were they the creators [of themselves]?Qur'an 52:35

My heart, Jubayr said, was about to leap out of my chest. He did not believe that night; Allah had His own timing for him, as He had His own timing for Wahshi, and for Hind, and has His own timing for you. But every one of them came in eventually, and every one of them was received. So this day that begins with a hill abandoned for a fistful of spoils, and runs through the worst loss the Prophet ﷺ had yet known, and through brothers cutting down brothers in the dust, ends on the one note that no setback can drown: that Allah guides whom He wills, in His own time, and that the very people who inflicted the deepest wound of his life were not for one moment placed beyond His mercy when at last they turned to Him. If the door was open to them, the Sheikh leaves you to feel it for yourself, then stop deciding it is closed to anyone, including you.

May Allah forgive the martyrs of Uhud and raise them in the highest gardens; may He gather us with Hamza, the lion of Allah and the master of the martyrs, and with Musab who held the banner with what was left of his arms; may He keep our feet where He has placed them when the spoils of this world are spread before our eyes, guard our anger when we are most wronged, and never let us decide that His mercy has run out, for ourselves or for anyone at all. And may He send His peace and His blessings upon Muhammad ﷺ, whose every sorrow He turned into guidance for us, and let us drink from his hand a drink after which there is no thirst.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 48: the martyrdom of Hamza, Uhud part 3 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (3:152, 3:153, 16:126, 52:35), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 49 of 104

The Battle of Uhud, part 4

Blood on his face, forgiveness on his lips

When this day of the seerah opens, the battle has already turned inside out. The Muslims who woke up winning are scattered across the plain gathering spoils, certain the war is over; Khalid ibn al-Walid's horsemen have swung back in from behind; and no one even saw the counterattack coming until it was already among them. Dr. Yasir Qadhi opens by showing you his workbench. What happened around the Prophet ﷺ in this single hour survives in ten or fifteen separate reports, and every early source threads them together in a different order, and how to fit them, what came first, how to fill the gaps, we will never know with full certainty, because we were not there. So what follows is one faithful way of stitching the pieces together: the firm as firm, the likely as likely, and some seams left showing where the reports go quiet.

This is day forty-nine, the fourth of five at the mountain, and it is the day of his blood ﷺ. The day a rock, an arrow, and a sword reached the most beloved face in all of creation, and the day the people around him showed exactly what they were built from.

Nine men between him and two hundred

Picture the field as the hour breaks open. Most of the army is dispersed in the certainty that it is finished, and the contingent of Quraysh is coming up behind them before anyone understands what is happening. One voice never stopped watching. The Prophet ﷺ was shouting that the enemy was at their backs, trying to pull them around to face it. The Qur'an keeps the exact sound of that moment, the climbing and the fleeing and the voice that would not stop calling from the rear:

[Remember] when you [fled and] climbed [the mountain] without looking aside at anyone while the Messenger was calling you from behind. So Allāh repaid you with distress upon distress so you would not grieve for that which had escaped you [of victory and spoils of war] or [for] that which had befallen you [of injury and death]. And Allāh is [fully] Aware of what you do.Qur'an 3:153

Now count who was actually beside him. Nine men. Two of the Muhajirun, Talha ibn Ubaydillah and Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas, radiyallahu anhuma, both of them of the ten given the glad tidings of Paradise, and seven of the Ansar whose names no book ever learned. Every other companion was somewhere else on that wide field, because the assumption everywhere was that the battle had been won. Nine men do not withstand two hundred. But a mountain can, and some of these Ansar, the Sheikh lets us assume, knew Uhud the way you know your own courtyard, while the Quraysh, staring up at a wall of rock, knew nothing at all. So the small band fell back and climbed, drawing the Prophet ﷺ toward shelter, trusting the height and distance to buy the minutes that mattered.

The pursuers kept closing, and a question began to repeat that should never have to be asked. Who will take on these men, and he will be my companion in Jannah? Every time, Talha answered first: I will. And every time, the Prophet ﷺ told him, stay where you are, O Talha, and sent one of the Ansar instead, keeping his best fighter for the end. And so, the Sheikh reconstructs, one by one the men of the Ansar walked back down the slope, very likely as decoys, drawing the hunt the wrong way while the Prophet ﷺ slipped into the rock. One by one, all seven died as martyrs. That, the Sheikh says, is exactly why we do not know their names: seven men of the highest rank of sacrifice in the religion, who spent their lives so that he ﷺ could keep his, and the books could not preserve so much as what they were called.

The most dangerous hour of the whole seerah

The shelter they were climbing toward is still in the mountain today. To call it a cave flatters it. It is barely an opening into the rock, not a chamber you walk into but a slither, a crevice with room for one body, maybe two. Someone in that group knew exactly where it was, and somewhere on that climb, with the Ansar falling behind him, the Prophet ﷺ reached it with Talha and Sa'd.

Stop here, because Dr. Yasir Qadhi stops here. The people of Ta'if were vicious beyond telling, but they meant to humiliate him ﷺ, not kill him. The cave of the hijrah held danger, but it was trackers who came one at a time, and even Suraqah wanted his prize alive. This is none of that. This is open war, no holds barred, armed men scouring the rocks below for the single purpose of ending his life. And so the Sheikh makes his judgment, and Allah knows best: in the entire seerah, there is perhaps nothing as critical, nothing as dangerous, as this exact point in time. Everything the religion was and would become balanced on a crack in a rock, two guards, and the protection of Allah alone.

Talha planted himself at the opening. When the Prophet ﷺ kept leaning out to read the field, Talha pleaded with him: may my mother and father be ransomed for you, O Messenger of Allah, do not look out, some stray arrow may strike you. My chest instead of your chest. And Sa'd knelt with his bow while the Prophet ﷺ did a thing that should rearrange how you picture him in this hour. He was gathering fallen arrows off the ground and handing them to his archer, one by one, saying, shoot, may my father and mother be ransomed for you. The companions said that phrase over him ﷺ all their lives, and no one ever deserved it as he did. But the direction matters: it means I would give up my own mother and father for your sake, and the narrations record that in his entire life the Prophet ﷺ never once combined both his parents in that phrase for another human being, except for Sa'd, on this day, at this bow. Sa'd carried that single sentence like a decoration until the day he died.

One more attacker came up the rock, and with the seven Ansar gone, Talha went out alone to meet him. He won the fight, but the man's sword took some of his fingers, and the pain pulled the old Arabic cry of hurt out of him. The Prophet ﷺ told him, gently, what that moment had been worth: had you said Bismillah instead, the angels would have lifted you up while the people watched. Not a rebuke for a wounded man, for there is no sin in crying out in pain. It was a measurement of how high Talha had climbed that day, how close the angels had been to carrying him into the sky, and how one word would have done it.

Others were finding their way up now, and among the first was a man of the Ansar. The Prophet ﷺ handed him a bow, collecting weapons off the ground for whoever could use them, and the man shot until the wood went soft in his hands. When his arrows were finished and the enemy's were not, he made his body the shield, reading each arrow out of the air and stepping into its path, until one came that he could only block with his face. It struck his eye. The Prophet ﷺ wept, drew the arrow out with his own hand, and prayed: Allah has protected Your Prophet with this man's face, so make his eye the better of his two and the most precise in sight. The man said afterward that it healed on the spot, and that for the rest of his life that eye saw more sharply than the one that was never touched.

Three wounds, and whose decision it is

How many times was the Prophet ﷺ wounded that day, and by whom? Here the reports themselves disagree. Some say once, some say twice; laid side by side, the Sheikh reads them as at least three separate wounds in three separate places, and the exact order is one of those gray areas where honest scholarship simply says, we were not there. What no report disputes is the blood.

A rock struck his face and split his lip, and there is a terrible irony in the hand that threw it. It belonged to Sa'd's own blood brother, riding that day with the Quraysh: two brothers at Uhud, one in the crevice bleeding to defend the Prophet ﷺ, and one below making him bleed, most likely catching the side of his face as they ran up to the cave. Then an arrow found the small gap in his armor and drove through into his cheek, dislodging a tooth and lodging in the flesh. That was the worst wound of the day, and it would not come loose. And a horseman swung a sword down at his head; a shield blunted the blow, but the strike still crushed the rings of his own helmet into his other cheek. The reports name two different men for that sword, two Abdullahs, and both names are left standing. Two wounds, then, one in each cheek, with iron buried in both: an arrowhead on the one side, the driven links of his own armor on the other.

And Sahih Muslim keeps the sound of the hour those wounds opened: the Prophet ﷺ wiping the blood as it ran down his face into his beard, and as he wiped he said it: how can a people succeed who have done this to their own Prophet? In another wording, how can they hope for Allah's mercy, when he was calling them to their Lord, calling them to Paradise, and this is what they returned to him? Read it slowly. He is not cursing them. He is bleeding, and reasoning, and the reasoning carries the edge of a verdict: surely, after this, there is no way they can be forgiven.

And when the believers returned to Madinah, revelation came down and answered him, in one of the bluntest sentences ever addressed to the most beloved of all creation:

Not for you, [O Muḥammad, but for Allāh], is the decision whether He should [cut them down] or forgive them or punish them, for indeed, they are wrongdoers.Qur'an 3:128

It is, the Sheikh says, a very harsh way even to phrase it: this is none of your affair, you have no share in it at all. Yes, they wounded you, and to say that much is the truth. But to go past it and decide that heaven will not forgive them is not the Prophet's ﷺ to decide. Whether Allah punishes or pardons belongs to Allah alone.

And here Sheikh Yasir stands on this one ayah and makes the claim of the night. If the ummah truly absorbed this single scene, he says, shirk would vanish from it entirely. Here is the Prophet ﷺ himself, the most beloved creation Allah ever made, told plainly and to his face that the file of forgiveness is not in his hands. His work is to deliver the message; the judgment of who is forgiven belongs to the One who sent him. So whoever pins his hopes on a saint, a grave, some righteous soul imagined as the real dispenser of pardon, with Allah reduced to merely ratifying his decisions, has the order of the universe exactly backwards. If the keys were not given to him ﷺ, they were given to no one beneath him. Take your asking, all of it, straight to Allah.

And the human truth sits beside the theological one. He ﷺ is a man. He bleeds, he feels the pain, he is angered by treachery, and he has every right to be; most of us would have said far worse. Then watch what the correction produced. The Prophet ﷺ turned his sentence completely around and began to pray for the very people who had done this to him: O Allah, forgive my people, for they do not know. And forgiveness came looking for them. The clearest proof stands at the head of the army that turned Uhud upside down: the man who led that charge would not die as the Prophet's ﷺ wounder. He would die as Khalid ibn al-Walid, Sayfullah, the drawn Sword of Allah, living evidence that the prayer was heard.

But where was the protection in all of this? One of the companions who was there swore to it: on the day of Uhud he saw two men in the whitest of white fighting in front of the Prophet ﷺ, fighting a fight no human being fights, men he had never seen before that day and never saw again after it. Angels. And angels do not miss an arrow. So the wounds were never a lapse in the guarding of him ﷺ; his life never once left Allah's keeping, and had it not been for that protection he would not have walked off the mountain at all. The injuries were measured, permitted, allowed for a wisdom folded inside them, scars that healed without ever debilitating him. He came down from Uhud wounded. He never came down broken.

And hold the severity of that all the way to nightfall. When he finally reached home, Fatima washed her father's face while Ali poured the water, and no matter how much Ali poured, the bleeding would not stop; by then he had been losing blood for the better part of half a day. So Fatima took a piece of date palm matting and burned it, crumbled the ash and worked it with water into a paste, and pressed it into the wound until it sealed shut. A daughter gluing her father's face closed with her own hands. That, in the end, is what this day cost him ﷺ.

The rumor, and the man who smelled Jannah

Somewhere in the same chaos, Mus'ab ibn Umayr fell. He shared the Prophet's ﷺ complexion, his features are said to have echoed him, and one report adds that he may have been carrying a cloak borrowed from him ﷺ. So when the same horseman who had swung the sword at the Prophet ﷺ now cut Mus'ab down, he looked at the man he had killed and believed he had done the unthinkable. He screamed it across the whole field: I have killed Muhammad ﷺ! And the lie ran through Quraysh, and through scattered knots of Muslims, like fire through dry grass.

Strangely, the lie carried a mercy inside it, which we only see looking back. By now perhaps four of every five of the enemy had already drifted off the field; only Khalid's smaller force was still fighting. Nobody wants to risk death once the goal is achieved, and the instant the word spread that the deed was done, the appetite for war died with it: the same false sentence that broke the believers' hearts began, quietly, to empty the field of their enemies. But the hearts were broken all the same. Some men wandered without direction. Some sat down where they stood and let their swords fall, because what is a sword even for now?

Then a man walked into that despair and refused it. Anas ibn al-Nadr, radiyallahu anhu, the uncle of Anas ibn Malik, found a group of companions sitting in their grief and asked why they were not fighting. They told him: have you not heard? The Prophet ﷺ has been killed. His answer deserves to be carved somewhere you will see it every day: even if Muhammad ﷺ has been killed, the Lord of Muhammad has not been killed. And what will you do with your lives after him? Get up and die upon what he died upon. Then, passing an old friend among the Ansar, he called out: by Allah, I can smell the fragrance of Jannah, coming to me from behind this mountain. And he prayed one of the most beautiful prayers ever spoken on a battlefield: O Allah, I seek Your forgiveness for what these have done, meaning the Muslims who had fled, and I free myself before You of what those have done, meaning the army of Quraysh. And he threw himself in.

They found him afterward with more than eighty wounds, his body so cut and disfigured that not one person could say who he was, until his sister came and knew him by the very tips of his fingers. A hadith in Bukhari preserves his stand, and over a soul like his Allah sent down what the ummah still recites:

Among the believers are men true to what they promised Allāh. Among them is he who has fulfilled his vow [to the death], and among them is he who awaits [his chance]. And they did not alter [the terms of their commitment] by any alteration -Qur'an 33:23

Among them, the ayah says, is the one who has already fulfilled his vow, and among them is the one still waiting his turn. Anas ibn al-Nadr had fulfilled his.

Found by the brightness of his eyes

Meanwhile the searchers who refused the rumor kept looking, hoping they would find him alive. The first man on the field to recognize the Prophet ﷺ was Ka'b ibn Malik, and you should hold onto that name, because his own mountain of a story is waiting years from now, at Tabuk. Helmet and mail had swallowed the Prophet's ﷺ face so you could not make out who the armored figure was, and Ka'b knew him anyway, by his eyes alone, by the brightness shining out of them. Joy did what joy does: he forgot himself and shouted at the top of his lungs, O Muslims, rejoice, the Messenger of Allah ﷺ is alive! The Prophet ﷺ signaled him to be quiet, because a hunted man does not announce where he stands. But the word was already running through the believers in reverse, the true word chasing down the false one that had run the same paths an hour before.

Ka'b reached him and handed over his own armor, perhaps because the Prophet's ﷺ was spent, perhaps so no enemy eye could pick out which armored man was him, since in those days men were known by their armor as much as their faces. Ka'b finished the day with no armor of his own and more than twenty wounds: the ledger of Uhud keeps recording the same entry, his pain ﷺ transferred onto people who volunteered to carry it.

Now they came up the rock in numbers, Abu Bakr and Umar and Ali and Zubayr and the rest. Abu Bakr told the story himself, years later. When the people rushed up, he said, there was already one man standing over him, fighting off everyone who tried to approach, and I said in my heart, O Allah, let it be Talha; if anyone is to have this honor, let it be Talha. And it was Talha. And while I was running, another man overtook me, flying up the mountain light as a gazelle out of sheer joy that the Prophet ﷺ was alive, and it was Abu Ubaydah ibn al-Jarrah. The two of them reached him together and saw the iron lodged in each cheek. Abu Bakr's telling remembers two arrowheads; other reports count one arrowhead and one driven helmet ring; the two wounds themselves are beyond dispute.

Abu Bakr moved to draw the first piece out, and Abu Ubaydah begged him, by the right of love, let me do this, and kept begging until the soft and gentle Abu Bakr gave way. And then think what he faced. The iron was sunk into the cheek of the Prophet ﷺ; to grip it with his fingers and pull risked tearing the wound wider. So Abu Ubaydah closed his teeth on the metal and rocked it loose, gently, gently, taking the pain into his own jaw instead of that beloved face, until the iron came free, and one of his own teeth came with it. Then he insisted on the second extraction too, and paid the same price again. Abu Ubaydah walked the rest of his life missing three, maybe four teeth, a gap he surely wore the way Sa'd wore his sentence. And the Sheikh leaves the arithmetic in your hands: if drawing the iron out cost a strong man his teeth, what did it cost the face that had carried it in, on both sides at once?

He is mine

Back in Makkah there had been a man whose hatred had a hobby. Ubayy ibn Khalaf mocked the Prophet ﷺ like no one else, and he kept a horse expressly for the purpose, boasting that he was feeding it the choicest grain to fatten it strong, so that one day, from its back, he would kill Muhammad ﷺ. His record was already long and filthy; he was among the men who had heaped the bloody innards of a slaughtered camel onto the Prophet ﷺ while he was in sujud. And to this boast, and only to this boast, the Prophet ﷺ ever gave an answer in kind: no, rather it is I who will kill you, insha'Allah.

At Uhud, Ubayy saw him from afar, most likely in this same stretch when only a few were near him, riding that very horse, and he wheeled it to charge. There is no outrunning a horseman, so the companions began to wall the Prophet ﷺ in with their own bodies, and he waved them off with a sentence that still raises the hair on your arms: leave him. He is mine. He took a spear from one of his men, not a sword, not a bow, a spear, read the charge, slipped the blow, and drove the point into the gap at the neck of Ubayy's armor. It caught in the armor and only went in a little, nicking the neck; by the plain arithmetic of the battlefield, a scratch. Ubayy wheeled back to his own lines screaming that Muhammad ﷺ had killed him.

They pulled off his armor, examined the small wound, and laughed: calm down, this is nothing, it will heal. He refused every word of comfort, and his reasoning is the part to keep. He told them: he promised me he would kill me, and by Allah, had he only spat on me, I would have died of it. Too late, and completely, he understood whose word he had spent his life mocking. He died on the road home, in a valley outside Makkah, never even reaching the city, buried in no man's land: the only human being ever killed by the hand of the Prophet ﷺ himself, a dishonor Allah reserved for him alone. And the books of sira add a chilling epilogue. Years later a traveler passing that valley by night saw a fire, and a figure inside it croaking thirst, thirst, and a voice warned him: give him nothing, for this is the one whom the Messenger of Allah ﷺ killed. A hadith seals it: no one earns a more severe anger from Allah than a man the Prophet ﷺ killed with his own hand.

A day for a day, and the answer for Hubal

As the fighting guttered out, the field began to belong to the women. Anas ibn Malik, still a boy, remembered them all his life: women of the believers with their garments hitched up so they could run, carrying water skins between the wells of Madinah and the wounded on the slope, washing wounds, nursing the dying. The verses of hijab had not yet been revealed, the lecture notes, and beyond that, necessity had its own rulings that day. Women did not usually march out with the armies, but Uhud was fought on Madinah's own doorstep, and so once the danger thinned its women came out, and did not wait to be asked.

Then, from below, the voice of Abu Sufyan, come to close the day as the leader of Makkah. He cried out as loud as he could: is Muhammad among you? Answer me. The Prophet ﷺ commanded the believers, do not answer him. Is the son of Abu Quhafah among you? He meant Abu Bakr. Silence. Is the son of al-Khattab among you? He meant Umar. Silence. And the Sheikh stops on the list itself. Here is a man still upon his idolatry, and yet as early as the third year he names, with perfect clarity, exactly who stands first and second after the Messenger ﷺ. How unfortunate are those who came later and could not see what even a pagan enemy saw on a battlefield. Hearing nothing, Abu Sufyan exulted: then we have killed them all. And that was more than Umar could carry. He roared back: you lie, O enemy of Allah; every one of those you named is alive, and Allah has left for you what will yet bring you grief.

Abu Sufyan answered with the ledger of war. A day for a day, he said, a day of Uhud for your day of Badr, and war goes in turns. Then, with a strange flat honesty, he called out that they would find some of their dead mutilated: I did not command it, he said, and I am not angry about it either. And Umar gave the dead their answer: they are not the same. Our dead are in Jannah, and your dead are in the Fire. And then Abu Sufyan reached for his god and cried out: be exalted, O Hubal! Hubal, the great idol of the Quraysh, the very stone their whole idolatry had grown up around.

And now the Prophet ﷺ, who had ordered silence over his own name and his own life, broke that silence for the name of Allah. Answer him, he said. They asked, what shall we say? He said, say: Allah is Higher and more Majestic. Abu Sufyan tried once more: we have al-Uzza, and you have no Uzza. The Prophet ﷺ said, answer him: Allah is our Protector, and you have no protector. One of the classical commentators stops here in wonder, and the Sheikh stops with him, because it is the perfect place to stop. When Abu Sufyan asked whether he ﷺ was even alive, the Prophet ﷺ would not grant him so much as the dignity of a reply; contempt was the fitting answer to a taunt about himself. But the instant an idol was raised over Allah, silence became impossible, and he ﷺ commanded the reply at once. Everything he swallowed for his own honor; nothing for his Lord's. Silence in its place, speech in its place, and he knew which was which. Abu Sufyan turned away with one last word, a promise to meet again at Badr a year from that day, and the army of Makkah rode off the field. The mountain fell quiet, and the hardest counting of the whole seerah, the long rows of the shuhada of Uhud, was waiting in the days just ahead.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this hour as a war story and leave it there, all spears and horses and narrow escapes. That would be to walk past the three questions it puts to us, and each one is meant to follow you home.

The first is about forgiveness, and who owns it. Bleeding from both cheeks, his blood running into his beard, the Prophet ﷺ said the most human thing imaginable: surely a people who did this cannot be forgiven. And the answer that came down was not a comfort but a correction, blunt as a closed door: not for you is the decision. If even he ﷺ was told that the file of pardon and Paradise was not in his hands, then it is in no one's hands beneath him: not a saint's, not a grave's, not any imagined intermediary's. The whole architecture of shirk collapses in this one scene. So when you have something to ask for, mercy, forgiveness, a way out, do not route it through anyone. Take it where he ﷺ was told to leave it. Take it straight to Allah.

The second is about protection, and what it does and does not promise. A companion watched two angels fighting before the Prophet ﷺ as no human fights, and on that same field he ﷺ took a rock to the lip, an arrow through the cheek, a sword crushing his helmet into his face. He was guarded perfectly and he bled badly, and there is no contradiction in it, because protection was never a promise of painlessness. His wounds were measured and permitted, blood with a wisdom folded inside it, while his life never for one instant left Allah's keeping. Hold that against your own scars. The hard thing that reached you was not proof that Allah had looked away. It may have been the most carefully guarded thing in your life.

And the third is about love, and what it looks like when it stops being a feeling and becomes a verb. Look at the bodies offered up on that slope: Talha's chest set in front of the Prophet's ﷺ chest at the crevice, an archer's arrows fed to him from the Prophet's ﷺ own hand, a man stepping into the path of arrows until one took his eye and thanking Allah for the trade, Abu Ubaydah's teeth left in the wound in exchange for the iron in that beloved face, Anas ibn al-Nadr walking toward eighty wounds with the fragrance of Jannah already in his lungs, Ka'b giving away his armor and keeping twenty wounds for himself. Not one of them stopped to deliberate. And here is the part that asks something of you, because his person is now beyond your shielding. You cannot put your chest in front of his ﷺ. But you can stand in front of his sunnah when it is mocked the way Ubayy mocked him, you can refuse to hand Allah's verdicts to any of His creation, and you can answer every Hubal of your own age, every false thing exalted over your Lord, with the words he commanded on that mountain: Allah is Higher and more Majestic.

So carry one thing down from Uhud into your ordinary days. When the worst news comes, and one day it will, say what Anas ibn al-Nadr said: even if everyone I leaned on is gone, the Lord of all of them does not die, and I will live and die upon what they lived and died upon. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, whose face bled for this religion and whose tongue asked forgiveness for the very people who made it bleed; gather us under his banner with Talha and Sa'd and the seven unnamed of the Ansar and the man who smelled Jannah behind the mountain, let us shield his sunnah now that his person is beyond our shielding, and give us at last to drink from his hand a drink after which there is no thirst.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 49: Uhud, part 4, the Prophet ﷺ wounded (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (3:153, 3:128, 33:23), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 50 of 104

The Battle of Uhud, part 5: the graves, the grief, and the verdict

Seventy graves, and a meeting at the fountain

The battle is over. What is left is everything a battle leaves behind: seventy bodies on the slopes, a city of women listening for hoofbeats, an enemy not yet far enough away to be safe, and a question that will be asked for fourteen centuries. Did we lose? Today Dr. Yasir Qadhi closes the five days of Uhud with its gentlest and its hardest scenes at once: shrouds and shared graves, wailing and a newborn, one more march on wounded legs, and an accounting that takes the word defeat and turns it inside out.

This is the last day at the mountain. By nightfall the martyrs will be in the ground and the Quraysh will be running for Makkah, frightened off by a rumor they never knew Allah had arranged. And before the chapter ends you will hear the Prophet ﷺ himself, eight years later and one week before his own death, still speaking of these seventy with the love of a man saying farewell. The arc of Uhud does not close at Uhud. It closes at a fountain.

Two shrouds and a drawn lot

The field was still raw when a woman came running across it. The Prophet ﷺ saw the figure in the distance and did not know her, saying only: the lady, the lady. Whoever she was, she should be spared what this ground would show her. It was Safiyya, his own aunt, the full sister of Hamza by the same mother and father, and the one who recognized her first was her son, Zubayr ibn al-Awwam. He ran to turn her back, and she, a grieving sister and a mother both, pushed him off her chest: I have no need of you now. She had heard her brother was dead, and she had heard what had been done to his body.

Then Zubayr said the only thing in the world that could have stopped her. It is not me, he said, the Prophet ﷺ is the one who says you should not go on. And she halted in her tracks. Hold the two halves of that scene together, because they are the measure of these people: agitated enough to shove her own son aside, and obedient enough to freeze mid stride at one word relayed from her nephew ﷺ. She handed Zubayr two cloths she had carried with her. I bought these for my brother, she said. Shroud him in them.

Zubayr went to wrap Hamza, radiyallahu anhu, and found lying beside him a man of the Ansar with no shroud at all, not even one. He could not bring himself to give his uncle two cloths while the Ansari had none, so the companions drew lots over them, and each martyr was buried in the cloth that fell to him. Sit with what that scene actually shows. The generation that carried this religion on its back was so poor that men cast lots over burial cloth, and so just that a gift bought with love for the Prophet's ﷺ own uncle was shared out with a stranger on the ground. Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi draws a quiet point of fiqh out of the grief. When the need of the whole community is this desperate, the public welfare can take precedence over a private intention, and what was donated for one man may be turned toward the many. It is wajib upon a community with the means to give every one of its dead at least a single shroud, and so the personal gift bent to the public need, and the family of Hamza accepted it. But, he is careful to add, the one who may make that call is the person in authority, not any hand that simply reaches out and takes.

Bury the one who carried the most Qur'an

Then the Prophet ﷺ walked the field and surveyed the dead, and when he came at last to Hamza he wept, until the reports say he sobbed. This was not the clean death of a soldier; it was mutilation, deliberate and personal. The nose, the fingers, the body opened, the liver torn out. No one on that field had been treated like Hamza, because no one's pain could reach the Prophet ﷺ the way Hamza's would. And for one moment his grief spoke as a vow: if Allah ever gives me victory over them, I will do to thirty of their dead what they did to this one man.

Heaven answered the vow at once, in a verse of Surah an-Nahl.

And if you punish [an enemy, O believers], punish with an equivalent of that with which you were harmed. But if you are patient - it is better for those who are patient.Qur'an 16:126

If you punish, then like for like, one for one, never thirty for one, and patience is better still. He ﷺ took the revelation whole. He never took that revenge, and from that day he outlawed the mutilation of the dead forever, friend or enemy, for as long as this ummah stands. The men who had desecrated his uncle were the very reason a mercy entered the law of war.

Seventy graves could not be dug one by one by exhausted, wounded men, so they buried two and three and four to a grave, as deep as they could manage. And he ﷺ gave one instruction that should stop you cold. Let the one who carried the most Qur'an be lowered in first. Even into the earth, the Qur'an goes first. Look, the Sheikh says, at the honor Allah gives the people of His Book, living and dead. This is the Book through which Allah raises some people and abases others; showing respect to the one who carries it is part of showing respect to Allah Himself, named in the sunnah beside honoring the grey-haired and the just ruler. In the most traumatic hour the community had ever lived through, with mutilated bodies waiting in shared pits, precedence still belonged to revelation.

Standing among those graves he ﷺ gathered the living and spoke to lift them, and among the words the books preserved he said: I will be a witness over these on the Day of Judgment. Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi untangles a misunderstanding that lasted through medieval Islam, where some scholars held that he had prayed the funeral prayer over the dead of Uhud. He did not, the Sheikh explains, and the narration in the Musnad of Imam Ahmad makes the difference plain: he made du'a for them, not janazah, for the martyr is beyond needing the funeral prayer. The supplication itself is preserved: praise of Allah for whatever He decrees, a plea for the good of this world and safety on the Last Day, that they die upon iman, and that Allah Himself take vengeance on those who had done this to them. Then he told the living what the dead had won, in hadith famous to every believer: the martyr is raised on the Day of Resurrection with his wounds as fresh as the moment he was struck, their color the color of blood and their fragrance the fragrance of musk. When the counting was done, the Muslims had lost around seventy, and in no battle did the Ansar ever pay more: Ibn Ishaq counted sixty five, Ibn Hisham added five to bring it to seventy, and the bulk of those dead were Ansar. Of the Muhajirun the two great losses were Hamza and Mus'ab ibn Umayr.

One prisoner out of three thousand

Three thousand men had marched on Madinah, and when the field finally cleared, the Muslims held exactly one prisoner of war, found cowering where the fighting had been. One, out of three thousand. There must be a story in that, the Sheikh says, and indeed there is, and it explains why Allah let this one man be the one humiliated.

His name was Abu Azzah, and he was a poet. He had been captured at Badr, his ransom set at four thousand dirhams, and he had begged the Prophet ﷺ for mercy: I am a man of only daughters, with no one to ransom me, be generous to me. The Prophet ﷺ, soft of heart, freed him for nothing at all, on a single condition: let me never find you raised against us again. Then, as the Quraysh were gathering for Uhud, Abu Sufyan came asking him for war poetry. At first Abu Azzah refused: I gave Muhammad ﷺ my word. So came the sweetening. Three thousand men cannot lose, Abu Sufyan told him; if we win, you share the spoils, and if you happen to fall, I will keep your daughters as my own and they will inherit my wealth. And he went.

He broke a merciful promise for money on either outcome, win or die, and Allah does not love the treacherous. Out of three thousand men, the one whom Allah let fall captive, and the one executed on that field, was the man who had betrayed a mercy shown to him. And the lecture sets a rule down beside the story, one that runs against every instinct: in this religion you may answer like with like in almost everything, an eye for an eye, a wound for a wound, but you may never answer treachery with treachery. Khayana is forbidden even as revenge. If a man betrays you, you may not betray him back, because betrayal itself is haram. Your word is a debt, and Abu Azzah died proving it.

A city learning how to grieve

Madinah had lined its streets, women and children scanning the returning ranks for the faces that were not among them. Then the wailing began, the loud, tearing kind, with beaten breasts and torn clothes and the cries of who will care for us now, how will we live without you. It rose from every quarter of the city at once, because seventy households had just been emptied in a single afternoon, and wailing had not yet been forbidden. Reaching his own home through that sound, the Prophet ﷺ said something that breaks the heart: but Hamza has no women to weep for him. Hamza had never married; there were no women of his own house to raise their voices for him. The women of the Ansar heard it, and they gathered at the Prophet's ﷺ door and wailed for Hamza. He came out and praised them and prayed good for them, and then, their kindness honored, he closed the door on the custom itself, and from that day wailing became haram in the sharia. Notice what was forbidden and what was not. He himself had wept over Hamza; the tears were never the crime. It was the screaming against the decree of Allah, the going-mad of grief, that ended at Uhud.

The grief kept surfacing in him ﷺ. Shortly afterward a son was born to an Ansari couple, and when the father came to ask what to name him, he answered: name him after the most beloved of names to me, Hamza. Madinah's newest life was given Uhud's heaviest loss to carry, and the ummah was taught something tender in passing, that keeping alive the names of the beloved who have gone before us, passing them down to our newborns, is a good way of love and remembrance. This was early; later he ﷺ would teach that the names most beloved to Allah are Abdullah and Abd ar-Rahman. But in this season, the name on his lips was Hamza.

He went in person to break the news where it would land hardest, and we may assume, the Sheikh says, that he chose those with the least family and the most need of consolation. He came to Hamna bint Jahsh and said: ihtasibi, brace yourself and seek your reward with Allah. One word, and inside it lives the whole of bad news softened by patience. For whom, she asked. Your brother, he said. She said inna lillahi wa inna ilayhi raji'un and made du'a for him, grieved but steady. Ihtasibi, he said a second time. For whom, she asked. Mus'ab ibn Umayr, he said. Her husband. And she screamed. Mus'ab, the once-pampered youth of Makkah who had given up everything and died owning a single short garment, was her husband, and she who had borne her brother's death with inna lillahi could not bear this. He ﷺ observed afterward, in words the books kept, that the husband holds a place in the heart of his wife that nothing else holds, a love Allah places there like a kind of miracle, which is why the mourning for a husband is four months and ten days where the mourning for any other is three.

And then there is the woman whose name no one wrote down. Father, brother, husband, all three taken at Uhud, her whole immediate family gone in one day, and she had also heard a rumor that the Prophet ﷺ himself was dead. As each blow landed she asked one question only: how is the Prophet ﷺ? He is well, they kept telling her, and she answered: until I see him. She would not be consoled by report. When her eyes finally found him ﷺ alive among the returning men, she spoke the line the ummah has never let go of: kullu musibatin ba'daka jalal. Every calamity after you is small.

Three days at Hamra al-Asad

An army that leaves can turn around, and on the road south the Quraysh were already debating exactly that. The Prophet ﷺ knew it without a single scout, knew it the way Allah taught him directly. So the very next morning, Sunday the sixteenth of Shawwal, with the city's wounds one night old, he called for a force to shadow the enemy and set one hard condition: only those who had stood at Uhud the day before might come. The three hundred who had turned back were not invited.
