Seventy wounded men answered, and he sent them ahead under Ali ibn Abi Talib to camp at a place called Hamra al-Asad, and gave them a piece of field-craft no academy ever taught him ﷺ. Watch what the Quraysh are riding, he said. If they have mounted their horses and left the camels, they are wheeling back for Madinah; if they are riding their camels and leading the horses, it is the long haul home to Makkah. They were riding the camels. He ﷺ joined the camp the next day and sat there three full days, the mauled army openly daring the victors to come back and finish what they had started. Allah set that march into the Qur'an, in a verse of Surah Al Imran.

Those [believers] who responded to Allāh and the Messenger after injury had struck them. For those who did good among them and feared Allāh is a great reward -Qur'an 3:172

Those who answered Allah and His Messenger with their wounds still open. Years later Aisha, radiyallahu anha, would recite this verse to her nephew Urwah ibn al-Zubayr and tell him: both of your fathers are in this verse, my nephew, Zubayr ibn al-Awwam and Abu Bakr, your father's father and your mother's. Both of them had marched out with the wounded to Hamra al-Asad.

Meanwhile the argument the Prophet ﷺ had foreseen was raging in the Quraysh camp. Ikrimah, the son of Abu Jahl, burned to turn back: we have neither killed Muhammad ﷺ nor done one thing worthy of genuine honor. As is so often the way, the elders were the cooler heads and the young men the hot ones, and Safwan ibn Umayya pushed back hard: do not do this. After what we did to their dead, they will be fuming, and those tribes of the Aws and Khazraj who stayed home will not stay home a second time, not after the killing we dealt their people. The Quraysh never knew the inner politics of the hypocrites; all they knew was that many in Madinah had not come out, and Safwan feared every one of them rising now. But Ikrimah's rhetoric was winning the room.

Then Allah moved a piece nobody was watching. Ma'bad al-Khuza'i was a chieftain of Khuza'ah, not yet a Muslim, but a man whose own sense of honor was disgusted by the mutilations, which violated even the pagans' code. He had just come to Madinah to pay the Prophet ﷺ a condolence visit, of his own accord, telling him plainly that he wished the loss had fallen on the other side. On the road out he crossed paths with Abu Sufyan, who trusted him as a fellow pagan fresh from Madinah and asked him eagerly: how did you leave Muhammad ﷺ and his companions? Ma'bad saw his moment. Without ever having heard Safwan speak, he painted Safwan's exact nightmare, color for color: they are coming for you, fuming with rage, and every man who missed the battle is marching beside them now, drums of war beating, sworn to have their vengeance. What do I advise? Flee as fast as your horses will carry you.

That sealed it. When a nobleman as respected as Ma'bad was this terrified, the Quraysh ran for Makkah, frightened off without a single finger lifted from the Muslim side, by a sympathizer the Prophet ﷺ had never sent and did not even know would meet them. War, the Sheikh reminds us, permits the deceiving of an enemy, and Allah's help came as it loves to come, min haythu la yahtasibun, from exactly where no one was counting. When the scouts confirmed the road south was empty, he ﷺ led the seventy home. The Battle of Uhud was over.

So was Uhud a defeat?

Now the accounting, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi refuses to sugarcoat the one true column of loss. The Quraysh lost twenty two men; the Muslims lost at least seventy, perhaps seventy five. Three to one. There is no softening that single statistic, and the Qur'an does not soften it either. It answers the believers' own stunned question in Surah Al Imran.

Why [is it that] when a [single] disaster struck you [on the day of Uḥud], although you had struck [the enemy in the battle of Badr] with one twice as great, you said, "From where is this?" Say, "It is from yourselves [i.e., due to your sin]." Indeed, Allāh is over all things competent.Qur'an 3:165

From where is this? From yourselves. And in passing the verse even fixes the count, for at Badr the believers had dealt the enemy exactly double, seventy killed and seventy taken captive; at Uhud, half of that came back upon them.

Then he opens the rest of the ledger, and the word defeat begins to wobble. Look first at the goals of the two sides. The Muslims set out to defend Madinah, and Madinah stood untouched; the Quraysh set out to obliterate Islam, and failed in every single manner, never even attempting the city. They came for surprise and found the believers armed and waiting, which so unsettled Abu Sufyan that he suspected a spy, and he was right, for the letter of al-Abbas had outrun him from Makkah. The opening of the battle was a Muslim rout of the Quraysh. The field at the end of the day was held by the Muslims; it was the Quraysh who withdrew. Prisoners: one to nothing, and one is more than zero. The pursuit: it was the wounded who chased the victors, three days at Hamra al-Asad while the Quraysh hurried home. And the trade route to Syria, the strategic prize this entire war is about, stayed exactly as choked as before. Ibn al-Qayyim, in his summary of Zad al-Ma'ad, lays down the closing argument from the enemy's own mouth: Ikrimah's confession that they had done nothing worthy of honor. No army that won talks like that.

So Uhud was not a victory, and it was not a defeat. It was an uneven thing, the Sheikh judges: the Muslims did not gain what they had hoped, but the Quraysh did not gain a fraction of what they came for. From every angle but the body count, Uhud reads as the believers' day. And as for that one real column of loss, Umar ibn al-Khattab answered it forever: qatlana fi janna wa qatlakum fi nar. Our dead are up there in Jannah, and your dead are down there in the Fire. The two are not the same.

The mirror of Badr

To understand what Uhud was for, the Sheikh holds it up against Badr like a mirror, and reads the two through their surahs, Anfal for Badr and Al Imran for Uhud. Of Badr, Allah said one of its purposes was to part the foul from the pure.

[It is] so that Allāh may distinguish the wicked from the good and place the wicked some of them upon others and heap them all together and put them into Hell. It is those who are the losers.Qur'an 8:37

At Badr the foul stood plainly across the field: disbelief facing faith in the open, mushrik against Muslim. Surah Al Imran gives Uhud the very same purpose, with one darker twist.

Allāh would not leave the believers in that [state] you are in [presently] until He separates the evil from the good. Nor would Allāh reveal to you the unseen.Qur'an 3:179

The pure are always the same. But this time the foul wore the faces of neighbors. Uhud's great unmasking was the unmasking of hypocrisy. Until that day the believers had kept a good opinion of the doubters among them, surely they cannot be so bad, surely they will stand with us when it truly matters, and they needed to see nifaq with their own eyes. So Allah let them see it, and named it in the surah.

And that He might make evident those who are hypocrites. For it was said to them, "Come, fight in the way of Allāh or [at least] defend." They said, "If we had known [there would be] battle, we would have followed you." They were nearer to disbelief that day than to faith, saying with their mouths what was not in their hearts. And Allāh is most knowing of what they conceal -Qur'an 3:167

If we had known there would be fighting. That is almost word for word what Abdullah ibn Ubayy had said when he pulled his three hundred back: as if there will really be a battle. Now match the mirror tighter still. At Badr, Allah had shown the enemy to the Prophet ﷺ as few in a dream, and Surah Anfal explains the mercy in it.

[Remember, O Muḥammad], when Allāh showed them to you in your dream as few; and if He had shown them to you as many, you [believers] would have lost courage and would have disputed in the matter [of whether to fight], but Allāh saved [you from that]. Indeed, He is Knowing of that within the breasts.Qur'an 8:43

You would have lost courage, and you would have disputed. Now read the verse of Uhud that the believers were given afterward, almost the same two words, but this time not as a danger Allah shielded them from.

And Allāh had certainly fulfilled His promise to you when you were killing them [i.e., the enemy] by His permission until [the time] when you lost courage and fell to disputing about the order [given by the Prophet (ﷺ)] and disobeyed after He had shown you that which you love. Among you are some who desire this world, and among you are some who desire the Hereafter. Then He turned you back from them [defeated] that He might test you. And He has already forgiven you, and Allāh is the possessor of bounty for the believers.Qur'an 3:152

The two diseases Allah had guarded them from at Badr, losing courage and disputing the command, they walked straight into at Uhud, and added a third: and disobeyed. At Badr, Sa'd ibn Mu'adh had said, march us to the ends of the earth, charge into the sea, and we are right behind you. At Uhud, Abdullah ibn Ubayy said, why should we listen to him when he did not listen to me, and three hundred backs turned. At Badr they were unarmed and willing; at Uhud they had every defense and were swayed by the spoils on the ground. Obedience and sincerity bought Badr its protection; disobedience and a hunger for the dunya bought Uhud its wound. Some of you, the verse says without flinching, desire this world.

And then the same verse does the thing only revelation would dare at the bottom of a catastrophe. He has already forgiven you. The file is closed inside the surah itself. Decades later, when the men who would murder Uthman ibn Affan flung Uhud in his face, you fled that day, he answered them: yes, I did, I was alone and I fled, and have you not read that Allah said He has already forgiven us? If Allah has pardoned it, who are you to reopen it? And Madinah lived that pardon to the letter. No one was reprimanded. No elder told the young men of the shura who had pushed to march out, I told you so. And Hudhayfa, whose own father had been cut down by Muslim swords in the chaos of the battle, lived on among the very men who had done it, forgave them, and gave the blood-money away to the poor. A pardon from heaven, honored on earth, exactly as the Prophet ﷺ taught: once the decision is made and trust is placed in Allah, you go forward, and you do not say if only, for if only opens the door of shaytan.

There remained one more wound to answer, the bitterest of all. Bleeding from the field, his daughter pressing the bandage to his face, the Prophet ﷺ had asked aloud how Allah could ever forgive a people who would do this to their own prophet. The reply, in Surah Al Imran, is startlingly blunt.

Not for you, [O Muḥammad, but for Allāh], is the decision whether He should [cut them down] or forgive them or punish them, for indeed, they are wrongdoers.Qur'an 3:128

Not for you is any of this matter. We excuse his anger, the Sheikh says, all of us, and Allah excused it too, yet the verse stands, and it stands as a guard around tawhid itself. Even the beloved of Allah ﷺ, the greatest of all creation, the seal of the prophets, the mercy to the worlds, does not hold the keys of guidance and punishment, because he is the best of creation and not its Lord. Then Allah showed why those keys are His alone, and the proof runs through the very names of these two battles. At Badr, every senior chief of the Quraysh who marched out was cut down and never came back. At Uhud, nearly every commander who had bloodied the Muslims was later led into Islam: Abu Sufyan, Ikrimah, Safwan, and the architect of the whole counterattack himself, Khalid ibn al-Walid, whom the very Prophet ﷺ he had wounded would one day name a sword from among the swords of Allah. Allah alone knew which of them carried faith inside, and Allah alone decides.

So the Sheikh gathers all five days of Uhud into a single sentence. Honor, izza, comes through sincerity and unity and obedience to Allah and His Messenger ﷺ, even outnumbered, even underarmed; and humiliation comes through disobeying Allah and His Messenger for a piece of this world. Victory is not owed to anyone, not to the pious, not even to the best human being who ever lived. It must be struggled for, every time, by everyone, with no exception at all. Rank is raised in this world and the next only through trial, only through patience and pain. He ﷺ had to bleed at Uhud, the Sheikh says, not for his own sake, for he knew all of this already, but for ours: so that every wounded believer who came after him would know that the road he limps is the road his Prophet ﷺ limped first, mocked worse than he is mocked, wounded worse than he is wounded, and still, through all of it, a mercy to the worlds.

Meet me at the fountain

Eight years pass. It is the last week of the Prophet's ﷺ life, the very year he will leave this world, and Uqbah ibn Amir tells us, in a hadith preserved in Bukhari, what he ﷺ chose to do with that week. He made a long, tender du'a for the dead of Uhud, the kind a man makes when he is bidding farewell to people standing right in front of him. Then he climbed the minbar, sick, near the end, and gave the last public sermon of his life.

I am your forerunner at the fountain, he ﷺ said. Ana faratukum ala al-hawd. I will be a witness over you, and our meeting place is the hawd; it is as if I am looking at it from where I stand right now. The Sahaba did not yet know what he knew, that death was very close. And then came the fear he chose to leave ringing in the ummah's ears, his last counsel from that minbar: I do not fear that you will fall into shirk after me. I fear that the dunya will be opened up for you, and that you will compete with one another for it. Uqbah said: that was the last time I ever saw the face of the Prophet ﷺ.

Hold the two ends of this long arc together now. The deepest wound of Uhud was never the seventy graves; it was the moment when hands reached for spoils and let go of an order, when the love of this world cracked an army that faith had been carrying to victory. And the final sermon of the very man ﷺ who had buried those seventy with his own grief returned to exactly that, and named it as the thing he feared most for us. Not idols. The dunya. Five days at one mountain, and the lesson he carried from its slopes all the way to his last minbar is the lesson he hands you tonight. The battle ends. The meeting place is set.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read Uhud's last day as the sad clearing-up after a tragedy, the graves and the grieving and the limping march home, and to close the book there. That would be to miss every question it puts to you. The fifth day at Uhud is the harvest day, where the Sheikh weighs the whole of it, and the weighing is meant for your hands, not the historian's.

Start where he ﷺ started, in the graves. The order of burial that day was set by the Qur'an: the one who carried the most of it was lowered in first, in the worst hour the community had ever known. Allah raises people through this Book and abases others by it, living and dead, and the honor it gave its bearers was given to no one else on that field. So ask yourself, honestly and without flinching, where the Book of Allah would place you if the order were called today. How much of it do you carry, and how much weight does it have in the actual hours of your life. The people of Uhud answered that question with their burial; you answer it with your days.

Then learn the shape of grief from a city that had to learn it overnight. He ﷺ wept over Hamza, and praised the women who came to weep at his door, and then forbade the wailing forever. Sorrow is allowed its tears; it is never allowed to scream against the decree of Allah. There is a shape to grief in this religion, and the whole of it fits inside one sentence the bereaved woman of that day reached for: inna lillahi wa inna ilayhi raji'un, to Allah we belong and to Him we return. And learn, too, when Allah has closed a file, to keep it closed. He has already forgiven you came down, and not one believer relitigated Uhud: no reprimands, no I told you so, and Hudhayfa gave away the blood-money of his own murdered father rather than hold the day against anyone. We are slower than they were to let things go, quicker to reopen the wound and to whisper if only. He ﷺ taught the opposite: once you have done what was right to do, go forward, and do not let if only open the door of shaytan.

But the question this day presses hardest is the one his last sermon pressed. Watch what actually beat the believers at Uhud. The line did not break for disbelief; it broke for booty. Some of you, said the verse, desire this world. And the final public fear of the man ﷺ who buried that day's dead was the same disease, not shirk but the dunya opening up and the ummah competing over it. So weigh whatever you are competing for right now, the thing you are tempted to break a little discipline to seize, the corner of the dunya you would bend a command to reach, against that sermon from that minbar. And weigh it, too, against the deepest comfort the day holds: that the best human being ever created bled, and buried his own uncle, and marched out again at dawn the next morning with seventy wounded men, because victory is struggled for and never owed, and there is no other staircase to rank than trial. If it had to cost him that, it will cost you something too, and the cost is not the punishment. It is the road.

So close the arc where the Prophet ﷺ closed it, at the minbar, one week before his passing, with the people of Uhud on his lips and the fountain before his eyes. O Allah, send Your praise and Your peace upon our Prophet Muhammad ﷺ; accept the martyrs of Uhud and join us with them in their Jannah; forgive us as You forgave the people of that day; guard our hearts from the dunya he feared for us; and gather us with him at his hawd for a drink after which there is no thirst. Ameen.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 50: the Battle of Uhud, part 5 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (16:126, 3:172, 3:165, 8:37, 3:179, 3:167, 8:43, 3:152, 3:128), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 51 of 104

The massacres of al-Raji and Bir Ma'unah

Two wells, eighty teachers, one night of grief

Uhud is only months behind them, and the desert has drawn exactly the wrong lesson from it. To the eye of a Bedouin watching from the wilderness, raiding by trade and by inheritance, the day at the mountain looked less like a defeat for Madinah than like proof that Madinah could be wounded at all, and that was invitation enough. Dr. Yasir Qadhi marks the shift carefully: the quarrel was no longer simply between the Muslims and Quraysh, one city against another. Something harder was setting in, a religious animosity glimpsed at Uhud and now widening into a war against Islam itself, and the tribes of the open country began to circle. The two stories that give this day its name are the incident of al-Raji and the incident of Bir Ma'unah, each named for the well beside which the killing was done, and each a massacre of the Prophet's ﷺ own teachers, lured out under a promise of safety and slaughtered for it.

Walk these hard pages slowly, because folded inside the treachery are some of the most luminous deaths the whole seerah owns: a body Allah Himself took charge of burying, a chained prisoner consoling the frightened mother of a child, and a man with a spear through his chest crying out, before he fell, that he had won. By one report, the news of both wells reached the Prophet ﷺ on a single night, and the grief in him turned into a posture of prayer the ummah has all but forgotten how to make.

One man against an army

Begin in Muharram of the fourth year of the hijrah, with a threat the city never saw arrive. The tribe of Hudhayl, one of the large Bedouin tribes of the north, had begun massing for a surprise attack on Madinah under its chieftain, Sufyan al-Hudhali. The Prophet ﷺ did not answer an army with an army. He sent one man, Abdullah ibn Unays, to cut the plot off at the head, to hunt down the chieftain and finish him so that the gathering force would dissolve. This was no expedition; it was a single sword sent into great danger. And the instructions read like prophecy: you will find him in the valley of Arana, the Prophet ﷺ told him, go now, for the time to strike is now.

Abdullah asked the only question a scout could ask. Messenger of Allah, what does he look like? I have never seen him. The Prophet ﷺ had never seen him either, but Jibril had described the man, and the sign he gave was one no spy could have furnished: when you see him, you will feel a terror at the sight of him greater than you have ever felt at any man. So Abdullah took nothing but his sword and rode toward the valley. Telling the story afterward, he said that when he glimpsed the chieftain in the distance, the fear landed exactly as foretold, a man who looked like something evil walking the earth, and he knew that Allah's Messenger ﷺ had spoken the truth.

Then, with the prayer time slipping away as he closed the gap, he did something no Muslim had ever done before him. He prayed while walking toward the chieftain, bowing with gestures of his head, unwilling to stop and stand and lose the hour. It was the first prayer of its kind in the history of Islam, an ijtihad he made on the spot, and the religion kept it. Dr. Yasir Qadhi pauses here, because the moment opens a window onto how the companions reasoned even while revelation was still descending. They made ijtihad in the Prophet's ﷺ own lifetime, extrapolating from what they knew of the Qur'an and the Sunnah to meet a situation no text had addressed, and when he heard of it he would sometimes affirm their reasoning and sometimes gently tune it, the way he laughed at the companion who, finding no water, rolled his whole body in the sand to purify himself and was shown the light wipe of tayammum: all you needed to do was this. This, the Sheikh notes, is the basis of a religion meant to remain livable in every time and place: the door of ijtihad, opened here in the lifetime of the one who brought the revelation.

Reaching the camp, Abdullah played the recruit. I hear you are gathering an army, he said, let me join it. The chieftain suspected nothing, and when the vulnerable hour came, the great man nearly alone in the valley with only a few about him, Abdullah finished what he had been sent to do. The moment the Prophet ﷺ saw him return he said: may this face be successful. The outcome was written all over him. Then he ﷺ gifted Abdullah a staff and told him it would be a sign between the two of them on the Day of Resurrection, some privilege folded into the gift and waiting for the meeting to come. Abdullah never let that staff out of his sight again, in peace or in war, and when he came to die he asked that it be buried with him in his grave.

Ten teachers walk into a trap

Hudhayl had lost its chieftain, and what it wanted now was worse than a battle. It wanted revenge by deception, the dirtiest of tactics, the Sheikh calls it, designed to get the Muslims killed by their own goodness. The tribe paid two neighboring tribes to stage a performance: delegations came to Madinah pretending to have embraced Islam and begged the Prophet ﷺ to send his most qualified men back with them to teach their new converts the Qur'an and the prayer, pressing for as many as he could spare. They knew the companions loved nothing better than to go and teach, and that he ﷺ would not refuse a call for teachers; it was the one request almost impossible to turn down, for teaching was the whole point of everything the religion was for. Between seven and ten men, most likely ten, volunteered, under the leadership of Asim.

At the well of al-Raji the performance ended. A hundred fighters rose out of the land and surrounded them, and the teachers understood in an instant that the conversion had been a lie and this was a trap. They scrambled to the top of a small hill and unslung their bows, because arrows hold an enemy at distance and none of them wished to throw his life away, and from above they answered the hundred with the only weapons they had. But ten cannot hold off a hundred forever, and the hundred offered the oldest lie war knows: come down, surrender, and your safety is guaranteed.

Asim, radiyallahu anhu, knew a detail that made surrender unthinkable. In one of the battles he had killed the husband of a pagan woman named Sulafa, and she had sworn an oath of grotesque precision: that she would not die until she had drunk wine from the skull of the man who killed him, and she had put a bounty of a hundred camels on that skull. Asim knew the tribe carried this private appetite for him, and so he called out his refusal, and the heart of what he said was not addressed to the hundred at all. O Allah, he prayed, I will not take their security. Inform Your Prophet ﷺ about us, that we were sincere and did not die as cowards. And as I have protected Your religion in the daytime, protect my body now that night is falling. Then he fought, with his bow until the arrows were gone, with his spear until it broke, with his sword until its edge died, and when there was nothing left to fight with, he fell. He died a shahid, and his dua hung in the air over the hill, waiting to be answered in full view of the men who had come for his head.

The instant the fighting ended, the warriors rushed the hill for one body above all the rest. And out of nowhere a swarm of wasps boiled up over Asim, stinging every face that came near, driving the men back from his corpse. They reasoned their way around it the way practical men do: wasps do not fly at night, they said, so we will simply wait, and take the skull after dark.

At sunset, water answered them instead. It had not rained. There was no river in that place. And yet a flood came surging out of the dry land, climbed the hill to where the body lay on its summit, lifted Asim up, and carried him away into the distance beyond every search. To this day, the Sheikh says, no one on earth knows where Asim is buried. Allah Himself took charge of his burial. He had asked his Lord to guard his flesh by night as he had guarded the religion by day, and his Lord honored the exchange to the letter, in front of the very people who had been promised a hundred camels for that skull.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi gives the moment its proper name. This is karamah, a mini miracle, granted not to prophets, for the miracle of a prophet is a mu'jizah, but to the righteous believers who follow them. It is a distinction he says he has drawn from this minbar many times. What matters here is that this single scene holds two karamat, the wasps and the river, and a third is already on its way to a starving prisoner in Makkah: a bunch of grapes in a season when there were no grapes in all the city.

Khubayb, the razor, and the grapes

Three men were left alive on the hill: Khubayb, Zayd, and Abdullah. The hundred swore them safety if they would come down, and so they came down, and the safety lasted exactly as long as it took to bind their hands. Abdullah named it aloud the moment the ropes went on, this is the first treachery, and he decided he would not be marched anywhere as merchandise. He refused every order, refused to walk, refused to be tied to a stick and led like an animal, until they killed him there at the side of the road because he simply would not move.

Here the lecture stops to settle the fiqh of that hill. Was it right to surrender, or right to fight on to the death? Permission was given for both, the Sheikh says; each man is rewarded for his choice, and which is more honorable depends on the circumstance. And Abdullah's refusal teaches a second ruling: a believer who does something he knows will get him killed at the hands of an enemy has not committed suicide. He did not raise the blade against himself; they did. To accept a death dealt by another's hand rather than submit to humiliation is courage, not self-destruction.

The other two were spared the road only because a living captive fetches money and a dead one fetches none. Khubayb was sold to Banu al-Harith in Makkah, blood for a man of theirs he had killed at Badr. Zayd was bought for a great sum by Safwan, whose father Umayyah, the old master and tormentor of Bilal, had been brought down at Badr by a group of the believers, and Zayd had been one of them. Both purchases had a single purpose, and it was not slavery. It was execution.

But Khubayb's captivity became a window his enemies never meant to open. When his death was announced, he asked his captors for two things: water to bathe, and a razor to clean his body, so that he might meet Allah purified. Look at where his mind was, the Sheikh says, hours from being killed: not on his family, not on his fate, but on standing before his Lord in a pure state. They granted it. And while the razor sat in his hand, a baby of the household crawled into his lap, and from across the room the child's mother froze in horror, certain that a condemned man with a blade would take his revenge on what was nearest. Khubayb looked up, almost insulted by the thought. Are you afraid I would kill this child? he said. By Allah, I would never do such a thing. He was the prisoner, he was the one about to die, and he was the one consoling her. That same woman testified ever afterward that she never saw a nobler prisoner than Khubayb, and that she had watched him, bound in chains and deliberately starved by his jailers, eating from a bunch of grapes in a season when there was not a grape to be found in all of Makkah. It was provision, she understood, arriving from a place no jailer could intercept.

At the execution ground he asked first to pray two rak'ahs. He prayed them with a steady heart, kept them short, and then turned to his killers with the reason: I would have prayed longer, except that you would think I was afraid of death. Then they killed him, and something outlived him on that ground. Khubayb is the first in Islam, and the Prophet ﷺ approved it in his own lifetime, of a sunnah the ummah still keeps: that any Muslim led out to execution prays two rak'ahs before he dies. May Allah spare us ever needing it, the Sheikh says, but Khubayb opened the door, and every martyr since has walked through it behind him.

A thorn in his place

Zayd was kept with the leaders of Quraysh, and when they brought him out to die, Makkah made a festival of it. The whole city took the day off to torture and kill one Muslim, treating the death of a single believer as an occasion for joy. He was bound and led to the lances, and before they did their work, Abu Sufyan leaned in with a question he must have thought was unanswerable. I ask you by Allah, tell me the honest truth, he said. Do you not wish, right now, that Muhammad ﷺ were here in your place, and you were safe at home with your family and children?

Zayd had nothing left to gain by lying and nothing left to lose by truth, and he answered with the iman only a believer carries. By Allah, he said, I would not wish that the Prophet ﷺ be pricked by a single thorn where he now sits, to ransom myself from this death, let alone that he stand here in my place. And Abu Sufyan, still years from his own Islam, spoke the sentence history filed away: I have never seen any man so beloved to his people as Muhammad ﷺ is to his companions.

Hold on to where that testimony comes from, the Sheikh insists. The companions did not narrate their own devotion; you do not learn the depth of their love for him from their own mouths. You learn it from the enemies of the message, watching the thing up close, who could not help but write down what they saw. Abu Sufyan would become a Muslim in time. But on this day he was an enemy, and even an enemy could see that no leader on earth was loved the way this one was loved.

Seventy of the best, on one man's word

In the same season, a door seemed to swing open in the other direction. Abu Bara, one of the senior chieftains of Najd, came down to Madinah and stayed a while. Najd was the other Arabia, the Sheikh reminds us, the vast interior of tribes the Hijaz barely knew. Abu Bara was visibly moved by what he saw, and the Prophet ﷺ tried his best to bring him to Islam, but the man hovered at the edge of it, impressed and undecided. And before he left he laid an offer on the table. If only you would send a group of your companions to the people of Najd to call them to this matter, he said, I am confident that many would answer. They will be under my word.

In the custom of the desert that word was not a private courtesy. When a senior chieftain extended his protection over a party of travelers, every tribe allied to him was bound by the same orf to honor it. So Abu Bara was opening the doors of Najd for da'wah and pledging the weight of his own seniority behind their safety, and he meant it sincerely, as events would prove. The Prophet ﷺ sensed the truth in him, and he was right about the man. What he could not have known was that the word would be broken by someone who had no right to touch it.

The stakes were enormous. Najd was bigger than the Hijaz, nearly double it, and the converts that might come from it could shift the entire balance of the religion. A golden opportunity, the Sheikh calls it, the green light given, and so the Prophet ﷺ sent seventy men. Set that number against the seven hundred who had stood at Uhud, and feel its weight: a full tenth of the city's fighting strength, sent out into the open desert armed with recitation. And not just any seventy. These were the qurra, the cream of the people of Suffa, the masjid's own resident students, men known for their Qur'an, men who would slip out at night to fill the water buckets of the Ansar and then come back to sleep on the floor of the masjid, owning no house in this world. Among them walked Haram, the uncle of Anas ibn Malik, who himself carried this account of them. A loss for the Muslims, the lecture reminds us, is a choosing for the chosen: Allah was selecting His shuhada from the very best of them.

I have won, by the Lord of the Kaaba

At the well of Ma'unah the party made camp and sent Haram ahead with a letter for one of the local chieftains: a greeting, an invitation to Islam, word that teachers had come under protection and were near. They could not have been received by a worse man. This was a chieftain who had once tried to negotiate his way into the religion as though it were a merger between equals: make me master of all the Bedouin lands while you keep the cities, he had proposed, or else name me your successor when you die. The Prophet ﷺ had refused outright, for prophethood is not a kingdom to be parceled out, and the refusal had been souring in the man ever since.

He did not even read the letter through. He flicked an eye at one of his henchmen, a silent signal, and while Haram stood waiting to be received, a man came up behind him and drove a spear between his shoulder blades until the iron came out through his chest. And then came the strangest victory cry in history. Haram felt the spear pass through him and called out: I have won, by the Lord of the Kaaba! Those were his last words, for he died at once. He had been longing for shahadah, and the instant it came he understood it and announced his triumph. One of the men standing there could not put the words down. What does a man win by dying? What kind of religion makes a person rejoice as the spear goes in? He carried the question from person to person, the Sheikh says, until it carried him into Islam.

Weigh the crime the way the lecture weighs it, sin stacked upon sin. Haram was an envoy, and an envoy is untouchable by the unanimous convention of all nations; to this day ambassadors are not harmed. Beyond that, he stood under the protection of a more senior chieftain, a protection it was never this man's business to break, and one report holds that the protector was in fact his own uncle. And the killing was done by stealth, a coded signal behind a guest's back, so that the man never saw it coming. Then, knowing the rest of the seventy were still camped at the well, the chieftain called on the surrounding tribes to help him finish them. Most refused, because Abu Bara's word still held them. But three clans agreed, and four to five hundred fighters closed the ring around seventy men who had come carrying books, not armor. The teachers tried to defend themselves, but they had not come for a battle, and one by one they were killed where they had camped, radiyallahu anhum. A cold-blooded massacre, the Sheikh says plainly, one that is unparalleled in the entire seerah.

Three escaped the ring, and their fates are a lesson in themselves. One, badly wounded, fell and was buried beneath the bodies of his brothers as they collapsed on top of him, so the killers never knew a living man lay under the pile; this was Ka'b ibn Zayd, and the series will meet him again two years from now at the Trench, where his shahadah finally finds him. The other two had been sent out on an errand and walked back to find vultures wheeling over the place their companions had been. They knew at once. The Ansari of the pair, al-Mundhir, refused to leave: my companions were granted shahadah on this ground, he said, and I will not be the man who outlives them only to go and tell their story. The Muhajir, Amr, argued the other way, that they should carry the news back to the Prophet ﷺ. In the end they walked in together. Al-Mundhir was killed, exactly as he had hoped to be, and Amr, for reasons of the chieftain's own, was released. Neither man was a coward; both walked into the same danger. But one had set his heart on shahadah and was given it, and the other on carrying the news home and was given that. You are handed what you intend, the Sheikh says, and here are two men handed precisely their intentions.

One night, one month of qunut

Amr's walk home added one more wound to the day. On the road he met two men of the killers' tribe coming the other way, and certain he was looking at the enemy who had just slaughtered his companions, he calmed them, waited for them to sleep, and killed them both. Then he found in their belongings a letter of protection from the Prophet ﷺ himself. The two had sought his aman and received it, and had taken no part in the massacre at all. Amr arrived in Madinah carrying the worst news of the year and his own terrible mistake folded inside it. The Prophet ﷺ took responsibility for the two deaths and paid the blood money to their relatives, and a principle was fixed for all time. Abide by your treaty; a mistake, once made, is owned and repaired; and no person wears the guilt of his tribe. These two were innocent of their people's crime, so their people's crime could not be laid on them. Collective guilt, the Sheikh says, pointing the lesson straight at our own politics, has no place in this religion: a man answers for what he did, not for what his nation or his tribe did.

Then the night itself. By one report, the news of al-Raji and the news of Bir Ma'unah reached the Prophet ﷺ on the very same night: eighty of his companions, his teachers, his Suffa students, men he had chosen by hand and lived among, betrayed and butchered at two different wells. And he ﷺ had not seen it coming. Hold that, because the lecture treats it as clear evidence about the unseen. He could describe the face of a chieftain he had never met and name the valley the man was standing in, because Allah informed him. And he could send seventy of his best toward Najd without any knowledge of their fate, because Allah had not. There is no question he ﷺ knew more of the ghayb than any of us, that he was shown portions of the heavens on the night of the Ascension that no one else has seen. But the unseen never belonged to him; it was only ever lent, in the measure Allah chose. Allah is its sole Knower, and He discloses it to none but the messengers He approves:

[He is] Knower of the unseen, and He does not disclose His [knowledge of the] unseen to anyone Except whom He has approved of messengers, and indeed, He sends before him [i.e., each messenger] and behind him observersQur'an 72:26-27

The grief in him ﷺ turned into a posture of prayer. For one full month, in every one of the five daily prayers, he stood after rising from the final ruku and made qunut: pleading for the martyrs, and calling on Allah against the treacherous clans, naming them one by one. And here the Sheikh stops the story for a sunnah he wants returned to the ummah. This is qunut al-nazilah, the qunut of calamity, and in the position he holds strongest it is not tied to any one prayer and is not a daily individual routine. It is the ummah's emergency supplication, raised standing in the last rak'ah after ruku whenever a general calamity afflicts the believers, and it has been badly neglected. When the Muslims bleed anywhere on earth, this is the sunnah that answers it. He named Syria and Palestine from his minbar in 2013. Fill in this year's names yourself, and pray the way he ﷺ prayed.

For a while, the martyrs themselves were given a voice. Verses of Qur'an were sent down about the men at the wells and recited among the believers, a report carried in Bukhari and Muslim, bearing their own message back to those they left behind: who will tell our brothers that we have met our Lord, pleased with Him as He is pleased with us? Then Allah lifted those verses, abrogating them for a wisdom He alone knows, leaving the ummah only their echo in the books and their meaning in the heart. The men at the wells did not lose.

As for the chieftain of the eye signal, the man even the Prophet ﷺ prayed against, a disease took him as is to be expected of one of his nature. It began under his arm and spread across his whole skin, a leprosy that drove him to delusion and made a pariah of him, until his own people abandoned him and he died alone in the desert, punished in this world before the next. Now set the two hearts of this day side by side and learn them. A chained prisoner with a razor in his hand who would not so much as frighten a baby, and a chieftain at the height of his power who murdered a protected guest with a flick of his eye. Such, the Sheikh says, is the way of the heart of iman beside the heart of kufr. The heart of iman is a heart of morality. The heart of kufr has none.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read al-Raji and Bir Ma'unah as two tragedies, file them under the cost of the early years, and move on. That would be to miss the questions they put to us, because the Sheikh refuses to leave either well as mere grief, and neither should we.

The first is about the price of the religion in your hands. Islam was never going to spread without sacrifice, without the loss of limb and life, and that loss is bitter to bear. The deen you inherited crossed this stretch of road on the shoulders of schoolteachers: men who filled other people's water buckets in the dark and slept on a masjid floor, men sold into execution grounds, a man speared through the chest while delivering a letter. They paid in blood for the thing you receive for free. Whatever carrying it forward asks of you today is light beside what it asked of them. Pay it gladly.

The second is about what iman looks like under pressure, and the day answered it already by holding two hearts up to the light: Khubayb chained and gentle, the chieftain free and merciless. Iman is the heart that stays moral exactly when treachery would be easy and unpunished. Watch which one you become when no one is watching and nothing would stop you, because that is where Khubayb and the chieftain parted ways.

The third is about how Allah treats those who hand themselves entirely to Him. The karamat are real, the Sheikh affirms, Allah's gifts to the faithful followers of His prophets. Asim asked his Lord to guard his body and his Lord guarded it past every search; Khubayb trusted Him with his last two rak'ahs and his last meal and was fed from the unseen. Hand Allah your everything, and your dignity becomes His to keep.

And two truths sit beside the wells that the day will not let you forget. You are given what you intend, so choose your intentions the way you would choose your ending: al-Mundhir intended shahadah and walked into it, Amr intended to carry the news home and was sent back alive, and neither was a coward. And no one wears another's crime, for the Prophet ﷺ paid the blood money for two innocent men of the killers' own tribe rather than let collective guilt stain them: a treaty is honored, a mistake is repaired, and a person answers only for his own deeds.

So take this day down into your ordinary one, and let it end where the Prophet ﷺ ended it: with grief that becomes prayer instead of despair. When calamity finds the believers anywhere on earth, do not look away. Stand in the night, raise your hands after ruku in the last rak'ah, and plead for them the way he ﷺ pleaded, until Allah is pleased. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, be pleased with the teachers of al-Raji and Bir Ma'unah and gather us with them in sincerity, guard this ummah from treachery as He guarded Asim from his enemies, and make us of those who, when His servants bleed, stand in the night and the prayer for them until He is pleased.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 51: the massacres of al-Raji and Bir Ma'unah (Memphis Islamic Center, 2013). Qur'an: Sahih International (72:26-27), verified via quran.ai.

The Seerah · 52 of 104

The Mothers of the Believers

Shelter, honor, and the answered du'a

A year has passed since Uhud, and the next campaign is already inked on the calendar: the expulsion of the Banu Nadir, the second of Madinah's three Jewish tribes. But before the seerah marches back to the field, Dr. Yasir Qadhi sets the war ledger down on purpose, because while the great political events unfolded, the Prophet's ﷺ own house was quietly filling, and those stories have been pushed aside too long. So today the door of his home opens, and we are asked to do what the battles never required of us: to learn, and to memorize, the names of the Mothers of the Believers, and to understand who he ﷺ married in these years, and why.

The honest way to walk this list is the way the Sheikh walks it, name by name, watching for the pattern that surfaces on its own. A widow disowned by her own family for her faith. A girl of about twenty whose husband was carried home from Uhud to die of his wounds. A woman the poor had named their mother before Islam ever reached her. A wife certain that no one alive could be better than the husband she had just buried. Run your eye down the names and you keep meeting the two hijrahs, the early sacrifices, records of faith almost too heavy to credit. Allah, the Sheikh says plainly, chose for His Messenger ﷺ women whose worth was already proven. And when the day returns to the field, it ends on a hot road home from Dhat al-Riqa, with a sad teenager on a worn-out camel and the kindest trick in the whole seerah.

Sawdah, who gave away her night

Begin where the grief was. After Khadijah, radiyallahu anha, died, it is related that for many months the Prophet ﷺ was not seen smiling, so devastated was he by her loss. It took a brave woman to say out loud what no one else would. Khawlah bint Hakim came to him and asked, simply, will you not marry, O Messenger of Allah? He asked whom she had in mind, and she had thought it through. If you wish for an elderly woman, Sawdah. And if you wish for a young one, Aisha. He ﷺ married them both within a month of one another, Sawdah at once, while for Aisha there was only the nikah, contracted in Makkah, with three and a half years to pass before she joined his household in Madinah, in the second year of the hijrah. Aisha cannot be told in minutes, and the Sheikh will not try; she deserves her own long series, and one day she will have it. Today belongs to the wives history tends to hurry past. So the counting begins, because hardly any of us has these names by heart: Khadijah the first, Sawdah the second, Aisha the third.

Sawdah bint Zam'ah had already given Islam everything it could ask. Her husband, al-Sukran ibn Amr, a brother of Suhayl ibn Amr, had taken her on the emigration to Abyssinia for the sake of their faith, and then died young, either there or just after the return, one of the few Companions we know by name who passed away in the Makkan years. When Sawdah accepted Islam her own family disowned her and cut her off, and in all of Makkah there was no one left to take her in. So about six months after Khadijah's death, most likely in Shawwal of the tenth year of the call, the Prophet ﷺ married her. It was compassion, given a name and a home.

She was the eldest of all his wives, a woman of large build who walked slowly, and we know it only because she tells us herself. At the Farewell Hajj she asked his ﷺ permission to leave Muzdalifah by night, ahead of the crowds, and he sent her on early with Ibn Abbas in a small party. From that one request the jurists draw a ruling that has stood ever since: on the night of Muzdalifah, the elderly, the weak, and those who tend them may leave early. Sawdah's slow walk became a mercy written into the law.

Then, years into the Madinah era, she began to fear her marriage might end, and what she did next is hers alone in all the seerah. She came to the Prophet ﷺ of her own will, with terms of her own making. I have no jealousy of your other wives, she said; my age is not for that. What I want is to be raised on the Day of Judgment among them, as your wife. So take my night, and give it to Aisha. He accepted, and it is related that around this it came down, in Surah an-Nisa, a surah that itself belongs to roughly these years:

And if a woman fears from her husband contempt or evasion, there is no sin upon them if they make terms of settlement between them - and settlement is best. And present in [human] souls is stinginess. But if you do good and fear Allah - then indeed Allah is ever, of what you do, Aware.Qur'an 4:128

The ayah is not for Sawdah alone; it is for every wife who fears her husband may turn from her, telling her she may negotiate, and that what the two of them agree upon is good. But notice what Allah arranged here for His Messenger ﷺ. Aisha was, after Khadijah, the most beloved to him, and he longed to spend more time with her, yet justice between his wives bound him. So it is as if Allah willed him a wife who had no need of her own night and would gift it freely, doubling his share with the one he loved most, and no one wronged in the trade. This, the Sheikh notes, shows plainly the permissibility of spouses negotiating that which is theirs by right. Sawdah died in the khilafah of Umar ibn al-Khattab, and lies in al-Baqi with her sisters.

Hafsah, the daughter of Umar

The fourth wife is Hafsah bint Umar, and hers is the marriage that falls exactly at this stage. Born some five years before the call began, she was married very young to one of the early converts, Khunays ibn Hudhafa, and emigrated to Abyssinia at his side. We know almost nothing of Khunays except the one thing that matters most: he was among the small band of Muhajirun, perhaps seventy in all, who stood at both Badr and Uhud, which marks him as one of the elite of the Companions. At Uhud he was severely wounded, carried back to Madinah, and died of those wounds. A martyr, the Sheikh stresses, just not on the battlefield. Hafsah was left a widow at nineteen or twenty, childless and lonely at the prime of her youth, and her father set out to do what fathers in that world did: find her a good man.

Umar went first to Uthman ibn Affan, himself newly bereaved, for his wife, a daughter of the Prophet ﷺ, had passed away in the very days of Badr. What do you think of Hafsah? It was understood what was being offered. Uthman asked for time, and after some days returned: I do not think I will marry at this time. To Umar this landed as a stinging rejection, for Uthman was a wealthy nobleman of every good quality, and unmarried besides. So Umar swallowed his pride and went to Abu Bakr with the same question. And Abu Bakr said nothing at all, not that day and not for days after. That silence, Umar would admit, wounded him more than Uthman's refusal had.

Both men were keeping the very same secret. The Prophet ﷺ had quietly mentioned Hafsah to them, taking their counsel, and neither could betray his confidence. By one report, found in the later biographical collections of the Companions rather than in Ibn Ishaq, Umar carried his frustration to the Prophet ﷺ himself, and the answer smiled with the secret it held: Hafsah will marry someone better than Uthman, and Uthman will marry someone better than Hafsah. So it unfolded exactly: Uthman, in time, married another daughter of the Prophet ﷺ, and Hafsah married the Prophet ﷺ himself. Afterward Abu Bakr came to excuse himself: the Prophet ﷺ had mentioned Hafsah to us, and I could not disclose his secret, and had he ﷺ left her, I would have accepted her myself. Mark the quiet lesson, the Sheikh says: even the Prophet ﷺ sought counsel before a marriage.

Aisha would say it herself: of all the wives, Hafsah was my real competition. The daughter of Abu Bakr and the daughter of Umar, each so much her father's child, close in age, quick, bold, sharp of tongue. And once the sharpness cost Hafsah dearly. Something passed between her and the Prophet ﷺ, serious enough that he considered divorce, and by one report pronounced it once. Then Jibril came down on her behalf: take her back, for she is sawwama, qawwama, a woman of much fasting and much standing in prayer, and she will be your wife in Jannah. Sit with how staggering that is. Heaven itself intervened in a marital dispute to defend her, and what it offered was not her lineage and not her wit, but her worship. Her piety saved her marriage. And here, the Sheikh says, is the lesson for any household that has known friction: weigh a spouse's sharp edge against iman and taqwa, which matter far more. Her own father weighed her less gently; finding her in tears after a later quarrel, he warned her that if a divorce ever came he would never speak to her again. Make peace, daughter, now.

And it is Hafsah whom history trusted with the Qur'an itself. She was one of the very few women of that generation who could read and write. When Umar was stabbed and died with no khalifah yet chosen, the master copy of the Qur'an, the grand mushaf first gathered in Abu Bakr's day, passed into her keeping, for her father had been holding it. When Uthman later needed it to make the copies he sent out to every city of Islam, he sent to Hafsah for it, and she sent it, and it was copied and returned, and she held it to the end. She died in the year 41 or 45 after the hijrah, and lies in al-Baqi. The daughter of Umar, keeper of the written Book.

The mother of the poor

The next name is the one this evening exists above all to rescue, because almost no one preserved it. Zaynab bint Khuzaymah was not of the Quraysh. She came from the Banu Hilal, a tribe of Najd, which means she had no kin in either city and almost no one to keep her story. She is not to be confused, the Sheikh is careful to note, with Zaynab bint Jahsh, the Prophet's ﷺ cousin from the Quraysh, whose long story is still some way ahead. This Zaynab was married to the Prophet ﷺ for less than a year, three months by one report, five or eight by others, and besides Khadijah she is the only one of his wives who died in his ﷺ lifetime. Even the handful of reports we have about her contradict one another, so little did the world trouble to record a woman with no clan behind her.

But two things about her survive everything. The first is her widowhood. By the best-known account she had come into Makkah through marriage, first to Tufayl ibn al-Harith and then, after he divorced her, to his brother Ubaydah ibn al-Harith ibn Abd al-Muttalib, a cousin of the Prophet ﷺ. And Ubaydah was no ordinary man: he was the eldest of the three champions who stepped out for the opening duel at Badr, beside Hamza and Ali, and the one struck down there, his leg severed, carried from the field to die of his wounds. The second thing that survives is her name. Long before Islam she was already called Umm al-Masakin, the mother of the poor, because she fed the hungry and gathered in the orphans when nothing yet commanded her to. That was her kunya before any revelation reached her, and it tells you the whole shape of her heart: a generosity that needed no scripture to teach it. The Prophet ﷺ married her around the third year of the hijrah, a woman with no protector and a heart of gold, and within months, in Rabi al-Awwal of the fourth year, she was gone. She was the first of his wives buried in al-Baqi, where nine of their graves now stand in a row. Khadijah alone lies elsewhere, in Hujun, in Makkah.

One tangent here is too remarkable to leave behind. Some books of history call Zaynab's mother the most noble mother-in-law in the history of mankind, and you have only to count her daughters to see why. She had five, and though she herself was not a Muslim, every one married a man of consequence and two became Mothers of the Believers: Zaynab herself, and her half sister Maymunah bint al-Harith, who in years to come would also marry the Prophet ﷺ. Among the others were Umm al-Fadl, the wife of Abbas and mother of Abdullah ibn Abbas; the younger Lubabah, mother of Khalid ibn al-Walid, which makes Khalid and Ibn Abbas cousins and Maymunah the aunt of both; Asma bint Umays, wife in turn to Jafar, then Abu Bakr, then Ali, who gave sons to all three; and Salma bint Umays, the wife of Hamza, whose orphaned daughter Umarah was lovingly fought over by Ali, Jafar, and Zayd, until the Prophet ﷺ gave her to Jafar, whose wife was the girl's maternal aunt, with a ruling that still stands: the mother's sister holds the place of the mother. One household, woven quietly into the whole story of Islam.

Something better than Abu Salamah

Now the name you already know. Umm Salamah, whose given name was Hind, the daughter of Abu Umayyah of the Makhzum, has walked through this series before. She made both emigrations, to Abyssinia and then to Madinah, and she was the very first woman to make the hijrah to Madinah at all. At Uhud she was among the women carrying water to the wounded on the battlefield. The Sheikh pauses over the pattern he has been pointing at all evening: run down the list of the Prophet's ﷺ wives and you keep meeting the two hijrahs, the early sacrifices, the costly records of faith. To emigrate twice was something very few women did, and yet you find it again and again among his wives, because Allah chooses those who are worthy to be chosen.

Her husband, Abu Salamah Abdullah ibn Abd al-Asad, was a cousin of the Prophet ﷺ from his mother's side, a man famous for the gentleness of his manners, and theirs was a marriage in which people could plainly see the love. Uhud wounded him gravely; the wound seemed to heal but never fully, and a few months later it took him. On his deathbed she tried to comfort him with a pact: she had heard that if a man of Jannah dies and his wife does not remarry, she is joined with him in Paradise, so let us promise one another that neither of us will marry again. Look at the tenderness of it, the Sheikh says: it is her husband who is dying, yet she frames the promise as mutual, to spare him, when in truth it is a promise only she would have to keep. Abu Salamah asked her, will you obey me? Gladly she said yes, as she always had. Then when I am gone, he told her, marry; do not remain alone. And then he turned to Allah with a du'a that ought to be taught to every husband: O Allah, grant her after me a husband better than me, who will care for her and never harm her, never cause her grief.

There is a second scene from before he died that makes the first unbearable to read. Once, Abu Salamah had come home glowing with something he had heard from the Prophet ﷺ, a hadith preserved in Sahih Muslim, and he told it to his wife: no servant is struck by a calamity and says, indeed we belong to Allah and to Him we return, O Allah reward me in my calamity and grant me better than it in its place, except that Allah rewards him and gives him something better than what he lost. It is the marvel of Allah's qadar, the Sheikh says, that of all the ways she might have learned those words, she learned them from her own husband's mouth. When Abu Salamah died, she remembered them, and she said the du'a. And then her honest heart finished the sentence in private: but who, she thought, could possibly be better than Abu Salamah?

The answer arrived in stages, and the first tells you the whole world of that first community. The very first man to propose to her, once her iddah was complete, was none other than Abu Bakr. And she declined him. Stop and let that register, the Sheikh urges: a widow turning down Abu Bakr, the noblest of men after the Prophet ﷺ. Among the Companions there was no such thing as a woman left to fend for herself, no stigma on a widow or a divorcee, no notion that a woman with children was somehow shelved; it was unthinkable to them that anyone should live without a spouse, and so the noblest men competed to honor exactly the women our own societies prefer to set aside. She declined because her heart still held that no one could equal Abu Salamah. And then another suitor came, and he came in person.

Three fears, three answers

Umm Salamah, radiyallahu anha, met the proposal of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ with the wisdom and the honesty that made her famous, for she was among the wisest of his wives, known too for her noble lineage and her beauty. The Arabic of her reply is more eloquent than any rendering, but its sense is this: how could I not be honored that you are interested in me? And yet there are three things you should know first, and she laid them on the table rather than rush to a yes. The first: I am a woman of strong jealousy, ghayrah, and you are a man who already has wives, and I fear it will draw out of me something that displeases you, and so displeases Allah. Look at the iman in that fear, the Sheikh notes: her worry is not for her own comfort but that she might sin. The second: I am a woman gone on in years. And the third: I have a family, four children, who come with me, and no one will care for them but the man I marry.

He ﷺ answered all three. As for your jealousy, I will ask Allah to remove it from your heart. As for your age, I am afflicted with the same calamity as you. Hold that line a moment: the Prophet of Allah ﷺ, in the middle of a marriage proposal, makes a gentle joke at his own expense to put a grieving widow at ease. It is his humor, his adab, his kindness all at once. And as for your children, he said, your family is my family. What a beautiful proposal, the Sheikh says, simply. They married around Shawwal of the fourth year of the hijrah, and Abu Salamah's deathbed du'a was answered in full, by the only answer that could possibly have been better than Abu Salamah.

And she repaid the ummah across a long life. It was Umm Salamah who, years later at Hudaybiyyah, would read the moment no one else could read, when the Prophet ﷺ was in a quandary and the Companions too distressed to obey. Do not argue with them, she counseled him; go out, say nothing, and shave your head and offer your sacrifice, and they will follow you. He took her advice, and they did. She outlived nearly all of them, dying in the year 59 in her late eighties, one of the very last of the wives to pass, with Abu Hurayrah leading her janazah, and she lies in al-Baqi. With her marriage he ﷺ had now married six; with Khadijah and Zaynab bint Khuzaymah gone, four were in the household. One story still waits ahead, that of Zaynab bint Jahsh, at whose marriage the verses of hijab would come down, one of the latter laws of the shari'ah, after the salah and the zakah and the fasting. The series will reach her soon.

The prayer of fear at Dhat al-Riqa

Then the lecture turns back to the field, and the field shows you the tide shifting. At the close of Uhud, Abu Sufyan had shouted his appointment across the valley: we meet again, Badr, one year from now, once and for all. So in Sha'ban of the fourth year the Prophet ﷺ marched out with around fifteen hundred Companions, gave the banner to Ali ibn Abi Talib, and camped at Badr, and waited more than a week. No army came. The Quraysh had mustered some two thousand men and even marched out, but their hearts were not in it, and they lost their nerve and turned home, pleading a year of drought and the need to feed their families. The Muslims, who had kept the appointment their enemies set, did not complain. That, the Sheikh says, is what iman against kufr looks like drawn on a map: the believers growing stronger, the Quraysh growing weaker.

The other expedition of these months is the Ghazwah of Dhat al-Riqa, against Ghatafan, a large and lawless Bedouin tribe of the north, known for raiding and highway robbery, who will return later as mercenaries in the armies of the Trench. The Prophet ﷺ led out seven hundred Companions; the two sides camped within eyesight for a week or two, and no battle came. As for when it happened, there is a real controversy, and the Sheikh handles it honestly. Ibn Ishaq places it here, just after Uhud, and we usually follow Ibn Ishaq as the most authoritative of the sira historians, which is why we treat it now; but men greater than Ibn Ishaq, Bukhari among them, place it years later, after Khaybar. One of the strongest reasons for putting it now is a grieving teenager you are about to meet, whose father had just died at Uhud and who had only just married, while the Prophet ﷺ did not even know he was wed.

No battle was fought, but Allah sent down at Dhat al-Riqa something the ummah carries to this day: salat al-khawf, the prayer of fear. An army within sight of its enemy cannot all turn its back to pray, or it would be cut down mid-prostration, so the Qur'an itself arranged the answer.

And when you [i.e., the commander of an army] are among them and lead them in prayer, let a group of them stand [in prayer] with you and let them carry their arms. And when they have prostrated, let them be [in position] behind you and have the other group come forward which has not [yet] prayed and let them pray with you, taking precaution and carrying their arms. Those who disbelieve wish that you would neglect your weapons and your baggage so they could come down upon you in one [single] attack. But there is no blame upon you, if you are troubled by rain or are ill, for putting down your arms, but take precaution. Indeed, Allah has prepared for the disbelievers a humiliating punishment.Qur'an 4:102

The imam prays the full prayer while the ranks behind him pray in shifts, one group standing guard with weapons in hand while the other prays, then trading places; that day it was done half and half, though the leader may decide the proportion by the danger. Stop and feel what this means. In the religion of Muhammad ﷺ, even an army within sight of its enemy is not excused the salah. It bends to the danger; it does not break. And one man showed what the prayer is worth. On the night watch, Abbad ibn Bishr took the first turn while Ammar ibn Yasir slept. Abbad stood to pray, and an arrow flew out of the dark and struck him. He drew it out and prayed on. A second came; he pulled that one out too and kept praying, and only when he feared he could no longer hold the watch did he wake Ammar. Why did you not rouse me at the first arrow? I was reciting a surah, Abbad said, and I did not want to break it; by Allah, were it not that I feared failing the duty the Prophet ﷺ entrusted to me, I would rather have died than cut that recitation short. It is said the surah was Yusuf. That is the sweetness the Companions found in salah.

The road home, and the kindest trick

The road home from Dhat al-Riqa carries two of the most beloved scenes in the whole seerah. The first comes on a blazing afternoon. It was the Prophet's ﷺ way to march after Fajr until the sun grew too fierce, then to halt and rest, and so the army scattered into whatever shade the valley offered, each man under his own tree, and slept, untroubled by any thought of attack. The Prophet ﷺ slept apart, beneath a tree, with his sword hung from a branch above him. A man of Ghatafan named Ghawrath had trailed the army, having promised his people he would kill him ﷺ. Seeing the camp asleep and the Prophet ﷺ alone, he walked unheard through the sleeping men, took the sword down, drew it, and stood over him as he ﷺ woke. Are you afraid of me? he demanded. No, said the Prophet ﷺ; and why should I be afraid of you? But you have a sword, the man pressed. No.

And as the Prophet ﷺ spoke, Allah cast fear into the man's heart, and he began to tremble until the sword fell from his hand. By another telling the question was, who will protect you from me, and the answer one word: Allah. When he said it, the sword dropped. The Prophet ﷺ picked it up, turned it, and asked him: now who will protect you from me? Be the better of the two who take the sword, Ghawrath managed, confessing his intent; I did not mean to show you mercy, so you be more merciful than I was. The Prophet ﷺ invited him to the shahadah. He would not accept Islam, only give a promise, freely made: I will never fight you, and I will never help anyone who fights you. And the Prophet ﷺ, with the would-be assassin's life in his own hand and the attempt a minute old, let him go. In Jabir's telling, carried in Bukhari, the Companions slept through the whole of it and woke only to him ﷺ calling them over, the scene already ended in forgiveness. Ghawrath went home saying: I have come to you from the best of all mankind. What leader, the Sheikh asks, would let such a thing go? And as for forcing Islam, there is no point in faith taken by the sword; the man promised not to fight, and that was enough.

The second scene begins with the slowest camel in the column. Jabir ibn Abdillah, radiyallahu anhu, sixteen or seventeen years old, sagged at the very back on a worn-out old camel, and under a far heavier load than that: his father had been martyred at Uhud, leaving debts and seven daughters, Jabir's sisters, with no one but him, and he the eldest, with nothing to his name but that one tired animal. Then a voice beside him: who is that at the back? And then, when he had named himself, why do I see you so sad, Jabir? The leader of seven hundred men had dropped back, alone, to ride beside the army's loneliest soldier, a teenage boy who was neither Abu Bakr nor Umar but just Jabir. He ﷺ drew the whole story out of him, then teased him the way you cheer a young man: so you have married! Why not a young girl, one to play with you and you with her, to make you laugh and you to make her laugh? No, Messenger of Allah, said Jabir: an older woman, a widow, someone to mother my seven sisters rather than add an eighth girl to a house that cannot bear one. And the Prophet ﷺ told him: asabta. You have done right. For marrying young or old, a virgin or a widow, each is right by its own circumstance, and Jabir's choice was right for his.

Then the trick began. The Prophet ﷺ told him to halt the dying camel, mounted it, said bismillah, and struck it the way camels are struck, and it surged to the front of the column like the fastest mount the boy had ever seen. Now Jabir was cheered. And the Prophet ﷺ said: sell it to me. Jabir, who needed that camel for his sisters' sake, refused him. Asked again, he flipped and offered it as a gift; and he ﷺ refused that too. Sell it. A dirham, he ﷺ offered, which for a camel is to offer five dollars for a car, and Jabir said no. Two, then. Still no. And so the haggling climbed, the Sheikh smiling as he tells it, all the way to forty dirhams, with Jabir laying down one condition: that he ride the camel home to Madinah first. He ﷺ slipped the boy a father's advice for that homecoming too, since Jabir had none left to give it: do not burst in upon your wife in the night; let the caravan's crier go ahead, so she has time to comb her hair and ready herself to meet you. Tenderness, taught out loud to a fatherless boy.

The next morning Jabir came to the masjid. Have you prayed two rakahs, Jabir? Pray first, then come for your business. So he prayed, and then the Prophet ﷺ told Bilal: weigh him out his forty dirhams, and add a little extra. Jabir took the money and turned to leave, and was called back. Where are you going? Home. But your camel is here; take it. Did you think, O Jabir, that I would haggle to cheat you out of it? The money and the camel are both yours. Ibn Hajar said this single hadith yields more than a hundred benefits, and the jurists have lived off it ever since: that bargaining is honest, that you may say no even to the best of mankind without shyness, that conditions may be set within a sale. But the Sheikh retells it, in the end, for what it shows of the Prophet's ﷺ own heart. The whole long ploy, the racing camel and the rising price and the call back at the door, was a gentle practical joke played on a proud, grieving boy, engineered so that Jabir could walk home with his camel and his dirhams and his dignity intact, given a great kindness without once tasting it as charity. That, the Sheikh says, is how he ﷺ gave.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this day as a list to memorize for next week's quiz, names and dates filed away, and to miss what each name is quietly asking of us. The Sheikh sets the battles aside on purpose so that we would look into the house, and the house has more to teach than the field.

Look first at why he ﷺ married these women, because the orientalist tells one story and the seerah tells another. Walk the list as the Sheikh walks it. Sawdah, disowned by her family for her faith, with no one in Makkah to shelter her. Hafsah, widowed at twenty when her husband died of his Uhud wounds. Zaynab, the mother of the poor, the widow of a martyr of Badr, without a relative in either city. Umm Salamah, a widowed mother of four whose dying husband begged Allah to send her someone better than himself. Every one was a marriage of shelter, of honor, of family, given to a woman whose sacrifice for Islam was already written, in an age when the noblest men competed to take exactly the widows and the children our own societies prefer to shelve. The house answers the polemic by simply being what it was. And it puts a question to us: whom has our culture taught us to quietly set aside, the widowed, the divorced, the woman with children, the one past her youth, and what would it cost us to honor them the way the Companions did?

Look next at how heaven weighed Hafsah. When her marriage shook and a tongue as sharp as her father's had carried her too far, Jibril came down not to recount her lineage or her wit but to testify to her fasting and her prayer, and to promise her Jannah. Her worship saved her marriage. We are quick to judge a spouse, a child, a friend by their edges, and slow to set those edges against their iman and their taqwa. Heaven did the opposite. Before you weigh anyone you love by their faults, weigh them the way Jibril weighed Hafsah, and let their worship outweigh their friction.

And then there is the du'a this whole day exists to put into your hands, the one Abu Salamah carried home from the Prophet ﷺ and Umm Salamah said over his body: indeed we belong to Allah and to Him we return; O Allah, reward me in my calamity and grant me better than it in its place. Say it first, the Sheikh insists, before the tears have dried, at the death, at the lost money, at the lost job, at every musibah, and trust Allah with the replacement. Umm Salamah said it through a heart that could not imagine anyone better than the husband she had buried, and Allah answered her with His own Messenger ﷺ. You will not be answered with a prophet. But you will be answered by the same Lord, who hears that du'a from you exactly as He heard it from her, and keeps His promise to give better than what He takes.

So take three things from this house down into your ordinary days. Honor the ones your world would set aside, as the Companions raced to do. Weigh the people you love by their worship before their faults, as heaven weighed Hafsah. And keep the du'a of calamity where your grief can reach it in the dark, and say it first of all. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, who sheltered the disowned widow and rode at the back of the column to find the saddest boy in the army, make our homes houses of settlement and worship and mercy as his ﷺ home was, reward us in our calamities and grant us better in their place, and gather us in Jannah with him ﷺ and the Mothers of the Believers: Khadijah, Sawdah, Aisha, Hafsah, Zaynab, Umm Salamah, and their sisters, radiyallahu anhunna. Ameen.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 52: the background of the wives of the Prophet ﷺ (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (4:128, 4:102), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 53 of 104

The expulsion of Banu Nadir

Allah came from where they never expected

A debt brought him ﷺ to their door, and almost everyone who has ever attacked this episode quietly drops that fact. He did not march on the fortresses of Banu Nadir to seize their land or settle a grudge. He walked south, with a handful of his closest companions, to ask a treaty partner for help paying a sum of money he himself had pledged on behalf of two men who were not even Muslims. That is how the day begins: not with an army, but with a debt.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi files this among the controversial topics of the seerah, and then, in the way he has promised to handle every controversy in the series, he walks straight at it. By the end of the evening he has built something you can hold in your hand: a betrayal answered not with vengeance but with ten days' notice, a siege that ended without a single arrow loosed, terms more generous than any empire of that age or this one would have offered, and an entire surah, the one Ibn Abbas refused to call by any name but the surah of Banu Nadir, retelling the whole affair from the side of the heavens. It is also a precursor. The Banu Nadir, once expelled, would become the chief architects of the great siege of al-Ahzab still to come; the men who plotted murder under their own wall would later ride to gather ten thousand against Madinah. So this is not only a story complete in itself. It is the fuse on something much larger.

A debt the whole city carried

Start where the Sheikh starts, with the blood money. In the fourth year after the Hijrah, in the month of Safar, came the twin disasters of al-Raji' and Bi'r Ma'unah, where Muslims sent out to teach were massacred.
