One survivor of Bi'r Ma'unah, the companion Amr ibn Umayyah, met two men on the road home as he made his way back, and he killed them. He believed he was striking at the very tribe that had butchered his brothers, part self-defense, part vengeance, both at once. But the tribe of Banu Amir was divided against itself. Its elder had personally guaranteed the safety of the Muslims; it was a rebellious nephew, struggling for power and ignoring his uncle, who had carried out the slaughter. The two men Amr ibn Umayyah killed belonged to the side that had kept faith. They were innocent, and their people held a covenant. So the Prophet ﷺ pledged their blood money in full: one hundred camels for each life, two hundred camels in all, a genuine fortune.

Here the Sheikh pauses on a point of law that most Muslims have never heard. Islamic law builds into cases of accidental manslaughter a mercy that pre-Islamic Arabia already understood: the blood money is not crushed onto one man's shoulders alone. A man who kills by accident fasts two consecutive months himself, but the hundred camels no single person could ever afford are shared by his whole community, each member paying a portion into a common fund, the man who did the deed carrying the largest share. Dr. Yasir Qadhi notes that this is precisely the kind of ruling modern jurists have barely begun to rethink for our own age, where a car accident can ruin a family in an afternoon. The wisdom is permanent: what afflicts the whole community, the whole community helps to carry.

The constitution of Madinah extended exactly this logic to the city. Whatever burden fell on it was borne by every signatory to the treaty, each clan paying its share until a fortune became a manageable contribution, and among the largest and wealthiest of those signatories stood the Jewish tribe of Banu Nadir. So when the Prophet ﷺ went to them, he went as a treaty partner calling in a treaty obligation, fully entitled to do so. Nothing about the request was hostile, or even unusual.

But the ledger with Banu Nadir already carried older entries, and the Sheikh lays them out so the reader sees how much tension had stacked up before this day. There was an early plot, recorded in the Musannaf of Abd al-Razzaq, in which a faction of Banu Nadir conspired to murder, in cold blood, some of the people of the Suffa, the devoted students of the mosque who were the closest thing the young community had to scholars. The plot leaked, Allah willed that no harm came of it, and because it was a faction and not the tribe, no one was punished. Then there was Uhud: the early sira authority Musa ibn Uqbah records that it was Banu Nadir who guided the Quraysh, telling an army that did not know the land where to camp and where best to meet the Muslims. And there was Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf, the poet, half Arab and half of Banu Nadir through his mother, counted among their elite, whose incitement against the Prophet ﷺ had finally cost him his life; eliminate Ka'b, and his tribe felt the loss as their own. Grievance upon grievance, and now, by the strongest reckoning, in Shawwal of the fourth year of the Hijrah, he ﷺ would walk into the middle of all of it carrying nothing but a request for money.

The rock above the wall

Picture what they walked into. The Jewish tribes of Arabia did not live as the Arabs lived. They built fortresses, real castles of stone, set among hectares of date palms with channels of water threading the green, an architecture the Arabs neither shared nor knew how to construct, each smaller clan keeping its own. The Prophet ﷺ took Abu Bakr, Umar, and a core of senior companions and made the day trip south to these plantations, and the welcome was warm to the point of flattery. It is about time you came to us for our help, one of them said, the favor turned around so that the host could feel he was the one doing the giving. Wait here, they said, while we prepare for you. And there was nothing in the world suspicious about it. Unexpected guests, an animal to be slaughtered, a feast to be laid out; of course it would take time. So he ﷺ sat down with his companions in the shade beside the fortress wall, exactly as the protocol of hospitality invited him to.

Above his head, the hospitality turned into a council of murder. He is sitting right beneath us, one of them said. This is our opportunity; let us take it. Every fortress keeps heavy stones ready at the top, because that is simply what a fortress is. One push, one rock lowered onto the head of the man sitting below, and the problem is solved, for what are the Muslims without him? Another voice warned of the backlash such a thing would bring. But the plan won the argument. And because everything was already in place, it would take only minutes.

Then Jibril came down with a single instruction, and the Prophet ﷺ obeyed it to the letter. He stood up and walked away. No word, no explanation, not so much as a glance back, all the way to Madinah. The companions sat there bewildered, watching him go; the hosts inside waited too, expecting their guest to be where they had left him. Nothing. Eventually the companions returned to the city, and there he ﷺ told them what Allah had told him: there had been a plan, and there had been a stone in it. Years later, the Sheikh notes, one of the last surahs to be revealed, al-Ma'idah, opened its long account of that people's broken trusts, and according to Ibn Abbas it begins with this very afternoon under the wall.

O you who have believed, remember the favor of Allāh upon you when a people determined to extend their hands [in aggression] against you, but He withheld their hands from you; and fear Allāh. And upon Allāh let the believers rely.Qur'an 5:11

Remember, the verse says, four years after the fact. Remember when their hands reached for you, and He held their hands back.

Ten days, and a hypocrite's oath

The answer to attempted murder was not an army. It was a notice. The Prophet ﷺ sent Muhammad ibn Maslamah back to Banu Nadir with the plot laid bare in detail: this is what you said, this is what was answered, I know exactly what passed between you. And with it came a single option. You have ten days to leave. Any man of Banu Nadir found after that would answer with his life. Muhammad ibn Maslamah had been their friend in the days of jahiliyyah, and they reached for that old friendship: how could you, of all people, be the one to bring this to us? His reply deserves to be carved somewhere a person can read it every morning. Islam has come and changed everything. The loyalties, the friendships, all of it now ran through Allah and His Messenger ﷺ. My friendship, he told them, is now with those who have Islam. Caught red-handed, with the plot exposed and nothing left to argue, they agreed to go.

Then a message slid in from inside Madinah, and it nearly undid everything. Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul, the head of the hypocrites, could not believe his friends and allies were being expelled. He was close to all the Jewish tribes and especially to Banu Nadir; Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf had been a personal friend. So he sent them a message, frank and harsh. No matter what happens, he swore, you will not leave Madinah. I will guarantee your protection. I will obey no one who tries to expel you, meaning the Prophet ﷺ himself. If you are fought, I will fight at your side. And the ultimate pledge: if you are expelled, I will go into exile with you. I have called my allies of Ghatafan, he added, and they will come two thousand strong.

Be honest with yourself, the Sheikh says, because this is the human heart of the trap. Without iman, you would be persuaded. The senior-most chieftain of the old Yathrib, a noble of jahiliyyah at the height of his standing, is offering you his own exile for your sake. What more could any ally on earth pledge? But Allah recorded both the promise and the verdict in a single ayah, and the verdict needs only four words.

Have you not considered those who practice hypocrisy, saying to their brothers [i.e., associates] who have disbelieved among the People of the Scripture, "If you are expelled, we will surely leave with you, and we will not obey, in regard to you, anyone - ever; and if you are fought, we will surely aid you." But Allāh testifies that they are liars.Qur'an 59:11

Allah testifies that they are liars, even as the words leave his mouth. And here is the bitter loop the Sheikh draws out and will not let you miss. Allah exposed the lie in the Qur'an, but Banu Nadir did not believe in the Qur'an in the first place. The one Book that was telling them the truth about their ally was the one Book they had rejected. So they trusted the liar instead.

By Allah, he is the one

The voice among them that took the bait belonged to Huyayy ibn Akhtab, one of the chieftains of Banu Nadir, and the truest portrait of the man comes, remarkably, from inside his own household. His daughter Safiyyah bint Huyayy radiyallahu anha would one day, after Khaybar still three years away, become a Mother of the Believers. She remembered being a girl of five or six, the beloved of her father and her uncle Yasir, the two men who never came home without making time to play with her and cherish her.

The day the Prophet ﷺ first arrived in the region, the two brothers went out to see this man with their own eyes, because they had been expecting someone: they had a premonition that a prophet was coming to revive their religion, and had long threatened the Arabs that when he came they would overcome them. What they had not expected was that he would come from outside their own people. Safiyyah remembered running to meet them when they returned, jumping for joy, and for the first time in her life neither her father nor her uncle so much as looked at her. They walked past their cherished little girl, gloomy and gray and silent. So she listened. Her uncle asked: is it him? Is he the one? Her father answered, glumly, from somewhere far down inside himself: by Allah, he is the one. And then the question that decides everything. What will you do? His answer: to be his enemy as long as I live. He could not accept a prophet who had not come from his own people. Safiyyah said, looking back across the whole of her life, that this was among the reasons Islam later entered her heart. As a child she had heard her own father recognize the truth and declare war on it in the very same breath.

Now Huyayy chose the promises of Abdullah ibn Ubayy over the ten days' notice, and he decided Banu Nadir would fight. But not everyone agreed with him. Inside the fortress there was a real argument, leave or stay, surrender or resist, exactly the disunity that the Qur'an would later describe with a precision that must have stung anyone who had been in that room.

They will not fight you all except within fortified cities or from behind walls. Their violence [i.e., enmity] among themselves is severe. You think they are together, but their hearts are diverse. That is because they are a people who do not reason.Qur'an 59:14

You think they are together, but their hearts are diverse. Huyayy prevailed over the others by force of will, and his messenger carried the answer back to Madinah with the arrogance of a slammed door. We have decided to stay. Do whatever you want. If'al ma bada lak, the Sheikh repeats, the height of arrogance: do whatever you can.

A siege with no help on the road

When the message was read to him, the Prophet ﷺ said Allahu Akbar, and the companions around him took up the takbir, because they understood at once that defiance meant the matter would now be settled completely, and they sensed it would settle in their favor. They armed themselves, and what happened next stunned Banu Nadir most of all. They had budgeted days for the Muslims to organize, perhaps a week. Mobilization took hours. The very same day, with some reports putting the number around seven hundred men, the Prophet ﷺ stood at the edge of their groves, and the siege had begun before Banu Nadir had finished disbelieving the news.

Then everyone waited. Banu Nadir watched the road for the two thousand spears of Ghatafan, and the road stayed empty. They watched for Abdullah ibn Ubayy, who had sworn to fight at their side and walk into exile with them, and he never once left his house. Seven days, some say ten, and in all that time not an arrow was loosed and not a sword was drawn, because they were waiting for a rescue that was never coming, and there was no possibility of this one tribe fighting the whole of Madinah alone.

In the middle of the siege the Prophet ﷺ did something almost casual in its confidence, and the Sheikh stops the narration here to make sure its weight lands. He left only a small contingent at the walls and marched the bulk of the army across the city to Banu Qurayzah, the third of the Jewish tribes. Not to fight them: to renew the constitution of Madinah with them yet again, to make them swear once more, with solemn oaths, that they would uphold the treaty, and to make certain the two tribes would not join hands while his back was turned. Banu Qurayzah affirmed it, for the third time now. And Dr. Yasir Qadhi insists you carry this scene forward, because a year and a half from now, when ten thousand men surrounded the city and a fifth column inside it could have meant the slaughter of the Muslims' own women and children, Banu Qurayzah would break exactly the oath they were renewing here. Critics brandish their fate more than any other episode, calling it the greatest smear on his honor, and they always skip scenes like this one: oath upon solemn oath, freely renewed a third time, then broken at the worst possible moment. What befell each of these tribes, the Sheikh says with the full weight of his method, came from its own actions, never from its blood and never from its Book. Even Banu Nadir here did not betray a fraction of what Banu Qurayzah later would. And the march made the point more brutally than any speech: the Muslims could pull most of their army off the siege and the besieged still could do nothing, cut off and alone, every promised ally failing them on schedule.

The palm trees and the frank answer

One pressure remained, and it was the one that finally broke them. The pride of Banu Nadir was their orchards, hectares of date palms in ordered rows, every channel of water and every amenity laid out with care, and the trees at last grown ripe. A date palm takes five to eight years before it gives anything at all; for the first years a family pours in labor and receives nothing, and only then, once the tree stands, does it yield for a decade or two. This was not merely wealth. It was effort, patience, the prize of a lifetime's work. So when the Muslims cut down and burned stands of the palms in full view of the walls, Banu Nadir watched their decades of labor go up in front of them, and they lamented and wailed, and threw a sharp accusation down from the fortress: you, who call to peace, what kind of peace is this that destroys orchards?

And the question landed not only on the besieged. Some of the believers felt it too, for the most practical of reasons: if these groves are about to pass into our hands, why destroy what we will inherit? It is a rare and striking moment, where the questioners inside the walls and outside them were almost asking the same thing. Allah Himself answered, and the answer is still recited today.

Whatever you have cut down of [their] palm trees or left standing on their trunks - it was by permission of Allāh and so He would disgrace the defiantly disobedient.Qur'an 59:5

Whatever you cut, and whatever you left standing on its roots, both were by the permission of Allah, and to humble the defiant. Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi is frank in the way he promises to be with every controversy in this series, and he sees nothing whatsoever to apologize for. It was a legitimate tactic of war. It broke the will of the besieged without spilling a single drop of blood. And the bulk of the four madhahib permit a legitimate army exactly this kind of measure. The trees did what ten days' notice could not: they bent a people who had refused to be bent.

Axes to their own houses

They surrendered unconditionally. Now measure the terms the Prophet ﷺ gave against the crime. Their chieftains, in formal counsel, by shura among themselves, had decided to assassinate him while he sat as their guest beneath their wall; this was not a hothead in the street but the considered decision of their leadership. And in answer he spared every life. The women and children untouched, no one taken captive, and they could take with them whatever their camels could carry. One condition only: leave the weapons behind. Frankly, the Sheikh says, this is extraordinarily generous, and he invites anyone to hold it up against how the empires of that age, or of our own, answer an attempt on the life of a head of state.

What Banu Nadir did next told everyone watching exactly who they were. So that no Muslim would ever live in them, they took axes to their own houses: the most advanced architecture in all of Arabia, fortresses the Arabs had never learned to build, the rooms where their children and grandchildren had been born, broken out of sheer spite by the very hands that had raised them. And the Qur'an reads it as a punishment fitted exactly to the deed.

It is He who expelled the ones who disbelieved among the People of the Scripture from their homes at the first gathering. You did not think they would leave, and they thought that their fortresses would protect them from Allāh; but [the decree of] Allāh came upon them from where they had not expected, and He cast terror into their hearts [so] they destroyed their houses by their [own] hands and the hands of the believers. So take warning, O people of vision.Qur'an 59:2

Read it slowly, the way the Sheikh reads it, with the day in your eyes. They thought their fortresses would protect them from Allah. How foolish can a person be, to imagine a wall of stone standing between him and his Lord? And Allah came upon them from where they had not expected, in the speed of a mobilization that took hours instead of days, in the cutting off of every ally they had counted on, and He cast terror into their hearts so that they destroyed their own houses with their own hands. So take warning, O people of vision. The surah adds that the exile was itself a concession: had Allah not already decreed their banishment, what reached them in this world would have been far worse, and the punishment of the Fire still waits for them beyond it.

Ibn Ishaq describes the departure as a spectacle the whole of Madinah gathered to watch. The wealth these fortresses had hidden astonished the companions: treasures, fabrics, brocades, jewels, stacked to the saddle horns until the camels groaned under the loads. The children walked so the camels could carry the goods. They would not leave the Muslims a single thing, not even, it is said, the carved doors of their houses, hauled away on top of the loads. And in the same breath the Sheikh pauses on the fairness of the religion: two or three of Banu Nadir chose Islam instead of exile, and instantly kept everything, homes and lands and possessions, their past enmity buying them no penalty at all the moment they entered the faith. The rest went mostly north, to Khaybar. And the surah names the day with a quiet, double-edged warning: the first gathering. First, because Khaybar was coming, where these same people would meet another reckoning three years on. And first, because beyond every gathering in this world waits the last Gathering of all.

The surah of Banu Nadir

The empty fortresses and the laden groves raised a question the companions had never faced. This wealth had been won without one horse spurred, one arrow shot, one wound taken. So it was not ghanimah, the spoils of an actual battle with their familiar shares. Allah revealed a different institution entirely for it: fay, wealth restored rather than seized, captured without fighting or bloodshed, and governed by rules of its own. The Sheikh is careful to add that these rulings, like much of the law of war, hardly apply in our times, having been framed for an unpaid army of volunteers riding their own horses, not the salaried armies of any modern state. But the principle inside the ayah is permanent, and he slows the lecture down to dwell on it.

And what Allāh restored to His Messenger from the people of the towns - it is for Allāh and for the Messenger and for [his] near relatives and orphans and the needy and the [stranded] traveler - so that it will not be a perpetual distribution among the rich from among you. And whatever the Messenger has given you - take; and what he has forbidden you - refrain from. And fear Allāh; indeed, Allāh is severe in penalty.Qur'an 59:7

So that it will not be a perpetual distribution among the rich from among you. By Allah, the Sheikh says, these few words are among the most powerful verses of Islamic economics ever revealed. Wealth in this ummah is meant to filter downward, to the orphan and the poor and the stranded traveler, not to circle endlessly among the elite. And he points, with real heat, at our own age, where the wealthiest one percent of humanity closes in on owning half of everything there is, and calls that exactly the loop this ayah was sent to break. The orphan, the poor, and the wayfarer are not an afterthought to charity in this religion. They are the policy. And the same verse carries one of the most explicit commands in the entire Qur'an, revealed about these very spoils and binding in everything he ﷺ ever said: whatever the Messenger gives you, take it; whatever he forbids you, leave it.

So what did he ﷺ do with the lands? He distributed them among the Muhajirun, the emigrants who had walked out of Makkah with nothing and lived ever since on the shoulders of the Ansar, and Ibn Ishaq adds that three poor men of the Ansar who owned no land and had fought in the battles were given a share as well. At a single stroke, the two halves of the city, the emigrants who had given up everything and the helpers who had carried them, stood closer to equal. And the Ansar, who might so easily have felt the wealth pass them by, were handed instead an ayah they would treasure above almost any other in the Book.

And [also for] those who were settled in the Home [i.e.,al-Madīnah] and [adopted] the faith before them. They love those who emigrated to them and find not any want in their breasts of what they [i.e., the emigrants] were given but give [them] preference over themselves, even though they are in privation. And whoever is protected from the stinginess of his soul - it is those who will be the successful.Qur'an 59:9

They love those who emigrated to them, they find no resentment in their breasts at what was given to others, they prefer others over themselves even with poverty sitting in their own house. The verse before promised the Ansar the land; this verse told them they had already won something better. You are the successful, it says, because you were protected from the stinginess of your own souls. And then a door swings open for everyone who came after, which is to say for us.

And [there is a share for] those who come after them, saying, "Our Lord, forgive us and our brothers who preceded us in faith and put not in our hearts [any] resentment toward those who have believed. Our Lord, indeed You are Kind and Merciful."Qur'an 59:10

We did not stand at Badr, we did not give up our homes in Makkah, but Allah wrote us into the surah anyway, as the ones who come after and ask forgiveness for those who went before and carry no resentment toward those who believed. The Sheikh adds the sharp edge the ayah implies: whoever carries hatred for the Companions has written himself out of this verse.

Step back now and look at the whole of what this short surah holds: the expulsion, the fortresses that could not protect, the terror cast into hearts, the cut palms, the institution of fay, the praise of Muhajirun and Ansar, the hope held out to every later believer, and the hypocrite whose empty promise Allah compares, literally, to Shaytan himself, who tells a man to disbelieve and then, once the man is ruined, announces that he is free of him.

[The hypocrites are] like the example of Satan when he says to man, "Disbelieve." But when he disbelieves, he says, "Indeed, I am disassociated from you. Indeed, I fear Allāh, Lord of the worlds."Qur'an 59:16

That is Abdullah ibn Ubayy exactly: promise everything, urge the ruin, and vanish when the moment comes to deliver, washing his hands of the people he doomed. This is why Ibn Abbas refused even to call it Surat al-Hashr. Hashr means the gathering, and that word almost always points to the Day of Resurrection; but in this surah it does not. Do not call it al-Hashr, he said. Call it the surah of Banu Nadir, for all of it came down about these days. And this is the very reason Dr. Yasir Qadhi built the entire evening around the surah, returning again and again to the conviction that runs through his whole class: one of the main purposes of studying the seerah is to understand the Qur'an. Once you have lived through the day of Banu Nadir, the surah stands up and walks. Read it tonight with this day in your eyes, and it will feel less like reading and more like remembering.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this episode as a grim political story, a tribe expelled, a siege won, and leave it sealed in the past. That would be to miss what it asks of us. Strip the day down to its beats and look at it once more.

A debt owed for two innocent strangers, honored at staggering cost. A guest seated against a wall, waiting in good faith for a feast, while his hosts counseled murder above his head. A Lord who told him ﷺ what was coming, and a Messenger who simply stood and walked away without a word. A notice of ten days where every empire of every age would have sent fire instead. A siege without a drop of blood, terms without vengeance, exiles leaving rich and free to carry away everything but their weapons. And then, settling over the whole event like light, an entire surah, so that no generation after would ever have to take anyone's word for what happened here. The Prophet ﷺ never needed treachery to win, and never once answered it in its own currency.

So take the threads down into your own days. Let loyalty find its true address: Muhammad ibn Maslamah loved his old friends and still delivered the notice, because Islam had come and changed everything, and when friendship argues with Allah and His Messenger ﷺ, friendship loses. Test every promise against its delivery, not against how grand it sounds, for Abdullah ibn Ubayy swore exile and war and two thousand spears and delivered nothing, and Allah compared that whole pattern to Shaytan: urge a man toward ruin, then step back and say, I am free of you. Trust nothing to stand between you and your Lord, no fortress and no wall, for they thought their fortresses would protect them from Allah, and whatever you shelter behind instead of Him will fail you at exactly the hour you need it most. And believe, as the Sheikh states it like a settled law of this path, that whatever you give up for the sake of Allah comes back larger: the Muhajirun walked away from their homes in Makkah without a single coin, and the groves of Banu Nadir landed in their hands.

So end the day inside the ayah that was written for you. O Allah, forgive us and our brothers who preceded us in faith, and put no resentment in our hearts toward those who believe. Make our loyalty run to You and Your Messenger ﷺ before every friendship, guard us from hollow oaths and from sheltering behind anything besides You, let what we surrender for Your sake return to us larger than we left it, and on the Day of the true Gathering, gather us under the banner of the one ﷺ who answered a rock on a wall with ten days of mercy. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger, who never needed treachery to win, and write for each of us a heart that recognizes the truth and surrenders to it, rather than recognizing it and declaring war in the same breath. Ameen.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 53: the expulsion of Banu Nadir (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (5:11, 59:2, 59:5, 59:7, 59:9, 59:10, 59:11, 59:14, 59:16), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 54 of 104

Banu al-Mustaliq

The dawn raid that ended in a wedding and a thousand freed

The days between the great battles of the seerah have revelations of their own, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi is careful not to let them slip past while the eye races toward the next clash of armies. Most books of sira, he notes, gather the battles into one long column and sweep the quieter happenings, the births and marriages and small commands, into a chapter at the very back; he tries to keep them where they fell, so the reader meets the Prophet ﷺ not only as a commander but as a teacher, a husband, and a grandfather. The season after Banu Nadir's expulsion is exactly such a stretch. A teenage scribe masters a foreign tongue in a fortnight. The last cup of wine is taken away for good. Sons of the Ansar, raised in another faith, are allowed to choose it, and heaven defends their right. A grandson is born and renamed from war to beauty. And then, at the end of all this gentleness, scouts ride in with word that a tribe by a desert pool is sharpening its spears.

What follows is among the strangest victories in the whole seerah: a battle won in a single morning, almost without blood, that ends in a wedding, a thousand captives walking free, and the enemy entering Islam entire, chief and tribe together. The Sheikh calls it one of the most bizarre and beautiful of the small stories of the Prophet's ﷺ life. The sweetness, as ever, is in the details, so this day is best walked slowly.

Two weeks to learn a language

Begin with the ordinary genius of a teenager. Letters still had to pass between Madinah and the Jewish tribes, and the Prophet ﷺ wanted eyes of his own on every line: he did not trust the correspondence to others, but wanted someone reliable who could read whatever arrived and write whatever he wished to send. He chose Zaid ibn Thabit, the Ansari boy who had been about eleven when the hijrah reached Madinah, presented to him ﷺ by a proud tribe as the child who had already memorized so many surahs of the Qur'an that he could recite them aloud as a small boy. Learn the language of the yahud, the Prophet ﷺ told him. Zaid bent his whole heart to it and came back having mastered Hebrew, reading and writing both, not merely conversation, in fifteen days. When the command comes from the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, you give it everything you have, and a fortnight later you own a language.

The choice was its own wisdom. Zaid was young, his mind still supple enough to take in a tongue whole, and he had grown up beside Jewish neighbors, as a boy even drifting into their madrasa, so the soil was already turned. And Allah was preparing something far larger than a postbag: this same mind would later be chosen, through Abu Bakr and Umar, as the principal compiler of the Qur'an after the Prophet's ﷺ passing. The arrangement of the mushaf in your hands is, under Allah, the fruit of Zaid's care. Heaven trains its instruments early.

The last cup

Around this same stretch of days, one of the longest conversations between heaven and Madinah came to its close. Wine was not forbidden in a single stroke; it was walked back in patient stages, because a people soaked in it for centuries could not be asked for everything at once. The first word came early in the Madani years, in a verse of al-Baqarah, which conceded that there was some benefit in wine and gambling but declared the harm in them far the greater: a nudge to keep away, with no command in it yet. Then an incident: a companion, still under the influence at prayer time, led the salah and mangled the recitation badly, and the verse of an-Nisa came down forbidding the believers to approach the prayer while intoxicated. With five daily prayers strung across the day, drinking now had nowhere left to live but the deep of the night.

Then, according to some reports, while the companions were camped outside the fortresses of Banu Nadir, the final verse arrived and shut the door for good.

O you who have believed, indeed, intoxicants, gambling, [sacrificing on] stone alters [to other than Allah], and divining arrows are but defilement from the work of Satan, so avoid it that you may be successful.Qur'an 5:90

The Sheikh notes that this last verse did not, strictly, belong to the affair of Banu Nadir; it simply happened to descend during that encampment. It named four things filth from the work of Shaytan and commanded that all four be shunned: wine, gambling, the stone altars of the idols, and the divining arrows of the old religion. Those arrows were no abstraction. They were the very kind once drawn before an idol when Abdul Muttalib cast lots over which of his sons would be given up. A society that had drunk for generations had been weaned step by careful step, until the last step asked for the whole thing at once, and Madinah, this time, gave it.

No compulsion, even for your own sons

Now a story most books of sira pass over, the Sheikh says, one that has to be reassembled from fragments scattered through the hadith collections, Abu Dawud among them, on the authority of Ibn Abbas. Before Islam, the Arabs of Yathrib lived in the long shadow of their Jewish neighbors. The yahud could read and write and carried scripture; the Arabs were idol-worshippers who could do neither. The Sheikh is blunt that the gap was real and cut both ways: one side felt superior and behaved as though it were, and the other felt inferior and knew that it was. Out of that complex grew a strange custom. A woman who kept losing her babies would vow that if Allah granted her a son who lived, she would give him over to the yahud, to be raised a Jew though he remained her own flesh. So a number of these boys, the sons of Ansari women, were handed in infancy to the Jewish tribes, most of all to Banu Nadir, on account of the close old ties between Banu Nadir and the Khazraj. They grew up inside Banu Nadir, Jewish in faith, married among Jews, grown men now in every sense, no longer the infants they had been.

When the orders of expulsion came down upon Banu Nadir, these sons prepared to march out with the only people they had ever known. Their birth parents, Muslims now, refused: we will not let our sons go; let them renounce Judaism and accept Islam and stay. The parents had reason to hope, for whoever of Banu Nadir embraced Islam kept his home and property and was not expelled at all; a number had done exactly that, and the door stood plainly open. But these young men believed what they had been raised to believe. And revelation came down, and this is the thing to notice well, on the side of the sons.

There shall be no compulsion in [acceptance of] the religion. The right course has become distinct from the wrong. So whoever disbelieves in taghut and believes in Allah has grasped the most trustworthy handhold with no break in it. And Allah is Hearing and Knowing.Qur'an 2:256

The Muslims were commanded to unhand their own children. The sons departed with their tribe, Jews by their own free word, and the most famous verse on freedom of religion in the entire Qur'an came down defending the right of Jewish young men to stay Jewish.

Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi refuses, as is his way, to dress the religion in borrowed clothes. He will not pretend Islam offers the freedom of the modern secular state, because it does not, and to claim otherwise would be apology rather than truth. That equal, creedless liberty in which every faith is treated as legally identical is a recent invention, he says, born in Europe out of Christendom's wars with itself, the Catholic and Protestant centuries of bloodshed that finally forced Europe to invent secularism simply to survive its own throat-cutting. Muslims never had that disease and never needed that cure. What Islam offered was different, and for its own age without parallel: we will say plainly that we believe you are wrong, and you will still live, worship, marry, and remain entirely yourself. Morally we hold your path to be in error; legally we protect it. And the verse that fixed that principle for all time came down to stop believing parents from forcing their own grown sons back into Islam: the plainest proof imaginable that the soul cannot be coerced.

The boy who was almost named war

Into these same months came the first grandchild. On the stronger of two datings, the Sheikh says, it was in Sha'ban of the fifth year of the hijrah that Fatimah gave birth to a boy. There is a real dispute here, which he lays out honestly: the majority hold that Hasan was born then, but some say it was Husayn, which would push Hasan back to Ramadan of year four. The two brothers arrived less than a year apart. He inclines, with the majority, to Sha'ban of the fifth year for Hasan.

The Prophet ﷺ was overjoyed at the news and called for the child: show me my son, bring me my son. He asked Ali what he had named him. Harb, said Ali: war, a fine and common name in the old days, a warrior's name. The Prophet ﷺ gently overruled the warrior: no, he is not war, he is beauty, he is Hasan. Eleven months later, when the second boy came, he ﷺ did not even wait to be asked. He simply folded the new name out of the first: Husayn, little Hasan, and that was that.

Renaming was his ﷺ settled habit, and Imam al-Bukhari preserves that he disliked harsh names and turned them sweet. An elderly woman who came bearing a name that meant something ugly left with its lovely opposite. And he did the same to names that praised their bearer: Umm Salamah's daughter Barrah, the pious one, a name that amounts to a woman testifying to her own piety, he changed to Zaynab, a fragrant flower of the desert, because a name should not boast of the virtue of the one who wears it. So his school of names ran two ways at once: turn the ugly beautiful, and turn the boastful neutral. Names should bless without bragging. For the newborn there was the adhan given softly in his right ear, and most of what we know about welcoming a child, the Sheikh notes, we know precisely because the Prophet ﷺ was instructing Fatimah over Hasan: shave the baby's head, give the weight of the hair in silver as charity. When Fatimah asked whether they should perform the aqiqah, he answered: no, I will do it myself. The grandfather would let no one else host it; he sacrificed the two sheep and sent out the invitations with his own hand.

And Madinah got to watch him ﷺ be a grandfather. A toddler Hasan would clamber onto his back while he was in sajdah, and rather than rise, the Prophet ﷺ simply let the prostration last until the boy had had his fill. Once, mid-sermon, the two small grandsons came tripping into the masjid in long red garments and fell; he ﷺ stopped the khutbah, came down, gathered them up one on each arm, and told the congregation he could not be patient enough to leave them stumbling. And once, holding Hasan on the minbar, he said words Bukhari preserves, as authentic as a report can be: this son of mine, and he always called them his sons, is a sayyid, an undisputed leader, and through him Allah will one day reconcile two great camps of the Muslims. Decades later, after Ali was assassinated, Hasan was pledged the khilafah, and rather than let his standoff with Mu'awiyah bleed the Muslims white in war, he gave up his own power on conditions. By the Sheikh's reckoning he was thereby the fifth of the rightly guided khalifas, and his abdication closed exactly thirty years of prophetic rule, down to the very month, as another sound hadith had foretold. And mark this above all, for it is the way of the people of the Sunnah: the Prophet ﷺ called both warring camps Muslims. One was nearer the truth, Ali nearer than Mu'awiyah, yet the other was sincere and not outside the faith. Believers can err and remain believers.

Dawn at the pool of al-Muraysi

South of Madinah, a day or two down the road toward Makkah, a tribe lived around a desert pool called al-Muraysi. These were Banu al-Mustaliq, keepers of one of the most prestigious idols in all of Arabia, Manat, one of the three great idols named in the Qur'an. They sat astride a busy caravan highway and had grown comfortable on the trade that passed their water. They had history, too, with the Prophet's ﷺ own house: an alliance with his grandfather Abdul Muttalib from the days of ignorance. It did not hold in his favor. When Quraysh marched, Banu al-Mustaliq remembered the old pact in reverse, siding with Makkah and helping against the Muslims at Uhud, against the very grandson of the man their fathers had sworn to. Their position was a standing danger, a stronghold for Quraysh set conveniently between the two cities. And now, with the trade road strangled and their income bleeding away, the decisive word arrived: scouts reported that their chief, al-Harith, was gathering men to launch a surprise attack on Madinah.

The Prophet ﷺ would not move on a rumor. He sent a companion the chief had never seen, who walked into the camp as a drifting bedouin and offered to join the raid for a share of the spoils. Al-Harith, hungry for strong fighting men, welcomed him gladly and promised him a portion. The plot was confirmed from inside its own tent. In the night the visitor slipped away and carried his answer home: it is true, it is true. The chief never thought to wonder where the stranger had gone. Only then did Madinah move. And the Sheikh draws a sharp line here, because he wants no confusion: this is open war, and every nation on earth has always spied on its enemies, so there is no fault in it. There is a difference between treachery, which Islam never permits even in war, and the lawful cunning of war, the stratagem that even the oldest manual of war ranks as nine-tenths of victory. Yet in his own person the Prophet ﷺ never once told a lie, not even with armies hunting him: asked on a journey where he was from, he ﷺ answered only, from water. The better way is always his way; but the cunning of war against a declared enemy is not a sin.

We have a report that it was a Monday, the second of Sha'ban, in the fifth year. More than seven hundred men and over thirty horses left Madinah, many fully armed, and fell upon al-Muraysi just after Fajr, before the tribe's morning had even begun. The shepherds were walking out with their flocks, the women going down to the water, the children drifting off to play, and the tribe was so utterly unprepared that when they saw the Muslims sweeping in they ran screaming back to their tents and surrendered. There was barely a battle to describe. Only a handful stood and fought; fewer than ten of the tribe were killed; the rest gave themselves up; and not a single Muslim fell to an enemy hand. Into Muslim custody came more than two thousand camels, more than five thousand sheep, and close to a thousand captives, most of them women and children.

The single Muslim death was friendly fire: in the confusion an Ansari mistook a man named Hisham ibn Subaba for an attacker and killed him, one of their own. Keep the footnote, the Sheikh says, because every name on the list of the conquest of Makkah has a story behind it. Hisham's brother Miqyas came up from Makkah, where he was no Muslim, and pretended to convert. He demanded the blood money for his brother and was given it, a hundred camels, for this was clear accidental manslaughter and the debt was owned without argument. That same night Miqyas murdered the Ansari who had killed his brother, took the camels, and fled back to Makkah. For that treachery his name went onto the short list of those excluded from the general amnesty on the day Makkah fell, to be taken dead or alive. And there is mercy even in that grim list: it held four men and two women, and in the end half were forgiven; Miqyas was one of those who were not.

And notice who marched on this expedition. Because everyone could see an effortless victory coming, a sure thing against a sitting target, the hypocrites volunteered at last in numbers, men who had managed to be absent from Badr and Uhud and every dangerous day before this. Abdullah ibn Ubayy himself came along. Hold that detail, because al-Muraysi matters almost nothing for the fighting and almost everything for what came after it, and the heaviest of that, the slander of Aisha radiyallahu anha on the road home, is where the next days of the seerah will live. Today belongs to the sweetest part.

First, though, a word about the calendar, because here the Sheikh openly parts ways with nine-tenths of the sira shelf. Ibn Ishaq, the authority most books follow, dated this expedition to Sha'ban of the sixth year, after the Battle of the Trench. But Dr. Yasir Qadhi judges this cannot stand, and his reason is precise. Sa'd ibn Mu'adh, the leader of the Ansar, walks through the story of the slander that follows al-Muraysi, and Sa'd died of his wound shortly after the Trench, in the fifth year. If al-Muraysi fell in year six, Sa'd would already be dead and the narration unravels in his hand. So the Sheikh sides with the earlier authority Musa ibn Uqba: Sha'ban of the fifth year, the very Sha'ban in which Hasan was likely born. The fourth year is impossible too, he adds, since that Sha'ban the believers were away at the second appointment of Badr. He is candid that the puzzle never fits perfectly whichever date one holds, and that scholars have wrestled this knot for twelve centuries, but the lessons of the day do not hang on the year. This is why you are reading this episode now, before the Trench, and not weeks from now.

A princess at the door

The captives were distributed among the army, as the custom of that world was, and the chief's daughter fell to an Ansari, Thabit ibn Qays ibn Shammas. Juwayriyyah bint al-Harith was about seventeen or eighteen, and she had no intention of remaining anyone's property. She struck a written agreement with Thabit to purchase her own freedom at a set price, the very arrangement the Qur'an commands the believers to honor.

And those who seek a contract [for eventual emancipation] from among whom your right hands possess, then make a contract with them if you know there is within them goodness and give them from the wealth of Allah which He has given you.Qur'an 24:33

This is the door, the Sheikh notes, that by one position in Islamic law the captive has the right to demand outright, at a fair price and with time to earn it. What Juwayriyyah did not have was the money. Her father had escaped the raid and was no captive; there was no one at hand to pay.

So she went looking for help, and she knocked, of all doors, on Aisha's, where the Prophet ﷺ was. Aisha tells the story herself, in the first person, with an honesty no one else would have dared. Juwayriyyah was sweet and lovely, she says; no one saw her without being captivated. And the moment I saw her at my door, Aisha admits, I hated her, because I knew the Messenger of Allah ﷺ would see in her exactly what I was seeing. Asked about that jealousy, the Sheikh declines to be scandalized: it is human nature, and the perfection of her faith was that her iman conquered it and let it go. Juwayriyyah stood and spoke like what she was, the daughter of a lord: I am Juwayriyyah bint al-Harith, the chief of his people. You see what has befallen me. I have contracted to buy my freedom from Thabit. Help me with it.

The Prophet ﷺ answered with an offer no captive in history has heard: shall I not give you something better than that? She asked what. He said: I will pay your contract myself, and free you, and marry you. He proposed then and there. She said yes. And so her freedom itself became her mahr; no bride has ever carried a dowry quite like it.

Then the marriage did what no command could have done. The word ran through the camp: the people of Banu al-Mustaliq are now the in-laws of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, and the in-laws of the Messenger of Allah cannot remain our slaves. One by one, hands opened, captive after captive set free, until every last soul of the tribe had been released, close to a thousand people, freed for the sake of one bride, with no order given from above and no price asked.

Her father al-Harith arrived soon after, carrying a ransom for his family and knowing nothing of how the world had already turned. He asked for his daughter back. And the Prophet ﷺ did something tender and enormous: he placed the decision entirely in her hands. If she wished to return with her father, he would not stop her. From this single act, the Sheikh notes, the jurists drew a whole ruling, that a husband may hand his wife the right to choose. And it was simply his ﷺ way; years before, he had given Zaid, the boy he loved, still legally unfreed at the time, that same open road back to his own father, and Zaid had chosen to stay.

Juwayriyyah chose to stay. And al-Harith stood and watched his own daughter, free, with the road home lying wide open, choose the Prophet ﷺ over her own father and people, and it undid him. There had to be a reason here, he thought. He embraced Islam, and because a chief goes nowhere alone, his whole tribe embraced Islam behind him. The Prophet ﷺ set al-Harith back at the head of his people and returned everything: the camels, the sheep, the property, all of it. Everything went back to exactly what it had been, except that the keepers of Manat were now a tribe of Muslims. Look where the morning began, the Sheikh marvels, and look where it ended. No one was forced, no one was plundered into faith; they saw this religion from inside its own house and walked in. And Aisha, who had every reason to say otherwise, gave the verdict that surely cost her the most: I know of no woman who brought greater blessing to her people than Juwayriyyah.

A man, not an angel

One question remains, and the Sheikh walks straight at it rather than around it. Was the Prophet ﷺ drawn to Juwayriyyah's beauty and her youth? Some pious voices rush to say never, as though desire itself were a flaw in him, as though he were somehow above being a man. The Sheikh refuses the apology firmly. Aisha knew her own husband better than we do, and she said plainly that he would see what she saw. The true perfection of the Prophet ﷺ, he insists, is not to be made into an angel but to be the best of men: a man who hungered and tired and bled at Uhud, a man whose desires were entirely real and moved only inside what Allah had made lawful. There is nothing haram in being drawn to a woman and proposing to her; the haram is only to act outside of marriage. He was drawn, so he proposed, through the lawful door, to a woman free to refuse, who later, handed the open road home, chose him again. To insist he was an angel, the Sheikh warns, is to lose the role model altogether, for an angel cannot be imitated. A Prophet who was a man, whose hungers obeyed him completely, is a guide a believer can actually follow.

There was vision in the marriage too, wide as a treaty. Marry the chief's daughter and an entire tribe becomes family, freed without ransom, honored without a single coin demanded of a people the war had already impoverished. Had the Muslims pressed Banu al-Mustaliq for the price of a thousand captives, the tribe would have been ruined; instead the message went out that these people were now kin, and should simply be freed. The Prophet ﷺ had sensed good in them and judged they would come to Islam, and within days the judgment proved true.

And the same day asks a harder question, which the Sheikh answers without a grain of sugar: what of the captivity itself? He will not even let the riqq of Islam share a single word with what was practiced in the America of two centuries ago, which he names among the very worst manifestations of bondage in all human history; to use one word for both, he says, is an insult to the Shariah. Look at the system this very episode has shown you. There is one source only: captives of a war not ended by ransom, absorbed into households rather than slaughtered or left to rot in camps, and eventually, again and again, set free. Kidnapping a free human being to sell, the engine of the later slave trades, earns in the Prophet's ﷺ own words the curse of Allah. The captive could contract for freedom, as Juwayriyyah did. And expiation after expiation in the sacred law repeats one remedy for the believer who breaks an oath, who errs, who slips: free a slave, free a slave, free a slave. The whole institution, the Sheikh stresses, is detachable: cut the entire chapter of riqq away and Islam stands perfect without it, which is precisely why no scholar on earth today calls for its return. And he is equally frank that in later centuries some Muslims did join the world's slave trades, and that this was flatly haram. The Shariah took an institution the whole world practiced, humanized it past recognition, and built the exits into its very walls.

Then put the fiqh down and end the day where the Prophet ﷺ kept finding his new wife: on her prayer mat. He left her there one morning after Fajr, and when he returned near the middle of the day, she had not moved, still in her place of prayer, still turning her tasbih. Have you remained like this since I left you? Yes, she said. So he ﷺ gave her, and through her gave us, four phrases heavy enough to outweigh a whole morning of sitting: glory and praise be to Allah, as many as the number of His creation, as much as the pleasure of His Self, as heavy as the weight of His Throne, as vast as the ink of His words. She was a woman of fasting and charity, the Sheikh adds; finding her fasting one Friday alone, he taught her not to single Friday out but to pair it with the day before or after. Juwayriyyah lived on in worship like that until around the fiftieth year of the hijrah, dying at about sixty-five, in the very same year, as it happens, that Hasan died: the day's first newborn and its freed princess leaving the world together.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to file al-Muraysi under battles and move on, a quick raid won before breakfast. That would be to miss almost everything in it. Strip the morning down and you find that the sword decided next to nothing; a marriage, a mahr of freedom, and a thousand unforced choices decided it all. The day puts its questions to us one by one.

First, it asks us to verify before we move. Seven hundred armed men sat ready in Madinah while one quiet envoy walked into the enemy's own camp and tested the rumor at its source; the Prophet ﷺ confirmed al-Harith's plot before a single blade left the city. We live in an age that forwards the alarming report before the cup of tea is cold. When you hear something grave about a person, the Prophet's ﷺ method is to check it where it began before you carry it a step further, and certainly before you act.

Second, it asks us to remember that faith is chosen, never forced. The most famous verse on the matter came down not to restrain unbelievers but to stop believing parents from dragging their own grown sons back into Islam. Offer the truth as beautifully as you can, with character that argues for it better than any sermon, and then leave the soul its own yes. Hearts are Allah's alone to open, and a faith pressed in by force was never faith at all. Banu al-Mustaliq entered Islam whole precisely because no one made them.

Third, it asks us to weigh our names and our words for blessing rather than boast. Harb became Hasan; Barrah became Zaynab. He ﷺ steered every name he could away from war, ugliness, and self-praise alike. What you call your children, and what you let be said of yourself, is a small daily dua, worth choosing with care.

Fourth, it asks us to take his humanity as the very point, not an embarrassment to be explained away. He was not an angel but the best of men, and his desires moved only inside the halal. A role model you cannot possibly imitate is no role model; this one you can follow, step for step, because he walked the same human ground you walk, and mastered it.

Fifth, it asks us to believe that one yes can free a thousand. Juwayriyyah's single choice turned a captive tribe into the Prophet's ﷺ family and loosed every chain among them, and Aisha said no woman ever brought her people more blessing. You will never see the full reach of a good decision made for Allah's sake.

And last, it asks us to weigh our dhikr and not merely to time it. Four phrases, said with a present heart, outweighed a whole morning on the mat. Keep the long sittings when Allah grants them, but never leave the heavy words behind: glory and praise be to Allah, as many as the number of His creation, as much as the pleasure of His Self, as heavy as the weight of His Throne, as vast as the ink of His words. O Allah, You who made the truth distinct from error, never let us force what only You can open, and make us callers whose character does the convincing. Bless our names and our children with a beauty free of boasting, loosen every bondage upon us, the outer and the inner alike, and write us among those who free and are freed. Send Your finest peace upon Muhammad ﷺ, who answered a captive's plea with something better than she dared ask, and let the next of his days, heavy as it is, find our hearts gentle and our trust in You whole.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 54: the Expedition of al-Muraysi and Banu al-Mustaliq (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (5:90, 2:256, 24:33), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration or date, the Sheikh's own weighing has been followed.

The Seerah · 55 of 104

The slander of Aisha, part 1

The night the lie was born

The expedition of al-Muraysi was barely a battle. The enemy was outnumbered, the victory so certain that not a single Muslim fell except one man killed by accident in the confusion. Dr. Yasir Qadhi does not teach this campaign for its fighting; he teaches it for the three stories that gather around it, and they grow heavier as they go. The first was a mercy: a marriage that set an entire captive tribe free. The other two belong to this day, and the second opens the door to the third, which the Sheikh calls one of the most traumatic episodes in the whole seerah: the slander of Aisha, radiyallahu anha, a lie aimed not at the Prophet's ﷺ armies but at the most intimate thing he had, the honor of his own home, the bond of a husband and his wife.

Before a single step of the story, hold its ending tight, because the story is built to make you forget it. Her innocence was not argued by lawyers or sworn to by witnesses. It was proclaimed by Allah Himself, in verses this ummah has recited for fourteen centuries and will recite until the Hour. Of every soul in Madinah the liars could have chosen, they chose the one woman whose vindication Allah would make into Qur'an. So what this day asks of you is not suspense about how it ends, but attention to how it begins: how a lie gets built, brick by brick, from a humiliated man, an exhausted army, a coincidence in broad daylight, and a city of idle tongues. Watch the machinery closely, because it has not changed, and one day it will come for someone you love, or for you.

A fistfight over water

There is a knot in the dates, and the Sheikh, true to his method, shows it rather than hides it. Did al-Muraysi, and so the slander, fall in Shaban of the fifth year of the hijrah, or of the sixth? Two figures named in the narrations cannot both have been present in the same year, yet both names sit in Sahih al-Bukhari with sound chains; the memories of narrators decades later got crossed. Dr. Yasir Qadhi follows the fifth year with most modern researchers, then sets the question down as a historical curiosity, because nothing in the story changes whichever way you choose. So we will tell it for the fifth, and not let the date distract us from what matters.

It began the way small wars always begin, over something too petty to remember. Two young men went out to draw water for the caravan, one of the Muhajirun who had emigrated from Makkah and one of the Ansar who had given them a home, and they fell into a dispute. No book records what it was about; the companions, in their wisdom, did not preserve such things. A kick was thrown, a punch came back. Then one cried out, O Muhajirun, come and help me, and the other cried, O Ansar, and grown men came running. Each side gathered behind its own, voices rose, tempers caught fire, and hands began to move toward weapons. In the best generation this ummah will ever produce, steel was very nearly drawn over a shoving match at a well.

The Prophet ﷺ heard the noise swelling and rushed from his tent to find his companions arrayed in two factions. He did not investigate. He buried the whole thing in a single sentence: are you back to the calls of jahiliyyah? Leave it, for it is rotten. Sit with the word he chose. Not unwise, not regrettable: rotten, decaying, putrid, the kind of thing the gut recoils from on sight. And notice what he ﷺ never once asked: who started it, who threw the first kick, whose grievance was older. Some disputes deserve a judge who descends into every detail; this one deserved a burial, because to dig through the pettiness would only feed it. His wisdom was to make grown men feel how foolish the affair was, and let it die. And it died.

Then the Sheikh presses the lesson into our own century, and it is one of the judgments most distinctly his across this night. Muhajir and Ansar are not tribal labels; they are honorifics the Qur'an itself bestows, an Islamic division with the highest credentials. And the very instant those honored names were used to pit believers against one another, they became, in the mouth of the Prophet ﷺ, jahiliyyah. Rotten. If a division the Qur'an sanctions turns putrid the moment it is weaponized, what of ours, which the Qur'an never sanctioned at all? Arab against Pakistani against Bengali against Egyptian; this village of the Punjab against that one. The believer is meant to flinch from racism the way he would flinch from rotting flesh, because that is the comparison his Prophet ﷺ reached for. And if the sahaba themselves, men and not angels, could flare into factions for an afternoon, then no community on earth gets to imagine itself immune.

The whisper at the rear of the army

Al-Muraysi carried a strange and dangerous cargo: the largest concentration of hypocrites ever to march under the banner of the Prophet ﷺ. The arithmetic that drew them was cynical and simple. The target was close, there was no harvest season to keep anyone home, and the victory was a foregone conclusion. So out they came in numbers, and at their head rode Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul, chief of the hypocrites of Madinah, on the one campaign he would ever join. His Islam had always been arithmetic too: he embraced it late, after Badr, among the very last, when it was finally clear there was no profit left in remaining a pagan. Before that his contempt had been open. This was the man who, when the Prophet ﷺ once rode past on his donkey, pinched his nose shut and said, do not bring this stench near me. The arrogance had been there from the first day, and now, with the Muslims swelling year after year, the old jealousy burned hotter than ever.

He had watched the brawl at the water with delight, and watched the Prophet ﷺ extinguish it with fury, for the division was exactly the wound he had hoped to see spread. So in his own tent, among his own people, the mask slipped all the way off. They now rival us in our own land, he fumed of the Muhajirun. It is exactly as the proverb says: fatten your dog, and it will turn and eat you. He was comparing the believers, and the Messenger of Allah ﷺ who led them, to a dog grown strong on his charity. Then came the sentence he would never outrun: by Allah, when we return to Madinah, the more honorable will surely expel the lower. And he rounded on his own circle: you did this to yourselves; you welcomed them into your land and split your wealth with them; had you withheld it, they would have scattered.

But sitting in that tent was a young man, barely more than a boy, whose heart was full of iman, and his name was Zayd ibn Arqam. He heard clear, unmistakable kufr, mockery of the Prophet ﷺ from the mouth of their supposed ally, and he could not carry it. He ran to his uncle, who took him at once to the Prophet ﷺ, and Zayd repeated what he had heard, word for word. Abdullah ibn Ubayy was summoned. And he swore. He stacked oath upon oath in the name of Allah that he had said no such thing. He is only a boy, he protested; will you take the word of a child over mine? The accusation was grave, the oaths were heavy, and outwardly the Prophet ﷺ let the matter rest. The Ansar were furious. And Umar, present and seething, wanted the hypocrite dealt with on the spot. Leave him, the Prophet ﷺ said. I will not have the people say that Muhammad kills his own companions.

The Qur'an quotes him word for word

Now watch what the Prophet ﷺ does with a camp full of inflamed tempers and a fresh scandal whispering through the tents. He gave the order to break camp and ride for Madinah, and then he simply refused to stop. The rest of that day, the whole of the night, nearly twenty hours of marching without a halt, until the army reached the outskirts of the city the next morning and dropped where it stood, sleeping the whole day away. A rumor is a fire, and its fuel is idle hours; a small thing tossed from one mouth to the next comes back doubled, every teller adding heat. So the Prophet ﷺ starved the fire of its oxygen. No campfire circles, no factions muttering into the dark, just the road and the long ache of walking, until every tongue was too exhausted to wag. He extinguished a brewing fitna with nothing but forward motion. And somewhere in that column walked the loneliest soul at al-Muraysi: a teenager whose truthful testimony had, in plain effect, been set aside in favor of a hypocrite's false oath. Zayd ibn Arqam would later say it was the worst day his mother ever bore him. Allah was about to answer his grief from the sky.

That very morning, on the doorstep of Madinah, revelation descended: Surah al-Munafiqun, the chapter of the hypocrites, in its entirety. It opens by naming their pious-sounding oaths for exactly what they were:

When the hypocrites come to you, [O Muḥammad], they say, "We testify that you are the Messenger of Allāh." And Allāh knows that you are His Messenger, and Allāh testifies that the hypocrites are liars.Qur'an 63:1

And then the surah does the thing the seerah never lets you forget. It takes the words Abdullah ibn Ubayy had hissed in the privacy of his own tent and prints them into the eternal Speech of Allah, quoted back to him and answered in the same breath. His instruction to withhold their money:

They are the ones who say, "Do not spend on those who are with the Messenger of Allāh until they disband." And to Allāh belong the depositories of the heavens and the earth, but the hypocrites do not understand.Qur'an 63:7

And his boast about who would expel whom:

They say, "If we return to al-Madīnah, the more honored [for power] will surely expel therefrom the more humble." And to Allāh belongs [all] honor, and to His Messenger, and to the believers, but the hypocrites do not know.Qur'an 63:8

He had told his circle the honored would expel the lowly; Allah answered that honor was never his to ration, but belongs to Allah, and to His Messenger ﷺ, and to the believers, and the hypocrites simply do not know it. The private venom of one bitter man is now recited in the mosques of the world, sealed into the Book until the end of time. Then the Prophet ﷺ sent for the boy. He took Zayd ibn Arqam gently by the ear and said: Allah has confirmed that this ear heard the truth. One night earlier, Zayd was the rejected witness of an entire army. Now he was the companion whose very hearing Allah Himself had vouched for, for all time.

And the restraint over ibn Ubayy paid a dividend no execution ever could have. Unmasked by the Qur'an rather than by a sword, many of his own followers were shaken loose from their hypocrisy and became sincere believers. Years afterward the Prophet ﷺ reminded Umar of the day he had begged to kill the man: had I killed him then, I would have driven many people away; but now, those same people, if I commanded them, would kill him themselves. And Umar answered: I know now that the judgment of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ carries more barakah than my own. Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi pauses for a teaching among the most important of the night, and the most easily abused. The Prophet's ﷺ restraint, weighing what outsiders would say of Islam and sparing a man who deserved punishment, is a foundational case of maslaha, the consideration of public welfare, which is a real source of Islamic law. Caring how Islam looks through your conduct, he says, is not vanity; it is his sunnah, and why the Muslim must be the most scrupulous and honest person in any room. But he keeps the caveat the moment demands, because it has become fashionable to swing maslaha like a crowbar against the Qur'an and the Sunnah themselves, setting aside a command that no longer suits. That, he insists, is the matter turned upside down, and all four schools agree: maslaha guides where the texts are silent, never against an explicit text. The proof is inside this very story: where the Prophet ﷺ had spoken, the good of the ummah lived precisely in obedience to him.

A son at the gate

The news reached the city before the army did, carried ahead by the crier who ran in to announce the return. So the talk was already spreading when a man came out to meet the Prophet ﷺ on the road. His name was Abdullah, and he was the son of Abdullah ibn Ubayy, and everything his father was not: a sincere, truthful believer. He had spent a sleepless night and arrived at perhaps the strangest request in the whole seerah. Messenger of Allah, he said, it has reached me that you intend to have my father killed. If you have resolved on it, then command me to do it, and I will bring you his head myself. He had grown up in jahiliyyah, and its old code still ran in his blood: if any other man kills my father, I do not believe I will keep my sword from the killer, and then I will have slain an innocent Muslim in revenge for a hypocrite, and earned Jahannam. So let the duty fall to the one man who could never seek vengeance for it.

Look hard at what is standing before the Prophet ﷺ: a son offering to obey Allah and His Messenger over the deepest loyalty jahiliyyah ever knew. And the Prophet ﷺ refused him, gently. No, he said. Rather, your duty is to be a good companion to your father, and we will treat him kindly for as long as he remains among us. The relief washed over the young man, but his anger had nowhere to go, and he spent it in his own way. He went and stood at the entrance to Madinah, and when his father came riding up, he barred the road with his own body. You were the one who said the honorable would expel the lowly? By Allah, you will not enter this city until the Messenger of Allah ﷺ gives you leave. And so the chief of the hypocrites waited at his own city's gate, blocked by his own son's arm, until the permission came down.

Hold both of them in your mind, because the story of this household is not finished with mercy. Years later, when Abdullah ibn Ubayy died, this same son came to the Prophet ﷺ and asked for his own shirt to shroud his father in, hoping it might draw down forgiveness. And the Prophet ﷺ gave it. He prayed the janazah over the chief of the hypocrites himself, and went down into the grave with his own blessed hands to lower the body. Only afterward did Allah reveal that His Prophet ﷺ must never again pray over such men, that even seventy pleas of forgiveness would not be answered for them. Until Allah Himself closed that door, the instinct of the Prophet ﷺ toward his worst enemy in Madinah was mercy, and more mercy. That is the heart the liars were about to take aim at. As for the son, he fell as a shahid in the wars of Abu Bakr's caliphate, a sincere believer to the end, while his father had died in his hypocrisy.

Her story, in her own voice

Now the seerah slows down, and you should feel in your chest why. This story wounds because it was designed to wound: the hypocrites had aimed past the armies and the politics at the most sacred thing a believer possesses, the intimate bond of a husband and his wife, the honor of a home. You cannot aim lower, the Sheikh says, and they went there anyway. Yet Allah folded a mercy into the heart of the pain. The fullest account of the slander that exists anywhere, running to pages in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim, is narrated in the first person by Aisha radiyallahu anha herself, decades later, detail by vivid detail. The hardest story in the seerah comes down to us in the voice of the one it hurt most, and the lecture does little more than translate her words and stand back. So will this telling.

Her narration opens with what sounds like housekeeping and is really a portrait of justice. Whenever the Prophet ﷺ meant to travel, she says, he would cast lots among his wives, and whichever wife's lot was drawn would accompany him. His fairness was so habitual that it governed even what no one on earth would have begrudged him deciding for himself. For al-Muraysi, the lot fell to Aisha, and so she rode out with him.

This was after the verses of hijab had been revealed, near the end of the fourth year, and here the Sheikh opens an important door, because the word hijab carries a meaning in the Qur'an our everyday speech has narrowed. In our usage, hijab means the headscarf. But the Qur'anic hijab revealed in these verses meant a physical curtain, a screen separating a person entirely, and that command was specific to the wives of the Prophet ﷺ, the Mothers of the Believers, an extra honor above what every believing woman observes. And so Aisha, unlike any other woman, could no longer simply ride in the open. She traveled inside a hawdaj: a small curtained canopy, a kind of miniature tent, lifted with her seated inside it and fastened onto the camel's back. Keep that canopy in your mind; the entire story is about to turn on it. Keep the distinction in its place too, because in our own day you will hear someone say the Qur'an's hijab was only ever for the Prophet's ﷺ wives, and walk away pleased as if they had dismantled something. They are half right about one word, the curtain, and wholly wrong about what they want it to mean: it is utterly false that covering itself was theirs alone, for the Qur'an commands the believing women generally, in plain words, to draw their khimar over their chests and their jilbab about themselves. A statement technically true about one word, aimed like a weapon at something false.

And mark whose night this was. When the order came to make camp one final time before Madinah, Abdullah ibn Ubayy was riding where the hypocrites always rode, at the rear of the column, freshly exposed by revelation, his honor in ashes, hunting for any wound he could give back. What comes next was not random cruelty; it was revenge, taken in the most cowardly way a man can take it, his darkness just dragged into the light before the entire ummah.

A necklace of onyx

When the camp settled for that last night, Aisha walked out, far beyond the lines of the sleeping army, to relieve herself in private. On her way back she touched her chest, and her heart dropped: her necklace was gone, its string snapped somewhere in the dark. It was a string of onyx, a whitish, translucent stone, barely more than polished rock. Stop on that, because the Sheikh does. The most beloved wife of the most beloved of all Allah's creation ﷺ owned no gold and no rubies. The wives of the emperors of Persia wore treasuries on their bodies; the wife of the Prophet of Allah wore common stone, the kind you might pick up off the ground, and she treasured it because it was his ﷺ gift to her. So she turned back into the darkness to look for it, and the search took a long time.

While she searched, far out of sight, the order to move ran through a camp of men who had barely slept in two days. The crew assigned to her camel lifted the hawdaj, curtained as always, felt nothing amiss in its weight, and set it in place. No one was permitted to look inside; no one would have dreamed of calling out to the wife of the Prophet ﷺ to ask whether she was within. So the camel went forward with an empty canopy, and not one man knew. And here is the thing the Sheikh will not let you miss: Aisha, narrating this fifty years later, with every right to blame the men who left her alone in a desert, refuses to. I was a young girl, she says, I had not yet put on weight, I was light; when they lifted the hawdaj they could not have noticed the difference. She is the one who was wronged, and she spends her own account making excuses for everyone in it.

She came back to an empty plain. Not a voice, not a soul; the whole army had been swallowed by the night. And there, lying exactly where her camel had knelt, was the necklace, found at last, now that it could do her no good at all. She did not scream, and she did not run blindly into the dark after the vanished column. She reasoned, calmly, like a believer: when they find me missing, they will come back, and this is the spot they will return to. So she sat down where she had been left, a young girl utterly alone in an open desert, without a drop of water, and her trust in her Lord was so settled that she lay down and fell asleep. Take the measure of that faith. Most of us could not close our eyes in such a place for terror; she slept like a child certain she has not been forgotten by the One who counts.

Not one word, and the birth of the lie

She woke to the sound of a man's voice raised in the remembrance of Allah. A companion named Safwan had slept clean through the army's departure, that being simply his nature, and was now making his own way back toward Madinah alone, far behind everyone else. Coming across the plain he saw a single figure asleep on the open ground, and he recognized her, for he had seen Aisha in the years before the curtain came down. The shock of it pulled the remembrance of Allah from his tongue, and it was that dhikr that woke her: a quiet sign, the Sheikh notes, of what that generation rehearsed, that even surprise came out of their mouths as remembrance rather than a curse. She woke, and at once covered her face with her jilbab. And then, she swears it herself, with an oath she would repeat the rest of her life: by Allah, he did not speak one single word to me.

He did not speak to her. He brought his camel near and made it kneel, then turned his back and walked away, so that she could rise and mount unseen. Then he took the lead rope and walked, on foot, leading the camel that carried the Mother of the Believers across the desert, hour after hour, until they caught up with the army just as it was about to enter Madinah.
