No questions, no conversation, no glance backward: a gentleman of this ummah doing the plainest right thing in the plainest, most careful way it could be done. Hold his name gently, the Sheikh says, because the liars would not. Safwan had not even married at this point in his life; he would later die a shahid in the conquest of Armenia, and his grave lies there to this day.

But they arrived in the full light of day, in front of everyone: one man on foot, leading a camel, and seated upon it the wife of the Prophet ﷺ. And the rear of an army is exactly where the hypocrites ride. Aisha herself marks the moment: that, she says, was when the rumor began, and the one who took it up was Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul. Out of an act of pure, innocent kindness, a humiliated man began, in broad daylight, to assemble his revenge.

In Madinah the fever took her: one whole month of it. She lay burning in her room, cut off from the circles of women where the talk of the city ran, and so, in the strange mercy of it, she heard nothing, while outside her door the lie did what lies do. It passed from mouth to mouth, swelling with every teller, because too few would do the one thing that kills a rumor: refuse to carry it. And here the Sheikh dwells on something he finds genuinely beautiful. In fourteen centuries of books of seerah, not one author who ever wrote this episode wrote down what the rumor actually claimed. They could not bring themselves to put it on the page. It is simply al-ifk, the lie, and the reader is trusted to need no more. Set that adab beside a culture that prints every sordid scandal across the front page, and learn the lesson our scholars carried in their very pens: what you spread, you normalize, and what you normalize, you slowly become. Even of true sins the religion commands, cover them; of a lie against the innocent, there was never the slightest question.

And here the lecture pauses, and so does the day, deliberately and mid-breath. She is home in Madinah, feverish, entirely innocent, and the very last soul in the city to know what is being whispered about her in the streets. The lie is loose and growing; Allah's answer has not yet come down. Tomorrow the whisper will reach her own ears at last, the hardest month of her young life will begin in earnest, and from above the seven heavens the vindication will start, slowly, to gather.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this day as a piece of ancient palace intrigue safely sealed in the past, and to feel nothing but pity for a wronged girl long since vindicated. That would be to miss every question it is pressing into your own life right now. A lie is built, not born whole, and this day shows you the assembly line. It needs a wounded ego, and there was Abdullah ibn Ubayy at the rear of the column with his honor in ashes. It needs a misread coincidence, and there was a kind man leading a camel in the daylight. It needs idle hours, the very thing the Prophet ﷺ had tried to march out of his army. And it needs carriers, mouths willing to pass the thing along and add a little heat, because too few would let it die in their hands. Look honestly at how often you have been one of those carriers, not the inventor of a lie but its courier, repeating what you could not verify about someone who was not there to answer. The machinery does not run without you, and it does not run if you refuse your link in the chain. Be the place where whispers end: refuse to carry what you would hate carried about you, and think well of the believers when the easy and exciting thing is to think the worst.

There is one image to carry down into your ordinary days: a young girl alone in a dark and empty desert, with no water and not a soul in sight, who reasoned that her Lord would not lose her, lay down, and slept. Around her, men were beginning to build a lie that would put her through the hardest month of her life. Above her, the One who would answer that lie in words this ummah still recites had already begun to write her vindication. She could see none of it; she simply trusted, and rested. Be like that in your own narrow nights. Do the plain right thing, like Safwan, and say nothing more. Tell the truth, like Zayd, and leave its confirmation to Allah. And when you have been wronged and cannot yet see the answer, sit down where your Lord has placed you, think well of Him, and rest in the certainty that He has not forgotten you. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, whose mercy reached even his enemies, guard our tongues from slander and our hearts from thinking ill of the innocent, and gather us on the Day every whisper is weighed with Aisha, the truthful daughter of the truthful, and with her beloved ﷺ.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 55: the return from al-Muraysi and the slander of Aisha, part 1 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (63:1, 63:7, 63:8), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 56 of 104

The Slander of Aisha, Part 2

When the answer came from above seven heavens

The campaign against Banu al-Mustaliq was over, and on the road home a small accident had happened that anyone could see was nothing at all. Aisha, radiyallahu anha, barely into her teens and the most beloved of the wives of the Prophet ﷺ, had been left behind when the caravan moved off in the dark, and was brought back on the camel of a chaste young companion, Safwan ibn al-Muattal, a man so reserved that in all the hours of that return he had spoken to her a single phrase and no more. She herself testified to it: she never heard one word from him beyond that. He walked ahead leading the animal while she rode, and caught the caravan before it reached Madinah. Nothing had happened, and everyone with eyes could see it.

But there was one man in Madinah who did not need anything to have happened. Abdullah ibn Ubayy, the head of the hypocrites, had just been stripped bare by the Qur'an itself, his lying exposed in verses Allah sent down testifying that the hypocrites swear to you while Allah knows they lie. His own people had begun to abandon him, and he was burning to get even, so low that he would do it in any way he could find. He looked at that innocent scene, a young woman and an upright young man on the road, and he saw a weapon. Out of it he built the ugliest lie in the entire seerah.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi opens this chapter by pausing over something genuinely amazing: in fourteen centuries, not one of the books of sira ever actually spells the slander out. None of them, to our own times, says explicitly what was alleged. This is the utmost respect the Muslims hold for the honor of the Prophet ﷺ, because to insult his wife is to insult him, and any man, the Sheikh observes, grows angrier when his wife is slandered than even she does, for it is his honor that is attacked. His household is dearer to the believers than their own families, and his wives are part of that household by the plain words of the Qur'an. So the lie is never named. Today it runs its full month, a young woman's tears finally run dry, and then Allah speaks. This is the day our mother was vindicated from above the seven heavens.

A month of not knowing

The whole city filled with whispers, and the one person it never reached was the one it was about. Aisha came home and thought nothing of the small delay on the road. Then she fell into an ordinary fever and lay bedridden for an entire month, while the rumor moved through Madinah like fire through dry grass and no one could bring themselves to tell her. The hypocrites had aimed at her precisely because she was the most beloved, the most innocent, the youngest, innocent in a way that, as the Sheikh puts it, did not yet even understand how evil people can become. She had never had a wicked thought cross her own mind, and so she could not imagine one in anyone else.

Only one thing troubled her, so small that only a wife would have caught it: the tenderness she was used to was missing. The Prophet ﷺ would come in, ask how she was, and leave, with none of the usual warmth. She wondered at it, let it pass, and had no idea that beneath that faint change a whole city was talking.

Hold that month from his side too, ﷺ, because the episode insists upon it, and it is one of the great themes the Sheikh draws from the whole story: the humanity of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. He is a human being, not a god and not a demigod, and on this matter no revelation had yet come down. For thirty days the best of creation carried the churning heart of a husband who has heard something said about his wife. His every instinct told him she was free of it, and he would defend her in public. But he could not decree the truth into the open by his own will, for the truth he needed came only from his Lord, and he would not drop filth like this onto the bed of a sick girl whose room sat beside his masjid. So he waited on Allah, and Allah, for a wisdom that would only be revealed at the end, kept him waiting.

The night she found out

When the fever finally broke she went out one night with Umm Mistah, a first cousin of Abu Bakr and so, to Aisha, almost an aunt. They walked to the open ground the women of Madinah used in those days before houses had washrooms inside them.

On the way back, Umm Mistah tripped on her own garment and, out of nowhere, burst out with a curse against her own son: may Mistah perish. Aisha was stunned: how can you say that about your own son, she demanded, a man who witnessed Badr? Three years on, to be one of the people of Badr was already the highest honor a believer could carry in the city. Look at her in that moment, the Sheikh says: she defends a believer's honor by pure reflex, knowing nothing of why his mother is angry, simply because her Prophet ﷺ had taught her not to speak ill of others behind their backs. And the cruelest part of it: she is shielding a man who has spent the last month wounding her honor, and she does not know it.

My dear child, said Umm Mistah, do you not know? And then she told her. Some accounts say Aisha fainted where she stood, unable to believe a human being could say such a thing about her. The sickness that came over her now was worse than the whole month of fever, grief doing what no illness had. And yet still she did not collapse in front of anyone. She asked the Prophet ﷺ for permission to go to her parents' house, to verify the news from the only two people she could break down in front of. He gave it, not knowing she had already learned everything. A newlywed barely in her teens, struck with the worst news of her life, and she composes herself and verifies before she reacts: a maturity, the Sheikh notes, that shames many three times her age.

She reached her mother and asked her straight: what are the people saying? Umm Ruman tried to lay it down gently. Be easy on yourself, my daughter; rarely is there a woman beautiful and beloved to her husband, with co-wives besides, except that they find things to say about her. It was meant as comfort. But comfort that does not deny is confirmation, and Aisha heard it as such. Subhan Allah, she said, have people actually said this? And she wept that whole night and into the morning, with no rest in her at all.

The goat and the dough

For a month he ﷺ had held the matter inside, partly to shield the sick girl in the room beside the masjid, and that very shielding had carried a hidden cost: while he kept silent to protect her, the fire in the background fed on the silence and grew. Her going out to her parents now set him free to move, and within a day or two everything happened at once. First he sought counsel. He called the two of his household he trusted most: Ali, his cousin and son-in-law, raised from boyhood in the house of Khadijah; and Usama ibn Zayd, the beloved son of a beloved father, a boy of perhaps thirteen or fourteen, born into the Prophet's ﷺ own home.

Sit with that, the Sheikh urges, because the men of this ummah in particular need to feel it: the wisest of all creation gathers a teenager to ask his advice. We have a real problem taking counsel from anyone, and here is our Prophet ﷺ broadening his own horizon by asking. Usama answered without hesitation: we know nothing of your family but good; this is not possible. Ali's answer was different in temperature: Allah has placed no narrowness upon you, and there are many women besides her; but if you want the truth, ask her servant girl, she will tell you what we cannot. Nothing Ali said was untrue, and his counsel was sound. But Aisha, telling the story years later, still lets you hear which answer had warmed her, for Usama had defended her outright while Ali had merely left the door of doubt ajar. There were small, human tensions between her and Ali, then and long afterward, that would surface one day at the Battle of the Camel. And the Sheikh is careful here: both of them, all their lives, kept those tensions strictly within the bounds of Islam and honor.

So Barirah was sent for, the servant girl whom Aisha had bought with her own money and set free, and who had stayed on out of pure love to serve her. Now this freed girl, herself young, stood trembling before the Prophet ﷺ, called to the witness stand of the gravest question in Madinah. He asked her directly: have you ever seen anything of her that would make you doubt? And out of her terrified, complete honesty came the entire case against the mother of the believers. The only fault I have ever seen in her, Barirah stammered, is that she is a young girl who falls asleep over the kneading of the dough, and the goat comes and eats it.

That was the whole file. A nap and a goat. You do not know, the Sheikh says, whether to laugh or to cry: the terror of the poor servant girl, racking herself for any sin she had witnessed and producing this; and the innocence of Aisha, shown even in the one fault that could be found, which turns out to be no fault at all but the proof of her purity. And when he ﷺ asked Zaynab bint Jahsh, the one co-wife who genuinely rivaled Aisha in his love, her answer rang out like a coin dropped on stone: O Messenger of Allah, I guard my hearing and my sight; I know nothing of her but good. Aisha never forgot it. Her religion protected her, she would say of her own rival; the jealousy was personal, and Zaynab never let it cross into her faith. Zaynab's own sister, Hamnah, trying to take her sister's side in the rivalry, fell into the talk instead, and suffered for it.

The day the masjid nearly split

With Aisha now out at her parents' house in al-Awali, a good forty minutes' walk from the center of the city, the Prophet ﷺ could at last take the matter public without it reaching her ears in the cruelest way. He stood before the gathered believers and said: who will excuse me concerning a man whose harm has reached even into my own family? By Allah, I know nothing of my wife but good, and they have named a man of whom I know nothing but good. Even now, defending his household before the whole congregation, not a single name crossed his lips ﷺ. Only adab. And in one breath he raised a shield over both of the slandered at once, his wife and the falsely accused Safwan.

Then a young chief of the Aws rose to answer, the famous narration carrying his name as Sa'd ibn Mu'adh. (The reports name two different men here, the Sheikh notes, and one must be a narrator's honest slip; the lesson does not change.) As for me, the man said, I excuse you. If this man is of my own tribe, the Aws, I will strike off his head; and if he is of our brothers the Khazraj, then command us and we will obey. Everyone knew who was meant, for the man behind it all, Abdullah ibn Ubayy, was a chief of the Khazraj. And so the leader of the Khazraj, Sa'd ibn Ubadah, until that very moment a righteous man, felt the old pre-Islamic blood surge up in him. You lie, by Allah. You will not kill him, and you could not. Had he been of your own tribe, you would never have wanted him dead. Another man of the Aws threw it straight back: you are the liar; we will kill him; you are only pleading on behalf of the hypocrites.

And there it was. In the Prophet's ﷺ own masjid, over his own open wound, the Aws and the Khazraj were rising against one another while the actual criminal sat untouched. The whole purpose of the gathering dissolved into my-people-against-your-people, and the Messenger of Allah ﷺ had to set his own pain aside and calm both sides himself. Mark something even here, the Sheikh points out: when Aisha narrates how Sa'd ibn Ubadah flared up, she pauses to defend him, insisting that before this one outburst he had been a righteous man and the tribal urge had simply gotten the better of him.

He ﷺ taught that four traits of jahiliyyah would never fully leave this ummah, and the very first is this one: blind partisanship for your own people, fighting for your tribe right or wrong. In our century, the Sheikh says, it has simply changed its name; we call it nationalism now, my country whether it is right or wrong. If the best generation that ever walked the earth could flare into it for a moment, with the Prophet ﷺ standing right in front of them, then none of us has any business assuming we are above it.

What the father of Yusuf said

Back in al-Awali, Aisha had now wept through a night and a day, until, she said, she thought her very liver would burst. A woman of the Ansar asked permission to enter, and simply sat beside her and wept with her: no questions, no speeches, just company in tears. We never learn her name. And sometimes, the Sheikh says, that is the entire substance of sympathy, and it is why our Sunnah is to be present with the grieving, because the burden lightens when it is carried alongside someone else.

Then, after a month of distance, the Prophet ﷺ himself came. He sat down, and even on this day he began as he began everything, with the praise of Allah. Then: O Aisha, I have heard such and such concerning you. If you are innocent, then Allah will declare your innocence. And if you have slipped into a sin, then seek the forgiveness of Allah and turn back to Him, for when a servant confesses his sin and repents, Allah accepts his repentance. Hear it slowly, for of all the things the Sheikh lingers over, this is among the most tender. At the rawest moment of his ﷺ own confusion, with the truth still hidden from him, there is no accusation in his mouth. He holds open two doors: if you are innocent, Allah will clear you; and if you have fallen, the door of repentance is open. Even where the sin would have carried the gravest weight in the law, he points her toward tawbah, because in this religion repentance is the key that fits every lock.

When he finished speaking her tears stopped completely, until she could feel the dryness in her own eyes. What rose in their place was not despair but a kind of righteous anger: that the question could even be entertained. She turned to her mother: answer the Messenger of Allah ﷺ for me. By Allah, said Umm Ruman, I do not know what to say. She turned to her father, Abu Bakr, caught between the Messenger of Allah ﷺ and his own daughter, and he said the same: by Allah, my daughter, I do not know what to say. There is no one in that room, the Sheikh marvels, for whom you do not weep; and one hypocrite, with one lie, had made losers of them all.

So the girl answered for herself. She reached for the example of a prophet who had once been wounded by a lie of his own family, and grief had stolen even his name from her memory. She was young, she explained afterward, and had not yet memorized much of the Qur'an, and could not summon the name Ya'qub at all. I can only say to you, she told them, what the father of Yusuf said. Then she recited his words when they brought him the false blood on the shirt:

And they brought upon his shirt false blood. [Jacob] said, "Rather, your souls have enticed you to something, so patience is most fitting. And Allāh is the one sought for help against that which you describe."Qur'an 12:18

So beautiful patience, she was saying, and Allah alone is the One whose help is sought against what you describe. And then she turned away from every one of them and faced the wall. She knew she was innocent, and she knew that Allah knew it. The very most she let herself hope for, she said, was that her husband ﷺ might be shown a dream in his sleep, for the dreams of the Prophet ﷺ were true. As for the Qur'an itself coming down about her, naming her, clearing her, she considered herself far too small a thing for that. She could not even imagine it.

Vindicated from above seven heavens

It came before anyone in the room had moved from their place. The weight of revelation descended upon him ﷺ then and there, the signs they had all learned to recognize: the head lowering, the heaviness, the sweat gathering on his brow like beads of pearl even on a cold day, as though a physical burden were pressing down on him. The room went silent around a girl who had turned her face to the wall.

When it lifted, the Prophet ﷺ was laughing for joy, and the first words out of his mouth were these: glad tidings, Aisha. Allah has declared your innocence. Her mother said: stand and thank him. And the wounded young heart answered: no, by Allah. I will not stand for him. I will stand and thank none but Allah, the One who sent down my innocence. A month of hurt was speaking, and who would not excuse her? And notice, even here, that her hurt itself pointed straight at the truth: the clearing had come from no one on the earth, not her husband and not her father, but from above the seven heavens, and she gave her thanks where the vindication had actually come from.

What had descended was the opening of Surah an-Nur, the Chapter of Light. The first ten of its verses were about her directly, and by the time the whole affair is fully addressed, the Sheikh says, more than twenty-five verses stand connected to this single incident: an entire passage of revelation that will be recited in the prayers of every generation until the end of time, carrying within it the innocence of Aisha radiyallahu anha. The opening verse set the frame for everything:

Indeed, those who came with falsehood are a group among you. Do not think it bad for you; rather, it is good for you. For every person among them is what [punishment] he has earned from the sin, and he who took upon himself the greater portion thereof - for him is a great punishment [i.e., Hellfire].Qur'an 24:11

And here the Sheikh plants the sharpest point of the entire episode, the thing he says it establishes beyond doubt: that long, silent month is itself a proof of prophethood and that the Qur'an is from Allah. Had the Qur'an been the composition of Muhammad ﷺ, his own imagination as his enemies claimed, the acquittal would have come on the first evening. No man tortures his own household, his in-laws, and himself for thirty days, for nothing, when a single sentence could end it. Instead the Messenger ﷺ hurt like any husband would and waited like any servant of Allah must, because Jibril does not answer to him; he comes only when Allah sends him. A false prophet clears his wife by sundown. A true prophet waits a month on his Lord. And so the very lie that was meant to shame his household ﷺ ended up proving that the Book is from Allah, exactly as the verse had said: do not think it bad for you, it is good for you.

Would you not like that Allah should forgive you?

The same passage drew out its consequences. The Qur'an fixed the punishment for unproven slander of this kind at eighty lashes, and the three believers who had let the lie pass across their tongues received it: Mistah, the poor man of Badr whose own mother had cursed him over it; Hassan ibn Thabit, the Prophet's ﷺ own poet; and Hamnah bint Jahsh. Not one of the three had invented a thing. Have you heard? was their entire crime, the news tossed from tongue to tongue like a ball that nobody holds. They fell, as so many of us fall, into the weakness of wanting to feel like the life of the gathering, repeating what they had heard, and it cost them a hadd. As for the instigator himself, Abdullah ibn Ubayy, he was left unpunished in this world, and the scholars read Allah's justice even in that: He did not want any worldly punishment to lighten the great punishment the verse had already promised him in the next life.

Then mercy was given the last word. Abu Bakr had been supporting Mistah for years, his own relative, a poor man and an emigrant, every description the Qur'an would shortly name. When that same Mistah's tongue had helped wound his daughter, Abu Bakr swore he would never spend a penny on him again. And who, the Sheikh asks, could blame him? Heaven did not blame him. Heaven invited him higher:

And let not those of virtue among you and wealth swear not to give [aid] to their relatives and the needy and the emigrants for the cause of Allāh, and let them pardon and overlook. Would you not like that Allāh should forgive you? And Allāh is Forgiving and Merciful.Qur'an 24:22

Would you not love, the verse asked him, for Allah to forgive you, the way you are now being asked to forgive? And Abu Bakr answered: yes, by Allah, I would love for Allah to forgive me. He brought back the stipend, made it better than it had been, paid it for the rest of his life, and gave the expiation due for breaking his oath. What kind of a religion is this, the Sheikh keeps exclaiming through this scene, that takes a father wounded in the honor of his own daughter and, rather than rebuke him, gently invites him to out-give his own wound, and then gets to watch him do exactly that?

As for Safwan ibn al-Muattal, the man they had accused: he swore by the One in whose hand is his soul that he had never in all his life so much as lifted the veil of a woman. During the bitter month his anger had once boiled over, and he had struck Hassan ibn Thabit with the flat of his sword, knocking the poet down. The case came before the Prophet ﷺ, and the Sheikh calls the ruling an astonishing display of this religion's fairness. Safwan's anger was for a real grievance, but he had wounded a man, and in Islam you do not take justice into your own hands, not even when it is your own honor at stake, as this was. So the judgment ﷺ went in Hassan's favor: the poet, who could by right have demanded an equal blow in return, was instead compensated with a garden, and forgave the rest for the sake of the Prophet ﷺ. The slandered man was not permitted to become a vigilante, and the slandering poet, already lashed for his tongue, still received his due for the blow. And Aisha herself, for the rest of her days, would never allow Hassan to be spoken against in her presence. Safwan died as he had lived; most scholars say he never married at all, and in the year eighteen after the hijrah he fell as a martyr on the campaign in Armenia.

The pure, daughter of the pure

One more house from that long month deserves the closing light, because Allah Himself shone it there. When the rumor reached the home of Abu Ayyub al-Ansari, his wife came to him and said only: have you heard what the people are saying? She did not even repeat the slander. And Abu Ayyub became angry and answered: Subhan Allah! It is not for us to speak of this; this is a mighty lie. Then the revelation came down carrying, in its very wording, the answer this one believer had already given in the privacy of his own home:

And why, when you heard it, did you not say, "It is not for us to speak of this. Exalted are You, [O Allāh]; this is a great slander"?Qur'an 24:16

What greater praise can a man ever receive, the Sheikh asks, than that his own manner of answering should be raised from above the seven heavens and held out to the whole ummah as the answer every believer should have given? He is not named in the Book, but his words are quoted in it, and they became the model. And catch the quiet lesson folded inside that praise: Abu Ayyub said it to his own wife. Marriage is not a license for gossip; the rules of the tongue do not stop at your own front door. We imagine, the Sheikh warns, that because we are married we may now speak freely about other couples. We may not. What the slanderers had done instead was the opposite, and the Arabic of the Qur'an captures it as something passed from mouth to mouth so quickly it was never even digested: the very picture of namimah, of tale-carrying, which the Prophet ﷺ taught is among the sins a person is punished for in the grave.

So where does the whole episode finally leave our mother? Exactly where Allah placed her: the pure, daughter of the pure; the innocent, daughter of the innocent; cleared by verses the ummah will recite in its prayers until the Hour. Which is why Dr. Yasir Qadhi closes the matter with the verdict the scholars draw, and does not mince a word of it: whoever, after all of this, accuses her of what Allah Himself declared her free of is not merely insulting a woman, he is denying the Qur'an, and that takes a person entirely out of the fold of Islam. It does not matter who they are or what they call themselves; Allah has freed her from above the seven heavens, and to accuse her now of any crime against her chastity is kufr.

And then, in the very same breath, he models the justice he has spent the whole night teaching. The group this question always seems to turn toward: their mainstream today, he insists, does not make this accusation. It survives only among a loud, very small fringe, and he says this as a man with open and deep disagreements with their theology, which is precisely what gives his fairness its weight. They may dislike our mother and say things about her that are far from kind, and even that is to be answered; but the specific accusation Abdullah ibn Ubayy made, the bulk of them do not make, and we are not permitted to hang the sin of the few upon the many. Be just, he reminds his listeners, for it is nearer to taqwa, and the One who taught us so does not allow even the defense of our own mother to make us liars about other people:

O you who have believed, be persistently standing firm for Allāh, witnesses in justice, and do not let the hatred of a people prevent you from being just. Be just; that is nearer to righteousness. And fear Allāh; indeed, Allāh is [fully] Aware of what you do.Qur'an 5:8

The day the ummah learned what a tongue can do, the Sheikh is saying, must not end with our own tongues running loose.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this story as a scandal that happened to other people long ago and close the book on it. That would be to miss every question it puts to us. The slander of Aisha radiyallahu anha is the heaviest door the seerah has yet walked us through, and the strangest thing of all is what was waiting on the other side of it: not scandal, but light, a whole surah named Light, and a handful of lessons the Sheikh gathers at the end of the night and presses into our hands.

Your tongue can cost you what no lie of your own ever did. Mistah, Hassan, and Hamnah invented nothing; they repeated. Have you heard? was the entire sin, and it earned each of them eighty lashes, because backbiting and tale-carrying are each a separate sin in this religion, heavier still when a woman's honor is the cargo. Mark the thing that should stop every one of us: what they passed along was, in a sense, truthfully reported. Tale-carrying does not even require a lie to be a sin. If the people of Badr could fall into it and be punished, our gatherings and our idle have-you-heards are not somehow safe.

Even the most beloved are tested, so no believer should expect a road without trial. If she, the most beloved wife of the most beloved of creation, had to walk through a month like this, then ease is not the measure of Allah's love. The more He loves a servant, the Sheikh reminds us, the more He tests him, and the trial raised her rank until the end of time. And do not think it was bad for you: that is Allah's own caption over the ugliest month of her life. The pain was entirely real and the good was entirely real, an entire surah's worth of it. We rarely get to read the good in our own trials as plainly as she eventually read hers, but the verse trains us to believe it is folded inside every musibah all the same.

So take the night down into your ordinary days. When every door on earth has shut, His door is still open: her mother had no words, her father had no words, her husband ﷺ was himself waiting on heaven, and she turned to the wall and said what the father of Yusuf said, and the answer came down before anyone had left the room. Be truthful to Allah and Allah will be truthful to you. Watch for the tribe-shaped reflex that flared even in the Prophet's ﷺ masjid, and name your own nationalism before it names you. And when someone wrongs the people you love, reach for the height Abu Bakr reached: out-give your wound, and forgive the way you would beg to be forgiven. May Allah send His peace and His blessings upon His Messenger Muhammad ﷺ, in whose household this trial fell and from whose patience we learn; may He be pleased with our mother Aisha, the pure daughter of the pure, vindicated from above the seven heavens; may He guard our tongues from the lie passed along so lightly, grant us at every shut door the beautiful patience of Ya'qub and the certainty of a girl who turned to face the wall, teach us to forgive as He taught Abu Bakr to forgive, and gather us with him ﷺ and with her in a place where no slander has ever entered.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 56: the slander of Aisha radiyallahu anha, part 2 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (12:18, 24:11, 24:22, 24:16, 5:8), verified via quran.ai. The narration is the Sheikh's; the phrasing is Buruja's.

The Seerah · 57 of 104

The Battle of the Trench, part 1 ﷺ

When all of Arabia marched on one city

There is a word for what is coming, and it should make the hair stand up. Khandaq means the trench, and that is the defense. But the battle has a second name, and the second name tells you the size of the danger. Ahzab. The gathered groups, the Confederates: not one tribe, not two in a passing alliance, but groups upon groups, every clan of consequence in the peninsula folded into a single host with a single aim. For the first time in the long, quarrelsome history of the Arabs, the tribes stopped fighting one another and turned, all together, to fight one man and the city that had taken him in.

This is the showdown the last four years have been building toward, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi opens it by slowing on a detail he usually waves past: the year. Normally, he says, it makes little practical difference whether a thing happened in this year or that. Here he lingers, because here the giants stand on both sides. One reading, resting on an explicit first-person report in Bukhari, places the battle in the fourth year after the Hijrah: Abdullah ibn Umar said he was fourteen at Uhud and turned away as too young, and at Khandaq he was fifteen and let through. Uhud was the third year, fifteen comes after fourteen, the arithmetic seems to settle it, and scholars as great as Imam al-Bukhari held to it; Ibn Hazm said there could be no doubt. And yet, the Sheikh notes, almost no one has even heard that opinion, because the masters of the seerah, the specialists who piece the whole story together report by report, nearly all settle on Shawwal of the fifth year. Their argument is not one text but the shape of the whole: the appointment Abu Sufyan had set for the year after Uhud, the death of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh that the histories fix soon after Khandaq, the way the pieces only fit in the fifth year. As for the boy who was fourteen then fifteen, who, the Sheikh asks, kept careful track of birthdays in that desert? The stronger view is Shawwal of the fifth year of the Hijrah.

A grudge that bought an army

It began, as so many catastrophes do, with a grudge. The tribe of Banu Nadir had once lived in Madinah, and they had been expelled, and they had earned it. Their leaders had agreed to murder the Prophet ﷺ as he sat at the foot of their fortress wall, by rolling one of the great boulders kept ready on the rooftops down onto his head. The plot was exposed before the stone could fall. They were sent out of the city with their lives and the famous condition: leave your weapons, leave your wealth, take only what your camels can carry. They even pulled the doors from their houses and lashed them to the beasts, because a door at least is something. They settled in Khaybar, not the other side of the world but less than two hours up the road, near enough that a man could ride there and back in a day.

But what a camel can carry is nothing beside what a camel must leave behind. They had owned acres upon acres, whole plains of date palms, wealth counted not in the tens of thousands they took but in the millions they abandoned. Land like that does not stop calling a man because he has been told to go. So Banu Nadir resolved to take it back, and being too few to take it themselves, they set out to rent the rest of Arabia to do it for them.

Their noblemen rode first to Makkah, a high delegation, every senior among them, and put a proposal to the Quraysh: let us strike Muhammad ﷺ together, and we will fund whatever the campaign requires. They were a strange audience for such a pitch, for the Quraysh did not entirely trust these people and did not entirely stop admiring them either. Here was a tribe with a Book, an ancient civilization, men who could all read in an age when the Quraysh by and large could not. So when the delegation came courting, Abu Sufyan was puzzled enough to say it aloud. You are the People of the Scripture, he said in effect, and you come to us. Whose religion is closer to the truth, ours or his?

It is a sharper question than it looks, and the Sheikh pauses to let it cut. To a pagan eye the faith of these visitors and the faith of the Muslims looked like two branches of one tree, and in truth they were close: the same God, the same prophets, laws that overlap in detail after detail. So the Quraysh, idol-worshippers who buried their daughters and circled their stone gods, asked the people of a revealed Book which path was nearer to heaven. And the delegation, with an alliance hanging on the answer, looked at the idolaters and told them: you are better guided than the believers, closer to the truth than Muhammad ﷺ and his companions.

Allah recorded that betrayal, the Sheikh says, and quoted it back to them word for word, and the believers recite it to this day.

Have you not seen those who were given a portion of the Scripture, who believe in jibt [superstition] and taghut [false objects of worship] and say about the disbelievers, "These are better guided than the believers as to the way"?Qur'an 4:51

They themselves believed in jibt and taghut, the very things their Book had come to destroy, and still they bowed to it all to bring down the truth. That, the Sheikh observes, is how far a grudge will carry a soul: a people of revelation, declaring idolatry the better guidance, because they wanted their date palms back.

The price of the harvest

With Makkah won, the delegation rode north to Ghatafan, the largest of the desert tribes and, by every account, the wildest of the Bedouin. Ghatafan had no real stake in this quarrel; their caravans did not live or die by the Syrian road the way the Quraysh caravans did, and irritation at the new power in Madinah is not a cause worth dying for. So Banu Nadir bought them with the only currency that moved such men: half the entire date harvest of Khaybar for a full year. A fortune, the Sheikh marvels, handed over to hire swords for a fight that was none of the hirelings' concern. These were mercenaries in the plainest sense, men with no banner but coin. It would curdle later, for the first to flee when things turned hard would be that same Ghatafan. But for now the bargain held.

Then the smaller tribes were brought in, clan after clan along the broken trade route, each contributing what it could: fighters, weapons, horses, armor, and, as was the custom of that world, slaves sent to fight in their masters' place. The Quraysh stood to gain on every front, not least in food, for they were not farmers and Banu Nadir sat on the date orchards of Khaybar. The bait was perfect: an offer fallen from the sky into the laps of a people who had hunted for a way back at the Prophet ﷺ ever since Badr, who had even backed out of their own appointed rematch the year before because they did not care to face these believers alone. Now they would not have to. Almost no one would.

For this was the secret of Banu Nadir, and the Qur'an names the pattern exactly. Their whole strength had ever been the fortress: thick walls, deep stores, a place to wait out any siege. Strip away the walls, as they had been stripped, and there was nothing underneath, no capacity to stand on their own feet and fight. The Sheikh reads it as a law that history keeps proving.

They have been put under humiliation [by Allah] wherever they are overtaken, except for a rope [i.e., covenant] from Allah and a rope [i.e., treaty] from the people [i.e., the Muslims]. And they have drawn upon themselves anger from Allah and have been put under destitution. That is because they disbelieved in [i.e., rejected] the verses of Allah and killed the prophets without right. That is because they disobeyed and [habitually] transgressed.Qur'an 3:112

A rope from Allah or a rope from men: a borrowed protection, an alliance bought or begged, never a strength of their own. Unable to fight by themselves, they assembled all of Arabia to fight for them.

Two banners over the desert

When the host finally converged on the agreed ground, the books reckon it at around ten thousand, under the command of Abu Sufyan, as at Uhud. Against it Madinah could field at the very most perhaps three thousand, and likely fewer. Three to one, four to one, the exact ratio hardly matters when the smaller number is your own.

And as that army gathered, something was happening that the Arabs had never witnessed, something the Sheikh wants us to stand still and see. Until this moment the Arabs had been a scattered, tribal people; at the very most two tribes might knot together for a season to crush a third, and that was the ceiling of their unity. Now ten and fifteen tribes marched as one, under banners, and there were only two banners on the whole of that field: the banner of Islam and the banner of disbelief. Every tribe of consequence had to choose a side. In barely more than a decade, a single man who had begun with a handful of frightened followers in the alleys of Makkah had reordered the entire peninsula into one stark confrontation. But on this day, the smaller banner flew over a city about to be sealed inside a ring of steel.

A foreign idea that saved the city

You cannot hide ten thousand men. The dust of them, the noise, the talk of every traveler, all of it reached Madinah long before the army did, and the Prophet ﷺ did what was his habit before Badr, before Uhud, and now before this. He gathered the companions in the masjid, set the whole situation plainly before them, and asked: what should we do?

The books, the Sheikh notes, record almost no proposals, and in his judgment that silence is itself the answer, because there was so little to propose. When an army four times your size is bearing down on your home, the only real question left is where to plant your feet and fight. The room must have been very heavy, until at last one man spoke who had never before been free to speak at such a gathering. Salman al-Farisi.

This was the first battle of Salman's life as a free man, and to feel the weight of his counsel you must remember the road that brought him to that room. A Persian by birth, he had walked away from the fire his people worshipped and gone searching for the true religion, attaching himself to one monk after another, living years with each and burying each as he died and asking him where to go next, until the last told him the final prophet was about to rise in a land of date palms, known by three signs: he would not eat from charity, he would accept a gift, and between his shoulders would be the seal of prophethood. Salman set out, was betrayed into slavery along the way, and was dragged to a Madinah he had never meant to reach, where he found the man, saw the seal, and believed. His master would free him only for an impossible price in date palms grown to fruit, and the Prophet ﷺ had set every seedling into the earth with his own hands so that Salman went free. By the time of this battle he was an old man, seventy at the least, a lifetime of seeking finally spent in the company he had crossed the world to find. He could give the one idea no Arab in that masjid possessed.

In my homeland, Salman said, in Persia, when we feared an enemy stronger than ourselves, we would dig a trench and hold the line behind it. No army of the Arabs had ever done such a thing. It was wholly foreign, a tactic of the empires to the north.

And the Prophet ﷺ took it. Dr. Yasir Qadhi stops here, because this small decision opens onto something that runs through the entire religion. In matters of worship and creed, he says, the believer takes nothing from anyone; there we have what Allah gave us, and innovation is a defect, not a gift. But in the things of this world, in technology and tactics and the plain knowledge of what works, the believer benefits from every people on earth and never once asks where the good idea was born. Wisdom is the lost property of the believer; wherever he finds it, he picks it up. The same Prophet ﷺ who would later seal his letters in wax because that was how the kings of the earth corresponded, now ordered a Persian trench dug across the open face of his city. This was not the way of the Arabs, and he did not care that it was not. It was useful and permitted, and that was enough.

The poet who could not lift a sword

Before a single spade broke ground, the Prophet ﷺ was already thinking several moves ahead, as he always did. Once every able man stood manning the trench, the city behind them would be hollow, its women and children undefended, and one tribe still living inside the walls, Banu Qurayzah, the last of the Jewish tribes of Madinah, held loyalties no one could fully vouch for. They had signed the treaty and renewed it more than once, sworn again only recently that they stood by the covenant of the city. But a sworn word is a thin wall when ten thousand are at the gate. So he sent the families to al-Fari, the great fortress of Banu Harithah of the Ansar, the strongest the city had, built in imitation of their Jewish neighbors' fortresses. To it went the women, the children, the very old and the blind, and with them a small boy named Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr, four or five years old, the first child born to the Muhajirun after the Hijrah. And one grown man went with them. Hassan ibn Thabit.

Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi turns aside into a story he calls an awkward one, and tells it anyway, carefully, on purpose, because there is too much benefit in it to leave buried, and because if his students do not hear it from him they will one day hear it twisted from someone worse. Hassan was the poet of the Prophet ﷺ. He was not a man of the battlefield. The companions said so plainly, one using a hard, blunt word that means simply he was not brave, that a sword in his hand made him tremble. He could not fight, everyone knew it, and so without anyone needing to say a thing he was placed where the families were.

One night during the siege, when the armies were already at the walls and Banu Qurayzah had begun to turn, the tribe sent two scouts to test whether the fortress of the families was guarded, meaning to fall on the women and children if it was not. Safiyyah bint Abd al-Muttalib, the aunt of the Prophet ﷺ, heard a man scaling the wall in the dark and whispering down to a companion below. She woke Hassan, the only man there, and told him to go deal with the intruder. He could not. O aunt of the Messenger of Allah, he said, may Allah reward you, do not shame me further; this is not my field. And Safiyyah did not argue. She wrapped a man's cloak around herself so her shape in the dark would read as a defender, took a dagger between her teeth, climbed down the wall, found the climbing man and killed him, and threw his severed head down to his companion below, who fled in terror, certain now the place was full of armed men. The fortress was saved by his believing it was guarded. When she came back up and told Hassan to go strip the dead man of his weapons, he answered that he had no need of them. Radiyallahu anhu.

Why, the Sheikh asks, tell such a thing at all? Not to mock, he says firmly, for to ridicule any companion is a sin, and he repeats it in case anyone misheard: this is no ridicule. He tells it for two reasons. The first is its simple humanity. We have a habit of making the companions superhuman, and the trouble with a superhuman is that you can no longer relate to him. It is healthy to know that some had weaknesses and even sins they repented from and were forgiven, because then their nearness to Allah stops being a locked door and becomes a road we too might walk. The second reason lands like a hand on the shoulder, and it turns on what came next in Hassan's life. This same man, with this plain weakness on his record, was the one the Prophet ﷺ stood up and commanded: rise, Hassan, and defend me with your tongue, and Jibril is with you. When the Quraysh attacked the Prophet ﷺ in verse, as Arabs warred in poetry before they warred with swords, it was Hassan's reply that struck home, and no one else's. Abu Bakr could not do it. Umar could not do it. Uthman, Ali, Abu Hurayrah, none of them could. He was given his own pulpit in the masjid for his poetry, not to preach but to answer the enemy in the one weapon they could not match, and an angel carried the words to him. Every one of us, the Sheikh says, has a role no one else can fill, and our weaknesses, even our sins, are no excuse to sit out the good we alone were made to do. When his moment came, Hassan, who trembled at a sword, did what the bravest of the companions could not. Do not let Shaytan whisper, the Sheikh warns, who are you to serve this religion, have you forgotten what you did. The honest Muslim owns his sin and refuses to let it silence him.

The Prophet ﷺ in the dust

Then they dug. The trench ran, by the careful reckoning of scholars who have walked the ground, something close to two kilometers along the one exposed face of the city, wide and deep enough to stop a horse, while the rest of Madinah was shielded by what Allah had already set in place: the harrah on either side, the black fields of volcanic rock where no army can march, and beyond them date palms too thick for ten thousand men with their horses and baggage to pass. There was exactly one open stretch wide enough to admit a host that size, and so it was the very size of the enemy that dictated where the trench must run. A smaller force could have slipped through anywhere, but ten thousand needed open ground, and the open ground was the one place that had to be sealed. Their numbers had become a weakness, and the believers dug to exploit it.

Ten men were set to each section. They had almost no slaves to spare, barely a week to finish, and the bitter cold of the Arabian winter to fight through, and they dug by hand, day and night, hungry the whole time, for the city's food was already running thin before the army arrived. And what the men remembered, decades on when they were old and wealth had finally come, was not the cold or the hunger. It was the sight of the Prophet ﷺ down in the trench beside them.

He did not stand at the edge and direct the work. Al-Bara ibn Azib said he saw the Prophet ﷺ carrying the dug earth until the dust of the trench had buried his chest, and al-Bara, who knew him, added that he was a man with much hair on his chest, and every bit of it was caked with dirt. He had stripped to the labor like any other man, as hungry as the hungriest of them. And when he saw how spent they were, he made dua: O Allah, there is no good except the good of the next life, so honor the Ansar and the Muhajirun. The believers sang it back to him in one voice.

Hold that picture against the verse the Qur'an had already revealed about the people who hid behind walls.

It is He who expelled the ones who disbelieved among the People of the Scripture from their homes at the first gathering. You did not think they would leave, and they thought that their fortresses would protect them from Allah; but [the decree of] Allah came upon them from where they had not expected, and He cast terror into their hearts [so] they destroyed their houses by their [own] hands and the hands of the believers. So take warning, O people of vision.Qur'an 59:2

They thought their fortresses would protect them from Allah. That was the expelled, trusting in stone, and the stone did not save them when Allah came at them from where they never looked. And here was the exact opposite of that trust made flesh: a Prophet ﷺ with no fortress at all, sweating in a ditch, whose whole defense was not in walls but in his Lord. As they worked they chanted together, every man lifting the final words in one chorus: by Allah, were it not for You we would never have been guided, nor given charity, nor prayed, so send down tranquility upon us and make our feet firm if we meet the enemy, for they have transgressed against us, and if they intend fitnah, we refuse. Ten thousand were coming over the horizon. The believers answered them with shovels and a song.

A wall of trust, and a siege ahead

Understand, finally, what the trench truly was, because the Sheikh is careful we do not mistake it for more than it was. It was not a fortress and was never meant to become one. It was a stalling tactic, an act, in plain words, of holy desperation. The city had been short of food before the first enemy tent went up, and now the believers were sealing themselves inside a ring they could not easily resupply across. They could not outlast ten thousand fed on the harvest of Khaybar. All the trench could buy was time, a stretch of days held open by a ditch and a prayer, and into that bought time the believers poured the one thing they had in abundance: their trust in a Lord who does not abandon His servants. Plan with everything in your hands, the day teaches, dig until the dust covers your chest, and then rest the outcome where it always rested, with the only One who decides it.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi leaves us here on purpose, the way a storyteller leaves you at the lip of the cliff. The trench is dug, and the dust is still on the Prophet's ﷺ chest. The women and children are walled inside al-Fari, where one scout already lies dead at the foot of the wall. Ten thousand men are massing on the only open ground, under two banners, and somewhere inside the city the last uncertain tribe is weighing which way to turn. What comes next, the Sheikh promises, is a string of wonders: the great rock in the trench that would not break until the Prophet ﷺ struck it himself and saw, by its sparks, the palaces of empires yet to fall; the little food that fed an entire army; the predictions he gave from the bottom of a ditch with the enemy at the gate. But all of that waits for the days ahead. Tonight the trench is finished, the cold is deep, the city is sealed, and a believing people who have run out of every option except trust stand at their ditch and lift their eyes to the horizon, and wait.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read the digging of the trench as a clever piece of military history, the day the Muslims out-engineered a bigger army. That would be to miss every question it puts to us, and it puts several.

It asks, first, what we do with wisdom that arrives from outside our own walls. The trench was a Persian idea in an Arab war, foreign to everyone in that masjid, and the Prophet ﷺ took it without a flicker of hesitation, because it was true and it worked and its birthplace was irrelevant. We are quick to do the reverse, to reject what is good because of where it came from, and just as quick to swallow what is harmful because it wears a familiar face. The believer's rule is cleaner than either reflex: in worship and creed we hold fast to what Allah revealed, and in the work of this world we take the good wherever we find it and never ask its pedigree. Wisdom is the lost property of the believer; go and reclaim what is yours.

It asks, second, what our weaknesses are allowed to cost us. Hassan ibn Thabit could not carry a sword, everyone knew it, and he himself never pretended otherwise. He had every excuse to stay small and silent. And when his moment came he stood and did what Abu Bakr and Umar could not, with an angel at his side. There is a thing only you can do, some good shaped exactly to your hand and no one else's, and the voice that tells you to sit it out because of your faults is not humility, it is the whisper of the one who wants the good left undone. Own your sin, and rise anyway. That is what a believer does with a flaw.

And it asks, last and deepest, whether we know the difference between effort and reliance, and how to give ourselves wholly to both. The believers dug a trench they knew, even as they dug it, could not hold forever. They took every means a desperate people could take, in the cold and the hunger, with the Prophet ﷺ himself buried to the chest in the dirt of it. And then, having done all of it, they did not lean on the ditch. They leaned on Allah, and sang it aloud that were it not for Him they would not even be guided. The expelled had trusted their fortresses and been found from where they never expected; the believers trusted no wall, only their Lord, and dug like men who knew that digging is part of trust and not its rival. This is the balance the Trench was built to teach, and most of us never quite learn it: to take every lawful means within reach and then to rest, truly rest, in the certainty that the result was never in our hands to begin with. The ten thousand held every visible advantage. The believers held a ditch, a prayer, and a Prophet ﷺ in the dust. But you already know, if you have read this far, in whose hands the outcome was always going to rest.

So carry one thing from this freezing night into your own days. When good comes to you from an unfamiliar source, weigh it by its truth and not its origin. When your weakness tempts you to sit out the good only you can do, stand up, faults and all, the way a poet who could not hold a sword once stood. And the next time you face something far larger than yourself, do every honest thing within your power until the dust covers your chest, then lift your eyes from the work to the One who decides what it comes to, and trust Him with the rest. May Allah make our feet firm when the armies of our own lives gather against us, fill the time our effort buys with tranquility from Him, and send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, who met ten thousand not first with a sword but with a shovel, and led his people from the bottom of the trench.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 57: the Battle of Khandaq, part 1 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (4:51, 3:112, 59:2), verified via quran.ai. The narration is the Sheikh's; the phrasing is Buruja's.

The Seerah · 58 of 104

The Battle of the Trench, part 2

Hunger, miracles, and the night the city was surrounded

The trench is already being dug. On the advice of Salman al-Farisi, the Prophet ﷺ has taken charge of the work himself, walking the ground along the exposed northern face of Madinah and, by one report, marking with his own hand the line the long ditch would follow. He divided the men into sections and managed the logistics the way he managed everything, from inside the labor and not above it. Now the digging is underway. And from the very first days, something is wrong: there is no food.

This is the second night of a siege that will take four to tell, and it is the part where everything tightens at once. The cold of a Madinan winter closes in. The hunger sharpens until grown men are tying stones to their stomachs. Out beyond the trench an army gathers that is larger than anything the Arabs have ever assembled, an alliance so wide the Qur'an gives it its own name. And before the siege is over, the believers will learn that the danger is no longer only in front of them. It is behind them too, inside the walls, among people they thought were bound to them by oath.

A trench dug on an empty stomach

It was winter, the hardest season Madinah knows. No fruit was ripening on the trees, the stores were already thin, and the cold of the place bit hard. Dr. Yasir Qadhi is honest with you here, and the honesty is part of the lesson: the books of seerah never quite explain why the shortage struck so suddenly and so completely from the very first days of digging. We are left to piece the reasons together. Perhaps it was the season, with nothing being harvested. Perhaps no one had prepared for a siege, laid in no extra supplies against the day the caravans would stop. Perhaps it was simply that every able man was now in the ditch instead of in the market, so trade halted and what little there was ran out fast. Most likely it was all of these together, a thing that took them so completely by surprise that the larders were bare before anyone could stock them. The plain fact, however we account for it, is that for the opening days of the work the companions of the Prophet ﷺ did not have enough to eat.

What they did have was barely food at all. Bukhari preserves the detail: the companions had dried barley that they would mix with a little oil, a coarse paste, and one of them admitted that it carried such a bad smell they had to force it down, eating it only because there was nothing else. Jabir radiyallahu anhu remembered days when the only thing to be found were the pits of dates, which they would put in their mouths simply to have something there. And then there is the image the whole episode turns upon. The Prophet ﷺ was down in the trench doing manual labor with the rest of them, and as he raised his arm to swing the pick his shirt lifted, and Jabir saw it: a stone bound tight against his stomach. In those days a hungry man would lash a rock to his belly and cinch it hard, partly to dull the gnawing, partly because the body, wound tight, half believes it has been fed. The leader of all of them was starving exactly as they were, and he had told no one. They learned it only because, for a moment, his shirt slipped.

A thousand fed from one small goat

Jabir could not carry what he had seen. He was newly married, only seventeen or eighteen, the same young companion of the famous story of the camel. He went home to his wife and told her plainly: I have seen something I do not have the strength to bear. The Prophet ﷺ is in that state. What do we have in the house? A little barley dough, she said, and one small goat, a kid really, too young to be worth slaughtering, so little meat would come of it. That is all we have. Then make the bread, he told her, and he slaughtered the kid and set it to cook. He went quietly to the Prophet ﷺ and whispered an invitation meant for a small table: my wife has prepared a little food, so come, you and one or two others, and eat with us.

And here the famous thing happened. The Prophet ﷺ stood and called out to the whole of the trench: O people of the trench, Jabir and his wife have made a meal for you, all of you come. Jabir went running home in something close to panic, because the food was meant for three or four men and now an army was rising to follow. But his wife asked one steadying question. Did you invite them, she said, or did he? He invited them, said Jabir. Then leave it to him, she answered, and her faith was perfectly calm. He knows what he is doing.

The Prophet ﷺ reached the food first, and he gave one instruction: do not lift the pot off the fire, and do not bake the bread, until I come. When he arrived, the meat was still cooking. He made du'a over the pot and the dough, and as a means of barakah put some of his blessed saliva into them, and asked Allah to bless it. Then he sent the people in to eat ten at a time, because the small room would hold only ten. A group ate their fill and left, and another ten came, and another, and the pot did not empty and the bread did not run out, until every last person there had eaten to satisfaction. Long afterward one of the narrators asked Jabir how many they had been. Around a thousand, he said. The whole people of the trench, fed from one small kid goat and a handful of barley dough that had been meant for a single household.

This, Dr. Yasir Qadhi explains, is the very essence of what barakah means: not more stuff appearing, but an increase in good placed into what is already there. The quantity looks unchanged, and yet Allah stretches it, so that a single plate feeds a thousand and a single dollar does the work of a hundred. When we beg Allah for barakah in our provision, this is what we are asking for, that the little He has given us be made to act as though it were limitless. The companions saw three or four such food miracles in this siege, the same pattern each time. Jabir's is the one remembered above all the others, for the sheer scale of it.

The rock that showed him three empires

Then the diggers hit a rock. It sat directly on the line the Prophet ﷺ had drawn, and no matter how the companions went down into the ditch one after another and hacked at it, it would not break. Some of them said the sensible thing: let us simply dig around it, the trench will be no weaker for a stone left in place. But one of them refused. No, he said, not until we ask him, because he is the one who showed us this line. Look, the Sheikh pauses here, at how meticulously they followed even the smallest matter of his Sunnah. It was only a stone, and the defense would not suffer if they curved the line around it. And still they would not move it on their own, because he had said the trench runs here, and so they went to ask rather than decide for themselves.

So they brought him down into the trench, and he took the pick. He struck the rock once, and a third of it crumbled away, and he said Allahu akbar, I have been given the keys of Sham, by Allah I can see its red palaces from where I stand. He struck a second time, and another third fell away, and he said Allahu akbar, I have been given the keys of Persia, I can see the white palace of al-Mada'in. He struck a third time and the rock shattered entirely, and he said Allahu akbar, I have been given the keys of Yemen, I can see its gates from this very place.

Sit with the moment in which those words were spoken. A starving, outnumbered band, penned behind a ditch in their own besieged city, bracing for the hour they might be overrun, and their Prophet ﷺ is standing among them naming the empires that will fall to them. And they fell, in exactly the order he gave: Sham first, in the days of Abu Bakr and the early reign of Umar; then Persia, the great empire of the Sasanians broken outright; then Yemen, begun in his ﷺ own lifetime and completed after him. Dr. Yasir Qadhi calls this what it is, a miracle no historian has ever truly explained. How could a band of desert tribesmen, fewer in number, poorer in arms, untrained against the great civilizations of the age, carve away half the Roman empire and topple the Persian one entirely? The historians have a thousand theories and not one sound explanation. The white pillars of al-Mada'in, the palace of Ctesiphon, the capital he described without ever having laid eyes on it, still tower against the sky today.

The word over Ammar

While they worked, the Prophet ﷺ watched young Ammar ibn Yasir radiyallahu anhu carrying twice the load of every other man, the same fierce and determined youth who had watched his own mother and father martyred in Makkah for refusing to renounce their faith. What Ammar had seen had made him this kind of man. Covered in dust, worn down, hungry like the rest, he drew from the Prophet ﷺ a word that would echo for decades: may Allah have mercy on you, the transgressing party will be the ones to kill you.

Keep the Sheikh's care with these words, because they are easy to mishandle. The Prophet ﷺ did not call them disbelievers, and he did not call them evil. He named a group that would step outside the bounds of the law, that would disobey the rightful authority of its time: in the wrong, yes, but believers still. Years later, in the bitter strife between Ali and Muawiyah, Ammar was an old man fighting on the side of Ali, and an arrow from Muawiyah's side struck him down. That side recognized the sign at once, because everyone remembered the prophecy: the transgressing party would kill Ammar. We hold that Ali was the one closer to the truth, and that Muawiyah made a sincere judgment in which he erred, and we ask Allah's forgiveness for him, for it was a mistake of judgment and not of faith. Some scholars note the saying may have been spoken twice, once at the building of the Prophet's masjid and again here at the trench. Either way, the words were planted in the soil of memory long before the day they grew true.

The Confederates, and the men who led them

This is the war the Qur'an calls al-Ahzab, the Confederates, and the name is the key that unlocks the whole battle. This was not a single army under a single commander. Quraysh came with four thousand men, the largest force they had ever assembled in their history, with three hundred horses and a great train of camels, and even that was only the core. Other tribes brought their hundreds and thousands, group stitched onto group by bribes and promises until something near ten thousand stood outside Madinah. Ahzab is the plural of hizb, a party or faction, and that is precisely the point: this was an alliance of factions, not a nation. Because there was no one leader they all obeyed, only a treaty of convenience, the whole thing was brittle. And that brittleness, the Sheikh notes, is exactly what Allah would later use to break them apart, for when there is no unity an alliance fragments at the first hard pressure.

Their leaders were a study in contrasts, and their later histories carry their own lessons. Over Quraysh stood Abu Sufyan, whose given name was Sakhr, now the most senior and noble man of his people after so many of their chiefs had fallen at Badr. He was about ten years older than the Prophet ﷺ, an enemy, yes, but a dignified one, who never once stooped to the gutter taunts of an Abu Jahl. The Sheikh draws a quiet rule from this, with its exceptions admitted: those who sank to crude insult generally showed a meanness that was never guided to faith, while those who kept some dignity in their enmity were, most of them, eventually guided. Abu Sufyan is the great example. He would accept Islam at the conquest of Makkah, and at Hunayn the Prophet ﷺ would hand him a fortune in spoils, until Abu Sufyan said: I fought you and you were the noblest of enemies, and I made peace with you and you are the noblest of friends. Generosity finished what war could not, and faith entered his heart in earnest. His son Muawiyah, born to that same hunger for leadership, would one day found a dynasty.

Beside Abu Sufyan rode rougher men. There was Uyaynah ibn Hisn, a coarse Bedouin chief, who once barged into the Prophet's ﷺ own home without seeking permission and, asked where his manners were, boasted that he had never in his life asked any man's leave to enter. After he left, the Prophet ﷺ named him for what he was: the fool who is obeyed by his people, and explained that the worst of mankind are those people are forced to be kind to only to be spared their evil. And there was Tulayha al-Asadi, leader of the Banu Asad, who stood that day outside Madinah unbelieving. After the Prophet ﷺ died, Tulayha would be one of those who claimed prophethood, until he was defeated and his tribe returned to Islam. He fled to Syria, and there, away from everything, he genuinely repented and came back seeking forgiveness, which Abu Bakr granted him. He carried his guilt openly for the rest of his life, begged Allah to accept him as a martyr, and died one at al-Qadisiyyah. The Sheikh holds his story up as real hope: if a man who went so far as to claim to be a prophet can repent and be received, then the lesser sins that weigh on us are no obstacle to a sincere return.

There is wisdom even in who refused to join. Ghatafan, the second largest contingent after Quraysh, had been promised half the date harvest of Khaybar by the Jewish leaders who had stitched the alliance together. Before the siege closed in, the Prophet ﷺ explored buying them off, sending word that they might withdraw in exchange for a third of Madinah's own harvest, and there is nothing forbidden in such a thing, paying to be spared an enemy's evil. He consulted the two chiefs of the Ansar, Sa'd ibn Mu'adh of the Aws and Sa'd ibn Ubadah of the Khazraj. They asked him a single question: is this something Allah has commanded you, or your own idea that we are free to weigh? It is my own idea, he indicated. Then no, they said. We never bowed to these people in the days of our ignorance, when we did not know Allah, so why would we hand them our wealth in humiliation now that Allah has honored us with Islam? Between us and them there is only the sword. And the Prophet ﷺ was pleased, and sent the messenger back with nothing. That, the Sheikh observes, is part of why Allah chose the Ansar to carry this religion: a people who had never been conquered, never been made to kneel, and would not learn the posture now.

The longest, most fearful nights

The believers camped with their backs to the mountain of Sila and the trench before them, with Madinah at their rear. The women and children were gathered into the fortress of one of the Ansar, a stronghold called Fari, for fear of treachery from inside the city. The trench itself had taken perhaps six days to dig, no more than two weeks, but the siege stretched on for the better part of a month, and across all those freezing days and nights the believers held the line of the ditch.

Aisha radiyallahu anha was there, in the Prophet's ﷺ own tent, for she would not be parted from him. She said afterward that she had witnessed many battles in which there was fighting and fear, but never one more exhausting, never one more terrifying for the Muslims, than this. Her fear, she said, was for the women and children at the mercy of the tribe inside the walls. Shifts of companions patrolled the entire length of the trench all night long, in the freezing dark, and their task was simply to call out Allahu akbar, Allahu akbar, so that any enemy creeping toward the ditch in the blackness would believe a great host stood waiting on the other side. There were no lights to string along miles of open ground. There were only voices in the cold, and Aisha said you could hear the takbir of the patrols going round and round until the dawn.

The Prophet ﷺ took a watch of his own, standing guard over a stretch of the valley like any of them. One bitter night Abbad ibn Bishr came to the tent in his armor, the clinking of it carrying through the dark, and offered to stand the Prophet's ﷺ shift so that he might rest. The Prophet ﷺ lay down, and he was so worn through with exhaustion that he fell at once into a sleep deep enough that they could hear him breathing. And through it all the men would step out into the cold to keep their watch, then duck back into shelter only long enough to thaw a little before going out again. Hungry, sleepless, frozen. It was precisely here, at the lowest point of their strength, that the heaviest blow fell.

The treachery within

Inside Madinah, behind the trench and the believers' backs, lived the Jewish tribe of Banu Qurayzah, still bound to the Muslims by the treaty of the city. Huyayy ibn Akhtab, chief of the already exiled Banu an-Nadir and among the bitterest enemies of the Prophet ﷺ, slipped back into the city he knew intimately, for the trench could keep out armies but not a single man who knew every lane of the place. He went to the leader of Banu Qurayzah, Ka'b ibn Asad, and set to work on him. At first Ka'b refused outright, reminding Huyayy that there was a covenant between his people and Muhammad ﷺ, and he refused three times over. But Huyayy knew his people, and knew what could move them, and he wore Ka'b down with promise after promise of immunity and protection and a share of the spoils, until at last the covenant was broken. Now the danger was no longer only the ten thousand in front of the ditch. There was a hostile force of perhaps fifteen hundred to two thousand fighting men inside the city, and the women and children barely shielded from them.

The Prophet ﷺ did not act on rumor. The treachery began as a fear, then a whisper, and he would not move against an entire tribe on a presumption. I fear something from Banu Qurayzah, he said. Who will go and find out? Az-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam stood up, alone, to go and spy on them, a very brave thing to do single-handed, and the Prophet ﷺ said the famous words: every prophet has a disciple, and my disciple is az-Zubayr. Az-Zubayr came back to report that the treaty was undone. But because certainty had to be total before any action, the Prophet ﷺ sent four leaders of the Aws who had been close to the tribe before Islam to put the question directly, telling them to announce it openly if the news was good but to give him only a hint if it was bad, so as not to break the believers' spirit. They went, and Ka'b ibn Asad met them with arrogance and denial. Who is Muhammad, he said, we have no treaty with him, and by one report he had already torn the document up. One of the four, enraged, began to curse him, and Ka'b answered that the matter between them was now beyond cursing. The four came back and confirmed it: the treaty was no longer named or honored, treachery plainly in the air.

Now picture it as the believers did. Ten thousand outside the ditch. Some two thousand turned hostile inside it. The women and children exposed, the food all but gone, the nights freezing, the men sleepless, and no sign anywhere of where help could possibly come from. This, so many of them said afterward, was the worst night of their lives. And it is precisely this moment that Allah Himself describes in Surah al-Ahzab:

[Remember] when they came at you from above you and from below you, and when eyes shifted [in fear], and hearts reached the throats, and you assumed about Allah [various] assumptions.Qur'an 33:10

They came at you from above, the Sheikh explains, meaning the Confederates massed beyond the trench, and from below you, meaning Banu Qurayzah risen within the city, the enemy now on every side. And the description of the heart climbing into the throat is no figure of speech invented by a poet. Anyone who has ever stood in a genuinely life threatening moment knows the sensation exactly, the heart straining as though it would force its way up and out.
