The believers reached that edge, and some among them, sincere companions, began to entertain the dark assumption the verse records, that Allah might not help them after all. The next ayah does not soften it:

There the believers were tested and shaken with a severe shaking.Qur'an 33:11

Shaken to the very core, and the Sheikh is careful to draw the right conclusion from it. To be shaken in a real crisis is not hypocrisy. It is not the serene certainty of an Abu Bakr or an Umar, which sits far above, but it is not the disease of the hypocrite either. It is only the proof that a believer has not yet reached the highest rank, that his trust, real and saving, is not yet flawless. Put yourself honestly in their place: weeks gone by, the food dwindling, ten thousand outside and an army within, and no human eye able to see the way out. Of course the heart trembles. And it was here, at this exact cliff edge, that the tide was about to turn. We must leave them on the very edge of it, the trench held through the dark by tired men shouting Allahu akbar at an enemy they could not see, the help not yet come and very near.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read part two of the trench as a sequence of wonders, the goat and the rock and the patrols in the dark, and to file it away as a stirring story safely sealed in the past. That would be to miss what it asks of us. Strip the siege down to the experience of the men who lived it, hungry, freezing, surrounded, betrayed from behind, and it stops being a tale and becomes a mirror.

He went hungry first, and told no one. The leader of the whole community tied a stone to his own stomach and swung a pick in the ditch beside the youngest of his followers, and they discovered his hunger only by accident, when his shirt slipped. This is the shape of real leadership, the kind the seerah keeps insisting on: not the man who is served from the front of the line, but the one who carries the heaviest load quietly from inside the trench and lets no one see what it costs him.

Then learn what to ask Allah for. One small goat, a kid not even worth slaughtering, fed a thousand men, because the Prophet ﷺ asked Allah to put barakah in it. We are forever asking for more, a larger sum, a longer day, a stronger body, when the companions were taught to ask for something subtler and greater: that the little already in our hands be stretched to do the work of much.

Follow the line he drew. They would not move a single stone out of the trench, though the defense would not have suffered, because he had marked the line and the matter was his to decide and not theirs. There is an entire discipline of love folded into that refusal: a precision in following him ﷺ that does not round off the small things we find inconvenient. The companions did not reason that way about a rock. We might ask whether we reason that way about far weightier things he left us.

And then there is the trembling itself, and what Allah said about it. He did not record that the believers at the trench felt no fear. He recorded the opposite, that their eyes shifted and their hearts climbed into their throats and they were shaken with a severe shaking, and then He called them believers in the very same breath. Hold onto that the next time a real crisis empties the ground from beneath you and you feel your faith shake. To be afraid in a genuine danger is not the failure. The failure would have been to let go of the rope. They trembled, and they held the line through the trembling, and they waited for Allah, and that holding through the fear is itself a station of faith. The Prophet ﷺ named Sham and Persia and Yemen while penned behind a ditch with nothing, and the keys he promised were real keys. They simply turned years later, long after the night that looked like the promise's opposite.

So take this night down into your own narrow places. When the burden falls, carry it quietly from inside the work. Ask Allah not only for more, but for barakah in the little you already hold. And when the ground shakes and the help is nowhere in sight and your own heart climbs into your throat, do not mistake the trembling for the end of your faith. Hold the line in the dark, keep calling on your Lord, and wait, because the help that came to the trench is the help of the One who does not change. May Allah, who fed a thousand from the little in Jabir's house, put barakah in our little; who steadied the hearts that reached the throats at the trench, steady ours when the ground shakes beneath us; and gather us with His Messenger ﷺ, who went hungry so that we might be fed.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 58: the Battle of the Trench, part 2 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (33:10, 33:11), verified via quran.ai.

The Seerah · 59 of 104

The Battle of Khandaq, part 3: the siege from within

The night the believers were shaken to the core

The trench held. Ten thousand men had ridden up to the edge of Madinah expecting an open plain and a quick slaughter, and instead they found a ditch they could not cross, dug across the one exposed flank of the city by a people who had never seen such a thing in the wars of the Arabs. So the great army did the only thing left to it. It sat down. We have ten thousand, the chiefs reasoned, we have supplies and we can fetch more, and the believers are trapped inside with nowhere to run. It is a waiting game now, and we will win it.

But a siege is not won at the walls alone. It is won, or lost, in the hearts of the people penned inside them, and it is to those hearts that Dr. Yasir Qadhi turns on this third day with the Battle of the Trench. The danger this time does not come charging on a horse. It slips into the city through a door at the back, it whispers in the gatherings of the faithful, and it bleeds slowly out of the arm of a beloved man. This is the longest night Madinah ever knew, fought entirely from within.

The whisper at the back door

Inside the walls there was still one tribe that had not joined the fight: Banu Qurayza, bound to the Prophet ﷺ by a written treaty, a covenant he had confirmed with them at least three separate times, the most recent only a few months before. They were the last loose piece on the board, and the enemy outside knew exactly what their defection would be worth. So a man came for them in the dark.

His name was Huyayy ibn Akhtab, a chieftain of the Jewish tribes already exiled from Madinah, and the father of the woman who would one day become the Prophet's ﷺ wife, Safiyyah. He knew Madinah well, and you cannot guard an entire city, so he made his way back inside and went straight to the fortress of Ka'b ibn Asad, the chief of Banu Qurayza. But word of his coming had reached the tribe, and it frightened them, because Huyayy at the gate could only mean one thing. So Ka'b shut the door and would not open it.

Huyayy stood outside and knocked. You bring nothing but bad omens, Ka'b called back through the door, your very presence here is misfortune; go away. I have a treaty with Muhammad ﷺ, he said, and I will not break my word, for I have never seen from him anything but truthfulness and faithfulness. But Huyayy kept knocking, kept begging, until at last he changed his weapon. The only reason you keep this door shut, he taunted, is that you are too stingy even to share your food with an old friend left alone out here in the night. It was crude, it was emotional blackmail, and it worked. The insult stung the door open.

And the instant it swung wide, the real assault began. I have brought you oceans of men, Huyayy told him, the chiefs of every tribe, all here with their armies, and they have sworn they will not leave until Muhammad ﷺ and his companions are wiped from the earth. He knew exactly which nerve to press. Deep down Ka'b wanted to side with his own people, and the only thing holding him back was the fear of treachery and defeat. So he hammered at that fear with numbers. How could a plan this large possibly fail? This tribe and that tribe, this many men and that many, the whole of Arabia at the gates. He pressed, and pressed, and pressed.

A man who sealed his own verdict

Ka'b's instinct was screaming the truth at him the whole time. He answered Huyayy with an Arab proverb sharp as a blade: you are a cloud that promises rain and gives only thunder and lightning. You make these fat promises, he was saying, but the end of it will leave us ruined. There was a sixth sense in him that would not quiet, this is not right, this is not right. But Huyayy would not let go. All through the night he kept at it, relentless, until finally he played the one card that could not be answered.

To prove how certain he was, Huyayy pledged that if the great armies left without finishing the work, he himself would stay behind inside the city and share Banu Qurayza's fate, whatever that fate turned out to be. He did not use the word hostage, but that was the meaning: I will throw in my own lot with yours. And here Dr. Yasir Qadhi makes you stop, because that is the line that decided everything. A man does not stake his own life on a plan unless he is certain it cannot fail. From Ka'b's vantage, how could ten thousand lose to a few thousand trapped behind a ditch? They had only to wait, and Huyayy's willingness to die with them if he was wrong was the final proof that tipped him over the edge.

By morning, Huyayy had won. The treaty was a written covenant, and Ka'b tore it up with his own hands. Hold onto that detail, the Sheikh insists, because people look at the punishment that will eventually come down on this tribe and tear it loose from everything that came before it. They look at the verdict without the context, without remembering how many times these people had confirmed their word. They had pledged peace in writing, three times over, the last time barely months earlier. And they tore it up at the single worst possible moment for the people they had sworn it to, with an enemy army of ten thousand camped at the gates. The road to the punishment ran straight through their own choice, and Huyayy, in his confidence, had pronounced his own sentence too. That pledge to share their fate would be honored to the letter. Allah would see to it.

Send me the wise ones

The first hint that something had gone wrong came from Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, watching the perimeter. He could see Huyayy slipping toward the fortress; he could not hear a word that passed. So he came back with a suspicion, not a fact, and for it the Prophet ﷺ gave him a title that stayed on him forever: every prophet has a disciple, and you are mine.

But a suspicion seen from a distance was not enough to act on. So the Prophet ﷺ chose his envoys with great care, the senior companions of the Ansar who had held the oldest and best ties to this tribe: Sa'd ibn Mu'adh, leader of the Aws, and Sa'd ibn Ubadah, leader of the Khazraj, with a small delegation of their elders. Go and confirm this news, he told them, and if it is true, do not announce it; only hint it to me. Notice why. He was not behind palace walls with secretaries guarding the way in, the way of emperors; he was out among his people on the front line, and anything said to him could be heard by everyone around him. If they have turned traitor, signal it quietly, he said, do not spread fear among the people and weaken them. But if they are still faithful, then shout it out for all to hear, so the people are put at ease.

The delegation found Banu Qurayza at their very worst, the most vulgar and foul mouthed the companions had ever seen them. We know no Muhammad, they sneered, and we have no treaty with him. It was a blatant lie, and everyone knew it, the very pinnacle of arrogance, from people who had physically torn that treaty to pieces only days earlier. Sa'd ibn Ubadah, known for his temper, flared and traded curses with them. But Sa'd ibn Mu'adh, the calmer of the two, held him back. My brother, he said, what stands between us and them now is far greater than any insult. Cursing will not help us. The two Sa'ds rode back and gave the Prophet ﷺ their coded signal: they simply named two tribes who in the past had feigned faith with Muslims only to gather them in and massacre them. That was the whole message, and he understood it at once.

When that signal reached him, the Prophet ﷺ said the last thing any heart on earth would have said. Glad tidings, he told them. Good news. He alone, with a trust in Allah no other soul could hold, looked at the worst report imaginable and saw something good buried inside it. This was the final tribe in all of Madinah still outside Islam, and their breaking of the treaty meant that, in time, the whole of the city would be made firm under the faith. Who else could take news like that and find in it a reason to rejoice? For everyone else, this was the worst night of the entire siege. There were now at least two thousand fighting men among Banu Qurayza, with a fortress of their own that they could move in and out of at will, and they sat inside the city walls, between the believers and their undefended women and children. How do you guard against an enemy already inside while another army of ten thousand waits beyond the ditch? This is the night Allah Himself describes, the hour when even the strongest of the companions felt their minds run wild.

[Remember] when they came at you from above you and from below you, and when eyes shifted [in fear], and hearts reached the throats, and you assumed about Allah [various] assumptions.Qur'an 33:10
There the believers were tested and shaken with a severe shaking.Qur'an 33:11

When the mask came off

Pressure separates people, and this night separated the believers from the hypocrites once and for all. The munafiqun came asking permission to slip home, with the excuse that their houses stood open and their families exposed. But Allah names the excuse for exactly what it was. Their homes were not exposed at all; they were simply too cowardly to meet the enemy, and they wanted only to flee.

And [remember] when the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is disease said, "Allah and His Messenger did not promise us except delusion,"Qur'an 33:12
And when a faction of them said, "O people of Yathrib, there is no stability for you [here], so return [home]." And a party of them asked permission of the Prophet, saying, "Indeed, our houses are exposed [i.e., unprotected]," while they were not exposed. They did not intend except to flee.Qur'an 33:13

One of them said it aloud in the gathering, dripping with contempt. Here was Muhammad, he scoffed, naming him flatly, and the flatness was itself a sign, for the believer does not call the Messenger ﷺ the way he calls any ordinary man. Here was Muhammad promising us the treasures of Kisra and Qaysar, the emperors of Persia and Rome, and now not one of us can so much as step outside to relieve himself for fear. The word he used was crude, and it was flung at the noblest of creation in the hardest hour of his life. That contempt drew a line straight through the Book itself, for Allah warned the believers never to address the Prophet ﷺ as they addressed one another, and in the same breath exposed the ones quietly slipping away under cover of the crowd.

Do not make [your] calling of the Messenger among yourselves as the call of one of you to another. Already Allah knows those of you who slip away, concealed by others. So let those beware who dissent from his [i.e., the Prophet's] order, lest fitnah strike them or a painful punishment.Qur'an 24:63

Now stand at the far end of that mocking sentence. Six or seven years later, not a lifetime, not even a full decade, the Muslims were eating from the dinner plates of the Persian emperor, exactly as the Prophet ﷺ had promised them, at the close of Abu Bakr's caliphate and the dawn of Umar's. The hypocrite could not see past his own fear, and so the promise looked to him like a delusion. The believer simply had to wait, and trust, and watch the word of Allah come true.

There is a second lesson folded into this night that the Sheikh calls one of the greatest benefits of the whole siege. The treachery was real and confirmed, no rumor and no exaggeration, and still the Prophet ﷺ said: keep it low, do not go telling everyone. Not because it was false, but because spreading even true evil corrodes the people who hear it. Islam does not sensationalize, even the truth. This stands in complete contrast to the world we live in, where every gruesome detail must be analyzed and exposed, and a heart fed a constant diet of crime and cruelty grows numb to it. Madinah was the ideal community, and still it was not a flawless utopia; even there, the most authentic reports tell us, ugly things sometimes happened. The way of the Prophet ﷺ was not to parade them but to keep them covered, so the public heart stays soft. Allah teaches the same restraint elsewhere: when a frightening report reaches people, the instinct is to spread it; the command is to carry it instead to those who can weigh it rightly.

And when there comes to them something [i.e., information] about [public] security or fear, they spread it around. But if they had referred it back to the Messenger or to those of authority among them, then the ones who [can] draw correct conclusions from it would have known about it. And if not for the favor of Allah upon you and His mercy, you would have followed Satan, except for a few.Qur'an 4:83

The instinct to expose, to analyze, to make every ugly thing public, is simply not the instinct Islam trains into you. It trains you to cover, to protect, and to keep the heart of your community soft.

The smallest count of all

Out at the trench, a handful of small skirmishes broke out. At one narrow point where no one stood on the far side, a band of five horsemen flung their mounts across and broke through. At their head rode Amr ibn Abd Wud, an elder of the Quraysh famous for his ferocity, a man who had wounded and killed at Badr, and who now wore the red turban that was his open boast of death, a sign known to both armies: come and fight me, and I will kill you.

He called out for a duel. A young man rose to answer, in his mid twenties, no match on paper for a warrior with decades of battle behind him: Ali ibn Abi Talib. The Prophet ﷺ held him back and told him to let an elder go. No elder rose. Amr called a second time, and a third, and each time Ali asked again, until the Prophet ﷺ saw the zeal in him and let him go. Amr knew the boy, had watched him grow up, and waved him off: go back, child, send me a man, I have no wish to kill you. And Ali answered him: but I have a wish to kill you. Enraged, Amr charged, and the two horses raised a wall of dust that swallowed them both, and the only word that came back out of it was a single Allahu Akbar. Ali had won, with a blow so swift it ended the fight before it began. Nearby, Zubayr struck down another of the attackers, Nawfal, with a blow so tremendous that the body split clean in two; when the companions marveled at his sword, he answered, it is not the sword, it is the arm.

When the Quraysh called out, offering to buy back Nawfal's corpse so they could bury it, the Prophet ﷺ refused their money. This corpse is filthy and so is its price, he told them; we have no need of the price of corpses, come and take it and bury it. From Badr onward he had given even his enemies the dignity of burial, and took nothing for it. And here is the wonder the Sheikh asks you to sit with. This was the largest army ever gathered against the Prophet ﷺ in his entire life, ten thousand strong, and yet the Battle of the Trench produced one of the very smallest counts of the dead of any major battle he ever fought. Three or four of the enemy, six or perhaps seven of the Muslims, most of those last by arrows showered in from across the ditch rather than by any direct fighting. You could nearly count the fallen on the fingers of one hand. The largest gathering, the smallest toll. It is a sign from Allah to reflect upon: when Allah fights directly on your side, even the numbers bow.

The arrow that found Sa'd

The true tragedy of the Trench was not any duel. It was a single arrow. Sa'd ibn Mu'adh, the chieftain of the Aws and one of the two envoys sent to Banu Qurayza, came to his mother's tent to bid her farewell before taking his place on the line. The verses of hijab had not yet come down, the Sheikh notes, for they would be revealed only months later, among the last commandments of all, a reminder that the faith was built up from its foundations first and its details last. So his mother could see him as he came, dressed in his armor, and she urged him not to linger: hurry, my son, you are late, everyone else is already at the front. That was the iman of these people, no dallying even in a mother's farewell. But as he left she worried aloud at how sparse his armor was, his chest covered but his arms left bare, for he could not afford more. An arrow struck him in the exposed upper part of his arm, exactly where she had feared, and drove deep. The man who shot it, aiming for the gap, cried out his own name in triumph: take that from me. Sa'd answered him only with a prayer against him. This was the wound that would not close. It bled, and bled, and bled, and in the end it would take his life.

Sa'd ibn Mu'adh was no ordinary man. He had accepted Islam early, at the hands of Musab ibn Umayr, and his standing among his people was such that when he embraced the faith his whole subtribe entered Islam out of love for him. It was Sa'd who, on the eve of Badr, had risen and spoken the words that steadied the entire army: go wherever Allah has shown you, and we are with you; we will never say to you what the people of Musa said to him, you and your Lord go and fight while we sit here. And the Prophet ﷺ carried this man in his heart so deeply that years after Sa'd was gone, gazing at the finest robe a foreign king had ever sent him, a Persian brocade so dazzling that the companions could not stop circling him to stare at it, he said only this: the handkerchief of Sa'd in Paradise is finer than this. The name simply rose to his lips, the measure of how much love was still there. Now Sa'd lay bleeding in a tent the companions raised especially for him, and the Prophet ﷺ would visit him there. And on what he did not yet know was the edge of death, Sa'd made his du'a. O Allah, if any war remains against the Quraysh, then let me live to fight them, for there is no people more hateful to me than those who have hurt Your Messenger ﷺ. But if You have ended the war between us, then take me as a martyr, and do not let me die until You have cooled my eyes with what becomes of Banu Qurayza. Hold that prayer in your mind. Every single word of it would be answered, and soon.

There was one more wound that night, though it fell on no body. On one of the days of the siege, which dragged on for around a month, the arrows and skirmishing along the ditch grew so relentless that the Muslims could not stop to pray. They did not refuse the prayer; they were so utterly consumed by survival that its time slipped past them before the thought of it could surface. This is one of only two times in the entire life of the Prophet ﷺ that a prayer was missed, and both were beyond his control. When it was finally done, the depth of his anger told you everything about where his heart truly lived. They kept us from the middle prayer, he said, until the sun had set; may Allah fill their houses and their graves with fire. Read that again. He was on a battlefield with ten thousand men at his gates, his own life in danger, and the thing that made him burn with rage was that he had been kept from a single prayer. Then he made wudu and prayed it after sunset, in its proper order. If this was his fury at missing one prayer under siege, the Sheikh asks, then what shall be said of the one whose business, whose entertainment, whose idle screen robs him of his prayers while he sits in perfect safety?

The stranger Allah sent

Around the twentieth day, with the siege grinding on and the believers worn thin, a man walked out of the darkness and into the Muslim camp. His name was Nu'aym ibn Mas'ud, of the tribe of Ghatafan, one of the very armies besieging the city. I have accepted Islam, he said, and no one among my people knows it. Tell me what to do, and I will do it. Out of nowhere, with the believers stretched to the breaking point, Allah had sent the one man on earth who could undo the entire alliance from the inside: a secret Muslim, trusted by the Quraysh, trusted by Banu Qurayza, and a member of Ghatafan, all three at once. This, the Sheikh says, is what it means that whoever has taqwa of Allah, He makes for him a way out and provides for him from where he never imagined.

You are only one man, the Prophet ﷺ told him, so go back, and do whatever you can think of to protect us. May I say things that are not strictly true, Nu'aym asked? And the Prophet ﷺ answered: war is deceit. Here the Sheikh stops to draw a careful line, because the word is so often abused. Deceit in war means the feint, the illusion, pretending to march one way while you turn the other; it is what every nation on earth practices, what the classic manuals of war have taught for thousands of years. It does not, ever, mean breaking a sworn oath, tearing up a treaty, or stabbing in the back a man to whom you gave your word. That is treachery, and Islam never permits it, not in war and not in peace. Nu'aym's genius was that his whole plan stayed on the right side of that line: he never broke a single trust, he only planted doubt.

To Banu Qurayza he went first, as the old friend he was. Do not fight alongside the Quraysh, he advised, until they hand you hostages from among their own nobles to keep inside your fortress as a pledge. For if this siege drags on and they tire of it, they will simply turn their backs and go home to Makkah, having lost nothing, and leave you here alone to face the consequences of breaking your treaty. It was sound advice, and they took it. Then he went to Abu Sufyan and the Quraysh, and warned them in confidence, as a man who owed them the truth: Banu Qurayza regret breaking their treaty, and they have secretly offered Muhammad ﷺ to seize some of your nobles and hand them over to be executed, as a peace offering to win back his favor. So if they send to you asking for hostages, send them no one, for it is a trap.

Within hours the seed had taken root. The Quraysh sent to Banu Qurayza proposing they join the fighting, and Banu Qurayza sent back demanding hostages first, and the Quraysh, hearing the very demand Nu'aym had foretold, refused to send a single man, now certain the tribe meant to betray them. Each side dug in, each now sure the other was a snare. The alliance of ten thousand, which the trench itself could not break, had begun to crack from within over a single planted doubt. And here Dr. Yasir Qadhi leaves you, on the night the tide first began to turn, with the army still camped beyond the ditch and nothing yet resolved. The rest, the wind that Allah would send, the lifting of the siege, and the verdict that Sa'd ibn Mu'adh prayed to live and see, waits for the days to come.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this third day of the Trench as a tense interlude, a stretch of waiting between the digging of the ditch and the lifting of the siege, and to hurry past it. That would be to miss the question it presses on us, because this is the day the whole battle is decided, and it is decided not at the walls but in the heart.

Look first at the glad tidings on the worst of nights. The treachery of Banu Qurayza was the darkest report of the entire siege, an enemy now inside the walls as well as outside them, and the Prophet ﷺ heard it and said: good news. His trust in Allah was so complete that he read catastrophe as the prelude to the whole city being made firm. Every other heart in Madinah heard only the disaster. He heard the promise underneath it. That is the believer's first instinct trained to its limit: when the report is grim, the question is not how bad is this, but where is the good that Allah has hidden inside it. We are quick to despair and slow to look for the mercy folded into a hard decree, when the order of his ﷺ heart was the reverse.

Look next at his silence. The betrayal was real and confirmed, and still he ﷺ ordered it kept quiet, do not go telling everyone, because spreading even true evil corrodes the people who hear it and hardens the heart of a whole society. We live in the opposite instinct, where every ugly thing must be exposed, and we mistake that exposure for honesty. Islam trains you to cover what you can, to protect the soft heart of your community, and to carry a frightening matter to those who can weigh it rightly rather than scattering it among people it will only weaken.

And look, finally, at the two things that burned in him under siege: a missed prayer, and a wounded friend. With ten thousand men at his gates and his own life in the balance, the thing that filled him with rage was that he had been kept from a single salah, and the deepest wound of the battle was not the loss of a fortress but the arrow in the arm of a man he loved, whose dying prayer he would watch Allah answer word by word. If a missed prayer could make the Messenger ﷺ burn with anger in the middle of a war, then let nothing smaller, no screen, no errand, no comfort, steal yours from you in the calm of peace. And if the promise of the emperors' treasures, sneered at by a frightened hypocrite, came true within a few short years exactly as it was given, then the promise of Allah does not fail; only our patience does.

So when your own trench closes in, with enemies seeming to press from above and below and your heart climbing into your throat, do everything you honestly can and then trust, as the believers of the Trench were made to trust, that Allah can send relief from a door no one was even watching. May Allah send His peace and blessings upon Muhammad ﷺ, who found good news in the worst of nights, make our hearts firm when eyes shift and hearts climb, guard our prayers when the world tries to steal them, and send us, as He sent the believers of the Trench, relief from where we never expected it. Ameen.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 59: the Battle of Khandaq, part 3 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (33:10, 33:11, 33:12, 33:13, 24:63, 4:83), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 60 of 104

The Battle of the Trench, part 4 ﷺ

The night Allah sent the wind

By the time this night arrives, the siege of Madinah is almost a month old. Ten thousand men are camped beyond the trench, the largest army Arabia has ever raised against the Muslims: the Quraysh on one front, the tribe of Ghatafan and its allies on another, and inside the city the treaty with Banu Qurayza has quietly collapsed, so that the believers must watch the wall at their backs as carefully as the army before them. They are cold, hungry, outnumbered ten to one, living on dirty water and a handful of dates, worn to the bone after twenty-five days that have ground their bodies down and tested their faith to its edge. And then, with no sword drawn and no battle fought, the whole alliance against them begins to come apart.

This is the night Allah ended it. It is a strange ending, with no charge and no clash of lines, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi tells it as a thing made of small pieces that no human eye could have fitted together: a man who became Muslim out of nowhere on the darkest night of the war, a single companion sent alone into the enemy camp, a quarrel over a Saturday, and above all of them, the One who sends the wind. Not one of those pieces looks like a weapon. Together they are the end of the siege.

The man Islam found in the dark

On one of the last nights of the siege, a man named Nuaym ibn Masud walked out of the confederate camp and crossed into Madinah, and to the end of his life he could not fully explain why. He tells the story himself, in the first person, and the Sheikh lingers on it because of how unusual it is. Allah, Nuaym says, simply threw Islam into his heart. That is his own phrase, and it carries the bewilderment of a man describing something that happened to him rather than something he decided: as if from nowhere, with no preacher at his side and no argument won, the faith was suddenly there. One night he was a pagan camped with the besieging army; the next he was crossing the lines to find the Prophet ﷺ, and waited until he had finished praying. Then the Prophet ﷺ turned to him and asked him why he had come.

That one question tells us something on its own, because it means the Prophet ﷺ knew his face. And of all the ten thousand men gathered beyond the trench, no man was placed quite like Nuaym. He was from Ghatafan, one of the besieging tribes, so his own people trusted him as one of their own. He used to sit and drink and dine with the leaders of Banu Qurayza and then buy his year's stock of dates from them, so they trusted him as an old friend and customer. And he had some old, neutral dealing with the Prophet ﷺ himself, never as an open enemy, enough that when he walked into Madinah the Prophet ﷺ recognized him at once. Every party in this war trusted him a little, and no party suspected him of anything. He had no idea what that was worth. Allah did, and Allah, the Sheikh says, had been arranging this man's life for years against a night Nuaym could not have imagined, drawing the threads of three hostile camps together into a single pair of hands.

Permission to deceive, never to betray

Nuaym offered his services, but admitted the difficulty plainly: no one knew he had become a Muslim. The Prophet ﷺ told him to go and do whatever he saw best, for war is deception. And here Dr. Yasir Qadhi stops to draw a line he will not let blur, because the meaning of what follows, and of what comes after it, depends on keeping it clean.

It is the custom of war the world over, he says, that war has no etiquette of honesty. Every people who have ever gone to battle has allowed the feint, the false impression, the misleading move, and Islam is no different in this. What Islam forbids, absolutely and by the consensus of the scholars, is something else entirely: treachery. To swear a covenant, to write a contract, to give your word of protection while in your heart you intend to break it, that is haram without exception, and the Prophet ﷺ never once in his life did it. Deceit is to indicate one thing and mean another in the open contest of war, where both sides know the game they are playing. Treachery is to betray a promise you actually made. The first is permitted; the second is forbidden; and the difference between them is the difference between a tactic and a sin.

Hold that distinction carefully, because the entire next episode, the fate of Banu Qurayza, will rest on which side of it the events fall. What Nuaym was about to do was clever deception against an enemy who had been given no covenant and no pledge, not the breaking of any oath. He had promised no one anything. He was simply about to tell each of three armies the one thing it was most afraid to hear.

How the alliance came apart

Nuaym went first to Banu Qurayza, since he was already inside the city and they were the easiest to reach. He came to them as the old friend they knew, and he warned them, as a friend warns. Your situation, he said, is more precarious than you realize. The Quraysh and Ghatafan are far from home; this is not their city and these are not their families. If the fighting turns hard, they can simply break camp and ride away, and then you will be left alone behind these walls with Muhammad ﷺ forever. Protect yourselves. Do not commit to the fight until they hand you seventy of their noblemen as hostages, men they cannot afford to abandon. It was, the Sheikh notes, a genuinely sound piece of advice, the kind a true friend might give.

Then he slipped across to Abu Sufyan and the chiefs of the Quraysh, and he told them the opposite secret, in confidence, because, he said, the closeness of our friendship demands that I warn you. Banu Qurayza, he told them, are having second thoughts. They want to repair their treaty with Muhammad ﷺ, and they have offered him seventy of your nobles as a peace offering, to be handed over and executed so that he will forgive them. So beware, he said: when they come asking you for hostages, and especially if they ask for your leading men, know that those are the very heads they have promised to deliver. Then he went to his own tribe, Ghatafan, and told them the same thing about themselves. Notice the craft in it, the Sheikh says: he set Banu Qurayza against the Quraysh and the Quraysh against Banu Qurayza, but never used his own tribe between the two, for a man cannot pretend to be neutral about his own people. With every party he played the one part that party would believe, and so in every direction he appeared to be exactly the honest friend each one needed.

It was a single rumour, told three ways, and it was devastating, because it did not invent a danger out of nothing. It took each ally's most reasonable request and turned it into the proof of a deadly plot. Now when Banu Qurayza asked for hostages, the Quraysh would hear an assassin's trap; now when the Quraysh hesitated, Banu Qurayza would hear the first move of betrayal. The siege that ten thousand men could not break, one new Muslim unstitched in a single night, simply by handing each side the truth it was already most afraid was true.

Saturday, and a quarrel that would not heal

It worked exactly as Nuaym had intended, and faster. Banu Qurayza sent an emissary to Abu Sufyan with a hard message: three weeks have passed and you have done nothing but talk. Let us set a date and attack from every side together and finish this matter of Muhammad ﷺ. But first, hand us seventy of your noblemen as surety, so we know you will not turn and ride away once the fighting becomes costly. It was, again, a sensible demand, the common sense of any wary ally; they asked for nobles and not slaves precisely because slaves would simply flee, while a man will not abandon his own chiefs. Abu Sufyan said he would think about it, sent them home empty-handed, and told his people exactly what Nuaym had warned him to expect: this is the trap, hand over no one.

The next day the cycle closed. Banu Qurayza were told what Abu Sufyan had said when their backs were turned, that he would not hand them so much as a single baby camel, let alone seventy of his nobles, because he was certain those men would be killed and delivered to Muhammad ﷺ. Imagine, the Sheikh says, how that lands on a tribe that has put its very survival on the line for this alliance: after all we have risked, you believe we mean to betray you? Each side now read the other's caution as treachery, and the suspicion fed on itself. One of Banu Qurayza's own men, Zubayr ibn Bata, said aloud the thing that should chill us, because it shows they understood their own position exactly: if the confederates leave, there will be nothing left for us but the sword. They knew the stakes. They knew what siding with the enemy from inside the city would cost them if the enemy failed.

Then Abu Sufyan forced the matter to a head. He sent a high delegation, led by no less than Ikrima the son of Abu Jahl, to issue an ultimatum: everyone attacks tomorrow morning, all sides at once, a surprise assault before any warning could reach the Prophet ﷺ. But the messengers reached Banu Qurayza on a Friday, and the next day was the Sabbath. Banu Qurayza refused to fight on a Saturday and asked to wait until Sunday, and even then would not move without their seventy hostages. To the Quraysh, who had never lived alongside the Jews and had never heard of a day on which a whole people does no work at all, this sounded like a transparent lie, a stall to buy a day in which to run and warn Muhammad ﷺ. Abu Sufyan summoned Huyayy ibn Akhtab, the man who had dragged Banu Qurayza into this war and was now sitting with the Quraysh, and demanded to know what this Sabbath was. Huyayy explained it was their law; Abu Sufyan would not hear it. What is Saturday, he raged, but the day between Friday and Sunday? He swore it was their treachery, that they were making a fool of him, and his anger turned dangerous enough that Huyayy slipped quietly away in fear for his life and went back to Banu Qurayza, pleading with them now to break their Sabbath and fight.

And here the Sheikh draws out a bitter irony. Huyayy, in his hatred of the Prophet ﷺ, was willing to violate the Sabbath, one of the very Ten Commandments his own scripture held sacred, just to strike at his enemy. But the tribe he was urging to sin refused him. We would rather be killed, they told him, than break the Sabbath. In their wrongdoing they still would not cross that line, though crossing it might have saved their lives; Allah had written the Sabbath for them as a binding law, and even on the wrong side of this war, they held it. Huyayy was so afraid of Abu Sufyan's rage that he did not dare return to the Quraysh, and stayed inside the fortress of Banu Qurayza, a decision that, as we will see, became his own death warrant. The alliance was finished, and not a single arrow had decided it.

The night the Prophet ﷺ asked for a volunteer

That same Friday night, the weather turned into the worst the companions had ever known. Clouds gathered, the cold came down hard, and a freezing wind rose and gathered until it was howling through the camp like thunder, dark and bitter and screaming, the kind of desert windstorm that fills the air with sand until a man cannot breathe and cannot see. After a month of hunger and dirty water and a few dates a day, the believers were spent past the edge of their strength. And into that darkness the Prophet ﷺ stood and asked: who will go and bring me news of the enemy, and he will be my companion in Jannah?

What can the Messenger of Allah ﷺ offer his exhausted, frozen men, the Sheikh asks, when he has no money, no camel, no comfort to give? He offers the only currency the people of iman truly want: a place beside him in Paradise. And no one answered. He asked a second time, and there was silence. A third time, and still not one man rose, not from cowardice but from sheer cold and hunger and dread, the body unable to lift itself after twenty-five days of siege. It was a general call for a volunteer, the companions understood it as such, and not one of them had the strength left to answer it.

Years later, an old companion named Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman would tell the story of that night to a gathering of young men in Iraq, and the way it came down to him is one of the most honest things in the whole seerah. The young men had been boasting. Had we been with the Prophet ﷺ, one of them said, we would have shown him what real fighting looks like; in another telling, the young man swore he would never even have let the Prophet ﷺ walk, but would have carried him on his own back. They imagined themselves braver than the companions. So Hudhayfah, who had actually been there, with Abu Bakr and Umar and Uthman and Ali ﷺ all around him in the dark, set out to put the boast in its place. Let me tell you about a night, young man. He described it exactly: the Quraysh above them and Banu Qurayza below them, their own families behind them and feared for, a cold and a darkness deeper than any night of their lives, a wind that sounded like thunder and ripped the cooking pots clean off the fires, a blackness so thick that a man could not see his own fingers if he stretched out his hand. And in all of that, three times the Prophet ﷺ asked, and three times not one of us, he said, answered with a single word.

Then the Prophet ﷺ called him by name. Hudhayfah, go. And here is the heart of the way Hudhayfah tells it, the detail the Sheikh fixes on: he does not dress himself up. Once he was named, he says, I had no choice but to rise, and he rose shivering and afraid. He had every reason, telling the story to admiring young men decades later with no witness left alive to correct him, to make himself the hero who leapt up gladly. Instead he confessed his fear, and confessed that only the direct command moved him. That honesty, the Sheikh says, is the very proof of his iman: he would not spin the smallest yarn in his own favour even when no one could have caught him. The Prophet ﷺ told him to bring back news but to give nothing away, neither himself nor the Muslims, and made a du'a over him: O Allah, protect him from before him and from behind him, from his right and from his left, from above him and from below him. And Hudhayfah says every trace of fear left his heart, and he walked out into the storm calm.

Inside the camp, and the wind of mercy

There was a wisdom in the choosing, the Sheikh points out, that only a man who knew his companions intimately could have managed. The Prophet ﷺ could not send Abu Bakr or Umar or Uthman or Ali ﷺ into the Quraysh camp; their faces were known, and they would have been recognized at once and killed. Hudhayfah was perfect for it. He was not from the Quraysh, but an outsider taken in: his father had migrated to Madinah and allied with the Ansar, so Hudhayfah was reckoned among them though of neither their blood nor the Quraysh's. He had not fought at Badr, and had reached Uhud too late to be marked by the enemy's eyes. He could walk into the lion's den entirely unseen. The Prophet ﷺ knew his men, the Sheikh says, and knew exactly which one fit which task.

Hudhayfah walked out into the howling dark, and Allah alone knows how he found the camp through that wind, but he found it, and slipped through the crowd until he saw the back of a man who sat surrounded the way a leader is surrounded. This must be Abu Sufyan, he worked out, though he had never seen Abu Sufyan in his life, which was the whole point of sending him. He had a clear shot; his hand went to an arrow, and the leader of the entire confederacy sat defenceless in front of him. And then he remembered the order: give nothing away. He lowered the arrow and let the moment pass. A while later Abu Sufyan stood and told his men that before he spoke, every man should take the hand of whoever sat beside him and make sure he was one of their own, for in that blackness no one could be certain who was who. Quick as anything, Hudhayfah seized the hands of the men on either side of him first, demanding who are you, who are you, so that by the time the question could come back to him it had already passed; he had taken the aura of authority and turned it on them. Then Abu Sufyan gave up. We are not in our own homes, he said, and cannot stay here. Our animals are dying, the wind will not let us keep a fire lit or a pot upright, Banu Qurayza have betrayed us, and I am leaving, whatever the rest of you choose. He mounted his camel and untied it, and as the beast lurched to its feet Hudhayfah had one last clear shot, Abu Sufyan utterly exposed on the rising camel, and again he let it go, because the Prophet ﷺ had told him only to watch. Abu Sufyan rode, and the great army broke up and streamed away into the dark behind him.

Hudhayfah came back through the storm and found the Prophet ﷺ doing what he always did when something weighed on him: he was praying. This was his sign, the companions noticed; whenever he was disturbed, he turned at once to prayer, for when every door of the world is shut, the Sheikh says, the door of Allah is open. He had been standing out in the cold, wrapped in a blanket of one of his wives against the freezing wind, begging Allah. They preserved his du'a from that night: O Allah, Revealer of the Book, swift in reckoning, defeat the confederates; O Allah, defeat them and shake them. He had asked Allah by name to send the armies of the wind and the clouds, and Allah had answered with the very storm that was, in that moment, tearing the enemy camp to pieces. When the Prophet ﷺ saw Hudhayfah standing there shivering, he drew him into the blanket at his feet until the prayer was done, and only then took the good news.

The favour of that night is exactly what Allah would later command the believers never to forget:

O you who have believed, remember the favor of Allāh upon you when armies came to [attack] you and We sent upon them a wind and armies [of angels] you did not see. And ever is Allāh, of what you do, Seeing.Qur'an 33:9

The wind, and an army no eye could see. On his way back, some reports say, Hudhayfah passed a band of horsemen in the dark, their faces wrapped, who told him to go and tell his companion that Allah had taken care of the fighting for him; these were the angels of that unseen army.

Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi shares a detail that turns the whole night tender. The Prophet ﷺ later said: Allah aided me with the wind of the Saba, and the people of Ad were destroyed by the wind of the Dabur. The Saba, the scholars explain, is the gentle wind, the one said to carry the scent of mercy and to gather the clouds for rain. The Dabur is the harsher, destroying wind, the one that wiped out an entire nation. Allah, who commands every wind, could have sent the destroying one and ended the Quraysh that very night. He sent the merciful one instead: enough to scatter the army and break the siege, but not enough to kill them. And the reason, the Sheikh says, is the most moving thing in the episode. Allah knew His Prophet's ﷺ love for his people, and knew that most of these very men, Abu Sufyan among them, would one day be guided to Islam; had the destroying wind come, they would not have lived to believe. So even in the armies He sent against an enemy, Allah was being kind to that enemy, sparing the very men who would one day stand in the ranks of the faithful. The wind that broke the greatest siege in Arabian history was a wind of mercy.

The siege that ended without a sword

By morning the confederates were simply gone. Not a tent left standing, not a pot, not a fire; the wind had torn the camp apart and the men had fled in every direction. Ten thousand strong, the largest army Arabia had ever assembled against the Muslims, withdrew having killed barely a handful of men, gained nothing whatsoever, and humiliated themselves in a way the Arabs would not soon forget. And the Prophet ﷺ said the words that mark the turning of the whole seerah: from now on we will march on them, and they will never again march on us. The tide had changed, officially and forever. After the Trench, the Quraysh would never again lay siege to Madinah; the believers had taken the offensive, and they would not give it back.

What had actually won it? Before any tactic, the Sheikh says plainly, it was two things: the iman of the believers and the du'a of the Prophet ﷺ. The Muslims were asked to do one thing only, to hold their ground and prove their faith, and when they proved it, Allah did all the rest. This is the meaning of the verse Allah revealed about that day:

And Allāh repelled those who disbelieved, in their rage, not having obtained any good. And sufficient was Allāh for the believers in battle, and ever is Allāh Powerful and Exalted in Might.Qur'an 33:25

Sufficient was Allah for the believers. He fulfilled His promise to defend them, aided His servant, and defeated the gathered armies by Himself, without the believers having to lift a sword. After this, the Sheikh notes, the Muslims took to repeating words we still say to this day: that Allah is true to His promise, that He aided His servant and alone defeated the confederates.

And around that core of faith and prayer, Allah had arranged every other piece like a master craftsman. The impossible conversion of Nuaym at the one hour it could matter. The Quraysh's total ignorance of the Sabbath, so that a true answer sounded to them like a lie. A month-long siege that had already ground the enemy's morale to nothing, their animals dying and their water gone. And then, at the end, the wind and the unseen army of angels. One secret convert, one brave companion in the dark, one quarrel over a Saturday, and one merciful wind, and the siege was over. Imagine, the Sheikh marvels, how perfectly the pieces fell into place; this is what it looks like when Allah Himself is fighting on your side.

But the war had left one piece still standing on the board. Banu Qurayza had broken their treaty and sided with the besieging army from inside the city, and they understood, even now, exactly what that meant. Their own man had said it aloud during the siege: if the confederates leave, there is nothing left for us but the sword. They had weighed the punishment that treason brings and gambled anyway, and the gamble had failed. The confederates had left. That reckoning comes next, and it is one of the most misunderstood moments in the entire seerah, an episode the Sheikh promises to face frankly rather than gloss over, because it was a punishment for what a people did and not for who they were. But that is tomorrow. Tonight the wind has done its work, the horizon is empty, and the Messenger of Allah ﷺ has folded a shivering companion into his blanket and finished his prayer in the cold.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read the last night of the Trench as a lucky turn of weather and leave it there, a storm that happened to arrive at the right moment. That would be to miss the question it puts to us. Look again at what actually broke the greatest army Arabia ever raised, and you will never read a hopeless-looking season of your own life the same way.

Guidance is Allah's to give, and He gives it suddenly. Nuaym had no Muslim beside him, no sermon, no slow turning of the heart anyone could trace; he says only that Allah threw Islam into his heart, out of nowhere, on the darkest night of the war, while his own tribe sat in arms against the believers. None of us knows whose heart Allah has already marked. So never write a single person off as too far gone, and never imagine that the people opposing the truth today cannot be standing inside it tomorrow. Most of the men who fled that wind would one day pray toward the same qiblah as the man they had come to destroy.

Keep the line between deceit and betrayal clean, because Allah does. Islam permits the feints of war against a declared enemy, but it forbids treachery absolutely: a promise made is a promise kept, even to an opponent, even when breaking it would profit you, and the Prophet ﷺ never once broke his word. Most of us will never fight a battle, but every one of us makes promises and gives our word, and the test is the same: do you keep faith when keeping it costs you? Nuaym deceived an enemy who had been promised nothing; he betrayed no one. Guard that distinction in your own dealings and you guard a piece of the Prophet's ﷺ own character.

Be honest even when no one is watching. Hudhayfah told the story of his own great night exactly as it happened, admitting his fear, admitting that he rose only because he was named, refusing to make himself the hero before an audience that would have believed any version he gave. He had no witness left to correct him and still would not flatter himself by a hair. That, the Sheikh says, is the very mark of his iman. Ask whether the account you give of yourself when no one can check it is as honest as the one a watching world would force on you.

And when every door of the world is closed, stand up and pray. On the worst night of the siege, frozen and besieged front and back, the Prophet ﷺ was found wrapped in a blanket in the cold, praying, asking Allah by name to send the wind; when something weighed on him, he turned straight to his Lord, as a first reflex and not a last resort. The believers were asked only to hold the trench and keep their faith, and Allah did the rest: He repelled the disbelievers in their rage so that they gained no good at all, and was Himself sufficient for the believers in battle. That is the balance this night teaches: do everything your faith and your strength allow, hold your ground with sincerity, and then rest, genuinely rest, in the certainty that the outcome was never in your hands.

So when your own siege feels endless, when you are cold and outnumbered and watching every horizon at once, remember the man praying in a blanket in the dark, and remember what he prayed. Ask, as he asked, knowing that Allah is true to His promise and sufficient for those who trust Him: O Allah, Revealer of the Book, swift in reckoning, defeat what gathers against me, and let me be among those who keep their ground until the wind of Your mercy comes. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, who loved even his enemies into guidance, be sufficient for us in our narrow places as He was sufficient for the believers at the Trench, and make us of those who do their part and trust Him with the rest.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 60: the Battle of the Trench, part 4 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (33:9, 33:25), verified via quran.ai.

The Seerah · 61 of 104

Banu Qurayzah: the treachery, the siege, and the arbitration of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh

Judged by the law they chose for themselves

This is one of the hardest days in the whole seerah, and one of the most lied about. So begin where Dr. Yasir Qadhi begins, with the one thing the critics always cut away: the context. For nearly a month the city of Madinah had been ringed by the largest army Arabia could assemble, the confederate host of al-Ahzab, Quraysh and Ghatafan and their allies, drawn up against a trench and a handful of believers. And in the middle of that terror one tribe inside the walls, Banu Qurayzah, bound to the Prophet ﷺ by a written treaty, had broken its word and opened negotiations with the enemy at the gates. The danger had never been the army alone. It had been the knife held at the city's back while it faced the spears at its front.

Then Allah lifted the siege without a battle. A bitter wind and a quiet sowing of mistrust between the confederate tribes did what no Muslim sword had to do; the great army woke one morning, struck its tents, and was gone. The Prophet ﷺ came home exhausted, after a month of fear and sleeplessness, and had not even set down his weapon before the next command arrived. Walk this day slowly, the way the Sheikh walks it, and you will see something the headlines never show you: a justice so scrupulous that the Messenger of Allah ﷺ gave away his own right to pronounce it, and handed it to a man the betrayed tribe themselves would have to call fair.

The war was not over

The morning after the great sandstorm, the believers woke to an empty horizon. The tents were overturned, the enemy gone, the confederates scattered without a single duel being fought. This is the moment of which Allah revealed:

And Allah repelled those who disbelieved, in their rage, not having obtained any good. And sufficient was Allah for the believers in battle, and ever is Allah Powerful and Exalted in Might.Qur'an 33:25

The Prophet ﷺ returned to his home around the middle of the day, the furnace hour of the Arabian afternoon, when the whole region shutters itself for the midday rest, and he began to unbuckle his armor. He had barely set down his sword when Jibril came to him, and the Sheikh lingers on how he came: dressed for war, a turban of soft brocade wound across his head, riding a mule whose saddle was draped in silk. Have you put down your weapon? Jibril asked. The Prophet ﷺ said yes. And the answer cut through the exhaustion of a month: the angels have not put theirs down. Allah commands you to go to Banu Qurayzah, and I am going ahead of you now to shake the earth beneath them.

There would not be a single day of rest. Imagine, Dr. Yasir Qadhi says, the longing for home after thirty days of mental siege, the relief of finally setting down the sword, and then the command to take it up again before your foot has crossed your own threshold. The Prophet ﷺ sent a crier through the lanes of Madinah with an order so urgent it became one of the most famous commands of his life, recorded in every book of hadith: let no one of you pray Asr except at Banu Qurayzah. Hurry. The Companions set out at once, the wounds of a month-long siege still on them, marching the two hours toward the fortress of the tribe that had betrayed the city.

Two honest readings of one command

On the road, that single order opened one of the most instructive episodes in the whole history of Islamic law. The Companions left Madinah in batches, some catching the message late, some delayed in arming again, and as they walked the sun began to slide down the sky. Asr was slipping away. Now what? One group reasoned that the Prophet ﷺ had meant hurry, that Asr at Banu Qurayzah was a way of saying make haste and not a command to let the prayer lapse past its appointed time, so they prayed Asr where they stood, on the road, and pressed on. The other group held to the words exactly as spoken: he said Asr at Banu Qurayzah, and Asr at Banu Qurayzah is what we will pray, even if the sun sets first and we make it up late. They prayed only after they arrived.

Two camps of qualified, sincere people read the same words two honest ways and could reach no consensus, so each acted on its own reasoning and neither rebuked the other. And when they came to the Prophet ﷺ and told him what had happened, that some had prayed on the road and some had prayed only after Maghrib, he criticized neither side. He simply let it be. Dr. Yasir Qadhi draws two threads out of that silence. The first is that when those who are truly qualified to weigh the texts arrive at different rulings, no one of them is bound to abandon his reasoning for another's, and neither should look down on the other. The literalist who clings to the plain word and the one who reads for the reason behind it are both walking a road the Companions walked first; this tension between the letter and the cause is not a flaw in the religion but an old and healthy current running through it since the first generation. The second thread is a single point that the day quietly settles: the truth itself is one. The Prophet ﷺ had intended one of the two meanings, not both, and a Companion who honestly missed it was not thereby a sinner but a rewarded seeker, for the one who strives and reaches the truth has two rewards, and the one who strives and errs still has one.

But the Sheikh fences this lesson with a caution he repeats with force, because in our age it is constantly abused: this is a conversation for those trained to have it, not for everyone with an opinion and a search engine. A man may have spent his life mastering the Qur'an and the sunnah and still know less about the direction of the qibla than a scout, because that is a different kind of knowledge; and a man who has mastered neither has no business pronouncing verdicts on either. When reputable scholars differ, that is the breadth of the religion. When the untrained crowd into the seat of judgment, that is its corruption. Let the qualified speak, and let the rest of us learn the difference between a scholar's disagreement and a stranger's noise.

The repentance tied to a pillar

The fortress was strong, the real architecture of the tribes of Madinah, of a kind the Muslims had no engines to break, so they ringed it and settled in to wait. Days dragged into weeks. Somewhere in the long siege the tribe sent out a request: they wanted to speak with one of the Companions by name, Abu Lubabah, an Ansari of the Aws who had been their close friend in the days before Islam, a man who used to sit in their fortress and was trusted among them. The Prophet ﷺ let him go in. Inside, the women and children crowded around him weeping, begging for mercy, and they put the question to him: should we surrender to Muhammad's judgment? Abu Lubabah said yes. But as the word left his mouth he drew his hand across his throat, signaling to them what surrender would mean.

He had not yet moved his feet from that spot, he said afterward, before he knew he had betrayed Allah and His Messenger. The Sheikh names exactly what the treachery was. It was not that he gave them a fact; it was that in the hour it mattered most, with everything at stake, his old affection pulled his loyalty out of place. This is a matter of wala, of where your allegiance lands when it is tested, and Abu Lubabah's had wavered toward the enemy of Allah and His Messenger after all that had happened. And so the verse came down for men in his position:

O you who have believed, do not betray Allah and the Messenger or betray your trusts while you know [the consequence].Qur'an 8:27

Abu Lubabah did not wait to be confronted. He walked straight out of the fortress, past the army, past the Prophet ﷺ without stopping, all the way back to the mosque in Madinah, and he bound himself to a pillar and swore he would not be loosed from it until Allah Himself accepted his repentance, and that he would never again set foot in the place where he had disobeyed his Lord. That pillar is called the pillar of repentance to this day. When the Prophet ﷺ heard, he said: had he come to me, I would have asked Allah to forgive him, but now he has bound himself, and the matter is between him and his Lord, for he has made a vow. And a vow to Allah, the Sheikh reminds us, must be kept, for of the righteous Allah says:

They [are those who] fulfill [their] vows and fear a Day whose evil will be widespread. Qur'an 76:7 Days later, at the dawn prayer, Umm Salamah saw the Prophet ﷺ come out smiling, and asked what had made him laugh; Allah, he said, has accepted Abu Lubabah's repentance. She hurried to carry the news. The Companions rushed to untie him, but he refused to be freed by anyone except the Prophet ﷺ himself, to be sure nothing was left undone, and so the Prophet ﷺ loosed the ropes with his own hands before leading the prayer. Al-Hasan al-Basri said the verse revealed for such men was the most hopeful in the entire Qur'an, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi asks us to memorize it, because it is about us:
And [there are] others who have acknowledged their sins. They had mixed [i.e., polluted] a righteous deed with another that was bad. Perhaps Allah will turn to them in forgiveness. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.Qur'an 9:102

People who own their sins, who have tangled good deeds with bad and do not pretend otherwise, and of whom Allah says, perhaps Allah will turn to them in forgiveness. And Ibn Abbas said that whenever Allah says perhaps, He does it.

The man who kept his word

Inside the fortress, the chief Ka'b ibn Asad laid three choices before his people. The first was the only one that could have saved them. Accept this man's religion, he told them, for by Allah we all know he is the prophet foretold in our own scriptures, and then we are all safe, every sin forgiven, for that is what Islam does. They refused. We will never leave the Torah, they said, we will never leave our religion. The Sheikh stops you here, because at the very end the open secret falls out of the chief's mouth and not one of them denies it. They knew. After all the signs, after all the years, they knew he ﷺ was the one their books had promised, and they would not follow him. There is no arrogance worse than that, to see the truth plainly and turn away from it anyway. His second option, that they kill their own women and children and then charge out sword in hand to the last man, they refused; his third, a surprise attack launched on their own Sabbath when no one would expect them to break it, they refused as a desecration. He despaired of ever wringing a single decision out of them.

So they tried instead to soften the terms. They sent a messenger to the Prophet ﷺ asking for what Banu Nadir had been given, to leave with their families and whatever their camels could carry; he refused. They sent again, offering to abandon every possession if only their lives were spared; he refused. Nothing less than unconditional surrender. And the Sheikh draws out what is easy to miss in that refusal. The Prophet ﷺ knew their fate was effectively sealed. He could have lured them out with a false promise of safety and then done as he wished, and saved everyone the siege. He would not. Treachery and lying are forbidden, the Sheikh stresses, even against an enemy you already know is doomed; the Messenger of Allah ﷺ was a man of his word to the very end, and would not buy a single advantage with a broken one.

And then a small thing happened on the last night that the Sheikh says holds the whole key to the day. One man of the tribe, Amr ibn Sa'da, slipped out past the guards. He was the one Qurazi, perhaps the only one, who had stood up when the covenant was broken and refused to break it: I will never betray Muhammad, he had said, though he never accepted Islam. The Companion on watch, Muhammad ibn Maslamah, recognized him, murmured a prayer that Allah forgive him for letting a man go, and let him pass. Amr vanished into the night and was never harmed; he reached Madinah, and by morning he was simply gone, and no one knows his end. When the Prophet ﷺ heard, he did not rebuke the guard. That, he said, was a man whom Allah saved because of his faithfulness. He died upon his own religion, and Allah spared him anyway, because he had kept his word. Hold onto that, the Sheikh says, and return to it, because it is the answer to everything the critics will throw at this day: these people were punished for what they did, not for who they were. Here is a man of the very same tribe and the very same religion, and because he alone kept faith, Allah made him a way out.

Judged by their own

When the tribe finally sent word that they would surrender, the Aws gathered around the Prophet ﷺ and began to plead. Banu Qurayzah had been their allies in the days of ignorance, their friends and confederates, and the Aws remembered it; they begged for the same mercy the Khazraj had won earlier for their own allies of Banu Nadir, who had been let go with what their camels could carry. But there was a world of difference between what Banu Nadir had done and what this tribe had done in the hour of the siege, and the Prophet ﷺ had not yet said a single word about their fate. He could feel the old tribal loyalty straining in the Aws, and rather than override it, he did something extraordinary.

Will you be satisfied, he asked them, if one of your own decides their case? They said yes. And he named Sa'd ibn Mu'adh, the leader of the Aws, the tribe's own ally and chief. Stop here, Dr. Yasir Qadhi says, because this is the part the world never tells. The Messenger of Allah ﷺ stepped down from his own right to pass the judgment and handed it to a man the betrayed tribe's protectors would have to accept as fair. He knew with certainty that if he had ruled, his ruling would have been obeyed without a murmur. But a true leader does not simply impose himself, even when he is the Messenger of Allah; there is a wisdom in carrying your people with you rather than dragging them. So the verdict would not be the Prophet's ﷺ. It would be the verdict of their own.

Sa'd was dying. A wound taken at the Trench had opened the great vessel of his arm and would not stop bleeding; he had spent the whole siege in the tent of Rufaydah, the Companion who had volunteered to nurse the wounded and tend the sick, the first field hospital in the history of Islam. They lifted Sa'd onto a mule and brought him, still bandaged and still bleeding after twenty-five days, and as he came his own people of the Aws pressed close, reminding him of the old friendship, urging him to be gentle, telling him he had surely been chosen so that he would show mercy. And Sa'd answered them: now is the time for Sa'd to fear no one's blame when it comes to Allah. The Sheikh sets that sentence against every man who reaches the end of his life craving a legacy, wanting to be spoken of well; here is a man at the door of death whose only thought is to be honest before his Lord. When he said it, the Aws understood which way he was going. As he arrived the Prophet ﷺ told the Companions, stand for your leader, the one and only time in the entire seerah he ever commanded such a thing, he who never let the Companions rise for him. Sa'd made his people promise that they would accept his verdict and not argue it, and they promised. Then, lowering his face before the Prophet ﷺ out of awe, unable to look up and speak to him so plainly, he asked him to promise too, that the verdict would stand. It will, the Prophet ﷺ said.

The verdict, and what it was not

Sa'd did not hesitate. He had been carrying this judgment for the better part of a month. His ruling: the fighting men would be executed, their property divided, and the women and children taken as captives. And the Prophet ﷺ said: you have judged by the judgment of Allah from above the seven heavens. The men were led out in batches to a place outside the city, and there they were executed. The books of seerah differ on the number, and the Sheikh hands you the range as he found it rather than choosing for you: Ibn Ishaq, the most authoritative, gives six to seven hundred and notes that some have exaggerated higher to eight or nine hundred; a narration in Muslim and Ahmad mentions around four hundred. Whatever the true figure, it was, Dr. Yasir Qadhi says plainly, not a small number. This was the adult fighting strength of an entire tribe. There is no softening that.

But hold the whole picture, because this is exactly where the lie lives. No children were killed. Of all the women, only one was executed, and only because she herself had killed a Companion during the siege by carrying a heavy grinding stone to the top of the wall and dropping it on him as he passed; Aisha, who sat with the captives, never ceased to marvel that this woman was laughing and joking even as her name was called for death. Everyone of the tribe who accepted Islam was spared. So were those for whom a Muslim asked clemency: the old man Zubayr ibn Batta, whose life a Companion begged in repayment of an old debt of mercy from the wars before Islam, though Zubayr, when he learned his friends and family were gone, asked in the end to be sent after them and was. Even one of the captives, Rayhana, the Prophet ﷺ would later free; she saw the gentleness with which she was treated, chose Islam over being passed to another, and chose to remain with him.

Now listen to how Dr. Yasir Qadhi answers the critics, because he does not flinch and he does not let you flinch either. This is the episode that has been smeared and weaponized more than any other, with the charge that the Prophet ﷺ and his religion are guilty of slaughtering a people for their faith or their blood. The punishment was harsh, the Sheikh says, and he will not pretend it was anything else; he sees no shame in admitting it was harsh, because harshness has its place. A religion whose rule is mercy and whose default is gentleness is the perfect religion precisely because, once in a great while, it will show severity, for there are lines a people cannot be allowed to cross and survive. A non-Muslim is free to call it harsh, and we will not quarrel with the word; we say it was the judgment of Allah, and that it was deserved. But the one charge that is a flat and academically indefensible lie, that no scholar of the seerah has ever so much as hinted at, is that they were killed for who they were. They were not. They were judged for treason in a time of war, after being warned again and again, three times over, the last of those warnings only months before. Cut away al-Ahzab, cut away the treaty and the betrayal in the hour of greatest danger, shout the number and erase everything around it, and you can make any atrocity out of any justice. Put the context back, and you see a tribunal. And the Sheikh presses the point home with their own scripture: in the book of Deuteronomy, a city that refuses an offer of peace and gives battle is besieged, and when it falls its fighting men are put to the sword while the women and children and livestock are spared as plunder, almost word for word the verdict Sa'd pronounced. They were judged, in the end, by the very law of the very book they had sworn they would never leave.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to let Banu Qurayzah be only a controversy, a hard fact to defend or to dread, and to walk away from it without letting it touch you. That would be to miss what the day is actually asking. Dr. Yasir Qadhi refuses to make it light, and he refuses to make it only an argument; underneath the siege and the verdict, the day is quietly putting a question to your own heart, and the question is about loyalty.

Watch where everyone's loyalty lands when it costs them something, because that is the spine of this whole episode. Abu Lubabah was a good man with a long record, a man of Badr and Uhud, and one gesture of misplaced affection in the wrong moment convinced him he had betrayed Allah and His Messenger, and he wept at a pillar until forgiveness came down. Amr ibn Sa'da was not even a believer, and because he alone kept the covenant when his whole tribe broke it, Allah opened a door in the night and walked him through it to safety. The one woman who was executed had, with her own hands, killed a man. The tribe that was judged had seen the truth, admitted it among themselves at the very end, and refused it out of pride. Every one of them was answered for what they did with their loyalty, not for the name they were born to. The Sheikh returns to this again and again, and you should carry it out of the chapter: where your allegiance falls in the hour it is hardest is where your allegiance truly is. It is easy to belong to Allah when belonging costs nothing. The test is the moment your old affections, your friendships, your fear, your wish to be thought gentle, all pull the other way, and you have to decide, like Sa'd on the mule, that now is the time to fear no one's blame when it comes to Allah.

And watch the Prophet ﷺ at the center of it, because he teaches a second thing that most of us never learn. He held every card. The judgment was his by right, his ruling would have been obeyed, and the law was on his side. And he gave the right away, handed it to the tribe's own chief so that no one could ever call the outcome unjust, because a leader who serves his people will surrender his own prerogative before he will let the weight of justice fall from a hand his people cannot trust. There is a whole ethic of power in that single decision. Most of us, given the right to decide, would seize it; he, given the right to decide, gave it to someone else and called the result the judgment of Allah from above the seven heavens.

So the day ends not on a battlefield but at a graveside. Sa'd ibn Mu'adh had made one last du'a as his wound bled, that Allah let him live to settle the affair of Banu Qurayzah, and Allah granted it, and then, his work done, he died only days later, at thirty-seven.
