When the Companions bore his body they said they had never carried anyone lighter, and the Prophet ﷺ told them why: seventy thousand angels had come down to carry him, angels who had never before descended to the earth. At the graveside in al-Baqi the Prophet ﷺ said Subhan Allah, and all of al-Baqi said it with him; then he said Allahu Akbar, and they followed; and he told them this was the servant for whom the Throne of the Most Merciful had shaken and for whom the gates of heaven had opened, and that even he, even Sa'd, had felt the grave press him once and then release him, so that if anyone were ever to be spared that pressing it would have been this man. And the Prophet ﷺ said into the earth: may Allah reward you with good, leader of your people; you have fulfilled your promise to Allah, and Allah will fulfill His promise to you. That is the note the day leaves ringing. A dying man who spent the last blood in his body being faithful when faithfulness cost him everything, and a Prophet ﷺ who would rather give away his own authority than wield it over a people who could not trust him. Whose loyalty do you carry, the day keeps asking, and is it Allah's, even when it is hard? O Allah, make us faithful to our word with You as Sa'd was faithful, let our loyalty be Yours in the very moment it costs us most, forgive us as You forgave Abu Lubabah at his pillar, and gather us with Sa'd ibn Mu'adh among the prophets, the truthful, the martyrs, and the righteous, and how excellent are those as companions.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 61: Banu Qurayzah (Memphis Islamic Center, 2013). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (33:25, 8:27, 9:102, 76:7), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry differing numbers and narrations, the Sheikh's own weighing has been followed.

The Seerah · 62 of 104

Minor expeditions after the Trench ﷺ

The small skirmishes where the biggest hearts turned

After the Trench, the seerah goes quiet for a while, and the quiet is honest. Roughly eighteen months lie between the great siege that nearly broke Madinah and the treaty that would change everything at Hudaybiyyah, and into those months fall more than a dozen minor skirmishes and expeditions, none of them, Dr. Yasir Qadhi is careful to say, anywhere near as significant as the battle before or the truce after. He chooses four or five, sets the rest aside as names the very advanced student can look up, and tells these few not for their politics, which were small, but for what happened inside them, which was not small at all. The strange thing about these unremarkable days is what filled them. Not conquest. Conversion. A king who walked into Madinah hating that blessed face ﷺ and could not walk back out the same man. An assassin who came for blood and left a brother. A son-in-law so scrupulous he repaid every borrowed coin before he would so much as say the shahada. The Prophet ﷺ rarely lifted a sword in any of these stories. Mostly he was simply who he was, and people standing near him found they could not stay who they had been.

So this is a chapter about the small days, and about the unreasonable size of what they can hold, the question they put quietly to every one of our own ordinary weeks.

The king tied to a pillar

It began with a routine success. A detachment of about thirty companions had been sent against a rebellious tribe in central Arabia, most likely in the sixth year of the hijrah, and they did exactly what they were sent to do and came home. The expedition itself is not the story; what they crossed on the road back is. A small convoy moved through the open desert, and something about it did not sit right, the signs of wealth and the air of rank on men of obvious importance out in the middle of nowhere with no proper guard. The companions took them captive and brought them to the Prophet ﷺ without the faintest idea whom they had caught.

It was Thumama ibn Uthal, chieftain of Banu Hanifah, one of the largest tribes in all of Arabia. To grasp who that is, Dr. Yasir Qadhi reaches for the only scale that fits: an Abdul Muttalib type of figure, the big man, a chief whose tribe was roughly the size of Quraysh itself. There was no royalty among the Arabs, but if there had been, Thumama was royalty. He had not been neutral in the war; he had stood with the pagans, and he had publicly vowed that if he ever got the chance he would kill the Prophet ﷺ himself. He had been travelling unguarded only because he never imagined the danger, certain Quraysh would never harm him and the Muslims were far away. But the Prophet ﷺ had once made a du'a that Allah would give him power over Thumama, and here, carried in as a prisoner by companions who did not recognize him, was the answer to that prayer. They did not know him. The Prophet ﷺ knew him at once.

There was no prison in Madinah, so they tied his hand to a pillar of the mosque itself, and this enemy chieftain who had sworn to kill the Prophet ﷺ now sat bound inside the Prophet's ﷺ own masjid. Then the Prophet ﷺ gave an instruction that set the whole tone of what followed. Treat him well. That evening he told his wives to send Thumama food from his own household, not because his family ate better than the companions but for the message carried with it, the bearer announcing where it came from: this is from Muhammad ﷺ. A man who had promised murder was being fed from the table of the one he had promised to kill.

Each morning the Prophet ﷺ came and asked the same question. What do you have to say, Thumama? And each morning Thumama answered not like a beggar but like a king. If you kill me, he said, you kill a man whose blood is heavy, and you will have war on your hands. If you forgive me, you forgive a man who recognizes a favor and repays it. And if it is money you want, name your price. There was no pleading in it, only the level confidence of a man who knew his worth and was not afraid to die. The Prophet ﷺ invited him to Islam; Thumama said nothing, and the Prophet ﷺ let him be. The next morning, the same; the third morning, again. The reports do not spell out what those three days did to him, but the Sheikh asks us to do the obvious arithmetic. What does a man absorb, tied to a pillar in the Prophet's ﷺ masjid for some seventy-six hours? He hears the adhan and the prayers, the Qur'an through the night; he watches the manners of the people; he sees exactly what this religion is. Nobody preached him a sermon; the place simply went on being itself in front of him. And on the third day the Prophet ﷺ said the thing nobody expected. Release him.

Three days to change a life

Thumama stood up, walked out a free man, went to a grove of date palms, and took a bath. Then, though he could have ridden away, he walked back in and said it, still addressing the Prophet ﷺ by his bare first name because no one had yet had time to teach him the etiquette of the believers. By Allah, Muhammad, there was no face on this whole earth more hateful to me than yours, and now there is none more beloved. There was no religion I despised more than yours, and now there is none dearer. There was no city I hated more than your city, and now it is the most beloved of cities to me. Three days, and the entire architecture of his hatred had been overturned, hatred of the man, the faith, the place, every wall of it down.

He explained himself: he had been on his way to perform the pilgrimage when the Muslims crossed his road, and he asked whether he should continue, a believer now. The Prophet ﷺ told him to go on. And here Dr. Yasir Qadhi draws the lesson the whole episode was built to teach: gentleness gets you what harshness never will. The Prophet ﷺ took no ransom, though Thumama offered any sum, and made no threat, though he held the man's life in his hand. He fed him from his own table and handed him back his freedom, the freedom of a man who had vowed to kill him. What does that do to a heart? It does what no sword could. Strength had not opened Thumama. Dignity had.

That same evening the companions brought him the lavish meals he had eaten for days, and he ate only a little and stopped, to their astonishment. The Prophet ﷺ explained: a man who ate this morning with the stomach of a disbeliever now eats tonight with the stomach of a believer, for the disbeliever eats with seven stomachs and the believer with one. Islam had reached even into the way he sat at a meal.

Then Thumama turned a single conversion into a strategy he never planned. He went on to Makkah and, by Ibn Ishaq's report, became the first person to enter the city openly proclaiming the talbiyah cleansed of the idols Quraysh had added to it, and when they surrounded him with half-drawn swords for it, only the calmer voices saved him, warning that to kill the chieftain of Banu Hanifah was to bring the whole tribe down on their necks. Thumama left that confrontation enraged, and his rage took a devastating shape. By Allah, he swore, not a single grain of wheat will reach you from the north until the Prophet ﷺ himself commands me to allow it. The grain road ran through his country, and he closed it. Weeks passed, then months, until the city was reduced to a famine food the Sheikh could barely find described, resorted to only in droughts because it was so wretched, made by the surviving reports of camel's blood and camel's hair. At last Abu Sufyan had to swallow his pride and write to the Prophet ﷺ, and even half-starving he could not keep the arrogance out of his pen: you claim you preach kindness to kin, and here you are letting us waste away.

And the Prophet ﷺ, at open war with these very people, wrote to Thumama to let the wheat resume. He could have said no, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi notes that no one could have faulted him: these were the people who had exiled him, tried to assassinate him, cut off his own community's supplies only months before. But the Prophet ﷺ would face Quraysh on the battlefield and would not starve their women and children. The Sheikh has a phrase for it, and it is the texture of the whole religion: strictness tempered with pragmatic mercy. He reopened the road, and Makkah ate again.

The assassin with treachery written on him

Abu Sufyan, by now the de facto senior leader of all Quraysh as the older generation died off, was not finished. He announced a bounty for anyone who would kill the Prophet ﷺ, and a bedouin came forward to earn it. Abu Sufyan equipped him with a camel, supplies, weapons, and a cover story: he would ride to Madinah pretending he wished to accept Islam, the usual pretext, the known way of getting close. He made the journey in six hard days, walked into the mosque, and began his rehearsed lines.

The Prophet ﷺ looked at him and said: this is a man with treachery written upon him. The instant the words were spoken the companions tackled him to the floor, and as they brought him down a hidden dagger fell from his belt, the dagger you do not carry into an audience with the leader unless you mean to use it. There was the whole plot, on the ground beside him. The Prophet ﷺ gave him a choice: tell me the truth and I will let you go. The man told it all, the bounty, the scheme, Abu Sufyan's hand behind it. And the Prophet ﷺ kept his word and freed him, and the would-be assassin, given his life by the man he had come to murder, embraced Islam on the spot. We do not even know his name; he is one of those footnotes the seerah keeps. But the pattern is unmistakable, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi sets it deliberately beside the killings that ran the other direction in these same months. In those days this was a two-way street: Quraysh sent assassins, and the Muslims, as we are about to see, sent their own. And here a man who walked in to kill walked out a believer, disarmed not by the companions but by the mercy of the one he had targeted.

A targeted killing, told honestly

Among these months falls a story Dr. Yasir Qadhi calls one of the favorites of those who want to attack the Prophet ﷺ, and one he frankly does not find troubling at all; he refuses both to sensationalize it and to apologize for it. It is the killing of Abu Rafi', Sallam ibn Abi al-Huqayq, one of the wealthy chiefs of Khaybar. Sallam was no bystander. He had been among the principal financiers of the coalition that besieged Madinah at the Trench, whose vast army was largely hired mercenaries, men with no stake in the fight who wanted only their pay and were the first to scatter when Allah's help came. Sallam had paid for them, and a man who had financed the destruction of Madinah once would do it again. This time the Prophet ﷺ did not order it. A party of the Ansar came with the proposal themselves, eager because the Aws had earlier dealt with Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf and the Khazraj felt the score stood one to nothing. And this was genuinely dangerous, far more than the killing of Ka'b who had lived an hour away. Khaybar lay two and a half or three days off, and every clan there sat behind the thick fortified walls the Jewish tribes had mastered and the Muslims had not yet learned to breach. The Prophet ﷺ granted permission on one firm condition: harm no woman, harm no child.

Five men went, led by Abdullah ibn Atik, chosen because he spoke the language of Khaybar like a native; you cannot carry out such a thing stuttering in plain Arabic where everyone around you speaks fluently. As the gates were closing at nightfall, Abdullah made himself visible to the guard at a distance, pretending to be a man relieving himself, just another straggler. The impatient guard called to him to hurry, and he slipped through, hid until full dark, then opened the gate and let the others in. They found Sallam's chamber, in the highest room of the house, and killed him. His wife cried out, and one of the men raised his sword to silence her, then remembered the order and sheathed it. On the way out, in the rising alarm, their weak-eyed leader missed a step and broke his foot, and the others carried him through the night while the whole settlement screamed behind them. They reached Madinah without a single casualty, and the Prophet ﷺ passed his hand over the foot and it was healed.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi will not flinch from what this was, and he will not let the modern reader posture over it either. These were different times with different norms, a declared state of war. This was a targeted killing, in the language we now use, the same kind of act Quraysh had just attempted in the Prophet's ﷺ own mosque with the bedouin and his dagger; it ran in both directions, simply how warfare worked. No scholar in the history of Islam, he stresses, ever licensed an individual to take such matters into his own hands. And the modern world, with its own drones and surgical strikes, has forfeited any clean standing to be scandalized by something fourteen centuries old. Deal with your own present, he says in effect, before you arraign the distant past.

It was on a related and larger expedition, against Banu Lihyan, the tribe responsible for the treacherous massacre of the companions at ar-Raji', that Allah legislated something that would outlast every skirmish of these months. Banu Lihyan, warned by their scouts, fled, and there was no battle. But Quraysh sent a small force to observe, and a moment came that taught the believers a permanent lesson. When the Muslims stood to pray, one of the watching pagans said: if only they would do that again, for there, with the whole army bowing and prostrating, would be the perfect moment to fall upon them in a single charge. They would wait, he was told; another prayer was coming. And in the interval Allah sent down the prayer of fear, salat al-khawf.

And when you [i.e., the commander of an army] are among them and lead them in prayer, let a group of them stand [in prayer] with you and let them carry their arms. And when they have prostrated, let them be [in position] behind you and have the other group come forward which has not [yet] prayed and let them pray with you, taking precaution and carrying their arms. Those who disbelieve wish that you would neglect your weapons and your baggage so they could come down upon you in one [single] attack. But there is no blame upon you, if you are troubled by rain or are ill, for putting down your arms, but take precaution. Indeed, Allāh has prepared for the disbelievers a humiliating punishment.Qur'an 4:102

The choreography is laid out in the verse itself: the army splits, one group prays behind the imam while the other stands guard with its weapons, and then they trade, so the believers are never both unarmed and in prostration at once. The pagans had hoped to catch them defenseless in the very act of worship; Allah answered with a form of worship that could not be caught, the one place in the whole Qur'an where the steps of a single prayer are written down.

The son-in-law and the necklace

Then comes a story plain in its events and unforgettable in its heart. Zayd ibn Harithah was sent with about a hundred and seventy men to intercept a Quraysh caravan returning from Syria, and he took the whole of it, wealth and camels and captives. Among the prisoners was Abu al-As ibn al-Rabi'.

To understand why the mosque would soon hold its breath over this captive, you have to know who he was. Abu al-As was the nephew of Khadijah, his mother her elder sister Halah, and he was married to Zaynab, the eldest of the Prophet's ﷺ four daughters. The match had been Khadijah's own doing, and the Prophet ﷺ, who the Sheikh notes would never refuse Khadijah anything, had given Zaynab to him. He was no hater of the faith. When Quraysh pressed every man married to one of the Prophet's ﷺ daughters to divorce her, the others gave in, but Abu al-As refused; offered any woman he wanted in exchange, he answered that no one could ever take Zaynab's place. He had fought at Badr on the Makkan side under compulsion, with no heart in it, and been taken prisoner there too.

That earlier captivity is the part that breaks you open. After Badr, when Abu al-As was a prisoner, Zaynab had sent her ransom from Makkah: a necklace that had belonged to her mother Khadijah. The Prophet ﷺ saw it among the ransoms and the memories came flooding back, the whole of his life with Khadijah rising up in a piece of jewelry, and he asked the companions, if they saw fit, to release this captive and return his daughter's necklace, for the ransom was theirs to give, not his to command. They freed Abu al-As gladly and sent the necklace back. But the Prophet ﷺ had asked one thing in return: send me Zaynab. And Abu al-As, a pagan still, had kept his word and sent her on to Madinah.

Now, years later, he was a captive a second time, and this time it was Zaynab's voice that rang out. After the dawn prayer, from the women's side of the mosque, she called, loud enough for every soul present to hear: O Muslims, I am Zaynab, daughter of Muhammad ﷺ, and I have granted my protection to this man. When the prayer ended the Prophet ﷺ turned and asked, did you hear what I heard? They said yes. By Allah, he said, I had no knowledge of this until now, no more than any of you. And then he taught them the principle the whole ummah would carry forever after: the believers are as one in the protection they extend; the least and lowliest among them may grant a pledge of safety, and all the rest are bound to honor it. So Abu al-As was protected on his daughter's word.

There remained his caravan, which was not only his own wealth but goods entrusted to him by many in Makkah. The Prophet ﷺ told the companions who held it that if they saw fit to return his property it would be more beloved to him, but that the choice was theirs and he would not force it. They gave it all back. Some of the Ansar, more zealous than the instruction warranted, tried to attach a condition the Prophet ﷺ had not set: would Abu al-As not accept Islam before they handed over the goods? He refused them flatly, and his refusal is its own magnificence. What an evil suggestion, he said, that I should change my faith out of greed for my property and betray the trust placed in me. He would not be bribed into the shahada.

And then Abu al-As carried it all back to Makkah, every last dirham, and went through the people asking: has anyone entrusted me with anything I have not returned? Until they all said no, you have discharged every trust. Of the whole caravan, he was the one man who brought the entrusted wealth home intact. And only then, with every obligation cleared and his honor beyond accusation, did he announce his Islam openly before Quraysh, so no one could ever say he had done it for his life or his money. He returned to Madinah a Muslim, and he and Zaynab were reunited. Of all his sons-in-law, Dr. Yasir Qadhi notes, this is the one of whom the Prophet ﷺ said: he spoke to me and was truthful, and he promised me and he kept his promise.

There is a subtle fiqh knot in the reunion the Sheikh does not skip. The authentic report says the Prophet ﷺ returned Zaynab to Abu al-As without a new marriage contract, even though years had passed in which she was Muslim and he was not. By the majority position, when a wife accepts Islam and her husband does not embrace it within her waiting period the marriage is dissolved, and a later conversion requires a new contract. But a minority, championed by Ibn al-Qayyim among others, took the report at its word: such a woman, if she has not married another in the interval, may simply resume married life with her husband once he accepts Islam, and the Sheikh inclines to this as the plain sense of the case.

The gift the size of a hill

The last story Dr. Yasir Qadhi tells is the one that gave a whole stretch of these expeditions its name: the army of al-khabat, the withered leaves. Some three hundred men under Abu Ubaydah ibn al-Jarrah were sent to intercept a Quraysh caravan and missed it, and the long march wore their supplies down to nothing. Abu Ubaydah rationed what was left, the same portion to each man, and the portion shrank until it was a single date a day, and then even that ran out. They sucked on the date pits for the faint taste, and they stripped the withered leaves off the thorn bushes, the khabat, and ate those, simply to stay alive. The expedition is remembered by that hunger.

Then they reached the shore, near a coastal town not far from Madinah, and Allah laid a gift before them the reports can barely describe. A whale, al-Anbar, lay beached and dead, a creature the like of which they had never seen. More than ten men, by one count thirteen, sat together inside the socket of its eye; they planted one of its rib bones upright in the sand, and Abu Ubaydah mounted the tallest camel in the army and rode beneath that single bone without lowering his head. For a month, three hundred starving men ate from this one animal until they grew fat again, and then they packed what meat remained and carried it home.

But it troubled them, and the trouble is itself a window into how seriously they took the law of Allah. They had eaten a creature already dead, that no one had slaughtered or even caught alive. So they brought the leftover meat to the Prophet ﷺ and asked him plainly: was it lawful, what we ate? He asked whether any remained, and when they brought it, he ate of it himself.

Lawful to you is game from the sea and its food as provision for you and the travelers, but forbidden to you is game from the land as long as you are in the state of iḥrām. And fear Allāh to whom you will be gathered.Qur'an 5:96

This, by Dr. Yasir Qadhi's knowledge, is the one and only time in his entire life that the Prophet ﷺ tasted seafood, and he ate it not from hunger but as a teaching, plain and physical, that the food of the sea is lawful. It need not be ritually slaughtered; it need not even have been caught alive, for this one was found dead on the sand. By naming the very greatest creature of the sea as halal, the Prophet ﷺ settled the matter for everything smaller: if the enormous whale, washed up and never hunted, is lawful, what doubt remains about the fish, the shrimp, the crab? The lesson teaches itself downward from the largest case, and it rests on the verse Allah revealed.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to skim these months as filler, the interval a lesser telling hurries through from one great battle to the next. That would be to miss the question they put to us, which may be the most personal the whole seerah asks, because most of our lives are made of exactly these days, not of Badr or the Trench.

Look at what actually changed in this quiet stretch. Not borders. Hearts. A chieftain came in chains, having sworn murder, and left unable to live without the face he had hated, undone by three days of plain dignity. An assassin came with a hidden dagger and a rehearsed lie, and left a brother. A son-in-law was praised by the Prophet ﷺ for repaying every entrusted coin before he would even say the shahada: he promised, and he kept his promise. None of this was won by force. The Prophet ﷺ was simply, relentlessly, who he was, and people could not stand near that and remain unchanged. That is the first thing to carry home: you do not need a great occasion to change a life, and you will almost never be given one. You will be given small encounters and small kindnesses, and it was on exactly such days that the biggest hearts in Arabia turned.

And notice the balance the Sheikh keeps pointing to, the one most of us never quite manage. The Prophet ﷺ was at open war with Quraysh, had faced them four times in the field, and yet when Thumama's blockade was starving their women and children he reopened the grain to the very people who had exiled and hunted him. Strictness tempered with mercy, firm toward the enemy yet unwilling to let the helpless waste away, and in him those were never two things but one. We tend to collapse into one pole, all softness or all severity; he held both at once, and that tension is the very texture of the religion. Notice too how often, across these stories, the du'a of the Prophet ﷺ is simply answered: he asked Allah for power over Thumama, and Thumama was carried into his mosque as a prisoner. It is a sign of his rank with his Lord, and a quiet invitation, for the One who answered His Prophet ﷺ so completely is the same One you are asking. Bring Him your small needs on your small days.

So take this from the small days. Be gentle where you could so easily be harsh, because gentleness opens what force never will. Keep your word and pay back every trust, down to the last coin no one is watching, as Abu al-As did before he would even claim the faith. Honor the safe-conduct you have given, because the pledge of the least believer is sacred. And believe that your minor days are not minor, that the same Lord who turned a king's hatred to love in seventy-six hours, and freed an assassin into faith, is at work in the unremarkable hours of your life too. O Allah, who answered Your Prophet's ﷺ every supplication, soften our hearts as You softened Thumama's, keep us true to our word as Abu al-As was true, make us strict only where we must be and merciful wherever we can, and gather us with Your beloved ﷺ on the Day we are all gathered to You.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 62: the minor expeditions between Khandaq and Hudaybiyyah (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (4:102, 5:96), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 63 of 104

The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, part 1

The road to a victory no one could see yet

There is an event in the seerah that Allah Himself calls a victory, and almost everyone guesses the wrong one. Ask a room full of Muslims what al-fath was, the clear opening, the manifest conquest, and most will picture the day the gates of Makkah swung open and the Prophet ﷺ rode in at the head of ten thousand. But Surah al-Fath, the surah of the opening, did not come down in the year Makkah was taken. It came down two years earlier, on a dusty plain outside the sanctuary, over a treaty so bitter that the believers who signed it walked away feeling humiliated. Not a sword was drawn. The pilgrims were turned back from a House they had crossed a desert to reach, and made to put their names to terms that read, to every eye on that plain, like a surrender. And while they were still swallowing it, Allah called it a clear victory.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi opens this part of the seerah by setting that gap squarely in front of us, because it is the lesson the whole arc turns on: what Allah names a triumph and what a watching crowd calls a defeat are not always the same thing. Hold that gap in your mind for the next stretch of the story, because everything in it will test it. And he flags, gently, why of all the episodes in the seerah this one speaks so directly to us. Hudaybiyyah is the story of a community that enters into an agreement, keeps its word, and then has to stand and watch injustice unfold under the cover of that very agreement, with no power to strike back, with nothing left to do but raise its hands and ask Allah to act where it cannot. For Muslims living as a minority, bound by treaties and laws not of their making, there is hardly an episode in the entire life of the Prophet ﷺ that lands closer to home. We will reach the heart of it in the days to come. Today is only the road in.

A victory with no battle in it

The Sheikh notes a small puzzle in how the companions themselves remembered this event, because it tells you what they understood it to be. Most of the later writers call it the treaty of Hudaybiyyah, or the peace of Hudaybiyyah. But the companions, the ones who were there, overwhelmingly called it the ghazwah of Hudaybiyyah, the expedition, the campaign. That word is strange here, because a ghazwah is normally the name for a battle the Prophet ﷺ himself led, and at Hudaybiyyah no battle was ever fought. One of them, listing the campaigns he had joined, counted Hudaybiyyah among them as the fifth, a battle with no fighting in it. Why?

Because, the Sheikh explains, the fighting did not happen, but everything except the fighting did. The believers came a hair's breadth from war; they took an oath to fight to the death and stood ready with the swords half drawn in their hearts. It is as if they had fought, and so they gave it the name of a battle they were spared. And there is a second reason it earns that heavier name: the event was so vast in its consequences that it overflows the small plain it happened on. It was a conquest in everything but bloodshed, and naming it a ghazwah, the companions reached for a word large enough to hold what they had lived through.

Indeed, We have given you, [O Muhammad], a clear conquestQur'an 48:1

A dream of the Ka'bah

What set the whole journey in motion was a dream. Sometime in the sixth year after the Hijrah, the Prophet ﷺ saw himself making tawaf around the Ka'bah, standing in the white of ihram, his hair shaved, the rites of umrah complete. And the dreams of the prophets are true. He read it as it was meant: an instruction from his Lord to go to the House. He had not seen Makkah in six years, and neither had the companions who had left everything they owned inside it, the homes and the graves and the streets of their childhood, and walked away with nothing.

The Qur'an would later confirm the dream by name, in plain words, promising that they would enter the Sacred Mosque in safety, heads shaved, fearing no one. But the Sheikh asks us to notice something folded into that same ayah. The dream said they would enter. It did not say when. The believers had assumed it meant this year, and the assumption was natural, and it was also their own. Later, when the year ended very differently from how they had pictured it, one of them would come to the Prophet ﷺ and ask him outright: did you not tell us we would make tawaf and shave our heads? And he ﷺ answered him: I told you, but did I say it would be this year? Allah shows His Messenger the truth, and still keeps the timing of it folded inside His own knowledge. The dream was real. The waiting was real too, and learning to live in the space between them is most of what faith is.

Certainly has Allah showed to His Messenger the vision [i.e., dream] in truth. You will surely enter al-Masjid al-Haram, if Allah wills, in safety, with your heads shaved and [hair] shortened, not fearing [anyone]. He knew what you did not know and has arranged before that a conquest near [at hand].Qur'an 48:27

The ones who stayed behind

The Prophet ﷺ announced his intention openly, to the people of Madinah and to the Muslim tribes scattered around it: a peaceful umrah to Makkah, and whoever was able to come should come. By now the territory of Islam had grown large; Badr, Uhud, and the Trench were behind them, and the lands near Madinah had largely entered the faith. The Emigrants prepared at once, understanding what it would cost in time and what it might cost in blood, and preparing anyway. But the bedouin tribes nearer the desert held back. And when the travelers finally returned, these same men came forward with their excuse ready and rehearsed: our wealth and our families kept us occupied, so ask Allah to forgive us.

Allah exposed them in the Qur'an before they could finish the sentence. They say with their tongues, He said, what is not in their hearts. And then He named the real reason, which was far darker than busyness. You thought, He told them, that the Messenger and the believers would never come back to their families, ever. You looked at fourteen hundred unarmed men in pilgrim's cloth walking straight toward the city that had twice marched out to slaughter them, and you did the arithmetic, and you bet against Allah. You thought an evil thought, and it ruined you. Their fear, the Sheikh notes, did not merely keep them comfortable and alive at home. It robbed them of the single greatest honor available to their generation, an honor that was, at that very moment, being handed to the men who had gone.

Those who remained behind of the bedouins will say to you, "Our properties and our families occupied us, so ask forgiveness for us." They say with their tongues what is not within their hearts. Say, "Then who could prevent Allah at all if He intended for you harm or intended for you benefit? Rather, ever is Allah, of what you do, Aware.Qur'an 48:11
But you thought that the Messenger and the believers would never return to their families, ever, and that was made pleasing in your hearts. And you assumed an assumption of evil and became a people ruined."Qur'an 48:12

Because those who walked anyway, who put their families and their fields and their very lives behind the command and went, were lifted. The companions, the Sheikh reminds us, were never a single flat rank; they rose in degrees, Badr above what came after, and Hudaybiyyah above what came after that. Of the people gathered with him on this journey the Prophet ﷺ would later say that no one on the face of the earth was more righteous than they were, and anyone present received, from that day, an automatic elevation in standing among the companions. That is the quiet machinery of faith the Sheikh keeps pointing at, the principle that runs through the whole religion: the more you trust Allah, the more He entrusts to you. The men who stayed home to keep themselves safe kept nothing of worth; the men who walked toward the danger were named the most righteous alive.

Leaving in white, on purpose

Fourteen hundred companions set out from Madinah. The number alone tells a story the histories never spell out but cannot hide: Badr had been a few hundred, Uhud and the Trench more, and now a single umrah draws fourteen hundred. The tide was turning even in raw count; the believers were no longer a hunted handful but a people numbering in the thousands.

And everything about the way they left was a deliberate message. The Prophet ﷺ timed the departure to the first of Dhul-Qa'dah, the opening day of the sacred months, the four months in which the ancient law of Ibrahim forbade all fighting, in which a man could meet his own father's killer at the Ka'bah and not lift a hand against him. He waited for the first of those months on purpose. He left in ihram, the white cloth of a pilgrim, not the iron of a soldier. He did not feint in one direction and double back, the way he masked his every military march; he went straight at Makkah, in the open, so that no living soul could mistake what he intended. By every custom the Quraysh themselves claimed to revere, they were forbidden to turn a pilgrim away in the sacred months, and in all their history they never once had. The Prophet ﷺ was, in effect, daring them to be the first.

And he brought the animals. Seventy camels, consecrated, garlanded, marked out with the signs the Arabs all recognized as offerings dedicated to Allah and to His House. Here the Sheikh lingers on a sunnah the ummah has all but forgotten, the consecration of the sacrificial animal, the hady, decorated and set apart so that everyone who saw it knew it had been given to Allah and could not be touched. It was an act the Arabs read instantly as a sign of piety, and they honored it. The Prophet ﷺ took seventy on this journey and would later take a hundred for his Hajj, because it was his way never to repeat a good deed at the same measure but always to climb. The camels were a true act of worship, offered for Allah's sake and nothing else. And they would also, in the days ahead, soften the hearts of the men Makkah sent out to negotiate, who would look at all those garlanded beasts and say that it was not right to turn such people away from the House. Trust in Allah, and every reasonable precaution, walking side by side.

That [is so]. And whoever honors the symbols [i.e., rites] of Allah - indeed, it is from the piety of hearts.Qur'an 22:32

Cautious to the last detail

The Prophet ﷺ never walked in blind, and this journey shows it at every turn; his whole life, the Sheikh says, demonstrates a man who knew Allah would provide and yet did everything in his power to make the plan sound. He chose a single scout to slip ahead into Makkah and learn what the Quraysh were preparing, and he chose him with care: not a famous Emigrant whose face the whole of Makkah knew, not a known Helper, but a man from the tribe of Khuza'ah, a neutral clan, a man who had not even announced his Islam and whom no one in Makkah would think to suspect. He posted lookouts around the caravan. And the heavier weapons of war, the armor and the fighting blades, he kept packed away in the baggage train, unworn, while the travelers carried only the lighter swords a man takes for the road. A pilgrim in ihram cannot dress for battle; the state of consecration does not allow it. But a wise man does not march defenseless toward enemies who have twice tried to kill him either. And here, the Sheikh points out, the two reports in the books of seerah, one saying the Muslims were armed and one saying they were not, are reconciled exactly: they were not wearing the weapons of war, and the weapons were nonetheless with them, riding in the baggage, ready if the day demanded them.

Then the scout came back with hard news. The Quraysh had heard. They had armed themselves, put on the leopard skins they wore as a public declaration of war, and sent out a cavalry force under Khalid ibn al-Walid, still on their side in those days, to camp on the road at a place called Ghamim and block the pilgrims from reaching the city. They had even brought their women and children, as they had done at Uhud, to announce that they had no intention of retreating.

And here the Sheikh asks us to listen closely to what the Prophet ﷺ said when the news reached him, because the words open a window straight into his heart. Woe to the Quraysh, he said, war has consumed them. What would they lose if they left me to the rest of the Arabs? If the Arabs overcome me, that is the very thing they wanted, and they are rid of me at no cost. And if Allah grants me victory over the Arabs, they will enter Islam with their honor intact, sharing in the triumph. Why must my own people be the ones to fight me? By the One in whose hand is my soul, I will not stop until Allah makes His religion victorious, or this neck of mine is severed. The Sheikh holds those words up against every accusation ever thrown at the Prophet ﷺ, that he was a man hungry for war. Hear him. This is a man grieving that his own blood will force a war upon him, a man who fought only when he was made to and to whom the prospect of fighting his own kin was a wound. He loved his people with the plain love a man has for his own, and he could not understand why they would choose the loss.

The council and the road of thorns

With Khalid's cavalry sitting across the direct road, the Prophet ﷺ stopped the caravan, praised Allah, and laid the whole situation before the companions, the way a true leader does, the Sheikh observes, rather than simply handing down an order that men obey with half a heart. He even offered his own first idea: strike at the desert tribes who had marched out alongside the Quraysh, and draw the enemy into a fight on those terms. Then he asked what they thought.

It was Abu Bakr, radiyallahu anhu, who answered. You came out, Messenger of Allah, intending only the House of Allah, not war and not the killing of anyone. So let us press on straight toward the Ka'bah as we set out to do, and fight only those who try to stop us. The Prophet ﷺ liked it better than his own first thought, and he changed his plan on the spot: then move on, in the name of Allah. A small thing, the Sheikh says, and an enormous one. A leader who rules by veto is obeyed grudgingly and without heart; a leader who consults raises men who follow with genuine enthusiasm, because the plan became theirs in the consulting. And it is also, the Sheikh notes, one of the clearest proofs that the Prophet ﷺ exercised his own considered judgment, his ijtihad, in worldly affairs, and that Allah permitted him to, for here he holds an opinion, hears a better one, and lets it go.

But the new route was brutal. To reach an alternate road into Makkah and slip around Khalid's army entirely, a guide who knew the country led them down through a valley of thorns and black volcanic rock, an entire plain that tore at their feet until they bled. And to carry his exhausted, bleeding people through it, the Prophet ﷺ told them that this place was for them what the gate had been for the Children of Israel, the gate they were commanded to enter humbly, asking forgiveness, and have their sins forgiven. Whoever passes through this valley, he ﷺ said, will have all his sins forgiven, except, he added, the man with the red camel. The forgiveness gave them the strength to go on, and the strange exception set them wondering. They searched, and found the man at the rear of the caravan, calling out for a lost red camel. They told him to come along and be forgiven, whatever he had done; he answered that finding his camel mattered to him more than going to their companion to have his sins forgiven. Not everyone walking beside the companions was a companion in heart. The Sheikh ties it, carefully, to the Qur'an's own plain acknowledgment that among the bedouins around Madinah were some whose faith had not truly entered, men the believers did not know but Allah did. And just as carefully he refuses to let that general fact be turned, as some try to turn it, against the great companions whom revelation praised by name. A single nameless man chasing a stray camel is one thing; Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, Ali, the ones the Qur'an and the Sunnah honor directly, are another entirely, and no vague verse about hidden hypocrites may be stretched to touch them. Whoever accuses such men of treachery, the Sheikh says plainly, only exposes the treachery in his own heart.

The camel that would not rise

By the time they finally emerged from the valley of thorns the night was falling, and they came out onto a plain called Hudaybiyyah, on the very edge of the sanctuary of Makkah, where a direct road runs down into the city. And there, as they arrived, the Prophet's ﷺ camel, Qaswa, knelt and would not move. The companions called to her the way the Arabs call a camel up, hal, hal, get up, move on, and she would not stir. They grew frustrated, because she was blocking the whole caravan and no one would ride ahead of him. Qaswa has become stubborn, one of them said. The Prophet ﷺ corrected him at once: Qaswa has not become stubborn, and that is not in her nature. Rather the One who held back the elephant has held her back too.

The Sheikh draws two things out of this, and both are easy to walk straight past. The first is the comparison itself. In the Year of the Elephant, Allah had halted Abrahah's elephant at the edge of Makkah, refusing to let it advance against the House. Now a camel is halted at the edge of Makkah. In both cases an animal is stopped, and stopped so that the sanctuary will be honored; the same hand was moving in both. There is wisdom in this stopping, the Prophet ﷺ was telling them, as there was in that one, so put your trust in Allah. The second thing is smaller and just as sharp. Look at how he ﷺ leapt to defend the honor of an animal against a careless word, refusing to let a gentle camel be slandered as stubborn when stubbornness was not in her. And the Sheikh turns it on us like a blade: if the reputation of a camel deserves to be guarded from a careless accusation, what about the reputation of a human being? Remember that, he says, the next time you hear someone smeared, the next time a careless word is about to leave your own mouth.

So where Qaswa knelt, they made camp, because that was plainly where they were meant to stop. And the one well on that plain had all but run dry, only a little water left at the very bottom. The people complained of thirst, their animals parched after a full day crossing the valley, far off the main road with its known wells. The Prophet ﷺ took an arrow from his quiver and had it placed down into the well, and he gargled water in his mouth and returned it into the bucket and sent it down, and the water rose and gushed until fourteen hundred people and all their animals drank their fill, and the men who had gone down inside had to scramble out so as not to drown. One more sign, among so very many, of the blessing Allah poured through his hands ﷺ, taking the little and making it more than enough.

And camped now at Hudaybiyyah, a single stone's throw from the Ka'bah he had seen in his dream, the Prophet ﷺ made a vow that would carry the weight of everything still to come. By the One in whose hand is my soul, he said, the Quraysh will not ask me for any condition in which they honor the sacred things of Allah except that I will grant it to them, so long as it spares bloodshed.

Hold that sentence, because it is a door into the rest of the story. Any condition, he ﷺ said, any condition at all, as long as it keeps the sacred things sacred and breaks no part of the religion and avoids the shedding of blood. The Sheikh reads it as the Prophet ﷺ deliberately steeling his companions for what was bearing down on them, because the terms the Quraysh were about to demand would be so bitter, so seemingly one-sided and humiliating, that even Umar ibn al-Khattab, radiyallahu anhu, the strongest and boldest of them, would not be able to make sense of them. He was preparing their hearts in advance, telling them before the blow landed that he had already decided to absorb it. The terms, the tears, the moment the firmest of the companions came undone, and then the slow and staggering turn by which Allah would name the whole of it a clear victory: that is where this road is leading.

Out on the plain, Khalid's scouts saw the Muslim camp and understood that the pilgrims had slipped around the cavalry, and the Quraysh were forced back toward Makkah to think again. Soon emissaries would begin crossing the open ground between the two camps. But the negotiations belong to tomorrow. For tonight, fourteen hundred travelers sit in white at the boundary of the sanctuary, the cuts from the valley still fresh on their feet, drinking from a well that should have been empty, waiting on a city that has never once in its history turned a pilgrim away, and is about to.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read the road into Hudaybiyyah as throat-clearing, the dull march before the real drama of the treaty, and to hurry past it. That would be to miss the questions it has already, quietly, put to us before a single term has been written down.

Begin with the gap the Sheikh set in front of us at the start, between what Allah names a victory and what a crowd calls a defeat. These fourteen hundred had a true dream of the Ka'bah, set out in obedience to it, walked openly and in peace, brought offerings garlanded for the House, did everything right, and they were about to be turned back from the door with terms that would taste like ash. And Allah would send down an entire surah to call it a clear victory while their feelings were still telling them they had lost. How often we do the reverse, rendering our verdict on a day the moment it disappoints us, writing defeat across an event before Allah has finished writing on it. The road into Hudaybiyyah asks us to wait for His ruling before we pronounce our own, because the very thing breaking our hearts may be the opening we would have begged Him for if we could have seen the end of it.

Then there is the way he ﷺ traveled, which teaches the balance most of us never quite manage. He sent a shrewdly chosen scout, posted lookouts, packed the weapons of war separately, and changed his plan when Abu Bakr's was better. He did, in short, every reasonable thing a careful man could do, and not one of those precautions was the smallest contradiction of his trust in Allah, because he also leaned his whole heart on his Lord and named the One who stopped the elephant as the One who had stopped his camel. Tie the camel, and trust the One who made the camel. We are forever collapsing that balance to one side, either scheming as though the outcome sat entirely in our own hands, or sitting idle and calling our negligence reliance on Allah. He ﷺ held both at full strength at once, and so must we.

And learn, finally, the smaller mercies threaded through the day. Learn from the bedouins who stayed home that fear keeps you safe and costs you everything, that the ones who hung back lost the highest honor of their generation not for anything they did but for the going they were too frightened to do. Learn from the man with the red camel that you can walk the entire distance beside the believers and arrive nowhere, if your heart was always somewhere else, chasing its own stray camel. And learn from Qaswa that if the Messenger of Allah ﷺ would not let an animal's good name be spoken against unjustly, then the careless word you were about to pass along about someone is heavier than you think. So take these down into your ordinary days. Wait for Allah's verdict before you write your own. Do everything in your power, and then rest your whole heart in His. Be of those who go when He calls, guard one another's honor as the Prophet ﷺ guarded his camel's, and learn, tonight, at the edge of a sanctuary you cannot yet enter, to trust the ending of a story before you are allowed to see it. May Allah, who held back the elephant and held back the camel, hold back from us every harm we cannot fend off ourselves, open for us the openings we could never imagine, and never let us read defeat into a day that He has written as a victory.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 63: between Khandaq and Hudaybiyyah, part 1 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (48:1, 48:27, 48:11, 48:12, 22:32), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing has been followed.

The Seerah · 64 of 104

The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, part 2

The envoys who came to argue and left convinced

When we left him last, his camel had knelt on the open plain of Hudaybiyyah and refused to rise, and he ﷺ had read in that refusal the will of his Lord and made his camp where the animal stopped. Fourteen hundred men were settled in their tents now, at the very edge of the sacred precincts, dressed in the two white cloths of ihram, their sacrificial animals garlanded and waiting, carrying nothing that could be called a weapon of war. They had come for umrah and nothing else. And on the far side of the water, behind a wall of armed men, sat a Makkah that would not let them in.

A word of warning before we walk into these days, the same warning Dr. Yasir Qadhi sets down first. These are among the hardest pages in the entire seerah to put in order. We do not have one connected account of Hudaybiyyah; we have a scatter of small reports, this companion remembering one scene, that companion another, and no chain that lets us say which came first and which came after. So what follows is one careful reconstruction, offered the way the Sheikh offers it, with Allah knows best written over the order of events. What is not in doubt is the shape of these days, and the shape is unforgettable: a procession of messengers crossing the sand, and almost every one of them arriving to argue and leaving unable to argue anymore.

The raid that drew no blood

Begin with an incident that has no date, because no narration gives it one. It may have fallen on the first day of the standoff or the last; the Sheikh sets it down on its own precisely because it cannot be slotted anywhere with confidence. It is preserved in Sahih Muslim, and it opens with eighty armed men.

While the camp slept, eighty fighters of the Quraysh crept down from the heights above Hudaybiyyah and timed their charge for the grey hour around the dawn prayer, hoping to fall upon a field of tents before a single Muslim could lift a hand. Picture what that raid was built to do. Eighty armed men breaking into a sleeping camp, striking fast and fleeing fast, and out of fourteen hundred there is no counting how many would have been cut down, two hundred, three hundred, before the raiders melted back into the hills. It was the kind of attack that ends with the valley full of the dead.

It ended with no one dead at all. The Muslims were not the easy prey the raiders had imagined; they had posted scouts, and the scouts saw the eighty coming. So the hunters became the hunted. Instead of surprising the camp, the eighty found themselves surrounded, disarmed, and marched in to the Prophet ﷺ without the spilling of a single drop of blood. An astonishing thing, the Sheikh calls it: eighty armed men forced to surrender and not one of them killed.

And then comes the twist that tells you everything about the man they had come to kill. He ﷺ forgave them. Every one of the eighty, captured in the act of a dawn ambush, he pardoned and sent home to Makkah unharmed. Sit with how strange that is. Every legal system that has ever existed, Sheikh Yasir points out, would have called this open and shut: you were attacked in your sleep, you may answer the attacker with the sword, and not a soul on earth would have blamed you. The eighty expected nothing less. And he let them walk away.

Allah named that restraint a gift, and recorded it in the Qur'an. Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi reads the verse closely, and notice that it cuts in two directions at once.

And it is He who withheld their hands from you and your hands from them within [the area of] Makkah after He caused you to overcome them. And ever is Allah, of what you do, Seeing.Qur'an 48:24

Read it slowly. He withheld their hands from you: eighty armed men fell on a sleeping camp and not one believer was harmed, and Allah claims that protection as His own doing. But then, and this is the half we are slow to see, He also withheld your hands from them. Because the Muslims, surrounding eighty raiders caught in an unprovoked attack, might very well have wanted to kill them, and would have been entitled to. And had even one of those eighty been struck down in that valley, the entire peace would have died with him, and Hudaybiyyah was about to become, after the conquest of Makkah itself, the greatest victory Allah would ever give this ummah. The mercy that spared eighty would-be killers was the very mercy that saved everything that came after. One drop of blood, and the whole future closes.

Why the surah is called the conquest

Hold onto something small that turns out not to be small at all. The whole of Surah al-Fath, the surah whose name is the conquest, was sent down about this, not about the day Makkah opened its gates, but about Hudaybiyyah, revealed on the road back to Madinah when it was over.

Many a Muslim hears the word fath and thinks at once of one thing only, the conquest of Makkah, because fath is fath and surely the conquest is what is meant. Sheikh Yasir corrects the reflex, gently but firmly. Fath means any opening, any victory. When you speak of al-fath without qualification, yes, you mean the day Makkah opened. But this surah, in its entirety, has nothing to do with that day. It came down about a treaty, and the victory it crowns was won by men who never unsheathed a sword.

Indeed, We have given you, [O Muhammad], a clear conquest.Qur'an 48:1

Carry that verse with you as the messengers begin to move, because nothing about the coming days is going to look like a conquest. It is going to look like a quarrel with no exit, an enemy too proud to yield over a thing that costs them nothing. And over all of it, before a single clause has been agreed, Allah has already written the word.

Budayl, and the offer the Quraysh would not hear

The first man to step into the gap between the two camps belonged to neither of them. Budayl was a chief of Khuza'ah, a tribe that lived in those hills and leaned quietly toward the Prophet ﷺ, though Budayl himself had not yet entered Islam; that would come in time. For now he was simply a neutral man, a noble Arab whom both sides trusted, and one who did not want a slaughter on the ground where his people lived.

He came into the camp carrying a warning. The sons of Ka'b, he said, naming the legendary ancestors of the Quraysh, are camped on the far side of the water, armed to the teeth, and they have sworn they will die before they let you set foot in the Haram. Muhammad, the message ran underneath, calm yourself, there is an army waiting over there. And he heard back the answer the Prophet ﷺ would give, almost word for word, to every envoy who came after him. We have not come to fight, he said. We have come to honor this House. War has worn the Quraysh down and worn us down alongside them; if they wish for peace, let them have peace, and if they wish for war, then by the One in whose hand is my soul, I will fight them for this matter until my neck is severed and Allah's decree is done. And then he laid out a way through, reasonable and generous: let the Quraysh step out of their city for a few days, let us enter and perform our umrah and leave, and then the city is theirs again. It was an offer the Quraysh would, in the end, accept, though only on their own bitter terms and only the following year.

Budayl carried the offer back across the sand. And here the Sheikh marks a detail that has not aged a day. The foolish among the Quraysh would not even let Budayl speak. We have no reason to listen to you, they told him, you have nothing to tell us we do not already know. The same shut ears we still meet, says Dr. Yasir, the people who have decided everything about you before you open your mouth, who do not care how reasonable the words might be because their minds are made up in advance. Arrogance is so foolish it blinds even a man's own intellect. The wise among them overruled the fools and let Budayl talk, and he told them the Prophet's ﷺ offer in full, and added his own counsel: O Quraysh, you are being hasty with this man, your anger is getting the better of you, he has not come to fight. The seeds of doubt were being sown in the enemy's own camp, and Budayl was only the first to sow them.

And the Quraysh gave the answer they would cling to, white-knuckled, to the very end. By Allah, they said, he will never enter upon us, and the Arabs will never say that he forced his way in. Not reason. Not theology. Pride, the Sheikh says plainly, nothing but pride. The whole standoff, when you dig to its root, was a wounded sense of honor: the Arabs must never be able to whisper that Muhammad walked into Makkah and the Quraysh could not stop him.

Urwa, the beard, and the curse of Abu Bakr

Then came Urwa ibn Mas'ud, a chief of Thaqif from Ta'if, Makkah's rival sister city in the mountains. Before the Quraysh would let him go as their envoy he had to prove his loyalty, and watch how an Arab does that: am I not like a father to you, he asked them, a son to you? Have I ever wronged you? Did I not avert bloodshed for you on such a day, did I not bring about reconciliation on another? He laid out his record like a resume, until they were satisfied that his heart was with them, and only then let him go. He reached the Prophet ﷺ and heard the same patient speech the others had heard, and he answered it with insult dressed up as advice.

Urwa looked over the fourteen hundred and saw, through the eyes of a man raised on the law of the tribe, nothing but a rabble. Muhammad, he said in effect, who are these people around you? By Allah I do not see men that I recognize, I see a medley of strangers thrown together, and the moment a sword is drawn they will scatter and abandon you and leave you standing alone.

Here Dr. Yasir steps back to teach something he wants carried far beyond this scene, because Urwa's slander follows a pattern that never dies. Look at what Urwa accuses the Prophet ﷺ of: breaking the ties of kinship, bringing a strange new theology, dragging in a crowd of outsiders nobody knows. And in every one of those smears there is a kernel of truth wrapped in layers of falsehood. From Urwa's pagan vantage the Prophet ﷺ truly was, in a sense, cutting across the old tribal bonds; his religion truly was new; he truly had come with people of many tribes. This, says the Sheikh, is the way of it with every accusation hurled at this religion across every century. There is always a small seed of something real, buried under a mountain of lies, and were it not for that seed nobody would ever swallow the lie. They called him a magician, because there were miracles; a poet, because the Qur'an is beautiful beyond speech. And the lesson is sharp: before we open our mouths to defend our faith against the accusations of our own age, we had better understand the kernel the other side is seeing, or our defense will never reach them.

But there was more in Urwa's words than a misreading. There was a claim that the believers would abandon their Prophet ﷺ at the first flash of a blade. And from behind the Prophet ﷺ that claim was answered by a curse so coarse that the Sheikh says he cannot bring himself to repeat it in the masjid, a savage retort flung at al-Lat, the idol that Ta'if guarded and Urwa's own city revered. And the man who said it was the last man on earth you would expect to hear such words from. Urwa, stung, demanded to know who had spoken, and was told: it was the son of Abu Quhafah. It was Abu Bakr, the gentlest of them, the shyest, the softest-spoken, the most modest man in the camp.

This is where Sheikh Yasir lingers, because the anger teaches as much as the gentleness does. Abu Bakr was not insulted for himself; nothing said that day had touched his own honor. He was told that the Muslims would forsake the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, and from stillness to fury in an instant, the kindest of all of them lost his temper and cursed, using words that in any other moment would have been unthinkable from his lips. Some things, the Sheikh says, are worth losing your temper over. He could absorb an insult to himself in silence, but the suggestion that he and his brothers would abandon the Prophet ﷺ set the gentlest heart in the camp ablaze. And the Prophet ﷺ did not rebuke him. It marks a rare exception, not a rule, the Sheikh is careful to add; the general way is to keep far from such speech, but once in a long while there is a thing that nothing else will say.

As the two men talked, Urwa kept reaching out to take hold of the Prophet's ﷺ beard, the old Arab gesture of one equal to another. And each time his hand came up, the flat of a sword came down on it, harder each time, and a voice told him: take your hand away from the Messenger of Allah ﷺ before it does not come back to you. Urwa looked up to find the man behind the helmet, and it was his own nephew. Mughira ibn Shu'bah had come to Islam carrying a dark past, a man who had once shared in the killing of travelers on the road for their goods, until Islam reached his heart and he came to Madinah a changed man. The Prophet ﷺ had accepted his Islam and refused his stolen wealth, telling him plainly that Islam wipes away what came before it but the blood-money and the plunder he could not bring among them, a judgment, the Sheikh notes, that is itself a quiet refutation of all who ever called these early Muslims highwaymen. Now Mughira stood as a bodyguard at the Prophet's ﷺ side, and being of the same blood as Urwa, he was the one man near enough in kinship to dare bring a sword down on an uncle's hand.

What an outsider saw

Urwa went back to the Quraysh, and what he reported is, in the Sheikh's words, one of the most beautiful passages in the whole of the seerah, and beautiful precisely because the man who spoke it was not yet a believer. He had no reason on earth to flatter them. He simply described what his own eyes had seen and could not explain.

O people, he said, I have entered the courts of kings. I have stood before Caesar of Rome, before Khusro of Persia, before the Negus of Abyssinia. By Allah, I have never seen a king honored by his people the way Muhammad ﷺ is honored by his companions. He does not so much as spit but one of them catches it in his hands and wipes it over his face and his skin. He does not make his ablution but they nearly come to blows over the water that runs from him. When he speaks they drop their voices in his presence, and they will not look him full in the face out of awe of him. If he so much as wishes for a thing, they race one another to do it before the words have left him. And then the man who had come to scold delivered his verdict: I have looked them over for you, O Quraysh, and if you want the sword, they will give you the sword, but I do not believe you can beat this man.

Here Dr. Yasir lingers longest, because he loves this turn in the road. The same man, in a single hour, going from who are these people, they will flee you at the first sword, to I have never seen anything like the way they love him. And mark how it happened. The Prophet ﷺ never argued Urwa into it; he never countered a single point. He simply was who he was, surrounded by people who loved him as no court on earth has ever loved a king, and a pagan chief saw it and could not unsee it. This, the Sheikh tells you flatly, is the real weapon of Hudaybiyyah. Not arms. Not numbers, for the Quraysh outnumbered them three to one. Iman, raw and visible, a faith so real that strangers who did not share it could feel it coming off the believers like heat. Our chief weapon, he presses it home, was never the strength of our hands but the belief in our hearts, and when that belief is true, the rest falls into place behind it. The Quraysh, hearing their own envoy go soft on them, snapped at him too: if any other man had said this, we would have answered him. And still, even now: he will not enter upon us this year.

The camel, the bedouin, and a coalition coming apart

Watch what the Prophet ﷺ does next, because Sheikh Yasir wants you to see the strategy working underneath the patience. It was time to send a man of his own toward the city, and to make the message of peace unmistakable he chose a companion named Khirash and mounted him on his own she-camel, an animal the people of Makkah would recognize on sight, so they would know the sender even before the messenger spoke. They recognized the camel and their rage spilled over. They hamstrung the animal where it stood, mobbed Khirash, and very nearly killed him, until a steadier voice talked the crowd down and sent him back alive. So a peaceful envoy of his own would not work either.

Then a man named Hulays stepped forward, a leader of the Ahabish, the great web of tribes around Makkah whom the Quraysh leaned on for their numbers and their muscle. And as Hulays drew near, the Prophet ﷺ recognized him from a distance and turned to his companions with an instruction that was pure understanding of the human heart: this is a man from a people who venerate the sacrificial animals, he said, so send the camels out to meet him. Hundreds of camels, garlanded and marked for the sacrifice, more than five hundred of them, the sheep and the goats besides, poured down the valley toward Hulays, and the Muslims raised their voices in the chant of the pilgrim in ihram. Hulays never even reached the camp. He saw a whole valley flooded with consecrated animals and pilgrims crying out their devotion, turned on his heel, and went straight back to the Quraysh: by Allah, it is not permitted to keep these animals from the House of Allah. They sneered at him, you are nothing but an ignorant bedouin, our mistake was ever to send you, and Hulays, stung by the contempt in it, swore by Allah that not one man of the Ahabish would stand with them. Another ally gone.

This, the Sheikh says, is the long patient game of a master, and you can watch the board change. One tribe at a time, the coalition is peeling away from the Quraysh, the Khuza'ah leaning off, Thaqif's envoy gone soft, now the whole bloc of the Ahabish swearing off the fight, until soon the Quraysh will stand alone and have no choice left but to talk. And notice his method with Hulays, because it is the heart of how this man called people to Allah. Hulays had a soft spot for the sacred animals, so the Prophet ﷺ showed him the animals, true and real, nothing staged, only the plain reality of why they had come. Meet people where their hearts already are; find the good a person already honors, and speak to that. And the Sheikh adds a word against the small-mindedness that creeps into some of us: there is righteousness in every people, even among these pagan chiefs who stood up for what was right when their own side was wrong, and the Prophet ﷺ welcomed it wherever he found it. A man need not share your creed to recognize a wrong when he sees one.

Sending Umar, and the man sent instead

Now it was the Prophet's ﷺ turn to send an envoy of his own straight into the city, into the lion's den itself, for in six long years no Muslim had walked into Makkah as a free man. It would take courage, and so he chose Umar, perhaps for exactly that, his strength and his fearlessness, the very man who had once announced his own Islam by daring anyone in Makkah to come and stop him.

And Umar, of all people, asked to be excused, not out of fear but out of clear eyes. Messenger of Allah, he said, my enmity with the Quraysh is well known, and I have no clan left in Makkah to stand at my back and protect me; they will kill me. Then, instead of simply refusing, he pointed to a better man. Uthman, he said, has more standing in their eyes than I do, and his people are strong and near to them. For in that world a man was shielded not by his own arm but by his tribe, and Uthman's tribe could cover him where Umar's could not. And the Prophet ﷺ took the counsel, the way he took good advice all through his life, weighing the word of a sincere man even when it changed his own plan, because firmness in principle was never the same thing as stubbornness in detail. So he turned to send Uthman ibn Affan into Makkah instead.

And there, with Uthman walking toward the city and his fate inside it still unknown, Dr. Yasir stops the story mid-stride. What that errand would cost, the rumor it would breed, and the pledge it would summon from fourteen hundred men under a tree, all of it waits for the days ahead. For now, hold the picture as it stands: a camp that came in peace, an enemy too proud to allow it, and one man after another crossing the sand to argue and turning back half-convinced.

What this day asks of our faith

Not a single battle was fought across these days, and yet by the end of them you have watched a war being won, and that runs against everything the world teaches about strength. A night raid was answered with forgiveness. A coalition of proud tribes came apart, not under attack, but one envoy at a time. A pagan chief was sent to scold and walked home in awe. And underneath all of it lay a weapon the Quraysh never thought to fear: a faith so real that even those who did not share it could feel it and could not stand against it.

So begin where the Sheikh begins, with the eighty who were spared. He ﷺ had every right under heaven and earth to answer that dawn ambush with the sword, and he forgave instead, and Allah called that restraint a gift, because a single drop of blood in that valley would have killed a victory greater than any battle could have won. We are quick to take what is ours by right and slow to ask what the forbearance might be worth. Sometimes the strongest thing a person can do is the very thing he has the full right to do and chooses not to. Ask, the next time you are wronged and entitled to strike back, what your restraint might be guarding that your anger cannot see.

Then sit with the real weapon. The Prophet ﷺ never won Urwa with an argument; he won him by being who he was among people who loved him as no king was ever loved. The people around you are reading the iman in your life long before they weigh anything you say, and a faith that is visible reaches hearts that no debate will ever open. And when you do speak, speak the way he ﷺ spoke to Hulays: find the good a person already honors and meet them there, with nothing bent and nothing staged. There is righteousness in every people; look for it, name it, and build on it.

And learn the gentleness and the anger together, because they are not opposites. The softest companion in the camp erupted in fury, and the Prophet ﷺ let it stand, because Abu Bakr was not burning for himself but for the Messenger of Allah ﷺ; love is what decides when to be calm and when to burn. Then learn the humility of leadership from the man at the center of it all, who heard Umar say send someone else and listened, because even the best plan can be bettered by a sincere word, and to be firm in your principles is not to be stubborn in your details.

So take one thing from this day of no battle down into your ordinary one. Be firm in what is true, and do not bend it to be liked or to be left alone. Be merciful to those who come against you, the way he ﷺ was merciful to eighty men caught with swords in their hands. Reach for the good in people wherever you find it. And let your faith in Allah be the thing that speaks for you before you have said a word, trusting, as this whole day teaches, that the truth, given time, always wins. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, who answered a raid with forgiveness and an enemy's pride with patience, make us firm upon what is true and soft toward His creation, and let the iman in our hearts speak before our tongues do.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 64: the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, part 2 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (48:1, 48:24), verified via quran.ai. Where the reports of these days resist a single chronology, the Sheikh's own careful reconstruction has been followed, and Allah knows best.

The Seerah · 65 of 104

The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, part 3

A pledge under a tree, a treaty that hurt to sign

Fourteen hundred believers are camped at Hudaybiyyah, on the very edge of the Haram, in ihram, with their sacrificial animals beside them and not a single thought of war in their hearts. They came north from Madinah to perform umrah, to walk around the House they had been driven away from, and nothing more. But the Quraysh have sealed every road into Makkah, and three or four envoys back and forth have gone nowhere. Now it is the Prophet's ﷺ turn to send a man of senior rank, someone the Quraysh cannot simply wave away, and what unfolds from that one decision becomes one of the most extraordinary days in the whole of the seerah: a rumor, an oath beneath a tree that the Qur'an itself would carry to the end of time, and then a treaty so lopsided that the ink of it would land, at the last, on the sound of a man dragging his chains.

Umar steps back, and Uthman steps forward

The Prophet's ﷺ first thought was Umar ibn al-Khattab. The reason is exactly what you would expect: physical strength, an intimidating presence, the kind of standing that lets a man walk into Makkah and not be pushed around. But Umar gave an answer that opens his heart to us. He did not refuse out of fear. Dr. Yasir Qadhi will not let that misreading stand for a moment: the last person on earth anyone could ever accuse of cowardice is Umar, the most fearless of men from before Islam until the day he died. Had this been a matter of ego, of making history, he would have been first in the line saying let me go, whatever happens. What he said instead was that his enmity with the Quraysh was simply too well known. They remembered exactly what he had done at Badr and exactly whom he had killed, and there was no clan of his left in Makkah to protect him. His own tribe, Banu Adi, had largely died out; there was no one to shield him if the Quraysh chose violence. And if they killed the envoy, the whole purpose of the mission, a treaty, an entry into Makkah, would be lost. Send someone they genuinely look up to, he said, someone without my baggage. And he named the man: Uthman ibn Affan.

Understand what this is. It is the opposite of ego. Umar took himself out of the spotlight so that the mission would actually succeed, and pointed instead to a man with the lineage to be safe and the dignity to be heard. Uthman was of Banu Umayyah, and Banu Umayyah and Banu Hashim, the Prophet's ﷺ own clan, were the two great rival houses of Quraysh, descended from brothers. After Ali, who was Banu Hashim himself, Uthman was among the closest in lineage to the Prophet ﷺ of all the early companions. And unlike Umar, he still had powerful relatives alive in Makkah who could stand for him. He had no baggage and he had protection. He was the right choice, and the strong thing Umar did was to see it and step aside. To call this a flinch, the Sheikh says, is to misunderstand the entire life of the man.

Into the city, under a cousin's protection

Uthman, radiyallahu anhu, rode toward Makkah and entered by the road called Baldah, where the Quraysh had men posted to block every approach to the city. They stopped him, they mocked him, and they told him to turn back: there would be no negotiation, no hope of his entering at all.
