He was on the point of returning when, in that very group, his own cousin recognized him. The man was Aban ibn Said, an older and well respected figure, and their fathers had been brothers, so Uthman was his first cousin. Whether his heart softened at the sight of how his kinsman was being treated, or whether old respect simply surfaced, Aban climbed down from his horse and put Uthman up on his own saddle and declared him under his protection. You will enter, he said, and you will meet whomever you wish to meet, and I am your guarantor.

This was the visa of that world. In the Arabia of the time, you did not enter a hostile city on your own; you entered under the aman, the guarantee, of a man who would vouch for you and answer for your safety. So Uthman rode into Makkah on his cousin's own mount, an honor in itself, and Aban personally took him to the leaders of Quraysh. He met Abu Sufyan, who was his own father's cousin, and he met the other chiefs, and every one of them refused him the same thing in the same words: the Muslims would not enter this year, for the Arabs must never be allowed to say that Muhammad ﷺ had forced his way in over them.

The Prophet ﷺ had charged Uthman with a second errand as well, and it is one we should not pass over. If he managed to get inside, he was to seek out the believers still trapped in Makkah, the men and women who had accepted Islam but had no means to escape the persecution, and tell them that Allah was aware of their situation and would soon make a way out for them. Uthman found them. Some of them, the Sheikh notes, were still in chains. There was a whole group of Muslims who had never been able to make hijrah to Madinah, not because they did not long to, but because they were being tortured and held and had no way to flee. And Uthman, in the middle of his own dangerous mission, carried them the Prophet's ﷺ promise: Allah knows, and relief is coming soon.

The rumor that changed everything

The meetings dragged. A thing that should have taken an hour or two stretched across most of a day, leader after leader, each one perhaps taking half an hour or forty five minutes, until the whole afternoon was gone. We do not know the exact timings, and the Sheikh admits frankly that this is one of the frustrations of the seerah, that the early reports so rarely tell us how long things took. But realistically it ran the better part of a full day, and from the camp at Hudaybiyyah it looked like nothing so much as a sealed black box. Uthman had walked in, and not one word had come back out.

And this, Dr. Yasir Qadhi says, is simply how human beings work. As the hours stretched far beyond anything anyone had expected, the silence began to breed. Somebody wondered aloud, what if they have killed him. Somebody else took that thought and hardened it, most likely they have killed him. As it grew later still, the small thing gathered weight, rolling and gathering steam the way a rumor does in every society in every age, until it had become almost a solid fact: surely, if he were alive, he would have come back by now. They were not going to keep so noble a man as a prisoner, and they were not going to simply send him home, so he must be dead. The dread settled over the whole camp, and the people quietly made up their minds that their envoy had been killed.

When that conviction reached the Prophet ﷺ, and it seemed the Muslims had truly accepted it, he did not let it pass. This was too much, he said: you do not send an emissary, one of their own kin, on an honorable mission, and have him killed, and then simply turn around and go home. We will not leave, he said, until we have settled this with them. And a crier went out through the camp with the call that would define the day: Jibril had come down to the Prophet ﷺ, and he was summoning all of them to give him their pledge.

The pledge of Ridwan, under the tree

Sit for a moment with the circumstance before the oath is given, because the Sheikh insists that we feel it. Fourteen hundred men, worn out from the long road north, low on water, their riding animals exhausted. They had set out for a peaceful pilgrimage, so even if some of them carried a sword or some light arms on the caravan, this was in no sense an army equipped for war; there had been no mustering, no donning of armor, none of the preparation a real battle demands. And facing them, fresh in their own city, were the Quraysh, at least three times their number, armored, fed, with unlimited supplies and every advantage of ground. To pledge to fight now was to pledge, with open eyes, to almost certain death. You are outnumbered, outmaneuvered, outflanked on every side.

And so, one by one, the companions came to the Prophet ﷺ as he sat with his back against a tree, and each man took his ﷺ hand in both of his and swore: that he would not turn back, that he would not flee the field, that he would fight, if it came to it, until death. To stand and die rather than run. This is the oath our tradition calls Bay'at ar-Ridwan, the pledge of Allah's good pleasure, and it carries that name because of the verse Allah revealed about it, the verse the Sheikh draws our eyes straight to:

Certainly was Allah pleased with the believers when they pledged allegiance to you, [O Muhammad], under the tree, and He knew what was in their hearts, so He sent down tranquility upon them and rewarded them with an imminent conquestQur'an 48:18

Read the middle of it slowly. He knew what was in their hearts. This is Allah Himself testifying that not one heart among those fourteen hundred was hiding a coward, that every man there was inwardly ready to do exactly what he swore. The tree itself is named in the Qur'an; Allah ties His pleasure to a real place and a real afternoon. Only one man held back, a hypocrite who, when the crier called, crouched down behind his camel in its shade so that no one would see him, and Allah exposed him for it. Everyone else gave his hand.

And here is the detail that ought to stop you where you stand. Uthman was not there. He was still inside Makkah, still presumed dead, the very man whose absence had triggered the whole oath. So when the companions had finished, the Prophet ﷺ took his own left hand, said this is the hand of Uthman, and placed it into his right, and gave the pledge on Uthman's behalf. As if to say: if Uthman were here, this is what he would do, so let me stand in his place and do it for him. The hand of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ standing in for the hand of an absent man. The Sheikh lingers here, and we should too: after this, how could any tongue ever utter a word against Uthman, when to insult his hand in that pledge is to insult the very hand of the Prophet ﷺ that stood for it?

Whose hand was over their hands

What did the people of the tree earn for what they did? The Prophet ﷺ told them on the spot: you are the best people on the earth right now, none on the face of the earth better than you. And later, after the return to Madinah, in a hadith recorded in Sahih Muslim, he said that no one who gave the pledge beneath that tree would ever enter the Fire of Hell. As a single body, the Sheikh explains, the people of Hudaybiyyah stand second only to the people of Badr in all the ranks the Prophet ﷺ ever praised; above them are only individuals, the ten given glad tidings of Paradise by name, but as a group there is no group higher than Badr, and then this. And the Qur'an honors the moment in a way that should make the skin prickle:

Indeed, those who pledge allegiance to you, [O Muhammad] they are actually pledging allegiance to Allah. The hand of Allah is over their hands. So he who breaks his word only breaks it to the detriment of himself. And he who fulfills that which he has promised Allah He will give him a great reward.Qur'an 48:10

They had thought they were pledging to a man sitting under a tree. Allah tells them they were pledging to Him. It was not merely that their hands rested in his ﷺ; Allah declares that His own hand, in the manner that befits His majesty, was above theirs. What an honor, the Sheikh says, that should move us to a kind of righteous envy: that these companions gave their oath directly into the hand of the Messenger ﷺ, and over that, the hand of Allah.

This is why, in the way of Ahl as-Sunnah, the people of Hudaybiyyah are untouchable, and why Uthman in particular is beyond the reach of slander. There is a narration the Sheikh relates from al-Bukhari that draws it all together. A Khariji once came to Abdullah ibn Umar, radiyallahu anhu, hunting for faults in Uthman, with the harshness whose seed reaches back to that earliest sect that first reviled and then killed him. He fired his three accusations. Did Uthman not flee at Uhud? Was he not absent from Badr? Was he not missing from the pledge of Ridwan? To each, Ibn Umar answered, by Allah. Yes, Uthman withdrew at Uhud, but Allah testified in the Qur'an that He has forgiven them, so I testify that he is forgiven. Yes, he was absent from Badr, but the Prophet ﷺ himself had commanded him to remain in Madinah to nurse his sick wife, the Prophet's ﷺ own daughter, who lay dying, and he still gave him a full share of the spoils, so he counts among the people of Badr. And as for Ridwan, you fool, the entire pledge only happened because of Uthman, and the Prophet's ﷺ own hand stood in for his. Now, said Ibn Umar, go back to your people and tell them that.

Suhayl comes, and Allah makes it easy

News of the pledge reached Makkah, and the effect was not what the Quraysh might have wished. According to one report, when they heard that this small, tired, lightly armed band had bound themselves by oath to fight to the death, they trembled in their own houses. A handful of believers with iman could make the mightiest tribe in Arabia afraid; that is what the pledge did. So they sent Uthman back unharmed and moved to negotiate in earnest before the Muslims could act. When the camp learned he was returning, some of them said, how fortunate Uthman is, he must have entered Makkah and surely performed tawaf while he was there. But the Prophet ﷺ said, I do not think he would do tawaf while we are kept out here. And so it was: Uthman answered the suggestion with shock, that he would never circle the House while the Messenger of Allah ﷺ stood barred outside it, that had they kept him in Makkah a full year he would not have done tawaf until the Prophet ﷺ did it first. The Sheikh pauses on the longing buried in that exchange, on how deeply these exiles ached for the House they had been driven from, that they could even envy a man for a circuit they only imagined he had made.

The Quraysh first sent a man whom the Prophet ﷺ, seeing him approach, named as a treacherous sort, and the talks with him went nowhere; he came with conditions no one could accept and no real authority to settle anything. And then, from the distance, the final delegation appeared, and at its head walked the man who would actually break the deadlock: Suhayl ibn Amr. Suhayl was no mere messenger but a member of the inner council of Quraysh, one of their great orators and statesmen, sent precisely because he knew what to say and how to say it. And when the Prophet ﷺ saw him, he drew a good omen straight from his name. Suhayl comes from the root sahula, which means to make a thing easy. Now, he said, your matter has been made easy for you.

The Sheikh stops here to teach us what a good omen actually is in Islam, because it is widely misunderstood. The Prophet ﷺ rejected superstition outright. There is no such thing as a black cat that brings misfortune, no broken glass, no unlucky number, no ill fate hiding in an object; to believe that such things can harm you, he taught, is a kind of shirk. But he loved al-faal, the good omen, which is nothing other than optimism in Allah, and he set two conditions on it: that the sign be positive, and that it be tied back to Allah. There is no negative counterpart; if you make du'a and then hear thunder, that is not a sign your du'a was refused, for it does not work in the other direction. But to make du'a on a grey and clouded day, and then to see the sun break through, and to take heart that your hardship will lift as those clouds are lifting, that is faal, and it is good, because a believer ought already to be hopeful in his Lord, and anything that lifts that hope is welcome. The Prophet ﷺ heard the name Suhayl, read ease into it, and ease is exactly what came: with his arrival the long impasse finally broke.

The treaty, word by word

The Prophet ﷺ called for Ali ibn Abi Talib, radiyallahu anhu, to write. And notice, the Sheikh says, that the Prophet ﷺ negotiated as a leader and not as a meek man begging for terms. He dictated first; he set the language; every clause began on his tongue. He started: write, in the name of Allah, ar-Rahman, ar-Rahim. Suhayl objected at once: I do not know this Rahman; write as we are accustomed, in Your name, O Allah. And the Prophet ﷺ conceded it. Then he dictated: this is what Muhammad, the Messenger of Allah, has agreed upon with Suhayl ibn Amr. Suhayl objected again: had we accepted that you were the Messenger of Allah, we would never have fought you in the first place; write your own name and your father's, Muhammad ibn Abdullah.

Ali had already written the words the Messenger of Allah, and he could not bring himself to scrape them out with his own hand. Who is this man, his anger rose, that I should erase rasul Allah from this page? So the Prophet ﷺ asked him to point to the very spot, and in those days a document was leather and there was no erasing ink, only a blade or a thumb to rub the writing away, and the Prophet ﷺ took it himself and with his own hand scraped the words off, and then told Ali, gently, write Muhammad ibn Abdullah.

Look closely at what just happened, the Sheikh says, because there are two kinds of love in this one moment. Ali honored the Prophet ﷺ precisely by refusing to strike out his title; that refusal was itself a form of reverence. And the Prophet ﷺ honored Ali in turn by not forcing him, by understanding his dilemma and reaching for the document himself rather than commanding a young man to do what his heart could not. Neither of these was a concession of faith. Denying him the title on a sheet of leather changed nothing of the reality of who he was. These were concessions of pride, and the Prophet ﷺ had already told his companions plainly, so they would understand exactly what was and was not at stake: by Allah, the Quraysh will not ask me for any condition that honors the sacred ties of Allah except that I will grant it to them, so long as there is no bloodshed in the sacred precinct and no fighting in the sacred months. He was giving up the appearance of victory to hold on to the substance of peace.

The condition, and the chains

Then came the term that cut. The main thing Suhayl had been sent to secure was simple: the Muslims would not enter Makkah this year. The Prophet ﷺ pressed, and Suhayl conceded a single point in return, that they could come the following year, but on Quraysh's own terms. And then Suhayl added a condition that was, for him, as personal as it was political: that no man who defected from Quraysh to the Muslims, even one who had become a Muslim, would be allowed to stay; he must be sent back. But any man who left the Muslims for Quraysh would not be returned. The companions erupted. How could they hand back a believer who had fled to them, who had chosen them and chosen faith? Glory be to Allah, they said, what an unjust condition. Ali's pen stopped. The commotion swelled and the minutes dragged. This was a test, and Allah willed that it would not arrive as an abstraction but with a face, and a voice, and a sound.

For Suhayl had two sons who had broken his heart over Islam. The elder, Abdullah, had been a secret convert; when the army of Quraysh marched out to Badr he walked in its very ranks under their noses, and at the first camp, when the two sides could see one another, he slipped across to the Muslims and fought against his own people, leaving his father to bear that shame as a prisoner of war while his son stood among the victors. The younger son was Abu Jandal. When Abdullah fled to Madinah, Abu Jandal's own faith became plain, and Suhayl was determined not to lose a second son. So he had locked him away and had him tortured, chained in a dungeon, deprived of food and water, commanded to worship the idols: I have lost one son, he said, and I will not lose you until you return to our ways. That had gone on since Badr, four and a half years, and Abu Jandal had not broken, and Suhayl had not relented.

And at that very moment, with the ink of this clause still wet, Abu Jandal had finally found a way out of his chains, no horse and no provisions and no road to anywhere, only the desperate hope of reaching the Muslims he had heard were camped outside the city. He came stumbling toward the camp, the fetters still on his ankles, crying out, O Muslims, not knowing that his own father sat among them, not knowing that the very condition being argued over was about him. They heard the clanking of his chains before they saw his face. And Suhayl turned to the Prophet ﷺ and said: this is the first one you will hand back to me, by our agreement.

There the day ends, on the cruelest sound in the entire seerah, the dragging of a tortured believer's chains and a father pointing at his own son to be returned to the dungeon. The harder verses of this trial are still to come; this is only where the Sheikh sets it down for the week.

And yet, woven invisibly through all of it, was a promise the companions could not yet see. This very year, the year that read to them like surrender and ended on the clinking of fetters, Allah would call by a name they would have wept to hear: a clear victory. And He tied to it a reward they could not begin to imagine on that bitter afternoon:

And much war booty which they will take. And ever is Allah Exalted in Might and Wise.Qur'an 48:19

The spoils were over the horizon, at Khaybar, the greatest material victory in the history of Islam to that point, just a few short chapters of their lives away. The Sheikh draws the line he has drawn from the beginning of this seerah: when you stand up for Allah and put your trust in Him, He returns to you far more than you gave up, even in this world before the next. At Badr they were promised one of two good things and given both. Here they were handed a treaty that looked like defeat and promised a conquest with treasure to follow. But that afternoon, none of them could see it. They only heard the chains.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this day as a story of heroism rewarded, the brave companions under the tree, the noble envoy, the wise Prophet ﷺ, all of it safely resolved long ago. That would be to miss the question it puts to us, because this is the day the seerah teaches you what trusting Allah actually costs.

Start with Umar, because the day begins with him. He was offered the spotlight and he handed it away. The strong thing, the truly brave thing, was not to insist on going and making history; it was to see that his presence would sink the mission and to point at Uthman and disappear. We are quick to read every opportunity as ours and slow to ask whether our being in the room actually serves the thing we claim to care about. Umar set his own ego down for the sake of the Muslims, and the Sheikh holds him up for exactly that. Ask, the next time you reach for a role, whether you want it for the work or for yourself, and whether the work might be better served by your stepping back.

Then there is the heart of the pledge, the line that should be carried out of this whole episode: He knew what was in their hearts. The people of the tree pledged expecting to die, and they walked away from that afternoon having won nothing visible at all, no battle, no entry, no umrah, only a treaty that stung. And Allah called them the best people on the earth and barred the Fire from them and declared His pleasure with them, for the willingness in their hearts, recorded by Him before the result was ever known. Hold onto that when your own efforts come to nothing the world can see. Sometimes the reward is for the readiness, weighed by the One who knows your heart, long before, and quite apart from, the outcome.

And then there is the treaty itself, and Abu Jandal's chains, which is where the day refuses to comfort us. Look at what these companions were asked to accept: their worship turned away, their dignity bargained down on a sheet of leather, a tortured brother in faith reaching them only to be handed back. Nothing about it felt like victory. The Prophet ﷺ read ease into Suhayl's name on a day that brought, to every eye in that camp, the opposite of ease, and he was right, and they could not yet see how. This is what faal really is, and what tawakkul really is: optimism tied to Allah on the very day the evidence points the other way. Be hopeful, and tie that hope to Him, for there is no bad omen in a name or a number or a clouded sky, only the certainty that the One arranging your affair is arranging it in mercy even when it reads as loss.

So take this day down into your own narrow places. When your plans come out like Hudaybiyyah, when the door is shut and the terms are unfair and you cannot find the victory anywhere in it, give your hand the way they gave theirs under the tree, and sign what looks like surrender, and wait for the horizon to prove your Lord right. He who knew what was in their hearts knows what is in yours. May Allah make us of those whose hearts He knows and is pleased with, send down His tranquility upon us when the outcome frightens us, and let us trust the ending He has written when all we can hear, for now, is the clinking of the chains. And may He send His peace upon His Messenger Muhammad ﷺ, whose hand was the hand of an absent man, and over whose hand was the hand of Allah.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 65: the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, part 3 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (48:18, 48:10, 48:19), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing has been followed.

The Seerah · 66 of 104

The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, part 4

The defeat that Allah called a clear victory

They had marched for ten days. They had camped outside Makkah for the better part of three weeks. They had come in white, unarmed, carrying nothing but sacrificial animals and a longing for the House that some of them had not seen in six years. And on this day, the sixty-sixth of the seerah, they would put their names to a treaty that landed on every one of them like a slap across the face, then turn their camels around and ride home without once touching the Kabah.

This is the hardest day in the whole journey so far, and the reason is not the lost pilgrimage and not the wounded pride. It is one boy. It is the day a believer in chains broke out of a Makkan dungeon, dragged himself bleeding into the Muslim camp begging to be saved, and was sent back. Dr. Yasir Qadhi pauses the entire narrative over what the Prophet ﷺ did in that moment, because he says he has found nothing else like it in the whole of the seerah. To understand why the Companions nearly could not obey, and why Allah named the whole bitter affair a clear victory before they had even reached Madinah, you have to stand in the dust at Hudaybiyyah and watch it happen.

The boy in the chains

The terms were still being argued. Nothing had yet been written. Suhayl ibn Amr, the Quraysh negotiator, had forced through the cruelest clause of all: any man who fled Makkah for Madinah, even one who came as a Muslim, would be handed straight back, while any Muslim who abandoned the faith and ran the other way, back to Makkah, would never be returned. A one way street, and everyone could see it. A number of the Companions began objecting out loud while the clause was still being spoken, which was not even their place, because a negotiation between the Prophet ﷺ and Suhayl was no occasion for the ranks to interject. The very fact that they could not hold their tongues tells you how deep this cut. Every muhajir in that camp knew the clause was aimed at people exactly like himself. Each of them had once fled their family, their tribe, their torturers, and found the believers waiting to embrace them in Madinah. Had this clause existed six years earlier, there would be no Islam in Madinah at all. The steady trickle of converts escaping the city would be choked off completely, and the surge that built the umma would never have come. They felt it, and they said so.

And then, into that exact five minutes of back and forth, came Abu Jandal. He was Suhayl's own younger son. While his father sat negotiating, this boy had been kept in a Makkan dungeon and tortured for two or three years, and one son already lost to Madinah was the reason Suhayl wanted this clause so badly. Somewhere in those years of darkness Abu Jandal had surely worked out how to slip his chains, but escape to where? Out into the streets of a city that hunted men like him, with no mount, no money, no road to the north? It had been hopeless. Then word reached him that the Muslims were camped just outside the walls, and he understood that the one chance of his entire life had arrived. He broke loose and dragged himself the last distance to Hudaybiyyah with the chains still locked on his wrists, the lash marks open and bleeding across an exposed and emaciated chest, and he threw himself among the believers crying out: O Muslims, save me. O Muslims, rescue me. Across the camp his father saw him and knew him at once, and turned to the Prophet ﷺ: this one, Muhammad, is the very first you return to me under our terms.

Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi stops you, because of what the Prophet ﷺ did next. He did not simply explain that the treaty bound him. He pleaded. Give him to me as a gift, he asked Suhayl, make this one an exception and we will write the clause in after. Suhayl refused. He asked again. And again. Three times, four times, the Messenger of Allah ﷺ begging a pagan for the body of a single tortured young man, and the Sheikh says plainly that nowhere else in all his reading of the seerah has he found the Prophet ﷺ pleading like this for anything. Suhayl would not yield an inch: this is the condition, take it or there is no treaty, and if you refuse me on my own son I will never agree to anything with you. And Abu Jandal, watching his father on the one side and the Prophet ﷺ on the other and grasping at last which way it was going, cried out the question that has echoed down fourteen centuries: will you return me to the idolaters when I have come to you as a Muslim? Do you not see what they have done to me? Do you not see what they have done to me?

Be patient, Abu Jandal

The Prophet ﷺ could not save him. So he gave him the only thing left to give. He turned to the boy directly and said: be patient, Abu Jandal, and seek your reward with Allah, for Allah will surely make for you a way out, and an exit, from this. Trust your Lord, and He will free you. It was not rescue. It was a promise of rescue, handed to a boy in chains by the one man on earth he had crossed a city to reach.

Then Umar ibn al-Khattab, radiyallahu anhu, could bear it no longer. He walked over to Abu Jandal, repeated the Prophet's ﷺ own words, be patient, and then added something far more militant. The blood of these pagans, he told the boy, is worth nothing at all, and as he said it he let his eyes drop to the sword at his own side, a silent invitation: here it is, take it, use it. But the men in front of Abu Jandal were his own father and Suhayl's companion, and to lift that sword was to strike his own father down, and in the end, for the simplest and most human of reasons, the boy could not do it. The most the camp could win for him was a single concession wrung from one of the Quraysh delegates: that he would no longer be tortured as he had been. And so Abu Jandal was put back in his chains and led away toward Makkah, and the whole camp watched him go.

The wound never fully closed. Years later, on the day of Siffin, when two Muslim armies stood on the very edge of slaughtering one another, an old and pious Companion tried to pull them back from the brink by reaching all the way back to this morning. Accuse your own opinions before you accuse the Qur'an and the Sunnah, he told them, blame your own understanding first, for I remember myself on the day of Abu Jandal, and had I been able to reject the command of the Prophet ﷺ that day, I would have done it. Notice, Dr. Yasir Qadhi says, that he does not even call it the day of Hudaybiyyah. He calls it the day of Abu Jandal. Of everything that happened on this long and crushing day, that is the scene that branded itself into a Companion's memory for the rest of his life: a tortured believer thrown back to his torturers before their eyes, and themselves unable to stop it.

The terms nobody wanted

Once the clause stood, the rest of the document was set down. There would be peace for ten years, and the phrase they used was that there would be no armor and no swords between the two sides, which in their language simply meant no fighting at all. Both sides were now free to enter into alliance with any other tribe in Arabia they wished, and whatever bound the two parties would bind their allies too. As for the pilgrimage the Muslims had marched all this way to perform: not this year. Let no Arab ever say the Muslims had forced their way into Makkah by strength. They would turn back now, and next year the Quraysh would empty the city for three days and the Muslims could come, perform their rites, and leave. Ali ibn Abi Talib, radiyallahu anhu, wrote the document out, and witnesses signed for both sides, Abu Bakr and Umar and others for the Muslims, Suhayl and his delegation for the Quraysh.

And here Dr. Yasir Qadhi asks you to lift your eyes off the humiliation for a moment and see the thing the Companions, drowning in their pain, could not yet see. For the first time in the entire story, the Muslims were being treated as an equal. You do not sit down to write a treaty except with a power you recognize, and here were the Quraysh, the masters of Arabia, forced to the same table to negotiate as equals with the very religion they had spent two decades trying to strangle in its cradle. After Badr, after Uhud, after the trench, the Muslims had been upgraded, and this treaty was the proof of it. But notice, the Sheikh says, what is happening underneath the words. On one side of that table sits a faith, the umma, and on the other a tribe, a bloodline, the Quraysh and whoever allied with them. In Islam the thing that binds you to your brother is the religion itself, stronger than kinship, stronger than nation, stronger than tribe; in every other society, then and now, something always trumps the faith, and for the Quraysh it was the tribe. With this one treaty the whole of Arabia was quietly being sorted into two, Islam on one side and everything else on the other, and the Arabs would soon have to choose which side of the line they stood on. This was the precursor to the conquest of Makkah and to the day, only a few years off, when idol worship would be wiped from the face of the Arabian Peninsula entirely. They had wept over a defeat. They had signed the opening line of their victory.

Are you not the Messenger of Allah?

The anger in the camp had to go somewhere, and it went where the deepest love so often hides. Umar came to the Prophet ﷺ and fired off a series of questions to which he already knew every answer. Are you not the Messenger of Allah? Yes. Are we not upon the truth, and our enemies upon falsehood? Yes. Then why, Umar pressed, do we give up the lower hand in our religion and let them trample over us like this? And the Prophet ﷺ answered with the only answer there was: I am the Messenger of Allah, and I will not disobey Him, and He will help me. I do not know where this is leading, the answer means underneath; I know only that my Lord commanded it, and He will not abandon me. Against a reply like that there is no argument, and Umar knew it, so he switched lines entirely. The vision, he said, the dream you told us in Madinah, that we would make tawaf around the House. And the Prophet ﷺ asked: did I ever tell you it would be this year? No, Umar admitted. Then you will go, he said, and you will make tawaf, only not this year. The vision was true; the timing had simply never been promised.

Then Umar went and put the very same questions to Abu Bakr, radiyallahu anhu, and here is the marvel the Sheikh wants you to catch. Abu Bakr had not heard a single word of the Prophet's ﷺ replies, and yet he gave Umar the same answers, point for point, almost word for word, and then added something sharper than the Prophet ﷺ himself had said: man, hold fast to the stirrup of his saddle, for by Allah he is the Messenger of Allah and he will not disobey his Lord, and there is no salvation for you anywhere else. Dr. Yasir Qadhi lingers here on a side of Abu Bakr we are forever forgetting. We picture him soft and weeping and gentle, and he was all of that, but when the moment called for a man to be a rock, no one in the umma was firmer, not even Umar. This is his rank: the one human being with the standing to put Umar in his place, the same way he would steady the whole shaking umma on the day the Prophet ﷺ died, when Umar lost himself in grief and it was Abu Bakr, and only Abu Bakr, who could cut him back to his senses. Nobody else on earth had that audacity.

And the Sheikh is careful and clear about Umar, because there are those who have tried to wield this day against him. Umar's anger was never about a bruised ego or wounded tribal pride. It was rage on behalf of the religion he loved, the fury of a man who could not stand to watch Islam, as he understood it, humiliated and trampled. That is a misjudgment born of love, not a sin of arrogance, and there is a world of difference. It is the error of the man who loses his camel in the desert, finds it again, and in his wild joy blurts out something close to blasphemy, and is forgiven, because emotion overran him. Umar overstepped, and he knew it, and he spent the rest of his life making it up, freeing slaves, fasting, praying through the nights, heaping good deed upon good deed in the hope of forgiveness for that one day. Real repentance, the Sheikh notes, is exactly this: not words alone but a change in what the hands do. Neither the Prophet ﷺ nor Abu Bakr ever held the outburst against him; they understood it for exactly what it was, the misfire of a heart that loved too fiercely. And there is a quiet permission buried here too, that to argue a point respectfully with the one in charge, to bring another view without contempt, is something the religion allows, for Umar did it to the Prophet ﷺ himself, three and four ways, and was corrected only on where it had carried him, never on the arguing itself.

Stand up and shave

It was over. The Prophet ﷺ told them to stand, slaughter their sacrificial animals where they were, shave their heads, and turn for home, the pilgrimage abandoned at the threshold. And then came the thing that Dr. Yasir Qadhi says, again, he has found nowhere else in the entire seerah. The Prophet ﷺ gave a direct command, and not one Companion moved. He said it a second time. No one stirred. A third time, and still the whole camp sat frozen, too crushed to lift a hand, every man waiting on the man beside him, the dead weight of a heartbroken crowd. He went back into his tent, and his wife Umm Salamah, radiyallahu anha, saw the agitation plain on his face, and when he told her that he had commanded his people three times and not one had answered, she gave him the wisdom of a single moment. Say nothing more to them, O Messenger of Allah. Go out, call your barber, and shave your own head where they can see. When they watch you do it, they will follow. He took her counsel, walked out, called for his barber, and put the blade to his own hair in full view of the camp, and the instant they saw it the spell broke and they fell over one another to do the same. He made dua three times for those who shaved their heads clean and only once for those who merely trimmed, and from that the scholars drew the ruling we still carry, that shaving is the more rewarded of the two.

Two new things were born on this day that became the permanent law of every Muslim after. The first was what a pilgrim does when he sets out for the sacred House and is then prevented from reaching it. Wherever you are stopped, by a closed road, a plague, a war, a crisis of any kind, you slaughter what you brought, you shave, you come out of the state of ihram right there, and you make the pilgrimage up later. The Companions had never once seen this done, for the simple reason that it had never needed doing, and across the whole life of the Prophet ﷺ this is the only time he set out for the House and did not reach it. Down all the centuries since, whenever pilgrims have been turned back by politics or plague or poverty, this is the path they have walked, and it was paved at Hudaybiyyah.

The second new thing came when the believing women began to emigrate. To send a man back to be tortured was hard enough, but what was to be done when a woman fled to Madinah in faith? The Prophet ﷺ did not know, and Allah revealed the answer:

O you who have believed, when the believing women come to you as emigrants, examine [i.e., test] them. Allah is most knowing as to their faith. And if you know them to be believers, then do not return them to the disbelievers; they are not lawful [wives] for them, nor are they lawful [husbands] for them. But give them [i.e., the disbelievers] what they have spent. And there is no blame upon you if you marry them when you have given them their due compensation [i.e., mahr]. And hold not to marriage bonds with disbelieving women, but ask for what you have spent and let them [i.e., the disbelievers] ask for what they have spent. That is the judgement of Allah; He judges between you. And Allah is Knowing and Wise.Qur'an 60:10

The women were to be tested, and if found true in faith they would not be sent back, their former husbands repaid the dowries they had given, and the women lawful to marry once their waiting period had passed. The reason they could stay turned on the same razor's edge that ran through the whole treaty: the Prophet ﷺ bound himself to the exact letter of what was written, and the letter, the words Suhayl himself had insisted upon, said that no man who came would be kept, and said nothing at all about women. The spirit of the clause surely meant any person, and the Quraysh would have meant it so, but the letter said man, and Allah held them to their letter. Once again a clause the Quraysh wrote to wound the Muslims had a door in it they never noticed, and through that door the believing women walked free.

A clear victory

On the long ride home Umar rode heavy with the weight of what he had said. He brought his camel alongside the Prophet ﷺ and greeted him with salam. No reply. He greeted him a second time. Nothing. A third time, the most a man may repeat it, and silence, and Umar's heart fell through the floor: may my mother lose me, I am finished, my words have ruined me. Then a rider came back along the line: the Messenger of Allah ﷺ is calling for you. Umar went forward braced for the worst thing he had ever heard, and instead he found the Prophet's ﷺ face shining, lit up with a joy that poured off it, and on his tongue a surah that had just come down whole and entire from the heavens, four and a half pages of revelation descending at once, the surah named al-Fath, the Victory, and it opened with the words:

Indeed, We have given you, [O Muhammad], a clear conquest.Qur'an 48:1

Is this a victory? a Companion asked him, the very question that the chains of Abu Jandal had planted in every heart in that camp. Yes, the Prophet ﷺ told him, it is a victory. And Umar, the same Umar who days before could see nothing in any of it but defeat and disgrace, set his camel racing up and down the lines crying out that Allah had granted them a clear victory. The Prophet ﷺ said of this surah that it was more beloved to him than everything the sun rises upon, that nothing on the face of the earth could have gladdened him as it did.

And Dr. Yasir Qadhi draws out a delicate and weighty point that the Companions taught us by the sheer speed of their turnaround, for Umar's reversal was instantaneous, a complete turn the moment the revelation reached his ears. It shows, the Sheikh says, that they had always grasped a difference we must never confuse: the Prophet's ﷺ own human judgment, the decisions he made as a man, which they were permitted to question with respect as Umar had done all day, and the command of Allah, binding the instant it descends, beyond all argument. All through that bitter day they had wondered only whether these terms were the best to be had, whether this was his ﷺ own reading that might have gone another way. They obeyed regardless, because obedience to him ﷺ was never the question; the wondering ended in a heartbeat the moment Allah Himself came down and settled it.

Like a plant that delights the sower

The surah closes by painting a portrait of the Companions themselves, and the Sheikh makes sure you feel exactly why Allah chose this hour to praise them. He likens them to a seed that a farmer plants, that pushes out its tender shoots, and grows, and hardens, and stands at last tall and firm and proud upon its own stalk until it gladdens the very one who sowed it. And the sower in this likeness is the Prophet ﷺ, made glad by what his Companions had become.

Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah; and those with him are forceful against the disbelievers, merciful among themselves. You see them bowing and prostrating [in prayer], seeking bounty from Allah and [His] pleasure. Their sign is in their faces from the effect of prostration [i.e., prayer]. That is their description in the Torah. And their description in the Gospel is as a plant which produces its offshoots and strengthens them so they grow firm and stand upon their stalks, delighting the sowers, so that He [i.e., Allah] may enrage by them the disbelievers. Allah has promised those who believe and do righteous deeds among them forgiveness and a great reward.Qur'an 48:29

It had been a brutal test. They were emotional, they were heartbroken, they came within a breath of being unable to obey. And when it came down to the final thing, they obeyed. The Sheikh asks you, honestly and without flattery, to picture any other community on the face of the earth following its leader through a single day like that one: the long march, then the ihram with no House at the end of it, then the humiliating terms read out one by one, then Abu Jandal thrown back to his torturers before their eyes, and still to stay, still to slaughter and shave and turn for home at his word. Allah looked at the tree He had grown in the harshest of soil, and He praised it in words that would be recited until the end of time.

Then came the proof that the whole bitter treaty had been a victory wearing the mask of a defeat. A believer named Abu Basir fled to Madinah, the first to test the new clause. The Quraysh sent two men after him, and the Prophet ﷺ, holding to the exact letter of his word, handed him over, telling him plainly: you knew the treaty when you came, and I will not be treacherous, so return. On the road back Abu Basir flattered one of his captors into letting him handle his sword, struck him dead with it, and sent the second man fleeing in terror all the way to Madinah. Abu Basir followed and stood before the Prophet ﷺ, who would not formally shelter him but turned away and spoke into the air a line of wonder rather than command: what a warrior he is, if only he had someone to help him. Abu Basir understood the hint perfectly. The Prophet ﷺ would not break the letter of the treaty by keeping him, but neither would he hand him back a second time: the meaning was, get out of my sight before another party comes for you. So he fled to the coast, to a spot on the trade road near the shore below Makkah, and set up camp alone. Then word reached Abu Jandal, that same boy whose chains had broken every heart, and he escaped once more and made his way, not to Madinah which he still could not reach, but to Abu Basir's camp. Two legends of the convert community in one place, and the men who could not reach Madinah began to find their way to them, one and two and ten and twenty, until they were seventy and eighty strong, a free company on the trade road raiding every Quraysh caravan bound for Syria.

And it could not be stopped, because of a knot the Quraysh themselves had tied. Their own clause kept these men from any treaty with the Prophet ﷺ, which meant the ten years of peace did not cover them, which meant they were no one's responsibility and no one's to call off. Within a year and a half the bleeding was so severe that the Quraysh sent a delegation to Madinah and, by the bonds of kinship, begged the Prophet ﷺ to take these men in and end the raids. The very people they had fought tooth and nail to keep out of Madinah, they were now pleading with him to take in. You cannot outwit the best of planners. He sent for them, and they came home to the umma, and the one who led that whole caravan of believers back into Madinah was Abu Jandal himself, the boy in the chains, whose rejection had been the cruelest moment of the day. Perfection, the Sheikh calls it: the very wound became the victory, and the hand that had been turned away led the procession home.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to file Hudaybiyyah away as a clever piece of statecraft that happened to work out, a humiliation that history later vindicated, and to leave it there. That would be to walk past the question it is holding out to each of us. This is the day faith is at its very hardest, and the reason is precise: it is the day faith looked exactly like loss. A believer in chains was sent back. A pilgrimage was abandoned at the doorstep of the House. The Companions sat in the dust too crushed to lift a hand. And every last piece of it, Allah named a clear victory before they had even reached home.

So sit a moment with Abu Jandal, because his iman is the lesson most easily missed. The Sheikh cannot think of a harder test in the whole seerah than to be turned away, for the sake of a political agreement, by the very Prophet ﷺ you believe in. We lose a little money, we scrape a car, some small thing slips through our fingers, and our certainty wobbles. He was handed back to his torturers by the one man he had crossed a city to reach, and he held firm, and out of nowhere Allah's help came from a direction no one could have scripted, and he rode back into Madinah not as a fugitive but as the honored leader of a whole company of believers. Whoever truly puts his trust in Allah, Allah will be enough for him. That is not a slogan; it is the literal arc of this boy's life inside a single year.

And learn the difference between Umar and Abu Bakr, for both loved the same Prophet ﷺ with the same fierce love, and the day pulled something different out of each. Umar's love came out as protest, a heart so pained on Islam's behalf that it overran his manners, and he was forgiven and spent his life mending it. Abu Bakr's came out as stillness: I do not understand this, but my Lord knows and His Messenger ﷺ knows, and I do not, so I will hold fast to the stirrup of his saddle and not let go. When the revelation came down it did not have to convince Abu Bakr of anything; he was already there. That is the quieter, harder faith, the one that can sit inside an unbearable decision without being shown the reason first.

So carry one thing from this day into your own Hudaybiyyah when it comes, the closed door, the unanswered dua, the outcome you would never in a thousand years have chosen. Accuse your own opinion before you accuse the wisdom of your Lord, as that Companion begged two armies to do on the brink of war, for when your feelings and the decree collide, it is your feelings that are mistaken. Be patient as Abu Jandal was told to be patient, knowing that patience is not the surrender of rescue but the trusting of its timing. And when something looks like the worst defeat of your life, remember that the clause built to choke the religion became the camp that brought the Quraysh begging, and what broke their hearts at the threshold of Makkah was the exact shape of the victory that won them the whole peninsula. May Allah grant us the steadiness of Abu Bakr and the patience He promised Abu Jandal, let us blame our own eyes before we ever doubt His wisdom, and show us, as He showed them, that the thing that broke our hearts was the very thing that won the war. And may Allah send His peace and praise upon His Messenger Muhammad ﷺ, who held fast when every heart around him was breaking, and was right.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 66: the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, part 4 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (48:1, 48:29, 60:10), verified via quran.ai.

The Seerah · 67 of 104

The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, part 5: the clear victory

How the defeat was named the clear victory

They were riding home, and their hearts were not with them. The companions had set out for Makkah to make umrah, dressed in the white of pilgrims, the sacrificial animals walking ahead of them garlanded for slaughter, and they had been turned back at the very edge of the sanctuary without setting foot inside it. They had watched the Prophet ﷺ sit across from the envoys of Quraysh and sign a treaty that read, line by line, like surrender. Come back next year, not this one. Hand back to us any man of ours who flees to you, but we will keep any of yours who comes to us. And erase from the top of the page the words Messenger of Allah. Even Umar ibn al-Khattab, radiyallahu anhu, had come close to losing himself over it, and would carry the memory of his own conduct that day as a private terror for the rest of his life. So the army rode north in silence, certain they had been handed a humiliation. And then, somewhere on that open road between Makkah and Madinah, the sky opened.

A whole surah came down at once. Dr. Yasir Qadhi asks us to stop and feel how rare that was. The Qur'an, as a rule, did not descend in finished pieces; it came an ayah here, a passage there, threaded into events over months and years. Surah al-Baqarah took something close to a decade to complete. Surah al-Fath was the exception. It came down whole, from its first word to its last, in a single descent, and it came down neither in Makkah nor yet in Madinah but in the saddle, on the way back, while the grief was still fresh in the men who carried it. It is counted among the Madani revelations because it came after the hijrah, though no Madani ground was beneath its feet when it arrived. And its very first word took everything these men had just lived through and turned it inside out.

A surah that named the defeat a victory

Indeed, We have given you, [O Muhammad], a clear conquestQur'an 48:1

Not a setback. Not a loss. A conquest, an opening, a clear and manifest victory. The word Allah chose was fath, and He set it at the head of the surah like a verdict handed down before the appeal had even been filed. These men felt they had been defeated. Allah told them, before they had reached their own doorsteps, that what they had actually witnessed was a triumph.

Then He explained why. The surah goes on to say that this was given so that Allah may forgive what came before of the Prophet's ﷺ fault and what will follow, and complete His favor upon him, and guide him along a straight path. The Sheikh pauses on a question that should puzzle us. What does a peace treaty have to do with the forgiveness of sins? The answer he gives is one of the gentlest in the whole seerah. The grief itself was the gift. The anguish the Prophet ﷺ carried at Hudaybiyyah, the sting of being turned from the House he loved more than any place on earth, was not Allah pressing him down. It was Allah lifting him higher. Every honor laid on him and every hardship asked of him were the same hand raising him in rank and wiping away what could be wiped away. A few verses on, Allah names the harvest of it all:

[And] that He may admit the believing men and the believing women to gardens beneath which rivers flow to abide therein eternally and remove from them their misdeeds - and ever is that, in the sight of Allah, a great attainment.Qur'an 48:5

So the treaty that read like a surrender was, in the ledger of heaven, a path to forgiveness for the Prophet ﷺ and to Paradise for those who stood with him. The men riding home in shame were being told that they had just walked through one of the great openings of their lives, and had simply not had eyes to see it yet.

He knew what was in their hearts

Out at Hudaybiyyah, when the rumor had spread that Quraysh had killed the Prophet's ﷺ envoy, he ﷺ had called the believers under a tree and they had put their hands in his hand, one after another, swearing to stand with him to the death. It was the bayah that history would call the Pledge of the Tree, the Bayah al-Ridwan, the pledge of the good pleasure of Allah. Now, on the road home, Allah reached back to that moment and told them what their hands had truly touched.

Indeed, those who pledge allegiance to you, [O Muhammad] - they are actually pledging allegiance to Allah. The hand of Allah is over their hands. So he who breaks his word only breaks it to the detriment of himself. And he who fulfills that which he has promised Allah - He will give him a great reward.Qur'an 48:10

They thought they had given their word to a man. Allah told them they had given it to Him, that His hand had been over theirs as they swore. No small comfort to men forced to fold up their swords and ride away from a fight they were willing to die for: their loyalty had not been spent on a lost cause; it had been received, directly, by the One whose causes are never lost.

That same surah then draws a hard line down the middle of the ummah, and the contrast is one of the quiet miracles of the whole revelation. On one side stood the believers who had marched out for Hudaybiyyah and put their hands in the Prophet's ﷺ hand. On the other stood the Bedouin who had stayed behind in the safety of their tents, calculating that the Muslims were walking to their deaths and wanting no part of it. Allah told the Prophet ﷺ exactly what these men would say to his face when he reached Madinah, and He told him before they said it.

Those who remained behind of the bedouins will say to you, "Our properties and our families occupied us, so ask forgiveness for us." They say with their tongues what is not within their hearts. Say, "Then who could prevent Allah at all if He intended for you harm or intended for you benefit? Rather, ever is Allah, of what you do, Aware."Qur'an 48:11

The excuse had not yet been uttered. No one had stood before the Prophet ﷺ and offered it. And here was the Qur'an reciting it back, word for word, before the man who would say it had even opened his mouth. How, the Sheikh asks, could it have been known, unless from the One who knows what a heart holds before the tongue dresses it up? And Allah went further still, past the excuse to the thing underneath it:

But you thought that the Messenger and the believers would never return to their families, ever, and that was made pleasing in your hearts. And you assumed an assumption of evil and became a people ruined."Qur'an 48:12

There it was, exposed. They had not stayed home for their property and their families. They had stayed home because they believed the Prophet ﷺ and his companions were going to their slaughter, and some part of them had found that belief comfortable. Two hearts, the same event, weighed perfectly by the One who sees inside them both, and it was the believers under the tree whom He turned to address:

Certainly was Allah pleased with the believers when they pledged allegiance to you, [O Muhammad], under the tree, and He knew what was in their hearts, so He sent down tranquility upon them and rewarded them with an imminent conquestQur'an 48:18

This is the verse the believers would hold onto for the rest of their days, and that later generations would recite over the names of those present at the tree. Allah was pleased with them. He knew what was in their hearts when they swore, and what was there was true, and so He poured down sakinah, a settling calm, over men who had every reason to be in turmoil, and He promised them a victory close at hand.

What was that nearer victory? The Sheikh points ahead to Khaybar, the great fortified stronghold of the remaining hostile tribes to the north, which would fall within weeks of the return, its spoils richer than anything the Muslims had ever gathered. The peace with Quraysh had freed their hands to deal with it. But the surah spends a verse, too, on a battle that never happened at all.

And if those [Makkans] who disbelieve had fought you, they would have turned their backs [in flight]. Then they would not find a protector or a helper.Qur'an 48:22

Notice what Allah is doing, the Sheikh says. There was no battle at Hudaybiyyah; no swords crossed. And yet Allah describes, with perfect certainty, how a fight that never took place would have ended: the disbelievers turning their backs, finding no one to protect them, the believers left standing though they were fewer. Allah knows the past, the present, the future, and even the shape of a future that will never arrive. It was one more reassurance to men who had walked away from a fight, told now by their Lord that they had never been the ones in danger.

The believers no one could see

And then the surah answers the question that must have burned in every heart riding home. Why hold us back at all? Why not push into Makkah, take the sanctuary by force, finish what they started? The Muslims had been willing; they had pledged their lives under the tree for exactly that. The answer Allah gives is a reason not one man in the camp could have known.

They are the ones who disbelieved and obstructed you from al-Masjid al-Haram while the offering was prevented from reaching its place of sacrifice. And if not for believing men and believing women whom you did not know - that you might trample [i.e., kill] them and there would befall you because of them dishonor without [your] knowledge - [you would have been permitted to enter Makkah]. [This was so] that Allah might admit to His mercy whom He willed. If they had been apart [from them], We would have punished those who disbelieved among them with painful punishmentQur'an 48:25

Inside Makkah, the Sheikh explains, were believing men and believing women, hiding their faith, unable to declare themselves. Had the fighting started, they would have been swept up on the side of Quraysh against their will, their Islam a secret no Muslim sword could read, and the army would have struck down its own brothers and sisters without ever knowing whose blood was on the blade. They would have entered Makkah as conquerors and woken to a grief they could never undo. So Allah held the swords back. The treaty that looked like the Prophet ﷺ being cheated of his rightful pilgrimage was Allah shielding the believers no one could see, and shielding the army from a wound to its own conscience, and giving time for those hidden ones, and others still to come, to be brought into His mercy. The clear victory was already at work, doing its quiet labor, before a single soul in that camp could have named it.

And there was one more thing they could not see, the thing that had started the whole journey. It had all begun with a dream. The Prophet ﷺ had seen himself and his companions entering Makkah in safety, making tawaf around the Kaaba, their heads shaved as pilgrims shave them, all fear gone. That vision was the reason the whole company had set out, and the reason the turning-away cut so deep. Where was the dream Allah had shown him? Had it failed? At the close of the surah, Allah answers.

Certainly has Allah showed to His Messenger the vision [i.e., dream] in truth. You will surely enter al-Masjid al-Haram, if Allah wills, in safety, with your heads shaved and [hair] shortened, not fearing [anyone]. He knew what you did not know and has arranged before that a conquest near [at hand].Qur'an 48:27

The dream was true. Every detail of it would come to pass, exactly as shown: the entering, the safety, the shaved heads, the absence of fear. It had never been cancelled. It had only been scheduled, set for a year not yet arrived, and in the gap between the promise and its keeping Allah was arranging a nearer conquest to carry them toward it. He knew what you did not know. What you cannot see, the Sheikh reminds us, is not the same as what is not there.

The proof was in the numbers

So how, in the end, did a treaty that broke the companions' hearts become the greatest victory of the entire seerah? Through the one thing the Muslims had never once been granted in all their years of struggle: peace. For the first time, Quraysh had sat across a table from the Prophet ﷺ as an equal party, and treaties, the Sheikh observes, are only ever struck between equals; otherwise it is not a treaty but a command from the strong to the weak. The very act of negotiating was Quraysh conceding, perhaps without meaning to, that this was no longer a rebellion to be crushed but a power to be reckoned with. And then peace did what war never could. Believers and idol-worshippers could travel the same roads, trade in the same markets, sit and argue without the shadow of a coming battle. And when people can finally talk, Islam spreads the way it spreads best: not down the edge of a sword, but by persuasion, by a man hearing the truth plainly and finding he cannot refuse it.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi brings forward al-Zuhri here, one of the earliest and greatest scholars of this religion, from whom so much of the seerah itself reaches us. Al-Zuhri said there had never been a victory in Islam before Hudaybiyyah greater than it. His reason was exactly this: that the people were at last at peace, so they mixed and spoke and Islam was mentioned among them, and no person of sound mind heard about it in those two years except that he entered it. The Sheikh sets that alongside the arithmetic of Ibn Hisham, the historian who refined Ibn Ishaq's biography into the standard the world still reads. At Hudaybiyyah the Prophet ﷺ had brought roughly fourteen hundred men. Two years later, when he marched back toward Makkah, he had ten thousand. Islam had been preached for some nineteen years to gather that first fourteen hundred; in the two years of peace that followed the treaty, the numbers more than doubled. Where did the other eight or nine thousand come from? They had converted in the quiet that Hudaybiyyah bought. A treaty had done in two years what nineteen years of struggle and battle had not. That is how a peace can be a larger victory than any war.

And there was one more thing the treaty did, the largest of all: it opened the road straight to the conquest of Makkah. Without Hudaybiyyah, the Sheikh says plainly, there is no conquest of Makkah. It was the very terms of the treaty, and Quraysh's eventual breaking of them, that would later give the Prophet ﷺ the opening to march on the city and take it almost without bloodshed. The humiliation at the edge of the sanctuary was the first step of the walk that ended with the keys of the Kaaba in his hand.

Hate a thing, and it is good for you

Of everything Hudaybiyyah teaches, this is where the Sheikh lingers longest, because for him this episode was always the most relevant in the entire seerah, the story he chose to teach before he had ever taught the rest of the life. Sometimes things happen, and on the surface they look like nothing but chaos and harm and ruin, and Allah knows what you do not.

Battle has been enjoined upon you while it is hateful to you. But perhaps you hate a thing and it is good for you; and perhaps you love a thing and it is bad for you. And Allah knows, while you know not.Qur'an 2:216

The whole episode is a lesson in tawakkul, in handing your judgment back to the One who can see the road you cannot. The companions could not see it. The treaty looked to them like surrender after surrender. Even the Prophet ﷺ, when Umar pressed him, did not lay out the wisdom of it; he said only that he was the Messenger of Allah and that Allah would not abandon him, without yet being shown how. He hated the grief of it as any man would, and he trusted anyway, because trust is not the absence of grief but the choice to lean on Allah inside it. You may hate a thing, and it is the best thing that ever happened to you, and you will only learn which it was later, when the field has grown.

Beside this sits the lesson the Sheikh states as bluntly as he states anything in the seerah: never, ever assume you know better than the Qur'an and the Sunnah. Umar himself said afterward that the way he had behaved at Hudaybiyyah frightened him for the rest of his life, and he gave charity and fasted and freed slaves to make up for the words he had spoken. And he said the thing that should be carved into every believing heart: that if any man other than the Prophet ﷺ had told him to fold up his sword and hand the Muslims back, he never would have obeyed. He had come that close. But in the end he submitted, because once Allah and His Messenger have spoken with an explicit word, your opinion is no longer an opinion. Accuse your own understanding, the Sheikh says, before you accuse the text. Allah's word is fact; ours, at its very best, is a guess. Hudaybiyyah is what it looks like to trust the One who sees the whole road when you can only make out the next step in the dark.

A treaty even with the worst enemy

Hudaybiyyah left behind a body of law and guidance that scholars have mined ever since, and the Sheikh draws out its boldest ruling first, the one he considers the most relevant of all for Muslims of his own time and place. The Quraysh were not neutral, not pacifists, not some well-meaning tribe that had simply misunderstood. They were the worst enemy this ummah will ever have, and the Sheikh means that precisely: no enemy since has fought the Prophet ﷺ as directly as they did. Every later foe attacked Muslims; only Quraysh attacked the Messenger of Allah himself, face to face. They tried to kill him in his own bed. They drove him from the city he loved. And still, with all of that between them, he ﷺ made peace with them and held to it. If a treaty was permissible with Quraysh, then a treaty is permissible with anyone less, which is everyone.

From this the Sheikh turns to the Muslims of his own land, and he is careful and he is firm. Different parts of the ummah, he says, may rightly hold different agreements; what binds one community is not what binds another, and he speaks only for the people among whom he lives. A believer who carries a passport and a visa has entered into a covenant, and a believer does not betray a covenant, however much injustice he sees, because the believer is never the double-faced man who promises peace and then strikes. So when your heart bleeds, as it should, for the wronged of the ummah, for the prisoner in chains and the bombed and the displaced, remember Abu Jandal, who came stumbling into the camp in his chains, the marks of torture on him, begging the Muslims not to send him back, and whom the Prophet ﷺ, bound by the ink still drying on the treaty, could not keep. That is why Umar lost himself. And remember that there was a wider good Allah was arranging in that very moment that no one in the camp could yet see, a peace that would double the ummah and open Makkah, bought at the price of one man's chains.

The Sheikh is unsparing about the alternative. To act on raw emotion, to lash out, to strike a marketplace and cut down the innocent in the name of grievance, is neither Islam nor even sense. It is forbidden by the plainest reading of the religion and foolish by the plainest reading of the world, because it invites a ruin that falls hardest on the very people it claims to defend, just as the Prophet's ﷺ patience at Hudaybiyyah won what rashness never could. Trust Allah. Keep your covenant. Do the good in your reach and make du'a for the rest. And leave the outcome to the One who took the bitterest humiliation in the story and named it, with His own word, the clearest of victories.

Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah

The surah that opened by calling the treaty a clear conquest closes by drawing a portrait, and there is a final, perfect irony in its first words. At Hudaybiyyah, Quraysh had forced the Prophet ﷺ to strike the title Messenger of Allah from the top of the document, refusing to let it stand. Now Allah wrote it into the eternal, unerasable word, where no pen of Quraysh could ever reach it.

Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah; and those with him are forceful against the disbelievers, merciful among themselves. You see them bowing and prostrating [in prayer], seeking bounty from Allah and [His] pleasure. Their sign is in their faces from the effect of prostration [i.e., prayer]. That is their description in the Torah. And their description in the Gospel is as a plant which produces its offshoots and strengthens them so they grow firm and stand upon their stalks, delighting the sowers - so that He [i.e., Allah] may enrage by them the disbelievers. Allah has promised those who believe and do righteous deeds among them forgiveness and a great reward.Qur'an 48:29

The title they made him erase, written down forever. And around him a community drawn in a single breath: hard against falsehood, tender with one another, bent low in bowing and prostration, the mark of worship on their faces. Then the image the Sheikh finds most fitting of all, the parable Allah draws from the Gospel. They are like a seed that sends out its tender shoot, and the shoot grows firm and thickens and stands tall upon its stalk, until it amazes the very farmers who sowed it, and enrages those who hoped it would wither. That was the ummah at Hudaybiyyah: a fragile shoot of fourteen hundred, mocked, turned away at the door, certain it had lost. And that is the ummah Allah promised would rise into something that astonishes the world. The defeat at Hudaybiyyah was the soil. The clear victory was the harvest, and fourteen centuries on we are still standing in the field He grew.

What the clear victory asks of our faith

It would be easy to read the aftermath of Hudaybiyyah as a happy ending, a tidy proof that things work out, and file it away. That would be to miss the blade of it. The companions did not get a happy ending in the moment. They got a treaty that broke their hearts, a brother in chains they had to surrender, a pilgrimage denied, and a long ride home in the certainty that they had been beaten. The victory was real the entire time, and not one of them could see it. That is the part that should keep us awake.

Because your own Hudaybiyyah will not announce itself either. It will come as the door that closes in your face, the prayer that seems to go unanswered, the plan that collapses, the loss you cannot make sense of. And everything in you will name it a defeat, the way Umar named it a defeat, the way the whole camp did. The lesson of this day is not that the pain was unreal. It was real; Allah Himself tied the treaty to the forgiveness of sins precisely because of the grief it carried. The lesson is that the pain and the victory were the same event, and that the One who could see the hidden believers in Makkah, and the eight thousand converts not yet made, and the keys of the Kaaba two years down the road, was arranging all of it through the very thing that looked like ruin. You may hate a thing, and it is good for you, and Allah knows while you know not.

So take three things from the road home down into your ordinary days. When something you cannot stand is decreed for you, refuse to rush to the verdict that it is bad for you; remember that the clearest victory in the seerah arrived wearing the face of a defeat. When the text is plain and your heart rebels against it, do what Umar finally did and accuse your own understanding before you accuse the word of your Lord. And when your heart bleeds for a wrong you cannot right, keep your covenants, do the good within your reach, make du'a for the rest, and refuse to let raw emotion drive you into a ruin that wounds the innocent. Above all, learn the one thing this whole arc was sent to teach. Tie your camel and trust your Lord, do every lawful thing in your power, and then rest, genuinely rest, in the knowledge that the One who sees the whole road has already named the ending. May Allah, who turned the grief of His Messenger ﷺ into the clear victory and wrote his title into the word that never fades, give us eyes that trust Him in the dark, hearts that bow to His word over our own, and a share in the harvest He promised would one day delight the One who planted it.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 67: the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, part 5 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (48:1, 48:5, 48:10, 48:11, 48:12, 48:18, 48:22, 48:25, 48:27, 48:29, and 2:216), verified via quran.ai.

The Seerah · 68 of 104

The Battle of Khaybar, Part 1

The fortresses fall, and the banner is given to Ali

The ink on the treaty of Hudaybiyah was barely two weeks dry. For years the great danger had always come from the south, from Makkah and the Quraysh, and now the south was bound by a ten year peace. For the first time since the hijrah the Prophet ﷺ could turn his back on Makkah and look the other way, to the north, and there, two hundred and thirty kilometres up the road, sat a problem he had been carrying for a long time. Its name was Khaybar. Dr. Yasir Qadhi notes how little rest the community took: barely a fortnight after they staggered home from Hudaybiyah, the Prophet ﷺ was already thinking about the next step, already planning, already moving. This is the seerah of a man who never let a victory become a resting place. He used the peace he had just won to settle the one account that was still open.

The threat that moved to Khaybar

To understand why the Muslims marched north, you have to remember who was waiting for them there. Over the years in Madinah, the relationship with its three Jewish tribes had broken down one tribe at a time, and the Sheikh runs through the list as a recap, because Muslims so often blur these names together. The Banu Qaynuqa were the first, expelled in the month of Shawwal after Badr, when they sent the Prophet ﷺ a veiled threat that he had only beaten soft men and would find them another matter. The Banu Nadir came next, expelled after Uhud, when they plotted to drop a stone on his head as he sat against their wall and Jibril came to warn him away. And the Banu Qurayza were last, not expelled but judged, for their treachery during the siege of the trench. With that, Madinah no longer held a hostile tribe inside its walls.

But the exiles had not vanished into the distance. The leaders of the Banu Nadir, men like Huyayy ibn Akhtab, the father of Safiyyah who would soon become the Prophet's ﷺ wife, and Sallam ibn Abil Huqayq, had simply moved up the road to the nearest stronghold, and from Khaybar they never stopped working. It was these exiles who had helped arm the confederate armies at the trench, and it was they who had slipped into Madinah during that siege and talked the Banu Qurayza into breaking their covenant. Now the Quraysh were neutralised, and Khaybar was the single remaining power in the local vicinity that would seize any chance to come back for what it had lost.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi refuses to dress the reason up. There was no army marching on Madinah that day, no plot already in motion. This was a preemptive strike, and he says so plainly, in the way he is known for, without beating around the bush. This was simply the law of that entire world, Muslim and Christian and Jewish and pagan alike: every tribe waited for its moment to move on another, which is exactly why the people of Khaybar had built fortresses in the first place. You do not raise walls like theirs in a land of perfect peace. There was no treaty between Khaybar and Madinah to honour, no covenant being broken. He is careful to add that the law of that age is not a law we are free to import into our own; where there is a treaty, the believer is bound by the treaty. But on that day, in that world, there was no treaty, and there was only the question of who would move first.

Khaybar was a place worth fighting for and a place hard to take. It was a lush, fertile oasis sitting atop a vast underground river, the same hidden kind of current that fed Madinah itself, among the largest date producing regions in all of Arabia, and populated entirely by Jewish tribes with no Arab clans among them. They had done in Khaybar what they had done everywhere they settled: they had built fortresses, the finest and most famous in the whole peninsula. The Sheikh notes that this skill points back to their origins, for the art of raising great fortresses and dams belonged to the south, to Yemen, and the Arabs of central Arabia had never mastered it. The Jews of Khaybar kept the craft secret and taught it to no one, so their walls stood unrivalled.

And Khaybar was not one fortress but many, at least eight or nine and perhaps fifteen in all, scattered across two halves of the oasis, each a walled town holding a few hundred souls with an entire small tribe behind its gate. To men who had only ever fought in the open desert, these walls were a wholly new kind of war. How do you fight a wall? They had no engines to break stone, no answer yet to a fortress that simply refused to open. But the Sheikh stops to point out the flaw the Jews never foresaw in all their genius for building: locked separately inside their own strongholds, they could not unite. Their walls were perfect against a small raiding party, but against a disciplined army of sixteen or seventeen hundred men, each fortress of two or three or five hundred would face the whole force alone, and one by one that is what happened. Their ingenuity had a gaping hole in it, and in that hole lay their downfall.

What an evil morning

The Muslims set out in high spirits, roughly sixteen or seventeen hundred of them, and the books of Bukhari and Muslim remember how they marched: shouting the takbir at the top of their voices, screaming Allahu Akbar, Allahu Akbar, la ilaha illa Allah until the desert rang. The Prophet ﷺ turned and gentled them. O people, he said, be easy on yourselves, for the One you are calling upon is not deaf and not absent. He hears you, He sees you, and He is nearer to you than your own jugular vein. In another wording: He is with you. Lower your voices. There is no need to strain them, for Allah is with you wherever you are and hears the whisper as clearly as the shout.
