He timed the approach with a commander's instinct. Khaybar lay close enough to reach in under two days of marching, and he camped the last night just short of the oasis and moved the army before dawn, so that Khaybar would wake to find the danger already on its doorstep. At sunrise the farmers came out as on any other day, carrying their plows and axes and tools, expecting nothing but their fields, and they saw the army and froze. They dropped their instruments and ran back through the gates crying out that Muhammad and his forces had come, Muhammad and his army has arrived. The Sheikh pauses on that cry, because it tells you something. They were surprised, yes, but they were not innocent and not bewildered. They knew at once who would come and why. The premonition in their panic was the panic of people who had done something they should not have done and had always known the day of collection would arrive.

As the gates slammed and were sealed, the Prophet ﷺ said the words that every book of seerah and every book of hadith preserves:

Allahu Akbar, Allahu Akbar, Khaybar has been destroyed. Indeed, when we descend upon the open ground of a people, then evil is the morning of those who were warned.

The siege had begun. Khaybar was not a city to be taken in a day; it was a campaign of at least eight or nine separate battles, some lasting a single day and some dragging on for ten, fought fortress by fortress across both halves of the oasis. It was brutal, close, unfamiliar work. The defenders did what fortress dwellers do, the things we have all seen in the films: they hurled stones and heavy logs and rained arrows down through the slits, and the Muslims, who had never faced this before, did not at first know what to do against it.

At the great fortress of Naim the fighting ground on for ten full days. Abu Bakr took the standard and led, then Umar took it after him, and still the walls held. Then Mahmud ibn Maslamah of the Ansar came too close to the base of the wall, and the defenders dropped a massive log down upon him and crushed him where he stood. He died a martyr, and his death sat heavily on the whole army; to watch a man killed in that manner, with no enemy to even strike back at, was a bitter and demoralising thing. And as each fortress finally fell, its survivors who could still ride would gallop to the next one still standing, so that the strongholds that remained only swelled with fighters as the campaign wore on. The Muslims were learning, slowly and at a price, exactly what it costs to break a wall.

The banner given to Ali

After the long, draining day at Naim, the Prophet ﷺ made an announcement at the night prayer that changed the mood of the entire camp. Tomorrow, he said, I will hand the banner to a man who loves Allah and His Messenger and whom Allah and His Messenger love, and at his hands Allah will grant us the victory. Umar ibn al-Khattab would say afterward that never once in his whole life did he long to lead as he longed to lead that night. Every man in the camp went to sleep hoping the banner would be placed in his hand.

In the morning the Prophet ﷺ prayed Fajr, turned to the gathered army, and asked: where is Ali ibn Abi Talib? They told him Ali was lying in his tent, his eyes so swollen and infected he could not even open them. Bring him to me, he said. When Ali was led out, the Prophet ﷺ applied his blessed saliva to those eyes, and they were healed on the spot, as though there had never been anything wrong with them. Then he placed the banner in Ali's hand and told him: go forth in the name of Allah, and do not turn back.

Ali set off, then stopped. There was a question he needed answered, but to turn around and ask it would have meant disobeying the order not to turn back. So without turning his head he shouted the question over his shoulder: O Messenger of Allah, on what terms do I fight them? The answer the Prophet ﷺ gave is one Dr. Yasir Qadhi lingers over, because it lays bare what these expeditions were actually for. Fight them, he said, until they bear witness that there is no god but Allah and that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah. If they say it, their lives and their wealth are protected from you, and their account is with Allah. Then the line that reframes the whole campaign: by Allah, if Allah guides a single soul through you, it is better for you than a whole herd of red camels. Ten days of siege, a martyr crushed at the wall, a fortress still unbroken in front of him, and the goal the Prophet ﷺ names for his commander is not the land and not the plunder. It is one heart turned toward Allah. The Sheikh says it as plainly as it can be said: the psychology behind these expeditions was never money or fame or fortune. It was dawah. It was the calling of people to their Lord.

The duel and the door

When Ali reached the fortress, its champion came out to meet him. Marhab was a famous and ferocious warrior, bold enough to call for single combat in front of both armies. The first Muslim to answer, Amir ibn al-Akwa of the Ansar, he cut down, and Amir fell a martyr. Then Ali himself stepped forward. By rights the commander should have stayed back and let others duel, but Ali took the challenge on, and he killed Marhab. It broke something in the defenders, the first great blow of Khaybar, the first time their pride cracked, and soon the fighting spilled out from behind the walls into the open ground.

Then comes the moment every Muslim child has heard. In the thick of the fighting, one of the enemy commanders struck the shield clean out of Ali's grip and left him exposed. Ali turned to the great gate of the fortress, took hold of the door, tore it from its hinges, and carried that single door as his shield through the rest of the battle before he cast it aside. Abu Rafi, who was there and watched it, said that afterward seven of them together took hold of that same door and tried to lift it and could not move it. Dr. Yasir Qadhi names this for exactly what it is, a karama, a small miracle that Allah granted to Ali, and he turns to the gathering with a charge he clearly means: we are the true followers of Ali ibn Abi Talib, and we give him every ounce of credit that Allah and His Messenger gave him, and we are never miserly or stingy in honouring him. Here was a man whom Allah loved and whom the Messenger ﷺ loved, who loved them both in return, one of the very greatest of the companions, radiyallahu anhu.

Three deaths, three lessons

Khaybar is remembered for its fortresses, but the Sheikh stops the army's advance again and again to stand over a single man, because each one teaches something the walls never could.

The first lesson came wrapped in mercy. When the fortress called as-Sab was taken after three days, it was a deliverance timed to the very hour, for the Muslims' food had run out entirely and they had nothing left. Inside, the fortress was stockpiled with grain and provisions and water, enough to feed the whole army for the remainder of the long campaign. They lived off that one fortress to the end of Khaybar.

The second lesson was a slave, owned by the people of Khaybar, who had heard there was a man claiming prophethood and came to ask him to his face what he was about. The Prophet ﷺ told him: I am a prophet. And what is a prophet, the man asked, what have you brought? So the Prophet ﷺ explained Islam to him, and the slave accepted it on the spot. Then he asked the practical question of a man who owns nothing: my master gave me these sheep to graze, what do I do with them now? Return them, the Prophet ﷺ told him, for it is not permissible for us to take what was entrusted to you. The Sheikh refuses to let the honesty of this slip past. The Prophet ﷺ was at that very moment at war with the man's master; had the master's fortress fallen, the master and his flock and all he owned would have become lawful spoil. But that is not how property is taken, and a trust is a trust even when its owner is your enemy in the field, so the sheep had to go back. The Prophet ﷺ rubbed the heads of the flock and told the man where to send them, and with the baraka of his dua the sheep returned to their master on their own. The slave came back and fought until a stray arrow killed him. Standing over his body, the body of a man who owned nothing in this world but the loincloth that covered him and who never once prayed a single prayer, because the time for prayer had not yet come after he believed, the Prophet ﷺ lowered his gaze and turned his eyes away. When they asked him why, he said: his two wives from Paradise have come to greet him.

The third man taught the hardest lesson of the three. There was a Bedouin fighting so fiercely that the whole army marvelled and said: there goes a man of Paradise walking the earth. No, said the Prophet ﷺ, he is of the people of the Fire. One companion could not reconcile it, for if a man fighting like that was bound for the Fire, who among them could hope for anything, and so he followed the man to see. The Bedouin fought ferociously, but when an arrow wounded his hand so that he could fight no more, he set the hilt of his sword against the ground and threw himself upon the point and killed himself. He had not been fighting for Allah at all; he had been fighting for his own name, his own glory, the legacy and the fame of being called a mighty man, and when he could fight no longer he could not bear it. The Prophet ﷺ explained what it meant: a man may do the deeds of the people of Paradise as people see them, until the decree overtakes him and he seals his life with a deed of the Fire; and a man may seem to do the deeds of the Fire, until the decree overtakes him and he seals his life with a deed of Paradise. Deeds are judged by their endings. Al-amalu bil-khawatim. Guard the ending, the Sheikh reminds us, and ask Allah for a good one.

Surrender, and a meal that never left him

Fortress after fortress fell across both halves of the oasis, until only the largest stronghold remained, packed now with everyone still left alive who had fled there for protection. This one was too big to storm, so the Muslims simply camped outside it and waited, and a solid two weeks went by until the people inside understood there was nowhere left to run and no way to hide, and they sought terms. They petitioned the conqueror, calling him not Messenger of Allah but Abul Qasim, and made their case: your people do not know how to work these lands or coax their harvest from them, and we know Khaybar inside out, so let us stay and farm it for you and give you a share. Back and forth the terms went until they were settled. The people of Khaybar would hand over half of everything the oasis produced; all the labour and maintenance and cost would remain entirely their own burden, not split but wholly theirs; and the treaty would last only as long as the Muslims wished it to, to be ended whenever they chose, with no long term guarantee at all.

The Prophet ﷺ agreed, and the Sheikh draws out the foresight in it. The Muslims had neither the numbers nor the experience to leave hundreds of men behind to farm an oasis this vast; Khaybar would have needed a thousand to run it, and the community had no thousand to spare. So the people who knew the land kept the land, and half of its enormous harvest came to the Muslims year after year, the largest single source of wealth Islam had yet known. Scholars later differed over whether Khaybar was a conquest or a surrender, and the Sheikh follows Ibn al-Qayyim and the majority: it was a conquest, for had they truly wished to surrender they would have done so at the start, and only sought terms once every other fortress had fallen and they saw their own turn coming. Years afterward, under the caliphate of Umar, with Islam spread across the world and no further need to keep them there, the open ended clause was finally invoked and the Jews were resettled elsewhere, ending the Jewish presence in central Arabia for good.

Then came the meal. One of the families sent the conqueror a gift, a whole roasted lamb, as a conquered people will send a gift to honour a new ruler. But the woman who prepared it had quietly asked around which cut of meat the Prophet ﷺ loved best, and she was told it was the shoulder. So she laced the entire animal with a powerful poison and concentrated the worst of it in the shoulder. When the Prophet ﷺ and the companions sat to eat and he put a piece in his mouth, he stopped at once and called out for everyone to stop eating: the shoulder of this lamb has told me that it is poisoned. Allah let the meat itself speak to him. He did not swallow. But one companion, Bishr ibn al-Bara, had already eaten, and the poison took hold of him, and within days he died. The Prophet ﷺ summoned the family and questioned them, drawing out one true answer after another until they admitted it, and then he called the woman, who answered with bitter honesty: he had killed her people, her husband and her kin, and she had wanted revenge; and she had reasoned that if he were a false prophet the world would be rid of him, and if he were a true one the poison could never harm him. Dr. Yasir Qadhi sets that reasoning against the Qur'an's own portrait of those who knew exactly who he was and rejected him anyway:

Those to whom We gave the Scripture know him as they know their own sons. But indeed, a party of them conceal the truth while they know it.Qur'an 2:146

They recognised him, the Sheikh says, the way a man recognises his own children, and still they chose pride over the truth they could see. It was the same arrogance the Qur'an records of them elsewhere, the cool certainty that the Fire could never really hold them:

And they say, "Never will the Fire touch us, except for a few numbered days." Say, "Have you taken a covenant with Allah? For Allah will never break His covenant. Or do you say about Allah that which you do not know?"Qur'an 2:80

The Prophet ﷺ forgave what had been done to him personally; he did not retaliate for his own sake. But when Bishr died, justice for Bishr was another matter, and the woman was put to death in retaliation for the companion she had killed. As for the Prophet ﷺ himself, the poison never fully left his body. Four years later, on his deathbed, he would tell Aisha that he could still feel the effects of that meal from Khaybar, that he felt it now in his very heart, and that this was the hour the poison had finally reached it. His death was already written; the poison was one of the causes Allah decreed to make that passing more painful, and he carried it, uncomplaining, to the last days of his life ﷺ.

The day Jafar came home

On the very day Khaybar was finally won, a second joy arrived that nearly eclipsed the first. Jafar ibn Abi Talib, the Prophet's ﷺ beloved cousin, walked back into his life after more than a decade away, returning from Abyssinia with some fifty Muslim men and women who had emigrated there in the earliest, hardest years of the dawah. Jafar was older than the young Ali, and the bond between him and the Prophet ﷺ ran deep and long. He rose to meet him, kissed him on the forehead, and said: I do not know which of the two delights me more today, the conquest of Khaybar or the coming of Jafar. A whole month of siege, the greatest wealth Islam had ever gathered, and still he weighed it all against the face of one man he loved.

These returnees had not lifted a sword at Khaybar, yet the Prophet ﷺ gave them a share of its spoils all the same, for Allah never wastes the sacrifice of those who sacrifice. Theirs had simply been a different war: more than a decade in a foreign land, a strange tongue and a strange people, even a civil war in Abyssinia that nearly swept them away, in which the Negus who sheltered them came so close to being overthrown that he had readied a secret ship for them in case he fell. The Sheikh tells the homecoming through Asma bint Umays, who had made that journey. When Umar gently teased her, calling her the seafaring lady of Abyssinia and claiming that he and the emigrants to Madinah had more right to the Prophet ﷺ because they had migrated first, Asma snapped, and took it straight to the Prophet ﷺ himself, declaring before the companions in the masjid that she would not eat or drink until she repeated to him exactly what Umar had said. He settled it at once: they had no more right to him than she did. You made one migration, he told Umar's camp, and these people made two. The riwaya says the Muslims of Abyssinia were never happier than they were that day.

And so Khaybar was the turning of a long tide. The largest land conquest of the Prophet's ﷺ life poured in: orchards and grain, weapons and armour and animals, the smaller settlements around the oasis and even distant Fadak, whose people were so frightened they sent their surrender before any army ever came near them, making Fadak a thing Allah gave to His Messenger ﷺ directly, with no battle fought, its proceeds set aside to care for his family. The wealth ran so deep that the Muhajirun finally returned to the Ansar the very land the Ansar had pressed upon them six years earlier, in the first year of the hijrah, when the emigrants had arrived with nothing and their hosts had given and given and given. Ibn Umar would say it as simply as it can be said: we never ate our fill until after Khaybar. The reward of the patient, the Sheikh reminds you, always comes, and for some of these believers it had been twenty years in the coming.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read Khaybar as a war story, a tale of siege engines and spoils, the biggest land conquest of the Prophet's ﷺ life, and to leave it there. That would be to walk straight past the people the Sheikh keeps stopping to show us, and to miss the questions they put to us.

He insisted the goal was a heart, not a haul. Ten days into a brutal siege, with a man crushed dead at the wall and a fortress still standing in front of him, the order he gave his commander was to call them to Allah first, because a single guided soul outweighs a whole herd of red camels. We are quick to forget what we are really fighting for, in our work and our arguments and our striving, and to let the prize become the land instead of the heart. He never once let it. Whatever you are pouring your effort into, stop and ask whether the aim is still a soul or has quietly become the spoils, because the difference is the whole of it.

He sent the sheep back in the middle of a war. He was at war with the slave's master, the man's flock would soon enough have been lawful plunder, and still a trust was a trust and the animals had to be returned to the very enemy he was fighting. Honesty did not pause for the battlefield, and it does not pause for anything. If integrity could survive that, with a war on and an army hungry, then it has no excuse to fail in the small and comfortable corners of our own lives, in the inbox and the ledger and the things no one would ever check.

And he carried the poison without complaint to the last week of his life. The same season that brought him the richest victory of his life brought him the meal that would help kill him, and the same man met both with the same steadiness, forgiving what was done to him even as justice was done for Bishr. Victory and pain arrived together, as they so often do in a life, and he did not let the pain sour the gratitude or the victory blind him to the wound. He simply bore it, and bore it quietly, for four more years.

There is a third man at Khaybar we should not leave without, the one the army was certain was bound for Paradise, who ended on the point of his own sword because his fight had never really been for Allah at all. Deeds are weighed by how they finish. A whole life can lean toward the light and still end in the dark, and a hard life can turn at the very end toward Allah and be saved. None of us knows which deed will be our last, and so the only safe prayer is the one the Sheikh keeps pressing into our hands: that Allah grant us a good ending, husn al-khatimah, and guard us from an evil one.

So take Khaybar down into your ordinary days. Make the aim a heart and not a haul. Keep your honesty when no one is enforcing it, the way he kept his with a war on. When victory and pain arrive in the same season, meet them with one steady face and bear the wound without complaint. And ask Allah, every day, for the ending you cannot guarantee yourself. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, who tasted the poison and forgave, who weighed the greatest conquest of his life against the face of one beloved cousin, who lowered his eyes before the wives of Paradise come to greet a slave; grant us the patience of those who waited long for His reward, and gather us under his banner on the Day the nations are gathered, upon the best of endings.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 68: the battle of Khaybar, part 1 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (2:80, 2:146), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 69 of 104

The marriage to Zaynab bint Jahsh

The one marriage Allah Himself contracted

There are evenings in this story that a teacher would rather walk past, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi opens this one by saying so out loud. He had been putting it off for a long time, and then he understood that there was no use in delaying it: you have to face the facts, lay them out, and deal with them in an honest, careful way. The marriage of the Prophet ﷺ to Zaynab bint Jahsh is, by his own description, even more sensitive than the affair of the so-called Satanic Verses, because hostile writers have dressed it up as something it never was, a love story, a scandal, and built a whole library of slander on top of it. So the Sheikh decides that you are better off hearing it from him, in full and in order, in the safety of a room where it can be explained, than stumbling onto a poisoned version on some website that wishes the Prophet ﷺ nothing but harm.

And so he does for this story exactly what he did for the Satanic Verses. He gathers the reports, one by one. He puts dates on them, names on them, asks who said what and in which century. He weighs the chains. And then he lets the truth stand on its own feet, with nothing hidden and nothing exaggerated. Underneath the noise there turns out to be something the slander never wants you to see: this was the one marriage in the history of the world that Allah contracted directly, from above the seven heavens, recorded in a Qur'an we still recite, in order to tear down a custom that had trapped people for centuries. Lead with that, and every other piece falls into its place.

The most eligible woman of Quraysh

Zaynab was not born Zaynab. She was born Barra, of the tribe of Quraysh, which is to say she was not of Banu Hashim but of the wider Quraysh, and her lineage made her proud. Through her mother she was a first cousin of the Prophet ﷺ himself, for her mother was his paternal aunt, the sister of his father Abdullah. She was younger than him by more than a decade, and by the reckoning of most of the books she was around thirty-five years old at the time of these events, though, as the Sheikh likes to remind us, no one in that age kept careful track of birthdays. She had been married once before, back in Makkah, and divorced, and she had emigrated to Madinah with her brother. A free woman again, available for marriage, and well aware of her own worth.

Then the Prophet ﷺ sent her a proposal, but not for himself. He proposed on behalf of Zayd ibn Harithah, the young man he had freed and raised as his own, the one everyone in Madinah still called Zayd ibn Muhammad. And Zaynab balked. I am the widow of Quraysh, she answered, the most eligible woman of my people, and you want me to marry Zayd? You can hear underneath the words exactly what she meant: a freed slave was beneath her, not of her caliber, not a match that could ever work. Her brother said the same thing, that Zayd was not worthy of their family. The Sheikh does not scold her for it. It is human nature, he says, woven into us, that a woman wishes to marry a man she can respect and look up to, just as a man so often feels unsettled if the woman he approaches has more wealth or standing than he does. Right or wrong is beside the point; this is simply how people are made. And so, from the very beginning, this match was in danger of collapsing before it began.

When Allah and His Messenger decide

The Prophet ﷺ insisted, and the earliest books of tafsir tell us that a verse came down for this exact standoff. Once Allah and His Messenger have settled a matter, no believing man and no believing woman has any choice left to make about it.

It is not for a believing man or a believing woman, when Allah and His Messenger have decided a matter, that they should [thereafter] have any choice about their affair. And whoever disobeys Allah and His Messenger has certainly strayed into clear error.Qur'an 33:36

The moment Zaynab and her brother heard it, their resistance dissolved. They said they were content with Zayd. And so the marriage went ahead, two people joined less by attraction than by obedience, which is rarely the firmest foundation a marriage can have. It did not flourish. Zayd and Zaynab were never an easy fit, and within a few years he was coming to the Prophet ﷺ to complain and to ask permission to divorce her, telling him of her sharp tongue and her sense of standing above him. Hold that ordinary, unhappy marriage in view as you read on, because everything the slander later twists is hiding somewhere inside it.

The verse, and the one name Allah wrote down

There is only one verse in the entire Qur'an that tells this story explicitly, and it is worth slowing down on every clause of it. The Sheikh says you should memorize it, or at least its meaning.

And [remember, O Muhammad], when you said to the one on whom Allah bestowed favor and you bestowed favor, "Keep your wife and fear Allah," while you concealed within yourself that which Allah is to disclose. And you feared the people, while Allah has more right that you fear Him. So when Zayd had no longer any need for her, We married her to you in order that there not be upon the believers any discomfort [i.e., guilt] concerning the wives of their claimed [i.e., adopted] sons when they no longer have need of them. And ever is the command [i.e., decree] of Allah accomplished.Qur'an 33:37

Read it slowly. Allah recalls the moment the Prophet ﷺ told Zayd: keep your wife and fear Allah, do not divorce her. And then comes the gentle, extraordinary rebuke: while you concealed within yourself that which Allah is to disclose, and you feared the people, while Allah has more right that you fear Him. There was something the Prophet ﷺ was keeping inside out of concern for what people would say, and Allah, who never lets His Messenger hide behind the opinions of anyone, announced that He Himself would bring it into the open. It is a verse so candid that, as we will see, the early Companions said that if the Prophet ﷺ had ever been going to suppress a single line of revelation, this would have been the line.

And here Dr. Yasir Qadhi stops you on a detail that most Muslims have lived their whole lives without noticing. Zayd ibn Harithah is the only Companion mentioned by name anywhere in the Qur'an. Not Abu Bakr, not Umar, not Ali, no one. Allah elsewhere refers to Abu Bakr by description, the second of two in the cave, which is honor enough, but it cannot be compared to this. Allah took this freed slave, once a piece of property bought and sold in the markets of Arabia, and wrote his name into the eternal book. It is a rank so towering that when the Prophet ﷺ later passed away, one of the Companions remarked that had Zayd still been alive, they would never have chosen anyone over him for the leadership. Most of us know little of Zayd, the Sheikh notes, only because he was martyred so early, in the eighth year, in a battle whose story is just weeks away in this series, and the grief of the Prophet ﷺ over him was visible and deep.

Then the verse delivers its purpose in one clause: so when Zayd had no longer any need for her, We married her to you. Allah married them. Not a guardian, not two witnesses, not a contract recited between people in a room. The nikah of the Prophet ﷺ and Zaynab was pronounced from above the seven heavens and recorded in the Qur'an itself. The whole world was witness, and no further witness was needed, no dowry-broker, no officiant, nothing. The verse came down and the matter was concluded. It is, the Sheikh says, the only marriage in the history of the world legitimized directly by Allah, the only marriage contract we still recite as scripture to this day.

Why a marriage had to break a custom

Read the verse once more and the reason for the whole painful episode rises to the surface: in order that there not be upon the believers any discomfort concerning the wives of their claimed sons. In pre-Islamic Arabia, adoption was absolute. A man who adopted a boy gave him his own name, and the adopted son became a true son in every legal sense the society recognized. He inherited as a real son inherited. And his former wife was as permanently forbidden to the man who raised him as a real daughter-in-law would be to a father. Nobody, the Sheikh points out, denies the underlying principle here: a so-called adopted son is not the same as a biological son. The wife of your own flesh-and-blood son is forever forbidden to you; the husband of your own daughter is forever forbidden; these bonds of blood are real and unbreakable. But a child you have raised who is not of your blood does not become your flesh by the mere saying of a word.

So Allah chose to undo the false custom in the way a society feels most sharply: not with a sentence in a sermon, but with a living example at the very top of the community. The man everyone had called Zayd ibn Muhammad would be called Zayd ibn Harithah again, by his real father's name. And the Messenger of Allah ﷺ himself would marry the former wife of the man he had once adopted, to prove in the most public way imaginable that the old taboo had no foundation in the religion of Allah. It cost him something personally, this public example, and it settled the question forever for every believer who came after.

A few lines later the same passage seals the deeper truth about who he ﷺ is.

Muhammad is not the father of [any] one of your men, but [he is] the Messenger of Allah and seal [i.e., last] of the prophets. And ever is Allah, of all things, Knowing.Qur'an 33:40

He is not the father of Zayd, nor of any of your men; the bonds were never what the Jahiliyyah imagined. He is the Messenger of Allah and the seal of the prophets, the last of them, with no prophet to come after. The Sheikh draws your attention to the shape of the whole passage: nearly a page and a half around this verse is pure praise of the Prophet ﷺ, including the celebrated verse a little further on in which Allah tells us that He and His angels send their blessings upon him, and commands the believers to do the same. The slander wants this story to be about a scandal. The Qur'an makes it about the end of false fatherhood, the finality of prophethood, and the honoring of the Messenger ﷺ.

Two versions, and an answer that does not flinch

Now the Sheikh does the work he spent an entire day doing, from nine in the morning until the hour of the lecture, in his own library, with forty or fifty books of tafsir open in front of him. He traces, century by century, in chronological order, what the scholars actually said the Prophet ﷺ was concealing in his heart. There turn out to be exactly two readings, and he names them plainly: version A and version B.

Version A is a story. What the Prophet ﷺ concealed, on this reading, was a quiet human inclination toward Zaynab once he had seen her, and an unspoken wish that the unhappy marriage might end, a wish he would not voice for fear of what people would say. Version B is not a story at all but an interpretation: Allah had already informed the Prophet ﷺ that Zaynab would one day become his wife, and that foreknowledge was the thing he kept inside, fearful that people would accuse him of marrying the former wife of his adopted son.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi is scrupulously honest about where each reading comes from, because the chronology is the whole point. Version A is the standard of the earliest authorities. Muqatil, whose tafsir is the oldest in print, records it, though as a mere storyteller he is cited only to show what people were saying around the year 150, not as a reliable scholar. But then comes at-Tabari, who died in the year 310, the great encyclopedist and most orthodox bastion of Sunni tafsir, and he records version A as essentially the only version, tracing it back through Qatadah, the foremost authority of his generation, and through other early scholars, all stating that what the Prophet ﷺ hid was the wish that Zayd would divorce her. After at-Tabari, the Sheikh names a procession of major commentators across the next centuries, including al-Wahidi, az-Zamakhshari, al-Baghawi, all carrying version A as if it were simply the received account. Version B appears in the printed books only later, in the fourth and fifth centuries, and slowly gains ground. By the time you reach the later masters like Ibn Kathir, who died in 774, and Ibn Hajar, who died in 852, version A is no longer mentioned at all; they say only that some of the earliest reports are better left uncited, and they cross them out without repeating them.

And here is where the Sheikh refuses to take the easy exit. Even if version A is correct, he says, following al-Baghawi who said it plainly six centuries before him, there is no sin in it whatsoever. Notice, he insists, what the Qur'an does and does not say. It never tells the Prophet ﷺ to repent. It never calls what he did a wrong. It does not say he made a mistake or that he must seek refuge. It says only that he concealed something out of embarrassment, and that he should have feared Allah over the people. A passing inclination of the heart that a man never acts upon is simply not a sin; love and attraction are not things a person controls, only his actions are. And the Prophet ﷺ did exactly what a believer is meant to do with such a feeling. He turned away. He told Zayd to keep his wife. He kept his own counsel. That, the Sheikh says, is not a flaw in the man. That is the role model itself: a human being with the same pulls you and I feel, who never once let one of them carry him into wrong.

A human prophet is the prophet we can follow

What the Sheikh will not accept, and he is emphatic about it, is the move that many modern speakers make: declaring version A a forgery slipped into the books by orientalists and heretics to defame the Prophet ﷺ. It does not behoove us, he says. We Muslims love a conspiracy theory, we love to blame this on that and that on this, but version A is woven all the way through our own earliest tradition, narrated by our own most reliable scholars, carried in the very same chains that at-Tabari relies on for some eighty percent of his entire tafsir. You cannot tear it out and remain honest with your sources. Follow version B if it sits easier on your heart, follow version A if you find it stronger, even hold some blend of the two, as some scholars did. All of it is within the fold of the tradition, and not one of the readings places a single sin on the Prophet ﷺ.

Then he draws the lesson all the way out, and this is the heart of the evening. There has always been a tension in this ummah, he reminds you, and it persists to this day: between those who lift the Prophet ﷺ into a near-mystical being with no human needs at all, who does not eat or drink or cast a shadow, and those who seize on his humanity in order to attack him. The straight path runs between the two, and it is the path the Prophet ﷺ marked out himself when he said, I am only a human being. A messenger who feels what you feel and masters it is a messenger you can actually imitate. Turn him into an angel with no inclinations, and you have quietly emptied his life of any example for your own. He points to the marriage to Juwayriyyah as proof of the same humanity, narrated soundly in the books: her own people said that the moment they saw her they understood what the Prophet ﷺ would see in her, and so it was. Subhan Allah, the Sheikh says, what in the world is wrong with a prophet who has the ordinary inclinations of a man and never once obeys the wrong one? That is not something less than perfection. That is exactly what perfection looks like.

And he lands it where the slander can never follow him. When the heart is corrupt, he says, it can take the most innocent story and turn it into filth; and when the heart is pure, an innocent story stays innocent. Those who set out to disparage the Prophet ﷺ never needed Zaynab's story at all; they will twist anything they are handed, and we have seen them do it again and again. And those who love him have no trouble here whatsoever. They have, the Sheikh adds with a small smile, far bigger problems to worry about than which version a believer prefers.

The night hijab came down

The wedding itself, in the beginning of the fifth year after the Hijrah, was the most generous feast the Prophet ﷺ ever gave for any of his wives, a fact recorded in all the books of hadith. Anas ibn Malik was sent out with a guest list, and then told to widen it and widen it again: now call the people of the courtyard, then those in the masjid, then anyone you meet on the road, then anyone at all. There was bread and meat until everyone in Madinah had eaten his fill, the simple staple food of the Prophet's ﷺ own table, for there was no rice in Arabia in those years; rice reached the Companions only after the conquests of Persia and Rome. Anas marveled that the people ate from a single small dish, more than seventy men from it, and when they had finished it held as much as it had at the start.

And it was on that very night that the verses of hijab came down. Before this evening there had been no command of covering as the Shariah would later define it, for the simple reason that the ruling had not yet been revealed; the women of the believers did not yet cover in the way the law would soon require. It arrived here, tied to this wedding.

O you who have believed, do not enter the houses of the Prophet except when you are permitted for a meal, without awaiting its readiness. But when you are invited, then enter; and when you have eaten, disperse without seeking to remain for conversation. Indeed, that [behavior] was troubling the Prophet, and he is shy of [dismissing] you. But Allah is not shy of the truth. And when you ask [his wives] for something, ask them from behind a partition. That is purer for your hearts and their hearts. And it is not [conceivable or lawful] for you to harm the Messenger of Allah or to marry his wives after him, ever. Indeed, that would be in the sight of Allah an enormity.Qur'an 33:53

Do not crowd uninvited into the homes of the Prophet ﷺ, the verse instructs, and do not linger over your conversation after a meal has ended, for it troubles him though he is too gentle to say so; and when you ask his wives for something, ask them from behind a partition, for that is purer for the hearts of everyone. And Zaynab would carry the honor of this night with her for the rest of her life. She would say to the other wives, with the pride a co-wife is allowed, that their families had married them to the Prophet ﷺ, but as for her, Allah had married her to him from above the seven heavens.

The longest hand

Aisha, radiyallahu anha, who of all people had the most reason to see Zaynab as a rival, left us instead some of the highest praise Zaynab ever received. Zaynab, she said, was the only woman who ever truly competed with her for standing in the eyes of the Prophet ﷺ. And yet, during the long ordeal of the slander against Aisha, when Zaynab was asked about her and could so easily have wounded a rival with a careless word, she answered only: I will protect my hearing and my sight, I know nothing of her but good. Her own sister, out of love for Zaynab, had been among those who let the rumor spread, but Zaynab herself would not touch it; her taqwa, Aisha said, held her back. Years later Aisha would speak about her in a single flowing paragraph preserved in Sahih Muslim: she had never seen a woman more devout in her religion than Zaynab, nor more fearful of Allah, nor more truthful in speech, nor more devoted to her relatives, nor more generous in her charity, nor more tireless in drawing near to Allah, except that she had a temper that flared quickly and just as quickly was gone. Imagine, the Sheikh says, the wife of the Prophet ﷺ saying that of another wife.

She was called the mother of the poor and the mother of the orphans, and she was known among all the wives as the most generous of them. The Prophet ﷺ had once told them, when they fell to debating which of them would be the first to follow him in death, that it would be the one with the longest hand. For years afterward they would line up against a wall and measure their arms one against another, until at last they understood what he had meant: not the longest arm, but the hand that reached furthest in giving. In the time of Umar, when the wives of the Prophet ﷺ were placed in the highest category of the public stipend and the great sum of twelve thousand dirhams was poured out before Zaynab in coins, she sat where she was and sent it away, handful by handful, this much to such a house and this much to another, until not a single coin remained and she had not gone to sleep. Umar heard of it, came to her door himself in wonder, and sent in another thousand; she gave that away too, and prayed that she might not live to see such wealth again, for money, the Sheikh says, is a trial. She passed away that very year, in the twentieth year after the Hijrah, the first of the Mothers of the Believers to die after the Prophet ﷺ and the first to be buried in that resting place where the others would join her. Umar himself led her funeral prayer. The life the slander wanted to soil was, from its first day to its last, the life of a saint.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to leave this evening as a successful defense, an answer filed away for the next time someone tries to ambush you with half a story. That would be to miss what it actually asks of you. Strip it down past the controversy and three things remain, each one meant to be carried out of the page and into an ordinary life.

The first is that obedience outranks preference. Zaynab did not want Zayd; she had every worldly reason to refuse, and she said so, and so did her brother. And then a verse came down telling her, and telling every believer after her, that once Allah and His Messenger ﷺ have decided a matter, there is simply no choice left to be exercised. Most of us are quick to move on what we want and slow to ask whether the door we are pushing on is one Allah has opened. The same surrender Zaynab was asked for is asked of you, and almost always in the very things you would not have chosen for yourself. The marriage that began in reluctant obedience ended in the highest honor any of the wives received. The order of those two facts is not an accident.

The second is that you are judged by what you do, not by what you feel, and that this truth is the very thing that makes the Prophet ﷺ a Messenger you can follow. The slander wants the story to be about a feeling. The Sheikh, walking patiently through six centuries of his own scholars, shows you that even on the oldest reading a feeling never acted upon is no sin at all, and that the man in the story did precisely what a believer should: he felt the pull, he turned his face away, he told Zayd to keep his wife, and he stepped nowhere near wrong. We spend so much energy ashamed of what passes through our hearts uninvited. Here is a Messenger of Allah ﷺ telling us, with his own life, that the heart's passing weather is not what we are weighed by; our deeds are. Make him an angel above all feeling and you lose your example. Keep him the most human of teachers and he becomes the proof that a real person, with a real person's inclinations, can live an entire life without once obeying the wrong one.

And the third is the matter the whole evening was built to settle: that the love we owe an orphan is real, and the bloodline we cannot invent for him is also real, and the two are not in conflict. Islam did not come to forbid you from raising a child who is not yours; it came to forbid you from lying about it. Zayd was loved, freed, raised, honored above almost everyone, his name written by Allah into the Qur'an forever. He simply was not turned into something he was not. Love the orphan, the Sheikh teaches through this verse, spend on him, give him your home and your heart, and still leave him his own father's name and his own lineage intact, for the caring is worship and the pretending was the custom Islam came to end. The whole episode, every painful and contested line of it, is Allah breaking that one lie from the very top of the community so that no believer beneath would ever have to carry its weight again.

So take this down into your own days. Surrender, in the things you would not have picked, to what Allah and His Messenger ﷺ have already decided. Stop arraigning yourself for the weather of your heart, and watch instead, with everything you have, what your hands and your tongue actually do. And love the ones in your care without ever needing to falsify who they are, for that is the dignity Allah Himself descended to defend. May Allah send His peace and His blessings upon His Messenger ﷺ, who feared his Lord above all people, and upon Zaynab the daughter of Jahsh, mother of the believers, whom Allah married to His Prophet from above the seven heavens; and may He let us love him as Aisha and Zaynab loved him, follow him as his Companions followed him, and gather us with him and with the Mothers of the Believers in the gardens He has promised them. Ameen.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 69: the marriage to Zaynab bint Jahsh (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (33:36, 33:37, 33:40, 33:53), verified via quran.ai. Where the books of tafsir carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own chronological weighing of them has been followed.

The Seerah · 70 of 104

The Battle of Khaybar, part 2

After the fortress falls, the heart that turns

The walls of Khaybar are the part everyone remembers. The fortresses of the north, the last organized Jewish power that had spent years arming the desert tribes against Madinah, had finally come down, oasis by oasis, until the strongest of them surrendered. It was a victory of the first order, and it ended a long shadow that had hung over the young community. And yet, when you sit with the days that followed, you begin to suspect the seerah is not really asking you to look at the walls at all.

Because what happened after Khaybar fell is where the Prophet ﷺ is laid bare. The army lingered. The fighting was over, the terms were being settled, the road home was long, and into that quiet stretch walked a ship's worth of long-lost family, a daughter of the enemy, a captive woman with every reason in the world to be bitter, and a frightened little girl on a camel. Strip away the siege and what is left is a man, and the man is more astonishing than the conquest. Dr. Yasir Qadhi spends a whole session on this aftermath, and by the end of it you have learned more about who the Prophet ﷺ was than any wall coming down could have taught you.

The cousin who came home from across the sea

On the very day Khaybar was conquered, a part of the Prophet's ﷺ heart that had been missing for more than a decade walked back into his life. These were the emigrants to Abyssinia, the believers who, in the darkest years of Makkah, had fled across the sea and taken refuge under the Najashi, the just Christian king whose land had sheltered them when their own city would not. At their head was the Prophet's ﷺ own cousin, Ja'far ibn Abi Talib, the brother of Ali, a man the Prophet ﷺ loved and had not laid eyes on in over ten years.

He stood and embraced him. And then he said a sentence the seerah will never quite let you put down. By Allah, he ﷺ said, I do not know which of the two makes me happier today: the conquest of Khaybar, or the coming of Ja'far. Sit with the scale he is using. On one side, a complete military triumph, the end of a strategic threat, an oasis of wealth and farmland falling into the hands of the believers. On the other, a single beloved face he had missed. And the Prophet ﷺ, standing in the flush of one of his great victories, could not say which weighed more. The Sheikh draws the line gently and lets it land: that is how he loved. A returning brother could balance a kingdom. We who lose sleep over our wins might ask how lightly we hold the people who are supposed to matter more than them.

A daughter of Abu Sufyan, alone in a foreign land

With that group from Abyssinia, in all likelihood, came one of the women who would become a wife of the Prophet ﷺ and a mother of the believers: Umm Habibah, the daughter of Abu Sufyan. Her story is one of the hard ones.

She and her husband had accepted Islam early, in the first wave, and had emigrated together to Abyssinia to escape the persecution of Makkah. He was, the Sheikh notes, one of a tiny handful of companions, men you could almost count on the fingers of one hand, who did the unthinkable thing: he abandoned the faith. In the land of the Christians he turned Christian, and not long after, he died. Umm Habibah was left utterly alone. A believing woman with a young daughter, stranded in a foreign country, no husband, no protector, and a father who was not coming to help, because her father was Abu Sufyan, the chief of the Quraysh, the very man leading the war against Islam. There was, on the face of it, no one in the world to take care of her.

So the Prophet ﷺ sent a proposal of marriage to her across the sea. And here the wisdom unfolds in two directions at once. Who would stand as her guardian, when her father was the enemy and no male relative of faith was near? The answer was the Najashi himself, the Muslim emperor in whose land she sheltered. The leader of the believers became the guardian for the one believer who had none. The king was overjoyed at the honor. He paid her dowry generously from his own wealth, on the Prophet's ﷺ behalf, without even being asked, lavished gifts upon her, and held the wedding feast there in Abyssinia. As for the wisdom of the match itself, the Sheikh says it is plain enough that you need say nothing more: she is the daughter of Abu Sufyan, the chief of Quraysh. There was no bridge to that house worth building more than this one. And so a lonely, abandoned believer, who had every reason to feel forgotten by everyone, was raised by Allah to be a wife of His Messenger ﷺ and a mother of the believers.

The gold of Kinana, and a question the Sheikh refuses to soften

Then comes an incident Dr. Yasir Qadhi will not sand the edges off, because, as he puts it, he is no apologist; he calls a spade a spade.

Part of Khaybar's surrender was a clear bargain. Unlike the tribes Madinah had exiled before them, the people of Khaybar had asked to stay on their land and work it, handing over half the produce, and the Prophet ﷺ had agreed, on conditions. Chief among them: they would surrender all of their gold, silver, weapons, and armor, and they would hide nothing. Conceal anything, and the treaty was void. But Kinana, one of the chiefs, killed in the fighting, had been a wealthy man, a man known to have possessed treasure chests of gold. And the gold could not be found.

His relative, Sa'yah, was brought and questioned: where is it? It is all gone, he said, spent on the war and the years. The Prophet ﷺ pointed out the plain arithmetic of it: Kinana had left Madinah only recently, and a fortune that size cannot be spent so fast; you are clearly lying. And still the man insisted, and insisted, and insisted. So the Prophet ﷺ handed him over to az-Zubayr and told him to extract the truth, and az-Zubayr did what was necessary, which is to say the man was roughed up, until very shortly he remembered: I think I saw Kinana going toward such and such a valley. They went to that valley, found ground freshly dug and turned over, and there was the buried gold.

This, the Sheikh acknowledges openly, is the moment some today seize on to accuse the Prophet ﷺ of condoning the torture of a prisoner. His answer is not to deny a word of what happened. It is to refuse the anachronism. This was the universal norm of that age, long before any Geneva convention had been imagined, let alone signed; every civilization on earth did this. The man was lying, he knew he was lying, and he knew the consequences. To haul a seventh-century world before a courtroom built in the twentieth, the Sheikh says, is not honesty, it is distortion. The principle he leaves us with is exact and worth keeping: where our law binds us to an accord we have actually entered into, we honor it to the letter; where no such accord ever existed, the Prophet ﷺ did nothing his entire world did not already do. There is no point, he says, in sugar-coating it. It is what it is.

Safiyyah, conquered and free

Now comes the heart of the day. Safiyyah was the daughter of Huyayy ibn Akhtab, the very chief who had spent years marshalling the Jewish tribes against Madinah, the man whose enmity had helped bring on the Battle of the Trench. She tells her own story, preserved in more than one narration, and it begins when she was a little girl.

She was her father's favorite, she said. Every day he and her uncle would come home from their fields and she would run out to them, laughing, and they would play with her. But one day, when she was around ten, they came home crushed, their shoulders dragging, and they walked straight past her as though she were not there. That was the day the Prophet ﷺ had arrived in Madinah, and they had gone to see him. She crept close and heard her uncle ask her father: is it really him? Yes, by the Lord, he answered, it is him. And what will you do? We will oppose him, said her father, as long as we live.

Years passed. Her tribe was exiled, she grew up, she married. And at Khaybar, where her people fought at the very front of the battle, more enraged than the rest because they had been driven from their homes, her father was killed, and her brother, and her husband. In the space of one twenty-day ordeal she was made an orphan and a widow at once. When the fighting ended and the captives were taken, Safiyyah fell, by the lot, to an ordinary soldier. But many of the companions came to the Prophet ﷺ and said a woman of her standing, the daughter of a chief, should not be left in the share of one soldier. So he ransomed her, paid her price, and took her himself.

What no one yet knew was what she was to be. Both were lawful: she could be kept as a slave, or freed and married. They watched to see how he ﷺ would treat her, and he showed them without a word. When it was time to leave Khaybar and she had to mount his great camel, he knelt, set one knee on the ground, and lifted the other so that his own thigh became a step for her to climb. And Safiyyah, with the quick dignity that marks her whole story, would not place her foot upon the Prophet ﷺ; she set her knee on his thigh instead and rose to the saddle. Then he took his cloak and covered her with it completely, and by that the companions understood: this is a veil for a wife, not for a slave. She was to be honored.

On their wedding night he said to her something deliberately, calculatedly harsh. Your father, he ﷺ said, was the most relentless of all the Jews in his enmity toward me, until Allah caused his death. It is a cruel thing to say to a new bride, and there was a precise reason for it. He was testing her, the Sheikh explains, watching for any hidden bitterness, any flicker of the desire for revenge, before he kept her, because a wife who carried hatred in her heart could not remain. And Safiyyah answered him with the Qur'an itself. Her father's enmity was her father's, she said, not hers; no soul carries the sin of another.

And no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another. And if a heavily laden soul calls [another] to [carry some of] its load, nothing of it will be carried, even if he should be a close relative. You can only warn those who fear their Lord unseen and have established prayer. And whoever purifies himself only purifies himself for [the benefit of] his soul. And to Allah is the [final] destination.Qur'an 35:18

Then he offered her a choice that sounds, on its surface, almost bitter. Accept Islam, and I shall keep you for myself; or remain in your religion, and perhaps I shall free you and you may return to your people. As she understood it, the choice was between staying a slave with him and walking away a free woman among her own. It was a test, the Sheikh says, sharper than the first. If she were not a true believer, why on earth would she choose to remain, even as a slave, with the man whose army had destroyed her family, when freedom and her own people lay open to her?

She answered that she had already inclined to Islam, and already believed in him, before he ever asked. My father and my brother are no more, she said, and notice her precision, the Sheikh adds, that she does not even mention the husband, passing over the awkward fact of the man also killed in the battle. Allah and His Messenger ﷺ, she said, are more beloved to me than being free and returning to my people. Never, the Sheikh says, in the whole history of humanity, do we find its like. A conqueror takes a woman whose father, brother, husband, and entire tribe his army has destroyed, and in place of hatred, vengeance, the wish to see him dead, she chooses his faith, and chooses to belong to him over going free. What does that show, he asks, and answers it plainly: it shows two things at once, that Safiyyah had a pure heart, and that Islam is true. So the Prophet ﷺ freed her then and there and married her, her freedom itself standing as her dowry.

There was a final detail he noticed only then. On her face was a fresh bruise. Where did this come from, he asked. From my husband, she said, Kinana. She had dreamed, before the battle, that the moon rose out of Madinah and came to rest in her lap, and when she told her husband, he struck her across the face: do you imagine the king of the Arabs is going to marry you? Allah, it turned out, had been preparing her heart for this all along. When Safiyyah later came to Madinah, the other wives, the Sheikh is honest, rushed to see this beautiful newcomer, and one of them remarked that she feared this one would take their husband's affection. It was Allah, in the end, who had won her, long before the army ever reached Khaybar.

The wife with no clan to defend her

Safiyyah came to Madinah with beauty and dignity but with no family and no faction behind her, and the household of the Prophet ﷺ, the Sheikh reminds us with great honesty, was not free of ordinary human jealousy. The wives had their alliances and their tensions, their two loose camps, and Safiyyah belonged to neither, because she had no one. That very aloneness, it seems, drew the Prophet ﷺ closer to her in sympathy.

Some of the wives looked down on where she came from. She came to him once in tears: they have taunted me, she said, for being a daughter of the Jews. Why did you not defend yourself, he asked, and then he taught her exactly what to say. Tell them, he ﷺ said, that your father is a prophet, Harun, and your uncle is a prophet, Musa, and you are married to a prophet. What do they have over you? Hold that answer up to the light. He does not merely comfort her; he hands her a dignity rooted in the prophets themselves, and turns the very lineage they mocked into her honor.

The Sheikh lingers on the depth of this love, because it shows itself most when she is wronged. Once, before Hajj, Safiyyah's camel fell sick and she had no mount, and the Prophet ﷺ asked another of his wives, who had spare camels and came from a more prestigious family, to lend her one. She refused, with a sneer at Safiyyah's origins, calling her by the slur again though she was now a Muslim. And the Prophet ﷺ was so displeased that he kept apart from that wife for the length of the journey, until she understood the size of her error, and only afterward, when they returned, did he reconcile with her with a gift. Even the best of households, the Sheikh notes, are not free of these human politics; what matters is whose side the Prophet ﷺ took, and he took the side of the one with no one to defend her.

And then there is the end. When the Prophet ﷺ lay dying, tossing in the agony of his fever, his wives gathered around him, not yet knowing he was about to leave them. It was Safiyyah who broke and cried out: how I wish I could carry your pain in your place. The woman whose father, brother, husband, and whole world his army had taken now wished only to take his suffering onto herself. Some of the other wives, in that moment, exchanged knowing looks, as if to say she did not mean it. And from his sickbed, the Prophet ﷺ told them plainly: she has spoken the truth. Where, the Sheikh asks, does a love like that come from? You would expect such a woman to be a vessel of bitterness and the wish for revenge. Instead, here she is at his deathbed, wailing that she would take his pain if she could. That is what enters a heart when iman enters it. A complete turning, a hundred and eighty degrees, and the seerah offers her to us as living proof of what faith can do to a soul.

The man who would carry his words to us

Khaybar's aftermath also quietly delivered the single most important narrator of hadith in the entire history of Islam.

Abu Hurayrah had come up from Yemen, a man of the tribe of Daws, making his way toward Madinah to find the Prophet ﷺ. When he learned that the Prophet ﷺ was at Khaybar, on the road north, he turned aside to come to him there, and arrived just after the conquest, in that same wave that brought the emigrants home from Abyssinia. For the Muslims returning from Abyssinia, the Prophet ﷺ granted a share of the spoils. For Abu Hurayrah and his small group, who had fought in nothing, he asked the companions' permission to give them a share too, and they agreed. So a man who never lifted a weapon at Khaybar walked away with a portion of its wealth, and with something incomparably greater.

He would accompany the Prophet ﷺ for only three years, the Sheikh marks the smallness of it, three years and a month, less than almost any famous companion. And yet he would transmit more of the Prophet's ﷺ words than any other human being who ever lived, with no exception at all. Number one, without rival. There is a wisdom in that worth a lecture of its own, the Sheikh says. For now it is enough to notice what the quiet aftermath of a battle had done: it had brought to the Prophet ﷺ the very channel through which an immense portion of his life, his sayings, his guidance, would one day reach the rest of us.

A frightened girl on his camel

One more incident, small and easy to pass over, and yet it may be the truest window of the whole day into the manners of the Prophet ﷺ.

A group of women of the Ansar had volunteered to come along to Khaybar, to tend the wounded, carry water, and help behind the lines. The Prophet ﷺ did not, as a rule, take women into battle, but Khaybar was so clearly going to be a victory, with so little danger, that he allowed them. Among them was a girl of perhaps seven or eight with no mount of her own, and so, on the way out, the Prophet ﷺ sat her on the baggage behind him, on his own camel.

When they halted and he told her to climb down, she froze, and would not move. She had just had her first menses, there, behind the Prophet ﷺ himself, and she was terrified and ashamed beyond words. Of all the times, of all the places, the Sheikh says, imagine the horror of it for a young girl: where, and behind whom. He told her to come down and she could not. So he looked, and saw the traces of blood on the saddle, and understood at once. Perhaps you are having your cycle, he said gently. She nodded. Do not worry, he told her, go and cleanse yourself, set your clothing right, and then bring some water and salt and wash this from the saddle, and take your place again. No anger. No embarrassment. No making her feel the thing she dreaded. Only calm.

Years later she would say that the only gift the Prophet ﷺ gave to the women on that journey, since there had been no fighting to earn a share of spoils, was a necklace he tied around her neck with his own hand. She wore it for the rest of her life and asked that it be buried with her when she died. This, the Sheikh says, is how the Prophet ﷺ met a moment that most men would have made unbearable for a child: he took the one who was frightened, the one with no power to protect herself from the shame of it, and he made her feel safe.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to file Khaybar under its conquest and move on, the way history files most things, under the thing that was won. The aftermath quietly refuses that. It keeps pulling your eye away from the walls and toward the hearts, and that is the question it puts to us.

He could not choose between a conquest and a cousin's face. Set Ja'far's return on one scale and Khaybar's surrender on the other, and the Prophet ﷺ, in his moment of triumph, found them level. We are the opposite by instinct. Our victories swell to fill the whole sky, and the people we love stand quietly in their shadow, certain they will keep. The Prophet ﷺ held his win lightly and his brother dearly, and the seerah leaves the scale sitting there for us to weigh our own lives against. Ask, honestly, which one of yours would tip.

And then there is Safiyyah, who is really the answer the whole day is built around. She had every earthly reason that a human heart can have to hate. Her father, her brother, her husband, her tribe, her whole world, taken in twenty days by the army of the man she now stood before. By every law of the wounded soul she should have been a sealed vault of vengeance. Instead she answered his hardest test with a verse of the Qur'an, that no bearer of burdens bears the burden of another, and chose his faith over her freedom, and lived and died loving him, and at his deathbed wished only to carry his pain. The Sheikh says it twice, because it is the lesson: this shows a pure heart, and it shows that Islam is true. Most of us carry burdens that were never ours to begin with, the resentments of our families, the bitterness of where we came from, the scores our people kept long before we were born. Safiyyah set hers down. No soul carries the sin of another. You will answer for your own heart, and for nothing that was placed in it by someone else, and you are free, this very day, to put down what was never yours to hold.

Notice, too, where his tenderness landed. Not on the powerful, but on the powerless. The lying chief who knew the consequences of his lie met the hard edge of his age; the world Khaybar belonged to was not gentle, and the Sheikh will not pretend otherwise. But the lonely widow, the taunted wife with no clan, the frightened child on his camel: to each of them the Prophet ﷺ gave dignity, calm, a defense, a necklace tied with his own hand. The measure of a person, this day teaches, is not how they handle the one who can fight back. It is how they treat the one who cannot. He met the moment each of them dreaded most, and he made the dreading one feel safe.

So carry three things out of the days after Khaybar. Hold your wins loosely enough that a beloved face can outweigh them. Set down the burden you have been carrying that was never yours, the inherited grudge, the family bitterness, the sin of someone who came before you, because no soul bears the burden of another. And in every room where you hold the power and someone else holds none, be the one who makes the frightened feel safe. May Allah fill our hearts with the love Safiyyah had for His Messenger ﷺ, free us of every bitterness we were handed and never chose, let no soul among us be made to carry a burden that is not its own, and gather us with His Prophet ﷺ and all those he loved.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 70: the Battle of Khaybar, part 2 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translation is from Sahih International (35:18), verified via quran.ai. The narration is the Sheikh's; the phrasing is Buruja's.

The Seerah · 71 of 104

Letters to the Kings

A man from the desert, writing to emperors

Stand back far enough to see the scale of it. Twenty years before this, the man at the center of the story was an orphan from a desert town, a trader with no army, no treasury, no throne, no map of the world beyond the caravan roads his uncles walked. Now he ﷺ sits in Madinah and dictates letters to the two superpowers who, between them, owned the known earth: to Heraclius, the Caesar of Rome, and to Chosroes the Sasanian, emperor of Persia. And not only to them. To the Negus of Abyssinia, to the Muqawqis who governed Egypt, to the Arab princes on the Roman and Persian frontiers, to the chiefs of Oman and a dozen tribes besides. The scholars have counted more than twenty-five such letters in all.

They were not sent in a single morning. Dr. Yasir Qadhi is careful about this: the letters went out at different times, to different men, and the historians simply gathered them into one chapter at roughly this point because it makes sense to read them together. They began around the truce of Hudaybiyyah, perhaps a little before Khaybar, and kept going until the end of his life ﷺ. What binds them is not a date but an ambition no orphan trader has any business holding: the conviction that the whole earth was now his audience.

This is the chapter of the letters. Who answered, who scoffed, who tore the page to pieces and what became of his kingdom. And at the heart of it, one of the most astonishing scenes in the entire seerah, told not by a believer but by an enemy: Abu Sufyan, dragged before the emperor of Rome and forced, against his own will, to testify.

Aiming at the whole world

The timing tells you everything. The Prophet ﷺ did not write to emperors while Makkah still had a sword at his throat; he waited until Hudaybiyyah had bought peace on the Makkan front, and only then turned outward, all the way outward, not to a rival tribe or a neighboring town but to the men who divided the map between them. And Dr. Yasir Qadhi asks you to notice not only who received a letter but who did not. The Prophet ﷺ wrote to Rome, to Persia, to Abyssinia, to Egypt, to the Arab rulers on the borders. He did not write to China, nor to the heart of Africa, though everyone in Arabia knew those civilizations existed, that beyond Abyssinia lay a whole continent and beyond Persia the lands of the Chinese. Why the silence toward them? Because there was no road of contact, no channel of trade or diplomacy along which a letter could travel and an answer return. This was not the fantasy of a man overreaching. It was a deliberate, pragmatic decision to write to exactly those powers with whom the Arabs already had dealings, and to announce that a new nation had risen, one fit to exchange ambassadors with Caesar and Chosroes. The message of Islam was being declared, on purpose, global. And here is the quiet miracle the Sheikh wants you to hold from the first page: within a single decade of these letters going out, there was not one land he ﷺ wrote to that had not entered the fold of Islam.

Read the letters side by side and a discipline emerges. Each one opens with the same line, Bismillah ar-Rahman ar-Raheem, and then, from Muhammad, the slave and messenger of Allah, to so and so. He names himself and he names his reader. He gives each ruler his proudest title, the great one of Rome, the great one of Persia, never a slight in the address. He states the entire religion in four or five plain sentences: there is one God, worship Him alone. No tangled theology, no display of learning. And then each letter is tailored, with real precision, to the man who will read it, as you are about to see. This is the etiquette of a head of state, and he ﷺ used it deliberately. Told that emperors will not even open a letter unless it is sealed in wax with the sender's own stamp, he had a ring engraved and his letters sealed, adopting the world's diplomacy without hesitation, because there was nothing of his religion in the old way of doing things and nothing forbidden in the new.

The Negus, who only needed a nudge

The first letter, and by far the one that truly landed, went to the Negus of Abyssinia, the just Christian king across the sea who, years earlier, had opened his land to the persecuted Muslims when Makkah hunted them and had refused to hand them back. Negus was his title, the way Caesar was a title; his name was Ashama. The Prophet ﷺ wrote to him about Isa, peace be upon him, summarizing in a few words what a Muslim believes: that he is the messenger of Allah, His spirit, and the pure word cast to Maryam. Then the gentle close: Aslim taslam, accept Islam and you will be safe. And he sealed the letter with the verse of Al Imran, the call to a word held in common, which you will meet again in the letter to Rome.

Say, "O People of the Scripture, come to a word that is equitable between us and you - that we will not worship except Allah and not associate anything with Him and not take one another as lords instead of Allah." But if they turn away, then say, "Bear witness that we are Muslims [submitting to Him]."Qur'an 3:64

Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi clears up a tangle that confuses many readers. People assume the Negus accepted Islam more than a decade earlier, on the day Jafar gave his famous speech in the throne room. But if he had become a Muslim then, why would the Prophet ﷺ be writing now to invite him? The likelier truth is that the Negus had long been sympathetic, leaning toward the faith without ever fully entering it, and this letter was the gentle push that finally brought him in. And notice what is conspicuously absent from his letter. The stern warning that closes the letters to Rome and to Persia, that the sin of your people will be upon you if you refuse, never appears here. The Negus was spared that line entirely. He did not need a threat. He needed only an invitation, and he answered it.

The proof of how much his faith meant arrived at his death, in the ninth year after the hijrah. On the very morning the Negus passed away in Abyssinia, the Prophet ﷺ turned to his Companions in Madinah and told them: your brother has died in the land of Habasha; come, let us pray over him. And they stood and prayed the funeral prayer for a man whose body lay an entire sea away, never washed by their hands, never carried on their shoulders. The Sheikh pauses here, because this is the single clearest instance in the whole seerah of salat al-ghaib, the funeral prayer for the absent, and the foundation of what he considers the soundest position in a long-disputed question of fiqh: that the absent prayer is offered for a believer when no one prayed over him where he died. The Negus had embraced Islam, but his people had not, so there was no one in Abyssinia to pray for their king, and the Muslims of Madinah prayed for him across the water.

Heraclius interrogates an enemy

The most famous of all these letters went to Caesar, and it is no minor report; it is preserved in both Bukhari and Muslim, agreed upon, of the highest authenticity. The Caesar of that day was Heraclius, who reigned from the year 610 to 641, and both Muslim and non-Muslim historians treat him generously. He was a true warrior and a shrewd politician, and, rare among emperors, a serious student of Christian theology, a man who understood the great controversy that had torn his religion for centuries, the question of who exactly Isa was, and who even tried, and failed, to engineer a compromise creed to reconcile its warring factions. He was no figurehead reciting prayers he did not understand. He knew what belief was, and how to recognize it.

Allah had already written this man's story into the Qur'an before the letter ever reached him. The Romans had been crushed by the Persians, who had swept through Iraq and Syria and taken Damascus, and even Jerusalem itself, so that for a span of years it was the Persians, not the Romans, who held the holy city. It looked like the end of Rome. And then the revelation came down.

Alif, Lam, Meem. The Byzantines have been defeated In the nearest land. But they, after their defeat, will overcome Within three to nine years. To Allah belongs the command [i.e., decree] before and after. And that day the believers will rejoice In the victory of Allah. He gives victory to whom He wills, and He is the Exalted in Might, the Merciful.Qur'an 30:1-5

The verse fell while Rome was on its knees, and then, exactly as promised, Heraclius accomplished a brave regrouping of his shattered armies, launched a counteroffensive, and by the year 628 had driven all the way to the gates of the Persian capital. All of it unfolded while the Prophet ﷺ was alive to watch the prophecy come true to the letter.

The letter to Heraclius traveled through the governor of Bosra, the small Arab town with Roman culture south of Damascus where the Makkan caravans traded, and it happened to find the emperor in Jerusalem. Whether the Prophet ﷺ knew Caesar would be there, the Sheikh says, we cannot tell; most likely it was simply the way Allah arranged it. And here the narration passes, astonishingly, into the mouth of an enemy. Abu Sufyan, still a pagan, still the chief of Makkah's opposition, happened to be trading in Syria when a crier summoned his party to the emperor's court. He did not understand why, until he found himself, a son of Quraysh, standing in the palace of Caesar before the emperor of Rome and all his dignitaries, the Arab translator at the ready.

Watch the emperor's tactic, because the Sheikh calls it the move of a genuinely intelligent ruler. Heraclius knew these Arabs were enemies of the man claiming prophethood, knew they would happily slander him. So how do you extract the truth from a hostile witness? You set him against his own companions. Heraclius asked which of them was the closest in blood to this man, and the answer was Abu Sufyan, a distant cousin, the nearest relation in the room. Sit him in front, the emperor ordered, and the rest behind him where he cannot see their faces, and he told the men at the back: if he lies to me, signal it. Abu Sufyan understood at once that any one of them would gladly catch him out for the prize and the honor of it, and he confessed afterward that the only thing that kept him from lying about the Prophet ﷺ was the certainty that his own companions would expose him on the spot.
