So the truth came out, one question at a time. Is he of noble family? Yes. Did anyone claim prophethood before him? No. Were his ancestors kings? No. Do the powerful follow him, or the weak? The weak, and they keep increasing. Does anyone abandon his religion once they enter it? No, not one. Have you known him to lie, or to break a promise, or to betray? No; we have a treaty with him now whose end we cannot foresee, the one thing Abu Sufyan could squeeze in. And what does he command? To worship Allah alone and abandon what our forefathers worshipped, and to pray, to speak the truth, to be chaste, and to keep the ties of kinship.

How could a man honest about gold lie about God

Then Heraclius did something extraordinary. He read every answer back, and out of an enemy's grudging testimony he reconstructed, point by point, a prophet. Dr. Yasir Qadhi walks through the emperor's reasoning, because it is a masterclass in how the truth defends itself.

His noble family, Heraclius said, is how every prophet of God comes, from a noble house among his people. That no one claimed prophethood before him means this is no fashion he is merely copying. That no kings stood in his lineage means this is no scheme to win back a lost throne. That the poor and the weak follow him first is the mark of every prophet, for the powerful have the most to lose from the truth and are the last to bow to it. That his followers grow and never leave is the mark of true belief, that once it settles in a heart it never departs.

And then the line the Sheikh lingers over, the hinge of the whole scene. You have told me, Heraclius said, that this man never once lied to you about gold and silver, never cheated you in a matter of money. So tell me: how could a man who would not lie to you over a coin turn around and invent the single greatest lie of all, a lie against God Himself? It does not add up. Sit with the strangeness of it. A pagan enemy is being handed the very logic of belief by a Christian emperor in a Roman court, and the proof is the Prophet's ﷺ own honesty, the honesty Makkah itself had testified to for forty years before they ever called him a liar.

Then Heraclius said it plainly. If what you say is true, this man will one day rule the very ground beneath my feet. We knew from our scriptures that a prophet was coming, but we did not expect him from among you Arabs; we looked for him elsewhere. And were I able to reach him, I would wash his feet with my own hands. Only now, after he had built the man in his mind from an enemy's words, did Heraclius call for the letter to be opened. It was short, like all the rest: from Muhammad, the slave and messenger of Allah, to Heraclius, the great one of Rome. Accept Islam and you will be safe; accept Islam, and Allah will grant you your reward twice over; but if you turn away, then the sin of the common people will be upon you. And then, once more, the verse of Al Imran.

The court erupted in uproar, and Abu Sufyan's party was rushed out. As they left, he muttered to his companions that the affair of this man had grown so great that even the emperor of Rome now stood in fear of him. That moment, Abu Sufyan would confess long afterward, was the first time Islam ever entered his heart, though he would not embrace it openly until the conquest of Makkah years later, and even then, the Sheikh notes without any disrespect, with some reluctance. As Allah Himself distinguishes them, those who came to faith late, after the victory was assured, are not in the same rank as those who came early, when it cost everything.

The spy from Tanukh, and an emperor who counted the cost

There is a sequel that Bukhari does not narrate but that the Musnad of Imam Ahmad preserves, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi insists you need it, because without it the rest of the Bukhari account makes no sense. Heraclius wrote back to Madinah, but the letter was a cover. What he truly sent was a spy: a Christian Arab he trusted from the tribe of Tanukh, whose name the histories never preserved, so the report is known simply as the hadith of the Tanukhi. The emperor gave him three things to verify. Does this man mention the letters he has sent to the kings of the earth? Does his speech somehow turn on the word night? And is there a strange mark upon his back, between his shoulders?

The man from Tanukh arrived, and the Prophet ﷺ, without so much as opening the emperor's letter, told him: I sent my letter to Chosroes, and he tore it, so Allah will tear his kingdom; and I sent my letter to Caesar, and he preserved it, so Allah will preserve his kingdom. The first sign, delivered before any question was asked. Then the letter was read aloud, and inside was a riddle: your scripture describes a Paradise as vast as the heavens and the earth; if that is so, then where is the Fire? And the Prophet ﷺ answered with a question of his own: where does the night go when the day comes upon it? The second sign, the word night placed exactly where the emperor had said to listen for it. The spy lingered for days, failing to glimpse the Prophet's ﷺ back beneath his garment, until at the moment of his departure the Prophet ﷺ turned, lowered his shirt, and let him see the seal of prophethood between his shoulders. Three signs sought, three confirmed, and the Tanukhi carried them home.

Only now does the second Bukhari scene make sense, the one readers so often mistake for the first. This is not the day of Abu Sufyan; it is a year or more later, after the spy's report has come back. Behind closed doors Heraclius gathered his senators and floated the question aloud: what would you say if I were to follow this messenger and enter his religion? The room bolted for the doors in fury; the empire was the very bastion of Christianity, and there would be no emperor who left the faith of Jesus. Seeing the danger, Heraclius laughed it off at once, told them he had only been testing their loyalty, and remained a Christian to the end of his days. He had seen the truth as clearly as a man can see it, weighed it against his crown, and kept the crown. And his own prediction came due faster than he can have imagined: within seven years of his standing in Jerusalem, Damascus and Syria and Palestine were carved away from his empire by, as the Sheikh puts it, a band of Bedouins out of the desert.

Chosroes tears the letter

Rome preserved the letter. Persia did the opposite, and the contrast is the spine of this chapter. The emperor of Persia was Chosroes, known across our cultures by his title Parvez, the last of the truly great Sasanian kings, painted by Muslim and non-Muslim historians alike as cruel and given to every excess. The Prophet ﷺ sent his letter through the governor of Bahrain: from Muhammad the messenger of Allah to Chosroes, the great one of Persia. Peace upon whoever follows the guidance. Accept Islam and be safe; and if you refuse, then the sin of the Magians is upon you. There was no verse of Al Imran in this letter, the Sheikh points out, and the omission is itself a lesson: that verse calls to the People of the Scripture, and Chosroes the fire-worshipper was no person of the Book.

The emperor was insulted to his core that a preacher from the deserts of Arabia would address him as though they were equals. He scoffed, and he tore the letter to shreds in the messenger's face. When word of it reached Madinah, the Prophet ﷺ said: he has torn my letter; may Allah tear his kingdom to shreds, every last shred of it. And so it unfolded. Chosroes, in his arrogance, ordered his governor in the Yemen to fetch this man from Madinah by force, as though two envoys could drag back the Prophet ﷺ whom all of Quraysh in their full strength had failed to stop. But before the order could be carried out, his own son rose against him in a palace coup, threw him into a dungeon, and starved him to death over days. When the two envoys finally stood before the Prophet ﷺ, he refused even to take the letter the first day, and then told them: go back to your master and tell him that my Lord has killed his lord, Chosroes, this very day. They returned to Persia to discover it had happened exactly so.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi stops hard on the dates here, and the point is sharp enough to draw blood. The Western records fix the death of Chosroes to late February of the year 628, and that lands precisely on the month in which the seerah places this conversation in Madinah. You cannot, the Sheikh argues against those who would dismiss such reports, fabricate a seam that fine: a prediction spoken in Madinah, matched to an emperor's death in Persia, down to the very season. And the larger collapse was just as exact. Within nine years the entire Sasanian empire, the very civilization that had so nearly erased Rome from the earth, imploded so completely that the sacred fires of Zoroaster went cold in the land of Zoroaster itself, and Persia became Muslim. The governor in the Yemen and his two envoys embraced Islam, and great numbers of their region with them.

Egypt, and a verse for the rest

The next batch of letters went out across the map. To the Muqawqis, the title of the ruler of Egypt, the Prophet ﷺ wrote a letter whose exact text the histories do not soundly preserve, but whose reply they remember well. The Muqawqis was courteous; he declined the faith, but sent gifts in return, among them the mule Duldul and the maidservant Mariyah, who would become the mother of the Prophet's ﷺ infant son Ibrahim. Of this ruler the Prophet ﷺ is reported to have said that the man had safeguarded his kingdom for a time by his courtesy, but that Allah would not let it endure. Letters went also to the rulers of Oman, who accepted, and to a long list of Arab chiefs; the scholars count more than twenty in all.

One of those letters carried a lesson worth more than its brevity suggests. It went to the tribe of Banu Hanifa, and among them to a man named Musaylimah, who would soon make his name infamous as the false prophet. Banu Hanifa sent back not a refusal but a bargain: we will accept Islam, they said, if you share your authority with us, if you give us a portion of the rule. The Prophet ﷺ answered with the Qur'an, the very words Allah had given to Musa before him.

Said Moses to his people, "Seek help through Allah and be patient. Indeed, the earth belongs to Allah. He causes to inherit it whom He wills of His servants. And the [best] outcome is for the righteous."Qur'an 7:128

The kingdom of God is not a seat to be split in a negotiation. You know, the Sheikh reminds you, what became of Musaylimah after that.

And here Dr. Yasir Qadhi offers the reading that is most distinctly his own, and the one he finds strongest. Each letter to Rome and to Persia warned its emperor that he would carry the sin of his people, and in the letter to Caesar that burden was named with a single word that has puzzled the scholars for fourteen centuries: al-Arisiyyin. It is not an Arabic word at all, and the classical commentators on Bukhari struggled with it, most guessing that it meant the common peasants. But the Sheikh follows the great Indian scholar Abul Hasan Ali Nadwi, who read it as the followers of Arius, the early Christian theologian who died around the year 336. Arius had taught a doctrine of Isa, peace be upon him, that, while not Islam, ran closer to its truth than any other Christian sect, denying his divinity; and for that his teaching was condemned and very nearly erased at the Council of Nicaea three centuries before the Prophet ﷺ was born, his books ordered burned until almost nothing of his own words survived. Two things make this reading compelling. First, al-Arisiyyin is precisely how Arabic would render the followers of Arius, and not at all how it would name peasants. And second, the parallel with Persia: there the burden was the sin of the Magians, a specific community, so the matching word to Caesar should name a specific community too, not a vague mass of commoners. If Nadwi is right, then the Prophet ﷺ, sitting in a desert where no living Arab had ever heard of Arius or his quarrel with Nicaea, was telling the emperor of Rome: there is a people among your Christians already primed to believe; block my message from reaching them, and their loss is on your account. It is a quiet proof of how far the awareness of this unlettered man ﷺ reached beyond the sands that raised him.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read the chapter of the letters as a stately diplomatic interlude between the battles, a list of envoys and replies, and move on. That would be to miss what it puts to us. Strip away the thrones and the seals, and four things in it are still asking something of your own life.

He thought in decades, not in days. Almost none of these letters produced an immediate result; only the Negus answered with his own faith, and even his people did not follow him in his lifetime. By every measure a politician would use, the campaign of the letters failed in the short term. And yet within ten years every single land he ﷺ addressed had come home to Islam. He was planting for a harvest he would not gather in person. We want our good deeds to bear fruit by the end of the week, and we abandon what does not. Aim far, and do not measure the seed by tomorrow's ground.

He spoke to every man in his own register, and honored each one with his best name. The letter to the Christian kings carried a verse about the People of the Scripture; the letter to the fire-worshipping emperor did not, because that verse would have meant nothing to him. He addressed Caesar as the great one of Rome and Chosroes as the great one of Persia, granting his enemies their proudest titles even as he called them to surrender their gods. And told that kings expect a sealed letter, he had a ring made and sealed his, without the faintest fear that courtesy might compromise his faith. Meeting people where they stand, in their own idiom and with their dignity intact, is not weakness; it is the manner of a prophet, and much of what we defend as religion was only ever custom.

He kept the message short, and clean, and unafraid. One paragraph. One God, worship Him alone. No tangled theology, no hedging, no padding to seem learned. The strongest invitation is almost always the simplest one, opened with the name of Allah and addressed plainly from you to them. We complicate the call until it is heavy enough to carry no one; he made it light enough that an emperor could read it in a breath and never forget it.

And at the center of it all stands the scene the Sheikh returns to, the one that should stay with you longest, because it is not a believer who delivers it. It is the truth defending itself through the mouth of an enemy. Abu Sufyan, who wanted nothing more than to slander the Prophet ﷺ before Caesar, could not, because his own honesty had a witness at his back; and from his grudging answers a Christian emperor assembled the proof of prophethood: a man who would not lie to you about a coin of silver will not invent a lie against God. This is what your own integrity is worth. When you are truthful in the small and unwatched matters of gold and silver, you become a witness for your own faith without saying a word, so that even those who stand against you are forced, in the end, to testify on your behalf. And yet Heraclius saw all of this, weighed it honestly, and still chose his crown over the truth he had just proven to himself. The whole of his tragedy was that he knew, and would not follow; for it is not enough to recognize the truth.

So carry one thing from the chapter of the letters down into an ordinary week. Plant something whose harvest you may never live to see, and trust Allah with the season of it. Speak to people gently, in their own words, by their best names. And guard your honesty in the smallest matters of money and speech, until your life itself becomes the letter, sealed and unmistakable, that invites others to the truth. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, who sat in a desert town and wrote to the masters of the earth as one certain that his Lord owned every throne among them, carry His word to the furthest ends of the earth in our own time as it reached the kings of his, and make each of us, in the small and unwatched corners of our lives, a witness He is pleased to accept.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 71: the letters to the various rulers (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (3:64, 30:1-5, 7:128), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 72 of 104

Umrah al-Qada

The promise of Hudaybiyyah, kept

A year before this month, the Muslims had set out for Makkah dressed for worship and been turned back at the edge of the sanctuary, never reaching the Ka'bah at all. The treaty they signed there at Hudaybiyyah, the one so many of them wept over because it read to them like a defeat, carried inside it a single quiet promise: not this year, but next, you may come, and the city will be yours for three days. This is the year the promise comes due, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi opens the story by counting umrahs in a way that turns the whole episode on its head before it has even begun.

They call it Umrah al-Qada, the umrah of making up. Qada means to make good a thing you owed, and on the surface this is the make-up for the umrah they were not permitted to complete. But notice what the Sheikh insists you not miss. In the ledger of Allah, that first umrah was never lost. At Hudaybiyyah the Prophet ﷺ and his companions had shaved their heads, slaughtered their animals, and been given the full reward of the umrah even though they never performed a single circuit of the Ka'bah. So when you are asked one day how many umrahs the Prophet ﷺ performed and you begin to count, you begin, strangely, with Hudaybiyyah, the one he did not actually do, because Allah counted it for him. Which means this one is in fact the first umrah he physically completed, and yet the second he was rewarded for: not a debt being collected, but a mercy handed back, twice over, to people who had once thought they were going home empty.

Two thousand, and this time armed

In the month of Dhul-Qadah, in the seventh year after the Hijrah, the Prophet ﷺ left Madinah for Makkah at the head of around two thousand Muslims. Set that beside the year before, when fourteen hundred had gone unarmed and been stopped on the road, and you feel the ground shifting. And this time they did not go empty-handed: they brought everything an army carries, horses, bows, armor, the full apparatus of war. Not to break the treaty. To be ready in case the Quraysh broke it first.

Word raced ahead to Makkah that two thousand armed Muslims were on the approach, and the Quraysh sent out a delegation to meet them, led by Mikraz ibn Hafs, the same envoy who had negotiated at Hudaybiyyah the year before. He came uneasy, and what he said is a confession dressed as a complaint. We have never once known you to be treacherous, he told the Prophet ﷺ, in anything small or great. So what is this? We agreed you would enter with nothing but sheathed swords, the bare minimum a traveler carries, and here you come with horses and bows and arrows and armor.

The answer was exact, a small masterclass in keeping a contract to the letter without surrendering your wits. We shall not enter Makkah, the Prophet ﷺ said, except as we agreed. We are not in Makkah yet. He had brought the weapons the whole way to the boundary of the haram, and there he halted them. He left the full arsenal outside the sacred precinct, posted two hundred men to guard the cache, and entered carrying only swords in their scabbards, exactly as the treaty stipulated. Then, so that no man would be cheated of the umrah, the guard was rotated: the eighteen hundred performed their tawaf while two hundred watched the weapons, and afterward they swapped. The Sheikh lingers on the double truth of it. The Prophet ﷺ did not trust the Quraysh, not after all they had done, so he kept his weapons within arm's reach. And he honored every word of his agreement, so he left those weapons at the line. Faithful to the contract, never naive about the people on the other side of it. Both at once, and the both-at-once is the lesson.

A city that locked its doors

When the Muslims came in, Makkah split three ways. Some families bolted themselves inside their houses for all three days. Another group gathered in Dar an-Nadwah, the assembly hall that doubled as their parliament, the largest house in the city, and shut the door behind them. And some left Makkah altogether, climbing out into the surrounding valleys and hills to camp until the visitors were gone. The reasons were a mix the Sheikh names plainly: partly politics, partly raw fear. Not one of them wanted to be in the city for this. They watched from behind doors and over ridgelines, waiting for it to be over.

And from inside that locked assembly hall, a rumor went out ahead of the Muslims to do its work on the crowd. The bulk of the Quraysh, the women, the elderly, all those who had not gone out to war, had not laid eyes on their emigrant sons and brothers and cousins in six and a half years, and in a society where family lived knotted together that is an ache. Someone decided to poison the reunion before it could happen. Madinah has ruined them, the whisper ran; its climate and its standing water and the diseases that come with them have left them thin and emaciated, weaklings now. And there was a real wound buried in the lie, which is why it could travel. When the companions first emigrated, the climate of Madinah, so unlike the dry sparse air of Makkah, had made them violently sick. Abu Bakr fell ill and thought he was dying. Bilal lay burning with fever, reciting poetry about how beautiful Makkah had been. The Prophet ﷺ had prayed, and Allah lifted the sickness of Madinah and cast it to an uninhabited place. The rumor took that true memory of a hard arrival and twisted it into a present lie: your emigrants have come home broken men.

When the Prophet ﷺ heard the rumor, he answered it, and his answer was not a word. It was a body, two thousand bodies, moving in front of the people who had told the lie. He commanded the companions three things at once. Raise your voices with the talbiyah, out loud, so the whole city hears it ring. Bare your right shoulders. And for the first three circuits of the tawaf, move with ramal, a brisk, near-running stride, as fast as a man can go without quite breaking into a sprint.

Picture the scene the Quraysh saw. Two thousand men in ihram, right shoulders bared, almost jogging around the Ka'bah, the talbiyah rolling off them in a single roar that filled the valley. From their rooftops and their hilltops the Quraysh watched, and the verdict came back from their own mouths: by God, there is nothing wrong with these people. We have never seen them stronger. And the Sheikh stops here to point out how much of what we still do was born in that single hour as an answer to a slander. The bared right shoulder and the quickened pace of the first three circuits, kept by every pilgrim to this day, were instituted right there, to refute a lie about a feeble ummah. So too the talbiyah: the Quraysh had one laced with shirk, claiming Allah had a partner who was His own, and the Muslims cut it clean at the truth, here am I, O Allah, here am I, You have no partner, and there they stop. That pure declaration of tawhid was now being broadcast at full voice over the heads of the very men who had driven its reciters out of the city.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi will not let this pass as mere spectacle, because it matters far beyond that day. This is image. This is, in his exact word for it, legitimate PR, and it is a standard thread of the seerah, not a departure from it. He gathers the parallels. This is the same Prophet ﷺ who at Uhud watched Abu Dujanah strut between the lines in his red turban and said of a gait that would otherwise look like pure arrogance, this is a walk Allah hates, except in a place like this. The same Prophet ﷺ who at Hudaybiyyah, learning that the envoy came from a tribe that revered the sacrificial camels, had the camels paraded before the man so he would see them garlanded and marked. The same Prophet ﷺ who now shows a watching Makkah, by the bared shoulder and the running feet, that his ummah is anything but broken. The lesson he draws is aimed straight at us. When people say Islam is violent, or harsh, or cruel, our task is to correct the picture they were handed, to show the accusation was false to begin with. And he draws the boundary as sharply as the permission. You do not soften the deen to please the audience; if a man objects to something Islam actually commands, you do not bend the religion to win him. You correct false charges; you do not negotiate away true ones. Guard the theology and the ethics untouched, and answer every lie told about them with the truth, in strength and dignity.

Home, after six and a half years

The books of seerah say almost nothing about those three days inside Makkah, and the Sheikh reads the silence the way a scholar should: nothing strange happened, or it would have been recorded. The Prophet ﷺ stayed, he prayed, and we can assume he led the companions in the five prayers before the Ka'bah while the Quraysh kept their distance and kept their word. Three quiet, peaceful days. But under that silence the Sheikh asks you to feel for something the records never thought to capture.

This was the city of his whole life. Fifty-three years of it. He ﷺ was walking past the house of Khadijah, past the corners where he had grown from an orphan boy into a man, past every street that carried a memory, the city of his mother and his father and his grandfather, the city of Abu Talib, the city of everyone he had loved and buried. Think, the Sheikh says, of how it feels simply to return to a house you have not lived in for years, how forgotten memories come flooding back the moment you cross the threshold. Now take that ordinary ache and multiply it by an entire lifetime, and by a forced exile under threat of death, and by the grave of the first woman who ever believed in him. We are not told what he felt. We are left only to imagine it, and the imagining, the Sheikh suggests, is its own quiet kind of worship, the heart leaning toward his heart across the centuries.

The marriage to Maymunah

On the way out, his uncle al-Abbas, radiyallahu anhu, made a suggestion that would close a chapter of the Prophet's ﷺ life: marry Maymunah. She was a widow now, a believing woman with no husband and no protector in a city of disbelievers she did not wish to remain among. And she was family by marriage: al-Abbas's wife, Umm al-Fadl, was her sister. Maymunah had asked her sister to find her a husband; the family turned to al-Abbas; and al-Abbas, now her guardian, brought the proposal to the Prophet ﷺ, praising her warmly. He knew her from the old Makkan days, and he agreed. So Maymunah became his wife.

She would be the last woman the Prophet ﷺ ever married, and, decades on, the last of the Mothers of the Believers to pass away, dying in the year sixty-one of the Hijrah. And there is a small aching symmetry the Sheikh pauses over. The marriage was settled at a place called Sarif, just outside Makkah, where the caravan would camp on the way home. Many years later, an old woman now, returning toward Makkah to perform an umrah of her own, Maymunah died at that very same Sarif, and was buried there, where she lies to this day. She married at Sarif and she was laid to rest at Sarif, a whole lifetime folded between the two.

Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi handles a famous knot of fiqh with care, because it has been misread for centuries and it touches the honor of a great companion. The Prophet's ﷺ cousin Ibn Abbas reported that the marriage contract was concluded while the Prophet ﷺ was in the state of ihram. The report is authentic, which is precisely why it stirred such a long debate, since by the law one does not contract a marriage while in ihram. But the cleanest resolution comes from the one person who would know this marriage best, the bride herself: Maymunah said plainly that he ﷺ had already exited ihram when he married her, that he was in the state of hil on the day of the contract. The Sheikh's verdict is gentle and firm at once. This was simply an honest mistake on Ibn Abbas's part, corrected by Maymunah, and there is no shame whatsoever in saying so. A companion is not infallible in his reports; only the Prophet ﷺ is protected from error. To note that one narration was mistaken, and to follow the better-placed witness, is not to diminish Ibn Abbas in the slightest; it is simply how the truth is established. The consummation, in any case, came only later, at Sarif, by everyone's account.

A feast they would not stay for

On the third day the Quraysh came with a single message: the time is up. And the Prophet ﷺ, who did not want to leave, tried to renegotiate. Would it really harm you, he asked them, if I stayed among you a little longer? I have just married. Let me be a groom in your city, and let us lay out a great feast tomorrow, and you come and eat with me. He was offering the people who had exiled him a wedding celebration, using his own marriage to win a few more days in the city that held his heart. The Quraysh would not bend. We have no need of your food, they said, with an edge the Sheikh notes was frankly a little rude. Three days are up. Peace be upon you, now go. So he honored the treaty and left without incident, completing the marriage only afterward, at Sarif.

But the Sheikh draws out the thing the Prophet ﷺ was reaching for in that offer, because it is the heart of who this man was. These were the same people who had besieged Madinah at the Trench, who had done every single thing they had done across twenty years. And now, for the first time, the upper hand was unmistakably his, and his instinct in that position of strength was not to crush them. It was to feed them. He used his own wedding as dawah: let me throw you a party, break bread at my table. Because, the Sheikh says, when you sit and eat with someone, hatred loosens its grip and anger settles. The Prophet ﷺ no longer needed to stand on the defensive; he could afford, at last, to be generous, and generosity here was not softness but strategy, the strategy of the strong. The Quraysh were too hard of heart to take the offered hand, and the Sheikh suspects their leaders knew why they refused: they could not risk their people feeling something warm toward the Muslims, the boycott of the heart dissolving over a shared meal. But the hand was extended all the same. And the Sheikh ties it without a seam to our own lives: this is who we are, a people who will sit and eat even with those who oppose us, hoping a hard heart might soften over food.

The orphan everyone wanted

As the caravan filed out of Makkah, a small girl, six or seven years old, came running after them, calling out: O my uncle, O my uncle, do not leave me here, take me with you. She had been living in Makkah with extended family since her father's death, and now she ran from them toward the departing Muslims, a child somehow choosing this caravan over the only relatives she knew. She was Umayrah, the daughter of Hamza, the Prophet's ﷺ uncle and the martyr of Uhud. Her mother was the sister of the famous companion Asma bint Umays, and the threads of kinship run so thick through this small society that, as the Sheikh notes, by his new marriage to Maymunah the Prophet ﷺ had even become a kind of uncle to the running girl. Everyone in Makkah, in the end, was related to everyone.

Ali ibn Abi Talib, radiyallahu anhu, took her by the hand and gave her to his wife Fatimah to raise. And then it became a happy quarrel among the greatest men of the ummah. Zayd ibn Harithah stepped forward to claim her: the Prophet ﷺ made me the brother of Hamza when we paired the emigrants and the helpers, so his daughter is my charge. Ja'far ibn Abi Talib stepped forward too: she is my cousin, Hamza is my uncle, and more than that, my wife Asma is the girl's maternal aunt. Three of the noblest men alive, each pressing his right to take in one small orphan girl, each insisting that he, above the others, was the one most fit to care for her.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi will not let you walk past this, because it is one of the quiet revolutions of the whole seerah. Fifteen years earlier, he says, no one would have fought for this child. A girl, and an orphan, in the Makkah that buried infant daughters in the sand, she would have been fortunate merely to be kept alive. And now three of the finest men in the world are competing for the honor of raising her, each marshaling his merits, each certain he has the most right. That is what Islam did to the worth of a girl in a single generation. Measure a faith by what it does for the smallest, most forgettable person in the room, and watch what this one did. The Prophet ﷺ settled it with exquisite tenderness, raising each man up before he chose, so not one of them would walk away feeling cast aside. To Ali: you are from me and I am from you. To Zayd: you are our brother and our freed servant and our trusted one. To Ja'far: you resemble me most, in your features and in your manners. Only after lifting all three did he give the verdict, awarding the girl to Ja'far on a principle that passed into the law of Islam: the maternal aunt holds the place of the mother. Ja'far's wife was the girl's aunt, and so she would be raised as near to a mother as she could come.

The last to come before the door opened

This was, the Sheikh says, the final psychological blow. The Conquest of Makkah was now just around the corner, and the proof of how completely the tide had turned lies in what happened in the very months after Umrah al-Qada: three of the most important men in all of Makkah crossed over to Islam, and the Sheikh insists you read it as no coincidence. They had seen the two thousand with their own eyes, heard the talbiyah of tawhid roll over their rooftops, and understood that the contest was effectively decided.

Amr ibn al-As came first, by one of the strangest roads in the entire seerah. The brilliant diplomat of the Quraysh decided after the Trench that Makkah's fall was only a matter of time, and resolved to wait it out under the Negus rather than ever live under Muhammad ﷺ. He sailed to Abyssinia, and there, in the king's court, he asked leave to kill an envoy the Prophet ﷺ had sent. The Negus struck his own nose in fury and revealed the thing Amr had never suspected: that he, the emperor who was supposed to be Amr's refuge from this Prophet, was himself a secret believer. Follow this man, the Negus told him, for by God he is upon the truth, and he will be victorious over his enemies as Moses was victorious over Pharaoh. And the Sheikh marks the marvel of it: this is the one recorded case of a tabi'i, one who lived in the Prophet's ﷺ time but never saw him, giving dawah to a man who would become a companion. Amr embraced Islam there in the court, overwhelmed that the truth had run ahead of him to the ends of the earth and was waiting when he arrived. He told no one, made his excuses, and turned back toward Makkah.

On that desert road he met two other men with their bags packed and their faces set the same direction, and the meeting sealed the most consequential conversion of the three. Khalid ibn al-Walid was the finest soldier the Quraysh had ever produced, the son of al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah, chief of the Banu Makhzum: the proud poet the Quraysh once sent to pronounce a verdict on the new revelation, who instead retreated into his house to scheme against it, and whom the Qur'an itself exposed:

Leave Me with the one I created alone And to whom I granted extensive wealth And children present [with him]Qur'an 74:11-13

The father had plotted in the dark, and his Lord had answered the plotting:

Indeed, he thought and deliberated. So may he be destroyed [for] how he deliberated.Qur'an 74:18-19

That was the line al-Walid was born into. But the son took a different road. Khalid told his own conversion in the first person for the rest of his life: when Allah desired good for me, he said, He cast the love of Islam into my heart. He had fought in every battle against the Prophet ﷺ and sensed, every single time, that he was being turned back, that something beyond his strength stood in his way. He had broken the Muslim line at Uhud; he had been sent to intercept the Prophet ﷺ on the road to Hudaybiyyah and been quietly outmaneuvered, a sign, the Sheikh notes, that the better commander was the one with Allah in his sight. After Hudaybiyyah it became plain to him that this man would prevail, and he asked himself where he could possibly go. Then came Umrah al-Qada, and a letter from his own brother al-Walid ibn al-Walid, already a Muslim, carrying the news that the Prophet ﷺ had asked after Khalid by name, had said a man of his caliber should not be kept from Islam, and wished him among the believers. It has become as clear as daylight, Khalid said at last; how long will we keep denying him? He set out for Madinah, and on the road he found Amr, and with them came the third: Uthman ibn Talha, of the clan that held the very keys of the Ka'bah.

The three of them walked into Madinah together, and the Sheikh asks you to see them as a single sign written by the hand of Allah: the custodian of the sacred house, the master strategist, and the master statesman, the religious, military, and political leadership of the Quraysh, all surrendering at once. Read that as decree, he says, not as chance. When Amr came to give his pledge, he held back the Prophet's ﷺ hand and made a condition first: that all his past sins be forgiven, that he begin with a clean slate. And the Prophet ﷺ told him the threefold mercy that is the born Muslim's hope as much as the convert's: do you not know, O Amr, that Islam erases what came before it, that the Hijrah erases what came before it, and that the Hajj erases what came before it? They had come in late, after years on the other side. But Allah had already drawn, in His Book, the very line that made room for them, distinguishing those who gave and fought before the conquest from those who came after, and then, in the same breath, promising the best of rewards to both:

And why do you not spend in the cause of Allah while to Allah belongs the heritage of the heavens and the earth? Not equal among you are those who spent before the conquest [of Makkah] and fought [and those who did so after it]. Those are greater in degree than they who spent afterwards and fought. But to all Allah has promised the best [reward]. And Allah, of what you do, is Aware.Qur'an 57:10

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read Umrah al-Qada as a victory lap, a triumphant little episode tucked safely between Khaybar and the Conquest, and move on. That would be to miss what it is really teaching: how to carry strength once you finally have it, and how long a promise from Allah can take to ripen without ever spoiling.

Begin with the weapons left at the line. The Prophet ﷺ trusted his Lord completely and did not for one moment trust the Quraysh, holding both truths in the same hands, carrying the full arsenal to the boundary of the haram and then leaving it there. We tend to collapse into one error or the other: a naive trust that calls all caution a failure of faith, or an anxious caution that leaves no room for trust at all. His way was to tie the camel and rely on Allah, to keep every weapon ready and to honor every word of the agreement, and the both-at-once is the discipline most of us never quite learn. Faithful to the letter, never naive about the people. That is the sunnah of the strong believer.

Then there is the bared shoulder and the running tawaf. A lie had been told about the believers, that they were weak and broken, and the Prophet ﷺ answered it not by arguing but by demonstrating, correcting the false picture without altering a single thing about the religion itself. This is the narrow path we walk when our faith is slandered. We do not change the deen to win approval, and we do not sulk in indifference to how it is seen; we answer the lie with the truth, in dignity and strength, guarding the line between refuting a false charge and bending to a true command. The accusation is false; the religion is not on trial; show them what is actually there.

And finally there is the wedding feast no one came to, the orphan girl three great men fought to raise, and the latecomers Allah promised the best reward. Watch what the Prophet ﷺ did the moment the upper hand was his: he offered his enemies a meal, he raised a forgotten child with tenderness, he opened the door wide to the men who had fought him hardest and told them their slate was wiped clean. He had every reason, by the world's accounting, to be harsh, and he chose generosity, because generosity was the strategy of a man who no longer had anything to fear. The lesson under all of it is patience that does not curdle into bitterness. A year before, these same believers had walked away from Makkah empty-handed, clutching a treaty that looked like a humiliation. They held on, they trusted the promise, the promise came due, and the city that had cast them out locked its doors and watched them circle the house of their father Ibrahim. What looks like a setback in the hand of Allah can be the very doorway to everything. So when your own road bends away from what you wanted, hold the promise and keep your hands open. And if Allah ever hands you the upper hand over someone who wronged you, remember the feast offered to the Quraysh, and that the strongest thing a believer can do with power is to extend, instead of a sword, a place at the table. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, who kept every promise his Lord gave him, who returned to the most beloved of cities not to take revenge but to worship and to invite, and who taught us that there is room, until the very last moment, for the latecomer to come home.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 72: Umrah al-Qada (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (74:11-13, 74:18-19, 57:10), verified via quran.ai. Where a companion's narration was weighed against another, the Sheikh's own judgment of the reports has been followed.

The Seerah · 73 of 104

The Battle of Mu'tah, part 1

Three commanders, one flag, and the sword that would not fall

Before the grand finale of the conquest of Makkah, the seerah pauses for one more story, and it is unlike any that has come before it. For the first time the Muslims will march not against an Arab tribe but toward the edge of the Roman world, the superpower of the age, the only non-Arab enemy the believers would ever face while the Prophet ﷺ still walked among them. They will set out expecting one foe and meet many. And they will go led, by his ﷺ own arrangement, by three commanders named one after another, because he understood before they left that not all of them were coming home.

The sahaba did not call it a ghazwah. Dr. Yasir Qadhi is careful here at the very threshold of the story, because the name itself carries a lesson. A ghazwah, by the definition of the early scholars, is a battle the Prophet ﷺ personally took part in, and he did not march north to Mu'tah. Most of the early and even the medieval historians broke their own rule and called it one anyway, struck by the sheer size of the army that went and the staggering identity of the enemy. But the companions themselves had a better name. They called it the expedition of the commanders, because in no other battle of his entire life did the Prophet ﷺ ever appoint so many leaders, one to follow the next. Mu'tah is simply the name of a small plain in what is today the kingdom of Jordan, where the modern Arabs call the village al-Mazar, the place of visitation, because the three commanders who fell there lie buried in its earth to this day.

An envoy murdered at the border

It begins with a crossed line. The Prophet ﷺ had sent a letter north, carried by his envoy al-Harith ibn Umayr, headed for the Roman emperor himself. To reach Rome you had to pass through the lands of the Arab Christian tribes who served the empire as its frontier, and the largest of these were the Ghassanids, a people originally of Yemen who had migrated north, taken on Christianity, and felt more kinship with the Romans than with their pagan Arab cousins. They were vassals: subservient clients who did whatever the empire asked, and were protected in return. So when the Prophet's ﷺ envoy came through, the Ghassanid chieftain Shurahbil seized him. And when al-Harith said what every envoy in history has said, I am a messenger, you cannot touch me, here is my letter for the emperor, the chief ignored all of it. He had al-Harith tied to a stake, brutally tortured, and then, with his own hands, killed him.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi asks you to feel the full weight of this, because the seerah turns on it. Ambassadors are not killed; that has been the rule of humanity for as long as there have been nations, and it holds to this day, when even bitter enemies expel an envoy rather than harm him. To order a minion to do the deed would already be a grave violation. To take the spear yourself and drive it in, after the man has been bound and tortured, is something cruder still: a deliberate, vulgar insult, a chieftain seven hundred kilometers away saying, this is what I think of you, and you are too far off to do anything about it. It was a declaration of war in the plainest terms. And now that the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah had at last secured the homeland, so that the Prophet ﷺ no longer had to fear a knife at his back from Quraysh, he was finally free to answer it. Scholars after him would search for deeper reasons, and one rings true as a wisdom even if it was not the direct cause: Mu'tah laid the psychological groundwork that this message was global and would not stop at the Arabs, a first domino before Tabuk and the conquests of Sham. But the earliest and strongest reason is the simplest one. A messenger had been murdered, and the murder demanded a reply.

The expedition of the commanders

In Jumada al-Ula of the eighth year after the hijrah, the Prophet ﷺ called for volunteers, and he told them plainly where they were going, because this was the farthest any Muslim army had ever traveled: a month and a half on the road, there and back, to the very borders of Sham. There could be no ambiguity, for a man needed to know what he was committing to before he committed. Around three thousand answered. Sit with that number for a moment, the way the Sheikh does. At Badr, six years earlier, the believers were three hundred. Even at Hudaybiyyah, a year and a half before, they were perhaps fourteen or fifteen hundred. In six years the army had not doubled or tripled but grown tenfold, and these were only the ones who marched out; there were others left behind in the city. Madinah was becoming a metropolis, the capital of a civilization that had not yet been born.

Then the Prophet ﷺ did something he had never done before and would never do again: he named three commanders, each to follow the last. Zayd ibn Haritha would lead. If Zayd fell, Ja'far ibn Abi Talib. If Ja'far fell, Abdullah ibn Rawaha. And in one narration he added a fourth instruction, that if Abdullah too were killed, the Muslims should choose a leader from among themselves on the spot. No other expedition in his entire life was given a chain of command like this. The arrangement itself was the warning. He knew, in a way the volunteers did not yet know, that this was no ordinary raid, and that he was sending some of the most beloved men in Islam toward a danger from which they would not all return.

Why Zayd, and the cousin's quiet hurt

Of all the men he could have placed first, the Prophet ﷺ chose Zayd ibn Haritha, and Aisha, radiyallahu anha, later explained why no one should be surprised: the Prophet ﷺ never once sent Zayd on an expedition except as its commander, never as second to anyone, and had Zayd outlived him, she said, no one would ever have been chosen above him. Think of who is saying this. Aisha is the daughter of Abu Bakr, and still she testifies that Zayd would have stood first. This is the Zayd who, as a boy given the choice between freedom beside his own father and slavery beside Muhammad ﷺ, chose to stay, saying the love this man has shown me, no one else on earth could ever show. He was so woven into the household that for years the people simply called him Zayd ibn Muhammad, until Allah revealed that adopted sons are not your true sons and must be called by the names of their own fathers.

Call them by [the names of] their fathers; it is more just in the sight of Allāh. But if you do not know their fathers - then they are [still] your brothers in religion and those entrusted to you. And there is no blame upon you for that in which you have erred but [only for] what your hearts intended. And ever is Allāh Forgiving and Merciful.Qur'an 33:5

After that verse he was Zayd ibn Haritha again, by his father's name; and yet, the Sheikh reminds us, he holds an honor no other companion holds, for he is the only one of them whom Allah named directly in the Qur'an. Abu Bakr himself comes only as a pronoun, his companion, in the cave. Zayd alone is named. To place such a man at the head of the column was the most natural thing in the world.

And yet Ja'far ibn Abi Talib, the Prophet's ﷺ own cousin and the elder brother of Ali, felt a small sting at being placed second. The Prophet ﷺ sensed it, as he sensed everything in the hearts of those he loved, and consoled him with a single sentence: you do not know, perhaps Allah has written something better for you in this. It was true beyond anything Ja'far could have guessed. Coming second in line was exactly how he would earn the death that made his name unforgettable, the death that placed him among the most honored martyrs in all of Islam. The hurt of being second became the doorway to a glory the first place could never have given him.

The farewell, and the poet who longed to die

The Prophet ﷺ handed Zayd a white banner, the standard the army would march under, no flag of surrender but simply the colors an army moves behind, and he walked the three thousand out to Thaniyyat al-Wada', the hill of farewell, where Madinah had always accompanied its travelers to the edge of leaving. There he gave his commanders their final counsel, to be firm and not to turn back, and made dua for them. And there the third commander, Abdullah ibn Rawaha, was seen weeping.

Asked why he wept, he gave an answer that has nothing of cowardice in it. I am not crying out of love for this world, he said, nor out of any attachment to it. I am crying because of a verse Allah has revealed, that every single one of us shall pass over it, meaning the Fire, a promise from Allah that will surely be kept.

And there is none of you except he will come to it. This is upon your Lord an inevitability decreed.Qur'an 19:71

He had suddenly seen himself at that crossing, the Sheikh explains, and the tears were for that, not for the road ahead. He had managed the leaving of Madinah; how would he manage the crossing over the Fire? That was the thought that broke him.

This was Abdullah ibn Rawaha: the great poet of the believers, an Ansari and among the elite of the Ansar who had pledged at Aqabah. He had fought at Badr, and he was the one the Prophet ﷺ sent ahead from there to carry the news of that first victory to Madinah, riding into the city crying out the names of the fallen chiefs of Quraysh until people thought him deranged. He was the poet whose verses against Quraysh the Prophet ﷺ defended when Umar bristled at poetry near the sacred precinct, saying let him be, for his words strike them harder than a shower of arrows. All through the journey north this man composed poetry begging Allah for shahada, for a mighty sword blow that would make the blood gush, so that travelers passing his grave one day would say: Allah guided this man to be a warrior, and how rightly guided he was. One night, riding beside young Zayd, who had been raised in his household, his verses about his own death grew so moving that the boy burst into tears at the thought of losing him. Abdullah, ever light of heart even on the road to his death, swatted him gently with his camel stick and teased: you fool, what would it cost you if I get my martyrdom, and you get the camel to yourself on the way back? That, in the end, is exactly what happened.

An enemy beyond anything they expected

The Ghassanids panicked when word reached them that an army larger than any that had ever left Madinah was on its way. They sent emissaries flying to every ally they had: the Christian Arab tribes of Lakhm, Judham, and others, and a contingent from the Romans themselves. The classical books then pile up the numbers until they become almost unimaginable, a hundred thousand, even two hundred thousand Romans alone. Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi asks you to think critically, the way he does throughout the seerah. He is not accusing the early historians of lying; it is simple human nature to look at a vast crowd and read its number far too high, a thing so well documented that in our own day two rival news channels will count the very same political rally and report figures wildly apart. The whole of the northern Arab tribes did not number a hundred thousand fighting men, and the Romans, who would not have thrown such a host even against the Persians, would certainly not have committed it against a band of Arab Muslims they did not yet take seriously. The more honest estimate is perhaps ten thousand at the very most. Whatever the exact count, one thing is beyond doubt: the Muslims were heavily outnumbered, and worse, they had not come prepared for this enemy at all.

For the Romans were a different breed of soldier altogether: better armed, better trained, better mounted, a professional military class beside the tribal fighters of Arabia. The sahaba knew it, and the knowledge stopped them in their tracks. When the news reached the marching column, the army halted and camped for two full days in a valley of what is now Jordan, debating what to do. This was not what they had set out for. They had expected the Ghassanids alone, against whom three thousand believers were enough; now they faced double or triple that, with a Roman contingent among them, and a hard choice split the camp in two. One group said: let us write to the Prophet ﷺ and wait for his command, even if it costs two weeks, and do whatever he tells us. The other said: we came to send a message and we have sent it, we stepped onto their land and frightened them, let us go home. The decision hung in the balance.

Zayd turned to Abdullah ibn Rawaha and asked his opinion, and this itself is a moment the Sheikh pauses on: in the hard hour you turn to the people of wisdom and seniority, not to the novices. There was tactic in it too, for Zayd surely knew already what Abdullah would say, but he wanted the most eloquent tongue in the army to say it. Abdullah stood, and gave a speech that settled everything. My people, he said, what you are afraid of is the very thing you came out looking for. You are afraid of death, but is martyrdom not exactly what we want? Your goal itself has become the thing standing between you and your goal. We have never won by numbers or by weapons, but by this Islam with which Allah has honored us, and He has promised us one of two beautiful outcomes. So let us go forward and meet whichever of the two He decrees.

Say, "Do you await for us except one of the two best things [i.e., martyrdom or victory] while we await for you that Allāh will afflict you with punishment from Himself or at our hands? So wait; indeed we, along with you, are waiting."Qur'an 9:52

It was the rousing word the army needed. They rose and moved on. And here is a spirit the Sheikh wants you to see with perfect clarity, because it is easily and dangerously misread. Later in the fighting, when a wide-eyed Abu Hurayra, a recent convert tasting his very first battle, marveled aloud at the size of the enemy host, an older companion answered him simply: you were not with us at Badr, and we did not win that day because we were many. The sahaba expected to win. Wanting shahada is not the same as walking into death on purpose. Abdullah ibn Rawaha longed for martyrdom with his whole heart and still fought a ferocious battle to live, still chose ground with water at hand and a line of retreat behind him. They were brave, not suicidal, and the difference, the Sheikh insists, is everything. They went forward confident of victory, ready for either of the two beautiful things, and afraid of neither.

Three commanders fall, one after the other

At Mu'tah the armies met, on that small plain the Muslims themselves had chosen, almost certainly for its access to water and its line of retreat, and the white banner the Prophet ﷺ had pressed into Zayd's hand became the center of the entire battle. Zayd charged from the front, and because the flag was in his hand the enemy converged on him from every side, for the banner is what an army hunts. He fought surrounded until he fell, a martyr, the first of the three exactly as the Prophet ﷺ had named him.

The banner passed to Ja'far ibn Abi Talib, and Ja'far fought one of the most ferocious fights ever recorded in the whole history of Islam. When his horse was hamstrung beneath him he leapt clear and fought on foot, deep in the enemy, the flag in one hand and his sword in the other. A blow severed the hand that held the banner; before the standard could touch the ground his other hand swept across and caught it. They cut off that second hand too, and still he would not let it fall: he bent, gathered the banner against his chest with the two stumps of his arms, and held it upright until a Roman soldier came at him from behind with one of those great Roman swords and cleaved him almost in two. When they searched for his body afterward, Abdullah ibn Umar, who fought in the battle, said they found Ja'far beneath a heap of the dead, and counted more than ninety wounds across him, stabs and cuts and arrow marks, around fifty in his front and forty in his back. Pause over that grim arithmetic, the Sheikh says, and ask why a man would count the wounds of a corpse. It is not morbid; it is a badge of honor. Every one of those wounds faced the enemy; not one was earned in flight. Years later, whenever Umar ibn al-Khattab passed the children of Ja'far, he would greet them with a name that is now part of the faith: O sons of the one with two wings. For the Prophet ﷺ said he had been shown Ja'far in Paradise, given two wings in place of the two hands he lost, flying wherever he wished.

Then the banner came to Abdullah ibn Rawaha, and here the seerah hands you something so human it should stop your heart. The poet who had begged for death the entire journey, who had wept at the farewell longing for the crossing, now stood at the very edge of it, and hesitated. He turned upon his own soul and fought it like an enemy on the field. I swear, O my soul, he said, you will go forward, or I will force you forward. The people have gathered, the clamor has risen, so what is the matter with you? Did you not used to long for Paradise? You were nothing but a drop of fluid in a despised vessel; what makes you so precious to yourself now? Then he stopped arguing, plunged into the enemy ranks, and died a martyr, the third of the three.

Do not read his pause as fear getting the better of him, the Sheikh insists, and his refutation here is worth carrying out of the chapter, for some have tried to mock this scene. Read it instead as the very definition of bravery. Bravery is not the absence of fear, for a man who feels no fear is not truly human at all. Bravery is feeling the fear and conquering it. Abdullah felt it, mastered it, and went, and which of us could even have lifted the sword he carried, let alone walked it into that host? Three of the most beloved men in Islam had now fallen, one after another, in the exact order the Prophet ﷺ had named them in Madinah before they ever set out. The white banner lay in the dust of Mu'tah, and the army, leaderless in the middle of the fight, needed a hand to lift it. Who reached for it, and what he did with it, is tomorrow's story. But mark this much now: the hand that would close around that flag belonged to a man who, only weeks before, had been among the most dangerous enemies the Muslims had ever faced, whose sword had bled them at Uhud. Islam forgives everything, the Sheikh notes, and the hour calls for whoever the hour calls for. Tomorrow we learn his name.

Grief in Madinah, hundreds of miles away

And here is the speciality of this episode, the thing that sets it apart from every battle before it. As the fighting raged some eight hundred kilometers to the north, the Prophet ﷺ sat in the masjid in Madinah and described it as it was happening, in real time, before any messenger could possibly arrive. Anas ibn Malik narrated it, and the report is in Bukhari, rigorously authentic. The Prophet ﷺ told the gathered companions: Zayd took the flag and was struck, then Ja'far took it and was struck, then Abdullah took it and was struck, and Anas said his eyes were overflowing with tears as he spoke, until at last he said: then a sword from the swords of Allah took it up, and Allah granted them victory through him.

When the confirmed news arrived from the north, the Prophet ﷺ sat down, and the grief upon him was plain to everyone who saw it. Think of who he had lost in a single afternoon. Zayd, who had been in his household for some thirty years, since before the prophethood, the closest thing to a son a man ever had until Allah revealed otherwise. Ja'far, whom he had embraced and kissed on his return from Abyssinia, saying he did not know which made him happier, that reunion or the conquest he had just won. Abdullah, his companion and poet from the early days of Madinah. He was a man in shock, and the seerah does not hide it. Aisha watched from behind her curtain as a man came reporting that the women of Ja'far's house were wailing, and the Prophet ﷺ, three times over, sent him back to quiet them, and three times the man returned having failed, until at last, worn down and grieving, the Prophet ﷺ said: then go and throw dust in their mouths.

The Sheikh unpacks this gently, because it can bewilder a reader meeting it for the first time. It is an Arabic expression of sheer exhaustion, not a literal command; the Prophet ﷺ was a man under the weight of loss, the wailing of the bereaved is forbidden in Islam but a thing these women had not yet been tested against, and the messenger kept returning with a problem no one on earth could fix. Notice too how delicately Aisha tells it: she says only that the man mentioned their weeping, covering the fault of the women rather than naming the wailing for what it was. And notice how fiercely she defends her husband. She turned on the man herself, from the corner of the room: may Allah put dust in your own nose, for you neither did what he told you nor spared him this suffering. No one defends a husband like his wife when she means to, and she meant to. It is one of the most human images in the whole seerah: a Prophet ﷺ undone by love and loss, sitting down under the weight of it, and a wife fierce in his defense. His grief takes nothing at all from his certainty; it only shows you how much love a single heart can carry. And on that grief the story pauses, with the sword of Allah still to be named, and the unexpected hand still closing, far to the north, around a banner in the dust.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read Mu'tah as a stirring tale of valor and leave it there. That would be to miss the questions it presses into the reader, for this is among the hardest and the tenderest days in the whole life of the Prophet ﷺ, and it asks four things of anyone who sits with it honestly.

It asks you to plan as though the worst were real, and to trust as though Allah held everything, and to know these are not opposites. The Prophet ﷺ named three commanders in a row because he faced the danger with open eyes, not because his trust in Allah had wavered for an instant. Careful preparation and reliance upon Allah are two hands of the same faith, and he gave us both in a single decision. We are quick to imagine that to prepare thoroughly is somehow to doubt, when the Prophet's ﷺ own example is the reverse: prepare for the worst, and place the outcome with Allah.

It asks you to understand what courage actually is. Abdullah ibn Rawaha begged for martyrdom every mile of the journey, then stood at its threshold and hesitated, then turned on his own trembling soul and drove it forward into Paradise. A person who feels no fear is not brave; he is merely unfeeling, less than fully human. The courage is in the going, in the conquering of a fear that is genuinely there. Whatever you most dread in obedience to Allah, the going is the bravery, and the fear you feel on the way is not its enemy but its raw material.

It asks you to stop counting. You were not with us at Badr, the old companion told the wide-eyed Abu Hurayra, and we did not win that day because we were many. When you find yourself outmatched, outnumbered, sure the odds have already decided the thing, the seerah turns the question around: it was never about the count. It was about the faith. And it asks you, in the same breath, never to confuse loving Paradise with throwing your life away. These men longed for shahada and still fought with everything to win, still chose ground with water and a way back; wanting death in Allah's path never once meant courting it recklessly, and anyone who reads their longing as a license for despair has read it exactly backward.

And last, it asks you to let even the grief teach you. The most God-conscious man who ever lived wept openly for three friends fallen far away, sat down under the weight of it, lost his words, and was defended by a wife who loved him. Grief is not weak faith. It is the heart of a believer breaking honestly and beautifully, and it sits in him alongside a certainty that never cracks. So take this day down into your own. Plan for the hardest thing and hand the result to Allah. Feel your fear and go anyway. Weigh your faith, not your odds. And when loss comes, let your heart break without shame, knowing that the tears of the Prophet ﷺ took nothing from his trust in his Lord. O Allah, send Your peace upon Your Messenger ﷺ, and upon Zayd, and Ja'far, and Abdullah, and all who fell at Mu'tah; grant us their love of meeting You, their courage in the face of fear, and a good ending after which there is no thirst, and save us from the Fire that every one of us must pass over.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 73: the Battle of Mu'tah, part 1 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (33:5, 19:71, 9:52), verified via quran.ai.

The Seerah · 74 of 104

The Battle of Mu'tah, part 2

How a retreat became the Sword of Allah

Yesterday the three commanders fell one after another, Zayd, then Ja'far, then Abdullah ibn Rawahah, and a single black banner kept being lifted out of the dust and pressed into the next pair of hands. Today the seerah finishes the story of Mu'tah, and it does not finish the way we want our stories to finish. There is no charge that turns the field, no rescue from the south, no miracle that bends the day back toward the Muslims. There are three thousand believers stranded in the far north against an empire, with no commander left to lead them and no victory left to win. What happens next is something quieter than triumph, and in its own way harder: a young man who had been a Muslim for only a few months looks at an impossible field and decides that the only victory still on offer is to bring the army home alive. By the end of it he will carry a title that reaches all the way into the records of the Roman court.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi begins the way an honest historian must, with a warning about the evidence. For the whole of this day, he reminds us, we really have only two or three explicit narrations from which the entire story must be derived. The picture cannot be painted in broad confident strokes; it has to be drawn from a few hard lines, each one weighed, the firm reported as firm and the doubtful as doubtful, and the gaps left honestly as gaps. Mu'tah tests that discipline more than most days, because so little of it was ever written down.

The man who broke nine swords

The clearest of those hard lines comes from Khalid ibn al-Walid's own mouth. On the day of Mu'tah, he would later say, nine swords broke in my hand, until nothing was left to me but a single Yemeni blade. Sit with what a man must be doing to shatter nine swords in one battle, one after another, swinging each until it failed and reaching for the next. That is not the fighting of a soldier holding a position. That is the fighting of a man holding an entire line together by the force of his own arm, and it tells you, even with no other narration, exactly what kind of struggle was keeping three thousand men from being overrun.

Khalid had seen what no one else wanted to say out loud. There was no real victory to be had on this field, none at all. The Romans and their Christian Arab allies were too many; the Muslims could not break them and could not hope to. The only victory still possible, he understood, was to preserve the Muslim army from complete destruction, to save it from massacre. So he fought a two part battle. First came a sharp, sudden push, a quick short term attack whose entire purpose was to make the Romans pause in their onslaught, to stagger them just long enough to break their momentum. Part of how he did it, in the one detail that survives, was to position his archers to blunt the advancing line. One of those archers was an old man whose arm was still strong but whose eyes had failed him. He told his two companions to lift him onto a shield and carry him as high as they could, and he would shoot as hard as he could while they were his eyes: turn me this way, turn me that way. He could no longer see the enemy, but he could still loose an arrow, and he would not be left out of the work.

By the time night fell, Khalid had managed to pry the two armies apart, just past the reach of a bow and arrow. Through the darkness the two forces could still see one another, but no longer kill one another; they were within sight but out of range. The Muslims took shelter behind a hill, safe at least for the night, and only then, in that pause, could the bodies that had lain on the field all day finally be gathered and buried. The three leaders, Zayd ibn Harithah and Ja'far ibn Abi Talib and Abdullah ibn Rawahah, were laid, the books say, together in a single grave.

A storm of dust, and a way home

About the next morning, the classical books fall silent. Sheikh Yasir is careful here, and the carefulness is itself a lesson. Nothing explicit about the second day appears in the early sources he could find. What he did find, in one of the later books, was a strategy, and he hands it on exactly as that: a tactic preserved by a later author, source unknown, that fits everything else we know about Khalid but that cannot be traced back with certainty. The honest historian neither swallows it whole nor throws it away. He sets it on the table and tells you where it came from.

The strategy was this. Khalid needed the Romans to hesitate long enough for three thousand exhausted men to slip away, because to retreat in full view of the enemy is to be hunted down and cut to pieces on the open road. The one thing that reliably makes an army stop is the fear of reinforcements. So Khalid manufactured exactly that fear. He sent a body of his companions far out across an expanse of sand, spread thin, with instruments to beat up the dust as they drove forward through it, so that from a distance it looked for all the world like hundreds of fresh horsemen riding in from Madinah. The Romans and the Christian Arabs saw the dust cloud gathering on the horizon and did the sensible thing. They paused. They were not about to throw themselves on the Muslims if a second army was about to arrive at the Muslims' back. And in those few hours of hesitation, the Muslim army disappeared, withdrew, and reached safety. Had they tried to leave before the eyes of the Romans, they would have been followed and massacred on the road. The illusion of help that was never coming bought them their lives. On the way home, the army passed the same village that had earlier ambushed and killed one of their own, and there they took their justice from it; the books are not precise about what they did, only that the wrong was answered.

Then Sheikh Yasir asks you to stop and sit with the arithmetic, because the arithmetic is the heart of the whole matter. Out of three thousand men, facing a vastly superior fighting force, the names we have of the fallen number around fifteen, with perhaps another five or ten whose names were never recorded. Less than one percent of the army. Faced with those odds, against that enemy, the fact that more than ninety-nine of every hundred men came home is, in its own strange way, an extraordinary thing. It is not the arithmetic of a rout. It is the arithmetic of a rescue.

Victory, loss, or something in between

So was Mu'tah a victory or a defeat? The Sheikh lays out three honest answers, and refuses to flatten them into one. The first group of scholars called it a victory, and not a small one. Among them were great experts of the classical tradition, and their evidence ran along three lines. The Muslims by and large returned successfully, with less than one percent lost. They carried some booty home with them, which is true; we will come to one of those stories. And, decisively for them, the Prophet ﷺ himself, when he had described the falling of the three commanders one after the other with tears in his eyes, then said that a sword from the swords of Allah took the banner, and Allah granted them victory. The argument from there is almost a theological one: if the Prophet ﷺ called it a fath, a victory, then a fath it is, in the same way that Allah names a thing and the thing is so. Nothing more need be said.

A second group, the historians, and every non-Muslim chronicler who wrote of the day, called it a defeat. They looked at it through a military and political lens, and through that lens the verdict was plain. The Muslims lost three of their most significant leaders, one after another. They lost the banner. They had to retreat while the Romans held their ground, and the one who retreats and leaves the field, generally speaking, is not the victor. A third group called it neither, a draw, neither side a clear victor. This was the position of more than one careful scholar, and their reasoning was that at the end neither side launched a frontal assault on the other, neither side took prisoners, the dead were roughly equal, and both armies simply returned home to their own lands.

Here the Sheikh does something you will watch him do again and again across this series. He declines to force the day into a single shape. Each of the three opinions, he says, is correct from where it stands. If the Prophet ﷺ called it a fath, then it is a fath, though not in the narrow military sense; it may be a victory of one kind and not another, the way Hudaybiyyah was a long term victory that felt, in the moment, like anything but. The historians have a real point: the Romans kept their land, the Muslims conquered not a single inch, and they went home. And the third group is weighty too, because the enemy did not annihilate the Muslims but let them go. So the Prophet ﷺ called it a victory, and it was, in the truest sense available to it: the lives that were saved are the victory, not an inch of conquered ground.

Then Sheikh Yasir names something braver, and it is one of the most distinctly his judgments of the whole episode. Part of the trouble, he says, is that many of our own historians want every single battle to come out shining for the Muslims, and in wanting that they miss the wisdom and the mercy hidden in the battles that do not. It is actually a great benefit, he insists, that some battles are not so positive, because Allah is teaching us, through His own beloved companions, that you will not always be handed everything on a silver platter. Sometimes you have to struggle. Sometimes even the best generation that ever lived makes a sound judgment that does not pay off in the short term. And if you only ever read the seerah as one unbroken chain of triumphs, then on the day your own life refuses to come out shining, you will have nothing to hold. Whereas if you have seen even this generation struggle, you find a strange comfort waiting for you exactly when you need it. Loss honestly told is not a wound to faith. It is a steadying of it.

Not runaways, but the ones who will return

The Prophet ﷺ welcomed the survivors home and rejoiced at their return. But within a few days the joy curdled into something ugly. A smear campaign began against the men who had come back, and we know about it because of one quiet detail preserved in the books of hadith. The Prophet ﷺ noticed that a companion named Salamah had stopped appearing in the masjid, and he asked after him: what is the matter, is he well, why do I not see him? Salamah's wife answered him. He has not come out of the house, she said, because every time he steps outside, people make fun of him and rebuke him. They call after him: O you who ran away, O you who fled, have you fled from the path of Allah? And so Salamah sat in his house, hiding from the mockery.

Some of those voices, the Sheikh notes, may have belonged to hypocrites looking for any wound to open. Some may have belonged to earnest believers stung by a kind of overzealous anger, men who had not gone and could not understand how anyone could turn his back on the enemy and live. It was probably a mixture of both. The men who had actually fought and survived were being branded cowards who had abandoned the field. The Prophet ﷺ ended the entire campaign with a single word. He took the slur they were using, al-fararun, the runaways, the ones who flee, and he changed one letter of it. No, he said, they are not the fararun. They are al-karrarun, the ones who will return and fight again. With that one change he turned an accusation into an honor and shut every mocking tongue. They had not fled as cowards. They had saved themselves so that they could come back and stand another day. This, the Sheikh says, was from the wisdom and the long sight of the Prophet ﷺ, to take the very word being used as a weapon and reforge it into praise.

And this ruling, he is careful to add, is not a softening of the religion but its very law, and he asks plainly that it not be misunderstood. Genuine, legitimate martyrdom is a goal every believer carries in his heart; no one denies that, and the Prophet ﷺ himself made it something to long for.
