But you do not walk into a battle simply wanting to die, throwing your life away with your sword doing nothing, because if every warrior did that there would be no army and nothing left of Islam to defend. The overzealous thought, how dare you turn your backs; the ones who had fled felt the guilt of it, which is why Salamah sat at home wondering whether he should have stayed and died. The Prophet ﷺ settled it for all of them. Foolish martyrdom is not bravery; reckless death is not courage. And Allah Himself had already drawn the line in the Qur'an, forbidding flight from the battlefield while permitting the tactical withdrawal:

O you who have believed, when you meet those who disbelieve advancing [in battle], do not turn to them your backs [in flight].Qur'an 8:15
And whoever turns his back to them on such a day, unless swerving [as a strategy] for war or joining [another] company, has certainly returned with anger [upon him] from Allah, and his refuge is Hell - and wretched is the destination.Qur'an 8:16

Allah does not permit fleeing out of cowardice. He does permit swerving as a maneuver of war, and joining back up with one's own forces to fight again. The men of Mu'tah had done exactly the second thing, and not the first. They were al-karrarun, the ones who would return.

After today, let no one weep

Then the Prophet ﷺ turned to the grief inside his own family. Send food to the family of Ja'far, he commanded, for something has befallen them that has left them too occupied to cook for themselves. And so food was prepared and sent to them. After three days he went to them himself. Abdullah ibn Ja'far, who was only six or seven at the time and who narrates the memory as a grown man, never forgot what he saw. The Prophet ﷺ came and called the children to him, and their hair was matted and wild; the very word in the hadith is that they looked like little chicks, every hair on end, because their mother, having just lost her husband and already traumatized by all that we heard last week, was in no state to comb their hair. The Prophet ﷺ saw it and took charge even of this. He called for a barber and had their hair seen to himself. Then he spoke to each child in turn, and to each he gave something that made the child feel singled out and held. To Muhammad ibn Ja'far he said, this one looks just like my uncle Abu Talib. To Abdullah he said, this one looks like me and behaves like me. He took the boy's hand, the hand of the one who would now be the man of the house, raised it, and prayed three times: O Allah, bless the progeny of Ja'far, and bless Abdullah in everything his hands touch, in all his buying and selling and dealings. And as for their father, he told them, who had been killed with both his hands cut off, Allah has given him two wings in place of his two hands, and he flies wherever he wishes in Paradise.

Their mother was Asma bint Umays, and Sheikh Yasir lingers on her story because of what it quietly buries. She was Ja'far's wife; after he was martyred she married Abu Bakr, and from him came Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr; and after Abu Bakr passed away she married Ali. Three of the greatest men of this ummah, in turn, and children from each. There was no stigma of divorce or widowhood among the companions the way it would later harden in Muslim societies, where a widowed woman was treated as though she might as well live out her days alone. That attitude simply did not exist among them, and a famous, gentle anecdote proves it. Two of Asma's sons, Muhammad ibn Ja'far and Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr, half-brothers through their mother, began to boast about whose father was nobler. Ali sat watching, and to tease his own wife, whose two former husbands were precisely the two men in question, he said: let your mother be the judge. With perfect wisdom Asma answered: as for the young men, the best is Ja'far, and as for the senior, wise men, the best is Abu Bakr. She had named both as the finest of their kind, leaving neither son defeated. And Ali laughed: then what have you left for me? Read that scene, the Sheikh says, and look for the tension certain groups insist must have lain between these families. Here is Ali, married to Abu Bakr's former wife, joking about whether Abu Bakr or his own brother Ja'far was the nobler man, and teasing her when she answers too well. It is so plainly the joking of a household at ease with itself that the supposed enmity becomes ludicrous to imagine.

There is a whole school of manners folded into this passage. When death visits a household, it is a sunnah for those close to the family to prepare food for them; the grieving should not be made to cook. It is a sunnah to visit with words of consolation, but not to linger, for it is disliked to turn the house of mourning into a long social gathering; Jarir the companion said they used to count gathering at the bereaved house and being fed there as part of the forbidden wailing. And there is a limit on grief itself. Mourning is permitted for three days, the legitimate grief of a heavy heart, of tears, of not feeling like eating, of taking time away. To go beyond three days into wailing, shrieking, beating oneself, crying out claims that border on disbelief, who will care for me, how will I live, that is forbidden. Only the widow keeps a longer term, four months and ten days, and even hers is a term of dignity, not of wailing. So even here, the Sheikh notes, the Prophet ﷺ could not perfectly control the grief in his own extended family in the first raw days; he tried, through a messenger, three times, and when it could not be stopped he let it be. Then on the third day he came himself and gently closed it: after today, let no one weep over my brother. Now was the time to return to life. Time, as we all learn, heals every wound.

The Sword of Allah, named in Rome

There is a strange and beautiful footnote to Mu'tah that almost sounds too good to be true, and Sheikh Yasir tells you he found it so striking that he went and downloaded the source to read it with his own eyes. The earliest Byzantine chronicler to write about the Muslims at all was an aristocratic monk named Theophanes, who died around the year 820, and who composed a vast chronicle drawing on Arab sources that no longer survive, sources no other Roman writer of his age could reach. His account of the Muslims is full of the usual distortions and antiquated stereotypes; he gets the year wrong, placing the battle in the first year of Abu Bakr's caliphate, and he says the Prophet ﷺ appointed four commanders when it was three. He calls the village Mu'tah, mentions that the brother of Heraclius was stationed there, and even records a detail not found in the Muslim sources at all, that the Romans had learned the day of the battle from a spy in their pay and chose to strike when the Muslims would be distracted. But buried in his garbled account is a single sentence that stops you. The one whom they call the Sword of God, he writes, was the one who got the army away. Khalid. Sheikh Yasir says he could hardly believe it until he held the page in his own hand: the title the Prophet ﷺ had given this man at Mu'tah had traveled, within a few generations, all the way into the records of the Roman Empire, so that a Christian monk writing of his own empire's wars knew the Muslims called Khalid the Sword of God.

That title, Sayfullah, the Sword of Allah, is the thread that ties the whole day together. A man who had been a Muslim for only a few months walked onto a hopeless field, broke nine swords in his hand, and brought an army home, and from that day forward he was Allah's own drawn blade. Which is exactly why, the Sheikh reminds you, Khalid had to die in his bed and not on a battlefield. On his deathbed he wept, and told a visitor to look him over, front and back: you will not find a hand's breadth of my body without a scar or a bruise from battle, and yet here I am dying like a sick old man. He had warrior's blood, and a warrior longs to fall in the field, and he could not understand it. But the later scholars explained what Khalid could not see. He was the Sword of Allah, and no enemy is permitted to break a sword of Allah; only the One who unsheathed it may sheathe it again and lay it to rest. For Khalid to be struck down as a martyr would have been a breaking of that sword, and so he was kept from it. He was too precious for the field. He received, the scholars trust, the reward of the martyr without the martyr's death.

And there is one more lesson the Sheikh draws, this one from a quarrel after the battle. A man from among the helpers, one of the volunteers from Yemen, had killed a Roman officer in gleaming golden armor who was wreaking havoc in the Muslim lines, attacking him with little more than a sword and a piece of tanned goatskin he had fashioned into armor. By the law of the spoils, the gear of the man you kill is yours, and so he took the officer's horse, weapons, and armor. Khalid, now the commander, judged it too much for one soldier and ordered some of it given to the general fund. The man protested that this was the sunnah of the Prophet ﷺ, and he was right; but Khalid was new to Islam and made an honest mistake, not knowing or not believing the rule, and he did not take anything for himself. When the matter reached Madinah, the Prophet ﷺ ruled in the soldier's favor and ordered Khalid to return all of it. But at that the man turned and mocked Khalid: see, didn't I tell you, now you have got what you deserved. And the Prophet ﷺ, angered by the arrogance of it, reversed himself on the spot: in that case, do not give it to him. Will you not leave my commanders to me? The soldier had been right about the law, and his arrogance was so ugly that the truth slipped out of his hands. Here is the warning, the Sheikh says: a man can be upon the truth and carry it with such insolence that his arrogance turns the ruling against him. Be right, and be humble, or your being right will not save you. And from the Prophet's ﷺ words, will you not leave my leaders to me, we learn another thing: Khalid had been appointed not by the Prophet ﷺ but by the choice of the people on the field, and yet the Prophet ﷺ called him my commander, my leader, because the leader the believers rightly choose becomes a leader to be honored as if appointed from above.

The frontier that opened

Step back from the day and you can see what Mu'tah actually did. Nearly every battle of the seerah runs south of Madinah; Mu'tah was the great battle to the north, the largest and, in the Prophet's ﷺ lifetime, the first and only time the Muslims and the Romans met in the field at all. With it, the reach of the young Muslim state had touched the very edge of the Roman world, and a real fear had been planted in the hearts of the Christian Arab tribes along that frontier. If three thousand men could do this much damage and still escape, what would happen when the Prophet ﷺ himself came north? The series will reach exactly that answer at Tabuk, where they will not dare to fight him at all. Mu'tah was also the school in which the Muslims, and Khalid above all, first learned the Roman way of war, the experience that Allah would later use to open the lands of Rome after the Prophet's ﷺ passing.

And remember the timing, because it frames everything that comes next. Mu'tah falls at the very beginning of the eighth year of the Hijra, and the conquest of Makkah is only months away, in Ramadan of that same year. In the year and a half since the treaty of Hudaybiyyah, every serious threat to the Muslim community has been dismantled. The northern frontier has been warned. The threats to the south have been answered. And Quraysh, in Makkah, the proud city that drove him out, is by now an old dog without teeth, all empty noise and quiet defections, its own chief once boasting that he would march down and show the Muslims who was master and now doing nothing at all, because he had seen what they could do. Even Quraysh's own statesman could read the board, which is why he was already slipping away to Madinah ahead of the end. Mu'tah was not the conquest of a single inch of ground. It was a message written in dust and blood on the road home: do not imagine that you can touch us. And it was the last thing standing between the Prophet ﷺ and the day he would walk back, at the head of his people, into the city that had cast him out.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to file Mu'tah away as a grim chapter and move on, one of the least remembered episodes of the whole seerah precisely because it does not end in a clean triumph. That would be to miss the questions it presses on us, and they are questions for ordinary days, not just for battlefields.

It asks us to learn that saving lives can be the victory. The Prophet ﷺ called Mu'tah a fath, not because the Muslims took any ground, but because more than ninety-nine of every hundred men came home. We are trained to recognize victory only in conquest, in the visible win, the banner planted on captured earth. But there are days when the bravest and most faithful thing a person can do is to live, to withdraw with honor, to preserve what can be preserved and fight on another day. Know the difference, the Sheikh teaches, between giving up and regrouping. Allah forbids turning your back in flight; He permits swerving as a maneuver of war and rejoining your own forces. Reckless death is not courage, and a wise retreat is not cowardice. The men they called runaways were really the ones who would return, and the Prophet ﷺ defended them with a single changed letter.

And it asks us, most of all, to make peace with the battles that do not shine. This is the judgment Sheikh Yasir keeps returning to, and it is the one to carry out of the chapter. We want every story of the believers to end in triumph, and in wanting that we miss the mercy buried in the ones that do not. Allah let even the best generation that ever walked the earth struggle, bury three beloved men in a single grave, limp home to be mocked, precisely so that on the day your own life refuses to come out shining you would not be left thinking that faith and ease must always travel together. When the Prophet ﷺ found his companions weeping for the fallen, he told them not to grieve too long, for the example of his ummah is like a garden whose owner prunes away the leaves and the branches so that each year's harvest comes finer than the last. Loss, in the hands of Allah, is not the end of the garden. It is how the garden grows. So when your own day arrives that will not come out shining, remember the men they called runaways, who were really al-karrarun, the ones who would return; remember Ja'far, who lost two hands and was given two wings; and remember that the One who unsheathed the Sword of Allah is the only One who decides when any of His servants is laid to rest. O Allah, send Your praise and peace upon Muhammad ﷺ, give Ja'far and the martyrs of Mu'tah their wings in Your Paradise, keep us from ever fleeing the path that leads to You, and on the day the banners are raised, gather us beneath his.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 74: the Battle of Mu'tah, part 2 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (8:15, 8:16), verified via quran.ai.

The Seerah · 75 of 104

The Expedition of Dhat as-Salasil

A new Muslim leads the elders, and an elder steps aside

After a pause of a few weeks, the seerah resumes, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi opens not with a new event but with a long breath and a backward glance. We are in the eighth year of the Hijrah now, and the Prophet ﷺ is sixty-one years old. Almost the entire story has been told, and he is nearing what the Sheikh calls the pinnacle of his whole life: the conquest of Makkah. Everything from this point forward will be a footnote to that morning. So before the great event arrives, the Sheikh gathers the whole road into one telling, because to feel the weight of the conquest you have to remember everything it cost to reach it.

For forty years the people of Makkah had loved him ﷺ and trusted him, certain he would one day be among their leaders, if not the leader of them all. Then he began to preach one God and the equality of all people, and the same men turned into his worst enemies, not because he had wronged them but merely because of a new belief. They fabricated against him lies they knew were lies. They called him a madman, a magician, a fanciful poet. And the Sheikh pauses over one accusation they never made: they never once said he was insincere, never said he wanted money or power. That charge would not stick to him, and the silence is its own testimony. The only explanation they could invent for a man who would not bend was that he had gone mad.

The whole road, in one glance

They could not touch him personally. He was the grandson of Abd al-Muttalib, of the elite of Banu Hashim, and in a culture that held noble blood sacred, no one could lay a hand on him. So they took out their cruelty on the weak instead, torturing and killing the early Muslims, until at last they tried the slow weapon of a boycott: we will sell you nothing, not food, not water, not clothing. How does a clan live when no one will sell it bread? The believers withdrew into a valley and starved by their own city's decree. The persecution drove some to flee across the sea to Abyssinia. The hatred only sharpened, until they were choking him in public and throwing the filth of a slaughtered animal upon him as he prayed.

When his uncle Abu Talib died, his protection died with him, and Abu Lahab descended to a level lower still. He would follow his own nephew from gathering to gathering, calling out behind him to the visiting tribes: do not listen to him, he is my nephew and I can vouch that he has lost his mind. Imagine the pain of it, the Sheikh says, a man stationed outside your own door, your own uncle, waiting to walk after you and undo every word you speak. Then Abu Lahab, irritated beyond patience, did the unthinkable for an Arab: he disowned a member of Banu Hashim and withdrew his support entirely. The Prophet ﷺ went to Ta'if seeking shelter and was driven out. And when at last the Quraysh, after thirteen years, resolved to do the one thing every Arab held sacred against, to gather and murder one of their own, the Hijrah came. He left the land of his birth, the land of his ancestors, the land of Ibrahim and Hajar before him, and he spoke to the city as he went: you are the most beloved of all lands to me, and had my people not expelled me, I would never have left you.

Even in Madinah they would not let him be. Badr broke them: the Sheikh calls it the single greatest victory of the whole era after the conquest itself, the day every senior leader of Quraysh was killed or taken, the day they never truly recovered from. Then the Confederates, when for the first time in their history the tribes united not around blood but around an idea, marshalled an army the Muslims could never have matched, and still Allah protected the city, and Quraysh learned that even all of Arabia could not take Madinah. Hudaybiyyah, which wore the face of a setback, turned every clause against them and bought two years of peace in which more people entered Islam than in the previous ten, the plainest proof that this religion spread through contact and not through the sword. In that peace the Prophet ﷺ cleared the board. Khaybar fell, the wealthiest and best-fortified power that could have opposed him, and with it came money, weapons that were hard to come by in that age, a steady income from its fields, and the final elimination of any serious threat within the Hijaz. Then Mu'tah, where a small force stood against the Roman superpower itself and came home all but untouched, more than ninety-five percent of the army intact. The Sheikh calls it a victory not of the sword but of morale, of perception, and he reminds us that more than half of any war is perception. The Arabs had never dared fight the Romans; the Muslims had, and walked away unharmed. By now Makkah was perhaps two-thirds empty, its noblemen dead or departed, more than half its people already gone to Madinah, and every domino was tipping toward home.

Not the famous battle, and not the point

A few weeks after Mu'tah, in the cold of that same winter, in roughly Jumada or Rabi of the eighth year, came the expedition the books call Dhat as-Salasil. And here the Sheikh stops to clear up a confusion the advanced students will feel at once. This is not the great Battle of Dhat as-Salasil from the reign of Umar, the one that helped break the Sasanian empire of Persia. The two share a name for entirely unrelated reasons. The later, famous battle was named Salasil, which means chains, because the prisoners taken were so many they were bound together in chains. Ours is named for a small pond, a watering hole called Salasil, that lay near where it took place. So if you have heard of a Battle of Dhat as-Salasil, the Sheikh warns, do not assume you know this one; you studied the famous battle of Umar's time, not this small expedition in the lifetime of the Prophet ﷺ.

And for us, he says, the smallness is exactly the point. We are not here for the politics of which tribe was subdued or where its territory lay. With all these minor expeditions, the benefit for beginners is never the military history; it is the small human moments folded inside them, the stories that carry a lesson you can hold in your hand. The target this time was the tribe of Quda'a, a relatively large clan far up in the north, their territory almost touching what is now Jordan, set a little to the northeast of Madinah. Quda'a had aided the enemy at Mu'tah, and this march was the answer to that aid.

A commander of three months

The Prophet ﷺ sent for Amr ibn al-As, and Amr himself narrates what happened in the first person. He commanded me to put on my garments and my armor and come to him, Amr said, so I did, and when I came he was performing wudu. He looked me up and down, taking my measure, and then he said: I wish to appoint you as the leader of an army, and Allah will keep you safe and give you much, and I am hopeful you will return with a great deal of wealth.

Now Amr was the freshest of new Muslims, barely two or three months into the religion, one of the very last to convert before the conquest, among the final batches whom Allah had blessed to enter Islam just in time. He was of the nobility of Quraysh, raised in luxury, the son of a man against whom the Qur'an itself had spoken and who had now died upon his disbelief. So when the Prophet ﷺ spoke of wealth, something in Amr flinched, as though he had been faintly insulted. O Messenger of Allah, he said, I did not accept Islam in order to become rich. I accepted Islam to be a Muslim, and to be with you. It is the kind of sentence that tells you a man is sincere, and it came from the same Amr who once confessed that whenever the Prophet ﷺ looked at him, he felt he must surely be the most beloved person to him. He had even asked, one day, who is most beloved to you, and heard Aisha named, and then her father, and he kept asking and never heard his own name, until he stopped for fear of where the list would end. He was a late convert, low on any roll of precedence, and yet such was the warmth the Prophet ﷺ made every companion feel that Amr believed himself the dearest of all.

The reply the Sheikh lingers over is a gift to anyone who has ever felt uneasy about chasing a living. How excellent is pure wealth for a righteous man, the Prophet ﷺ told him. There is nothing wrong with wanting money, he was teaching, and nothing wrong with having it, on two conditions: that the wealth itself is clean and lawful, and that the man who holds it is good. Amr had misunderstood, reading a rebuke where none existed, and the Prophet ﷺ corrected him gently. People of wealth, after all, can accomplish what those without it cannot. Money is one of the great tests Allah has left to this ummah, the Prophet ﷺ said elsewhere, but the believer does not flee a test; he asks his Lord to help him pass it. He does not say I want no money at all. He says: O Allah, grant me wealth that is pure, and let me spend it for Your sake. And the man entrusted with this command was deliberately one of the early generation, of whom Allah would later distinguish in His Book:

Not equal among you are those who spent before the conquest [of Makkah] and fought [and those who did so after it]. Those are greater in degree than they who spent afterwards and fought.Qur'an 57:10

By the testimony of the Qur'an itself, the Sheikh notes, men like Amr, who entered before the conquest and fought, stand at a higher degree than those who came after. And it is to such a man, sincere and newly arrived, that the army was now handed.

No fires in the dark

Amr was given three hundred men and told to take the tribe by surprise. He was new to Islam, but he was no new soldier. He had been raised since boyhood in the household of a chieftain, a man of a soldier's upbringing, close in age and temperament to the likes of Khalid ibn al-Walid, the younger and harder generation of Quraysh. So he marched with the instincts of a born commander. It was deep winter, and he moved only by night, never by day, so that no word of his coming would reach the enemy ahead. And through that bone-deep desert cold he forbade his men to light a single fire. When they grumbled, he told them flatly that anyone who lit a fire he would throw into it.

Later, when the men carried this complaint to the Prophet ﷺ, Amr explained himself, and his reasoning was sound. O Messenger of Allah, he said, our numbers were small, three hundred against a great tribe, and I did not want the enemy to see how few we were. We traveled in the dark and we hid ourselves. The desert cold, the Sheikh adds from his own experience, is a different cold from the cold of snow; they say it reaches the bones. Amr asked his men to endure it rather than betray their weakness, and he was right to.

At the edge of Quda'a's land he saw that three hundred would not be enough, so he withdrew and sent to Madinah for reinforcements. The Prophet ﷺ dispatched two hundred more, bringing the force to five hundred, and among the new arrivals were Abu Bakr and Umar and other senior companions. In command of the reinforcement he placed Abu Ubaydah ibn al-Jarrah, one of the ten promised Paradise, one of the great elite of the sahaba. And to Abu Ubaydah he ﷺ gave a single instruction for the moment the two forces met: when you reach your companion, make sure the two of you agree, and do not disagree. Whatever happens, stay united.

One of the ten steps down

When the reinforcements arrived it was time to pray, and the iqamah was called, and Abu Ubaydah stepped forward to lead. And here, the Sheikh explains, we must understand something about that age. The one who led the prayer led the army; the imam of the salah was the imam in every sense. This was not like later times, when anyone might be pushed forward to lead a prayer. In early Islam the leader led the prayer himself, gave the call, gave the sermon, and the symbolism was deliberate and total. So when Abu Ubaydah moved to lead, he was assuming command, as he naturally expected to, being by every measure the senior man.

Amr stopped him. No, he said, you came as reinforcements; I am the commander here, and you are over the two hundred you brought, but I am over the army. Some of the companions bristled at this. Abu Ubaydah is our leader, they said, you may lead your three hundred. And the tension was real, the Sheikh insists, because by every standard Abu Ubaydah was the greater man: senior in Islam, far ahead in Qur'an, of higher rank in every way, set against a boy of three months whose memorized Qur'an could hardly be compared and whose precedence did not exist. Both men, in a sense, were right. Abu Ubaydah was more qualified. Amr had been placed in charge by the Prophet ﷺ himself.

And then Abu Ubaydah remembered the last thing he had been told. The Messenger of Allah ﷺ commanded me to agree with you and not to differ, he said, so even if you disobey me, I will obey you, so that we do not fall into discord. And he stepped down. He let Amr lead the prayer, and lead the army after it, and he swallowed his own pride for the sake of one thing: unity.

The Sheikh stops the march here to break an image we carry wrongly. We have to shatter the picture of the sahaba as superhuman, he says. They were human, the very best of humans, which is a different thing entirely, and as humans they felt exactly what we feel. Abu Ubaydah knew he was more qualified, and he was. Amr knew he had been put in charge, and he had been. The lesson is not that these men had no egos; the lesson is what they did with them. The greater of the two, the one ahead in knowledge and in faith, was the one who let go. That, the Sheikh says, is true leadership: not necessarily to be the one in charge, but to sacrifice your own standing for the wholeness of the community. To step on your own ego so the people stay one.

And folded inside that lesson is a second. In Abu Ubaydah's contingent stood Abu Bakr and Umar, who by the consensus of the ummah are greater than Abu Ubaydah himself, let alone greater than Amr. And they prayed behind a Muslim of three months without a word of complaint. From this, the Sheikh draws a point of creed: to be placed in charge, a man need not be the single best person present. The scholars have a name for it, the leadership of a lesser man over those who are better than him, and it is permitted. This is one of the differences between us and certain sects who insist the leader must be the most virtuous soul on earth. We say that is impossible and was never the case in history, and here is the proof: the two greatest men of the ummah after the Prophet ﷺ, praying in the ranks behind a new convert, because authority is a role and not a verdict on rank.

The cold and the verse

Amr himself tells the next story, and he is not ashamed to tell it on himself, the Sheikh notes, because there is too much benefit in it to keep quiet. On one of the freezing nights of that expedition, Amr woke in a state of major ritual impurity. To take a full bath in that cold, in the open desert with no shelter, he feared, might be the death of him. So he made tayammum, the dry purification with clean earth, and led his companions in the dawn prayer. When the army returned to Madinah, this too went onto their lengthening list of complaints: he led us in prayer while in a state of janabah.

The Prophet ﷺ summoned him. And notice, the Sheikh says, that he asked before he judged: is it true that you led them in prayer while in a state of impurity? He did not condemn on the strength of a complaint; he sought the other side first. Amr answered with the Qur'an. O Messenger of Allah, he said, I heard Allah say:

And do not kill yourselves [or one another]. Indeed, Allāh is to you ever Merciful.Qur'an 4:29

And I feared that bathing in that cold would be the death of me, so I made tayammum instead. And the Prophet ﷺ laughed, Amr said, and said nothing against me.

Small as it looks, the Sheikh draws a great deal from this night. By the agreement of the scholars, he says, this establishes that tayammum is valid even when water is present, if using the water would genuinely harm you. Until this point in the seerah, the companions had made tayammum only when no water could be found; this is the first time, while the Prophet ﷺ lived, that a companion had water in plenty and deliberately set it aside, and was not rebuked for it. The two recognized excuses the jurists derive are precisely these: a deadly cold like Amr's, or a wound or skin ailment that water must not touch. And there is something deeper still. Amr was faced with an explicit command, that one in a state of janabah must bathe, and he understood that this command could not possibly have been meant to destroy him, and he found in another verse the room to apply the first with wisdom. He was no rigid literalist who would apply a text even unto his own death. Between the one who says the text means only its surface and the one who reads it in the light of his circumstance, the Sheikh observes, there has always been a tension, present even among the companions, some more literal and some more open, and far sharper in our own day. Our religion asks us to be faithful to the text and alive to the situation at once. It is a delicate balance with no easy formula, and Amr, on a freezing night, walked it well.

He greeted them, and they killed him

The expedition itself was no battle. When Quda'a saw five hundred riders bearing down on them they dropped everything and fled, and the Muslims came home safe and rich, exactly as the Prophet ﷺ had promised at the outset: you will be kept safe, and Allah will give you much. The tribe was too large to subdue entirely, but the message was sent, and fear was set in their hearts, and from that day the north was no threat. Coming so soon after Mu'tah, this expedition effectively secured all of northern Arabia, at least in perception, and several tribes there entered Islam and an alliance formed. But there is one more incident the books attach near here, in one of the small expeditions of those months before the conquest, and it is the heaviest thing in the whole episode. The Sheikh sets aside the names of the tribes, for there is little benefit in them, and tells it for the verse Allah revealed about it.

A party of believers was sent against a tribe that threatened the Muslims, and on the way they passed a man who lit up with joy at the sight of them and called out the greeting of peace, the unmistakable sign of a Muslim. He was a secret believer among a people who had not accepted Islam. The others lowered their weapons at his greeting. But among them was a man named Muhallim ibn Jaththama, who carried an old grudge against him from the days before Islam, some petty vendetta of the Jahiliyyah. The rest accepted the man as a Muslim; Muhallim did not. He decided the greeting was a mere pretense, that the man was only feigning Islam to save himself, and he attacked him alone, killed him, and seized his belongings as spoils. When the news reached the Prophet ﷺ, Allah sent down the verse that exposed the true motive of the deed:

O you who have believed, when you go forth [to fight] in the cause of Allāh, investigate; and do not say to one who gives you [a greeting of] peace, "You are not a believer," aspiring for the goods of worldly life.Qur'an 4:94

The verdict came from the heavens, the Sheikh says: it was not faith that moved your hand. It was a vendetta, and a hunger for what the man owned. Allah laid bare the intention Muhallim had hidden behind a claim of zeal.

And the Sheikh does not soften the ending. When Muhallim's people later begged the Prophet ﷺ to ask Allah's forgiveness for him, he refused, in one of the very few refusals of his entire life, because of what Allah had disclosed to him of the man's heart. It is said that within a few days Muhallim died. When his people buried him, the next morning the earth had cast his body back to the surface, lying face down. They dug again and buried him again, and again the earth would not hold him, and a third time the same. At last they gave up, laid his body in a ravine, and heaped stones over it. And the Prophet ﷺ said something that turns the whole story toward us. The earth swallows up people worse than him, he said. His crime was not the worst ever committed; the ground covers far worse. But Allah wished to warn you through him, to teach you the sanctity of a human life. You looked at a man saying peace, and you decided his heart for him, and Allah alone knew what was within it. Here the Sheikh lets the episode land where it must, on the gravest of its lessons: a soul is not yours to weigh.

What this day asks of our faith

It is a small expedition, the kind a history book would skip in a sentence, and yet look at everything it leaves in your hands. Strip it down and four plain teachings stand out, each of them about how a believer carries himself among other people and before Allah.

The first is that clean wealth is a good thing and not a guilty one. How excellent is pure wealth for a righteous man. We are quick to feel that there is something unspiritual about wanting to provide, that the pious heart should want nothing of this world, and the Prophet ﷺ corrected that misunderstanding in a single sentence to a man who felt it as sharply as we do. There is no shame in earning, on two conditions: that the money is lawful and that the heart is right. Money is one of the great tests of this ummah, and the believer does not run from a test. He asks his Lord to help him pass it. O Allah, grant me provision that is pure, and let me spend it for Your sake.

The second is that the strongest move in the room is often to step on your own pride. Abu Ubaydah was the greater man and the senior leader, ahead of Amr in Islam and in Qur'an and in standing, and he is the one who stepped down. He let go of being right so that the army would stay one. We tell ourselves that to yield is to lose, when the seerah shows the opposite: that the larger soul is the one that can set its own claim aside for the sake of the whole. And beside that lesson sits a third, that the leader need not be the best person present. Abu Bakr and Umar, the two greatest of the ummah after the Prophet ﷺ, prayed behind a Muslim of three months and did not murmur. Authority is a role, not a ranking of souls. Do not refuse to follow the one placed over you merely because you can name someone you think is better; the companions could name two, and they prayed behind Amr anyway.

The fourth is to verify before you judge. Twice in this single episode the principle stands out. The Prophet ﷺ did not condemn Amr on the strength of his soldiers' complaints; he called him and asked what had happened, for there are always two sides and the proof comes first. And when Muhallim killed a man over a greeting, the very verse Allah sent down commanded the believers to investigate, to make certain, before they decide that someone is not what he says he is. Which opens onto the heaviest lesson of the day, the one the Prophet ﷺ drew over a grave the earth would not close. A man said peace, and someone decided his heart was a lie and took his life over it, and the ground itself rejected the killer. You do not get to read what is inside another person. That knowledge belongs to Allah alone, and a single human soul is sacred beyond your power to weigh it.

So carry these out of the freezing dark of that winter and down into your ordinary days. Ask Allah for provision that is pure and for deeds He will accept. When your pride and the community's unity stand on opposite sides of a small argument, be the one who steps down. Follow the one placed over you without insisting he be the finest soul alive. And the next time you are tempted to decide what is in someone else's heart, to brand a greeting a lie or a believer a pretender, remember the body the earth threw back three mornings running, and stay your hand. May Allah send His peace and blessings upon His Messenger Muhammad ﷺ, who tested a new believer gently, who asked before he judged, who taught us that clean wealth is no shame and that the larger heart is the one that yields, and who showed us, over a grave that would not close, that a single human soul is sacred and is not ours to weigh.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 75: the recap and the expedition of Dhat as-Salasil (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (57:10, 4:29, 4:94), verified via quran.ai. The narration is the Sheikh's; the phrasing is Buruja's.

The Seerah · 76 of 104

The conquest of Makkah, part 1

A broken treaty, and the man who came to beg

For two years the treaty of Hudaybiyyah had held, and inside its quiet something strange had happened. The peace that Quraysh had forced on the Prophet ﷺ at the edge of Makkah, the peace that read on parchment like a defeat, had turned into the widest door the religion had ever known. Men who had spent their lives swearing they would never believe sat down with Muslims now, traded with them, argued with them, and one by one were undone by what they heard. Islam grew faster in those two years of peace than it had grown in all the years of war. And so when this day arrives, the day the conquest of Makkah begins, it does not begin with a Muslim drawing a sword. It begins with a treaty tearing apart in the dark, an old blood feud no one had buried, and a murder committed inside the sacred Haram itself.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi opens this episode by reaching back, because the men who break the peace will defend themselves in front of the Prophet ﷺ by reciting the whole history of Arabia, and you cannot follow their plea unless you know what they knew. So the story begins not with the raid but with the names, the lineages, the treaties their grandfathers had sworn. The Arabs forgot none of it. They carried their genealogy and their alliances in their chests, generation upon generation, and very soon every thread of it would be laid out before the Messenger of Allah ﷺ as an argument for war.

A clause that armed both sides

Go back to the treaty itself. One of its clauses gave both sides, Quraysh and the Muslims, the right to take any Arab tribe into alliance, and whatever side a tribe joined, the terms of the treaty reached over it too. No warfare. The peace was not just between two armies; it was meant to cover everyone they gathered under them. So the tribes chose their side. The tribe of Khuzaah, many of whom had by now accepted Islam, came in with the Prophet ﷺ. The tribe of Banu Bakr, still wholly pagan, came in with Quraysh. Two tribes camped on the outskirts of Makkah, now bound to opposite ends of the same fragile peace, with a hundred years of unsettled blood between them.

Khuzaah was no small clan, and the Sheikh dwells on who they were, because they were tied to the Prophet ﷺ himself. They were one of the legendary old tribes of Arabia. It was Khuzaah who had once expelled the tribe of Jurham, the in-laws of Ismail, from the custodianship of Makkah, and Khuzaah who then ruled the sanctuary for some three hundred years, a vast honor for any tribe. And it was a chieftain of Khuzaah, generations back, who first carried idolatry into Arabia. He had traveled north to the Amalekites of Syria, a mighty people he imagined giants, and asked them the secret of their power, and they told him it lay in the idols they worshiped. So he begged one from them and brought it home, and that idol was Hubal, the very Hubal that became the principal god of the Kaaba, the Hubal that Abu Sufyan would one day cry out for over the dead at Uhud, only to hear the Prophet ﷺ answer back: Allah is higher and greater. All of this was Khuzaah's history. And there was more, woven into the Prophet's ﷺ own blood: his great grandmother, several generations removed, was a daughter of the chieftain of Khuzaah, married into the line that would become Banu Hashim. The Arabs remembered even that, a woman's lineage five generations gone, and they would bring it to his door.

There was a treaty in that history too. When the Prophet's ﷺ ancestor took back Hubal and the Kaaba passed at last into the hands of Quraysh, the two tribes did not fall into endless war. They stayed on cordial terms, and in time his own grandfather, Abdul Muttalib, swore a formal pact with Khuzaah, recorded in the old books in deep poetic Arabic: that the two tribes would be as one, for as long as the sun rose and shone hot over the land, generation after generation, each coming to the other's aid against anyone who brought them harm. A pact meant to outlive everyone who signed it. Hold onto that pact. The man who comes to Madinah will lay it on the table as though the ink were still wet.

The night raid, and a word at the Haram

Banu Bakr and Khuzaah had been killing each other for a hundred years, the way the old tribes did. One death answered by ten, ten by a hundred, names kept on a list and handed down to sons who had not yet been born when the first blood was spilled. Islam had forced a pause. The treaty of Hudaybiyyah had split the two tribes onto opposite sides and frozen the feud in place. But nothing had healed. The list was still there, the dead still uncounted, and in the eighth year of the Hijra, Banu Bakr decided the time had come to settle it.

They knew this meant breaking the treaty, so they went first to Quraysh. Their noblemen came and explained plainly: we want our revenge, and we know we are bound by the peace, so give us permission for a single raid. And here the Sheikh plants a point he will not let pass. Quraysh did not merely look the other way. Some of their leaders quietly handed Banu Bakr weapons, almost certainly for a share of the spoils, since there was no other reason on earth they would arm them. And one of them laughed off the danger with a line that would haunt Makkah: do not worry, attack at night, and how will Muhammad ﷺ ever find out? They knew. They approved. Their own elders armed the raid. This is not a story, the Sheikh insists, of guilty Banu Bakr and innocent Quraysh standing by. Quraysh were party to the breaking of their own treaty.

So Banu Bakr struck. They fell on a camp of Khuzaah in the dead of night at a watering hole called al-Watir, just outside the boundaries of the Haram, expecting the easy raid of the desert code, where you take the camels, perhaps wound a man, and ride away before anyone is truly hurt. It went wrong. The alarm went up, the men woke, the women and children began to scream, and what should have been a quick theft became a slaughter. More than twenty of Khuzaah were killed, an enormous number for one tribe in one night, where a raid that took even a single life was rare. And one death among them poisoned everything that followed. A man of Khuzaah broke and ran for the sanctuary, thinking only that if he could reach the Haram he would be safe, because even the pagan Arabs held that no blood could be shed on that ground. He made it to the edge of the sacred precinct, turned, and called out the name of the man chasing him: I am in the Haram now, fear your Lord. The man of Banu Bakr answered him with a blasphemy, that there is no Lord today, no religion, call on no one, and cut him down on holy ground. The treaty was not merely broken. It had been broken with a knife inside the sanctuary of Allah.

The poem that moved him

News like that cannot be buried, and Allah, the Sheikh notes, completely exposed and humiliated them through it. The chieftain of Khuzaah set out at once for Madinah with a delegation of forty men, and when he stood before the Prophet ﷺ he did what the Arabs did best in a moment that mattered: he recited a poem. A single page of it, in classical Arabic so old and deep that Dr. Yasir Qadhi pauses to admit that he himself had to sit and translate it slowly, that none of the Arabs around him could have rendered it on sight, because the tongue it was composed in reaches back past anything spoken today. It is preserved in Ibn Hisham. And it is a masterpiece of pleading.

He built his case in three claims, and every one of them was a thread of the history the Sheikh had laid out. First, the blood: you and I share an ancestor, he reminded the Prophet ﷺ, your own great grandmother was of us, we are kin. Then the pact: your grandfather Abdul Muttalib swore a treaty with my tribe, that we would be as one as long as the sun rose, generation after generation, and that bond is still owed. And then the present: we are under your treaty now, we came in on your side at Hudaybiyyah. Kin, and sworn, and allied, three claims twisted into verse. Then he turned to the slaughter, and described how his people were cut down even as they bowed in prayer, how nothing covered them, not even the Haram. This, the Sheikh says, is how news traveled in Arabia. You did not send a messenger; you composed a poem, and the better it was, the faster it spread, until it was on every tongue from one end of the peninsula to the other. This was their CNN, their Fox News, a single brilliant page that could move a nation.

It moved the Prophet ﷺ. He was visibly stirred by what he heard, and he told them plainly, twice over, that they would be helped: you shall be helped, you shall be helped, and then an oath in the Arab idiom, may Allah never help me if I do not help you, a phrase not meant literally but as the strongest possible promise that aid was coming and there was no question of it. And as he spoke, a cloud was gathering on the horizon, and he read it as a sign, the promise of victory for Khuzaah. This too the Sheikh stops on. Islam encourages the good omen, al-fal, and the Prophet ﷺ loved it. There is no such thing in this religion as a bad omen, no black cats, no broken mirrors, no knocking on wood, all of that superstition bordering on shirk. But a good sign is permitted with two conditions: that you read something hopeful into it, and that you tie the outcome back to Allah and not to a charm. You make your dua, you see a rainbow, and you let your heart say, perhaps Allah is answering me. That is allowed, and more than allowed.

An ultimatum, and a refusal

What did the Prophet ﷺ do next? Here the Sheikh weighs two reports, the way the seerah must always be weighed. Most scholars read Ibn Ishaq to mean that he simply began preparing his army from this moment and marched, treating Quraysh's complicity as an automatic breach of the treaty, which was entirely his right; there is nothing wrong with that reading, for Quraysh had knowingly broken the peace. But there is a second report, carried by Ibn Hajar and others, that Dr. Yasir Qadhi himself leans toward, partly because it traces back soundly and partly because it shows the Prophet ﷺ doing exactly what he always did: trying, one last time, to spare the bloodshed.

In that account he sent Quraysh an ultimatum, and a remarkably fair one. Three choices. Pay the blood money for the slain of Khuzaah. Or cut off your alliance with Banu Bakr and abandon them to whatever they have brought on themselves. Or prepare for war. As reasonable, the Sheikh says, as anyone could ask. Cut loose a tribe that betrayed the peace, or pay for the dead they killed under your protection, or fight. And Quraysh refused on both counts. The blood money for more than twenty dead would have come to two thousand camels, a staggering fortune; to grasp it, recall that the entire spoils of Badr had not reached even a few hundred camels. Two thousand would have wiped them out, left them, in their own words, with nothing but the clothes on their backs. And they would not abandon Banu Bakr, because Banu Bakr were now the only pagan tribe left anywhere near them. Every other tribe in the region had gone over, fully or in large part, to Islam. Cut Banu Bakr loose, and Quraysh, the old masters of Arabia, would be utterly alone. That refusal is its own measure of how far the world had already turned. The lords of Makkah were down to a single ally, and they clung to it because it was all they had left.

Abu Sufyan goes door to door

When the younger hotheads of Quraysh slammed the door on any compromise, refusing to pay and refusing to give an inch, Abu Sufyan understood what was coming, and he made a decision no one would have predicted. He would go to Madinah himself. He would ride into the Prophet's ﷺ own city and try to renew the treaty of Hudaybiyyah in person, and Quraysh, knowing they had broken it, put him in charge of saving it by any means he could. Sit with the picture, because the Sheikh asks you to. This is Abu Sufyan, the chieftain of Quraysh, the man who had commanded the army at Uhud and the army at the Trench. In all the years of this conflict he had entered Madinah only at the head of soldiers, only to attack. Now he rode in alone, unarmed, to beg.

He went to the Prophet ﷺ first, the Sheikh judges, because no other order makes sense, and got nothing. Not a yes, not a no, only silence. Whatever he asked, the Messenger of Allah ﷺ gave him no answer at all, and Abu Sufyan walked out sick with worry, because silence told him the peril was real and the matter already decided. So he went looking for old connections. He found Abu Bakr, radiyallahu anhu, a friend from before Islam, and asked him to intercede with the Prophet ﷺ. Abu Bakr told him flatly: I cannot help you, I can do nothing for you. He found Umar, radiyallahu anhu, once his business partner, and Umar did not even soften it; he mocked the man outright: you think I would go and speak for you before the Messenger of Allah ﷺ? Never. Two doors, shut. He went to Ali, radiyallahu anhu, the closest of them all to him in blood, since Abu Sufyan too was of Banu Hashim's wider line, and found him at home, with Fatima nearby caring for their little son Hasan. Oh Ali, he pleaded, you are the nearest man in this whole city to me in lineage, go to the Prophet ﷺ for me, do not let me go back to my people humiliated and empty handed. But Ali would not interfere. The Prophet ﷺ has decided a matter, he said, and we do not turn him from his decision once it is made.

How amazing, the Sheikh marvels, is this sight. Ali is barely more than a youth in Abu Sufyan's eyes, half his age, and yesterday Abu Sufyan was the man instigating armies and commanding the field, and now here he is going from door to door, pleading with Abu Bakr, begging Umar, reduced to entreating the young Ali. And it gets lower. Desperate, he turned to Fatima, radiyallahu anha: oh daughter of Muhammad, could you not send this little boy of yours to ask his grandfather for protection on behalf of Quraysh? And he even promised it, that the child would be honored as a chief, a sayyid, until the end of time, not knowing that the Prophet ﷺ had already named Hasan exactly that, a master of this ummah, by a word from heaven that no man like Abu Sufyan could grant or withhold. She refused him gently: my son is far too young to grant anyone protection, and no one gives protection against the Messenger of Allah ﷺ; we are not a people who go against his decree. And here the Sheikh draws out the point that explains the very marriage the next door will turn on, the reason no guardian needed to act for the Prophet ﷺ in any matter, for as Allah has said:

The Prophet is more worthy of the believers than themselves,Qur'an 33:6

His own daughter turns him away

There was one door left, and it was the one that broke him: the door of his own daughter. Umm Habiba, Ramla bint Abi Sufyan, had not seen her father in something like thirteen or fifteen years. She had emigrated to Abyssinia for the sake of her faith in the middle of the Makkan years, and there her husband had done the rare and ruinous thing of abandoning Islam and dying upon his apostasy, leaving her stranded, a believing woman alone in a far country with no home to return to, for she would not go back to her pagan father in Makkah. And in that distant land the Prophet ﷺ had sent a proposal of marriage for her, and the Negus himself had stood in as the one to arrange it, paying her dowry and hosting the wedding feast in his own court while the Prophet ﷺ was a sea away. She had only lately returned. Now her father, still a pagan, walked into her house in Madinah.

As he moved to sit down, she reached out and pulled the Prophet's ﷺ bedding away so that he could not sit on it. He looked at her, half wounded and half mocking: am I unworthy of the bedding, he asked, or is the bedding unworthy of me, which is it? It is the bedding of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, she said, and you are an idolater, and you will not sit on it. One more door, shut, and this time by his own child, who would not let him so much as rest his weight on a cloth out of honor for the Prophet ﷺ. He muttered that some evil must have touched her since she left him, that she had lost her senses, and he walked back out into the street.

Out of every option, he returned at last to the one piece of counsel Ali had given him on his way: go to the masjid, Ali had told him, and announce that you grant Quraysh your protection, for you are the chief of Kinana, the noblest man of the whole confederation, and perhaps that is something. Protection, amaan, was a real institution in that world. Any member of a tribe could extend safe passage on behalf of his people, and Islam itself kept the practice and honored it, so that even a woman, even a child not yet of age, could grant a man protection and have it stand. So Abu Sufyan rose in the masjid and declared aloud that he had given Quraysh his protection. But it was hollow, and everyone knew it, with the Prophet ﷺ having said nothing and not a soul standing to second him. He had granted a safety that was not his to give. He rode home having achieved precisely nothing, and when Quraysh heard his account, every door refused, every man turned away, they told him to his face: you have been made a fool of. There was nothing else I could do, he answered. That was the only thing left. And the Sheikh asks you to look, simply to look, at what nine years had done. Once, at the gates of Makkah, this man and his city had denied the Prophet ﷺ even the protection of an entry. Now the chieftain of Quraysh stood in the streets of Madinah begging for protection he could not buy at any price, refused by everyone, refused by his own daughter, and going home to be laughed at for trying. Allah, the Sheikh says, would let him live to taste this, and one day to accept Islam and be forgiven. But first he had to swallow, just a little, what he had spent years dealing out to others.

An army gathered in secret

Now the Prophet ﷺ called the people to arms. And when the leader of the believers issues a general call to muster, the Sheikh explains, answering it becomes an obligation on every able man, all the more so when that leader is the Messenger of Allah ﷺ himself. So the word went out: every man who could march would march. But he would not say where. He gave them the days they needed to settle their affairs and ready their families, and through all of it he kept the destination sealed. Not from his closest companions. Not from Abu Bakr, the dearest of all of them. It is preserved that during those days of preparation Abu Bakr came to his own daughter Aisha, who was packing the Prophet's ﷺ provisions for the journey, and asked her where he was going, and she answered honestly that she had no idea. If Aisha, packing the very bags, did not know, and Abu Bakr, asking her, did not know, then no one did. Such secrecy, the Sheikh says, has almost no parallel in the whole seerah. And the reason was not any doubt about the loyalty of those he trusted. It was the plain wisdom that a secret told even to the most faithful soul, in all innocence, does not stay a secret. One person tells one trusted friend, swears him to silence, and the friend tells one more, and the city knows by morning. Some things are kept only by telling no one at all.

He did more than stay silent. He sent a small scouting party of eight men north, toward Syria, to plant the impression that the great army was bound that way, and it was a believable feint, for the recent fighting in the north was fresh in everyone's mind, and the campaign of Tabuk would in fact draw him north the following year. The word spread that the army was headed up north, and the whole misdirection held. Only at the very last moment, the Sheikh says, quite literally the day before they set out, did the Prophet ﷺ reveal the truth: they were marching south, on Makkah. And this, exactly this, is the meaning of his famous words that war is deceit. The Sheikh halts here deliberately, because the hadith is twisted by some Muslims and by the Islamophobes alike into a license to lie, a charge that nothing a Muslim says can be trusted. It is no such thing. The Prophet ﷺ did not lie. You are never permitted to give an oath you mean to break, or to sign a treaty or a contract knowing you will betray it; that is treachery, and it is forbidden absolutely, in every circumstance. Deceit in war means only this: you let the enemy believe you are marching north while you march south. There is no broken word in it, no betrayed trust, only the camouflage and misdirection that every army on earth practices. The line is bright, and the Sheikh wants it kept bright: strategy is allowed, treachery is forbidden, and the two must never be confused.

And then they came. Not a single emigrant stayed behind, not one of the famous helpers, for Madinah was now their home and Makkah a city many of them had been driven from; and the outlying tribes poured in by the thousand, one tribe sending a thousand men, another seventeen hundred, until ten thousand believers stood under his banner. The Sheikh asks you to stop and sit with the arithmetic, because it is staggering. Roughly seven years before this, at Badr, the Muslims had numbered about three hundred, and the Prophet ﷺ had stood in the night and begged his Lord that if this small band were destroyed there would be no one left to worship Allah on the earth. A year or two before this, ten thousand of the enemy had come down on Madinah at the Trench, and again he had turned to Allah, for they could not be fought. And now that same number, ten thousand, marched out behind him. He left on the tenth of Ramadan and reached the outskirts of Makkah on the nineteenth, ten thousand strong, in nine days, an almost impossible speed for so vast a host, when even a small swift caravan took seven.

The letter that almost changed everything

On the eve of that march, as the secret finally broke and the destination became known, one of the great companions made the single worst mistake of his life. The moment he learned the army was bound for Makkah, he sat down and wrote a letter in secret to the people there, warning them to brace themselves, that the Prophet ﷺ was coming, that they would be attacked and should prepare. His name was Hatib. And, the Sheikh promises, his is a long story and a beneficial one, full of wisdom and full of mercy: why a believer of his rank, a man of Badr, would do such a thing, and why the Prophet ﷺ, knowing all of it, would choose in the end to forgive him.

That is where this day leaves us, on the very edge of everything. The treaty is broken, the proud chieftain has been sent home a fool, the deceit has held, ten thousand are on the road covering the desert at impossible speed, and a single secret letter is racing ahead of them toward Makkah, carrying the one warning that could undo it all. We pick up the cliffhanger from here.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this day as politics, the clever maneuvering of a statesman closing in on his enemy, and to leave it there. That would be to miss what it asks of us. Strip it down and four things stand out, and not one of them stays in the seventh century.

He kept a word he had not even given. The treaty that bound the Prophet ﷺ to Khuzaah was not his treaty; it was his grandfather's, sworn before Islam, before he was born, in the dark days of idolatry. And he honored it, because a binding agreement stays binding as long as it holds nothing forbidden, and Abdul Muttalib's pact held nothing forbidden. Faithfulness to a promise, the Sheikh teaches plainly here, does not expire with the one who made it. We treat our commitments as ours to renegotiate the moment they grow inconvenient; he treated even an inherited word as a debt that had come due. Ask what promises you are quietly letting lapse because the person who made them, or the version of you who made them, feels long gone.

He drew the brightest possible line between strategy and treachery, and he never once let it blur. War is deceit, he said, and then he showed exactly what that did and did not mean: misdirect the enemy all you like, send scouts north and march south, wear the camouflage every army wears, but never, ever break an oath or betray a contract or go back on your given word. The difference between cleverness and treachery is the difference between a feint and a lie, and most of the cruelty in the world lives in the place where people pretend they cannot tell them apart. In your own dealings, in business and in speech, the test is simple: did I mislead an opponent's expectations, which is allowed, or did I break my word, which is forbidden? Keep that line as bright as he kept it.

He watched power turn, and so should you. The Sheikh will not let the image of Abu Sufyan go, and neither should we. Yesterday he led armies; today he wanders Madinah with his hands out and is refused at every door, including the door of the daughter who was once his. No standing on this earth is permanent, none of it. The man on top this year is the man begging next year, and the only security that does not crumble is security with Allah and His Messenger ﷺ. Hold whatever you have been given, your position, your influence, your good name, lightly, and humbly, because it was always on loan.

And he guarded a secret as an act of wisdom, not suspicion. He withheld the destination from the people he loved and trusted most, not because he doubted them but because he understood how even an innocent word travels, swearing a friend to silence and watching the city know by dawn. Some matters are kept best by telling no one at all. There is a discipline in that for all of us, in an age that confuses sharing with sincerity and treats every withheld thing as a betrayal. Not every truth is owed to every ear, and the strength to carry something alone, in trust, is its own kind of faith.

So take this day down into your ordinary life. Keep the word you gave, and even the word you only inherited. Misdirect an opponent if you must, but never break a promise, and never blur the line between the two. Look at the proud man begging at the closed door and loosen your grip on whatever standing you imagine is yours. And when something must be held in silence, hold it. The treaty broke, the chieftain was humbled, and ten thousand marched, but the lesson the whole episode rests on is older and quieter than any of it: power passes, promises do not, and the only standing that lasts is standing with him ﷺ. May Allah send His peace and His blessings upon His Messenger ﷺ, keep us true to every word we give, gather us under his banner the day the nations are gathered, and let us be of those who march behind him and never against him.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 76: the conquest of Makkah, part 1 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translation is from Sahih International (33:6), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 77 of 104

The Conquest of Makkah, Part 2

The letter in the braids, and the man Badr saved

The army was being raised in the dark. The Prophet ﷺ had told no one where this march was going, not the men sharpening their blades, not the clans counting their riders, not even, until almost the last hour, his closest companions. Eventually he had to say it, because you cannot send an army anywhere without telling it to prepare, and so a day or two before they moved he let the destination out: Makkah. But for weeks before that he had carried one quiet du'a above every other plan, a du'a Dr. Yasir Qadhi sets at the very center of this whole campaign: O Allah, do not let my plan reach them. Everything was riding on Makkah not seeing this coming. The city that had hunted him out of his own home eight years earlier was not to know that ten thousand were forming up against it until they were already at its gates.

And then one of his own men sat down and wrote Quraysh a letter to warn them.

This is the story of that letter. It will take up almost the whole of this chapter, because, the Sheikh says, it is one of the most significant stories in the entire seerah, dense with benefit, and at the same time one of the most dangerously misused stories Muslims carry. People reach for it today to justify killing other Muslims. So we have to walk it slowly, scene by scene: who the man was, how Allah exposed what no human eye could have found, what he said when the sword was already at his neck, and why the Prophet ﷺ would not let it fall.

A believer writes to the enemy

His name was Hatib ibn Abi Balta'a, and he did not belong to Quraysh by blood. According to the strongest report he was a mawla from Yemen, a man of immigrant, second-class standing in Makkah, attached as a client to one of the Qurayshi houses. By another report he was a freed slave who had bought his own liberty. The two accounts agree on what matters: Hatib was not a Qurayshi noble. He was an outsider who had heard the truth, accepted Islam, and made the hijrah to Madinah, and the second report quietly explains the wound at the heart of his story, because if he had been a slave then his family had been slaves too, and they could not simply pack up and follow him north. However it happened, when Hatib left Makkah, his children stayed behind, and by one narration his mother stayed with them. He had no tribe back there to stand over them. Every other great Muhajir had clansmen in Makkah who would never let their own blood be touched. Hatib had no one.

So when word reached him that the Prophet ﷺ was preparing to move, Hatib wrote to the people of Makkah. Ibn Ishaq does not preserve the wording, but one or two other books carry the gist, and it is unmistakable. By one version he hedged, telling them only that the Muslims were marching with an army and might be coming their way, so take your precautions, which would mean he wrote before the destination was even announced. By the fuller version he named it outright: Muhammad is coming for you with an army like the night, and the image is poetic, the night being dense and dark and crowded, so picture the sheer mass of the host, and he is rushing toward you like a torrent. And then, woven into the warning itself, a line that gives the whole man away. By Allah, even if he came to you alone, Allah would still give him the victory over you, for Allah will keep His promise to him.

Sit with that sentence, because it will matter at the end. Even as he spilled the secret, Hatib could not stop confessing his own certainty: it will not save you, he is telling them, he will win no matter what you do. This was not a man who had lost his faith in the Prophet ﷺ. This was a man whose faith and whose fear were at war inside him, and on this one night the fear got his hand to the pen.

He had a letter that could undo an entire campaign, and he needed it carried in total secrecy. So he found a travelling woman, a mawla herself, no one famous, a name the books barely record, precisely so that no eye would follow her. He paid her, and he did not tell her what the letter said. Her one task was to smuggle it to Quraysh and breathe a word of it to no one, and her pay waited on its safe delivery. She took it, untied her hair, laid the folded paper against her scalp, and braided it back in until it was buried in the plaits, hidden where she was sure no one would ever look. Then she set out for Makkah. No one had watched Hatib write it. The carrier herself did not know its contents. There was, the Sheikh says plainly, no human way on earth that this letter was ever going to be found.

The secret that could not be kept

There was no human way. So Allah sent Jibril.

The Prophet ﷺ was told the whole of it from above the heavens: there is a letter, here is the woman who carries it, she is on such-and-such a caravan on such-and-such a road, go and stop her and bring it back. He dispatched riders at once, among them Ali, with a description so exact it could only have come from revelation. Ride out to Rawdat Khakh, just beyond Madinah. You will find a woman travelling with a certain party. She is carrying a letter. Take it from her and bring it to me. Look, the Sheikh says, at the precision of a knowledge that comes straight from Jibril: not a hunch, not a search of the roads, but the place, the company, and the cargo named in advance.

They galloped out and caught the caravan exactly where he had said. They demanded the letter. The woman told them she had nothing, that she did not know what they were talking about. So they searched. They went through every item she carried, piece by piece. They made the camel kneel and searched it, searched the saddle, turned over her belongings, and found nothing, because there was nothing to find anywhere except in her hair. And here one of the riders drew a line worth memorizing and carrying for life. By Allah, he said, the Messenger of Allah ﷺ has not lied to us, and we have not lied to you. Either you produce this letter, or we will strip you and search you ourselves. There was not a flicker of doubt in him. Follow the chain of his certainty: Jibril does not lie to the Prophet ﷺ, and the Prophet ﷺ does not lie to us, so the letter exists, whatever our search has turned up so far. The absence of the letter in her bags did not shake his trust by a hair. It only meant they had not yet looked in the right place.

When she saw they meant it, she asked them to turn around. Pause on her, because the Sheikh does, almost ashamed on our behalf. Here is a non-Muslim woman, alone on an open road, surrounded by armed men, threatened with being stripped, and what she will not surrender even now is her modesty. She would not let strange men see her hair. She made them turn their backs, took off her head covering, unwound the braids herself, and handed over the letter. Covering the hair, the Sheikh notes, was not in those days a Muslim marker at all; it was simply what every woman of every civilized society did, in Arabia and in Rome and, two centuries ago, here in America. And it is a sorrow, he says, that so many of us today have let go of the very shyness that this woman clung to with men standing over her ready to search her body. She was brought back to the Prophet ﷺ with the letter. She told the truth: a man had paid her, she did not know what the letter contained, she had simply done as she was hired to do. And she was let go, because she had not knowingly committed any crime. She is not mentioned again.

O you who have believed, do not take My enemies and your enemies as allies, extending to them affection while they have disbelieved in what came to you of the truth, having driven out the Prophet and yourselves [only] because you believe in Allah, your Lord. If you have come out for jihad [i.e., fighting or striving] in My cause and seeking means to My approval, [take them not as friends]. You confide to them affection [i.e., instruction], but I am most knowing of what you have concealed and what you have declared. And whoever does it among you has certainly strayed from the soundness of the way.Qur'an 60:1

The sword, and the man under it

Now the letter lay in the Prophet's ﷺ own hand, and the handwriting was Hatib's, and Jibril had already named him. There was nothing left to establish. The evidence could not be more complete: revelation from heaven, a witness, a confession waiting to happen, the document itself. And still the Prophet ﷺ summoned Hatib and asked him: did you do this?

Stop here, the Sheikh insists, because the lesson is enormous and most of us trample straight over it. The case was airtight. Jibril had told him everything. He did not need a single word more. And yet he called the man in and gave him the chance to answer to his face. If even here, with the proof total and divine, a man is still asked before he is condemned, then when you hear something about someone, however damning it sounds, you confirm it to him directly and you hear his side before you let a single word of verdict leave your mouth. This is not softness. This is the law of how a believer handles an accusation.

Hatib did not deny it. It is my letter, he said. And in the same breath Umar ibn al-Khattab was on his feet, his hand reaching for his weapon: O Messenger of Allah, let me strike the neck of this hypocrite, for he has betrayed Allah and His Messenger. But the Prophet ﷺ said, leave him. And then Hatib spoke, and the thing to notice is what he fought for. He did not fight for his life. He fought for his faith. O Messenger of Allah, he said, by Allah, I have not changed, I have not abandoned my religion, I did not prefer disbelief over Islam. I did not do this out of any love for the disbelief of Quraysh. I knew with certainty that Allah would give you the victory and that you would not be harmed, that my letter could not touch you. I wanted only one thing: a hand with Quraysh, some favor they would remember, so that they would protect my family, because every one of your other companions has clansmen in Makkah to guard his relatives, and I have no one there to guard mine.

Read that again, and see a man arguing for his iman with a drawn sword waiting at his head. He never once says spare me. He almost certainly believed he was about to die and had made his peace with it. What he could not bear was to die with the word disbeliever fixed to his name, and so he spent every breath he had on that and on nothing else. Then the Prophet ﷺ gave his verdict: he has told you the truth. Say nothing about him but good.

Perhaps Allah has forgiven the people of Badr

Umar was not satisfied, and his second objection was sharper than the first, because it had shifted ground. O Messenger of Allah, he said in effect, even granting that he has faith, he is a spy, he has betrayed the trust placed in him, so let me deal with him. The Sheikh draws the line between the two objections with care, because it is the hinge of the whole episode. The first time, Umar spoke as though Hatib had stepped out of Islam altogether, as though this act were itself disbelief, and the killing he asked for was the killing of an apostate. The second time he set the question of faith aside entirely and argued the law: this is treason, and the penalty for treason is death.

And Umar was not wrong about the principle. Betraying your own people to an invading enemy has been counted among the gravest crimes there is, across nations and across centuries, right down to our own day. The Sheikh reaches for a recent example so no one walks away muttering that Islamic law is harsh: in living memory, on this very soil, a husband and wife were put to death for selling secrets to a foreign power, and the judge told them they had done something worse than murder. No serious nation treats treachery as a small thing. Umar was reading the law correctly.

But the Prophet ﷺ answered with something that no law book could contain and only he could know. Did he not witness Badr? And what can tell you, Umar, that Allah has not looked upon the people of Badr and said: do as you wish, for I have already forgiven you? At that, the man who a heartbeat earlier had wanted a sword drawn dissolved into tears. Umar wept, and he said: Allah and His Messenger know best. We carry a picture of Umar as all hardness and edge, and the Sheikh asks us to correct it here, because under that sternness was a heart this tender, a heart that one sentence about the forgiveness of Allah could melt straight through.
