His harshness, the Sheikh adds, was itself a mercy the ummah needed in its season; it was no accident that the great conquests came in the years of his rule. Sometimes a community needs the strict hand, and Allah gave it one when it was needed. But the strict hand here gave way at once to the soft heart underneath it.

And it was at this moment, the books record, that Allah sent down the opening of Surah al-Mumtahanah, the chapter named for exactly this kind of test, a chapter whose very name speaks of the woman, and the believer, who must be examined. Hatib had not betrayed the believers out of love for the enemy or hatred of the faith; he had done it out of fear for his children. But Allah was not only judging one frightened man on one night. He was teaching the whole ummah, to the end of time, where the line sits, and reminding every believer who would ever read it that whatever they bury in secret, He already knows it: I am most knowing of what you have concealed and what you have declared.

Judge a person by the whole of his life

Because of one sentence, say nothing about him but good, Hatib lived on for more than twenty years, and not once in all that time did anyone throw this letter in his face. Not once. The Sheikh wants you to feel the sheer weight of that authority. Here was a leader so trusted that a single ruling from his lips could take a man's worst hour, the very worst thing he ever did, and seal it permanently out of reach, so that an entire community would cover it for the rest of that man's life. Hatib went on to serve. The Prophet ﷺ later chose him as his own emissary, sending him with a letter to the ruler of Egypt. He fought on. He died years afterward in the caliphate of Uthman, his standing whole and unblemished. And the Sheikh adds a quiet aside that lands like a benediction: generations later, one of Hatib's own descendants became a leading student of Imam Malik and helped carry the Maliki school across North Africa and into Andalusia, where it remains the dominant school to this day. Such, he says, are the works of Allah: from the man who wrote the letter, a line that would teach the religion to a continent.

This, the Sheikh says, is precisely how we are taught to weigh people: by the sum total of who they are, the good and the bad together, never by their worst three minutes. The righteous person who slips once is not the same as the habitual sinner who does the identical thing. There is even a hadith, the Sheikh notes, for the people of standing in a community, not the people of knowledge necessarily but the movers, the ones who carry it and serve it and fund its mosques: when such a one slips, overlook it, cut him some slack. We live in an age, he warns, that does the exact opposite, hunting through a person's thousand good hours for the five bad minutes and holding up only those. And he says it about himself, knowing he is among those picked apart this way: a scholar who speaks every day, now permanently recorded, will inevitably say something wrong somewhere across a thousand hours, and to comb for those minutes and parade them while going blind to all the good is not zeal. It is, he says, a disease of the heart.

The Sheikh folds several of his other benefits in alongside this one. The first is the infinite knowledge of Allah, that nothing escapes Him: a letter braided into a stranger's hair on an empty road, and He exposed it. It is the same Lord, the Sheikh reminds us, who heard the woman who came to dispute about her husband while Aisha sat in the very same room, behind a curtain, straining to catch her words and unable to, and Allah heard that complaint from above the seven heavens and answered it in the Qur'an. He hears what is whispered an arm's length away and unheard; He reads what is folded into a braid and unseen. The second is that du'a is the believer's true weapon. The march on Makkah stayed hidden, above all the secrecy, for one reason: the Prophet ﷺ had asked Allah to keep it hidden. O Allah, do not let my plan reach them. That du'a was the real precaution, and it was answered. The third balances it: even when Allah is handing you a miracle, He still wants your effort. He could have struck the woman down with lightning, or lost her camel in the desert until she died of thirst. Instead riders had to mount and gallop and search. Give your hundred percent, the Sheikh says, and leave the rest to Allah, because even when the help is entirely His, the effort He asks for is still yours.

The story they turn into a weapon

Here the Sheikh slows down and tells you, without flinching, that this is where the story has been turned into one of the most politically loaded passages in the entire seerah, and he does not pretend the hard question away. The question is real: what is the status of a Muslim who helps an enemy that is invading the Muslims? Hatib himself is not in doubt, because the Prophet ﷺ vouched for him directly. The doubt is about everyone after him. What of someone in our own time who aids an invading force against a Muslim land, who feeds it information, or works for it, or fights in its ranks?

There are two main answers, and the Sheikh lays them both out with respect. The first is the stricter one: that to aid an invading enemy against the Muslims is itself an act of disbelief, by that action alone. Its proof is a verse of Surah al-Ma'idah:

O you who have believed, do not take the Jews and the Christians as allies. They are [in fact] allies of one another. And whoever is an ally to them among you - then indeed, he is [one] of them. Indeed, Allah guides not the wrongdoing people.Qur'an 5:51

Whoever allies with them is one of them: this camp reads the words to mean that aiding the enemy expels a person from the faith outright. It is the position, the Sheikh says, of a number of serious and respected scholars, among them Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, who counted aiding the disbelievers against the Muslims among the very nullifiers of Islam, and Ahmad Shakir in Egypt, writing under British occupation, who ruled that whoever supported the British in any way at all, even by approving of their rule in speech, had left the fold of Islam. The Sheikh is explicit and generous here: he respects this position and the scholars who hold it; they have every right to their reading; he does not dismiss it.

But he leans, in the end, toward the more careful reading of Ibn Taymiyyah, and he draws it straight out of Hatib's own words. If a man aids the enemy out of love for their disbelief and hatred of the believers because they are believers, that is disbelief on its own, even if he never lifts a finger, because the heart alone has already done it. But if he aids them for a worldly reason, for his family, for property, out of a private fear, then it is a grave sin that wounds his faith without tearing him out of it. And that, exactly, is Hatib. He did not love the idols of Quraysh; he loved his mother and his children. He preferred their safety over the confidence of Allah and His Messenger in that one moment, and Allah named the act a grave straying, a wandering far from the path, yet never once called him a disbeliever, and the Prophet ﷺ guarded both his life and his name. The whole of Hatib's defense, the Sheikh points out, is precisely this argument: I have not changed, my heart is still full of iman, I did this out of fear, not out of love for kufr.

And this, the Sheikh says, is where the real danger lives in our own age, and he is blunt about it to the point of putting himself in the line of fire. The groups he calls jihadist, using the word, he is careful to say, as a plain academic label and not as praise, because what they do is not the jihad Allah and His Messenger laid down, almost all reach for the strictest verdict. They cut the strict ruling out of the book and paste it onto any Muslim who will not follow their line, until people who pray, who fast, who keep the Shariah are branded disbelievers and declared lawful to kill, simply for disagreeing. He has been accused of disbelief himself, he says, a hundred times over, for saying exactly this. And his answer is the beating heart of the whole episode: a ruling is not Google, to be copied and pasted. The work of the scholar is not to read a line in a book and assume it lands on the situation in front of him; it is to weigh the actual human being, his motive, his circumstance, his ignorance or his compulsion, the way the Prophet ﷺ weighed Hatib before he judged him. An act of disbelief, the Sheikh reminds us, does not by consensus make a person a disbeliever, because there may be a misreading in the heart, as there was in Hatib, who genuinely believed his letter could do no harm; or sheer ignorance, which Allah excuses, as He excused the terrified man who told his children to burn his body so his Lord could not gather him; or compulsion, as with the companion forced to utter words of disbelief while his heart stayed firm. To strip the faith off a praying Muslim so casually, the Sheikh says, is itself the way of the very fanatics the early Muslims feared, and it has done the ummah more damage than the invading armies ever did. When chaos comes, he says, hold your tongue. The Prophet ﷺ warned that whoever calls his brother a disbeliever, the word returns to one of the two of them; if it does not land on the one accused, it comes back on the one who said it. Err, always, on the side of caution. Whoever prays as we pray, faces our qiblah, and eats our slaughter is a Muslim, and Allah will never punish you for having been too slow to cast a believer out of the faith.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this as a spy story with a merciful ending and leave it there. That would be to miss the questions it presses against our own hearts, and there are several, because the Sheikh drew several out of one evening.

Hatib hid his secret where no human being could ever have found it, and Allah found it. Nothing you fold away in the dark is folded away from Him. He hears the whisper you yourself cannot make out across a small room, and He reads the letter buried in a braid on an empty road. Ask, then, how you would live this single day if you truly carried the knowledge that you are seen: every private message, every hidden account, every thing done where no eye reaches. The man who lives as one who is watched, because he is, has already won half the battle with himself.

And notice the order of the Prophet's ﷺ own striving. The campaign stayed secret not finally because of clever security but because he had asked his Lord to keep it secret, and then he sent the riders out anyway. Du'a first, and then the full weight of human effort, and never one without the other. We are quick to exhaust our own plans and slow to ask, or quick to ask and too lazy to move. His way was both at once: beg Allah to do what only Allah can do, and then gallop as though it all depended on you. He wants your hundred percent, and then He wants you to rest the rest in Him.

Then there is the way a man was judged. With the proof total and revelation itself naming him, the Prophet ﷺ still turned to Hatib and asked, did you do this. If the case that airtight was still put to the man's own face, then we have no excuse, ever, for the verdict we pass on someone behind his back on a fraction of the evidence. Confirm it to the person. Hear his reason. There may be a whole world in it you cannot see from outside. And when you have heard it, weigh him as the Prophet ﷺ weighed Hatib: by the sum total of his life, his Badr and his letter together, not by his worst three minutes pulled out and held to the light while the thousand good hours go dark. To see only the failure, the Sheikh warned, is a sickness of the heart, not a sign of zeal.

And last, and heaviest, there is the thing Hatib fought for. With a sword at his neck he never once said spare me. He spent his last breath defending his iman, because dying was bearable to him and dying labelled a disbeliever was not. Hold that against the ease with which the word is thrown today. The man who pleads for his faith and not his life when he thinks he is about to die is not a man who hates his Lord, and the Prophet ﷺ knew it, and shielded him, and sealed his name as good for twenty more years. Be slow, then, terrified-slow, to put a praying soul outside the faith. Leave that weighing to those truly qualified for it, and for the rest of us, when the fitna is thick and the tongues are loud, the safest and most beloved thing before Allah is often silence.

So take this evening down into your ordinary days. Live as one who is seen, because you are. Ask Allah first and then work as though it is all on your shoulders. Hear a person out before you judge him, cover his slip the way the Prophet ﷺ covered Hatib's, and weigh him by the whole of his life. And guard your tongue from declaring against anyone who prays to Allah, for the word you throw has a way of coming home. O Allah, You are peace, and from You is peace, and to You peace returns; make our hearts firm upon Your religion, keep our tongues from casting out those who pray to You, gather us among the forgiven people of Badr, and let us meet our Prophet ﷺ with nothing on our record that he would not wish to see. Ameen.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 77: the conquest of Makkah, part 2 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (60:1, 5:51), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing has been followed.

The Seerah · 78 of 104

The Conquest of Makkah, Part 3

The march back, and the men who came home before the gates fell

The army has formed, and the road north is open. On the tenth of Ramadan, in the eighth year after the Hijrah, the Prophet ﷺ rides out of Madinah at the head of ten thousand and turns toward the city that once drove him into the dark. The march is a secret kept so tightly that Makkah itself does not yet know he is coming. Letters have been intercepted, mouths have been sealed, and the largest force Arabia has ever seen is moving across the desert almost without a rumor to announce it.

But the famous part of this story, the city that falls without a battle, is still days away. What the road delivers first is not a battlefield. It is men. Four of them, every one once an enemy of the Prophet ﷺ, every one now hurrying in the opposite direction to reach Islam before it arrives without them. Today belongs to them. Dr. Yasir Qadhi frames the whole of this lesson around their conversions, because before the conquest had begun at all, Islam was already winning back the people who had hated it longest, one man at a time, on an open road. It is a day about the door that stays open until the very last moment, and about who is small enough to walk through it.

Leaving, and the man no one remembers

Before he had gone a mile, the Prophet ﷺ made a quiet decision that is easy to ride straight past. He left Madinah in the care of a companion almost no one has heard of, before this day or after it: Abu Ruhm al-Ghifari. There is a lesson hidden in that name alone. The plain fact, the Sheikh notes, is that every major companion you can name marched on Makkah; the great names were all on the road. And yet someone still had to mind the city, to keep the home behind the army standing. The someone was not a famous man. History barely records him. There is no shame in being the one who holds the home front while the others ride out to glory, and the choosing of an unknown man to guard Madinah is the Prophet's ﷺ own way of saying so.

He ﷺ and his companions were fasting, for it was Ramadan, and he did not break that fast inside the walls of the city. He waited until they reached a place called Kadid, just outside Madinah, and only there did he break it, and the people broke theirs along with him. The Sheikh pauses on this small scene, because the vast majority of scholars build a ruling out of it. Travel does not begin from inside your own city. You are not permitted to shorten your prayer or to skip the fast of a travel-day while you are still within your own walls. So if your journey falls in Ramadan, you begin that day fasting, and you keep fasting until your city is genuinely behind you. Dr. Yasir Qadhi makes it concrete for the rest of us, who no longer travel by camel: if your flight leaves at three in the afternoon, you cannot break your fast in the Memphis airport, or in any airport, because the airport is still inside the city. You cannot shorten your prayer in your own house before you set out. The departure begins where the city ends, and not one step before.

The last man ever to emigrate

They were barely two days out, still less than halfway and perhaps no more than a quarter of the way, when they passed the valley of al-Juhfah. Coming the other way, from Makkah toward Madinah, was a small caravan: women, children, and at its head the uncle of the Prophet ﷺ, al-Abbas, traveling with his wives and his family. He was coming as a muhajir. He had no idea any of this was happening. The march was a secret so complete that all of Makkah was in the dark; an uncle on the road to join the Muslims certainly had not been told.

And so Allah handed al-Abbas an honor that no human being after him would ever receive. The Prophet ﷺ was the seal of the prophets; there is no prophet after him. Al-Abbas, by meeting the army at exactly this place, became the seal of the emigrants: there is no hijrah after the conquest of Makkah, and he made it under the wire, never even reaching Madinah, his intention to emigrate carrying him over the line just as the gate was closing. The Sheikh notes a hadith on this, that the Prophet ﷺ told him you are the last of the emigrants just as I am the last of the prophets; the report is weak, but, as he reminds us, there is no harm in using a weak narration in history, only in law. The Prophet ﷺ rejoiced at the sight of his uncle, told him to settle his family somewhere safe and then catch up, and al-Abbas, riding light and alone, soon overtook the slow-moving host.

When exactly al-Abbas entered Islam is a question the scholars argued over for pages, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi lays out their whole range without forcing one of them on us. Some placed it impossibly early; some at the ransom after Badr, when he was a prisoner of war and the Prophet ﷺ told him of money he had buried in secret that no one alive could have known of; some before the conquest of Khaybar; and some right here, on this road. What the Sheikh will say with confidence, setting the academic question aside, is this: after the Battle of Badr, al-Abbas knew Islam was true. Whether he declared it then or kept it hidden, serving for years as the Prophet's ﷺ man inside Makkah, his heart had already turned. This was the uncle the Prophet ﷺ had once named at Badr among the three not to be killed; this was the man whose grief at false news of a Muslim defeat, and whose joy at the truth, had given him away. He waited a long time. He did not, in the end, wait too long.

The cousins who were turned away

Two more men slipped out of Makkah in panic and fear, and hurried to meet the army before it could reach the city. They did not emigrate to Madinah; they simply came to submit on the road, about a day's journey out. Both were first cousins of the Prophet ﷺ. Both had spent years wounding him. Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith was the son of al-Harith, the eldest of the Prophet's ﷺ uncles, and he was a poet, and in the years in Makkah he had aimed his poetry at the Prophet ﷺ like a weapon, composing verse after verse to ridicule the message and mock the man. In that age there was no crueler form of slander than this. The other cousin, Abdullah ibn Abi Umayyah, had once stood before the chiefs of Quraysh and thrown a sneer in the Prophet's ﷺ face: I will never believe in you, he said, until you climb a ladder into the sky in front of me and bring back down a book and a covenant we can read with our own eyes, and even then, I am not sure I would believe. The height of arrogance, and delivered in the open parliament of Quraysh, before all their leaders. These were men the Prophet ﷺ had grown up beside, blood relatives bound by the old cousin's camaraderie, and they had turned on him when he was most in need of them.

That exact mockery, the Sheikh notes, is written into the Qur'an. Allah recorded the demands of the deniers, every escalating one of them, and answered them all in a single dignified line placed in His Prophet's ﷺ own mouth:

And they say, "We will not believe you until you break open for us from the ground a spring Or [until] you have a garden of palm trees and grapes and make rivers gush forth within them in force [and abundance] Or you make the heaven fall upon us in fragments as you have claimed or you bring Allah and the angels before [us] Or you have a house of ornament [i.e., gold] or you ascend into the sky. And [even then], we will not believe in your ascension until you bring down to us a book we may read." Say, "Exalted is my Lord! Was I ever but a human messenger?"Qur'an 17:90-93

The spring from the ground, the garden with rivers running through it, the sky torn down in fragments, Allah and the angels brought out in ranks, the house of gold, the climb into heaven, the book carried back down in hand: every impossible thing the deniers demanded, gathered and recorded forever, and met with one calm sentence. Exalted is my Lord. Was I ever but a human messenger? The demand for a spectacle is answered not with a spectacle but with the dignity of a servant who knows exactly what he is and what he is not.

Now these two cousins came begging. They did not go to the Prophet ﷺ directly. They went to his wife Umm Salamah, radiyallahu anha, the sister of one of them, and asked her to carry their request inside, to intercede for them and tell him that his cousins had come in remorse. She felt for them, after all they were her own kin, and she went in and laid their plea before him. The answer came back hard: I have no need for them. I have no need to see them, and no wish to. He ﷺ would not even grant them an audience.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi stops here deliberately, because this scene stings people who only know the gentle Prophet ﷺ, the one who pardoned and pardoned and pardoned again. How could he refuse them outright? But the Sheikh answers it plainly. You cannot govern, you cannot establish justice, you cannot protect a community, by always and only turning the other cheek. Any creed that preaches nothing but endless forgiveness collapses the moment it must build a society, because people will take advantage of it without end. So in the life of the Prophet ﷺ the general rule was mercy, the standing law was forgiveness, but there remained, deliberately, a fear in it: that to cross the line all the way had a cost, and that crossing it was not always met with a smile. He treated people, too, according to who they were; he was gentler with a raw bedouin than with the senior companions he held to a higher standard, and these two were no strangers but cousins who knew exactly what they were doing when they did it. And there was a second wisdom folded inside the refusal, the Sheikh adds, one that the next hour would reveal: it was a test. Turn an insincere man away at the door, and he grows angry and leaves, his pride wounded, muttering that if you do not want him he does not want you. Turn a man away who has finally understood what Islam truly is, and he will not leave. He will stand at the door and refuse to go. It is the difference, the Sheikh says, between Iblis, who was rejected and grew arrogant, and Adam, who fell and begged to return.

I will not go back

Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith did not leave. He raised his voice where he knew the Prophet ﷺ would hear it, and he took his young son Ja'far by the hand and lifted him up, and called out: if you will not see me, then by Allah, my son and I will walk out into the desert until we die of hunger and thirst. I am not going back to Makkah. You may call it emotional blackmail, the Sheikh says, and you would not be wrong, but it worked, because the heart it was aimed at was a soft and gentle one. This was his cousin, and the small boy was his cousin's son, his own nephew, a child he had perhaps never even met, but blood all the same. The histories say the Prophet ﷺ felt the softness rise in him at the sound of his cousin begging, and he let the two of them in.

Abu Sufyan had a poem ready, as he always did, but this one had turned all the way around. The Sheikh says the loss of these poems in translation is among his own regrets in teaching the seerah in English, for they are full of a profound beauty that even fluent modern Arabic cannot fully unlock. One line of it ran, in its meaning: a guide other than myself guided me to Allah, a guide whom I kept repelling at every opportunity I could find, and still he kept on guiding me. When Abu Sufyan reached that line, the Prophet ﷺ struck him lightly on the chest, the brotherly way you push someone you love, and said: yes, you repelled me every time you could. The grief he had carried showed for a moment in those words, the hurt of a man whose own cousin had turned a weapon on him. And then he forgave him, fully. This same Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith would prove the depth of his new faith within weeks, at the Battle of Hunayn, standing firm beside the Prophet ﷺ when others broke and ran, and shaming those who fled.

So three men of the old enemy had already come home before the city was even in sight: al-Abbas the uncle, and the two cousins, Abu Sufyan ibn al-Harith and Abdullah ibn Abi Umayyah. The conquest had not begun, and the hardest hearts were already turning.

Ten thousand fires in the dark

Twenty kilometers from Makkah, roughly a single day's journey out, the army reached a place called Marr az-Zahran and made its final camp. In the morning they would march in. And here the Prophet ﷺ gave an order that says everything about how far the world had turned: light your fires. Every man, light his own. There was no longer any need to hide their numbers, no need for the old caution that had shadowed every earlier expedition. Ten thousand fires bloomed across the plain until the dark itself seemed to catch and burn, campfire upon campfire as far as the eye could reach.

Makkah was a shadow of the city that had driven him out. Fifteen years of emigration, of warriors lost, of tribe after allied tribe accepting Islam, had hollowed it out. The Sheikh reckons it held perhaps a fifth, even a sixteenth, of the people who had filled it when the message first came; the rest had emigrated or fallen, and more than three quarters of Quraysh's old allies had themselves become Muslim. The few who remained could not field an army, and the allies who might once have come would not. Dr. Yasir Qadhi names the reversal in a single breath, and it is worth stopping over: only three years earlier, at the trench around Madinah, the whole of Arabia had massed at the gates of the Muslims to wipe Islam off the earth. Now Islam stood at the gates of Makkah, and Quraysh had no one left to call. The table had turned, completely, and all of it belonged to Allah and His Messenger.

Al-Abbas, the brand-new emigrant whose conversion Quraysh still did not know about, felt the old pity for his former people stir in him as he looked at those fires. He had been one of them only yesterday. He begged the Prophet ﷺ to let him ride out and try to spare Makkah a battle, to give the people one last chance to come out and submit before the army came in. The Prophet ﷺ gave him his own mule to ride, and sent him into the night.

The chieftain on the Prophet's mule

In the dark al-Abbas overheard three men who had crept out of Makkah to scout the strange fires on the plain. The loudest of them was Abu Sufyan ibn Harb, the great chieftain of Makkah, and the Sheikh is careful to keep him distinct from the cousin of the same name: this is the famous Abu Sufyan, the father of Mu'awiyah, the last senior leader Quraysh had left, for Badr and the years after it had taken all his peers. He and his companions could make no sense of what they saw; it never once occurred to them that the Muslims could be this many. One of them guessed wildly that some friendly tribe must have come to defend Makkah, and Abu Sufyan, who knew the tribes better than any of them, answered that they had neither the courage nor the numbers to be this. Al-Abbas called out to him by name. This, he said, is the army of Muhammad ﷺ, and these are not your helpers, and if he takes the city by force tomorrow, your head will be the first to fall. Come with me, and I will ask him to spare you.

Abu Sufyan, seeing no other road, climbed up behind al-Abbas on the Prophet's ﷺ own mule, and the two of them rode straight through ten thousand men, every one of whom would have killed him on sight and gladly. At every campfire a voice challenged them out of the dark, and every time al-Abbas answered, this is al-Abbas on the mule of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, and every time they were waved on without another word, because to ride the Prophet's ﷺ own mount was a sign not just of protection but of trust, a sign that he must have sent you on some errand, and no man dared question it. So they passed through the whole camp untouched, until they came upon Umar ibn al-Khattab, radiyallahu anhu, who recognized the chieftain at once and leapt up calling for his execution: praise be to Allah, who has delivered the enemy of His Messenger to us with no treaty and no covenant to protect him. Al-Abbas threw out a tribal argument, that Umar would never want to kill a man if he were of Umar's own clan. And Umar shut it down cold. Listen to what he said, the Sheikh urges, for it is one of the great declarations of love in the whole seerah. By Allah, Umar told him, the day you accepted Islam was more beloved to me than the day my own father would have, had he lived to accept it, and only for one reason: because your Islam brought the Prophet ﷺ more joy than my own father's Islam ever could have. How dare you, then, play the tribe card with me. The Prophet ﷺ ended the standoff before it could harden: take him to your tent, al-Abbas, and bring him to me in the morning.

All that night al-Abbas worked on him, the two of them going back and forth, al-Abbas pressing, Abu Sufyan resisting, and at dawn al-Abbas brought him in. Isn't it time, the Prophet ﷺ asked him, that you came to know there is no god but Allah? Abu Sufyan answered first with the highest praise an Arab tongue can offer, may my mother and father be your ransom, how gentle you are, how merciful, how good to your kin, for I would never have treated you as you are treating me. And then he admitted the truth that had finally broken through: by Allah, had there been any other gods beside Allah, they would surely have done something for me by now. He had seen it at last. And the Sheikh draws out, with some urgency for the young especially, how slow that seeing had been. These were men who had watched the Prophet ﷺ at close range for twenty-three years and still half-believed in their idols. It is very hard, he says, to abandon the theology you were raised in, very hard to walk away from a culture you hold dear. Faith is almost never a five-minute conversation. Abu Sufyan accepted that there is no god but Allah, and then balked again at the second half of the testimony: as for Muhammad, I am not yet sure. At this al-Abbas, out of patience after the long night, told him bluntly to submit or be killed in the morning. Now mark this carefully, the Sheikh insists: it was not the Prophet ﷺ who threatened him, for we do not threaten people into faith and it is forbidden to do so; it was an exhausted friend at the end of his rope, saying come now, I have done all I can, the rest is on you. And reluctantly, half-convinced, Abu Sufyan said the full shahada. For we hold Islam to be true, the Sheikh explains, and so when a man half-accepts it, we let him in, trusting the truth itself to win the rest of him over in time. And in time, it would.

It is not a kingdom

Al-Abbas knew his man. O Messenger of Allah, he said, Abu Sufyan is a man who loves his pride; give him something to hold onto. So the Prophet ﷺ granted him an honor woven exactly to his nature. When Makkah is entered tomorrow, he announced, whoever stays within the Sacred Mosque is safe, and whoever stays inside his own home is safe, and whoever enters the house of Abu Sufyan is safe. His house, alone among the houses of Makkah, would become a sanctuary. You meet a person where his dignity lives, the Sheikh observes, and a proud chieftain was handed a way to stand tall inside his own surrender, to be not merely a man spared but a man whose roof now sheltered others.

Then the Prophet ﷺ sent al-Abbas to hold Abu Sufyan at a narrow neck of the valley, where the whole army would have to file past him as it marched on the city. And banner after banner went by. Who are these, Abu Sufyan kept asking, and al-Abbas named them, tribe after tribe he had never in his life imagined gathered under a single command, until Abu Sufyan could only marvel that no one on earth could stand against such a host. Last of all came the contingent of the Muhajirun and the Ansar, ringed close around the Prophet ﷺ himself, so thick with armor that, the histories say, you could see nothing but the gleam of steel. Abu Sufyan stared at it and turned to al-Abbas: the kingdom of your nephew, he said, has become very great. And al-Abbas corrected him with one sentence that holds the entire meaning of the day inside it: it is not a kingdom. It is prophethood. It is not power seized and held; it is the gift Allah gives to a messenger, and it has brought him all of this.

Stand where Abu Sufyan stood, the Sheikh urges, and feel the turn of it. What a scene, he says, what a melodramatic sight: ten thousand believers marching in, and the chieftain of Quraysh watching every banner pass and at last submitting. Eight years before, this same Prophet ﷺ had slipped out of this very city in the dead of night with a single companion, Abu Bakr beside him and no one else, and hidden in a cave for three days while Quraysh hunted him for his life and put a price on his head. Now he returns at the head of ten thousand, and the last great chieftain of his enemies watches the army of Allah pass by and gives up his idols. Where are the proud ones now, the Sheikh asks, the ones who tortured the believers and plotted in their assembly hall and drove the Messenger ﷺ out into the desert? Gone, every one of them, fallen at Badr or scattered to the wind. From where to where had Allah carried His Prophet ﷺ in less than a decade. The matter belongs to Allah, and to no one else, and it always did.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to remember the conquest of Makkah only for the city that fell without a battle, and to let the road there blur past on the way to the gates. That would be to miss the question this day puts to us. Strip it down and four men stand in it, and not one of them deserved the door to still be open. An uncle who emigrated with no time to spare. Two cousins who had cut the Prophet ﷺ with poetry and a public sneer. A chieftain who had fought him for twenty years and submitted in the last hour of the last night. All four found the door open anyway. That is the mercy walking quietly underneath the whole conquest: he came back not for vengeance but to gather the ones who were finally ready, even the ones who had hated him longest.

The first thing this day asks is that you believe the door stays open later than you think. Al-Abbas crossed the line into the honor of hijrah with almost no margin at all, the very last man ever to hold that title, his intention carrying him over just as the gate swung shut. Abu Sufyan, the great chieftain, said the shahada in the final dark before the city fell, reluctant and half-convinced, and Islam took him in. As long as you are still breathing, you are not too late to come home to Allah. Do not let the years you wasted, or the things you said, or the length of your turning away, persuade you that you have missed your chance. These men had wasted more, said worse, and turned away longer, and the door was still open.

The second thing it asks is that you learn to read your own sincerity by what it is willing to endure. He ﷺ turned the cousins away, the Sheikh tells us, partly as a measured act of governance, for unchecked mercy cannot rule, and partly as a test. The insincere man, refused, grows angry and leaves; the sincere man, refused, stands at the door and will not go, the way Adam begged to return while Iblis only swelled with pride. When something good costs you, when the thing you want pushes back and makes you wait at the door, the staying is the proof. Ask yourself whether your faith is the kind that leaves when it is not flattered, or the kind that refuses to leave at all.

And the third thing it asks is patience, both with the people you love and with the slow turning of your own heart. Men watched the Prophet ﷺ for twenty-three years, the Sheikh reminds the eager among us, and still half-believed in their idols at the end of it. Faith is rarely a quick conversation. You cannot argue a heart into the truth in five minutes, and you should not expect to. Plant, and be gentle, and let Allah open the way in His own time, with others and with yourself. And when a heart does turn, even reluctantly, even half-convinced like Abu Sufyan's, let it in, and trust the truth to finish the work. Honor people, too, where their dignity lives, as the Prophet ﷺ honored a proud man with a house of refuge: you can be firm on the truth and still leave a person standing tall, and doing both at once is the Prophetic way.

So take this road down into your ordinary days. Believe the door is still open for you, because it is, as surely as it stood open for an uncle out of time and a chieftain out of arguments. Do not give up on the people whose hearts are turning slowly, and do not give up on your own. And ask Allah for the one thing none of these four men could ever have guaranteed themselves, the gift of dying upon the faith they reached so late: O Allah, You who turned the hearts of Your Prophet's ﷺ oldest enemies into his ummah in a single night, turn ours fully toward You and keep them turned, and let us live as Muslims and take us back to You as Muslims, gathered at last under the banner of the one who came home to Makkah in mercy. May Allah send His peace and blessings upon His Messenger ﷺ, who returned to the most beloved of cities not to repay its cruelty but to open its door, and who left no one behind who was willing to walk through it.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 78: the Conquest of Makkah, part 3 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translation is from Sahih International (17:90-93), verified via quran.ai.

The Seerah · 79 of 104

The conquest of Makkah, part 4

Go, you are free

It is the morning of the 20th of Ramadan, in the eighth year after the hijrah, and the people of Makkah do not know that the end of their world is one hour away. Ten thousand men are standing at the edge of the city, the largest army the Arabs had ever assembled, and not one watchman has run ahead with the news. Dr. Yasir Qadhi asks us to sit with how impossible that is, because in that Arabia word of a single caravan reached a town before the camels did; it was simply how the desert worked. Yet here is a force of ten thousand, arriving at the very doors of Makkah, and the city sleeps on. There is only one explanation, and the Sheikh gives it plainly: when the Prophet ﷺ had asked Allah to conceal his march from Quraysh, Allah concealed it, and kept it concealed to the literal minute the army walked in. It is humanly impossible to hide ten thousand people from a city. So it was not a human doing. It was a miracle, quiet and total, and the men who lived through it never forgot that the silence around them had been arranged in heaven.

Twenty one years of mockery, of boycott in the valley, of torture in the sun, of exile and of war had led to this single sunrise. The man Makkah had driven out was about to walk back into it as its master. What he does in the hours that follow is the reason this day is counted among the very highest points of his whole life ﷺ, and it has almost nothing to do with the size of the army behind him.

An army no one saw coming

The surrender had come the night before. Abu Sufyan, the lifelong leader of Quraysh and for two decades the architect of its wars, had finally accepted Islam in the Muslim camp, after al-Abbas spent an entire night reasoning with him in the dark. By morning his loyalties had turned, and al-Abbas, the recent convert himself, his heart still half attached to the people he had only just left, sent him galloping back through the gates with one urgent task: warn Makkah not to fight. There is no winning this, only blood if they try.

So while Abu Sufyan rode for the city, the Prophet ﷺ ordered the army drawn up. The books of sira keep a long, detailed roll of which clan marched where and who carried which banner, names that mean little to most of us now, but the shape of it is simple enough. There were three great contingents, each with its own smaller companies folded inside. Over one he set the Ansar, over another the Muhajirun, and in the middle rode a mixed body with the Prophet ﷺ himself among them. Khalid ibn al-Walid led one wing; Sa'd ibn Ubadah, one of the two chiefs of the Ansar and the man in whose house the Ansar would one day gather after the Prophet's ﷺ passing, carried the banner of his people.

And over the whole army he laid down one command above every other: sheathe your swords. Do not fight anyone. Do not kill a soul unless you are first attacked, and then only in your own defense. Hold that sentence in your mind, the Sheikh says, because the world has never seen its like. An army the size of a nation was marching on a city under strict and explicit orders not to make war on it.

A banner taken back

As the columns moved in, something in the old tribal blood rose up in Sa'd ibn Ubadah, and he began to chant as he went: today is the day of slaughter, today the day of the great battle, today the sanctuary itself would lose its sanctity and blood would be lawful in it. The word he used was halal. The haram, he was crying, would today be made halal. Understand what those words meant, because the whole life of that ground hangs on them. The sacred precinct is called the haram precisely because things permitted everywhere else become forbidden inside it: you may not hunt its game or pluck its wild trees, and you may certainly not spill blood within it. Even in their paganism, Quraysh had never once declared the haram open for killing. Now the standard-bearer of the Ansar was shouting through its streets that its sanctity had ended.

Abu Sufyan heard it and was alarmed, and he hurried to the Prophet ﷺ: how can he say the haram will be made halal today? The Prophet ﷺ answered that Sa'd had erred, kadhaba in the old classical sense, which here means not that he lied but that he had spoken wrongly, gotten it mistaken. And quietly, without a public rebuke, he had the banner lifted from Sa'd's hand and placed instead in the hand of a man of Quraysh. Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi points to a precision in the Prophet's ﷺ judgment made in the space of a single moment. Sa'd intended no evil; he had simply been carried away, and the heat of the old days had risen in his voice on exactly the wrong morning. But the men leading the entry into the sacred city, the Prophet ﷺ saw at once, should be the city's own people, the Quraysh, so that never afterward could anyone say that outsiders had come to conquer the house of their fathers. He read the danger, weighed it, and adjusted the entire order of the march in an instant. That is ijtihad on the spot, the Sheikh notes: he reasoned, saw the harm coming, and moved to head it off before it arrived.

So the contingents poured in from the west of the town and from the east, its two great gateways, swords down, while Abu Sufyan ran ahead through the lanes crying the strangest call to arms the Arabs ever heard. He did not call them to battle. He called them to surrender. Here is Muhammad, he shouted, he has come with an army you cannot fight, so do not even rise to meet it. Come to my house and you are safe. The very last refuge the Prophet ﷺ had granted him the night before, whoever enters Abu Sufyan's house is safe, Abu Sufyan now turned into the only refuge worth taking, and pressed it on his people in the street. His own wife, the formidable Hind, darted out of the crowd, seized him by his beard, and cursed him in public with words so vile the old books record them half in wonder, calling him a coward, a useless fat man, demanding the people kill this lazy, treacherous leader of theirs. Abu Sufyan held his ground. Woe to you, he told the crowd, do not let her push you into a rash thing; an army has come that you cannot fight. When one of them asked what use a single house could be to a whole city, he finally told them everything: whoever shuts his own door is safe, whoever reaches the haram is safe, whoever enters my house is safe. And the people scattered, some to their homes, some racing to the sanctuary, some to the two or three houses thrown open as public refuge that day. The message had carried. Makkah was laying down its arms.

Barely a drop of blood

Not quite all of it. A small knot of Quraysh could not stomach surrender, and with perhaps half an hour's warning they snatched up whatever weapons lay to hand and threw themselves at one of the incoming flanks. At their head was Ikrimah, the son of Abu Jahl, and with him two of the last great chiefs still living, Safwan ibn Umayyah and Suhayl ibn Amr. It was not a battle. The books of sira spare it a line or two, and even those lines make clear it was nothing organized, only a chaotic, hopeless rush of men who had no time to think. They struck the wing that Khalid ibn al-Walid was leading. A dozen or so of Quraysh were killed, by one count thirteen or fourteen, by another perhaps twenty, and two or three of the Muslims fell, and it was over almost before it began.

And so, Dr. Yasir Qadhi says, Makkah was finally conquered on the 20th of Ramadan in the eighth year of the hijrah, with barely any loss of life at all. After twenty one years of suffering, the Prophet ﷺ returned as the undisputed master of the city of his birth, the greatest city in the history not merely of Islam but of all humanity, and almost no one had to die for it. Pause on that, because this is simply not how cities fall. Conquest in the human story means siege and sack, fire and slaughter and the long settling of scores. This is the one conquest ever won by an army marching in under orders not to fight, and the Sheikh wants us to feel how unprecedented it stands, before and since: a city honored by being taken without war, by a commander who forbade his men to draw their swords as they entered it.

The conqueror with his head down

He came in from the high northern side of the town, the place still known today, mounted on his camel and dressed in his armor, with a red turban wound and tucked beneath his chin rather than streaming behind him. And here is the image the Sheikh asks us never to lose. The Prophet ﷺ lowered his head, lowered it and lowered it, until his blessed forehead was almost touching the back of the camel, and as he rode he was glorifying Allah and reciting Surah al-Fath under his breath: we have given you a manifest victory. Empires send their kings into a captured city with their chests thrown out and their chins lifted high, drunk on the moment. The most successful conqueror who ever lived came in bent nearly double over the neck of his mount, the living picture of a servant who knows exactly Whose victory this is and that none of it is his own. Neither before this day nor after it, the Sheikh says, has any conqueror entered a city he had taken in such a posture of humility before God instead of pride before men.

He rode straight to the Ka'bah, the house his father Ibrahim had raised, and began his tawaf still mounted, a staff in his hand and no sword at all. Around the house stood the idols of Quraysh, more than three hundred and sixty of them, very nearly one for every day of their year. Each time he passed one, he pointed the staff toward it, and it fell: forward if its face was toward him, backward if its back was turned, every last one toppling at the gesture of a man who never touched it. A miracle of Allah, Dr. Yasir Qadhi calls it, worked through the pointing of a stick. And as the idols came down he recited the verse that the moment was built to carry:

And say, "Truth has come, and falsehood has departed. Indeed is falsehood, [by nature], ever bound to depart."Qur'an 17:81

He touched the Black Stone with the staff as he came round to it, and kept circling, while the precinct filled and filled with the people of the city, believers and pagans pressed together, until the whole of Makkah stood gathered in one place around the man on the camel, waiting to learn what he would do with them.

Cleansing the house

When the tawaf was done he called for the keys, and with his own blessed hands ﷺ he unlocked the door of the Ka'bah and stepped inside. The interior was no purer than the courtyard had been. There were small idols of precious materials, ornaments, painted images on the walls, even depictions of angels, whom the pagans had imagined as the daughters of God. He gathered them out and broke them, throwing down and cracking apart everything he found, until the inside of the house was as clean as the truth it had been built to hold.

On one wall he came upon a painting of Ibrahim, and in the painted hands were the divining arrows the pagans cast before their ventures, and it grieved him. May Allah ruin them, he said, what has Ibrahim to do with this? Ibrahim was no caster of arrows, no diviner of fates. He was a man of pure and upright faith who surrendered himself wholly to Allah, neither Jew nor Christian and never for an instant an idolater, and the Qur'an itself draws the line clean:

Abraham was neither a Jew nor a Christian, but he was one inclining toward truth, a Muslim [submitting to Allah]. And he was not of the polytheists.Qur'an 3:67

Then every image on those walls was wiped away.

Watch the order of what he did, the Sheikh says, because the order is the whole seerah pressed into a single afternoon. The first act of this conqueror is not to settle accounts, not to divide spoils, for there are no spoils here, no captives taken, no land seized, no plunder carried off; the conquest of Makkah was unique in that nothing of the kind was taken at all. The first thing he does is worship Allah and tear down what had been worshipped beside Him. He cleanses the outer idols, then the inner ones, then the painted filth on the walls, and only after the house of Allah has been returned to the purity Ibrahim built it for does he turn at last to face the people. He attends to his Lord before he attends to anything in the world, even in the hour of the greatest victory a man was ever given.

Go, you are free

Now he stood in the open doorway of the Ka'bah, on its very threshold, and turned to face them. Imagine the scene, the Sheikh says, because words cannot reach it. Behind him is the house of Allah with its doors flung wide. Before him are gathered more than ten thousand of his own followers and, packed in among them, the thousands of Quraysh who had hunted him, mocked him, tortured his companions in the heat, and driven him out of his home. The doors are open. The man they wronged for twenty one years is standing in them, and every single soul in that precinct is utterly, completely in his power.

He began with the only thing that ever mattered: there is no god but Allah alone, He has fulfilled His promise, He has aided His servant, and He alone has defeated the confederates. Then he abolished the age that had ended. Every claim and custom and conceit of jahiliyyah, the whole inherited hierarchy of who is born above whom, the arrogance of blood and tribe, all of it, he declared, is gone, struck down, finished. He recited to them what Allah had revealed:

O mankind, indeed We have created you from male and female and made you peoples and tribes that you may know one another. Indeed, the most noble of you in the sight of Allah is the most righteous of you. Indeed, Allah is Knowing and Aware.Qur'an 49:13

All of you are from Adam, he told them, and Adam was from dust. The only rank left standing in this new order is taqwa: the one nearer to Allah is simply the one who fears Him more. Of all the old honors of Makkah he kept just two alive, the custodianship of the Ka'bah and the watering of the pilgrims, and let the rest fall away with the idols.

Then came the question. He turned to the Quraysh and asked them, with what the Sheikh calls flawless and perfect psychology, what do you think I will do with you? Hear everything that one gentle sentence does at once. It tells them, without a word of boasting, that their fate now rests entirely in his hands; he establishes the rank he has earned over them, but he establishes it by asking rather than by declaring. And underneath the gentleness lies the unspoken weight of all they have done, you have wronged me terribly, you deserve to be punished, said not by accusing them but by leaving the silence for them to fill. He does not say how could you, or why did you. He simply asks, and lets the answer rise out of hearts that had always known the truth of him and had been too proud or too afraid to say it. And they said it now, with their own tongues: you are a noble brother, and the son of a noble brother; you will do nothing but good. Then he gave them the very words Yusuf had once given the brothers who sold him, the words that had been revealed to comfort him long ago in Makkah when he was the one persecuted and alone, a promise that a day of power would come and that on that day he was to forgive as Yusuf forgave. No blame upon you today, he told them. May Allah forgive you. Go, you are free.

Words cannot do justice to the scene, the Sheikh admits, and lets it stand. The man at the height of all earthly power, with the people who had ruined his every year standing helpless before him, reached not for the sword but for the gentlest sentence in the Qur'an, and set a whole city free.

Even the exceptions were forgiven

There was still the matter of the keys. When Uthman ibn Talha brought them, Ali ibn Abi Talib asked the Prophet ﷺ to grant his own clan, Banu Hashim, the honor of holding the custodianship of the Ka'bah together with the watering of the pilgrims, joining a hundred years of divided privilege into one. The Prophet ﷺ would not. He called Uthman ibn Talha forward and placed the keys back into his hands: take them, this is a day of faithfulness and of giving back what is owed. Allah had commanded the returning of trusts, and the Sheikh notes that the scholars read this very moment into the verse:

Indeed, Allah commands you to render trusts to whom they are due and when you judge between people to judge with justice. Excellent is that which Allah instructs you. Indeed, Allah is ever Hearing and Seeing.Qur'an 4:58

From that day to this, across the whole long history of the religion, no one has dared take the key from the descendants of Uthman ibn Talha. The door has changed and the key with it, but the trust has stayed in that one family, generation after generation, exactly where it was placed on the morning of the conquest.

And there was a short, ugly list. Before he entered, the Prophet ﷺ had named a handful of people, somewhere between six and nine by the differing counts of the early books, who were not covered by the general amnesty and could be killed wherever they were found. They had earned it. There was Ikrimah, the son of Abu Jahl. There was Abdullah ibn Khatal, who had accepted Islam, then murdered the companion sent with him and fled back to Makkah a renegade, and set two slave singing-girls to composing poetry mocking the Prophet ﷺ, the height of propaganda in that age. There was a man who had feigned Islam only to carry out a revenge killing. There were the two who had brutalized the Prophet's ﷺ own daughters as they tried to leave Makkah, driving a spear at the camel of Zaynab until she fell and miscarried, and threatening his two younger daughters on the road. And there was Wahshi, who had killed his beloved uncle Hamza at Uhud. Of these, Abdullah ibn Khatal clung to the very curtains of the Ka'bah begging sanctuary, and even so was put to death, for some crimes the day's mercy did not reach.

But here is the detail the Sheikh most wants us to carry away: even the exceptions turned out to be exceptions. Of that whole list, more than half were forgiven in the end. Ikrimah, the son of the man the Prophet ﷺ had called the Pharaoh of this ummah, fled by ship toward Abyssinia, and when a storm fell on the vessel and the pagan captain cried out that only Allah could save them now, it struck Ikrimah that gods who cannot help a drowning man have no claim on a living one, and he vowed that if he were spared he would put his hand in the Prophet's ﷺ hand and find him forgiving. He was spared, he came back, he was forgiven, and he died years later a martyr on the field against the Romans. Abdullah ibn Abi Sarh, the renegade scribe whose lie about the revelation orientalists still repeat, was hidden by his foster brother Uthman ibn Affan and brought forward to beg pardon; the Prophet ﷺ let a long, heavy silence fall before he accepted, and that man went on to govern Egypt and die in righteousness. One of the two singing-girls was forgiven. Habbar, who had caused Zaynab's miscarriage, was forgiven when he came repentant. Even Wahshi was forgiven, though the Prophet ﷺ asked him afterward never to let him see his face again, the wound of Hamza being what it was. From a city of some two thousand souls, after twenty one years of war, the Sheikh reckons that only about four were ever actually put to death. Mercy was the rule. Punishment was the rare exception. And even most of the exceptions, in the end, were wiped away.

What this day asks of our faith

It is tempting to read the conquest of Makkah as the triumphant last act of a long war, a victory to admire from a safe distance and then set down. That is to miss the question it presses against our own lives. Strip the day to its core and it stops being history and starts being a mirror.

He bowed lowest at the exact moment he stood highest. At the summit of everything Allah had given him, with a city and an army in his hand, he came in with his forehead nearly on his camel's back, glorifying his Lord. We do the reverse almost by instinct: a small success lifts our chin, a little power straightens our spine. His ﷺ life teaches the opposite reflex, that the larger the victory Allah grants you, the lower your head should go in thanks, because the victory was never yours to puff out your chest over. Ask, the next time something goes gloriously right for you, whether your first motion is upward in pride or downward in gratitude.

He worshipped before he ruled. The first thing the conqueror did was not to claim, divide, or avenge; it was tawaf and tawhid, the cleansing of the house before a single word to the people. There were no spoils that day because the day was not about spoils. We are quick, in our own moments of victory, to rush straight to the business of winning, to the settling and the spending and the scorekeeping, and slow to turn first to Allah. He ﷺ turned to his Lord first, and let everything in the world fall into its right place behind that.

And then, standing in the open door with every door of revenge swung wide before him, he chose the sentence of Yusuf. This is the heart of it, and the hardest part to live. Anyone can be gentle with nothing to lose; the Prophet ﷺ had been merciful at Ta'if when he was weak and alone and bleeding, and now he was merciful again when he held an army and a city and the unquestioned right to punish, and the Sheikh sets those two days side by side on purpose, because mercy at the height of strength is the rarer and truer thing. It is easy to forgive the person who can still hurt you; it is something else entirely to forgive the person who is now wholly in your hand. Most of us never reach Makkah in our small kingdoms, our homes and arguments and old grudges, the moment when we finally hold the power and the other is finally helpless before us. When we do, the question is the one he answered: what will you do with them now that you can do anything? He returned the trust to the family that held it even when he could have kept it. He left the door open even for the names marked for death, and watched the son of his fiercest enemy walk back through it and die a martyr. So take this day down into your ordinary life. Bow lowest when you stand highest. Put Allah first in the moment things go right. Return what is not yours to keep. And when someone who has wronged you is at last at your mercy, reach past the sword for the gentler word, and say what he said: no blame upon you today; go, you are free. May Allah, who softened His Prophet's ﷺ heart on the day he could have crushed them all, soften ours toward those who have wronged us, gather us under his banner ﷺ on the day every soul is gathered, and let him say of us on that day, no blame upon you today.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 79: the conquest of Makkah, part 4 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (17:81, 3:67, 49:13, 4:58), verified via quran.ai. The narration is the Sheikh's; the phrasing is Buruja's.

The Seerah · 80 of 104

The conquest of Makkah, part 5

The day the city he was driven from came to his hand

Makkah has opened. The valley that drove this man into the desert is now spread out beneath him, and there is no army left to fight, no wall left to breach, no question left in the whole city except the oldest one a wronged person ever asks: now that you have them, what will you do? He is standing at the doors of the Ka'bah, the house at the center of the world he was born into and exiled from, and the people who hunted him are pressed around him, watching his face to learn the shape of the reckoning. By every law their century knew, this was the hour of revenge. He gives them one sentence instead, and then the rest of the day fills up with something so much gentler and stranger than vengeance that we are still turning it over fourteen centuries later: a voice on the roof of the Ka'bah, a line of hands on a hill, and a slow trickle of old enemies finding their way home one heart at a time.

This is the fifth of our days inside the conquest, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi sets it down carefully, the way the whole seerah has to be set down: the events of these weeks reach us as scattered reports, each one needing to be weighed and fitted to the others, much of it spread across nineteen or twenty days the narrations do not always date. So we tell it the honest way, scene by scene, never importing into the city a single thing the city did not give us. What it gives us, on this day, is mercy at the height of power, which the Sheikh names from the start as the whole lesson the conquest exists to teach.

Go forth, you are free

Stand in the scene for a moment, because the symbolism is too obvious to walk past. The doors of the Ka'bah are thrown open behind him, and in front of him are the families of those who tortured his companions, the sons of those who plotted his death in the assembly hall, the woman who had cut open the body of his uncle on the field of Uhud. Quite literally the doors of Allah are standing open, and the Messenger of Allah ﷺ is at the threshold of them, and he is not blocking the way in, he is calling a beaten city through it.

He tells them: go forth, you are free. And with the words goes the open invitation that the open doors had already spoken: anyone who wishes may enter Islam, the house is open and so is its Lord's mercy. In the last days the Sheikh had walked through the short list of those the Prophet ﷺ had not granted blanket pardon, and even of that handful, six or seven or eight, the great bulk were in the end forgiven and only a very few were carried out. That is the rule of the whole religion laid bare in one afternoon: mercy and tolerance and compassion are the rule, and strictness is the rare exception, shown only so that a line exists and people know not to cross it. On this day the exception was almost invisible and the rule was everywhere. He held an entire city in his hand at the one moment he could finally have closed it, and he opened it instead.

The voice on the roof

Then he gave one of the most famous instructions of his life. He told Bilal radiyallahu anhu to climb to the top of the Ka'bah, to the highest point in Makkah, and to call the adhan from there. This is the one detail of the conquest that has traveled everywhere, the thing a person who knows almost nothing of this religion will still somehow know: that Allah honored Bilal to be the first human being ever to call the adhan over the holiest of houses, lifted onto its very summit to proclaim the testimony of faith.

Sit with what that voice had carried before it ever reached the roof. It was the voice that used to cry out the oneness of Allah in the valleys of Makkah while his owner crushed him under the noon sun on the burning sand for refusing to stop, the voice that would not break no matter what was laid on the chest beneath it. The Sheikh draws out the lesson he says we have all heard since we were young and which is true no matter how many times we hear it: how amazing that this exact voice, the one persecuted in these valleys for testifying to Allah, is the voice Allah and His Messenger now chose to raise, literally and figuratively, above the Quraysh who had once stood over it. An Abyssinian with no lineage, no clan, no standing among the Arabs, sent up onto the high place to announce on behalf of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. And the adhan is no small thing to be given: it summarizes the whole religion, the oneness of Allah and the truth of His Prophet, condensed into the call itself.

Here the Sheikh hands you a principle worth memorizing even if you never learn another word of Arabic, a maxim he calls al-jazaa min jins al-amal: you are dealt with as you deserve to be dealt with, good for good and bad for bad, your reward bearing the very character of the deed that earned it. The one who held his voice firm for the sake of Allah at the hour of humiliation is the one whose voice is raised at the hour of honor. Hold firm in the hard years, the principle says, and trust Allah with the repayment, because the repayment will be cut from the same cloth as the patience.

Below the roof, the elite of Quraysh stood listening, and three of them said three things they thought no one would ever repeat. Attab ibn Asid, the full brother of Abu Jahl, said he thanked God his father had died before this day, so that the old man would not have to watch this black man chosen above them, a sentence with the whole disease of jahiliyyah breathing inside it, a man glad his father was dead rather than see Bilal on the roof. Al-Harith ibn Hisham said that if he truly believed this man was upon the truth, he would follow him, which was his way of saying he did not believe it. And Abu Sufyan, a Muslim now for only a few hours, held his tongue and said: I will say nothing, for if I spoke, these very stones would tell him what I said. Later, as the Prophet ﷺ left the Haram and passed the three of them, he repeated their words back to them, letter for letter, exactly what each man had said while no one was near. Al-Harith took the testimony of faith on the spot. You must be a prophet, he said, for no one heard us and no one carried it to you.

That third man is worth pausing on, because the Sheikh does. Abu Sufyan's sentence is one of the strangest in the seerah: he believed that if he spoke, the Prophet ﷺ would somehow know, which means he already believed the man was a prophet, and yet his soul had not yet bent. His head had arrived and his heart was lagging behind. This is faith arriving in levels, the Sheikh says, and we will see it again before the day is done, and we see it still: people enter this religion and their Islam is at first mediocre, and we accept it and leave their affair to Allah, and some of them go on to outstrip the ones born inside the faith. Islam, for a soul not raised in it, can take time to settle.

The hill where it ended, where it began

From the Ka'bah he made his way to the hill of Safa and sat down somewhere upon it, and there he received the bay'ah, the oath of allegiance, from everyone willing to embrace Islam. They came in hordes, lining up and crowding around him, hand after hand after hand after hand. The Sheikh says this is the primary picture of a promise Allah had made, that very ayah coming to life in front of you:

And you see the people entering into the religion of Allah in multitudes,Qur'an 110:2

And the place could not have been chosen more perfectly. Safa is where the public call began, nearly twenty years before, the day he ﷺ climbed it and called his clans by name to warn them, and they turned on him. How apt, the Sheikh says, that where the dawah begins, there it should end. The whole circle closes on the same stone. He once stood up on that hill before a hostile city, and now he sits down on it to receive that city home, the matter that opened two decades ago coming around to its close, and the bulk of the people of Makkah accepting Islam in this single stretch of the day.

The door that closed behind them

One exchange on that hill became a ruling for the entire ummah. A companion, an earlier emigrant, brought forward his own blood brother, newly arrived, and said: O Messenger of Allah, this is my brother, grant him the reward of the hijra as you granted it to me. He wanted for his brother exactly what he had been given himself, which is the very heart of this religion, to want for your brother what you want for yourself. But the answer he received is among the most famous things said during the conquest, recorded in Bukhari and Muslim: there is no hijra after the conquest of Makkah.

The Sheikh is careful about what this does and does not mean, because most of us have never even heard of the ruling it abrogated. For some seven or eight years, the migration to Madinah had been an obligation on every single Muslim alive: you made hijra or your Islam was deficient. The day Makkah opened, that obligation was lifted. The one and only Hijra, the great communal migration that had defined a whole generation, was complete, and it could not be joined late, so its unique reward could no longer be earned by anyone arriving now. This does not abolish all migration: there will still be personal hijras, a believer or a group fleeing persecution for the safety of their faith, in every age. But the general Hijra of the ummah was finished. What remained in its place, the Prophet ﷺ said, was jihad and good intention, any righteous deed done sincerely. If the brother truly longed for that rank, the road ahead was wide open: the campaigns against Rome and Persia were about to begin, and a sincere heart could still carry him there. The real conquests had not even started yet.

The women's pledge, and Hind behind the veil

When the men had finished, the women of Quraysh were called, and they gathered before him, and he took their pledge upon the conditions Allah lays out in the Qur'an:

O Prophet, when the believing women come to you pledging to you that they will not associate anything with Allah, nor will they steal, nor will they commit unlawful sexual intercourse, nor will they kill their children, nor will they bring forth a slander they have invented between their arms and legs, nor will they disobey you in what is right, then accept their pledge and ask forgiveness for them of Allah. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.Qur'an 60:12

Among them, hidden behind a full face veil so that no one recognized her, stood Hind bint Utbah, the wife of Abu Sufyan, the woman who only hours before had been clutching her husband's beard and crying for the city to fight, and who carried a far older history than that: it was Hind who had done to the body of Hamza radiyallahu anhu at Uhud what every listener knows and dreads to repeat. She knew her history, and she was afraid of it, and the veil was her cover. The Sheikh pauses only to note what the scene incidentally proves: the face veil was known and worn by women in the time of the Prophet ﷺ. Whether it is obligatory is a separate scholarly question; the claim that the niqab is some later medieval invention smuggled into Islam is simply false, and the seerah shows it plainly.

But Hind could not hold her tongue, and her tongue gave her away. When he said do not associate anything with Allah, she spoke up from the crowd that he was demanding of the women something he had not demanded of the men, an assumption that was simply wrong, born of imagining this was a political conquest rather than a religious one, that he was now their ruler but surely would not touch their gods. The men had been told to give up their idols too. Seeing no support, she relented: very well, if you insist, we give it. When he said do not steal, she shot back that she used to take from Abu Sufyan's wealth, bit by bit, without his knowing, and was that theft or not? Abu Sufyan, hearing his wife give herself away and more anxious than she was, hurried to announce that whatever was past, he had forgiven, the past, he was careful to say, not the future. And the Prophet ﷺ said: are you Hind? She admitted it, and begged his pardon, and asked Allah to forgive her.

He did not punish her, and he did not say you are forgiven. He simply moved on. The Sheikh stops you here, because this is the kind of scene a person who has not studied the seerah can find hard, until he sees what is actually in it. It is easier to forgive a crime done to yourself than one done to someone you love, and harder still when the one wronged is dead and beyond your power to console. He neither struck back nor pretended the wound was nothing. He let it go and let her go, and his very silence, his refusal to receive her into his presence, was itself the lightest possible weight laid on a woman who had earned far worse. Mercy and an honest wound sat together in the same heart, and that, the Sheikh says, is exactly the perfection of it.

She was not finished. When he said do not commit unlawful relations, she snapped, does a free woman do such a thing? a line that says something true even about pagan Arabia, that decent families guarded their honor and held this crime to be beneath them. And when he said do not kill your children, came the answer that undid the gathering: as for that, we raised them as little ones and you killed them as grown men at Badr. It was so sharp and so unexpected, this barb out of nowhere, that one of the great companions seated behind the Prophet ﷺ burst out laughing and toppled over onto his back. Even they, the Sheikh notes, could take a joke; it shows you their humanity. And when the pledge was done, he sent the women to give the oath through Umar radiyallahu anhu, for it is narrated in every book of hadith that the Prophet ﷺ never once took a woman's hand in pledge. His word to them was the pledge itself: my speech to one of you, he would later tell a woman who reached out her hand, is as my speech to a hundred. The best example is the example, and where one of us cannot rise to it, we keep it as the ideal rather than bend it to ourselves.

The fear of the Ansar

Quietly, off to the side, the Ansar of Madinah began to ache. They watched him returned to the city of his birth, watched his kin and his cousins and the whole of Quraysh pouring into Islam, and a murmur went around among them: now that he has come home, softness for his own people has taken hold of him, he will settle back among them and forget us. The Sheikh asks you to see clearly what was at the root of it. It was not really jealousy. It was love, the particular ache of people who fear that the one they love is drifting back to where he came from and will leave them behind.

Jibril came down and told him what they had said. And yet the first thing the Prophet ﷺ did was go to them and ask, did you say this? though he already knew, word for word, from heaven. The Sheikh dwells on the courtesy of it. When a stranger or an acquaintance comes to us reporting that someone spoke ill of us, we are quick to make ourselves judge and jury and executioner in a single breath, no benefit of the doubt offered, the matter closed before the accused has said a word. Here was a report that could not possibly be doubted, delivered by the angel of revelation, and still he gave the Ansar the chance to speak, because Allah had sent the report to him precisely to quell a fitna before it grew, and the manners of the Prophet ﷺ were to ask even when he already knew. They confessed: yes, O Messenger of Allah, we said it.

Then he reminded them who he was. Had he not, years before at Aqabah, when they asked him whether he would leave them once the victory was his, promised them that his blood was their blood and his destruction their destruction, that his life was their life and his death their death? Who did they take him for, that he would break that promise now? He had emigrated to them and with them and for them; he belonged to them in living and in dying, gathered into them and no longer merely a man of his own clan. The Ansar wept, and asked his forgiveness, and pleaded that they had spoken only out of what they feared and saw, and were wrong. And the Prophet ﷺ told them that Allah and His Messenger believed their excuse.

The ones who came in slowly

The Prophet ﷺ stayed in Makkah about three weeks, and across those nineteen or twenty days the holdouts came in, one reluctant heart at a time. The narrations do not date each conversion, so we hand them on as bits and pieces, and they all teach the same thing, the thing Allah Himself said to the bedouins:

The bedouins say, "We have believed." Say, "You have not [yet] believed; but say [instead], 'We have submitted,' for faith has not yet entered your hearts. And if you obey Allah and His Messenger, He will not deprive you from your deeds of anything. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful."Qur'an 49:14

Islam arrives in levels, the Sheikh reminds you, and Allah tells the bedouins as much, that they should not yet claim belief when they have only submitted, for faith had not entered their hearts. He saw it then and we see it now, and our part with hearts still on the road, including our own, is to be the more patient and the more generous.

There was Fadala, a young man of Quraysh about whom little else is known, so enraged by the defeat and all he had watched that he hid a dagger and resolved to assassinate the Prophet ﷺ, a suicide mission with no thought of surviving it.
