He crept up behind him during his tawaf, and the Prophet ﷺ, who made a great deal of tawaf across those days, turned and said: is this Fadala? What were you intending? Nothing, Fadala lied, I was only remembering Allah. The Prophet ﷺ laughed at the open lie, and laid his hand on Fadala's chest, and Fadala said afterward that the instant the hand touched him, no one on the face of the earth was more beloved to him than the man before him. The whole plot dissolved into faith between one breath and the next.

There was Safwan ibn Umayyah, son of Umayyah ibn Khalaf and one of the leaders of Quraysh, who tried to fight, and when fighting failed, fled toward the sea at Jeddah meaning to board a ship and drown himself rather than live under this. His cousin Umayr went to the Prophet ﷺ and begged a guarantee of safety, and was given the very turban the Prophet ﷺ had worn entering Makkah as a token Safwan would recognize, and caught him at the shore. Safwan came back, too afraid even to dismount, calling out across the Haram to confirm the safety with his own ears, and was granted four months to decide, asked nothing of his heart. Later, after Hunayn and its vast spoils, the Prophet ﷺ saw him gazing at an entire valley of livestock and said, take it, it is yours, and Safwan said that such a gift could come only from the heart of a prophet, a man who could not be made to care for the world, and the most hated of men to him became the most beloved. People who love this world, the Sheikh observes, are sometimes won only when they are shown a love stronger than it, and then they recognize the sincerity, and faith enters.

There was Suhayl ibn Amr, the proud negotiator of Hudaybiyyah, a senior leader of the old generation, who did not flee but locked himself in his house in terror and sent his eldest son to plead for protection, saying he knew of no man still living who had wronged the Prophet ﷺ more than he had. He was granted safety, and the Prophet ﷺ told the companions: when Suhayl comes, do not stare at him in anger, not even in your faces, for he is a man of intelligence and honor, too intelligent to stay ignorant of Islam, and when he sees its truth he will come to it. And so he did, in the months after Hunayn and Ta'if. And there was Abu Quhafah, the aged blind father of Abu Bakr radiyallahu anhu, perhaps the oldest man alive in Makkah, led in by the hand by his granddaughter Asma to give the pledge. The Prophet ﷺ said: why did you not leave the old man in his house? We would have come to him. The dignity of the elder honored, the Sheikh notes, not for his own rank but for his son's, for the one who shows no respect to our elders is not of us, and you treat a person by what he and his family have done, and there is nothing un-Islamic in that. And when the blind old man's hand was placed in the hand of the Prophet ﷺ, Abu Bakr wept, and said he wished the hand he was watching were the hand of the Prophet's own uncle Abu Talib rather than his own father's, that he would gladly have traded his father's Islam for the joy it would have given the Prophet ﷺ to see Abu Talib believe. One by one, across three patient weeks, the city he had been driven from came home.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read the conquest as the triumphant end of a long war and leave it there, the good men winning at last. That is to miss the question it actually puts to us. The conquest is the hour almost no one survives with their soul intact: total, unanswerable power over the very people who hurt you. He ﷺ walked into that hour and walked out the other side gentler than he went in, and the test he passed in it is the test set quietly for every one of us, on a far smaller scale, more often than we would like.

Power is for mercy. He held an entire city at his mercy and said, go forth, you are free, and let the rule of his whole religion be visible for an afternoon: compassion as the standard, strictness only the rare exception that keeps a line in place. The measure of a person's faith was never what he does when he is wronged and helpless. It is what he does the moment he could finally get even, and chooses not to. Most of us are tested not with a conquered city but with a small advantage suddenly fallen into our hands over someone who wronged us, and the question is the same.

You are paid in your own coin. The voice that cried tawheed under torture in these valleys was the voice raised above the Ka'bah at the hour of honor, good for good, the reward cut from the same cloth as the deed. Hold firm in the hard years and you are trusting Allah for a repayment that will carry the very character of your patience. And forgiveness, the day teaches, does not mean pretending. He forgave Hind without ever saying you are forgiven, and let her pass, neither striking back nor declaring the wound healed. You can release a hurt to Allah without forcing your mouth to say it never happened; mercy and an honest grief can live in one heart, as they lived in his.

Faith comes in levels, and so we are patient. Some entered that day and soared; some, like Abu Sufyan, stood with belief in their heads and not yet in their hearts; some, like Safwan and Suhayl and Fadala and the old blind father led in by the hand, came in slowly across three weeks, dragged the last distance by a touch on the chest or a valley of gifts or sheer unhurried courtesy. Allah told the bedouins not to claim more than they had yet reached, and the lesson for us is to be the more generous and the more patient with every heart still on the road, beginning with our own. And underneath it all, the promise he gave the Ansar when they feared he would forget them: my life is your life, my death your death. He is yours. Belong to him ﷺ, and trust that the one who answered a conquered city with an open door, and a frightened tribe with that promise, does not forget his own. May Allah let the heart that has been wronged answer with the mercy he answered with, open for us the doors He opened for Makkah, and gather us among those who come to him, however slowly, and are received.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 80: the conquest of Makkah, part 5 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (110:2, 60:12, 49:14), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 81 of 104

The Conquest of Makkah, Part 6

From the lowest of the low to the highest of the high

The gates are open. The idols are down. The amnesty has been declared, and the man Makkah once tortured and exiled is now the undisputed master of the city that drove him out. So what does a conqueror do with a city that is finally his? You would expect a settling of scores, a redistribution of houses, a parade. For nineteen quiet days the Prophet ﷺ shows you the opposite, and almost none of it looks like conquest. He pitches a tent in the valley of his worst memory. He welcomes home the last few who come in. He gives the haram back its holiness, teaches the new Muslims their religion, pays blood money for a mistake that was not his, and then, gently, is told that his own work on earth is nearly finished. This is the close of the long Makkah arc, and it lands not on triumph but on mercy, law, and the quiet shape of a life bending toward its end.

The last ones home

Two more came in at the very end, and they tell you what the whole conquest had been for. The first was Umm Hani, the Prophet's ﷺ own cousin, a daughter of Abu Talib and full sister of Ali ibn Abi Talib. She had known him since childhood; she had watched the religion rise from inside his own family, and she had held back from it all those years, embracing Islam only now, at the conquest. Her story has nothing to do with belief and everything to do with the texture of these days. Two of her in-laws, men with old blood between them and Ali, had fled in terror during the conquest, certain that Ali would take revenge on them. When they had nowhere left to run, they sent word to her, and she took them into her house and locked the door, on her own brother. Ali was enraged. How dare she come between him and the men he was hunting? He threatened to harm them.

So the next morning Umm Hani went straight to the Prophet ﷺ. Because she was family, she was let through to his tent, and she found him in the middle of a ghusl, washing behind a cloth that his daughter Fatima held up to shield him; there were no private rooms in those days, no separate facilities, only a corner of a tent and a curtain held by someone you trusted. He ﷺ asked who had come. Umm Hani, she said, and he lowered the cloth, looked at the cousin he had not seen come into the fold all these years, and said: welcome, Umm Hani. She poured out the whole story, that she was a Muslim now, that she had given protection to her in-laws, that Ali was threatening them, that she had come straight to him to appeal. And he ﷺ answered with a sentence that still astonishes: we grant protection to whomever you have granted it.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi stops you on how open that law is. From this single exchange the scholars of fiqh derived that any Muslim at all, man or woman, free or slave, can extend a personal pledge of safety, an aman, to any individual, and the entire community is then bound to honor it. A single woman's word held the whole victorious army back. This is the law of Islam, and it is, the Sheikh says, a strikingly lax and open system, unprecedented in its age. Any person could say, this one is under my protection, and all the Muslims would have to obey. It grew out of a faith that came to gather people in, not to settle scores. And there is a small treasure folded into the same visit: when he ﷺ finished his ghusl, Umm Hani watched him pray eight units of the forenoon prayer, salat al-duha. It is the only report we have from the Prophet's ﷺ own life of how many units he prayed for that prayer, because he kept it in the privacy of his home and his wives, understanding it as habit, never thought to count it for us. Umm Hani counted. Eight.

The second latecomer was the man who had spent his whole life trying to keep people out. Abdullah ibn al-Zibara was the official poet of the Quraysh, a man of the Banu Sahm, and his weapon was verse. In an age when poetry was the press, the propaganda, the public verdict all at once, he was the voice that hurled poem after poem at the Muslims, before Badr and after it, before Uhud and after it, trading blows with Hassan ibn Thabit while the Prophet ﷺ told Hassan to answer him and that Jibril was with him. When Makkah fell, Ibn al-Zibara fled to Najran with his cousin and his closest friend, too uncomfortable in his own skin to stay near the Prophet ﷺ even though his name was not on any list. And Hassan, hearing the poet had run, gloated. He wrote a page-long poem, preserved in Ibn Ishaq, the most scathing personal attack he ever composed: what a coward, he flees to Najran, the whole world curses him, he and his children are cursed until the Day of Judgment.

But the poem landed somewhere it was never aimed. Ibn al-Zibara read it and grew not defiant but depressed, because he agreed with it. He had run. He had been a coward. And lying there in a strange city among a strange tribe, he began to turn the message itself over in his mind, not the insult, the content. Within a few days he had packed his belongings to return to Makkah a Muslim. His cousin was bewildered, and a little betrayed: we came all this way together, and now you will abandon me here alone among strangers? And Ibn al-Zibara gave the answer that tells you everything had turned: why should I stay among a strange tribe in a strange land when I can go back to my own kith and kin, my own cousin, who is the best of all mankind?

I see the light of faith upon him

Picture the scene the Sheikh draws for you. The Prophet ﷺ is sitting before the Ka'bah with the companions around him when a figure appears far off on the road, walking toward them. Before anyone can tense or reach for a weapon, he ﷺ says: that is Ibn al-Zibara, and I see upon him the light of faith. So no one says a word. And before the Prophet ﷺ himself can speak, the poet reaches him and says it first, out of his own mouth: peace be upon you, O Messenger of Allah. I bear witness that there is no god but Allah, and that you are the Messenger of Allah.

Then he confessed the whole campaign of his life, and even in translation the Sheikh asks you to feel the weight of his words, because the language is pregnant with meaning. I rode horses, he said, and I traveled on camels, and I walked on foot to oppose you. Everything I had, my tongue, my efforts, all of it spent against you. I even fled to Najran to escape you. And still Allah wanted good for me. He spoke of the shame of it, of how Allah had made him see at last how ignorant he had been, venerating and sacrificing to a stone that cannot think, an idol that does not even know it is being worshipped. The Prophet ﷺ welcomed him and told him what he told them all: Islam wipes away everything that came before it. Every sin you committed is erased and made clean. And for the rest of his life the poet who had cursed Islam composed poem after poem in its praise, as an expiation for what he had done, until one day, after a poem recited in front of him, the Prophet ﷺ took off his own cloak and laid it across the man's shoulders. To a poet that was the highest honor imaginable, and the Prophet's ﷺ personal cloak was a gift he gave to almost no one. Much of Ibn al-Zibara's later poetry, unlike Hassan's, has been lost, and the Sheikh confesses he does not know why; only fragments survive. But the scholars remembered him as a great poet who, after Islam, cancelled with his verse the very evil he had once spread with it.

Two latecomers, then: a woman who sheltered her own family's enemies, and a poet who had cursed the Prophet ﷺ in public for the better part of two decades. Both folded into the mercy at the last hour, without a single word of reproach. That is the texture of the days after the conquest.

A tent in the valley of the boycott

He ﷺ stayed in Makkah for nineteen days. The reports differ, some say fifteen, some twenty, but Bukhari says nineteen, and that is the count the Sheikh follows. And in all that time the conqueror of the city had nowhere of his own to stay. The question came up at once: whose house would he occupy? Ali wanted to reclaim the family home, the house of Abu Talib, the very house the Prophet ﷺ had grown up in. Why, he asked, would they not simply go back to their own house? And the answer he received was a fair man's answer: did Aqil leave us any house to return to?

Understand the reference. Abu Talib had died upon the religion of his fathers, a disbeliever, with four sons. The two eldest, Talib and Aqil, had not embraced Islam in those years; only the younger two, Ali and Jafar, had. So when Abu Talib died, his estate passed, by the plain law, to Talib and Aqil, and Ali and Jafar inherited nothing of the house, because a Muslim does not inherit from a disbeliever. Then Talib died, and Aqil sold the whole property to someone else, a legitimate sale. So when Ali asked to take the house back, there was nothing to take; it belonged now to a buyer who had paid for it lawfully, and the Prophet ﷺ would not lay a hand on property that had been sold in good faith. He was even, the Sheikh notes, a little wounded by it, a little critical of Aqil, who had sold away the family home; if Aqil had left it for them, they would have had somewhere to go. But Aqil sold it all. From this single episode the scholars drew a clean ruling of inheritance, that a disbeliever is inherited by disbelievers, that Muslims do not take from them, and that lawfully sold property cannot afterward be seized back. The conqueror of the holiest city on earth had, in that city, no house at all. (Aqil himself, the Sheikh adds, did accept Islam at the conquest, and then lived a long and quiet life, never much involved in great affairs, and passed away without fanfare.)

So he did something that is one of the quiet wonders of the seerah. He pitched a tent. The place he chose was a small valley five minutes' walk from the Ka'bah, a place called al-Hujun, and every person who lives in Makkah to this day knows exactly where al-Hujun is; ask any Makkan and he will point to it. But al-Hujun was not just any spot of ground. Al-Hujun was where the Quraysh had once gathered in their secret meeting to enact the boycott, to draw up and sign the document that sealed the Prophet ﷺ and his clan behind years of hunger and isolation. It was the lowest of the low, the worst thing the Quraysh ever did to him, plotted on that exact ground. And now he returns to that exact ground, not as the besieged but as the undisputed master of the city, and lives there. Dr. Yasir Qadhi will not let you walk past the symbolism. Look, he says, at how Allah tests people and then honors them. Allah tested him through what happened at al-Hujun, and in that very same place Allah raised him to the highest of the high. From the lowest of the low to the highest of the high, in the precise spot where it had hurt the most. That is the perfect justice of Allah, written into the geography of one small valley.

He gave the haram back its sanctity

In one of the earliest sermons of those days, perhaps the very morning after the conquest, he ﷺ gathered the people and reminded them what their city is. O people, Allah has made Makkah sacred. He made it sacred the day He created the heavens and the earth, and it shall remain sacred until the Day of Judgment. No blood may be shed in it, human or animal; you cannot fight in it, you cannot hunt in it, you cannot even pluck its plants or its leaves. Everything Allah has placed there is to be left as it is. It was never lawful to fight in before me, he told them, nor will it be lawful after me, and for me it was made lawful for only a single hour of a single day, the hour in which I entered it; and now that hour is over, and Makkah has returned to its sanctity as it was before. He even gave them the answer to the objection: if anyone ever says to you that the Prophet himself fought in Makkah, tell him that Allah made it lawful for His Messenger and has not made it lawful for you. So deeply did the companions take this in that some of them would not so much as brush an insect off their clothing in the haram, unwilling to disturb any living creation of Allah within its sacred bounds. And Allah Himself, the Sheikh reminds you, warns of a painful punishment for the one who merely intends wrongdoing there.

Indeed, those who have disbelieved and avert [people] from the way of Allah and [from] al-Masjid al-Haram, which We made for the people - equal are the resident therein and one from outside - and [also] whoever intends [a deed] therein of deviation [in religion] by wrongdoing - We will make him taste of a painful punishment.Qur'an 22:25

Then came the cleanup of a pagan economy. Every home in Makkah held idols, and every home held wine. And the city had run a brisk and lucrative trade: they would take any wood or any stone, paint it, carve it, and sell it to the Bedouins and pilgrims coming from outside as an idol from Makkah, which fetched a higher price because a Makkan idol carried prestige, a shirk souvenir from the holy city. The Prophet ﷺ forbade the worship and the drinking, of course, and when the people, stuck now with their stockpiles, began to offer the idols and the wine cheaply to outsiders to be rid of them, he forbade that too. Allah and His Messenger, he said, have forbidden the sale of wine, of carrion, and of idols. When Allah forbids a thing, He forbids its price. The principle is simple and clean: what is not good for you is not good to hand to someone else for money. You do not get to say, it is forbidden for me but I will profit by selling it to you. So the idols were smashed and the wine was poured out into the streets, and from the conquest of Makkah a permanent rule of fiqh was born, one that every student still learns: what is forbidden to use is forbidden to sell.

And he taught them, in the most memorable way, that the law does not bend for rank. A noblewoman of the powerful Banu Makhzum, the tribe of Abu Jahl, was caught stealing, and her people, knowing the Prophet's ﷺ tender heart for him, sent Usama ibn Zayd, the beloved son of his beloved freedman, to intercede and ask that she be let off because she was a woman of high station. He ﷺ was furious. Do you intercede, he said, concerning one of the punishments set by Allah? Then he stood before all the people and gave the line we know by heart: by Allah, if Fatima the daughter of Muhammad were to steal, I would cut off her hand. We do not operate so that the rich and the famous go free while the poor and the lowly are punished. The high and the low stand on one level before the truth. And there is a gentle coda that Aisha preserved: that very woman lived a good life afterward and would later come to Aisha with petitions and requests of her own, which Aisha would carry to the Prophet ﷺ, and they would be granted, like anyone else's. Because once a punishment has been carried out, the Sheikh says, a person is not branded a criminal for the rest of their days; the matter is closed, and they move on.

Allah knows where to place His message

Here is the thing about the conquest that the Sheikh most wants you to grasp: it changed everything, and not because of a battle. Arabia in those days had no central government; every tribe was its own little province, its own small city-state. But the conquest of Makkah was understood as the conquest of the central nervous system of the whole peninsula, because on one thing every Arab agreed, no matter how far they lived from the fight, that Makkah was the holiest city on earth, the city of Ibrahim and Ismail, the city to which all of them traced their heritage, the city Allah had once shielded from the army of the elephant. The tribes far from the conflict had been watching from the sidelines for fifteen years to see how it would end. Their reasoning, as al-Qurtubi records it, was direct: Allah once protected this city from the people of the elephant. If Allah now allows this man to take it, that settles the question. It shows he is exactly who he says he is, a prophet, and there is no stopping him.

And when a sign comes to them, they say, "Never will we believe until we are given like that which was given to the messengers of Allah." Allah is most knowing of where [i.e., with whom] He places His message. There will afflict those who committed crimes debasement before Allah and severe punishment for what they used to conspire.Qur'an 6:124

Dr. Yasir Qadhi reads the wisdom of the choice back to you, and it is worth slowing down for. You cannot argue with the grandson of Abd al-Muttalib. You cannot accuse a man born and raised in the holiest city on earth, from the cream of its noblest lineage, of grasping for status he was not owed; he already had every honor the Arabs could confer by birth. His own people had cast him out, and now he had come back and taken not some foreign land but his own city, the holiest city of all, on its own terms. Imagine, the Sheikh says, how the Arabs would have felt if some outside tribe had conquered Makkah. But this was a man of Makkah returning to Makkah. No rival could match the choice from any angle, not lineage, not birthplace, not the symbolism of the Ka'bah itself. Allah is most knowing of where He places His message. He chose this man, in this city, with this lineage, for reasons no one could ever contest.

Entering the religion of Allah in crowds

And so it happened exactly as Allah had said it would. From the conquest onward, tribe after tribe, many of them with names that had never once appeared in the whole story because they had never once fought, began sending delegations to declare their Islam. No armies marched out to compel them. They simply came, in such numbers that the next two years, from the eighth year through the tenth, are remembered as the Year of Delegations: every second or third day a new tribe arriving at his door. Look at what this means, the Sheikh says. The Prophet ﷺ never fought more than ninety percent of the Arabs of his time; he fought only the Quraysh and the few tribes allied to them. All the rest, central Arabia, the south, the far north, the eastern and western reaches, came in without a single battle. Across a peninsula of hundreds of thousands of people, paganism was wiped out completely, every trace of it gone, and all of it inside fifteen years. It is, he says plainly, a miracle, a verse you could watch happen with your own eyes.

When the victory of Allah has come and the conquest, And you see the people entering into the religion of Allah in multitudes, Then exalt [Him] with praise of your Lord and ask forgiveness of Him. Indeed, He is ever Accepting of Repentance.Qur'an 110:1-3

But the Sheikh saves the deepest reading of this surah for last, and it belongs to Ibn Abbas. Years later, in the caliphate of Umar, the teenage Ibn Abbas, the Prophet's ﷺ young cousin, was being seated among the gray elders of Badr, and some of the senior companions objected: we have sons as old as this boy, and you do not bring them. So Umar, to show them why, gathered them and asked them all what this surah meant. Most gave the obvious answer: when Allah blesses you and grants you victory, thank Him and seek His forgiveness. Then Umar turned to the boy and asked him. They have not spoken correctly, Ibn Abbas said. Rather, Allah is informing His Prophet ﷺ that when Makkah is conquered, his time on earth is about to come to an end, and so he is told to prepare to meet Allah by increasing his worship and his seeking of forgiveness. And Umar said: that is all I know of it too. The conquest was the pinnacle, the Sheikh says, and from the pinnacle there is only the descent toward home. Surat an-Nasr is the beginning of the end; it tells him, for the very first time, that death is near. And barely two years after the conquest, he ﷺ passed away.

Sit with that the way the Sheikh asks you to. The best of all creation, the most generous man who ever lived, the least sinful of all human beings, is told at the very summit of his mission to meet his end with more prayer and more istighfar. So where does that leave you and me? He ﷺ was given a hint that his end was near. We will be given no such hint. The honest response is the only one there is: to begin the worship and the seeking of forgiveness now, today, while there is still time to begin.

What even a perfect victory costs

There was still work to do in those nineteen days. He ﷺ sent out small parties from Makkah to invite the neighboring tribes to Islam and to bring down the great temple idols, the public idols that whole regions venerated, while every household was simply commanded to destroy its own. Khalid ibn al-Walid was sent to destroy al-Uzza, and when he arrived, her own custodian, seeing what was coming, flung his axe around her neck and told her to defend herself, then fled; she did not defend herself, and Khalid destroyed her. Within a few days the famous idols of the whole Hijaz were gone. But this greatness, the Sheikh is careful to say, was marred by a small tragedy, and it was not from the Prophet ﷺ; it was the mistake of a brand-new Muslim, a reminder that in this world there is no perfection, that even at the highest moment of the religion something will go wrong, and that a human being is always prone to error.

Khalid had a checklist of expeditions, and one item on it was the tribe of Banu Jadhima, a day's distance from Makkah. But Khalid had a personal history there: in the days of ignorance this tribe had killed his uncle, and there was old blood between them. So when he appeared on their horizon with an army behind him, the Banu Jadhima thought attack, and a group of them reached for their swords. When other members of the tribe, understanding what was really happening, tried to announce their submission, they said it clumsily, with the old word, sabana, sabana, we have become Sabians, the way the Arabs used to call the new religion. The senior companions present caught the meaning and held back. But Khalid, already provoked, and not yet taught the sacred rule that a person's surrender is taken at its word, that you do not open up a man's heart to check, the rule the religion had learned from the story of Ali and the man who spat at him, and from Usama killing a man who said the shahada at the last moment, Khalid did not understand. He struck, and he killed men who should never have been killed. Senior companions like Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf refused his order to continue, because in Islam a leader is obeyed only in what is right, and this was a clear case of disobeying a commander who had commanded a wrong. Khalid, who was their military superior though junior to them in faith, grew angry and cursed Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf.

When the news reached the Prophet ﷺ, he did something striking. He stood, faced the Ka'bah, raised his hands, and said: O Allah, I am innocent before You of what Khalid has done. I did not command him to this. He sent Ali at once with a large sum of wealth to pay the full blood money to every family, generously, person by person, and to apologize on his behalf. And when Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf came complaining of how Khalid had cursed him, the Prophet ﷺ gave the words we always quote: do not curse my companions, for by Allah, were one of you to spend the like of Mount Uhud in gold, he would not reach a handful, or even half a handful, of what one of them gave. Dr. Yasir Qadhi points to who was being told this. It was said to Khalid, himself a great companion, a man who would become one of the most respected figures in the religion. And if Khalid, for all his greatness, could not reach the rank of the earliest believers, then where, the Sheikh asks, does that leave the rest of us? The hadith we use to honor the companions was spoken to a companion. It should raise our estimate of them higher, not lower.

Allah has written [i.e., decreed], "I will surely overcome, I and My messengers." Indeed, Allah is Powerful and Exalted in Might.Qur'an 58:21

And this is the theology the whole long arc has been driving toward. Allah has decreed: I will surely overcome, I and My messengers. The man they tortured and exiled, against whom they plotted assassination, came back in eight years and took the capital of Arabia with almost no blood spilled, and from that conquest the whole peninsula entered Islam in crowds. But the seerah, the Sheikh reminds you, has mostly been the downs, not the ups. This victory came after the buried daughters and the strangled children of the early persecution, after the boycott and the years of hunger, after Uhud and the broken treaties, after the dead at Mu'tah whom the Prophet ﷺ himself wept for. Allah does not let the effort of the sincere go unrewarded, but He asks for the effort first; no one is handed the reward sitting at home, waiting for the blessing to fall on him. You stand, you strive, you endure, and then Allah does not waste it. The conquest is the proof of the promise. And yet even now the work was not finished. One major city still stood near Makkah, a city built on a high plateau behind thick walls and its own water, a city no army of that day yet knew how to take by siege. It was the city that had pelted him with stones and turned its children loose on him, the city he had once stood over with the power to destroy and chose instead to forgive: Ta'if. And before Ta'if, in a narrow valley, there waited a battle that would test the twelve thousand at the very moment they felt invincible. The valley was called Hunayn. That is where the next chapter begins.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read the conquest of Makkah as a story that ends in a parade, the great vindication, the hero home at last, and to close the book there. That would be to miss almost everything the days after the surrender are trying to teach. Strip them down and you find not triumph but four hard disciplines, and every one of them is aimed straight at us.

The first is that mercy keeps its door open to the very last. A woman who locked her door on her own brother to shelter his enemies, and a poet who had cursed the Prophet ﷺ in verse for twenty years and then run away, both came in at the end, and both were welcomed without one syllable of reproach. From a distance, before the poet could even speak, he ﷺ saw the light of faith upon him. No one, this tells you, arrives too late. No past is too dark. While the door is still open, it is open all the way, and the only question is whether you will walk through it. Ask yourself who you have decided is beyond welcome, and then look again at the man walking up the road to the Ka'bah.

The second is that power is for justice, not revenge. The moment he finally had the upper hand over the city that had wronged him was the exact moment his fairness was tested, and it held. He would not seize back a house that had been lawfully sold, even though it was his childhood home and the buyer had been an enemy. He would have cut off his own daughter's hand for theft rather than let rank buy an exemption. When you are weak, fairness is cheap; you cannot afford otherwise. The test comes when you have won, when you could bend the rule and no one would stop you. That is when this faith asks you to hold the line, because the high and the low stand on one level before the truth.

The third is the small, unglamorous law born in those streets: what is forbidden to use is forbidden to profit from. The wine was poured into the dust, not quietly sold to outsiders for a fair price. When Allah forbids a thing, He forbids its price, because what is not good for you is not yours to hand to another for money. It is a question about your income and your hands. How much of what you take is clean all the way down, and how much of it travels to you over something you would never touch yourself? Clean earning, this day says, is not a side matter of the faith; it is the faith.

And the fourth is the deepest, the one Ibn Abbas read out of a short surah while the elders read only the surface. At the summit, the very pinnacle of his life and mission, with the holiest city on earth in his hand and a whole peninsula turning toward him, the first thing Allah asked of His beloved ﷺ was not celebration but preparation: exalt your Lord, praise Him, and seek His forgiveness, for your work is nearly done. The best of creation was told to meet his death with more prayer. He was given a hint. We will not be. So the lesson he was told to take is the lesson left for us, and it is urgent precisely because we have no warning: that the higher you are raised the lower you should bow, that the right response to every blessing is more worship and more istighfar, and that the only honest time to begin preparing to meet Allah is now, before the conquest of your own life is behind you and the descent has quietly begun.

So carry these four out of Makkah and down into your ordinary days. Leave the door of mercy open to someone you had closed it on. Be most fair in the moment you have the most power to be unfair. Make your earning clean enough that you would put your own hands on every part of it. And meet your blessings the way he ﷺ was taught to meet his greatest one, on your knees, in praise and in seeking forgiveness, as a person whose time is shorter than he knows. May Allah keep our hearts firm upon His religion, gather us among those who entered it in crowds, forgive us as He taught His Prophet ﷺ to seek forgiveness at the height of his honor, and let us follow him through whatever valley comes next. And may Allah send His peace and His blessings upon Muhammad ﷺ, who forgave the city that exiled him, pitched his tent in the valley of his deepest wound, and was raised there from the lowest of the low to the highest of the high.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 81: the conquest of Makkah, part 6 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (22:25, 6:124, 110:1-3, 58:21), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 82 of 104

The Battle of Hunayn, Part 1

When their numbers pleased them, and the earth closed in

Makkah had fallen, and not with a war. The Kaaba stood cleansed, the idols toppled and shattered, the city that had hunted the Prophet ﷺ for thirteen years now lay open and forgiving beneath him. He ﷺ stayed nineteen days settling its affairs, and the believers who had come up from Madinah could have been forgiven for thinking the long ordeal was over. The hardest thing any of them had ever imagined, the recovery of the sacred city, was behind them. What more, they might have asked, could be left to do?

Then, while he ﷺ was still camped in Makkah, the news came in. A great army was gathering in the mountains, the largest the Arabs had ever raised, and it was marching for them. What followed in the valley of Hunayn is one of only three battles Allah names in the whole of the Qur'an. It was the day the Muslims were more numerous than they had ever been, twelve thousand under one banner, and the day they came nearer to ruin than they had at Badr or Uhud or the Trench. Dr. Yasir Qadhi calls it the battle that humbles the strong. Day eighty-two is the ambush, the rout, and the rally, and the lesson buried in all three: numbers save no one.

The last stand of the old gods

The only city near enough to threaten Makkah was Ta'if, the mountain stronghold of the tribe of Thaqif, and Thaqif had read the conquest more clearly than the Quraysh ever had. With the sacred city in Muslim hands, there was no longer anything in all of Arabia standing between the worship of one God and the whole of the peninsula. If paganism was ever going to make its stand, it would have to be now, before monotheism swept the rest of the land the way it had swept Makkah.

So Thaqif joined hands with the great bedouin confederation of Hawazin and began sending emissaries out to every pagan tribe within reach, and two motives ran braided together through their summons. The first was the fear of a dying religion: they would defend the old gods before the old gods were gone. The second was older and far more familiar to the Arab heart. For centuries Thaqif had been second only to the Quraysh in prestige, and now, with the Quraysh vanquished, Thaqif saw its chance to slip into the vacuum left behind, to seize Makkah and become the new custodians of the Kaaba. They wanted to save paganism, and they wanted to rule it.

Sheikh Yasir Qadhi pauses here to let the size of what happened next land, because it had never happened before. Tribe answered tribe, and the host that assembled was more than twenty thousand fighters: Hawazin and Thaqif at the core, perhaps ten thousand, the other ten thousand drawn from a crowd of smaller tribes whose names the Sheikh leaves aside lest the story drown in them. It was the largest gathering of pagan Arabs in the entire history of Arabia. Sit with that. Since the Arabs had been a people they had been scattered tribes, divided by bloodline and feud, and nothing on earth had ever united them but the pride of fathers and forefathers. Now, for the first time, something else had pulled them into a single army. This was not a tribal raid. It was a religious war, polytheism gathering its full strength for one final stand against the worship of the one God. The numbers, the Sheikh notes, had been climbing like this through the whole seerah, Badr to Uhud to the Trench, each larger than the last, until at Hunayn the count reached a height the Arabs had never seen and would never see again under the banner of their idols.

The man sent to count them

The Prophet ﷺ heard the rumors and did what he always did when a hard decision lay ahead: he sent for knowledge before he sent for swords. He dispatched a companion named Abdullah ibn Abi Hadrad, a recent convert from that very region, calculating that the gathering tribes would not yet know he had become a Muslim, and that in a crowd of twenty thousand no one would watch any single man. Abdullah walked in among them, fell into step as though he belonged, and counted. He came back shaken: never had he seen so many men, twenty thousand on the march, and he told of their armor and weaponry, for the people of Ta'if had always held an edge over other tribes. They lived high on a fertile plateau, naturally walled, well fed, and they had built real fortresses and fielded real armies. These were not desert raiders but known, hardened fighters.

When Abdullah laid this before the believers, the Prophet ﷺ called his senior companions to council, as was his unbroken habit, and asked them what they should do. But Abu Bakr and Umar and the rest sat astonished, almost silent. They had not been bracing for war. This was meant to be a season of joy; Makkah was theirs, the long struggle won, and now they were told the largest battle of their lives was about to begin. In the confusion Umar said aloud that perhaps Abdullah was simply mistaken, and Abdullah took it as an accusation of lying and flared back: if you think me wrong, you have thought someone better than me wrong before, meaning the Prophet ﷺ himself in the days before Umar's Islam. Umar complained to the Prophet ﷺ, who answered gently that indeed Umar had once been astray, and Allah had guided him to Islam, and left it there. He took no side. He simply let the heat go out of the room.

The Sheikh draws something tender out of this small quarrel. The companions were not angels. To differ is human, and to grow sharp with one another is something you find in every society that has existed; in His wisdom Allah preserved for us the most perfect human community there has been, and it is one with its small frictions intact. Neither man sinned, and it did not last, for when the matter turned to the protection of the religion these same men closed ranks into a single unbreakable force. It does not make a Muslim a bad Muslim to stumble into a moment of anger. And it shows us the Prophet ﷺ at his most quietly masterful, not adjudicating who was right, but cooling the quarrel until it dissolved.

Ask me for a lucky tree

He ﷺ would not march against twenty thousand with the ten thousand who had come from Madinah alone. So he turned to Makkah itself, the city he had conquered only days before, and roughly two thousand of its men joined the army, some Muslims of a single day, some not yet Muslim at all. He needed armor and weapons, and he asked for them openly. He went to Safwan ibn Umayya, still inside a two-month period of grace and not yet a Muslim, and asked to borrow his armor. Are you taking it by force, Safwan asked, or asking me for a loan? A loan, the Prophet ﷺ told him, guaranteed: if anything is damaged or lost, you will be repaid its worth. From this single exchange, the Sheikh notes, the scholars draw a settled principle of Islamic law, that what you borrow you are responsible for, and if it is harmed in your keeping you must make it good. He borrowed from Safwan and from others, every loan guaranteed and, when it was over, every loan repaid. So an army of roughly twelve thousand set out, more men than the believers had ever fielded, better armed than they had ever been, on the sixth of Shawwal in the eighth year, and four days later it reached the valley of Hunayn.

On the road out of Makkah the army passed a great green tree standing alone in the desert, a magnificent canopy in a land of stone and sand. The pagans called it dhat anwat, the tree you hang things on. Once a year they would gather around it in festival, sacrifice to it, and drape their weapons in its branches for good luck before riding to battle, trusting the tree to carry them home alive. Some of the men walking past it now were brand new Muslims, a week into the faith, and the memory tugged at them. Messenger of Allah, they called out, why not make for us a tree like theirs?

The Prophet ﷺ was stunned. Subhanallah, he said, and then swore by the One in whose hand his soul rested: you have said exactly what the people of Musa said to him. And he answered them by reciting what Allah had recorded of that older folly, when the Children of Israel, freshly delivered through the parted sea, came upon a nation bowing to idols and turned to their own prophet with the same request.

And We took the Children of Israel across the sea; then they came upon a people intent in devotion to [some] idols of theirs. They [the Children of Israel] said, "O Moses, make for us a god just as they have gods." He said, "Indeed, you are a people behaving ignorantly.Qur'an 7:138

They had not asked for an idol, the Sheikh is careful to say. They had asked for a charm, a tree to hang their weapons on. But to the Prophet ﷺ the two requests were one and the same, sworn upon with the most solemn oath he knew, not because the words matched but because the heart of them matched exactly. For what is a god, in the end, but the thing you turn to for help, the thing you reach for when you are afraid, the thing you trust to protect you and to bring you good? Hang your safety on a tree or a stone or a colored bead, and you have handed a piece of your trust to something that was never going to save you, and that is the very root from which idolatry grows. Only Allah protects. We laugh at the black cat and the wood we knock on, the Sheikh adds, and forget their cousins in our own homes: the hand of five fingers on the wall, the turquoise eye against envy, the amulet tied around the neck. To believe that an object on your wall guards you from the evil eye is, in essence, to keep a small idol, for you have given it your hope and your fear, so that when something happens to it you panic as though your protector has fallen.

And yet, the Sheikh points out, watch what the Prophet ﷺ did and did not do. He named the act, and named it without softening: he called the request a form of shirk, and swore to it. But he did not call the man a mushrik. He did not throw the new Muslim out of the faith, did not brand him an idolater, because the man simply did not know yet. He had left paganism a week ago and had not yet understood what Islam asked of him; ignorance was his excuse, and the Prophet ﷺ extended it to him. Here is a distinction, the Sheikh insists, that the ordinary Muslim almost never makes and badly needs to learn: a person can commit an act of shirk without being a mushrik, an act of kufr without being a kafir, because there are conditions to be met and excuses to be removed before any such verdict falls on a human being, and that weighing is the work of scholars, not of laymen passing sentence on each other. The deed may be named for exactly what it is. The person, new or unaware, is taught with patience, not cast away.

How can twelve thousand be beaten

The size of the army went to its head. One of the men looked out over the host, the twelve thousand, the horses and camels and weaponry such as the believers had never owned, and said aloud what others were quietly feeling: how could we possibly be defeated, when we are twelve thousand? It reached the Prophet ﷺ, and it troubled him deeply. He answered at once. Do not say such a thing, he warned, for there was once a prophet among the prophets, a man whose name we are not given, who looked upon his own army and felt that same swell of pride at the sheer mass of men behind him. And as he looked, Allah sent down a pestilence out of the clear sky, and the army was destroyed where it stood, before it had so much as met an enemy.

The Sheikh sets this against an image still fresh in the listener's mind. Only weeks before, the Prophet ﷺ had entered Makkah at the head of the greatest force he had ever commanded, his head bowed so low in humility that it nearly touched the back of his mount. That was the posture of true victory, a conqueror folding himself small before the One who gave the conquest. This boast in the valley was its exact opposite, the head lifted in pride rather than lowered in gratitude, and it was spreading from man to man, freely, the Sheikh adds, precisely because so many in the ranks were new, a week into Islam and some not yet truly in it at all, and had not yet learned where strength actually comes from.

The valley that closed its jaws

Hunayn was Hawazin's own ground, and they knew every fold of it. The chieftain leading them was Malik ibn Awf, a young man of about thirty, newly raised to command, with everything to prove in his first great battle. And here, the Sheikh says, an old human drama played itself out, the clash of youth and age every society has known: the young certain they know best, the old who usually do.

Among the host, led by the hand because he was blind with age, was one of the most senior and noble men of the tribe, a former chief named Durayd ibn al-Simma, brought not to fight but for the blessing and wisdom of his presence, a living memory of a hundred battles. When they set him down he listened, and what he heard alarmed him. I can hear babies crying, he said, and goats bleating, and camels lowing. Who has brought the women and children and all the herds to a battlefield? They told him it was the new chief, and Malik was summoned. Why, Durayd demanded, had he dragged the whole of his people, their families and property and cattle, into a war? Because, the young man answered, he wanted every man to fight with his wife and children and wealth at his back. And the blind old warrior rebuked him without mercy. This was a foolish move, and a dangerous one. Your women and children and goats will not win you this war. A man who is going to lose does not win because his family is behind him; wars are won by men with swords, and by nothing else.

Then Durayd asked by name after two or three of the bravest subtribes, the ones a wise commander would want at his side, and was told that not one had come. He understood at once. Those men had weighed this war, judged it a loss, and stayed home, and they had judged rightly. Go back, Malik, he said. Do not fight. And if you will not heed that, then at least send the precious ones home behind their fortress walls, for if you win you will go home to them, and if you lose, at least they will be safe. It was entirely sensible counsel, and the people of Hawazin began to waver toward it.

But the thirty-year-old chief needed to prove his manhood. So he mocked the old guard, told him he had lost the plot and should leave the war to the young. And when he saw the people still hesitating, Malik played his last card, and it reveals exactly the kind of leader he was. By Allah, he swore before them all, if you do not obey me, I will drive my sword through myself right here in front of you. With that blackmail he silenced them, and the families and the flocks stayed. The age-old folly of youthful zeal over elder wisdom, the Sheikh observes, and on this day Allah willed that it serve the Muslims, for all of it, the families, the herds, the wealth, was about to fall into their hands.

Then Malik laid his trap, and it was a clever one. Knowing the Muslims would have to pass through a narrow valley, he posted bands of archers in the nooks and crevices along both heights of the pass, hidden the length of the valley on ground only he and his men knew. At the far end he set a small token force from Hawazin that looked arrayed for battle. That was the bait: let the Muslims rush toward it, let it break and flee as though beaten, drawing the whole army forward certain of an easy victory, and then, when the bulk of them were deep in the pass with walls on both sides, give the signal. The archers above would loose volley after volley into men who could neither see the bowmen nor reach them, while the twenty thousand below charged from the front. A double assault, from above and from ahead, with nowhere to run.

It unfolded exactly as drawn. In the half light before dawn the Muslims surged into the valley, the bait broke and ran, the army poured forward sure the day was won. And then the signal went up, a great shout, and in an instant the arrows fell from every height at once while the enemy charged from the front, and the great army shattered. This is the precise moment Allah names in His Book, the only mention He makes of a battle alongside Badr in the Qur'an by name.

Allāh has already given you victory in many regions and [even] on the day of Ḥunayn, when your great number pleased you, but it did not avail you at all, and the earth was confining for you with [i.e., in spite of] its vastness; then you turned back, fleeing.Qur'an 9:25

They fled, hundreds upon hundreds, helter-skelter, no man caring for any other, a mob in full panic. The Sheikh names why it broke so completely. So many were the newest of Muslims, a week old in the faith, and some not Muslim at all; the ambush was utterly unexpected, for they had walked in certain of victory; and arrows are a terror we can barely imagine, falling out of nowhere from above, impossible to see in time, while the sheer mass of twelve thousand meant most had no armor to turn a single shaft. So they ran, scrambling for any cover from the sky raining death. Their great numbers, in which they had delighted only hours before, availed them nothing at all, and the wide earth shrank around twelve thousand men until there was nowhere left to stand.

I am the Prophet, this is no lie

In the heart of the rout, the Prophet ﷺ did not move. While twelve thousand men streamed past him in blind panic, he pressed his white mule forward, toward the enemy, into the arrows, as though the whole army were fleeing around a single still point, and called out over the chaos in a voice meant to carry: Come to me. I am the Messenger of Allah. I am Muhammad, the son of Abdullah. And then the lines the seerah has never forgotten, that ring down fourteen centuries: I am the Prophet, this is no lie. I am the son of Abdul Muttalib.

He was reaching them, the Sheikh explains, with both hands at once. With the first he held out the truth of his prophethood, the call no believer could ignore. With the second he held out a name, and the name was deliberate. Abdul Muttalib was the most beloved of all the Arabs of the age before Islam, the grandfather every tribe in the peninsula honored, and the men fleeing through that valley were in large part Makkans of a single week's faith who had revered that name their whole lives long. So the Prophet ﷺ combined the two loyalties on purpose, the loyalty to Islam first and above all, and beneath it the loyalty of blood, because for these men a name they had loved since childhood might pull them back when nothing else could. The sentiment of tribe, or in our age of nation, is not evil in itself, the Sheikh adds; like any sentiment it can be turned to good or to ill, and here he ﷺ turned it to good, so long as it never rises above the pride of faith.

His uncle Abbas was beside him, famous for a voice that carried like a horn, and the Prophet ﷺ told him to call the companions back by name, tribe by tribe, beginning with the people of the pledge under the tree, who at Hudaybiyah had vowed to fight without weapons. Abbas roared their names into the valley, one tribe after another, until the walls rang with it. And the Sheikh describes what happened then as men waking from a daze. As each man heard his own name called across the chaos, he came to his senses, asked himself where on earth he was running to, and turned. Here we are, they cried, here we respond to your call, and they came flocking back. Among those who had never fled at all was the Prophet's own cousin, the other Abu Sufyan, the son of al-Harith, gripping the stirrup of the mule in his armor, so close in blood that when the Prophet ﷺ asked who he was, he answered: I am the son of your mother, the cousin who weeks earlier had wept for forgiveness and been forgiven, and who now, when all the world ran, had stayed.

And not far off were men whose faith was so thin it nearly tore through. When the Muslims first broke, the brother of Safwan ibn Umayya said with satisfaction that today, at last, the magic spell of Islam would be broken, for they had always accused the Prophet ﷺ of sorcery. Safwan, not yet even a Muslim, snapped at him to be silent: by Allah, I would rather be ruled by a man of Quraysh than a man of Hawazin, pure tribal feeling and nothing of faith in it. This, the Sheikh says, is exactly the mindset the Prophet ﷺ was contending with as he reached for the grandfather's name as well as the Lord's: he was holding the line of an army half of which barely believed.

Then He sent down His tranquillity

As the companions surged back around him, the Prophet ﷺ bent down from his mule, took up a handful of dust and pebbles, and flung it toward the enemy with the words: may the faces be disfigured. It is reported as a sign, the Sheikh says, that their sight clouded and the archers on the heights could no longer find their mark, and the tide of the day began to turn. He raised his hands and prayed, and the believers, seeing their Prophet ﷺ unmoved at the center of the storm, pressed forward at last.

And this is where the Qur'an's own account of the rout comes to rest, and Allah tells the believers what no eye on that field could have seen.

Then Allāh sent down His tranquility upon His Messenger and upon the believers and sent down soldiers [i.e., angels] whom you did not see and punished those who disbelieved. And that is the recompense of the disbelievers.Qur'an 9:26

The whole lesson of the day is folded into the silence between those two verses. First the earth closes its jaws on twelve thousand men who had let their great host please them until it pleased them right into a trap, and the wide world shrank to nothing around them. Then, in the very next breath, the help comes down: the sakinah poured straight into the hearts of the Messenger ﷺ and the believers in the instant their courage had failed, and with it soldiers no human eye could see. Not when the army grew. Not when the swords were counted. The victory descended the moment the believers stopped fleeing and turned back to their Lord, and it descended from Him alone, unearned and unseen.

The dust is still hanging in the air, the day not yet won, and the Sheikh leaves the rest, the rout about to run the other way, the spoils that will test the believers more sorely than the arrows did, for the day to come. But the turn has already happened, the way it always happens: not when the numbers swelled, but when the men who had trusted them came home to the One who had been calling their names all along.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read Hunayn as a near disaster narrowly survived, a battle story with a happy ending, and to leave it sealed in the past. That would be to miss the question Allah is putting to us by naming this day, of all days, in His eternal Book. He did not name it to record a victory. He named it to expose a heart, and the heart it exposes is ours.

Numbers are not the help. This is what the whole day was built to teach, and Allah taught it by giving the believers, just once, everything the world says you need to win, and then letting it avail them nothing, so that no believer after them would ever again confuse the help of Allah with the size of what he holds in his hand. Ask yourself honestly what you are leaning on: the size of your savings, the breadth of your network, the strength of your body, the count of those who agree with you. All of it is a gift, and none of it is the Helper. The moment you mistake the gift for the Giver, you are standing in the valley at Hunayn with the arrows about to fall.

Watch the charms you reach for. The new Muslims wanted only a tree, and the Prophet ﷺ swore by Allah that it was the very wish of a people who had asked their prophet for an idol, because a god is simply whatever you turn to for protection and help. So look at what you actually turn to when you are afraid, the bead, the eye on the wall, the superstition you would be embarrassed to defend out loud, and ask whether you have quietly handed a sliver of your trust to something that was never going to save you. Only Allah brings good and turns away harm. Strip every rival from that conviction and let it stand alone.

Name the deed, be gentle with the person. The Prophet ﷺ called the request shirk and did not soften it, and in the same breath refused to call the man a mushrik, because the man did not yet know. Hold both of those at once, because we are forever collapsing them, either excusing the error to spare the person or condemning the person to be done with the error. He ﷺ did neither. He named the wrong clearly, taught the man patiently, and left the judging of souls to those with the knowledge to judge. Be that exact with yourself, and that merciful with everyone else.

And stand when everyone else runs. Twelve thousand men fled, and one man pressed his mule forward into the arrows and called them back by name. Steadiness in the worst moment, the Sheikh reminds us, is not the absence of fear; it is a trust in Allah that simply outlasts the fear. When the thing you counted on melts away and the wide world goes suddenly narrow around you, do the one thing the army at Hunayn finally did. Turn back toward the One calling your name through all the noise. The tranquillity and the unseen soldiers did not come down on the strong or the many. They came down on the few who turned around. Whatever has broken and run in your life, the help of Allah waits on the other side of turning back to Him. O Allah, send down upon us Your tranquillity as You sent it upon Your Messenger ﷺ and the believers on the day of Hunayn, make firm our feet when the ground gives way beneath us, strip from our hearts every reliance but reliance on You, and let us be among those who turn back to You before the day is lost. Ameen.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 82: the battle of Hunayn, part 1 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (7:138, 9:25, 9:26), verified via quran.ai. The narration is the Sheikh's; the phrasing is Buruja's.

The Seerah · 83 of 104

The Battle of Hunayn, Part 2

The turn, the unseen army, and the largest spoils ever taken

Hold the picture exactly where the last day left it. The valley of Hunayn is a narrow throat of land, and at dawn twelve thousand believers had poured into it certain of an easy morning. They were the most numerous army the Muslims had ever fielded and the best equipped, mail and arrows borrowed by the hundred from the people of Makkah, the men rested from nineteen unhurried days in the conquered city. And for the first time in their lives some of them had felt the thing a believing army can least afford to feel: that they could not lose. The trap snapped shut. Arrows fell from every rim of the valley at once, the herds the army had driven before it stampeded back through its own ranks, and the great host came apart and ran.

Not all of it. In the middle of the panic one man would not move. The Prophet ﷺ pressed his white mule toward the very arrows that were emptying the valley, and around him the army had narrowed to the smallest of circles, Abu Bakr and Umar, Uthman and Ali, his uncle Abbas, a scattering of the Ansar. Twelve thousand had become a dozen. This second day of Hunayn is the story of what happens after a man refuses to flee: the turning of the rout, the descent of an army no eye could see, the heart of an assassin changed in mid gallop, the largest fortune the Muslims would ever take, and the long stubborn road it opened toward Ta'if.

When they heard their own names

Abbas had a voice that carried like a horn, and the Prophet ﷺ set it to work. He did not call to the army as a crowd, which would have been a single cry swallowed by a valley full of screaming. He called them back the way a shepherd gathers a scattered flock, one band at a time, clan by clan and name by name. O company of such and such. O sons of so and so. And the men, blind with panic and running for the rocks, heard their own names rise out of the valley behind them, and it was as if they woke from a bad dream and asked themselves where they thought they were running to. They turned. They came back, first in trickles and then in a flood, re forming around the still center of that white mule.

Then the Prophet ﷺ bent to the floor of the valley, took up a fistful of pebbles and dust, and flung it into the faces of the enemy. He had done this once before, on the morning of Badr, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi draws the line between the two days deliberately: the same hand, the same gesture, the same result. The archers on the heights faltered, their sight clouded, their volleys broke their rhythm, and in that gap of confusion the believers gathered tighter and tighter around the Prophet ﷺ with every breath.

This is the day Allah singled out by name in His Book, one of only a handful of moments in the entire seerah that the Qur'an narrates as it happened. He reminds the believers first that He has carried them through field after field, and then He turns to this one valley and refuses to look away from their worst hour in it:

Allāh has already given you victory in many regions and [even] on the day of Ḥunayn, when your great number pleased you, but it did not avail you at all, and the earth was confining for you with [i.e., in spite of] its vastness; then you turned back, fleeing.Qur'an 9:25

One verse holds the whole morning, the Sheikh points out, and holds it with an unflinching honesty that should stop us. The confidence is in it, the cockiness, the numbers that had pleased them and bought them nothing, the wide valley that suddenly had no room in it for a fleeing man, and the backs that turned, named and recorded forever. Allah does not draw a veil over the moment His servants failed Him. He writes it into the Book that will be recited until the end of the world, so that the lesson of it can never quietly be lost: that the day you trust your own numbers is the day the earth shrinks around you.

An army no eye could see

And then, in the very next breath, the verse turns, and so does the day.

Then Allāh sent down His tranquility upon His Messenger and upon the believers and sent down soldiers [i.e., angels] whom you did not see and punished those who disbelieved. And that is the recompense of the disbelievers.Qur'an 9:26

Notice the order, because the order is the whole teaching. The help did not come down while the army was boasting that it could not be destroyed. It came down in the instant their courage had collapsed and they had begun to crawl back toward the One calling their names. First Allah sent His sakinah, His own tranquillity, settling onto the Messenger ﷺ and onto the believers whose hearts had just been torn open by fear, until terror gave way to a steadying calm. And then He sent down soldiers that no human eye could see. As at Badr, the angels came to Hunayn, and Allah states it plainly in His Book in His own words, so that not one believer for the rest of time could mistake where the rescue had come from. It came from above, not from the borrowed armor and the rested limbs below.

And as at Badr, the Sheikh notes, some of those present were given a glimpse of it. Jubayr ibn Mutim, by now a Muslim, the son of that Mutim ibn Adi whose protection had once sheltered the Prophet ﷺ in the worst of the Makkan years, was watching when the two armies met. He saw what he could only describe as a great black mass come down out of the sky, dense and dark, and then break apart and scatter across the field the way a poured storm of ants spreads over the ground. He understood at once what he was seeing: the help of Allah, descending to fight for the believers. The blackness was not a colour but a density, a host so packed together that it looked like a single shadow until it spread.

But the moment Dr. Yasir Qadhi lingers over longest belongs to a man who had come to Hunayn for the exact opposite of faith. Shaybah ibn Uthman was a distant cousin of the Prophet ﷺ, of Banu Abd al-Dar, one of the sister clans of Banu Hashim. His father Uthman had been killed at Uhud fighting on the side of the pagans, and the wound of it had never closed. Shaybah had accepted Islam only days earlier, at the conquest of Makkah, and his own honesty about it is what makes the story so piercing: his Islam, he would say, was still only on his tongue and had not yet reached his heart. So when he saw the Prophet ﷺ standing exposed on his mule while the whole army fled around him, the old grief rose up and saw its chance. He took his spear, turned his horse, and spurred it at the Prophet's ﷺ back to finish, in the chaos where no one would see, what Uhud had begun.

And then, he said, out of nowhere a wall of light rose up between him and his target, so blinding that he had to throw his arm across his face, and his eyes burned in his skull. The Prophet ﷺ turned. Whether he saw the angels in that light or saw only a man galloping at him with murder in his face, he understood in an instant exactly what was happening, and he did not reach for a weapon. He raised his hands. O Allah, drive the devil away from Shaybah. O Allah, guide Shaybah. Three times. And Shaybah said that faith came pouring into his heart on the spot, in the space of that single charge, so that by the time his horse reached the end of its gallop the man who had set out to assassinate the Prophet ﷺ had wheeled around and was fighting at his side against Hawazin and Thaqif. He lived a righteous life from that day. It is one of the great answered prayers of the seerah: the Prophet ﷺ, with an assassin's spear at his back, spent his breath not on saving himself but on begging Allah to save the soul of the man holding it.

The rout that ran the other way

Now the whole field tipped. Hawazin and Thaqif, it became clear, had wagered everything on a single tactic, and they had no second one in reserve. The plan had been simple and, for one terrible hour, brilliant: bury the Muslims under arrows from the heights, watch them break, then pour down on a fleeing, scattered enemy and cut them apart. It was a flawless plan A. There was no plan B. So when the believers refused to stay broken, when they re formed and came charging back uphill at full speed behind their Prophet ﷺ, the enemy reached for the next move and found nothing there. The rout simply reversed its direction. The same panic that had emptied the valley of Muslims now emptied it of pagans, every man fleeing headlong for the mountains and the wadis.

The Prophet ﷺ would not let them gather themselves again. Twenty thousand scattered men can become twenty thousand reassembled men by nightfall, so he commanded the companions to pursue them hard into the valleys and up into the heights and give them no quarter of rest in which to stop and regroup. Out of that command came a long string of small pursuing expeditions, two hundred sent down one wadi, three hundred up another, and in the chasing some of the best of the companions fell.

One of them was the uncle of Abu Musa al-Ashari. Sent in command of a pursuit, he was struck down by a thrown javelin. His nephew reached him with the shaft still standing in his chest, bent close, and learned from his dying lips the name of the man who had thrown it. Abu Musa went after that man at once and dealt with him, then came back to his uncle. I have taken care of him, he said, now what can I do for you. Pull it out, his uncle answered, and when the javelin came free the bleeding took him fast. With his last breaths he sent his salam to the Prophet ﷺ and begged him to ask Allah for his forgiveness. When Abu Musa carried the message back, the Prophet ﷺ rose from where he lay with his shawl about him, called for water, made wudu, turned to the qiblah, and lifted his hands so high that the white of his underarms could be seen, and he prayed for the dead man by name, that Allah forgive him his sins and raise his rank. And me, Messenger of Allah, said Abu Musa, ask for me too. So he prayed for Abu Musa as well, that Allah forgive him and bring him on the Day of Judgment to a noble place. Abu Musa's son Abu Burda, who became one of the great scholars of the generation after the companions, would treasure that hadith for the rest of his life: that the Prophet ﷺ had made dua, with his own raised hands, for his father and his grandfather both.

She should not have been killed

Walking the battlefield in the aftermath, among the fallen, the Prophet ﷺ came upon the body of a woman who had been killed in the fighting, and the sight of it stopped him cold. This was wrong, he said. She should not have been killed. Who did this. He was told that it was Khalid ibn al-Walid, already sent on ahead in command of a pursuit.

Catch Khalid, the Prophet ﷺ ordered, before he goes any further, and tell him: he is not to kill any woman, any child, or any servant. The last word in that command the scholars have turned over with great care, the Sheikh notes, taking it to mean the hired help, or the elderly, or anyone living on the edges of a fighting camp who has not himself come out to fight. In that age there was no separate class of people called civilians; every grown man was, in effect, a soldier. But the people who were not soldiers, the women, the children, the servants and the old, the Prophet ﷺ placed firmly and explicitly outside the reach of the sword, and he did it not in a lecture hall but on a battlefield still warm from a fight that had nearly killed him.

This, the Sheikh emphasizes, is one of the foundation stones that the books of fiqh return to again and again when they take up the law of war, and it is one of the most urgent rulings in the seerah for our own time. On a field that he had every human reason to think of as nothing but war, the Prophet ﷺ plainly forbade the killing of non combatants. He did not wait to be asked the question. He saw a single body that should never have been there, and before he did anything else he sent a fast rider to make certain it would not happen a second time. The believer's anger, even on the day of battle, never erases the believer's limits.

The largest spoils ever taken

Hunayn brought the Muslims the greatest war booty they had ever laid eyes on, and the reason was the enemy's own catastrophic miscalculation. Before the battle, the young chieftain of Hawazin had ordered the tribes to drag their entire world to the field, wives and children, flocks and herds, everything they owned, on the theory that a man fights to the death when his family is watching from just behind the lines. The elder among them had warned it was folly, that a woman penned behind an army is no use in winning a battle and only a prize to be lost if the battle is lost, but the young man had his way. So when Hawazin broke and fled, they left all of it standing in the valley. The counts the Sheikh hands on are staggering: some six thousand captives, women and children; more than twenty four thousand camels; over forty thousand goats. Reckon it in the money of our own day and you are speaking of tens of millions, a fortune the Muslims had never before come within sight of. The Prophet ﷺ had every camel and every captive gathered and brought to a valley called Jiranah, on the road between Ta'if and Makkah, and set an armed guard at the head of it, for there was a great deal here that the defeated would dearly want back.

Two small scenes survive from the gathering, and both of them are about him ﷺ more than about the gold. As the captives were led in, one of the women began to cry out that she was no ordinary prisoner, that she was the sister of their own companion. Brought before him, she said she was Shayma, his foster sister from the earliest days of his childhood, of the household of Halima who had nursed him in the desert. Prove it to me, he said gently, for he had not laid eyes on her in more than half a century; he was sixty now and had last seen her when he was a small boy. She said: you still carry a mark on my back, from the day you bit me as a baby while I was carrying you on my hip. He knew her then, and laughed, and gave her the choice to stay with him in honour or to return to her people loaded with gifts. It is even narrated, in a chain the Sheikh is careful to flag as broken and uncertain, fifty fifty whether it is sound, that his foster mother Halima herself was among those brought to him, an old woman near eighty by now, and that the Prophet ﷺ rose to his feet for her, took the cloak from his own back, and spread it on the ground for her to sit upon, which in that world was the very highest honour a man could pay. Whether or not she ever accepted Islam the narration does not say. That he honoured the woman who had nursed him, the Sheikh says, is certain.

And the Sheikh pauses on one detail it would be easy to read straight past. Only four Muslims were killed at Hunayn, a tiny toll for so vast a day, yet many were wounded, and among the wounded were all four of the men who would one day, each in his turn, lead the ummah after the Prophet ﷺ: Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, and Ali. Every one of the four future caliphs shed his blood in this one valley, and every one of them was among the handful who had not run but stood their ground around the white mule when twelve thousand fled. There is a quiet argument in that fact about who was fit to lead. Hunayn also shares something with only one other day in all of history, the Sheikh observes: it is one of just two battles that Allah names outright in the Qur'an, the other being Badr. The Qur'an mentions other conflicts, the confederates at the trench among them, but only as armies and not as named battles. Badr and Hunayn alone are named. And the parallel runs deeper than the naming. Badr was the victory of faith and Hunayn the victory of fortune; one a boost to the spirit and the other to the hand; one the proof that Allah can win a war with three hundred, the other that He can fill the believers' arms when He chooses. In both, the help came down from the sky. In both, the believers strained to the very end of their own effort, and then stood back and watched Allah finish what they could not.

On to Ta'if

The scattered enemy ran in every direction, but the people of Thaqif did not melt into the wilderness like the rest of the tribes. They ran home, to the one place on earth they trusted, to Ta'if: their green city nestled high in the cool of the mountains, ringed with massive walls, fed by its own streams, heavy with grapes and fruit, the proud old rival of Makkah that had everything Makkah had except the Kaaba and had answered that one lack by piling up wealth and fortification instead. They had stocked the city against a year long siege and built engines of defence along its walls, cauldrons of burning oil to pour down on any attacker, the like of which no other city in Arabia possessed. And so, with the great fortune still under guard at Jiranah, the Prophet ﷺ turned and marched the army straight from the field of Hunayn to the walls of Ta'if.
