The scholars of seerah, the Sheikh notes, usually tell Hunayn and the siege of Ta'if as a single story, because they fell back to back against the same enemy, and they argue over whether to count them as one event or two. In truth they are two distinct things: a battle in the open valley, and then a siege at a city wall.

And here Dr. Yasir Qadhi draws out something that should reshape how we picture the Prophet ﷺ as a commander. Long before any of this, while he was still in Makkah and had only just heard that Hawazin and Thaqif were arming against him, he had quietly sent two or three of the companions away to a distant tribe, Jurash, to learn the machinery of siege warfare, weapons the Muslims had never in their history used: the catapult, the battering ram, and the great shield the soldiers call the testudo, the turtle, a roof of shields under which men can advance to a wall while burning oil and arrows rain down from above. That is foresight, the Sheikh says, a man thinking three steps ahead. While the battle of Hunayn was still entirely in front of him, unfought, he was already preparing for the walls of Ta'if that lay beyond it. And it worked to the letter. By the time he reached the city, those companions had returned with the knowledge, and for the first time anywhere in that part of the world, the Muslims raised a catapult against a wall.

So the same army that had nearly shattered in a valley at dawn now stood, within days, before the most fortified city in Arabia, fielding engines of war it had never before owned. The companions camped first too close, and were driven back under volleys of arrows, until they pitched at the spot where the great mosque of Ta'if, Masjid Ibn Abbas, stands to this day. They tried the catapult, and the battering ram, and once pushed the turtle up to the very wall, only to have the burning oil find its way through and scatter the men out from under it into the open arrows. The walls held. After some days the Prophet ﷺ judged it time to lift the siege, but the younger companions, drunk on the victories of Makkah and Hunayn and feeling themselves on an unbroken roll, begged to stay and try once more, and he let them, for a wise leader, the Sheikh draws out, does not force his judgment on his people like a dictator even when he is the Messenger of Allah and knows best. They stayed, lost more men, and at last quietly agreed it was time to go.

And when they were leaving, the companions came to him and said: Messenger of Allah, make dua against Thaqif. This was the very city that had once driven him out and set its children to stone him bleeding through its streets, the city that, even after the conquest of Makkah, still stood stubborn and defiant behind its walls. If any people in all of Arabia had earned a curse, it was these. The Prophet ﷺ raised his hands, and every man around him raised his hands certain that the destruction of Thaqif was about to be sealed, and he said: O Allah, guide Thaqif. O Allah, bring Thaqif to us. He prayed for them, not against them. The goal of this religion, the Sheikh says, was never vengeance and blood; it was always mercy and guidance. And Allah answered the dua. Thaqif would come, of their own feet, and accept Islam the following year. The fortune still waited at Jiranah; the men of Hawazin had not yet come to claim back what was theirs; and the way the Prophet ﷺ would choose to divide that staggering wealth, in a manner that would test the hearts of even his closest companions, is the story of the days to come.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to take the second day of Hunayn as a war story with a happy ending, the rout reversed, the angels down, the treasure counted, and to file it away as a great escape that worked out. That is to walk past everything it is trying to put to us. Slow down at three places in it and you will not read your own hard mornings the same way again.

The first is the order of the verse, and the order is the entire lesson. Allah did not send the sakinah and the unseen soldiers down onto an army boasting that it could not be destroyed. He sent them down at the lowest point of the day, the instant the believers stopped fleeing and began to turn back toward the One calling their names. Their numbers had pleased them and availed them nothing, and the wide earth had shrunk around them; and only then, only after the turning, came the help. We are forever waiting for the rescue to arrive so that we can then turn back to Allah, when the whole shape of this day says the reverse: turn back first, in the dust and the panic and the shame of it, and the help comes down onto the turning. Whatever has scattered in your life, the sakinah is waiting on the far side of facing Him again.

The second is a man on a horse with a spear, riding to kill. The Prophet ﷺ had every right to strike Shaybah down, or to call for help, or simply to flee. Instead he lifted his hands and spent his breath on the one prayer that no one watching would have thought to make: O Allah, guide Shaybah. And by the end of that single gallop the assassin was a brother, fighting at his side. We are quick to write people off, to decide that this heart or that one is finished, an enemy past saving. The Prophet ﷺ looked at a spear aimed at his own back and saw a soul that Allah could still soften, and asked Him to soften it. Ask yourself who you have already given up on, and whether you have ever once raised your hands and begged Allah to guide them the way he begged for the man riding to kill him.

And the third is the strangest pairing of all, and it sits at the very heart of Hunayn. The same Prophet ﷺ who watched an unseen army descend from heaven to fight for him had, weeks earlier and hundreds of miles away, quietly sent men off to learn how to build a catapult. He did not say: Allah will send angels, so why prepare. He prepared to the last bolt and the last shield, and then he leaned with his whole weight on Allah, and when the help came it came from above and not from the engines below. This is the balance the whole battle was teaching, and the one the army had forgotten at dawn when its numbers pleased it: reliance on Allah is not the enemy of effort, it is what all our effort is meant to rest upon. Tie the camel of every lawful preparation, learn the catapult, count your numbers, sharpen your sword, and then know in your bones that the victory was never once in the numbers or the sword, but came down, as it came down at Badr and at Hunayn, from a place no eye can see.

So carry the turning down into your ordinary days. When something in your life breaks and runs, turn back to Allah before you see any sign of rescue, because the help waits on the turning. Pray for the person you have most given up on, by name, the way he prayed for Shaybah. Prepare like a man who knows that preparing is part of trust, and then rest, genuinely rest, in the knowledge that the outcome was never yours to hold. And remember, when your own winning streak stalls at some wall that will not fall, that even in his hour of greatest glory the Prophet ﷺ was given Hunayn but not Ta'if, was shown that this world is never a clean and total victory, so that we would learn to seek the only victory that is. May Allah send down upon us His tranquillity as He sent it upon His Messenger ﷺ and the believers at Hunayn, soften the hardest hearts toward His guidance as He softened Shaybah's in mid charge, keep us merciful when we hold the power to be cruel, and let every victory He gives us be one we trace back, with the whole of our hearts, to Him alone.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 83: the battle of Hunayn, part 2 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (9:25, 9:26), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 84 of 104

The Conversion of Ka'b ibn Zuhayr

The poet under a death sentence, and the cloak that saved him

Makkah has fallen, and the Prophet ﷺ is back in Madinah. The idols are coming down across Arabia, the tribes are sending their delegations, and the question that hangs over these last months of the eighth year is what to do with the men who had fought Islam with the most dangerous weapon of all. Not the sword. The tongue. In a world without newspapers and without screens, the poet was the propaganda machine, and a single barbed ode could travel a thousand miles on the lips of strangers and turn a whole people against you. The poets of Quraysh knew this, and they had used it without mercy. Most of them, by now, had been killed at Badr or executed at the conquest, or had repented and come in. But one name still hung over the desert, the most famous of them all.

About two weeks after the conquest, that man, the single most famous poet alive in the Arabian Peninsula, walked quietly into the mosque. No one knew his face. And there was a death sentence on his head.

The greatest poet alive

To feel the weight of this story you have to know who Ka'b was. His father was Zuhayr ibn Abi Sulma, a name that should ring a bell from anyone's schooling in Arabic, one of the authors of the Mu'allaqat, the seven hanging odes the Arabs reckoned the very summit of their poetry. He ranked among the greatest of all the Jahili poets, in the company of Imru' al-Qays, the absolute peak of the pre-Islamic art. He died before the coming of Islam, when the Prophet ﷺ was still a young man, and never met him. This is his son. Ka'b was born and raised in that house, and grew into the premier poet of the whole Arabian Peninsula in his own generation. Whatever gift his father had carried, the son carried it too, and the Arabs knew it.

And he had used that gift as a weapon against the Prophet ﷺ. He composed poems attacking him. When his own brother accepted Islam, Ka'b grew angrier still, and wrote a harsh satire lampooning his own blood for converting. And then, when he had run out of things to say, he crossed the one line you do not cross with the early Muslims: he began to speak of the Muslim women, in vulgar and unspeakable terms. After that, there was no mercy left for him. It was understood, among the believers, that this man's life was forfeit. He was not of Quraysh; he was from the tribe of Muzayna, up north of Madinah, and he had never set foot in the city. Everyone knew his name. No one had ever seen his face.

What turned him was not a threat from his enemies. It was a letter from the brother he had ridiculed. After the conquest of Makkah, that same brother wrote to him a heartwarming letter, full of a tenderness Ka'b had done nothing to earn. You have no choice left, the brother told him. Makkah is conquered, your life is at stake. Either flee the whole Peninsula, or else come, accept Islam, and repent, because the Prophet ﷺ accepts the repentance of whoever repents. Sit with the shape of that, because Dr. Yasir Qadhi pauses on it. Ka'b had done the cruelest thing a poet could do, a satire against his own brother, and the Muslim brother answered the cruelty with kindness. He wrote not to gloat over a fugitive but to save him, and he made the way back sound easy, because in truth it was: the door is open, just walk through it.

And Ka'b thought for a long time. In that long thinking he realized something honest about himself, something a lesser man would never have admitted: he did not actually believe in the idols. He never had. He had been clinging to culture and custom, to the way his fathers had always done things, and not to any deep-seated conviction in stone gods at all. Once he saw that clearly, the decision made itself. He wanted to become a Muslim. So he set out for Madinah, the city he had never seen, where his name was a legend and his face was unknown.

Banat Su'ad, and the cloak

He came into Madinah carefully. He spent the night, it is said, in the house of a distant acquaintance who knew to keep his arrival a secret. In the morning he prayed Fajr in the congregation, one more worshipper in the rows, his face meaning nothing to anyone around him. And when the prayer was done, he rose and walked up to the Prophet ﷺ and placed his own hand into the Prophet's ﷺ hand, and still he did not say who he was.

He spoke instead as though he were a messenger carrying a plea from someone far away. Ka'b ibn Zuhayr, he said, is seeking your protection; he has come repenting for what he has done, and wishes to accept Islam. If he comes to you like this, will you accept him? And the Prophet ﷺ said: yes, I will accept him. Only then did the poet let the truth out. I, he said, am Ka'b ibn Zuhayr.

The name landed in the mosque like a thrown stone. One of the Ansar shot to his feet to cut the man down where he stood, this poet who had defamed their women, now within arm's reach. But the Prophet ﷺ stopped him. Let him be, he said. He has come as a Muslim, repenting and accepting Islam. Leave him. And it was over. The death sentence that had hung over Ka'b for years, the verdict no one had thought to lift, dissolved in a single sentence from the only mouth that could speak it. He had come as an enemy and was received as a brother, in the space of one breath.

Then Ka'b, the poet to the bone, asked the thing a poet would ask. Messenger of Allah, may I recite something for you? He had a poem ready. The Prophet ﷺ told him to go ahead, and Ka'b recited one of the most famous poems in all of seerah literature. The classical books name it after its opening words, Banat Su'ad, "Su'ad has departed." Later generations gave it another name: Qasidat al-Burdah, the ode of the cloak. And here Dr. Yasir Qadhi stops to untangle a knot that confuses Muslims to this day, because there are two Burdahs, and people are forever mixing them up.

This is the original Burdah. Burdah means cloak, and the poem earns the name from what happened at its end. It is in deep, difficult classical Arabic, so hard that it is hardly ever recited today; the Sheikh, no stranger to Arabic, says plainly that these lines are almost impossible to translate without immense help, and that most native Arabs would be no better off, the words and styles simply beyond the reach of modern Arabic. Then there is the second Burdah, the one everyone has heard, composed by al-Busiri some six hundred years later, the famous one sung by Pakistanis and Arabs and Turks and every ethnicity on earth. But it rests on al-Busiri's own claim to have seen the Prophet ﷺ in a dream and been given the cloak there, which comes from no one but the author himself and raises its own questions. That is not the poem of this night. The Burdah of this night is Ka'b's, recited to the living face of the Prophet ﷺ.

Like every classical ode, it opens with a lost love, a woman who has packed up and walked away, the love story a veil never meant literally. What matters, and what the Sheikh dwells on, is the last third, where the veil drops and the poet speaks straight to his own danger. I have been told, Ka'b says, that the Messenger of Allah has threatened me, and yet to receive pardon at his hands is the thing most hoped for of all. Slow down, he pleads, be careful, before someone strikes me down, by the One who gifted you this Qur'an with all its warnings and its good counsel. Do not believe everything the rumor-mongers say about me, even though they say a great deal, for I am not as guilty as they claim. And then he stands inside his terror and names it without flinching: I am standing now in a place where, if an elephant could see and hear what I see and hear, it would be trembling in sheer terror, unless the Messenger ﷺ should grant me respite.

And then comes the line, the most famous of this long ode and one of the most famous in the whole history of Islamic poetry, the one Dr. Yasir Qadhi takes a particular and personal pride in. The Prophet ﷺ, Ka'b declares, is a light from which guidance is sought, a drawn sword among the swords of Allah, a muhannad. A muhannad is an Indian blade. In seventh-century Arabia the finest steel on earth came from India, the very pinnacle of the metalworker's art, so to call the Prophet ﷺ a muhannad was the highest praise a poet had in him to give: the perfect blade, unsheathed in the path of Allah. The Sheikh, an Indian himself, lingers here with open delight, for here at last is a line in which the people of the subcontinent can take a share of the honor: the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, likened to the steel of their own land.

The poetry was so beautiful that the Prophet ﷺ was moved, and he did the thing that gave the poem its name forever. He took off his own cloak, his Burdah, and draped it over the poet, the single greatest honor you could bestow on a man of verse. And he told Ka'b one thing more: you wounded the Ansar with your tongue, so now praise them. And Ka'b did, in lines the books still record. The man who had come into the city with a death sentence on his head walked out of it wearing the mantle of the Prophet ﷺ, and he died a Muslim, the most famous poet of his age, gathered in at last by the hand of the one he had once attacked.

Adi, who was sure it was either a prophet or a king

In these same final months of the eighth year, while delegations arrived and the new order took shape, the Prophet ﷺ was sending small expeditions out to the smaller tribes to bring them in and pull down the idols that still stood. One such expedition, under Ali ibn Abi Talib, reached the tribe of Tayyi in the north, destroyed their great idol, and brought captives back to Madinah. Among them was a woman who loudly demanded to be heard. I am the daughter of Hatim, she announced, the daughter of Hatim al-Tai. And that name carried its own weight, for Hatim al-Tai was the most famous generous man the Arabs ever knew, the very byword for openhandedness, whom the desert still tells stories about fourteen centuries later. For her father's sake alone, the Prophet ﷺ set her free, gave her wealth and a mount and safe passage, and let her go wherever she wished.

Her brother was Adi ibn Hatim, the chieftain of Tayyi, who had not stayed to face the Muslims. This son who plainly did not share his father's character had prepared an escape, and the moment the advance reached him he abandoned his tribe and fled north with his family, all the way to the Roman frontier. Adi was a Christian, comfortable and well connected, on good terms with the Roman emperor, living a cushy life on the edge of the greatest empire of the day.

When his sister reached him, after rebuking him roundly for leaving her, she gave him a piece of clear thinking. Go back down to this man Muhammad ﷺ, she said. He is one of two things and no third: either a prophet, in which case the sooner you believe the better, or a king, in which case you might as well go and win his favor. And the very fact she would grant he might be a prophet, the Sheikh points out, showed which way she was already leaning. So Adi, the man who would later say there was no one on earth more despised to him than the Prophet ﷺ, made his decision the honest way: let me go and see for myself. If he is speaking the truth, I will listen, and if he is lying, it will do me no harm.

I know your religion better than you

Adi walked into Madinah with no protection at all, carrying the cool certainty that no one would dare lay a hand on the son of Hatim, and he was right; the honor of that name still covered him, just as it had freed his sister. People recognized him in the streets, a crowd gathered, and they brought him to the Prophet ﷺ. Accept Islam, the Prophet ﷺ told him, and you will be safe. But Adi answered that he already had a religion he followed, that he was a Christian, as if to say: why would I trade down to your faith? I am no pagan, go preach to the pagans. He said it a second time, and a third. And then came a line that Dr. Yasir Qadhi calls a classic of the Prophet's ﷺ dawah: I know your religion better than you know it. I know the message of Jesus better than you do.

And he proved it, gently, with two questions and a third. Are you not the chief of your people? Yes. Do you not take a quarter of their wealth in tax? Yes. And do you not know that your own religion forbids you that? Adi was ashamed, because the Prophet ﷺ had laid his finger on the exact spot where Adi was not even living by what he claimed to believe. I became embarrassed, Adi said afterward, and I knew at that moment that he was a prophet, because he had picked out my hypocrisy at once. Here the Sheikh draws the lesson out: we are the ones truly following the teaching of Jesus, who was a practicing servant of God, who prayed and fasted, never ate the flesh of swine, was circumcised, and lived by the law he was given. Who, among all of humanity, still does that and honors Jesus? Then the Prophet ﷺ was plain about the rest, that those who took Jesus, or Ezra, as more than servants of God had gone astray. He did not soften it, because, as the Sheikh insists, you cannot prove one faith true without disputing the others, and the respectful debate is the honest one, not the comfortable fiction that every creed is equally valid.

This man is not a king

Then the Prophet ﷺ took Adi by the hand and walked with him toward his own house. And on the way, this is Adi telling the story himself, an old woman with a small child stopped him in the road with some need, and the Prophet ﷺ stood there in the open street and stayed with her until her need was met. Adi watched the whole thing, and a sentence formed itself in his mind: this man is not a king. A king does not halt in the road for a poor old woman when a chieftain waits at his side, knowing exactly who that chieftain is.

They reached the house, and there was a single worn-out mat inside, nothing more. The Prophet ﷺ laid it down and said, sit. But Adi was embarrassed, because it was plainly the only thing in the room. No, said Adi, you sit on it. And the Prophet ﷺ insisted, until Adi sat on the mat and the Prophet ﷺ lowered himself onto the bare ground of his own house. This humility, the Sheikh says, is what first cracked Adi's heart open. Within five minutes he had ruled one of his two possibilities out entirely.

Then the Prophet ﷺ read him, the way you read a man you have understood to the bottom, and went straight to the real obstacle. He began on the simplest ground: do you know of any god besides Allah? No. Any being mightier than Allah? No. Then those who set up partners with Him are mistaken and misguided. And then the heart of it: perhaps, the Prophet ﷺ said, you are not accepting Islam because of the state of the people around me. There it was, named out loud. Adi had come from the edge of the Roman Empire, the premier civilization on earth, down into a Madinah that did not yet look like a civilization at all, and he was judging the truth of the message by the condition of the people who carried it. So the Prophet ﷺ answered every worry in turn. You are worried about safety, he said in effect, and safety is coming: the day is coming when a woman will travel alone from al-Hira to make tawaf at the Kabah, fearing nothing and no one but Allah. You are worried about poverty, and the wealth of empires is coming: the day is coming when the treasures of Kisra son of Hurmuz, the emperor of Persia, will be opened and shared out among you, and a man will walk the streets with his charity in his hand, looking for anyone poor enough to take it, and find no one at all.

And Adi, who lived past the age of a hundred and twenty, narrated this hadith himself as an old man, and added: I have seen two of these three with my own eyes. I saw the woman travel safe and alone from al-Hira to the Kabah, and I was among those who shared out the treasures of Kisra when we conquered his capital. As for the third, he said, by Allah it will surely come. Adi ibn Hatim accepted Islam.

What Hatim's son was told about his father

Adi came back to the Prophet ﷺ many times after that day, and on one of those visits he asked the question a loving son would ask. My father, he said, used to keep the ties of kinship, feed the people, and be generous to everyone. He was speaking of Hatim al-Tai, the very legend of Arab generosity, the name the desert still honored above all others. Will any of that benefit him? And the Prophet ﷺ gave an answer as gentle as it was exact, a single authentic sentence: your father wanted something, and he got what he wanted.

He wanted fame. He wanted to be praised, remembered, made a legend of generosity, and Allah gave him precisely that, to the letter, down to our own day, fourteen centuries on, when we still tell his stories. He got exactly what he was working for. Here Dr. Yasir Qadhi holds up a hard and clarifying teaching. The good that a person does purely for fame, or status, or the praise of people, is paid out to them in this world, in full, exactly as they wished it: the people have already paid the bill, and there is nothing waiting on the other side. It is only the deed done for the sake of Allah that is kept, treasured up for the next life. Allah says it plainly:

But whoever desires the Hereafter and exerts the effort due to it while he is a believer - it is those whose effort is ever appreciated [by Allāh].Qur'an 17:19

And there is nothing unjust in this, the Sheikh insists, nothing illogical at all. When a person never once acted for Allah, why should Allah reward him in the Hereafter? Let him collect, instead, the wage he was actually working for, and he will collect it, every coin. Many of the most beloved names in human history have already been paid in full, here, where they wanted to be paid.

The closing of a year

These were the last months of the eighth year, and the shape of a state was hardening out of what had been, only recently, a single besieged city. For the first time the Islamic polity was being organized along political lines: the Prophet ﷺ was appointing governors over regions, sending teachers to teach the Qur'an and the prayer, and dispatching collectors to gather the zakat, with one man sometimes carrying all three offices at once. And he kept sending out parties to tear down the idols that still stood. He sent Jarir against a great idol in the south called Dhul-Khalasa, a temple so brazen that its people had built a rival structure and called it a Kabah, to compete with the true one in Makkah; Jarir burned it to the ground. He was not at peace, the Sheikh notes, knowing idols were still worshipped in the Sacred Land, and he wanted idolatry gone from the Peninsula entirely. This is the work behind a verse that would come down within a few months:

O you who have believed, indeed the polytheists are unclean, so let them not approach al-Masjid al-Ḥarām after this, their [final] year. And if you fear privation, Allāh will enrich you from His bounty if He wills. Indeed, Allāh is Knowing and Wise.Qur'an 9:28

And inside his own household, a single year held everything a human life holds: a marriage, a divorce, a death, and a coming birth. The Sheikh raises the marriage and divorce only because it is well known to students of hadith and he refuses to be accused of hiding anything, while being honest that its real cause was simply never recorded, as such private matters rarely are. A woman the Prophet ﷺ had married sought refuge from him on the very first night, before the marriage was consummated; the words were grave, and he answered them with perfect dignity: you have sought refuge in One who is great, and sent her back to her family. He raises it, the Sheikh says, chiefly to break a stubborn myth, the claim that the Prophet ﷺ never divorced. He did, and there is no shame whatsoever in it. The majority of the Mothers of the Believers had been widowed or divorced before he married them, and the stigma our cultures pile onto divorce has no root in the Qur'an or the Sunnah. Sometimes two people simply do not suit one another, and that is not a sin.

But the death that year was the heaviest weight of all: Zaynab, his eldest daughter, radiyallahu anha. Years before, at the very time of the Hijra, she had been violently thrown from a camel and never recovered from the injury, and now, after eight long years of patient suffering, she passed away, around thirty-one years old. This was the daughter who had once sheltered her own husband, Abu al-As, with her own voice, standing up in the mosque to announce that she had granted him her protection, and the Prophet ﷺ, who had not known of it until that moment, honored her word and gave the man back to her. Three of the Prophet's ﷺ four daughters died during his own lifetime, and Zaynab was among them. A forming empire on the outside, governors and collectors and falling idols across the whole Peninsula; and on the inside, the ordinary, breaking griefs of a man who was, through all of it, a father burying his child.

What this day asks of our faith

Two strangers walked into Madinah in the same season, one carrying a death sentence and one carrying contempt, and the same Prophet ﷺ disarmed them both. It would be easy to read these as two charming conversion stories and move on, and that would be to miss what each is quietly asking of us.

Start with Ka'b, and with the brother who wrote the letter. Ka'b had done the very worst a poet of his world could do; by the standards of his society he was beyond reach, his life already forfeit. And the move that saved him was not a threat from an enemy but a kindness from the man he had most wronged, an open hand extended to a person who had earned only a closed fist. We tend to believe that those who have hurt us, or hurt what we love, are best met with hardness, that mercy is what you offer those who deserve it. The brother's letter says otherwise, and the Prophet's ﷺ let him be says otherwise, and the cloak off his own back says it loudest of all. No one's record is past the reach of a sincere return. Ask yourself who, in your own life, you have already written past forgiving, and whether you would have the largeness to draft that letter.

Then there is Adi, who ruled out a king before he had heard a single point of theology. He did it by watching: a man who stopped in a public street for a poor old woman while a chieftain waited, a man who sat on the bare ground so his guest could have the only mat. The argument that opened Adi's heart was not an argument at all. It was a character. People read who you are long before they weigh anything you say, and the most persuasive case you will ever make for what you believe is the way you treat the person who can do nothing for you. The Prophet ﷺ then found the real objection beneath the polite one, naming the worldly weakness Adi was secretly judging him by: people rarely tell you the true thing holding them back, and the patient, loving move is to find it and speak to it.

And underneath both stories runs the hardest teaching of the day, the one that came in the answer about Hatim. Your father wanted something, and he got what he wanted. The good we do for the eyes of people is real good, and it is fully paid, here, in admiration and the warmth of being well thought of, and then it is gone. Only what was done for Allah is kept. This is not a comfortable thought, because so much of what we do is done at least partly to be seen doing it, and the line between serving God and serving our own reputation runs straight through the middle of our best deeds. So sit, before this day closes, with the plainest possible question: of the good I did this week, how much would I still have wanted if no one had ever known? That portion, and only that portion, is being treasured up. The rest has already been spent.

So take three things from this season down into your days. Write the letter you think the other person has not earned, and leave the door of return open even for the one who slammed it. Let your character carry your faith further than your arguments ever will. And weigh, honestly and often, who you are really working for, so that what you build is kept and not merely paid. May Allah send His peace and blessings upon His Messenger ﷺ, the light from which guidance is sought, who pardoned the poet who came against him and clothed him in his own cloak, and who sat upon the ground so a guest could rest. O Allah, accept our repentance as You accepted the poet's, clothe us in the garment of taqwa as You clothed Your poet in his, and let everything we do be for You alone, so that it is kept for the Day we meet You.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 84: the conversion of Ka'b ibn Zuhayr (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (17:19, 9:28), verified via quran.ai.

The Seerah · 85 of 104

The miracles of the Prophet ﷺ

Over a thousand signs, and one that never ends

The story stops here for a night. Hunayn is behind them, the long road to Tabuk is ahead, and Ramadan is about to fold the lecture closed for the year, so Dr. Yasir Qadhi steps out of the timeline and asks a question that has been sitting silently under the whole seerah from the first verse in the cave of Hira. Not a battle tonight, not a treaty, not a delegation. Just this: what made people believe him ﷺ in the first place? What did Allah place in the hands of this one man that the rest of mankind could not match?

The Arabic word for it is mu'jizah, and the Sheikh begins by turning the word over in his hand, because its root is the surprise that frames everything that follows. The same root gives the word for the old, the frail, the one grown weak with age. A mu'jizah comes from weakness. It is named for incapacity. And the reason is exact: a miracle is precisely the thing that the rest of mankind is too weak to reproduce, an event that breaks past what people are accustomed to, granted at the hands of someone who claims to be a prophet, in order to prove the claim true. That is the whole definition. A supernatural occurrence, given to a man who says he is sent by Allah, that demonstrates he is.

And it is no accident in the life of one prophet. It is the sunnah of Allah across all of them. No prophet was ever sent without signs; carrying a miracle is part of what it means to be a prophet at all. So the question for this evening is not whether he ﷺ had them. The scholars who wrote whole books on this counted past a thousand documented wonders in his life, with one master of the science putting the soundly narrated ones at more than twelve hundred. The question is what they were for, and which of them was meant, in the end, for us. The Sheikh gathers them into ten categories and a handful from each, and he saves for last the one miracle you can still pick up with your own two hands.

A miracle was never the only proof

Before a single wonder, the Sheikh clears away a misunderstanding that has tripped people up for centuries, because if you do not clear it first, everything after it will be misread. Some have claimed that the one and only thing that proves a prophet is a miracle. It sounds reasonable until you test it, and the test is simple. If a miracle were the only proof, then every single person who ever believed must have believed because they saw one. So ask: did Abu Bakr, radiyallahu anhu, see a miracle before he believed? He did not. Did Umar? He did not. Did the majority of the early companions? They did not. Look around any masjid today at the converts sitting in it, and most of them came to the religion without ever witnessing a sign at all.

So the Sheikh draws a firm line through the claim. A miracle is one proof among several, not the only one. The character of the prophets proves them. The truthfulness of the way they live proves them. The weight and the content of the message they bring proves them. A sincere heart can be convinced by the dignity of a life and the beauty of a word, with no wonder in sight, and most believing hearts across history were convinced exactly that way. By the end of an evening of marvels you will be tempted to think that the miracle is the engine of faith. It is not. It is one door among many, and for most people it was never the door they walked through.

There is a second truth here, harder than the first, and the Qur'an states it without softening: miracles do not help the arrogant. They benefit two kinds of people only. The first are those who already believe, in whom a sign deepens certainty into something unshakable. The second are the open and the sincere, the ones who come honestly and say, prove it to me, show me, and then submit the moment they are shown. To a heart that has already decided to reject, no sign on earth is ever enough. Hold both of those truths in one hand as the wonders begin, the Sheikh says, because they are the lens through which the whole night must be read.

And listen to what the Quraysh actually demanded of him, because you will hear that the problem was never a shortage of proof. Make a spring gush out of the ground for us, they said. Grow us a garden of date palms and grapevines with rivers running through it. Drop the sky on us in pieces. Bring Allah and the angels to stand here in front of us. Build yourself a house of gold, or climb a ladder up into the heavens, and even then, they added, we will not believe in your climb until you bring back down a book out of the sky that we can hold in our hands and read for ourselves. The Qur'an records the demand and the answer it was given in the same breath:

And they say, "We will not believe you until you break open for us from the ground a spring Or [until] you have a garden of palm trees and grapes and make rivers gush forth within them in force [and abundance] Or you make the heaven fall upon us in fragments as you have claimed or you bring Allāh and the angels before [us] Or you have a house of ornament [i.e., gold] or you ascend into the sky. And [even then], we will not believe in your ascension until you bring down to us a book we may read." Say, "Exalted is my Lord! Was I ever but a human messenger?"Qur'an 17:90-93

One line of reply for the whole catalogue of their demands: glory be to my Lord, was I ever anything but a human messenger? He never claimed to be more. And Allah lays the arrogance underneath the demands completely bare. The trouble was never that the proof was too thin; it was that the heart had already shut.

And [even] if We opened to them a gate from the heaven and they continued therein to ascend, They would say, "Our eyes have only been dazzled. Rather, we are a people affected by magic."Qur'an 15:14-15

Give them the very thing they ask for, a gate of heaven thrown open in broad daylight, and let them spend the day climbing up through it, and they will come down and tell you their eyes were drugged, that the whole thing was a spell. The Sheikh says he has heard the modern translation of that verse with his own two ears, from anyone determined in advance not to believe. Show me God himself, they say, and then I will accept. It is the same demand the Quraysh made fourteen centuries ago, and the Qur'an answered it before it was ever spoken again.

Nothing in front of a closed heart

Why open a lecture about miracles by insisting, at length, that miracles sometimes do nothing at all? Because, the Sheikh says, by the end of the night you will hear wonder after wonder and you will want to ask: how could anyone witness all of this and still walk away? And the Qur'an has already answered the question before you can finish asking it. The verse he calls one of the most clear-cut in the whole Book on the subject of human arrogance:

And even if We had sent down to them the angels [with the message] and the dead spoke to them [of it] and We gathered together every [created] thing in front of them, they would not believe unless Allāh should will. But most of them, [of that], are ignorant.Qur'an 6:111

Send down the angels in ranks, let the dead climb out of their graves and testify, gather every created thing in all the world before their eyes, and a heart that has decided will still not move, not because the evidence ran short but because evidence was never the point. And the demand that exposes them is the oldest and the proudest one of all:

And those who do not expect the meeting with Us say, "Why were not angels sent down to us, or [why] do we [not] see our Lord?" They have certainly become arrogant within themselves and [become] insolent with great insolence.Qur'an 25:21

Notice who speaks that line, the Sheikh says: those who do not expect to meet their Lord, who do not actually care about religion in the first place. Show us our God, they say, as if they had the standing to set Him a summons. Allah names it for what it is, arrogance and insolence, and asks through them the question they should have been asking themselves: who do you imagine you are, that the signs all around you are not enough, and that some grander sign would somehow be?

So a question people ask about the seerah answers itself. Why were the Quraysh not simply shown more miracles, enough to overwhelm them? Because more would not have helped, and because more would have destroyed them. Allah does not deal in spectacle for its own sake; there is nothing of idle play in how He runs His creation. When the Quraysh swore they would believe if only the mountain were turned to gold before their eyes, the Sheikh relates, the Prophet ﷺ was offered exactly that, on a single condition. If the sign came and they still refused it, there would be no more reprieve; the matter would be sealed and they would be finished. And he ﷺ chose to leave them their time instead, the longer road of patience over the one decisive blow, and that choice, the Sheikh says, is itself a window into his mercy. It is also the reason most of the wonders you are about to meet unfolded in front of believers, and only a rare few in front of the men who came to mock. To pour signs onto arrogance is to pour out destruction, and Allah, out of mercy, gave the proud only enough to know the truth, and then let them be.

When the things of the earth answered him

The first category is the strangest to a modern ear and the gentlest in tone: the wonders worked through lifeless things, through stones and pebbles and food and wood. He ﷺ said that there was a stone in Makkah he still recognized, one that used to greet him with peace, give him salam, before he was ever given prophethood at all. The companions sitting close around him at a meal heard the food in his blessed hand say subhanallah as he lifted it toward his mouth. And once, in a gathering that held Abu Bakr and Umar and Ali among others, he took up a handful of gravel, small pebbles, and as they lay in his palm the whole company heard them praising Allah aloud. He passed the pebbles to Abu Bakr, and in his hands they went on praising. To Umar, and they kept praising. To the others, and still the sound continued, every man in the circle hearing it. Then he ﷺ set them down, and when the companions picked the same stones back up there was nothing, only silence in their hands.

Sit with what that scene is actually showing, because the Sheikh draws the meaning out and it is larger than the wonder. It is not that the Prophet ﷺ taught the stones to speak. It is that everything in creation is already glorifying its Maker, ceaselessly, all the time, and we are simply built so that we cannot hear it. There is nothing that does not praise Him. When his blessed hand closed around those pebbles, the veil between the people and the rocks lifted for a moment, and the companions heard what creation never once stops saying. Then he set them down, and the veil came back, and the praising went on exactly as before, only now beyond the reach of human ears again. You are sitting, this very moment, inside a worship you cannot hear, surrounded by it on every side, invited into it but largely deaf to it. The pebbles were not the lesson. The silence afterward was.

The trunk that wept for him

And here the Sheikh slows almost to a stop, because this one he lifts out of its category and gives a place of its own, for the sheer enormity of it. In the earliest days of Madinah, when the masjid was first built and some palm stumps had to be cleared to make room, one trunk was left standing near the front, and the Prophet ﷺ took to giving the Friday khutbah leaning against it. He stood there and spoke against that trunk for years, off to one side of the masjid, because you do not get to choose where a tree happens to grow. Then, a few years on, when some wealth had begun to come to the community, the companions asked his permission to build him a proper minbar, a pulpit of three steps, and one of the women of the Ansar lent the skilled carpenter among her household to carve it. Carpentry was a rare craft in that land. The new pulpit was set in the middle of the masjid, and the trunk was left behind at the side.

The first Friday he ﷺ climbed those new steps, more than a thousand people were packed into the masjid, shoulder to shoulder. And the whole congregation heard a sound. Not the creak of old wood: the crying of a camel. The loud, broken sobbing of a baby camel, rising from the abandoned trunk. The Prophet ﷺ came down from his new pulpit, walked across to the trunk he had stood against all those years, and put his arms around it, holding it and patting it the way you would gentle a weeping child, until the crying stopped. He said afterward that had he not gone to it and quieted it, it would have kept weeping until the Day of Judgment. Then he gave the command that the trunk be taken up and buried beneath the minbar, where the histories say it rests to this day, somewhere under the place where the imam stands.

The Sheikh stresses how certain this report is. It is not one of the gentle, loosely chained stories that the seerah carries alongside its firm ones; it is mutawatir, narrated by so many companions at once, more than a thousand eyewitnesses in that single packed room, that doubt has nowhere to stand. And he tells us that one of the early scholars could never recite this hadith without breaking down in tears, and would say, through his weeping: a tree cried because it missed the Prophet ﷺ and the knowledge that he used to spread leaning against it. And then the scholar's question, which the Sheikh hands straight to us: what, then, is the excuse of our hearts? We who never once sat in front of him, who never heard a single khutbah from those lips, and yet so often feel nothing at the mention of him at all. A trunk had more longing for him than we manage on our best days.

Water from his fingers, food that would not run out

By sheer count, the Sheikh says, the largest category of all is food and water, and the seerah is so thick with these that he can only gather a few. The shape of them is always the same: a little is set before a multitude, and the little becomes enough, and there is more left at the end than there was at the start. At the treaty of Hudaybiyyah the water ran out entirely, and the Prophet ﷺ put his hand down into the last of what remained in the vessel, and from between his fingers the water came welling up and flowing out, like a spring, like an opened tap, until in one narration more than three hundred people had made their wudu from it. Dr. Yasir Qadhi pauses on a verdict that one of the scholars of al-Andalus passed on this very wonder. It was, he said, a greater sign than Musa striking water from the rock. Because water does, on rare occasion, seep out of stone; you will find it now and then, even if only a trickle, and Musa was given it in abundance. But water has never once, anywhere, gushed out from flesh and bone. That is a thing the world had never seen and has not seen since.

And then the one that the whole city of Madinah stood witness to, on the day of the Trench. The men were starving as they dug, the great confederate army massed around them, and Jabir, radiyallahu anhu, had heard the weakness of hunger in the Prophet's ﷺ own voice. He went home to whatever his household could find, one small goat and a little flour, enough perhaps for a handful of people, and slaughtered the goat and set his wife to kneading the dough, meaning only to whisper a quiet invitation to the Prophet ﷺ and one or two of the companions. But the Prophet ﷺ rose and announced it to the entire trench: people, Jabir has made a feast for you all, come. Fourteen hundred starving men. Jabir ran home in a panic, certain he was ruined, and his wife asked him what he had done. The Prophet ﷺ had done it, he told her, not him. Then he ﷺ arrived, spat baraka into the dough and blessed the pot, and gave the instruction that the bread was not to be drawn from the oven, nor the meat lifted from the fire, until he was there to serve it. And then the army ate, batch after batch after batch, the company filing in and eating its fill and leaving, until every last man of the fourteen hundred had been fed. And when the last of them rose and left, there was more food in that house than when they had begun. Thousands in Madinah saw it.

The moon, split in two

The tenth and final category is the one the Sheikh reserves for everything that fits nowhere else, the miscellaneous, and into it falls one of the greatest signs ever granted to any prophet who ever lived. In the early years in Makkah, the Quraysh challenged him, one of the very few times a wonder came at their own daring. If I were to split the moon in two, he ﷺ asked them, would you believe in me? They said yes. And the moon split, the two halves drawing apart so that one stood on one side of the mountain and one on the other, in plain sight of them all, with the peak between the pieces. The Qur'an itself records it, without a trace of hedging:

The Hour has come near, and the moon has split [in two].Qur'an 54:1

Dr. Yasir Qadhi notes that an early scholar weighed this sign against all the others and ranked it at the very top, above the wonders of every prophet before. The reason is striking. Every other miracle, of every other messenger, took place down here, on the earth we know and walk and can reach. This one happened in the heavens, out beyond our world entirely, in a place no human hand will ever touch. That is what set it above the rest: it was worked in the sky itself, where none of us can go. And still, the Qur'an tells you in the very next breath exactly what the proud did with a sign hung in the heavens for all of them to see:

And if they see a sign [i.e., miracle], they turn away and say, "Passing magic."Qur'an 54:2

They looked up at the broken moon, turned their faces away, and called it a passing spell, magic that would soon wear off. Precisely as Allah had said the closed heart always would. The greatest sign in the sky, and arrogance reached for the same one word it always reaches for. The lesson the Sheikh opened the night with closes the loop here: it was never about the size of the proof.

The miracle still in your hands

And then the Sheikh saves the greatest for the very last, the one he says eclipses every other and is, all by itself, worth more than all the rest combined, infinitely more. To see why, look at what the wonders of this whole evening have in common. The stone that gave salam, the pebbles that praised, the trunk that wept, the water from his fingers, the army fed from one goat, the moon broken over the mountain: every single one of them was witnessed by other people, in another time and place. We believe in them, and we are right to. But we did not see them. We were not standing in that masjid, not kneeling at that well, not looking up at that sky. They lit a moment and then they passed, and the moment is gone.

There is one exception, and only one. When a person comes and asks how he can possibly know that this religion is true, there is a single miracle you can physically place into his hands and say: here. Read this. Listen to its recitation. Sit with what it says. The Prophet ﷺ said that every prophet before him was given signs by which his people came to believe in him, and that the sign he himself was given was the revelation, the Qur'an, and that on account of it he hoped to have the largest following of any prophet on the Day of Judgment. After a whole evening of wonders, the Sheikh lingers on the wording: the only miracle Allah gave me. Clearly he ﷺ was given thousands. But the miracle of the Qur'an is so bright that beside it the others seem almost to fade from view, the way the others recede in front of this one. It has no expiry and no border. It carries no constraint of time or of place. It is the living miracle, renewed every time a child opens his mouth to recite it, and it is the one sign a Muslim can still hold out to a doubter today, when every other wonder can only be reported, never shown. And so the Sheikh closes the evening on the line he has been walking toward all along: in truth, a Muslim needs no miracle beyond the Qur'an, and beyond the life of the Prophet ﷺ itself, for his life is miracle enough.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to leave an evening like this one merely amazed, a parade of wonders admired and then set down, and to walk away no different than you came. That would be to miss every question the night is actually putting to you. The Sheikh framed the whole hour so that the wonders would point past themselves, and they point straight at us.

Begin where he began, because it is the most freeing thing in the lecture. Proof, by itself, rarely moves a heart that has decided. Allah says it in His own words: send the angels down, let the dead speak, gather every created thing before their eyes, and the arrogant will still refuse. So stop waiting for the one perfect argument that will finally crack someone open, and stop tormenting yourself that you must not have found it yet. Faith lands on the humble and slides off the proud, and that has been true since the Quraysh stood looking at a moon in two pieces and called it a trick of the eye. Carry the truth honestly and leave the hearts to the One who turns them.

And remember, against your own restless craving for a sign, that belief was never mainly about miracles in the first place. Abu Bakr believed without one. Umar believed without one. The truthfulness of his ﷺ life, the dignity of his character, the weight of what he brought, that was enough for the best of this ummah, and it is a door still standing open to anyone sincere. If your own iman feels thin, the answer is almost never to go hunting for a wonder. It is to sit closer to the life and the word that convinced the companions, until they convince you the same way.

Then let the smaller wonders do their quiet work on you. The pebbles praised in his palm because everything is already praising, always, and we are simply too deaf to hear it; you are standing this moment inside a worship the whole creation is offering, and you were invited to join it out loud. And the trunk, the trunk above all. A piece of dead wood wept because it missed him ﷺ and the remembrance he spread while leaning against it, and the scholar who could not recite that report without crying asked the only question that matters: what excuse, then, do our hearts have? We can still pick up his words. We can still recite what he recited, still walk where his sunnah walks. A tree longed for him with more feeling than we manage, and we have so much less reason to have grown cold. Let that shame you, the Sheikh would say, but gently, the way he ﷺ patted the trunk until it stilled, not into despair but back toward warmth.

And then hold, with both hands, the one gift that was meant for you and not for them. The moon split for a generation that is gone; the water ran from his fingers for men who have long since met their Lord. You were not there for any of it. But the Qur'an is in your home tonight. It is the living miracle, the one sign that did not fade with the eyes that first saw it, the one you can open right now and the one you can place in a doubter's hands and say, simply, read this. It deserves more than a high shelf and a month of attention once a year. So take one thing from this evening of wonders down into your ordinary days: let your eyes see the signs the proud were blind to, in the praising of the stones and the patience of the Prophet ﷺ and the mercy that gave the arrogant their time. And let your heart settle, finally, on the miracle that never ended. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, the one whose very life is sign enough, keep our hearts firm upon His Book, count us among those whom the Prophet ﷺ will recognize and call to his fountain, and let us drink from his hand a drink after which there is no thirst.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 85: the miracles of the Prophet ﷺ (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (17:90-93, 15:14-15, 6:111, 25:21, 54:1-2), verified via quran.ai. The narration is the Sheikh's, the phrasing is Buruja's.

The Seerah · 86 of 104

Lessons from Hunayn and Ta'if

What the largest victory came to teach

Some battles are won so completely that the danger only arrives afterward. After three and a half months away from the seerah, Dr. Yasir Qadhi comes back not to push the story forward but to circle back over ground he feels was crossed too quickly. We were rushed, he admits, through Hunayn and Ta'if, and there were points of benefit left on the field. So this is not a chapter of events. It is a chapter of wisdom mined out of events, and the wisdom turns out to be sharper than the fighting.

Set the scene again. Makkah fell in Ramadan of the eighth year of the Hijrah, and one by one the great names of Quraysh came in, Abu Sufyan, Suhayl ibn Amr, Ikrimah ibn Abi Jahl, slowly at first and then all of them. The Prophet ﷺ broke the idols of Makkah and of the towns around it, and then word reached him of an offensive: the proudest tribe left standing after Quraysh, the people of Thaqif in their mountain city of Ta'if, had allied with their cousins of Hawazin and marched out more than twenty thousand strong to crush the Muslims first. The two armies met at Hunayn, and the day opened in disaster. The new converts, fresh from Makkah and untested, broke and fled at the first shock, and the Prophet ﷺ held his ground with the senior companions until the line re-formed and the rout swung the other way. The Hawazin men ran and left everything behind, their wives and children and herds and wealth, the single largest body of spoils in the entire history of the seerah, because their young commander had overruled his elders and dragged the whole tribe, families and property and all, onto the battlefield. The army then marched on Ta'if and laid siege, and Ta'if did not break. The Prophet ﷺ lifted the siege and turned back toward Madinah, and over the people who had defied him he said only this: Allah will guide them. They will come. Within a year they did. Now go back, the Sheikh says, and let the battle teach.

Where you put your trust

The Qur'an names only two battles by their own names, Badr and Hunayn, and it names Hunayn not to celebrate it but to correct it.

Allah has already given you victory in many regions and [even] on the day of Hunayn, when your great number pleased you, but it did not avail you at all, and the earth was confining for you with [i.e., in spite of] its vastness; then you turned back, fleeing.Qur'an 9:25

For the first time in the whole struggle the Muslims were the larger army, and their numbers pleased them, and in that quiet pleasure something slipped. The confidence of the host rested, for a moment, on its own size rather than on the One who hands out victory. So the earth, for all its width, closed in on them, and they ran. The verse names the cause without mercy: a heart that leaned, briefly, on the wrong thing.

Sheikh Yasir lifts this clean out of the seerah and sets it in the center of theology, because it is the lesson lying underneath all the others. Trust, real trust, must rest in the Causer of causes and not in the cause itself. Allah made causes real, and to neglect them is foolishness, not faith; the Prophet ﷺ gave the whole balance in a single line: tie your camel, then put your trust in Allah. You tie the camel. But the heart does not cling to the rope. Lean your heart on the alarm system itself, on the salary, on your own qualifications, as though these were what truly kept you, and you have made the means into a small idol. This is not the major shirk that takes a person out of Islam, for no Muslim worships his alarm; it is the hidden, lesser shirk of a heart that has quietly moved its reliance from God to the thing God gave it. And like the numbers at Hunayn, the thing will not save you when you need saving.

Then he draws a distinction with real tenderness in it. There are two kinds of causes, physical and spiritual. A physical cause is one any human being uses regardless of faith: medicine, strength, money, the alarm on the wall. A spiritual cause is supernatural and tied to a belief: for the believer, du'a and the Qur'an. Your heart must not attach itself to a physical cause, because that goes against tawhid. But it may lean fully on a legitimate spiritual cause, because a spiritual cause is by its nature a connection to Allah, and to lean on it is to lean on Him. So the believer works the world with both hands and keeps his heart looking upward. That, the Sheikh says, is the precise thing Hunayn was sent to teach, and he calls it what it is: not seerah anymore, but aqidah.

Mercy for the one who does not know

On the road out to Hunayn the army passed a great spreading tree that the pagan Arabs had once revered, hanging their weapons on its branches for luck, as though the wood would carry them to victory. A man called Abu Waqid al-Laythi, a Muslim of perhaps a single day, who had not yet memorized a verse or sat in one lesson, called out: O Messenger of Allah, make for us a tree like that one, a Dhat Anwat of our own, the way we used to have in the days of ignorance. And the Prophet ﷺ swore by Allah that this was the very thing the Children of Israel had said to Musa, when he had just brought them through the sea and they passed a people worshipping idols: make for us a god as they have gods. It was major shirk, in plain words, the request for an idol from the lips of a man who days earlier had been a pagan.

And yet watch what he ﷺ did not do. He did not tell Abu Waqid to renew his shahada, did not declare him fallen out of the faith. He corrected him and he taught him, because the man was new and simply did not know. There is a root of faith, asl al-iman, the genuine wish to submit to Allah and His Messenger, and it can sit in a heart still tangled with deep ignorance. A person who has that root, who truly wants to submit but does not yet know what submission forbids, may be excused by Allah for what he says out of that ignorance, even when the thing he says is itself shirk. It is the exact inverse of the refusal of Iblis, who understood perfectly and would not bend: asl al-kufr is to refuse to submit, knowing; asl al-iman is to want to submit, not yet knowing to what.

He brings it straight into our own streets, and names the cases without flinching, because a sanitized faith is no help to anyone. A young Muslim raised far from knowledge says that anyone good will enter Paradise, no matter what they believe, that every path leads to Jannah. To say this, the Sheikh insists, is kufr, because it denies the kalima from both ends: it imagines gods who may be worshipped beside Allah, and salvation for those who reject His Messenger ﷺ. To declare the forbidden permitted is itself disbelief, even though to fall into the forbidden is merely sin: to drink is haram, but to say drinking is fine is kufr. The error is grave and he will not shrink the word for it. But the person in front of you, raised in ignorance and meaning no rebellion, is not to be hurled out of Islam. He is owed exactly what Abu Waqid was owed: the error named honestly, and then patience, wisdom, and good manners in the teaching. To the elders quick to excommunicate, the Sheikh says: calm down. Yes, it is kufr. And no, you do not answer it by telling a confused believer he is no longer a believer.

The night the Ansar wept

When the spoils of Hunayn were divided, the Prophet ﷺ poured them onto the newest Muslims of Makkah. A hundred camels here, ten thousand somewhere else, fortunes by the million in the reckoning of that world, until men who had fought him for twenty years walked away overnight as the wealthy elite. Abu Sufyan, al-Aqra ibn Habis, the chieftains of Quraysh, became rich beyond anything they had owned. And the Ansar, the men of Madinah who had sheltered Islam for years and bled for it and defended the Messenger of Allah ﷺ with their bodies, were given nothing. Not a single coin.

And they felt it. The Sheikh asks you to put yourself among them honestly, because any one of us, he says, would have lost our minds: twenty years of enmity rewarded with fortunes, and the ones who carried the religion on their backs not even called. Word moved through the Ansar with a dangerous edge: when there is fighting to be done we are the first on the line, but when the wealth is handed out, he gives it to his own people, the Quraysh. To be disappointed in the Messenger of Allah ﷺ is in itself something close to kufr, and it shows you how human this moment was. Then the Sheikh plants his feet and refuses to look away from it, because, he says, we do the companions a quiet violence when we turn them into angels. Make the Sahaba into angels and they can no longer be our role models, for they are no longer of our kind. They were the best generation of human beings, kuntum khaira ummatin ukhrijat lin nas, and they were human beings.

You are the best nation produced [as an example] for mankind. You enjoin what is right and forbid what is wrong and believe in Allah. If only the People of the Scripture had believed, it would have been better for them. Among them are believers, but most of them are defiantly disobedient.Qur'an 3:110

They wanted what you and I want. The greatness was never that they felt nothing; it was that they could be reminded, and would turn. So the Prophet ﷺ called them into a tent, alone, every non-Ansari sent away, and spoke to their hearts. Did I not come to you when you were astray, and Allah guided you through me; poor, and He enriched you; divided against one another, and He joined your hearts? Then he set the choice before them: are you not pleased that the people return to their homes with sheep and camels, and you return to your homes with the Messenger of Allah ﷺ among you? And he gave them the words the Sheikh lingers over: I am one of you. Were it not for the Hijrah, that I was born where I was born, I would be one of the Ansar in truth; and if all mankind walked down one valley and the Ansar walked down another, I would walk the valley of the Ansar. It was exactly what they needed to hear. They wept until their beards were wet and begged his forgiveness. The waswasa had come, and the faith had answered it. That, the Sheikh says, is the very believer the Prophet ﷺ described: the believer can be weak, but when he is reminded, he remembers.

Reading each person where they stand

Watch how differently he ﷺ treated three groups inside one short campaign, because the difference is not favoritism but the religion's own common sense. To the Ansar, secure in their faith, he gave no money at all and a private speech that broke them open. To the new chieftains of Quraysh, whose hearts he was still binding to Islam, he gave fortunes. And to the rough Bedouins who came afterward demanding their share, he gave only tokens until the wealth ran out entirely.

On the battlefield itself he reached for a tool he had never used before and would never use again. When the Quraysh converts wavered and fled, he did not call them back with a verse. He called on the one memory that would move these particular men: I am the Prophet, this is no lie; I am the son of Abdul Muttalib. He invoked his lineage. And the Sheikh marks the tension in it, because the Prophet ﷺ himself warned that four matters of ignorance would cling to his Ummah, and the first he named was pride in ancestry. So he is not boasting, for he cannot disobey what he himself taught. He is reading them. He is true: he is the grandson of Abdul Muttalib, their own legendary chieftain, and for these men in this instant the name of that chieftain was the rope that would pull them back. Notice the order: I am the Prophet comes first, and only then I am the son of Abdul Muttalib. The very same wisdom governs how a believer handles pride of nation or origin today; there is nothing inherently wrong in belonging to a land and a culture, and a thing that can curdle into sin can, used rightly, become a bridge.

And then the human reality of it cut the other way. Later, pressed by a throng of Bedouins shouting for money, money, money, he was forced back into a stand of thorny desert shrubs until his cloak was torn from his shoulders and fell to the ground. The companions did not shield him as bodyguards, because these were Muslims asking for what was theirs. He said: give me back my cloak. By Allah, if I had coins as many as these thorns I would share them all among you, and you would not find me a miser. He was a man pressed and frustrated, and entirely just in it, with every right to treat this crowd differently from the dignitaries of Quraysh. Aisha, radiyallahu anha, preserved the command beneath all of it in the very first hadith of the introduction to Sahih Muslim: the Messenger of Allah ﷺ ordered us to place people in their proper stations. You honor the man of standing as a man of standing and answer the rude as the rude have earned, and the naive notion that everyone must be treated identically is not the Sunnah.

When to forgive, when to call down justice

At the walls of Ta'if, worn down by a siege that had dragged on for weeks and cost Muslim lives, the companions came to him with a request: O Messenger of Allah, make du'a against Thaqif. They were not inventing some strange new thing. These companions had seen him pray against people before: against the confederates when the Asr prayer was delayed at the Trench; against the men who once threw the filth of a slaughtered animal upon his back, every one of them later killed at Badr; against the tribes who betrayed and slaughtered his teachers at ar-Raji' and Bi'r Ma'unah, a full month of qunut raised against them. They knew the door was open, and were asking him to open it again.

But over Ta'if he chose mercy: O Allah, guide Thaqif, and bring them to us. And the Sheikh uses the contrast to clear away a soft modern myth, that a Muslim may never speak a du'a against anyone. He is, by his own description, no apologist, because a false picture of our tradition only sets up a crisis of faith for the day a reader meets the real one. The Qur'an itself is full of supplication against the wrongdoer. Musa prayed against Pharaoh; Nuh prayed against his people. It is true that the tender-hearted prophets, Ibrahim and Isa, prayed for their misguided peoples, and both responses are genuinely prophetic; Nuh and Musa were of the sterner cast, as Umar was beside Abu Bakr. The general rule leans toward mercy, and at Ta'if mercy won the day. But against a true tyrant, a Pharaoh of our own age drowning the innocent, it is not merely permitted to pray for his downfall, it can be the more fitting prayer of the two. The Prophet ﷺ taught that the supplication of the wronged rises to Allah with no veil between it and Him, even against a fellow Muslim who has stolen your wealth or knowingly slandered your name. If that door is open against a wrongdoing brother, how much more against the open tyrant. Honesty here protects the believer; it does not endanger him.

And yet mercy was also the verdict where strict justice could have been demanded. There was a real crime at Hunayn: to flee the battlefield is counted among the seven deadly sins the Prophet ﷺ named, and whole groups of the new Muslims had done exactly that at the first clash. So when the dust settled, Umm Sulaym, radiyallahu anha, was furious. She had been among the small band that held firm, a woman with no sword who pulled a hand-dagger from her belt and vowed to drive it into the stomach of anyone who came near, and the Prophet ﷺ had seen her do it. Now she came hot with anger at the men who had run: O Messenger of Allah, execute these cowards who fled. And he answered her with a sentence that is itself a principle of leadership: O Umm Sulaym, Allah has sufficed us and been good to us. They had sinned, yes, gravely. But the day was won, the danger was past, and what would be gained by killing a thousand men over a battle already decided? Let it be. The past is the past. There is a knowing, the Sheikh observes, that recognizes when an offense, however genuine, is better buried than avenged, and the strength to choose the burying.

How he won the hearts of his enemies

Now the masterstroke, and it is pure reading of the human heart. Hawazin, unlike Thaqif who had run back behind the walls of Ta'if with many of their families saved, had lost everyone: every wife and child captured, every herd taken, the whole tribe scattered with nothing left. The Prophet ﷺ judged that a people stripped that bare would have to come back and negotiate, so he held their captives and property at the valley of Ji'ranah, set two hundred men to guard them, and delayed the division as long as he could, marching to Ta'if and back while he waited. But Hawazin delayed too, and a commander cannot hold an army's spoils forever. At last he distributed the people and the property among the companions, and only then, days later, did Hawazin arrive as Muslims, asking for their families back.

He could not simply unwind it. A man handed ten thousand the day before, or a captive worth a fortune, does not gladly give it back the next morning; the attachment is there, and the wealth is lawfully his. So he managed it with a delicacy the Sheikh calls human psychology in its finest form. Which is dearer to you, he asked them, your families or your wealth? Their families, of course; was it even a question? He had known they would say it. Then he taught them the move. Tomorrow, after the prayer, when ten thousand hearts are still soft from worship, stand and announce that you have accepted Islam, and ask me to intercede with the Muslims, and ask the Muslims to intercede with me, to return your children. The right time, the soft hour after salah, an appeal to the heart and not a command, because he did not wish to command at all; the wealth was the people's right, and to order them to surrender it would be neither just nor likely to move them to generosity. He wanted them to give freely, so he arranged the moment in which giving freely would feel natural.

When they made their plea, he rose at once: as for what belongs to me and to the family of Abdul Muttalib, it is returned to you. The Muhajirun called out that theirs too was given back, for the sake of Allah, and the Ansar stood and said the same. Some of the new converts, within their rights, wanted payment, and he did not shame them; he promised them the first share of the next spoils, until every captive was ransomed home. And then he asked after their leader, the very Malik ibn Awf who had instigated the war and overruled his elders to drag the families to the field. He had fled into Ta'if. Tell him, the Prophet ﷺ said, that if he comes to me as a Muslim I will return his family and his wealth, and give him a hundred camels on top of it. For a man who had lost his tribe, his family, his fortune, and his rank, it was no choice at all. Malik came, took his shahada, and was reinstated as chief of his own tribe. The man who lit the fire went home a Muslim leader of his own people, everything restored to him but the idols.

The Sheikh lets the wonder of it land. What other story in the history of the world tells of an enemy who attacks you, loses, and is sent home with his family, his wealth, his rank, and a religion? It was not the first time, either; he recalls Banu al-Harith and the kin of Maymunah, prisoners freed and restored in the same way. One of the new chieftains, al-Aqra ibn Habis, went back to his people and said it plainly: by Allah, this is truly a Prophet, for no king would give away all he had and care nothing for poverty. That is what generosity does; it was kindness, not the sword, that was carrying Arabia into Islam, because Islam is the truth and however a heart is brought near it, the truth seeps in. Their faith might be weak now, not yet entered into their hearts, but it would enter, and their descendants are the righteous Muslims of every land since.

The bedouins say, "We have believed." Say, "You have not [yet] believed; but say [instead], 'We have submitted,' for faith has not yet entered your hearts. And if you obey Allah and His Messenger, He will not deprive you from your deeds of anything. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful."Qur'an 49:14

And folded inside the mercy is a quieter wisdom the Sheikh asks you not to miss: he gave Hawazin back their own man as their leader. People follow a leader of their own kind, who knows their lineage and their ways, and who is better fit to rule a people than the one who has ruled them already. So the chieftain of Jahiliyyah was handed back his chieftaincy, made the leader of the very tribe he had led before, with nothing changed but that the idols were gone and the people were Muslim.

What the largest victory leaves us

Behind every prisoner of war at Ji'ranah stood a long and uncomfortable question, the question of bondage, riqq, and the Sheikh meets it head on, because honesty is the only thing that protects faith. He will not even use the English word slavery, because the chattel horror that word conjures, one of the worst manifestations the institution ever knew anywhere, was never the thing the Sharia regulated. Bondage was a universal practice that no civilization before Islam had placed a single law upon. Islam was the first to fence it in: narrowing its only lawful source to the captive of a legitimate war who is not ransomed, where every other culture seized free men off the road and sold them; commanding that the bondsman be fed and clothed as your own brother, until often you could not tell servant from master; and opening avenue after avenue out of it, as the expiation for broken oaths, as one of the eight lawful uses of zakat, as one of the surest deeds by which a believer ransoms his own neck from the Fire. It was a framework, the Sheikh stresses, that governed the institution wherever it existed yet never required it to exist; remove it entirely and the Sharia stands whole, which is why he knows of no scholar today who calls for its return. And the Prophet ﷺ himself never kept a single bondsman as a personal servant. Every one he had, he freed, and many, once freed, simply stayed and served him for love.

The result of it all, Hunayn and Ta'if together, was decisive in a way no plan of men could engineer. The whole of the Hijaz was now Muslim; idolatry was gone from public life, and Ta'if was a small island of paganism in an ocean of tawhid that would surrender within months. Hunayn, the Sheikh says, was the last battle Islam would ever fight against the idolaters of Arabia.
