After it the campaigns turn outward, toward Rome and Persia and Egypt; within the Peninsula, Arab idolatry simply ceased to exist, less than a year from the conquest of Makkah, after more than two decades of struggle. A whole land cleansed of shirk inside a single generation. No human plan does that; this was a miracle no human could bring about, and our Lord brought it about. Among the last of the great names to come in was the most famous poet alive in all of Arabia, Ka'b ibn Zuhayr, whose celebrated ode opens, as the old Arabian poems always did, with long verses of love and longing for a woman named Su'ad. The Prophet ﷺ let those verses stand uncorrected, though elsewhere he had stopped a poet mid-line when the words strayed into kufr, telling young girls singing on Eid to drop the verse that claimed a prophet knows the unseen of tomorrow. There is wisdom even in that restraint: a thing need not be perfectly sanitized before it may be left alone, and the poem climbed in the end to its famous praise of him ﷺ as a light by which the world is lit and a drawn sword among the swords of Allah.

What this day asks of our faith

A recap is never only a recap. Going back over a battle long since won, the Sheikh has handed us a set of questions sharp enough to cut into an ordinary week, and the largest victory of the seerah turns out to have come wrapped in the hardest lessons of it.

Begin where the verse begins, with the numbers that pleased them. We are forever doing the same arithmetic, counting the salary, the health, the qualifications, and feeling safe in the count. Hunayn is the day the count failed. Use every lawful means with both hands and neglect none of them, for neglect is not faith; but do not let the heart move in to live with the means. Let it lean only upward, on the One who makes the means work and can unmake them in an instant, and keep for your du'a and your Qur'an the reliance you withhold from the medicine.

Then sit with how human the best of them were. We flatten the companions into angels and then wonder why they cannot reach us, when their whole value as guides is that they wanted what we want and were pulled where we are pulled. The Ansar felt the sting of being passed over, and the marvel was not that the feeling never came but that a single honest reminder turned it to tears. Ask which reminder you have been refusing to hear. And learn from how he answered them, and from Abu Waqid, and from the crowd in the thorns, that wisdom is not the flat machinery of treating everyone the same; it is reading the soul in front of you and placing it in its proper station, fortunes for one heart, a private word for another, patience for the ignorant.

And carry out of this day the strangest of its lessons, what to do with an enemy at your feet. He returned the families, the wealth, even the rank, and sent the man who lit the fire home as a Muslim chief, and a watching enemy looked at that and said, this can only be a Prophet. Generosity carried a whole peninsula into Islam where the sword alone never could, and the believer who can forgive a closed offense and let the past stay past has laid hold of the very tool the Messenger of Allah ﷺ used to win the hearts of those who once raised their hands against him. So take one thing down into your ordinary days: put your trust above the gifts and never in them, let yourself be reminded and then turn, read the people God places before you, and reach for mercy before you reach for the debt. May Allah make our trust rest in Him and not in what He gives us, soften our hearts the moment we are reminded, and let us treat His servants with the mercy our Prophet ﷺ showed even to those who had come to kill him.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 86: a recap and the lessons of Hunayn and Ta'if (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (9:25, 3:110, 49:14), verified via quran.ai.

The Seerah · 87 of 104

The Expedition of Tabuk, Part 1

The army of hardship, and the men who gave everything

Hunayn is behind him. Ta'if is behind him. In the six months since he ﷺ withdrew from the walls of Ta'if, central Arabia has fallen almost entirely into his hands, and the small tribes that still held out have begun to come in. He did not march on them himself; he sent the companions out in seven or eight small expeditions to the lands around Makkah and Madinah, partly to bring in the last of the tribes, and partly to pull down the great public idols, Dhul-Kaffayn and al-Fuls and others, that still stood in the open. And then, into the hottest part of the year, with the harvest about to come in and no enemy massing on any horizon that anyone could see, the order came: march north. A thousand miles, on foot and on thirsty camels, through the desert in the heat of late July.

This is the last campaign the Prophet ﷺ would ever lead. There would be no ghazwah after it; from here the road runs only to his farewell Hajj, to his final illness, and to the morning the companions would have to carry Islam without him. And they would remember this expedition by a name heavier than any victory. Not the expedition of Tabuk. The army of hardship, jaysh al-usrah.

The idols and a caution for our times

Before the road north opens, Dr. Yasir Qadhi pauses on those months of idol-breaking, because they are seized on in our own day and turned into something they were never meant to be. Notice, he says, that the Prophet ﷺ did not pull down a single public idol until he held political authority over the land. He did not begin his dawah twenty years earlier by smashing the gods around the Kaaba; he began with the call to Allah, and only after the conquest of Makkah, only after central Arabia was wholly his, did the destruction of the public idols begin. The ones torn down were public icons of shirk, idols in the open square, not the private temples of a settled community.

And there is a second caution folded into the first. By the consensus of the scholars the Jews and the Christians, and by the ruling of Umar ibn al-Khattab the Zoroastrians too, were always permitted to keep their faith and their houses of worship inside the Islamic state. The history bears this out: across the Umayyads and the Abbasids and the Ottomans, the most unexpected minorities survived for centuries inside Muslim lands, tolerated so long as they kept the peace and did not parade their shirk in the public square. The Sheikh is blunt about why he lingers here. The groups in our own time who lift these incidents to justify destroying whole peoples or sites have torn them from their context: they have neither the authority the Prophet ﷺ had, nor the careful limits he kept. The seerah does not license them. It refutes them.

Why march at all?

Here is the strange thing, and the Sheikh is honest that after teaching the seerah many times he has never found a clean answer: there was no obvious reason to go. The classical historians offer causes, and he weighs them one by one, and most of them fall apart in the hand. A report that the Jews of Madinah goaded the Prophet ﷺ northward to be rid of him, telling him a true prophet would have to go up to Syria, the land of the prophets and of the gathering on Judgment Day? It cannot stand. There were no Jews left in Madinah by the ninth year. And the verse cited for it, in Surah al-Isra, is a Makkan verse, revealed years before Tabuk was ever conceived; it has nothing to do with this campaign.

A report that forty thousand Roman troops were marching south to crush the Muslims, summoned by the Christian Ghassanid chief who had heard a false rumor that the Prophet ﷺ was dead? The narration is shaky, and the logic worse. The Romans, who ruled millions, had no interest in a desert they could have swallowed any time in the last three or four centuries. They cared nothing for the Arabs and saw in them no threat. To imagine them sending a legion down into that heat is, the Sheikh says plainly, nonsensical.

What lingers is a smaller, realer fear. The Ghassanids, the Christian Arab tribe on the Roman frontier, had spilled Muslim blood before; they had killed an envoy, and they had a hand in the deaths at Mu'tah, where Ja'far fell. The fear of them was genuine. The Sheikh recalls the day the Prophet ﷺ withdrew from his wives for a month and the whole city panicked, and a man burst in on Umar with the words "something has happened," and Umar's very first guess was: has the king of the Ghassanids attacked? That reflex tells you the threat was real in their minds. But even this does not answer the only question that matters, and the Sheikh keeps returning to it like a man pressing on a sore tooth: why now? Why in July, when no army on earth, Ghassanid or Roman, would come down through that furnace? Revenge could wait for January. The harvest did not have to be abandoned. Nothing in the visible world demanded that the march happen in the worst month of the year.

And so the Sheikh lands where the story itself pushes him, and he offers it as his own conclusion, take it or leave it: there was no immediate threat. This was a command from Allah, given plainly, to test these companions before their Prophet ﷺ would leave them.

Two more things point that way. The first is how the Prophet ﷺ went about it. Every other campaign he had kept under a veil, sometimes even feinting in the wrong direction to keep his enemies guessing, so that the blow fell before they knew it was coming. Tabuk he announced. He sent, al-Waqidi tells us, messengers to the surrounding tribes and even back to Makkah, calling the able-bodied men out by name. No surprise, no concealment, an open and declared march that the whole of Arabia could watch assembling. A man does not advertise an ambush. He advertises a test. And the second is a verse:

O you who have believed, fight against those adjacent to you of the disbelievers and let them find in you harshness. And know that Allah is with the righteous.Qur'an 9:123

The earliest commentator we possess, at-Tabari, read this as the very instruction that turned the Muslims first against the Arabs nearest them, and then, once Arabia was settled, northward toward Rome. Fight those adjacent to you, those who ring you about, and let them find in you firmness. It reads, the Sheikh suggests, almost like a map drawn in advance: the central Arabs are conquered, the rest will follow on their own, and now the gaze of the young ummah lifts past the desert to the frontier in the north, where the Ghassanids sit in the shadow of the Romans. The danger of the Ghassanids was never really the Ghassanids; it was the empire behind them. The Prophet ﷺ understood the verse exactly as Allah intended it, and Tabuk was the first step of that turning. He reads it all against Surah at-Tawbah, the surah revealed almost entirely around this expedition, so blunt and so relentless in its call to march that one of its very names is Surat al-Qital, the surah of fighting. Allah was forging the men who would carry Islam out of Arabia after the Prophet ﷺ was gone. The test came first. Everything that the ummah became flowed from whether they passed it.

Go forth, light or heavy

Read Surah at-Tawbah with Tabuk in mind, the Sheikh asks, and you will feel an urgency you find nowhere else in the Qur'an. This was no quiet, voluntary call like the early battles. Going was fard ayn, an obligation on every healthy adult man around Madinah, even the new Muslims of Makkah, excused only by genuine sickness or a hardship that could not be moved. And the verses that came down do not coax. They confront.

O you who have believed, what is [the matter] with you that, when you are told to go forth in the cause of Allah, you adhere heavily to the earth? Are you satisfied with the life of this world rather than the Hereafter? But what is the enjoyment of worldly life compared to the Hereafter except a [very] little.Qur'an 9:38

What is wrong with you, the verse asks, that when you are called to march you sink into the ground, heavy, clinging to home? And the warning that follows is sharper still, that if they will not go forth Allah will punish them and put a different people in their place, and they will not harm Him in the least:

If you do not go forth, He will punish you with a painful punishment and will replace you with another people, and you will not harm Him at all. And Allah is over all things competent.Qur'an 9:39

And then the line that leaves no man an exit, whatever his state, however little he has:

Go forth, whether light or heavy, and strive with your wealth and your lives in the cause of Allah. That is better for you, if you only knew.Qur'an 9:41

Light or heavy: whether you ride out fully armed or with nothing at all, whether the going is easy on you or it costs you everything, go. And here the Sheikh stops you, because these are exactly the verses the extremist groups love to lift and aim at whatever cause they please, cutting and pasting them as though they were sent down for their own war. The verse, he insists, belongs to the moment it descended. Allah revealed this for Tabuk. The words are eternal and there will be a thousand causes across the centuries where they carry weight, but no one gets to staple this specific command onto his own banner and tell his followers that if they do not march with him Allah will punish them. That kind of specificity belongs only to the battle the verse came down for. To pretend otherwise is to forge a revelation in your own name.

The army of hardship

There was never a battle at Tabuk. No clash of armies, no swords drawn against swords, not a drop of blood spilled in anger. Imam al-Bukhari, when he reaches this campaign in his book of the seerah, gives it both of its names in a single heading: the expedition of Tabuk, and it is the expedition of hardship. The companions, the Sheikh notes, called it by that second name far more often than the first. And they were right to, because the enemy this time was not an army. It was the road itself.

Count the weights stacked on these men. It would be the largest force Arabia had ever raised. The sources reach for twenty thousand, even thirty, and the Sheikh, ever careful, reminds us that crowds always swell in the retelling. It is human nature, he says, not lying: you glance at a great gathering and the zeros multiply if the crowd is on your side, and they fall away if it is not, and the companions kept no roster and counted no heads. Twenty thousand may well be too large for that time and place. Call it simply the biggest army the Prophet ﷺ ever led. A larger army means more mouths, more water, more of everything, carried across more distance, for this was the farthest he ﷺ ever marched at the head of men, close to a thousand miles, and a journey there and back of better than a month, twenty-five days at the least. And it fell in late July, when Madinah bakes past a hundred and ten degrees, when the people who live there abandon work through the middle of the day and shelter through the worst of it because no body can labor in such heat. The Sheikh lived in those lands for ten years, and he calls the summer heat there surreal, a thing you cannot function inside of.

Then the cruelest weight, the one we can barely feel today. August was harvest. And here the Sheikh slows down to make us understand something we have forgotten how to understand. For nearly all of human history, people did not know what they would earn from one month to the next. The salary, the steady paycheck that arrives on the same day every month no matter the weather, is a blessing so recent and so strange that most of us cannot grasp life without it. The agrarian society of Madinah lived hand to mouth, week to week, and the harvest was not the bonus on top of the income; the harvest was the income, the single great paycheck on which a family lived the entire year. To march now was to walk away from the only money that was coming. The Prophet ﷺ was not merely asking these men to risk death in battle. He was asking them to abandon their bread, in the worst heat of the year, for a journey with no enemy waiting at the end of it. That is why they named it the army of hardship.

Those who followed in the hour of difficulty

The Qur'an gives this expedition its own name, and it is not Tabuk. It is the hour of difficulty, sa'at al-usrah:

Allah has already forgiven the Prophet and the Muhajireen and the Ansar who followed him in the hour of difficulty after the hearts of a party of them had almost inclined [to doubt], and then He forgave them. Indeed, He was to them Kind and Merciful.Qur'an 9:117

Qatada, the foremost student of Ibn Abbas, said plainly that this was Tabuk: they set out toward Syria in the blazing heat and were tested almost past bearing. How tested? Two or three men would be rationed a single date for the whole day, the Sheikh recounts, splitting one date between them, and when the flesh of it was gone they would pass the pit from mouth to mouth, each man sucking it for whatever ghost of taste or nourishment remained, real or imagined, because it was better than nothing.

And the thirst was worse than the hunger. Umar ibn al-Khattab himself, asked years later by Ibn Abbas to describe the hour of difficulty, told the story. They reached a place so dry, he said, that they thought their throats would collapse, that they would simply die. Men went out hunting for water and came back with nothing, more spent and more parched than before. Some slaughtered their own camels to wring the moisture from the stomach, a bitter and brackish thing, salt more than water, and the desperation that drove them to it meant they would now have to walk the whole long way home on foot, having killed the very animal that was to carry them.

It was here, at the edge of dying, that Abu Bakr came to the Prophet ﷺ and begged him: ya Rasul Allah, make du'a for us. We need a miracle. The Prophet ﷺ said, if you wish, and raised his hands. And Umar swore that he had not yet brought them down before the sky darkened and the clouds gathered and the rain came pouring, until every man in that army had filled every container he carried, and the throats that were collapsing drank their fill.

He took the means first

Notice what the Sheikh notices in that rain, because it is one of the judgments he wants carried out of this story. The Prophet ﷺ did not raise his hands the moment the army grew thirsty. He let them reach the very edge: the slaughtered camels, the collapsing throats, the certainty of death. He, of all people, knew that the miracle was his to call. Why would he hold it back so long? Why did it take Abu Bakr, finally, to prod him into asking?

Because he was teaching every one of us a law deeper than any single day in the desert. The Sheikh names it akhdh bil-asbab: you take the means. You struggle, you exhaust your effort, you do the hard and bitter human work to its very end, and only then, when you have given everything you have, does the help of Allah arrive. The Messenger of Allah ﷺ was a man to whom miracles came; that was never in doubt. But he would not lean on them. The miracle came, but never at the start. Only after the struggle. Only after the sacrifice. That is the pattern of the whole seerah, the Sheikh says: at the very end, yes, the help descends, but not before the bitterness has been tasted to the bottom.

And there is mercy folded into that law for the rest of us. Most of us will never raise our hands and split open the sky over a dying army. But the believers who walk his ﷺ road, who put in the effort and taste the bitterness of it and refuse to quit, will see their own miracles, quieter ones, less blatant, arriving in their own lives at the very moment they have nothing left to give. Things will happen to them, the Sheikh promises, that they will recognize for what they are. Allah blesses the ending, not the wishing. He blesses the ones who paid the price first.

Whoever finances the army of hardship

Once the Prophet ﷺ understood the march had to happen, the first thing he did was raise the money for it. And here a beautiful detail surfaces, the Sheikh says: only six months earlier he had held all the spoils of Hunayn and Ta'if in his hands, a fortune, and he had spent every coin of it in the path of Allah, keeping nothing for himself and nothing in the treasury. That was his way to the end. So now the war had to be financed from nothing, from the ground up. He stood on the mimbar and called out: whoever finances the army of hardship will be given Jannah. And the companions came, a bag of gold here, a fistful of coins there, until the piles rose in the masjid.

Then came Uthman ibn Affan, may Allah be pleased with him. A caravan of his, near a hundred camels, had just returned laden from a northern trade, the whole of his recent fortune arriving at once. And when he heard that promise he gave all of it, thousands of gold coins, poured out before the Prophet ﷺ. The Prophet ﷺ turned the coins over in his hands, and over again, and said: whatever Uthman does after this day will not harm him. Words so weighty that years later, when the rebels had surrounded his house and meant to kill him, Uthman would stand and recall them in his own defense, and Ibn Abbas would throw them back at the besiegers: your Prophet ﷺ said that whatever this man does will not harm him, so who are you, with your invented list of complaints, to stand against him?

And then the famous contest. Umar, who had heard the same promise from the Prophet's ﷺ own mouth, hurried home thinking, today at last I will outdo Abu Bakr, and brought half of everything he owned, leaving the other half for his family, which was already a thing no one was even obliged to do. Then Abu Bakr arrived, and what he brought was clearly more, clearly all of it. What did you leave for your family, the Prophet ﷺ asked him. And Abu Bakr answered: I left them Allah and His Messenger. After that, Umar said, I will never try to beat him again; there is no competing with that kind of trust. Understand what the Sheikh wants you to see in this. Tabuk was being paid for not by men who had plenty to spare, but by men who emptied their hands of the only paycheck they had, with no certainty of when the next would come, and trusted that Allah would refill them.

And one last scene from the gathering, because it shows how human these days were, and how honestly the seerah keeps that humanity. Abu Musa al-Ash'ari was sent by his clan to ask the Prophet ﷺ for camels to ride to Tabuk, for no man could cross that desert on foot; the riders went two and three to a beast. He came not knowing that the Prophet ﷺ was in a state of anger that day, troubled most likely, the Sheikh suggests, by the hypocrites and their excuses, the test the story would soon reach. So when Abu Musa made his request, the answer came sharp: wallahi, I will not give you anything to ride. He went away crushed, certain he had done something wrong, and told his clan that the Prophet ﷺ had refused them and he did not know why.

Barely any time passed before Bilal came calling him back. The Prophet ﷺ handed him six fine camels he had just purchased: take these two, and these two, and these two. Abu Musa hesitated, confused and uneasy, for the Prophet ﷺ had sworn by Allah that he would give him nothing, and here was the gift in his hands. It had to be explained to him, and the explanation is the lesson: when the Prophet ﷺ spoke, he genuinely had nothing to give, and the words came out heavier than the situation deserved; when the means arrived, he remembered Abu Musa and gave. An oath sworn in that way, the Sheikh notes, over a thing not truly intended, does not bind a person, just as Allah says that He does not take us to task for the idle, careless oaths of our tongues, but only for what our hearts have firmly resolved. And there is a tenderness in Abu Musa's unease, his reluctance to simply take the camels after a qasam had been spoken; it is the conscience of a companion who would not step lightly over an oath in the name of Allah.

So the funds were gathered, the camels found, and the largest army Arabia had ever seen began to assemble in the killing heat. But the hardship of jaysh al-usrah was never only thirst and distance and abandoned harvests. Among those who hung back, and even among some who marched, a deeper trial was waiting, the test of the hypocrites and their excuses, the men whose hearts the heat and the distance exposed. And that is where the road to Tabuk turns next.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to file Tabuk away as the campaign where nothing happened, the long hot march that ended without a battle. That would be to miss the whole point of it. There was no battle precisely because the battle was never the test. The test was whether twenty thousand men would move when moving cost them everything and explained nothing, and they passed it so completely that no enemy was even needed to prove what they were made of. The Qur'an remembers them by name, not as conquerors, but as the ones who followed him ﷺ in the hour of difficulty, and tells us that Allah turned to them in mercy.

Sit first with the strangeness of the command. No army was massing. No threat could be seen. The wisest student of the seerah, after teaching it many times, cannot find a worldly reason for the march, and concludes there was none: it was Allah's word, and Allah's word alone, that sent them into the desert in July. We are forever waiting to understand before we obey, demanding that the reason be visible to us before we will move. The men of jaysh al-usrah were asked to do the opposite, to march on the command and trust that the wisdom was real even when it was hidden, and the whole future of the ummah was built on their willingness to do it. Ask yourself how much of your own obedience waits, quietly, on first being shown the point of it.

Then sit with how they gave. Uthman emptied a caravan. Umar gave half of all he had. Abu Bakr gave everything and left his family Allah and His Messenger. And the weight of what they did is not in the size of the piles, but in the fact that this was their bread, the one paycheck of the whole year, given away into the heat with no guarantee that another would ever come. We are generous, most of us, out of our surplus, from the part we will not miss. The army of hardship was financed out of need, by men who trusted that the Hand which asked them to empty their pockets would not leave them empty. The measure of a gift, this day teaches, is not what it adds to the pile but what it costs the one who gives it.

And sit, last, with the rain. The Prophet ﷺ held back the miracle until the camels were slaughtered and the throats were closing and a man as great as Abu Bakr had to beg him to raise his hands. He could have called the clouds on the first day. He waited, on purpose, so that we would learn the law beneath the mercy: take the means, do the hard human work to its very end, and only then rest in what Allah will send. This is the balance most of us never quite find, forever wishing for the rain without walking the dry road that comes before it. The help of Allah is real, and it is coming, but it comes to the ones who paid the price first. So take one thing from this army that won no battle and may have done the hardest thing the companions ever did. Move on Allah's word before you can see the reason for it. Give from your need and not only from your surplus. And the next time you find yourself at the edge of a dry place, with your throat closing and your strength gone, do every lawful thing in your power, and then raise your hands the way they did, knowing that the One who held the rain until His Prophet ﷺ asked for it has not forgotten you. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, who emptied his hands for this religion and asked his companions to empty theirs, count us among those who follow him in every hour of difficulty, and grant us, after every struggle, a mercy from Himself after which there is no thirst.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 87: the Battle of Tabuk, part 1 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (9:38, 9:39, 9:41, 9:117, 9:123), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 88 of 104

The Expedition of Tabuk, Part 2

The man who would not lie his way out

Tabuk was the last expedition of the Prophet ﷺ, and almost the whole of the ninth surah of the Qur'an came down because of it. There was no battle there in the end, no clash of armies, no swords drawn. The enemy never came out to meet them. And yet Dr. Yasir Qadhi spends lecture after lecture inside Tabuk, because the fighting that mattered was not on any field. It was inside the hearts of the men who had to decide whether to march at all. Today the seerah slows almost to a stop and follows just one of those men, the whole way through, because his is one of the most beloved and most painful stories in the entire life of the Prophet ﷺ, and he told it himself.

His name was Kab ibn Malik. He failed. He had every reason on earth to go and he stayed home anyway. What he did after that failure is the reason we still tell his story fourteen centuries later, and the Sheikh walks it line by line, paragraph by paragraph, because Kab narrated it in the first person, to his own son, holding nothing back. It is a story about the one mistake a good man cannot lie his way out of, and exactly what it costs to tell the truth when a lie would be so much easier.

A confession told to his own son

Kab ibn Malik was no ordinary believer. He was of the elite of the Ansar, one of the small band who pledged their lives to the Prophet ﷺ at Aqabah, before the hijrah, before Islam had a city of its own to shelter in. He belonged to the tribe of Banu Salima, the same tribe that once asked the Prophet ﷺ for permission to move their homes closer to him in Madinah, away from the far edge of the city where they lived. He ﷺ told them to stay where they were: O Banu Salima, your footsteps to the mosque are being recorded for you. Every track you leave is written down. Some of the early scholars, the Sheikh notes, read one verse of Surah Yasin as a promise made to people exactly like them, that Allah counts not only what a soul sends ahead but the very prints it leaves behind.

Indeed, it is We who bring the dead to life and record what they have put forth and what they left behind, and all things We have enumerated in a clear register.Qur'an 36:12

So when Kab's story reaches us, it reaches us from a man whose feet had been on the right road his entire life, a man of Aqabah, a man who never missed a march. He lived long, into old age, and his eyes failed him at the end, and it was his son Abdullah who would take him by the hand and lead him about. And it is Abdullah who carries the story forward: I heard my father tell of his staying behind from Tabuk. The whole chain runs through a son repeating, word for word, his father's account of the worst thing his father ever did.

Sit with how rare that is, the Sheikh says, because most of us spend a lifetime hiding our failures from our children. We want our sons and daughters to see a man who never stumbled. Kab did the opposite. He sat his own son down and told him, in effect, let me tell you about the time your father got it badly wrong, because the lesson was never in the falling. The lesson is in how a believer climbs back up. Nobody is perfect, and a father who pretends he is teaches his child nothing. A father who confesses, and then shows what repentance looks like, teaches him everything.

No excuse, and he knew it

Kab opens his account with a kind of pride, and the Sheikh is careful to call it the good kind, not the arrogance that is always forbidden, but the legitimate satisfaction of a believer taking honest stock of a life spent in service. I was never absent from any expedition the Prophet ﷺ fought, Kab says, except Tabuk. There was Badr, but no one was ever blamed for missing Badr, because Badr set out as a raid on a caravan, not as a battle anyone was obliged to join. And then he adds something striking: I would not trade my night at Aqabah for Badr itself, even though the people speak more of Badr. This is a man reciting his record. Abu Bakr and Umar, radiyallahu anhuma, would do the same from time to time, the Sheikh notes, quietly listing what they had given to the religion. It is not vanity. It is a soul preparing the case it will carry to the Day of Judgment, and the question it puts to us is uncomfortable: when did we last sit and ask what good we have actually done for the sake of Allah, rather than spend the whole accounting on the dunya?

But naming his record only makes what came next harder, not easier. Tabuk was unlike every march before it. The Prophet ﷺ, who normally concealed his destination and let the enemy guess until the last moment, this time announced it plainly and gave his reasons: the heat was punishing, the distance was the farthest the Muslims had ever attempted, the enemy was vast, and it was the season the dates were ripening and the shade had never been sweeter. Because the journey demanded so much preparation, surprise was set aside. Everyone knew where they were going, and going was an obligation on every able-bodied man. There was no slipping under the radar. To stay behind from Tabuk without a reason was not a lapse. It was a major sin.

And Kab had nothing to hide behind. I had never been stronger or wealthier than I was right before Tabuk, he admits to his son. By Allah, I had never owned two riding camels until that expedition, and then I had two. The Sheikh dwells on the weight of that. There were exactly two excuses a man could lawfully offer: a body too weak to travel, or a purse too empty to equip oneself. The companions financed their own expeditions; there was no paid army, and a single camel, shared among two or three men, was the bare minimum for the road. Most companions owned none at all; even the Prophet ﷺ had not owned a camel until the night of the hijrah, when he bought one from Abu Bakr. And here was Kab with two of his own, and his full health besides. Two camels, the Sheikh says, was like owning two cars in a place where almost no one had even one. Kab had no excuse, and he says so himself, plainly, without flinching: I had no excuse at all.

The weapon called tomorrow

So why did he stay? Not from cowardice, and not from any decision. From the smallest, most ordinary thing in the world. Kab is painfully honest with his son: when the Prophet ﷺ set out, the fruit was ripening and the shade was sweet, and my heart leaned toward my orchard and my comfort more than toward the march. I would go out each day meaning to make my preparations, buy my supplies, settle my affairs, and I would come back having done nothing, telling myself there is no rush. There is always tomorrow.

Then the army formed up and left, thousands of them, so many that Kab says no register could have held all their names. The march of so large a body would be slow; a single rider could still have overtaken them in a day or two. So Kab told himself he would equip himself in the morning and ride out and catch them. Morning came, and he did nothing. Another day, and nothing again. How I wish I had done it, he tells his son, the regret still raw after all the years. By the third day it was hopeless. One rider cannot close a three-day gap. The chance was simply gone, and he had let it go without ever once choosing to.

The Sheikh lingers here, longer than almost anywhere, because this is the trap that catches the rest of us, not just Kab. Procrastination, he says, is among the most effective weapons the shaytan owns, and one of the early scholars described exactly how it operates: every time a door of good swings open in front of you, two other doors open beside it to compete, the door of perhaps and the door of soon. Perhaps I should do it differently, and I will get to it shortly. Open those two doors enough times and you have done nothing at all, while believing the whole while that you fully intend to. The entire Qur'an, the Sheikh reminds us, is built on verbs of motion: race, hasten, rush to your Lord, stand up and warn. Faith itself is action. Kab never resolved to disobey. He kept saying tomorrow until tomorrow ran out, and that is how most of us lose the things we mean to do, not in one defiant choice but in a hundred soft deferrals that each felt like nothing at the time.

Two garments and a man who would not gossip

Now Madinah emptied, and Kab wandered through it like a man in a ghost town. The only faces left were men known for their hypocrisy and men the war had excused, the elderly, the infirm, the weak. It grieved him to the bone. Out at Tabuk, meanwhile, the Prophet ﷺ made camp for the better part of a month, and one day, looking around the gathering, he asked: what has Kab ibn Malik done? A man from Kab's own tribe of Banu Salima answered with a sneer, his fine clothes and his self-admiration have held him back. At once another companion rounded on the speaker: what an evil thing to say. By Allah, O Messenger of Allah, we know nothing of Kab but good. And the Prophet ﷺ said nothing, and let the matter rest.

Notice who history remembered, the Sheikh says, and notice who it forgot. The man who defended his absent brother is named in the report and honored in it forever. The man who tore Kab down is left nameless, swallowed by the very gossip he tried to spread. Do you not think the Prophet ﷺ knew both their names? Of course he did. And still the report hands one down and lets the other vanish. The Sheikh counts this as one of perhaps a dozen places across the whole seerah where evil is deliberately not named, where the companions choose to cover a sin rather than broadcast it, a discipline he says runs in the exact opposite direction to a culture that builds entire magazines and television shows on gossip. And he hangs a hadith on the moment, one he asks us to memorize: whoever defends the honor of his brother in his absence, Allah will shield his face from the Fire on the Day of Judgment. That very defense, he ﷺ said, becomes your own defense before the Fire. So the next time a man is torn apart in a gathering and you know him to be good, the Sheikh urges, speak for him. Let that word you spend on an absent brother be the word that is spent for you when you most need it.

The smile of an angry man

The Prophet ﷺ turned the army for home, and Kab's mind began racing. He searched for every excuse he could invent, and he even started gathering the counsel of the shrewdest elders of his family, assembling a story that might carry him through. Then word came that the Prophet ﷺ had reached the city, and in an instant every false excuse dissolved in Kab's hands. He knew with total clarity that no lie was going to carry him out of this, that nothing invented could ever save him. So he made his decision: he would tell the truth, only the truth, and let it land wherever it fell.

When the Prophet ﷺ arrived he first prayed two units of prayer in the mosque, a sunnah of the returning traveler that the Sheikh notes we have very nearly forgotten, and then he sat to receive the people. The stragglers came the next day in a long line, more than eighty of them, and one after another they swore their false oaths and begged forgiveness. The Prophet ﷺ accepted each excuse on its surface and left their secrets to Allah. Then Kab's turn came. And here Kab gives us a detail the Sheikh calls one of the most beautiful windows into the heart of the Prophet ﷺ in the whole seerah. He came to me, Kab says, and he smiled at me, the smile of an angry man. When we are truly angry, the Sheikh observes, we will not give a person even the smallest smile; we set our lips straight and let them feel it. But the Prophet ﷺ, even displeased, could not bring himself to scowl in a brother's face. And yet it was not the warm smile, and Kab read it instantly, because you cannot fully hide your heart even behind a smile. This was disappointment, and the disappointment was itself a kind of honor, because it meant the Prophet ﷺ held Kab to a higher standard than the men who had filed past before him. The hypocrites were expected to fail. Kab was not.

Come here, the Prophet ﷺ said. What kept you back? Did you not buy a mount? And here, even now, the Sheikh marvels that the Prophet ﷺ knew: he had been watching this companion who was not even of the inner circle, and he knew Kab had a second camel. Kab answered with everything he had. O Messenger of Allah, if I were sitting before any other man on earth, I have been given a quick and persuasive tongue, and I could talk my way clear of his anger. But by Allah, if I tell you a lie today to win your pleasure, I know Allah will soon make you angry with me by exposing it. And if I tell you the truth, and you are displeased with me today, I can still hope for a good outcome from Allah. By Allah, I have no excuse. I have never been stronger or more able than the day I stayed behind. And the Prophet ﷺ said: as for this one, he has told the truth. Get up, and wait until Allah decides your case.

The Sheikh stops the whole narrative here to draw out the theology buried inside Kab's words, because it is, he says, one of the most important points in the entire story. Kab understood that it was Allah's pleasure he ultimately needed, not the Prophet's ﷺ; that even a lie that won the Prophet ﷺ over would not save him if Allah was not pleased, and that Allah could forgive him even while the Prophet ﷺ remained displeased. That is exactly backward from how some later groups came to speak, attributing to the Prophet ﷺ command over Paradise and Hell. The Prophet ﷺ is the greatest of all creation, the most merciful and the most beloved, but he is not divine, and he is not the one who decrees a soul to the Garden or the Fire. That belongs to Allah alone. Kab, in the rawest moment of his life, had his theology perfectly in place.

Fifty nights as a stranger in his own city

As Kab walked away, his own tribesmen swarmed around him and followed him all the way home. By Allah, they said, we never knew you to commit a sin before this. Why could you not have done what the others did, made some excuse, and let the Prophet ﷺ ask forgiveness for you? They pressed him so hard, and the way out looked so easy, that Kab nearly turned back to retract his confession and lie. Then he stopped and asked the question that saved him: did anyone else tell the truth as I did? They said yes, two men said exactly what you said, and they were answered as you were answered. Who are they, he asked. They named two men, the Sheikh notes, who had both fought at Badr, both righteous, both men in whom there was blessing. And the moment Kab heard their names, his choice was made: if these two are with the truth, then I am with them. Not with the eighty and their excuses. With the two.

The Sheikh ties this directly to the command Allah would send down, and to a clean, hard lesson inside it.

O you who have believed, fear Allāh and be with those who are true.Qur'an 9:119

Truth is not measured by the number of people standing on its side, he says. Eighty smooth tongues weighed less, in the sight of Allah, than two honest ones. Kab chose his company by their character and not by their count, and that is the choice the verse asks of every believer: not be with the many, but be with the truthful.

Then the order came. The Prophet ﷺ forbade the Muslims to speak a single word to the three of them, and to them alone. Notice, the Sheikh says, that the hypocrites who lied walked away untouched in this world; not one of them was punished here. The punishment fell on the three believers who had told the truth. And this is the wisdom of it, he explains, a wisdom that runs through every hardship a believer ever meets: a believer's suffering in this world erases what he would otherwise owe in the next, while the hypocrite is left alone now to face the full reckoning later. So Madinah closed its mouth. No police enforced the order, no informers, no watchers, nothing at all but iman, and an entire city of thousands obeyed as one, simply because the Prophet ﷺ had said so. That kind of obedience, the Sheikh observes, can only grow from faith. And the earth, in Kab's own words, grew tight around him for all its vastness. He had become a stranger in his own land.

His two companions shut themselves inside their homes and wept. But Kab was the youngest of the three and the most spirited, so he forced himself out among the people. He prayed in the mosque, he walked the markets aching for a single soul to return his greeting, and he would come before the Prophet ﷺ and give his salam, then watch his lips, desperate to see them move in reply. They never moved. When Kab prayed nearby he would steal a glance and find the Prophet ﷺ watching him, and the moment Kab looked, the Prophet ﷺ would look away, so that Kab came to know, by that stolen glance, that there was mercy waiting somewhere behind the silence. At last, unable to bear it, Kab climbed the garden wall of his dearest friend and cousin Abu Qatada, the way he always had, and gave his salam. Abu Qatada did not answer. I ask you by Allah, Kab pleaded, do you not know that I love Allah and His Messenger? Abu Qatada was silent. He asked again, and a third time, begging now, weeping, and his cousin answered only into the air: Allah and His Messenger know best. Kab broke down and wept, and climbed back over the wall.

The Sheikh draws a line from this to our own age. The world now recognizes total isolation as a form of torture; those who have endured solitary confinement sometimes say they would take physical pain over being unseen, unspoken to, treated as though they do not exist. Kab's was a gentler version, he walked free and was never caged, but it was a punishment all the same, and it was tearing him apart. And the whole of it, the Sheikh insists, was mercy in disguise. Allah was not casting these three away. He was using fifty nights of silence to lift them higher than they had ever stood.

The letter from a king, and a wife sent away

Then the world came testing, the way it always does at the lowest moment. A merchant from the north was moving through the market of Madinah calling out, who can show me to Kab ibn Malik, and the people pointed Kab out to him. He came and handed Kab a letter. It was from the king of Ghassan, the very people Tabuk had marched against. Their spies in Madinah, perhaps among the hypocrites, had reported that one of the elite of the Ansar had been cast out and shunned, and the king moved at once. We have heard, the letter ran, that your companion has treated you coldly, and Allah has not made you to live in a place where you are humiliated and your worth is wasted. Come to us, and we will console you and honor you. A door had swung open: leave the people who will not so much as look at you, and be a prince among their enemies.

Why would a king reach for a single shunned man, the Sheikh asks, and the answer is layered. To crack the unity of the Muslims. To wound the Prophet ﷺ and the companions by taking one of their own. To win a trophy defector paraded before the world, the way nations have always prized the one who crosses over. And to plant a source inside the city. The offer was a weapon, and it arrived aimed at Kab in his weakest hour.

Kab read it, and said one thing to himself: this too is part of the test. And he carried it straight to his oven and burned it. The Sheikh draws out why this is so much greater than it first looks. It is one thing to pass a test through iman; it is another thing entirely to recognize, in the moment, that you are even being tested, and that recognition takes knowledge. Iman lets you hold the line. Knowledge lets you see where the line is, and why it has been drawn, and what the enemy is really after. Kab understood exactly what Ghassan wanted from him, and rather than hold the letter and risk it whispering to him again, he burned it on the spot, so there could be no slow reconsidering in a dark hour. The best of all states, the Sheikh says, is iman joined to knowledge: faith to carry you through the trial, and knowledge to show you the trial for what it is.

On the fiftieth morning, the final blow landed. A messenger came with the Prophet's ﷺ command that Kab separate from his wife. Kab did not ask why, or for how long. He asked only one thing: do I divorce her, or what? Just keep apart from her, he was told, and do not approach her. So he sent her to her family. Look at the wonder of that, the Sheikh says: fifty nights of being slowly unmade, and now a fresh and heavier test arrives without warning, and Kab's only instinct is to leap to obey, to ask not why but how. That is iman. Kab's wife went to the Prophet ﷺ herself and asked leave to stay and care for him, for he is an old man, she said, with no servant; would you mind if I serve him? The Prophet ﷺ permitted her to feed him, but said there must be nothing between them. And she answered, by Allah, he has no desire for anything; he has done nothing but weep from the day this began until now. Some of Kab's family urged him to seek the same permission for himself. Kab refused. I am the youngest of the three, he said. What would I say? What excuse could I make? And so he held the line alone, the hardest way, asking for nothing.

And [He also forgave] the three who were left alone [i.e., boycotted, regretting their error] to the point that the earth closed in on them in spite of its vastness and their souls confined [i.e., anguished] them and they were certain that there is no refuge from Allāh except in Him. Then He turned to them so they could repent. Indeed, Allāh is the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful.Qur'an 9:118

Here the Sheikh, having reached the end of the lecture, stops the story on its cliff edge, and so the seerah stops with him. Ten more nights would pass after the fiftieth. On the morning of the sixtieth day, after two full months, Allah would turn to the three who were left behind and accept their repentance. The verse above records that ending. But the lifting of the punishment is for the next day to tell. Tonight Kab is still weeping against his wall, certain there is no refuge from Allah except in Allah Himself, the earth closed tight around him for all its width. The punishment was never the point. The mercy waiting on the far side of it was, and tomorrow you will hear it arrive.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read Kab's story as a sad interlude and move on. That would be to miss everything it puts to us. Strip it down and it becomes a mirror, and almost no one walks away from it unmarked.

He never decided to disobey. That is the first thing, and the most frightening, because it means the ruin came without a villain. Kab did not choose to miss Tabuk; he chose, over and over, to wait until tomorrow, and tomorrow quietly ate the chance. The Sheikh calls procrastination the favorite weapon of the shaytan, the door of perhaps and the door of soon swinging open beside every good deed until you have done nothing while fully meaning to do everything. Look honestly at your own delayed prayers, your postponed repentance, the good you keep promising yourself you will get to. When the good is in your hands, move, because the cost of waiting is rarely a decision you will remember making, only a chance you will spend the rest of your life wishing back.

He told the truth when the lie was easy, and he had the rarest reason to lie. He could speak; he had the gift of a quick tongue and eighty men modeling the smooth way out, and his own tribe pushing him toward it. And still he refused, because he understood that a lie which pleases today buys Allah's anger tomorrow, while the truth, however much it costs now, leaves the door of forgiveness open. That is the whole arithmetic of honesty, and we get it backward constantly, trading a hard truth for a soft lie and calling it wisdom. And he chose his company by character, not by number: be with those who are true, the verse says, not be with the many. Two honest men of Badr outweighed eighty smooth excuses, and Kab knew which side of that scale he meant to stand on.

And when the enemy's letter came at his lowest hour, he did not merely resist it; he recognized it. This too is a test, he said, and burned it before it could whisper twice. The Sheikh's distinction is one to carry for life: iman holds the line, but knowledge shows you where the line is and why it was drawn, so that the door which opens at your weakest moment does not fool you into walking through it. Pair the two, and you will not be sold an escape that is really a snare.

So take Kab down into your ordinary days, tonight, before the rest of his story even arrives. Do the good in front of you now, before tomorrow swallows it. Tell the truth when the lie would be easier and cheaper and kinder to your pride. Keep the company of the honest few rather than the comfortable many. And when the earth grows tight around you for all its vastness, when the walls of some hardship close in and you cannot see the way out, hold to the certainty that held Kab against his wall through fifty silent nights: that there is no refuge from Allah except in Him. O Allah, do not let us be people who wait for tomorrow when the good is in our hands today. Make our tongues truthful when the lie is easy, keep us among the truthful, and when we are confined and shunned and afraid, turn to us as You turned to the three who were left behind, and gather us with them, and with Your beloved Prophet ﷺ, in the mercy that was always waiting on the far side of the trial. Ameen.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 88: the expedition to Tabuk, part 2 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (36:12, 9:119, 9:118), verified via quran.ai. The narration is the Sheikh's; the phrasing is Buruja's.

The Seerah · 89 of 104

The story of Ka'b ibn Malik

The dawn that broke after fifty nights

Yesterday we left Ka'b ibn Malik at the very bottom of it. He had told the truth when a lie would have cost him nothing. He had stood before the Prophet ﷺ and said plainly that he had no excuse for staying back from Tabuk except his own delay, that he had not been sick, had not been poor, had simply kept saying tomorrow until the army was gone. The Prophet ﷺ had ordered him boycotted, and Ka'b had watched an entire city look through him as though he were not there. Forty nights of that, and then the order came that even his wife should leave him. The king of Ghassan sent a letter offering him a home and a place of honor among a people who would not shun him, and Ka'b read it, recognized it for the trial it was, and fed it to the fire. By the end he could no longer bear the walk to the masjid, and a man who had given his Bay'ah at Aqaba was reduced to praying Fajr alone on his own rooftop.

This is where the chapter resumes, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi tells us this is one of his favorite stories in the whole seerah, and that it deserves to be one of ours. The reason is in where it ends. Ka'b is not finally set free by a man telling him he is forgiven. He is set free by Allah Himself, who announces the acceptance of his repentance from above the seven heavens, in words of the Qur'an that are still recited to this day. A believer who loved Allah and His Messenger ﷺ committed a real sin, endured a punishment that broke him, and was forgiven completely, by name, in a Book that will be read until the end of time. That is why the story is told. Today is the dawn of it.

Fifty nights, and a voice from the mountain

Ka'b would describe those days, decades later, not by reciting a verse but by pointing at it, and the Sheikh finds that detail beautiful: a man so soaked in the words Allah revealed about him that he narrates his own life through the Qur'an without quoting it. He would say only that he was in the state Allah described, and his listeners knew exactly what he meant. Allah had said of the three of them that the wide earth, for all its vastness, had closed in upon them until there was nowhere left to stand, and their own selves had become a burden too heavy to carry, and they had understood the one thing the whole ordeal was built to teach them.

And [He also forgave] the three who were left alone to the point that the earth closed in on them in spite of its vastness and their souls confined them and they were certain that there is no refuge from Allah except in Him. Then He turned to them so they could repent. Indeed, Allah is the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful.Qur'an 9:118

There is no refuge from Allah except in Allah. You cannot save yourself from Him by running anywhere except toward Him. That, Sheikh Yasir says, was the entire purpose of the fifty nights: to bring three honest men to the place where the world had shrunk to a single door, and that door was the One who had closed all the others.

Ka'b had tried, in the beginning, to keep walking among the people, going to the masjid, moving through the marketplace while no one returned his greeting. But after a while even that became unbearable, and he withdrew into his house and stopped going out at all, until the loneliness was such that he prayed even Fajr by himself on his roof. And it was there, one morning, sitting in exactly the grief the verse describes, worried about himself and about everything, that he heard a man who had climbed to the top of Jabal Sal'. This is the mountain you see as you leave the Prophet's masjid, and it had taken the man some twenty minutes to climb it after the announcement at Fajr. From the summit, across the whole distance, the man cried out at the very top of his voice: Ya Ka'b ibn Malik, abshir. Be glad. The sentence stopped there. Glad of what? There was no need to finish it, because in all the world there was only one thing it could mean. Ka'b fell straight into prostration where he sat. He knew. The relief of Allah had come.

The city becomes a festival

The Prophet ﷺ had announced after Fajr that Allah had accepted the repentance of the three, and Madinah simply erupted. People scattered in every direction to be the first to carry the news. Some ran to the other two men. Some climbed the mountain to shout it down to Ka'b. And a horseman came galloping the whole way from the masjid to Ka'b's house, with no reward waiting for him, no tie of tribe between them, nothing to gain at all except the joy of being the one to say it to his face. The Sheikh dwells on this. Look, he says, at the genuine love that ran through that generation: a man would gallop across the city on his own horse for nothing, simply because his brother had been saved, and to him that was reason enough.

Ka'b, overflowing, wanted to give the rider something, and discovered he had nothing left in the house worth giving. Across those fifty nights he had quietly given his money away in charity until the coins were gone; he still had his land and his garden, but of cash and clothing the house was empty. All he owned now was the garment on his back. So he took off his outer cloak and gifted it to the man, and then had to knock on a neighbor's door to borrow something to wear to the masjid. The Sheikh lingers here on the poverty of the Companions, and turns it back on us. Gift one of us a used shirt today and we would be insulted, he says, while to the Companions a worn garment was a treasure, because most of them owned only one, and the Prophet ﷺ himself owned two. Our own closets are bursting, and we should be honest about what that has done to our gratitude.

Then Ka'b walked, in his borrowed cloak, into a city that had turned his private deliverance into its own celebration. The people received him in waves, embracing him, congratulating him on Allah accepting his repentance, calling out glad tidings to him at every turn. This was not Ka'b's joy alone; it was an Eid for the whole community. And Sheikh Yasir asks the question that makes it land: what did the rest of the believers have to do with the forgiveness of three men? Why should the whole city rejoice over a matter so private? The answer is the entire point. When the bond of brotherhood is that strong, your brother's rescue is felt as your own rescue. The community grieved as one body while the three were cut off, and now it rejoiced as one body that they had been brought back.

Talha stood up

Ka'b reached the masjid and found the Prophet ﷺ seated with the people gathered around him. And out of all the muhajirun in that gathering, one man rose to his feet, hurried over, took Ka'b's hand, and congratulated him: Talha ibn Ubaydillah, radiyallahu anhu, the famous companion of Makkah. No one else among the muhajirun stood. It was not that the rest had done anything wrong, the Sheikh is careful to note. They were happy for him; they simply stayed seated. But Talha stood.

Ka'b is narrating this as a blind old man, perhaps seventy-five years of age, looking back across forty years, and he says he never forgot that gesture as long as he lived. One small act of kindness, offered at the moment a person has been hurt and abandoned, can change a heart for the rest of its life. Just a handshake. Just rising to your feet. The Sheikh draws it straight out and presses on it, because it is so easy and so cheap and so rarely done: at times of distress you discover who your friends truly are, and the one who comes with a word of comfort when the whole world has turned away is the one you remember to your grave. It costs almost nothing to be that person. It is never, ever forgotten.

Is this from you, or from Allah?

Ka'b greeted the Prophet ﷺ, and the face of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ shone with joy until it was like a piece of the full moon. That description recurs across many narrations, the Sheikh notes, in the hadith of Ka'b and in others: when the Prophet ﷺ was happy, his face beamed like the moon. And he said to Ka'b: be glad with the best day you have seen since the day your mother gave birth to you. The best day of your whole life. And why? Because Allah had announced his forgiveness, and what more could a servant want than that.

Then Ka'b asked something that tells you everything about the faith of that generation. O Messenger of Allah, he said, is this from you, or from Allah? And the Prophet ﷺ answered: no, it is from Allah. Sheikh Yasir holds this exchange up as the very heart of tawhid. Ka'b loved the Prophet ﷺ more than he loved his own self, and yet he drew a clear, bright line between the two: the pleasure of the Messenger ﷺ is a tremendous thing, an immense thing, but it is not Heaven and Hell. The One who owns forgiveness, who alone decides Paradise and the Fire, is Allah. A word of pardon from the Prophet ﷺ would have been good; but Ka'b was waiting for the word that decides eternity, and that word could come from only one place. This, the Sheikh says, is exactly what the Companions understood by living alongside the Prophet ﷺ, and it is exactly what is lost when later generations place him on a pedestal he himself never claimed. They did not go to him for the things that belong to Allah, because they knew him as a man, the greatest of men, and a Messenger, but a man.

It was the same conviction Ka'b had voiced when he first confessed. He had told the Prophet ﷺ that if he had wished, he could have talked his way into pleasing him with a smooth excuse, but he could never have deceived Allah, and Allah would have exposed him in the end; whereas if he angered the Prophet ﷺ by being honest, he could still hope that Allah would forgive him. So he had told the truth and waited. Now the waiting was over, and the verdict had come, not from the man in front of him, but from above the seven heavens.

Keep some of your wealth

Overflowing with gratitude, Ka'b made an offer. O Messenger of Allah, he said, because Allah has accepted my repentance, I will give away everything I own in the path of Allah. All of it. He would keep nothing for himself. And here the wisdom of the Prophet ﷺ pulled him gently back from the high of the moment: keep some of your wealth for yourself, that is better for you. So Ka'b said he would hold on to his share from Khaybar, which was a good and steady source of income, and give the rest away. Khaybar had been the largest fortune the Companions gained, and the Ansar, who had received nothing of the spoils of Hunayn, had a real portion of it.

The Sheikh draws two threads out of this small exchange. The first is that we should not act on raw emotion or a spiritual high. Ka'b at his peak wanted to strip himself completely bare, and the Prophet ﷺ steadied him, told him to think, told him to keep enough for himself and his family, because it is not from this religion to give your whole estate to charity and leave your wife and children in poverty. And if someone objects that Abu Bakr, radiyallahu anhu, once gave away literally everything he owned, the answer is easy: that is precisely why he was Abu Bakr. Not even Umar reached that station. We should be honest about our own level and not mistake a moment of feeling for the rank of a Siddiq. The second thread is gentler. When something good happens, thank Allah with charity. A raise, a graduation, the birth of a child: meet your blessings by giving. Ka'b already knew this in his bones, which is exactly why his first instinct upon being forgiven was to give everything away. The Prophet ﷺ did not correct the instinct. He only adjusted the amount.

I will never tell a lie again

Then Ka'b made the vow that became his legacy. O Messenger of Allah, he said, Allah saved me only because I told the truth. So part of my repentance is that I will never tell a lie as long as I live. He had stood at the very edge of ruin and watched the hypocrites lie their way to a hollow, momentary safety, and he had chosen honesty when it cost him everything he had; and honesty was precisely the thing that rescued him. So he bound himself to it for the rest of his days.

He told his son, decades later, that he did not know of any Muslim whom Allah had tested in truthfulness more severely than him from that day onward. Chance after chance came to him where a lie would have been easier, and each time he remembered his promise and held to it, and he never once told a lie intentionally to the day he was speaking those words. And then, even there, at the very end of a long life, his humility shows through. He said intentionally, the Sheikh points out, because he could not be certain he had never said something he believed to be true that later turned out to be false. An old man, nearly blind, with perhaps a year of life left in him, was still guarding his tongue, still refusing to overstate even his own faithfulness, still asking Allah to protect him for whatever days remained. That caution, the Sheikh says, is itself the mark of sincere repentance: a heart that, forty years after the slip and certain of its forgiveness, still will not take its own salvation for granted.

Sheikh Yasir gathers the whole lesson into a single hadith. The Prophet ﷺ said to hold fast to truthfulness, for truthfulness leads to righteousness, and righteousness leads to Paradise, and a man keeps telling the truth and striving to tell the truth until he is written with Allah as a Siddiq, a truthful one. And he warned against lying, for lying leads to wickedness, and wickedness leads to the Fire, and a man keeps lying until he is written with Allah as a liar. The Prophet ﷺ tied honesty directly, in a single chain, to Jannah. Ka'b had simply lived the hadith out across forty years of his life.

The verse came down

What Ka'b had not yet heard, in the moment he asked is this from you or from Allah, were the actual words. He would hear them only afterward, when Allah revealed his forgiveness in the Qur'an itself. Allah turned in mercy to the Prophet ﷺ and to the muhajirun and the ansar who had followed him out into the crushing heat and hardship of Tabuk, when the hearts of a group of them had nearly faltered.

Allah has already forgiven the Prophet and the Muhajireen and the Ansar who followed him in the hour of difficulty after the hearts of a party of them had almost inclined [to doubt], and then He forgave them. Indeed, He was to them Kind and Merciful.Qur'an 9:117

And then, in the very next verse, the words about the three who were left, the verse Ka'b would spend his life pointing to. Ka'b said that Allah had never granted him a blessing greater than Islam itself, except this one: that on the day he could have lied, he did not. Because had he lied, he would have been destroyed alongside the hypocrites, whom Allah described in the harshest terms He ever used for any people. Notice, the Sheikh says, how Ka'b attributes even his own honesty back to Allah: not "I was strong enough to tell the truth," but "Allah protected me from telling a lie." The believer traces all good back to its source; it is the disbeliever who credits himself.

To show the contrast, Ka'b then recited the verses about those very hypocrites, the ones who would meet the returning army with false oaths so that the believers would simply turn away and leave them in peace.

They will swear by Allah to you when you return to them that you would leave them alone. So leave them alone; indeed they are evil; and their refuge is Hell as recompense for what they had been earning. They swear to you so that you might be satisfied with them. But if you should be satisfied with them, indeed, Allah is not satisfied with a defiantly disobedient people.Qur'an 9:95-96

The Sheikh reads Ka'b's choice of these verses as deliberate. The liars and the truth-teller stand side by side in a single surah, and they end in two completely opposite places: the ones who swore false oaths consigned to the Fire, and the one who told the costly truth named among the forgiven. Allah even commanded that the Prophet ﷺ never again seek forgiveness for those hypocrites; their case was closed against them. Ka'b's case was opened in mercy.

And here, on one word of his own verse, the Sheikh says, Ka'b became a teacher of tafsir. Most renderings translate alladhina khullifu as the three who were left behind, as though it merely meant they had lagged behind the marching army. Ka'b corrected this. Khullifu, he taught, does not mean we remained behind; it means our verdict was held back, suspended, left hanging. The hypocrites were not in any state of suspension, because the Prophet ﷺ had outwardly accepted their oaths and consigned their hearts to Allah, so their affair was closed. But the three honest men were deliberately placed in limbo, their judgment deferred, no ruling made in their case at all, until Allah Himself ruled on them. That, Ka'b explained, is what the word carries: not the three who lagged behind the expedition, but the three whose case was left open and unresolved until Heaven decided it.

What the story leaves in your hands

Imam Bukhari placed this hadith under the Battle of Tabuk, which makes perfect sense, for that is where it happened. Imam Muslim placed it under the chapter of repentance and its blessings, which makes just as much sense, because that, in the end, is what the story actually is. Ka'b committed a real sin, the Sheikh is unflinching about this: disobeying a direct command of the Prophet ﷺ at a time when going out was an obligation upon him, and the fifty-night boycott was a heavy and genuine punishment, not a trivial thing. And still, after all of it, he was forgiven, completely, by name, in a Book recited until the Day of Judgment. A story like that, the Sheikh says, is precisely the kind we are permitted and encouraged to tell, the story of the sinner who repented, because it gives the rest of us hope and something to hold on to.

So before the time ran out, Sheikh Yasir drew the threads together, and they are threads to carry down into ordinary days. That victory comes at the darkest hour: relief arrived for Ka'b only when the wide earth had closed upon him and he could not bear another night, so the believer stays patient and trusts that the way out is around the corner precisely when there seems to be no way out at all. That genuine repentance keeps its ache: Ka'b, forty years on and certain of his forgiveness, still wished he had simply gone with the army, and that lingering regret is not weakness of faith but the very sign that the tawba was real. That procrastination is the quiet danger running underneath the whole story: Ka'b kept saying tomorrow, tomorrow, until the army was gone, so every time you feel the pull to delay a good deed, remember Ka'b and do it now. And that when good news comes, the prostration of gratitude, sajdat ash-shukr, is the right response, the way Ka'b fell into sajda the very instant the call reached him from the mountain, without wudu, without delay, simply turning to face his Lord in thanks. The Prophet ﷺ never commanded it in so many words, but the Companions did it, and earlier prophets did it, and Ka'b did it on his rooftop, and so it is a sunnah we should revive.

Hold on, then, to the shape of this story, because you will need it. A man who loved Allah and His Messenger ﷺ still failed, still slipped, still spent fifty nights as a stranger in his own city with the walls of the earth pressing in on him. And the road back was not a clever excuse or a powerful intercessor or a letter from a king; it was the plain truth, spoken when a lie would have been so much easier, and a heart that ran toward Allah when there was nowhere else left to run. That door is never locked. So if today the earth feels narrow around you, take the lesson Ka'b spent his whole life teaching: there is no refuge from Allah except in Allah. Tell Him the truth. Wait at His door. The relief comes at the hour you are certain you cannot survive another night of it. O Allah, You are the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful, who forgave the three when the wide earth had closed upon them; turn to us as You turned to them, save us by our honesty as You saved Ka'b, and write us among the truthful who are gathered with Your Messenger ﷺ. Ameen.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 89: the story of Ka'b ibn Malik (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (9:117, 9:118, 9:95-96), verified via quran.ai.

The Seerah · 90 of 104

The Expedition of Tabuk, Part 3

Who gave everything, and who gave nothing

There is no battle at Tabuk. Dr. Yasir Qadhi keeps returning to this, almost as a refrain, because it is the strangest thing about the whole campaign: the seerah has spent week after week here and is not yet half finished, and in all of it not two armies ever meet. The Romans do not come. No swords are crossed. And yet these are some of the richest weeks of the entire story, because Tabuk was never really an expedition against Rome. It was a sieve. The call goes out for the hardest, hottest, longest march of the Prophet's ﷺ life, the army they would name Jaysh al-Usra, the army of difficulty, and the moment that call goes out it begins to sort the people of Madinah into what they actually are. Not by tribe, not by wealth, but by what the heart does when it is asked to give.

So the events of these days do not arrive in a tidy line. The Sheikh tells them the way the histories preserve them, as a cluster of smaller incidents, each one a small mirror held up to a particular kind of soul. Watch who empties his house into the cause and who empties his imagination of excuses. Watch a man carry a heap of gold across the city on his shoulder, and another invent a fear so flimsy it embarrasses the page. Watch grown believers weep because they were too poor to be allowed to come. The same city, the same call, and a distance between the two responses that the Sheikh measures with a phrase he uses more than once: as wide as the heavens and the earth.

The day Umar tried to beat Abu Bakr

The Prophet ﷺ stood and asked the people for charity, and he kept asking, and something rose in Umar ibn al-Khattab, radiyallahu anhu, that he had never quite managed to feel before. Today, he thought, is the day I beat Abu Bakr. He went home and gathered half of everything he owned and brought it back.

Sit with how much that is. There were no salaries then. The whole architecture of a monthly paycheck, of money arriving on a known day and arriving again two weeks later, is a modern thing that has quietly reshaped how we think about giving. For the companions, wealth came in unpredictable bursts: a dinar today, then nothing for weeks, then perhaps a hundred from a single transaction and then silence again for years. To hand over half, in a world like that, was to hand over half of an unknown and unrepeatable future. The Prophet ﷺ saw the bag, knew Umar was not a rich man, and asked him gently what he had left for his family. The same amount again, Umar answered. Half for them, half for Allah. There is a tenderness in the question itself, the Sheikh notes: we are not asked to give beyond our means, and the family has the first claim.

Then Abu Bakr, radiyallahu anhu, came, carrying a bag of his own, and he was no richer than Umar. The Prophet ﷺ asked him the same question. What did you leave your family? And Abu Bakr said the line that ended the contest before it had truly begun. I have left them Allah and His Messenger. The bag held everything. And Umar said to himself, by Allah, I will never beat you at anything.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi stops here on something easy to rush past, because it is, by his telling, a mentality the Muslims have very largely lost. The companions monitored one another. Not out of envy, but as runners in a race, each straining to see how much the next man prayed and fasted and gave, so as not to be left behind on the way to Allah. The Ansar would complain, half in awe, that the wealthier emigrants prayed as they prayed and fasted as they fasted but gave in charity what the Ansar had nothing to match. That is competition, but a competition turned entirely upward. We do this instinctively for the dunya, the Sheikh says: we bend over backward to win the promotion, to be noticed, to be top in our small niche, and we make sure everyone sees us doing it. Allah aims that very same hunger at something infinitely better. The Qur'an does not merely permit it; it commands it, using the exact verb for a footrace:

Race [i.e., compete] toward forgiveness from your Lord and a Garden whose width is like the width of the heavens and earth, prepared for those who believed in Allah and His messengers. That is the bounty of Allah which He gives to whom He wills, and Allah is the possessor of great bounty.Qur'an 57:21

And here is the detail the Sheikh sharpens to a point. That racing verb, the Qur'an reserves for the next world. It is never aimed at this one. When the subject is the dunya, the language goes soft and slow: when the Friday prayer is finished, it says, disperse and seek of the bounty of Allah, walk away gently. Run to Jannah; walk to the dunya. Abu Bakr's measure, the Sheikh is careful to add, is his alone. It is an example our religion holds up, not a rule the rest of us are bound to meet.
