Even Umar did not reach it, and the general command remains to give in balance, neither extravagant nor stingy. But the racing itself, the upward straining, the watching of one another not to envy but to be spurred, that is the lesson, and it is one we should grieve having mislaid.

A pile of gold on his shoulder

Uthman ibn Affan, radiyallahu anhu, had come to Madinah years before with nothing but the clothes on his back, and through patient buying and selling had built himself into one of the richest men in the city. The call for Tabuk happened to fall just as a great caravan of his returned from Syria, the whole of it financed by him. He did not hesitate. He sold the entire thing on the spot, in the marketplace, the moment it arrived, camels and saddles and all the goods upon them, lock, stock, and barrel, to the buyers gathered there, and walked away with around a thousand gold coins. A fortune in any age, and an almost unimaginable one in his. He had no bag for it. So he gathered the coins into the fold of his thawb, the heavy real wool of it bearing the weight where cotton would have torn, hoisted that load onto his shoulder, and carried it across Madinah, the one city on the face of the earth where a man could walk with a fortune on his back and fear no one. No guard. No escort. This was the Madinah of the Prophet ﷺ.

He poured it out in front of the Prophet ﷺ, a literal heap of gold on the ground, and the Prophet ﷺ began turning the coins over with his hand, and said the words the companions would still be quoting some thirty years later: nothing Uthman does after today will harm him. By another sound account it was a pledge made aloud, before the caravan had even come home. As the Prophet ﷺ kept calling, who will give, who will give, Uthman stood and said he would give a hundred camels with all their loads, and the Prophet ﷺ kept calling, and he raised it to two hundred, and the Prophet ﷺ kept calling, until he said three hundred camels with everything on them, the very number the returning caravan would turn out to hold, as though he had given it away before it arrived. And the Prophet ﷺ came down from where he stood and said: Uthman has no sin after what he has done today. Decades later, when the rebels surrounded his house demanding answers for trivial things, it was to this day that the aged Uthman pointed, and the companions present confirmed it: the Prophet ﷺ had indeed said it.

He was not alone in his largesse. Abdur-Rahman ibn Awf, who had likewise arrived in Madinah with empty hands and built a fortune from nothing, brought a great quantity of grain. And the Sheikh pauses here to press a point our own times badly need to hear. There is nothing wrong in Islam with wealth. Nothing at all. Not if you are the kind of person who pours it out like this. Money in the hand of a generous believer is a blessing and an instrument, and a man like Abdur-Rahman is precisely the role model for it. The danger was never the gold. The danger is the heart that closes around it and will not let it go.

Two handfuls of dates

On the other side of the very same fundraiser stood the hypocrites, who gave nothing, and could not bear that anyone else gave anything. They were not merely stingy; they worked to discourage others from giving, and they mocked whatever was given. If a man gave a great deal, they sneered that he was showing off. If a man gave a little, they scoffed at how useless so small a gift could be. Sometimes openly, in front of the crowd, and sometimes with a nudge and a wink passed between them across the gathering. It is, the Sheikh observes drily, the same sickness still alive among us, the watching of other people's charity in order to belittle it.

There was a man of the Ansar who had nothing to give. So he spent an entire night at the cheapest, most thankless labor there is, hauling water up from a well by hand to sell it, and by morning he had earned two small handfuls of dates for the whole night's work. He brought one handful to the Prophet ﷺ. Here, he said, is half of everything my night was worth; the other half is for my family. And a hypocrite stood up, in front of the whole crowd, and sneered: does Allah really have any need of these few dates? And a second one scoffed that the poor man needed the dates far more than Allah did, so why was he making a spectacle of his poverty, why did he not give the charity to himself. You can feel, the Sheikh says, exactly how that landed on a man who had just poured out his entire night.

It was over this precise insult that Allah sent down the verse the hypocrites should have feared above all others. They imagined they were the ones mocking; Allah turned the mockery back upon them and promised them a punishment to match:

Those who criticize the contributors among the believers concerning [their] charities and [criticize] the ones who find nothing [to spend] except their effort, so they ridicule them - Allah will ridicule them, and they will have a painful punishment.Qur'an 9:79

The handful of dates was never the point. The whole of it was the effort behind the handful, a night of a poor man's sweat, and Allah saw that effort, and weighed it, and defended it by name in a Book that would be recited until the end of time. There was another man too, whose name the histories barely keep, who came and said: Messenger of Allah, I have no money to give you, but I give you my honor, my dignity, that I will defend you against any who slander you. And the Prophet ﷺ said: I have accepted your charity. Even that which cost no coin at all, offered sincerely, was received and recorded.

Permit me, and do not tempt me

If they would not give their money, they would not give their bodies either, and so the excuses came, in a steady and inventive stream, some offered before the march and some saved up for the return. The Sheikh lingers on the most absurd of them. The Prophet ﷺ turned to al-Jadd ibn Qays, one of the chiefs of the hypocrites, and invited him plainly and warmly: O Jadd, will you not come out with us to fight the Romans? And al-Jadd asked to be excused, and gave this as his reason: by Allah, my own people know that I have a weakness for women, and I fear that if I lay eyes on the women of the Romans I will lose my head and not be able to control myself, so let me stay behind. A man inventing a fear of foreign women in order to dodge a sacred struggle. The Prophet ﷺ simply turned away from him and let him sit.

And Allah answered that exact excuse, almost word for word:

And among them is he who says, "Permit me [to remain at home] and do not put me to trial." Unquestionably, into trial they have fallen. And indeed, Hell will encompass the disbelievers.Qur'an 9:49

It is straight into the trial that they have already fallen, head first, by the very lie they told to escape one. The man who begged not to be tempted had walked himself into the deepest temptation there is, the abandoning of the Messenger ﷺ at his hour of need. And Surah at-Tawbah, densely woven with the hypocrites' own words and moods, draws the whole picture of them:

Those who remained behind rejoiced in their staying [at home] after [the departure of] the Messenger of Allah and disliked to strive with their wealth and their lives in the cause of Allah and said, "Do not go forth in the heat." Say, "The fire of Hell is more intense in heat" - if they would but understand.Qur'an 9:81
So let them laugh a little and [then] weep much as recompense for what they used to earn.Qur'an 9:82

Do not march out in this heat, they told one another, pleased with their own cleverness, lounging at home while the believers prepared to bake in the desert. And the answer comes back with a terrible quiet: the fire of Hell is hotter, if only they understood. They were laughing their way toward the very thing they were so carefully trying to avoid.

And there is a tenderness folded into this section that is easy to miss entirely. Allah, in the midst of exposing the liars, turns and gently corrects the Prophet ﷺ himself:

Allah has pardoned you, [O Muhammad, but] why did you give them permission [to remain behind]? [You should not have] until it was evident to you who were truthful and you knew [who were] the liars.Qur'an 9:43

The Prophet ﷺ was so merciful, so disinclined to think ill of anyone, that he accepted every excuse the moment it was offered. The Sheikh reads these words not as a rebuke at all, or barely one: notice that the pardon comes first, before the question. It is the language of a Lord protecting His beloved ﷺ from men who were taking advantage of his mercy, a gentle steadying hand laid on the one who was too generous with his trust for his own people's good.

The ones who wept to be left behind

Now hold the hypocrites in one hand and these people in the other, because the contrast is the whole point. There was a group of believers, perhaps seven, perhaps a dozen, the histories do not fix the number, whom the sources came to call al-bakkaun, the weepers. They had neither the money to equip themselves nor a camel of their own to ride, and the Prophet ﷺ had already spent everything buying mounts for others until there was nothing left. They kept coming back to the marketplace, day after day, to the very last day before the army moved, still hoping for a beast to carry them. And at last the Prophet ﷺ had to tell them the truth: he could find nothing left to carry them on. The Qur'an itself records the moment they turned away:

Nor [is there blame] upon those who, when they came to you for you to take them along, you said, "I can find nothing upon which to carry you." They turned back while their eyes overflowed with tears out of grief that they could not find something to spend [for the cause of Allah].Qur'an 9:92

One man, the Sheikh says, was overjoyed at a false excuse. Another wept at a true one. The distance between those two states of faith is the heavens and the earth. The hypocrite was forgiven nothing and rejoiced; the weeper was excused entirely, blameless, free to stay with a clear conscience, and he wept for the opportunity he was missing. Between the two there is no measuring.

And their longing was not wasted. The Prophet ﷺ told the army, while it was deep in the march, that back in Madinah there were men who shared in every single step they took, every valley they crossed, who were with them the whole way. They asked, astonished: even while they sit in Madinah? Even while they sit in Madinah, he said, because they were held back by nothing but a genuine excuse. Their intention was so pure that it earned them the full reward of a march they never made. Make the intention, the Sheikh urges, even for the good you cannot afford to do. If I had the means, I would, said with a sincere heart, is itself written down as a good deed, and how many of those we let slip away unspoken.

And some closed the gap with their own feet. Abu Khaythama, after the army had gone, walked into his cool garden where his wives had spread a rug in the shade of the date palms and sprinkled water on it to cool it and laid out a meal, and the sight of his own comfort struck him like a blow: what am I doing here in this ease while the Messenger of Allah ﷺ bakes in the desert? He would not even sit down. He saddled his camel that instant and rode out alone to chase the army. Abu Dharr's camel failed him, fell sick or was not returned to him, and rather than stay he hoisted his baggage, the load meant for a camel's back, onto his own shoulders and set out on foot, alone, walking for days, until on the second or third day a lone trudging figure in the heat resolved, as the army watched, into a man on foot carrying a camel's burden. When the Prophet ﷺ saw each of them coming in the distance he hoped aloud that it would be them, because he was always watching, always knew exactly who was missing and who was present. Of Abu Dharr, walking by himself, he said words that would come true to the letter many decades later: may Allah have mercy on Abu Dharr; he walks alone, he will die alone, and he will be raised alone.

And so it was. Abu Dharr held views on wealth and its hoarding so austere that they brought him into friction with much of the community, until in the time of Uthman he withdrew to live in the wilderness with his wife and an aged servant. When he lay dying his wife wept that they had not even cloth enough to shroud him, and he comforted her: do not cry, for I heard the Prophet ﷺ say that one of us would die in the wilderness and a band of believers would pray over him, and every other man in that gathering has died among his family or in a city, and I alone am left. Go, he told her, and sit my body by the caravan road; someone will come. And out of the empty desert a great caravan came, and among its riders was Ibn Mas'ud, who wept to recognize the prophecy he had heard with his own ears, and they gave of their own garments for the shroud and buried the lone companion and prayed over him there. He walked alone, and he died alone, and a band of the faithful was sent to lay him down, exactly as it had been foretold.

The masjid built to divide

The hypocrites who could not spare a single coin for Tabuk somehow found both the funds and the energy to build, before the Prophet ﷺ had even departed, an entire masjid of their own, finished and standing, within walking distance of his. Their enthusiasm and their wealth, the Sheikh observes, were never truly exhausted; they were simply never for Allah and His Messenger. They were for their own circle, their own elitism, a place where they could gather and say what they liked without the believers nearby to check them.

And behind it lay a darker plot. Years before, a Madinan chief named Abu Amir, called the monk for his pre-Islamic Christian devotion, a man the city had once revered for his apparent integrity, had wanted Madinah's leadership for himself. When the Prophet ﷺ arrived and the people gave their hearts to him instead, Abu Amir's true face showed: it had been the fame he loved, not the faith. He refused Islam, fled to Makkah after Badr, and at last to the court of Caesar in Rome, an Arab willing to live among the Romans and scheme against his own. And from there he wrote to his old friend Abdullah ibn Ubayy: build me a base in Madinah, a foothold from within, so that Roman troops and I together can move against the Muslims. The new masjid was meant to be that headquarters, with Abdullah ibn Ubayy to lead it. The Prophet ﷺ, the Sheikh notes, made dua against Abu Amir that he would die a stranger, far from family and friend, and so he did, alone in Rome.

So when the builders came to the Prophet ﷺ, sweetly, and asked him to come and pray in their new masjid and bless its opening, pleading that it was only for the weak and the elderly and for the rainy nights when the walk to his masjid was hard, he sensed that something was wrong. He did not yet know Abu Amir's letter, but he knew the timing, the money spent on a needless masjid within sight of his own while the army went unfunded, and the recent verses exposing these very men. He would not say yes, and in his gentleness he would not bluntly say no. He told them only that he was busy now, preparing to travel, and that he would see to it, if Allah willed, when he returned. He never said he would come. The masjid stood and operated for about a month while he was at Tabuk, and he never once set foot in it. On the journey home, Jibril came down with the verdict:

And [there are] those [hypocrites] who took for themselves a mosque for causing harm and disbelief and division among the believers and as a station for whoever had warred against Allah and His Messenger before. And they will surely swear, "We intended only the best." And Allah testifies that indeed they are liars.Qur'an 9:107

Allah named the place Masjid al-Dirar, the masjid of harm, and laid bare its four poisons in a single verse: harm, disbelief, division among the believers, and a hidden station of ambush for one who had already declared war on Allah and His Messenger, which was Abu Amir. They will swear they meant only good, Allah said, and He Himself testified that they were lying. The Prophet ﷺ sent men ahead to tear the building down and burn it to the ground before he reached the city, and so the last great organized scheme of the hypocrites ended in ashes.

When a masjid is not a masjid

Allah did not leave the matter at a condemnation. He set a second masjid against the first, in the very next verse:

Do not stand [for prayer] within it - ever. A mosque founded on righteousness from the first day is more worthy for you to stand in. Within it are men who love to purify themselves; and Allah loves those who purify themselves.Qur'an 9:108

The companions tied this praise partly to the masjid of Quba, whose people were known for their unusual care with cleanliness; when they were asked why Allah had singled them out for loving to purify themselves, they explained their custom of always using water. And a sound report has two companions disputing in the Prophet's ﷺ own lifetime which masjid the verse meant, taking the question to him directly, and he swore by Allah that it was his own masjid in Madinah. The Sheikh gives the reading the scholars settled on as the one that holds both: every masjid raised from its first day upon taqwa is the masjid Allah is praising here, and every masjid built to do harm is the masjid He is condemning. A building of brick and prayer-niches is only ever as honest as the hearts that raised it.

And from this Dr. Yasir Qadhi draws a warning so urgent that he states it twice. Yes, in principle, a masjid can still today become a masjid of harm, built by the wrong people for the wrong ends. But that is a verdict for Allah to pass, not a label for us to throw. He has seen it happen too many times: a community splits, a second masjid opens down the road, and the first congregation brands the second a masjid of dirar simply because its people disagreed with them. That, he says plainly, is arrogance, and it is dangerous, and it is deeply un-Islamic. To disagree with a scholar, even to leave and pray behind another, even to build your own masjid because you prefer a different teacher, is a believer's right, and it does not make him a hypocrite. Breaking away from a group of people is not breaking away from the Prophet ﷺ. To call that second masjid dirar is to accuse its founders of plotting outright kufr, the way Allah accused Abu Amir, and if you truly believed that, the Sheikh says, you would be obliged to go and burn it down, as the Prophet ﷺ did. Since you will not, you do not believe it. So live and let live. If they are sincere, their masjid will flourish; if they are not, Allah will expose them in this world before the next. Do your own work, and leave others to theirs.

What this day asks of our faith

There is no battle at Tabuk, and that is exactly why it asks so much of us. A battle would let us off easily; we could admire the courage of men long dead and feel that the test was theirs, not ours. But Tabuk does not test the sword arm. It tests the thing every one of us carries into every ordinary day: what the heart reaches for when it is asked to give something it would rather keep.

Watch the two rows it sorted the city into, because they have not changed in fourteen centuries. In one row stands the man who carried a fortune of gold across Madinah on his shoulder, and beside him the man who labored a whole night for two handfuls of dates and gave one, and beside him the weepers who had nothing at all and turned away in tears that they could not give more. Different wealth, identical hearts: every one of them emptying himself toward Allah and grieving only that he could not pour out more. And in the other row stand the comfortable and the clever, who rejoiced at staying home, invented fears, sneered at the poor, and built a masjid to divide, laughing their way toward a fire hotter than any desert. You already know, reading it, which row you would want your name in. The whole difficulty of the religion is to live so that it would actually be there.

So take three things from a campaign that never drew a sword. First, learn again to race upward. The companions watched one another's prayer and fasting and giving the way we watch one another's careers, not to envy but to be pulled forward, because Allah aimed the verb for a footrace at forgiveness and Jannah and never once at this world. Run to Him; let the dunya be the thing you merely walk toward. Second, make the intention even for the good you cannot reach. The weepers shared in every step of a march they never marched, because the heart that sincerely says I would, if I could, has already done something Allah writes down. Stop letting your missed chances pass in silence. And third, guard your tongue from the easy cruelty of judging another's offering, whether it is a man's small charity or a community's separate masjid. The handful of dates was defended by name in the Qur'an, and the rush to brand a rival masjid as a masjid of harm was called arrogance by the Sheikh who has watched it tear communities apart. Give what you can without shame, never shame what another gives, and leave the weighing of hearts to the One who alone can see them.

May Allah make our hearts of the row that empties itself for Him and not the row that hoards, grant us a truthful intention even for the good we cannot afford, keep us from ever mocking a small gift or shaming a sincere one, and gather us under the banner of His beloved ﷺ among those who answered when He called. May He send His peace and His blessings upon Muhammad ﷺ, who spent everything he had and asked his own price be paid in full, and who watched the road behind him for every soul that was missing.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 90: the expedition to Tabuk, part 3 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (57:21, 9:79, 9:49, 9:81, 9:82, 9:92, 9:43, 9:107, 9:108), verified via quran.ai.

The Seerah · 91 of 104

The expedition to Tabuk, part 4

The army finally leaves, and the road teaches

For three weeks and more the seerah has circled the Battle of Tabuk without once leaving Madinah. We have heard the call to march in the punishing heat of late summer, watched the hypocrites peel away one excuse at a time, and sat with the three sincere men who simply stayed behind and would pay for it. All of it happened inside the city, before a single camel turned its head north. Today the army finally moves, and here is the thing to hold onto as it does: Tabuk is barely a battle at all. There is no clash of swords waiting at the end of this road. What waits is a string of small, luminous incidents strung along the journey itself, and the lessons come not from combat but from the road. This is not like Badr or Uhud, one great event told from many angles. It is many little events, and each one is its own lecture.

So watch what happens between the city gate and the well of Tabuk. A young man is left behind and breaks down in tears. A companion finally maneuvers his camel close enough to ask the Prophet ﷺ the only question that had ever truly kept him awake. A Christian emperor sends an old man across the desert with a letter in one hand and three secret tasks in the other. None of it is a battle. All of it is the seerah at its most human, and at its most instructive.

The one left to guard the house

Whenever the Prophet ﷺ left Madinah, he appointed someone to run the city in his absence, and for Tabuk that man was Muhammad ibn Maslamah. But this journey was different in scale. It would stretch close to two months there and back, the longest the Muslims had ever undertaken, and a household cannot be left to fend for itself that long. So the Prophet ﷺ turned to Ali ibn Abi Talib, his cousin, the boy he had raised in his own home, now his son-in-law and the husband of his daughter, and told him to stay behind and take charge of the family, to see to their needs and protect them while the army was away in the far north.

The hypocrites pounced. This was meat for them. They began putting it about that Ali had been left behind because he was a burden, no real fighter, and the Prophet ﷺ did not want him slowing the army down. Of all the men in Madinah to accuse of cowardice, they chose Ali, whose bravery was already a byword. He was a young man, and a young man feels this kind of taunt in his bones. It cut him deeply, and he did what a man stung in his honor does: he put on his armor, took his sword in hand, and rode out hard after the army until he caught it outside the city. Messenger of Allah, he said, are you leaving me behind with the women and children, while the hypocrites mock me and say you only left me because I am not fit to fight?

Dr. Yasir Qadhi asks you to feel the irony before you draw anything else from it. Here is Ali, weeping for the chance to march into danger, so desperate to risk his life for Allah that he chases the column down to beg his way in. And there are the hypocrites, snug at home behind their false excuses, the men who would not march for anything, and yet they are the ones with the gall to call Ali a coward. That, the Sheikh notes, is the permanent signature of hypocrisy in every age: they accuse others of the very crime they themselves are guilty of. The man who will not fight names the brave man a coward. It is a tell, and once you learn to read it you will see it everywhere.

Like Harun was to Musa

The Prophet ﷺ consoled him with a sentence that has echoed down fourteen centuries. Are you not content, he said, that you are to me as Harun was to Musa, except that there is no prophet after me? And then, to settle the wound the hypocrites had opened, he said it without ornament: they are liars. I left you behind only to take charge of those I have left behind, meaning my own family. Do not give the hypocrites another thought.

This is one of the most sensitive narrations in the whole seerah, and Sheikh Yasir slows right down to handle it with care, because of all the hadith that touch Ali, this is the one a certain group leans on hardest to argue that Ali was meant to lead the ummah after the Prophet ﷺ. So the honest path is to hold two things at once, firmly. The first: as Sunni Muslims, we affirm every single virtue this hadith confers on Ali, and we affirm it gladly, because it sits in our own most authentic books. The honor is real, and we do not wish it smaller. But the second is just as real: we read the narration whole, in its own words and its own moment, and not as a slogan torn loose from both.

Read whole, what does it say? Harun was Musa's brother, his closest helper, the one nearest to him in all the world, and the comparison the Prophet ﷺ reaches for is exactly that closeness, that brotherhood. This is how near you are to me. That is the point of the words, a statement of love and kinship, not a charter of political succession. And look at the proof sitting inside the very same incident: the Prophet ﷺ did not leave Ali in charge of Madinah at all. He left Muhammad ibn Maslamah over the city. Ali was chosen for one role, charge of the household, precisely because he was family. Who do you trust with your wives and children for two months: a stranger, or your own son-in-law? The Prophet ﷺ said as much in the same breath: I have left you over my family. Faithful to his telling, Sheikh Yasir keeps the blessing and the boundary together. The honor for Ali stands, undiminished. The conclusion some try to build on top of it simply does not follow from the words.

The mound called farewell

The army moved out past a small hill on the northern edge of Madinah, a place nearly every Muslim has heard named and almost none realize is an actual spot on the map. It is called Thaniyyat al-Wada, which means, near enough, the mound of farewell, or the pass of goodbye. It earned the name honestly. When a caravan was setting out to the north, the families would walk alongside it as far as this little rise, embrace their travelers there, wave them off, and turn back home from its crest. It was the last place you saw someone before the desert took them. So the army climbed past it and went on.

And here Dr. Yasir Qadhi pauses to correct a memory that almost every Muslim carries, and carries lovingly. There is a famous song, beloved across the entire ummah: Tala al-badru alayna, the full moon has risen over us, from Thaniyyat al-Wada. It is placed, almost universally, at the Hijrah, the moment the Prophet ﷺ first arrived in Madinah and the city poured out to greet him. The trouble is that it cannot belong there, and the Sheikh gives two decisive reasons. First, the geography is wrong: the mound lies to the north, but at the Hijrah the Prophet ﷺ was arriving from the south, up from Makkah, so the city could not have lined it to receive him. And second, the content is wrong: at the Hijrah the great majority of Madinah was not yet Muslim, so there was no city of believers waiting to sing of the rising moon. The welcome that song describes, a whole Muslim city rising in joy to greet him ﷺ coming home from the north, fits this moment, the return from Tabuk, and very little else. The song is true and beautiful. It is simply pinned to the wrong day.

Past the mound, the Prophet ﷺ halted the march and reorganized the entire army, assigning the battalions, naming their commanders, distributing the flags. The number of men is a guess, the Sheikh reminds us, because no one counted; some books say thirty thousand, a figure he treats with healthy skepticism, but even at half that it was the largest force the Muslims had ever fielded. Al-Waqidi devotes pages to which tribe followed which leader, and the Sheikh waves the catalogue away as more than we need, but he keeps the lesson buried inside it. The Prophet ﷺ organized the men by their tribes. Islam came to bury blind partisanship, the fanaticism that made a man fight for his clan whether it was right or wrong; and yet it does not erase the natural affinity of people who share a town, a tongue, a homeland. You meet someone from your own city in a strange land and feel a warmth that rests on nothing but shared origin, and that warmth is no crime. It is how human beings are made, and a wise commander, far from fighting it, puts it to work: men stand firmer beside the brothers they know.

The question Mu'adh fell sick over

On the march, after the dawn prayer one morning, the sun began to climb and the riders began to nod off on their camels. As the men dozed, their mounts wandered apart, each drifting after whatever grazing caught its eye, until the tight column had spread loose across the desert. Mu'adh ibn Jabal was trying to keep close to the Prophet's ﷺ camel when his own animal lurched; he yanked the reins, the beast reared back, and the sudden movement spooked the Prophet's ﷺ mount and sent it bolting forward. The Prophet ﷺ had his turban wound across his face against the sun. He drew it back to see who had startled his camel, saw that it was Mu'adh, and instead of any rebuke simply called him near. And Mu'adh tells it himself: I drew so close that our two saddles were almost touching.

Sit with the sweetness of this. Mu'adh suddenly finds himself alone with the Prophet ﷺ in the open desert, the rest of the army scattered far behind, just the two of them riding stirrup to stirrup. And his first instinct is not small talk. It is to seize, with perfect manners, the chance he had been waiting for. Give me permission to ask you something, he said. There is a question I have turned over until it made me ill: tell me of a deed that will admit me into Jannah, something only you can tell me, so that I need never ask anyone else.

The Prophet ﷺ marveled at the question. You have asked about something tremendous, he said, and repeated it three times, for he would repeat the weighty things so they would sink in. And yet, he went on, it is easy for whomever Allah makes it easy. Then he gave the answer, the whole religion folded into a single breath: you worship Allah alone and associate nothing with Him, you establish the prayer, you give the zakah, and you hold to that until you die. And then, says Sheikh Yasir, he offered Mu'adh the entire architecture of the faith in one image. Shall I show you the head of the whole matter, its pillar, and the summit of its hump? Its head is Islam itself; its pillar is the prayer; and the summit of its hump is jihad, the striving in the path of Allah. Remember where they were when these words were spoken: riding north toward Tabuk, expecting a Roman army to rise over the horizon. Every syllable of it landed with the weight of where they were going.

And he ﷺ sealed it with the saying the enemies of Islam love to twist out of all recognition: I have been commanded to fight the people until they testify to the oneness of Allah and that Muhammad is His Messenger, and they establish the prayer and give the zakah; and if they do that, their lives and their property are protected from me. The Sheikh keeps the context the distorters always cut away. This is the Prophet ﷺ explaining, to a companion riding beside him, why he is marching to Tabuk against a hostile power massed on the frontier: an answer to the question why are we on this road, not a standing command to make war on the whole world until the end of time. And the proof that the scholars understood it exactly this way is written across the centuries that followed: the empires of Islam, the Umayyads, the Abbasids, the Ottomans, kept settled borders and lived at peace across them. They did not read this hadith as an order of perpetual conquest, because it never was one.

Twenty days, and a morning sermon

The army reached Tabuk and stayed twenty days. Throughout the whole of it, the Prophet ﷺ shortened his prayers, praying the four-unit prayers as two, and from this single well-attested fact a sprawling discussion in Islamic law was born: how long may a traveler keep shortening his prayer before he counts as settled? The Sheikh lays the schools out honestly, none claiming certainty. The Malikis and Shafiis hold to four days; the Hanafis to fifteen; some read the twenty days at Tabuk itself as the ceiling. And a fourth view, which Sheikh Yasir finds strongest and which Ibn Taymiyyah championed, sets no fixed number at all: shortening tracks your circumstance, not a square on the calendar. The genuine traveler, living out of a saddlebag with no settled home around him, may go on shortening for as long as that lasts; the man comfortably at home in his own house, even for two days, is no traveler at all. And the twenty days at Tabuk prove no ceiling for an ordinary trip, because Tabuk was a state of war, where every dawn might bring an attack and no one knew the day of return. That uncertainty is its own category, and you cannot stretch it to cover the man who flies somewhere knowing he will be there exactly five days.

On their very first morning at Tabuk, after the dawn prayer, the Prophet ﷺ stood and delivered a sermon. The long chain that carries it whole is weak, and Sheikh Yasir says so frankly, but he reads it out anyway, for parts of it are firmly authentic and the whole is a window onto how he ﷺ actually spoke. It is short, sharp lines, each one a lecture you could live a year inside. The most truthful speech is the Book of Allah. The firmest handhold is the word of taqwa. The best of ways is the way of Ibrahim, and the best of paths is the Sunnah of Muhammad ﷺ. The best of affairs are those most firmly settled, and the worst of them are the newly invented matters.

And on it runs. The worst excuse is the one offered at the moment of death, and the most evil of regrets is the regret on the Day of Judgment. The higher hand, the one that gives, is better than the lower hand that takes. A little that suffices you is better than a great deal that distracts you. The richest wealth is the contentment of the heart, and the noblest provision a traveler can carry is taqwa. He ﷺ closed it the way mercy closes everything: O Allah, forgive me and forgive my ummah, and I ask Allah's forgiveness for me and for all of you.

The king found among the cattle

Tabuk turned out to be a campaign of treaties rather than a clash of armies. While the main force held the camp, the Prophet ﷺ sent Khalid ibn al-Walid north with a few hundred companions to Dumat al-Jandal, near what is today the city of Sakaka in al-Jawf, hard against the Syrian frontier. The tribe there was Kindah, a great and ancient Christian Arab people, originally of Yemen, famed for their courage, with long-standing ties to Caesar, and prestigious enough that, rarely among the Arabs, they called their chieftain a king, a malik. Their king bore a name strange to our tongues: Ukaydir. And the Prophet ﷺ told Khalid exactly how he would find him: not at the head of his army, but away from it, out among the cattle.

And so it fell out, with an almost comic precision. One night the king's herd came battering against the palace door until his wife could not sleep and demanded he deal with the racket. Ukaydir went down with three or four servants, judged the beasts were simply hungry, and led them out himself to graze before dawn, no army, no guard. Khalid came upon him there with three hundred and fifty companions and took him without a fight. Ukaydir did not accept Islam at that moment, though it is said he did later; but he agreed to break his alliance with Rome, pay the jizyah to the Prophet ﷺ, and never raise a hand against the Muslims.

Three or four more northern tribes followed his example, and this, the Sheikh suggests, may be the deepest political wisdom of the entire expedition, the real reason Allah willed it. A vast Muslim army sitting at Tabuk made the calculation plain to every frontier tribe at once. Men like Ukaydir could not hope to fight such a force alone, and so one after another the northern Arabs cut their ties to Rome and came to terms with the Prophet ﷺ. The northern door was quietly sealed against the empire, without a major battle being fought. Ukaydir later sent gifts to show his new loyalty, among them a robe woven through with gold thread, so fine the companions had never seen its like and circled around it in wonder. The Prophet ﷺ saw their amazement and said, gently: are you impressed by this? The handkerchiefs of Sa'd ibn Mu'adh in Jannah are finer than this. Sa'd, who had died of his wound after Banu Qurayzah and whose funeral the angels attended, owns napkins in Paradise that put this emperor's robe to shame.

The envoy with three secret tasks

The story Dr. Yasir Qadhi saves for last is one of his favorites in the whole seerah, and he tells it with relish. Heraclius, the emperor of Rome, sent a second letter to the Prophet ﷺ at Tabuk, carried by an old man of the Christian Arab tribe of Tanukh, from Hims in Syria, who by the time he related the tale was nearly a hundred years old. But before the envoy ever set out, Heraclius did something behind locked doors. He gathered his patriarchs and bishops, bolted the chamber, and tested them. You have seen, he said, how far this man's affair has spread, and our own scriptures foretell that what is under my feet will become his. So why do we not follow him, or at the least make peace with him, for he is a true prophet? The clergymen recoiled in horror and bolted for the door, desperate to tell the people that Caesar meant to abandon Christianity. He called them back and soothed them: it was only a test, to see how firm you are in your faith. He had no intention of converting, and never did. He simply wanted to know who this Prophet ﷺ truly was.

So Heraclius found a sharp Arabic speaker with a good memory, the Tanukhi, and gave him the letter along with three secret tasks. First: does this man mention the earlier letters I have sent him? Second: when he reads my letter, does he say anything of night, anything of darkness? And third: look at his back, and see whether there is something there that should not be on the back of any man. The envoy rode south to the well of Tabuk, where the Prophet ﷺ sat among his companions. And here is a detail that says everything: the Tanukhi could not pick out the leader. The Prophet ﷺ wore nothing to set himself above the men around him, no finery, no mark of rank, and the envoy had to ask which of them was the chief. They pointed him out. He came and set the letter down, and the Prophet ﷺ left it unopened. First he invited the man himself to the faith of his father Ibrahim: you are an Arab, embrace the religion of your forefather. The envoy answered with the practiced logic of a diplomat: I am the ambassador of a nation that has its own religion, and I cannot change my faith while on its business; I will go back and consider the matter. The Prophet ﷺ laughed softly, and recited the verse Allah had revealed for exactly this kind of moment, that guidance was never his to hand out, but Allah's alone:

Indeed, [O Muḥammad], you do not guide whom you like, but Allāh guides whom He wills. And He is most knowing of the [rightly] guided.Qur'an 28:56

Then, with the letter still unopened on the ground, the Prophet ﷺ told the envoy what three kings had done with the letters he had sent them. Kisra, the emperor of Persia, tore my letter to pieces, so Allah will tear his kingdom to pieces. The Negus's son tore my letter, so Allah will tear his kingdom apart, and the Sheikh pauses to be exact, for this was not the righteous Negus over whom the Prophet ﷺ had prayed the funeral prayer, but his son, after whom a civil war dissolved that Abyssinian kingdom. But your master Heraclius kept my letter safe, so his rule will endure as long as there is good in it. The envoy started. This was the first sign, the earlier letters, named exactly as Heraclius had said to watch for. He drew an arrow from his quiver and quietly carved a note into the leather so he would not forget.

Then Mu'adh read the emperor's letter aloud, and inside it was a riddle. You invite me, Heraclius had written, to a Paradise as wide as the heavens and the earth. So where, then, is the Fire? The Prophet ﷺ answered without a heartbeat's pause: subhanallah, where does the night go when the day comes? The second sign, night and darkness, spoken just as the emperor had predicted. The envoy carved his second note. And as the man rose to leave that night, having been hosted by one of the Ansar, the Prophet ﷺ called him back, drew aside his garment from his shoulders, and said: look at what your master sent you to find. Between his shoulder blades was the seal of prophethood, a small raised mark the size of a pigeon's egg, an unusual growth of hair set perfectly between the shoulders. The third sign. The old man of Tanukh went home with all three of his master's questions answered, and the Sheikh leaves him there, at the very edge of belief, a stranger who had come to test the Prophet ﷺ and found, written on his back and spoken from his lips, everything his emperor had been too proud to follow.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to feel a little cheated by a chapter of the seerah with no battle in it, to read past these incidents as filler between the great events. That would be to miss what the road north was put here to teach. Strip Tabuk down and it is not a war story at all; it is a syllabus, and every milestone along the way is asking something of us.

Start with Ali in his armor, weeping to be let into danger, and the hypocrites at home calling him the coward. When people who have plainly failed a test grow loud in accusing you of failing it, recognize the pattern: they are describing themselves. Hypocrisy projects, and the believer's protection against it is simply to know it on sight, so that the accusation, when it comes, tells you nothing about you and everything about the one who threw it. Then sit with how the Prophet ﷺ answered Ali, because that answer carries its own discipline. He honored Ali as Harun was to Musa, a genuine and towering virtue, and he neither shrank it to avoid being misread nor inflated it into a claim no one had made. Love a true thing enough to keep it exactly its true size. That is harder than it sounds; we are forever either embarrassed by a truth or stretching it past what it can bear.

And then there is Mu'adh, riding stirrup to stirrup with the Prophet ﷺ in the empty desert, finally alone with the one person who could answer the question that had made him ill. Ask yourself what you would say if you had that moment. Mu'adh knew; he had carried his question so long it had worn a groove in him: what gets me into Jannah? And the answer was the whole religion in a sentence, worship Allah alone, establish the prayer, give the zakah, and hold to it until you die. Here is the quiet mercy in it: you already have the answer. The deed that admits you to Paradise was handed to you long ago, and the only question is whether you are living it. Get your one question ready, then notice that the answer is already in your hands. And keep, while you are at it, the discipline the Sheikh kept with the hard hadith on that same ride. I have been commanded to fight the people is quoted by the enemies of this religion with its context surgically removed. Keep the context. Truth survives only when we refuse to read it in fragments, and that refusal is owed to every hard thing, ours and others'.

Last, hold the Tanukhi at the edge of belief and the verse the Prophet ﷺ recited to him. The most beloved of all who ever called to Allah wanted that old man to believe, and could not make him; Allah revealed it to His own Messenger as a settled fact, that you do not guide whom you like, but Allah guides whom He wills. There is a deep rest in that for anyone who has poured themselves into calling a person they love toward the good and watched them turn away. The calling is yours; the heart was never yours to turn. Do your part sincerely, then lay the outcome down where it always belonged, with the One who alone is most knowing of the rightly guided. So take the road's own lessons into your ordinary days. Name hypocrisy when it accuses you, and keep your peace. Keep every true thing at its true size. Ask the question you have been carrying, and live the answer you already have. And call to the good with everything in you, then set the heart of the one you love gently back in the hands of its Lord. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, who wore nothing to set himself above his companions and bore the seal of prophethood between his shoulders, keep our hearts firm upon His religion, and gather us in the company of the one we have walked beside these many days.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 91: the expedition to Tabuk, part 4 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translation is from Sahih International (28:56), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry weaker chains, the Sheikh's own weighing of their reports has been followed.

The Seerah · 92 of 104

The expedition to Tabuk, part 5

Twenty days, no battle, and a road that pointed at the future

There was no battle at Tabuk. The army had marched the best part of a month north, into a heat and a hardship that would mark this as the most difficult campaign of the Prophet's ﷺ life, and at the end of the road the enemy never came. The tribes of Ghassan stayed home. The Roman columns never formed. And so the Prophet ﷺ made camp in a place where, on the surface, nothing happened, and stayed twenty days. Dr. Yasir Qadhi gathers the scattered reports of those twenty days and the long journey home, and asks us first to notice that the seerah hands us this stretch not as one connected story but as a scatter of small incidents, with no way of knowing which night any of them fell on. So we tell them as they were handed down: a pool refilled by a hand, a sermon counting five gifts and six signs, a burial in the dark, a road past the ruins of a punished people, and the mockers the Qur'an exposed before they could speak. The point of Tabuk was never the destination. It was the rehearsal, and by the end of these days you will see exactly what the Prophet ﷺ was rehearsing his people for.

Twenty days, and a pool that filled to the brim

We do not know how many marched. The reports say ten thousand, fifteen, twenty, thirty thousand, and the honest answer the Sheikh gives is that there was no register and no one counted. What is known with certainty comes from a different kind of detail, the kind the seerah preserves precisely because no one would invent it. For all twenty days, the Prophet ﷺ prayed his four-rakah prayers shortened to two, the way a traveller prays. Hold onto that, because it carries a quiet ruling that will surface again before this chapter closes.

There was a small pool of water in the area, and he ﷺ had given a plain instruction about it: do not touch it. One or two of the men reached it before he arrived and used it anyway, not out of defiance but out of haste, and when he found what they had done he was angry and rebuked them. They had disobeyed by accident, but they had disobeyed, and a small disobedience is still a disobedience. Then he ﷺ took some water in his hand, rinsed his mouth, and returned it to the pool. That little was all it took. The water rose to the brim, and an army of tens of thousands drank from it for the length of the stay. It is said that a masjid still marks the spot outside Tabuk to this day, Masjid at-Tawbah, with a well at the very place he ﷺ camped, though Sheikh Yasir adds that he has never been there himself and cannot vouch for it. The correction and the mercy, notice, came in the same breath: he rebuked the haste, then fed the whole army from his own rinsed mouthful. That is the texture of his leadership, and it runs through every scene that follows.

Five things given to him alone

One day, after the prayer, he ﷺ stood and told them that Allah had that very day given him five things given to no prophet before him, and counted them out.

The first: he was sent to all of mankind, where every messenger before him had been sent only to his own people. The second: Allah aided him with awe, fear cast into the hearts of his enemies at the distance of a full month's journey, and Tabuk, he said, was the living proof of it, for Ghassan and the empire of Rome had been so terrified across a month of road that they never marched at all. The third: the spoils of war were made lawful for him, when no nation before him had been permitted to keep them; in the times before, the Sheikh reminds us, a fire would descend from the sky and consume the captured booty as the sign that the deed had been accepted by heaven, while to his ummah alone the spoils were left as a provision. The fourth: the whole earth was made for him a place of prayer and a means of purification, so that wherever a believer stands when the prayer falls due he may purify himself and pray, where the nations before could worship only inside their houses of worship under far stricter rules, granted no tayammum from clean earth when no water could be found.

And the fifth he held back. Allah told him to ask, and he could have asked for anything at all. He chose to save his request as an intercession for his ummah on the Day of Judgment, the day they would need it more than they had ever needed anything. He kept his one open wish for the worst hour of his people, and spent it on them.

And here, standing closer to the great empires of Rome and Persia than he had ever stood as a prophet, he made a promise his companions could barely hold: that Allah had given his ummah the treasures of them both. To men who were a single desert city ringed by superpowers, it can only have sounded impossible. Within a few short years it was history.

Count six before the Hour

On one of those nights he ﷺ gave a teaching that Bukhari preserves, among the most sobering things he ever said. Count six things, he told them, before the Day of Judgment.

The first is my death, he said, that the leaving of the Prophet ﷺ from this world is itself a sign of the Hour. The second is the conquest of Jerusalem, which came to pass less than two years after he was gone. The third is a plague that will take the people the way a certain sickness sweeps through a pen of camels, the kind that, once it touches one animal, leaves the whole herd dead. The fourth is wealth grown so heavy that a man will be handed a hundred dinars, far more than a year's income for most of the companions hearing him, and will be vexed that it is only that much. The fifth is a trial, a fitna, that will not leave a single household of the believers untouched. And the sixth, the heaviest of all, is a truce with a great power that holds while a common enemy is fought and then turns to treachery once that enemy is beaten, ending in a war beneath eighty banners, twelve thousand men under each, an army the like of which the world has never seen.

Here the Sheikh stops the lesson to teach us how to hold it. We do not, he says, nail a specific prediction of the Prophet ﷺ to a specific headline of our own day and declare the two one and the same, because Allah knows best and we do not. We may say our times appear to rhyme with the warning; what we may never do is put a sentence in his ﷺ mouth that he did not say, or claim as certain what he left as a sign. The teaching is given to make a heart sober, not a tongue certain. These are calamities we are taught to dread and to flee, not events we await, even as we believe that whatever he ﷺ foretold will surely come.

The only man a prophet prayed behind

One of those twenty nights the Prophet ﷺ went out from the camp to relieve himself, far from the people as he always did, and Mughirah ibn Shu'bah went with him carrying the water and a spear. When he ﷺ had finished, Mughirah poured the water for him to make wudu. The night was bitterly cold, deep winter, and he ﷺ was wearing a heavy Roman cloak with narrow sleeves that would not push up past the elbow. So rather than leave any part of his forearms dry, he ﷺ drew his arms out from underneath the cloak entirely and washed them fully from below, then wiped over his turban and his leather shoes, telling Mughirah to leave them on, for he had put them on in a state of purity. Sheikh Yasir lingers on the cold and the tight sleeve on purpose. We are not permitted to be lazy with the water. Every part the wudu requires must be reached by it, even when it is freezing and the clothing fights you. That is precisely the hour when how you worship is being measured.

By the time the two of them walked back, something had happened in the camp that he ﷺ had not arranged and would not undo. He had taken his time, the time for the prayer had come, and the companions had refused to delay it even for him. They could not know how long he would be, and the prayer was too heavy in their hearts to push back a moment longer, so they had begun, with Abdur-Rahman ibn Awf standing as their imam. He ﷺ arrived to find them already a rakah in, and rather than interrupt them or signal them to start over for his sake, he quietly joined the rows behind a companion and prayed a full unit following him.

When Abdur-Rahman realised, in the dark and the rising murmur, who was standing behind him, he moved to step back and yield his place. The Prophet ﷺ motioned to him: stay where you are. So he completed the prayer as imam, and the Prophet ﷺ made up the rakah he had missed afterward. The Sheikh marks what this means. Abdur-Rahman ibn Awf, radiyallahu anhu, carries an honour that as far as we know belongs to no other human being in all of history: he is the only person a prophet of Allah ever prayed behind for a complete unit of prayer. There is a famous moment near the end, in his ﷺ final illness, when Abu Bakr was leading and the Prophet ﷺ came out and sat beside him, but Abu Bakr, out of pure love, stepped back and refused to lead him, saying it was not for the son of Abu Quhafah to stand in front of him. So that privilege, in its fullness, was never his. It was Abdur-Rahman's alone, given by accident on a cold night in the north, because the companions would not delay their prayer even for the man they loved more than themselves.

A grave dug in the dark

Another night, Ibn Mas'ud woke and saw a fire burning in the distance, and gathered around it the Prophet ﷺ, Abu Bakr, and Umar. He went toward it and found that a companion had died: Abdullah Dhul-Bijadayn, the man of the two coarse garments, and the name was the whole of his story. When he accepted Islam his tribe turned him out with nothing, refusing him even the wages he had earned, and he left owning a single cheap cloak. When he reached Madinah he was so ashamed of his poverty that he tore that one cloak in two, so it would look as if he owned two garments, tying one around his waist and throwing the other over his shoulders. That was all anyone knew of him: not one of the famous, not one of the elite, a man whose only distinction was a torn cloak and a new faith, dead of natural causes on a cold night far from home.

And the Prophet ﷺ went down into the grave himself. Hand him to me, he told Abu Bakr and Umar, and they passed the body down, and he laid Abdullah into the earth with his own hands, and as he settled him he said: O Allah, I am pleased with this servant of Yours, so be pleased with him. Ibn Mas'ud, watching the Prophet of Allah ﷺ on his knees in the grave of a man no one could place, said afterward that he wished with his whole heart that he had been the one lying dead in that grave, to be received like that.

Think, the Sheikh says, of what a scene like that does to an army. The Messenger of Allah ﷺ himself, woken in the freezing dark for a man no one of rank would have noticed, climbing down into the grave to lay him in by hand and begging Allah to be pleased with him. The men saw that, and it is part of why they had followed him into the hardest march of their lives and would follow him into harder still. He did not lead them from above. He buried their forgotten with his own hands, and let them watch him do it.

The road home, and the ruins of Thamud

Then the long road back, and on it the miracles came again, the way they had for the length of the campaign, every time the provisions thinned out under twenty thousand mouths. The increasing of food and water, Sheikh Yasir notes, is among the most often repeated of the Prophet's ﷺ signs across the seerah, and Tabuk alone holds four or five of them. Water poured from between his fingers ﷺ until the men drank from his hand as from a fountain. On a parched night, a du'a for rain that had fallen by morning. And when the animals could carry no more, he ﷺ had the camels brought before him in a line and patted them and prayed: in the name of Allah, O Allah, carry upon these those who travel in Your path. The narrator, Fadalah, said that by the time they reached Madinah the beasts were so strong and full of fire that the men had to fight to hold them back.

And the road passed al-Hijr, the dead cities of Thamud, the people of Salih ﷺ, whose homes are carved not of wood or brick but straight into the living rock of the mountains, rooms and chambers and even beds cut into the stone by means no one fully understands. They are older than Ibrahim ﷺ himself, among the most ancient settlements known in Arabia, and they still stand. Some of the men hurried in to explore. The Prophet ﷺ had them called back, and asked them why they would want to walk into the homes of a people upon whom the punishment of Allah had descended. We are amazed at these ruins, O Messenger of Allah, they said. Shall I not tell you of something more amazing, he answered: a man among you, telling you what was and what will be. Your prophet, in other words, is the greater sign by far, more amazing than any punished city in any valley. Then he covered his face with his cloak, lowered his head, quickened his pace, and would not pause anywhere in that valley. Do not enter upon a people who wronged themselves and were punished, he told them, except that you are weeping, lest what struck them strike you. Some of the men had already drawn water from the old wells. Pour it out, he ﷺ told them, give it to the animals, and the dough you kneaded with that water, feed to the animals as well; this is the punished water of a punished place, and it is not for you.

And he turned the warning toward the heart of Thamud's ruin: they had demanded a miracle and then defied it. Allah had brought a great she-camel out of the mountain before their eyes, one that drank the whole settlement's water on one day and gave them milk enough to feed them all on the next. You think yourselves powerful, the Sheikh frames it, for carving rooms into a mountain; Allah brought living flesh out of that same rock. And still they hamstrung her, and the punishment came as a single sound from the heavens that silenced every one of them at once. So do not, he ﷺ warned his own people, go asking Allah for miracles; that valley demanded its sign and was destroyed by it.

When his own she-camel went missing on that road, the search held up the whole army, and one of the hypocrites sneered, well out of his hearing: here is a man who claims revelation comes to him from the heavens, and he does not even know where his own camel is. The words never reached the Prophet ﷺ by any human mouth, for the man was at the far end of the army; but a man who shared that hypocrite's tent was sitting with him when revelation informed him of it. He ﷺ stood and said: some of you have said such and such. By Allah, I am only a human being, and I know nothing except what Allah tells me, and Allah has just now told me that my camel is held fast by its harness, caught on a tree in such and such a valley. Go and bring it. They went, and it was exactly there. He knew nothing of the unseen, the Sheikh notes, except what his Lord disclosed to him, and his Lord disclosed a very great deal. The same tongue that found the camel by revelation had just confessed, I am only a human being. Both halves are the lesson.

The jokers and the verse that named them

Among the thousands on that road were hypocrites, for an army of twenty thousand was never going to be all of the elite, and on the way home a knot of them grew loud and crude, laughing past every limit. He really thinks we are going to conquer Rome and Persia, one of them mocked, who does he think he is. It was, on any human reckoning, an absurd thing to claim, the Sheikh notes, a handful of desert people against empires six hundred years old, and that very absurdity is where faith and its absence parted ways: to a believer it was a promise, to these men a joke. Then another said something fouler, sneering at the reciters of the Qur'an in their midst, calling them cowards with fat bellies. A companion overheard it and rounded on them: you are lying, O enemy of Allah, and I will carry your words to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ this instant.

He set off from the rear of the army toward the front to report it, and by the time he reached the Prophet ﷺ, revelation was already descending; the companions could see it in the lowered eyes and the weight that settled over him. The mockers, suddenly terrified that Allah would expose their hearts, came galloping their camels up behind the man to invent an excuse before he could even speak. Picture it, the Sheikh says: the messenger running, the hypocrites racing their mounts behind him, and at the head of the army the revelation coming down. And before the companion could get a word out, before the man's stammered apology was even finished, the Prophet ﷺ was already reciting the words that had just been sent, without so much as turning to look at the man clinging to his stirrup. The verse quoted the excuse back to him before he made it.

And if you ask them, they will surely say, "We were only conversing and playing." Say, "Is it Allah and His verses and His Messenger that you were mocking?"Qur'an 9:65

Make no excuse, the revelation went on; you have disbelieved after your belief. The man hung onto the stirrup of the moving camel, dragged along beside it, pleading, and the Prophet ﷺ would not turn his head, only kept reciting: do not give me your excuses.

Here the Sheikh halts the whole army to put something in our hands and make us carry it home, because he judges it among the most urgent lessons of the entire campaign for the world we now live in. We believe in the sacred. We believe certain things are simply off limits to a joke. You may question, you may argue a matter out, you may ask how a thing can be so, all of that is open. But to mock and ridicule Allah and His Messenger ﷺ is not something a heart with real faith in it is even able to do; the skin recoils at the thought. So the Qur'an does not call it a slip to be patched over. It calls it disbelief after belief, and the man who does it knowingly has, by the verse's own words, to come back to Islam and begin again. The Sheikh names this a red line precisely because it is being erased, and says it is most important of all to teach to a generation raised where nothing is held sacred and a cruel joke about the religion can feel like harmless fun. It is not harmless. The Qur'an even forbids us to mock the false gods of others, lest they turn and mock Allah; how much more, then, the One we worship and the Messenger we love.

A road that pointed at the future

There was one more plot on that road, among the strangest in the whole seerah, reaching us in only a narration or two. Travelling by night, the Prophet ﷺ had drawn apart from the main body with two or three companions onto a high, narrow place above a cliff. In the dark, perhaps fourteen or fifteen men with their faces covered charged his camel, trying to drive it over the edge. He ﷺ brought it down swiftly and safely into the valley, and they scattered and fled. Hudhayfah, who was guarding him, asked who they had been. Hypocrites, he ﷺ said, who wanted to throw me from the cliff. Their faces were hidden, no one recognised them, no punishment followed, and most of the army never even knew it had happened.

After perhaps two months away, perhaps closer to three, he ﷺ came home, and the whole of Madinah poured out to meet him at Thaniyyat al-Wada, the hill of farewell to the north of the city, where the women and the children sang the famous welcome that has echoed down the centuries. And here the Sheikh corrects a misconception most of us were raised inside. That song, Tala' al-Badru alayna, belongs to this homecoming from Tabuk, not to the hijrah from Makkah, as it is so often told. The hill is to the north and the hijrah arrived from the south; and on the day of the hijrah the city was not yet Muslim, where now the entire city was, and so the entire city came out. He went to his masjid, prayed two rakahs, and at once began receiving the delegations of those who had stayed behind.

And then the Sheikh saves for the very end the wisdom of the whole campaign. Why march an army a full month north, through the hardest conditions of his life, when there was no enemy waiting to be fought? The honest answer, he says, is that the only reason we can be sure of is that Allah commanded it, as a test. The Surah is plain about it: when you are told to go forth, you do not cling to the earth.

If you do not go forth, He will punish you with a painful punishment and will replace you with another people, and you will not harm Him at all. And Allah is over all things competent.Qur'an 9:39

That, the Sheikh says, is the heart of it. Simply to go, to leave the ripening harvest and the cool shade and the family and walk a month into the desert and a month back for no battle at all, was harder than any fighting would have been. The man who can obey when the reason is not yet visible can certainly fight when the time comes. The going was the test, and the believers passed it where the fighters were never even examined.

And it was a rehearsal. This is the second half of the wisdom, and the deeper one. The Prophet ﷺ took his people by the hand and led them to the exact northern land they would have to return to and conquer within a year and a half, after he was gone, against the Byzantine empire itself. The little tribal treaties he secured along the way were real, but they were never reason enough to move twenty thousand men a month north; the reason was the road itself, learned once with him leading so the next time they could walk it without him. On his deathbed, with two or three days of his life remaining, his mind was still set on that same direction: he was readying the army of Usamah ibn Zayd to march north. The first land the ummah conquered after him, under Abu Bakr, was Sham, the very north he had just walked them to and home from. He marched them to the edge of the world they would inherit without him, showed them the road, and then he let them go.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to file Tabuk away as an anticlimax, the great campaign that fizzled, a long hot march to nowhere with no battle to remember it by. That is to miss everything it was built to teach. The whole point of Tabuk is that the test was the going, not the fighting, and that is a test we sit for far more often than we ever sit for a battle.

Start with the smallest thing and the largest, held together in one breath at the pool. He ﷺ was angry at men who touched the water in nothing worse than haste, because a small disobedience is still a disobedience; and then he rinsed his own mouth into that same pool and filled it to the brim for tens of thousands. We tend to split those two apart, to think mercy means letting small things slide and discipline means hardness. He held them in one hand: correct the wrong honestly, then pour out more than they could have asked for.

Then the cold night and the tight sleeve, which sounds like a footnote and is not. Offered every excuse a freezing man could want, he ﷺ pulled his arms clean out of a heavy cloak rather than leave a hand-span of forearm dry in his wudu. We are not permitted to be lazy with the water. The worship that counts is the worship you do not rush when it is cold and inconvenient and no one would blame you for cutting it short, because the cold is the test and the convenience is the bribe. And in the same camp, he ﷺ climbed down into the grave of a man whose entire biography was a torn cloak, and asked Allah to be pleased with him, and an army watched and never forgot. Honour the one no one is counting, bury the forgotten with your own hands, and you will be followed through anything; for status in the eyes of people was never once status with Allah.

And keep the sacred sacred. The hypocrites laughed, and the laugh turned out to be a line they could not come back across, named from the heavens as disbelief after belief while one of them still dragged at the stirrup inventing his excuse. The Sheikh asks us to carry this one home most of all, into a world that holds nothing holy and teaches our children that a joke can be aimed anywhere. You may question and you may argue; you may not mock and ridicule Allah and His Messenger ﷺ, because a heart with faith in it cannot. Teach the young there is a red line before the world persuades them there is none.

And underneath all of it, the thing the Surah commands and the campaign was built to drill: when you are told to go, go. The hardest obedience is not the one whose reason you can see, the battle in front of you, the danger you understand. It is the long march with nothing visible at the end of it, the leaving of the harvest for a road that points at no enemy, the order whose wisdom you will not be shown until later, if ever. That is the obedience he ﷺ drilled into them on the way to a future they would have to inherit without him. So take this down into your ordinary days: do the small thing right when it is cold and costly, honour the one nobody is counting, keep one thing too sacred to be laughed at, and when the command is clear but the reason is hidden, be of those who go. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, who buried the forgotten with his own hands and led his ummah to a future he would not walk into, make us of those who go when He says go, keep the sacred sacred in our hearts, and gather us under his banner on the Day the nations are gathered.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 92: the expedition to Tabuk, part 5 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (9:65, 9:39), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 93 of 104

Tafsir of Surat at-Tawbah

The surah with no mercy at its head, and the most merciful man at its close

Tabuk is over. The army that marched into the heat of the northern desert has come home, the test has done its quiet work, and the seerah pauses now to do something it has not done before: it stops walking and sits down to read. Roughly two thirds of an entire surah of the Qur'an was sent down for this one expedition, and so before the story moves on to the last year of his life ﷺ, Dr. Yasir Qadhi turns the whole lesson into a reading of that surah, ayah by ayah, against the events we have just lived through. The chapter is Surat at-Tawbah, the chapter of repentance, and it is one of the very last portions of the Qur'an to descend; there is barely a year of the Prophet's ﷺ life left when its verses come down.

It is a strange and heavy surah, and the Sheikh does not soften that. It opens without the basmalah, the only chapter in the entire Qur'an that does. It names almost no one, and yet it exposes everyone who hid. It carries some of the harshest language in the Book against those who broke faith. And before any of that, before the surah even begins as a surah in our reading, the story it produced begins with a funeral: the death of the man who spent a lifetime trying to wound the Prophet ﷺ, and the mercy that buried him anyway.

The funeral of the man who hated him

There is one incident left from the season of Tabuk, and it happened within a month of the army's return. Abdullah ibn Ubayy ibn Salul fell ill, and everyone understood he was dying. To grasp the weight of his deathbed you have to remember who he was. Before Islam ever reached Yathrib, this man was being groomed to be its leader, not a king, for the city had no king, but the senior-most figure among its chiefs. The other elders who might have rivaled him had been killed in the civil wars before the hijrah or had died in the year or two after the Prophet ﷺ arrived, until the only great pre-Islamic politician left standing was Abdullah ibn Ubayy, by now an old man in his seventies.

He did not embrace Islam out of conviction. He was not at Aqaba; he came in with the very last batch of converts, after Badr, when paganism had been swept out of Madinah and a man either accepted Islam or left. He was forced, and his record under Islam shows it. He turned back at Uhud with a third of the army, complaining that since the Prophet ﷺ had not taken his counsel, why should he fight. At the trench of al-Ahzab he was the frightened one, going from Companion to Companion asking whether they could not see that all of mankind had gathered against them this year, trying to spread his terror through the ranks, and Allah answered by raising the believers' iman instead. On the road home from a later expedition he said openly that when they returned to Madinah the honorable would drive out the lowly, meaning himself the honorable and the Prophet ﷺ the lowly. And he lit the first match of the slander against Aisha radiyallahu anha, the worst of all his crimes. Every one of these things sits in the Qur'an. He is the ringleader the Book describes without ever writing his name, the one it calls, in a word that has no clean English equal, the one who took the largest share of it.

And yet, dying, he sent for the Prophet ﷺ and asked him to seek forgiveness for him. The Sheikh stops here, because that single request is the whole psychology of the hypocrite laid open. Why would a man who spent his life mocking the Prophet ﷺ want him at his deathbed at all? Because at some level he knew exactly who Allah was, knew Islam to be true, and at another level was too proud to ever truly bow. That is what makes the hypocrite, in the end, worse than the open disbeliever: the plain pagan does not know, and an utterly ignorant man might find some excuse before Allah, but this man knew, and chose his pride, which is why the Qur'an places the hypocrites in the lowest depth of the Fire.

When the request came, Umar ibn al-Khattab radiyallahu anhu could not contain himself. Messenger of Allah, he said, will you visit him, when he is an enemy of Allah? Notice what the Prophet ﷺ did not do. He did not correct Umar, did not say no, he is not an enemy of Allah. He said only that he hoped, through this kindness, that Allah might draw to faith a thousand of those who still looked up to the dying man. The thousand is not a literal count; Abdullah ibn Ubayy did not have a thousand followers. It is an expression for the many whose iman was still weak and who watched their old leader to see how the Prophet ﷺ would treat him. So the Sheikh draws out the principle the Prophet ﷺ was teaching in the act itself: Islam takes the long view, weighs the good against the harm, and will extend warmth even to a man it has every reason to despise, if a greater good for the community lies on the other side of that warmth.

He went. There are two reports, and the Sheikh holds that both may be true: that the dying man himself asked for the Prophet's ﷺ shirt to be used as his shroud, and that his son later asked for it too; in the first case the request was made in life, in the second the garment was actually handed over after death. What is certain beyond doubt is that the Prophet ﷺ gave his own shirt for the burial. The Sheikh ties it back to the lesson of the day before, on barakah: the shirt was sought because barakah clung to anything that had touched him ﷺ.

Then the body was brought to the masjid for the funeral prayer, and as the Prophet ﷺ rose to lead it, Umar took hold of his garment to stop him. He began listing the dead man's record back to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ: will you pray over him after he did this, and this, and this? It is one of the most extraordinary scenes in the whole seerah, a Companion reaching out and physically halting the Prophet ﷺ, reminding him of a verse of the Qur'an, and asking, is it not forbidden now to pray for the hypocrites? The Sheikh pauses to note what this reveals, and what it does not. It is not the modern slogan of free speech, which is a different idea. It is something quieter and sturdier: that even the Messenger of Allah ﷺ could be respectfully challenged by those who loved him, and that he would hear the challenge without crushing the one who raised it. Set that beside the rulers of our own age, the Sheikh says, where a hundredth of such a word would mean you were never seen on the street again, and you begin to feel what kind of man Umar was standing in front of.

The Prophet ﷺ answered that Allah had given him a choice, and he had chosen, and then he recited the verse:
