Ask forgiveness for them, [O Muhammad], or do not ask forgiveness for them. If you should ask forgiveness for them seventy times, never will Allah forgive them. That is because they disbelieved in Allah and His Messenger, and Allah does not guide the defiantly disobedient people.Qur'an 9:80

What he ﷺ drew from it is precise. Allah had not commanded him to refrain; Allah had said the forgiveness would not be granted even at seventy askings, but the command itself, ask or do not ask, left the door open. So he chose to ask. If I knew, he said, that asking seventy-one times would gain their forgiveness, I would ask seventy-one. Then he prayed over the man, and walked with the body to the grave, and it is recorded that he climbed down into the grave himself ﷺ and helped to bury the one who had been such an enemy to him. The Sheikh lets the weight of it settle. The Prophet ﷺ had, as we would say, no lost love for this man; in all his years Abdullah ibn Ubayy had done nothing but injure him. And still, now that he was dead, the Prophet ﷺ did not want the Fire even for him. This is the heart of the day's first lesson: you do not wish the Fire of Hell upon even your worst, worst enemy.

The verse that drew the line

After that burial, the line came down from heaven:

And do not pray [the funeral prayer, O Muhammad], over any of them who has died, ever, or stand at his grave. Indeed, they disbelieved in Allah and His Messenger and died while they were defiantly disobedient.Qur'an 9:84

Never again pray over one of them; never again stand at his grave. Read quickly, it sounds like a rebuke, as though the Prophet ﷺ had done something he should not have. But the Sheikh asks you to notice the order of events, because the order is the mercy. Allah let His Messenger ﷺ pour out his tenderness one final time, gave his shirt, prayed the prayer, stood at the grave, and only then closed the door. The verse did not snatch the act back; it sealed it after it had been completed.

And here the Sheikh insists on the part most people miss, because to miss it is to misuse it. This verse never reached down to us. The Prophet ﷺ could be forbidden from praying over specific hypocrites because Jibril had named them to him; he knew, by revelation from the heavens, whose professed Islam concealed real disbelief. We have no such knowledge, and we never will, because hypocrisy is hidden by its very nature, which is exactly what the word means. So the ruling that governs us is the opposite of a license to judge. When a person who openly professed Islam dies, we wash him, we pray the funeral over him, and we bury him among the Muslims, and whatever was truly in his heart we hand over to Allah. The verse drew a line that only the Prophet ﷺ could see, and the day's second lesson is to leave the hidden hearts of others to the One who made them.

The surah with no basmalah

Now the seerah steps back to read the chapter whole, and the first thing anyone notices about Surat at-Tawbah is what is not there. Every other surah of the Qur'an opens with bismillah ar-Rahman ar-Rahim, in the name of Allah, the Most Merciful, the Especially Merciful. This one does not. It is the only surah without it, and it is also among the last to be revealed, descending in the ninth year of the hijrah with only a year of the Prophet's ﷺ life remaining. The Sheikh is careful to add that the surah was not sent down all at once or in its written order: the first thirty-odd verses came later, around the pilgrimage season, and what we are reading, from verse thirty-eight onward, is the Tabuk material. The Qur'an, he reminds us, was never arranged in the order of its revelation; verses came down and the Prophet ﷺ told the scribes precisely where each one belonged.

So why no basmalah? Two answers come down from the Companions, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi gives each its full weight. The first is a report in the collection of at-Tirmidhi from Uthman ibn Affan radiyallahu anhu, the Companion who oversaw the compiling of the mushaf. He was asked why he had placed at-Tawbah next to al-Anfal with no basmalah dividing them, when the two surahs were revealed so far apart, al-Anfal early, around Badr, and at-Tawbah at the very end. Uthman answered that at-Tawbah was among the last surahs to be revealed, that the matter of where exactly it belonged was not fully clear to them, and that because its subject was the same as al-Anfal, fighting and striving in the cause of Allah, they set the two together and did not write a basmalah between them, uncertain whether the two were one surah or two.

The Sheikh notes that this is advanced ground, the kind that can unsettle before it settles. From this very report, a small number of the early scholars, among them Qatada, the student of Ibn Abbas, actually held that al-Anfal and at-Tawbah were a single surah, which is why some of the earliest counts list one hundred and thirteen chapters rather than one hundred and fourteen. They were not claiming a surah was missing; they were joining two into one. But that view stood almost entirely alone, and the ummah settled, with no real disagreement remaining, on two distinct surahs.

The second answer comes from Ali ibn Abi Talib radiyallahu anhu, asked the same question by one of his household. This, he said, is a surah in which Allah severs His ties with the idolaters; it opens with a declaration of disassociation, a freedom from any covenant with them, and it would not befit such a chapter to begin with the name of the Most Merciful. The whole opening, Ali observed, is harsh against those who reject Allah and His Messenger, and so mercy was simply not the right door by which to enter it. Both readings, the Sheikh says, carry a real legitimacy: the placement was genuinely uncertain to the Companions, and the tone of severance made the basmalah an ill-fitting threshold.

And the surah teaches one more thing in passing, something the Sheikh draws out because it touches the integrity of the whole Qur'an. There are two kinds of ordering: the order of the verses within each surah, and the order of the surahs within the Book. On the first there is no dispute. The Prophet ﷺ himself, under revelation, set every verse in its place; you can feel it, the Sheikh says, in the way each surah holds a rhythm and a flow from one ayah to the next, a coherence that runs underneath even a translation. He even pauses on a striking witness from outside the tradition, a non-Muslim German scholar of the Qur'an who, against another orientalist claiming the text had been tampered with by later scribes, argued that this was plainly false, because the internal architecture of each surah demonstrates a single coherent design preserved from the Prophet's ﷺ time. She does not believe the Qur'an divine, the Sheikh notes; she believes it preserved, which is its own kind of testimony. But the order of the surahs is a different matter. That appears to have been arranged by the Companions, who each kept their own arrangement in their private copies, until it was standardized in the time of Uthman, after which it became binding on the ummah to keep. So the flow you feel within a surah is divine, set by the Prophet ﷺ; the sequence of the chapters is the Companions' trust, faithfully kept.

What is the matter with you?

From verse thirty-eight, the surah turns to Tabuk itself, and it opens like a hand laid on the shoulder that then begins to tighten:

O you who have believed, what is [the matter] with you that, when you are told to go forth in the cause of Allah, you adhere heavily to the earth? Are you satisfied with the life of this world rather than the Hereafter? But what is the enjoyment of worldly life compared to the Hereafter except a [very] little.Qur'an 9:38

What is the matter with you, that when the call to march comes, your bodies sink to the ground? The severe and beautiful warning begins here and recurs throughout the surah, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi stops at once to build a fence around it, a fence he says is not optional. These are powerful verses of jihad, revealed for a specific command at a specific moment in history, and it is impermissible, haram, for any Muslim to lift them out of that moment and stamp them onto his own private cause, declaring that Allah revealed them for him. Yes, the Sheikh grants, every legitimate cause has a share of their spirit; no one denies that. But to claim this verse points at my particular faction and my particular expedition is to say that Allah is, in effect, revealing the verse to you, and that, the Sheikh warns plainly, is precisely the road that leads to extremism. The verses belong to Tabuk. Read in their context, they instruct; read into one's appetite, they destroy. That is the day's fifth lesson, and the Sheikh states it as a warning, not a footnote: never weaponize a verse of the Qur'an.

Then the command becomes plain:

Go forth, whether light or heavy, and strive with your wealth and your lives in the cause of Allah. That is better for you, if you only knew.Qur'an 9:41

Light or heavy: healthy or sick, young or old, rich or poor, fully equipped or empty-handed, go. The Sheikh marks how this set Tabuk apart. Unlike Badr, unlike many earlier expeditions that were voluntary, every able-bodied believer was bound to this one. And he returns to the question that has shadowed this whole episode from its first lesson: why? No real enemy ever materialized at Tabuk; no battle was fought; nothing of substance was found when the army arrived. The verses answer it. The march was a command and a test, deliberately set, to raise the bar so high that this same generation, after the Prophet ﷺ was gone, would carry the struggle out to the ends of the earth. Tabuk was Allah sorting the believer who goes from the one who clings to the ground, and the surah is the record of that sorting.

If you do not aid him, Allah already has

Then comes a verse that reaches back across nine years and sets it down in the middle of the largest Muslim army yet assembled:

If you do not aid him, Allah has already aided him when those who disbelieved had driven him out [of Makkah] as one of two, when they were in the cave and he said to his companion, "Do not grieve; indeed Allah is with us." And Allah sent down His tranquility upon him and supported him with soldiers you did not see and made the word of those who disbelieved the lowest, while the word of Allah, that is the highest. And Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise.Qur'an 9:40

If you will not help him, Allah is telling them, then know that Allah has already helped him, on a night when there was no one to help, when he was one of only two men, hunted, hiding in the dark of the cave of Thawr, saying to his companion: do not grieve, indeed Allah is with us. At Tabuk there were more Muslims gathered than ever before in the history of the religion, and Allah turns to that vast army and asks, in effect: do you imagine I need your numbers? I stood by him when he had no numbers at all, when he was one against a city, and I sent down tranquility upon him and armies you could not see.

The Sheikh lingers over two things in the Arabic. The first is the phrase that calls the Prophet ﷺ the second of two, thaniya ithnayn. It carries no ranking, he stresses, no suggestion of a hierarchy between the two men; it is simply how Arabic says one of two people, exactly as one says the third of three, a picture of two figures alone in the dark and nothing more. The second is the word companion, sahibihi. This, the Sheikh says, is the verse, the one that settles the matter for all time, that names Abu Bakr radiyallahu anhu the companion of the Prophet ﷺ in the cave. There is no other verse in the Qur'an that calls a specific, named person the Prophet's ﷺ companion by this description. And so the Sheikh states the conclusion with the firmness it deserves: anyone who denies that Abu Bakr was the companion of the Prophet ﷺ has contradicted the plain testimony of the Qur'an itself, and to contradict the Qur'an on a matter this clear is to step outside belief in the Qur'an. The cave that held two men now anchors a verse that no one can argue around.

Exposed by their fear of being exposed

Much of what follows is a long, unsparing exposure of the hypocrites who stayed behind, and the readings the Sheikh walks through are unforgettable precisely because the Qur'an is so exact about the human heart.

There were the men who came to be excused with the flimsiest of pretexts. One begged to be left at home lest the sight of foreign women on the campaign tempt him into sin, and Allah answered that with the excuse of fleeing the trial, he had fallen headlong into the trial itself. There were those who swore by Allah that only the lack of means kept them home, and Allah said that even as they swore, they were destroying themselves, because He knew they were lying. And there were the men who, had they actually marched, would only have spread discord through the ranks. Here the Sheikh draws out a distinction the verse makes with surgical care. Allah names not two kinds of people but three: those who sow the discord, those steady enough to resist it, and between them the dangerous middle, the soft hearts who would have listened and been swayed. Sit with that, the Sheikh urges. For every Companion whose name has reached us, there were thousands whose names we will never know, ordinary believers who had not climbed to the rank of an Abu Bakr or an Umar, and some even of that generation could be moved by an evil whisper. So what of ours. The verse is a mirror held up not only to the hypocrites of Madinah but to every age that came after.

And against the whole landscape of their fear, the Qur'an sets the believer's single, steadying line, one Dr. Yasir Qadhi calls among the most beloved verses in the entire Book:

Say, "Never will we be struck except by what Allah has decreed for us; He is our protector." And upon Allah let the believers rely.Qur'an 9:51

Nothing will ever reach us, the believer says, except what Allah has already written for us, and He is our protector, our guardian, and on Him we rely. The Sheikh unfolds the courage folded inside it. We are waiting, he says, for only one of two beautiful endings: victory in this world, or martyrdom into the next. So we win in either case, while the hypocrite, who fears death above all, loses in both. Say this verse to yourself when the dunya frightens you. That is the day's fourth lesson, and the Sheikh names the exact ayah: hold the believer's line.

Then the surah turns its sharpest edge on the mockery of the road home, the jokes some of them made at the expense of the Prophet ﷺ and the revelation, and when they were confronted and swore they had only been chatting and joking among themselves, Allah cut straight through the alibi:

And if you ask them, they will surely say, "We were only conversing and playing." Say, "Is it Allah and His verses and His Messenger that you were mocking?"Qur'an 9:65
Make no excuse; you have disbelieved after your belief. If We pardon one faction of you, We will punish another faction because they were criminals.Qur'an 9:66

The Sheikh draws the deepest point out slowly, and it is a point about eloquence as much as about justice. The hypocrites lived in a particular terror: that a surah would descend laying bare what was hidden in their hearts. Imagine the strange double-mindedness of that, he says, men who at some level knew that Allah knew their secret, and who trembled that He would publish it. And the eloquence of the Qur'an is this: Allah exposed them by narrating their fear of being exposed. He described the dread itself, named the thing they were hiding by describing how desperately they hid it, and that was enough. They mocked the Messenger ﷺ for being too lenient, for listening to everyone, and Allah turned even that back on them, that his listening was a mercy to them, for had he dealt with them as they deserved they would not have been left alive. Respect for the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, the Sheikh says, is itself a branch of iman, and to mock him, to ridicule or belittle him, is something no Muslim heart can do; that line, too, the surah makes absolute.

The mercy at the close

Before it ends, the surah lets the light back in. It tells of the three Companions whose case was held in suspense, true believers who had simply failed to march and then refused to lie about it, their verdict delayed while all of Madinah was commanded to keep its distance from them, until Allah turned to them in mercy and forgave the men who had owned their mistake and told the truth. And the Sheikh notices the order in which the forgiveness is announced, because it is tender. Before declaring the three forgiven, Allah first declares the Muhajirun and the Ansar already forgiven, those who followed the Prophet ﷺ in the hour of hardship, the hour of Tabuk, even after a party of them had nearly inclined to waver. He reassures the obedient first, and only then turns to the three.

And then, after a surah of severance and exposure, of excuses laid bare and ties cut off, the very chapter that refused to open with mercy closes upon the most merciful of all men:

There has certainly come to you a Messenger from among yourselves. Grievous to him is what you suffer; [he is] concerned over you and to the believers is kind and merciful.Qur'an 9:128

A Messenger from among yourselves, one of you: your suffering weighs upon him, whatever harms you pains him, he is forever anxious for your good, and to the believers he is gentle and merciful. The Sheikh ties it straight back to where this whole day began, to the graveside of Abdullah ibn Ubayy. You saw it with your own eyes, he says: a man grieved at the punishment awaiting even a hypocrite, eager that even his bitterest enemy might somehow be spared. That is the Prophet ﷺ this verse is describing. The surah that would not begin with the name of the Most Merciful ends by calling the man at its center kind and merciful, and the two facts, the severance and the mercy, are meant to be held together and not balanced away.

The final word of the surah is given to him as the believer's whole posture compressed into two breaths:

But if they turn away, [O Muhammad], say, "Sufficient for me is Allah; there is no deity except Him. On Him I have relied, and He is the Lord of the Great Throne."Qur'an 9:129

If they turn away, say: Allah is enough for me; there is no god but Him; upon Him I have placed my trust, and He is the Lord of the Mighty Throne. With that sentence the Tabuk material comes to its close, and the seerah, the Sheikh tells us, now stands near the very end of the road: the bulk of the Qur'an revealed, the laws of Islam all but complete, Makkah secured, and the tribes of Arabia beginning to enter the religion in crowds. What remains is the last handful of months of his life ﷺ.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read a tafsir lesson as information, a set of facts about a surah, and walk away knowing more. Surat at-Tawbah refuses that. It puts hard questions to the one reading it, and the Sheikh draws them out as questions, not data.

Begin where the surah's story begins, at the grave. The Prophet ﷺ had no love left for the man being lowered into it; Abdullah ibn Ubayy had spent his every breath under Islam wounding him. And still he ﷺ gave his own shirt for the shroud, prayed the funeral, and climbed down into the grave with his own hands, because now that the man was dead he would not wish the Fire even on him. Whatever you are still carrying against someone who wronged you, measure it beside a Prophet ﷺ who buried his bitterest enemy and hoped he might yet be spared. Carry less.

Then hold the line that runs through the surah against the line that runs through your own days. The hypocrites had an excuse for every command, the flimsiest of them sworn to with the name of Allah, and the Book exposed them by narrating the very fear they hid. The Sheikh turns it on us before we can feel superior: count the excuses you reach for when Allah asks something of you, the prayer deferred, the wealth withheld, the command adhered to heavily as though your body were sinking to the ground, and notice how thin the smallest of them is. And learn from the same surah not to point these blades outward. The Prophet ﷺ alone was told who the hypocrites were; the verse forbidding their funerals never reached us. We were never given the right to read another person's hidden heart, only the duty to watch our own. We pray over whoever professed Islam, and we leave the rest to Allah.

And then there is the verse the Sheikh calls among the most beloved in the Qur'an, the one he tells you to keep on your tongue. When the dunya frightens you, when the numbers are against you and the way is dark, say what the believer says: never will we be struck except by what Allah has decreed for us; He is our protector. It is the same trust that held two men steady in a cave with the whole of Quraysh hunting them, and it is offered now to an army of thousands and to you. We wait for one of two endings, and we win in either, while the one who fears only this world loses in both.

So take the day's two gifts down into your ordinary life. Carry less in your heart against those who wronged you, because the most merciful of men buried his enemy with his own hands. And carry the believer's line on your tongue when the world frightens you, because the surah that would not open with mercy chose to close on the man who was mercy itself. May Allah send His peace and blessings upon Muhammad ﷺ, the Messenger from among ourselves whom our suffering grieved, make us of those who followed him in the hour of hardship, who owned their mistakes and found His mercy, and who say with full hearts, when the world turns away, that Allah is sufficient for us, the Lord of the Mighty Throne.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 93: the tafsir of Surat at-Tawbah and Tabuk (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (9:80, 9:84, 9:38, 9:41, 9:40, 9:51, 9:65, 9:66, 9:128, 9:129), verified via quran.ai.

The Seerah · 94 of 104

The Year of Delegations, Part 1

The year Arabia came knocking

The Battle of Tabuk is behind us, and the ground has shifted under the whole peninsula. The Muslims marched north to meet the Romans and the Romans never came; the Ghatafan never came; and an army that fights no one and rides home unvanquished is, by the only reckoning the desert understood, the winner. Add that to what came before. Makkah has been conquered. Quraysh, the one tribe under whose banner all the others might ever have gathered, has been subdued. Hunayn has been won. And so, quite suddenly, there is no longer a flag in Arabia for anyone to rally under, no capital to resist from, no leadership to unite behind. The smaller tribes look around and understand the arithmetic: they cannot stand alone, and there is no longer anyone left to stand with. So they do the only thing left. They send delegations to Madinah. The ninth year of the Hijrah carries a name for this season, am al-wufud, the year of delegations, and it is where Dr. Yasir Qadhi turns now, near the close of the whole series, with not many months of the Prophet's ﷺ life left to tell.

Why this year carries the name

Before a single delegate arrives, the Sheikh hands you a puzzle to hold. If the ninth year is the year of delegations, why do the histories record delegations arriving as early as the fifth, around the time of the Trench, and continuing in a steady stream right up to the last month of the Prophet's ﷺ life? The delegations did not begin in the ninth year at all. They simply reached their peak in it. After the conquest of Makkah and the victory at Hunayn, the whole political landscape of the peninsula changed, and the frequency of delegations climbed to its height. That is why the year is named for them, and why the scholars gather the bulk of them and discuss them all at this one juncture: there were so many, scattered across so many years, that to pause and narrate each in its place would have been unbearably repetitive. The lists run past a hundred and twenty tribes. The Sheikh has no intention of marching you through all of them; he will illustrate perhaps a tenth, the handful that carry a lesson worth keeping, and let the rest stay in the books for those who want them.

And a delegation, he is careful to insist, did not always mean a tribe embracing Islam. It meant a tribe sending two or three representatives to negotiate. Some of those negotiations ended in faith, freely and joyfully given. Some ended in a hesitant faith that wavered and took years to settle. Some, it turned out, were pure hypocrisy, the tribe only pretending and then abandoning Islam the very moment the Prophet ﷺ died, so that his death alone exposed what they had really been. And some sought nothing more than a treaty: we will not become Muslim, but we will not fight you either. At this stage that was still permitted; a pagan could yet live in Arabia. Only later, after this year, would that door close, when Surah at-Tawbah would come down and give the idolaters four months around the sacred precinct, after which all of Arabia would enter Islam and paganism would be finished. But for now the door stood open, and through it came every kind of visitor, down to the delegation of pure threat: Musaylima the liar, who came at the head of his people from Yamama and offered, with a straight face, to split the earth in half, one half for the Prophet ﷺ and one for himself. Not every delegation was the same. Each, the Sheikh says, was its own story.

The tribe that heard before anyone came

Begin where the Sheikh begins, with one of the very earliest delegations of all: the tribe of Abd al-Qais. They lived in the region the early sources call Bahrain, which in those days meant not the island we know by that name but the whole eastern coast of Arabia, the land of Dammam and Dhahran, what we now call the eastern province, pressed right up against the empire of Persia. Some were Christian, some pagan, and they sent two delegations: the first in the fifth year, reckoned by some the first delegation ever to come, and a second later in am al-wufud itself.

Here is the thing that should stop you. In the fifth year, a tribe sitting on the very doorstep of Persia already knew the details of Islam, and not one Muslim had ever reached them. No envoy of the Prophet ﷺ had been sent that far, no Companion, no preacher. The message had traveled the whole width of Arabia on its own, and they had heard enough to be interested, interested enough to gather a band of new converts and send them across the desert to Madinah. Abd al-Qais are remembered for it ever after as the first tribe outside the Hijaz to accept Islam voluntarily, without an army at their gates, without ever being invaded, a matter of pride they carried for generations. One day, the Sheikh relates, the Prophet ﷺ was delivering the khutbah and told his Companions a delegation was coming, the best people of the east, and they would be the best of the east for the simplest reason: there were no Muslims at all further east than they. A day or two later Umar ibn al-Khattab, radiyallahu anhu, was standing in the marketplace when a band of strangers arrived, and he leapt up out of sheer joy, rushed to embrace them, and gave them the good news of what the Prophet ﷺ had said about them before they had even come.

The whole delegation rushed to the Prophet ﷺ, all of them except the youngest, a man history remembers by his nickname, al-Ashajj, the one with the wound, for an old fracture on his head. His given name is uncertain; it is the nickname that survived, because of what he did. While the others hurried ahead in their excitement, brand new Muslims desperate to lay eyes on him ﷺ, al-Ashajj hung back. He washed himself, put on good clothes, perfumed himself, and only then, composed and unhurried, came forward to meet the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. The Prophet ﷺ looked at him and told him he had in him two qualities Allah and His Messenger love: al-hilm and al-anaah. Forbearance, the Sheikh explains, the mastery of one's own temper, and anaah, the patience that does not act in haste, that thinks a thing through before it moves. The two are cousins, almost one quality from two sides: a man whose temper is cool does not lunge, and a man who pauses to think gives his temper the time to cool. And al-Ashajj asked one of the most intelligent questions in the whole seerah. These two qualities, he said, did I work to develop them in myself, or did Allah create me with them? And the Prophet ﷺ told him Allah had implanted them in his very nature.

Sheikh Yasir lingers here, because any parent knows the truth of it in their bones. You raise children under one roof, with one upbringing, and one comes out quick to anger and another calm from the cradle; you can see it when they are still babies. Some of who we are is shaped, and some is simply given. So what did al-Ashajj do with the part given to him for free? He thanked the One who gave it: all praise to Allah, who created in me qualities He loves. Not a flicker of pride, only gratitude that his Lord had built something beloved into him at no cost of his own. A man who answers himself like that, the Sheikh says, is a man you can already tell will go far.

The delegation also had a problem they laid out plainly. Between their land and Madinah lay the tribe of Mudar, still pagan, still hostile, so that the only safe time to travel was during the sacred months, the ashhur al-hurum, when warfare was forbidden across all of Arabia. But it cut both ways: once they went home, they could not return for a year. So they asked the Prophet ﷺ for something to carry back, teach us something that will take us to Jannah and that we can teach our people. An intelligent tribe, the Sheikh repeats, asking exactly the right question. He ﷺ commanded them to have faith in Allah, then asked: do you know what faith in Allah is? To bear witness that there is no god but Allah, to establish the prayer, to give the zakat, and to fast Ramadan. And there he stopped, with four, not five; he did not mention the Hajj, because this was still the fifth year and it had not yet been made obligatory. Then, because Abd al-Qais were a people of cold lands who fermented their own drinks, they asked what they could drink, and he ﷺ forbade them their intoxicants by name, listing the kinds they brewed one after another, the precise drinking customs of a people on the far edge of the peninsula whom he had never met. They went home carrying their four pillars and their prohibition, and Abd al-Qais are remembered as the first to build a masjid outside the Hijaz and the first to gather for Jumuah anywhere beyond Madinah.

The blunt bedouin who made the Prophet ﷺ swear

Some delegations came to negotiate a treaty rather than to believe, and one of those involved a tribe you already know without knowing it: Banu Sad ibn Bakr, one of the sub tribes of the great Hawazin, and the tribe of Halima al-Sadiyya, the woman who nursed the Prophet ﷺ as an infant in the desert. The Hawazin, the Sheikh reminds you, were the bedouin half of a pair: they and Thaqif of Taif go back to the same ancestor, but one line settled into the town and the other stayed out among the tents, and it is the bedouin who take in the children of Quraysh to nurse, which is how Halima came to him in the first place. These were her people, then, and they sent an envoy who was not himself a Muslim, sent only to arrange a treaty, in the days before Thaqif itself would come. His name was Dimam ibn Thalaba, and he was a bedouin to the bone: hairy, gruff, rough, with two long braids hanging at the sides of his head the way the desert men wore them, the way you could still find them wearing them in old photographs a century ago.

He rode his camel straight up to the door of the masjid, made it kneel right there in plain view, and barged in demanding, where is the son of Abd al-Muttalib? And here, the Sheikh says, pause and see one wisdom of why Allah chose for His Prophet ﷺ the most honored lineage in all of Arabia. This is an unlettered bedouin from a distant tribe, and yet he knows the name Abd al-Muttalib, and knows the man claiming prophethood is his grandson. Lineage opened the door before a word of the message was spoken; a man more willing to listen because the one he had come to hear came from someone his whole world already revered. The Prophet ﷺ answered that he was the one. And Dimam, with no softness in him at all, looked at him and said: you are Muhammad? Then, before anything else: I am going to be hard with you in my questions, so do not be angry with me. He had at least that much courtesy, to ask leave to be blunt. Go ahead, the Prophet ﷺ told him, ask whatever you wish.

What followed was a strange, blunt interrogation, a hadith that has always tickled people for its sheer rough manner. Your envoy came to us, Dimam began, and told us Allah has sent you. He spoke the truth, said the Prophet ﷺ. Then the bedouin built his suspense the way a simple mind builds it, brick by heavy brick. Who created the sky? Allah. The earth? Allah. The mountains? Allah. Then, gathering it into one oath: I ask you by the One who created the heavens and the earth and raised the mountains, did Allah send you to us? And the Prophet ﷺ swore that yes, Allah had sent him. The bedouin trusted an oath where he did not yet trust a messenger, so he made him swear to each command in turn. Did Allah command you to tell us to pray five times a day? Yes. To pay the zakat? Yes. To fast? To make the Hajj, now that the command had come? Yes, to each. Then Dimam swore by the One who had sent him with the truth that he would do exactly this, no more and no less, not adding to it nor subtracting from it by a hair, and just as abruptly as he had stormed in, he stormed back out. He shall enter Jannah if he is truthful, the Prophet ﷺ said, for this is the bare floor of the faith: the five pillars kept, the major sins avoided, and a man who does that has done his part. And when Dimam reached home he tore down his people's idol with his own hands and began to preach. Ibn Ishaq records, from one of the Tabiun, that no single envoy was ever a greater blessing to his community than this rough man was to his: by him, his whole tribe came to Islam. And the Sheikh will not let the baraka of it pass without naming where some of it surely came from, that this was Halima's tribe, the family that had once carried the Prophet ﷺ as a nursing infant, and that Allah poured a particular blessing into them through that old bond.

Among these delegations sits a quiet miracle, told by another tribe. Muzaynah came, four hundred of them, the whole tribe at once to embrace Islam. They studied with the Prophet ﷺ, and when it was time to make the long journey home, he ﷺ told Umar ibn al-Khattab, radiyallahu anhu, to give them the provisions they would need for the road, four hundred men, weeks of travel. Umar said, O Messenger of Allah, I have only a single sack of dates at home; that will never be enough for four hundred. The Prophet ﷺ simply told him again: go and provide for them. And Umar answered only, I will do it. Whatever you say, I will do it. He went back to his house, put the whole weight of his trust on that command, and found his store room filled with dates to the ceiling, heaped, Ibn Ishaq says, like a camel kneeling and filling the room. He called the four hundred, and every one filled his travelling sack, and the last man to leave looked back and saw the pile sitting exactly as high as before the first man had touched it, undiminished, as though no one had eaten from it at all. Not every delegation ended in wonder. This one did.

The tribe that came boasting

Then came a delegation of ten men from Banu Asad ibn Khuzayma, and they came loudly. They walked into the masjid and announced themselves: O Messenger of Allah, we testify to the shahada, and we are Muslims. And we want you to know we came to you without you ever sending anyone to us, and we accepted Islam without you ever having to fight us. We are not like the other Arabs. We traveled to you through the darkness and through the cold. On and on they went, the Sheikh says, bragging about the favor they imagined they had done him ﷺ by believing, holding their faith over his head like a gift he ought to be grateful for.

And Allah answered them directly, in an ayah Sheikh Yasir ties to this very tribe:

They consider it a favor to you that they have accepted Islam. Say, "Do not consider your Islam a favor to me. Rather, Allah has conferred favor upon you that He has guided you to the faith, if you should be truthful."Qur'an 49:17

They were Muslims, the Sheikh is clear, their faith was real, only thin, iman not yet settled deep in their hearts. But they had the order of things exactly upside down. They thought they had done the Prophet ﷺ a favor by entering Islam; Allah turned it around and showed them the favor runs the other way. No one does Islam a kindness by accepting it. If you are truthful in your faith, then be grateful that Allah chose you for it, for the guidance is the gift, and you are the one who received it. Islam is not a favor you grant. It is the favor granted to you.

The delegation that came to kill him ﷺ

The darkest delegation belonged to Banu Amir ibn Sasaa, the tribe behind the massacre at the well of Maouna, where Muslim teachers sent out at the tribe's own request had been betrayed and slaughtered. One of its chiefs, Amir ibn al-Tufayl, a name that had come up before in this story, came to Madinah among the delegates with no intention whatsoever of believing. He had come to murder the Prophet ﷺ. He had arranged it with his man Arbad ibn Qays: I will keep him occupied with questions, and when I give you the signal, draw your blade and strike. Most likely, the Sheikh judges, a poisoned blade, for an ordinary dagger does not reliably kill, and these were people who knew poison and had used it against him ﷺ before, in the sheep at Khaybar; one cut, and the deed would be done. The Sheikh is honest about the dating, which the delegations rarely give us cleanly: he suspects the attempt actually predates the conquest of Makkah, since once Makkah had fallen it is hard to imagine anyone still thinking an assassination could change anything. But it sits with the delegations in the telling.

Amir kept pressing the Prophet ﷺ for a private audience, just the two of them, and the Prophet ﷺ kept refusing him, telling him there would be no private meeting until he accepted Islam. The conversation went back and forth. Three times Amir gave Arbad the secret signal to strike. Three times Arbad did nothing at all. When it became clear the private moment would never come and the plan had failed, Amir threw off all pretense and laid his arrogance out in the open. I give you three choices, he told the Prophet ﷺ. Take the people of the towns and leave me the people of the open country, and we will divide the leadership between us. Or make me your heir, to rule after you. Or it is war, a thousand stallions and a thousand mares against you. The Prophet ﷺ refused all three, and as the man finally left, he ﷺ raised his hands and prayed: O Allah, suffice me against Amir ibn al-Tufayl, and guide his people. A prayer against the one man, and a mercy for the whole tribe behind him. This dua, the Sheikh urges, is one to memorize and keep, Allahumma-kfini, O Allah be sufficient for me, against this trouble, against this enemy, even naming him, for whenever a particular person threatens us, this is the prayer to make.

Outside, Amir rounded on Arbad in fury. You are the strongest warrior I have, the very reason I chose you; why did you fail me? And Arbad gave an answer that explains everything. Every time you gave me the signal, he said, I could see no one but you. I could not see Muhammad at all. Did you want me to cut you down? He had not refused out of cowardice or mercy; Allah had simply hidden His Messenger ﷺ from the assassin's eyes, the way the seerah has shown before, the way Allah set a barrier before those who plotted in the early days and behind them and covered them so they could not see. The man stood in the same room and could not find the Prophet ﷺ to strike him. As for Amir, the curse landed fast. On the journey home he lodged for a night in a house of ill repute, and in the dark a spreading sickness fell on him and ran through his body in a single night; realizing he was dying, and dying in a shameful place, he was too ashamed to be found there, so he mounted his horse and fled into the night, and his body was never found.

And Arbad met an end stranger still. He made it home, where his people asked where he had been. He told them he had just come from the man who claimed to be a prophet, and then he said something vile about Allah, words the Sheikh refuses to repeat, a plain and open insult to his Lord. The next day Arbad walked out among his community on his camel, and lightning came down from the heavens and consumed him and his camel where they stood, the two of them turned to ash in front of the whole tribe. Sheikh Yasir reads this as the very meaning of three verses in Surah ar-Rad that the books of tafsir apply to this man. First the warning that nothing repels Allah's punishment once He wills it:

For him are successive [angels] before and behind him who protect him by the decree of Allah. Indeed, Allah will not change the condition of a people until they change what is in themselves. And when Allah intends for a people ill, there is no repelling it. And there is not for them besides Him any patron.Qur'an 13:11

Then the lightning itself, the thing that came down on Arbad, set in the sky for fear and for hope:

It is He who shows you lightning, [causing] fear and aspiration, and generates the heavy clouds.Qur'an 13:12

And then the thunderbolt, and the One who sends it down on whom He wills, even as such men stand arguing about Him:

And the thunder exalts [Allah] with praise of Him, and the angels [as well] from fear of Him, and He sends thunderbolts and strikes therewith whom He wills while they dispute about Allah; and He is severe in assault.Qur'an 13:13

The thunder itself praises Allah, the Sheikh says, and the angels tremble in His praise, and into all that praise He sends the thunderbolt to strike whom He wills, even while men dispute about Him. Arbad disputed about Allah, insulted Him, and was struck out of the very sky he had mocked. Allah is severe in His assault, and a man who had come to help kill His Messenger ﷺ learned it the hard way, incinerated before his own people.

Tamim al-Dari and the creature on the island

One last delegation, and a famous one. Tamim al-Dari was a Christian from a tribe in the north who came to Madinah and embraced Islam, and he carried a story the Prophet ﷺ found so striking that he gathered the people in the masjid simply to hear it told. The Companion Fatima bint Qays, who narrates it, remembered hearing the call go out, as-salatu jamiah, come to the prayer, outside the usual times; she came and stood in the front row of the women, and when the Prophet ﷺ finished he climbed the pulpit, beaming, his face bright with pleasure. Stay where you are, he told them, and let me tell you why I called you. By Allah, I did not call you for any sermon or lesson. I called you only to tell you the story of Tamim al-Dari, a Christian who has become a Muslim, and who has told me something that agrees with what I have been telling you about the Dajjal.

Tamim told of sailing with thirty men, tossed by the waves and lost a full month at sea, until at sunset they washed up on a strange island. They took their small boat ashore and met a beast so covered in hair that you could not tell its front from its back. Terrified, they asked what it was. I am al-Jassasah, it said, and sent them on to a monastery where, it told them, a man was waiting to hear from them. Inside they found an enormous man, a giant, bound in iron, his hands chained to his neck, his legs shackled from knee to ankle. He questioned them about specific places in the Levant: the date palms of Baysan, whether they still bore fruit; the lake of Tabariyya, whether it still held water; the spring of Zughar in Syria, whether its water still ran and its people still grew crops. And he asked about the unlettered prophet who had appeared in Arabia, whether the Arabs had fought him and how it had gone. When they answered that he had left Makkah, settled in Yathrib, and prevailed over the Arabs near him, the chained giant told them it would be better for them to obey him, and then named himself. I am the Dajjal, he said, and soon I will be permitted to come out and travel the earth, and I will not leave a single town unentered, staying in each for forty nights, except for Makkah and Tayba; both are forbidden to me, and every time I try to enter one of them, an angel with a drawn sword will turn me back at every road. The Prophet ﷺ struck his pulpit with his staff and said: this is Tayba, this is Madinah; had I not told you he would never enter it? Then he pointed, correcting any guess that the creature lay in the western sea or the Arabian sea: no, he is in the east, in the east, in the east.

Now the Sheikh does something honest with you, because this hadith is recorded in Sahih Muslim and yet it sits uneasily with much of what we otherwise know. He keeps the report exactly as it stands, refusing to reject what an imam of Muslim's rank graded sound; but he also keeps the unease, his own and that of scholars before him. His own teacher, Sheikh Ibn al-Uthaymeen, used to say that something in his heart told him this hadith was troubled, while warning in the very same breath, correctly, that you never reject a hadith merely because your heart dislikes it; the heart is no measure of authenticity. The measure is other evidence, and that is where the real difficulty lies. Several authentic reports, in Bukhari and Muslim both, seem to pull against this one. There is the night the Prophet ﷺ told his Companions that not a single soul then alive would remain upon the earth a hundred years later, with no exception made, hard to square with the Dajjal already alive and chained on an island. The well known descriptions make him a short, stocky man, not a giant; they say he will be born in the future to a couple awaiting a child, not bound across the sea already; they dwell on his one blind eye and the word kufr written between his eyes, none of which appears in Tamim's account. And the long episode of Ibn Sayyad, the strange youth of Madinah whom Umar swore to his dying day was the Dajjal, makes little sense if the Dajjal were known to be locked on a distant island. For reasons like these, a small number of scholars, Rashid Rida of Egypt and Ibn al-Uthaymeen among them, have flagged this single report as problematic, not out of personal taste but against the wider weight of the texts. The Sheikh adds that he himself looked into the chain that very day and found a real weakness in it, the narration passing through a narrator known to have drawn at times on the storytellers, and that another student of Ibn al-Uthaymeen has written a whole study tracing the same difficulty through every chain.

The Sheikh leaves it precisely where a careful believer must, and the discipline of where he leaves it is itself the lesson. If the Prophet ﷺ truly said this, then it is true and we accept it without the slightest hesitation; our intellects and our cultures and our comfort have no vote. The only question, and it is a real one, is whether he truly said it, and on that there hangs an honest question mark. What is not in question, he reminds you firmly, is the one fact the whole report was told to confirm: the Dajjal will never enter Makkah or Madinah. That is reported by at least six Companions through many separate chains, established beyond any doubt. The strange detail of the chained giant on the island wobbles. The protection of the two sacred cities does not.

What this day asks of our faith

This is the year Arabia stopped resisting and started arriving, and the handful of delegates the Sheikh has set before us are not a parade of tribal names to be skimmed and forgotten. Each one puts a question to us, quietly, across fourteen centuries.

Compose yourself before you come to Allah. The whole tribe of Abd al-Qais rushed in, new Muslims so eager they could not wait, and there is something beautiful in that eagerness; but it was the one who paused that the Prophet ﷺ praised, the young man who washed and dressed and perfumed himself and arrived calm, and was told he carried two qualities Allah loves. We are a people who lunge, who fire off the angry reply and rush the heavy decision. The seerah holds up al-Ashajj and asks whether we can slow down before the things that matter most, whether we can come to our prayer and to our Lord with a temper that has had time to cool. And it asks something gentler too: that we make our peace with how we were made. Allah built al-Ashajj calm by nature, and al-Ashajj answered with thanks rather than pride. Some of who you are is given, not earned. Thank Allah for the good He set in you for free, and work patiently, without despair, on the part that is yours to shape.

Ask for what you can carry home. Abd al-Qais did not ask for everything; they asked for the floor that holds a whole people up, the testimony and the prayer and the zakat and the fast, simple enough to teach an entire tribe a year's journey away. There is humility in learning the essentials well enough to pass them on, and a trap in always reaching for the advanced and the obscure while the foundations stay shaky. Learn the floor, build on it, and carry it to others.

And then there is the favor, and who is doing it for whom. Banu Asad came boasting they had done the Prophet ﷺ a kindness by believing, and Allah turned the whole thing over from above the seven heavens: do not count your Islam a favor to Me; Allah did you the favor, by guiding you to it. It is a verse to hold against the heart on the days we feel we are doing our religion a service by keeping it, that our prayers and our restraint are gifts we bestow. They are not. The guidance was the gift, given to us, and gratitude is the only fitting response. No one does Islam a kindness by entering it. Islam is the kindness done to them.

Carry, too, the prayer he made over the man who came to murder him. With a poisoned blade somewhere in the room and an assassin waiting on a signal, the Prophet ﷺ did not curse the tribe; he prayed against Amir alone and asked guidance for Amir's people in the very same breath. Curse the harm, and pray for the people. Even at the sharpest moment a man can face, his mercy reached past the one who wronged him to the many who merely followed, and that is the prophetic temper we are asked to grow toward: hard on the harm, soft on the human beings caught around it.

And keep the discipline the Sheikh modeled over the story of the Jassasah, because it will guard you in an age that loves to throw away whatever it finds uncomfortable. A hadith is weighed by evidence, never by taste. The Sheikh kept the report in Sahih Muslim and kept the scholars' unease, and let neither one swallow the other, insisting all the while that the only valid scale is other texts, never our own comfort or our own era's instinct. Hold your honest questions honestly, but hold them to the right standard, and never make your own heart the judge of what your Lord and His Messenger ﷺ may say.

The waves are only beginning. Still to come, in the next two days, are Daws and the prayer the Prophet ﷺ turned from a curse into a mercy, the proud tribe of Thaqif bending at last in Taif, the false prophet who wanted half the earth for himself, and the Christians of Najran and the curse none of them would dare to risk. For now, let today's prayer be his prayer ﷺ, the one he raised over the man who came to kill him: O Allah, be sufficient for us against all that would harm us, guide those who wrong us before You judge them, soften the hardest hearts the way You softened a whole peninsula until it came knocking at the gates of Madinah, and let us come to You composed and grateful, like the young man who washed and perfumed himself before he ever reached Your Messenger ﷺ. Ameen.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 94: the Year of Delegations, part 1 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (49:17, 13:11, 13:12, 13:13), verified via quran.ai. The narration is the Sheikh's; the phrasing is Buruja's.

The Seerah · 95 of 104

The year of delegations, part 2

The tribes come to Madinah, one wave at a time

Once Makkah fell, the Arabs understood which way the wind had turned, and they came. From the fifth year of the hijrah onward they had been trickling in, but in the ninth they arrived in a flood: so many delegations, and so loosely dated, that even the earliest historians gave up setting each one in its proper month. Ibn Ishaq and the others gathered them under a single heading and let the calendar blur, because this was the season the delegations came. We call it the year of delegations, and to tell it honestly is to tell it as they preserved it, thematically, with the chronology bending back and forth, since some of these tribes had actually sent word in the seventh and eighth years.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi walks us through three of them, and no two open the same way. A Yemeni chieftain who plugged his ears with cotton so he would not have to hear the Prophet ﷺ, and heard him anyway. A prince of royal blood, mourning a stolen throne, told he would be given something better. And the one tribe in all of the Hijaz that had held out to the bitter, bloody end, finally coming to give up its idol. Three doors into Islam, not one swinging on the same hinge. Read them together and you begin to see how patiently Allah opens the same door for very different hearts, and how patiently His Prophet ﷺ held it open for each.

The chieftain who stuffed his ears

Go back, first, to a year we cannot quite name, the seventh or eighth of the dawah, when the Prophet ﷺ was still being hounded in the streets of Makkah and the Quraysh had made it policy to warn every visiting dignitary against him. In that climate came Tufayl ibn Amr ad-Dawsi, chieftain of the Yemeni tribe of Dos, a poet and a man accustomed to weighing matters for himself. The Quraysh received him warmly, as they would any chief, then leaned in with the warning they gave everyone: be careful, one of our own has become a magician, his speech splits fathers from sons and brothers from brothers, you grow mesmerized just by listening. They said it so insistently, Tufayl remembered, that he grew genuinely afraid. When he went to the Kaaba to present himself before the idols, he stuffed his ears with cotton, so that nothing of the Prophet ﷺ could get through.

But the Prophet ﷺ was standing there in prayer, reciting the Qur'an, and cotton does not block everything. A few words slipped past the wool, and Tufayl had never heard anything like them. So he began to reproach his own cowardice the way an intelligent man does: you are a poet, a thinking man, you know truth from falsehood. Listen to him; if it is good, take it, and if it is bad, leave it. He pulled the cotton from his ears, went to the Prophet ﷺ, and told him plainly what the Quraysh had done and that he now wanted to hear the rest. The Prophet ﷺ sat him down, taught him what Islam is, and recited to him. It is said it was only the last three short surahs, al-Ikhlas, al-Falaq, and an-Nas, and Tufayl was so moved that he accepted Islam on the spot, an early Makkan convert in the thick of the persecution.

Then he asked for something that would echo down the centuries. Make du'a for me, he said, and make du'a for my tribe. And the Prophet ﷺ raised his hands and said, O Allah, guide the tribe of Dos. Three times he made that du'a for a people who had not yet believed. Whether he raised it in Makkah that day or years later in Madinah the historians are not sure, and Sheikh Yasir is content to leave it open, because the point is not the place but the prayer: the Prophet ﷺ asking guidance for a whole tribe of strangers on the word of one man newly come to the faith. And then the instruction Tufayl was sent home with, the one that frames everything after it: go back to your people, and be gentle with them. Do not be harsh. Be gentle, and perhaps they will be guided.

Gentleness, and the man it brought

Tufayl went home and was gentle, and it worked exactly as the Prophet ﷺ had promised. The very day he returned, his father, his mother, and his wife entered Islam, one after another. He was beloved among his people, the chieftain they trusted, and so the faith spread quietly through them until some eighty families of Dos had accepted Islam at his hands. There is a measure of his standing in something he offered the Prophet ﷺ while the persecution still raged: come to my fortress in Yemen, he said, emigrate to my land and my protection. A safe refuge, offered freely, before Madinah was ever on the horizon, and the Prophet ﷺ declined it, for Allah had not given him permission to go there, having already willed Madinah for him ﷺ and no other door.

In the end Tufayl did not come to Madinah merely as a delegate. He gave up the chieftainship, left his land and his lineage and his honor, and emigrated, and so strong had iman grown in his people that many of them pulled up their lives and came with him. Stop on that, because the Sheikh asks us to. This was no persecuted man fleeing for safety, but a chief with status and inherited rank, with everything to keep, and he walked away from all of it for the sake of nearness to the Prophet ﷺ. It is easy to say in a sentence. Sit with what it would have cost.

And here is the thread the whole story was quietly carrying. Among the people guided to Islam through Tufayl, back in Yemen, was one man who would become one of the greatest legacies this ummah has ever known: Abu Hurayrah. He accepted Islam at Tufayl's hands and emigrated with that final wave, most likely in the middle of the eighth year, which means he was with the Prophet ﷺ for barely two years before the end. Two years, and yet he is called the preserver of the sunnah, the companion who narrated more hadith than any other, more than five thousand of them, when even those nearest the top of that list come in below four thousand. How? He explained it himself. He had given up marriage and the pursuit of a livelihood to simply stay in the masjid, near the Prophet ﷺ, with no food and no comfort, so as to miss nothing. He was so hungry, he said, that he would sometimes stop a companion in the road and ask a question whose answer he already knew, only hoping the conversation might carry them as far as that man's door, where a meal might be offered. From a poverty like that came the richest harvest of narration in the history of the religion.

Now hold the two men together. Every reward Abu Hurayrah earned for preserving the sunnah, Tufayl ibn Amr has a share in it, because Tufayl is the one who guided him. This is where Sheikh Yasir presses hardest, and returns more than once: never trivialize a single good deed you do. Most people have never even heard the name Tufayl ibn Amr. And on that obscure name hangs a share in every hadith Abu Hurayrah ever taught, every prayer prayed because of a sunnah he preserved, every reward still accruing fourteen centuries later. You never know which small thing Allah is quietly building something enormous upon.

A prince told he would be given better

The second delegation came from royal blood, and it belongs squarely in the ninth year. Wa'il ibn Hujr was from one of the small kingdoms of Yemen, that corner of Arabia which, unlike the rest, was never wholly tribal but studded with little thrones, perhaps a remnant of the age of the Queen of Sheba. His grandfather and great-grandfather had been among its kings; he was, in the plainest sense, a prince. And before he even arrived, the Prophet ﷺ announced to the companions that there was coming to them one of the noblemen of Yemen, one of its princes, of his own free will, no one having pressured or fought him, wanting nothing but to enter Islam. He told them so that they might honor the man. Three days later Wa'il arrived, exactly as foretold, one of the quiet miracles of the Prophet ﷺ that the seerah keeps handing us without fanfare.

And the Prophet ﷺ honored Wa'il as Sheikh Yasir says he honored almost no one else. He brought the young man up onto the minbar beside him, and spread out his own cloak for Wa'il to sit upon, the old Arab gesture of the highest respect, something he did only rarely. And then Wa'il opened the wound he had carried all the way from Yemen: my family, he said, have taken my right from me. The throne. Some cousin had taken the seat Wa'il believed was his, the way royal families have always quarreled over a chair, each prince certain he is the one qualified to sit in it. To this grief over a lost kingdom the Prophet ﷺ answered with a single sentence that should reach every one of us: I will give you better than that.

Better than a throne. Better than a kingdom. Because we know, from the explicit hadith of the Prophet ﷺ, that the very lowest person in Jannah will be told to wish, and wish, and wish, and will then be given everything he wished for and ten times the whole of this world besides, when no king who ever lived held even a tenth of the dunya in his hands. So a claimant to a throne was told, plainly and gently, do not grieve the crown your cousin took; I have something better waiting for you. And the Sheikh turns it straight toward us: so do we all, if we are faithful to Allah. Whatever you have given up for His sake, He is not bargaining you down to something smaller. He is trading you up to something worth more than an entire kingdom.

There is a tail to this story that Sheikh Yasir cannot resist, and it carries its own quiet warning. The Prophet ﷺ gave Wa'il authority over a district of Yemen, befitting his blood, and sent a young escort along to settle the affairs and return: a youth barely eighteen, named Muawiyah ibn Abi Sufyan. On the road Wa'il rode his camel while Muawiyah walked alongside in the heat, and the young man asked if he might ride behind him. It is not befitting, said Wa'il, still wearing his old royalty like a second skin, that someone like you should ride with kings. Then at least lend me your sandals, Muawiyah said, for his own were worn through and the stones were burning. It is not befitting that a king's sandals should touch your feet, came the reply. Take shade, instead, from the shadow of my camel. Some forty years and more would pass. Muawiyah became the ruler of the Muslims, the first to found a dynasty, while the little kingdoms of Yemen had long since dissolved into nothing under the caliphate. And Wa'il came to him then, as a petitioner needing the ruler's favor, and reminded him of that day on the road. Be careful, the Sheikh says, who you are stingy with. You never know how the one walking beneath you today will be standing tomorrow.

Urwah, who was killed for calling his people

Now the great story of the day, the most significant delegation of the entire ninth year, and it begins with the one tribe that had refused, to the very last, to bend. Thaqif, the people of Ta'if. This was the tribe the battle and siege of Hunayn had been fought against, the tribe that had once driven the Prophet ﷺ bleeding from their streets, the last holdout of paganism in the whole province of the Hijaz. When the Prophet ﷺ had finally lifted the siege of Ta'if and turned away, the companions urging him to press the attack, he said, let them be, they will come to us. And eventually they realized he was right, that they had no road left open but Madinah. Yet something stood between them and that road and made them terrified to take it: a blood debt of their own making.

The man at the center of it was Urwah ibn Masud, one of the most respected leaders Thaqif had. You may already know him. He is the envoy Quraysh sent to the Prophet ﷺ at Hudaybiyyah, the very man who went back and reported, in the height of eloquence, that he had sat in the courts of Caesar and Khosrow and the Negus and had never seen a people revere a leader as the companions revered Muhammad ﷺ. They would not let his ablution water fall to the ground before they caught it, nor a single hair of his fall without rushing for it; they sat around him with their eyes lowered, as though birds were perched upon their heads. A man of that standing, describing what he saw as a disbeliever, amazed in spite of himself. He is even alluded to in the Qur'an: when the Quraysh sneered that revelation, if it had to come at all, should have gone to one of the two great men of the two great cities rather than to Muhammad ﷺ, Urwah was one of those two great men they meant.

After the siege of Ta'if, Urwah caught up with the Prophet ﷺ on the road back to Madinah and entered Islam, before they ever reached the city. By the custom of the day the Prophet ﷺ would have expected him to emigrate, his tribe being unbelievers; but Urwah asked instead to be sent home, to call his own people. The Prophet ﷺ warned him: I fear your people will kill you. They love me more than they love their own daughters, Urwah answered; if they found me asleep they would not even wake me. He went home, and at dawn he climbed to the roof of his house and called the adhan, the open declaration of his Islam, and an arrow came out of the dark and struck him. His own people had killed him. As he lay dying his family pressed him, the way of the Arabs: what of revenge, what of the blood money, it is your right. Nothing, he said. This is a gift Allah has honored me with; He chose me as a martyr. You will do nothing at all. And bury me with the martyrs of Hunayn, outside the family graves, with the companions of the Prophet ﷺ whom we ourselves had killed. Look at how loyalties had turned: a chief of Thaqif asking to be laid not among his own ancestors but among the very Muslims his tribe had fought. And when the news reached the Prophet ﷺ, he said that this man was to his people as the man of Ya-Sin was to his.

And there came from the farthest end of the city a man, running. He said, "O my people, follow the messengers.Qur'an 36:20

The one who came running to his city to call it toward the messengers, and was killed by his own people for it. There is something more here that the Sheikh lingers over: the Prophet ﷺ had read Thaqif more truly than Urwah, their own leader, had read them. Urwah was certain they would never harm him; he was too beloved. But a man inside a thing is half-blinded to it, while one outside weighs it fairly, and the Prophet ﷺ, with both his prophethood and that clear outside eye, saw what the blood would cost before it was ever spilled.

Ten days of bargaining

So Thaqif came, in Ramadan of the ninth year, but as frightened men rather than as believers. The blood of Urwah sat heavy on the tribe's conscience, and each of their leaders kept pushing the journey onto the next, afraid that to go to Madinah was to be called to account for killing one of their own, until at last six or seven of the seniors went together with their entourage. They were so eaten by guilt and so certain of poison that they would not touch a meal until their Quraysh go-between, a distant cousin of the Prophet ﷺ named Khalid ibn Saeed, had eaten from it first before them. And when they reached the Prophet ﷺ they ignored every protocol they had been taught, greeting him with the salutation of the days of ignorance and addressing him by his bare name, the very height of disrespect, when Allah Himself had told the believers not to address the Prophet ﷺ as they address one another. All of it announced that iman was not yet in their hearts. And he ﷺ overlooked every bit of it, never taking a word of it personally, and this was of his perfection ﷺ. He ordered a guest tent pitched for them inside his own masjid, doubling their shelter and their honor.

Then the haggling began, and it ran more than ten days, because they had come to negotiate, not to submit. Their first anxiety, tellingly, was for their stomachs and their wealth, in that order. What about interest, they asked, when all our profit is in riba? Allah has forbidden it, came the answer, with the verse quoted to them; you keep your principal and nothing beyond it. What about fornication, when we are travelling merchants always on the road? Forbidden. What about wine, when our land is famous for its grapes and our winters bitterly cold, surely there is an exception? No exception. One of their leaders said outright that they could never face their people and tell them riba and zina and wine had all been stripped away at once. But another asked the only question that mattered: what is the alternative? An army within the month, and the loss of everything. And then one of them steadied the rest with the most beautiful argument of all. Look at the people gathered around the Prophet ﷺ, he said: they used to live exactly as we do, and they gave it all up and they are living fine. If they could do it, so can we. That, says the Sheikh, is the quiet power of a healthy ummah: a man on the threshold sees the thing can be done, because he watches it being done all around him.

Only once their appetites were settled did they remember the thing that should have come first of all: their idol. Al-Lat, the goddess of Ta'if, second in prestige in all of Arabia only to Hubal. Could they keep her three years, they asked. No. Two? No. One? A month? Down and down they bargained, and the answer never changed: she must come down. Then they asked to be excused from the prayer itself, and the Prophet ﷺ told them there is no good in a religion that has no prayer in it, no such thing as a Muslim who does not pray. Then they asked to be excused from wudu, because of the cold; no, again. This is the very impulse the Qur'an answered when the Quraysh sneered that the revelation should have gone to one of the great men of the two great cities, as though greatness were theirs to assign and Allah's mercy theirs to parcel out.

And they said, "Why was this Qur'an not sent down upon a great man from [one of] the two cities?"Qur'an 43:31
Do they distribute the mercy of your Lord? It is We who have apportioned among them their livelihood in the life of this world and have raised some of them above others in degrees [of rank] that they may make use of one another for service. But the mercy of your Lord is better than whatever they accumulate.Qur'an 43:32

Of all the demands, the prayer was the hardest for proud men to yield, because to bow the back felt to them like surrender. And surrender, of course, was exactly what it was.

The condition that was no condition

Then came the request Sheikh Yasir lingers on longest, because the scholars are still turning it over. Excuse us from zakat and from jihad, they said. And the Prophet ﷺ answered them: you are excused from zakat and from jihad. But once they had departed, days later, he said of them, they shall give zakat, and they shall go for jihad. What happened there?

The scholars give two readings, and Sheikh Yasir lays both out without forcing a verdict. The first is that this was something special to the Prophet ﷺ alone: knowing by revelation that iman would soon fill their hearts and they would come to do both willingly, he simply let the flawed condition stand, as a prophecy more than a permission, and no leader after him could ever grant the like, because none after him receives revelation about what a people's hearts will become. The second reading takes it as a precedent: that the political leader may accept an incorrect condition from a brand-new Muslim for a temporary time, then enforce the full deen once faith has settled, on the understanding, borne out by history, that everyone who genuinely enters Islam comes in the end to live it from within. Theological and closed, or legal and repeatable? The scholars differ. Both readings agree, the Sheikh stresses, on the destination: Thaqif did in time give the zakat and go for the jihad, and their descendants are practicing Muslims to this day.

And here he turns it, gently and at length, toward us. When someone comes toward Islam still carrying things that are not yet Islamic, be lenient, he says, ultra lenient, far more than you would ever be with a settled Muslim. Do not justify the haram, never call the forbidden permitted, but cut such a person a million times more slack than you would cut yourself, because at the start iman is only a glimmer, and harshness and the demand for everything-at-once will snuff it out and turn the person away for good. Bring them instead into the fold, into the masjid, near the Qur'an, where they can listen and the faith can grow. Then, when iman has taken root, they will become better Muslims on their own; and even if they never fully do, a Muslim carrying that one sin is far better off than a soul that never came in the door at all. He reaches for the case that makes it real: a woman wants to embrace Islam but her husband will not, and she has children. You do not meet her at the threshold with a demand that she leave her family, unless her iman is already strong enough to bear it. Faith first, perfection later: that is the wisdom hiding inside a stubborn tribe's ten days of bargaining.

The idol comes down

They stayed in Madinah fifteen days, and around the tenth they entered Islam at last, and because it was Ramadan they fasted alongside the Prophet ﷺ and broke their fast in his masjid, Bilal carrying them their suhur in the dark and their iftar at dusk. New to it and over-careful, they kept misjudging the hours. At suhur they held back from eating, sure the dawn had already broken, until Bilal told them he had just left the Prophet ﷺ eating; at sunset they held back from breaking the fast, sure the light was still in the sky, until Bilal told them the Prophet ﷺ had already broken his. An anxious, beautiful first fast. And quietly, through those days, the youngest member of the delegation had become the most eager of all. Uthman ibn Abi al-As would slip out of the elders' tent to sit with the Prophet ﷺ memorizing the Qur'an, and with Abu Bakr learning how to pray, while the seniors haggled. So when the delegation was about to leave, Abu Bakr suggested to the Prophet ﷺ that Uthman be made their leader, and the Prophet ﷺ agreed: the youngest of them raised over all the seniors, because he loved the Qur'an the most and had hungered for Islam the hardest.

Al-Lat still had to fall. The Prophet ﷺ sent Abu Sufyan and Thaqif's own nephew al-Mughirah ibn Shu'bah to bring her down. The later books carry a story Sheikh Yasir clearly enjoys, not found in the earliest sources but preserved in the histories. Al-Mughirah, something of a prankster, asked Abu Sufyan whether he might play a joke on the watching crowd. The whole tribe had gathered, tense and grieving, the women weeping and the elders wailing over their goddess, certain that some catastrophe would strike the man who dared touch her. Al-Mughirah swung his axe, let out a great cry, and fell flat on his face. The tribe erupted with joy: did we not warn you, the goddess has struck him down. Then he sprang back to his feet. You fools, he said, and turned back to the work, hewing al-Lat down blow by blow while the keeper of the idol swore the earth would swallow him the instant he reached her base, until there was nothing left of her at all.

On the very spot where she had stood, the Prophet ﷺ ordered Uthman, now the leader of his people, to build a masjid, and it stands in that place to this day. The temple's long-accumulated hoard of gold and silver, for every pagan shrine becomes a kind of bank over the years, the offerings piling up untouched, was gathered and given to the poor and to those weighed down by debt. And so the proudest holdout in all the Hijaz finally came in. The blood debt was set down, the goddess was rubble, a masjid rose where she had stood, and in time, exactly as the Prophet ﷺ had foretold, Thaqif gave the zakat and went for the jihad of their own accord. The long story of their paganism was over.

What this day asks of our faith

Three delegations, three kinds of heart, and not one came in the same way, yet every one came in. That is the first thing the year of delegations asks us to see. Tufayl reasoned his way toward the door, an honest man who would not reject a thing before he had tested it, and pulled the cotton from his ears. Wa'il was led to the door and seated in honor, and had to be taught, slowly, that what waited beyond it was greater than the throne he grieved. Thaqif had to be all but carried through it, bargaining over every inch, and ended exactly where the gentlest of them had hoped. Allah opened one door for three unlike hearts, and the Prophet ﷺ was patient enough to hold it open for each at the speed that heart could manage. Ask which of the three you are with the people in your own life who are still finding their way, and whether you are holding the door or letting it swing shut.

Be gentle when you call, because gentleness is what lets a new flame catch. Go back, and be gentle with them, the Prophet ﷺ told Tufayl, and a tribe came in. And do not trivialize the small good you do, because you cannot see what Allah is building on it. One obscure chieftain guided one man in Yemen, and that man was Abu Hurayrah, and a share in every hadith he preserved runs back to a name most of us had never heard until today. The kindness you think too small to matter may be the hinge of something you will not learn the size of until you are shown your own record.

And carry home the lesson the Sheikh presses hardest of all, the one that runs under the whole story of Thaqif. Faith first, perfection later. With anyone whose iman is still only a glimmer, the new Muslim, the one inching back toward the masjid, the one tangled in things not yet untangled, cut a million times more slack than you would ever cut yourself. Do not bless the haram, but do not stand at the threshold with a list of demands either, because harshness puts the flicker out, while a soul brought near the Qur'an grows into the rest in time. The Prophet ﷺ excused a stubborn tribe from zakat and jihad to get their hearts in the door, and then they gave both willingly. Get the heart in; the rest tends to follow. O Allah, guide the hearts that are still finding their way as You guided Dos, give us better than every throne we have let go of for Your sake, make us gentle callers to Your path, and gather us with Your Prophet ﷺ and with all those who answered him. Ameen.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 95: the year of delegations, part 2 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (36:20, 43:31, 43:32), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 96 of 104

The year of delegations, part 3: Musaylima, Yemen, and the Christians of Najran

A false prophet, a distant miracle, and the curse no one would risk
