The books of seerah list more than a hundred and ten delegations by name, and for the great mass of them all that has come down to us is a single line: this tribe came. They sent their men, they heard, they went home, and history kept only the fact of their arrival. Over three sittings Dr. Yasir Qadhi has worked through that long roll and lifted out the few that earn their own telling, and on this third and last day he closes the arc. A fourth, he admits, would only have been a recital of tribal names, so he gathers here the most important of what remains: six or seven embassies, and one of them the strangest in the whole record. For most of them came at last to submit. This one came to make a demand.

By the end of the day you will have met the first man in the history of the Arabs ever to call himself a prophet, watched a miracle land in a far valley while two confused envoys stood in Madinah not understanding a word of it, listened to a tribe from Yemen ask the oldest question there is, and seen sixty Christians offered a curse so heavy they turned their camels around and rode home rather than touch it. And underneath all of it runs one quiet astonishment the Sheikh keeps returning to: in a single year, with no armies on the march, the whole of Arabia came. That had never happened before in the life of the world.

The man who wanted half the prophethood

Begin with the bizarre one, because it is the most unforgettable. Out of Yamama, in the central uplands of Arabia, came the delegation of Banu Hanifa, a large Christian tribe, and at their head an old man named Musaylima, perhaps in his late sixties, perhaps already past seventy. In his younger years he had travelled all the way to Jerusalem to study Christianity, learned the language of the Romans and taken on their customs, and come home to a reverence that only grew with the decades. By the time Islam reached his country he was a man the entire region of Yamama looked up to, and his tribe carried him into the Masjid shielded behind fine cloths, a procession fit for a king, the whole pomp of a man told he is great for so long that he has come to believe it.

That is the thing the Sheikh wants noticed first, and he names it as one of the great dangers of power: once a man tastes it, it gets into his head, and he comes, quite literally, to feel that people owe him something. Musaylima had watched his peninsula fracture along the new fault line of Islam, watched tribe after tribe go over, watched even a nobleman of his own people, the chieftain Thumama ibn Uthal, who had once been tied to a pillar of the Masjid for three days and emerged a Muslim with such grace that he swore to choke off Makkah's grain until the Prophet ﷺ released it, carry his subtribe over with him. And the more Islam spread, the harder the old man's heart set. He could not imagine, after a lifetime of being venerated, becoming subservient to anyone.

So Banu Hanifa did not come to submit, and Musaylima sent his terms ahead of him: name me your successor and make me a partner in the prophethood, the way Musa made Harun his partner, and then I will follow you. Otherwise, no. He set the condition before he had even declared himself, which tells you, the Sheikh says, that the claim was already alive in his heart. And he had the audacity to put it to the Prophet's ﷺ face. The Prophet ﷺ happened to be holding a branch he had picked off a tree, the idle way a person holds something while he talks, and he gave Musaylima his answer through it: by Allah, if you asked me for this stick, I would not give it to you. You want command after me, and I would not hand you this worthless branch. Allah will deal with you, and humiliate you. And then: you are the one Allah warned me about.

Two bracelets of gold

That last sentence has a story folded inside it. Years later, when Ibn Abbas had grown into an old man, he went to Abu Hurayra to ask about a dream the Prophet ﷺ had once referred to; Ibn Abbas had been a boy of around twelve when the Prophet ﷺ died, and much of his vast knowledge came to him secondhand, gathered from the elder companions. Abu Hurayra gave him the hadith, and it is preserved in Bukhari. As he slept, the Prophet ﷺ had said, he saw two bracelets of gold upon his arms. Gold is forbidden for the men of this umma, and the wrongness of it troubled him even in the dream. Then he was inspired to blow upon them, and at his breath the two broke loose and flew away. He understood them at once: two liars who would rise after him, each pretending to a share of what was his and possessing none of it.

Musaylima was one of those two bracelets. He became the first of the false prophets, exactly as the Prophet ﷺ had foretold in another hadith: after me you will see liars, and the worst of them will follow others who came before them. The Sheikh stops over a quiet fact that is easy to skate past. Before this Prophet ﷺ, the Arabs had never so much as heard of the idea of a man claiming prophethood; it was not a category they possessed. When Heraclius interrogated Abu Sufyan with his careful questions, one of them was whether such claimants were common among the Arabs, and the answer was plain: no, never. The notion belonged to the Jews and the Christians; the children of Ishmael had no tradition of it at all. It took the towering success of the truth to summon all the counterfeits. The copycats only came once there was something worth copying.

And copy he did, down to the style of the Qur'an itself. Musaylima was, to the best of the Sheikh's knowledge, the first human being not merely to claim prophethood but to try to imitate the sound of revelation, and his attempts were so hollow that there is no harm in passing one on as the nonsense it is. There is the line addressed solemnly to a frog: O toad, daughter of two toads, purify what you purify, you neither muddy the water nor keep the drinker from drinking. They believed in those days that toads cleansed water, and this was the height of what the man could set against the Book of Allah. One of the Arabs who passed through his country, not yet a Muslim himself, heard a recitation of it and told him to his face: by Allah, I know you are a liar. Even an idolater could hear the difference. When Banu Hanifa's two messengers later carried Musaylima's letter to Madinah, declaring the earth now split between him and the Prophet ﷺ, half and half, the Prophet ﷺ heard their open profession of the lie and said: were it not that envoys are not to be killed, I would strike the necks of you both. The Sheikh draws a point of law from the restraint. The immunity of the ambassador is older than any one religion and honoured across them all, the same principle the world today calls diplomatic immunity, a law that does not arise from the Sharia and yet which the Sharia affirms because it serves the common good. The Prophet ﷺ kept it even with men who had come to insult him.

Musaylima's end belongs to the wars of apostasy, after the Prophet ﷺ had passed, and the Sheikh says every Muslim should know the hand that killed him. It was Wahshi, the Abyssinian who had once thrown the javelin that killed Hamza, radiyallahu anhu, at Uhud. Wahshi took up the same weapon and went out against the false prophet, saying that he would atone for the best man he had ever killed by slaying the worst, and so the spear that had taken the Lion of Allah took the first of the liars.

Allah's camel, sacrificed at Jurash

Not every visitor that year came swollen with himself. One of the tribes of Azd, a great people of Yemen, accepted Islam, and the Prophet ﷺ set one of his companions over them as commander, charging him to bring in a neighbouring tribe still upon idolatry, the people of a place called Jurash. While that campaign was quietly unfolding far to the south, Jurash, knowing nothing of it, sent two of its own envoys north to Madinah to take the measure of the Prophet ﷺ and learn whether he would grant them a truce. The timing is the whole point: the two men set out before the attack, so there was no possible way for them to know what was about to happen to their people behind their backs.

They came before him, as delegations came every day, and he asked them which tribe they were from. From Jurash, they said. And he told them, plainly: Allah's camel is being sacrificed at Jurash, even as we speak. They did not understand him; it was a riddle in their ears, this talk of a slaughtered camel. But Abu Bakr and another companion leaned in and pressed the meaning on them: do you not see what he is telling you? Your tribe is being overrun this very moment, far away. Your one hope now is to beg him to ask Allah to save them. So the two men begged, suddenly afraid, and the Prophet ﷺ raised his hands and prayed that the people of Jurash be guided. The envoys carried their confusion the whole way home, and when they arrived they pieced it together: at the very hour they had stood before him in Madinah, the Muslim commander had overcome their tribe and stood on the edge of a slaughter, then chosen, of his own will, to forgive and spare them. There had been no massacre. The whole tribe took it for the sign it was, and the next delegation that came up from Jurash came not to bargain over a truce but to enter the religion whole.

Faith is Yemeni

Many of the embassies of that year came for no reason but to learn: to sit and memorise the Qur'an, to study the religion, to carry questions of their own and lay them before the Prophet ﷺ. Through these one-time visitors a surprising number of the standard reports of fiqh and even of creed have reached us, narrated by men who saw the Prophet ﷺ once in their lives and went home to teach their people what he had said. The most striking question of them all came from the Banu al-Harith of Yemen, and it is kept in both Bukhari and Muslim.

The contrast that frames it is sharp. A delegation of the Banu Tamim, from the crude and hardened north, had come and been greeted by the Prophet ﷺ with glad tidings of Paradise, and they had answered by asking instead for money, since money was the only good thing they could imagine being offered. The Prophet ﷺ fell silent and did not lower himself to their level, which is itself a lesson in his manners: when a man misunderstands you out of coarseness, you need not descend to answer him. Then the men of Yemen arrived, and to them he said, accept the good news, since Banu Tamim would not. And they answered as the others had not: we accept it, and we have come all this way to ask you about the very beginning. How did Allah create it all? How did everything start? These, the Sheikh observes, were minds of real depth, reaching past every smaller question to the greatest one imaginable.

The Prophet ﷺ told them there was Allah, and nothing before Him, and that He created the heavens and the earth while His Throne was upon the water. The Sheikh lingers on one thing the wording quietly teaches, and only that one, since the hadith has generated whole volumes of theology he sets aside today. The heavens and the earth are not the sum of Allah's creation. There was a Throne already, and water already, before this world was made; our universe is not His only handiwork. And then comes the detail that breaks the heart of every student of hadith. Imran ibn Husayn, narrating it, was drinking it in when someone called out that his camel had bolted, and he jumped up and ran after it across the sand, and caught neither the camel nor the rest of the hadith. How I wish, he said for the rest of his life, that I had let the camel go. And how the Sheikh wishes it too, because that delegation went home and did not become carriers of hadith, and so the answer to the oldest question trails off where a fleeing camel cut it short, for a wisdom Allah kept to Himself.

It is a piece of why the people of Yemen drew such tenderness from the Prophet ﷺ. The people of Yemen have come to you, he said in a hadith in Muslim, with the softest of hearts and the best of souls. Faith is Yemeni, and wisdom is Yemeni. And he prayed, O Allah, bless us in our Sham and in our Yemen, calling both lands ours at a time when neither was yet under Muslim rule. So heavy is that praise that the Sheikh notes a promise kept in Muslim: that the Prophet ﷺ will stand at his Hawd, his fountain, beating back the crowd with his staff until room is made, and the people of Yemen will be the first given to drink from his hand.

Make it easy, do not make it hard

A few months on, after Abu Bakr had led the Hajj of the ninth year, one of the kings of Yemen accepted Islam, and the Prophet ﷺ sent Muadh ibn Jabal, radiyallahu anhu, down to him as his judge and deputy. He honoured Muadh as few were ever honoured: he walked on foot beside him while Muadh rode, giving him his counsel for the long road south. You are going to a people of the Book, he told him, so call them first to the testimony of faith; and if they answer, then to the prayer; and if they answer, then to the charity. And near the end, gently, almost in passing: it may be that you will not see me after this year of mine. Neither of them named what that was, but it was a farewell, and Muadh would indeed never see him again.

His last word to Muadh is the one the Sheikh asks every listener to take home and keep. Make things easy, and do not make them hard. Give people glad tidings, and do not drive them away. Bring hearts together, and do not split them apart. People stand at different levels, the Sheikh explains; your work when you reach them is to meet each one where he is, fill in the gaps and draw him closer, never to turn him off the road by flinging a hard truth at the wrong person at the wrong moment. It grieves the Sheikh how many carry the religion the opposite way, speaking a thing that may even be true but speaking it without wisdom, to ears not ready for it, and slamming shut a door that gentleness would have eased open. And here is Muadh ibn Jabal, a scholar among scholars, sent off with the command to be easy and gentle. If Muadh was told this, the Sheikh asks, then who exactly are we, that we should be harsh in the name of Allah's religion?

The Christians of Najran

Now the long account, and the most instructive of all. Months earlier the Prophet ﷺ had written to Najran, a Christian province in the south with close ties to Rome, addressing its people in the name of the God of Ibrahim and Ishaq and Yaqub and laying three roads before them: accept Islam, pay the jizya, or stand and fight. They gathered to weigh it. Some said openly that a prophet had long been foretold from the line of Ishmael, that there were clear signs of him in their own scriptures, and that perhaps this was the man. Others said that even if he were no prophet but only a king, they had better gauge his strength first. So they agreed to send a delegation north and judge for themselves: sixty men, one of the largest embassies the Prophet ﷺ ever received, where most numbered two or five or ten. Among them were two dozen of their nobility, a handful of bishops, and over them all a patriarch, their senior most figure.

They arrived dressed unlike any Arabs the companions had ever laid eyes on, in the manner of the Romans, and the companions marvelled that they had never seen a delegation like it: their bearing, their manners, their clothing, all of it foreign. When the hour of their own prayer came upon them inside the Masjid, the companions moved to stop them, and the Prophet ﷺ told them to let it be. So in the holiest Masjid in Madinah the Christians of Najran turned to the east, as the Christians of old turned, and said their prayers facing one of the walls, and no one disturbed them. The Sheikh marks the moment, and will return to it.

Then the dialogue began, and it ran for some days, perhaps three or four, back and forth over Isa, the son of Maryam. At the end of one long day they set down their hardest question, the one they thought unanswerable. If you grant, as we do, that Isa was born of a virgin with no father, then who is his father? The Prophet ﷺ did not answer from himself. He told them: wait, and let Allah answer you. And the next day the opening of Surah Al Imran came down, and within it the answer that dissolves the whole difficulty, for it shows that a birth without a father proves nothing of divinity at all:

Indeed, the example of Jesus to Allāh is like that of Adam. He created him from dust; then He said to him, "Be," and he was.Qur'an 3:59

Adam had neither father nor mother, the Sheikh draws out the force of it, and no one calls Adam the son of God. If creation without a father made a man divine, Adam would have the better claim, fashioned as he was from dust by nothing but a word: Be. Isa needs no father, because Allah needs only to say to a thing, be, and it is.

The curse no one would risk

Still the arguing would not end, and so Allah gave the final move. It is the verse the scholars call the verse of mubahala, of mutual invocation, and it stands a little further on in the same Surah:

Then whoever argues with you about it after [this] knowledge has come to you - say, "Come, let us call our sons and your sons, our women and your women, ourselves and yourselves, then supplicate earnestly [together] and invoke the curse of Allāh upon the liars [among us]."Qur'an 3:61

The logic of it is merciless in its simplicity. If you are so certain you stand upon the truth, here is a clean way to settle it: bring your children and your women and your own selves, and we bring ours, and together we call down the curse of Allah upon whichever party is lying about Him. Both cannot be right. Either Isa is the son of God, or he is not, and the side that lies about Allah will bear the curse. And the Sheikh explains why the verse summons the children and the wives before the men: because it is far more agonising to watch the punishment of Allah fall upon one's children and wife than upon oneself, and a man sure of his cause would not gamble his little ones on a lie. The Prophet ﷺ made his own family ready, his daughter and Hasan and Husayn and Ali, prepared to walk out and stake the people he loved most on the truth of what he carried.

And the next morning, the Christians of Najran withdrew. Among themselves they had reasoned it to its end: if this man truly is a prophet, and we go through with this curse, we will be wiped from the face of the earth. So they came back and said, we decline the mutual curse, and we will accept the jizya. We will not leave our religion, but we will not stand in that field against you. The Prophet ﷺ set them a measured tribute, a fixed amount of cloth and silver each year, and bound it, in his characteristic wisdom, only for as long as it suited both sides, never forever, always with the clause that either party might one day end it. They asked him, further, for a man to judge their internal disputes, since they had seen the Muslims to be honest, and he sent them one of the companions.

The Sheikh draws the lessons out as gently as the day asks. The mubahala, first, is a last resort and never an opening; it is almost always a threat held in reserve rather than a thing carried out, the final word at the very end of legitimate argument, and not something Muslims are encouraged to reach for. Then there is the patriarch. On the road home, the most senior man among the sixty was overheard by his younger brother forbidding anyone to speak against the Prophet ﷺ, and when the brother asked why, he admitted it outright: because he is the prophet, the one foretold. Then why did you not follow him? And the old man gave the truest answer in the whole account: because of the honour and the wealth and the standing the Roman emperor has given us, which following this man would cost. The brother was so shaken to hear it said aloud that he broke from his people then and there and came to Madinah a Muslim. The bulk of mankind, the Sheikh observes, simply follows the men it looks up to; the senior had seen the truth and the rest had only followed the senior, which is exactly why the Prophet ﷺ always reached first for the chieftains, knowing that when the head of a people turns, the people turn behind it.

And then there is that wall in the Masjid, and the Christians praying toward it unmolested, which the Sheikh holds up as the very height of what he calls realistic pluralism. The Prophet ﷺ tolerated the People of the Book and never once forced a soul among them. He did not tolerate idol worship, and would not grant the idolaters a single day's delay in tearing down their idols, but a guest who believed differently he honoured, and fed, and housed, and let pray in his own house of worship in his own direction, even knowing the words being said were words no Muslim could call a valid prayer. This is not the soft lie that every path is equally true, which is in the end a nonsense, since each faith holds the others wrong on the very things that matter most. It is something firmer: clear that there is one road to Allah, and just as clear that no one is dragged down it. Let the Christian keep his religion and the Jew his, and let the dialogue be as firm as it needs to be, firm enough at its furthest edge to call down the curse of Allah, and still no hand is ever laid on anyone. This was tolerance with its eyes open, certain of one truth and tender with every soul.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this last day of the delegations as a string of curiosities, a false prophet and a far-off miracle and a curse declined, and to file it away as the colourful tail end of a great year. That would be to walk past the questions it sets quietly in front of each of us. The year of delegations does not close with a battle. It closes with a doorway, and tribe after tribe walking through it, the proud and the seeking, the pagan and the Christian and the half convinced, until the whole peninsula stood gathered under one rule for the first time in the history of the world, and not one sword had been raised to gather it. The fruits of patience, the Sheikh says, are always sweet. Allah kept His promise to His Prophet ﷺ. He keeps it still.

Look hard at Musaylima before you look anywhere else, because his ruin is the most ordinary of all and the nearest to us. No enemy destroyed him. His own importance destroyed him. A lifetime of being revered had convinced him, by slow degrees, that the world owed him something, until at last he believed it owed him a share even of prophethood, and he was offered for it not a kingdom, not a title, but a worthless stick the Prophet ﷺ would not so much as place in his hand. Watch, then, what a little reverence does to a soul, because it does its work quietly, and it does not spare the religious. Guard your heart from your own status before your status hollows it out as it hollowed his.

Take, too, the last thing the Prophet ﷺ ever said to Muadh, and carry it the way it was given. Make things easy and do not make them hard; give people glad tidings and do not drive them away. If the gentlest possible instruction was the one entrusted to one of the greatest of the companions, it is the one entrusted to us, who are so much smaller and so much quicker to be harsh in Allah's name. A true thing flung at the wrong heart at the wrong hour closes a door that mercy would have opened. And learn the lesson of the Christians of Najran alongside it, for they stood in front of the Prophet ﷺ himself, heard the Qur'an answer their hardest question out of the unseen, and many still would not believe, holding instead to their honour and their wealth. If sincere people would not all be convinced by him ﷺ, do not break yourself when your own da'wah does not land. You are asked to be clear and to be kind. You were never asked to force a single heart, because guidance was never in your hands; it is with Allah, who let those Christians pray toward their own wall in his Masjid and sent them home in peace with their treaty intact, firm in one truth and merciful to every soul, and the two were never opposites in him.

So leave the day with the prayer those many delegations came so far to ask of him. They crossed deserts not to bargain but to beg a single du'a from his mouth, the lightest thing in the world to ask and the heaviest to be granted. O Allah, keep our hearts firm upon Your religion as You kept firm the hearts of those who came to him ﷺ from every horizon. Make us easy upon other people as You commanded Your Prophet ﷺ to be easy. Gather us among the people of the softest hearts and the best of souls, and let us be of those given to drink from his hand at his Hawd, a drink after which there is no thirst again. May Allah send His peace upon His Messenger ﷺ, who would not hand a liar a worthless branch nor deny a guest a place to pray, and who waited in patience until, in a single year, the whole of the earth he was sent to came walking to his door.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 96: the year of delegations, part 3 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (3:59, 3:61), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry weak or bizarre reports, the Sheikh's own weighing of them has been followed.

The Seerah · 97 of 104

Maria and Ibrahim

The son he held, the day even the sun was blamed

By this point in the seerah we are almost at the end of his life, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi turns aside from the great public events, the campaign to Tabuk, the long parade of delegations, to catch up with something quieter that has been waiting in the background all along. It spans two and a half years, the eighth, ninth, and tenth of the Hijrah, and at its center is a single small life: the birth and the death of the Prophet's ﷺ infant son, the boy he named after his own father, Ibrahim. To tell that story honestly we have to tell the story of the child's mother first, Maria the Copt, and around her sit several of the reports that modern readers are made to stumble over. The Sheikh chooses to walk straight into them rather than around them, and so will we.

There is a reason for that choice, and he says it plainly before he begins. Some of this story gets quietly dropped from modern seerah books. Most contemporary authors simply gloss over it, and in a world before the internet, the Sheikh observes, that silence might once have passed for tact. Today it does the opposite. There is no point hiding information or pretending it does not exist, because you will not get anywhere by it. A young brother or sister logs on, reads a verse or a hadith they were never taught in Sunday school, and the shock of it shakes their faith loose, not because the report is a lie but because no one who loved them ever told them it was there. They had built a sheltered picture, and reality cracked it.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi speaks here from his own experience, because the phone calls are real. More than once a young man has called him already having decided he is no longer a believer, quoting incidents lifted straight out of the books of seerah. He heard them not from a teacher but from people with an agenda, who lined up one narration from this year and another from that year, ignored the thousand stories that show the beauty of the Prophet ﷺ, and concentrated on the two or three that sting. And the hard thing, the Sheikh says, is that they are not even lying. They quote directly from the sources. The only thing they hide is the context.

So the choice in this chapter is deliberate. Tell it openly, attribute it honestly, and let it stand inside the belief that frames everything: that this man ﷺ was the Messenger of Allah, and that what Allah sanctioned for his time and place was permitted, even where the ummah has long since agreed to leave the practice behind. It is not the Sheikh's job, nor anyone's, to distort what happened. It is his job to convey and to teach. If people accept it, they accept it; if they reject it, that is not a rejection of him but of Allah and His Messenger ﷺ. And honestly, he adds, most of what follows is not even that difficult once it is placed in its world. The discomfort is ours, projected backward. The man at the center of it did nothing wrong.

A gift from Egypt

After Hudaybiyyah, in roughly the eighth year of the Hijrah, the Prophet ﷺ sent letters calling the kings and rulers around him to Islam, and one of them went to the ruler of Egypt. The Arabic sources call him the Muqawqis, but that is a title, like Caesar or Kisra, not a name, and Egypt in those days was no independent kingdom. It sat under Byzantine rule, and this man was not a king at all but a patriarch the Romans had appointed, for there was no separation of church and state then and the empire installed its religious men to govern. Looking through the books of history, the Sheikh identifies him as Cyrus of Alexandria, the Arabicized Jurayj, the man who would prove to be the last Byzantine prefect over Egypt, since the Muslims conquered the land within twenty years. He was a Melkite Christian, of the tradition that would later become the Greek Orthodox church, and he was no minor figure; he was a genuine theologian who wrote on the nature of Christ, a man still admired in that church to this day.

No record of his reply survives the way Caesar's does, but everything in his conduct points one way. He did not tear the letter up. He did not mock it. He answered with courtesy and sent back a small fortune: a thousand measures of gold, fine garments, honey, a mule named Duldul that the Prophet ﷺ would keep and ride for years, a servant named Mabur, and two Coptic Christian sisters, Maria and Sirin. The two young women, the books say, had been daughters of a noble family who, by a custom of that church not unlike the one we read of with the family of Maryam and Zakariyya, had been gifted into the care of the church, and so it was the patriarch who now sent them on. Reading the polite letter and the lavish gifts together, the Prophet ﷺ remarked that this man had safeguarded his rule by his courtesy, but that his rule would not last; he would die a natural death, untouched in this world for his kindness, and his line would end with him. Within twenty years Egypt fell, and he was indeed its last Byzantine prefect. It was, in its way, a quiet prophecy.

A man cannot keep two sisters at once, so the Prophet ﷺ gave Sirin to the poet Hassan ibn Thabit, radiyallahu anhu, and she bore him a son, Abd al-Rahman. Maria he kept for himself.

Honest about a hard thing

Here is the part the Sheikh refuses to soften. No amount of sugar-coating removes the reality that Maria and her sister were not wives of the Prophet ﷺ. No marriage was contracted. Maria held the status the tradition calls milk al-yamin, and which the Bible's own vocabulary calls a concubine. We know almost for a fact that she later embraced Islam, but she came as a Christian and was not a Muslim when she arrived, and in the Sharia a free Muslim woman could never have been taken into that status in the first place. We all carry a bias on this subject, the Sheikh says, himself included, and it is a fair one: we believe this man ﷺ to be a prophet, so we trust that what he did was sanctioned by Allah for its time. Someone who does not share that belief, he will not pretend to argue into it.

What he will do is set the wider world straight, because the objection is never really about the Prophet ﷺ alone. The basic pattern of the biblical prophets, on the Bible's own account, was many wives and many concubines; David is given over seven hundred, Solomon more than a thousand women between wives and concubines. Against that, a man who lived more than twenty-five years of his adult life with one wife and married plurally only in his fifties and sixties is hardly the scandal. That is a fair point to put to a believing Christian or Jew. But fewer and fewer people today hold those scriptures sacred, and the Sheikh meets that honestly too. He reads aloud from a modern rabbi answering a questioner disgusted that Solomon used masses of laborers in bondage to build the Temple, and the rabbi's reply is the same one a Muslim must give: history is brutal, and it strips us of our most cherished illusions about the past. We are all, the rabbi writes, children of the anti-slavery movement of modern times, and our discomfort with the institution is only two or three centuries old. To read the seventh century through the eyes of the twenty-first and then condemn the people who lived in it is fair to none of them.

And the way the institution lived in Islam was simply not the way it lived elsewhere. The Sharia forbids outright the enslaving of a free person; there was one narrow channel, captives of a war who were not ransomed, and nothing else. There is no counterpart here to the cruelty of the American plantation, where free people were hunted, shipped, and worked worse than cattle, a brutality the Sheikh calls perhaps the most monstrous manifestation of the institution in human history. Maria had her own home and was treated with dignity, and the sons of such women in this tradition became caliphs and kings without the faintest stigma. And it is finished. Sheikh Yasir is blunt that he knows no scholar of our tradition calling for its return; the ummah has agreed to move past what was once permitted, exactly as other faiths have. He cites his own late teacher, asked during the Bosnian war, a war the world itself ruled a legitimate defense, whether captives could be taken in the old way, and the answer was a flat no, not in our times. That is the frame. Inside it, the grief is allowed to be just grief.

The sword sent to find the truth

Maria was a stranger in a strange land, alone, with little Arabic, and rumors are cheap. Word began to spread that Mabur, the servant who had come with her from Egypt and was said to be a relation of hers, was visiting her, with everything that whisper was built to imply. This is no report from an obscure corner of the tradition; it is in Sahih Muslim, in nearly every book of the Sunnah. The Prophet ﷺ gave Ali ibn Abi Talib, radiyallahu anhu, his sword and sent him to deal with the man, for anything touching the household of the Prophet ﷺ fell to Ali, who was of that household.

Ali asked a sharp and intelligent question before he went. Should he carry out the command, he said, as a man who simply obeys an order, or should he go as one who uses his own eyes and ears, who sees and hears what an absent commander cannot? In other words: do you want me to execute, or to investigate? Go, he was told, as one who sees and hears. Find out. On that single phrase the scholars divide. Some hold that the Prophet ﷺ had a right here to judge as no one else does, even without the trial and the two witnesses the Sharia normally demands. Others, and this is the reading Sheikh Yasir leans toward, hold that Ali was sent to frighten the man and to investigate, not to execute, which is precisely why he was told to use his eyes rather than just his blade.

Ali found the man, in a date grove by one account, and the man, terrified at the sight of the sword, was exposed, whether because he fell trying to climb away or because he revealed himself to prove his innocence. Either way it became plain on sight that he had been mutilated, made a eunuch, as some peoples did to their servants, a thing the Sharia forbids absolutely even with a lawful slave. The accusation collapsed where it stood. There was nothing in it, and could be nothing in it. Ali returned and told the Prophet ﷺ that the rumor was empty, and it died.

Last night a son was born to me

Late in the eighth year of the Hijrah, Maria gave birth to a boy, and the Prophet ﷺ was overcome with joy. We do not know exactly when she entered Islam, the Sheikh searched the books and could not date it, but one may fairly assume that by the time her son was born she had long since become a believer, living in the household of the Prophet ﷺ as she did. He came to the masjid lit up, and the companions could read the happiness on his face. A son was born to me last night, he told them, and I have named him with the name of my father: Ibrahim.

Then he said something about Maria that opens a window onto the whole subject. Her son, he said, has set her free. In the Sharia, a concubine who bears her master a child becomes umm walad, mother of the child. The child is fully legitimate, equal to every other child of the father in name, in inheritance, in standing; and the mother can no longer be sold or transferred or treated as property, and she goes free the moment her master dies. So Islam, the Sheikh observes, was already crowding the institution toward its own end, hemming it in until it could barely breathe. How do you keep as property the very woman who has given you a son? It becomes forbidden. Maria was lifted, by her child, out of the only status she had ever held among them.

There was a small competition among the women of the Ansar over who would nurse the boy, an eagerness to help that has all but vanished from our time but was once a common kindness between mothers, and one of them was given the role, with a stipend for it, since it was understood as work that deserved its wage. Ibrahim lived something under a year and a half, between roughly fourteen and eighteen months by the reports. And of that span almost nothing is recorded. No dramatic episode survives, only a baby and a father seen carrying him through the city. This is one of the quiet sorrows of how the seerah reached us: we have only what the companions chose to pass on, and sometimes, the Sheikh says, that silence simply means there was nothing more to tell than a man delighting in his son, at the most adorable age a child ever has, the months of waddling and laughing and grunting recognition before the harder years begin.

The eyes weep, the heart grieves

In the first quarter of the tenth year, with less than a year of his own life remaining, word reached the Prophet ﷺ that the child was dying. The signs had begun, and those around him understood what they meant. He went out to Maria's house in al-Aliya, the quarter of Madinah that still carries the name fourteen centuries on, and some of the companions came with him. He took Ibrahim into his hands. The baby was wheezing, the breath catching and rattling, the pangs of death plainly upon him. And the tears began to fall down the face of the Prophet ﷺ.

It startled them. You too, Messenger of Allah? one asked, as though to say: even you weep? That shock is itself a piece of evidence, for it tells you how rarely they ever saw him cry in public; they were almost asking whether he, too, was human enough for this. And he answered with words the ummah has carried to every graveside since. The eye weeps and the heart grieves, but we say only what pleases our Lord. And then, turning to the child: were it not for the truth that this is a decree which must come to pass, and that the last of us will follow the first, we would have grieved for you far more than this. Ibrahim, you are leaving, but I am coming after you, and we will be gathered.

Grief, the Sheikh draws out carefully, was never the thing forbidden. Sabr does not mean an empty face or a heart of stone. It governs the tongue and the limbs, not the feeling itself. You may feel the loss in full, you may let the tears come, and none of that is a breach of patience. What sabr forbids is that you wail against the decree, that you strike yourself or tear your hair, that you cry out why is this happening to me. By and large, he notes with relief, most Muslim societies have abandoned those old pre-Islamic practices of beating oneself in grief, though they linger in a few cultures still. The Prophet ﷺ prayed the janazah over the infant, with four takbirat, and Ibrahim was buried in al-Baqi, in a grave still known today. And here the Sheikh asks us to stand back and look at the whole shape of this man's life: a father who died before he was born, a mother lost in childhood, then his grandfather, then Abu Talib, then Khadijah his wife of twenty-five years, and every one of his children but Fatimah laid in the earth before him. Ibrahim was the last of those griefs, less than a year before his own. Allah was raising his rank, and making of him a mercy and a model for everyone who would ever stand at a small graveside after him.

The day the sun was blamed

On the very day Ibrahim died, within hours of the child's passing in the morning, the sun went into eclipse over Madinah. The eclipse itself is recorded in Bukhari and Muslim. And the people did what grieving people do: they reached for the most beautiful meaning within reach. The sun itself, they said, is grieving for the Prophet's ﷺ son; the heavens are veiling their own light out of sorrow for his sorrow. The news ran through the city, and it was a tender, loving thing to say.

Here is the moment, and the Sheikh holds you on it, because to him it is one of the clearest proofs of the man's sincerity in the entire seerah. The Prophet ﷺ had everything to gain by saying nothing. Let the people believe the cosmos itself wept for his child; what could it possibly cost him? Let the rumor spread, let the world conclude that the sun darkened for the death of his son. But he could not, because he was a teacher of the truth before he was a grieving father. He gathered the people and delivered a sermon, recorded in Bukhari and Muslim: the sun and the moon are two of the signs of Allah. They do not eclipse for the death of anyone, nor for anyone's birth. When you see this, hasten to the remembrance of Allah, and pray. On the morning he could most easily have accepted a miracle offered in his own honor, with his infant son freshly dead in the next room, he stood and refused it and called it what it was, a coincidence in the order Allah set into the heavens. A false prophet does not do that. To the Sheikh this is worth a thousand arguments: a man inventing a religion seizes such a gift; this man gave it back over the body of his own child.

And there was a deeper reason the boy could not grow to be a man, written before he was even born. A verse had already come down, in Surah al-Ahzab, and the Sheikh asks us to notice its precision. Allah did not say the Prophet ﷺ was the father of no child at all. He said:

Muḥammad is not the father of [any] one of your men, but [he is] the Messenger of Allāh and seal [i.e., last] of the prophets. And ever is Allāh, of all things, Knowing.Qur'an 33:40

The word is rijal, grown men, men come of age, not the general words for a child or an infant that the Qur'an uses elsewhere. Had Ibrahim lived into manhood, the verse would have been contradicted; instead the child remained exactly what the Arabic so exactly leaves room for, a boy who never reached it. Some companions said that had Ibrahim lived he would have been a prophet, and Sheikh Yasir is careful here: the soundest reading makes that the reflection of Anas ibn Malik and others, their own reasoning that the son of the Prophet ﷺ must surely be a prophet, and not a statement of the Prophet ﷺ himself. To it the Sheikh adds a wisdom of his own. There was a mercy in his leaving no surviving son. Look at the strife that grew even around his blessed line through his daughter, the factions that exalted his descendants past what is human; a living male heir would have become a throne, a crown passed down by blood, and faith was never meant to travel that way. We are all children of prophets, sons and daughters of Adam and of Nuh, and it saves none of us. Whoever's deeds hold him back, the Prophet ﷺ said, his lineage will not push him forward.

A human being, and no sin in it

One last report belongs to Maria, and there is no leaving it aside, because Allah placed it in the Qur'an itself. It is reported in Bukhari and beyond. One day, with Hafsa away, the Prophet ﷺ had Maria with him in Hafsa's room, and Hafsa returned sooner than expected and saw Maria leaving. She was hurt and she was angry: in my house, on my day. He ﷺ kept gentling her and calming her, until, to settle her, he swore that he would not go near Maria again. I have made her forbidden to myself, he said. And he asked Hafsa to keep it between the two of them. She went straight to Aisha and told her.

Sheikh Yasir does not dress this up, and he does not let it wound you either. There was nothing unlawful in any part of it. Maria was lawful to him; Hafsa had every right to feel exactly what any wife feels, and he understands it, every husband in the room understands the fear of a wife's distress and what it can move a man to do. The co-wives were human beings; Hafsa and Aisha were close as friends one day and at odds the next, as hearts are. What the episode dismantles is only the false picture, the imaginary Prophet ﷺ who never desired, never relented under a wife's tears, never moved a beloved away to keep the peace, as in fact he moved Maria out to al-Aliya, away from the houses by the masjid, to spare the friction. He did all of these things, and not one of them is a sin. He is the perfect human, the Sheikh insists, but a human, and that is precisely what makes him reachable. Build instead a superhuman who never wept and never wanted, and the picture shatters the first time a hard report arrives; the real man, who buried his children and still smiled at a newborn, is unbreakable.

Then Allah revealed the opening of Surah at-Tahrim, gently correcting His Prophet ﷺ for forbidding to himself what Allah had made lawful merely to please a wife:

O Prophet, why do you prohibit [yourself from] what Allāh has made lawful for you, seeking the approval of your wives? And Allāh is Forgiving and Merciful.Qur'an 66:1

The surah ordained the expiation that frees an oath, and then it turned to address the two wives themselves, naming no names but leaving no doubt:

If you two [wives] repent to Allāh, [it is best], for your hearts have deviated. But if you cooperate against him - then indeed Allāh is his protector, and Gabriel and the righteous of the believers and the angels, moreover, are [his] assistants.Qur'an 66:4

If Allah had wished this private, the Sheikh points out, He would have kept it private; instead He made it Qur'an, recited until the end of time, because there is something in it for us. Notice too, he says, the rank it quietly reveals: this is a household whose affairs Allah Himself guards, a husband whose protectors, should anyone conspire against him, are named as Allah, then Gabriel, then the righteous believers, then the angels. The young Ibn Abbas, a boy of twelve or thirteen at the time, waited years for the chance to ask Umar who the two women were, and when he finally caught him alone and put the question, Umar answered with the honesty of a man naming his own daughter: Hafsa and Aisha. If anyone could have refused the question it was Umar; he answered it plainly, because Allah had not hidden it. So the Prophet ﷺ broke the oath, gave the expiation, and Maria returned to him. The man at the center of it never once did anything wrong. He was only, gloriously, real.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this chapter only for its hard reports, to treat it as a list of difficulties to be defended, and to miss what it actually does to the heart. We are near the end of his life now, and this is among the entries that bring him closest to us. Strip it down and four things stay with you.

The first is that tears are not weakness. The companions were startled to see him ﷺ weep, as though grief were beneath a man like him, and he corrected them on the spot. The eye weeps and the heart grieves; sabr lives in the tongue and the hands, not in a dry face. We have somehow inherited the opposite lesson, that faith means feeling less, that the strong believer swallows the loss and shows nothing. He showed us a better way over a wheezing infant in his own hands: feel the loss in full, let the tears fall, and guard only what you say and what you do. You are allowed your grief. What you owe Allah is that your grief never curses His decree.

The second is that he taught the truth before he served himself. On the worst morning of that last year, with the easiest miracle in the world being handed to him, the whole city ready to believe the sun had darkened for his son, he gathered the people and gave it back. It would have cost him nothing and flattered him plainly, and he refused it, because a teacher of the truth does not let even a beautiful lie stand in his own favor. Ask yourself how often you reach instead for the kinder story, the version that makes you look better, the silence that lets a flattering misunderstanding live. He could not do it on the day it would have comforted him most. Choose the true thing over the flattering one, especially when the flattering one is about you.

The third is that we must not build a Prophet ﷺ who never existed. The picture that shatters under one hard report, the man who never wept and never wanted, was never the one the tradition handed us; we constructed him ourselves, out of a misplaced reverence, and then left our children defenseless when reality arrived. Love instead the Messenger ﷺ the books actually describe: a man given a son in his sixties who could not stop smiling, who wept openly and was startled to be asked why, who felt the pull of what was lawful to him and answered a wife's hurt by moving a beloved out of sight. None of it diminishes him. It is the opposite. A superhuman you cannot weep beside; this man you can.

And the fourth is that lineage saves no one. There was a mercy, the Sheikh said, in his leaving no surviving son, because faith was never meant to pass by blood, and the proof is written across the centuries in the descendants of prophets who sold what their fathers died for. Whoever's deeds hold him back, he ﷺ said, his lineage will not push him forward. Your descent earns you nothing before Allah; your deeds earn you everything. So take this day down into your own losses. Let your tears come, and guard your tongue. Owe the truth even when a softer story is yours for the taking. And love the real Messenger ﷺ, the one who buried nearly everyone he loved and still met his Lord saying only what would please Him. May Allah send His peace upon Muhammad ﷺ, who said the eye weeps and the heart grieves; may He gather us and our own losses with him in the gardens where no child is buried and no eclipse falls; and when our turn comes, may He let us, too, say only what pleases Him.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 97: Maria the Copt and the death of Ibrahim (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (33:40, 66:1, 66:4), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 98 of 104

The Marital Dispute and the Verse of Choice

One month apart, and a choice between two worlds

There is one season in the whole of his ﷺ life when the strain inside his own home grew serious enough that he withdrew from it. One. In fourteen centuries of being held up as the most perfect of husbands, this is the single time it ever reached that point, and Allah sent down Qur'an because of it. Dr. Yasir Qadhi does not even know for certain when it happened. The reports give clues that pull against each other, some pointing to the fifth year, some later, and he is content to leave it where the sources leave it: an incident we know happened, told in vivid detail, without a date pinned to it. What matters is not the year. What matters is the shape of it, and the way it ends.

So we approach it the way the Sheikh approaches it, gently, and with our eyes open to the humanity of everyone in the story. Because that is the first thing the day teaches. The Prophet ﷺ is the best of all men, but he is a man, not God, not divine, a human being who loved and was loved and could be hurt in the way that married people are hurt. His wives are the best of all women, the mothers of the believers, but they are still wives in a marriage with its ordinary friction. What they wanted was permitted. What he chose was higher. And out of that narrow gap came a month of distance, a bare little room with a fiber mat in it, and a verse that still asks every reader who meets it to choose.

What the mothers of the believers asked for

For most of his life there was almost nothing to ask for. He ﷺ was born an orphan; his father had already died, and there was no one to provide for him. His first work was as a shepherd, herding the flocks of Makkah for a few coins, pennies for an entire day's labor in the sun. He lived crowded into the house of Abu Talib among the other children, and the first small relief came only with his marriage to Khadijah, when at last he had a home of his own. Even then it was no life of luxury. He did not so much as own a riding animal until the Hijrah; he could not afford one, and until then had no need of one. The wealth of the world simply was not his.

Then, slowly but surely, it began to come, not to him, but to the ummah around him. After Badr, more than seventy captives were ransomed back, each for thousands of silver coins, and the tide began to turn. After the conquests the first truly large break came at Khaybar, whose fields were handed over, and the gift of Fadak, a sister settlement, which became his ﷺ own personal income and the main source that fed his household from the seventh year onward. Then came Hunayn, the largest accumulation of wealth the young ummah had yet seen, and from all of it he kept, for himself, not a single coin. The Law allowed the leader his share; he gave it all away, by choice. And as the believers around them began to live a little more comfortably, his wives, may Allah be pleased with them, asked for the same. A better house, perhaps, better furnishings, a portion of the standard that was rising on every side.

Hear this clearly, because Dr. Yasir Qadhi makes it the heart of the episode and repeats it so that no one can mistake it. The exact words were never recorded, and it is right that they were not: this was the private speech of a home, and who would write down what a wife says to her husband behind their own door? But what they asked for was not a sin. It was not even disliked. It was halal, the most natural human wish in the world, and wanting it took nothing whatsoever away from their noble rank. Let no one ever feel, in the telling of this, that we are diminishing the mothers of the believers, for we are not. Two of the wives gathered the others into a shared request, the way any case is stronger when more than one voice carries it, and they were asking their husband, who genuinely did have access to wealth, for a fair portion of what was already his. Nothing in the story lowers them. The whole weight of it falls on a man who had decided, for our sake, to live with far less than he could have had.

The women of Madinah

The story comes to us mostly in the first person, from Umar ibn al-Khattab, radiyallahu anhu, because two of the wives at the center of it were Hafsa, his own daughter, and Aisha. His son-in-law Ibn Abbas had been desperate to ask him about the whole affair, and out of respect for Umar's time he waited more than a year for the right moment, never barging in, until one day on a journey he was helping Umar make wudu and finally put the question to him. And Umar told him the whole of it.

He set the scene with something that had once unsettled him. In Makkah, he said, we were a people who dominated our women. Then we came to Madinah and found a people whose women answered them back, and our women began to learn it from theirs. One day he grew angry with his wife over some matter, and she answered him, defending herself, and he rebuked her: how dare you reply to me? And she shot back, why are you so surprised? The wives of the Prophet ﷺ answer him too, and one of them will keep her distance from him for the whole of a day. This was not Hafsa's mother, but another of Umar's wives, and the very fact that she knew it tells you something: a community always knows, in broad strokes, the texture of the homes within it, and the household of the Prophet ﷺ was no exception.

It stopped Umar cold, and he went straight to Hafsa. Do you answer him back? Do you leave him for the day? Yes, she said. Both. And here the Sheikh asks you not to flinch but to feel the humanity of it. Strip away the superhuman picture some of us carry, he says, and remove it from your mind, because it is false: he ﷺ is the best of men, but a man, and his wives are the best of women, but wives. This is the portrait of a real marriage, with its back-and-forth, its quiet half-days, its ordinary irritation, and a husband ﷺ who tolerated all of it without ever once imposing on his wives the harsh Makkan customs he had grown up with. Things were permitted to them that would have been unthinkable, even a kind of disbelief, from anyone else, precisely because they were his wives. Umar, frightened for his daughter, rebuked her hard and told her never to answer him back and never to press him for anything she needed, to come to him, her father, for any extra she wanted instead. And then he said the thing that reveals his fear: do not let the standing of your companion deceive you. Aisha is more beautiful in his eyes and more beloved to him than you are; she can get away with what you cannot. The one we love most, he understood, is forgiven what others are not. It is how parents are with a cherished child, and Umar knew his daughter did not have that margin.

Something worse than war

Then one night, after Isha, when the city had gone dark and people had already lain down to sleep because there was no light to keep them up, Umar's neighbor came pounding on his door. In those days a knock at that hour meant only one thing, and Umar rushed out certain of the worst the times could hold: have the Ghassanids attacked? They were a standing fear along the northern frontier, an enemy that might descend on Madinah at any time. No, the man said. Something worse than that has happened. The Messenger of Allah ﷺ has divorced his wives.

Sit with that for a moment, because Dr. Yasir Qadhi will not let it pass quickly. For these Companions, an enemy army on the march was the lesser disaster. A crack in the household of the Prophet ﷺ was the greater one. A communal catastrophe would wound them; a personal grief of his ﷺ wounded them more, because they loved him so completely that the breaking of his home outweighed the threat of war. By morning the rumor had swept all of Madinah. When Umar came to the masjid after Fajr he found the people gathered there, turning pebbles over in their hands, waiting, weeping, an entire community undone by the thought that his ﷺ home might be coming apart. The young Ibn Abbas, a boy of ten or eleven, remembered that morning to the end of his life: the families of the wives all in tears, the masjid full, the whole city pulled into a single private sorrow.

Umar went to the homes first. He found his daughter Hafsa weeping and rebuked her: did I not warn you that this would happen? Then he went to the apartment of Aisha and called out at the curtain, daughter of Abu Bakr, have you now gone so far in troubling the Prophet ﷺ? But Aisha had a tongue as quick as his own, and she answered him back: son of al-Khattab, go and see to your own vessel. Hafsa is as much in this as I am; worry about your own daughter. He learned, then, that this was not the fault of one of them but of both. And he learned something else: the Prophet ﷺ had not gone to any of his wives at all. He had withdrawn alone to a small chamber above the masjid, reached by a ladder, a bare little room he otherwise almost never used.

The marks of the mat

This attic room is mentioned in the books of seerah almost nowhere except here, and that is how we know it existed: a small private space the Prophet ﷺ would climb to occasionally for solitude, never to live in, holding little more than a mat and a vessel of water. Now he had gone there to be alone. A servant stood at its entrance, and Umar asked him three times to seek permission to enter before it was granted. He raised his voice the third time so the Prophet ﷺ would hear, and he made sure his purpose was understood: I have not come as a father-in-law to plead for Hafsa. If the Messenger of Allah ﷺ commanded me to strike off her head, he said, I would obey. I come only as a friend, to learn what has happened.

Inside was the scene that has reached every Muslim child since. Some barley, dried, a mere handful of it. A half-tanned skin of water. A vessel in the corner for the body's needs, and almost nothing else. The Prophet ﷺ was reclining on a mat woven from the rough fiber of the date palm, his upper garment set aside, and when he turned to face Umar the coarse weave had pressed its marks into the skin of his side. The most honored man on the face of the earth, and his bed had left lines in his body. Umar looked at the bareness of it and wept.

His first question was the only one that mattered: O Messenger of Allah, have you divorced your wives? No, the Prophet ﷺ said. Allahu akbar, Umar breathed, and from this the Sheikh notes a small recurring sunnah: that one says Allahu akbar on hearing good news. Then, to soften the air, Umar told the whole story of his own wife answering him back and how the women of Madinah had taught his women to do the same, and the Prophet ﷺ smiled, and Umar took heart, and asked leave to sit and talk, for he had been standing tense the whole time. The Prophet ﷺ smiled again and let him. Here the Sheikh pauses on the gentler, wiser Umar we so rarely picture, the man who reads a friend's pain and sets out simply to lift it. Then Umar lifted his head and looked around the bare room and could hold it no longer: O Messenger of Allah, make du'a that Allah expands your provision. Persia and Rome live in luxury, and they do not even worship Him; how can you, the chosen one, live like this? And he began to weep again. The Prophet ﷺ sat up. Are you in doubt, O son of al-Khattab? Are you not pleased that they should have this world, and we the next?

Dr. Yasir Qadhi stops the whole episode here, on that one line, because it answers a question that still gnaws at us. Why are the believing nations so often not the richest? Why is the wealth and the technology and the GDP so often elsewhere? Part of the answer, he says, is right here: those people may have been handed the good of this life precisely because they have no share waiting in the next. Every good deed a disbeliever does, and they do many, must be repaid somewhere, and if not in the Hereafter then here, in ease and prosperity and comfort. And the deeper point is simpler still. Success is not measured in coin. It is not weighed by what a people own. It is weighed by faith, by ethics, by virtue, and by that measure true success was lying right there on the palm-fiber mat. Umar's answer was instant: ask Allah to forgive me. It was a mistake even to have wished for more.

A choice between two worlds

What the Prophet ﷺ had done, Umar now learned, was take an oath, an ila, to keep apart from his wives for one month. The fiqh of it is precise, and the Sheikh lays it out: a husband may swear off intimacy with his wife for a fixed period, up to a maximum of four months, to let a heated dispute cool while both sides think it through. It is permitted, and it is wise, one of the surest ways to defuse a quarrel that has grown too hot: step apart, let the heat drain, let each side come to see how far it went. The Prophet ﷺ used this device exactly once in his life, for one month, and he lived it out in that bare room.

On the twenty-ninth day he returned. Aisha had been counting every single day, in her distress, waiting for the thirtieth, anxious that he might come back early and break his oath to Allah before its time. So when he came on the twenty-ninth she corrected him: you swore a month, and only twenty-nine days have passed. Gently he taught her that a month can be twenty-nine days as well as thirty. And then, beginning with her, he laid before each wife the verse Allah had just sent down. He told Aisha not to be hasty about it, to go and consult her parents before she answered, knowing that her mother and father would never in this world tell her to leave him.

The offer was stark and merciful at once:

O Prophet, say to your wives, "If you should desire the worldly life and its adornment, then come, I will provide for you and give you a gracious release. But if you should desire Allāh and His Messenger and the home of the Hereafter - then indeed, Allāh has prepared for the doers of good among you a great reward."Qur'an 33:28-29

If you want this world and its adornment, the wealth he truly did have access to is yours: take it, with a gracious, gentle release, no harshness, no bitterness, simply a beautiful parting. But if you want Allah and His Messenger ﷺ and the home of the Hereafter, then Allah has prepared an immense reward for the doers of good among you. This was the whole point of it, the Sheikh says. He ﷺ had chosen to be the model who lived with nothing, and so anyone who would remain his wife had to choose that life with him; it was not appropriate for the Messenger of Allah to live as kings live. Aisha did not even pause. What is there to consult about, she said, when the matter is this clear? I choose Allah and His Messenger ﷺ and the Last Day. She would not pray over it, would not go to her parents; some choices are too plain to need deliberation, and her answer showed not only her maturity but her iman. And every single one of the mothers of the believers chose exactly as she did, though a genuine fortune was within their reach. Not one of them considered, for a moment, leaving him.

And Allah was his protector

There is a tender footnote Umar tucks into the story, back when he was sitting in that bare room. If you ever do decide to part from any of your wives, he had told the Prophet ﷺ, know that Allah is with you, and His angels, and Jibril, and Mikail, and I am with you, and Abu Bakr, and all the believers are with you. Umar used to say that on three occasions in his life Allah sent down revelation that agreed with something he had said, and this was one of them. He had stood there as a father-in-law and declared himself, against his own daughter if it came to it, on the side of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ.

For at the opening of Surah at-Tahrim, Allah Himself declared it:

If you two [wives] repent to Allāh, [it is best], for your hearts have deviated. But if you cooperate against him - then indeed Allāh is his protector, and Gabriel and the righteous of the believers and the angels, moreover, are [his] assistants.Qur'an 66:4

Read in our own day the verse can sound severe, but the Sheikh keeps it exactly where the verse keeps it: this is a husband loved by the very heavens, his back covered by the Lord of all the worlds, by Jibril, by the righteous among the believers, by the angels behind them, even in the privacy of a domestic quarrel. And notice the door the verse leaves wide open. It opens with repentance, with hearts turning back, the whole dispute invited to fold into mercy rather than rupture. The matter was never meant to end in divorce. It was meant to come home.

And there is one last touch that the Sheikh will not let us miss, because it is the fairness of the man in its purest form. When one of the wives quietly asked him ﷺ not to tell the others that she had chosen him, hoping that perhaps some of the rest might then choose differently, he refused. Allah sent me as a teacher, he said, not as one who makes things hard. He would not use his deeper love for one of them as a reason to be unjust to the others, would not let any wife be quietly disadvantaged so that another might gain. We know, all of us know, that he loved Aisha more than the rest; and even that love never once tipped him into unfairness. The most beloved marriage in all of creation, and its justice held even here, even in its hardest hour.

What this day asks of our faith

Dr. Yasir Qadhi lands the whole long evening on the simplest thing in it. Why would a man with access to the treasures of Persia and Rome choose a palm-fiber mat that left marks on his skin, and then ask the people he loved most in the world to live simply alongside him? To sacrifice for yourself is one thing, and any of us can manage it; to ask it of your wives and children is another thing entirely. He did it not out of poverty but on purpose, deliberately, to set the standard for an entire ummah. He had been offered, the authentic hadith tells us, the rank of a prophet-king, and he chose instead to be a servant-messenger who would humble himself before his Lord, and after that he was never again even seen to eat while reclining at his ease. That choice, the Sheikh says, is itself a sign of his prophethood. No ordinary man, given the keys to the world, hands them back. He was never meant to live as a king, so he lived as a servant, and gave everyone who loved him the same clear choice the verse gives us.

And around that center the Sheikh gathers what this day teaches about marriage, and asks us to receive it without flinching. In a society where it was simply normal for a man to strike his wife, where everyone did it and it was not even questioned, Aisha swore by Allah that the hand of the Prophet ﷺ never once rose against any woman or any servant. When the quarrel grew too hot, it was he who left the home, not she, and the Sheikh draws the line plainly: you never put a woman out of her house. The man is the one who steps away. The wives of the Prophet ﷺ went nowhere; they stayed in their homes, and he withdrew. Cultures differ, he reminds us, in how men and women carry themselves, and Islam allows a wide spectrum of that, asking only that its outer limits be kept. And a marital trouble is not a shame to be buried in silence. The whole of Madinah knew that his ﷺ home was in difficulty, and he was not embarrassed by it, because a problem carried into the light, brought to family or to wise counsel, is a problem that can be healed. It is the men, especially, who hide such things and so let them fester; he ﷺ did not hide his.

So let this day ask of you what it asked of the mothers of the believers. They were laid the two worlds side by side, comfort now or Allah and His Messenger ﷺ and the everlasting home, and they chose, freely, with a fortune within reach, and to the very last of them they chose him. Ask yourself how much of your own striving is for the adornment of a life that ends, and whether, set plainly beside the home that does not, you would choose as Aisha chose, too quickly even to deliberate. Measure your life, as the Sheikh says, not by its GDP but by its faith. Carry your own home with the gentleness he carried his, stepping away to let the heat cool rather than wounding, never embarrassed to seek help. The best marriage in the history of the world was not the one without storms. It was the one whose single serious storm, in a whole lifetime, was weathered so beautifully that we are still learning from it fourteen centuries on. May Allah make us of those who choose Him and His Messenger ﷺ over every passing comfort, who hold our homes with mercy and our hearts with patience, and who are gathered with the mothers of the believers in the home of the Hereafter, the great reward He has promised the doers of good.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 98: the marital dispute and the verse of choice (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (33:28-29, 66:4), verified via quran.ai.

The Seerah · 99 of 104

The Hajj of Abu Bakr ﷺ

The year the idols left the pilgrimage forever

Makkah had fallen the year before. In Ramadan of the eighth year of the hijrah the Prophet ﷺ had entered the city he was born in, and the three hundred and sixty idols crowding the Kaaba had come down, one after another, until the House stood clean. You would think the pilgrimage would follow at once. It did not. There was a season between Ramadan and the next Dhul-Hijjah when the Muslims could have gone up for hajj, and they did not go, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi wants you to sit with why, because the reason is not the one you would guess.

It was not that they were too busy. A duty as weighty as hajj is not set aside for a crowded calendar. The honest answer is harder and plainer: Makkah was not yet safe. Taif sat an hour up the road, a large and bitterly hostile city that had not yet submitted, still armed and still watching. You delay an obligation for danger, not for delegations. So in that eighth year there was no formal pilgrimage out of Madinah at all. A small group of Muslims under the local governor of Makkah performed the rites, the first time believers had openly made hajj since the dawah began, but the Prophet ﷺ and the great companions in Madinah stayed where they were. The city had been conquered, but a conquest is not yet a cleansing, and the time was not right.

By the ninth year it was. Taif had finally accepted Islam, the great northern threat had been answered at Tabuk, and the road south lay open. So the Prophet ﷺ decided that this year the pilgrimage would finally be set in order, and he made a decision that surprised everyone: he would not lead it himself. He sent Abu Bakr radiyallahu anhu at the head of three hundred Muslims, with twenty-five animals marked for sacrifice, to take charge of the hajj of that year. And as the caravan pulled out of Madinah, the proclamation that would end the age of idolatry forever was still up in the heavens, not yet sent down.

Why he sent Abu Bakr and stayed

Two things had to be true before the Prophet ﷺ would stand at the head of a pilgrimage, and in the ninth year only one of them was. The idols were gone, yes. But pagans were still streaming into Makkah from every corner of Arabia, carrying with them the customs of the days before the light, and one of those customs he would not so much as stand beside.

He said so plainly. There are people, he told Abu Bakr and the others, who still circle the Kaaba naked, and I do not wish to perform hajj until that is brought to an end. That was the whole of his reason, and it was reason enough. It was not befitting for the Messenger of Allah ﷺ to take his place in a pilgrimage shot through with that kind of vulgarity. So he held back one more year, and he sent his closest friend ahead in his place.

There was a second thing the Sheikh is careful to set out. The pilgrimage of the ninth year would still be performed in the old order, the way the Arabs had always done it, because the Prophet ﷺ had not yet taught them the corrected rites. That lesson he was saving for the year to come, his own farewell hajj, when he would show the ummah for all time how the rite was meant to be done. So Abu Bakr radiyallahu anhu goes ahead not to perfect the hajj but to lay its foundation: three hundred believers behind him, twenty-five animals before him, and a single charge to carry out. He goes to make certain that from this year forward, Makkah belongs to one worship and one only. No more idolaters. No more idol rites. Ever again.

The custom he would not stand beside

It needs to be understood, because the modern reader recoils from it and rightly so, that the people who circled the House with nothing on did not think of it as shame. They thought of it as piety. It was not everyone, and not at every hour. The Sheikh estimates, frankly guessing at the proportion, perhaps one in a hundred, a display of extra devotion that grew more common in the dense crowds of the hajj season and more common still in the dark of night. The Quraysh themselves abstained from it, but not out of any decency. They abstained out of arrogance, because they held themselves to be the elite people of the Haram and decided the rules of the sanctuary simply did not bind its own custodians.

Where could such a thing have come from? The Sheikh walks you to the very page of the Qur'an that answers it, in Surah al-A'raf, and the whole passage before the answer is about clothing. It tells how Shaytan worked to strip Adam and his wife and expose them, and then Allah speaks, and what He says is that He sent clothing down.

O children of Adam, We have bestowed upon you clothing to conceal your private parts and as adornment. But the clothing of righteousness, that is best. That is from the signs of Allah that perhaps they will remember.Qur'an 7:26

Dwell on the claim inside it. We are the one creation in this physical world that wears something beyond what we were born in, and that covering is not an accident of culture. It was, quite literally, sent down. It is a gift from above, a sign of Allah. And then, a few lines on, comes the indictment of the people who would tear that gift off in His own sanctuary:

And when they commit an immorality, they say, "We found our fathers doing it, and Allah has ordered us to do it." Say, "Indeed, Allah does not order immorality. Do you say about Allah that which you do not know?"Qur'an 7:28

A fahisha, the Sheikh explains, is a sin of a particularly vulgar or lewd nature, and circling the House unclothed was exactly that. Notice how Allah handles the two excuses the pagans reach for. The first, that their forefathers did the same, He lets stand without rebuttal, because it happened to be true. Their fathers really had done it. But a true fact is not therefore a valid reason, and Allah simply lets the dead weight of it lie there. The second excuse He crushes outright: that Allah Himself had commanded the practice. No. Allah does not command immorality, whatever a people may claim in His name.

And the justifications these pilgrims actually gave should make the hair on your neck stand up, because we have heard them in our own time. They said they wished to come before Allah the way their mothers gave birth to them, returned to a pure and original state, unspoiled by anything man had added. The Sheikh stops here on purpose, because that is, word for word, the logic of the modern movement that wants to strip down in the name of nature, the very same appeal to a supposedly innocent natural state. Their other excuse was that it was shameful to circle the House in the same garments they had sinned in, so the garments had to go. Both arguments sound almost reasonable. That is precisely the danger. It is proof, the Sheikh says, of how the human mind, left alone with no revelation to check it, will manufacture a justification for absolutely anything it has already decided to do. You want to walk naked around the holiest house on earth, you will find a noble-sounding reason. You want to do far worse, you will find one for that too. That is why guidance had to be sent down: not because the intellect is too weak to reason, but because it is strong enough to rationalize whatever the heart already wants.

A living lesson for whoever lives among it

Before the proclamation comes down from heaven, the Sheikh pauses to take something out of all this for us, and it is one of those moments where the seerah reaches across fourteen centuries and lays a hand on your shoulder. For the first fifty-three years of his life, the Prophet ﷺ lived in a Makkah where this happened in public, women among the pilgrims included, and there was nothing in his power to do about it.
