He lowered his gaze. He fought his own battle. He did not pack up and leave the city because sin was visible in its streets.

So hold this, the Sheikh says, and he is speaking especially to the earnest young man who has convinced himself that merely living surrounded by temptation is itself a sin, that he must flee to some purer land where his soul will be safe. There is no purer land. Go east and you will find as much corruption or worse, and in any case the screen in everyone's pocket has flattened the entire map, so that the billboard you escaped is now in your hand wherever you stand. The Prophet's ﷺ own example settles the matter. When you are the minority, when you cannot change what surrounds you, you do not abandon your place. You guard your own eyes and you keep your own heart, and as long as you are protecting yourself to the best of your ability there is no sin upon you for being there. Presence in a flawed society is not the sin. Surrender to it is.

Bara'ah comes down

Abu Bakr had been gone from Madinah only a matter of hours, the caravan not yet arrived, when the opening of Surat at-Tawbah was revealed: a declaration aimed like an arrow straight at the pagans of Arabia. It is one of the very last surahs to come down, and it begins in a way no other surah does. It opens without the basmala, without "In the name of Allah, the Most Merciful, the Most Compassionate." That is no oversight, and the Sheikh relays the classical explanation for it. The basmala, it was said, is what you write at the head of a covenant of peace and protection. You do not open a proclamation of severance with the formula of mercy and bond, when the proclamation itself announces that Allah has cut His ties. There is, the Sheikh notes, no word in English as total as bara'ah. It is the washing of the hands. It is the end of every relationship. In English we say I have washed my hands of this matter, and that is the sense of it: I have nothing more to do with you.

[This is a declaration of] disassociation, from Allah and His Messenger, to those with whom you had made a treaty among the polytheists.Qur'an 9:1

What it ends is the whole web of treaties Makkah had signed over the years, in the sixth year, the seventh, the eighth, agreements made with this tribe and that. All of them are now to be made null and void. But Islam does not break a treaty by ambush, and this is a point of the Sharia the Sheikh will not let pass. You cannot tear up a covenant and attack in the same breath; it is forbidden, a betrayal of the very etiquette of faith. You must give notice. So notice is given, and it is generous:

So travel freely, [O disbelievers], throughout the land [during] four months but know that you cannot cause failure to Allah and that Allah will disgrace the disbelievers.Qur'an 9:2

Four months. Travel where you wish, in complete safety, anywhere across the land and not merely in your own tribal territory, for the grace has been widened beyond the borders of the old pacts. Settle your affairs, see your families, sell your property, pack what you own. And this grace, the Sheikh stresses, is not the gesture of a power too weak to act. Allah is not incapacitated. He is choosing to give notice, choosing to be generous, and warning at the same time that none of them can escape Him or thwart His decree. Four months, and then the bond is gone.

The verse they call the sword

Here Sheikh Yasir slows almost to a stop, because of all the ayat in the Qur'an this is the one most torn from its place and waved in Islam's face. This is the verse Fox News reaches for, the verse Spencer and Geller and every islamophobe quotes and labels the verse of the sword. Read alone, in a single line, it sounds like a sentence of death on the whole world: then kill the polytheists wherever you find them. Read in its place, fenced on both sides by what the same revelation says, it is something else entirely, and the Sheikh insists with real force that you read it in its place.

Start with the verse directly before it, which carves out its own exception with its own hand:

Excepted are those with whom you made a treaty among the polytheists and then they have not been deficient toward you in anything or supported anyone against you; so complete for them their treaty until their term [has ended]. Indeed, Allah loves the righteous [who fear Him].Qur'an 9:4

Any tribe that held a fixed-term treaty and kept it honestly, never falling short, never backing an enemy against the Muslims, is to have that treaty honored in full to its appointed end. Not abrogated. Not shortened. Honored, because Allah loves those who keep faith. The four months were for the open-ended pacts; the conditional treaties with their own honored terms are left exactly as they stand. We are, the Sheikh says, a people who are fair to those who are fair to us.

Only now, with that exception already in place, does the verse itself arrive:

And when the inviolable months have passed, then kill the polytheists wherever you find them and capture them and besiege them and sit in wait for them at every place of ambush. But if they should repent, establish prayer, and give zakah, let them [go] on their way. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.Qur'an 9:5

Read it whole and watch what the islamophobe always cuts away. First the timing: when the inviolable months have passed. Nothing whatsoever happens during the four months of grace; the clock has to run out first. Then the scope. This is not a decree against humanity. It is for the Arabian Peninsula, for the sanctuary, for the ending of open idolatry in the one land on earth where Islam would not allow it to remain. Two doors stood open to the pagan of Arabia: give up your idols, or leave the peninsula. It was never convert or die, the Sheikh says with great care, it was only ever leave Arabia or accept Islam. And the proof is bolted onto the second half of the very same verse: but if they repent and establish prayer and give zakah, then let them go their way, for they are now your brothers and Allah is Forgiving and Merciful. The sentence that begins with the sword ends with an open door.

Then the verse right after it seals the matter past all argument:

And if any one of the polytheists seeks your protection, then grant him protection so that he may hear the words of Allah [i.e., the Qur'an]. Then deliver him to his place of safety. That is because they are a people who do not know.Qur'an 9:6

A pagan, even one standing under that very threat, who comes and asks for protection, is to be granted it. You shelter him. You let him hear the words of Allah. And then, whether or not he believes, you escort him safely to wherever he feels safe, and he goes on his way unharmed. The Sheikh lands the point like a hammer: not one single human being lost his life because of the verse of the sword. Not one. It was a warning, and the warning worked. It was a notice clear enough and grace generous enough to frighten an entire peninsula into a choice, and that, exactly that, is how idolatry vanished from Arabia. He will not soften the goal, and he is firm about this too: it was the intent of Islam that open idol-worship end in this land, and it did end. But he will not let the verse be ripped away from the mercy that Allah fastened to both its sides.

Why Ali carried it

When the surah came down, someone suggested the simplest thing: just send it ahead to Abu Bakr, who was already on the road with the caravan. The Prophet ﷺ refused. None shall convey this on my behalf, he said, except a man of my own household. So he gave Ali ibn Abi Talib his own personal camel, the way a ruler hands a trusted envoy his ring or his staff as the sign that this man speaks with the ruler's own authority, and sent him galloping after the caravan to recite the proclamation to the people at the time of hajj. Ali radiyallahu anhu had not been among the original three hundred. He was sent for this one sacred task.

And [it is] an announcement from Allah and His Messenger to the people on the day of the greater pilgrimage that Allah is disassociated from the disbelievers, and [so is] His Messenger. So if you repent, that is best for you; but if you turn away, then know that you will not cause failure to Allah. And give tidings to those who disbelieve of a painful punishment.Qur'an 9:3

When Abu Bakr saw Ali riding up on the Prophet's ﷺ own camel, he asked at once the only question that mattered: am I being replaced, or am I still the leader of this hajj? Ali answered him plainly. You are still the leader; I have only come to recite Surat at-Tawbah. And the two of them traveled on together in the one caravan.

The Sheikh is even-handed about the dispute this episode has fed for centuries. Our brothers among the Shia read it as among the clearest proofs that Ali, not Abu Bakr, should have led the ummah after the Prophet ﷺ, that here is the Prophet ﷺ declaring that only Ali could represent him. But the answer, the Sheikh says, is simple and it is sitting right in front of us. By the custom of the Arabs, a treaty made by a chief, or broken by him, had to be enacted by a man of his own blood, so that the stubborn pagans, still steeped in a culture where lineage was everything, could raise no objection and could not say they would not accept the word of some lesser man. The Prophet ﷺ removed every possible excuse. That is why a member of his household had to carry the breaking of the covenant. And the two giants ride in a single caravan, Abu Bakr radiyallahu anhu the overall leader of the pilgrimage, Ali radiyallahu anhu sent for one specific errand under that leadership, by Ali's own word. We love Ali, the Sheikh says, as he deserves to be loved, and we are more truly his partisans than those who claim the name, for we believe in him as he is worthy to be believed in. We grant him every honor that is genuinely his, this great one included, with no stinginess at all. But that a man is chosen to deliver a message is not the same as a man being appointed to lead a nation.

Four announcements that closed an age

So Ali and Abu Bakr move together through Mina and proclaim it to the gathered pilgrims, pagan and Muslim alike. First the opening verses of the surah, so that every idolater present knows the clock has started and four months are running. Then four announcements that drew the borders of a new world.

No one enters Jannah but a believer. No one circles the Kaaba naked, ever again. No idolater performs the pilgrimage after this year. And every treaty with the pagans is dissolved, with four months' notice, unless it carried its own honored, fixed term. The Sheikh lingers on the first of these, that no disbeliever will enter Paradise, because it was placed first on purpose, announced to the very people who were about to scatter back across Arabia carrying word of what they had heard. It is, he says, the one principle of Islam our age finds hardest to swallow and is quickest to water down: that salvation runs through this path, and not through every path at once. But a religion that saves no more than any other religion is a religion that saves nothing in particular, and there would be no reason on earth to follow it rather than another. In this world, the Sheikh says, you have your way and I have mine, and there is no compulsion. But the next world is not a place where every contradiction is true at the same time. Monotheism and idolatry cannot both be right. The pilgrims were being told, as plainly as it could be told, that there is one road to Allah's pleasure, and this was it, and this was their final chance to take it.

And this was the only hajj in all of history in which Muslims and pagans performed the rites side by side, the last time the two would ever stand together at the House. By the very next year the idolaters were gone for good. The Sheikh calls it one of the most astonishing turns the world has ever seen: in roughly twenty years an entire civilization set down a heritage of more than three thousand years and took up a new faith. There are Arab Christians to this day, and there were Arab Jews until living memory, and Islam tolerated both, the People of the Book living under its protection. But Arab pagans simply ceased to exist. The idolatry that had ruled the peninsula since before memory was wiped from it, and it was wiped not by the sword in the end but by a warning so clear and a grace so generous that a whole people chose. And the Prophet ﷺ, in the last full year of his life, stood and watched the entire arc of his mission, everything he had carried since the first trembling night in the cave of Hira, come to rest.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read the hajj of the ninth year as a tidy administrative chapter, a treaty wound up and a custom abolished, and to move on. That would be to miss what it presses on us. There are four things here worth carrying down into an ordinary life.

The first is how Allah ended the treaties, because it tells you something about how a believer is meant to handle even an ending. He did not let the covenants lapse by ambush. He gave four full months of notice, safe passage anywhere across the land, and He honored to its last day every fixed-term pact that had been faithfully kept. If even severance is done with faithfulness, with notice and fairness and a kept word, then so is everything smaller that you owe to anyone. The way you end a thing, a job, a partnership, a friendship, is part of your din, not an exception to it.

The second is how to read the hard verse. The verse of the sword is fenced on both sides by mercy: a four-month grace before it, an open door of protection after it, an exemption for every tribe that kept faith, and a promise of brotherhood for anyone who repents inside the very same line. Whenever a single fragment of revelation is held up to you to prove something ugly, about your own faith or anyone's, the discipline this day teaches is to ask what stands on either side of it. The fragment torn loose is a weapon. The verse read whole is guidance. They are not the same text.

The third is the thing the Sheikh draws out of the naked tawaf, and it is perhaps the most unsettling, because it is about your own mind. People found pious-sounding, nature-loving reasons to strip themselves bare at the holiest house on earth. The intellect, left to itself, is not too feeble to reason. It is strong enough to build a beautiful argument for whatever the heart has already chosen, and the more clever a person is, the better the argument. That is exactly why guidance had to come down from above and not up from within. When you next find your own reasoning arriving very smoothly at the conclusion your desire wanted all along, remember the pilgrim who undressed in the name of purity, and be suspicious of the smoothness.

And the fourth is the gentlest, and it sits in how the Sheikh handles Ali. Ali carrying the proclamation was a true and a magnificent honor, given for a real and sufficient reason. The Sheikh neither shrinks it nor inflates it. He gives it every ounce of its due and not one ounce more. Love that is honest does exactly that: it grants every blessing its full weight without stretching it into a claim it was never meant to bear. We can honor those we love without inventing things for them, and we should, because the inventing, however affectionate, is in the end a kind of lie.

So take these down into your days. End things the way Allah ended those treaties, with notice and fairness and a word kept to the last. Read whole what people hand you in pieces. Distrust your own cleverness when it agrees a little too readily with your appetite. And love the people you love truthfully, giving them all that is theirs and nothing that is not. May Allah, who ended an age of idols with mercy folded into the warning, never let us read His words to wound, only to be guided by them, and may He gather us at the last under the banner of the one He sent as a mercy to the worlds ﷺ.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 99: the verse of the sword and the hajj of Abu Bakr (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (7:26, 7:28, 9:1, 9:2, 9:3, 9:4, 9:5, 9:6), verified via quran.ai.

The Seerah · 100 of 104

The Farewell Hajj

One pilgrimage, and a goodbye the people did not yet understand

For nine years he ﷺ lived in Madinah and did not make Hajj. The House had been cleansed, the idols thrown down, the Quraysh broken open to Islam, and still he waited. Then, in the tenth year of the Hijrah, he announced that this year he would go, and the word ran out across Arabia like water finding every crack in the rock. From every tribe and every valley they rose and walked toward Madinah, not to fight and not to trade, but only to stand where he stood and do what he did. The city swelled. They kept coming along the whole length of the road, until Jabir radiyallahu anhu, an old blind man recounting it seventy years later, could still feel the press of it: he looked in front and saw nothing but riders and walkers, looked behind and to his right and to his left and saw the same, humanity as far as the eye could carry. Never in his entire life had the Prophet ﷺ stood before a larger gathering of human beings.

He would make the pilgrimage exactly once. Everything the ummah would ever know about how to perform Hajj, every ruling and every fine detail argued over in the centuries since, comes out of these few days, because the companions watched his every step and copied it. And underneath the joy of the largest crowd he ever addressed ran something the people around him did not yet feel: he knew he was saying goodbye.

Take your rites from me

When exactly Hajj became an obligation is a question the scholars have long turned over, and Dr. Yasir Qadhi lets the debate stand rather than flatten it. Some hold that the verse came down years earlier and the Prophet ﷺ simply delayed, which is why they permit a Muslim with the means to wait a year or two; others hold that the command came only in the ninth or early tenth year and that he went as soon as it was revealed, which is why they say Hajj must not be delayed once a person can afford it. The honest answer, the Sheikh says, is that we do not know for certain when the verse descended. What is not in doubt is the verse itself, the one that lays the duty of the House on every soul who can find a way to it.

In it are clear signs [such as] the standing place of Abraham. And whoever enters it shall be safe. And to Allah from the people is a pilgrimage to the House, for whoever is able to find thereto a way. But whoever disbelieves, then indeed, Allah is free from need of the worlds.Qur'an 3:97

He told the people plainly: take your rites of Hajj from me. He understood with complete clarity that whatever he did on these days, the ummah would do until the end of time, and so he moved through every act deliberately, an example in every footstep. He prayed in the masjid, left Madinah on the twenty-fifth of Dhul-Qadah, stopped just outside the city and prayed and entered the state of ihram, and turned toward Makkah. The journey took ten days at the steady pace of a great caravan, neither hurried nor slow, and along the entire road the crowds thickened. By the time it was over the people had already given these days a name. They were calling it the farewell pilgrimage, Hajjat al-Wada, while he ﷺ was still walking among them, sensing the goodbye in it without quite grasping what the goodbye would mean.

The name, the Sheikh is careful to note, came from the companions themselves, in his lifetime. On the day of sacrifice he would stand and say again and again, O Allah, bear witness, and he would bid the people farewell, and they began to murmur that this must be a farewell. The word was on their tongues. But its weight had not yet landed in their hearts, because the thing it pointed to, that he ﷺ would soon be gone, simply did not click. He arrived at the edge of Makkah at night and would not enter a city in the dark, nor enter it weary; he camped, slept, woke, prayed, and then, as ihram permits, washed and bathed before going in. On the Sunday morning he entered the sacred city for the last pilgrimage of his life.

The day of Jabir

The fullest record of these days is one long hadith, four pages of it preserved in Sahih Muslim, and the way it reaches us tells you something about the love that carried it. Jabir ibn Abdullah had grown into a blind old man, one of the last living companions, when the great-great-grandson of the Prophet ﷺ, a young descendant through Ali and Fatimah, came to sit with him. Jabir asked who each of his visitors was, and when he learned who this young man was he stood, placed his hand on the boy's head, and opened his shirt and laid his palm on his chest, blind eyes searching, wanting to feel the living thread that ran back to the Messenger ﷺ and to draw a blessing from it. Then he said: ask whatever you wish, my nephew, you are welcome. And the young man asked the one thing he had come for. Tell me about his Hajj.

So Jabir told it, and the brand of it is everywhere in how Muslims pray and walk and supplicate to this hour. He held up nine fingers, a blind man counting in the air, and said that for nine years the Prophet ﷺ did not make Hajj, then announced it in the tenth. At the Kabah he ﷺ touched the Black Stone and made his tawaf, hurrying the first three rounds and walking the last four, then prayed two rakahs behind the Maqam of Ibrahim, reciting in them the command to take that station as a place of prayer. Then he went out to Safa, and at its foot he recited the verse, saying as he began: I begin with what Allah began with.

Indeed, Safa and Marwah are among the symbols of Allah. So whoever makes hajj to the House or performs umrah, there is no blame upon him for walking between them. And whoever volunteers good, then indeed, Allah is Appreciative and Knowing.Qur'an 2:158

He climbed Safa until he could see the Kabah, faced it, raised his hands, and praised Allah for fulfilling His promise and aiding His servant and granting him the city without a battle ever being fought for it. Then he descended toward Marwah, and where the valley floor dipped low between the two hills he ran, the very place where our mother Hajar once ran in her desperate search for water, where she had to hurry because in that hollow she could no longer see her child. He ran where she ran. The whole rite of sai is the footsteps of a frightened mother, made holy, repeated by a hundred thousand and by every pilgrim since.

He went out onto the plain

On the eighth he led the people to Mina, and there he prayed each prayer at its proper time but shortened, teaching the pilgrim's prayer by performing it. On the morning of the ninth, the day of Arafah, he did something the old Quraysh would never have done. They had invented a private elitism for themselves: too holy, they imagined, to step outside the sacred Haram with the common pilgrims, they used to stop at the very edge of the boundary, at a place called Muzdalifah, and refuse to go out to the plain of Arafah at all. You measly mortals go and stand at Arafah, was the unspoken posture; we are the elite of the House, and we will not leave it. The new Muslims among the Quraysh assumed he ﷺ would keep their old habit, and when he sent word to pitch his tent at Namirah, on the very line where the Haram ends and Arafah begins, they expected him to stop there.

He did not stop there. He camped a stone's throw from the line, and the moment the sun crossed its peak and the time of Dhuhr came in, he called for his camel and rode straight out onto the plain itself, into Arafah, with everyone else. The Sheikh draws the lesson out: this was the death of the old elitism, the leveling of the Quraysh's invented rank, done not by argument but by the Prophet's ﷺ own feet carrying him out among the people. He came to the valley of Uranah, gathered the enormous crowd, and addressed them. Then Bilal called the adhan, and he prayed Dhuhr and Asr together, shortened, with that single call and two iqamahs, and prayed nothing in between.

Then he went to the foot of the mountain, faced the qiblah, and raised his hands. And he did not lower them. The Sheikh asks you to sit with this, because anyone who has stood at Arafah knows the body gives out after twenty or thirty minutes in that furnace of heat: you grow tired, you need to move, you need shade and water. He ﷺ stood from the afternoon until the sun went all the way down, until the last yellow light drained out of the sky and the disc slipped below the horizon, and his hands stayed lifted the entire time, asking and asking and asking. That, the Sheikh says, is the essence of the whole pilgrimage. You read it and your mind cannot hold it: hours of standing, hours of dua, nothing held back. When the sun was fully gone he mounted his camel, and he set Usamah ibn Zayd behind him, and he pulled the rein of his camel close so the animal could not rush, and he moved off toward Muzdalifah with his hand out to the crowd, calling to them over and over: slow down, slow down, there is no need to hurry. He who never had to fear being jostled, who could have ridden through any press of people, slowed his own camel to teach a stampeding ummah to be gentle. Even his pace was a mercy.

This day I have perfected your religion

It was there, on that plain, on the day of Arafah, that the verse came down. The Prophet ﷺ stood among a gathering so vast that the early scholars, having no real way to count it, simply settled on a round hundred thousand and admitted they were guessing. And into that ocean of believers, with not a single idol left worshiped anywhere in the peninsula and the whole of Arabia gathered as one to Islam, Allah revealed that today He had perfected the religion.

This day I have perfected for you your religion and completed My favor upon you and have approved for you Islam as religion.Qur'an 5:3

The Sheikh is careful, here, to guard against a common error. This is not the final verse of the Qur'an. About three months of his ﷺ life remained, and other verses would yet come down, some of them from Surah al-Baqarah among the last to be revealed. But what a moment for these words to descend: the religion declared complete and the favor declared full at the very peak of Islam's glory, when the Arabs had come in whole and the idols were all thrown down. The faith was finished not in defeat or in struggle but at its highest summit.

And the day held a second gift the people barely noticed: that Arafah, that year, fell on a Friday. Long afterward a Jewish man would come to Umar ibn al-Khattab radiyallahu anhu and say, you have a verse in your Book that, had it come down to us, we would have taken its day as a festival. Which verse, Umar asked. The man recited this one, the perfecting of the religion. And Umar answered him: I know exactly when it was revealed. Our Prophet ﷺ was standing at Arafah, on the day of Arafah, and it was a Friday. It was, the Sheikh notes, already a day of celebration twice over: the greatest day of the Hajj falling on the greatest day of the week.

It is worth knowing, as he points out, that the famous wall-poster image of one long Farewell Sermon is really a gathering of several. He ﷺ spoke at Arafah, and again at Mina on the days that followed, the tenth and the eleventh and perhaps the twelfth, taking every chance a crowd that size offered to drive his message home. What the books preserve from Arafah is the gist of it, and a companion in a distant tent later swore that he could hear the Prophet's ﷺ voice clearly even from there, a voice carried, it seems, beyond what is natural, so that Allah let the furthest pilgrim hear the words straight from his mouth.

Everything of the old days is under my feet

Listen to me, he ﷺ began, for I do not know whether I will ever meet you again after this year. The premonition was plain in the words. Then he set the law of the new ummah against the lawlessness of the old. Your blood and your wealth are sacred to one another, he said, as sacred as this day of yours, in this month of yours, in this land of yours. The law of the jungle that had ruled Arabia, where the strong simply took, where men killed and plundered and seized, was finished. Every one of you, he was telling them, has a life and a property as inviolable as the Kabah itself. And then he said it in the harshest, clearest way the Arabic allowed: everything of the days of ignorance is under my feet. The feuds, the customs, the rituals, the idol-worship, the old grievances written in blood, all of it trampled, obliterated, gone beneath his foot.

And then he proved he meant it in the most costly way a man can choose: against his own family. The first blood-debt I cancel, he said, is the blood of my own kin. A boy of the family had been killed in a feud between two tribes, and a hundred camels were owed in blood money, and the Quraysh were ready to go to war to collect it. He wiped it out, first, his family's own claim. Then he turned to interest. The first riba I abolish, he said, is the riba owed to my uncle al-Abbas. His uncle had been a wealthy lender, owed a great deal of accumulated interest from before the prohibition came down. The Prophet ﷺ erased it in front of everyone: pay him back his principal, not a single coin more. The Sheikh lingers here, because this is leadership by example at its purest. My own family will not profit from a feud, and my own family will not profit from interest, so that no man in that crowd could ever say the new rules were made for the weak and bent for the strong.

Then he turned to something a seventh-century sermon had no earthly reason to mention, and he gave it a whole passage of its own: fear Allah concerning women. He reminded them that a wife is taken under the trust of Allah, made lawful by His word and His name, that she has rights over her husband as he has rights over her, that she must be provided for in fairness according to his means. There was no social pressure on earth pushing him ﷺ to defend women that day, no movement to placate, no audience demanding it. He did it, as the Sheikh puts it, for one reason: no community rises while its women are crushed, no ummah flourishes while half of it is wronged. In a world where men beat their wives as a matter of unquestioned habit, every word he spoke to lift and protect them was leaps beyond the age. Finally he gave them the anchor that holds when everything else is gone: I have left among you that which, if you hold fast to it, you will never go astray, the Book of Allah. Then he asked them the question that mattered most to him. You will be asked about me, so what will you say? And they answered as one that he had conveyed the message, fulfilled the trust, and been sincere to them. He raised his fingers to the sky and said, three times: O Allah, bear witness. O Allah, bear witness. O Allah, bear witness. He wanted his ummah, on the Day when every messenger is questioned about his people, to be the ones who testify for him that he did his work.

All of you are from one father

On the tenth, the day of sacrifice, he spoke again, and this time he opened by making the crowd uneasy. What month is this, he asked, and they hesitated, the question so obvious they feared a trap behind it, until he answered himself: is it not Dhul-Hijjah? What day is this? The day of sacrifice. What land is this? The sacred land. Each obvious answer was a hook, because every Arab felt in his bones how holy this month, this day, this ground all were, and on that feeling he hung the lesson: then know that your lives and your wealth, and your honor too, your reputations safe from slander, are as sacred to one another as this day of yours in this land of yours. He had added honor now to blood and wealth. And he warned them sharply: do not return after me to disbelief, striking one another's necks. He told them to carry the message onward, the one present to the one absent, for perhaps the one who receives it later would understand it better than the one who first heard it.

Then came the words the Sheikh calls genuinely revolutionary, preserved as an authentic hadith. All of you are from Adam, and Adam is from dust. There is no superiority of an Arab over a non-Arab, nor of a non-Arab over an Arab, nor of one color over another, except by piety. The Sheikh stops the whole narrative to weigh what this is. No philosopher, no thinker, no ruler in all of recorded human history had ever stood and declared every human being equal. Every society everywhere, by race or by blood or by tongue, had believed itself the better stock, and had built that belief into its laws and its pride. And the one man who said the opposite, plainly, before a hundred thousand witnesses, was the man with the noblest lineage among all the Arabs, the descendant of the keepers of the House, the one human being in that crowd with the most to lose by leveling rank to dust. That it came from him ﷺ, of all people, the Sheikh says, is itself a sign that it did not come from him but from above him. Fourteen centuries later the world is still struggling, still failing, to catch up to that one sentence. We know in our hearts that it is true, and we have not yet learned to live it.

A hundred camels, and a knife he would not lift

He ﷺ had sent word to Ali radiyallahu anhu, who had been serving as a judge in Yemen, to bring up a hundred camels for the sacrifice and to meet him for the Hajj. On the day, the Prophet ﷺ took the knife himself and slaughtered sixty-three of them with his own hand, one camel, it is noted, for every year of his life, for he was sixty-three; and he gave the rest to Ali to complete the hundred. One narration adds a wonder: that as he approached the herd with the blade, the camels did not flee the way animals always flee the knife, but pressed toward him, each one straining to be the first slaughtered by the hand of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. From every one of the hundred a piece of meat was taken and cooked in a single pot, and he ﷺ ate from the meat and drank from its broth, teaching the ummah by doing it that the sacrifice is eaten, and shared, and given to the poor, the way Muslims keep it to this day.

Then a small moment that says everything about the man. He went to the well of Zamzam, where his own relatives of the Banu Abd al-Muttalib were drawing water for the pilgrims, an honored family duty, and they offered to step aside and let him draw it himself. He declined, and he gave his reason out loud: if I were to draw the water, the people would crowd in to take this task from you, because they would want to do whatever I do. So I will not. He understood, more sharply than anyone, how fiercely the ummah loved to follow his every motion, and rather than disrupt the men at their work or set off a stampede of imitation around the well, he simply took the cup they handed him and drank. Even his restraint was an act of care. He would not let his love of an act become a burden on the people who already held it.

The well of Khumm

One last incident closes these days, and the Sheikh handles it with deliberate care, because it is read very differently across the schools, and he is firm that the difference is not in the facts but in what is read into them. Ali radiyallahu anhu had come from Yemen leading his men and the gathered wealth and charity of the region, and the hundred camels. As he hurried ahead to reach the Prophet ﷺ, the man he had left in charge of the party handed out new garments from the collected charity to the whole group, fine Yemeni cloth, as a kind of reward. Ali returned, saw it, and was angered, because that charity was not theirs to give away, and he ordered the clothes returned to the collection. The men were stung, and the moment the Hajj was over they came to the Prophet ﷺ complaining about Ali.

So at a place on the road back, a well called Khumm, the Prophet ﷺ rose to Ali's defense and rebuked the complaint warmly. Whoever takes me as a friend, he said, let him take Ali as a friend; Ali is to me as Harun was to Musa. He told them plainly to stop bringing complaints against him, for Allah loved Ali and Ali had done right. And he repeated the charge about his household, the same words from Arafah folded around his family: I leave you the Book of Allah, and fear Allah concerning my family. The Sheikh affirms every syllable of this. None of it is denied, none of it hidden, all of it a genuine and high honor for Ali; Sunni Islam, he says, has never been embarrassed by the virtues of Ali nor competed with anyone over them, for his merits are more than can be counted. But the setting is the setting. These were words to defend a wronged man from unjust complaints, not a contest over who would lead the ummah after him ﷺ. And the simplest proof is the silence that followed: when the Prophet ﷺ passed away only months later and the companions gathered to settle the leadership, not one person, not even Ali's own supporters, raised the day of Khumm as a claim to succession. It had never been about that. We affirm the honor in full, and we do not read into it a politics that was never there.

What the Farewell Hajj asks of our faith

It would be easy to read the Farewell Hajj as a grand finale, a triumph, the religion crowned at the summit and sealed in glory, and to leave it there. That would be to miss what it puts to us. This was the faith handed over, finished and entire, on a plain crowded to the horizon, with the words this day I have perfected for you your religion still settling over the heads of the ummah. And it was a goodbye dressed as a victory. He ﷺ stood before more people than he had ever addressed in his life, and what he gave them was not a victory speech but a will, the last instructions of a man who knew he was leaving, to a people who did not yet know it.

Look at how he chose to spend that platform, because the choice is the lesson. He did not boast of conquest. He made every Muslim's blood and wealth and honor sacred, and then, to prove the new law was not for the weak alone, he abolished the feuds of the old days starting with his own family's blood-debt, and the interest of the age starting with his own uncle's wealth. He defended the women in his ummah's care when not a soul on earth was pressing him to. He declared every human being equal, no Arab above a non-Arab, no color above another, except by piety, the one man with the most to lose by saying it, saying it anyway. And he left them the rope that does not fray, the Book of Allah. Ask which of these you have actually taken into your own life. Whose debt have you forgiven before demanding what you are owed? Which woman in your care have you honored when no one was watching? What quiet ranking of yourself above another are you still carrying that he ﷺ trampled under his foot at Arafah? How long is your dua, really, before your body tells you to stop?

And then there is the standing itself, the heart of it all. He raised his hands at the foot of that mountain in the afternoon heat and did not lower them until the last light was gone from the sky. Hours of asking, of a Prophet whose past sins were forgiven and whose future was secured, pouring himself out before his Lord as though he had everything still to beg for. That is the measure he leaves us: not the minutes we can spare for Allah, but the hours; not the prayer we rush through to get back to our lives, but the standing we refuse to cut short. Give Allah the hours.

So take this from the hundredth day of his life ﷺ down into your ordinary one. You are nearly at the place in the story where the ummah will have to learn to walk without him beside it, and he spent his last great gathering teaching it exactly how. Forgive a debt that is yours to demand. Lift someone you could keep beneath you. Stand longer in dua than is comfortable, and do not lower your hands when your body asks you to. Keep your hands on the Book he left, every day and not occasionally, for it is the only thing that was promised to keep you from going astray. And then answer him the way the hundred thousand answered him at Arafah, fingers lifted, three times over: O Allah, we bear witness that he conveyed the message, fulfilled the trust, and was sincere to this ummah to the very end. O Allah, send Your peace and Your blessings upon Muhammad ﷺ, who said goodbye before we understood it was goodbye; gather us where the nations are gathered under his banner, let us drink from his hand at the Pool, and keep our hands on the rope he left us until the day we meet him. Ameen.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 100: the Farewell Hajj (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (3:97, 2:158, 5:3), verified via quran.ai. The narration is the Sheikh's; the phrasing is Buruja's.

The Seerah · 101 of 104

The death of the Prophet ﷺ

The day the light was laid in the earth

There is no harder day in the whole of the seerah than this one, and there is no day that asks more of our adab. We have walked with him ﷺ for a hundred days, from before his birth in a quiet house in Makkah to the plain of Arafah where a hundred thousand and more stood weeping at his last sermon, and now we have come, with heavy hearts, to the morning the ummah woke to a world that no longer had him in it. So read this slowly. Read it the way you would sit with someone you love in their final hours, lowering your voice, keeping salawat moving on your tongue, asking Allah to let the sweetness of these pages be a closeness to His Messenger and not merely a knowing of his story.

He ﷺ returned to Madinah from the Farewell Hajj in Dhul-Hijjah of the tenth year, and he did not leave it again. He had ridden out of Makkah the moment the rites were done, on the very evening of the last day of Hajj, and once he entered his city he stayed in it until he was carried into its earth. A few quiet weeks passed. Then, in the house of one of his wives, the fever began, and the last ten days of the most beloved life that has ever been lived began to unfold. Dr. Yasir Qadhi taught this seerah for ten years, and this is where it ends, and where he ends it, we end too.

The last command: an army pointed at the horizon

Before the illness, there was a final order. Toward the very end of the month of Safar he called the people to arms and made ready an expedition aimed at the lands of the Romans, at Syria and at Palestine, and at the head of it he placed Usama ibn Zayd, a young man of seventeen or eighteen. Usama was the son of Zayd, the freed slave whom the Prophet ﷺ had once adopted and loved as his own, the same Zayd the companions said no one would have hesitated to follow had he still been alive. And Usama was the son of Umm Ayman, one of the very few souls left on the earth who remembered the Prophet's ﷺ mother and father, the woman who had cared for him as an infant when he was newly orphaned. Usama had been born in the Prophet's ﷺ own home and raised under his eye, and he was loved so dearly that the people simply called him the beloved, the son of the beloved.

Murmurs spread through the city. The boy was too young, they said, and his father had been a freed slave, and how could such a one lead the senior companions, Abu Bakr and Umar among them. It was, the Sheikh notes, a real objection in their hearts, and racism is one of the four diseases the Prophet ﷺ warned would linger in his ummah until the end of time. So he gathered them and answered it head on: if you criticize his leadership now, you criticized his father's leadership before him, and by Allah his father was worthy of it, and this boy is worthy of it, and he is among the most beloved of all people to me after his father. There was a hard wisdom in the choice as well, for Usama's father had been martyred by the Romans at Mu'tah, and who better to send against them than the son seeking the place his father fell.

Usama marched out and camped a day or two beyond Madinah. Then word reached him that the Prophet ﷺ had fallen ill, and the expedition halted to wait for him to recover. He never did. And here Dr. Yasir Qadhi asks us to sit with what it means that Allah chose for His Messenger to pass with this order still standing in the field. A man who held the whole of Arabia in his hand would not let Islam stop at Arabia's edge. The faith was never meant to be an Arabian thing; it was meant for the whole earth, and for Jerusalem above all after Makkah and Madinah. With the last strength of his life he aimed his companions outward, at the horizon, at the holy land itself. Then he was called home before the army could move, and within a few short years that very force became the first Muslim army to break the Romans and open the road to Syria and to Jerusalem. He laid the foundation and did not live to walk on it, and somehow that is exactly the point.

The signs no one let themselves read

The warnings had been there all along. We can look back now and see them lined up plainly, but when they were given, not one of them landed, and the Sheikh explains why with the most human reason there is: we do not let ourselves think about the death of the people we love, and the more we love them, the less we can bear the thought. So how could anyone alive have made themselves imagine a world without him ﷺ. When Allah had said it to His Prophet, as clearly as words can say a thing, the companions had heard it and never once pictured the day.

Indeed, you are to die, and indeed, they are to die.Qur'an 39:30

So explicit, and still it did not register, because they could not let it. When Abu Bakr recited verses like this one on the day of the Prophet's ﷺ death, one companion asked, almost in disbelief, is this truly in the Qur'an, and another said it was as though he were hearing it for the very first time.

The quieter signs came thick in his last year. In Ramadan of the tenth year, Jibril came and reviewed the Qur'an with him not once, as he had done every Ramadan of the prophethood, but twice, with no explanation given, only the weight of something changed. When Surah an-Nasr was sent down, with its word that the great victory had come and the people were entering Allah's religion in crowds, and its command to glorify the Lord and seek His forgiveness, it was Ibn Abbas, still a young man, who understood what the elders did not: this was a gentle notice that the work was complete and the time to meet the Lord was near. The Prophet ﷺ told Mu'adh, as he sent him off to Yemen, that he might never see him again, and walked him out on foot while Mu'adh rode, and Mu'adh wept. He went out to the martyrs of Uhud and prayed over them like a man saying farewell. Late one night he woke his servant Abu Muwayhibah and walked to the graveyard of Baqi, and stood among the buried and greeted them and said, in effect, rest easy, and wait for me, for I am soon to follow. And he told that same servant that Allah had offered him a choice, the keys of this world and a long life with Paradise at its end, or to meet his Lord now, and that he had already chosen his Lord. Not one of the companions let the meaning of any of it through. They heard the words of a man saying goodbye and went on hoping it was only a hard season that would pass.

The fever, and the chamber he asked to rest in

The fever took hold in the house of Maymunah, around the first days of Rabi al-Awwal. It was a fierce illness, and it must be remembered that they had nothing for it, no medicine to dull the pain the way a single tablet dulls ours; the Prophet ﷺ himself had called fever a portion of the Fire that Allah lets a believer taste in this world, and a fever in that age could kill a person by its sheer ferocity alone. For a few days he kept moving from house to house, fair to his wives to the very last even when his body could barely carry him. Then, when he grew too weak, he asked their permission, though by his rank before Allah he had no need to ask any of them, to be allowed to stay and rest in a single chamber. They all gave it gladly. It was the chamber of Aisha.

And here the Qur'an had already spoken the thing the heart cannot speak.

And We did not grant to any man before you eternity [on earth]; so if you die - would they be eternal?Qur'an 21:34

If any soul in all of creation had a claim to live forever, the Sheikh says, it was him ﷺ, and even he was returning to his Lord. In that chamber Aisha would recite over him the prayers of protection he had taught her and blow them gently upon him, and keep a vessel of cool water at his side, drawing her hand through it to wipe his burning forehead, for the strongest of the companions said afterward that his fever burned like the fever of ten men together.

Settling every debt, and the friend who wept

When the fever climbed, he bound a cloth tightly around his throbbing head and asked to be carried out to the people, leaning between his uncle al-Abbas and his cousin Ali. The masjid was already full, and stayed full, because his illness was no secret and the companions who lived too far to keep watch had simply begun sleeping in the masjid out of pure worry, the way you cannot sit still in your own house when someone you love is suffering nearby. He could no longer stand on the minbar, so he sat upon it, and gently, deliberately, he began to put his affairs in order.

He warned them, with his own grave already on his mind, that Allah had cursed the peoples before them who took the graves of their prophets as places of worship, and told them plainly not to do that to his. Then he said the thing that pierced them. If I owe any one of you a debt, let him come and take it from me now. If I have ever struck anyone unjustly, here is my back, take your right from me in this world before the Day of Judgment comes. And he asked, and asked, and asked again, until at last a man, mortified, admitted that the Prophet ﷺ had once promised three silver coins to a beggar on his behalf and never repaid him, and would not have said a word except that the asking went on so long he feared the sin of staying silent. Three small coins, long since forgiven in the man's heart, and the Prophet ﷺ had them paid at once. This is the man who held the whole of Arabia, making certain he would meet his Lord owing no one anything at all.

Then he gave an order whose meaning they could not yet read. Every private door that opened from a companion's house into the masjid was to be shut and sealed, every single one, save one. The door of Abu Bakr would stay open. The houses had been built flush against the walls of the masjid, and it had been permitted all those years for their owners to enter through their own doors; now all of them were closed forever, and only Abu Bakr's remained. Dr. Yasir Qadhi reads it the way the companions would only read it later, when their eyes were clearer: of all of them, Abu Bakr was being singled out, brought near, and quietly prepared.

In that same address he told them, in the third person, of a servant of Allah who had been offered the choice between this world and what is with his Lord, and who had chosen what is with his Lord. Most of the masjid simply marveled at how fortunate that servant was, to be asked and to answer so well. Only one man understood, and he began to weep, and could not stop, weeping hard in front of them all, because Abu Bakr alone grasped that the servant was the Prophet ﷺ himself, and that the man he loved more than his own life was telling them goodbye.

The Prophet ﷺ looked at him before the whole gathering and said: do not weep, Abu Bakr. The one I trust most in his companionship and his wealth is Abu Bakr, and were I to take from all of mankind a khalil, a dearest and closest friend, it would be Abu Bakr. But Allah has already taken me as His khalil, so I cannot take another. Yet between us there is the brotherhood of Islam and its love. It is among the tenderest moments in the entire seerah: a dying man gathering the strength to comfort the friend who could not yet bear to say out loud the thing he already knew.

Go and tell Abu Bakr to lead the people

Soon he could no longer stand long enough to lead the prayer himself. He tried. He rose, and fainted, and was revived with water poured over him, and asked, have the people prayed, and was told they were waiting for him, and tried to rise again, and fainted again, longer this time, and tried a third time, until in one narration he had attempted it seven times and his knees simply would not hold him. Sit with that, the Sheikh says, and feel the shame of our own neglect: a dying man, burning with fever, collapsing again and again because he cannot bear to miss leading his people in prayer. Then he said it: tell Abu Bakr to lead the people in prayer. In ten years in Madinah, no one had ever led the prayer while the Prophet ﷺ was present in the city, not once, and the companions did not miss the weight of what they were hearing.

Aisha, hearing her own father named, tried gently to turn it aside. My father is a soft-hearted man, she said; when he stands in your place and recites he weeps, and the people behind him will not be able to hear. Choose someone else. It was not a lie, but it was not her true fear either. She did not want her father blamed for seeming to push himself forward, and she dreaded that his standing in the Prophet's ﷺ place would be welded, in people's memory, to the loss that was coming. She drew Hafsa in to plead the same case. And from his sickbed he saw clean through all of it: tell Abu Bakr to lead the people; Allah and the believers will refuse anyone but Abu Bakr; you are like the women around Yusuf. So Abu Bakr led the prayers.

Then, a day or so later, the Prophet ﷺ found a little strength, and was carried out while the rows were already standing, and seated beside Abu Bakr. Abu Bakr stepped back to give him his place, but he motioned him to stay, and so the Prophet ﷺ prayed seated, with Abu Bakr standing at his side and the rows following Abu Bakr's movements. Understand what the people's eyes saw and what was truly happening, the Sheikh says. To every eye in the masjid, Abu Bakr was the imam. In reality, the true imam was the one sitting silently beside him, and Abu Bakr was taking his lead from the Prophet ﷺ alone. Dr. Yasir Qadhi calls this the clearest sign of all the quiet signs, given a day and a half before the end. And he draws out its precise restraint: the Prophet ﷺ never named a successor in plain words, because he did not want to fix forever the custom of kings appointing their heirs. He left it open, and indicated everything without commanding it, so that the ummah after him could choose its leaders in more ways than one, which is exactly what it did.

The last smile, the miswak, and the Highest Companion

On the morning of Monday, the twelfth of Rabi al-Awwal, while Abu Bakr led the dawn prayer, the Prophet ﷺ drew back the curtain of Aisha's chamber and looked out upon his ummah standing in its rows. They had not seen his face in two days. Anas said they nearly broke their prayer for the joy of it. He was smiling, his face beaming, and Abu Bakr stepped backward thinking he was coming forward to lead, but he motioned him to stay where he was, and let the curtain fall. That was the last the companions ever saw of his blessed face ﷺ, smiling at the sight of his people at prayer. And what a fitting last sight it is, the Sheikh says, that the final image they kept of him was joy over the very thing he had spent his last breath defending.

Then the fever climbed higher. Fatima came, and seeing her father in such pain she cried out how it grieved her, oh my father, how your suffering wounds me, and he told her gently: your father will suffer nothing after this day. She was the last of his family left to him; he had buried his mother, his father, his grandfather, his uncle, and every one of his children but her, and now he drew her close and whispered something that made her weep, that Jibril had reviewed the Qur'an with him twice this year and that his time had surely come. Then he whispered again, and she smiled through her tears, for he had told her she would be the first of his household to follow him, and the mistress of the women of Paradise. She wept at the parting and smiled at the reunion, because, the Sheikh marvels, she did not want a single day of life that did not have him in it.

Aisha cradled his head against her chest. Her brother Abdur-Rahman came in holding a fresh miswak, and the Prophet ﷺ looked at it, and she understood, and softened it in her own mouth and gave it to him, and he used it with a vigor that astonished her, clean and presentable to the very end, readying himself to meet Jibril and his Lord. Then his eyes lifted upward and his lips began to move, and Aisha leaned close to hear. With those whom Allah has blessed, he was saying, the prophets and the truthful and the martyrs. O Allah, forgive me, and have mercy on me, and join me with the Highest Companion. He said it three times. The very last words were al-rafiq al-a'la, the Highest Companion, and then he was gone, a little after the sun had risen on that Monday, his head against her, on her day, in her chamber. Of all that Allah ever gave her, Aisha said, this was among the greatest: that he passed between her neck and her chest, with the trace of her own miswak still in his blessed mouth.

Whoever worshipped Muhammad ﷺ

When the news broke, the companions came apart. Umar, the mountain of a man before whom the city trembled, could not accept it; he stood in the masjid and swore the Prophet ﷺ had not died but had gone to his Lord as Musa once went, for forty days, and would return, and he would strike the neck of any man who dared say otherwise. It was not a denial of the truth so much as a heart that could not survive the truth. Abu Bakr was away; he had seen the smile that dawn and gone home for the first time in a week, certain the worst had passed. Now he rode back hard, and did not even stop in the masjid, but went straight to his daughter's chamber, uncovered the Prophet's ﷺ face, bent and kissed his forehead, and wept: how good you are in life and in death, O Messenger of Allah. Then he covered him, and steadied himself, and walked out to the people.

He told Umar to sit, and Umar, lost in his shock, would not. So Abu Bakr stood, and even now, on the worst morning of his life, his love kept him from climbing to the top of the minbar where the Prophet ﷺ had stood; he took the lower step, as he would for the rest of his days, and praised Allah, and said the words that held the ummah upright when it could no longer hold itself: whoever used to worship Muhammad ﷺ, let him know that Muhammad ﷺ has died; and whoever worships Allah, then Allah is alive and never dies. And he recited the verse that had come down years before at Uhud, on the day they had first feared they had lost him.

Muḥammad is not but a messenger. [Other] messengers have passed on before him. So if he was to die or be killed, would you turn back on your heels [to unbelief]? And he who turns back on his heels will never harm Allāh at all; but Allāh will reward the grateful.Qur'an 3:144

Umar said afterward that his legs gave way beneath him and he sank to the ground, and it was as if he were hearing the verse for the very first time, as though it had been revealed in that instant. The grief was real and bottomless. But the faith did not turn back on its heels. Allah was still Allah, and the ummah, with its heart broken clean in two, held its ground.

Now they had to bury him. They were uncertain even how to wash him, until, as they sat exhausted, a sleep fell over them and a voice none of them could place said: wash him in his garments. They woke remembering the same words, and so they washed him ﷺ with his clothes on, and shrouded him in three white Yemeni garments with no turban, and could not agree where to bury him until Abu Bakr recalled what he had once heard: that a prophet is buried in the very place where he dies. So the grave was dug into the floor of Aisha's chamber, beneath the bed he had lain in. There was no khalifa yet to lead a funeral prayer over him, so the people of Madinah came to him in waves across two full days, the men, then the women, then the children, group after group entering that small room and praying over him alone, while outside the tribes flooded in by the tens of thousands. Anas, who had seen the Prophet ﷺ ride into Madinah on the brightest day the city ever knew, said the day they buried him was the darkest of their lives, that the light went out of Madinah and a man could put out his hand and feel he could not see it, and that by the time they finished laying him in the earth they no longer recognized their own selves. They felt that empty.

And in those same final days he left the ummah a consolation meant to outlast every grief that would ever come. Whoever among my ummah is struck by a calamity, he said, let him be consoled by the calamity that befell them through me, for no calamity will ever strike anyone greater than the calamity of losing me. The loss of the Prophet ﷺ, the Sheikh explains, is the largest disaster the ummah has ever suffered or ever will; measure every sorrow of your own life against it, and let it be the grief that makes all your other griefs bearable.

There is one word more from these last days, and it crosses fourteen centuries to find us where we sit. He told the companions, near the end, how he longed to meet his brethren. Are we not your brethren, they asked. No, he said, you are my companions. My brethren are those who will come after you, who never saw me and yet believed in me, and one of them would give up all his wealth and all his family just to see me once. He was speaking of people not yet born. He was, if we dare to hope it, speaking of us. He never saw us, and he loved us, and he asked Allah for us by name in his heart. The seerah does not truly end in a grave in Madinah. It ends in that longing, and it turns the longing back upon us as a question we have to answer with our lives.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be possible to read this day as a sad and beautiful story safely sealed in the past, and to close the book a little heavier and otherwise unchanged. That would be to miss everything it is asking. The hardest day in his life ﷺ leaves us, strangely, the gentlest and clearest set of instructions, and they run straight out of how the Sheikh tells it.

He prepared even as he suffered. With the fever burning through him, his last public acts were to settle every debt, to free his conscience of any wrong by offering his own back to be struck, to give away the last seven coins he owned because he could not bear to meet Allah holding them. He died owning nothing of this world, the man who ruled Arabia, with a borrowed lamp burning in the house that night, and he lacked nothing, because he had spent his whole life rich in everything that does not rot. Ask, then, how you are measuring your own wealth, and whether you could meet your Lord today owing no one anything, with your accounts of money and of grievance alike closed clean.

And he never let go of the prayer. He collapsed reaching for it. His last instruction to the ummah from that minbar was to guard the prayer and to fear Allah regarding the weakest people in their care. His last smile was at the sight of his people standing in their rows. If the prayer mattered this much to him ﷺ at the very end of the most complete life ever lived, what excuse can any of us hold up at the easy beginning of ours.

Then there is the truth that held the world steady on the morning it broke. When the ummah was coming apart and even Umar could not stand, Abu Bakr did not steady them with comfort but with tawhid: whoever worshipped Muhammad ﷺ, he has died; whoever worships Allah, Allah never dies. Love him with the whole of your heart, give the world for one sight of his face the way his brethren would, and worship the One who sent him and will never leave you. That is the line that divides faith in a man from faith through a man to his Lord, and it is the line the whole religion was waiting, in that hour, to see whether the ummah would hold.

So take this day down into your ordinary life. Live so that you owe no one, in coin or in conscience. Guard the prayer the way a dying man guarded it. And love him ﷺ enough that his Sunnah is visible in how you wake and speak and pray and forgive, because he told us his brethren are exactly those who never saw him and believed, and that love is still on offer to anyone who will pay its price. O Allah, send Your praise and peace and blessing upon our Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. Let us live upon his Sunnah and die upon his love, gather us in his company on the Day the nations are gathered, let him know us and be glad to see us, and let us drink from his blessed hand a drink after which we never thirst again. Ameen.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 101: the death of the Prophet ﷺ (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (39:30, 21:34, 3:144), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 102 of 104

The mosque and the grave of the Prophet ﷺ

The most honored resting place on earth

There is no patch of ground on this earth more honored than the one we are about to stand beside. It is a grave, and it is a home, and for fourteen centuries it has been the place a believer's heart turns toward after the Kaaba itself. Near the very end of our hundred-day walk with him ﷺ, Dr. Yasir Qadhi does something he has not quite done before in the whole series. He sets aside the battles and the sermons and the great public turning points, and takes us inside the house instead. He shows us the bed. And then he tells us what happened to that bed, and to the body laid to rest beside it, across the long centuries that came after.

It is a strange and beautiful way to say goodbye to the man whose life we have followed. Not with a sword raised or a verse revealed, but with a room about ten feet by twelve, with a roof of palm leaves that let the rain through, and inside it the most beloved human being who ever lived. The Sheikh is honest from the start that much of what he draws cannot be known with certainty. The models built by modern architects and displayed in the museums of Madinah are careful attempts, no more, to reconstruct a thing the sources only half preserve. So we should hold the picture the way he holds it: as much as can be gleaned, drawn faithfully, and Allah knows best where the lines blur.

A house you could touch the walls of

When the Prophet ﷺ came to Madinah he built his mosque first, and against its back wall he raised homes for his family: small chambers of unbaked clay, one for Sawda, one for Aisha, and as the years passed more for the rest of the mothers of the believers. He married his daughter Fatima to Ali in those same first days, and built a house for them right beside his own. Ali, the Sheikh notes, kept two houses, one near the mosque and one out in the Quba district, but it was the nearer one, Fatima's house, that sat among the apartments of the Prophet ﷺ, and there the two of them were married.

Then the qibla was turned. The houses that had stood behind the mosque now stood in front of it, and a new prayer-niche was set on the southern wall. There was no covering at first over the open court, so they built a lattice of date-palm trunks and roofed it with woven palm leaves, the ordinary roofing of that age. The mihrab itself was never carved into any wall; it began as a tree stump and then a small pulpit, which is why, on the careful models, you will not find it cut into the stone. Of all those clustered chambers, only Aisha's opened directly into the mosque. Her doorway and the house of Allah shared a single threshold; the others had doors that were eventually closed off.

Do not picture anything grand. The Sheikh asks you to measure it honestly. The room, the actual living chamber, was about three by three and a half meters, which is ten feet by twelve. That is the size of a rug you would roll out in a living room, he says, not the size of the room: the rug. Inside that single span was the sleeping, the cooking, the storing, the whole of a life. There was a low door, perhaps seventy centimeters across, and a ceiling around a meter and a half, so low that some of us today would have to bow our heads to step inside.

Around the cluster of chambers there seems to have run a low clay wall, a barrier for the privacy of his wives, and it is to this that the Sheikh believes Allah points when He speaks of those who would rudely call out to the Prophet ﷺ from behind the apartments.

Indeed, those who call you, [O Muḥammad], from behind the chambers, most of them do not use reason.Qur'an 49:4

The wall let him move from one wife's house to the next without ever stepping out among strangers. There is a gentle dispute among scholars, the Sheikh notes, over whether a narrow alleyway ran between Aisha's wall and the mosque, or whether the two walls were one and the same. He weighs a small clue: in Sahih Muslim, the Prophet ﷺ once asked Aisha to pass him his prayer mat from the mosque, and she answered that she was in her monthly cycle and could not enter, and he told her that her menses was not in her hand and to reach in and pass it anyway. That she could simply put her hand through and draw the mat in suggests her room sat right against that shared wall. But whether a sliver of an alley lay between them or not, the Sheikh calls it plainly: a trivial question, pleasant to wonder about, impossible to settle, and Allah knows best. What is certain is that there was a curtain, not a solid door, over the inner opening, and a curtain implies a wall to hang it from. It was that very curtain he ﷺ would lift on the last morning of his life to look out one final time on his people at prayer.

Buried where he slept

The bed was a mattress stuffed with the fibers of the date palm, resting on a frame of stripped branches the way a wooden frame serves ours. Over the years the stuffing thinned, and it is narrated that those who loved him tried to replace it with something softer, and he ﷺ refused and asked for his old worn one back. There was no kitchen and no second room. They kept jugs of water hung high on the walls, out of reach of rodents; a grinding mill, for we know from the account of the slander that Aisha would fall asleep over the grinding of the barley; a small fire for cooking and warmth; a staff carved from a branch; and swords and shields hung on the wall as every household of that time kept them. Even at ten feet by twelve the room was cluttered, the Sheikh says, so crowded with the few things a life needs that when the Prophet ﷺ rose to pray at night there was not room enough to prostrate until he gently nudged Aisha to draw in her feet.

For the rest, there was a chamber pot for the night, and otherwise they walked out beyond the city, once every two or three days, because they ate so little that they needed no more. The roof is the detail the Sheikh dwells on with something close to wonder. In the whole lifetime of the Prophet ﷺ his roof was never made of solid material. It was only intertwined palm leaves, never watertight, so the rain came through. And more than the rain, the cold. The Sheikh, who lived in Madinah for ten years, can attest to it: the cold there is a sharp, particular desert cold, and a roof of loose leaves cannot hold any warmth against it. The man Allah had honored above all creation was sheltered from neither the rain nor the cold of his own home. A solid roof was built over those houses only after he ﷺ had died. This is the house the Sheikh wants burned into your memory before he tells you what came next.

When the Prophet ﷺ passed away, the companions did not know at first where to lay him, until Abu Bakr, radiyallahu anhu, recalled a thing the Prophet ﷺ had told him: that every prophet is buried where he dies. So his bed was lifted, and they dug his grave in that very spot, in the floor of Aisha's chamber. They dug it as a lahd: a shaft straight down, then a niche cut into the side toward the qibla, the body laid gently into the niche and the opening sealed with bricks so that no earth would ever fall upon him. There are two lawful forms of grave, the Sheikh explains, the lahd with its side-niche and the shaqq, a straight trench with a hollow at the bottom, the form the law requires in places like Memphis and Houston where he has buried the dead. The majority hold the lahd to be the sunnah, and one of their proofs is simply this: the Prophet ﷺ was buried in a lahd, and Allah would have chosen for His Prophet only the best. He was not undressed. Over the garments he already wore they placed three white cloths, and they laid him down.

The woman who lived beside the graves

Aisha, radiyallahu anha, went on living in her own house, with the grave of her husband ﷺ resting in one corner of it. When her father Abu Bakr was dying he asked to be buried beside his companion, and she gave the space gladly, for it was her father and the Messenger of Allah ﷺ; now there were two graves in her home, and she lived on beside them for another decade.

Then Omar, radiyallahu anhu, was stabbed, and the doctors told him plainly that the wound would kill him. Knowing he was dying, he sent his son Abdullah with a message framed in breathtaking humility. Tell Aisha, he said, that Omar, not the caliph, but a man named Omar, asks her permission to be buried alongside his two companions. And he added a condition that reveals the whole man: if she hesitates even slightly, leave, do not argue and do not press her, for it is her house and her right. She had been keeping that last space for herself. She gave it away without a pause. I wanted it for myself, she said, but I will prefer Omar over my own soul. So a third grave was opened in her room, and Aisha hung a curtain across it and lived in the small space that remained, drawing the curtain out of modesty, because the third man was neither her husband nor her father.

Sit with what the Sheikh lingers over here, because it visibly moved him. For roughly twelve years this woman ate and slept and prayed in a single room while, a curtain's width away, lay the graves of her husband the Messenger of Allah ﷺ and her own father, with Omar beside them. When Aisha herself was dying, far later, in the time of Muawiyah, in the year fifty-seven after the Hijra, she asked not to be buried there at all, though a space yet remained, but to be laid among the other wives of the Prophet ﷺ in al-Baqi. Why, we are not told. Perhaps out of deference to Omar, the Sheikh offers, perhaps because she felt it more fitting to rest among the women, and he will not guess further. As for the fourth and final space in the room, there are narrations in the books of hadith that Isa, returning at the end of time, may one day be buried there. But every one of those reports is weak or very weak, the Sheikh is careful to say, so we do not affirm it as certain; we say only that it is found in the sources, and Allah knows what will be.

Walls raised over the chamber

For about ninety years the houses simply stood. The wives of the Prophet ﷺ had left no biological descendants, so after they passed no one lived in the chambers; the property fell to the care of the family of the Prophet ﷺ. Then the caliph al-Walid ibn Abd al-Malik, who ruled from eighty-eight to ninety-six after the Hijra, resolved to expand the mosque in all four directions. And that raised a grave problem, because to the east stood the chambers of the mothers of the believers, untouched for nearly a century. He insisted that the land be purchased at any cost from whoever held it, and that the mosque be made to enclose it all, the grave included.

Why he insisted, the Sheikh weighs carefully and refuses to flatten. One reading is that he was simply a ruler doing what rulers do. Kings have always wanted to leave their mark by raising glorious mosques, and the Sheikh notes drily that there is a ruler in our own time, whom he will not name, building one of the largest mosques in the world out of state funds while his people go hungry. On this reading al-Walid cared nothing for the houses of the wives; he wanted a magnificent mosque, and no one would dare object to a man with the power of the caliphate, especially when, by ninety after the Hijra, not a single companion remained alive in Madinah. They had all passed, the very last of them dying around a hundred and ten after the Hijra, and none of those in Madinah.

But there is a second reading, and the Sheikh confesses he finds it more persuasive: there was politics in it. The Umayyads carried a deep and bitter rivalry with the family of the Prophet ﷺ, and Karbala, the slaughter of Husayn, lay only a decade in the past. The descendants of the Prophet ﷺ were profoundly loved, as is only human, and Ali ibn Husayn, the gentle survivor who as a sick young man in his tent had outlived the massacre of his whole male line, would sit in his ancestor's house, which opened straight onto the mosque, and at times even preach from there into the gathered congregation. Imagine it, the Sheikh says: the descendant of the Prophet ﷺ addressing the people with the very chamber of the Prophet ﷺ as the backdrop behind him. To a throne nervous about its own legitimacy, there was one simple answer, to remove the house. The Sheikh names this for what it is, the corrupting pull of power, the greatest trial known to man, and he asks Allah to protect us all from ever sitting in such a seat, because when a throne feels threatened, every sacred thing becomes negotiable.

Senior scholars of Madinah objected, the foremost among them Saeed ibn al-Musayyib. But notice precisely why, because the Sheikh corrects a misreading some in our own age have spread. Saeed did not object on some theological ground about a grave standing inside a mosque. He objected because he wanted the people to keep seeing the rough, bare simplicity of the houses of the Prophet ﷺ, so that their hearts would stay soft. Within ninety years, civilization had already come to Madinah: two-story homes, fine embroidered fabric, money pouring in from the conquered empires of Rome and Persia. Saeed wanted a living reminder, in plain view, of how the Messenger of Allah ﷺ had actually lived, and he understood that tearing the houses down would take that lesson away forever. The ruler did not care to keep it. The chambers were demolished, every one of them, except Aisha's.

And as the workmen dug at her wall, one of them cried out in fright and the young governor overseeing the work nearly cried out with him, for two feet were protruding from the earth, fresh and whole after ninety years, and they feared these were the feet of the Prophet ﷺ himself. A scholar of the family was summoned, Abdullah ibn Abd Allah, and he reassured them: do not fear, O Governor, these are the feet of our grandfather Omar. They covered them over and built on. The detail, the Sheikh notes, suggests they had broken into the original wall after all.

The pentagon, the thefts, and the fires
