The young governor who oversaw the building was none other than Omar ibn Abd al-Aziz, who would himself become caliph fifteen years later and is remembered as one of the most God-fearing of all rulers, though at this early stage of his life, the Sheikh is candid, he was still an ordinary Umayyad prince, praying and fasting as all did but without the deep love of the faith that came to him later, eager like any governor to please the throne. Around Aisha's chamber he raised a solid structure of thick black blocks, the very stone the Kaaba is built from, with no door at all; whether the original walls of her house were preserved inside this casing or replaced by it is a small dispute the Sheikh leaves open, hoping the original was kept but unable to be sure.

Then, around that, Omar ibn Abd al-Aziz built a second enclosure, and he built it five-sided, a pentagon, on purpose. The scholars give two reasons. First, so that no one praying in the mosque would ever face it squarely, as though it were a qibla. Second, so that no newcomer to Islam, in an age when people were converting in great numbers, would see a black cube in Madinah and mistake it for a second Kaaba. That pentagon would stand for some five hundred years, and to this day, the Sheikh stresses, no one has ever entered the innermost chamber it guards.

The books of history then carry stories, three or four or five of them, of attempts to steal the body of the Prophet ﷺ for prestige. The earliest are ascribed to the deranged Fatimid caliph al-Hakim, and these the Sheikh does not doubt, given the man. But the most famous tells of two men who came to Madinah posing as devout worshippers, fasting and withdrawn, living near the chamber while they secretly dug a tunnel toward it, paid by the Crusaders to seize the body, until the ruler Nur al-Din Zangi, the man who would raise up Salahuddin, was warned three nights running in a dream by the Prophet ﷺ himself, rode to Madinah, found them out at a feast he threw for the whole city, and poured molten lead around the grave to seal it forever. It is a great story, the Sheikh says, told in many a medieval book. And then, honestly, he sets it down. He has researched it, and he cannot believe it. The earliest reference appears more than two centuries after the supposed event, around the year seven hundred and seventy-two, in an age when histories were already being written and would not have missed it. And a man responsible for the sanctuary of the Prophet ﷺ today, whom the Sheikh questioned directly, told him plainly there is no lead barrier of any kind around the grave. He treats it as a later invention to glorify a beloved figure, and Allah knows best.

Two fires, however, are beyond doubt. In the year six hundred and fifty-four after the Hijra a lamp-keeper went in to refill the oil, had an accident, and the flame caught a curtain, and the curtain a pillar, and the whole mosque burned, the roof collapsing inward, and even the original pulpit of the Prophet ﷺ was lost in it. The people wrote to the caliph in Baghdad for guidance, for no one had entered the chamber in five or six hundred years, but it was the very year of the Mongol sack of Baghdad, when over a million were slaughtered and the caliph himself was about to lose his life, and no answer ever came. So out of sheer reverence they simply rebuilt the mosque around the chamber, daring not to disturb what had fallen inside, and it was the rising sultan Baybars, the freed slave who became king and turned back the Mongols at Ayn Jalut, who financed the rebuilding. A second great fire came in eight hundred and eighty-six, when on a dark and stormy night a bolt of lightning struck the minaret as the muezzin was climbing to give the call, killing him and setting the roof ablaze, and again the whole mosque had to be raised anew.

The green dome and the grille

Out of those two rebuildings came the shapes a visitor sees today. After the first fire, Baybars raised a square wall around the chamber and set upon it, for the first time in the history of Islam, a dome over the grave; there had never been one before, not in six centuries. It was a wooden dome, and it stood two hundred years until the second fire took it.

After that second fire, the greatest scholar then living, al-Samhudi, who died in nine hundred and eleven after the Hijra and wrote volumes on the history of Madinah, was sent into the inner chambers to clean and restore them and to make sure the structure of Omar ibn Abd al-Aziz still held. He was the first human being in some five hundred years to enter, and the last for the centuries since. He wrote of it in trembling first-person prose. He stepped only to the rear threshold of the blessed room and would go no further, out of awe, not setting foot inside. He caught a fragrance unlike anything he had ever known, and he asked what else one would expect of such a place, where the angels descend and the baraka gathers; anyone who denies that there is something there, he wrote, he could only feel sorry for. He stood as long as he was able, sending salam upon the best of the prophets ﷺ and then upon the nearest of his two companions, and made all the dua he could. He found the graves settled almost flat, only the mound of Omar at the back still slightly raised, a little reddish gravel scattered over them, and the earth, when he took a handful, still moist as if freshly turned. From his hand the wooden enclosure was replaced with one of metal, the green lattice grille that pilgrims look through to this day. It was built in eight hundred and eighty-six, which is fourteen eighty-one of the common era, when Columbus had not yet crossed the ocean and Granada had not yet fallen, and it is six hundred years old.

And the famous dome itself: Baybars's wooden dome was rebuilt in solid form by the same sultan who set the grille, and for most of its long life it was not green at all. It was bare metal first, bright where the sun struck it, then painted blue, then white. Only around twelve hundred and fifty-three after the Hijra, which is eighteen thirty-seven of the common era, in the reign of Sultan Mahmud, was it painted green for the first time. The green dome of Madinah, the Sheikh reminds us, is barely a century and a half old, and there is no religious meaning whatsoever in the color. A curtain has been hung around the grave since the early Abbasid era, first gifted by the mother of Harun al-Rashid; the cloth that covers it now is woven in the very same Makkah workshop that weaves the covering of the Kaaba, and it is changed once in every ten years.

Where we stand and give salam

So when you finally come, and may Allah let every one of us come, here is what is real and what is imagined. From outside the grille the men give their salam, walking past the three points that face the three graves: peace to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, then to Abu Bakr a little behind him, then to Omar. You stand perhaps ten feet from him ﷺ. The only ones ever permitted into the outer hallway, six feet nearer and no closer, are the cleaners, who are themselves students of knowledge chosen by a committee, and visiting kings and their highest guests. Through the lattice you will see only the curtain. There is no house to glimpse, no graves to make out. Anyone who claims to have seen more than the curtain, the Sheikh says firmly, it is a figment of their imagination. No one enters the innermost chamber. There are no keys to it, there is no door into it, and no one ever should.

Keep the adab the companions kept. The body of the Prophet ﷺ was left exactly where he died, untouched, out of the utmost respect, and that reverence is the inheritance every generation since has guarded, through fire and flood and the schemes of kings. Even when the great fire brought the roof down upon the chamber, no one would climb in to clear it, because they had not been given leave. You do not need to touch a wall or press your eye to the grille to be near him. You were near him from the first day of this journey, every time his name crossed your lips and you sent your salawat.

What this day asks of our faith

We began this seerah, a hundred days ago, with a single question: who is he ﷺ? We answered it then with his specialities, the list of all that set him apart, the most praised soul in creation, the mercy sent walking, the first to pass through the gate of Jannah. Today we end it standing quietly outside a small room in Madinah, having walked the whole of his life, and the answer is no longer a list at all. It is love. And the room itself puts three questions to us before we leave it.

Measure your wants against his room. Ten feet by twelve, a roof that let the rain and the cold straight through, and inside it the most honored soul in all creation, who turned away the softer mattress and asked for his worn one back. Saeed ibn al-Musayyib begged a caliph not to tear that house down, not over any point of law, but so the people would keep seeing, with their own eyes, how the Messenger of Allah ﷺ had actually lived, and let it keep their hearts soft. The house was taken from the eyes of the people in the end. Let it not be taken from yours. The next time your own appetite for more grows loud, the Sheikh says, remember the chamber Saeed fought to save, and ask how much of what you crave the most beloved of Allah's creation ever had at all.

Then there is Aisha, who kept the last grave in her room for her own body and gave it away the moment a dying man named Omar asked for it, preferring him over her own soul. The thing you are quietly saving for yourself is sometimes the very thing you were meant to give away. And there is the discipline the Sheikh modeled all evening, loving the Prophet ﷺ with his whole heart and still setting down, gently and firmly, the beautiful tale of the molten lead and the warning dream, because it does not hold up to the sources. Reverence is never a license to repeat what is not sound; the love that honors him is the love that tells the truth about him.

And last, the thing the whole long history of that chamber teaches, from the palm-leaf roof to the bare metal dome to the green paint that is younger than the telegraph: the grave needs no decoration to be honored. There was no dome at all for six centuries, and the green is barely a hundred and fifty years old, and the color means nothing. What honors that room is not its color but the One who lies beside it ﷺ, and the salam this ummah has never once stopped sending. So you do not need to reach Madinah to be close to him. A hundred days you have walked beside him, and the nearness was always in the salawat, and that you can send from anywhere on the earth. Send it now, the way al-Samhudi sent his at the threshold of that fragrant room, the way his ummah has always sent it, and carry it with you out of these hundred days and into the whole of the rest of your life. O Allah, send Your praise and Your peace upon our Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, the most honored of all who ever lived; let us stand one day at that blessed grille and give him our salam in person; gather us under his banner with Abu Bakr and Omar and all who loved him; and convey to his soul, from this ummah that has never stopped saying his name, our greeting and our peace.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 102: the mosque and the grave of the Prophet ﷺ (Memphis Islamic Center, 2015). Qur'an: Sahih International (49:4), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing of their chains has been followed.

The Seerah · 103 of 104

The incident of the scrolls

Bring me pen and paper, and the question that followed

We are nearly at the end now. Only a handful of days separate us from the moment the most beloved of all creation ﷺ would leave this world, and before we reach that Monday there is one Thursday we are obliged to sit with. It was a Thursday so heavy that Ibn Abbas, radiyallahu anhu, who carried its memory all his life, could not speak of it in later years without weeping, and he gave it a name that tells you everything about how it felt to those who lived it: the greatest calamity. Not the calamity of a battle lost, not famine, not the death of a child. The greatest calamity, in the reckoning of a man who saw the Prophet ﷺ with his own eyes, was a few words exchanged over a piece of parchment.

Dr. Yasir Qadhi opens this lecture with a confession of reluctance. He would rather not go back into this history at all, he says, because in the end what happened, happened, and reading blame backwards into the past changes nothing that was. But this is not a question a believing life can simply step around. Sooner or later it finds every Muslim who loves this religion. At some point someone leans in and asks: did this happen, did that happen, why did they do it this way and not another. So we go back, carefully, to the place where the two great strands of Islam first part company, and we look at one specific incident, slowly and honestly, the way it deserves to be looked at. It is sensitive ground. It will be walked as such.

Why we cannot simply pass over it

Most tellings of the seerah do not stop at the passing of the Prophet ﷺ. They carry on a little further, to the choosing of Abu Bakr and to the army of Usama marching north toward Syria, because those events grow directly out of his final days and cannot be cut loose from them without leaving the story hanging. And it is here, in this same stretch, that something else has to be faced. What surrounds the death of the Prophet ﷺ is the most contested ground in the whole of the seerah, the very seam where Sunni and Shia first separate. You cannot tell it honestly while pretending one side of the conversation does not exist. To narrate these days with integrity is to acknowledge that there are two readings, and to say plainly which one we hold and why.

There was an earlier incident already, in the final months, that some read very differently from us: the day at the pool of Ghadir, when the Prophet ﷺ spoke of Ali, radiyallahu anhu. And here Sheikh Yasir wants no confusion about the Sunni posture, because it is generous, not grudging. We affirm that day one hundred percent, he says. It is in our own books and we have no difficulty with a word of it. When you put it back into its setting, the Prophet ﷺ was defending Ali, raising him up against those who had found fault with him, declaring that whoever takes me as a protector takes Ali as a protector too, that to irritate Ali is to irritate him ﷺ. We embrace all of that. What we do not read into it is a formal appointment to leadership, and the reason we do not is that the whole texture of those last days points another way. The Prophet ﷺ kept turning, again and again, toward Abu Bakr. He insisted Abu Bakr lead the prayer in his place. When for a moment they could not find him and Omar, radiyallahu anhu, stepped forward instead, the instruction came back firm: no, find Abu Bakr, and let him lead. Never once in his life had the Prophet ﷺ commanded another man to lead the prayer while he himself was alive and present in the masjid. For us, the Sheikh says, you cannot get more significant than that. So we come to the incident of Thursday already standing in the light of everything around it.

What a Thursday it was

The incident is known by two names: the incident of Thursday, and the incident of the scrolls, the qirtas being the parchment, the papyrus, the surface they wrote upon. And before a single word of controversy, Sheikh Yasir plants a flag he wants no one to walk past. This report is in Bukhari and in Muslim. It is fully authentic. We as Sunnis affirm it one hundred percent. It is in our own books, narrated in our own chains, and there is nothing here to hide and nothing here to deny. The difference between the two sides was never whether it happened. It is only, ever, how it is read.

Ibn Abbas is the one who hands it down, and the longest version is his. As the pain of the Prophet ﷺ grew sharp and heavy upon him, he said: bring me a writing, and I shall write for you something after which you will never go astray. Bring me pen and paper, we would say in plain English, and I will set down for you a thing that will keep you from misguidance forever. And in that room, among the people who loved him most in all the world, there was hesitation. One voice, and the books of hadith do not name whose it was, said: the Prophet ﷺ is overcome by his pain, he is suffering, and we have the Book of Allah, and that is sufficient for us. We need nothing besides the Book of Allah. Others said no, ask him what he wishes, bring it to him, let him write. And the voices went back and forth across the bedside, rising against one another. When the Prophet ﷺ saw them differing in front of him, he said: get up and leave me, for it is not befitting that you should dispute in my presence.

Here Sheikh Yasir pauses to clear away a misreading before it can take root. The Shia tradition, he notes, identifies that one hesitating voice as Omar, and adds that someone in the room suggested the Prophet ﷺ was speaking deliriously from fever. But our books of hadith do not name the speaker at all, not as Omar, not as anyone. To build a mountain of accusation on a name that the authentic narrations never supply is to build on sand. What the sound report gives us is a voice, a hesitation, a dispute, and the Prophet's ﷺ displeasure at being disputed over. No more than that, and we are not entitled to manufacture more.

Then, that same day or soon after, the Prophet ﷺ gave three final instructions. Expel the idolaters from the Arabian Peninsula. Honor the visiting delegations the way I have always honored them, receive every newcomer to Islam as I received them. And a third that the sub-narrator confessed, with the plainness of an honest man, that he had simply forgotten, lost to us now forever in the telling. Later scholars suggest it may have been the command to send the army of Usama, or the warning that his grave should never be made into an idol, since both of these are recorded in other narrations of his final days, but no one can say for certain. And Ibn Abbas, recalling it across the years, would let the grief rise in him and say that the calamity, the whole calamity, was what came between the Messenger of Allah ﷺ and the writing of that parchment.

Before the controversy, what the day teaches

Imam Bukhari recorded this single incident in seven separate places across his collection, and Sheikh Yasir lingers on why, because before any argument is opened there are quiet lessons in this scene that both sides of the divide hold in common. They belong to all of us.

That the Prophet ﷺ reached for pen and paper at all is, the scholars say, among the proofs that recording sacred knowledge is beloved and commanded. He who had brought a revelation preserved first in hearts now reached, at the very end, for ink. Write your knowledge down, the scene says to us, before it slips away the way that third instruction slipped away from a man who was standing right there. That his last counsel made room for the delegations tells the ummah how to receive those who come seeking this religion: with warmth, with hospitality, with generosity, exactly as he had received them. And that he disliked the disputing, openly and to their faces, tells us how ugly division is in the sight of Allah, and how much uglier it becomes when it breaks out in the presence of guidance itself.

And there is one more tenderness the scholars draw from these moments, almost too gentle to notice. Do not sit too long at the bedside of the sick. A visit to the ill should be a light thing, a checking-in, brief and kind, not a weight laid upon a body already carrying pain; lingering becomes a burden, however lovingly meant. Even out of the hardest scene in the whole seerah, even from a deathbed, the ummah was still being taught how to love one another well. This is the mercy of it: that nothing of him ﷺ was ever wasted, that even his suffering kept teaching.

The first question: what did that voice mean

Now the controversy itself, and Sheikh Yasir frames it not as a verdict to be pronounced but as three honest questions to be answered in turn. The first: how do we understand the one who said the Book of Allah is sufficient for us, the voice our tradition takes to be Omar, who turned the moment aside rather than rushing to fetch the pen and ink.

He spent real time, he tells us, gathering how the classical scholars answered this, and across the centuries they are remarkably of one mind on the spirit of it. Al-Khattabi, who died in the year 388, held that Omar never for a moment imagined the Prophet ﷺ was mistaken; rather, seeing the severity of the fever, he feared the Prophet ﷺ might in his pain say something the ummah would later find vague, or that an enemy of the religion might one day twist, and he knew that the Companions did at times reason aloud with the Prophet ﷺ when circumstances allowed it, as they had done at Badr over the placement of the army and at Hudaybiyyah over the terms of the treaty. Al-Qadi Iyad, who died in 544, and al-Qurtubi of Cordova, who died in 676, read it the very same way: a command can carry within it signs that soften it into a request, and Omar must have perceived something in that moment that led him to judge it so, and for that sincere effort of judgment he is rewarded whether he read it rightly or not. Ibn al-Jawzi and Ibn Hajar narrated these positions and agreed with them, that out of care and concern for the Prophet's ﷺ pain Omar felt this was not the hour to draw him into any further matter. And Imam al-Nawawi went further still, saying the scholars had agreed this incident displays Omar's understanding and his foresight, that he feared a thing being written down which people might fail to live up to and then be held to account for, and that he leaned on the Qur'an's own testimony that the religion was already whole and complete.

And here is where Sheikh Yasir refuses, in his own words, to sugarcoat or to play the apologist. We Sunnis look at this, he says, and we are biased, there is no denying it, just as the other side is biased in its turn. But our bias is not arbitrary; it is built upon a track record. Twenty years of Omar stand behind this Thursday, and years of his justice as a leader still lay ahead of it, and the Prophet's ﷺ own words ring over the man: that had there been a prophet after him it would have been Omar, that Shaytan himself flees the path that Omar walks, that there had been inspired men before and if there were one in this ummah it would be Omar. When you love someone, and they do something ambiguous, you read it in the best light you can find. When you do not love them, you read the very same act in the worst. The Sheikh will not pretend the action is crystalline. Omar's stance here is genuinely ambiguous, he says; the scholars' explanations are good attempts and no more than attempts, for none of them were in that room and neither were we. What we know of the man asks of us a leap of faith: that whatever he saw, he had a very good reason for it, even if the reason is lost to us. Those who do not love him will not make that leap. That, stated honestly and without dressing it up, is the true heart of the disagreement.

The second and third questions: what, and why the silence

The second question sets Omar's motive aside and asks something different: what did the Prophet ﷺ actually want to write? One answer is the simplest and lies right in the text. He wanted to set down the three instructions themselves, and when the writing did not happen, he simply spoke them aloud instead, so that nothing of substance was in fact lost. Many scholars hold exactly this.

But there is an older and weightier reading, and Sheikh Yasir gives it real space. Sufyan ibn Uyaynah, who died in the year 198, and others noticed that only a few days earlier, in the very beginning of this same final illness, the Prophet ﷺ had said something recorded just as firmly in Bukhari and Muslim. This was the day Aisha, radiyallahu anha, had complained of a headache and he ﷺ had answered that his own was worse. On that day he said: call for me your father, Abu Bakr, and your brother, that I may write a document, for I fear that someone will aspire to leadership and say I have more right, but Allah and the believers will refuse anyone other than Abu Bakr. Read the two scenes together, separated by only three or four days, and the scrolls may well have been pointing the exact opposite way from what is often claimed: toward Abu Bakr, not away from him. This was the considered view of Sufyan and of more than one early scholar, and Ibn Taymiyyah, who devoted ten volumes to these very differences, held the document to have been precisely this, a writing in favor of Abu Bakr. From our tradition, the Sheikh notes plainly, there is simply no evidence at all for the other reading. We cannot follow a claim our own sources nowhere support.

Then comes the third question, and it is the one he finds most decisive of all, the one that closes the matter even if you grant every doubt about the first two. Set Omar's motive entirely aside; let it be whatever the other side wishes it to be. Everyone agrees the incident of Thursday took place on a Thursday. The Prophet ﷺ passed away the following Monday. That is Thursday evening, and then a full Friday, a full Saturday, a full Sunday, and into Monday: roughly four whole days. Now, if this writing was as world-altering as the claim requires, if everything turned upon it, then how did four days pass with nothing further said? He ﷺ was not a man who ever abandoned a charge from his Lord; never once in his life did he set down something Allah had commanded him to carry. And through all four of those days the very people most beloved to the other side, and beloved to us as well, Ali among them, were constantly at his side, and he said nothing further to any of them either. Ibn Hajar makes exactly this point: it is not possible that the Prophet ﷺ would leave a matter of that magnitude hanging, uncommented, for four days. Imam al-Mazari narrows it to two possibilities, and both of them shut the door. Either Allah had commanded the writing, in which case the dispute of a few Companions could never have stopped it, so it must have been abrogated by Allah Himself; or it was the Prophet's ﷺ own considered judgment, which after the unseemly disputing he simply chose, of his own will, to let go. There is no third road. And on either road, had the matter truly been of that towering importance, it could not have been left undone. To claim otherwise is, in effect, to claim that the Prophet ﷺ failed in his mission, and that no believer of any persuasion can say.

Through all three questions Sheikh Yasir keeps returning to one gentle anchor, the thing Omar himself is said to have leaned on. The religion was already complete. Allah had declared it before this Thursday ever came:

This day I have perfected for you your religion and completed My favor upon you and have approved for you Islām as religion.Qur'an 5:3

The faith was finished, the favor was full, and nothing essential could have hung upon a page that was never written.

What was clear, and where we land

There is one more thing that settles the Sheikh's heart on this, and it is not an argument about the scrolls at all. It is everything else those final weeks were quietly saying. Over and over, in his last days, the Prophet ﷺ kept pointing toward Abu Bakr. He insisted that Abu Bakr lead the prayer, and when at first they could not find him and Omar rose to take his place, the word came back: no, find Abu Bakr, let him lead. Never in his entire life had the Prophet ﷺ ordered another to lead the prayer while he himself was alive and in the masjid; for us, the Sheikh says, you cannot get more significant than that. And in the year he could not make the Hajj, it was Abu Bakr he sent as leader of the pilgrims; when Ali was sent afterward, Ali himself made it plain that he came not as the commander of the Hajj but to deliver a particular message. In the two things the Prophet ﷺ ever delegated, the prayer and the pilgrimage, the same name kept surfacing of its own accord.

So where do we land? The incident of the scrolls is real, affirmed, never denied, recorded in our own most trusted books, and read very differently by different hearts. Sheikh Yasir, who says of himself that he is no apologist and calls a spade a spade, leaves us with three honest conclusions and no more. That Omar, by the sheer weight of who he was across a lifetime, must have had a sound reason we were simply not there to witness. That the writing, if it named anyone at all, leans by the evidence toward Abu Bakr and never once toward the other claim. And that whatever a person decides about the first two, four silent days make it impossible that something of that magnitude was merely lost to a quarrel at the bedside. Underneath all of it sits the deeper truth that Allah Himself declared, the truth that dissolves the panic the whole question can stir up:

We have not neglected in the Register a thing.Qur'an 6:38

Nothing was neglected. Nothing was left out. The religion reached us whole and entire. We were never orphaned by a missing page.

He ends as he began, refusing to inflame it. What happened, happened. We hold our reading with conviction and we hold it with adab, the dignity of restraint, and we do not let a grief fourteen centuries old curdle into something that poisons the very love the whole story was meant to plant in us. That is the discipline the day demands: to be honest about the disagreement, settled in our conviction, and gentle in our manner all at once.

What this day asks of our faith

It would be easy to treat the incident of the scrolls as a debate to be won, a place to score a point against someone who reads it differently, and to walk away from it harder than we came. That would be to miss entirely what the Sheikh is doing with it. He is not handing us a victory to brandish. He is handing us a heart to protect.

The first thing the day asks is that we affirm what is authentic even when it is hard to hold. The incident of Thursday is in our own books, and the honest believer affirms it one hundred percent rather than wishing it away. Faith has never needed us to deny the difficult; it asks the harder and nobler thing, that we read the difficult with knowledge and with honesty, and trust that the truth can bear being looked at squarely. A religion that asked us to look away from its own authentic reports would be a fragile thing. Ours does not. It invites the gaze.

The second thing it asks is that we know our own bias and own it out loud, the way the Sheikh does. We read Omar through twenty years of his character and through the Prophet's ﷺ own praise of him, and that is a lean, an inclination, and we should call it what it is rather than dress it as cold neutrality. There is more strength in saying we love this man and so we read him generously than in pretending we arrived at our verdict from nowhere. And flowing straight out of that, the third thing: that some matters ask of us a leap of faith. We were not in that room. None of us saw what Omar saw. What we know of him asks us to extend the benefit of the doubt we would beg for ourselves, to assume that a man of that stature, doing something ambiguous, had a reason worthy of him even when the reason is hidden from us. To love someone well is, in part, exactly this, to give them the reading we would want given to us on the day our own act looked unclear from the outside.

And then the day asks the quiet, ordinary things that have nothing to do with the controversy at all, and perhaps these are the ones to carry home. That we write our knowledge down, because he ﷺ reached for pen and paper at the very end, and because we have all watched a precious thing slip away the way that third instruction slipped from a man who was standing right beside the Prophet ﷺ when it was spoken. That we visit the sick gently and briefly and do not make a burden of our love. That we treat the division it cost so dearly as the ugly thing he ﷺ named it to be, never to be reopened lightly for the pleasure of being right. And above all, that we rest in the truth Allah declared before that Thursday ever dawned: that the faith was perfected, the favor completed, nothing in the Book neglected. Whatever the disputes of history, you were not handed an unfinished religion. You were handed a whole one.

So take one thing from this heavy day down into your ordinary life. When you meet a contested piece of this religion's past, do not let it make you cruel, and do not let it make you doubt the completeness of what reached you. Hold your conviction, and hold your manners with it. And then turn, as this day turns the believer, to the only safe ground there is, and ask of Allah the thing the heart feels it needs most when history grows turbulent and loyalties pull in different directions: a heart that will not swerve.

[Who say], "Our Lord, let not our hearts deviate after You have guided us and grant us from Yourself mercy. Indeed, You are the Bestower."Qur'an 3:8

O Allah, do not let our hearts deviate after You have guided us, and grant us from Yourself mercy; You are the Bestower. Keep our love for Your Prophet ﷺ and for everyone he loved unshaken by the quarrels of men long dead. Gather us with him on the Day the nations are gathered. And let no disagreement of this passing world ever come between us and the writing he truly left us, the one after which we will never go astray: Your Book, and his guidance, complete and entire, with nothing neglected and no missing page. May Allah send His peace and blessings upon His Messenger ﷺ, who in the last hours of his life reached for ink so that we might never stray, and grant us hearts steady enough to be worthy of the religion he completed among us.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 103: the incident of the scrolls (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (5:3, 6:38, 3:8), verified via quran.ai. The narration and the weighing of these reports are the Sheikh's; the phrasing is Buruja's.

The Seerah · 104 of 104

The Election of Abu Bakr

When the first of the two stood in his place

One hundred and four days ago we began before his birth, with a single question: who is he ﷺ? We did not yet know the lineage, or the cave of Hira, or the wound at Ta'if, or the long road north to Madinah. We have walked the whole of it since, year by year, and now we arrive at the far side of the entire life, on a Monday morning in Madinah, only hours after that life has ended. His blessed body ﷺ has not yet been washed. And already the ummah he left behind is standing before the first question of a world without him: who leads now?

Dr. Yasir Qadhi opens this last session with a careful warning, and we are bound to keep it. Today, he says, is not only history; it is also theology, the very place where the divide between Sunni and Shia Islam begins. So we will walk it the way he walks it: slowly, with respect for everyone whose name appears in it, raising our voices at no one and reading the worst into no heart. This is the election of Abu Bakr as-Siddiq, radiyallahu anhu, the first Khalifah of Islam. It is where the seerah closes, and where the age of the companions opens.

Where the divide begins

The Sheikh does not rush past what is at stake. Every Muslim is intimately aware that this moment, the choosing of the first leader after the Prophet ﷺ, is where Sunnism and Shiism part ways. The Sunni position, he says plainly, is that Abu Bakr was the most suitable man for the role, and that this is a religious matter and not merely a political one. The other group holds otherwise. He is not here to lay out the Shia evidences and argue them; that is a different class. Yet you cannot honestly tell this story while pretending the theology is not in the room with you.

Within Sunni Islam itself, he sets out three views on how Abu Bakr came to lead. The first holds that the companions, recognizing he was the best among them, simply chose him, with nothing explicit from the Prophet ﷺ on the matter. The second, narrated from al-Hasan al-Basri and others, holds that the Prophet ﷺ indicated it indirectly, never naming him outright but leaving clear hints that any attentive heart would catch. The third is a small minority view, held openly by the great Andalusian scholar Ibn Hazm: that the Prophet ﷺ explicitly commanded it. The Sheikh is candid that he searched for another name to attach to that third view and could find almost no one beyond Ibn Hazm. And he notes the symmetry that makes it interesting: it is the exact mirror of the Shia claim, which says the Prophet ﷺ explicitly commanded that Ali, radiyallahu anhu, should lead.

Then he does what a careful teacher does. He collapses the first two views into one. They are not truly opposed, he says. The whole body of hadith praising Abu Bakr as the greatest of the companions is itself an indication of who should lead; to honor a man that highly is already to point at him. So the honest Sunni position, the one he asks us to carry, is this: the Prophet ﷺ did not stand up and name a successor in plain words, but he left enough light in the room that the companions understood. Faithful to that light, the rest of the story unfolds.

The second of the two

Over the centuries, scholars searched the Qur'an itself for indications of Abu Bakr's standing. The Sheikh is honest that many of those readings are a reach; one famous mufassir, he notes, worked his way out of the very first verse of the Qur'an, al-hamdu lillahi rabbil alameen, by a long and convoluted path, until he too arrived at Abu Bakr. There is a great deal of that sort of thing, and he does not find most of it convincing. But two verses, in his judgment, carry real weight.

The first is the ayah of the cave. As the Prophet ﷺ and Abu Bakr lay hidden from the men of Makkah in the cave of Thawr, Allah revealed the verse that names him forever:

If you do not aid him, Allah has already aided him when those who disbelieved had driven him out as one of two, when they were in the cave and he said to his companion, "Do not grieve; indeed Allah is with us." And Allah sent down His tranquility upon him and supported him with soldiers you did not see and made the word of those who disbelieved the lowest, while the word of Allah, that is the highest. And Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise.Qur'an 9:40

Here is the argument the scholars drew from it, and the Sheikh finds it sound. If Allah Himself names Abu Bakr as the second of the two, his companion in the cave, the one told do not grieve, then when the first of the two is gone, who naturally stands in his place? The second. And the word khalifah means precisely that: linguistically, a khalifah is the one who takes over when another departs, who steps into a vacated place. The very word of the office is built into the verse. When number one is no longer here, number two takes the stand. He calls this, frankly, not a bad evidence at all.

The second verse names the foremost believers and ranks them in their order:

And the first forerunners among the Muhajireen and the Ansar and those who followed them with good conduct, Allah is pleased with them and they are pleased with Him, and He has prepared for them gardens beneath which rivers flow, wherein they will abide forever. That is the great attainment.Qur'an 9:100

The Sabiqun, the first forerunners, are praised here as the best, and they are listed in sequence: the earliest of the Muhajireen, then the Ansar, then those who followed. And who, the Sheikh asks, is the very first adult man of the Muhajireen to embrace Islam? Abu Bakr. He stands at the front of the front rank. These two readings, he says, are the ones with genuine substance; the rest he leaves to the side. Hold the cave, then: two men, one threshold, and the quiet logic that when the first crosses over, the second remains.

The dream and the open door

Beyond the Qur'an there are so many ahadith on the rank of Abu Bakr that one could fill whole lectures with them. The Sheikh selects those that touch leadership directly, and the most beautiful is a dream the Prophet ﷺ reported, preserved in Sahih al-Bukhari. I was at a well, he said ﷺ, drawing water as long as Allah willed that I draw. Then Abu Bakr came and drew a bucket or two, and in his drawing there was some weakness. Then Umar ibn al-Khattab came, and the bucket turned in his hands into the great vessel used for watering whole herds; the Prophet ﷺ said he had never seen a leader draw water the way Umar did, until the people brought their camels back to the pens, watered to their fill.

The symbolism, the Sheikh says, is plain and profound. After the Prophet ﷺ departed, Abu Bakr took the rope and kept the water flowing for a year or two, exactly the span of his Khilafah, a bucket or two. And the weakness in his drawing was no fault of his own; the whole ummah trembled and very nearly fractured in his time, the tribes that broke away, the false claimants, the wars of apostasy, the wound that nearly killed the religion in its cradle. By the grace of Allah, this one man held it together and healed it; had it not been for Allah, and then for His choosing Abu Bakr, the ummah would have splintered and the Islam we know would not exist. He drew exactly long enough to steady the rope, and exactly long enough for Umar to come and turn the well into a flood. For the conquests that followed under Umar remain, in fourteen centuries, unequalled; a band of Arabs who could not read, with no empire behind them, erased the Persian empire from the map and carved away half of Rome, and to this day, the Sheikh marvels, historians scratch their heads at how. We know how. The Prophet ﷺ had told a man who tore up his letter that Allah would tear up his kingdom, and within ten years it was gone. One man steadied the well; the next let it overflow.

But the single strongest indication of all, the Sheikh says, came in the Prophet's ﷺ final illness, when he commanded that Abu Bakr lead the prayer. For three days and nights the dying Prophet ﷺ, feverish and drifting in and out of consciousness, sent again and again for Abu Bakr to stand where he himself had always stood. And once, when those around him tried to substitute another man, he stirred and grew firm: stop him, he said, and bring Abu Bakr, for Allah and His Messenger refuse anyone but Abu Bakr. This was no ordinary question of who would lead a single salah; it was a demonstration of who would stand in his place when he was gone. And in the last sermon he ever gave from the minbar, lowered now and unable to stand, he gave another sign. The houses of the companions had been built around the masjid, many with small private doors opening straight onto it. Close every one of those doors, he ﷺ said, except the door of Abu Bakr. Every household lost its private access. One man kept his.

So why, with all of these signs laid one upon another, did the Prophet ﷺ never simply say the words, when I die, let Abu Bakr lead? Here the Sheikh offers the answer he finds strongest, and Allah knows best. Had the Prophet ﷺ named a successor outright, it would have hardened into binding law that every leader must name the next, and that is a door better left open than commanded shut. Look, he says, at how the four rightly guided Khalifahs each did it differently. Abu Bakr named Umar outright. Umar named a council of six and told them to choose among themselves. Uthman named no one, and there was a measure of confusion before the people settled on Ali. And later still, Muawiyah, radiyallahu anhu, the last of the companions to hold the office, named his own son, and so opened the door of dynasty. The Sharia, the Sheikh notes, permits every one of these methods; even naming a son does not invalidate a Khilafah, for if it did, the ummah would have had no valid leadership for most of its history. It is simply the least ideal of the ways. The Prophet ﷺ left the matter open on purpose, and so he indicated without commanding.

The shade of Banu Sa'idah

Now to the morning itself. The Prophet ﷺ passed away after the Fajr prayer on Monday, the twelfth of Rabi al-Awwal. Within a few hours, on that same Monday, the Ansar began to gather at their old meeting place, a covered shelter, a saqifah, in a garden belonging to Banu Sa'idah, one of the great clans of the Khazraj. It had been their gathering place since the days of jahiliyyah, the way every Arab community kept a place to come together. The Sheikh is careful here, and asks us to be careful with him. There was no announcement, no summons, no scheme. It is simply human nature, he says, that when a shared calamity strikes, people come together to ask one another what happens now. And so the conversation turned, as it could only turn, to who would take charge of the ummah, because everyone understood that a people cannot live without a leader.

The name that rose among them was Sa'd ibn Ubadah, radiyallahu anhu, the senior figure of the Khazraj and one of the most honored men of the Ansar. He was a man of Badr, one of the chosen representatives at the second pledge of Aqaba, and famous, as his father and grandfather had been before him, for an open-handed generosity that fed scores of the poor of the Suffah at a time. As his people spoke of him, he lay among them ill with a fever, wrapped in a cloak. There was nothing dishonorable in any of it, the Sheikh stresses. The Khazraj were the larger of the two Ansar tribes, and the first of Madinah to accept Islam, and they felt their weight. Each side genuinely believed itself the more deserving, and there is, he says, an element of truth in every one of those feelings.

Word of the gathering reached the masjid, where Abu Bakr and Umar still were. Let us go to our brothers among the Ansar, Umar said. Abu Bakr agreed. And here the Sheikh offers a theory, naming it openly as his own: that Abu Bakr did not simply happen to be accompanied by the right men, but deliberately chose his delegation. For it is no accident that the two who walked beside him were Umar and Abu Ubaydah ibn al-Jarrah, two of the ten promised Paradise. Meanwhile Ali, Abbas, and the family, radiyallahu anhum, were occupied with the most natural duty of that morning: the inner affairs of the household and the washing and preparation of the Prophet ﷺ. There is nothing here to be awkward about, the Sheikh says plainly, and no conspiracy to imagine; to suppose a plot is, in his word, ludicrous. Ali was barely in his early thirties, while Abu Bakr and Umar and Uthman were nearly twice his age. He was not yet on anyone's mind for the role, not out of any slight, but because those who had known him since he was a child do not suddenly see the child as the elder. His time would come, when he had grown into it, and the ummah would be honored to have him. We admire and respect every blessing in him.

The leaders and the helpers

Only one eyewitness ever told us what happened beneath that shelter, and that is Umar himself, who narrated it years later, during his own Khilafah, to crush a rumor that had begun to spread: that Abu Bakr's election had been a hasty, thoughtless accident, and that another man might have been chosen had it been thought through. When Umar heard that whisper he was incensed, gathered the companions, and told them exactly how it had gone. So the scene we have is the scene Umar gave, in the first person.

A speaker from the Ansar rose and made their case, and he made it with grace, no insults, only honest pride. We are the Ansar, the helpers of Allah, he said, the ones who gave this religion shelter and defended it; and you Muhajireen are dear to us, but you came to us a few at a time, and you are few. From their vantage it was simple arithmetic: the Ansar numbered in the thousands, the senior Muhajireen perhaps a tenth of that, and surely the many should lead the few. Umar said that as the man spoke, he had a sharp reply taking shape in his own mind, and was on the point of rising to deliver it, knowing even as he readied it that it carried an edge Abu Bakr would not have liked. But Abu Bakr pressed him gently back down and rose himself. And Umar, who feared making no man angry but feared making Abu Bakr angry, held his tongue. By Allah, Umar admitted afterward, Abu Bakr was wiser and gentler than I would have been, and there was not a single point I had prepared except that he said it, or said it better.

For Abu Bakr did not begin by diminishing the Ansar. He began by honoring them. This is the spirit of the ayah of the Sabiqun, the foremost among both the Muhajireen and the Ansar, and Abu Bakr recalled every virtue the Qur'an and the Prophet ﷺ had granted the Ansar, including the saying that had all of mankind taken one path and the Ansar another, he ﷺ would have taken the path of the Ansar. He gave them their full due, and only then did he turn, and turn directly to Sa'd. You were sitting before the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, he said, on the day he himself said that the leaders of this matter shall be from the Quraysh, the righteous of them leading the righteous of mankind. I was there; you were there. And the Arabs, with the tribal pride of their age still in them, would accept leadership from the Quraysh as they would accept it from no one else. By pointing to Sa'd, and then setting before him a hadith they had both heard, he closed the door without ever pushing on it. For how does a believer argue with a hadith of the Prophet ﷺ once it is laid before him? And Sa'd, in a moment the Sheikh holds up as a mark of his iman, answered: you have spoken the truth. You are the leaders, and we are the helpers.

Stretch forth your hand

Then came the moment the Sheikh calls pure wisdom of leadership. Abu Bakr did not nominate himself; that was never his way, and it was not the fitting way. Instead he reached out and took the hands of the two men he had brought with him, Umar and Abu Ubaydah, and said to the gathering: I have nominated these two for you; choose whichever of them you wish. This too was wisdom. The Ansar had not been looking through that door at all; they had been thinking of themselves, of Sa'd. Had Abu Bakr said choose this one, they would have felt cornered. By opening a door they had not even been looking through, and inviting them to pick freely between two of the best of mankind, he made it easy to walk through.

This was the one line of the whole speech that Umar said he hated, that his own name had been put forward at all. By Allah, Umar said, it would be more beloved to me to have my neck struck, where there is no sin in the striking, than to lead a people while Abu Bakr is among them. There was a flurry then, voices rising the way they rise in any crowded gathering, and someone proposed a compromise: two leaders, one from the Ansar and one from the Muhajireen. But that cannot work, the Sheikh notes, any more than a ship can sail with two captains. And it was here that Umar, who had the loudest and deepest voice in the gathering, cut clean through the noise. Stretch forth your hand, O Abu Bakr. Abu Bakr put his hand out. Umar took it and gave him the pledge of allegiance, the very first to do so, and the Muhajireen and the Ansar followed, hand after hand.

The Sheikh, faithful to his task, does not hide the harder edge of the story, for part of his duty, he says, is to show us what is not always on the rosy side of the picture. There are narrations that Sa'd ibn Ubadah was not content with the outcome, that he felt himself pressed into it; you have forced me, one report has him say, and the answer comes back that it is better to be brought along with the community than to be let go and become a cause of division. If such a report is authentic, the Sheikh says, there is nothing wrong in it, for it is no sin to have wished, for good and halal reasons, to serve and to lead. Other scholars hold even these reports baseless. As for the wild fabrications, that Sa'd refused ever again to pray behind Abu Bakr, that the jinn later killed him and were heard boasting of it, the Sheikh sets every one aside as the stuff of partisan sources and bizarre legend, on which our religion rests not at all. Sa'd quietly withdrew to Sham in the end and passed away there a few years on, a man of Badr and of Aqaba, honored to the last.

The first khutbah of a Khalifah

By Tuesday, barely a day after the Prophet ﷺ had passed, and with his blessed body ﷺ still lying in the house of Aisha awaiting burial, the people gathered in the masjid to give their public pledge before the noon prayer. Umar stood first and undid the rumor at its very root. What I said yesterday, he told them, about imagining the Prophet ﷺ would outlive us all and be the last of us to go, I did not find that in the Book of Allah, nor did the Messenger ﷺ ever tell it to me; it was only my own thought, and I was wrong in it. But Allah has left among you His Book, by which the Prophet ﷺ was guided, so hold fast to it and you will be guided. And Allah has gathered your affair upon the best of you, the companion of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, the second of the two when they were in the cave. So rise, and give him your pledge.

And they did, one after another, until Abu Bakr rose to deliver a sermon the Sheikh calls the most powerful ever given by any political leader after the prophets. It is only three short lines, and he lets them stand almost bare, because nothing a man could add would improve them.

O people, Abu Bakr said, I have been put in charge of you, though I am not the best of you. So if I do well, then help me; and if I do wrong, then correct me. Truthfulness is a trust, and lying is a betrayal. The weak among you is strong in my sight until I secure his right for him, and the strong among you is weak in my sight until I take from him what is owed. Never does a people abandon striving in the path of Allah but that Allah strikes them with humiliation; and never does indecency spread among a people but that Allah envelops them in His punishment. Obey me so long as I obey Allah and His Messenger ﷺ; but if I disobey Allah and His Messenger, then you owe me no obedience. Now rise, and pray, may Allah have mercy upon you.

The seerah we have walked for a hundred and four days had taught this man exactly how to carry power: as a trust laid upon him, with his own smallness named out loud in the first breath of it. That is not how the world teaches men to take a throne. It is how the Prophet ﷺ had taught him to take a trust.

The age of the companions begins

Within a single day, every question had been settled and the ummah had its leader. Only then was the Prophet ﷺ washed and prepared, and so many thousands came to pray over him ﷺ that the burial was pushed deep into the night; he was laid to rest where he had lived, in the house of Aisha, in the small hours the Muslim calendar already counts as Wednesday. And with that, the Sheikh says, the story of the election of Abu Bakr is complete.

He closes the way a teacher of mercy closes a hard subject, and his closing is itself part of the lesson. We respect every one of the companions, he reminds us, even where we might not agree with some judgment one of them made, for they were human and a difference of ijtihad among them is no stain. We do not impugn their integrity, and we trust that each sought the good of this ummah. And when the later disputes among them arise, the ones that brought drawn swords and lost lives among men we all love, his counsel is the counsel of Imam Malik and Imam Ahmad before him: that was a blood Allah kept our swords out of, so let us keep our tongues out of it too. Not every knowledge softens the heart; some of it only hardens, and these companions are far too dear to us that we should pick over their wounds.

And so the seerah ends here, not with the Prophet ﷺ alone, but with the people he left behind taking their first steps into the world he had spent twenty-three years preparing them to carry. This is what a true teacher leaves behind: not a void, but a people ready. The story does not close in grief; it closes in continuity, in a hand stretched out and a pledge given, in the first of the firsts rising to take up what the Prophet ﷺ had set down. One hundred and four days with him ﷺ is over. Loving him, and following those who loved him, never has to be.

What this final day asks of our faith

It would be easy to read this last morning as an administrative footnote, a question of governance settled and filed, and to close the book feeling the story is over. That would be to miss what it asks of us, standing as we do on the far side of the whole life with the question of how to carry it forward now in our own hands.

The first thing it asks is the way we hold our own small portions of authority. Abu Bakr took charge of an entire ummah and opened his first words with I am not the best of you. He did not grasp the office as a prize won; he received it as a trust feared, naming his own smallness before anything else, putting the weakest of the people first in his care and the strongest last, and binding his own claim on obedience to a single condition: only so long as I obey Allah. Most of us are never handed an ummah, but every one of us is handed something, a household, a class, a team, a name others trust. Ask how you carry it. As a prize that proves you, or as a trust that frightens you a little because you know Who will ask after it.

The second thing it asks is how we begin when we need to move another heart. Umar had the sharper reply ready, and it would have been true, and it would have failed. Abu Bakr won the room by honoring the Ansar first, and because he had honored them, the one hard thing he came to say landed soft. You do not change a heart by first showing it everything it has gotten wrong; you change it by first showing it the respect it is owed, and then the truth you came to bring arrives among friends. We are quick with our corrections and slow with our honor, and the order of his wisdom was the reverse.

And the third thing, the one this final day asks more than any other day before it, is whether our love of the Prophet ﷺ is large enough to outlive him. The companions woke to the worst morning of their lives, with his body ﷺ not yet washed, and they did not collapse into the loss; gently, faithfully, they carried on, because he had built them to. He is not here for us to follow with our eyes, but he left us his Book, his Sunnah, and the road his companions walked after him, the firsts of this ummah, the first to believe and the first to give and the first to fall, each one a door he opened and they walked through. So end the way his ummah has always begun again after every loss. O Allah, send Your praise and Your peace upon Muhammad ﷺ, the second of the two, the most praised of all creation. Be pleased with Abu Bakr and Umar and Uthman and Ali, and with all of his companions. Keep our tongues from what You kept their swords from, gather us among those who loved him and followed those who loved him, and let us drink from his hand, on the day of thirst, a drink after which we never thirst again. The age of the Prophet ﷺ has closed. The age of his companions has begun, and we are invited to walk it too.

Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 104: the election of Abu Bakr (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an translations are from Sahih International (9:40, 9:100), verified via quran.ai. Where the histories carry more than one narration, the Sheikh's own weighing has been followed, and the disputes among the companions are left, as he leaves them, to Allah.
